8.1.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.1.title: Board Approval of Technology Risk Appetite
8.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the board has formally approved a technology risk appetite document that is explicitly linked to the institution's enterprise-wide risk appetite statement — a generic or standalone technology risk policy without this linkage is a primary red flag. Examiners will look for evidence of substantive board deliberation rather than rubber-stamp approvals, checking board and committee minutes for the quality of discussion on technology risk tolerances and KRI thresholds. A key concern is whether risk ownership is clearly assigned at a named-individual or role level, with documented acceptance criteria for residual risks, rather than being diffused across departments with no accountable owner. Examiners will also test whether KRIs are actively monitored and reported to the board on a regular cycle, and whether breaches of tolerance have triggered documented escalation and response. Finally, examiners assess whether the technology risk appetite has been reviewed with sufficient frequency and rigour relative to material changes in the institution's technology environment, threat landscape, or business strategy.

evidenceItems.name: Board-Approved Technology Risk Appetite Statement
evidenceItems.description: The formal technology risk appetite statement approved by the full board, demonstrating explicit alignment with the institution's enterprise risk appetite statement. This document defines the nature and level of technology risk the institution is willing to accept in pursuit of its strategic objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: The document contains a direct cross-reference or mapped linkage to specific statements in the enterprise-wide Risk Appetite Statement (RAS), with version numbers cited

Risk appetite dimensions are clearly articulated across multiple technology domains (e.g., cybersecurity, IT availability, third-party technology, data integrity) with qualitative and quantitative statements for each
The document bears a board resolution number, approval date, and signatures or recorded assent from the full board (not delegated to a committee alone)
Version history shows a structured review cycle with evidence that prior versions were superseded following deliberate board review rather than administrative update
The statement distinguishes clearly between risk appetite (willingness to accept risk) and risk tolerance (quantified boundaries), with both explicitly articulated
commonGaps: Technology risk appetite exists as a subsection of the IT policy rather than as a standalone board-approved document, making it unclear whether the board specifically approved the appetite
The document references the enterprise RAS in general terms without mapping specific technology risk categories to corresponding enterprise-level risk appetite dimensions
Quantitative tolerance thresholds are absent, leaving appetite statements purely qualitative (e.g., 'low appetite for cyber risk') with no measurable boundary for escalation
Board approval is evidenced only by a Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee resolution, with no full board resolution on record
The document has not been updated following material changes such as cloud migration, new digital product launches, or significant cyber incidents
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board resolution register and signed board papers
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Riskonnect) — risk appetite module
Enterprise Risk Management department — enterprise RAS and technology risk appetite mapping documents
SharePoint / document management system — policy library with version control history
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with board resolution attachment

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Tolerance Register with Quantified Thresholds
evidenceItems.description: A structured register or schedule that translates the board-approved risk appetite into specific, quantified risk tolerances for defined technology-related events. This demonstrates compliance with clause 8.1(a) by showing the board has considered impact on business operations and customers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Tolerances are defined for discrete technology event categories (e.g., unplanned system downtime, RTO/RPO breaches, cybersecurity incidents, data loss events, third-party outages) with numeric thresholds for each
Each tolerance entry specifies the impact dimension assessed — operational, financial, reputational, and customer impact — showing the board considered all relevant perspectives per clause 8.1(a)
Tolerance levels are stratified (e.g., green/amber/red thresholds or tiered numeric ranges) to support graduated escalation and response
The register is directly traceable to the board-approved Technology Risk Appetite Statement through shared reference numbers or an explicit mapping table
Board or Risk Committee minutes reference deliberation on specific tolerance values, evidencing active consideration rather than administrative adoption
commonGaps: Tolerances are defined only for IT availability (uptime %) while omitting cybersecurity, data integrity, and third-party risk categories, creating uneven coverage
Customer impact dimension is not assessed in tolerance-setting, focusing solely on internal operational impact in breach of clause 8.1(a)'s explicit requirement
Tolerance thresholds exist in the risk appetite document but have not been cascaded into operational risk registers or system monitoring dashboards, creating a disconnect between policy and practice
The register lists tolerances without a corresponding breach escalation matrix, leaving ambiguity about what action is required when a tolerance is exceeded
Tolerances have not been reviewed or updated following material changes to the institution's technology infrastructure or product portfolio
suggestedSources: GRC platform — risk register and tolerance management module
Technology Risk Management team — risk tolerance schedule annexed to the risk appetite framework
IT Operations — SLA and OLA registers that embed system-level tolerances
Business Continuity Management team — RTO/RPO registers cross-referenced to board-approved tolerances
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Ownership and Residual Risk Acceptance Register
evidenceItems.description: Documentation that identifies named risk owners for each technology risk category and establishes the criteria and approving authority for the acceptance of residual risks. This directly evidences compliance with clause 8.1(b) by demonstrating clear accountability and a structured residual risk acceptance process.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each technology risk category in the risk register has a named risk owner identified by role title and individual name, with no risks assigned to a generic group or department without individual accountability

A residual risk acceptance framework is documented, defining escalation criteria based on risk rating thresholds (e.g., risks rated High or above require CRO and board approval; Medium requires Risk Committee)
Accepted residual risks are recorded with the name and role of the approving authority, the date of acceptance, the rationale, and a defined review or expiry date
The approving authority matrix for residual risk acceptance has been formally approved by the board or board-level Risk Committee
Risk owner attestations are on record showing owners have acknowledged their accountability for assigned technology risks on at least an annual cycle
commonGaps: Risk ownership is assigned at departmental level (e.g., 'IT Department') rather than to a specific role holder, making accountability non-enforceable and failing the clear accountability requirement of clause 8.1(b)
Residual risk acceptances are approved verbally or via email without a formal documented record in the GRC platform or risk register, leaving no audit trail
The approving authority matrix for residual risk acceptance has not been approved by the board — it exists only as an operational management document
Accepted residual risks have no review or expiry date, meaning risks accepted years ago remain on the register without reassessment
Risk owner assignments have not been updated following organisational restructuring, resulting in named owners who have left the role or the institution
suggestedSources: GRC platform — risk register with ownership fields and residual risk acceptance workflow
HR system — to validate current incumbents against named risk owners
Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee — minutes and papers referencing residual risk acceptance decisions
Technology Risk Management team — risk ownership framework document and acceptance log
evidenceItems.format: GRC platform export (PDF/Spreadsheet) with supporting committee minutes

evidenceItems.name: Key Risk Indicator (KRI) Framework and Monitoring Reports
evidenceItems.description: The documented KRI framework defining indicators selected to monitor technology risks against board-approved tolerances, together with periodic monitoring reports demonstrating active use of KRIs for board-level oversight. This evidences compliance with clause 8.1(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: The KRI framework document lists each KRI with its definition, data source, measurement frequency, threshold levels (green/amber/red), owner, and mapping to a specific technology risk category and tolerance

KRIs cover both existing risks (e.g., patch compliance rate, system availability) and emerging risks (e.g., AI/ML model risk indicators, third-party concentration metrics), demonstrating forward-looking risk monitoring
Board or Board Risk Committee receives a KRI dashboard or report at each meeting cycle, with minutes evidencing that KRI status was discussed and not merely noted
Amber and red KRI breaches are accompanied by documented management responses and remediation timelines in the board report
KRI effectiveness is reviewed periodically — at least annually — with evidence that indicators are retired or added in response to changes in the technology risk environment
commonGaps: KRIs exist for IT operations (uptime, incidents) but do not cover cybersecurity, data risk, or third-party technology risk categories, leaving material risk areas unmonitored at board level
KRI reports are prepared and reviewed only at management level; the board receives summarised dashboards without underlying KRI data, preventing meaningful board oversight
KRI thresholds are not linked to board-approved risk tolerances — thresholds are set operationally without reference to the tolerance register, breaking the governance chain
Emerging risk indicators are absent from the KRI framework, meaning the board has no forward-looking visibility of new or evolving technology threats
KRI breaches in the amber or red zone are reported to the board but without management commentary or remediation actions, leaving the board without the information needed to exercise oversight
suggestedSources: GRC platform — KRI module with threshold configuration and historical trend data
Board Secretariat — board and Risk Committee papers containing KRI dashboards
Technology Risk Management team — KRI framework document and data dictionary
IT Operations and Security Operations Centre (SOC) — source data feeds for KRI calculation
evidenceItems.format: PDF dashboard reports and supporting spreadsheet/GRC data export

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Resource Adequacy Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing that the board has ensured sufficiency and appropriate deployment of resources for technology risk management, as required by clause 8.1(d). This includes budget allocation, staffing, and tooling decisions reviewed and endorsed at board level.
whatGoodLooksLike: An annual technology risk resource assessment is presented to the board or board-level committee, covering headcount, budget, tools, and skills adequacy against the institution's risk profile and risk appetite
Board or Risk Committee minutes show substantive deliberation on technology risk resourcing, including challenge of management proposals rather than passive endorsement
Budget allocations for technology risk, cybersecurity, and IT resilience are traceable to board-approved budgets with a clear linkage to the technology risk appetite and identified risk owners
Skills gap analyses for the technology risk function are documented and presented to the board with remediation plans (training, hiring, outsourcing) and tracked to completion
Resource adequacy is revisited when material changes occur (e.g., new technology initiatives, significant incidents, changes in threat landscape), evidenced by out-of-cycle board papers or updates
commonGaps: Technology risk resourcing is decided at management level and presented to the board only in the annual budget cycle without a dedicated risk-lens assessment, failing the board's obligation to 'ensure' sufficiency under clause 8.1(d)

The board approves an aggregate IT budget without visibility of the specific allocation to technology risk management and cybersecurity functions, preventing meaningful oversight of resource sufficiency
Skills adequacy assessments are not conducted, meaning the board has no evidence base on which to assess whether deployed resources are 'appropriate' as required by clause 8.1(d)
Resource adequacy is assessed only quantitatively (headcount, budget) without qualitative assessment of skills, tools, and organisational placement of the technology risk function
No linkage exists between the technology risk appetite and resource allocation decisions — high-appetite risk areas are not demonstrably receiving proportionate resources
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board and Risk Committee papers on IT and technology risk budgets
Finance department — approved IT and cybersecurity budget allocations by cost centre
HR/People function — technology risk and IT security headcount and skills data
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Chief Risk Officer (CRO) office — resource adequacy assessments and gap analyses
evidenceItems.format: PDF board papers with supporting spreadsheet budget schedules

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Appetite Periodic Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the board has reviewed the technology risk appetite at regular intervals with sufficient deliberation, and that the review considered changes in the risk environment. This evidences compliance with clause 8.1(e) and demonstrates the dynamic, not static, nature of board oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined review cycle for the technology risk appetite is documented (e.g., annual minimum, or triggered by material events) and has been consistently adhered to, with review dates on record

Board or Board Risk Committee minutes for each review cycle contain substantive deliberation — including challenge, discussion of environmental changes, and rationale for retaining or amending appetite statements — not merely a resolution to 'note and approve'

Each review cycle includes a structured assessment of changes in the risk environment (e.g., new cyber threats, regulatory changes, technology transformation programmes, incident experience) presented as board papers
Version control records show changes made to the technology risk appetite following each review, with a change log explaining what was amended and why, or a documented rationale for no change

Out-of-cycle reviews are evidenced when triggered by material events (e.g., significant cyber incident, major technology failure, regulatory directive), showing the board's responsiveness to a changing risk environment
commonGaps: The technology risk appetite is reviewed only as part of an annual policy review cycle with no specific board deliberation — the review is administrative rather than substantive, failing the 'sufficient deliberation' requirement of clause 8.1(e)

Review records exist but show no evidence of environmental context being presented to the board — the board approved a rollover of the prior appetite without analysis of whether the risk environment had changed

There is no documented trigger mechanism for out-of-cycle reviews, meaning material changes in the technology environment (e.g., a major ransomware incident industry-wide) do not prompt appetite reassessment
The change log between appetite versions is absent, making it impossible to determine what, if anything, changed between review cycles and why
Review intervals are longer than 12 months for a material or systemically important institution, or the review has lapsed beyond the documented cycle without a recorded rationale for the delay
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board calendar, board paper archive, and resolution register for technology risk appetite review items
GRC platform — policy management module with version history and review workflow audit trail
Technology Risk Management team — review working papers, environmental scan reports, and change logs
Risk Committee Secretariat — Risk Committee minutes and action item tracker for appetite review follow-up items
evidenceItems.format: PDF board/committee papers, minutes, and version-controlled policy documents

auditTips: Trace the governance chain end-to-end before examination: start from the enterprise Risk Appetite Statement and verify that specific technology risk appetite statements can be mapped to it by reference — examiners will test this linkage and a break in the chain is an immediate finding.

Pull board and Risk Committee minutes for the last three review cycles and read them critically for evidence of substantive deliberation on technology risk appetite items; highlight passages where the board challenged management, requested clarification, or amended proposals — passive 'noted and approved' resolutions are a red flag examiners will flag.

Validate that every risk owner named in the technology risk register is a current incumbent in the named role — cross-check against HR records before submission, as stale ownership assignments signal that clause 8.1(b) accountability is not operationally maintained.

Reconcile KRI thresholds in the monitoring system or GRC platform against the approved tolerance values in the tolerance register — mismatches between operational monitoring thresholds and board-approved tolerances indicate the KRI framework under clause 8.1(c) is not actually calibrated to the board's intent.

For each accepted residual risk in the register, verify that the approving authority recorded matches the authority level stipulated in the residual risk acceptance framework — approvals made below the required authority level are a direct breach of clause 8.1(b) and should be remediated before examination.

Prepare a single traceability matrix that maps each sub-obligation in clause 8.1(a) through 8.1(e) to the specific evidence items, document references, and page numbers you will present — examiners work systematically through sub-clauses and a pre-prepared matrix demonstrates institutional rigour and accelerates the examination process.

Identify and document any material changes to the technology environment (cloud adoption, new digital products, significant incidents, threat intelligence developments) that occurred in the last review period, and confirm each is addressed in the most recent technology risk appetite review board paper — unexplained gaps between environmental changes and appetite revisions are a primary line of examiner inquiry under clause 8.1(e).

8.2.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.2.title: Board Oversight Responsibilities
8.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners evaluating clause 8.2 focus on whether board oversight is substantive rather than ceremonial — they will look for evidence that the board actively challenges and interrogates IT and cyber strategic plans rather than rubber-stamping management submissions. A key red flag is board minutes that record approvals without any documented discussion, questions raised, or conditions imposed on management. Examiners will trace the three-year coverage of IT strategic plans and verify that plan approval dates, review cycles, and board resolution records are consistent and unbroken. They will also scrutinise whether the TRMF and CRF are formally endorsed by board resolution and whether subsequent board papers demonstrate ongoing monitoring of framework implementation against defined milestones. On policies, examiners compare the last review date of each IT-related policy against the three-year threshold and look for evidence that review depth was calibrated to operational complexity — a superficial 'no changes required' sign-off on a materially changed environment is a finding in itself.

evidenceItems.name: Board-Approved IT and Cybersecurity Strategic Plan
evidenceItems.description: The formally approved IT and cybersecurity strategic plan document covering a rolling period of no less than three years, together with all board resolutions, covering papers, and version history demonstrating periodic review. This evidence demonstrates that the board has discharged its obligation under 8.2(a) to approve and periodically reassess strategic alignment of technology direction with business objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: The strategic plan explicitly covers a minimum three-year horizon with dated milestones, KPIs, and budget envelopes aligned to documented business objectives
A board resolution or signed approval page bears the board's formal endorsement, with the approval date clearly recorded and traceable to the corresponding board meeting minutes
Version history or a change log shows at least one prior approved iteration, with a summary of material changes between versions reviewed and accepted by the board
Board meeting minutes for the approval session include substantive recorded discussion — questions raised by directors, management responses, and any conditions or directives attached to approval
The plan cross-references the institution's TRMF and CRF, demonstrating that technology strategy and risk appetite are aligned at board level
commonGaps: Strategic plans cover only 12–18 months despite the mandatory three-year minimum, often because they are conflated with annual IT budgets or operational roadmaps

Board approval is evidenced only by a generic resolution number with no accompanying paper trail showing the board actually reviewed plan content, leaving ambiguity about whether approval was substantive

The plan is not updated after major organisational or risk environment changes (e.g., a core banking migration, pandemic-driven digitalisation), resulting in a document that is technically 'current' but factually obsolete
Business objectives referenced in the strategic plan are vague or absent, making it impossible to assess whether IT direction genuinely supports institutional strategy
No evidence of interim board-level progress reviews between formal approval cycles, suggesting oversight is periodic rather than continuous
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — formal board papers, resolutions register, and signed approval documents
Chief Information Officer (CIO) or Chief Technology Officer (CTO) office — master copy of the IT strategic plan with version history
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Riskonnect) — strategic plan artefacts linked to governance workflows
SharePoint or institutional document management system — IT strategy document library with access-controlled version control
Board committee (e.g., Board Risk Committee or Board IT Committee) — sub-committee minutes where strategy is first reviewed before full board endorsement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (strategic plan document) + PDF (board resolution and minutes extract)

evidenceItems.name: Board Endorsement Records for TRMF and CRF
evidenceItems.description: Formal board resolutions, board paper submissions, and meeting minutes evidencing the board's endorsement of the Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) and Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) as required under RMiT paragraphs 9.1 and 11.2. This evidence demonstrates compliance with clause 8.2(b) and provides the documentary chain linking framework existence to board-level accountability for their effective implementation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Separate, clearly identifiable board resolutions formally endorsing the TRMF and CRF by name, with endorsement dates and the names of directors present recorded

Board papers submitted for each endorsement include a meaningful executive summary of framework scope, key risk appetite statements, and how the framework addresses RMiT obligations — not merely a cover page forwarding a large technical document

Post-endorsement board reporting (quarterly or semi-annual) demonstrates active oversight of framework implementation: progress against deployment milestones, exception reports, and corrective actions tracked to closure

Minutes record the board's specific inquiries about framework adequacy — for example, whether controls cover cloud or third-party risk — and management's responses, showing genuine challenge rather than passive acceptance
Any material amendments to the TRMF or CRF after initial endorsement are brought back to the board for re-endorsement, with a summary of what changed and why
commonGaps: The TRMF and CRF exist as documents but the board endorsement was conducted at management committee level (e.g., EXCO or Risk Management Committee) and never formally escalated to or ratified by the full board

Board papers presented for endorsement are excessively technical and contain no executive summary, raising doubt about whether non-executive directors could meaningfully evaluate what they were endorsing
Endorsement records exist but post-endorsement implementation reporting to the board is absent, meaning the board approved frameworks it never subsequently monitored
The TRMF and CRF are endorsed as a single bundled resolution with no distinct treatment, making it unclear the board understood them as two discrete frameworks with separate regulatory anchors
Framework endorsement records are stored in an ad hoc manner (email chains, informal memo) rather than as formal board secretariat records, creating evidentiary gaps during examination
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — resolutions register, certified true copies of board minutes, and board paper archives
Chief Risk Officer (CRO) office — TRMF and CRF master documents with amendment history
Board Risk Committee Secretariat — sub-committee endorsement papers and minutes where frameworks are first deliberated
GRC platform — framework governance module linking documents to approval workflows and review schedules
Compliance or Regulatory Affairs unit — regulatory submission records where TRMF/CRF are referenced in BNM correspondences
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board resolution certified by Company Secretary) + PDF (board paper and minutes extract)

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Risk Assessment Reporting to the Board on Critical IT Systems
evidenceItems.description: A structured record of risk assessment reports presented by senior management to the board (or a designated board committee) covering critical IT systems and emerging technology adoption, including the controls and resources applied to mitigate identified risks. This evidence demonstrates the board's fulfilment of clause 8.2(c) — that it actively requires and evaluates management's demonstration of robust, comprehensive risk assessments rather than accepting conclusions at face value.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined reporting cadence (e.g., quarterly) is established and evidenced, with board-level dashboards or risk reports covering critical IT systems identified under the institution's criticality classification framework
Reports include quantified or rated risk exposures, control effectiveness scores, and residual risk positions — not only lists of risks without assessment depth

Emerging technology risk assessments (e.g., AI/ML models, cloud-native services, open banking APIs) are presented with a distinct section addressing novel risk vectors and the adequacy of existing control frameworks to address them
Board or committee minutes record challenge questions directed at management regarding risk assessment methodology, completeness of critical system scope, and sufficiency of mitigating resources
Tracking tables within reports show closed findings from prior periods and open items with accountable owners and target dates, demonstrating continuous rather than point-in-time oversight
commonGaps: Risk reports presented to the board are high-level status summaries with no visibility into the underlying assessment methodology, preventing the board from evaluating whether assessments are truly robust
The definition of 'critical IT systems' is not formally documented or consistently applied, resulting in scope gaps — some critical systems are assessed while others of equal significance are omitted

Emerging technology risk assessments are absent or treated as an afterthought despite the institution actively deploying AI, cloud, or API-based services, creating a material blind spot at board level

Resource adequacy assertions in reports are qualitative statements ('adequate resources are in place') with no supporting evidence such as headcount data, budget allocations, or third-party capability assessments
The board does not formally record its acceptance or rejection of reported risk positions, meaning there is no evidence the board exercised judgment on the adequacy of management's risk controls
suggestedSources: CIO/CTO and CISO offices — risk assessment reports and board presentation decks on critical system risk
Board Risk Committee Secretariat — minutes and action logs from committee sessions where IT risk is reviewed
GRC platform — risk register extracts tagged to critical IT systems, with control effectiveness ratings and owner assignments
ITSM tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — change and incident records used to substantiate risk positions reported to the board
Internal Audit — IT audit reports and findings registers that corroborate or challenge management's risk assessment representations to the board
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board risk report) + PDF/XLS (risk register extract) + PDF (board/committee minutes)

evidenceItems.name: IT Policy and Framework Review Register
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register or log documenting all IT-related frameworks, policies, and guidelines subject to the three-year mandatory review cycle under clause 8.2(d), including each document's owner, last review date, next scheduled review date, review depth rationale, and board or designated authority sign-off. This evidence demonstrates that the institution has operationalised the review obligation systematically and that review depth is calibrated to operational complexity and risk environment changes.
whatGoodLooksLike: The register enumerates every IT-related policy, framework, and guideline in scope, with unique document identifiers, version numbers, effective dates, and review due dates clearly populated for each entry

For each completed review cycle, the register records not only the approval date but also the review depth rationale — e.g., 'substantive review due to core banking migration' or 'limited review; no material operational or risk environment changes' — demonstrating proportionate assessment

Reviews completed within the three-year threshold are evidenced by linked approval records (board resolution, committee minutes, or delegated authority sign-off consistent with the institution's governance matrix)

Policies approaching or past their review due date are flagged with escalation status and owner accountability, showing the institution actively manages the register rather than treating it as a static artefact
The register is reconciled at least annually against the institution's master document library to identify newly created policies not yet captured in the review schedule
commonGaps: The register exists but is incomplete — certain IT-related guidelines (e.g., acceptable use policies, vendor management guidelines, BYOD policies) are not tracked, creating undocumented review gaps

Review dates are recorded but there is no evidence of the review itself having been conducted — the policy document version is unchanged, no reviewer comments exist, and no approval record is available

Review depth is uniform across all documents regardless of the institution's operational complexity or recent risk environment changes, indicating a tick-box approach rather than the risk-calibrated review required
Policies are reviewed by management-level committees without board visibility, and there is no mechanism for the board to confirm that review quality and completeness meet its expectations

The register is maintained as an uncontrolled spreadsheet with no access log or version history, making it impossible to demonstrate that entries have not been retrospectively altered prior to examination
suggestedSources: Compliance or Regulatory Affairs unit — policy governance register and review schedule
GRC platform policy management module (e.g., OneTrust, ServiceNow Policy and Compliance) — automated review cycle tracking with workflow approvals
SharePoint or document management system — document library with metadata fields for review dates and version history
Board Secretariat — approval records for policy reviews that required board or board committee sign-off
Internal Audit — policy gap assessments conducted as part of annual IT audit planning, which may identify unreviewed documents
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet/GRC system export (policy register) + PDF (supporting approval records per policy)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Application Submissions to BNM with Supporting Risk Assessments
evidenceItems.description: Records of technology-related applications submitted to BNM (e.g., new product approvals, cloud adoption notifications, digital banking licence applications) together with the underlying risk assessments and the board's review or endorsement of the risk management position presented in each submission. This evidence demonstrates compliance with clause 8.2(e) — that the institution's risk management framework actively supports and produces robust risk assessments for regulatory submissions rather than treating them as standalone compliance exercises.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each BNM submission package includes a clearly identified risk assessment section that is traceable to the institution's TRMF and uses the same risk taxonomy, rating scales, and control language as internal risk management documents

Board or board committee minutes record review and endorsement of the risk management position in the submission prior to filing, with directors given the opportunity to challenge assumptions or request additional analysis

Post-submission correspondence with BNM (queries, additional information requests, conditions imposed) is documented alongside management's responses, creating a complete regulatory interaction record

A submissions register tracks all technology-related applications to BNM, including submission date, application type, current status, and risk assessment reference number, enabling end-to-end traceability
The risk assessment accompanying each submission reflects a current view — prepared or refreshed within a defined period before submission — and is not a recycled assessment from a prior engagement
commonGaps: Risk assessments attached to BNM submissions are prepared in isolation by a project team using different risk rating criteria than those in the TRMF, creating inconsistency that examiners can readily identify by cross-referencing documents

Board endorsement of the regulatory submission risk position is absent — submissions are filed by management without explicit board awareness, meaning the board cannot demonstrate it ensured framework adequacy as required
The submissions register is incomplete or does not exist, making it difficult to demonstrate a consistent and systematic approach to regulatory technology applications across the institution

Risk assessments accompanying submissions focus exclusively on technology risk while omitting cyber resilience, third-party, and operational continuity dimensions that BNM examiners expect to see addressed holistically
There is no feedback loop from BNM's responses or conditions back into the internal risk management framework, missing an opportunity to demonstrate adaptive governance
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance unit — BNM correspondence files, submission packages, and regulatory interaction logs
Board Secretariat — board or board committee resolutions and minutes endorsing specific regulatory submissions
CIO/CISO offices — project-level risk assessment documentation linked to specific technology initiatives submitted to BNM
GRC platform — regulatory submission module or risk assessment repository with traceability to submission artefacts
Legal and Secretarial — formal submission letters, acknowledgement receipts from BNM, and condition fulfilment records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (BNM submission package including risk assessment) + PDF (board endorsement records) + Spreadsheet (submissions register)

evidenceItems.name: Board IT Governance Committee Charter and Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: The formal charter or terms of reference (ToR) for the board-level committee responsible for IT and technology risk oversight (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Board IT Committee, or Board Audit and Risk Committee), together with evidence of its operation — attendance records, meeting frequency, and action item logs. This evidence demonstrates the structural governance mechanism through which the board discharges its collective oversight responsibilities under clause 8.2 and provides the institutional context for evaluating all other evidence items.
whatGoodLooksLike: The charter explicitly assigns responsibility for IT strategic plan review, TRMF/CRF oversight, senior management challenge on risk assessments, and policy review cycle monitoring — directly mirroring the obligations in clause 8.2(a) through 8.2(e)

Membership composition includes at least one director with documented IT or cybersecurity expertise or experience, and the charter requires this competency to be considered in director appointments or co-option
Meeting frequency is sufficient for meaningful oversight — at minimum quarterly — and attendance records confirm quorum is consistently achieved with full member participation

The charter is reviewed and re-endorsed by the full board within the three-year cycle and any amendments are approved by board resolution, demonstrating that the governance structure itself is subject to the same review discipline it oversees

Action item logs from committee meetings trace IT risk and governance issues from identification through to board-level closure, with escalation triggers defined for issues that require full board attention
commonGaps: The committee ToR is generic and does not specifically enumerate IT strategic plan approval, TRMF/CRF oversight, or policy review monitoring as standing agenda items, relying instead on ad hoc escalation from management
No director on the committee has demonstrable IT or cybersecurity competency, and the institution has not sought external expertise to supplement board capability in this domain

Committee meeting frequency is insufficient — meetings occur only annually or semi-annually — meaning the board's ongoing oversight of rapidly evolving technology and cyber risks cannot be demonstrated between meetings
The ToR has not itself been reviewed within the three-year cycle, creating an ironic governance gap in the very document that governs IT oversight

Action item logs are maintained informally without target dates, accountable owners, or escalation records, making it impossible to demonstrate that the committee actually drives closure of identified governance issues
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — certified current version of committee ToR, board resolution approving the ToR, and attendance registers
Company Secretary — director profile records evidencing IT/cybersecurity competency for relevant committee members
Board committee Secretariat — meeting packs, minutes, and action item registers for the relevant oversight committee
Compliance unit — governance framework documentation mapping board committee responsibilities to RMiT obligations
Human Resources or Talent Management — board skills matrix demonstrating technology governance competency in board composition
evidenceItems.format: PDF (committee charter/ToR with board approval resolution) + PDF (meeting minutes and attendance registers) + Spreadsheet (action item log)

auditTips: Cross-reference the IT strategic plan approval date against the corresponding board resolution in the resolutions register — examiners will verify these independently, so any date discrepancy between the plan cover page, the board paper, and the minutes extract will be treated as a governance control weakness rather than an administrative error

Before examination, map every IT-related policy and guideline in the institution's document library to the policy review register and confirm that each entry has a completed review record within the last three years; policies that fall outside the window should be reviewed and approved immediately, with the completion date and scope of review documented explicitly

Prepare a traceability matrix that links each clause 8.2 obligation to specific board minutes, resolutions, and report references — examiners will ask for this cross-reference on day one and having it pre-built demonstrates institutional preparedness and signals substantive rather than reactive compliance

Review board and committee minutes from the past three years and confirm that IT risk and strategic technology items appear as standing agenda items with recorded discussion; where minutes are sparse on IT matters, work with the board secretariat to ensure future minutes capture director questions and management responses in sufficient detail to evidence genuine challenge

For BNM technology application submissions, pull every submission filed in the past three years and confirm that each has an accompanying risk assessment using TRMF-consistent methodology and a board or committee endorsement record; any submission lacking either element should be retrospectively documented with a contemporaneous note explaining the governance pathway that was followed

Conduct a dry-run interview with the board chair or a non-executive director designated as IT oversight lead — ask them to describe, without reference to documents, how the board satisfies each obligation in clause 8.2; gaps in their narrative will reveal where institutional memory and formal documentation are misaligned and where coaching or documentation improvement is needed before examination

Verify that the TRMF and CRF endorsement records specifically name both frameworks and are not subsumed into a broader risk framework approval; BNM examiners treat the TRMF (RMiT paragraph 9.1) and CRF (paragraph 11.2) as distinct regulatory requirements and will look for distinct endorsement artefacts for each

8.3.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.3.title: Board-Level Technology Committee
8.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will first verify that a formally designated board-level committee — not a management-level body — carries explicit accountability for technology oversight, checking board resolutions and the institution's governance charter for unambiguous language of designation. A common red flag is reliance on the full board or on management committees (e.g., EXCO or IT Steering Committee) as substitutes for a dedicated board-level structure. Examiners will then scrutinise committee terms of reference and member profiles to confirm that at least one director possesses substantive technology experience, distinguishing genuine competency from superficial credentials such as a single short course. Meeting frequency, attendance records, and quality of minutes will be assessed to determine whether technology oversight is substantive and ongoing rather than ceremonial. Examiners are also alert to gaps between what the ToR prescribes and what committee activity records demonstrate in practice.

evidenceItems.name: Board Resolution Designating Technology Committee
evidenceItems.description: A formally passed board resolution that explicitly designates the specific committee (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Board Technology Committee) as responsible for technology oversight under RMiT. This is the foundational instrument establishing the obligation and demonstrates board-level intent and authority.
whatGoodLooksLike: Resolution is passed by the full board and recorded in official board meeting minutes, not merely noted by management
The resolution explicitly names the designated committee and uses language that mirrors the RMiT obligation — 'oversight over technology-related matters'
Resolution references the applicable regulatory framework (RMiT) or date of policy issuance, evidencing regulatory awareness
The resolution is undated and clearly precedes or coincides with the institution's RMiT compliance effective date
Any subsequent amendments to the designation (e.g., renaming or reconstituting the committee) are each supported by a fresh board resolution
commonGaps: Technology oversight responsibility is embedded within a broader committee mandate (e.g., Board Risk Committee ToR) without a discrete board resolution specifically designating it for technology under RMiT
The resolution delegates technology oversight to a management-level committee such as EXCO or IT Steering Committee rather than a board-level body
Minutes reflect that the matter was 'presented to' or 'noted by' the board but no formal resolution was passed or recorded
The resolution exists but has not been updated following material changes to the committee's composition, scope, or name
Resolution language is generic ('oversee all risk matters') and does not specifically address technology as a distinct oversight domain
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — official board minute books and resolution registers
GRC platform (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect, MetricStream) — board resolution repository
SharePoint or document management system under Board Governance folder
Legal/Compliance department — regulatory compliance register
evidenceItems.format: PDF (certified extract of board minutes or standalone board resolution, signed by Company Secretary)

evidenceItems.name: Committee Terms of Reference (ToR)
evidenceItems.description: The current, board-approved Terms of Reference for the designated committee, setting out its mandate, scope, composition requirements, quorum, reporting lines, and delegated authorities. The ToR is the primary governance instrument that operationalises the board resolution and must explicitly reflect technology oversight obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: ToR explicitly states that the committee is the board-designated body for technology oversight and references RMiT or equivalent regulatory obligation

Technology-related responsibilities are enumerated in sufficient detail (e.g., reviewing technology risk appetite, overseeing major technology projects, approving IT strategy) rather than captured in a single generic line
Composition clause specifies the minimum requirement for at least one member with technology experience and competencies, consistent with RMiT 8.3 language
ToR prescribes minimum meeting frequency, quorum requirements, and escalation protocols to the full board
ToR carries a version history, effective date, and evidence of board approval (e.g., board minute reference or chairman's signature)
commonGaps: Technology responsibilities are listed in the ToR but are indistinguishable from general risk or audit duties, with no specific technology oversight mandate
Composition clause does not mention the requirement for at least one member with technology experience, meaning compliance with this element cannot be demonstrated from the governing document
ToR has not been reviewed or updated within the past two years, predating significant changes in the institution's technology risk profile or regulatory guidance
The ToR is approved at management level (e.g., signed by CEO) rather than ratified by the full board
Reporting obligations in the ToR are unclear or absent — no requirement for the committee to escalate technology matters to the board
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — governance documents register
GRC platform — policy and charter management module
Board sub-committee SharePoint site — Governance & Policies library
Compliance department — regulatory document inventory
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (board-approved, version-controlled document with effective date and approval signatures)

evidenceItems.name: Technology-Competent Member Profile and Credentials
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing that at least one committee member meets the RMiT requirement for technology experience and competencies. This includes the director's curriculum vitae, professional qualifications, board skills matrix, and any supporting evidence of relevant experience, used by examiners to substantively assess whether the competency threshold is genuinely satisfied.
whatGoodLooksLike: The designated technology-competent member's CV demonstrates substantive industry experience (e.g., prior CIO/CTO/CISO role, technology firm executive, or senior engineering leadership) rather than peripheral technology exposure
A board skills matrix formally maps each director's competencies against required domains, clearly identifying the member(s) satisfying the technology criterion
Professional credentials are current and relevant (e.g., CISM, CISSP, CRISC, CEng, relevant technology-focused directorship training) and copies are on file
The competency assessment is reviewed and affirmed annually as part of the board effectiveness review, with documented conclusions
The institution maintains a succession plan identifying how the technology competency requirement will be maintained should the qualified member vacate the seat
commonGaps: The identified 'technology-competent' member's background is tangential (e.g., used IT systems as a business user) and does not demonstrate substantive technology leadership experience
The board skills matrix exists but technology competency is not a mapped dimension, making it impossible to cross-reference fulfilment of the RMiT requirement
Credentials on file are outdated or the member completed a single short technology awareness course that does not constitute the 'experience and competencies' standard
No succession or contingency plan exists for maintaining the technology competency requirement if the qualifying member resigns or steps down
The institution identifies multiple members as collectively satisfying the requirement but cannot point to a single individual who individually meets the threshold as specified in RMiT
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — director profiles and declaration files
Human Resources / Board Nominations Committee — board skills matrix
Compliance department — fit and proper assessment records
GRC platform — governance and board competency module
evidenceItems.format: PDF (CV with supporting credential copies; board skills matrix as PDF or spreadsheet export)

evidenceItems.name: Committee Meeting Minutes and Attendance Records
evidenceItems.description: Certified minutes from all committee meetings within the review period, together with attendance registers, demonstrating that the committee is actively discharging its technology oversight mandate. Examiners use these records to evaluate whether oversight is substantive — evidenced by informed deliberation, challenge, and direction — or merely procedural.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes reflect substantive deliberation on technology-specific agenda items (e.g., cyber incident reviews, technology risk appetite, major project status, vendor risk) rather than perfunctory noting of management reports
The technology-competent member is consistently present and their contributions or questions are recorded, evidencing active engagement
Meeting frequency meets or exceeds that prescribed in the ToR, with no unexplained gaps over the review period
Minutes record decisions, escalations to the full board, and action items with owners and due dates, demonstrating committee accountability
Minutes are formally approved at the subsequent meeting and signed by the committee chair, with the company secretary's certification
commonGaps: Minutes are generic and templated, recording only that management presentations were 'noted' without evidence of board-level challenge or direction on technology matters
The technology-competent member has poor attendance rates and is absent from critical meetings where technology strategy or risk matters were deliberated
Meeting frequency is lower than stipulated in the ToR (e.g., ToR requires quarterly meetings but records show only two meetings in a twelve-month period)
Technology agenda items are brief standing items appended to broader risk or audit committee meetings with no dedicated time allocation or separate technology-focused deliberation
Action items from prior meetings are not tracked or closed out in subsequent minutes, suggesting the oversight cycle is incomplete
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — certified committee minute books
GRC platform or board portal (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect) — meeting records and attendance logs
Committee chair's secretariat — draft and approved minutes archive
SharePoint — Board Committee Meetings folder
evidenceItems.format: PDF (certified meeting minutes with attendance register, signed by Company Secretary and committee chair)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Reporting Package to Committee
evidenceItems.description: Management papers and reports submitted to the designated committee for technology oversight purposes, demonstrating the information flow that enables the committee to discharge its mandate. These papers evidence that the committee receives sufficient, timely, and relevant technology intelligence to exercise meaningful oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are structured, recurring, and technology-specific — covering technology risk profile, key risk indicators, major incidents, project portfolio status, and vendor risk — rather than being ad hoc or embedded within general management updates
Report content is calibrated for board-level consumption: executive summaries with clear risk ratings, trend analysis, and specific requests for committee decision or endorsement
Reports are submitted ahead of each committee meeting with sufficient lead time for members to review, consistent with the ToR or committee calendar
The reporting package includes a standing section on emerging technology threats and regulatory developments relevant to the institution's risk profile
Reports reflect two-way accountability: management actions in response to prior committee directions are tracked and reported back
commonGaps: Technology reports submitted to the committee are identical to those presented at management-level EXCO or IT Steering Committee, with no board-level adaptation or executive summary
Reports are provided at the meeting itself without pre-circulation, preventing informed deliberation and undermining the quality of oversight
Technology reporting is bundled within the Chief Risk Officer's general risk report with minimal dedicated technology content, making it difficult to evidence standalone technology oversight
Reports focus exclusively on IT operations metrics (uptime, help desk volumes) and omit strategic technology risk, cyber risk, or regulatory technology matters
There is no consistent reporting template or schedule; frequency and content vary materially between meetings with no documented rationale
suggestedSources: CIO / CISO office — board/committee reporting templates and distribution records
Company Secretariat — board paper repository
GRC platform — committee reporting module
SharePoint — Board Committee Papers library
ITSM / risk management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Archer) — report generation and distribution logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers with version control, distribution metadata, and receipt confirmation from Company Secretary)

evidenceItems.name: Annual Board Effectiveness Review — Technology Oversight Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Extracts from the institution's annual board and committee effectiveness review that specifically assess the performance of the designated committee in discharging its technology oversight mandate and the adequacy of technology competency on the committee. This evidence demonstrates a continuous improvement loop and sustained regulatory compliance over time.
whatGoodLooksLike: The effectiveness review explicitly evaluates the designated committee's performance against its technology oversight mandate, with findings and recommended improvements documented
Technology competency of the committee is assessed as a distinct dimension, with a conclusion on whether the RMiT 8.3 requirement is currently met
Review findings are presented to the full board and formally accepted, with improvement actions tracked to completion
The review process is conducted by an independent facilitator (external or internal audit) at least annually, lending objectivity to the assessment
Prior-year findings on technology oversight have been acted upon, evidencing a functioning improvement cycle rather than a compliance-for-audit exercise
commonGaps: Board effectiveness reviews do not include a technology oversight dimension, meaning compliance with RMiT 8.3's ongoing obligations cannot be demonstrated beyond point-in-time documentation
The review addresses committee effectiveness generically (e.g., meeting conduct, minutes quality) but does not evaluate whether technology oversight is substantively adequate
Technology competency sufficiency is not re-assessed annually, so the institution cannot confirm that the RMiT requirement continues to be met as membership changes
Improvement actions identified in the review are not formally tracked or reported to the board, breaking the governance loop
The effectiveness review is conducted only when triggered by external examination rather than as a standing annual practice
suggestedSources: Board Nominations and Remuneration Committee — board effectiveness review reports
Internal Audit department — governance review workpapers
External facilitator's report (where applicable)
Company Secretariat — board improvement action tracker
GRC platform — governance and assurance module
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal effectiveness review report with board acceptance minute reference and action tracker)

auditTips: Begin your evidence assembly by tracing the designation chain: confirm that a board resolution exists, that it designates a specific board-level (not management-level) committee, and that the ToR of that committee explicitly reflects technology oversight — any break in this chain is an immediate finding. Cross-reference the resolution date against the institution's RMiT effective date to confirm timely compliance.

Do not accept a combined Board Risk Committee or Board Audit Committee as automatically compliant — examine the ToR and meeting minutes to verify that technology oversight is a substantive, enumerated mandate rather than absorbed incidentally within broader risk or audit scope. BNM examiners will probe whether 'technology matters' receive dedicated agenda time and informed deliberation.

For the technology-competent member requirement, prepare a concise one-page narrative for each qualifying director that maps their career history to substantive technology leadership milestones, supported by their CV and relevant credentials. Avoid relying solely on the board skills matrix — examiners will ask follow-up questions and may request the underlying evidence.

Conduct a pre-examination dry run: select three recent committee meetings at random and verify that the agenda, pre-circulated papers, minutes, and action log are complete, consistent, and traceable. Gaps in this paper trail — missing pre-circulation records, unsigned minutes, or unresolved action items — are low-hanging findings that can be remediated before examination.

Cross-reference the technology reporting package against the issues and decisions recorded in meeting minutes: if minutes record deliberation on a cyber incident but no incident report appears in the pre-circulation pack, examiners will question whether the committee had adequate information to exercise oversight. Ensure the paper trail of information provision is complete.

Confirm that the institution's organisational chart and governance map clearly distinguishes the designated board-level committee from the management-level IT Steering Committee or equivalent, with explicit reporting lines between them. A common examiner query is whether the board-level committee receives independent reporting or merely ratifies management decisions — the governance documentation must make this distinction unambiguous.

Prepare a single consolidated governance summary document (one to two pages) that maps each RMiT 8.3 obligation — designation, board-level status, technology mandate, and competency requirement — to the specific evidence item and document reference. Present this as your opening exhibit during examination; it frames the evidence package, demonstrates regulatory awareness, and reduces the risk of examiners drawing adverse inferences from disorganised evidence.

8.4.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.4.title: Board Discussion on Technology and Cyber Risk
8.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the board is genuinely engaged with technology and cyber risk — not merely receiving reports in a passive or ceremonial manner. Examiners scrutinise board meeting minutes to verify that cyber risk discussions are substantive, recurrent, and cover strategic, reputational, and liquidity dimensions, not just operational IT matters. A key red flag is thin or templated minutes that suggest agenda items were noted without meaningful deliberation, particularly where no board-level questions, challenges, or decisions are recorded. Examiners also verify that cybersecurity awareness training is structured, institution-specific, and completed by all board members — generic e-learning modules with no evidence of attendance attract scrutiny. Where external expert input is not used, examiners expect a documented rationale; where it is used, they will request evidence of the expert's credentials and the substance of their contribution to board discussion.

evidenceItems.name: Board Meeting Minutes — Technology and Cyber Risk Agenda Items
evidenceItems.description: Approved minutes from full board meetings documenting the discussion of technology risk and cyber threats, demonstrating that the board actively deliberates on these topics at regular intervals and not merely receives reports without engagement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes record substantive board discussion including questions raised, concerns noted, and decisions or directives issued — not merely 'noted and received'
Cyber risk appears as a standing agenda item or is demonstrably covered at every board meeting cycle, with frequency consistent with the institution's risk profile
Minutes explicitly reference strategic, reputational, and liquidity dimensions of cyber risk, not just operational or IT-operational matters
Minutes capture the board's assessment of the potential impact of an extreme cyber incident on stakeholders, including customers, counterparties, and regulators
Minutes are formally approved at the subsequent meeting and bear the signatures of the chairperson and company secretary
commonGaps: Minutes record cyber risk items as 'presented and noted' with no evidence of board deliberation, challenge, or follow-up action assigned
Cyber risk discussion is relegated to board committee level (e.g., Board Risk Committee) with no escalation or reporting back to the full board
Minutes omit coverage of strategic, reputational, or liquidity risk dimensions, focusing only on technical incidents or IT operational KPIs
Frequency of discussion is irregular — cyber risk appears in some quarters but is absent from others, with no documented reason for omission
Minutes are not formally approved or are maintained only in draft form, reducing their evidentiary weight
suggestedSources: Company secretariat records and board paper repository (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect, or SharePoint board portal)
GRC platform board module where meeting minutes are stored and version-controlled
Board Risk Committee minutes where cyber risk is first deliberated before escalation to full board
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed approved minutes) or DOCX with tracked approval; board portal system export

evidenceItems.name: Technology and Cyber Risk Board Reporting Pack
evidenceItems.description: The periodic management report or dashboard submitted to the board covering technology risk and cyber threat updates, demonstrating that the board receives regular, structured, and sufficiently detailed updates to inform meaningful discussion.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are produced on a defined and consistently maintained frequency (e.g., quarterly or per board cycle) with no unexplained gaps
Content covers current threat intelligence, material incidents, key risk indicators (KRIs), and forward-looking cyber risk trends relevant to the Malaysian financial sector
Reports include explicit coverage of strategic, reputational, and liquidity risk implications of cyber threats, not just technical incident counts
Each report is version-controlled, date-stamped, and formally tabled as a board paper with a reference number traceable to the corresponding meeting minutes
Reports demonstrate escalation of significant findings with recommended board-level decisions or approvals clearly identified
commonGaps: Reports are heavily technical (e.g., SIEM alert volumes, patch counts) without translating cyber risk into business or financial impact terms that the board can act on
Reporting frequency is inconsistent — some periods have no formal board-level cyber report, with information instead embedded in general management updates
Reports lack forward-looking threat intelligence; they summarise past incidents only without situational awareness of emerging threats
The same report template is reused across periods with minimal substantive updates, suggesting a compliance-of-form rather than genuine risk communication
Reports are not formally tabled as board papers and cannot be cross-referenced to specific board meeting minutes
suggestedSources: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or CIO office — board reporting pack preparation
GRC platform reporting module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, LogicGate)
Board paper repository on company secretariat platform or SharePoint board portal
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) function for integrated risk reporting that includes cyber
evidenceItems.format: PDF board paper; PowerPoint presentation deck; GRC platform dashboard export

evidenceItems.name: Board Cybersecurity Awareness and Training Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that all board members have participated in relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programmes, including training content, attendance, completion, and assessment outcomes where applicable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Attendance records or completion certificates exist for every individual board member, including independent and non-executive directors, with no members exempted or with unexplained gaps

Training content is institution-specific and contextually relevant — covering threats pertinent to the Malaysian financial sector (e.g., business email compromise, ransomware, third-party risk) rather than generic cybersecurity awareness
Training is refreshed at least annually and updated to reflect the current threat landscape, with version history maintained
Records include training date, duration, format (in-person, facilitated session, e-learning), and — where applicable — assessment scores or knowledge check outcomes

Training programme is approved by a relevant governance body (e.g., Board Nomination & Remuneration Committee, or the board itself) and linked to the institution's broader cybersecurity awareness framework
commonGaps: Training records exist for executives but are absent or incomplete for non-executive or independent board directors
Training consists solely of a generic online module (e.g., a 15-minute e-learning video) not tailored to the financial sector or the institution's specific risk profile
No records of training completion are maintained — only training invitations or calendars, with no confirmation of actual attendance
Training frequency is insufficient — the same single programme from a prior year is presented as current, with no refresh to reflect evolving threats
Training content is not documented or retained, making it impossible to assess whether the curriculum meets the standard of 'relevant cybersecurity awareness'
suggestedSources: Human Resources Learning Management System (LMS) — board director training records
Company secretariat — board induction and continuing development records
CISO or cybersecurity function — facilitated board briefing session materials and attendance registers
External training provider — certificates of completion and programme syllabi
evidenceItems.format: PDF completion certificates; LMS system export (CSV/PDF); signed attendance registers; training presentation decks with version date

evidenceItems.name: External Expert Engagement Records for Board Cyber Briefings
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of instances where external cybersecurity experts provided input to board-level discussions on cyber risk, including the expert's credentials, the substance of their contribution, and how their input influenced board deliberation or decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Records identify the external expert by name, organisation, and relevant qualifications or credentials (e.g., CISSP, CISM, relevant threat intelligence or regulatory expertise)
Engagement scope and key findings or recommendations presented to the board are documented, not merely the fact of the engagement
Board minutes cross-reference the external expert's input, confirming it was received and discussed at board level
Where external experts are not engaged, a documented board-level rationale exists explaining why internal capability is deemed sufficient
Engagements are periodic and risk-driven — triggered by significant threat developments, post-incident reviews, or material changes in the cyber risk landscape
commonGaps: No external expert input is obtained and no documented rationale explains the omission, particularly where the institution's internal cyber expertise is limited
External expert reports are received by management but never formally presented to or discussed at the board level
Expert credentials are not recorded, making it impossible to assess whether the input meets the standard of 'external expert' as contemplated by the clause
Engagements are one-off (e.g., a single briefing years ago) with no evidence of periodic or ongoing expert input aligned to the evolving threat landscape
Expert input is generic industry content (e.g., a public threat report) rather than tailored analysis relevant to the institution's specific risk profile
suggestedSources: CISO or CIO office — vendor and consultant engagement records
Procurement system — contracts and statements of work for cybersecurity advisory engagements
Board paper repository — external expert presentation decks tabled at board meetings
Company secretariat — records of external presentations made at board or board committee meetings
evidenceItems.format: PDF expert reports and presentations; signed engagement letters or terms of reference; board paper cover sheets referencing the expert input

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Risk Discussion Time Allocation and Board Calendar Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that the board allocates sufficient time to discuss cyber risk as a substantive agenda item, distinct from general board business, including board meeting agendas, time allocation records, and any dedicated cyber risk sessions or tabletop exercises conducted at board level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board meeting agendas designate cyber risk as a named, standing agenda item with a specified time allocation, not embedded within a general 'risk update' item

Where an extreme cyber incident scenario is discussed, the agenda and minutes reflect dedicated time for scenario analysis covering strategic, reputational, liquidity, and stakeholder impact dimensions
Board or board committee has conducted at least one cyber crisis simulation or tabletop exercise, with participation records and outcomes documented
Annual board work plan or calendar explicitly schedules cyber risk as a thematic deep-dive item at least once per year in addition to routine reporting cycles
Time allocated to cyber risk discussion in agendas is proportionate to the institution's risk profile and is demonstrably increasing over time as the threat landscape evolves
commonGaps: Cyber risk is consistently the last agenda item with minimal time allocated, and minutes suggest it is routinely deferred or time-compressed due to earlier agenda overrun
No evidence of any dedicated cyber risk deep-dive session or scenario exercise at board level — all discussion is confined to brief updates within broader risk agenda items
Agendas do not specify time allocations for individual items, making it impossible to demonstrate that 'sufficient time' was allocated as required by the clause
Tabletop exercises or crisis simulations are conducted at management or operational level only, with no board participation or board-level scenario discussion
Board work plan does not identify cyber risk as a planned thematic topic, suggesting discussion is ad hoc rather than systematically planned
suggestedSources: Company secretariat — board and board committee meeting agendas and annual board work plans
Board portal platform (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect) — meeting scheduling and agenda management records
CISO or cybersecurity function — records of board-level tabletop exercises or cyber crisis simulations
GRC platform — board governance calendar and risk committee scheduling records
evidenceItems.format: PDF meeting agendas; board work plan calendar (PDF/XLSX); tabletop exercise reports and attendance registers

evidenceItems.name: Board Skills Matrix — Cybersecurity Competency Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A board skills matrix or competency assessment demonstrating that the collective board possesses or has access to sufficient cybersecurity and technology risk literacy to engage meaningfully with cyber risk discussions, supporting the institution's approach to board-level cyber governance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Skills matrix explicitly includes cybersecurity, technology risk, and digital literacy as assessed competency dimensions alongside traditional financial and governance competencies
Matrix is reviewed and updated at least annually, with refreshes triggered by changes in board composition or material shifts in the technology risk landscape
Where the board has identified gaps in collective cyber competency, documented remediation actions are in place — such as targeted training, external expert engagement, or board composition plans
Individual director self-assessments or independent assessments underpin the matrix ratings, providing an evidential basis for competency claims
The matrix and its implications for board effectiveness are discussed at Board Nomination & Remuneration Committee level and reflected in the annual board effectiveness review
commonGaps: Skills matrix omits cybersecurity and technology risk as competency dimensions, addressing only traditional board skills such as finance, legal, and industry expertise
Matrix is static and has not been updated following changes in board composition or evolution of the institution's technology risk profile
Competency ratings are self-assessed without supporting evidence, and all directors are rated as competent in cybersecurity regardless of actual background
Identified gaps in board cyber competency are not linked to any remediation plan — training records or external expert engagement cannot be traced back to gap closure
The skills matrix is prepared for recruitment purposes only and is not actively used as a governance tool to inform board development or external expert engagement decisions
suggestedSources: Board Nomination & Remuneration Committee — board skills matrix and director competency records
Company secretariat — board composition and director profile documentation
Human Resources — board director development plans and training needs analysis
Annual Report — board composition and skills disclosure (as a cross-reference point)
evidenceItems.format: PDF or XLSX skills matrix; PDF board effectiveness review report; director profile summaries

auditTips: Cross-reference board meeting minutes against the board reporting pack index: every cyber risk report tabled should produce a corresponding substantive minute entry. Where reports are tabled but minutes record only 'noted', flag this as a compliance risk and work with the company secretariat to improve minute-taking standards before examination.

Compile a board cyber training tracker that maps each individual board member (by name and role) against training completed, date, duration, and content version. BNM examiners will ask for evidence by individual director — a consolidated register prevents gaps from being discovered during the examination itself.

If the institution has not engaged external cybersecurity experts at board level, prepare a formal documented rationale approved by the board or a relevant committee explaining why internal capability is sufficient. An undocumented absence of external expert input is harder to defend than a reasoned, documented decision.

Review the past 12 months of board meeting agendas and calculate the average time formally allocated to cyber risk discussion. If the allocation is visibly insufficient relative to the institution's risk profile, work with the company secretariat to restructure the board work plan to include at least one annual cyber deep-dive session with a recorded extended time block.

Ensure the board skills matrix is updated to explicitly include cybersecurity and technology risk as a competency dimension. Map identified gaps to specific remediation actions (training completed or external expert engaged) so that the matrix functions as a living governance tool rather than a static recruitment document.

For institutions that have not conducted a board-level cyber tabletop exercise, prioritise scheduling one before the anticipated examination window.
