Retain the exercise scenario, facilitation materials, participation register, and post-exercise report as evidence that the board has discussed the impact of an extreme cyber incident on stakeholders — this directly addresses Clause 8.4(b).

Prepare a clause-by-clause evidence map linking Clause 8.4(a), (b), and (c) to specific document references (paper numbers, minute references, training completion records) so that during examination you can respond to examiner questions with immediate, traceable citations rather than searching across multiple systems.

8.5.subsection: Responsibilities of the Board of Directors
8.5.title: Board Audit Committee Technology Oversight
8.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the Board Audit Committee exercises substantive, informed oversight of the internal technology audit function — not merely procedural sign-off. Examiners will scrutinise BAC meeting minutes to confirm that technology audit matters are discussed with depth and that BAC members demonstrate sufficient technological literacy to challenge audit findings. A primary red flag is a BAC that delegates technology audit oversight entirely to management without independent review. Examiners will also assess whether corrective action tracking is a standing agenda item with documented follow-through, and will cross-reference audit issue registers against subsequent BAC minutes to verify that gaps are not silently deferred or closed without evidence of remediation. The frequency and scope of technology audits relative to the institution's risk profile is a key evaluation point, with examiners looking for documented rationale when high-risk technology domains are audited infrequently.

evidenceItems.name: BAC Meeting Minutes — Technology Audit Agenda Items
evidenceItems.description: Approved minutes from BAC meetings demonstrating substantive discussion of technology audit activities, findings, and oversight decisions. These records establish that the BAC is actively engaged in technology audit governance rather than passively receiving reports.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes contain dedicated technology audit agenda items at every meeting, with named BAC members recorded as asking specific questions or raising concerns about findings
Discussion captured in minutes reflects understanding of technical risk concepts such as access control gaps, patch management backlogs, or third-party system risks — not just high-level summaries
Minutes record BAC decisions on audit scope changes, resource allocations for the internal audit function, or escalation of critical findings to the full Board
Corrective action status updates appear as a standing agenda item with explicit BAC acknowledgement of open versus closed items and expected closure dates
Minutes are formally approved at the subsequent BAC meeting and signed by the BAC chairman, with a clear audit trail showing version history
commonGaps: Minutes are templated and generic, recording only that technology audit reports were 'noted' without any substantive discussion or BAC deliberation captured
Technology audit items appear irregularly or are bundled under a catch-all 'internal audit update' with no differentiation of technology-specific matters
Corrective action tracking is absent from minutes, making it impossible to verify BAC oversight of gap closure without separate evidence
Minutes lack attribution of questions or comments to individual BAC members, preventing examiners from assessing whether members with technological competence are actively engaged
Significant time lags between the BAC meeting date and the approval of minutes (exceeding 60 days) undermine the reliability of the record
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Company Secretary office — official approved minutes repository
GRC platform (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect, or SharePoint Board portal) where meeting packs and approved minutes are stored
Internal Audit department for draft minutes prior to formal approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed and approved), with corresponding meeting packs in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Annual Technology Audit Plan — BAC-Approved
evidenceItems.description: The formally approved annual internal technology audit plan, evidencing that the BAC has reviewed and endorsed the audit scope, methodology, procedures, and frequency. This document demonstrates BAC fulfilment of its obligation to ensure appropriate audit scope and scheduling relative to the institution's technology risk profile.
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit plan explicitly maps audit engagements to technology risk domains (e.g., cybersecurity, cloud, third-party IT, core banking systems, BCP/DR) with clear rationale for prioritisation and frequency
BAC approval is documented by a resolution or formal sign-off in meeting minutes, with the plan version and date referenced, confirming the BAC reviewed — not merely received — the plan
Plan includes a risk-based methodology narrative explaining how audit frequency is calibrated to inherent risk ratings of each technology domain
Resource allocation section confirms that staffing levels and competency mix are sufficient to execute the planned engagements within the audit year
Mid-year plan revision process is documented, with any scope changes requiring BAC notification or approval depending on materiality thresholds defined in the audit charter
commonGaps: Audit plan is approved by the Chief Audit Executive or Audit Committee without a clear record of BAC-level review, creating ambiguity about whether BAC fulfilled its own obligation
Scope is defined at a high level (e.g., 'IT general controls audit') without specifying systems, domains, or risk-based rationale, making it impossible to assess adequacy
Frequency of technology audits is uniform across all domains regardless of risk ratings, suggesting a compliance-calendar approach rather than a genuine risk-based methodology
Plan does not account for emerging technology risks introduced during the year (e.g., new cloud migrations, new third-party integrations) and is not refreshed mid-year
No evidence that the BAC challenged or requested modifications to the proposed plan — approval appears rubber-stamped without deliberation
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — annual audit planning documentation
GRC platform (e.g., TeamMate, AuditBoard, or RSA Archer) for audit universe and plan management
Board Secretariat for the BAC resolution or minute extract confirming plan approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded BAC approval resolution or cross-referenced minute extract

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Staff Competency Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the BAC has assessed and is satisfied with the competency of internal audit staff to perform technology audits. This includes qualifications, certifications, training records, and any BAC-level review of the audit team's capability profile relative to the institution's technology complexity.
whatGoodLooksLike: A competency matrix mapping each technology auditor's qualifications (e.g., CISA, CISSP, CEH, cloud certifications) against the technology domains in the audit universe, reviewed and acknowledged by the BAC
BAC minutes or a formal BAC paper record that the committee reviewed the technology audit team's capability profile and was satisfied — or that it directed management to address identified gaps
Annual training and professional development records for technology audit staff, with a summary provided to the BAC confirming CPE hours and skills currency
Where internal capability gaps exist, evidence of co-sourcing or specialist engagement arrangements reviewed and approved by the BAC, with defined scope and oversight controls
Succession planning or talent pipeline documentation for key technology audit roles, evidencing BAC awareness of staffing risk to the audit function
commonGaps: Competency assessment is managed entirely within the Internal Audit function without any formal BAC review, resulting in no evidence of BAC satisfaction with staff adequacy
Certification records exist but are not aggregated into a capability summary for BAC consumption, leaving a gap in evidence of BAC-level oversight

Technology audit team lacks certifications relevant to the institution's highest-risk technology domains (e.g., cloud auditing where the institution is heavily cloud-dependent), with no compensating co-sourcing arrangement
Training records show generic IT training rather than technology-audit-specific upskilling, undermining the assertion of 'adequate competence' for audit work
BAC receives staff headcount data but not a qualitative competency assessment, meaning the BAC cannot demonstrate it evaluated adequacy of skills — only adequacy of numbers
suggestedSources: Human Resources — personnel files, training records, and certification databases
Internal Audit department — audit team competency matrix and CPE tracking logs
Learning Management System (LMS) for training completion records
Board Secretariat for BAC papers and resolutions referencing competency reviews
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (competency matrix), PDF (certifications and training records), PDF/DOCX (BAC paper and resolution)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Issue Register and Corrective Action Tracking Log
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register of technology audit findings and associated corrective action plans (CAPs), demonstrating that the BAC exercises effective oversight of remediation progress. This is the primary evidence that the BAC is ensuring 'prompt closure of corrective actions to address technology control gaps' as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register captures all open technology audit findings with severity ratings, root cause categorisation, CAP owner, committed closure date, and current status — updated prior to each BAC meeting
BAC meeting materials include a structured exception report highlighting overdue CAPs, repeat findings, and findings that have been re-rated or deferred, requiring explicit BAC acknowledgement
Evidence of BAC directing escalation or escalation timelines for high-severity findings that have breached their committed closure dates, captured in meeting minutes
Closure validation process is documented — showing that CAP closure requires evidence of remediation reviewed by Internal Audit before the finding is marked closed in the register
Trend analysis is presented to the BAC at defined intervals (e.g., quarterly) showing aging of open items, repeat-finding rates, and domain-level concentration of control gaps
commonGaps: Register exists but is maintained solely by management with BAC receiving only a summary count of open and closed items — no visibility of individual high-risk findings or overdue CAPs
CAP closure is self-certified by the business unit that owns the control gap, without Internal Audit validation, making BAC oversight of 'effective closure' unsubstantiated
Findings are routinely deferred beyond original committed dates without formal BAC acknowledgement or revised closure rationale, indicating ineffective oversight
Repeat findings (same control gap identified in successive audits) are not flagged distinctively in the register or escalated to the BAC, masking systemic control weaknesses

Register tracks findings at a generic 'IT audit' level without distinguishing technology control gaps from financial or operational findings, preventing meaningful BAC oversight of technology-specific remediation
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., AuditBoard, MetricStream, RSA Archer, or ServiceNow GRC) — audit issue management module
Internal Audit department — issue tracker and CAP management files
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) for technology remediation task tracking linked to audit findings
Board Secretariat for BAC meeting packs containing issue register extracts
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (current register), PDF (BAC meeting pack extracts showing register summaries)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Reports Submitted to BAC
evidenceItems.description: Completed internal technology audit reports formally submitted to and received by the BAC, demonstrating that audit findings and conclusions are escalated to the appropriate governance level. These reports confirm the existence of a functioning technology audit function operating under BAC oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are addressed or copied to the BAC and include an executive summary calibrated for a board-level audience alongside technical findings, enabling informed BAC oversight without requiring deep technical immersion
Each report clearly states the audit scope, period covered, methodology, overall rating or opinion, and individual finding severity ratings consistent with the institution's defined rating scale
BAC acknowledgement of receipt and review is captured in meeting minutes, with specific reports referenced by engagement name and date — not just a generic 'audit reports reviewed' notation
Reports include management responses and agreed CAPs with committed dates, enabling the BAC to track remediation accountability from the point of initial finding disclosure
Distribution list and submission date are recorded on each report, providing an audit trail confirming timely escalation to the BAC rather than delayed or selective disclosure
commonGaps: Reports are submitted to the Audit Committee but BAC members have delegated review to a sub-committee or management layer, with no evidence of direct BAC engagement with technology audit findings
Reports lack an overall audit opinion or rating, presenting findings as a list without context on severity or systemic risk — limiting the BAC's ability to prioritise oversight attention
High-severity or critical findings are verbally briefed to the BAC Chairman without formal written submission, creating gaps in the documentary record of BAC oversight
Management responses in reports are vague or commit only to 'ongoing monitoring' without specific remediation actions or measurable closure criteria
Significant time lag between report finalisation and BAC submission (exceeding 60 days) is not explained or escalated, indicating a breakdown in the reporting chain
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — finalized audit report repository
GRC platform or SharePoint — audit report document management library
Board Secretariat — BAC meeting pack archives confirming which reports were tabled at each meeting
evidenceItems.format: PDF (finalized, signed audit reports with management responses)

evidenceItems.name: BAC Technology Audit Oversight Charter or Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: The BAC's formal Terms of Reference (ToR) or charter explicitly assigning responsibility for technology audit oversight, including the specific obligations enumerated in RMiT 8.5. This document establishes the governance framework within which all other evidence items operate.
whatGoodLooksLike: ToR explicitly assigns the BAC responsibility for overseeing the internal technology audit function, staff competency, audit scope and frequency, and corrective action closure — directly mirroring the language of RMiT 8.5

ToR specifies the minimum frequency of BAC review for technology audit matters (e.g., quarterly reporting of CAP status, annual review of audit plan) with defined escalation triggers for urgent matters
Document has been reviewed and approved by the full Board within the past 12 months, with the review date, approving resolution, and version number recorded
ToR defines the BAC's authority to direct the Chief Audit Executive on technology audit resourcing, scope adjustments, or co-sourcing arrangements without management interference
ToR includes a mechanism for the BAC to commission special technology audits or thematic reviews independent of the annual plan when risk indicators warrant it
commonGaps: BAC ToR references 'internal audit' oversight broadly without specifically calling out technology audit as a distinct responsibility, creating ambiguity about whether RMiT 8.5 obligations are formally assigned
ToR has not been reviewed or updated in more than two years, failing to reflect the institution's current technology risk profile or regulatory developments such as RMiT itself
Technology audit competency oversight is assigned to the BAC in the ToR but operationally delegated to the Chief Audit Executive without a reporting or escalation mechanism back to the BAC

Corrective action oversight responsibilities are described in vague terms ('monitor remediation') without specifying reporting frequency, escalation thresholds, or BAC decision-making authority for overdue items
ToR is a standalone document not cross-referenced to the Board Charter, the Internal Audit Charter, or RMiT obligations — creating potential gaps or conflicts between governance instruments
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — current approved BAC Terms of Reference
Board portal or SharePoint governance library for policy and charter documents
Legal and Compliance department for the register of Board-level governance instruments and their review dates
evidenceItems.format: PDF (Board-approved, signed, with version history and review date)

auditTips: Cross-reference BAC meeting minutes against the technology audit issue register to verify that every high and critical severity finding raised during the examination period has a corresponding BAC discussion entry — any finding that exists in the register but is absent from minutes is an immediate red flag that the BAC is not exercising effective oversight of corrective action closure.

Request the BAC Terms of Reference first and map each obligation in RMiT 8.5 verbatim (staff competency, audit scope, procedures, frequency, and corrective action closure) to a specific clause in the ToR — if any obligation lacks a clear ToR assignment, the governance framework itself is deficient and downstream evidence will not remedy this structural gap.

When reviewing competency evidence, match the technology audit team's certification and skills profile against the institution's highest-risk technology domains by cross-referencing the annual technology audit plan's risk universe. A team with only legacy IT audit certifications auditing a cloud-heavy or API-driven institution will not satisfy the 'adequate competence' standard regardless of headcount.

Examine the aging profile of open corrective actions in the issue register and identify any items that have been deferred more than once — then check whether each deferral was explicitly approved by the BAC with documented rationale, or whether deferrals were granted by management without BAC visibility. Repeated management-only deferrals indicate the BAC's oversight of 'prompt closure' is nominal rather than effective.

Request the last three years of annual technology audit plans alongside the corresponding year-end completion reports to assess whether planned audits were actually executed. A pattern of low completion rates or frequent scope reductions without BAC-documented rationale suggests the BAC is approving plans it cannot or does not enforce, undermining its oversight effectiveness.

Assess whether the BAC receives technology audit reporting in a format that enables genuine oversight — look for executive summaries with clear risk ratings and trend data, not just raw technical findings. Then verify in minutes that BAC members asked substantive questions; if minutes show only 'noted' responses to every report, probe whether the BAC understands its oversight role versus its receipt-of-information role.

For co-sourced or outsourced components of the technology audit function, confirm that co-sourcing arrangements were reviewed and approved at BAC level (not just management level), and that the BAC receives findings from co-sourced engagements through the same reporting and issue-tracking pipeline as internal engagements — gaps here indicate the BAC's oversight perimeter does not cover the full technology audit function.

8.6.title: Senior Management Accountability for Technology Risk
8.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether senior management actively owns technology and cyber risk management rather than delegating it passively downward. Examiners specifically verify that board-approved TRMF and CRF frameworks are translated into operational policies and procedures that are quantitatively aligned with the stated risk appetite and tolerance thresholds — generic or aspirational policies that lack measurable controls are a primary red flag. On crisis management, examiners scrutinise whether escalation triggers are explicitly calibrated to customer impact metrics (disruption duration, customer volume, transaction value) and whether those thresholds have been exercised in drills. For board reporting, examiners look for evidence that KPI dashboards are substantive and timely, not retrospective or sanitised, and that the board is genuinely enabled to exercise oversight. Common red flags include undated or unsigned policies, KPI reports with consistent green ratings and no trend analysis, and crisis plans that lack quantified escalation thresholds.

evidenceItems.name: TRMF and CRF Implementation Policy Register
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated register of all operational technology risk and cyber risk policies and procedures that derive from the board-approved TRMF and CRF, demonstrating the translation of framework directives into enforceable day-to-day controls. Each policy entry must trace back to a specific TRMF/CRF section and reference the applicable risk appetite or tolerance limit it enforces.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each policy document contains an explicit cross-reference field mapping it to the TRMF/CRF section it implements, with version numbers aligned
Policy statements include quantitative risk tolerance thresholds (e.g., maximum acceptable system downtime, patch SLA windows) consistent with board-approved appetite statements
The register shows a documented review cycle (at minimum annual) with evidence of senior management sign-off — dated signatures or digital approval records in the GRC platform
Policies are tiered by criticality with mandatory rollout evidence (staff acknowledgement logs, training completion records) demonstrating operational embedding
Change history within the register shows policies were updated within 90 days of any board-level TRMF/CRF revision, maintaining continuous alignment
commonGaps: Policies exist but contain no explicit linkage to TRMF or CRF sections, making it impossible to demonstrate translation from board-approved frameworks
Risk appetite thresholds in operational policies differ from or are absent in the board-approved appetite statement, indicating inconsistency rather than cascading alignment
Policies carry an approval date but no evidence of senior management review after the most recent TRMF/CRF update, suggesting the register is stale
Procedures exist for some technology domains (e.g., patch management) but are absent for others covered by the CRF (e.g., threat intelligence, incident classification), creating coverage gaps
Policy documents are stored in departmental silos with no consolidated register, making it impossible to demonstrate comprehensive coverage during examination
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) — policy module with version control and approval workflows
SharePoint or Confluence policy library with metadata tagging for framework cross-references
Document Management System (DMS) maintained by the Chief Risk Officer or CISO office
Board Secretariat records linking board-approved TRMF/CRF versions to downstream policy revision logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (individual policies) + Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (register/index)

evidenceItems.name: Crisis Management Plan with Escalation Thresholds
evidenceItems.description: The documented crisis management plan (or business continuity and IT disaster recovery escalation annex) that defines quantified escalation triggers for service disruptions based on customer impact dimensions: duration of disruption, number of customers affected, and monetary value of impacted financial transactions. Evidence must show that the plan is operationally active, not merely a document artefact.
whatGoodLooksLike: The plan contains an explicit escalation matrix with numerical thresholds for each of the three BNM-specified customer impact dimensions — duration (e.g., >2 hours), customer count (e.g., >5,000 customers), and transaction value (e.g., >RM 10 million) — mapped to escalation levels and required notifiers
Senior management escalation paths are named by role (not individual) with documented contact protocols and defined response time SLAs at each escalation tier

The plan has a documented test or simulation date within the last 12 months, with an after-action review showing that escalation timelines and thresholds were validated against the three impact criteria
The plan references integration with the customer communications protocol and regulatory notification obligations (e.g., BNM incident reporting timelines), demonstrating end-to-end crisis response
Version history shows the plan was reviewed and updated following any actual service disruption incident, with changes traceable to lessons-learned records
commonGaps: Escalation thresholds are described qualitatively (e.g., 'significant disruption') rather than with quantified values for duration, customer count, and transaction value, making objective escalation decisions impossible
The crisis management plan exists but its escalation section omits one or more of the three customer impact dimensions specified in clause 8.6(b), particularly transaction value
The plan has not been tested via tabletop exercise or live drill within the past 12 months, so there is no empirical evidence that escalation triggers function as designed
Escalation paths are person-specific rather than role-based, rendering the plan unreliable when named individuals are unavailable
After-action reviews from past incidents show that actual escalation deviated from the documented thresholds without a formal plan update to reconcile the discrepancy
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management (BCM) team repository — crisis management plan and BCP/DR documentation
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM, Jira Service Management) — major incident records with escalation audit trails
BCP exercise and drill records maintained by the operational resilience or BCM function
Operations Control Centre or NOC logs capturing historical escalation event timelines
Incident Management System capturing customer impact metrics at time of escalation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (plan document) + Spreadsheet (escalation matrix) + Exercise report (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Service Disruption Incident Records with Customer Impact Metrics
evidenceItems.description: Historical records of actual service disruptions that document the three customer impact dimensions — disruption duration, number of customers affected, and number and value of financial transactions impacted — used to validate that escalation decisions were triggered correctly in accordance with the crisis management plan thresholds.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each major incident record contains a timestamped log of the disruption duration, a quantified customer impact count sourced from channel or system telemetry, and a calculated transaction impact figure (count and value) produced at the time of the incident

Records show that when thresholds were breached, escalation to the appropriate senior management tier occurred within the SLA defined in the crisis management plan, with timestamps evidencing timeliness

Post-incident review reports are linked to each major incident record and contain a root cause analysis, a customer impact assessment, and confirmation that regulatory notification obligations were assessed
The incident classification system reflects the three-dimensional customer impact taxonomy consistent with clause 8.6(b), enabling direct comparison between actual events and escalation thresholds
Aggregated incident trend data is maintained at the portfolio level, allowing senior management to identify recurring disruption patterns that may warrant risk appetite review
commonGaps: Incident records document system-level metrics (e.g., server uptime) but not customer-facing impact — customer count and transaction value are absent or estimated rather than measured
Disruption duration is recorded as resolution time rather than customer-impact duration, obscuring the actual period during which customers could not transact
Escalation timestamps are missing or inconsistent, making it impossible to verify whether escalation met the timeliness obligation in the crisis management plan
Post-incident reviews were not completed for all major incidents, or were completed significantly after the event, reducing their evidentiary value
Incidents below the formal escalation threshold are not documented, creating a blind spot that prevents assessment of whether thresholds are calibrated appropriately
suggestedSources: ITSM platform major incident module (ServiceNow, Remedy, Jira) — incident records with escalation audit trails
Banking system transaction monitoring dashboards capturing impacted transaction volumes and values during downtime windows
Digital channel analytics platform (e.g., mobile banking, internet banking ops data) for customer impact counts
Operations Control Centre or NOC daily/incident logs
Customer complaints management system cross-referenced to disruption windows for corroboration
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF/CSV) + Post-Incident Review reports (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Risk KPI Reporting Pack
evidenceItems.description: The recurring reports submitted by senior management to the board (or Board Risk Committee) providing updates on key performance indicators for technology risk and cyber risk controls. These packs demonstrate that senior management fulfils its obligation under clause 8.6(c) to keep the board adequately informed to enable oversight and informed performance review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are produced at a regular, pre-defined cadence (e.g., monthly or quarterly) with no unexplained gaps, and submission dates are documented against the board meeting schedule

Each report contains both current-period KPI values and historical trend data, enabling the board to assess trajectory rather than point-in-time status — positive or negative trend commentary is provided by senior management
KPIs are directly mapped to the risk controls specified in the TRMF/CRF, with explicit reference to board-approved risk appetite thresholds so the board can interpret performance in context

Reports include qualitative commentary from senior management on material control weaknesses, emerging risks, or threshold breaches — not just RAG status indicators — demonstrating substantive engagement
Board acknowledgement or minutes excerpts confirm that the reports were tabled, reviewed, and that board questions or directives arising from the review are recorded and tracked to closure
commonGaps: KPI reports are produced but KPIs are not linked to the board-approved risk appetite statement, so the board cannot assess whether performance is within tolerance
Reports consistently show all-green RAG status with no narrative explanation of risk trends, suggesting sanitisation rather than genuine risk transparency
Board meeting minutes do not reference the technology risk KPI report or record any board discussion, indicating the report may have been tabled but not substantively reviewed
Reports are backward-looking only with no forward-looking risk indicators or emerging threat commentary, limiting the board's ability to exercise proactive oversight
The frequency or timing of reports is ad hoc rather than scheduled, preventing the board from conducting regular and consistent performance review
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board pack archives and meeting minutes confirming report tabling and discussion
GRC platform reporting module — scheduled KPI dashboards with export and distribution logs
CISO or CRO office — quarterly/monthly technology risk report production records
Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee minutes and action logs
Risk appetite monitoring dashboards (e.g., from Archer, MetricStream, or Power BI connected to GRC data)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board pack) + System-generated KPI dashboard export (PDF/XLSX) + Board minutes excerpt (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Technology Risk Mandate and Delegation Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing that senior management (collectively or through named roles such as CTO, CISO, CRO) holds formally assigned accountability for day-to-day technology and cyber risk management, including the internal governance structure through which this accountability is exercised and delegated. This provides the foundational evidence that primary responsibility is vested in senior management as required by clause 8.6.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board-approved or board-ratified terms of reference for the Management Risk Committee, IT Risk Committee, or equivalent body explicitly assign technology and cyber risk ownership to named senior management roles with defined accountability boundaries

Individual senior management role mandates (e.g., CTO, CISO, COO job descriptions or authority matrices) include explicit accountability for technology risk management consistent with clause 8.6 obligations
A documented escalation and delegation matrix shows how technology risk decisions flow from senior management downward to operational teams, with clear authority limits at each tier
Evidence of senior management active participation in technology risk forums — attendance registers, signed-off risk decisions, or action items attributable to senior management in committee minutes
The delegation framework is reviewed and reaffirmed annually, with records showing that any changes to roles or accountabilities were approved by the board
commonGaps: Technology risk accountability is described in board-level TRMF documents but is not translated into specific senior management role mandates or committee terms of reference that create enforceable individual ownership

The management committee responsible for technology risk oversight has terms of reference that have not been updated following organisational restructuring, resulting in accountability gaps or misaligned role assignments
Committee meeting records show infrequent senior management attendance or decisions being deferred to working-level staff, undermining evidence of active senior management oversight
Delegation below senior management is undocumented, creating ambiguity about authority limits and preventing auditors from tracing accountability through the organisation

The accountability framework covers technology risk generally but does not explicitly address cyber risk as a distinct category, inconsistent with clause 8.6's dual reference to technology and cyber risks
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board-approved committee terms of reference and management committee charters
Human Resources — senior management role profiles, authority matrices, and delegated authority frameworks
IT Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee — meeting minutes, attendance records, and action logs
GRC platform — risk ownership registry linking risk categories to accountable senior management roles
Organisational governance documents — governance framework or operating model documentation maintained by the Chief Governance Officer or equivalent
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (terms of reference, role mandates, delegation matrix) + Committee minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Risk Appetite Alignment Evidence — Policy vs. Board-Approved Thresholds
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence demonstrating that the operational policies and procedures implemented by senior management are quantitatively consistent with the board-approved risk appetite and risk tolerance levels, fulfilling the specific requirement in clause 8.6(a) that implementation be 'consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance'. This is a critical linkage document that examiners use to test whether the framework cascade is substantive.
whatGoodLooksLike: A traceability matrix or compliance mapping document explicitly cross-references each operational policy control parameter (e.g., RTO/RPO targets, patch SLA, vulnerability remediation windows) against the corresponding board-approved risk tolerance limit

Where operational thresholds are more conservative than the board-approved tolerance, there is documented rationale from senior management explaining the tighter boundary and confirming it does not create unintended operational risk
The risk appetite statement itself contains technology and cyber risk metrics that are measurable and observable, enabling meaningful comparison with operational policy parameters
Any policy that deviates from or cannot be directly mapped to the risk appetite statement is accompanied by a formal exception record approved by senior management with a remediation timeline
The alignment mapping is updated whenever the board revises the risk appetite statement, with version-controlled records showing synchronised updates to both appetite and policy documents
commonGaps: The board-approved risk appetite statement contains only qualitative technology risk descriptors (e.g., 'low tolerance for cyber incidents') with no quantitative thresholds, making policy alignment unmeasurable and impossible to evidence

Operational policies contain specific numerical controls (e.g., 72-hour patch deployment SLA) but no documentation links these figures to the board-approved appetite, leaving the alignment implicit rather than demonstrated
The risk appetite statement was last revised more than 12 months ago and does not reflect current technology or cyber risk exposures, suggesting that policy alignment is based on an outdated baseline
Exceptions where operational policies exceed or fall short of the appetite thresholds are not documented or approved, creating unexplained inconsistencies that examiners will flag
Different business units apply different operational thresholds for the same risk type without a documented rationale, indicating fragmented rather than enterprise-wide appetite alignment
suggestedSources: Risk Management Division — board-approved risk appetite statement (current and prior versions)
GRC platform — risk appetite monitoring module with policy parameter mappings
CISO and CRO office — TRMF/CRF implementation traceability matrix or compliance mapping document
Policy Management System — policy metadata including appetite threshold references
Internal Audit — prior audit findings on risk appetite alignment for remediation evidence
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (traceability/mapping matrix) + PDF (risk appetite statement) + PDF (exception records)

auditTips: Before examination, produce a clause 8.6 evidence map — a single document that lists each sub-obligation (8.6(a), 8.6(b)(i-iii), 8.6(c)) and points to the specific evidence item, page reference, and custodian for each. BNM examiners work methodically through the clause; institutions that can respond to each obligation point immediately reduce examination friction and demonstrate maturity.

Verify that the crisis management plan's escalation thresholds are expressed as concrete numbers for all three clause 8.6(b) dimensions before examination — duration in hours, customer count as an integer, and transaction value in Ringgit. Pull the last three major incident records and manually check whether actual escalation timestamps match what the plan prescribes; unexplained deviations are among the most commonly cited findings.

Cross-validate the board KPI reports against board meeting minutes. Examiners will compare the two: if a report is dated for a board meeting but the minutes contain no reference to technology risk KPIs or any board commentary on the data, the report will be treated as tabled but unreviewed. Prepare a one-page summary showing each report submission date mapped to the corresponding board minute reference and any directives raised.

Trace at least three operational policy controls back to the board-approved risk appetite statement in a documented matrix and have this available on day one of examination. Focus particularly on controls relating to system availability (RTO/RPO), vulnerability and patch management timelines, and incident classification — these are the areas most likely to be probed when examiners test clause 8.6(a) alignment.

Conduct a pre-examination gap check on senior management committee attendance records for the last 12 months. Thin or inconsistent attendance by the CTO, CISO, or CRO at the IT Risk Committee or equivalent forum is a red flag that examiners use to challenge whether senior management holds genuine day-to-day ownership of technology risk, as opposed to delegating it entirely to working-level staff.

Prepare a version-controlled policy lineage document showing the date of the most recent board TRMF/CRF approval, the date each downstream policy was subsequently updated, and the approving senior manager. If the gap between framework approval and policy update exceeds 90 days for any material policy, prepare a documented explanation and evidence of interim controls — examiners will interpret unexplained lags as evidence that the clause 8.6(a) implementation obligation was not met promptly.

Run a parallel tabletop exercise focused specifically on the three customer impact escalation triggers in clause 8.6(b) within 60 days before any anticipated examination window, and retain the exercise scenario, participant list, escalation decisions made, and after-action review as examination-ready evidence. This provides contemporaneous proof that the crisis management plan is operationally embedded, not merely documented.

8.7.title: Cross-Functional Technology Committee
8.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the cross-functional committee is substantive in composition and function rather than a nominal governance construct. They will verify that membership genuinely includes senior management from cyber, technology, and major business units — not delegates or alternates as the norm — and that the committee exercises real oversight rather than rubber-stamping decisions already made at the working level. Examiners pay particular attention to whether board updates on key technology matters are timely, structured, and demonstrate escalation discipline, and will test this by tracing specific incidents or strategic changes back through the reporting chain. The policy deviation approval process is a frequent red flag area: examiners look for evidence that deviations are subjected to a documented, rigorous risk assessment before approval, and that material deviations are formally reported to the board with appropriate follow-through. Committee meeting cadence, quorum records, and the quality of minutes are used as proxies for whether governance is genuinely active.

evidenceItems.name: Committee Charter / Terms of Reference (ToR)
evidenceItems.description: The formal founding document that constitutes the cross-functional technology committee, defines its mandate, membership criteria, quorum requirements, meeting cadence, reporting lines, and scope of authority — directly demonstrating that senior management has formally established the committee as required by Clause 8.7.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter explicitly names the committee's mandate in terms that map to the three sub-clauses (a), (b), and (c) of 8.7, with no material omissions

Membership criteria unambiguously require representation at senior management level from cyber, technology, and each major business unit, with a named incumbent and a defined escalation path if the primary member is unavailable

Quorum rules specify that a minimum cross-functional quorum (e.g., at least one cyber, one technology, and two business unit representatives) must be present for binding decisions, preventing single-function dominance
Charter is formally approved by the Board or a Board-level committee and carries a version history showing it has been reviewed at least annually
Reporting obligations are explicit, including frequency and format of board updates and the threshold definition for 'material deviation' triggering board escalation
commonGaps: Charter exists but was never formally approved by the Board or a Board committee, leaving its authority ambiguous under examination
Membership clauses allow business unit representation by Head-of-Department level staff rather than senior management, undermining the cross-functional senior management requirement
The term 'material deviation' is undefined, making it impossible to demonstrate consistent application of the escalation obligation in sub-clause (c)
Charter has not been reviewed or updated since initial adoption, failing to reflect subsequent organisational restructuring or changes in technology risk profile
Reporting cadence to the board is absent or expressed vaguely (e.g., 'as required'), rather than specifying a minimum frequency aligned with board meeting cycles
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Company Secretarial records for the formally approved charter document
GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) governance module where committee charters are maintained
SharePoint or intranet governance repository under Technology Risk or Corporate Governance section
Compliance or Internal Audit document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Committee Membership Register and Attendance Records
evidenceItems.description: A living register of current committee members by name, title, and business unit, supplemented by meeting attendance logs, demonstrating that the committee is cross-functional, senior in composition, and actively convening as required by Clause 8.7.
whatGoodLooksLike: Membership register lists each member with their formal title (confirming senior management grade), functional area (cyber, technology, or specific business unit), date of appointment, and date of any substitution with rationale

Attendance records for every meeting show quorum was achieved in accordance with the charter, with no pattern of recurring absences from any single function that would suggest a function is effectively unrepresented
Where a permanent member was absent, the record documents whether a formally authorised alternate attended, and whether any decisions requiring that function's input were deferred
The register reflects actual organisational structure — business units represented match those identified as major in the institution's own operating model documentation
Attendance data can be reconciled against the meeting minutes, confirming that members recorded as present signed or were named in the minutes
commonGaps: Attendance records show that one or more functions (commonly cyber) consistently send working-level staff rather than the designated senior management member, with no formal alternate mechanism
The membership register has not been updated following senior management changes, so named members no longer hold the roles that qualified them for membership
Business units represented on the committee do not include all units the institution's own organisational chart would classify as major (e.g., retail banking or wealth management absent)
Quorum was not achieved at several meetings where decisions were nonetheless recorded, creating a validity risk for those decisions
No documented process exists for maintaining the register, so membership drift occurs without formal approval or record
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Company Secretarial function for appointment records
HR system (e.g., Workday, SAP SuccessFactors) for role title verification of named members
Meeting management platform or calendar system for attendance logs
GRC platform governance module for committee membership tracking
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export; PDF attendance registers signed or acknowledged per meeting

evidenceItems.name: Committee Meeting Minutes and Decisions Log
evidenceItems.description: Formal minutes for every committee meeting capturing agenda items, substantive discussions, decisions made, and action items assigned, providing the primary evidence that the committee is exercising active oversight of technology plans and operations as required by sub-clause (a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes are structured, dated, and reference the specific agenda items, demonstrating a deliberate rather than ad hoc meeting agenda that includes standing items aligned to sub-clauses (a), (b), and (c)

Discussion sections reflect genuine deliberation — not mere noting of reports — with recorded questions, dissenting views, or requests for further information that show members are exercising independent judgment
Each decision is recorded with sufficient context to understand what was approved, who proposed it, and on what basis, enabling retrospective audit of the committee's reasoning
Action items are assigned to named individuals with due dates, and subsequent minutes include a standing agenda item tracking closure of prior actions
Minutes are formally confirmed (signed or acknowledged) at the following meeting, establishing a clear chain of authenticated records
commonGaps: Minutes are template-thin — they record that presentations were 'noted' without capturing any substantive discussion or challenge, providing no evidence of genuine oversight

Decisions on technology plans or policies are recorded without the supporting rationale, making it impossible to demonstrate that the committee applied judgment rather than rubber-stamping executive proposals
Action items are recorded inconsistently or without ownership, and follow-up tracking is absent, so the committee's effectiveness in driving implementation cannot be demonstrated
Minutes are finalised and signed weeks after the meeting, undermining their contemporaneous reliability as evidence
Gap exists between meeting frequency prescribed in the charter and actual meeting cadence evidenced by minutes, with no documented explanation for missed meetings
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Technology Governance Office for final signed minutes
SharePoint committee workspace or dedicated governance portal where draft and final minutes are stored
GRC platform action tracking module for cross-referencing decision and action item closure
Email records from committee secretary as corroborating evidence of distribution
evidenceItems.format: PDF (final signed) with DOCX working drafts retained; action log as Spreadsheet or GRC export

evidenceItems.name: Strategic Technology Plan with Committee Oversight Evidence
evidenceItems.description: The institution's current strategic technology plan together with records demonstrating that the cross-functional committee oversaw its formulation and is actively monitoring implementation, satisfying the specific obligation in sub-clause (a).
whatGoodLooksLike: The strategic technology plan document itself is version-controlled and carries a formal approval record showing it was reviewed and endorsed by the committee before submission to the board

Committee minutes contain references to specific strategic technology plan agenda items at multiple points in the planning cycle, showing the committee's role in formulation rather than only final sign-off
Implementation dashboards or progress reports are presented to the committee at a defined cadence, with minutes recording the committee's review, challenge, and any direction given

Associated technology policies and procedures are listed in an inventory that is maintained by or reported to the committee, demonstrating the oversight scope extends to policies and procedures as required by sub-clause (a)
Where strategic technology plan milestones were missed or materially revised, the committee's deliberation on root causes and remediation is documented
commonGaps: The strategic technology plan was formulated by the technology function alone and brought to the committee only for information after the fact, with no evidence of committee oversight during formulation
Implementation reporting to the committee is infrequent or ad hoc, with no standing agenda item, meaning the committee cannot demonstrate effective oversight of implementation

Technology policies and procedures are managed entirely within the technology function and are not formally within the committee's oversight scope, leaving sub-clause (a)'s reference to 'associated policies and procedures' unaddressed
The strategic technology plan presented to the committee differs in material respects from the version approved by the board, with no committee record explaining the delta
Progress reporting uses RAG status without underlying KPIs, making it impossible for the committee (or an examiner) to verify the basis for status assessments
suggestedSources: Technology Strategy or Planning function for the current strategic technology plan document
GRC platform or project management tool (e.g., Jira, MS Project, ServiceNow PPM) for implementation tracking data
Policy management system for the inventory of technology policies and procedures
Board papers archive where the committee-approved plan was subsequently submitted to the board
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for the plan; Spreadsheet or system export for implementation dashboard; PDF for committee approval records

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Update Reports
evidenceItems.description: The formal reports or board paper packages through which the cross-functional committee discharges its obligation under sub-clause (b) to provide timely updates to the board on key technology matters, together with evidence of actual board consideration.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board papers on technology matters are presented at a regular, defined cadence (e.g., every Board meeting or every quarter) and are attributed to the committee rather than to management ad hoc, establishing the committee's role as the primary conduit

Reports cover both strategic technology matters (plan progress, investment decisions) and operational risk matters (significant incidents, emerging threats, control weaknesses), demonstrating breadth of oversight
The definition of 'key technology matters' is documented in the charter or a standing operating procedure, ensuring consistent and objective judgments about what warrants escalation
Board minutes or resolution records confirm the reports were received, discussed, and that any board directions were recorded and fed back to the committee
A timeliness standard is met — for example, significant technology incidents are reported to the board within a defined period after occurrence, not only at the next scheduled board meeting
commonGaps: Technology updates to the board are provided by the CTO or CIO directly, with the committee's role in preparing or endorsing the report absent from the paper's provenance, leaving sub-clause (b) unattributed
Reports are heavily formatted summaries that lack sufficient detail for the board to exercise meaningful oversight — key metrics, incident summaries, or risk trends are absent
No documented threshold or definition exists for 'key technology matters', making the selection of topics for board reporting appear arbitrary under examination
Board papers on technology are presented irregularly, with gaps of more than one board cycle, undermining the 'timely' requirement of sub-clause (b)
Board minutes do not record any substantive discussion of technology reports, suggesting they are presented for noting only, raising questions about effective board oversight
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat for board paper packages and board minutes
Technology Governance Office for the committee-authored or committee-endorsed report drafts
GRC platform for the technology risk and incident data that feeds into board reports
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) for incident data used in reporting
evidenceItems.format: PDF board paper packages; PDF board minutes as corroborating evidence

evidenceItems.name: Policy Deviation Approval Records and Risk Assessments
evidenceItems.description: The end-to-end records for each instance where the committee approved a deviation from a technology-related policy, including the deviation request, the risk assessment, the committee's decision, and (for material deviations) the board escalation record — directly evidencing compliance with sub-clause (c).
whatGoodLooksLike: A deviation request form or workflow captures the specific policy requirement being deviated from, the business or operational justification, the proposed compensating controls, and the risk assessment conducted before submission to the committee

Risk assessments accompanying deviation requests are substantive — they identify specific risks arising from the deviation, quantify or qualify impact and likelihood, and document the adequacy of compensating controls — rather than being a checkbox exercise

Committee minutes record the deliberation on each deviation request, including any conditions attached to approval, time-limits on the deviation, and the requirement for a closure plan to return to policy compliance
A materiality threshold for board escalation is defined, applied consistently, and documented; each deviation record includes an explicit materiality determination with rationale
A deviation register is maintained and reviewed by the committee at a defined cadence, showing open and closed deviations, with ageing of unresolved deviations tracked and escalated
commonGaps: Deviations are approved by the technology function or individual risk owners without routing through the committee, meaning sub-clause (c)'s committee approval requirement is structurally bypassed

Risk assessments accompanying deviation requests are superficial — they state that risk is 'acceptable' without articulating the specific risks, their magnitude, or the adequacy of compensating controls
No materiality threshold is defined, so determinations of which deviations require board escalation are subjective and inconsistent, and there is no audit trail of materiality judgments

Material deviations are summarised in aggregate in periodic board reports rather than reported individually with sufficient detail for the board to exercise oversight, diluting sub-clause (c)'s reporting obligation
Open deviations are not tracked to closure; deviations approved as temporary persist indefinitely with no committee review, demonstrating that the approval process is not effectively managed
suggestedSources: GRC platform policy exception/deviation workflow (e.g., Archer exception management module, ServiceNow Policy and Compliance Management)
Risk function for risk assessment templates and completed assessments
Committee secretariat for deviation-specific agenda items and decision records in minutes
Board Secretariat for board papers where material deviations were escalated
Policy management system for the deviation register and open item tracking
evidenceItems.format: GRC system export or PDF for deviation requests and risk assessments; PDF committee minutes as decision evidence; Spreadsheet deviation register

auditTips: Before the examination, trace at least three recent policy deviations end-to-end from the original request through the risk assessment, committee approval, and (where applicable) board escalation — BNM examiners frequently request this walkthrough to test whether sub-clause (c) operates in practice, not just on paper.

Prepare a one-page committee composition map showing each member's name, formal title, functional area, and grade classification against the institution's senior management definition; examiners will cross-reference this against the HR system or organisation chart to verify that 'senior management' is not being interpreted loosely to include middle management.

Conduct a pre-examination reconciliation between the committee's meeting minutes and the board papers on technology matters, confirming that every key technology matter identified in committee minutes has a corresponding board update within an appropriate timeframe — unexplained gaps are a common finding under sub-clause (b).

Review the strategic technology plan's version history and committee paper record together: if the plan was substantially revised after committee endorsement but before board approval (or vice versa), ensure the committee's involvement in the revision is documented; examiners look for evidence that the committee was not bypassed mid-process.

Prepare a summary deviation register that is examiner-ready — listing every deviation approved in the past 24 months, the approving committee meeting date, whether it was classified as material, whether it was escalated to the board, and its current status (open/closed) — and verify the register is consistent with the actual minutes and board papers to avoid contradictions during the examination.

Test the 'timely' requirement of sub-clause (b) empirically: identify the three most significant technology events (incidents, strategic decisions, or control failures) in the past 12 months and map the date of occurrence to the date the board received a report; if any gap exceeds your institution's defined timeliness standard or spans more than one board meeting cycle, address the gap and document the remediation before examination.

Ensure the committee charter's definition of 'material deviation' is aligned with the institution's broader materiality framework used for other regulatory reporting obligations — inconsistency between materiality thresholds across different governance documents is a credibility risk that examiners may exploit to question the robustness of the institution's governance framework.

9.1.title: TRMF Integration with Enterprise Risk Management
9.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will test whether the TRMF is genuinely embedded within the institution's ERM rather than existing as a parallel or siloed framework. The primary red flag is a TRMF document that references ERM in name only — examiners will trace whether technology risks actually flow into enterprise-level risk reporting, board risk appetite statements, and consolidated risk registers. Examiners will interview the CRO, CIO, and technology risk owners to assess whether ERM and TRMF governance cycles are synchronised, including common escalation thresholds, shared committee structures, and integrated risk taxonomies. They will also verify that the Board and senior management receive consolidated risk reporting that meaningfully aggregates technology risk alongside credit, market, and operational risk. A TRMF that cannot demonstrate a live, bidirectional linkage to the ERM — evidenced through committee minutes, risk reporting, and policy cross-references — will be assessed as non-compliant regardless of how comprehensive the standalone TRMF document may be.

evidenceItems.name: TRMF Policy Document with ERM Integration Provisions
evidenceItems.description: The formal, board-approved Technology Risk Management Framework document that explicitly defines how the TRMF operates as a component of the institution's ERM. This document must articulate the linkages between technology risk governance and the overarching enterprise risk governance structure, including shared definitions, escalation paths, and reporting lines.
whatGoodLooksLike: The TRMF document contains a dedicated section explicitly referencing the ERM framework by name, describing how TRMF governance hierarchies map to ERM committee structures
Technology risk appetite thresholds within the TRMF are directly derived from and cross-referenced to the Board-approved enterprise risk appetite statement
The document defines a common risk taxonomy that aligns technology risk categories with the institution's enterprise risk classification scheme
Escalation and reporting obligations in the TRMF explicitly reference enterprise-level risk governance forums (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Group Risk Committee) as upstream recipients
The document version history shows periodic review aligned with ERM framework review cycles, demonstrating that both frameworks evolve in concert
commonGaps: TRMF document references ERM only in a generic opening statement with no substantive linkage to governance structures, reporting lines, or risk appetite
Technology risk appetite limits in the TRMF are set independently without traceability to the enterprise risk appetite statement approved by the Board
The TRMF uses a technology-specific risk taxonomy that does not map to the institution's enterprise risk taxonomy, creating aggregation blind spots
TRMF review cycle is not synchronised with the ERM review cycle, resulting in misaligned policies that examiners can easily identify through version date comparisons
The TRMF document lacks a governance map or diagram showing how technology risk governance bodies connect to enterprise risk governance bodies
suggestedSources: Enterprise Risk Management / Group Risk function document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, Archer, ServiceNow GRC) policy module
Company Secretariat for board-approved policy version with approval signatures
Internal Audit shared drive for the most recently reviewed version
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — formal policy document with version control header, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: Integrated Enterprise Risk Register Showing Technology Risk Aggregation
evidenceItems.description: The enterprise-level consolidated risk register maintained by the ERM function that includes technology risks sourced from the TRMF process. This register demonstrates that technology risks identified and assessed under the TRMF are not siloed but are elevated into and visible within the institution's enterprise-wide risk inventory.
whatGoodLooksLike: The enterprise risk register contains a clearly labelled technology risk category or domain, with individual risk entries traceable back to TRMF-identified risks in the technology risk register

Each technology risk entry in the enterprise register shows the risk owner, inherent rating, control effectiveness, and residual rating using the same methodology applied across all enterprise risk categories
The register demonstrates a regular update cadence (at minimum quarterly) with timestamps showing that technology risk entries are refreshed in line with TRMF review cycles

Material technology risks (e.g., cyber, third-party IT, operational resilience) appear in the enterprise register's top risk heat map or watchlist, showing genuine aggregation rather than selective inclusion
The register includes a cross-reference or hyperlink to the detailed TRMF technology risk register, establishing a traceable parent-child relationship between enterprise and technology risk records
commonGaps: Enterprise risk register contains a technology risk section that is sparsely populated or contains only generic placeholder entries that do not reflect actual TRMF assessments
Technology risks in the enterprise register are maintained by the IT department independently rather than being formally sourced from and reconciled with the TRMF process
Risk ratings in the enterprise register for technology risks differ from those in the TRMF technology risk register with no documented rationale for the discrepancy
The enterprise register is updated annually while the TRMF risk register is updated more frequently, creating a stale picture of technology risk at the enterprise level
High-residual technology risks identified in the TRMF process are absent from the enterprise register's escalated or top risk listings, indicating a breakdown in the aggregation workflow
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk register module (Archer, MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, or equivalent)
Group Risk / Enterprise Risk Management function — consolidated risk register files
Risk Management Information System (RMIS) if the institution operates one
Internal Audit working papers from the most recent ERM or technology risk audit
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (PDF/XLSX) — must include risk ID, category, owner, rating, and last-reviewed date

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Risk Committee Minutes Evidencing Technology Risk Reporting
evidenceItems.description: Minutes from the Board Risk Committee (BRC), Board Audit and Risk Committee (BARC), Management Risk Committee (MRC), or equivalent governance forums that demonstrate technology risks are reported to and deliberated at enterprise governance levels. These minutes show that technology risk information from the TRMF feeds into the highest levels of risk oversight in the institution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes from at least four consecutive quarterly meetings contain a standing agenda item for technology risk, with substantive discussion of TRMF-sourced metrics, key risk indicators, or emerging technology threats

Minutes record specific decisions, challenge questions, or directives from board or senior management members regarding technology risks, demonstrating active oversight rather than passive receipt of reports
Technology risk dashboard or TRMF status report is listed as a tabled paper in the minutes, with an appended copy of the paper enabling examiners to verify the quality of reporting

Minutes reflect escalation of specific technology risk items from the management-level technology risk committee upward to the board risk committee, demonstrating the escalation pathway functions in practice
Minutes are signed, dated, and formally adopted at the subsequent meeting, establishing their authenticity as governance records
commonGaps: Technology risk is addressed only as a sub-item within the operational risk or internal audit agenda items rather than as a standalone reporting line, obscuring its materiality
Minutes record only that a technology risk report was 'received and noted' with no evidence of board or management engagement, challenge, or decision-making
Technology risk reporting to governance forums occurs ad hoc or only when incidents arise, rather than on a regular scheduled basis embedded in the committee calendar
The technology risk reports tabled at governance forums are sourced from the IT department's own reporting rather than from the TRMF process, breaking the ERM integration chain
Minutes do not reference specific risk thresholds, key risk indicators, or appetite limits from the TRMF, making it impossible to verify that governance oversight is informed by the framework
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — board and committee minute books
Board portal or board pack distribution system (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardPad)
Group Risk function — management risk committee records
Chief Risk Officer's office — governance reporting archive
evidenceItems.format: PDF — signed minutes with appended tabled papers; board portal export acceptable

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Risk Appetite Statement with Technology Risk Thresholds
evidenceItems.description: The Board-approved enterprise risk appetite statement (RAS) that includes explicit technology risk appetite metrics or thresholds, demonstrating that technology risk tolerance is defined at the enterprise level as part of the ERM framework and cascaded into the TRMF rather than existing independently within the technology function.
whatGoodLooksLike: The enterprise RAS contains a dedicated technology or operational technology risk appetite section with quantitative and qualitative thresholds (e.g., maximum acceptable system downtime, cyber incident frequency tolerance, third-party IT concentration limits)
Technology risk appetite thresholds in the enterprise RAS are directly referenced or reproduced in the TRMF, with an explicit statement that TRMF limits are derived from the enterprise RAS
The RAS was approved by the Board and is dated within the last 12 months or shows a formal annual review confirmation, demonstrating that technology appetite remains current
The enterprise RAS includes a monitoring mechanism that attributes responsibility for technology risk appetite reporting to a named function (e.g., CIO, CRO, Technology Risk Committee)
Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) used in TRMF monitoring can be mapped back to the appetite thresholds in the enterprise RAS, showing coherent metric design across both frameworks
commonGaps: The enterprise RAS addresses credit, market, and operational risk appetite but omits technology risk or addresses it only through a generic operational risk residual category
Technology risk appetite thresholds exist within the TRMF but were set by the technology risk function independently without board-level endorsement through the enterprise RAS
The enterprise RAS contains technology risk appetite statements that are so broad or qualitative (e.g., 'low tolerance for technology failures') as to be unmeasurable and unenforceable
The RAS has not been updated to reflect new technology risk exposures (e.g., cloud adoption, AI deployment) that have occurred since its last formal review
There is no documented process for cascading enterprise RAS technology thresholds into TRMF-level operational limits, leaving the connection implicit rather than auditable
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — board-approved RAS with resolution reference
Group Risk / ERM function — risk appetite framework documentation
Chief Risk Officer's office — RAS monitoring and breach reporting records
GRC platform — risk appetite module linked to risk register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — board-approved document with resolution number, approval date, and CRO/Board Chair signature

evidenceItems.name: TRMF–ERM Integration Mapping or Governance Architecture Document
evidenceItems.description: A structured document — typically a governance framework map, integration charter, or architecture diagram — that explicitly illustrates how the TRMF and ERM interact at the process, reporting, and governance levels. This evidence demonstrates that the institution has consciously designed the integration rather than claiming it incidentally.
whatGoodLooksLike: The document includes a visual governance hierarchy or process flow diagram showing how TRMF processes (risk identification, assessment, monitoring, reporting) connect to corresponding ERM processes at defined integration points

Integration points are described with sufficient specificity to identify the responsible parties, the data or output exchanged, and the frequency of exchange (e.g., 'TRMF risk register entries escalated to enterprise register quarterly by Technology Risk Manager')
The document identifies shared governance forums that serve both TRMF and ERM purposes (e.g., a Management Risk Committee that receives both technology and enterprise risk reports on a common agenda)
The mapping document is formally owned and maintained by a named function (e.g., Group Risk or Technology Risk) and shows a version history confirming it is actively maintained
The document references both the ERM framework and the TRMF by their current approved versions, confirming that the integration mapping is current and applies to the live frameworks
commonGaps: No standalone integration mapping document exists — the institution relies on verbal assertions that integration occurs without documented evidence of how the linkage is structured
The governance map shows committee structures but does not describe the information flows or outputs that pass between TRMF and ERM governance layers
The mapping document was produced specifically in anticipation of a BNM examination and lacks version history or operational artefacts (e.g., meeting records) confirming it reflects actual practice

The document describes integration at a high level but cannot be reconciled with actual practice — for example, it states quarterly aggregation but risk registers show no evidence of updates at that cadence
The mapping covers reporting integration only and does not address integration of risk appetite, taxonomy, methodology, or incident escalation processes
suggestedSources: Group Risk / ERM function — framework governance documentation
Technology Risk function — TRMF supporting documentation
Enterprise Architecture or GRC team — governance framework libraries on SharePoint or Confluence
Internal Audit — framework review working papers that may contain independently produced mapping assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or Visio/PowerPoint diagram exported to PDF — must include version number, owner, and approval date

evidenceItems.name: Periodic ERM Review Reports Incorporating TRMF Outputs
evidenceItems.description: Formal periodic risk reports (quarterly, semi-annual, or annual) produced by the ERM or Group Risk function that consolidate technology risk information sourced from the TRMF alongside other enterprise risk categories. These reports demonstrate in practice that the TRMF is an active input into enterprise-level risk visibility and decision-making.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports contain a technology risk section that references TRMF-sourced data including risk register status, KRI performance, control effectiveness ratings, and open risk treatment actions
The technology risk section is presented on a consistent basis across multiple reporting periods, evidencing a systematic and embedded reporting routine rather than ad hoc inclusion

Reports show trend analysis of technology risk over time (e.g., movement in residual risk ratings, KRI trends), demonstrating that TRMF outputs are used to inform enterprise-level risk trajectory analysis

Material changes in technology risk posture reported in the TRMF (e.g., emergence of a new critical vulnerability, a significant third-party IT failure) are reflected in the enterprise risk report in the same or the immediately subsequent reporting period
Reports are produced and distributed by the ERM or Group Risk function (not the IT or Technology Risk function alone), confirming that technology risk information is owned at the enterprise level
commonGaps: Enterprise risk reports contain a boilerplate technology risk paragraph that does not change meaningfully between periods, suggesting that TRMF outputs are not actually being reviewed and incorporated
Technology risk data in enterprise reports is sourced from IT department self-reporting rather than the formal TRMF process, bypassing the framework and undermining its integrative purpose
Reports are produced but are not formally tabled or referenced in governance committee meetings, meaning the integration exists on paper but not in governance practice

The technology risk section of enterprise reports is limited to cyber and information security, omitting other technology risk categories (e.g., IT operational risk, third-party technology risk, change and project risk) that should be covered by the TRMF

There is a systematic lag between TRMF risk assessment updates and their appearance in enterprise risk reports, indicating a broken or manually dependent data flow that creates a reliability risk for oversight
suggestedSources: Group Risk / ERM function — periodic risk reporting archive
