Chief Risk Officer's office — management and board reporting library
Board portal — historical board and committee pack archive
GRC platform — automated report generation module if applicable
evidenceItems.format: PDF — formally issued report with distribution record; board pack attachment is acceptable as corroborating evidence

auditTips: Before the examination, perform a side-by-side reconciliation of the TRMF document and the ERM framework document to identify every point where one references the other — examiners will do this, and any reference that appears in one document but cannot be located in the other will be flagged as a gap in integration

Prepare a one-page integration summary matrix for the examiner team that maps each key TRMF process (risk identification, assessment, monitoring, reporting, appetite-setting) to its corresponding ERM counterpart, including the responsible owner and frequency — this proactively demonstrates that integration is designed rather than incidental

Pull the last four quarters of Board Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee minutes and tag every instance where technology risk from the TRMF is discussed, deciding, or actioned — if you cannot find substantive entries in at least three of the four quarters, treat this as a red flag requiring remediation before the examination

Cross-check that the technology risk appetite thresholds currently in use within the TRMF appear verbatim or with explicit traceability in the enterprise risk appetite statement — any threshold in the TRMF that cannot be traced to a board-approved enterprise document is a compliance exposure under this clause

Interview the CRO and CIO jointly prior to the examination to confirm they can articulate how the TRMF integrates into the ERM without referencing documents — examiners will conduct these interviews separately, and inconsistent answers between the CRO and CIO on governance structure or reporting flows are a significant examination red flag

Verify that the enterprise risk register and the TRMF technology risk register are reconcilable — run a comparison to confirm that every risk in the TRMF register rated 'high' or above appears in the enterprise register, and document any exceptions with a rationale; unexplained omissions signal to examiners that the aggregation process is not functioning

Ensure that the TRMF and ERM framework documents share a common review schedule and that the most recent versions of both were reviewed in the same cycle — version date mismatches of more than six months between the two framework documents will prompt examiners to question whether integration is genuinely maintained or was a one-time design decision that has since diverged

9.2.title: TRMF Minimum Requirements
9.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the TRMF is a living, operationalised document rather than a shelf policy — they will cross-reference the framework's stated responsibilities against actual governance minutes, escalation records, and risk register updates to verify that roles are genuinely exercised. A key red flag is a TRMF that defines technology risk narrowly, omitting emerging technology risks such as AI, cloud, or API dependencies, which examiners will probe using Appendix 9 as a benchmark. Examiners pay particular attention to whether information asset classification is comprehensive and consistently applied across all systems including third-party-hosted assets, and whether the institution can demonstrate scenario analysis outputs have materially informed BCP/DR posture. The completeness of incident management procedures — specifically the link between detection, response timelines, and customer impact reporting — is a recurring examination theme, with examiners expecting evidence of post-incident reviews that feed back into the risk framework.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Management Framework (TRMF) Document
evidenceItems.description: The master TRMF policy document approved by the Board or Board-level Risk Committee, incorporating all minimum elements specified under Clause 9.2(a)–(k). This document establishes the institution's authoritative definition of technology risk, governance structure, risk appetite, and the methodological basis for all downstream technology risk activities.
whatGoodLooksLike: The document explicitly defines 'technology risk' in a manner consistent with BNM's definition and includes sub-categories covering cybersecurity, IT operational, data integrity, and emerging technology risks
All eleven elements of Clause 9.2(a)–(k) are addressed in clearly labelled sections with cross-references to supporting policies, procedures, and tools
Version history shows regular Board or Board Risk Committee approval, with the most recent review dated within the past 12 months and change rationale documented
The TRMF explicitly references Appendix 9 of RMiT and includes provisions for assessing and governing emerging technologies such as AI, cloud services, and open APIs
The document contains a clearly mapped RACI matrix or equivalent showing technology risk responsibilities from the Board down to operational technology teams and across the three lines of defence
commonGaps: The TRMF exists as a standalone policy but lacks integration with the institution's broader Enterprise Risk Management framework, creating inconsistent risk appetite definitions across domains
Emerging technology risks (e.g., generative AI, cloud-native deployments) are not addressed, leaving a visible gap against Appendix 9 obligations
The document has not been updated following material changes such as new cloud migrations, acquisition of digital subsidiaries, or significant third-party onboarding
Responsibilities are described at a high level without clear ownership at function or role level, making accountability unenforceable during an incident or audit
Scenario analysis and incident management are referenced conceptually but not substantiated by links to specific procedures, rendering those sections non-operational
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) — Policy Management module
SharePoint or enterprise document management system under the Risk & Compliance or IT Governance library
Board Secretariat records for approval minutes and resolution extracts
Chief Risk Officer or Chief Information Security Officer office document repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata; Board approval resolution as separate PDF

evidenceItems.name: Information Asset and System Risk Classification Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive inventory of all information assets and technology systems, each classified by criticality level (e.g., Critical, High, Medium, Low) in accordance with Clause 9.2(d). The register underpins risk prioritisation decisions, controls allocation, and recovery planning, and must cover both internally hosted and third-party-managed assets.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every system and information asset in scope — including cloud-hosted, vendor-managed, and subsidiary systems — is listed with a documented criticality rating and the business function it supports
Classification methodology is defined in the TRMF and consistently applied, with evidence of a formal classification review cycle (at minimum annually or upon material change)
Critical systems are clearly flagged and linked to BCP/DR plans, RTO/RPO targets, and relevant third-party contracts
The register captures interdependencies between assets, including upstream and downstream data flows and integration points with third-party service providers
Change history within the register shows assets have been added, reclassified, or retired in response to technology changes, acquisitions, or decommissioning events
commonGaps: The register covers core banking systems but omits shadow IT, departmental applications, or recently onboarded SaaS platforms, leaving material gaps in the asset universe
Criticality ratings are self-assessed by application owners without independent validation or consistent scoring criteria, resulting in systematic under-classification
The register is maintained as a static spreadsheet with no audit trail, making it impossible to demonstrate that classifications were current at a point in time during an examination
Third-party hosted assets are excluded from the classification exercise on the basis that they are 'vendor-managed', contrary to RMiT expectations
The register does not link asset criticality to risk controls, creating a disconnect between classification outcomes and actual control deployment
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management (ITAM) system (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Freshservice, Device42)
GRC platform — Asset Registry or Information Asset Register module
IT Architecture team's application portfolio records
Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform for externally hosted assets
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (Excel/CSV); PDF report for formal review submissions

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Register with Assessment Methodology Evidence
evidenceItems.description: The institution's active technology risk register, demonstrating risk identification (including emerging technology risks per Clause 9.2(c)), measurement, and assessment in accordance with Clause 9.2(e). Supporting artefacts must include the risk scoring methodology, inherent and residual risk ratings, control effectiveness assessments, and evidence that the register is kept current under Clause 9.2(h).
whatGoodLooksLike: Risks are identified across all technology domains including cybersecurity, IT operations, data management, third-party dependencies, and emerging technology, with Appendix 9 risk categories explicitly addressed
Each risk entry contains inherent risk rating, applicable controls with effectiveness ratings, and resulting residual risk, demonstrating a rigorous measurement methodology consistent with the TRMF
The register is updated at a defined frequency (e.g., quarterly) and upon trigger events, with timestamps and owner sign-off visible in the audit trail
Risk assessment methodologies (qualitative, quantitative, or hybrid) are documented and applied consistently, with calibration evidence such as loss event data or industry benchmarks where used
Risks approaching or breaching risk appetite thresholds are flagged and linked to management actions, escalation records, and committee reporting
commonGaps: Emerging technology risks such as AI model risk, open banking API exposure, or cloud concentration risk are absent from the register despite the institution operating these technologies
Risk ratings are not supported by a documented methodology — assessors apply subjective scores without a calibrated likelihood/impact scale, making assessments incomparable across periods
The register is refreshed annually but shows no evidence of interim updates despite material incidents, system changes, or new vendor onboarding occurring between cycles
Control effectiveness is assumed rather than tested — controls are rated 'effective' without reference to audit findings, penetration test results, or monitoring data
Risk owner fields are populated with team names rather than named individuals, diffusing accountability and making risk ownership unenforceable
suggestedSources: GRC platform — Risk Register module (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow IRM, LogicGate)
Technology Risk team's working files and risk assessment workbooks
Internal Audit reports referencing risk register findings
Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee reporting packs
evidenceItems.format: GRC system export or Excel workbook; methodology documented in PDF/DOCX policy annex

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Governance Structure and Committee Reporting Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing the governance and reporting arrangements required under Clause 9.2(b), including committee terms of reference, meeting minutes, escalation records, and management information (MI) reports that demonstrate technology risk is actively overseen at multiple organisational levels and across the three lines of defence.
whatGoodLooksLike: Terms of reference for technology risk governance bodies (e.g., IT Risk Committee, Board Risk Committee) explicitly reference technology risk oversight responsibilities and quorum requirements, with membership spanning business, technology, risk, and audit functions
Committee minutes from the past 12 months demonstrate substantive discussion of technology risk items, with decisions, actions, and escalation trails documented and followed up
Technology risk MI reports presented to senior management and Board include risk profile dashboards, KRI trends, material risk exceptions, and control failure notifications
Escalation records show that risks breaching appetite thresholds have been reported upward in a timely manner, with evidence of Board or senior management response

The three-lines-of-defence model is operationalised — first-line risk and control self-assessments, second-line independent challenge records, and third-line internal audit coverage of technology risk are all evidenced
commonGaps: Committee minutes record that technology risk was 'discussed' but contain no substantive content, action items, or decision rationale, suggesting the governance process is perfunctory
MI reports focus exclusively on IT operations metrics (e.g., uptime, patch rates) without translating these into risk-based language or linking them to risk appetite statements

The escalation framework exists on paper but there are no records of escalations having occurred, even during periods with known incidents or elevated risk, indicating the framework is not used in practice
Technology risk governance sits exclusively within the IT function with no evidence of second-line Risk Management or Compliance challenge, undermining the three-lines model
Board-level technology risk reporting is consolidated into a broader enterprise risk report with insufficient granularity for examiners to assess Board oversight of technology-specific risks
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — committee minutes and resolutions archive
Risk Management function — Board and management reporting packs
GRC platform — risk reporting and dashboard module
IT Governance Office — committee charter and terms of reference documents
evidenceItems.format: PDF (committee minutes, MI reports, ToR); dashboard exports from GRC or BI platforms

evidenceItems.name: Critical Third-Party and Interdependency Mapping Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation satisfying Clause 9.2(i) identifying critical third-party service providers, their connected parties, and the interdependencies that support delivery of critical technology functions. This includes the third-party risk inventory, dependency maps, contractual risk provisions, and evidence that these interdependencies are actively monitored within the TRMF.
whatGoodLooksLike: A maintained register of all technology service providers classified by criticality, with clear rationale for criticality designation and linkage to the information asset classification register

Dependency maps or architecture diagrams illustrate the flow of critical functions through third-party infrastructure, identifying single points of failure and concentration risk (e.g., multiple critical services hosted with a single cloud provider)
Contracts with critical third parties include provisions for audit rights, incident notification obligations, BCP/DR requirements, and data security standards consistent with RMiT expectations
Connected parties of critical third-party providers (i.e., their own subcontractors and cloud infrastructure providers) are identified and the risk of cascading failure is assessed
Periodic third-party assurance reviews (e.g., SOC 2 reports, on-site assessments, questionnaire-based reviews) are on file for all critical providers and findings are tracked to closure
commonGaps: The third-party register captures vendor name and contract value but does not classify criticality, making it impossible to prioritise oversight or satisfy the 'critical third party' identification requirement
Connected parties and sub-processors of critical vendors are not identified — the institution's third-party risk exposure therefore extends beyond what is being managed
Dependency mapping exists at a conceptual level in architecture documents but has not been translated into operational risk records that can be used during incident response or scenario analysis
Outsourcing and cloud vendor contracts predate RMiT and lack required provisions for audit rights, incident notification, and BCP testing participation
Third-party assurance reviews are conducted at contract initiation only, with no evidence of ongoing monitoring commensurate with the criticality of services provided
suggestedSources: Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform (e.g., Prevalent, OneTrust, Aravo, or GRC module)
Procurement and Vendor Management — contract repository and vendor registry
IT Architecture team — system integration and dependency diagrams
Legal and Compliance — outsourcing register required under BNM guidelines on outsourcing
evidenceItems.format: Excel/GRC system export for register; PDF for architecture/dependency maps and assurance reports

evidenceItems.name: Scenario Analysis and Incident Management Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Artefacts demonstrating compliance with Clause 9.2(j) and (k) — specifically, completed scenario analysis exercises testing the institution's capacity to recover critical systems under severe conditions, and the incident management policy with supporting records showing it is operationalised. This evidence set must show both forward-looking preparedness (scenario analysis) and retrospective operational evidence (incident records and post-incident reviews).
whatGoodLooksLike: Scenario analysis exercises are documented with clearly defined severe-but-plausible scenarios (e.g., ransomware encrypting core banking systems, cloud provider regional outage, critical vendor insolvency), including assumptions, testing outcomes, and identified capability gaps with remediation plans

Scenario analysis outcomes are formally fed back into the TRMF risk register and BCP/DR plans, with evidence of the feedback loop (e.g., updated risk ratings, revised RTO targets, or enhanced controls following exercise findings)
The incident management policy specifies roles, escalation thresholds, communication protocols, and target resolution timelines aligned with the criticality classification of affected systems

Incident records from the past 12 months demonstrate the policy is followed — each material incident has a corresponding ticket with classification, response timeline, customer impact assessment, and post-incident review

Post-incident reviews for significant events show root cause analysis, lessons learned, and verified closure of remediation actions, with evidence that patterns are identified and fed into risk management processes
commonGaps: Scenario analysis is conflated with standard DR testing — exercises test technical recovery procedures for known failure modes but do not explore severe, multi-factor stress scenarios or systemic shocks as envisaged by Clause 9.2(j)
Scenario analysis outputs are not formally documented or are treated as internal working papers rather than governance artefacts, leaving no evidence trail for examination purposes

The incident management policy defines process steps but does not specify quantified response timelines tied to system criticality classifications, making it impossible to assess whether response performance meets stated standards
Post-incident reviews are completed for major incidents only, with no systematic review process for medium-severity events that in aggregate may reveal systemic control weaknesses

There is no demonstrable link between incident management lessons learned and updates to the TRMF, risk register, or controls library — the incident process and risk management process operate as silos
suggestedSources: IT Service Management (ITSM) platform — incident management module (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM, Jira Service Management)
Business Continuity and DR team — exercise reports, scenario planning workbooks
CISO office — incident response playbooks and post-incident review records
Risk Management function — records of risk register updates following incident or exercise findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scenario analysis reports, incident management policy, post-incident reviews); ITSM system exports for incident logs

auditTips: Map every sub-clause of 9.2(a)–(k) to a specific section of the TRMF document before the examination — prepare a compliance matrix that an examiner can use to navigate directly to the relevant policy provision for each obligation, as examiners will test whether the framework is internally coherent and complete rather than simply asserting coverage

Prepare a version-controlled TRMF change log showing all amendments made since the last BNM examination, with the business or regulatory trigger for each change documented — this demonstrates the framework is actively maintained rather than static, and pre-empts examiner questions about whether recent technology changes (e.g., cloud adoption, new digital products) have been reflected in the framework

Reconcile the information asset classification register against the critical third-party register and the scenario analysis scope before the examination — any critical system that appears in the asset register but is missing from third-party oversight records or scenario analysis scope will be identified as a gap by examiners, so ensure these registers are consistent and mutually referencing

Pull a sample of ten to fifteen incidents from the ITSM platform for the past twelve months and verify that each has a complete record covering classification, response timeline against SLA, customer impact statement, and post-incident review — examiners routinely request incident samples and trace them end-to-end, so incomplete records on any sampled incident will raise questions about the robustness of the entire incident management process

Validate that emerging technology risks are explicitly represented in the technology risk register by cross-referencing Appendix 9 of RMiT against currently deployed or piloted technologies — if the institution uses AI, open banking APIs, or multi-cloud infrastructure but these do not appear as named risk categories in the register, address this gap before examination as it is a consistently cited finding

Ensure that governance evidence (committee minutes, escalation records, MI reports) forms a traceable chain from operational risk data to Board oversight — examiners will follow a specific risk from the register through to a committee discussion and up to Board-level reporting; breaks in this chain (e.g., a material risk with no committee discussion) indicate that governance arrangements are not genuinely operationalised

Conduct a dry-run scenario analysis walkthrough with senior management two to three months before the expected examination window, using a ransomware or cloud provider failure scenario, and document the output formally — this simultaneously satisfies the Clause 9.2(j) evidencing requirement and ensures that senior management can speak credibly to the institution's preparedness posture when questioned by examiners

9.3.title: Independent Technology Risk Management Function
9.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the technology risk management function is genuinely independent — structurally separate from IT operations and business lines — and not merely a paper construct. Examiners scrutinise organisation charts, reporting lines, and terms of reference to verify the function has direct access to the board or board-level risk committee without business-line interference. A key red flag is where the technology risk function sits within the CIO or IT division hierarchy, indicating a lack of independence. Examiners also test whether the function actively implements both the TRMF and CRF (not just owns the documents), by reviewing evidence of its involvement in critical technology project governance and escalation records. Finally, examiners verify that independent views were actually provided on third-party assessments — not simply rubber-stamped — by looking for documented dissent, qualifications, or conditions attached to opinions furnished to the board and senior management.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Management Function Charter / Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: The formal mandate document establishing the independent technology risk management function, defining its scope, authority, reporting lines, independence safeguards, and responsibilities for implementing the TRMF and CRF. This document demonstrates that the function exists by design and has been formally authorised by the board.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter explicitly states the function is independent of IT operations and business lines, with a reporting line to the CRO or board risk committee — not the CIO
Responsibilities for implementing the TRMF and CRF are enumerated verbatim and mapped to specific roles within the function
Charter grants the function unfettered escalation rights to the board and senior management, with defined escalation timelines (e.g., within 24 hours for critical issues)
Document is board-approved, carries a version history, and has been reviewed within the last 12 months
Scope explicitly covers enterprise-wide technology risk, including third-party and outsourced arrangements, not limited to internal IT systems
commonGaps: Charter exists but reporting line shows the function head reports to the CIO or a technology business unit, undermining independence
Responsibilities reference the TRMF in general terms without specifying how the CRF is implemented, leaving cyber resilience obligations unclear
Document has not been reviewed or updated since the institution's last major structural reorganisation, making it factually inaccurate
Escalation thresholds and timelines are absent, leaving the function without a defined trigger for board-level escalation
Charter was approved by management rather than the board, insufficient for a board-mandated governance structure under RMiT
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board-approved governance documents and committee charters
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — policy and charter repository
SharePoint/Intranet policy library — HR-published organisational mandates
Company Secretary records — board resolutions approving the charter
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with board approval signature page and version control table

evidenceItems.name: Organisation Chart and Reporting Line Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Current and historical organisation charts demonstrating the structural independence of the technology risk management function from IT operations and business divisions. This evidence corroborates the charter by showing actual reporting relationships and headcount dedicated to the function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organisation chart shows the technology risk function head reporting directly to the CRO or board risk committee, with no dotted-line dependency on the CIO
Function has dedicated full-time staff with technology risk specialisations (not shared resources borrowed from IT operations)
Chart is dated, version-controlled, and reflects the current state at the time of examination
Job descriptions for key roles within the function explicitly exclude operational IT responsibilities, reinforcing independence
Historical charts are retained to demonstrate continuity of independence over time, not just at point-in-time examination
commonGaps: Function staff appear on both the technology risk and IT operations charts, indicating shared or dual-hatted roles that compromise independence
Organisation chart is undated or reflects a prior structure, making it impossible to confirm current arrangements
Head of technology risk function has a primary reporting line to the CIO with only an advisory line to the CRO, which BNM treats as insufficient independence
Function is staffed by a single individual with no backfill or succession, calling into question enterprise-wide coverage capability
Headcount is entirely contractor-based with no permanent employees, raising concerns about institutional knowledge and accountability
suggestedSources: HR information system (e.g., Workday, SAP HCM) — live organisational structure exports
Board Secretariat — approved governance structure documentation
Compliance or risk management department — internally published risk function org charts
Job description repository on HR portal or SharePoint
evidenceItems.format: PDF org chart export with effective date; supplemented by HR system screenshot

evidenceItems.name: TRMF and CRF Implementation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that the technology risk management function actively implements the Technology Risk Management Framework and Cyber Resilience Framework — not merely maintains them as documents. This includes implementation plans, control assessment outputs, gap remediation tracking, and periodic review records attributed to the function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual TRMF and CRF implementation roadmap owned by the technology risk function, with milestones, owners, and completion status tracked in a GRC platform
Control self-assessment or independent control testing outputs show the function assessed controls across all business units enterprise-wide, not limited to selected segments
Remediation action logs demonstrate the function tracked issues to closure and escalated overdue items to senior management
CRF implementation evidence includes cyber resilience testing schedules (BCP/DR, red team exercises) initiated or overseen by the function
Board or risk committee reports from the function reference specific TRMF/CRF implementation progress with quantitative metrics (e.g., percentage of controls assessed, number of gaps closed)
commonGaps: TRMF and CRF documents exist but there is no implementation plan or activity log showing the risk function drove their operationalisation
Control assessments were performed by IT operations staff rather than the independent risk function, negating independence
CRF implementation evidence is limited to the IT disaster recovery team with no involvement or oversight documented from the technology risk function
Gap remediation tracking is maintained in an unstructured spreadsheet with no version control, making it impossible to verify completeness or timeline
Enterprise-wide scope is not demonstrated — coverage evidence only references headquarters or the core banking system, omitting subsidiaries and third-party-hosted environments
suggestedSources: GRC platform (RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) — control assessment and remediation modules
Technology risk function's SharePoint site — TRMF/CRF implementation workplans
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — risk-related change and remediation tickets raised by the function
Board and risk committee meeting packs — technology risk management reports
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export; PDF board reports; PDF/DOCX implementation plans

evidenceItems.name: Critical Technology Project Advisory and Escalation Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the technology risk management function was involved in advising on critical technology projects and that issues with potential impact on the institution's risk tolerance were escalated in a timely manner. This covers project risk assessments, risk opinions, and escalation memos attributed to the function.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined list of critical technology projects exists and the technology risk function's involvement is recorded in project governance documentation (e.g., project initiation documents, steering committee minutes)

Risk opinions or advisory notes issued by the function for each critical project are dated, addressed to the relevant steering committee or senior management, and reference specific risk tolerance thresholds
Escalation records show issues were raised within the institution's defined escalation timeline, with evidence of board or senior management acknowledgement and response
Post-implementation reviews of critical projects include a technology risk function sign-off or independent assessment of residual risk
A register of critical project escalations is maintained, showing issue description, date raised, recipient, resolution, and closure date
commonGaps: Technology risk function was not engaged until late in the project lifecycle (e.g., pre-launch only), with no evidence of early advisory involvement at project initiation or design phases
Escalation records exist but are informal email threads with no structured log, making it impossible to verify timeliness or completeness
The institution cannot define what constitutes a 'critical technology project' — no criteria or threshold documented, meaning scope of advisory obligation is undefined
Risk opinions are generic and do not reference the institution's stated risk tolerance or appetite levels, making them non-evidential for RMiT purposes
Escalations were directed to the CIO rather than senior management or the board, contradicting the independence requirement and failing the timeliness test for board-level awareness
suggestedSources: Project Management Office (PMO) system (e.g., MS Project Online, Jira, ServiceNow PPM) — project risk logs and steering committee records
GRC platform — technology risk project advisory register
Board and risk committee secretariat — escalation memos and board paper acknowledgements
Technology risk function's document management repository — advisory opinions and sign-off records
evidenceItems.format: PDF advisory memos, meeting minutes, escalation register (spreadsheet or GRC export)

evidenceItems.name: Independent Views on Third-Party Assessments
evidenceItems.description: Documented opinions and independent views provided by the technology risk management function to the board and senior management on third-party technology risk assessments, including vendor audits, outsourcing risk reviews, and external penetration test reports. This demonstrates the function fulfils its advisory role on third-party matters independently of business units sponsoring those third parties.
whatGoodLooksLike: A register of third-party assessments reviewed by the technology risk function exists, cross-referenced to corresponding independent opinion documents submitted to the board or senior management

Independent views are clearly attributed to the technology risk function (not the business unit owning the vendor relationship) and contain substantive analysis, qualifications, or conditions — not just endorsements
Where the function disagreed with a third-party assessment finding, the dissent is documented and the resolution is traceable in subsequent board papers or management decisions
Board or risk committee papers include a standing agenda item for technology risk function views on material third-party assessments, with minutes reflecting deliberation
Independent views are provided within a defined SLA (e.g., within 30 days of receipt of third-party assessment), with evidence of timely delivery
commonGaps: Third-party assessment reports are presented to the board by the business unit relationship owner with no independent view from the technology risk function attached
The function's review is limited to a brief cover email rather than a substantive opinion document, insufficient to demonstrate genuine independent analysis
Independent views are only provided for outsourced critical services and do not cover cloud service providers, fintech partnerships, or other technology third parties
No SLA or process exists for the technology risk function to receive third-party assessment reports, meaning reviews are ad hoc and potentially untimely
Board minutes do not record any deliberation on the technology risk function's independent views, suggesting they were submitted but not considered, undermining the purpose of the requirement
suggestedSources: Vendor management system or third-party risk management platform (e.g., Prevalent, OneTrust, RSA Archer TPRM) — assessment reports and review records
Board Secretariat — board and risk committee papers and minutes containing technology risk function opinions
GRC platform — third-party risk register with linked independent review records
Technology risk function's document repository — opinion letters and advisory notes on third-party assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF opinion letters/memos; board paper extracts (PDF); third-party risk register export

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Reporting by the Technology Risk Function
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports submitted directly by the independent technology risk management function to the board or board risk committee and to senior management. These reports demonstrate the function's direct access to governance bodies, the substance of its independent views, and its enterprise-wide oversight role — distinct from operational IT reporting.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are clearly authored and submitted by the technology risk function (not the CIO or IT department), with the function head's signature or attribution confirmed in board minutes
Content covers enterprise-wide technology risk exposure, TRMF/CRF implementation status, critical project risks, and third-party risk — not merely IT performance metrics
Reporting frequency aligns with the board-approved reporting calendar (typically quarterly minimum) with no unexplained gaps in the reporting history
Reports include risk tolerance breach notifications or near-breach alerts where applicable, demonstrating active monitoring against approved thresholds
Board minutes confirm that reports were received, discussed, and actioned — not merely noted — evidencing genuine board engagement with technology risk function outputs
commonGaps: Technology risk reporting to the board is embedded within the CIO's IT operations report, preventing identification of independent risk function outputs
Reports focus on IT service performance metrics (uptime, incidents) rather than technology risk exposure and control effectiveness, conflating operational and risk reporting
Reporting frequency is annual or ad hoc, insufficient to demonstrate continuous enterprise-wide oversight and inconsistent with BNM expectations for material risk functions
Board minutes record technology risk reports as 'noted' with no evidence of discussion, challenge, or action, suggesting the function's independence has no practical effect on governance
Risk tolerance breach reporting is absent from historical reports, either because thresholds have never been tested (unlikely) or because the function lacks a mechanism to detect and report breaches
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board risk committee and full board meeting packs and minutes
GRC platform — automated risk reporting module with distribution logs
Technology risk function's document management system — report drafts, final versions, and submission records
Senior management risk committee secretariat — management-level technology risk reports and attendance records
evidenceItems.format: PDF board papers and meeting minutes; supplemented by report distribution logs from GRC or document management platform

auditTips: Map each sub-obligation in clause 9.3(a), (b), and (c) to a dedicated evidence item before the examination — examiners will test each limb separately, and a gap in any one (e.g., no documented independent views on third-party assessments under 9.3(c)) will result in a finding even if the function otherwise exists and is active

Conduct a structural independence test internally before BNM arrives: trace the reporting line of the technology risk function head from their position to the board and confirm there is no IT operations or business-line node in that chain — any dependency will be immediately visible to examiners reviewing the org chart

Pull the last 12 months of board and risk committee papers and verify that technology risk function reports appear as standalone agenda items with the function head as named author — if reports are embedded in CIO packs or attributed to IT management, restructure reporting before the examination

Cross-reference the critical technology project register against the technology risk function's advisory log — for every project meeting the institution's criticality threshold, confirm a dated advisory note or risk opinion exists; gaps in coverage are a common examination finding under clause 9.3(b)

Prepare a third-party assessment matrix listing all material vendor and outsourcing arrangements, the date of the most recent assessment, the date the technology risk function reviewed it, and the date an independent view was submitted to the board — examiners will sample from this matrix and request the underlying documents on the spot

Test the escalation process end-to-end with a documented walkthrough: identify a historical instance where a technology risk issue approached or breached the institution's risk tolerance, and compile the complete escalation trail from detection by the function through to board acknowledgement, including timestamps — examiners assess timeliness, not just existence of escalation

Ensure the TRMF and CRF both explicitly designate the independent technology risk management function as the implementation owner, and that implementation activity logs in the GRC platform are attributed to that function rather than IT operations — if ownership is ambiguous in the framework documents, update them before the examination and document the rationale for the change

9.4.title: Designation of Chief Information Security Officer
9.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the CISO appointment is substantive rather than nominal — they probe for genuine independence from day-to-day IT operations, which is frequently violated when the CISO also holds a CTO or Head of IT role, even under a different title. Examiners will scrutinise the reporting line structure to confirm the CISO has a direct or functionally independent path to the board, not merely through the CIO. Red flags include CISOs who cannot produce evidence of board-level engagements, who lack external professional certifications, or whose resource allocation (headcount and budget) is controlled entirely by the technology operations function. Examiners will cross-reference the job description, organisational chart, board papers, and the CISO's curriculum vitae to triangulate whether the authority, independence, and competency obligations under sub-clauses (a) through (d) are simultaneously met in practice.

evidenceItems.name: CISO Formal Appointment and Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: The formal instrument designating the CISO, including the board or senior management resolution approving the appointment, the signed terms of reference or mandate, and the job description. This demonstrates that a CISO has been formally designated with explicit accountability for the technology risk management function as required by the opening obligation of clause 9.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or Board Risk Committee resolution explicitly naming the incumbent and designating the role as responsible for the technology risk management function, dated and minuted
Terms of reference clearly articulating the CISO's authority to set policy, direct remediation activities, and escalate risks to the board independently of the CIO or COO

Job description that enumerates all sub-clause (c) competency domains: emerging and core technology skills, audit and governance, risk management, strategic planning, IT/cybersecurity program execution, and third-party risk management
Appointment letter or contract addendum confirming the CISO's reporting line (e.g., to CEO or Board Risk Committee) and distinguishing this from the technology operations reporting line
Annual review or re-affirmation of mandate signed by the CEO or board chairperson, demonstrating the designation is actively maintained rather than a one-time exercise
commonGaps: Appointment exists only in an HR system record with no accompanying board resolution, making it impossible to demonstrate the board's awareness or endorsement of the designation
Terms of reference are silent on authority — the CISO can advise but cannot independently escalate to the board or halt high-risk technology changes
Job description conflates the CISO role with the CIO or Head of IT role, failing to satisfy the independence requirement in sub-clause (a)
Appointment documents were never updated following an organisational restructure, so the current reporting line in practice differs from what the formal document states
No distinction is made between the technology risk management function (CISO) and the technology operations function (CIO/CTO), leaving examiners unable to determine independence
suggestedSources: Board secretariat — board and Board Risk Committee meeting minutes and resolutions
Human Resources Information System (HRIS) for employment contract and role classification
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) under role governance or policy management module
SharePoint/Confluence governance document repository under 'CISO Charter' or 'Technology Risk Governance'
Legal/Compliance department for executed appointment instruments
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board resolution, signed appointment letter, signed terms of reference); DOCX (job description)

evidenceItems.name: Organisational Chart Demonstrating CISO Independence
evidenceItems.description: Current and point-in-time organisational charts showing the CISO's position within the institution's governance hierarchy, specifically demonstrating structural independence from day-to-day technology operations as required by sub-clause (a). This evidence is critical for BNM examiners to assess whether the independence obligation is structurally embedded.
whatGoodLooksLike: Org chart clearly shows the CISO reporting to the CEO, CRO, or directly to the Board Risk Committee — not to the CIO, CTO, or any Head of Technology Operations

The technology operations function (e.g., IT Infrastructure, Application Development, IT Service Desk) sits in a separate branch of the hierarchy, confirming no overlap with the CISO's direct span of control
Chart is dated, version-controlled, and approved by the CEO or CHRO, demonstrating it is an authoritative record rather than an informal diagram
Where a matrix or dual-reporting structure exists, a supplementary note or policy addendum explicitly describes how independence is preserved and conflicts of interest are managed
Historical org charts are retained for the full 7-year period, allowing examiners to verify independence was maintained through prior reporting periods and organisational changes
commonGaps: The org chart shows the CISO as a direct report of the CIO, which structurally undermines independence and is one of the most common findings in BNM technology risk examinations
The CISO carries dual responsibilities — acting as both CISO and Head of IT Operations — with no compensating controls documented to manage the conflict
Org charts are undated or exist in multiple inconsistent versions across departments, preventing examiners from establishing an authoritative picture
The CISO's team (security analysts, SOC) is shown as part of the IT Operations tower rather than under a discrete security governance function
No org chart is available for prior periods, making it impossible to demonstrate continuity of independence across the examination window
suggestedSources: HR department — HRIS org chart module (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors, Workday)
Corporate Affairs or Board Secretariat for board-approved governance structures
GRC platform governance module for approved governance framework diagrams
SharePoint document library under 'Corporate Governance' or 'Organisational Structure'
Annual Report appendices (for publicly filed versions as corroborating evidence)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (approved and signed org chart); Visio/PowerPoint as supplementary working copy

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Technology Risk Reporting Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the CISO has, in practice, apprised the board and senior management of current and emerging technology risks as required by sub-clause (b). This includes board papers, management committee minutes, risk dashboards, and CISO briefing materials submitted to governance bodies, evidencing that the reporting obligation is discharged on a regular and substantive basis.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or Board papers containing a dedicated CISO or technology risk section, authored or presented by the CISO, covering both current threat landscape and emerging risks (e.g., AI-driven threats, cloud concentration risk) with at least quarterly frequency
Minutes of Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body recording the CISO's attendance, the topics presented, and the board's deliberations or decisions arising from the technology risk update

Management Risk Committee or EXCO papers showing the CISO's regular (at minimum monthly) technology risk reporting to senior management, including key risk indicators, incident summaries, and remediation status
Evidence that emerging risks are distinguished from operational risk updates — e.g., dedicated sections on threat intelligence, regulatory horizon scanning, or emerging technology risk assessments

CISO's written attestation or escalation records showing instances where the CISO independently raised a risk concern to the board, demonstrating that the reporting is substantive and not merely ceremonial
commonGaps: Technology risk updates to the board are presented by the CIO rather than the CISO, meaning the independence of the risk reporting line is not demonstrated in practice

Board papers contain only a brief operational IT update rather than a structured risk appraisal covering both current and emerging technology risks, failing the 'emerging risks' element of sub-clause (b)
CISO reporting occurs ad hoc rather than on a scheduled cycle, with gaps of six months or more between board-level risk briefings
Minutes do not record the CISO's name or the substance of the technology risk discussion, making it impossible to evidence that the obligation was discharged

Risk reporting is limited to cyber incidents already occurred, with no forward-looking emerging risk content (e.g., no discussion of AI threats, supply chain attacks, or regulatory technology changes)
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — Board and Board Risk Committee meeting packs and minutes
Risk Management department — Management Risk Committee and EXCO meeting papers
GRC platform — technology risk dashboard and automated risk reporting module
SharePoint 'Board Papers' library or equivalent document management system
CISO office — presentation archives and risk briefing decks
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers, minutes, CISO presentations); Excel/Dashboard export (risk indicators)

evidenceItems.name: CISO Qualifications, Certifications, and Competency Profile
evidenceItems.description: Documentary evidence of the CISO's professional certifications, educational qualifications, work experience, and continuing professional development, directly demonstrating compliance with sub-clauses (c) and (d). This establishes that the designated individual possesses the requisite technical skills, governance and risk expertise, and appropriate certifications mandated by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Curriculum vitae on file showing demonstrable experience across all sub-clause (c) domains: core and emerging technology knowledge, audit and governance, risk management, strategic IT/cybersecurity program management, and third-party risk management — with specific roles and durations cited
Copies of current, valid professional certifications appropriate to the CISO role, such as CISSP, CISM, CISA, CRISC, or equivalent, with expiry dates confirmed as valid and renewal records maintained

Evidence of continuing professional development (CPD) activities within the examination period — training records, conference attendance, professional body membership, or CPD logs — demonstrating skills currency in emerging technologies
Internal competency assessment or skills gap analysis conducted at appointment and periodically thereafter, with a documented plan to address any identified gaps

Where the CISO's background is stronger in governance than technical domains (or vice versa), documented compensating measures such as a dedicated technical deputy or access to specialist advisors, with evidence those arrangements are active
commonGaps: The CISO holds certifications that have lapsed or are not renewed, which is a direct violation of the 'appropriately certified' requirement in sub-clause (d) and a straightforward finding for examiners

Certifications held are limited to a single domain (e.g., only CISA for audit) without coverage of cybersecurity, risk management, or technology strategy, leaving sub-clause (c)'s multi-domain competency requirement unaddressed
No evidence of CPD or skills currency in emerging technologies — the CISO's qualifications predate significant technology shifts (e.g., cloud, AI) without supplementary training records
CV on file is outdated, covering a prior role rather than the current CISO position, so the experience profile cannot be validated against the current mandate

Third-party risk management experience is absent from both the CV and certifications, which is specifically enumerated in sub-clause (c) and increasingly scrutinised by BNM given outsourcing and cloud adoption trends
suggestedSources: HR department — personnel file including CV, qualification certificates, and professional development records
HRIS training and certification module (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors Learning, Workday Learning)
Professional certification bodies directly (ISACA, (ISC)², SANS) for verification of certification status
CISO office — CPD log and training attendance records
GRC platform — skills registry or governance role competency module
evidenceItems.format: PDF (certificates, CV, CPD log, competency assessment report); HRIS system export

evidenceItems.name: CISO Resource Allocation and Budget Authority Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution ensures the CISO has sufficient resources as required by the clause's opening obligation, including dedicated budget authority, headcount control over the information security function, and access to tools, systems, and external expertise. This evidences that resource sufficiency is structurally guaranteed rather than contingent on discretion of the technology operations function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Approved annual budget with a discrete cybersecurity or information security line item under the CISO's ownership, separate from the general IT operations budget, with sign-off from the CFO and board
Headcount plan for the security function approved by the CISO and ratified by senior management, with actual vs. planned headcount tracked and any vacancies escalated through a documented process
Evidence that the CISO has independent procurement authority for security tools, external consultants, and threat intelligence services up to a defined threshold, without requiring CIO co-approval

Resource adequacy review conducted at least annually, benchmarked against industry norms (e.g., percentage of IT budget allocated to security) or regulatory guidance, with findings reported to the Board Risk Committee
Documentation of instances where the CISO requested additional resources and the outcome — demonstrating that the authority to escalate resource insufficiency to the board is exercised in practice
commonGaps: The cybersecurity budget is a sub-line within the IT operations budget controlled by the CIO, meaning the CISO cannot independently allocate resources without the CIO's approval — directly undermining resource independence
No dedicated headcount budget exists; the CISO relies on shared IT staff whose allocation is prioritised by IT operations management rather than security requirements
Resource allocation is not documented in board or management papers, so examiners cannot verify that the board has assured itself of resource sufficiency as part of its governance oversight

The institution cannot produce a benchmark or adequacy assessment — resource levels are determined by historical precedent rather than a structured analysis of security risk and regulatory requirement

Security tool procurement requires joint approval from the CIO and CISO, with no documented escalation path when the two functions disagree, leaving resource decisions vulnerable to IT operations influence
suggestedSources: Finance department — approved annual budget documents and cybersecurity cost centre records
HRIS — headcount and workforce plan for the information security function
Procurement system (e.g., SAP Ariba, Coupa) — purchase orders and approval workflows for security-related expenditures
GRC platform — resource adequacy assessment or risk appetite reporting module
Board papers — annual budget approval minutes and any CISO resource escalation records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board-approved budget, resource adequacy report); Excel (budget tracker, headcount plan); Procurement system export

evidenceItems.name: CISO Independence Controls and Conflict of Interest Policy
evidenceItems.description: Policies, procedures, and operating records that institutionalise the CISO's independence from day-to-day technology operations as required by sub-clause (a), including conflict of interest declarations, role separation controls, and any compensating measures where organisational constraints exist. This demonstrates that independence is embedded in governance design, not merely asserted.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formally approved policy or governance framework that explicitly prohibits the CISO from holding concurrent operational technology responsibilities (e.g., infrastructure management, application deployment, IT service delivery) and defines permissible versus impermissible dual roles
Annual conflict of interest declaration signed by the CISO, reviewed by the CRO or board audit committee, confirming no undisclosed operational technology responsibilities during the reporting period

Documented process for the CISO to independently commission or receive internal audit and external assessment findings on technology controls, without routing these through the CIO or IT operations management

Evidence that the CISO has objected to, escalated, or placed conditions on technology decisions driven by operations (e.g., sign-off withheld on a high-risk system deployment), demonstrating that independence is exercised in practice and not merely structural
Where functional overlap is unavoidable (e.g., in smaller institutions), a board-approved exception document with time-bound compensating controls and a remediation timeline accepted by the board
commonGaps: No written policy addresses the separation of the CISO role from technology operations, leaving independence contingent on informal convention rather than enforceable governance rules

The CISO is also a member of the Change Advisory Board (CAB) or equivalent operational IT governance body in an approving (rather than advisory) capacity, blurring the line between oversight and operations
Conflict of interest declarations are not collected from the CISO, despite being standard practice for other C-suite and risk function roles, creating an evidentiary gap
Internal audit reports on technology controls are routed through the CIO before being presented to the CISO, undermining independent access to assurance information

The institution relies on an assertion of independence without documented evidence of the CISO ever exercising that independence (e.g., no record of escalation, objection, or conditional sign-off on technology decisions)
suggestedSources: Compliance and Risk Management department — technology risk governance policy and role separation controls
Board Secretariat — conflict of interest declaration register
Internal Audit department — technology audit reports and terms of engagement with the CISO
GRC platform — policy management module for CISO independence policy
Change management system (e.g., ServiceNow) — CAB membership and CISO participation records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (governance policy, conflict of interest declarations, exception approvals); System export (CAB participation records)

auditTips: Map every sub-clause obligation to a named evidence item before the examination: sub-clause (a) requires independence documentation (E2, E6); sub-clause (b) requires board reporting records (E3); sub-clause (c) requires a competency profile (E4); sub-clause (d) requires certification evidence (E4). Presenting evidence pre-indexed to clause obligations signals maturity to examiners and reduces the risk of items being overlooked under examination pressure.

Verify the CISO's reporting line in the org chart matches what is stated in the board resolution and the terms of reference — examiners routinely find inconsistencies between the formal appointment document (which says the CISO reports to the CEO) and the actual org chart (which shows a dotted line to the CIO). Reconcile these before the examination.

Confirm all professional certifications are current and not lapsed by pulling a certification verification report from each issuing body (ISACA, (ISC)², SANS) no more than 30 days before examination. Do not rely on the certificate document alone — examiners have been known to check online certification registries during fieldwork.

Review the most recent 12 months of board and management committee papers to confirm the CISO — not the CIO — is the named author or presenter of technology risk items. If the CIO has been presenting technology risk updates, prepare a transition plan and be ready to explain the remediation timeline, as this is a common finding that examiners will press on.

Conduct a resource adequacy self-assessment benchmarked against peers (e.g., cybersecurity budget as a percentage of IT spend, security FTE per RM billion of assets) before the examination. BNM examiners increasingly ask institutions to justify their security resource levels against risk exposure — having a documented, quantitative basis for resource decisions is more defensible than a qualitative assertion.

Stress-test the CISO's independence by reviewing the past 12 months of Change Advisory Board records, technology project sign-offs, and vendor onboarding approvals. If the CISO's name appears as an operational approver rather than an independent reviewer, this is an independence breach under sub-clause (a) that should be remediated before examination and disclosed proactively if remediation is incomplete.

Prepare a consolidated CISO profile document — a single pack containing the appointment resolution, org chart extract, terms of reference, CV, certification copies, CPD log, and last three board risk presentations — that can be handed to examiners on day one. Institutions that can produce this integrated pack quickly demonstrate control maturity; those that take days to assemble it create the impression of poor governance.

9.5.title: CISO Responsibilities for Information Protection
9.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the CISO role is substantive rather than nominal — they look beyond the existence of a title to verify that the individual actively formulates, owns, and drives policy under the TRMF and CRF, and is not merely a figurehead. Key red flags include CISO policies that are unsigned or clearly drafted by external consultants without internal tailoring, board and senior management reports that contain no meaningful security risk analysis, and evidence of compliance gaps that the CISO failed to escalate. Examiners will cross-reference Board Risk Committee minutes, CISO-authored policy documents, and management reporting packs to confirm the CISO maintains an active, informed view of the institution's evolving technology security risk profile in relation to its actual business operations. Particular scrutiny is applied to whether the CISO's advisory function meaningfully influences strategic decisions, or whether security concerns are systematically deprioritised without documented rationale.

evidenceItems.name: CISO Policy Ownership Register and Approved Policy Suite
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated register linking each information security and technology policy to the CISO as formal policy owner, accompanied by the approved policy documents themselves. Demonstrates that the CISO has fulfilled the obligation under clause 9.5(a) to formulate appropriate policies for the effective implementation of the TRMF and CRF.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy register explicitly names the CISO as owner for all TRMF- and CRF-related policies, with version history, approval dates, and next-review dates visible
Each policy document carries the CISO's signature or digital approval stamp alongside Board or senior management endorsement, demonstrating formal authorisation
Policies reference specific TRMF and CRF objectives and map directly to BNM RMiT requirements, showing deliberate alignment rather than generic boilerplate
Review cycle is documented (typically annual or triggered by material changes) and evidence of prior review rounds is retained, showing ongoing stewardship rather than a one-time exercise

Policy suite covers the full scope of information asset protection including data classification, access management, incident response, and third-party risk, with no material gaps relative to the institution's risk profile
commonGaps: Policy ownership is attributed to IT or Risk departments rather than the CISO, making it impossible to demonstrate CISO accountability under clause 9.5(a)
Policies reference TRMF or CRF in name only, with no substantive mapping to framework objectives or regulatory requirements, indicating copy-paste adoption without institutional tailoring
Approval records show rubber-stamp sign-off with no evidence of CISO review or challenge, undermining the credibility of ownership
Policy register exists but has not been updated following material changes in the institution's technology environment or new BNM issuances, leaving stale documents as the current reference
Policies covering CRF obligations are absent or incomplete, leaving a documented gap that examiners will treat as a direct clause 9.5(a) failure
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, or equivalent) — policy management module
SharePoint or institutional intranet policy library with version control
Board and senior management approval minutes referencing specific policy documents
CISO office document repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy documents; Spreadsheet or GRC-exported policy register

evidenceItems.name: TRMF and CRF Implementation Evidence Pack
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence that the CISO has operationalised both the Technology Risk Management Framework and the Cyber Resilience Framework within the institution, including implementation plans, gap assessments, and closure tracking. Validates the formulation obligation in clause 9.5(a) extends to effective implementation, not merely policy drafting.
whatGoodLooksLike: A structured implementation or programme plan exists for both TRMF and CRF, with milestones, owners, and status tracking attributable to CISO-led governance
Gap assessments against RMiT requirements are documented and dated, showing the CISO conducted or commissioned a formal baseline review prior to implementation
Remediation actions from gap assessments are tracked to closure with evidence artifacts (change records, audit reports, sign-off emails), demonstrating active enforcement rather than passive planning
Implementation progress is reported to senior management or Board Risk Committee at regular intervals, linking this evidence to the advisory obligation in clause 9.5(c)
Both frameworks are treated as living programmes with periodic re-assessment rather than point-in-time compliance exercises
commonGaps: Implementation is documented for TRMF but not for CRF, or vice versa, leaving a partial compliance posture that examiners will flag as a material gap

Gap assessments are undated or attributed to external consultants with no evidence of CISO review, making it unclear whether the CISO has genuine understanding of the institution's compliance position
Remediation tracking shows items marked as closed without supporting evidence artifacts, raising questions about the integrity of the closure process
Implementation plans exist but have not been updated following changes in the institution's technology landscape or new BNM guidance, indicating the programme has stalled
No linkage between TRMF/CRF implementation status and management reporting, severing the connection between policy formulation and the advisory obligation
suggestedSources: GRC platform — risk treatment and control implementation modules
Project management tools (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) tracking RMiT compliance workstreams
Board Risk Committee and IT Steering Committee papers and minutes
Internal audit working papers on TRMF/CRF compliance assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX implementation plans; Spreadsheet gap trackers; GRC system exports; Meeting minutes

evidenceItems.name: Compliance Enforcement Records and Exception Management Log
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating the CISO's active enforcement of information security policies, technology-related regulatory requirements, and framework obligations. This includes policy exception approvals, non-compliance findings, escalation records, and remediation tracking. Directly evidences clause 9.5(b) responsibilities.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal exception management process exists with the CISO as the designated approving authority for policy exceptions, and all approved exceptions are logged with risk justification and time-bound expiry
Non-compliance findings from internal audits, vulnerability assessments, or self-assessments are tracked in a central register with CISO-acknowledged action plans and closure evidence
Evidence of CISO escalation of persistent or high-severity non-compliance to senior management or the Board, demonstrating active enforcement rather than passive awareness
Regulatory correspondence (e.g., BNM directives, MAS equivalent) is tracked against internal compliance actions with the CISO named as accountable owner
Closed exceptions are reviewed at expiry and either renewed with fresh justification or remediated, showing the exception process does not become a permanent bypass mechanism
commonGaps: Exceptions are approved by IT management rather than the CISO, undermining the CISO's enforcement authority and creating accountability confusion under clause 9.5(b)
No central exception log exists; exceptions are managed ad hoc via email threads that are difficult to retrieve and present to examiners
Non-compliance findings are documented but show no evidence of CISO acknowledgement, escalation, or action ownership, suggesting the CISO is not actively enforcing compliance
Expired exceptions have not been reviewed or renewed, indicating exceptions are being used indefinitely without oversight

Regulatory compliance tracking is siloed within the Compliance or Legal function with no visible CISO involvement, contrary to clause 9.5(b)'s requirement for CISO enforcement of technology-related regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: GRC platform — exception management and risk treatment modules
ITSM tool (e.g., ServiceNow) — change and exception request workflows
Internal audit issue tracking system
CISO office correspondence and escalation records
Regulatory submissions register maintained by Compliance function
evidenceItems.format: GRC system exports; Spreadsheet exception log; PDF/DOCX audit reports and escalation memos

evidenceItems.name: CISO Management Reporting Pack — Technology Risk and Security Advisories
evidenceItems.description: A set of periodic reports and briefing materials prepared by or on behalf of the CISO for senior management and Board Risk Committee consumption, addressing technology risk exposure, security developments, and the institution's evolving technology security risk profile relative to its business and operations. Directly evidences clause 9.5(c) responsibilities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are produced on a regular cadence (at minimum quarterly) and addressed to named senior management recipients, with evidence of delivery and receipt (e.g., meeting minutes, read receipts, or tabled agenda items)
Content includes a forward-looking assessment of how changes in the institution's business or technology environment affect its security risk profile, not merely a retrospective summary of incidents
Key risk indicators (KRIs) and key performance indicators (KPIs) are tracked over time with trend commentary authored or reviewed by the CISO, demonstrating analytical depth
Reports include explicit CISO recommendations or advisory positions on strategic matters, with evidence that senior management has acknowledged or responded to those recommendations

Material developments — such as new threat intelligence, regulatory changes, or significant incidents — are reflected in reports with appropriate prominence, showing the CISO maintains a current and contextualised view of the risk landscape
commonGaps: Reports are produced by the IT Risk team with minimal CISO involvement, and examiners cannot establish that the CISO personally reviewed or endorsed the content before submission to senior management
Report content is backward-looking (incident counts, patch statistics) with no advisory commentary on emerging risks or strategic implications, failing to meet the clause 9.5(c) advisory obligation
Reports are prepared but no evidence exists that they were actually presented to or discussed by senior management, making the advisory function unverifiable
The institution's technology security risk profile is described in generic terms with no connection to specific business lines, products, or operational changes, indicating a superficial analysis
Ad hoc briefings on significant security events are verbal and undocumented, leaving no audit trail of the CISO's advisory function during material incidents
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee and Management Risk Committee meeting packs and minutes
CISO office reporting templates stored on SharePoint or equivalent
GRC platform — risk dashboard and reporting module
Email distribution records or board paper submission systems
Executive security briefing decks maintained by the CISO office
evidenceItems.format: PDF/PPTX board and management papers; Meeting minutes confirming tabling and discussion

evidenceItems.name: CISO Role Charter and Formal Appointment Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation establishing the CISO's appointment, organisational positioning, delegated authorities, and scope of responsibilities including those under clause 9.5. This is foundational evidence confirming the CISO role is structurally empowered to fulfil all three obligations — policy formulation, compliance enforcement, and senior management advisory — rather than being a nominal designation.
whatGoodLooksLike: A board-approved or senior management-approved CISO role charter explicitly enumerates responsibilities that map to clause 9.5(a), (b), and (c), with no ambiguity about scope
The CISO's formal appointment is evidenced by a board resolution, employment contract addendum, or equivalent, with a clear effective date and reporting line to the CEO or Board
Delegated authority matrix grants the CISO enforcement powers over technology and information security matters, including authority to escalate to the Board without interference from the CTO or CIO
The charter is reviewed and reaffirmed at least annually or upon material changes in the CISO's responsibilities or the institution's risk profile

Organisational chart clearly positions the CISO with appropriate independence from the IT function, consistent with BNM's expectation that security oversight is not subordinated to operational technology delivery
commonGaps: The CISO role charter describes responsibilities in generic terms (e.g., 'oversee security') without specifically addressing TRMF, CRF, or the advisory obligation to senior management as required by clause 9.5
The CISO reports into the CTO or CIO rather than directly to the CEO or Board, creating a structural conflict of interest that undermines independence and enforcement authority
No formal appointment documentation exists beyond a job description, leaving the CISO's authority unclear and difficult to substantiate in examination
The charter has not been reviewed following significant changes in the institution's technology environment, regulatory landscape, or organisational structure
Delegation of authority is insufficiently specific, leaving the CISO without clear power to enforce compliance or escalate non-compliance contrary to clause 9.5(b)
suggestedSources: Board secretariat — resolutions and governance documentation
Human Resources — employment records and role specifications
Corporate governance framework and organisational design documents
SharePoint governance library — CISO charter and delegation matrices
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX board resolution or appointment letter; Role charter document; Organisational chart

evidenceItems.name: Technology Security Risk Profile — Current and Historical Assessments
evidenceItems.description: Documented assessments of the institution's technology security risk profile, produced or commissioned by the CISO, showing how the profile evolves in relation to business and operational changes. This is core to the clause 9.5(c) obligation to advise senior management on 'developments in the financial institution's technology security risk profile in relation to its business and operations'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk profile assessments are conducted at least annually and following material changes in the institution's business (new products, acquisitions, technology migrations) with the CISO named as accountable owner
Each assessment explicitly links technology risk ratings to specific business lines, products, and operational processes, demonstrating contextualised rather than generic risk analysis
Trend data across multiple assessment periods is maintained, allowing examiners to verify that the CISO tracks the direction of travel in the institution's risk exposure over time

Threat intelligence inputs (including sector-specific intelligence from BNM JCERT, FS-ISAC, or equivalent) are visibly incorporated into the risk profile, showing the CISO maintains awareness of the external threat environment
Assessment outputs feed directly into the CISO's management reporting under 9.5-E4, creating a traceable chain from risk identification to senior management advisory
