commonGaps: Risk profile assessments are asset-centric (listing vulnerabilities and systems) without mapping risks to business impact or operational dependencies, failing the 'in relation to its business and operations' requirement of clause 9.5(c)
Assessments are conducted annually on a fixed schedule regardless of material business or technology changes, indicating a compliance checkbox approach rather than genuine risk management
No historical assessments are retained or retrievable, making it impossible to demonstrate how the CISO has tracked and responded to the institution's evolving risk profile over time

Threat intelligence is not incorporated into risk profile assessments, leaving the CISO's advisory function disconnected from the external threat environment relevant to Malaysian financial institutions
Risk profile outputs are not used to inform the CISO's management reporting, severing the link between risk assessment and the advisory obligation under clause 9.5(c)
suggestedSources: GRC platform — risk register and risk assessment modules
Threat intelligence platform (e.g., MISP, ThreatConnect) — institutional risk overlays
Vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable, Qualys) — risk-rated outputs reviewed by CISO
Business continuity and enterprise risk management documentation
Board and management risk committee papers referencing technology risk profile updates
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX risk assessment reports; GRC system exports; Spreadsheet risk registers with trend data

auditTips: Before examination, compile a single CISO accountability matrix that maps each clause 9.5 obligation — policy formulation (a), enforcement (b), and senior management advisory (c) — to specific named evidence artifacts with document references and locations. Examiners work through obligations methodically and this prevents scrambling for evidence under time pressure.

Validate that the CISO's name appears as author, owner, or approver on all key policy documents, exception approvals, and management reports — not merely as a recipient or secondary signatory. BNM examiners specifically test whether CISO accountability is nominal or substantive by tracing names across document trails.

Cross-reference Board Risk Committee and Management Risk Committee minutes against the dates of CISO-authored reports to confirm that reports were formally tabled and discussed, not merely distributed. Minutes should contain enough detail to demonstrate that senior management engaged with the CISO's advisory content, not just acknowledged receipt.

Review the CISO's reporting line on the current organisational chart and confirm it reflects appropriate independence from the CTO and CIO functions. If the CISO reports through the technology function, prepare documented rationale for this structure and evidence of compensating governance controls, as this will attract examiner scrutiny under clause 9.5(b)'s enforcement obligation.

Conduct a pre-examination dry run in which the compliance team requests from the CISO office every piece of evidence that would be needed to satisfy each clause 9.5 sub-obligation. Gaps identified during this rehearsal — missing exception logs, outdated policy reviews, undocumented verbal briefings — can be remediated before examination without the pressure of an active review.

Ensure the technology security risk profile assessments explicitly reference named business lines, products, and recent operational changes (e.g., cloud migrations, new digital channels, vendor onboarding). Assessments that read as generic IT risk inventories will be treated by examiners as failing the 'in relation to its business and operations' requirement of clause 9.5(c), even if technically comprehensive.

Prepare a consolidated evidence binder organised by clause 9.5(a), (b), and (c) sub-obligations, with a cover index, rather than presenting raw files from multiple systems. This signals to examiners that the institution has a mature understanding of its own compliance obligations and reduces examination duration by enabling examiners to locate specific artifacts without extended discovery sessions.

10.1.title: Technology Project Governance
10.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised governance arrangements that are genuinely proportionate to project risk and complexity — not merely whether a governance policy exists on paper. Examiners will trace specific technology projects through their life cycle to verify that oversight roles were actively exercised, that authority structures were respected in real decisions, and that risk assessments were conducted at meaningful junctures rather than as a one-time checkbox. Red flags include generic governance frameworks applied uniformly regardless of project risk tier, project committees with no documented evidence of deliberation or challenge, risk assessments completed only at project initiation, and steering committees whose membership or attendance records suggest passive participation. Examiners also probe whether escalation paths from project teams to senior management and the board are clearly defined and demonstrably used when risk thresholds are breached.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Project Governance Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: The overarching policy document that defines the institution's technology project governance framework, including risk-tiering methodology, mandatory governance structures by project tier, and the principles governing authority and reporting. This demonstrates the existence of a documented, board-approved governance regime commensurate with project risk and complexity.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines risk-tiering criteria (e.g., budget thresholds, regulatory impact, strategic significance, technical complexity) with distinct governance requirements mapped to each tier
Roles such as Project Sponsor, Project Manager, Steering Committee, and independent Project Assurance are clearly defined with non-overlapping accountabilities and explicit authority limits
Policy mandates escalation thresholds and describes the reporting chain from project team to EXCO and Board IT Committee with defined cadence
Document carries formal approval signatures from the Board or a delegated Board committee, with version history showing periodic review
Policy cross-references related frameworks including IT risk management, vendor management, and change management policies to prevent governance gaps at handoff points
commonGaps: Governance requirements are uniform across all projects with no risk-tiering, meaning a minor system patch and a core banking replacement carry identical governance overhead or, worse, insufficient oversight
Roles and responsibilities section is generic and aspirational with no mapping to specific named roles or organisational units within the institution
Policy has not been reviewed or updated in more than two years, rendering it silent on cloud-native, agile, or vendor-managed delivery models
Authority limits for project approvals are absent or inconsistent with the Delegation of Authority framework, creating ambiguity about who may approve scope or budget changes
Document exists but lacks evidence of Board or EXCO endorsement, undermining its standing as an enforceable governance requirement
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — policy register module
SharePoint intranet policy library under IT Governance or Technology Risk
Board secretariat records for evidence of Board IT Committee approval
IT Steering Committee or EXCO meeting minutes referencing policy endorsement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: Project Oversight Roles and Responsibilities Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured record — typically a RACI matrix or responsibility assignment document — that maps all required project governance roles to named individuals or positions for each active and recently completed technology project. This demonstrates that oversight responsibilities are operationally assigned and not merely stated in policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI matrix covers all governance touchpoints including initiation approval, stage-gate reviews, risk assessment sign-off, change control authority, and project closure, with named role-holders for each
Independent project assurance or quality assurance roles are assigned separately from project delivery roles, with documented scope of independence
Role assignments are updated at project phase transitions and any changes are version-controlled with rationale recorded
For high-risk or complex projects, the register shows involvement of second-line risk and compliance functions as reviewers or approvers at defined milestones
Register is maintained centrally in a project portfolio management tool and is accessible to governance bodies for real-time visibility
commonGaps: Project Sponsor role is listed but the individual named has not attended any steering committee meetings, indicating nominal rather than active oversight
RACI matrices exist only for large projects; smaller but operationally significant projects have no documented role assignments
Second-line functions (Technology Risk, Internal Audit) are absent from governance structures despite projects carrying material risk to operations or data
Role assignments have not been updated following staff changes, meaning former employees remain listed as accountable parties
Responsibility assignments conflate the Project Manager and Project Sponsor roles in the same individual, eliminating an essential layer of independent oversight
suggestedSources: Project Portfolio Management (PPM) tool (e.g., Microsoft Project Online, Jira Portfolio, Planview)
Project charter documents held in SharePoint or document management system
HR system for validation of role-holder employment status and reporting line
IT Steering Committee terms of reference and membership lists
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system-generated RACI export; PDF project charters with role sections

evidenceItems.name: IT Steering Committee and Project Governance Meeting Records
evidenceItems.description: Minutes, attendance records, and decision logs from governance bodies that provide project oversight — including IT Steering Committees, Project Steering Committees, and Board IT Committee briefings. This demonstrates that authority and reporting structures are actively exercised throughout the project life cycle, not merely established on paper.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes record substantive deliberation including questions raised, challenges made to project status or risk positions, and rationale for decisions — not merely motion-and-resolution summaries
Attendance records confirm that quorum requirements are consistently met and that sponsor-level accountable parties are present at key decision points
Escalation items from project teams are visibly tracked through governance meeting records, showing the reporting chain functioning as designed
Minutes reflect stage-gate decisions with explicit conditions or remediation actions attached to conditional approvals, and subsequent records show closure of those conditions
Meeting cadence aligns with project risk tier as required by policy — higher-risk projects show more frequent and senior governance engagement
commonGaps: Minutes are templated and formulaic with no evidence of substantive debate or challenge, suggesting rubber-stamp governance rather than active oversight
Attendance records show frequent absence of the designated Project Sponsor or senior risk representatives without documented delegation
Escalated risk or issue items appear in one set of minutes but are not tracked to resolution in subsequent records, indicating governance gaps
Steering committee meetings occur only at project initiation and closure, with no governance touchpoints during the delivery phase where risks materialise
Minutes are produced weeks after meetings, reducing their reliability as contemporaneous evidence of governance decisions
suggestedSources: Board secretariat system for Board IT Committee and EXCO records
SharePoint or Teams for project-level steering committee minutes
Outlook calendar records for meeting attendance corroboration
ITSM or GRC platform action tracking for escalation log evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF minutes with wet or digital signatures; attendance registers as PDF or spreadsheet

evidenceItems.name: Technology Project Risk Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented risk assessments conducted at defined stages throughout the technology project life cycle — including initiation, design, pre-implementation, post-implementation, and at significant change events. This directly evidences compliance with the clause obligation to perform risk assessments throughout the project life cycle, not as a one-time exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessments are structured documents that identify technology, operational, regulatory, and third-party risks specific to the project, with inherent and residual risk ratings and named risk owners
Evidence shows risk assessments were repeated at each major phase gate, with prior assessments updated to reflect changes in risk profile as the project progressed
Risk treatment plans are attached to each assessment with defined actions, owners, and target dates, and subsequent records show tracking of treatment completion
For high-risk projects, assessments include input or sign-off from second-line Technology Risk function, evidencing independent review rather than self-assessment only
Risk assessments are formally approved by the appropriate authority level as defined by project risk tier, with signatures or system approval records retained
commonGaps: A single risk assessment is completed at project initiation and never revisited, meaning risks that emerged during design or testing phases are unrecorded and unmanaged
Risk assessments use generic risk categories not calibrated to the specific technology, architecture, or regulatory context of the project
Residual risk ratings are recorded but no evidence exists that residual risks were formally accepted by an appropriately authorised individual
Risk assessments for vendor-delivered or third-party-managed projects omit third-party and supply chain risk dimensions
Assessments are completed by the project delivery team without any independent second-line review, compromising objectivity and thoroughness
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform risk register (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow Risk, MetricStream)
Project Management Office (PMO) SharePoint repository — project artefact folders
Technology Risk function records for independent risk review sign-offs
ITSM change management system for risk assessments linked to project change requests
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX risk register or assessment template with version history; GRC platform export

evidenceItems.name: Project Authority and Reporting Structure Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation establishing the hierarchical authority structure for technology projects — including delegation of authority matrices, project approval thresholds, and defined reporting lines from project teams through senior management to the Board. This demonstrates that authority structures are explicit, enforceable, and aligned with the institution's broader governance framework.
whatGoodLooksLike: Delegation of authority matrix specifies approval thresholds for project initiation, budget changes, scope changes, and early termination, mapped to role or seniority level with monetary and risk-significance criteria

Reporting structure diagram or narrative clearly shows the path from project team to PMO, to IT Steering Committee, to EXCO, and to Board IT Committee, with defined reporting frequency and content requirements at each level
Project dashboard or portfolio reporting templates demonstrate standardised upward reporting, including RAG status, key risks, issues, and milestone progress
Instances of authority being exercised — such as EXCO approval of a project exceeding budget threshold — are evidenced by meeting records or approval workflows in the PPM tool

Authority structure is consistent with the institution's broader Delegation of Authority framework endorsed by the Board, avoiding conflicts or gaps between project governance and enterprise governance
commonGaps: Authority thresholds for project approvals are not defined, meaning project managers or heads of IT approve projects of any size without escalation to EXCO or Board
Reporting structure exists in the governance policy but actual project status reports are sent to different recipients or at different frequencies without documented rationale
Board IT Committee receives project portfolio updates only annually, providing insufficient visibility for active oversight of material technology projects
Budget and scope change authorities are not aligned with the enterprise Delegation of Authority, creating a parallel — and potentially weaker — approval regime for technology projects
Reporting templates vary by project team, making portfolio-level reporting inconsistent and preventing meaningful aggregated risk visibility for senior governance bodies
suggestedSources: Corporate Secretariat for Delegation of Authority framework documents
PMO for project reporting templates and distribution lists
Board IT Committee terms of reference for reporting obligations
PPM tool for evidence of approval workflows and authority exercise
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX authority matrix; organisation chart; Board-approved terms of reference

evidenceItems.name: Project Portfolio Register with Risk Classification
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register of all technology projects — active, completed, and decommissioned — that includes risk classification, governance tier assignment, and current status. This demonstrates that the institution has implemented a systematic, portfolio-wide approach to project governance rather than applying oversight selectively or ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register captures all technology projects above a defined threshold, with fields for project name, sponsor, budget, risk classification, governance tier, stage, and key milestone dates
Risk classification is applied using a documented, consistent methodology and is reviewed at each stage gate to reflect changes in project complexity or risk
Register is maintained in real time within a PPM or GRC platform, with access controls ensuring only authorised parties can update risk classifications or governance tier assignments
Portfolio-level reports derived from the register are produced for IT Steering Committee and EXCO at defined intervals, enabling aggregate risk visibility
Register includes completed and cancelled projects with closure records, enabling trend analysis and post-implementation review tracking
commonGaps: Register is maintained in a spreadsheet with no access controls or version history, making the integrity of classification decisions unverifiable
Minor or business-unit-initiated technology projects are excluded from the register, creating blind spots in the institution's technology risk portfolio
Risk classifications are assigned at project initiation and never updated, meaning projects that escalated in complexity retain a low-risk classification
Register exists but is not formally presented to governance bodies — it is used only by the PMO for administrative tracking rather than as a governance tool
No evidence of portfolio-level risk aggregation, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate awareness of cumulative technology project risk exposure to BNM examiners
suggestedSources: PMO project portfolio management tool (e.g., Planview, Microsoft Project Online, Jira Portfolio)
Enterprise GRC platform for risk classification records
IT Steering Committee and EXCO board packs containing portfolio dashboards
Finance system for budget actuals cross-referenced against project register entries
evidenceItems.format: System-generated portfolio report (PDF/Excel export) with timestamp; GRC platform screenshot or export

auditTips: Before the examination, select two to three technology projects of varying risk tiers completed within the past 24 months and assemble a complete governance evidence pack for each — initiation approval, RACI, steering committee minutes, risk assessments at each phase, and closure records. Examiners will trace individual projects end-to-end and inconsistencies between the governance policy and actual project records are the most common finding.

Map every governance requirement stated in the policy (risk tiers, mandatory committees, escalation thresholds) to a concrete artefact that demonstrates it was applied to real projects. If the policy mandates a pre-implementation risk assessment for Tier 1 projects but no such record exists for a recent Tier 1 project, prepare a written explanation or remediate before the examination.

Cross-reference steering committee attendance records against the RACI matrix and the institution's HR system to confirm that named Project Sponsors and senior accountable parties were genuinely present at key decision points. Examiners probe nominal versus active governance, and gaps in attendance without documented delegation are a reliable red flag.

Ensure risk assessments in project files reference project-specific risks rather than generic template language. Before the examination, review three to five risk assessments and verify that the identified risks are plausible for the specific technology, vendor, and regulatory context of each project — generic assessments are a common critical finding.

Verify that authority exercised in practice matches the Delegation of Authority matrix. Pull three examples of project budget or scope changes approved during the audit period and confirm the approver's role matches the threshold in the authority matrix. Discrepancies between documented authority and actual approvals indicate the governance framework is not operationally embedded.

Confirm the project portfolio register is current and comprehensive by cross-referencing it against IT capital expenditure records from Finance and change requests raised in the ITSM system during the audit period. Projects that consumed budget or generated change records but do not appear in the governance register indicate scope gaps that examiners will identify.

Prepare a concise narrative document — no more than two pages — summarising how the institution's project governance framework is calibrated to risk and complexity, with examples of how different governance requirements applied to a low-risk and a high-risk project respectively. This helps examination teams quickly understand the proportionality of the framework and reduces the likelihood of misinterpretation during interviews with senior management.

10.2.title: Technology Project Risk Assessment
10.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether technology project risk assessments are substantive documents that genuinely address each of the seven prescribed risk domains, rather than templated tick-box exercises. Examiners pay particular attention to whether resource and vendor competency assessments account for concurrent project load across the institution's portfolio, and whether complexity risk analysis demonstrates actual due diligence on integration points and data migration scope. A recurring red flag is the absence of documented security control adequacy reviews at each project phase gate, particularly where confidential customer data is involved. Examiners will also cross-reference the risk assessment against UAT defect logs, post-implementation review reports, and DR test results to verify that risks identified were actually tracked and mitigated. Institutions that lack evidence of formal risk assessment sign-off by appropriate governance bodies — such as the IT Steering Committee or a dedicated Project Risk Committee — are likely to receive supervisory findings.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Project Risk Assessment Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured risk register produced for each significant technology project, documenting identified risks across all seven RMiT 10.2 prescribed areas, with likelihood and impact ratings, mitigating controls, risk owners, and residual risk acceptance sign-off. This is the primary artefact demonstrating that the institution has systematically identified and addressed key project risks before and during implementation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register explicitly maps risks to each of the seven RMiT 10.2 sub-clauses (a) through (g) with no areas left blank or marked 'not applicable' without documented justification
Each risk entry records a risk owner, target mitigation date, current status, and a residual risk rating that has been formally accepted by an authorised approver
The register is versioned and dated at each project phase gate (initiation, design, build, UAT, deployment) showing risk profile evolution over the project lifecycle
Concurrent project load analysis is embedded or referenced, showing how resource constraints from parallel projects were factored into the risk rating for clause (a)
Register has been reviewed and formally endorsed by the IT Steering Committee or Project Risk Committee with documented minutes referencing the specific risk register version
commonGaps: Risk register is generic and not tailored to the specific project — identical boilerplate risks appear across unrelated projects with no customisation to actual system complexity or vendor profile
Security control adequacy (clause c) is addressed only at initiation and not revisited at subsequent phase gates, leaving cybersecurity risks unmonitored during build and testing

Concurrent project portfolio load is not quantified — the register acknowledges resource risk in principle but contains no data on how many other significant projects are competing for the same resources
Residual risk acceptance lacks appropriate authority — sign-off is at project manager level rather than a governance body with delegated risk appetite authority
DR readiness risk (clause g) is absent or deferred to post-implementation, meaning the risk assessment does not address operational readiness before go-live
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or equivalent) — project risk module
Project Management Office (PMO) SharePoint/Confluence site — project artefacts library
IT Steering Committee secretariat — approved risk register versions with meeting minutes
ITSM platform (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) — linked project issues and risk items
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or GRC system export (with version history)

evidenceItems.name: Vendor and Resource Competency Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing that the institution has formally assessed the adequacy and competency of both internal resources and vendors assigned to significant technology projects. This includes vendor due diligence reports, resource capacity analyses, and concurrent project load assessments, directly satisfying clause 10.2(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Vendor due diligence report includes credentials verification, reference checks, financial stability assessment, and a specific evaluation of the vendor's capacity given their existing client commitments
Internal resource assessment maps required skills and FTE capacity against available resources, with a named escalation path if resource gaps emerge during the project
A consolidated view of significant technology projects currently in-flight is maintained by the PMO and referenced in each new project's risk assessment to quantify concurrent load risk
Vendor contractual obligations include SLA commitments for resource availability and a right-to-audit clause allowing the institution to verify vendor staffing levels
Assessment is refreshed at major project milestones or when significant vendor or internal resource changes occur, with changes formally documented and communicated to the project sponsor
commonGaps: Vendor competency assessment is limited to commercial/procurement due diligence and does not evaluate technical delivery capacity or the vendor's concurrent commitments to other clients
Internal resource capacity planning is informal — no documented analysis comparing available skilled headcount against aggregate demand from concurrent significant projects
Assessments are conducted once at project initiation and never updated, despite known resource changes (e.g., key personnel turnover) during the project lifecycle
No evidence that the institution considered the number, size, and duration of other concurrent significant projects when accepting the risk rating for resource adequacy
Third-party subcontractors engaged by the primary vendor are not included in the competency assessment, creating blind spots in the resource risk picture
suggestedSources: Procurement/Vendor Management team — vendor due diligence files and scorecards
PMO — project portfolio dashboard showing concurrent significant projects
HR/Resource Management system — skills inventory and capacity utilisation reports
Contract repository (SharePoint or contract management system) — vendor SLA and staffing schedules
evidenceItems.format: PDF report with supporting spreadsheet annexes

evidenceItems.name: System Complexity and Integration Risk Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A dedicated technical risk assessment or section within the project risk register that evaluates the complexity of systems being implemented, covering use of unproven or unfamiliar technology, integration risk with existing systems, multi-vendor technology management, data migration scope, and system customisation extent. This directly addresses clause 10.2(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Technology novelty is explicitly assessed — documentation records whether the technology has been deployed in comparable Malaysian financial institution environments and what mitigations apply where it is unproven
Integration architecture diagram is referenced, with each integration point risk-rated based on criticality of connected systems and complexity of data transformation required
Data migration risk assessment quantifies the volume of records, identifies data quality issues from profiling exercises, and specifies cleansing rules and rollback data points
Where multiple vendor-proprietary technologies interact, a compatibility and dependency matrix is maintained and owned by a named integration architect
Customisation risk is quantified — the assessment records the extent of custom code relative to the vendor standard product and the implications for future upgrades and vendor support
commonGaps: Technology familiarity assessment is absent — the institution adopts new or cloud-native technology without documenting whether internal teams have the skills to manage post-implementation risks
Integration risk is assessed at a high level only, with no interface-level analysis of what happens if a downstream system is unavailable during migration or go-live
Data migration risk assessment focuses on technical execution but does not address the risk of migrated data quality defects impacting business operations or regulatory reporting post-cutover
Extensive system customisation is treated as a project delivery decision rather than a risk that increases long-term maintainability and vendor support risk

Multi-vendor technology landscape is not mapped — no single document identifies all vendor-proprietary components, their interdependencies, and the institution's ability to isolate or replace any one component
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team — architecture decision records and integration diagrams
Data Migration team — data profiling and quality assessment reports
PMO or project repository — technical design documents and customisation logs
GRC platform — technology risk entries linked to the project record
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded architecture diagrams; data migration plan as separate DOCX/XLSX

evidenceItems.name: Project Security Control Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that security controls were formally assessed for adequacy and correct configuration at each phase of the project lifecycle — not merely at initiation — to mitigate cybersecurity breaches and exposure of confidential data. This satisfies clause 10.2(c) and should demonstrate continuous security assurance from design through to post-implementation.
whatGoodLooksLike: A security requirements document is produced at project initiation and signed off by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or delegated Information Security team, specifying mandatory controls for the project
Security reviews or checkpoints are embedded into project phase gate criteria — go/no-go decisions at design, build, UAT, and deployment stages each require documented security sign-off
Penetration test or vulnerability assessment results are available for the new system prior to production deployment, with a tracked remediation plan for all high and critical findings
Data classification for information processed by the project is documented, with controls mapped to sensitivity level — particularly for projects touching customer PII or financial data
Post-implementation security review is conducted within 30-90 days of go-live and its findings are formally closed through the project or transitioned to BAU security management
commonGaps: Security assessment is conducted only once at project initiation and not revisited when significant scope changes or new data flows are introduced during the build phase
Penetration testing is deferred to post-implementation or omitted entirely for projects classified as 'internal-only', despite involving confidential customer data
Security control configuration is documented in design but there is no evidence that the actual deployed configuration was verified against the approved design before go-live
Projects involving third-party or cloud-hosted components do not include assessment of the vendor's security controls — the institution assumes vendor certification covers all residual risk
No formal process exists to ensure security findings identified during UAT are resolved before production deployment, creating a gap between identified risk and controlled go-live
suggestedSources: Information Security team — security assessment reports and penetration test reports
GRC platform — security control registers linked to project record
ITSM/project tool — phase gate checklists with security sign-off evidence
Cloud or infrastructure team — configuration baseline and hardening verification records
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports (security assessments, pentest reports); XLSX (control matrices); system-generated phase gate records

evidenceItems.name: User Requirements Specification and Change Control Records
evidenceItems.description: The approved User Requirements Specification (URS) or equivalent business requirements document, together with change request logs and scope change impact assessments, demonstrating that requirements were comprehensively defined upfront and that subsequent changes were formally governed. This addresses clause 10.2(d) by showing mitigations against scope creep and failure to meet business needs.
whatGoodLooksLike: URS is formally signed off by business owners, IT, Risk, Compliance, and where relevant, Legal, before development commences — sign-off page is version-controlled and retained
Requirements include functional, non-functional (performance, availability, security), regulatory, and data requirements, demonstrating comprehensive coverage of business and compliance needs

A formal change control process is evidenced — each scope change request is logged, impact-assessed for risk, cost, timeline, and compliance implications, and approved by an authorised governance body before implementation
Traceability matrix links each business requirement to a specific test case, providing evidence that all requirements will be tested and demonstrating no gaps between requirements and test coverage
Post-implementation review explicitly compares delivered functionality against the original approved URS, documenting any deviations and their business impact
commonGaps: URS is signed off by IT only, without formal business owner or compliance/risk endorsement, undermining its authority as a complete statement of requirements
Requirements are high-level and ambiguous — they describe outcomes rather than measurable acceptance criteria, making it impossible to definitively confirm requirements were met during UAT
Change requests are informally approved via email or verbal agreement without a documented impact assessment, meaning scope growth is not risk-assessed or tracked
Regulatory and compliance requirements (e.g., data localisation, reporting obligations) are absent from the URS, discovered late in the project lifecycle and resolved through costly rework
Traceability between requirements and test cases does not exist, so there is no way to demonstrate that all business requirements were tested and accepted before go-live
suggestedSources: PMO or project repository (SharePoint, Confluence) — URS and requirements documentation
ITSM or project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) — change request log and approval records
Business Analysis team — requirements traceability matrix
IT Steering Committee minutes — scope change approvals
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (URS with sign-off); XLSX or ITSM export (change log and traceability matrix)

evidenceItems.name: System and User Acceptance Testing Strategy and Results
evidenceItems.description: The documented testing strategy, test plans, test execution results, defect logs, and UAT sign-off records for the technology project. This evidence demonstrates that a robust testing regime was applied to reduce the risk of undiscovered system faults and functionality errors per clause 10.2(e), and that deployment and fallback strategies were exercised as required under clause 10.2(f).
whatGoodLooksLike: A comprehensive test strategy document covers System Integration Testing (SIT), User Acceptance Testing (UAT), performance/load testing, regression testing, and — where applicable — parallel run or simulation testing, with entry and exit criteria defined for each phase

UAT results include a named business sign-off from the process owner confirming acceptance, with a tracked defect log showing that all severity 1 and 2 defects were resolved or formally risk-accepted before go-live

Fallback and rollback procedures are documented and tested — evidence of a rehearsed fallback drill (e.g., from a staging or pre-production environment) is available, with test outcomes and timings recorded
Performance and load testing results confirm the system meets defined throughput and response time SLAs under peak load conditions representative of production, not just average load

A post-implementation review or hypercare report is produced within an agreed period after go-live, documenting production incidents, residual defects, and whether the system has stabilised as expected
commonGaps: UAT is compressed or bypassed due to project schedule pressure — test execution records show abbreviated cycles with large numbers of deferred defects accepted into production without formal risk acceptance documentation
Performance and load testing is conducted at unrealistically low volumes, meaning the system has never been validated against peak transaction loads that reflect actual or projected business demand
Fallback strategy exists as a document but has never been rehearsed — there is no evidence of a documented fallback drill, leaving the institution exposed if a production cutover fails
Defect management is informal — high-severity defects are closed in the ITSM tool without evidence of fix verification testing, making it impossible to confirm that resolutions were effective
UAT sign-off is obtained from IT rather than business process owners, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate that the system meets end-user business needs as required by clause (e)
suggestedSources: ITSM or test management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps, ALM/Octane) — test plans, execution results, defect logs
PMO or project repository — test strategy and UAT sign-off documents
Infrastructure/operations team — fallback drill records and cutover runbooks
Business units — UAT acceptance certificates signed by business process owners
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (test strategy, sign-off certificates); ITSM/test tool export (defect log, test results); XLSX (summary dashboards)

evidenceItems.name: Disaster Recovery Readiness Assessment Post-Implementation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that DR operational readiness was formally assessed following the implementation of new or enhanced systems, confirming that DR plans, RTO/RPO parameters, and recovery procedures have been updated and tested to reflect the post-implementation state of the environment. This directly addresses clause 10.2(g).
whatGoodLooksLike: An updated DR plan or BCP annex specific to the new or enhanced system is produced as a project deliverable, with RTO and RPO targets formally approved by senior management and consistent with the institution's overall DR framework

A DR test or drill is conducted within an agreed period after go-live (typically within 3-6 months), with documented test scope, execution results, gaps identified, and a remediation plan for any failures

DR readiness is included as a formal go-live criterion — the project phase gate checklist requires confirmation that DR procedures for the new system are documented and the operations team has been trained before production deployment

Integration dependencies of the new system with other critical systems are reflected in the DR plan — the plan addresses recovery sequencing and confirms that the new system's DR posture does not degrade the institution's overall recovery capability

DR plan ownership is formally transferred from the project team to the IT Operations or Business Continuity Management team as a documented project closure activity, with named accountable owners for ongoing DR maintenance
commonGaps: DR readiness is treated as a post-project BAU activity rather than a project deliverable — no DR assessment or updated DR plan is produced before go-live, leaving the institution unable to recover the new system in a disaster scenario

Existing DR plans are not updated to reflect the new system's dependencies and data flows, meaning the recovery procedures in the event of a disaster would be based on a pre-implementation architecture that no longer exists
DR tests conducted after implementation test the old architecture rather than the new system configuration, providing no assurance that the new system can be recovered within approved RTO/RPO targets

RTO and RPO targets for the new system have not been formally defined or approved — the institution inherits default infrastructure-level targets without assessing whether they meet business requirements for the specific system

Project closure does not include a formal handover of DR documentation to operations — DR plans exist as project artefacts only and are not integrated into the institution's BCM framework or tested on a recurring basis
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management (BCM) team — DR plan repository and test reports
IT Operations — system runbooks and recovery procedure documentation
GRC platform — BCM/DR module with system-level RTO/RPO registry
PMO — project closure checklist confirming DR handover completion
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (DR plan, test reports, handover certificate); GRC system records

auditTips: Map each of the seven sub-clauses (a) through (g) to specific evidence items before the examination — create an internal matrix showing which document satisfies which sub-clause, so examiners can be directed to precise artefacts rather than broad project folders. Gaps in the matrix are gaps in compliance.

Pull the institution's active project portfolio dashboard and cross-reference it against the risk assessments for each significant project — examiners will probe whether concurrent project load was quantitatively assessed, so ensure the concurrent load analysis is embedded in or explicitly referenced by each project's risk register, not held separately in PMO data that examiners may not see.

Verify that security control reviews (clause c) contain dated evidence at each project phase gate, not just at initiation. A common examination finding is a single security sign-off at project start with no evidence of re-assessment when scope changed or when the build deviated from the approved design — compile a phase-gate timeline for each project showing security checkpoints.

For every project that went live in the examination period, locate and prepare the fallback rehearsal record and the DR readiness assessment. Examiners specifically check clauses (f) and (g) by asking operational staff whether they have actually practised the rollback procedure — if staff cannot describe the fallback drill, the absence of records becomes a credibility issue compounding the documentary gap.

Review UAT defect closure records before the examination — identify any instances where high-severity defects were accepted into production and confirm that formal risk acceptance documentation exists, signed by an appropriate authority. Examiners interpret undocumented defect deferrals as evidence that testing robustness under clause (e) was not maintained.

Ensure post-implementation reviews (PIRs) exist for all significant projects completed in the examination window and that they explicitly address whether identified project risks materialised, whether mitigations were effective, and whether the system has stabilised. The PIR is often the best evidence that the risk assessment process was closed-loop rather than a pre-implementation formality.

Confirm that all risk assessments bear formal governance sign-off — at minimum from the Project Sponsor and a Risk or IT Steering Committee — and that the sign-off authority aligns with the institution's Risk Appetite Statement and delegated authority matrix. Risk assessments signed only at project manager level will be flagged as a governance control deficiency regardless of the quality of the underlying analysis.

10.3.title: Board and Senior Management Project Reporting
10.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on three core obligations embedded in this clause: (1) whether reports actually reach both the board and senior management — not merely one or the other — and whether there is documented evidence of review and not just receipt; (2) whether reporting is genuinely timely, with examiners scrutinising the lag between risk events and board-level awareness; and (3) whether reporting is sustained throughout the full implementation lifecycle of significant projects, not just at initiation or closure. A common red flag is a reporting structure that routes project risk updates only to a technology steering committee without formal escalation to the board or its delegated committee. Examiners also probe the definition of 'significant project' — institutions without a documented, consistently applied classification threshold are viewed as having a structural gap. The word 'ongoing' is treated strictly: ad-hoc or milestone-only reporting frameworks are considered non-compliant where no predefined cadence exists.

evidenceItems.name: Significant Technology Project Classification Policy
evidenceItems.description: A documented policy or framework that defines what constitutes a 'significant project' for the purposes of RMiT reporting obligations. This establishes the scope of which projects trigger board and senior management reporting requirements, and should include quantitative thresholds (e.g., budget, duration, systems impacted) and qualitative criteria (e.g., regulatory impact, systemic risk, customer-facing scope).
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy contains explicit, measurable thresholds (e.g., budget exceeding RM 5 million, duration exceeding 12 months, or impact on core banking systems) that are consistently applied
A documented register of all active projects assessed against the classification criteria, showing which were designated 'significant' and which were not, with rationale
The policy is approved by the board or a board-delegated committee, demonstrating governance ownership of the classification framework
The definition aligns with or cross-references the institution's broader risk appetite and technology strategy, not treated as a standalone compliance artefact
Version history and periodic review dates are present, showing the policy has been maintained and is not a one-time artefact produced for examination purposes
commonGaps: Classification criteria are entirely qualitative and subjective, enabling management discretion to exclude politically sensitive projects from board-level scrutiny
The policy exists but there is no corresponding register showing how it was applied to specific projects during the audit period
Thresholds were set years ago and have never been reviewed, meaning inflation or organisational growth has rendered them ineffective at capturing genuinely significant projects
The policy was drafted by technology teams and has never been formally approved at board or risk committee level, creating an ownership gap
No link between the classification policy and the escalation/reporting framework, so a project designated 'significant' does not automatically trigger the board reporting cycle
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) — policy repository module
SharePoint or Confluence governance document library maintained by the Chief Risk Officer or Chief Technology Officer office
Technology Project Management Office (PMO) policy documentation
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee approved minutes referencing adoption of the classification framework
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy document) + Spreadsheet or GRC system export (project classification register)

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Technology Project Risk Reports
evidenceItems.description: The actual risk reports submitted to the board (or a board-delegated committee such as the Board Risk Committee or Board IT Committee) and to senior management (e.g., EXCO, Management Risk Committee) covering risk status of significant technology projects. These are the primary artefacts demonstrating that reporting occurred, was timely, and contained substantive risk content rather than status-only updates.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are clearly addressed to or packaged as board/committee papers, with distribution lists confirming receipt by board members or designated senior management, not just technology teams

Each report contains a dedicated risk section covering key project risks, their likelihood and impact ratings, current mitigating controls, residual risk status, and any escalations — not merely RAG status

The report date and the date of the board/committee meeting are both recorded and the gap between risk event occurrence and board awareness is demonstrably short (typically within one reporting cycle)
Reports include trend analysis — comparing current risk status against prior period — demonstrating ongoing monitoring rather than point-in-time snapshots
There is a clear linkage between risk items raised in project-level reports and the risk items discussed in board minutes, confirming the report was actually reviewed and not filed without discussion
commonGaps: Reports submitted to the board are high-level project dashboards with RAG indicators but contain no substantive risk narrative, meaning the board cannot meaningfully review risk management
Reports are prepared for the IT Steering Committee (a management-level body) but are not further escalated to the Board Risk Committee, breaking the board-reporting obligation
Reporting is project-milestone-driven (e.g., at phase gates) rather than periodic, leaving gaps of several months during which the board had no visibility of risk developments
The same template report is submitted regardless of changing risk conditions, suggesting the report is a compliance formality rather than a genuine management tool
There is no evidence of the board or senior management acknowledging, questioning, or directing action on risk items raised in the reports, undermining evidence of 'review'
suggestedSources: Board secretariat system or SharePoint board paper repository (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect, or internal SharePoint board pack library)
Management Risk Committee or EXCO paper distribution records
Enterprise GRC platform — project risk reporting module with report generation logs
PMO reporting system (e.g., Microsoft Project Online, Planview, or Jira portfolio dashboards) where board-formatted exports are generated
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board/committee papers), with metadata confirming submission date and recipient distribution

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Meeting Minutes — Technology Project Risk Agenda Items
evidenceItems.description: Approved minutes from board-level committees (Board Risk Committee, Board IT Committee, or full Board) and senior management forums (EXCO, Management Risk Committee) that record the discussion and any decisions or directions relating to technology project risk reports. Minutes serve as the definitive evidence that reports were not merely received but were actively reviewed by the relevant governance body.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes reference the specific technology project risk report by name and date, confirming it was tabled and considered at that meeting rather than merely circulated
Minutes record substantive discussion points — questions raised by board or senior management members, management responses, and any directional guidance or decisions issued

Where risk escalations or threshold breaches were reported, minutes show a corresponding board or senior management response (e.g., approval of additional resources, direction to accelerate mitigation, or formal acceptance of residual risk)
Minutes are approved and signed by the chairperson, timestamped, and filed in a retrievable system — satisfying both governance and record-keeping requirements
A consistent cadence of agenda items on significant technology project risks is visible across successive meeting minutes, demonstrating the 'ongoing basis' obligation is met
commonGaps: Minutes record only that a report was 'noted' with no further discussion detail, which BNM examiners treat as evidence of passive receipt rather than active review
Technology project risk is bundled into a single 'risk update' agenda item with no specific reference to individual significant projects, making it impossible to demonstrate project-level oversight
Gaps exist in the meeting schedule — e.g., no board committee meeting for a quarter — during which significant project risks were not reported at board level, violating the ongoing basis requirement
Minutes from senior management forums show project risk discussion, but board-level minutes have no corresponding coverage, suggesting escalation did not reach board level
Minutes are drafted but approval is significantly delayed (e.g., approved at the following meeting three months later), undermining their reliability as contemporaneous evidence
suggestedSources: Board secretariat system — approved minutes repository (Diligent, BoardEffect, or SharePoint governance library)
Management Risk Committee secretariat records
Company Secretary office filing system
GRC platform — governance module where committee outcomes are logged against risk items
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed and dated approved minutes)

evidenceItems.name: Project Risk Reporting Calendar and Reporting Cadence Log
evidenceItems.description: Documentation that establishes the mandated reporting frequency for significant technology projects to the board and senior management, and a corresponding log or tracker confirming that reports were actually submitted at each scheduled interval throughout the project lifecycle. This is the primary mechanism for demonstrating that reporting occurred 'on an ongoing basis throughout the implementation.'
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal reporting calendar or schedule approved by the board or a delegated committee specifying minimum reporting frequency (e.g., monthly to management risk committee, quarterly to board risk committee) for significant projects

A compliance tracker or log showing each scheduled reporting date, the actual date of submission, the receiving body, and the report reference — allowing a direct comparison of planned vs. actual reporting

The reporting cadence is calibrated to project risk level — higher-risk phases of a project (e.g., cutover, UAT) trigger more frequent reporting than stable phases, demonstrating a risk-proportionate approach
The calendar spans the full project lifecycle from initiation through post-implementation review, with no undefined periods where reporting obligations are considered suspended
Any deviation from the scheduled cadence is documented with a formal explanation and compensating action, rather than simply absent from the log
commonGaps: No formal reporting calendar exists; reporting frequency is determined ad hoc by the project manager, resulting in inconsistent cadence and gaps during lower-activity project phases
The reporting schedule ends at go-live, with no post-go-live reporting period, despite ongoing implementation-phase risks during stabilisation

The cadence log shows consistent reporting to senior management but no scheduled reporting directly to a board-level committee, relying instead on management summarisation without direct board access to project risk data
Scheduled reports were skipped during periods of organisational change (e.g., leadership transitions, restructuring) with no compensating mechanism or formal deferral approval
The calendar exists for the current year only, with no historical records demonstrating ongoing compliance for projects that commenced in prior periods
suggestedSources: PMO governance framework documentation — reporting calendar appendix
Enterprise GRC platform — automated report scheduling and distribution log
SharePoint — PMO central repository with report submission timestamps
IT Steering Committee or Board Risk Committee terms of reference (which typically specify the minimum reporting frequency required)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (reporting tracker/log) + PDF (approved reporting calendar/schedule)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Project Risk Register for Significant Projects
evidenceItems.description: The live, maintained risk register for each significant technology project, demonstrating that project risks are being actively identified, assessed, and managed — and that the content of board and senior management reports is grounded in a structured risk management process rather than being prepared ad hoc for reporting purposes. The risk register provides the evidentiary foundation that the reports submitted to the board contain substantive risk data.
whatGoodLooksLike: The risk register is maintained throughout the project lifecycle with dated entries showing risks being added, updated, escalated, or closed — evidencing ongoing management rather than a static document produced at initiation

Each risk entry includes owner assignment, likelihood and impact ratings, current and target risk scores, mitigation actions with due dates, and a clear link to the escalation level (project team vs. senior management vs. board)

Risks designated for board or senior management escalation in the register are traceable to corresponding items in the board/committee papers, closing the loop between operational risk management and governance reporting

The register reflects the specific technology risks relevant to the project type (e.g., data migration risks, system integration risks, third-party dependency risks, cybersecurity risks during transition)
A named risk owner at senior management level is identified for escalated risks, confirming accountability sits at the appropriate level and is not diffused across the project team
commonGaps: Risk registers are prepared at project initiation and rarely updated thereafter, meaning board reports are based on stale risk data that does not reflect current project conditions

The register identifies risks but contains no evidence of active mitigation — action items are listed but have no completion dates, owners, or update history, suggesting the register is maintained for compliance appearance only

Escalation thresholds in the register are not defined or not applied consistently, meaning risks that should have been escalated to the board were managed at project team level without appropriate visibility
Multiple risk registers exist for the same project across different systems (e.g., PMO tool, GRC platform, spreadsheet) with inconsistent content, creating conflicting audit evidence

The risk register does not distinguish between project delivery risks and technology/operational risks, conflating schedule slippage with the substantive technology risks that RMiT requires the board to oversee
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform — project risk module (Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or Riskonnect)
PMO project management system — risk log module (Jira, Microsoft Project, Planview, or ServiceNow PPM)
SharePoint — individual project risk register files maintained by project risk coordinators
ITSM platform — change and project risk integration where applicable
evidenceItems.format: GRC system export or Spreadsheet (risk register), with version history or audit trail timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Risk Escalation and Board Reporting Framework for Technology Projects
evidenceItems.description: The documented governance framework or policy that defines how technology project risks are escalated from project teams through senior management to the board, including escalation triggers, reporting templates, responsible parties, and the delegation of authority structure. This framework is the institutional architecture that makes compliance with clause 10.3 systematic and repeatable rather than dependent on individual initiative.
whatGoodLooksLike: The framework explicitly names the board-level committee responsible for receiving significant technology project risk reports, and defines the minimum information requirements for each report submitted to that committee

Escalation triggers are quantified — e.g., any risk with a residual score above a defined threshold, any project milestone slip beyond a defined number of days, or any cost overrun beyond a defined percentage automatically triggers board reporting

Roles and responsibilities for report preparation, review, and submission are clearly assigned to named roles (e.g., Project Sponsor, Chief Risk Officer, Chief Technology Officer, Board Risk Committee Secretary) rather than generic teams
The framework has been tested via a walkthrough or tabletop exercise and the results are documented, demonstrating the institution has validated that the escalation chain functions as intended

The framework cross-references the institution's broader technology risk management framework and the board's terms of reference, demonstrating it is embedded in the governance architecture rather than a standalone compliance document
commonGaps: The framework describes escalation to senior management but is ambiguous about when and how information reaches the board, leaving a gap in the final escalation step that clause 10.3 specifically requires
Escalation triggers are defined in principle but no quantitative thresholds are specified, giving management excessive discretion to determine when the board needs to know about a risk
The framework was drafted by the PMO and has not been formally adopted or endorsed by the board or board-delegated committee, undermining its authority and enforceability

No template or minimum content standard is defined for board-level project risk reports, resulting in inconsistent quality that makes it impossible to confirm the board received the information needed to discharge its oversight obligation

The framework covers the reporting structure but contains no feedback loop — there is no documented process for the board's directions or decisions to be communicated back to the project team and incorporated into risk management actions
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform — policy and framework repository
Board Risk Committee or Board IT Committee terms of reference (which typically codify the escalation structure)
Chief Risk Officer office — technology risk governance framework documentation
PMO governance manual — escalation and reporting procedures chapter
SharePoint governance library maintained by the Company Secretary or Risk Management function
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (approved framework document) with board or committee approval evidence (e.g., signed cover sheet or minutes reference)

auditTips: Construct a traceability matrix before the examination that maps each significant technology project to: (a) the date it was classified as significant, (b) every board and senior management report submitted, (c) the meeting at which each report was reviewed, and (d) the corresponding minute reference — this single artefact answers the three core questions examiners will ask (who received reports, when, and how often) and demonstrates end-to-end compliance at a glance.

Pull a minimum 24-month sample of board and senior management meeting agendas for any committee with technology project oversight responsibility and verify that significant project risk appears as a standing or recurring agenda item — gaps in the agenda sequence are high-value findings for examiners, and you should identify and remediate them before the examination rather than explaining them during it.

Cross-reference the content of board reports against the corresponding project risk register entries for the same period: if the board report lists five key risks but the risk register for that week shows eight active high-rated risks, the discrepancy suggests the report was sanitised before reaching the board — a significant red flag that examiners will probe.

Validate that your institution's definition of 'significant project' has been applied consistently: take your full project portfolio for the last three years, apply the classification criteria mechanically, and compare the output against the projects that were actually subjected to board-level reporting — any project that meets the criteria but was not reported to the board represents a reportable gap that needs explanation and, if it is ongoing, immediate remediation.

Review the time-stamp evidence for report submission: examiners will ask whether reports were prepared before or after the board meeting, and whether the board had adequate time to review them. A report submitted to the board secretariat on the morning of the meeting it was discussed is weaker evidence than a report distributed five business days in advance — check your submission logs and distribution records to confirm the timeliness standard is met in practice.

Examine post-go-live reporting coverage: many institutions treat project completion as the end of their reporting obligation, but BNM interprets 'throughout the implementation' to include the stabilisation period following go-live. Confirm your reporting framework and calendar explicitly cover the post-implementation review period and that board reports were actually submitted during that phase for completed significant projects.

Before the examination, interview the board or senior management members who received the reports (or their representatives) to confirm they recall receiving and discussing project risk reports — if board members are unaware of having reviewed technology project risks, this suggests the reports were tabled without substantive discussion, which examiners treat as equivalent to non-reporting for the purposes of the 'review' obligation in clause 10.3.

10.4.title: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework
10.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework (ETAF) is a living, governed document rather than a one-time artefact. They will probe whether the framework is board- or senior management-approved, version-controlled, and actively used in system development and acquisition decisions. Key red flags include architectures that lack system interdependency maps or fail to identify single points of failure, frameworks with no traceability between technology components and the business functions they serve, and cases where the architecture documentation is materially out of date relative to the institution's actual infrastructure footprint. Examiners will also cross-reference the framework against BIA records, DR/BCP plans, and change management logs to verify that the architecture underpins operational resilience decisions. Institutions that treat the ETAF as a siloed IT document with no linkage to business strategy or longer-term technology roadmaps are likely to receive findings.

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework Policy Document
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy or charter that formally establishes the ETAF, defines its scope and objectives, assigns ownership and accountability, and describes the governance lifecycle for maintaining and reviewing the framework. This document demonstrates that the institution has formally committed to clause 10.4 obligations at a policy level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is approved by the Board or a senior governance committee (e.g., Board Technology Risk Committee or ITSC) with dated signatures and version history visible
Scope statement explicitly references all five functions mandated by clause 10.4(a)-(e) and maps them to named framework components

Governance provisions define mandatory review frequency (at minimum annual), trigger-based reviews upon material infrastructure changes, and a named document owner such as the CTO or Head of Enterprise Architecture
Policy includes clear linkage to related frameworks including BCP/DR, IT Risk Management, Change Management, and Information Security policies
Version control log shows at least one full review cycle has been completed since initial issuance, with evidence of changes incorporated
commonGaps: Policy exists but has never been formally approved by a governance body — exists only as a draft or working document with no sign-off trail
Scope is limited to infrastructure or network architecture only, omitting application architecture, data architecture, or security architecture dimensions required under clause 10.4(a)
Review cycle is defined in the policy but evidence of actual periodic reviews (minutes, sign-off sheets) cannot be produced
Framework ownership is assigned to a generic IT function rather than a named role, making accountability unclear during examination
Policy document predates significant infrastructure changes (e.g., cloud migration, core banking upgrade) and has not been updated to reflect the current environment
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture or IT Governance team SharePoint/Confluence repository
Board secretariat records and Board/ITSC meeting minutes for approval evidence
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate) policy register
IT Policy Management System with version control and approval workflow logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with tracked version history, approval signatures, and review log

evidenceItems.name: Baseline Architecture Inventory and Component Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured register of all baseline technology architecture components — covering infrastructure, platforms, middleware, and major applications — including the rationale for their adoption and their current lifecycle status. This directly evidences compliance with clause 10.4(a), which requires a comprehensive view of technology throughout the institution along with key rationale for component use.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all tiers of the architecture stack: network, compute, storage, platform, application, and data layers, with no material categories omitted
Each component entry includes a documented rationale for adoption (e.g., vendor support lifecycle, strategic fit, regulatory compliance requirement) rather than just a name and version
Component lifecycle status is current (active, end-of-life, scheduled for decommission) and reconciles with the institution's asset management and patch management records
Register includes cloud-hosted components and third-party managed services, not only on-premises infrastructure
Document is dated within the last 12 months and carries evidence of a formal accuracy review against the CMDB or asset inventory
commonGaps: Register captures on-premises infrastructure but omits SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS components, leaving the cloud footprint undocumented
Rationale column is blank or populated with generic descriptions (e.g., 'vendor selected') rather than substantive justification aligned to business or risk drivers
Register has not been reconciled against the CMDB or network discovery tool output, resulting in components present in production that are absent from the register
End-of-life and end-of-support dates are missing, preventing examiners from assessing technology obsolescence risk as required implicitly under clause 10.4(e)
Document is treated as an IT-only artefact with no linkage to the broader ETAF, making it difficult to demonstrate that it forms part of the mandated framework
suggestedSources: Configuration Management Database (CMDB) — ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, or equivalent ITSM tool
Enterprise Architecture repository (e.g., LeanIX, Ardoq, Sparx Enterprise Architect)
Network discovery and asset management tools (e.g., Qualys, Tanium, SolarWinds)
Vendor contract and software asset management (SAM) register maintained by Procurement or IT Asset Management team
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or structured export from EA repository / CMDB, with version date and review sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Technology Infrastructure and System Interdependency Maps
evidenceItems.description: Diagrammatic and narrative documentation of the institution's technology infrastructure, including system-to-system connectivity, data flows, integration dependencies, fallback and failover relationships, and security control placement. This is the primary evidence for clause 10.4(b) and directly supports single point of failure (SPOF) identification as explicitly called out in the verbatim text.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams exist at multiple levels of abstraction (e.g., logical, physical, and data flow) and are current to within 6 months or last material infrastructure change, whichever is more recent
Interdependency maps explicitly annotate fallback and failover relationships, including the capacity and RTO/RPO characteristics of each fallback path
SPOF analysis is documented as a formal output derived from the interdependency maps, with identified SPOFs tracked through to remediation or accepted risk decisions
Security controls (e.g., firewalls, WAFs, IDS/IPS, encryption boundaries) are overlaid on network and system diagrams, enabling examiners to verify defence-in-depth
Diagrams are reconciled with the DR/BCP plan to confirm that recovery procedures reference the same system relationships depicted in the architecture
commonGaps: Diagrams exist but are maintained informally by individual teams, resulting in inconsistent formats and no single authoritative source aligned to the ETAF
Fallback and failover paths are undocumented or described only in narrative BCP text without architectural diagrams to substantiate the claimed resilience
SPOF identification has never been formally conducted using the interdependency maps — the ETAF documents infrastructure but does not use it analytically
Security control placement is absent from architecture diagrams, requiring examiners to rely solely on security policy documents without architectural corroboration
Diagrams do not include third-party service provider connections, API integrations, or cloud on-ramps, which represent material dependencies in modern financial institution environments
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture repository (LeanIX, Ardoq, Sparx EA) for formal architecture diagrams
Network Operations Centre (NOC) documentation — Visio or Lucidchart network topology diagrams
ITSM / CMDB relationship maps (ServiceNow Discovery relationship visualiser)
Information Security team for security architecture overlays and zone diagrams
BCP/DR team for fallback architecture diagrams and recovery topology documentation
evidenceItems.format: Visio/Lucidchart/draw.io diagrams exported to PDF, with accompanying narrative documentation in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Business-Technology Mapping Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured mapping that traces technology components (infrastructure, systems, applications) to the business functions, organisational units, and data assets they support. This directly evidences clause 10.4(c) and is critical for demonstrating that the institution can perform business impact analysis (BIA) using the architectural framework as a foundation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix covers all material applications and technology services and maps each to at least one business process, one organisational unit, and the data classification of information processed
Criticality ratings for business functions (e.g., critical, important, supporting) are reflected in the mapping and are consistent with ratings used in the BIA and BCP/DRP

Mapping is used as a live input into the BIA process — BIA reports cite the technology mapping matrix, demonstrating that the ETAF actively supports impact analysis rather than being a standalone document
Data mapping includes regulatory data categories (e.g., customer PII, financial transaction records) aligned to the institution's data governance and privacy framework
Matrix is maintained under change management controls — updates are triggered by system decommissions, new application deployments, or organisational restructures
commonGaps: Mapping exists at the application level but does not trace down to infrastructure components, meaning a server or network failure cannot be mapped to its business impact without manual investigation
Organisational unit mapping is outdated and reflects a prior organisational structure, reducing confidence that the matrix would produce accurate BIA inputs
Data assets are absent from the mapping, making it impossible to use the framework to assess data-related business impacts as required under clause 10.4(c)
The mapping matrix is maintained by IT independently of the BIA process owned by Business Continuity — the two are not reconciled and contain conflicting criticality classifications
Third-party and outsourced technology services are not included in the mapping, creating blind spots for business functions that depend on vendor-delivered capabilities
