suggestedSources: GRC platform application register with business owner and criticality metadata (ServiceNow, Archer, SAI360)
Business Continuity Management team BIA working papers and system dependency questionnaires
Data governance repository or data catalogue (Collibra, Alation, or internal SharePoint data inventory)
Enterprise Architecture repository where application-to-business-capability mapping is maintained
IT Service Management team service catalogue with business owner assignments
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform report export, cross-referenced to current BIA documentation

evidenceItems.name: Architecture Principles, Network Governance Standards, and IT Security Policy Suite
evidenceItems.description: The set of documented architecture principles, network design standards, and IT security policies that govern how the institution designs, builds, and maintains its technology infrastructure and controls. This evidences clause 10.4(d) and demonstrates that the ETAF is a governing instrument, not merely descriptive documentation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture principles are formally documented (e.g., security-by-design, zero-trust network segmentation, cloud-first policy) with stated rationale and governance authority who approved each principle
Network infrastructure standards specify mandatory security controls for each network zone (DMZ, internal, management, DR), and compliance with these standards is subject to exception management

IT security policies (including access control, encryption, patch management, and vulnerability management) are explicitly tied to architecture design requirements rather than existing as standalone documents
Exception management process is documented and exceptions to architecture principles are formally tracked, risk-accepted by an appropriate authority, and time-limited
Standards and policies carry version histories showing they are actively maintained, with the most recent review dated within 12 months or after a material regulatory or threat landscape change
commonGaps: Architecture principles exist as high-level aspirational statements but have no enforcement mechanism — there is no process to verify that system designs conform to stated principles before deployment

Network standards and IT security policies are maintained as separate documents by different teams with no explicit cross-reference to the ETAF, making it difficult to demonstrate that they form part of the mandated framework
Security policies are generic and not differentiated by network zone or technology tier, providing insufficient guidance for architects and engineers designing specific system components
Exception management for deviations from architecture principles is informal or undocumented, meaning risk accepted through non-conformance cannot be demonstrated to examiners
Policies have not been updated to address cloud, containerisation, or API architecture patterns, leaving significant areas of the current technology environment ungoverned by the framework
suggestedSources: IT Policy and Standards register on SharePoint or GRC platform
Information Security team documentation repository for security policy suite
Enterprise Architecture team for formally approved architecture principles register
Change Advisory Board (CAB) or Architecture Review Board (ARB) records showing principles applied in design approvals
Network engineering team for network zone design standards and firewall rule governance documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy documents with version control, approval records, and review history

evidenceItems.name: Technology Architecture Roadmap and Evolution Plan
evidenceItems.description: A forward-looking document or set of documents that articulates the institution's longer-term technology architecture priorities, planned transitions, and investment decisions. This directly evidences clause 10.4(e) and demonstrates that the ETAF is not a static description of the current state but a governance instrument that guides the institution's technology trajectory.
whatGoodLooksLike: Roadmap covers a meaningful planning horizon (minimum 3 years) and identifies specific architecture evolution initiatives with rationale tied to business strategy, regulatory requirements, or risk reduction objectives
Technology debt and end-of-life component remediation are explicitly tracked in the roadmap with target decommission dates and interim risk mitigation measures

Roadmap has been presented to and approved by a senior governance body (Board, ITSC, or Technology Risk Committee) demonstrating that architecture evolution priorities are governed at an appropriate level
Roadmap is linked to the institution's IT budget planning cycle — planned architecture initiatives have corresponding budget allocations or are formally flagged as unfunded risks
Progress against roadmap milestones is tracked and reported periodically, with evidence that deviations or delays are escalated and risk-assessed
commonGaps: Technology roadmap exists as a slide deck used for internal planning purposes only, with no formal approval, no version control, and no structured review cycle
Roadmap focuses only on new capability delivery and omits technology debt remediation and end-of-life system replacement, leaving obsolescence risk unaddressed

Architecture evolution priorities are disconnected from the institution's strategic plan and budget cycle — there is no evidence that technology architecture informs or is informed by business strategy
Roadmap is maintained at business unit or product level rather than at the enterprise level, meaning there is no institution-wide view of architecture direction as required by clause 10.4(e)
Progress tracking against roadmap commitments is absent — the institution can produce a roadmap document but cannot demonstrate whether planned milestones are being achieved
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team three-year or five-year technology roadmap documents
Board Technology Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee papers and meeting minutes showing roadmap approval and progress updates
IT strategic planning documentation from the Strategy or Transformation Office
IT budget submissions and approved capital expenditure plans linked to architecture initiatives
Programme Management Office (PMO) portfolio register showing status of architecture roadmap initiatives
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or presentation deck with formal approval record; supplemented by programme status reports in spreadsheet or PMO tool export

auditTips: Before examination, conduct a self-assessment gap analysis by mapping each of the five sub-clauses (10.4(a) through 10.4(e)) to specific, named documents in your evidence library — any sub-clause that cannot be mapped to at least one formal, approved document represents a material gap that BNM examiners are likely to surface.

Verify that the ETAF document index is consistent across your GRC platform, SharePoint repository, and any evidence package you intend to submit — discrepancies between what your policy register lists as current and what you actually produce for examiners will raise credibility concerns about your governance processes.

Cross-reference the single point of failure analysis required under clause 10.4(b) against your most recent BIA and DR test results to ensure that SPOFs identified in the architecture are either remediated or have documented, risk-accepted compensating controls — examiners will specifically test this linkage.

Prepare a demonstration-ready walkthrough showing how the business-technology mapping matrix (10.4(c)) feeds directly into the BIA process: identify one or two recent BIA exercises where you can show the architecture mapping was used as a direct input, as this demonstrates the framework is operationally active rather than a paper exercise.

Review all architecture documents for currency relative to material infrastructure changes in the past 24 months — common examination findings involve cloud migrations, core system upgrades, or new third-party integrations that are reflected in operational systems but absent from the ETAF documentation, which examiners will identify by comparing diagrams against live CMDB or network discovery outputs.

Ensure the Architecture Review Board or equivalent governance body has documented records (terms of reference, meeting minutes, and decision logs) showing that architecture principles from the ETAF were applied in design approvals for recent system development or acquisition projects — this directly demonstrates the framework is used to govern system development as required by clause 10.4(d) and the clause's opening paragraph.

For the technology roadmap, confirm it has been formally presented to the Board or a Board-level committee within the past 12 months and that the meeting minutes explicitly reference it — BNM examiners view senior governance engagement with the roadmap as a key indicator that clause 10.4(e) is substantively met rather than nominally documented.

10.5.title: System Development Life Cycle Methodology
10.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has a formally adopted, board- or senior management-approved SDLC methodology that explicitly covers all eight mandated phases: requirement, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance, and decommissioning. Examiners pay particular attention to whether the methodology is genuinely integrated with enterprise architecture governance, risk management frameworks, and security-by-design principles — or merely cross-referenced in name only without substantive controls. A key red flag is the absence of security requirements gates at each SDLC phase, particularly during requirements elicitation and design, suggesting security is bolted on post-deployment rather than embedded. Examiners will also test whether the methodology is consistently applied across all system types — including third-party acquired systems and cloud-hosted platforms — and will sample project artefacts from recent deployments to verify real-world adherence versus paper compliance. Institutions that cannot produce phase-gate sign-off records, security risk assessments tied to individual system projects, or decommissioning procedures for retired systems are treated as materially non-compliant.

evidenceItems.name: SDLC Methodology Policy and Framework Document
evidenceItems.description: The master policy or framework document that formally defines the institution's adopted SDLC methodology, enumerates all mandated phases (requirement, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance, decommissioning), and articulates how the methodology integrates with enterprise architecture, risk management, and security principles. This is the foundational governance artefact against which all other evidence is benchmarked.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document explicitly names and defines all eight RMiT-mandated SDLC phases with entry and exit criteria for each phase gate
Policy includes a section on mandatory integration points with the Enterprise Architecture Review Board, Risk Management Framework, and Information Security Policy
Document is version-controlled, dated within the last two years or reviewed annually, and carries approval signatures from the CIO or equivalent senior management
Scope statement explicitly covers internally developed systems, acquired commercial packages, SaaS/cloud platforms, and outsourced development engagements
Policy references specific security principles (e.g., secure coding standards, OWASP, threat modelling) that must be applied during design and development phases
commonGaps: Policy covers only software development and omits acquisition, configuration, or low-code/no-code platform projects, creating unregulated pathways to production
Integration with enterprise architecture is described in vague aspirational language without defining mandatory EA review gates or approval workflows
Decommissioning phase is absent or limited to a single line, with no procedures for data disposal, licence termination, or dependency migration
Document has not been reviewed or updated in more than two years and does not reflect current cloud or DevOps delivery models in use
Security principles referenced are generic and not mapped to specific controls applied at each SDLC phase, making auditability of security integration impossible
suggestedSources: IT Governance or IT Policy repository on SharePoint or Confluence
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) policy module
CIO or Technology Governance secretariat document register
Board/ITSC committee meeting minutes confirming policy approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — formal policy document with version history, approval signatures, and review schedule

evidenceItems.name: SDLC Phase-Gate Artefacts and Project Sign-Off Records
evidenceItems.description: A sample set of completed SDLC phase-gate artefacts drawn from recent system development or acquisition projects, demonstrating that the methodology is operationally applied and not merely documented. Artefacts include requirements specifications, design documents, test plans, UAT sign-offs, deployment checklists, and post-implementation reviews. These records provide direct evidence of consistent methodology adherence across project portfolios.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each sampled project folder contains a complete set of phase artefacts with dated sign-offs from the project manager, business owner, and IT security representative at each gate
Requirements documentation includes a dedicated section capturing security and privacy requirements, linked to the institution's information security policies
Design artefacts include threat modelling outputs or architecture risk assessments reviewed and approved by the Information Security function
Test plans distinguish between functional testing, security testing (SAST/DAST), and performance testing, with results recorded and defects tracked to closure
Post-implementation review is completed within 30–60 days of go-live and includes lessons learned, residual risks accepted by management, and sign-off confirming closure
commonGaps: Phase-gate sign-offs are retrospective or backdated, indicating artefacts are produced for compliance purposes rather than as genuine project controls
Security review sign-off at design phase is missing or delegated to the project team rather than an independent Information Security function
Test evidence covers only functional UAT and omits security or vulnerability testing results, leaving no audit trail of pre-deployment security assurance
Artefacts exist for internally developed systems but are absent for commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) or SaaS acquisitions, revealing inconsistent methodology application
Post-implementation reviews are either not performed or are superficial checklists without documented residual risk acceptance by an accountable executive
suggestedSources: ITSM/PPM platform project workspaces (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow, Microsoft Project Online)
SharePoint or Confluence project repositories organised by system or release
IT Project Management Office (PMO) document archive
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records for approved deployments
evidenceItems.format: Mixed — PDF/DOCX for policy artefacts; spreadsheets or system exports for test logs and defect trackers; system screenshots for approval workflows

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Architecture Integration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the SDLC methodology is substantively integrated with the institution's enterprise architecture (EA) governance process, as required by clause 10.5(a). This includes EA review records, architecture exception logs, and technology roadmap alignment evidence showing that system development and acquisition decisions are evaluated against the institution's strategic architecture and technology standards.
whatGoodLooksLike: EA review request and approval records exist for all sampled projects, showing that no system proceeded to design without an architecture assessment
Architecture decision records (ADRs) or technology assessment reports are linked to specific SDLC projects and document alignment or approved deviation from EA standards
Technology standards register or EA pattern library is referenced in design artefacts, confirming developers and architects apply institutional standards rather than ad hoc choices
Architecture exceptions are formally logged, risk-assessed, time-bound, and approved by a named architecture review authority (e.g., Architecture Review Board)
EA governance outcomes are reported to senior management or the IT Steering Committee on a periodic basis, demonstrating active oversight of technology portfolio alignment
commonGaps: EA review is an informal conversation with no documented outcome, leaving no audit trail of architecture governance for individual projects
EA integration is described in the SDLC policy but the Architecture Review Board has no formal terms of reference, membership, or meeting records to substantiate its existence
Small or low-risk projects are blanket-exempted from EA review without a defined risk-based criteria framework, creating unmonitored gaps in architecture governance
Technology standards exist but are not referenced or enforced in SDLC artefacts, meaning integration is stated in policy but absent in practice
No mechanism exists to track or remediate architecture debt arising from approved exceptions, creating long-term strategic and security risk accumulation
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team SharePoint or EA tool (e.g., LeanIX, Ardoq, Sparx EA)
Architecture Review Board meeting minutes and decision logs
IT Steering Committee papers referencing EA portfolio reviews
Technology standards register maintained by the EA or CTO office
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for review records and ADRs; spreadsheet or GRC system export for exception register; presentation decks for ITSC reporting

evidenceItems.name: Security Integration Records Across SDLC Phases
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that security principles and requirements are embedded throughout the SDLC rather than applied only at deployment, as required by clause 10.5(c). This encompasses secure coding standards, security requirements specifications, threat modelling records, vulnerability scanning results, penetration test reports, and security sign-off at each phase gate. This body of evidence demonstrates the institution's implementation of security-by-design principles protecting CIA of customer and counterparty information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Security requirements are captured as a distinct, mandatory section in requirements specifications, including data classification, access control, encryption, logging, and applicable regulatory obligations
Threat modelling outputs (e.g., STRIDE analysis, attack surface maps, data flow diagrams with trust boundaries) are present in design artefacts and reviewed by Information Security
SAST and DAST scan reports with remediation evidence are present for all in-scope systems, and critical/high findings are resolved before deployment approval is granted
Penetration test reports from independent testers are available for internet-facing or high-risk systems, with findings tracked to remediation or formal risk acceptance
Security sign-off by an Information Security Officer or delegate is a mandatory gate at both design and pre-deployment phases, evidenced by named approvals in project records
commonGaps: Security requirements are conflated with functional requirements and not separately identified, making it impossible to verify security coverage at the requirements phase
Threat modelling is performed only for the highest-risk projects and is absent for systems handling customer data that are classified as medium risk
Vulnerability scanning is conducted but remediation is not tracked to closure before go-live, and waivers for unresolved findings are undocumented or lack risk owner approval
Penetration testing is procured reactively after incidents rather than as a standard pre-deployment gate for qualifying systems
Security sign-off responsibility is assigned to the project manager rather than an independent Information Security function, undermining the control's assurance value
suggestedSources: Information Security team's project engagement records and security review tracker
Vulnerability management platform exports (e.g., Tenable, Qualys, Checkmarx, Veracode)
Penetration test reports from third-party security firms retained by the institution
GRC platform security risk registers linked to individual SDLC projects
ITSM platform change records with mandatory security approval workflow steps
evidenceItems.format: PDF for test reports and design security reviews; spreadsheet or system export for vulnerability trackers and finding registers; system screenshots for approval workflow evidence

evidenceItems.name: Risk Management Integration in SDLC
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that risk management policies and practices are integrated into the SDLC as required by clause 10.5(b). This includes technology risk assessments conducted at key SDLC phases, risk register entries for systems under development, residual risk acceptance records signed by accountable risk owners, and evidence that risk outcomes influence project decisions such as design changes, enhanced controls, or project deferral.
whatGoodLooksLike: A risk assessment is mandatory at the design phase for all projects above a defined risk threshold, with results documented in the institutional risk register and linked to the specific project
Risk register entries for in-flight SDLC projects include inherent risk rating, controls identified, residual risk rating, and named risk owner accountable for acceptance
Risk treatment decisions demonstrably influence SDLC outcomes — e.g., design revisions documented as a result of risk findings, or deployment deferred pending control implementation
Risk assessments are updated at material change points in the project lifecycle (e.g., scope change, architecture change, vendor change) rather than performed once at initiation
Risk acceptance for residual risks is signed by an executive with delegated authority consistent with the institution's risk appetite and authority matrix
commonGaps: Risk assessments are performed at project initiation but not updated when material changes to scope, design, or third-party components occur mid-project
Risk management integration is limited to a checkbox on a project initiation form rather than a substantive assessment that informs design and control decisions
Residual risk acceptance is signed by the project manager rather than an independent risk owner with appropriate delegated authority, undermining risk governance
Risk register entries for SDLC projects are not linked to the enterprise risk register, creating siloed risk visibility and preventing portfolio-level risk aggregation
Risk assessment methodology applied at the SDLC level is inconsistent with the institution's enterprise risk management framework, producing incomparable risk ratings
suggestedSources: Enterprise or Technology Risk Register maintained in the GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow Risk, MetricStream)
Project risk logs maintained in PPM or project management tools
Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee meeting papers and minutes
Technology Risk function's SDLC engagement records and risk assessment templates
evidenceItems.format: GRC system export or PDF for risk register records; DOCX for risk assessment reports; PDF for risk acceptance sign-off memos or committee minutes

evidenceItems.name: System Decommissioning Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal decommissioning records for retired systems, demonstrating that the institution's SDLC methodology extends to the full system lifecycle including its end, as explicitly required by clause 10.5. These records evidence controlled retirement of systems including data disposition, licence termination, dependency migration, access revocation, and final security and risk sign-off, protecting the confidentiality and integrity of residual customer and counterparty data.
whatGoodLooksLike: A decommissioning plan or checklist exists for each retired system, signed off by the system owner, Information Security, Data Management, and IT Operations prior to shutdown
Data disposition records confirm that customer and counterparty data was either migrated to a successor system, archived per retention policy, or securely destroyed with a destruction certificate
Access revocation records confirm all user accounts, service accounts, and API integrations connected to the decommissioned system were disabled or removed prior to shutdown
Infrastructure decommissioning evidence (e.g., server decommission records, cloud resource termination logs, licence cancellation confirmations) is retained and linked to the system record
A post-decommission review or closure notice is signed by the system owner confirming all dependencies were resolved and no orphaned data or access paths remain
commonGaps: No formal decommissioning process exists; systems are simply switched off without documented data disposition, access revocation, or dependency assessment
Decommissioning checklists are completed but data destruction is not evidenced by a third-party certificate or witnessed destruction record, leaving residual data risk unsubstantiated
Legacy systems are placed in extended maintenance limbo — neither actively maintained nor formally decommissioned — creating unmanaged security and compliance exposure
Access to decommissioned system environments or their backups remains active post-shutdown due to absence of a formal access revocation step in the decommissioning workflow
Decommissioning records are not retained or are discarded with the system, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate historical compliance with lifecycle management obligations
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management (ITAM) system decommission workflow records (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Lansweeper)
Data Governance or Records Management team data disposition and destruction certificates
Identity and Access Management (IAM) platform access revocation audit logs
Vendor or cloud provider licence termination and resource deletion confirmations
Change Management system records for decommissioning change requests approved by CAB
evidenceItems.format: PDF for decommissioning plans, data destruction certificates, and closure notices; system export for CMDB records and IAM audit logs; email confirmations for vendor terminations

auditTips: Before the examination, conduct an internal project portfolio sample: select 3–5 recently completed SDLC projects spanning different risk tiers and system types (internally developed, COTS, SaaS), and verify that a complete phase-gate artefact set exists for each. Gaps in this sample will be the first place a BNM examiner looks, and self-identifying them allows remediation time.

Map each of the eight mandated SDLC phases in the RMiT verbatim text to a corresponding section or gate in your SDLC policy document. Prepare a one-page compliance matrix showing this mapping and have it ready as an opening artefact — examiners appreciate institutions that can demonstrate self-awareness of their regulatory obligations rather than requiring the examiner to construct the mapping themselves.

Specifically test the decommissioning phase, as it is the most frequently absent phase in Malaysian FI SDLC policies. Compile a register of all systems decommissioned in the past three years and confirm that decommissioning records, data disposition certificates, and access revocation evidence exist for each. If records are missing, escalate for retrospective documentation and formal risk acceptance before the examination.

Prepare a cross-reference matrix linking each SDLC phase to the specific integration touchpoint it has with (a) Enterprise Architecture, (b) Risk Management, and (c) Information Security functions. For each cell in the matrix, identify the artefact that evidences the integration and the approval authority. This matrix directly addresses the three integration requirements in clauses 10.5(a)–(c) and demonstrates integrated governance rather than siloed compliance.

Interview the PMO or project delivery team leads — not just the governance team — to validate that the SDLC methodology is understood and applied in practice. Examiners frequently interview project staff and find that documented policies are unknown to practitioners. Use pre-examination dry-run interviews to identify training or awareness gaps and close them before the examination date.

Pull vulnerability scanning and penetration testing records for all systems developed or significantly changed in the past 12 months and verify that critical and high findings are remediated or formally risk-accepted with documented justification. Examiners will cross-check scan results against change records and deployment approvals — unresolved critical findings in production systems are a significant red flag that can trigger deeper examination of the institution's security posture.

Validate that the SDLC methodology explicitly covers acquired or outsourced systems, not only internally developed code. Prepare evidence showing that third-party or vendor-developed systems undergo equivalent SDLC governance controls — including security assessments, EA review, and risk acceptance — before being admitted to production. Many institutions have robust internal development processes but uncontrolled vendor system onboarding, which constitutes a material gap under clause 10.5.

10.6.title: Rapid Development Security Requirements
10.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing this clause will prioritise evidence that security is not sacrificed for speed in agile or DevOps environments. They will look specifically for automation of security compliance reviews rather than manual gate-keeping, and will probe whether vulnerability discovery and testing are embedded in the CI/CD pipeline rather than bolted on at the end of a release cycle. A key red flag is any indication that rapid methodology is used as a justification to bypass standard security controls or governance approvals. Examiners will cross-reference pipeline configuration artefacts against actual release records to verify that automated security checks are enforced consistently, and will interview DevOps and security teams to confirm that tooling is not merely present but operationally effective. Institutions that cannot demonstrate automated enforcement — as opposed to manual checklists — are considered non-compliant with the automation obligation in the verbatim text.

evidenceItems.name: Rapid Development Security Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy or framework document that explicitly governs the use of rapid or agile development methodologies and mandates adherence to enterprise security, governance, and compliance requirements throughout the development lifecycle. This demonstrates that the institution has codified its obligation to maintain security standards in dynamic development environments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly names permitted rapid methodologies (e.g., Scrum, SAFe, Kanban, DevSecOps) and maps each to mandatory security controls
Document is approved by the CTO, CISO, and Board-level or senior management committee, with version history visible
Policy references integration with the enterprise security framework, change management policy, and RMiT obligations by clause number
Clear roles and responsibilities are defined for security champions, DevOps engineers, and the application security team within rapid development workflows
Policy includes a mandatory automated security gate requirement as a precondition for production deployment and is reviewed at least annually
commonGaps: Policy exists for traditional SDLC but contains no specific provisions for rapid or agile methodology, leaving teams to interpret security obligations informally
Document is approved only at department head level with no evidence of senior management or Board endorsement as required for a foundational security policy
Security requirements are described in general terms without specifying which controls are mandatory, automated, and non-bypassable in a CI/CD context
Policy has not been reviewed or updated since the institution adopted DevOps or containerised deployment practices, making it materially out of date
No accountability matrix links individual roles to specific security obligations, making it impossible to determine who is responsible for enforcing compliance
suggestedSources: GRC platform policy repository (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or equivalent)
SharePoint or Confluence policy library maintained by the IT Governance or Enterprise Architecture team
CISO or IT Risk office document management system
Board IT Committee or EXCO meeting minutes referencing policy approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with approval signatures, version control table, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: CI/CD Pipeline Security Automation Configuration Records
evidenceItems.description: Technical configuration artefacts and architecture documentation demonstrating that automated security compliance checks are embedded within the continuous integration and continuous delivery pipeline. This is the primary technical evidence of the automation obligation in the clause and shows that security gates are enforced programmatically rather than relying on manual review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Pipeline-as-code files (e.g., Jenkinsfile, GitLab CI YAML, GitHub Actions workflow) explicitly include stages for SAST, DAST, SCA, secret scanning, and container image scanning with defined pass/fail thresholds
Configuration shows that pipeline execution is blocked — not merely notified — when critical or high-severity findings breach the defined threshold, with evidence of enforced quality gates
Tooling inventory lists licensed and integrated security tools (e.g., SonarQube, Checkmarx, Snyk, OWASP ZAP, Trivy) and maps each to specific security control objectives
Access controls on pipeline configuration files are restricted to authorised DevSecOps personnel, evidenced by repository permission logs, preventing bypass of security stages
Architecture diagram shows end-to-end pipeline flow from code commit through security scanning to deployment approval, with named tooling at each automated security stage
commonGaps: Security scanning tools are installed in the environment but are not integrated into the pipeline as blocking gates — findings are reported but do not prevent deployment
Pipeline configuration files allow manual override or skip flags for security stages without requiring documented exception approval, enabling unauthorised bypass
Scanning tools are configured with permissive thresholds (e.g., only blocking on Critical severity) that do not reflect the institution's documented risk appetite or security standards
Secret scanning and software composition analysis (SCA) for third-party library vulnerabilities are absent from the pipeline despite the institution using open-source dependencies extensively
Pipeline configurations are not version-controlled or subject to change management, making it impossible to demonstrate the historical state of security gates at the time of any given release
suggestedSources: Source code management platform (e.g., GitLab, GitHub Enterprise, Azure DevOps) — repository settings and pipeline definitions
CI/CD orchestration platform (e.g., Jenkins, Bamboo, CircleCI) — job configuration exports
Application Security team or DevSecOps engineering team documentation
Security tooling vendor consoles (e.g., SonarQube project configuration, Snyk organisation settings)
evidenceItems.format: YAML/JSON pipeline configuration files, PDF architecture diagram, system-exported tooling configuration reports

evidenceItems.name: Automated Security Scan Results and Remediation Records
evidenceItems.description: Historical records of automated security scan outputs generated during the development pipeline, together with evidence of remediation tracking and closure. These records demonstrate that the automated security compliance review is not merely configured but is actively producing results that are acted upon, and that vulnerabilities are discovered and resolved before production release.
whatGoodLooksLike: Scan result logs are retained per release and per sprint, showing a consistent record of SAST, DAST, and dependency scan outputs with timestamps linked to specific pipeline run identifiers

Remediation records in the issue tracking system (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow) show that security findings above the risk threshold are assigned, tracked to closure, and linked back to the originating scan finding by ID

Trend data shows a measurable reduction in recurring vulnerability categories over consecutive sprints, demonstrating that findings are genuinely remediated rather than routinely suppressed or accepted
Exception or risk acceptance records exist for any findings that were accepted rather than remediated, each with documented business justification, risk owner sign-off, and defined review date
Audit trail confirms that no release proceeded to production with open critical or high findings outside an approved exception process, demonstrable via pipeline gate enforcement logs
commonGaps: Scan results are generated but stored only in the scanning tool console with no systematic export or retention, making historical evidence unavailable for prior release periods
Remediation records are maintained informally in spreadsheets or team chat channels rather than in a controlled issue management system, lacking the traceability required to link findings to fixes
A large proportion of findings are marked as accepted or suppressed with no documented risk owner approval, suggesting the institution is bypassing remediation rather than managing risk
DAST scan results are absent, with the institution relying solely on SAST, leaving runtime and integration vulnerabilities undiscovered before release

There is no evidence that scan results from one sprint inform security requirements or testing scope for the next, indicating findings are treated as isolated events rather than inputs to continuous improvement
suggestedSources: Application security tooling platforms (Checkmarx, Veracode, Snyk, OWASP ZAP reports)
ITSM or issue tracking system (Jira, ServiceNow) — security defect backlog and closure history
CI/CD platform build logs and artefact storage
GRC platform vulnerability register or application risk register
evidenceItems.format: System-exported PDF/CSV scan reports, ITSM ticket exports, pipeline build log archives

evidenceItems.name: Secure Release Gate and Deployment Approval Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that a formal, enforced security approval gate exists as a precondition for every production deployment arising from a rapid development cycle. This evidence shows that enterprise governance and compliance requirements are applied at the point of release, preventing the deployment of IT services that have not passed automated security validation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Deployment approval workflow records show that each production release required a security sign-off step, with the approver identity, timestamp, and reference to the associated security scan pass documented
Change management records in the ITSM system link each production release to a change request that explicitly captures the security gate outcome (pass/exception) as a mandatory field
Pipeline execution logs confirm that the deployment stage was only triggered after all preceding automated security stages returned a passing status, demonstrating technical enforcement of the gate
Emergency or expedited release records include a documented post-deployment security review with a defined completion deadline, evidencing that governance is maintained even under time pressure

Release dashboard or metrics report shows the proportion of releases blocked or delayed by security gates over the review period, demonstrating the gate is operationally effective and not merely nominal
commonGaps: Change management records exist but do not capture security gate outcomes as a mandatory field, making it impossible to confirm that security approval was obtained for individual releases
Emergency change process allows bypassing the security gate without any compensating controls or mandatory post-deployment review, creating a material gap in governance coverage
Deployment approvals are captured only as verbal or informal sign-offs in team meetings with no documented audit trail linking the approver to a specific security assessment outcome
The security gate exists in pre-production environments but is absent or unenforced in the production deployment pipeline, meaning the final deployment step is not governed
There is no distinction in the approval record between a full security pass and an accepted-exception release, making it impossible to assess the true risk profile of the production environment
suggestedSources: ITSM change management module (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management) — change request records with security approval fields
CI/CD platform deployment logs and approval audit trail
Release management team records and release governance committee minutes
DevOps portal or internal developer platform — release dashboard and metrics
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV), CI/CD deployment log archive, release governance meeting minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Access Control and Privileged Access Records for Development Environments
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that access to development, testing, and production environments within the rapid development workflow is controlled, reviewed, and aligned with least-privilege principles to prevent unauthorised access — one of the two explicit security objectives stated in the clause. These records demonstrate that the institution manages the elevated access risks inherent in dynamic development environments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Access control matrix for development, staging, and production environments is documented, showing role-based access with no direct developer write access to production outside a controlled deployment pipeline

Privileged access management (PAM) logs show that any elevated access to production infrastructure is session-recorded, time-bound, and requires a justification linked to an approved change or incident record
Quarterly or semi-annual access recertification records demonstrate that development team access is reviewed and attested by the relevant system owner and data owner, with revocations documented

Service account and pipeline credential management records show that CI/CD pipeline credentials are managed in a secrets manager (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) and are not hard-coded in pipeline configuration files
Segregation of duties controls are evidenced, confirming that developers cannot promote their own code to production and that the deployment action requires a separate, independent authorisation
commonGaps: Developers have direct write access to production databases or application servers as a matter of convenience, with no compensating controls or session monitoring in place
Access recertification is performed annually at best and is not specific to the rapid development team, meaning stale access accumulates between review cycles
CI/CD pipeline credentials are stored as plain-text environment variables in the pipeline configuration rather than in a secrets management solution, creating a material credential exposure risk
No segregation of duties control exists, allowing the same engineer who writes code to also approve and execute the production deployment, contrary to enterprise governance requirements
Production access granted for incident response or hotfix activities is not revoked after the event, leaving persistent elevated access that is not tracked or justified on an ongoing basis
suggestedSources: Identity and access management (IAM) platform — access provisioning and recertification reports (e.g., SailPoint, CyberArk, Microsoft Entra ID)
Privileged access management platform session logs (CyberArk, BeyondTrust)
Secrets management solution audit logs (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
ITSM access request and approval records
Source code repository permission reports (GitLab, GitHub Enterprise)
evidenceItems.format: IAM system-exported access reports (PDF/CSV), PAM session log exports, recertification sign-off records (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Security Testing Integration Evidence for Rapid Sprints
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that security vulnerability discovery and testing — including both automated tooling and where applicable structured security testing activities — are integrated into each rapid development sprint or iteration cycle rather than deferred to a single pre-release phase. This addresses the clause's specific obligation to ensure discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities is continuous and commensurate with the pace of rapid development.
whatGoodLooksLike: Sprint retrospective or definition-of-done records show that security testing completion is a mandatory acceptance criterion for each user story or sprint, with evidence of consistent application across multiple sprints

Penetration testing or red team engagement records exist for major releases or application components, with scope, methodology, findings, and remediation status documented and linked to the relevant development cycle
Security requirements are traceable from the product backlog through to test cases in the test management system, demonstrating that security is designed in rather than tested in retrospectively

Threat modelling artefacts (e.g., STRIDE analysis, data flow diagrams with trust boundaries) are produced for new features or architectural changes and are reviewed as part of sprint planning or design sessions

Security regression test suite is maintained in the automated test framework and is executed on every build, with test coverage metrics that include security-relevant test cases such as authentication, authorisation, and input validation
commonGaps: Security testing is conducted only once per quarter or at major release milestones, meaning the majority of sprints produce and deploy code without any security validation in the current review period
Penetration testing is performed by an internal team without independence from the development function, or is conducted infrequently with no clear linkage to the rapid development release cadence
No threat modelling is conducted for new features or system changes introduced through the rapid development process, relying entirely on automated scanning to identify design-level security flaws
Definition-of-done criteria for sprints make no reference to security testing, meaning security is treated as a separate workstream rather than an integral part of delivery quality

Security regression tests exist but cover only functional authentication flows and do not test for common vulnerability classes (e.g., injection, broken access control, insecure deserialisation) relevant to the institution's technology stack
suggestedSources: Agile project management platform sprint records (Jira, Azure DevOps, Rally)
Test management system (Zephyr, TestRail, Xray) — security test case library and execution history
Penetration testing engagement reports from internal red team or approved third-party security firms
Threat modelling documentation in Confluence, SharePoint, or architecture repository
CI/CD platform automated test result reports
evidenceItems.format: Sprint record exports (PDF/CSV), penetration test reports (PDF), threat model documents (PDF/DOCX), automated test result reports

auditTips: Request a live demonstration of the CI/CD pipeline execution for a recent sprint release, not just documentation — ask the DevOps team to show a pipeline run and point out each automated security stage, its configuration, and what would happen if a critical finding were raised. Documentation that describes automated gates that do not actually block deployment is a common and serious misrepresentation.

Cross-reference pipeline build logs against the change management system: for every production release in the sample period, confirm there is a corresponding pipeline run log showing all security stages passed (or an approved exception), and a change request with a security approval field populated. Gaps in this chain indicate releases occurred outside the governed process.

Specifically test the emergency change or hotfix pathway: request records of all emergency production deployments in the review period and verify each has either a security gate pass log or a documented post-deployment security review completed within the defined timeframe. Emergency pathways are the most common location for security governance to be circumvented in rapid development environments.

Interview both the application security team and the development team leads independently about the security gate process: discrepancies between their accounts of what is enforced, what can be overridden, and who holds override authority are strong indicators that documented controls are not operationally effective.

Verify that automated security tooling is current and correctly configured by reviewing the tool version, ruleset update date, and scan scope configuration — an outdated ruleset or a scan scoped to exclude critical application modules renders the automation obligation nominally met but substantively ineffective.

Examine the remediation backlog in the issue tracking system for the age and volume of open security findings: a large backlog of unresolved high-severity findings that are repeatedly deferred across sprints indicates that the institution has automated discovery but has not established the governance discipline to act on results, which fails the spirit of the clause.

Check that the scope of automated security testing covers all components of the application including third-party libraries, container base images, and infrastructure-as-code templates — institutions commonly have SAST in place for application code but have no controls over the supply chain components that may introduce vulnerabilities into the final deployed service.

10.7.title: Production Environment Segregation
10.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has implemented genuine, enforceable segregation between production and non-production environments — not merely logical or policy-based separation. They look for documented architecture diagrams supported by live technical evidence, particularly network topology, access control matrices, and cloud tenancy configurations. A primary red flag is shared administrative credentials or overlapping network segments between production and development or testing environments. Where cloud is in scope, examiners will specifically probe whether the institution can demonstrate that production workloads are isolated at the virtual host or hypervisor layer, not simply separated by naming convention or VLAN tagging alone. They also cross-reference change management logs against access records to detect any instances where development or test personnel accessed production systems directly.

evidenceItems.name: Environment Segregation Architecture Document
evidenceItems.description: A formal architecture document or network topology diagram illustrating the physical or logical boundaries between production, development, and testing environments, including network segments, security zones, jump hosts, and demarcation points. Demonstrates that segregation has been designed and approved at an architectural level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagram explicitly labels and separates production, UAT, staging, and development tiers with distinct network subnets or VLANs for each
Document is version-controlled, dated, and bears approval signatures from the CISO or CTO and the IT Steering Committee
Architecture narrative explains the rationale for the segregation design and maps it explicitly to RMiT clause 10.7 obligations
Diagram includes firewall placement and enforced traffic flow rules between environments, not just zone labelling
Document is reviewed and revalidated at least annually or upon any material infrastructure change
commonGaps: Diagram exists but shows only logical separation (e.g., separate folders or naming conventions) with no distinct network boundaries or access enforcement
Architecture document is outdated — reflects a legacy on-premises topology that does not account for current cloud or hybrid infrastructure
Production and non-production are depicted in separate zones, but shared services (e.g., Active Directory, DNS, NTP) are not scoped or risk-assessed
Document lacks formal approval trail, existing only as an informal Visio file without governance sign-off
Cloud components are absent from the diagram despite the institution operating hybrid workloads in scope
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team's SharePoint or Confluence repository
IT Steering Committee minutes and approved architecture review records
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) under Technology Risk domain
Infrastructure team's network management platform (e.g., SolarWinds, NetBox)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded network diagrams (Visio, Lucidchart, or draw.io exports)

evidenceItems.name: Access Control Matrix — Environment-Level Separation
evidenceItems.description: A documented and system-enforced access control matrix showing which roles and individual accounts have access to production versus non-production environments. Demonstrates that personnel access is restricted by environment boundary and that developers and testers cannot access production systems directly.
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix maps every role (developer, tester, DBA, sysadmin, DevOps engineer) to permitted environments, with production access limited to designated operations and DBA personnel only
Production access accounts are provisioned separately from development accounts — no shared identity is used across environment boundaries
Privileged access to production is gated through a Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution with session recording and time-limited access tokens
Access control policy explicitly prohibits developers and testers from holding standing access to production, enforced through Active Directory group policy or IAM roles
Quarterly access reviews are documented, evidencing that out-of-scope production access is revoked promptly
commonGaps: Developers hold persistent, standing access to production databases or servers for 'emergency debugging', with no compensating controls or formal exception approval
A single shared service account (e.g., 'svc_deploy') is used across both production and non-production pipelines, blurring the access boundary
Access matrix document exists but is not reconciled against actual IAM or Active Directory group memberships, creating discrepancies between policy and practice
No PAM tool is in place — production privileged access is managed via shared password spreadsheets or informal verbal handover
Access reviews are performed annually rather than quarterly, leaving stale entitlements undetected for extended periods
suggestedSources: Identity and Access Management (IAM) platform exports (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, CyberArk, BeyondTrust)
Active Directory group membership reports from IT Operations
GRC platform under Access Governance or Logical Access Control controls
Quarterly User Access Review (UAR) records from the Information Security team
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) access provisioning and deprovisioning tickets
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) with system-extracted IAM exports appended as supporting evidence; PDF for policy document

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Virtual Host Isolation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence specific to cloud-hosted environments demonstrating that production workloads and non-production workloads are not co-located on the same virtual host, hypervisor node, or cloud account/subscription/project. Directly addresses the cloud-specific obligation in clause 10.7.
whatGoodLooksLike: Production and non-production environments reside in completely separate cloud accounts (AWS), subscriptions (Azure), or projects (GCP), providing account-level blast radius containment

Cloud provider tenancy or dedicated host configuration reports confirm that production VMs or containers are not scheduled onto the same underlying physical or virtual host as non-production workloads

Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) templates (Terraform, CloudFormation, Bicep) are version-controlled in separate repositories per environment, with production deployments requiring elevated pipeline approvals
Cloud management console screenshots or API-exported inventory lists show environment tagging that is enforced through Service Control Policies (SCPs) or Azure Policy, not just labelling convention
For containerised workloads, production Kubernetes clusters are deployed on separate node pools or separate clusters from non-production, with namespace-level RBAC reinforcing the boundary
commonGaps: Institution relies solely on naming conventions or resource tags to distinguish production from non-production in the same cloud account, with no account-level isolation enforced
Cloud vendor's shared responsibility model is misunderstood — institution assumes the provider guarantees virtual host isolation without configuring dedicated tenancy or verifying co-tenancy policies
No documentation exists confirming whether the cloud service agreement includes virtual host isolation guarantees; co-tenancy risk is unassessed in the vendor risk register
Multi-tenant SaaS platforms used for core banking or payments are not assessed for virtual host isolation, as the obligation is incorrectly scoped only to IaaS environments
Cloud environment inventory is managed manually and is stale, making it impossible to produce an accurate and timely account of current production vs. non-production resource placement
suggestedSources: Cloud provider management console exports (AWS Organizations, Azure Management Groups, GCP Resource Hierarchy)
Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool reports (e.g., Prisma Cloud, Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Wiz)
Cloud vendor contracts and service agreements held by the Vendor Management or Procurement team
Infrastructure-as-Code repositories (GitHub, Azure DevOps, GitLab) showing environment-specific deployment pipelines
Cloud provider dedicated host or reserved instance configuration reports
evidenceItems.format: System-exported PDF or CSV inventory reports; cloud console screenshots; IaC repository links with commit history

evidenceItems.name: Network Segmentation and Firewall Rule Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Firewall policy exports, network access control lists (NACLs), and security group configurations demonstrating that network-level controls enforce the boundary between production and non-production environments, preventing unauthorised lateral movement or data exfiltration between tiers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Firewall rulesets explicitly deny all inbound and outbound traffic between production and non-production network segments by default, with only named exceptions approved through change management

Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) or security group rules in cloud environments are reviewed and exported, showing that production security groups do not permit ingress from development or testing subnets

A network segmentation review or penetration test report confirms that traffic flows between environments are limited to explicitly approved pathways (e.g., CI/CD pipeline agents connecting to deployment targets via a controlled jump host)
Firewall rule reviews are conducted at least semi-annually and documented, with unnecessary or overly permissive rules remediated within a defined SLA
Network diagrams are reconciled against actual firewall configurations — what is drawn matches what is deployed
commonGaps: Firewall rules between production and development segments were opened for a project several years ago and never closed — 'temporary' rules have become permanent without formal review
Cloud security groups or NACLs use overly broad CIDR ranges (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8) that inadvertently permit traffic from development subnets into production
No process exists to periodically review and recertify firewall rules; the ruleset has grown organically over years without pruning
Network segmentation is enforced at layer 3 but application-layer controls are absent, allowing application-to-application calls between environments via shared API endpoints
Evidence of rule reviews exists but remediation tracking is absent — issues identified in reviews are not demonstrably closed within acceptable timeframes
suggestedSources: Firewall management platform exports (e.g., Palo Alto Panorama, Cisco Firepower Management Center, Fortinet FortiManager)
Cloud provider security group and NACL configuration exports (AWS, Azure NSG, GCP VPC firewall rules)
Network Operations Centre (NOC) or IT Infrastructure team's configuration management database (CMDB)
Penetration test and vulnerability assessment reports from Information Security
ITSM platform firewall change request records
evidenceItems.format: System-exported configuration files or PDF reports; firewall policy spreadsheet exports (CSV/XLSX)

evidenceItems.name: Change Management Records Demonstrating Environment Gate Controls
evidenceItems.description: Change management records, deployment pipeline logs, and release approval workflows demonstrating that all changes to production are governed by a formal process and that no ad-hoc deployments bypass the production environment boundary. Provides evidence that segregation is operationally maintained, not just architecturally designed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every production deployment is traceable to an approved change request in the ITSM platform, with no orphaned deployments that lack a corresponding RFC
Change Advisory Board (CAB) or equivalent approval records show that production changes require multi-party sign-off, including business owners and Information Security where applicable
CI/CD pipeline audit logs demonstrate that production deployment pipelines are separate from non-production pipelines and require approvals that development and testing pipelines do not
Emergency change procedures explicitly require post-implementation review and retrospective documentation, preventing emergency access from becoming a routine bypass mechanism
A reconciliation between deployment logs and approved change records is performed regularly, with unexplained discrepancies investigated and reported to risk management
commonGaps: Developers perform hotfixes directly on production servers via SSH or RDP without raising a formal change request, citing urgency — emergency change procedures are either absent or unenforced
CI/CD pipelines deploy to production automatically upon merge to the main branch without a mandatory human approval gate, eliminating the change management control
Change records are raised retrospectively after the deployment has already occurred, undermining the pre-authorisation intent of the change management process
Pipeline logs are not retained beyond 30–90 days, making it impossible to demonstrate a 12-month history of controlled deployments during an examination
No reconciliation process exists between deployment events and approved change records — unapproved deployments would go undetected
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management, Jira Service Management)
CI/CD platform audit logs (e.g., Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions)
CAB meeting minutes and approval records from IT Governance
Version control system (e.g., GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) merge and tag history for production releases
Release Management team's deployment runbooks and post-implementation review records
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system-exported PDF or CSV reports; pipeline audit log exports; CAB minutes in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Production Data Masking and Non-Production Data Governance Policy
evidenceItems.description: Policy and technical evidence demonstrating that production data is not used in development or testing environments without appropriate masking or anonymisation. Evidences that the segregation boundary extends to data flows, not only network and access controls, and that sensitive customer data cannot be exfiltrated through non-production channels.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal data classification and non-production data handling policy prohibits the direct copy of production data into development or test environments without prior masking or synthetic data substitution

Technical evidence from a data masking tool (e.g., Delphix, Informatica Data Masking, IBM InfoSphere Optim) shows that masking routines are applied before any production dataset is provisioned to non-production environments
Test data management procedures specify approved synthetic data sets for each system, with traceability to the data masking tool outputs
Exceptions to the data masking policy require written approval from the CISO and Data Protection Officer, are time-limited, and are tracked in the GRC platform
Annual audits of non-production environments are conducted to detect any residual unmasked production data, with findings reported to the Information Security Committee
commonGaps: Developers routinely restore production database backups directly into development environments for debugging, without any masking, citing convenience and time pressure
Data masking policy exists as a document but no technical tooling enforces it — compliance relies entirely on manual adherence by individual developers
Masked data sets are created once and reused indefinitely across test cycles, meaning they may diverge significantly from current production data structures after schema changes
No formal exceptions process exists — unmasked production data use is either unknown to management or informally tolerated without documented approval
Audit of non-production environments for production data has never been performed, leaving the institution unable to confirm whether the data segregation obligation has been met in practice
suggestedSources: Information Security Policy repository (SharePoint or GRC platform) for data handling and test data management policies
Data masking tool audit logs and job execution reports (e.g., Delphix, Informatica)
Database Administration team's backup and restore request records from the ITSM platform
Data Protection Officer's records of exceptions and approvals
Non-production environment database inventory, obtainable from the CMDB or cloud asset management tool
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for policy documents; system-exported masking job reports (PDF/CSV); ITSM exception approval records

auditTips: Walk examiners through the end-to-end segregation architecture using a live demonstration where possible — pull up the cloud management console or network management platform during the examination and show production and non-production accounts or segments side by side. This demonstrates operational reality, not just documented intent.

Pre-reconcile your change management records against CI/CD deployment logs for the 12 months prior to examination. Any deployment event in production that cannot be traced to an approved change record is a critical finding — identify and document these before BNM does, and present a remediation timeline.

For cloud environments, obtain a written statement or portal evidence from your cloud provider confirming the tenancy model in use. If dedicated hosts are not configured, prepare a risk acceptance document approved by senior management acknowledging the shared tenancy model and the compensating controls in place — examiners will ask.

Conduct a targeted access control review specifically scoped to production systems at least 60 days before the examination. Revoke any standing developer or tester access to production that lacks formal exception approval, and document the clean-up as evidence of proactive governance.

Map each evidence item explicitly to the clause 10.7 obligation it satisfies using a compliance traceability matrix. Examiners respond well to institutions that demonstrate they understand which control addresses which regulatory obligation — it signals maturity and reduces examination time on foundational questions.

Perform a spot-check of non-production databases for unmasked customer data (PII, account numbers, transaction records) before the examination. If any is found, remediate immediately and document the remediation. An institution that self-identifies and corrects data leakage into non-production environments is viewed more favourably than one where BNM discovers it.

Cross-reference your firewall rule review records against the architecture diagram — examiners will compare what the diagram shows against what the firewall actually permits. Any discrepancy (e.g., a rule allowing SSH from a development subnet to a production host that is not shown in the diagram) will be treated as evidence of inadequate governance of the production boundary.

10.8.title: System Testing Methodology
10.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a formally documented, enterprise-wide testing methodology that is consistently applied across all system deployments — not merely ad hoc testing practices. Examiners will specifically probe whether test coverage extends beyond functional testing to include performance, security, and regression testing, and whether test outcomes demonstrably gate deployment decisions. A key red flag is the absence of documented user acceptance testing (UAT) sign-offs, which signals that 'meets user requirements' is not being formally validated. Examiners also focus heavily on the sensitive test data obligation: they will look for evidence that production data used in testing environments is either masked, anonymised, or subject to explicit authorisation controls, and that access to test environments containing such data is logged and restricted. Institutions that conflate 'testing occurred' with 'a sound methodology exists' are frequently cited — examiners expect a structured, repeatable framework with defined entry/exit criteria, roles, and escalation paths.

evidenceItems.name: System Testing Policy and Methodology Framework
evidenceItems.description: The overarching policy or framework document that defines the institution's mandatory approach to system testing across the SDLC, including testing types required, phases, roles and responsibilities, entry/exit criteria, and the link between test outcomes and deployment approval gates.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates testing phases (unit, integration, SIT, UAT, performance, security) with defined minimum coverage thresholds for each phase
Entry and exit criteria are clearly defined per testing phase, with explicit prohibition on proceeding to production without satisfying exit criteria
Document is Board or senior management approved, versioned, and reviewed at least annually with an audit trail of changes
Policy scope covers all deployment types including third-party packages, cloud services, and internally developed systems without carve-outs
Roles and responsibilities section assigns named accountabilities (e.g., Test Manager, Business Owner, CISO sign-off for security testing) rather than generic team references
commonGaps: Policy exists but applies only to major projects; BAU patches, configuration changes, and third-party upgrades are explicitly or implicitly excluded, creating a regulatory gap
Testing types are listed but without minimum coverage requirements, allowing teams to satisfy the policy with superficial test passes
Document has not been reviewed or updated following significant technology changes (e.g., cloud migration, agile adoption), making it materially inconsistent with actual practice
Entry/exit criteria are aspirational rather than enforceable — no approval workflow exists to prevent deployment when criteria are not met
Policy is owned by IT operations but has not been endorsed by Risk Management or the Board, undermining its standing as an institution-wide control
suggestedSources: IT Governance / Technology Risk team document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform policy register (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate)
Board or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes recording policy approval
SDLC framework documentation maintained by Enterprise Architecture or PMO
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — formally approved policy document with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Test Plans and Test Case Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Project-level test plans and associated test case libraries that operationalise the testing methodology for specific systems or releases, demonstrating that testing is structured, requirement-traceable, and planned rather than improvised.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each test plan contains a traceability matrix linking test cases back to specific functional and non-functional requirements, confirming coverage is deliberate and complete
Test plans define scope, out-of-scope items, test environment specifications, resource requirements, schedule, and risk/contingency provisions
Test cases include preconditions, step-by-step execution instructions, expected results, and pass/fail criteria — not just high-level scenario descriptions

Non-functional test cases covering performance benchmarks (response time, throughput under load) and security test scenarios (authentication, authorisation, injection) are present alongside functional cases
Test plans are version-controlled and formally approved by the Business Owner and IT Project Manager before test execution begins
commonGaps: Test cases exist only as informal checklists or spreadsheet rows with no link to requirements, making it impossible to demonstrate coverage of user requirements
Performance and security test cases are absent or treated as optional, leaving robustness claims unsupported by evidence
Test plans are created post-execution or backdated, which is detectable through metadata inconsistencies and absence of pre-execution approvals
Test case expected results are vague (e.g., 'system works correctly') rather than specific, measurable outcomes, preventing meaningful pass/fail determination
Test plans exist for greenfield projects but are not produced for significant change releases or third-party system upgrades
suggestedSources: Test management tools (Jira Xray, Zephyr, HP ALM/Quality Center, Azure DevOps Test Plans)
Project SharePoint or Confluence project wiki
PMO project artefact repository
Development team Git repositories (for BDD/automated test specifications)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for test plans; Spreadsheet or test management tool export for test case libraries

evidenceItems.name: Test Execution Results and Defect Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence of actual test execution outcomes including pass/fail results per test case, defect logs with severity classifications, defect resolution records, and re-test confirmation — collectively demonstrating that testing was genuinely performed and defects were resolved before deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test execution reports show date-stamped, tester-attributed results for each test case with actual outcomes recorded against expected results, not just a summary pass rate
Defect log captures all raised defects with severity/priority classification, root cause category, assigned owner, resolution date, and re-test outcome — forming a closed-loop audit trail
Critical and high-severity defects have documented disposition decisions (fix, defer with risk acceptance, reject) signed off by the Business Owner and Risk function before deployment proceeds
Defect closure rates and open defect profiles at deployment are recorded, with any deferred defects carried to a post-implementation remediation plan with target dates
Test execution metrics (total cases, pass rate, defect density, re-open rate) are summarised in a test completion report that feeds the deployment approval decision
commonGaps: Test results are stored only as summary dashboards or verbal sign-offs in meeting minutes, with no granular per-test-case execution evidence to support claims of coverage
Defect management is conducted informally via email threads or chat, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic tracking, severity classification, or resolution verification
High-severity defects are marked 'closed' in the defect log without documented re-test evidence, raising concerns about whether fixes were actually validated
Test completion reports exist but do not disclose open or deferred defects at time of deployment, presenting an incomplete picture to governance bodies
Defect records are purged or archived in inaccessible systems shortly after project closure, making retrospective examination impossible
suggestedSources: Test management and defect tracking tools (Jira Software, Azure DevOps, ServiceNow, HP ALM)
Project closure reports in PMO repository
Change Advisory Board (CAB) meeting packs which typically include test completion summaries
Automated CI/CD pipeline test reports (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Azure Pipelines)
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF/XLSX) from defect tracking tool; Test completion report in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: User Acceptance Testing (UAT) Sign-off Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation evidencing that business users validated the system against agreed requirements and accepted it as fit for purpose prior to deployment, directly satisfying the regulatory obligation that the system 'meets user requirements'.
whatGoodLooksLike: UAT sign-off is a named, dated signature (wet or digital) from an authorised Business Owner or delegated representative — not a generic IT team approval or automated system closure
Sign-off document references the specific version of requirements or business specifications against which UAT was conducted, establishing a clear chain from requirements to acceptance

UAT completion report distinguishes between full acceptance, conditional acceptance (with documented conditions and resolution plan), and rejection — nuanced outcomes that demonstrate genuine business engagement
Evidence shows business users actually participated in test execution (e.g., tester attribution in UAT test cases, UAT session attendance records) rather than rubber-stamping IT-conducted tests
For regulatory or compliance-driven systems, sign-off includes confirmation from the Compliance or Risk function in addition to the business user
commonGaps: UAT sign-off is issued by the IT Project Manager or Development Lead rather than a business representative, conflating the developer and the user and rendering the control ineffective
