Sign-off is a blanket approval email that does not reference specific requirements, versions, or test scope, making it impossible to verify what was actually accepted
Institutions conduct 'UAT' but it is indistinguishable from SIT — business users are not involved, test cases are purely technical, and sign-off is procedural rather than substantive
UAT sign-off is obtained after the system has already been deployed or during parallel run, rather than as a prerequisite gate to production deployment
Sign-off records exist for major projects but are absent for significant enhancements or regulatory-driven changes, creating unexplained gaps in the audit trail
suggestedSources: Project management repository (SharePoint, Confluence, Jira)
Change Management system deployment approval records (ServiceNow Change)
Project Steering Committee or CAB approval packs
Business unit document management systems
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with wet or digitally certified signature; or workflow-based approval record exported from project management tool

evidenceItems.name: Sensitive Test Data Authorisation and Handling Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Policy, procedures, and supporting records governing the authorisation, masking/anonymisation, access control, and monitoring of sensitive data used in test environments — directly satisfying the regulatory obligation for 'proper authorisation procedures and adequate measures to prevent unauthorised disclosure'.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal data masking or anonymisation standard exists and specifies which data classification levels require masking before use in non-production environments, with defined masking techniques (e.g., tokenisation, substitution, generalisation) per data type

Where production data is exceptionally approved for test use, a documented authorisation process requires sign-off from the Data Owner, Privacy/Compliance Officer, and CISO, with the business justification and duration recorded
Access to test environments containing sensitive data is restricted to named individuals via role-based access controls, with access provisioning and de-provisioning records maintained
Test environment access logs are retained and subject to periodic review, with anomalous access (off-hours, bulk downloads) generating alerts reviewed by the Security Operations function
Post-testing data disposal procedures are documented and evidenced — confirming that sensitive test data is purged or re-anonymised after the testing cycle concludes
commonGaps: Institution uses production data in test environments routinely without a formal authorisation process, treating it as an operational norm rather than an exceptional, controlled activity
Data masking procedures exist in policy but technical implementation is incomplete — spot checks reveal unmasked customer identifiers (NRIC, account numbers) in test databases
Access to test environments is managed by the development team without independent oversight, and access lists have not been reviewed or recertified since the environment was provisioned
No audit logging is enabled on test databases or file shares containing sensitive test data, making it impossible to detect or investigate unauthorised access retrospectively
Sensitive test data disposal is not documented or evidenced — test environments are simply decommissioned or left idle without confirmed data sanitisation, creating residual disclosure risk
suggestedSources: Data governance platform or data catalogue (Collibra, Alation)
Identity and Access Management (IAM) system provisioning logs (Active Directory, CyberArk, SailPoint)
Database Activity Monitoring (DAM) or SIEM audit logs (Wazuh, Splunk, IBM QRadar)
Data masking tool configuration and job execution logs (Informatica, Delphix, IBM Optim)
Privacy / Compliance team records for data handling authorisation approvals
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for policy and procedures; System exports (XLSX/CSV) for access logs, authorisation records, and disposal confirmations

evidenceItems.name: Test Environment Controls and Segregation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and technical evidence demonstrating that test environments are properly segregated from production, that environment configurations are controlled, and that access and change controls prevent contamination of production systems during testing activities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network architecture diagrams confirm logical and/or physical segregation between production and non-production environments, with firewall rules preventing direct data flows from test to production without controlled interfaces
Test environment provisioning and decommissioning follow a documented process with Configuration Management Database (CMDB) entries tracking environment status, ownership, and approved users
Environment-specific access control matrices show that production access rights are not replicated in test environments and that developers do not have elevated access to production systems

Test environment configuration is baselined and changes are subject to a lightweight change management process, preventing uncontrolled drift that could invalidate test results or introduce security gaps
Periodic reconciliation between CMDB test environment inventory and actually running environments is performed and evidenced, identifying and remediating unauthorised or forgotten environments
commonGaps: Test and production environments share common infrastructure (same database servers, shared network segments) with only logical separation via naming conventions, providing insufficient isolation
Developer accounts have standing access to both test and production environments, creating a pathway for accidental or deliberate contamination of production during testing
Test environments are provisioned informally and are not inventoried in the CMDB, resulting in shadow environments that contain sensitive data but are outside the control framework

Test environment configurations diverge significantly from production over time due to uncontrolled changes, causing tests to pass in environments that do not reflect production conditions and undermining robustness assurances
Decommissioned test environments are not confirmed as destroyed — virtual machine snapshots or database backups containing sensitive data persist on shared storage without oversight
suggestedSources: CMDB (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Freshservice) environment inventory records
Network Operations team — firewall rule sets and network topology diagrams
Cloud management platform (AWS Control Tower, Azure Management Groups, GCP Resource Hierarchy) for cloud-hosted test environments
IAM system — access provisioning records and access reviews for non-production environments
Virtualisation platform (VMware vCenter, Hyper-V) or container orchestration (Kubernetes namespace configurations) for environment lifecycle records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for architecture diagrams and procedures; System exports for CMDB records and access control matrices

auditTips: Construct a deployment traceability package for each system released in the examination period: for every change deployed to production, assemble the test plan, test results, defect log, UAT sign-off, and CAB approval into a single linked artefact. BNM examiners will ask to trace a specific deployment end-to-end — institutions that can do this in under 30 minutes demonstrate a mature methodology; those that cannot signal process weakness regardless of what policy documents say.

Before the examination, conduct a random sample check of at least 5-10 production deployments from the past 12 months and verify that each has a corresponding test completion report with UAT sign-off dated before the deployment date. This directly validates that the testing methodology is applied consistently, not just for high-profile projects, and will surface gaps before the examiner finds them.

Prepare a sensitive test data register that inventories all instances where production or sensitive data was used in non-production environments during the examination period, cross-referencing each instance against the authorisation record, masking status, access list, and disposal confirmation. Examiners specifically target the data handling obligation — an inability to produce this register suggests the control does not exist in practice.

Ensure that test environment access logs are readily retrievable for the examination period and have a documented review cadence. If the institution uses a SIEM (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk), prepare a saved query or dashboard that demonstrates ongoing monitoring of non-production environment access. The absence of monitoring evidence is treated by examiners as equivalent to the absence of the control itself.

Cross-reference the testing methodology policy against the institution's actual SDLC and change management procedures to identify any inconsistencies — particularly around agile/DevOps pipelines, cloud deployments, and vendor-managed systems that may operate outside the documented framework. Examiners will probe whether edge cases (hotfixes, emergency changes, SaaS updates) are genuinely covered, and any carve-out represents a regulatory gap.

Brief the Business Owners who signed UAT approvals in the examination period so they can credibly articulate their role in the testing process if interviewed. BNM examiners may conduct walkthroughs with business stakeholders to verify that UAT sign-offs reflect genuine business validation rather than procedural rubber-stamping — misalignment between documentation and stakeholder understanding is a significant red flag.

Validate that your test methodology policy explicitly addresses performance and security testing requirements, not just functional testing. Clause 10.8 requires the system to 'perform robustly', which examiners interpret as encompassing load, stress, and security validation. If performance testing results and security test reports (e.g., VAPT findings and remediation evidence) cannot be linked to the testing methodology and deployment approvals, the robustness obligation will be assessed as unmet.

10.9.title: Scope of System Testing
10.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether institutions have defined and applied a sufficiently comprehensive testing scope that is proportionate to the risk and complexity of the system under development or acquisition. A key red flag is a one-size-fits-all testing approach that mechanically lists all test types without demonstrating applicability decisions — examiners expect to see documented rationale for which test types were included or excluded for each release. Examiners scrutinise whether application security testing (e.g., VAPT, SAST/DAST) is consistently applied to customer-facing and internet-accessible systems, and whether stress and load testing results are tied to capacity planning decisions. They also verify that exception and negative testing has been performed, as this is frequently omitted by institutions that focus only on happy-path scenarios. Cross-referencing test scope coverage against the system's risk classification and change impact assessment is a standard examination technique.

evidenceItems.name: System Testing Strategy and Scope Document
evidenceItems.description: A formal document defining the testing scope for each system or significant release, identifying which test types from the RMiT 10.9 catalogue apply and providing documented rationale for any exclusions. Demonstrates that testing coverage decisions are deliberate and risk-based rather than arbitrary.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each release or project has a distinct test strategy document that explicitly maps to the applicable test types (unit, integration, UAT, security, stress/load, regression, exception, negative)
Exclusions are documented with clear, risk-based justification — e.g., stress testing excluded for a low-volume internal admin tool with defined SLA thresholds
The document is reviewed and signed off by the IT project manager, QA lead, and business system owner prior to test execution commencing
Test scope is linked to the system risk classification and impact assessment from the change management record
Version history is maintained showing how the scope was revised as requirements evolved during the project lifecycle
commonGaps: A generic test plan template is used across all projects without tailoring the test type selection to the specific system risk profile
Application security testing is listed as 'applicable' but no evidence exists showing it was actually planned, scheduled, or executed within the test cycle
Test strategy documents are completed retrospectively after testing has already commenced, undermining their credibility as governance artefacts
Exclusion rationale is absent — test types are simply omitted from the plan with no documented justification
The document is not formally approved and lacks evidence of stakeholder sign-off before test execution
suggestedSources: Project management platform (Jira, Azure DevOps, Confluence) — project wiki or attached test plan documents
GRC platform (ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — linked to change request records
SharePoint project governance library under the SDLC or change management folder structure
ITSM change records (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy) as attachments to the RFC (Request for Change)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Unit and Integration Test Results
evidenceItems.description: Automated or manual test execution records evidencing that unit testing (component-level) and integration testing (interface and data flow between modules) were performed. Demonstrates that defects are caught at the earliest and most cost-effective stages of the development lifecycle.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated unit test reports generated by CI/CD pipeline tools (e.g., JUnit, pytest, Jest) with pass/fail counts, code coverage percentage, and timestamps aligned to the build version
Integration test scripts reference specific interface contracts, API endpoints, or message queues being validated, with expected versus actual results documented
Test coverage metrics meet the institution's defined minimum threshold (e.g., ≥80% code coverage for critical modules) and exceptions are formally approved
Defects identified during unit and integration testing are logged in the defect tracking system with severity ratings and resolution status prior to UAT commencement
Test results are traceable to specific user stories, functional requirements, or system design specifications via traceability matrix
commonGaps: Unit testing is performed informally by developers with no structured output — verbal confirmation of testing replaces documented evidence
Integration testing focuses only on success paths and does not validate error handling, timeout scenarios, or degraded service responses between integrated components
CI/CD pipeline test reports are not retained or archived after deployment, making historical evidence unavailable during examination
Code coverage metrics are not tracked or are below the institution's own defined threshold without approved exceptions
Defects found during unit/integration testing are fixed and closed without being formally logged, removing the audit trail of issues discovered and resolved
suggestedSources: CI/CD pipeline tools (Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, Azure Pipelines) — build and test artefact repositories
Code quality platforms (SonarQube, Coverity) — unit test coverage dashboards and historical reports
Defect tracking system (Jira, Azure DevOps Boards, HP ALM/Quality Center) — defect logs with test phase attribution
Source code repository (GitLab, GitHub, Bitbucket) — pull request merge history with linked test run results
evidenceItems.format: System-generated HTML/XML test reports or exported PDF; defect logs as spreadsheet or system export

evidenceItems.name: User Acceptance Testing (UAT) Sign-Off Records
evidenceItems.description: Business-owner-led test execution records demonstrating that end-user representatives validated that the system meets business requirements and operational expectations before production deployment. Demonstrates segregation of testing responsibility between IT and business functions.
whatGoodLooksLike: UAT test scripts are authored by business representatives (not solely IT), covering end-to-end business workflows including data entry, processing, and output validation
UAT results are formally documented with individual test case pass/fail status, tester name, execution date, and observed versus expected outcome
Outstanding defects at UAT completion are risk-assessed and formally accepted or escalated by the business system owner with documented sign-off
UAT environment is isolated from production and populated with representative, masked, or synthetic data rather than live customer data
Final UAT sign-off letter or approval record is dated, signed by the authorised business representative, and referenced in the go-live approval documentation
commonGaps: UAT is conducted exclusively by IT staff, with no genuine business user involvement, negating the independence and business-validation purpose of UAT
UAT sign-off is obtained via informal email rather than a structured approval record, making it difficult to evidence during examination
UAT test cases cover only core happy-path scenarios and do not reflect edge cases, volume data, or real-world operational exceptions
UAT is conducted in the development or staging environment that has not been validated as representative of production configuration
Open defects at time of UAT sign-off are undocumented or accepted without a formal risk acceptance record from the business owner
suggestedSources: Test management tool (HP ALM/Quality Center, Zephyr for Jira, TestRail) — UAT execution and sign-off records
Project governance repository on SharePoint — UAT sign-off letters scanned as PDFs
ITSM change management record (ServiceNow) — UAT approval as a mandatory gate before change is approved for deployment
Email archive (Microsoft 365 Exchange) — formal UAT approval communications from business system owner
evidenceItems.format: PDF sign-off letter and spreadsheet/system export of test case results

evidenceItems.name: Application Security Testing Reports (VAPT/SAST/DAST)
evidenceItems.description: Technical security assessment reports covering vulnerability and penetration testing (VAPT), static application security testing (SAST), and/or dynamic application security testing (DAST) performed prior to production deployment. Demonstrates that security is embedded in the testing lifecycle and that known vulnerabilities are remediated or risk-accepted before go-live.
whatGoodLooksLike: SAST scans are integrated into the CI/CD pipeline and executed automatically on every build, with results reviewed by a security engineer and critical/high findings blocking deployment
DAST or VAPT is conducted by an independent team (internal security function or accredited third-party vendor) against a production-equivalent environment
The security test report includes a clear findings register with CVSS or institution-defined severity ratings, remediation recommendations, and retest confirmation for resolved findings
Residual risk acceptance for findings not remediated before go-live is formally documented and approved by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or equivalent authority
Security testing scope explicitly covers OWASP Top 10 categories for web applications and relevant threat vectors for the system's technology stack and deployment context
commonGaps: Application security testing is only performed annually or during major releases, with no security testing evidence for incremental or patch releases that introduce new code
VAPT reports are provided by a third party but remediation tracking is absent — there is no evidence of what was fixed, when, and how fixes were validated
Security testing is conducted against a development environment that does not reflect production configuration, rendering findings potentially incomplete or inaccurate
Critical or high-severity findings are carried forward to production without formal risk acceptance documentation from authorised management
SAST tools are deployed but findings are consistently suppressed or marked as false positives without documented security engineer review and justification
suggestedSources: Security testing platform (Veracode, Checkmarx, Snyk) — SAST scan reports and pipeline gate results
VAPT vendor reports — third-party penetration test reports with formal retest confirmation letters
ITSM vulnerability management module (ServiceNow VR, Qualys, Tenable.io) — findings register with remediation tracking
GRC platform — security risk acceptance records linked to individual findings and system deployment records
CI/CD pipeline artefact storage — automated DAST scan reports (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite Enterprise) generated per build
evidenceItems.format: PDF security assessment report; findings register as spreadsheet or GRC system export

evidenceItems.name: Stress, Load, and Performance Testing Results
evidenceItems.description: Technical test records demonstrating that systems were subjected to stress and load testing to validate performance under peak and beyond-peak conditions prior to production deployment. Demonstrates that capacity planning decisions are empirically grounded and that service degradation behaviours are understood before go-live.
whatGoodLooksLike: Load test scenarios are defined based on projected peak transaction volumes, concurrent user counts, and business event triggers (e.g., salary crediting day, regulatory reporting deadlines), not generic assumptions
Stress test results document the system's behaviour at breaking point — including the failure mode, recovery time, and whether graceful degradation mechanisms activated as designed
Performance baselines (response time, throughput, error rate) are defined before testing and test results are explicitly compared against these thresholds, with pass/fail determination documented
Test results are reviewed by both the IT infrastructure team and the business owner, with infrastructure sizing or application tuning actions traceable to test findings
Performance test evidence is retained alongside capacity planning records to demonstrate alignment between observed system behaviour and infrastructure provisioning decisions
commonGaps: Stress and load testing is documented in the test plan as applicable but execution evidence is absent — tests were either not performed or results were not retained
Load tests are run with unrealistically low concurrent user counts or transaction volumes that do not reflect actual peak business conditions
Test results show performance threshold breaches (e.g., response times exceeding SLA) but no remediation or risk acceptance record exists before the system was promoted to production
Stress testing is performed in isolation on individual components without testing integrated end-to-end system behaviour under load
Performance testing is treated as a one-time pre-launch activity with no evidence of regression load testing after significant code changes or infrastructure modifications
suggestedSources: Performance testing tools (Apache JMeter, Gatling, k6, LoadRunner) — test execution reports and raw results exported as HTML or CSV
APM platforms (Dynatrace, AppDynamics, Datadog) — performance dashboards and reports from test execution periods
Infrastructure monitoring platform (Grafana, Zabbix, PRTG) — resource utilisation reports captured during load test windows
Capacity planning records on SharePoint or ITSM — linking test outcomes to provisioning decisions
Cloud provider performance analytics (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) — if system is hosted in cloud or hybrid environment
evidenceItems.format: HTML/PDF test execution report with graphs; infrastructure monitoring export as PDF or image archive

evidenceItems.name: Regression, Exception, and Negative Test Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Test records demonstrating that regression testing (re-validation of existing functionality after changes), exception testing (handling of unexpected or erroneous inputs), and negative testing (system behaviour when conditions outside design parameters are applied) were executed. Demonstrates that the institution verifies system stability and robustness, not merely new feature correctness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regression test suites are maintained and version-controlled, with test case scope reviewed and updated at each release cycle to reflect the current production state of the system
Exception test cases explicitly cover invalid inputs, boundary values, null/empty fields, malformed data formats, and unexpected user action sequences with expected error handling outcomes documented

Negative test scenarios include unauthorised access attempts, privilege escalation, session management abuse, and API calls with missing or malformed parameters — particularly for customer-facing or financial systems
Regression test execution is automated where feasible, with automated suite run reports generated per release and deviations from baseline flagged for investigation
Test case traceability matrix maps regression, exception, and negative test cases to specific requirements and prior defects to demonstrate that known failure modes are continuously re-validated
commonGaps: Regression testing is limited to re-running the same subset of UAT scripts and does not cover areas of the system not directly modified, missing potential side-effect defects introduced by changes
Exception and negative test cases are absent from the test plan — testing is entirely focused on validating that correct inputs produce correct outputs, with no validation of error handling paths
Regression test suites are not updated when new features are released, causing the suite to become stale and no longer representative of the production system's full functional surface
Exception testing covers input validation at the UI layer only and does not test API-level or backend data processing error handling, leaving server-side vulnerabilities untested
Negative testing evidence is conflated with UAT results — there is no distinct record demonstrating that out-of-bounds and adversarial scenarios were specifically exercised
suggestedSources: Test management tool (TestRail, Zephyr for Jira, HP ALM) — regression, exception, and negative test case libraries with execution history
CI/CD pipeline — automated regression suite run reports archived per build or release tag
Defect tracking system (Jira, Azure DevOps) — defects raised during regression and exception testing cycles, linked to test case IDs
Requirements traceability matrix maintained in Confluence, SharePoint, or the GRC platform — linking test cases to functional requirements and historical defects
API testing platforms (Postman, REST-assured, Karate) — negative and exception scenario test collections with run results exported as reports
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system export for test case execution records; automated reports as HTML/PDF; traceability matrix as XLSX

auditTips: Map each system release in scope to a test scope decision register that explicitly records which of the seven test types (unit, integration, UAT, application security, stress/load, regression, exception/negative) are applicable, with documented rationale for any exclusions — this directly addresses the 'where applicable' qualifier in the clause and demonstrates that applicability decisions are deliberate, not accidental omissions.

Cross-reference the test scope coverage against the institution's system risk classification framework: high-risk or customer-facing systems should evidence all seven test types, while lower-risk internal tools may have fewer — an examiner will immediately question why a public-facing payment application omitted VAPT or stress testing, so prepare that justification in advance.

Establish a pre-production deployment checklist that gates each release on mandatory evidence submission — specifically requiring signed test reports for each applicable test type before the change approval board (CAB) authorises go-live; this creates a natural, auditable control point that demonstrates governance over the testing lifecycle.

Compile a testing evidence package for each significant release that consolidates all applicable test artefacts (test strategy, execution results, defect closure evidence, sign-off records) into a single retrievable location — during examination, the inability to quickly produce a complete evidence package for a specific release is itself treated as a governance weakness, independent of whether testing was actually performed.

Audit the application security testing programme specifically for completeness — examiners frequently find that institutions perform VAPT for new systems but have no evidence of security regression testing after subsequent releases; confirm that your SDLC policy mandates security testing at each major release and that the CI/CD pipeline enforces SAST gates that cannot be bypassed without a documented exception.

Validate that stress and load testing scenarios are anchored to real business volume projections — pull transaction volume data from your core banking or transaction processing systems and confirm that load test parameters exceed peak historical volumes by a defined margin (e.g., 120% of highest observed daily peak); testing at unrealistically low volumes is a common finding that undermines the credibility of the entire performance testing programme.

Ensure that exception and negative test cases are explicitly labelled and segregated from positive test cases in your test management system — examiners will specifically request to see evidence of negative and exception testing, and presenting a mixed test execution report where these cases cannot be distinguished from standard functional tests will require you to manually evidence the coverage, which is time-consuming and risks appearing incomplete.

10.10.title: Source Code Review for Critical Systems
10.10.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether a formal, documented source code review process exists and is consistently applied to all changes affecting critical systems — not selectively or on a best-efforts basis. A key concern is whether the institution has a defensible definition of 'critical systems' and whether that inventory is current and approved at an appropriate governance level. Examiners will probe whether reviews are substantive and technically credible, distinguishing between rubber-stamp approvals and genuine security-focused code analysis. Red flags include ad-hoc or developer self-review practices, absence of recognised secure coding standards (e.g., OWASP, CERT), and change records that show production deployments with no corresponding review evidence. The assessment will also extend to whether findings from code reviews are tracked to resolution before deployment, confirming that the review gate is real and not a post-hoc compliance exercise.

evidenceItems.name: Source Code Review Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy and associated operational procedure that mandates source code review for all changes to critical systems, specifying scope, reviewer eligibility, review standards, and mandatory gates before deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly scopes 'critical systems' by reference to the institution's approved critical system inventory and defines what constitutes a 'change' subject to review (including hotfixes and emergency patches)
Procedure specifies which recognised secure coding standards are adopted (e.g., OWASP Secure Coding Practices, CERT Coding Standards, SANS CWE Top 25) and how compliance with them is assessed
Reviewer qualification criteria are defined, including independence requirements — reviewers must be separate from the code author for critical systems
Policy is version-controlled, board- or senior-management approved, reviewed at least annually, and cross-referenced to the change management policy
Escalation and exception-handling procedures are documented, including who may approve a waiver and what compensating controls must be applied
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not explicitly mandate source code review for emergency or out-of-band changes, creating a loophole for the majority of high-risk deployments
The definition of 'critical systems' in the policy is vague or inconsistent with the institution's actual critical system register, making scope disputes possible during examination
Secure coding standards referenced are outdated (e.g., referencing OWASP 2013 rather than current guidance) or are named but not operationalised into a checklist or tool configuration
Reviewer independence requirement is absent, permitting developers to self-review their own code on critical systems
Policy has not been reviewed or updated following material changes to the technology landscape, such as adoption of cloud-native development or containerised workloads
suggestedSources: Information Security or Technology Risk department — policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, GRC platform such as ServiceNow GRC or Archer)
IT Governance or Change Advisory Board secretariat for approval records
Group or enterprise policy management system for version history and review cycle evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems Inventory
evidenceItems.description: The institution's approved register of systems classified as critical, which defines the scope of systems to which Clause 10.10 applies. This inventory must be current, formally approved, and used as the reference point for change and review gate decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory is formally approved by a senior governance body (e.g., Technology Risk Committee or CISO) and reviewed at least annually or upon material change to the technology environment
Each entry includes system name, business function, criticality classification rationale, system owner, and technology stack sufficient to inform what type of code review tooling is applicable
Inventory is actively referenced by the change management process — ITSM tickets for changes to listed systems automatically trigger the source code review requirement
Inventory aligns with the institution's Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery designations, providing consistency across regulatory domains
An audit trail exists showing how systems were assessed for criticality, including any systems that were considered but not classified as critical, with documented rationale
commonGaps: Inventory has not been updated to reflect recent system acquisitions, decommissions, or re-platforming projects, meaning the review scope used in practice does not match the approved register
Criticality classification is based solely on regulatory or financial thresholds and does not account for operational dependency or data sensitivity, leaving operationally critical systems unscoped
The inventory exists as a static document with no integration into ITSM or change management tooling, so its use as a gatekeeping mechanism depends entirely on individual awareness
No documented rationale exists for systems excluded from the critical classification, making it impossible for auditors to assess whether the scoping decision was reasonable
Ownership of the inventory is unclear, with no assigned accountable officer responsible for keeping it current
suggestedSources: Technology Risk or IT Governance team — maintained in GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow, OneTrust) or CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB)
Business Continuity Management team for cross-reference with BCP/DR asset register
Enterprise Architecture repository for system-of-record classification
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export with version history

evidenceItems.name: Source Code Review Records per Change
evidenceItems.description: Individual review records — one per change to a critical system — demonstrating that a substantive source code review was conducted, by a qualified independent reviewer, prior to the change being introduced to production. These are the primary transactional evidence of compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each record is traceable to a specific change request (CR number or ticket ID) and contains the reviewer's identity, date of review, systems and modules reviewed, and a clear approve or reject decision
Review records reference the specific secure coding standard or checklist used and document which checks were performed, not merely a pass/fail checkbox
Records show that the reviewer is independent of the code author — reviewer and developer names are both captured and do not match for critical system changes

Where automated static analysis tools (e.g., SonarQube, Checkmarx, Veracode) are used, the tool scan report is attached or linked, and the reviewer has explicitly assessed and signed off on the findings rather than auto-approving
The timestamp of the review approval precedes the change deployment timestamp in the ITSM system, confirming the review was a pre-deployment gate and not a post-facto exercise
commonGaps: Review records exist as informal email threads or verbal approvals with no structured documentation, making it impossible to demonstrate the substance of the review
Automated tool scan results are treated as sufficient evidence of review without any human assessment or sign-off, meaning findings may have been suppressed or ignored without accountability
Review records are missing for emergency changes or hotfixes, which are often the highest-risk changes precisely because they bypass normal controls
Reviewer identity is a shared account or team name rather than an individually accountable named person, preventing assessment of independence and qualification
The change deployment records in the ITSM system show deployment timestamps predating the code review approval, indicating the review was performed retrospectively
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — change request records with linked review artefacts
Code repository platform (e.g., GitLab, GitHub Enterprise, Bitbucket) — pull request or merge request approval history with reviewer comments
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools (e.g., SonarQube, Checkmarx, Veracode) — scan reports linked to build or deployment pipelines
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF or CSV) from ITSM and/or code repository platform

evidenceItems.name: Secure Coding Standards and Review Checklist
evidenceItems.description: The institution's adopted secure coding standard and the operational checklist or ruleset used by reviewers to assess whether code changes comply with recognised coding practices. This evidence demonstrates that reviews are substantive and standards-based, not ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: The standard explicitly references and adapts one or more recognised industry frameworks (e.g., OWASP Secure Coding Practices, CERT C/C++ or Java coding standards, NIST SP 800-218 Secure Software Development Framework)

The checklist is language- or platform-specific where applicable (e.g., separate checklists for Java microservices versus SQL stored procedures) and covers categories including input validation, authentication, cryptography, error handling, and dependency management
The checklist is formally version-controlled, approved by the CISO or equivalent, and referenced directly in the source code review procedure
Automated tool rulesets (e.g., SonarQube quality gate configurations) are documented and align with the manual checklist, ensuring consistency between automated and human review

The checklist has been updated within the past 12 months and specifically addresses risks relevant to the institution's technology stack (e.g., container security, API security, infrastructure-as-code if applicable)
commonGaps: A checklist exists but covers only generic OWASP Top 10 items without tailoring to the institution's specific languages, frameworks, or architectural patterns, reducing its practical utility
The checklist has not been updated in more than two years, meaning it does not address contemporary vulnerability classes relevant to the current technology environment
Automated tool rulesets are used in practice but have never been formally documented or approved, meaning the de facto review standard is opaque and cannot be assessed for adequacy
The checklist is optional or advisory in the procedure rather than mandatory, allowing reviewers to skip sections without documented justification
No evidence exists that reviewers have been trained on the checklist or that their understanding has been assessed, undermining confidence in the quality of reviews performed
suggestedSources: Information Security team — secure development standards maintained in SharePoint, Confluence, or equivalent knowledge management platform
Application Security or DevSecOps team — tool configuration repositories (e.g., SonarQube quality gate rules, Checkmarx query sets)
Training and Competency records from HR or Learning Management System for reviewer certification
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX checklist with version control; supplemented by tool configuration exports

evidenceItems.name: Code Review Finding Remediation and Closure Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that findings identified during source code reviews are formally tracked, assigned for remediation, and resolved before the change is permitted to proceed to production. This evidence confirms that the review gate has real consequence and is not a formality.
whatGoodLooksLike: A centralised defect or finding tracker (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow, or SAST tool defect management) captures all code review findings with severity classification, assigned owner, and due date
Evidence shows that critical or high severity findings block deployment — change records confirm that changes with unresolved high-severity findings were not promoted to production
Remediation records include a re-review or re-scan confirmation after the fix is applied, providing a closed-loop audit trail from finding to resolution
Risk acceptance decisions for findings deferred rather than remediated are documented with named approver, rationale, compensating controls, and expiry date — these do not exceed a defined threshold
Trend reporting on finding volumes, severities, and time-to-remediation is produced periodically and reviewed by Technology Risk or Governance Committee, demonstrating management oversight
commonGaps: Findings are recorded in the SAST tool but are not linked to the change request or deployment record, making it impossible to confirm findings were resolved before the change went live
Risk acceptance for unresolved findings is undocumented or approved at too low a level (e.g., by the developer's own team lead), with no independent oversight
Re-review after remediation is not consistently performed — the fix is assumed to be correct without validation, potentially introducing new vulnerabilities
No distinction is made between findings that block deployment and those that are informational, meaning the review process does not operationally prevent insecure code from reaching production
Finding data is not aggregated or reported to governance committees, so senior management has no visibility into systemic code quality or security risks across critical systems
suggestedSources: Application Security or DevSecOps team — defect tracking in Jira, Azure DevOps, or SAST platform defect management modules
Technology Risk Committee or equivalent governance body — meeting minutes and dashboards showing finding trend reports
Change Advisory Board records for evidence of deployment hold decisions linked to unresolved findings
evidenceItems.format: System export from defect tracker and/or SAST platform; governance committee minutes in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Reviewer Qualification and Independence Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that personnel conducting source code reviews of critical systems are suitably qualified and independent from the code authors, supporting the adequacy of the review as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined reviewer qualification framework exists specifying minimum experience, technical competencies, and any required certifications (e.g., CSSLP, GWEB, or equivalent) for reviewers of critical systems
HR or competency records confirm that assigned reviewers meet the qualification criteria, with training completion records for secure coding and application security topics
The institution maintains a current list of approved reviewers for critical system code reviews, reviewed and updated at least annually by the CISO or Security Architecture function
Code repository records (e.g., pull request history) show that for all critical system changes, the approving reviewer is a different individual from any contributor to that change
Where external reviewers or vendors are used, contracts include confidentiality obligations and the vendor's own qualifications are documented and assessed prior to engagement
commonGaps: No formal qualification criteria exist for code reviewers — any peer developer may approve changes to critical systems regardless of their security knowledge or experience
Training records are held only in informal email acknowledgements rather than an LMS, and cannot be produced systematically for a sample of reviewers during examination
The approved reviewer list has not been updated to reflect staff departures or role changes, meaning individuals who have left the security function may still appear as active approvers in the system
Independence is assessed informally — no automated enforcement in the code repository prevents a developer from approving their own changes, relying solely on manual discipline
For third-party developed components integrated into critical systems, no evidence exists that equivalent review standards were applied to the vendor's code before integration
suggestedSources: Human Resources — training records and competency assessments from LMS (e.g., Cornerstone, Workday Learning, or equivalent)
Information Security or CISO office — approved reviewer register
Code repository platform (e.g., GitLab, GitHub Enterprise) — branch protection rules and pull request approval configuration exports
Vendor Management or Procurement — third-party contracts and vendor security assessment records
evidenceItems.format: HR system export, approved reviewer register (PDF/Spreadsheet), repository configuration export

auditTips: Perform a transactional walk-through test: select a sample of 10-15 production changes to critical systems from the past 12 months across different system types and change categories (including at least two emergency changes), then trace each from the change request through to the code review record and deployment confirmation. Any chain that cannot be fully reconstructed will be a finding.

Validate that the critical systems inventory used to scope code review obligations matches the systems actually subject to review in practice — pull the ITSM change log for systems on the inventory and compare it against review records. Gaps between systems listed and systems with review evidence indicate either scope avoidance or an outdated inventory.

Test the independence control specifically: from the code repository pull request history, identify any instance where the approving reviewer is also listed as a commit author on the same change. Any such instance for a critical system is a direct breach of the independence requirement and must be remediated with evidence of compensating review.

Request the SonarQube or equivalent SAST tool's quality gate configuration and verify that the 'fail' conditions align with the institution's stated secure coding standards — many institutions claim tool-assisted review but have quality gates set to 'warn only', meaning no code change is ever actually blocked by the automated review.

Cross-reference code review finding remediation records against production deployment dates in the ITSM platform. Where a high-severity finding was raised and a deployment to the same system occurred within the same change window, obtain explicit evidence that the finding was resolved before deployment — this timeline check frequently uncovers instances where review was bypassed under time pressure.

Ask the compliance team to produce the most recent governance or risk committee report that includes code review metrics (finding volumes, severity trends, time-to-remediation). If no such report exists, the institution cannot demonstrate that management oversight of the code review control is operating — this is a governance gap independent of whether individual reviews are occurring.

For any third-party or vendor-supplied components integrated into critical systems within the review period, verify that the source code review procedure explicitly addresses vendor code and that evidence exists of either equivalent review or a documented risk acceptance signed at an appropriate level — vendor-supplied code is a common blind spot in otherwise mature code review programmes.

10.11.title: Independent Review of System Changes
10.11.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the financial institution has formalised, documented procedures that ensure system changes are reviewed and approved by parties independent of those who initiated or developed the change — mere peer review within the same team is a common red flag. Examiners will scrutinise the approval workflow to confirm that independence is structurally enforced, not just stated in policy. For material changes specifically, examiners will demand evidence that contingency and rollback plans were not only documented but actively tested before implementation, and that test outcomes were recorded and acted upon. A frequent finding is the absence of testing records for rollback plans, or plans that exist on paper but were never exercised. Examiners will also cross-reference change records against incident logs to identify material changes that caused disruption, then assess whether contingency plans were triggered and whether they were effective.

evidenceItems.name: Change Management Policy and Independent Review Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy document that defines the institution's change management lifecycle, including the formal requirement for independent review and approval of system changes. This demonstrates that a procedural framework exists, that independence is structurally defined (e.g., by segregation of duty rules), and that the policy is Board- or senior management-approved and periodically reviewed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines 'independence' — e.g., approver must not be from the same team or cost centre as the change initiator, with named roles or job functions listed
Change categorisation matrix is embedded in or linked from the policy, distinguishing minor, significant, and material changes with different approval thresholds for each
Policy references contingency plan requirements for material changes and mandates pre-implementation testing of rollback procedures
Document carries version control history, Board or IT Steering Committee approval signature, and a scheduled review cycle (at minimum annual)
Policy is cross-referenced to related procedures such as the Incident Management Policy, Release Management Standard, and BCP/DR framework
commonGaps: Policy defines independent review in generic terms without specifying which roles or committees constitute an independent approver, making structural independence unverifiable
Change categorisation criteria are absent or vague, so the heightened controls for 'material changes' are not consistently applied across the institution
Contingency plan obligations are buried in a separate document with no explicit link from the change policy, creating gaps in awareness and enforcement
Policy has not been reviewed or re-approved in over 12 months, or carries no evidence of senior management sign-off
Policy scope excludes cloud-hosted systems, vendor-managed platforms, or emergency changes, leaving significant change types ungoverned
suggestedSources: IT Governance SharePoint or intranet policy repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or local equivalent) policy register
Board IT Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes approving the policy
Chief Information Officer (CIO) or Head of IT Operations office
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Change Advisory Board (CAB) Terms of Reference and Membership Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal charter or terms of reference for the body responsible for independently reviewing and approving system changes (e.g., Change Advisory Board, Change Control Committee). This demonstrates that a standing, structured governance body exists with defined membership, quorum rules, and authority limits, and that membership is constituted to ensure independence from change implementers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Terms of Reference explicitly state that CAB members must not be the primary implementer of any change they vote to approve, with a formal conflict-of-interest declaration process
Membership spans multiple independent functions — e.g., IT Operations, Information Security, Business, Risk, and Compliance — preventing single-team dominance
Authority matrix defines which change categories require full CAB approval versus delegated approval, with material changes requiring CAB quorum as a minimum
TOR specifies meeting frequency (e.g., weekly standard CAB, on-demand for emergency changes) and mandates documented minutes for every session
Membership list is current, with named individuals and their functional roles confirmed against the latest organisation chart
commonGaps: CAB membership is drawn exclusively from IT operations staff, with no representation from Risk, Compliance, or Business units, undermining independence
Terms of Reference exist but have never been formally approved by senior management or the IT Steering Committee
No conflict-of-interest mechanism exists — change requestors routinely participate in approval votes for their own changes
Emergency or out-of-band changes bypass the CAB entirely with no compensating retrospective review documented
CAB is described in policy but meeting minutes cannot be produced, suggesting the body exists on paper only
suggestedSources: IT Governance or IT Operations department document repository
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) CAB configuration and membership records
IT Steering Committee or Board IT Committee secretariat for TOR approval records
HR or organisational chart system to verify member roles and reporting lines
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (TOR) plus system-exported membership roster

evidenceItems.name: System Change Records with Independent Approval Evidence
evidenceItems.description: The population of change tickets or change request records for a defined period (minimum 12 months), demonstrating that each change — and especially each material change — underwent independent review and received documented approval before implementation. This is the primary transactional evidence that the policy and CAB governance are operating as intended.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each change record shows a distinct requestor, implementer, and approver identity, with the ITSM system enforcing that the same user ID cannot fulfil more than one of these roles on a single ticket
Material changes carry a mandatory CAB approval record, including approver names, date of approval, and CAB meeting reference number linked to corresponding minutes
Change records include a completed pre-implementation checklist covering impact assessment, testing results, and rollback plan reference before the approved-for-deployment status is set
The ITSM system audit trail shows sequential workflow stages (Raised → Under Review → Approved → Implemented → Closed) with timestamps and user identities at each stage
A periodic management review report (e.g., monthly change metrics dashboard) is produced from the ITSM system summarising volumes, approval rates, and unauthorised change exceptions
commonGaps: Approval fields in the ITSM system are free-text rather than system-enforced, allowing a single user to self-approve changes without a workflow control
Change records for emergency or hotfix deployments are created retrospectively with no evidence of prior independent review
A material change classification exists in policy but change records show most high-impact changes logged as 'standard' or 'normal' to avoid the heavier approval process
CAB approval is recorded only as a date stamp with no record of who attended, what was discussed, or what conditions were imposed
Change records are purged or archived in a system that cannot produce an auditable export, making population testing impossible
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy, Freshservice)
CAB meeting minutes register maintained by the IT Governance secretariat
CI/CD pipeline approval logs (e.g., Azure DevOps, GitLab, Jenkins) for development-facing change gates
Release management register maintained by the Application Development or DevOps team
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/XLSX) or PDF report from ITSM platform with full audit trail

evidenceItems.name: Material Change Contingency and Rollback Plans
evidenceItems.description: Documented contingency plans (including rollback procedures) prepared specifically for material system changes, demonstrating that the institution has planned for unsuccessful implementation scenarios prior to go-live. Each plan should be change-specific or template-based with change-specific parameters completed, and must be approved as part of the change authorisation process.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each material change record references a uniquely identified contingency plan document that includes step-by-step rollback instructions, responsible owner, rollback decision authority, and maximum rollback time objective
Contingency plans specify clear rollback trigger criteria — e.g., specific error conditions, performance thresholds, or time limits after which rollback is initiated without further escalation
Plans identify the communication protocol during a failed implementation, including internal escalation paths and, where relevant, customer or regulatory notification obligations
Rollback plan is reviewed and signed off by the independent approver (CAB or equivalent) as a condition of change approval, evidenced in the change record
Plans are version-controlled and stored in a location accessible to on-call staff during the implementation window, with access confirmed in pre-implementation checklists
commonGaps: Contingency plans are generic templates with change-specific fields left blank, rendering them unusable as actual response guides during an incident
Plans exist for some material changes but are absent for others, with no systematic process to identify which changes qualify as material and therefore require a plan
Rollback procedures describe the goal (restore previous state) but not the steps, tools, or commands needed, making execution dependent on undocumented tribal knowledge
Contingency plans are stored only on the implementer's local drive or a non-versioned shared folder with no access controls, raising concerns about availability and integrity
Plans are approved at a point after the change is already implemented rather than as a pre-condition of go-live approval
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change record attachments section
IT Operations SharePoint or Confluence wiki (implementation runbooks)
Project management platform (Jira, Azure DevOps) linked to the release or deployment ticket
Configuration Management Database (CMDB) linked rollback documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX attached to or linked from the corresponding change record

evidenceItems.name: Rollback and Contingency Plan Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that contingency plans for material changes have been tested — not merely documented — prior to implementation. Test records may include pre-production environment test results, tabletop exercise outcomes, or simulation logs, and must show that test findings were reviewed and any deficiencies remediated before the change was approved for production.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test records are dated before the production implementation window and reference the specific change ID and contingency plan version being validated

Test outcomes document what was tested (full rollback execution, partial rollback, communication drill), the environment used (UAT, staging, DR), the result (pass/fail per step), and the elapsed time against the rollback time objective
Where test failures or deficiencies were identified, a remediation log shows the corrective action taken and a retest result before production approval was granted
Test records are signed off by an independent reviewer (e.g., a Test Manager or QA Lead separate from the implementer) confirming the rollback was validated

For high-frequency change types (e.g., database schema changes, core banking patches), a periodic tabletop or simulation schedule is maintained with results archived alongside individual change records
commonGaps: Contingency plans are marked 'tested' on the change record but no supporting test evidence (scripts, logs, sign-offs) can be produced to substantiate the assertion
Testing is performed only in a development environment that does not replicate production data volumes or system configurations, undermining the validity of rollback time estimates
Test records exist for some material changes but the institution cannot demonstrate a systematic, policy-driven requirement that testing is mandatory before approval
Test outcomes show failures that were not remediated, with the change approved for production regardless, indicating the test gate is not enforced
Rollback time recorded during testing significantly exceeds the maximum tolerable downtime for the affected system, with no compensating risk acceptance or plan adjustment
suggestedSources: Test Management platform (e.g., Xray for Jira, Azure Test Plans, HP ALM/Quality Center)
UAT or staging environment execution logs exported from CI/CD pipeline
IT Operations or Release Management team test sign-off registers
CAB meeting minutes that reference test results as a condition of approval
evidenceItems.format: System-exported test execution report (PDF/HTML) or signed test completion certificate (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Post-Implementation Review Reports for Material Changes
evidenceItems.description: Structured post-implementation review (PIR) reports prepared after material system changes, documenting whether the implementation was successful, whether any issues occurred, whether contingency plans were invoked, and what lessons were learned. This demonstrates a closed-loop governance process and provides BNM examiners with evidence that the institution monitors change outcomes and continuously improves its procedures.
whatGoodLooksLike: PIR is completed within a defined timeframe after go-live (e.g., within five business days) and the completion deadline is tracked in the ITSM system as a mandatory post-implementation task
Report documents the actual implementation outcome against the planned outcome, including any deviations, errors encountered, and whether rollback was fully or partially invoked

Where business disruption occurred, the PIR cross-references the incident record, quantifies business impact, and documents the root cause and corrective actions assigned to named owners with due dates
Lessons learned are formally captured, escalated to the CAB or IT Steering Committee, and traceable to subsequent policy or procedure updates — not filed and forgotten
PIRs are aggregated into a periodic management report (e.g., quarterly change effectiveness review) that is presented to senior management or the IT Steering Committee
commonGaps: PIR is performed informally or only verbally, with no written record produced or retained in the change record
PIRs are completed only when something goes wrong, rather than systematically for all material changes, making the process reactive rather than embedded
Lessons learned sections are consistently left blank or contain generic entries (e.g., 'implementation successful — no issues') without genuine analysis
PIR findings are not tracked to closure, so the same issues recur across multiple material changes without systemic remediation
PIRs are not presented to senior management, meaning leadership has no visibility into change implementation quality trends or recurring weaknesses
suggestedSources: ITSM platform post-implementation review task linked to the parent change record
IT Steering Committee or CAB meeting minutes where PIR summaries are tabled
Project Management Office (PMO) closure reports for programme-level material changes
IT Risk and Compliance team's change effectiveness monitoring dashboard or register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX report attached to the change record and tabled at governance forum

auditTips: Pull a statistically representative sample of change records from the past 12 months — including at least all changes classified as 'material' — and trace each from submission through independent approval to implementation, verifying that the approver identity is structurally independent of the requestor and implementer in the ITSM system rather than just nominally different.

Request a live demonstration of the ITSM change workflow to confirm that system-enforced controls (role separation, mandatory fields, approval gates) prevent self-approval — policy documents alone do not prove that independence is operationally enforced.

Cross-reference the institution's incident register against the change record population for the same period: any incident that occurred within 72 hours of a change implementation should have a corresponding PIR and, for material changes, a rollback plan invocation record or a documented decision not to invoke rollback with stated rationale.

For every material change in the sample, physically locate and review the contingency plan document — confirm it is change-specific (not a blank template), was approved before the implementation window, and carries a test completion record dated prior to production go-live; absence of any one of these three elements is a finding.

Interview the CAB chair or secretary and request the last three months of CAB meeting minutes; verify that minutes record individual attendee names (not just quorum counts), the specific changes discussed, any conditions imposed on approval, and that minutes are formally signed off — this distinguishes an active governance body from a rubber-stamp process.

Ask the institution to demonstrate how it identifies and classifies a change as 'material': request the classification criteria, then apply them to a sample of recently implemented changes to test whether any changes that should have been classified as material were downgraded to avoid heightened controls — systemic under-classification is a significant red flag for BNM examiners.

Verify that rollback test records reference the production-equivalent environment and that the rollback time achieved during testing falls within the maximum tolerable downtime for the affected system as documented in the institution's BIA; where testing was only performed in a non-representative environment, flag this as a gap in the adequacy of contingency plan validation.

10.12.title: Third Party Service Provider System Changes
10.12.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has operationalised contractual control over third party service providers (TPSPs) managing critical systems — not merely whether contracts exist, but whether those contracts are enforced in practice. Examiners specifically verify that advance change notification obligations are defined with measurable notice periods, that secure-by-design attestations are substantiated by evidence of the TPSP's SDLC controls rather than self-declarations alone, and that source code escrow or equivalent access arrangements are tested and not merely documented. A key red flag is the presence of boilerplate contract language without corresponding operational procedures, logs of received notifications, or evidence of the institution acting on TPSP change notices. Examiners will also cross-check whether the scope of 'critical systems' subject to clause 10.12 controls is consistent with the institution's own critical system register, and probe whether supply chain cyber risk from the TPSP propagating into the institution's environment has been considered and mitigated.

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Contracts with Change Notification Obligations
evidenceItems.description: Executed contracts or service agreements with TPSPs that develop or maintain critical systems, containing explicit clauses mandating advance notification of system changes that may impact the financial institution's IT environment, including defined minimum notice periods and escalation procedures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contract contains a dedicated change management clause specifying a minimum notice period (e.g., 30 days for major changes, 5 days for urgent patches) tailored to the criticality of the system
Notification obligations are bi-directional: TPSP must notify of planned changes, and the institution retains a right to approve, defer, or reject changes to critical systems
Contract defines the scope of 'changes' triggering notification (e.g., infrastructure upgrades, dependency library updates, architecture changes, deployment to shared environments)
Contract references an agreed change classification taxonomy (e.g., emergency, standard, major) with distinct notice requirements per class
Contracts are reviewed and re-executed or amended at regular intervals (e.g., annually or upon service renewal) with evidence of legal and IT risk sign-off
commonGaps: Contract contains generic change management language without specifying minimum notice periods, leaving 'sufficient notice' undefined and unenforceable
Change notification obligations exist only in the SLA schedule rather than the master agreement, creating ambiguity about legal enforceability
Contracts are outdated and predate the institution's formal classification of the system as 'critical', meaning the stricter 10.12 obligations were never incorporated
Notification scope is limited to outages or incidents and does not explicitly cover planned system changes, upgrades, or dependency modifications
No evidence of the institution having conducted a contract gap review against RMiT 10.12 requirements; contracts have never been tested against the clause's three sub-obligations
suggestedSources: Legal / Contract Management team — centralised contract repository (e.g., Ironclad, ContractSafe, or SharePoint contract library)
Procurement department — vendor agreement register with amendment history
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — third party risk module with linked contract artefacts
Technology Risk team — critical system register cross-referenced against TPSP inventory
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed contract) + DOCX (contract schedule or SLA annex)

evidenceItems.name: TPSP Change Notification Log and Institution Response Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational records evidencing that TPSPs have actually provided advance change notifications in accordance with contractual obligations, and that the institution has reviewed, acted upon, and tracked those notifications for critical systems over the review period.
whatGoodLooksLike: A maintained change notification register or ITSM ticket log (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) that records each TPSP-initiated change notice with date received, system affected, change description, scheduled date, and institution's disposition (approved/deferred/rejected)
Email or portal-based notification records from TPSPs that are timestamped and demonstrate compliance with contractual notice periods (e.g., notification received 35 days ahead of a major release)
Evidence of an internal review workflow triggered by each TPSP notification, including IT risk assessment sign-off and, where applicable, CAB (Change Advisory Board) approval
Documented instances where the institution exercised its right to defer or reject a TPSP change, demonstrating active governance rather than passive receipt of notifications
Periodic compliance reporting (e.g., quarterly) summarising TPSP change notifications received, SLA adherence rate, and any breaches of notice periods with remediation actions
commonGaps: TPSP change notifications are received informally via email and are not logged centrally, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic tracking or audit trail
The institution receives TPSP release notes or changelogs retrospectively (post-deployment) rather than in advance, indicating the contractual notice obligation is not being enforced
No evidence of internal review or risk assessment being conducted in response to TPSP change notifications; notifications appear to be filed without action
Change notification records exist only for infrastructure-as-a-service providers (e.g., cloud platforms) and not for application-layer TPSPs that develop or maintain bespoke critical systems
Gaps in the log for extended periods suggest either no changes occurred (unlikely) or notifications were not being systematically captured, with no explanation documented
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management) — change request module filtered by TPSP-originated changes
Email archive system — searchable records of TPSP change advisory communications
Technology Operations team — Change Advisory Board (CAB) minutes and approval records
GRC platform — third party risk event log linked to specific TPSP and system
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or ITSM system export (CSV/PDF) + Email records (PDF export)

evidenceItems.name: TPSP Secure-by-Design Attestation and SDLC Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that TPSPs developing or maintaining critical systems adopt secure-by-design principles in their software development lifecycle (SDLC), including the institution's process for verifying these claims as a contractual and ongoing due diligence obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contract or separate security annex requires the TPSP to maintain a documented SDLC that incorporates secure-by-design principles (e.g., threat modelling, SAST/DAST, security code review, dependency vulnerability scanning) with annual attestation submitted to the institution
TPSP provides supporting evidence of SDLC controls such as a current SSAE 18 SOC 2 Type II report, ISO/IEC 27001 certificate, or equivalent third-party audit report covering development practices

Institution has conducted or commissioned an independent technical assessment (e.g., application security audit, penetration test of TPSP-developed system) with results reviewed and remediation tracked

Evidence of supply chain risk analysis performed by the institution, identifying how vulnerabilities in TPSP development practices could propagate into the institution's environment, with mitigating controls documented

TPSP's secure-by-design attestation is reviewed and renewed at defined intervals (e.g., annually or upon major release) and is stored alongside the vendor risk file with a clear escalation path for non-renewal
commonGaps: Institution relies solely on TPSP self-declaration or a tick-box questionnaire to confirm secure-by-design adoption without seeking corroborating evidence such as audit reports or independent test results

Contractual obligation for secure-by-design exists but there is no defined mechanism for the institution to verify compliance — no attestation schedule, no right-to-audit clause, and no evidence of verification ever having occurred

TPSP's security certifications (e.g., ISO 27001) are accepted as sufficient but do not specifically cover development/SDLC practices, only operational security; the gap is not identified or compensated for

Supply chain cyber risk (i.e., risk that TPSP vulnerabilities propagate to the institution) has not been formally assessed; the institution cannot demonstrate awareness of this specific risk vector as referenced in the clause
Secure-by-design evidence was collected at onboarding but has not been refreshed; TPSP's current development practices may have changed materially since initial assessment
suggestedSources: Vendor / Third Party Risk Management team — TPSP due diligence files and security assessment records
GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC, OneTrust) — vendor risk assessments and TPSP security questionnaire responses
Information Security team — penetration test reports, application security assessment reports for TPSP-developed systems
Legal / Procurement — security annex to vendor contracts, right-to-audit provisions
TPSP-provided artefacts: SOC 2 reports, ISO 27001 certificates, secure SDLC policy documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF (audit reports, certificates, attestation letters) + DOCX (internal assessment reports)

evidenceItems.name: Source Code Escrow or Access Arrangement Agreement
evidenceItems.description: Legally binding arrangements ensuring that source code for TPSP-developed critical systems is accessible to the financial institution for business continuity purposes, including escrow agreements with third-party custodians or equivalent contractual access rights, together with evidence of periodic verification.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal source code escrow agreement is in place with a recognised escrow agent (e.g., Iron Mountain, NCC Escrow International) covering all TPSP-developed critical systems, with defined release conditions tied to business continuity trigger events (e.g., TPSP insolvency, material breach, cessation of support)

Alternatively, where escrow is not used, the contract grants the institution an unconditional right to receive and hold a copy of the source code, with defined update frequency to ensure the deposit reflects the current production version
Escrow deposit is verified at defined intervals (at minimum annually) through a technical verification exercise confirming the deposited code is complete, current, buildable, and documented

The institution has an internal or documented plan for how it would use the source code in a business continuity scenario, including identification of personnel or third parties with capability to maintain or transfer the system

Escrow arrangements and access rights are captured in the Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) for each critical system, with the TPSP identified as a single point of failure risk
commonGaps: No source code escrow or equivalent arrangement exists; the institution relies entirely on the TPSP's continued operation and goodwill for access to source code
