An escrow agreement exists on paper but the initial deposit was never completed, or the deposited code has not been updated in years and does not reflect the current production system
Escrow release conditions are defined so narrowly (e.g., only upon TPSP liquidation confirmed by court order) that access would be practically unavailable in a real business continuity scenario
The institution has never conducted a technical verification of the escrow deposit to confirm the code is complete, compilable, and representative of the live system

Source code accessibility is considered only for software developed entirely by the TPSP, without accounting for third-party libraries, APIs, or infrastructure-as-code components that are also critical for system reconstruction
suggestedSources: Legal team — escrow agreement documents and escrow agent correspondence
Technology / Application Management team — software asset inventory and source code repository access documentation
Business Continuity Management team — BCP/DRP documents referencing TPSP dependencies and source code access
GRC platform — TPSP risk profile with business continuity controls documented
Escrow agent portal or reports — verification certificates and deposit update confirmations
evidenceItems.format: PDF (escrow agreement, verification certificates) + DOCX (BCP/DRP excerpts)

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Register with TPSP Classification
evidenceItems.description: A maintained inventory of critical systems that are developed or maintained by third party service providers, forming the basis for determining which systems are subject to clause 10.12 obligations, with evidence that the register is kept current and reviewed against contractual controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal critical system register exists and is reviewed at least annually (or upon material change), with each system entry specifying whether development or maintenance is performed by a TPSP and identifying the relevant provider
The register uses a documented, risk-based criticality classification methodology aligned with BNM's definition of critical systems, not solely self-assessed without reference to regulatory guidance
For each TPSP-managed critical system, the register cross-references the applicable contracts and confirms whether clause 10.12 sub-obligations (a), (b), and (c) are covered by those contracts
Register is owned by a named accountable role (e.g., Chief Technology Officer or Head of IT Risk) and changes require formal approval with an audit trail of version history

The register is integrated with or linked to the institution's vendor risk management programme so that changes in TPSP status (e.g., new provider, contract expiry) trigger a review of critical system controls
commonGaps: Critical system register exists but TPSP involvement is not recorded as a field, making it impossible to systematically identify which critical systems are subject to clause 10.12 without manual cross-referencing

Register is updated reactively rather than maintained proactively; newly onboarded TPSP-managed systems are not added until after the next scheduled review cycle, leaving a compliance gap during the interim
Criticality classification is inconsistent — some systems classified as critical based on revenue impact only, without considering IT/operational resilience criteria relevant to RMiT
There is no formal linkage between the critical system register and the contract management system, so when contracts are renewed or amended the clause 10.12 controls are not re-verified

Register is held in an uncontrolled format (e.g., an individual's desktop spreadsheet) without version control, approval workflow, or access restrictions, undermining its reliability as a governance artefact
suggestedSources: Technology Risk / IT Governance team — critical system register or CMDB (Configuration Management Database)
GRC platform — asset register or application portfolio management module
IT Asset Management team — CMDB entries (ServiceNow, BMC Helix) with vendor and support model fields
Enterprise Architecture team — application landscape documentation
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC platform extract (PDF/CSV)

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Risk Monitoring and Periodic Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating ongoing monitoring of TPSP compliance with clause 10.12 contractual obligations throughout the engagement lifecycle, including periodic reviews, performance reports, and documented escalations or remediation actions where obligations were not met.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined third-party review cycle (e.g., quarterly SLA review, annual comprehensive risk reassessment) is documented and evidenced by meeting minutes, review reports, or scorecard records covering all clause 10.12 obligations

Evidence of the institution actively monitoring TPSP compliance with change notification obligations, including metrics such as notification timeliness and breach frequency, reported at governance level (e.g., IT Risk Committee)

Documented instances of the institution identifying and escalating non-compliance by a TPSP (e.g., late notification, lapsed secure-by-design attestation) with a resolution timeline and evidence of closure

Annual or event-triggered re-assessment of TPSP risk that explicitly evaluates continued adequacy of clause 10.12 controls in light of changes to the system, the TPSP's circumstances, or the threat landscape

Monitoring results and TPSP performance data are reported to senior management or the Board Risk Committee, demonstrating that third-party technology risk is subject to appropriate oversight at the governance level
commonGaps: Third party reviews are conducted but do not specifically assess clause 10.12 obligations — reviewers check financial health and general SLA performance but do not verify change notification compliance or secure-by-design attestation currency

Monitoring is entirely reactive: the institution responds to TPSP-initiated communications but has no proactive mechanism to detect when a TPSP fails to notify of a change or to identify TPSP-side security incidents that could propagate to the institution
Review records exist for TPSPs managing non-critical systems but the same rigour is not applied to TPSPs managing critical systems, contrary to the heightened obligations under clause 10.12

Non-compliance by a TPSP (e.g., failure to provide adequate notice before a change) has occurred but was not formally documented or escalated, giving the appearance of a functioning relationship while masking material control failures

Governance reporting on third-party technology risk does not disaggregate critical system TPSP performance from general vendor performance, making it impossible for senior management to assess the specific risk profile of clause 10.12-relevant providers
suggestedSources: Vendor Management / Procurement team — TPSP performance review records and SLA reporting dashboards
GRC platform — third party risk assessment workflow, periodic review logs, and issue tracking
IT Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee — meeting minutes and management information packs referencing TPSP performance
Technology Operations team — change management records cross-referenced against TPSP notification logs
Internal Audit team — prior audit findings and management responses related to TPSP governance
evidenceItems.format: PDF (review reports, committee minutes) + Spreadsheet (TPSP performance scorecard)

auditTips: Start by cross-referencing the institution's critical system register against its TPSP inventory to confirm that every TPSP-managed critical system is subject to a contract that specifically addresses all three sub-obligations in clause 10.12(a), (b), and (c) — a common examination failure is partial coverage where only the highest-profile vendors are contractually compliant while smaller niche providers managing critical components are overlooked.

Do not accept contract language at face value: pull the TPSP change notification log from the ITSM system and verify that actual notifications received conform to the contractual notice period. Calculate the average lead time between notification receipt and planned change deployment for a sample of TPSP-initiated changes; any instances below the contractual threshold represent enforcement failures that examiners will pursue.

For secure-by-design obligations, challenge the institution to produce substantive evidence beyond self-attestations — specifically ask for the most recent independent audit report (SOC 2 Type II, penetration test, or equivalent) covering the TPSP's development practices, and check whether the report scope and date align with the current version of the critical system. If only a security questionnaire is available, document it as a gap and recommend upgrading to evidence-based verification.

Test the source code accessibility arrangement by requesting either the escrow agent's most recent verification certificate or a demonstration that the institution can retrieve a current copy of the source code. For escrow arrangements, confirm that the release conditions are practically achievable — overly narrow conditions (e.g., requiring a court order) may render the arrangement ineffective for real-world business continuity scenarios.

Map each evidence item back to the specific TPSP and critical system it covers, and produce a compliance matrix (TPSP × obligation) before the examination. This matrix quickly surfaces white spaces — TPSPs where one or more of the three sub-obligations is not addressed — and allows the compliance team to remediate targeted gaps rather than conducting a broad review under time pressure.

Review the institution's Business Continuity Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan to confirm that TPSP dependency for source code access is explicitly identified as a risk scenario, with a documented response procedure. Examiners expect clause 10.12(c) to be operationalised within the BCM framework, not treated as a standalone contractual formality.

Check whether clause 10.12 controls are triggered and re-evaluated during the institution's TPSP lifecycle events — specifically at contract renewal, system re-scoping, and when a TPSP undergoes material changes such as acquisition, ownership change, or outsourcing of its own development to a sub-contractor. The absence of event-driven re-assessment is a persistent examination finding, as institutions that were compliant at onboarding may have drifted out of compliance without detection.

10.13.title: Critical System Decommissioning
10.13.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has a structured, documented process for decommissioning critical systems that explicitly accounts for customer and operational continuity. Examiners will probe whether contingency plans are not merely documented but have been actively tested and validated — untested plans are treated as non-existent for examination purposes. A key red flag is the absence of a formal criticality assessment that identifies which systems fall under this clause, as institutions that cannot articulate their critical system inventory cannot credibly demonstrate compliance. Examiners also look for evidence of stakeholder coordination across technology, operations, risk, and customer-facing units during the decommissioning lifecycle, and will scrutinise whether post-decommissioning reviews were conducted to confirm that adverse impacts were monitored and resolved. The 'S' marker signals that examiners expect substantive, auditable evidence rather than policy statements alone.

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Decommissioning Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy and supporting procedure document that governs the end-of-life lifecycle for critical systems, defining scope, roles, approval authority, mandatory steps, and regulatory obligations including customer impact assessment and contingency planning requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines 'critical system' consistent with the institution's broader IT risk taxonomy and BNM RMiT definitions, with a cross-reference to the critical system register

Procedure includes a mandatory pre-decommissioning checklist covering customer notification timelines, data migration/archival requirements, interface dependencies, and regulatory data retention obligations
Document ownership is assigned to a named role (e.g., Head of Technology Risk) with defined review frequency of at least annually or upon material change
Policy mandates that a contingency plan be produced, approved, and tested as a prerequisite before any decommissioning activity commences
Version history and board/senior management approval signatures are present, demonstrating active governance oversight
commonGaps: Policy exists for general system retirement but does not separately address 'critical systems' as a distinct category with elevated controls
Procedure does not specify minimum notice periods or communication obligations to customers before decommissioning takes effect
Contingency planning is referenced in the policy but not operationalised into a mandatory procedural step with defined ownership
Document has not been reviewed or updated within the past 12 months, rendering it potentially inconsistent with current system landscape
Approval authority is undefined or delegated too low, lacking evidence that senior management endorsed the decommissioning framework
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — Policy Management module
SharePoint or Confluence policy repository maintained by Technology Risk or IT Governance team
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes evidencing policy approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Inventory and Decommissioning Scope Register
evidenceItems.description: A maintained register of all systems classified as critical, including their decommissioning status, and for systems undergoing or having undergone decommissioning, a record of the scope assessment that determined applicability of clause 10.13 controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register identifies each critical system with attributes including system name, business function, criticality classification rationale, data sensitivity, customer-facing indicator, and regulatory relevance
Systems scheduled or recently decommissioned are flagged with decommissioning project reference, planned timeline, and accountable system owner
Register is reconciled against the institution's Configuration Management Database (CMDB) or asset inventory on a defined periodic cycle, with evidence of reconciliation dates
Criticality classification is reviewed at least annually and upon any material change to system function, dependency, or risk profile
Register is accessible to Technology Risk, Audit, and Compliance functions and shows a clear chain of custody for updates
commonGaps: Critical systems are not formally classified or the classification methodology is undocumented, making it impossible to demonstrate which systems are in scope for clause 10.13
Register is maintained as an ad-hoc spreadsheet with no version control, ownership, or defined update frequency
Decommissioned systems are removed from the register immediately upon retirement, leaving no auditable history of which systems were decommissioned and when
Register does not capture interdependencies and downstream systems, preventing a complete impact assessment for decommissioning planning
Classification decisions are not supported by documented rationale, making it difficult to defend scope inclusions or exclusions to examiners
suggestedSources: CMDB within ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management)
Technology Risk register maintained by Chief Technology Officer or IT Risk function
Business Continuity Management system where critical systems are enumerated for RTO/RPO purposes
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (Excel/CSV/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Decommissioning Project Plan and Impact Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A project-level document for each critical system decommissioning activity that captures the structured plan, business and customer impact assessment, dependency mapping, migration or data archival approach, and stakeholder sign-off prior to execution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Impact assessment explicitly addresses customer-facing risks, including service disruption scenarios, and documents agreed mitigation actions with owners and deadlines

Dependency mapping covers upstream and downstream system integrations, third-party interfaces, and data flows, confirmed through technical validation rather than relying solely on documented architecture
Project plan includes defined go/no-go decision gates with named approvers at each phase, and evidence that approvals were obtained before proceeding
Data migration and archival plan addresses regulatory retention obligations and confirms data integrity validation steps were performed post-migration
Stakeholder sign-off includes representation from Technology, Operations, Risk, Compliance, and relevant business lines, documented via formal approval records
commonGaps: Impact assessment is generic and does not specifically evaluate customer-facing services dependent on the system being decommissioned
Dependency mapping was performed at initial project kick-off but not refreshed prior to execution, resulting in undiscovered dependencies at go-live
Project plan lacks formal approval gates, with decommissioning proceeding based on informal agreement rather than documented sign-off
Data archival approach does not address regulatory retention periods, creating a risk of premature data destruction
Assessment focuses exclusively on technical impact and omits operational and customer experience dimensions required by the clause
suggestedSources: Project Management Office (PMO) system (e.g., Microsoft Project, Jira, Smartsheet)
Enterprise Architecture repository for system dependency and integration diagrams
Technology Change Advisory Board (CAB) records and meeting minutes
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX project plan with embedded or attached approval records

evidenceItems.name: Contingency Plan for Unsuccessful Decommissioning
evidenceItems.description: A documented, system-specific contingency plan that defines rollback procedures, escalation paths, decision criteria for invoking contingency, and recovery actions to be taken if the decommissioning is unsuccessful or causes adverse impact on customers or operations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contingency plan includes a clearly defined rollback or system reinstatement procedure with step-by-step technical instructions and estimated recovery time objectives
Decision criteria for invoking contingency are pre-agreed and documented (e.g., specific error thresholds, customer complaint volumes, transaction failure rates) removing ambiguity during an incident
Plan identifies named individuals and alternates responsible for invoking, executing, and declaring completion of the contingency, with current contact details
Plan addresses customer communication obligations during a contingency scenario, including escalation to public communications or regulatory notification if applicable
Plan is reviewed and approved by Technology Risk and relevant business line heads, with version date confirming it reflects the current system architecture at time of decommissioning
commonGaps: Contingency plan exists at a generic level in the business continuity framework but no system-specific plan has been produced for the decommissioning activity
Rollback procedures assume that the legacy system environment remains available but no verification has been performed to confirm this assumption holds at execution time
Decision criteria for invoking contingency are absent or vague (e.g., 'if issues arise'), leaving the invocation decision to ad-hoc judgement under pressure
Plan does not address customer communication during a contingency scenario, creating regulatory exposure if customers are adversely affected without notification
Plan was produced but not shared with all parties responsible for execution, evidenced by absence of distribution records or acknowledgement from key personnel
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management system or dedicated BCP/DRP repository
Technology Change Management records within ITSM platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy)
Technology Risk team's project risk register linked to the decommissioning project
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with named approvers and version date

evidenceItems.name: Contingency Plan Test Records and Results
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the contingency plan for unsuccessful decommissioning was formally tested prior to the decommissioning execution date, including test design, execution records, outcomes, deficiencies identified, and remediation actions taken.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test records document the test scope, date, participants, scenario exercised, and explicit confirmation that rollback or reinstatement procedures were validated end-to-end
Test results capture both successful outcomes and any failures or gaps identified, with a remediation log showing each gap was closed before decommissioning proceeded
Test was conducted in an environment sufficiently representative of production to provide meaningful assurance, with a documented rationale if a full production simulation was not feasible
Test completion was signed off by an independent reviewer (e.g., Technology Risk or Internal Audit) rather than solely by the project team executing the decommissioning
Test records are linked to the specific decommissioning project and contingency plan version that was tested, ensuring traceability
commonGaps: Contingency plan was produced but testing was waived or deferred due to project timeline pressure, with no documented risk acceptance or compensating control
Testing was performed informally with no structured test script, making it impossible to confirm what was actually validated and what the outcomes were
Test environment was materially different from production (e.g., reduced data volumes, missing integrations), and no assessment was made of whether test results provide valid assurance
Deficiencies identified during testing were noted but not remediated before decommissioning proceeded, with remediation deferred to a post-decommissioning activity

Test records exist but are held solely by the project team and were not formally submitted to Technology Risk or Compliance, making them unavailable for examination without significant retrieval effort
suggestedSources: IT Service Management platform test management module (ServiceNow, Jira Xray)
Project Management Office records for the specific decommissioning project
Business Continuity or Technology Risk team's testing schedule and results repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX test report or structured spreadsheet with sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Post-Decommissioning Review and Customer Impact Monitoring Report
evidenceItems.description: A post-implementation review conducted after decommissioning completion that documents whether the process achieved minimal adverse impact on customers and business operations, captures any incidents or issues that arose, and confirms all contingency and residual actions were resolved.
whatGoodLooksLike: Review is conducted within a defined period after decommissioning (e.g., 30 days) and references pre-defined success criteria established in the project plan to assess outcomes objectively
Customer impact monitoring data is included, drawing from complaint volumes, service desk tickets, transaction error rates, and customer feedback channels over the post-decommissioning period
Any incidents or service disruptions that occurred during or after decommissioning are documented with root cause analysis, customer impact quantification, and remediation actions taken
Review confirms that all data archival, interface disconnection, and licence termination activities were completed and verified, leaving no orphaned dependencies
Report is reviewed and accepted by Technology Risk and relevant business line management, with distribution evidence confirming senior management visibility
commonGaps: No formal post-decommissioning review is conducted; the project is closed once technical decommissioning steps are completed without assessing customer or operational impact outcomes
Review is performed but relies solely on subjective team assessment rather than objective data from monitoring systems, complaint logs, or service metrics
Customer complaints or service issues attributable to the decommissioning are not linked back to the project review, meaning lessons learned are not captured
Review is completed internally by the project team without independent validation, reducing its credibility as assurance evidence for examination purposes
Lessons learned from the decommissioning are documented but not fed back into the decommissioning policy or procedure, indicating a closed-loop process failure
suggestedSources: Customer complaints management system (e.g., CRM platform, contact centre records)
IT Service Management incident and problem management records
Project Management Office post-implementation review templates and archives
Technology Risk dashboard or operational risk event log
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX formal review report with management sign-off

auditTips: Compile a decommissioning activity log covering the past 3 years that lists every critical system decommissioned, the project reference, completion date, and a pointer to the evidence package for each — examiners frequently begin by requesting this list to determine the sample they will examine in detail

For each decommissioning in the sample period, assemble a linked evidence bundle (plan, impact assessment, contingency plan, test records, post-review) so that evidence can be traced end-to-end without reliance on multiple teams during the examination — gaps in the chain are an immediate red flag

Proactively verify that contingency plan test records are dated before the decommissioning execution date, as examiners will check this sequencing — a test record dated after the decommissioning date demonstrates that the clause obligation was not met at the time it was required

Cross-reference the critical system inventory against BCP/DRP critical system lists and the institution's RTO/RPO register to confirm consistency; discrepancies between these lists suggest the critical system classification process lacks rigour and will attract scrutiny

Review customer communication records for each decommissioning to confirm that affected customers were notified in advance with adequate notice periods; the absence of customer notification documentation is a common finding that directly contradicts the 'minimal adverse impact on customers' obligation in the verbatim text

Ensure that post-decommissioning reviews are independently validated — ideally by Technology Risk or Internal Audit — as self-assessed reviews by project teams are typically discounted by examiners when evaluating the credibility of evidence

Where any decommissioning contingency was actually invoked, prepare a detailed incident narrative including timeline, customer impact metrics, and resolution steps, as examiners will pay particular attention to real contingency events as the most direct test of whether the institution's controls functioned as required by the clause

10.14.title: Automated Development and Security Tools
10.14.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised—not merely procured—automated tooling across the full software development lifecycle, with particular scrutiny on code scanning and security assessment integration into CI/CD pipelines for critical systems. Examiners probe whether tool coverage aligns with the institution's threat landscape and the criticality tiers of its applications, looking for evidence that automated security gates actively block deployments rather than serving as advisory checks. Red flags include tooling inventories that lack evidence of active use (e.g., no scan reports, no pipeline execution logs), absence of automation in change management or software version control for critical systems, and a disconnect between the tools deployed and the institution's documented cyber risk appetite. Examiners also verify that the rationale for tool selection is documented, that outputs feed into risk and vulnerability management processes, and that the institution periodically reviews tool adequacy against emerging cyber threats.

evidenceItems.name: Automated Tooling Inventory and Governance Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register cataloguing all automated tools deployed across the software development lifecycle—covering development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, and version control—including tool purpose, vendor, version, licensing status, criticality tier coverage, and assigned owner. Demonstrates that the institution has deliberately governed its toolchain rather than acquired tools ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register explicitly maps each tool to one or more SDLC functions named in Clause 10.14 (development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, version control)
Each entry records which critical systems or application tiers the tool covers, enabling examiners to verify critical system coverage
Register is version-controlled with a clear review cadence (at minimum annual) and shows evidence of the most recent review sign-off by IT Security or CTO
Tool adequacy column documents rationale for selection and a date of last assessment against emerging threat landscape
Ownership is assigned to named individuals or teams, not generic roles, with escalation paths documented
commonGaps: Register exists but does not distinguish which tools are applied to critical versus non-critical systems, making it impossible to verify critical system coverage
Tool versions and licensing are not tracked, creating risk that outdated or unlicensed tools with known vulnerabilities remain in use
No review history or sign-off trail, suggesting the register is a point-in-time document rather than a living governance artefact
SDLC function mapping is absent or uses internal naming that cannot be cross-walked to the six functions enumerated in Clause 10.14
Decommissioned tools are removed without retention of historical records, creating audit trail gaps for past deployments
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or local equivalent) — Technology Asset Register or CMDB module
IT Security team — maintained as part of the security architecture artefacts
ITSM platform (e.g., Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy) — configuration item database filtered by tool category
SharePoint/Confluence — DevSecOps team knowledge base or toolchain runbook section
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF/CSV), with version history

evidenceItems.name: CI/CD Pipeline Configuration and Execution Logs for Critical Systems
evidenceItems.description: Pipeline-as-code definitions and execution history demonstrating that automated build, test, security scan, and deployment stages are enforced for critical systems. Provides direct evidence that automation facilitates 'timely security assessment of critical systems' as required by the clause, and that security gates are active rather than advisory.
whatGoodLooksLike: Pipeline definitions (e.g., Jenkinsfile, GitLab CI YAML, Azure Pipelines YAML) show mandatory security scan stages that block merge or deployment on policy violations, not just generate reports
Execution logs for the review period show consistent scan execution across all deployments to production environments for critical systems, with no unexplained gaps
Logs capture pass/fail outcomes, scan tool name, version, and ruleset version used, enabling reproducibility and trend analysis
Failed pipeline runs due to security gate violations are documented with evidence of remediation before re-run, demonstrating the control is effective
Pipeline configuration is itself version-controlled in the same repository as application code, with change history auditable
commonGaps: Security scan stages are present in pipeline definitions but configured with 'allow failure' flags, meaning failed scans do not block deployments and the control is ineffective
Execution logs are purged on short retention cycles (e.g., 30–90 days) making it impossible to demonstrate continuous compliance over the examination period
Critical systems are exempted from pipeline enforcement through manual deployment bypass procedures that are invoked frequently without documented approval
Logs show scan execution but do not record the ruleset or plugin version used, preventing assessment of whether scans were current against emerging threats
Pipeline coverage is incomplete — some critical system repositories lack enforced pipelines and rely on manual developer execution of security tools
suggestedSources: DevOps platforms: GitLab CI/CD, GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Azure DevOps — pipeline run history exports
Artifact repositories: JFrog Artifactory, Nexus — build promotion logs
ITSM change records cross-referenced with pipeline execution timestamps to verify automation was used for each change
DevSecOps team — pipeline-as-code repository in version control system
evidenceItems.format: System export (JSON/CSV/PDF), pipeline configuration files (YAML/Groovy) exported from VCS, with timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Code Scanning Results and Remediation Tracking Records
evidenceItems.description: Output reports from Static Application Security Testing (SAST), Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), Software Composition Analysis (SCA), and secret scanning tools, together with records showing how identified findings were triaged, assigned, and remediated. Demonstrates that automated code scanning produces actionable security assessments rather than unreviewed reports.
whatGoodLooksLike: Scan reports are produced for every release cycle of critical systems and are dated, tool-attributed, and linked to the corresponding change or release record in the ITSM system

A defined vulnerability triage and SLA policy exists and scan findings are tracked in a defect management system (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow) with severity, owner, target remediation date, and closure evidence
Trend reporting shows reduction in open critical and high findings over time, or documented risk acceptance with CISO/CTO sign-off for accepted residual risk
SCA reports identify open-source components with known CVEs and demonstrate that dependency updates or mitigating controls are applied within defined SLAs
Scan rulesets and policies are reviewed and updated at least annually or when significant new threat intelligence is received, with change history documented
commonGaps: Scan reports are generated and stored but there is no evidence of systematic triage — findings sit unassigned with no remediation tracking, making the scanning activity cosmetic rather than effective
Critical and high findings from prior examination periods remain open with no documented risk acceptance or escalation, indicating the process breaks down at remediation
SCA scanning is absent or covers only first-party code, leaving third-party library vulnerabilities undetected in critical systems
Scan configurations have not been updated to include new vulnerability categories or emerging threat vectors, and ruleset version history is not maintained
Results from different tools are siloed with no consolidated view, preventing assessment of aggregate security posture across the SDLC
suggestedSources: SAST tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx, Semgrep, Veracode — project dashboards and exported reports
DAST tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite Enterprise — scan report archives
SCA tools: Snyk, WhiteSource/Mend, Dependabot — vulnerability reports and PR remediation history
Defect tracking: Jira, Azure DevOps Boards — security vulnerability issue queue filtered by source tool
GRC platform — vulnerability management module with SLA tracking
evidenceItems.format: PDF/HTML scan reports, Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC export for finding tracker, with timestamps and version references

evidenceItems.name: Software Version Control Policy and Repository Access Audit Logs
evidenceItems.description: The institution's policy governing use of version control systems (VCS), supported by repository configuration evidence and access audit logs, demonstrating that all code for critical systems is managed under version control with appropriate access controls, branching strategies, and immutable audit trails.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy mandates VCS use for all critical system source code, infrastructure-as-code, and pipeline definitions, with no exceptions permitted without documented CISO approval
Branch protection rules are enforced on critical system repositories, requiring peer code review and passing CI/CD pipeline checks before merge to main/production branches
Access audit logs show that direct pushes to protected branches are blocked and that all access is authenticated via individual accounts (no shared credentials), enabling full attribution
Repository access reviews are conducted at least semi-annually with documented evidence of removal of stale or departed-employee access
Policy is reviewed annually with version history and sign-off by IT Security and a senior technology executive
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not explicitly mandate VCS for infrastructure-as-code or pipeline configurations, leaving automation scripts outside controlled versioning
Branch protection rules are not enforced consistently — some critical system repositories allow direct commits to main branch by administrators, bypassing the control
Access audit logs have insufficient retention or are not reviewed periodically, making it impossible to detect or demonstrate detection of unauthorised access
Service accounts and build agents have write access to production branches, creating a potential pathway for unauthorised code injection through pipeline compromise
VCS usage is not tracked against the critical systems list — some critical applications continue to use shared network drives or email for code distribution
suggestedSources: VCS platforms: GitHub Enterprise, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Bitbucket — repository settings exports, branch protection rule configurations, access logs
Identity and Access Management system — access provisioning and deprovisioning records tied to VCS groups
IT Security team — semi-annual access review sign-off records
Policy repository: SharePoint or Confluence — versioned VCS governance policy document
evidenceItems.format: PDF policy document, system-exported access logs (CSV/JSON), branch protection configuration screenshots or exports

evidenceItems.name: Automated Change Management Integration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that change management workflows for critical system deployments are integrated with or enforced by automated tooling—such as deployment gates linked to change advisory board approvals, automated rollback triggers, and deployment tracking in the ITSM system—demonstrating that automation supports controlled and auditable change processes rather than bypassing them.
whatGoodLooksLike: Deployment pipeline configurations show integration with the ITSM change management system, requiring an approved Change Request (CR) number before production deployment can proceed
Change records in the ITSM system are automatically enriched with deployment artefact details (build number, commit hash, scan results summary) from the pipeline, creating a linked audit trail
Automated rollback procedures are defined, tested, and documented, with evidence of at least one successful rollback test per review period for critical systems
Post-deployment verification steps are automated and results are captured in the change record, confirming successful deployment or triggering rollback without manual intervention
Emergency change procedures that bypass standard gates are logged, limited in use, and subject to post-implementation review with documented approval from a named senior authority
commonGaps: Change records and deployment logs exist independently with no automated linkage — reconciliation relies on manual data entry, creating opportunities for misrepresentation or gaps
Automated deployment tools are used but changes are still recorded manually in the ITSM system after the fact, undermining the integrity of the change timeline
Rollback procedures are documented but have never been tested in production or staging, and no test evidence exists to validate their effectiveness
Emergency change bypass procedures are used frequently (e.g., more than 5% of changes for critical systems) without adequate justification or post-implementation review
Automated deployment tools have production access credentials embedded or stored insecurely, creating a privileged access risk that contradicts the security intent of automation
suggestedSources: ITSM platform: ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management — change management module, deployment records, and CR-to-pipeline integration logs
Deployment orchestration tools: Spinnaker, ArgoCD, Octopus Deploy — deployment history and approval gate logs
CAB meeting minutes and approval records — IT Change Advisory Board documentation
IT Operations team — rollback test evidence and post-implementation review records
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV), pipeline deployment logs, CAB minutes (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Tooling Adequacy Review Against Emerging Cyber Threats
evidenceItems.description: Periodic review records demonstrating that the institution evaluates whether its deployed automated tools remain adequate against the evolving threat landscape, including assessment of new tool capabilities, emerging attack vectors not covered by current tooling, and decisions to adopt, retire, or enhance tools. Demonstrates the 'emerging cyber threats' responsiveness intent of Clause 10.14.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal review is conducted at least annually by IT Security with documented input from threat intelligence sources (e.g., BNM advisories, CISA KEV, MITRE ATT&CK updates) and outputs in a structured assessment report

Review report identifies specific emerging threat categories (e.g., supply chain attacks, AI-generated code vulnerabilities) and maps them to existing tool coverage, documenting gaps and proposed remediation
Decisions arising from reviews are tracked to completion — tool upgrades, new tool procurement, or documented risk acceptance with named approver and review date
Review findings and decisions are reported to the relevant technology governance committee (e.g., IT Risk Committee, Technology Steering Committee) with documented acknowledgement

Tool vendor security advisories are monitored and there is evidence of prompt response to critical tool vulnerabilities (e.g., patch applied within defined SLA, interim compensating controls documented)
commonGaps: No formal periodic review process exists — tool adequacy is assessed only reactively when a security incident occurs or an examiner raises a concern
Reviews are conducted but rely solely on vendor marketing materials rather than independent threat intelligence sources, producing superficial adequacy conclusions
Tool upgrade decisions are made informally without documented rationale, making it impossible to demonstrate that emerging threats drove tool enhancement
Governance committee reporting on tooling adequacy is absent — IT Security conducts reviews but findings are not escalated for senior management visibility or decision
Vendor end-of-life or end-of-support dates for scanning tools are not tracked, resulting in continued reliance on tools that no longer receive signature or rule updates
suggestedSources: IT Security team — annual tooling adequacy assessment reports and threat intelligence review records
GRC platform — risk register entries related to tooling gaps and remediation tracking
Technology governance committee minutes — IT Risk Committee or Technology Steering Committee records referencing tooling decisions
Vendor portals and advisory feeds — patch and advisory subscription records as evidence of active monitoring
Threat intelligence platforms (e.g., MISP, commercial TI feeds) — query history or subscription evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF assessment report, committee minutes (PDF/DOCX), risk register export (PDF/XLSX)

auditTips: Map your automated tooling inventory directly to the six SDLC functions named in Clause 10.14 verbatim text (development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, version control) and verify that critical systems as defined in your institution's criticality tiering framework are covered by each function — examiners will draw this matrix themselves if you do not provide it, and gaps will be immediately visible.

Validate that code scanning and security gate controls are genuinely blocking rather than advisory by pulling a sample of pipeline execution logs for critical system releases and confirming that at least one scan failure was encountered, investigated, and resolved before deployment — a record showing only green pipelines may indicate gates are not enforced or are systematically bypassed.

Cross-reference your ITSM change records for the examination period against pipeline execution logs and VCS commit history to ensure every production change to a critical system has a corresponding automated pipeline run — unexplained changes deployed outside the pipeline are a significant red flag for examiners and may indicate unauthorised or shadow deployment practices.

Prepare a concise walkthrough narrative for each critical system that traces a single release end-to-end: from code commit in VCS, through automated CI/CD pipeline execution (including scan results), to ITSM change approval, to production deployment and post-deployment verification. This demonstrates integrated automation rather than isolated tool usage and is the most effective way to communicate maturity to examiners.

Review and refresh your tooling adequacy assessment before the examination window, explicitly referencing recent threat intelligence events (BNM cybersecurity advisories, CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue updates, or relevant FSISAC alerts) and documenting how your current toolset addresses or has gaps against those threats — this directly satisfies the 'emerging cyber threats' language in the clause and signals proactive risk management.

Audit your emergency change and pipeline bypass records for the examination period: calculate the percentage of critical system changes that bypassed automated controls, ensure each bypass has documented approval from a named senior authority and a post-implementation review, and be prepared to explain the business justification. A high bypass rate without adequate controls is frequently cited in BNM examination findings under this clause.

Ensure that all scan tool versions, ruleset versions, and configuration baselines are documented and that you can demonstrate tools were kept current throughout the examination period — produce a version history log for each critical scanning tool showing when updates were applied, as examiners may probe whether tooling was allowed to fall out of date, which would undermine the 'timely security assessment' intent of the clause.

10.15.title: Third Party Software Supply Chain Risk
10.15.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has a structured, operationalised approach to identifying and managing risks introduced by third-party software components throughout the software supply chain — not merely documented intentions. Examiners will look for evidence that SBOM practices are automated and continuous rather than ad-hoc, and that open-source software (OSS) governance extends to repository access controls and active monitoring for leakages and vulnerabilities. Red flags include the absence of any SBOM tooling, reliance on manual component tracking, undocumented OSS usage in production systems, and OSS policies that exist on paper but are not enforced via technical controls or testing pipelines. Examiners will cross-reference the institution's third-party risk management framework, secure development lifecycle documentation, and incident records to verify that supply chain risk is treated as a live operational concern rather than a one-time procurement checkpoint.

evidenceItems.name: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: A formal, approved policy document mandating the generation, maintenance, and use of SBOMs for all systems utilising third-party or open-source software components. Demonstrates the institution's governance commitment to automating supply chain visibility.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates SBOM generation at build time and upon every significant dependency change, covering all production-facing systems
Procedure specifies the SBOM standard format used (e.g., CycloneDX or SPDX) and the toolchain responsible for generation (e.g., Syft, Trivy, OWASP Dependency-Track)
Policy defines roles and responsibilities: who owns SBOM maintenance, who reviews vulnerability alerts, and escalation paths for critical findings
Document is approved by the CISO or equivalent and reviewed at least annually, with version history and change log visible
Scope statement explicitly includes containerised workloads, third-party APIs integrated via SDK, and internally developed applications with OSS dependencies
commonGaps: Policy exists but applies only to internally developed software, excluding third-party COTS products and SaaS integrations with embedded components
SBOM generation is described as a manual process performed only at initial deployment, with no continuous or automated refresh mechanism
No defined standard format for SBOMs, resulting in inconsistent outputs across teams that cannot be centrally analysed or compared
Roles and responsibilities for SBOM ownership are absent or assigned to a team without the authority or tooling to act on findings
Policy was drafted once and not reviewed following major supply chain incidents (e.g., Log4Shell) that should have triggered a policy update
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform policy module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate)
CISO Office document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with approval signature page and version history

evidenceItems.name: SBOM Inventory and Vulnerability Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational records demonstrating that SBOMs are actively generated, stored, and continuously monitored for newly disclosed vulnerabilities across all in-scope systems. Evidences the 'automate identification and continuous monitoring' requirement of clause 10.15(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: A centralised SBOM repository (e.g., OWASP Dependency-Track, Anchore) with timestamped SBOM artefacts for all production systems, updated at each build or release cycle
Dashboard or report exports showing active vulnerability alerts mapped to specific components, with CVE identifiers, CVSS scores, and affected system names
Evidence of automated integration between the SBOM tool and the vulnerability database (NVD, OSV, GitHub Advisory Database), with update frequency of at least daily
Records showing that vulnerability alerts triggered defined workflows — e.g., ITSM tickets created in ServiceNow with priority aligned to CVSS severity within SLA thresholds
Trend reports demonstrating reduction in mean-time-to-remediate (MTTR) for SBOM-identified vulnerabilities over a rolling 12-month period
commonGaps: SBOM tool is deployed but alert notifications are not routed to any accountable team, resulting in findings that age without remediation action
Coverage is incomplete — SBOMs exist for new applications only, while legacy systems and containerised microservices are excluded from monitoring
Vulnerability records lack traceability to remediation actions; auditors cannot confirm whether a flagged component was patched, mitigated, or accepted as residual risk
SBOM exports are static point-in-time snapshots stored as files rather than live-monitored artefacts, defeating the purpose of continuous monitoring
No evidence of periodic reconciliation between the SBOM inventory and the institution's authorised software register or CMDB
suggestedSources: OWASP Dependency-Track, Anchore Enterprise, or equivalent SBOM management platform
CI/CD pipeline artefact store (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) — build logs with SBOM generation steps
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — vulnerability remediation tickets linked to SBOM findings
CMDB / Software Asset Management (SAM) system
evidenceItems.format: System export (JSON/XML SBOM files), PDF dashboard reports, Spreadsheet vulnerability register

evidenceItems.name: Open-Source Software (OSS) Security Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: A documented OSS governance framework establishing rules for the approval, usage, monitoring, and disposal of open-source components within the institution's technology environment. Directly addresses the clause 10.15(b) obligation to establish an OSS security policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines an OSS approval process including licence compatibility review, security vetting, and maintainer activity assessment before any new OSS component is permitted in production

Procedures cover secure coding practices specific to OSS integration, including pinning dependency versions, verifying package integrity via checksums or signed releases, and prohibiting direct use of unverified forks
Policy explicitly addresses source code repository access controls, including requirements for MFA on all developer accounts accessing third-party platforms (GitHub, GitLab, npm, PyPI)
OSS vulnerability assessment cadence is defined — e.g., automated daily scans plus a formal quarterly OSS security review with documented findings and sign-off
Policy includes a process for responding to OSS component abandonment (unmaintained libraries), including migration timelines and interim risk acceptance procedures
commonGaps: Policy focuses solely on licence compliance (legal risk) and does not address security risks such as typosquatting, malicious package injection, or compromised maintainer accounts
Secure coding practices described are generic and not tailored to OSS-specific risks; no reference to dependency pinning, integrity verification, or supply chain attack vectors
Policy scope excludes infrastructure-as-code dependencies (Terraform modules, Helm charts, Ansible roles), which are common OSS supply chain attack surfaces
No defined process for evaluating the security posture of OSS maintainers or project health indicators (e.g., commit frequency, CVE response history)
Policy approval is at team level only, lacking endorsement from Information Security or Risk Management, reducing its authority and enforcement capability
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Software Engineering / DevSecOps team documentation
GRC platform policy module
Legal and Compliance policy register (for licence compliance intersection)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with approval metadata and review history

evidenceItems.name: Source Code Repository Access Control Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that access to source code repositories hosted on third-party platforms is secured, access-controlled, and monitored. Addresses the clause 10.15(b) requirement to ensure 'secure access to source code repositories in third party platforms'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Access control reports from repository platforms (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) showing that MFA is enforced for all developer accounts, with no exemptions
Quarterly access reviews with documented approval, confirming that repository permissions adhere to least-privilege principles and ex-staff/contractor access is promptly revoked
Evidence of branch protection rules and code signing requirements, preventing direct pushes to main/release branches without peer review
Audit log exports from the repository platform showing privileged actions (admin changes, webhook modifications, secret access) reviewed by security operations
Evidence that organisation-level security settings (e.g., GitHub Advanced Security, secret scanning, dependency alerts) are enabled and monitored
commonGaps: MFA is configured as optional rather than enforced at the organisation level, allowing some developer accounts to operate without it
Access reviews are conducted annually at best and do not include contractor or third-party developer accounts, which may retain access post-engagement
No audit log monitoring is in place for repository platforms; privileged actions go undetected without SIEM integration or periodic log review
Repository secrets (API keys, credentials) are stored in plaintext within repository files or commit history, with no secret scanning controls active
Branch protection rules are inconsistently applied — enforced on main branches but absent on release or hotfix branches used in production deployments
suggestedSources: GitHub / GitLab / Bitbucket organisation security settings and audit logs
Identity and Access Management (IAM) system — developer account provisioning records
SIEM platform — repository audit log ingestion evidence (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, Wazuh)
Privileged Access Management (PAM) system
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/PDF audit logs), Screenshots with timestamps, Access review sign-off sheets (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Supply Chain Incident and Data Leakage Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational evidence that the institution actively monitors for data leakages, credential exposures, and malicious code propagation through the software supply chain. Addresses the 10.15(b) requirement for 'regular monitoring to prevent data leakages' and 'propagation of malwares across supply chain'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence of automated secret scanning tools (e.g., GitHub Secret Scanning, TruffleHog, GitGuardian) integrated into CI/CD pipelines, with alerts routed to the security team
SIEM or threat intelligence platform showing monitored indicators related to known compromised packages, malicious npm/PyPI packages, and supply chain attack IOCs
Records of periodic dark web or paste site monitoring for leaked credentials associated with developer accounts or internal code repositories
Documented response records for any detected leakages — including containment actions (credential rotation, package version pinning), root cause analysis, and post-incident review
Subscription to or integration with supply chain threat intelligence feeds (e.g., OSV, Sonatype OSS Index, Socket.dev) with evidence of operationalised alerts
commonGaps: Secret scanning is enabled on the repository platform but alerts are sent to a generic inbox that no team actively monitors, resulting in unactioned findings
Monitoring scope covers only the institution's own repositories and does not extend to third-party packages downloaded and used in build pipelines
No threat intelligence feeds specific to supply chain attacks are subscribed to; the institution relies solely on generic CVE databases that lag behind zero-day supply chain incidents
Data leakage monitoring is confined to DLP tools on the corporate network and does not cover external developer platforms where source code or secrets may be inadvertently exposed
Absence of documented response to any past supply chain alert, making it impossible to verify that the monitoring process is operationally effective rather than purely technical
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, Wazuh) — supply chain and repository alert dashboards
GitHub Advanced Security / GitGuardian / TruffleHog — secret scanning alert reports
Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) — supply chain IOC feeds
Security Operations Centre (SOC) — incident and alert handling records
DLP solution — data leakage detection logs
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF/CSV alert reports), Incident records (PDF), Dashboard screenshots with timestamps

evidenceItems.name: OSS Security Testing and Vulnerability Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that open-source software components undergo robust security testing and timely vulnerability assessment before production use and on a continuous basis thereafter. Addresses the clause 10.15(b) requirements for 'robust testing of open-source software and timely vulnerability assessment'.
whatGoodLooksLike: CI/CD pipeline configuration showing automated Software Composition Analysis (SCA) scans (e.g., Snyk, WhiteSource, OWASP Dependency-Check) as a mandatory gate before any build can be promoted to production
Vulnerability assessment reports with clear remediation timelines mapped to severity — e.g., Critical: 24 hours, High: 7 days, Medium: 30 days — with evidence of compliance against SLA
Penetration test or red team reports that specifically include supply chain attack scenarios (e.g., dependency confusion, malicious package substitution) with documented findings and remediation
Records of OSS component testing in an isolated sandbox environment before integration, including test plans, execution evidence, and sign-off from a security reviewer
Formal risk acceptance records for any known OSS vulnerabilities that cannot be immediately remediated, approved by an appropriate authority with documented compensating controls
commonGaps: SCA scans are run but configured in 'monitor-only' mode without build-breaking thresholds, allowing vulnerable components to proceed to production without remediation
Vulnerability remediation timelines are defined in policy but compliance against SLA is never measured or reported, leaving chronic overdue findings unaddressed

Security testing of OSS components is treated as part of general application testing rather than a distinct supply chain risk control, resulting in insufficient coverage of component-specific attack vectors
Risk acceptance for unpatched OSS vulnerabilities is informal or verbal, with no documented business owner approval, compensating controls, or review date
Penetration tests do not include supply chain attack scenarios, meaning the institution has no independent assurance that its OSS controls are effective against real-world supply chain threats
suggestedSources: CI/CD platform (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) — SCA scan pipeline configurations and build reports
Vulnerability Management platform (Snyk, Tenable.io, Qualys) — OSS component scan reports
GRC platform — risk acceptance register and vulnerability exception records
Penetration testing provider reports — archived by Information Security team
Change Management system (ServiceNow) — OSS testing evidence attached to change records
evidenceItems.format: PDF scan reports, Pipeline configuration exports (YAML), Spreadsheet SLA tracking, PDF penetration test reports

auditTips: Map every production system to its SBOM artefact before the examination — examiners will ask for specific systems and expect to be shown the corresponding SBOM with component listings and vulnerability status. A gap in coverage for even one critical system will be treated as a material finding.

Demonstrate continuous monitoring, not point-in-time snapshots: prepare time-series evidence showing SBOM-driven vulnerability alerts generated, routed, and closed over the past 12 months. Static reports dated at examination time will not satisfy the 'continuous monitoring' intent of clause 10.15(a).

Cross-reference your OSS policy against the actual OSS components listed in your SBOMs to verify that every approved OSS component follows the policy's vetting and testing process. Examiners may select components at random and trace them back through the approval and testing chain.

Prepare a single-page supply chain risk control summary that links each clause 10.15 obligation (SBOM adoption, OSS policy, repository access, leakage monitoring, secure coding, testing, vulnerability assessment) to a named control owner, a tool or system, and sample evidence. This enables examiners to assess completeness at a glance and reduces the risk of a gap being overlooked.

Validate that repository access controls are current by running a live access review in the days before examination — confirm that all former employees and contractors have had access revoked, MFA is enforced without exemptions, and audit logs are being ingested into your SIEM. Stale access or unmonitored audit logs are high-visibility red flags for examiners.

Review your incident and alert response records specifically for supply chain events: if secret scanning or SBOM vulnerability alerts were ever triggered, ensure you can demonstrate the full response lifecycle — detection, notification, containment, remediation, and post-incident review. An absence of any historical alerts will prompt examiner scepticism about whether monitoring is genuinely operational.

Ensure your OSS security policy explicitly references third-party platforms (GitHub, npm, PyPI, Docker Hub) and includes controls for infrastructure-as-code dependencies such as Terraform modules and Helm charts. Examiners familiar with recent supply chain incidents will probe whether the institution's scope extends beyond application code to the full build and deployment toolchain.

10.16.title: Shadow IT Risk Management
10.16.auditorFocus: BNM examiners scrutinise whether the institution has formalised, operationalised, and enforced a shadow IT policy rather than merely drafted one. Examiners will probe whether the policy is supported by a discovery mechanism — such as network scanning, cloud access security broker (CASB) logs, or endpoint DLP telemetry — that continuously identifies unsanctioned systems, applications, and cloud services. A primary red flag is a policy that exists on paper but lacks a corresponding register of identified shadow IT assets, remediation records, or escalation evidence showing that identified risks were actually treated. Examiners will also assess whether the institution can demonstrate staff awareness and accountability measures, and whether shadow IT findings feed back into the formal risk management cycle, including risk appetite statements and technology risk registers.

evidenceItems.name: Shadow IT Policy and Framework Document
evidenceItems.description: The board- or senior-management-approved policy that defines shadow IT, articulates the institution's risk appetite toward unsanctioned technology, establishes prohibited and permissible use boundaries, and assigns accountabilities across IT, risk, and business units.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy contains a clear, institution-specific definition of shadow IT covering on-premise software, SaaS applications, cloud storage, personal devices used for work data, and AI tools.
Document carries evidence of Board or Risk Committee approval with version history and review dates, with the most recent review within 12 months.
Policy explicitly mandates discovery, risk assessment, remediation, and escalation obligations with named role accountabilities (e.g., CISO, IT Risk Manager, Business Unit Heads).
Sanctions and disciplinary consequences for non-compliance by staff or vendors are clearly articulated and linked to the HR or vendor management policy.
Policy cross-references related controls such as the Software Asset Management policy, Cloud Risk Management policy, and Acceptable Use Policy to form a coherent control ecosystem.
commonGaps: Policy defines shadow IT narrowly (e.g., only on-premise software) and omits SaaS, personal cloud storage, or generative AI tools increasingly used by staff.
No evidence of periodic review or Board/senior management ratification — document bears an approval date more than two years old.
Accountabilities are assigned to generic roles or departments without named positions, making enforcement accountability ambiguous during examination.
Policy lacks a sanctions framework, reducing its deterrent effect and leaving the institution unable to demonstrate consequence management.
Document exists in isolation without cross-references to related policies, creating gaps where unsanctioned technology could fall outside any control boundary.
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or RSA Archer) policy repository
SharePoint or Confluence policy management intranet site
Board and Risk Committee meeting minutes and resolution records
IT Security or Technology Risk team document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Shadow IT Discovery and Scanning Records
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that the institution actively and continuously identifies unsanctioned systems, applications, and cloud services in use across the enterprise, including scan configurations, tool outputs, and discovery logs.
whatGoodLooksLike: Discovery is automated and recurring (at minimum monthly) using tools such as CASB, network traffic analysis (NTA), endpoint agents, or DNS query logs — with scan schedules and configuration evidence retained.
Scan outputs are timestamped and include classification of findings by risk level (critical, high, medium, low) and asset type (application, cloud service, device).
Coverage extends beyond the corporate network to remote working endpoints, BYOD devices, and public cloud environments used by staff.
Discovery results are reconciled against the approved software/application register to produce a delta report of unsanctioned assets.
Evidence shows that discovery outputs are reviewed by a named responsible party (e.g., IT Security team) within a defined SLA after each scan cycle.
commonGaps: Discovery is ad hoc or performed only during annual audits rather than on a continuous or scheduled basis, leaving prolonged blind spots.
Scanning tools cover only the corporate LAN and do not extend to cloud environments, remote workers, or third-party-managed infrastructure segments.
Outputs are raw tool exports with no triage, classification, or reconciliation against the approved asset register, making it impossible to evidence risk-based decision-making.
No retention of historical scan records, preventing trend analysis or demonstration of improvement over time to examiners.
CASB or DNS filtering tools are deployed but not configured to flag shadow SaaS usage, limiting discovery to only network-visible assets.
suggestedSources: CASB platform reports (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps, Netskope, Zscaler)
Network Access Control (NAC) or SIEM platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, QRadar) query logs
Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tool inventory reports
DNS filtering solution logs (e.g., Cisco Umbrella, Infoblox)
Vulnerability management platform asset discovery module (e.g., Tenable, Qualys)
evidenceItems.format: System-generated reports (CSV/PDF export) with scheduled run metadata

evidenceItems.name: Shadow IT Risk Register and Inventory
evidenceItems.description: A maintained register of all shadow IT assets identified through discovery activities, documenting the risk assessment outcome, business justification (if any), remediation action, and current status for each item.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register contains all fields necessary for risk-based decisions: asset name, owner, business unit, discovery date, data classification of information processed, risk rating, and remediation status.
Each entry has a documented risk assessment outcome and a clear disposition decision — sanctioned with conditions, blocked, or under remediation — with an accountable approver and date.
Register is actively maintained and reflects current state, with evidence of regular (e.g., monthly or quarterly) reconciliation cycles shown through timestamps and reviewer sign-offs.
High-risk entries (e.g., shadow IT processing customer or financial data) have escalation records showing they were raised to CISO or Risk Committee level.
Remediation items have target closure dates and actual closure dates, enabling examination of the institution's treatment timeliness and follow-through.
commonGaps: Register is populated from a single point-in-time exercise and has not been updated since, making it inaccurate and demonstrating absence of an ongoing process.
Risk ratings are applied inconsistently or without documented methodology, with examiners unable to trace how a risk level was determined for a given asset.
Disposition decisions are missing or indicate items are under review for extended periods without escalation, suggesting the institution lacks a functioning remediation process.
Register does not distinguish between shadow IT assets that process regulated or sensitive data (higher risk) and those that do not, indicating absence of risk-based prioritisation.
There is no linkage between the shadow IT register and the broader technology risk register or risk appetite framework, preventing enterprise-level risk visibility.
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk register module (e.g., ServiceNow Risk Management, Archer)
SharePoint list or Excel workbook maintained by IT Risk or IT Security team
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system exception log
IT Service Management (ITSM) platform risk or exception records (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (XLSX/CSV/PDF) with version history

evidenceItems.name: Shadow IT Remediation and Incident Records
evidenceItems.description: Records evidencing that identified shadow IT risks were actively treated through blocking, decommissioning, sanctioning, or migration to approved alternatives, including any incidents arising from shadow IT exposure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation records exist for each high- and critical-rated shadow IT finding, showing the action taken, responsible party, completion date, and evidence of verification (e.g., post-action scan confirming removal).
Firewall, proxy, or CASB block records are retained and cross-referenced to shadow IT findings, demonstrating technical enforcement rather than reliance on policy alone.
Where shadow IT was retrospectively sanctioned (e.g., a business-critical tool), records show a formal risk acceptance sign-off by an appropriate authority with conditions attached.

Any incidents (data exposure, regulatory breaches, or security events) attributable to shadow IT are recorded in the incident management system with root cause attribution to shadow IT and post-incident control improvements documented.
Remediation timelines are consistent with the risk rating — critical items closed within days, high items within weeks — demonstrating risk-proportionate response.
commonGaps: Remediation is documented for some findings but not others, with examiners unable to determine whether omissions represent completed actions or unmanaged risks.
Technical blocking evidence (firewall rules, proxy exceptions) is not linked to the shadow IT register entries, preventing verification that policy decisions were enforced at the control layer.
Sanctioned exceptions lack formal risk acceptance sign-off from an authority with sufficient seniority, or the sign-off predates the risk assessment, suggesting a rubber-stamp process.

Incidents with a shadow IT root cause are recorded in the incident system without the shadow IT attribution tag, preventing the institution from measuring the impact of shadow IT risk materialisation.
Re-scanning or post-remediation verification records are absent, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate that remediated items were actually removed from the environment.
suggestedSources: ITSM incident and change management records (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira)
Firewall and proxy change management logs
CASB enforcement policy activity logs
Technology risk exception approval records from GRC platform
Post-incident review reports held by Technology Risk or IT Security
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system exports (PDF/CSV) and firewall/CASB log extracts

evidenceItems.name: Staff Awareness and Training Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that employees across the institution have been informed of shadow IT risks, the policy requirements, and their personal obligations, including training completion records, awareness campaigns, and communication artefacts.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training records show organisation-wide completion (above 95%) of shadow IT awareness content, with role-specific modules for high-risk groups such as business analysts, project managers, and technology staff.

Training content is current (updated within 12 months) and reflects the institution's specific prohibited and permitted tool categories, including SaaS and AI tools relevant to the institution's context.
Awareness campaigns (e.g., intranet notices, phishing-style simulation alerts for shadow IT behaviour, town halls) are documented with distribution lists and dates.
Training records capture individual completion dates, assessment scores where applicable, and manager-level acknowledgements for business unit heads.
New joiner onboarding documentation includes shadow IT policy acknowledgement as a mandatory step, with records retained in the HR or LMS system.
commonGaps: Training completion rates are below 90% with no evidence of follow-up or escalation for non-completers, undermining the institution's ability to claim effective awareness.
Training content is generic cybersecurity awareness material that does not specifically address shadow IT or the institution's policy requirements.
Training records cannot be disaggregated by business unit or role, preventing demonstration of targeted awareness for higher-risk populations.
No evidence of refresher training or policy re-acknowledgement following material policy updates, leaving staff potentially unaware of changes to permitted and prohibited categories.
Awareness is limited to annual training with no supplementary communications, campaigns, or reminders throughout the year.
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) completion reports (e.g., Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors, internal LMS)
HR onboarding system policy acknowledgement records
Internal communications platform records (e.g., SharePoint intranet, email campaign tools)
Technology Risk or IT Security team training delivery records
evidenceItems.format: LMS system reports (PDF/CSV) and training material artefacts (PDF/PPTX)

evidenceItems.name: Shadow IT Governance and Reporting Records
evidenceItems.description: Management information (MI) reports, committee papers, and governance records demonstrating that shadow IT risk is periodically reported to and overseen by appropriate senior management and risk governance bodies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Periodic MI reports on shadow IT discovery findings, remediation status, and trend metrics are presented to the Technology Risk Committee or equivalent at minimum quarterly, with meeting minutes confirming discussion and action items.
