Reporting includes trend data showing whether shadow IT risk is increasing, stable, or decreasing over time — demonstrating a managed and improving control environment.
Senior management or Board Risk Committee papers reference shadow IT as part of the institution's overall technology risk profile, linking it to risk appetite thresholds.
Action items arising from governance discussions on shadow IT have owners, due dates, and evidence of follow-up in subsequent meeting records.
Annual policy review outcomes, including any changes made in response to new shadow IT threat vectors (e.g., generative AI tools), are documented in governance records.
commonGaps: Shadow IT is not reported as a distinct risk category in management reporting — it is subsumed under general IT risk with no dedicated metrics, making it invisible to governance oversight.
Reporting is produced but not evidenced as having been presented to a governance committee — meeting minutes do not reference shadow IT or show that findings were deliberated.
Metrics reported are outputs (number of scans conducted) rather than outcomes (number of risks identified, remediated, or accepted), limiting meaningful governance oversight.
There is no evidence of governance escalation when shadow IT risk breached a defined threshold, suggesting risk appetite triggers are either absent or not operationalised.
Annual policy review is not documented in governance records, and the policy shows no changes over multiple years despite a rapidly evolving shadow IT landscape (e.g., rise of AI tools).
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Committee and Board Risk Committee meeting minutes and papers
GRC platform management reporting module or dashboard exports
Technology risk dashboard reports distributed to senior management
CISO or CTO periodic technology risk reports
Annual policy review sign-off records from the GRC platform or document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF committee papers and minutes; dashboard exports (PDF/XLSX)

auditTips: Before examination, run a freshness check on the shadow IT register: verify that every open or in-remediation item has been actioned within its stated target date, and that the register reflects the most recent discovery cycle output. Examiners will probe the gap between discovery findings and register entries as evidence of process integrity.

Prepare a control narrative document that maps the end-to-end shadow IT lifecycle — discovery → risk assessment → remediation → governance reporting — to specific tools, responsible teams, and policy references. This enables examiners to trace the process without relying on verbal explanation alone.

Cross-reference shadow IT findings against the incident register to identify any historical incidents attributable to unsanctioned technology. Proactively prepare a summary of lessons learned and control improvements implemented, as examiners will assess whether the institution learns from shadow IT risk materialisation.

Ensure CASB or DNS filtering tool configurations are documented and current, and that block lists include categories relevant to Malaysian financial sector risks such as personal file-sharing (Google Drive, Dropbox), consumer AI tools, and personal email accessed from corporate devices. Examiners may request live demonstrations of detection capability.

Validate training completion data in the LMS against the current employee headcount roster — completion percentages calculated against stale headcount figures are a common gap that undermines the training evidence. Ensure the non-completer follow-up process is documented and evidenced.

Prepare a reconciliation artefact showing the delta between the approved software/application register and shadow IT discovery outputs, with each unsanctioned item's disposition clearly documented. This directly demonstrates the institution's ability to identify risks as required by the clause's verbatim obligation.

Review the most recent governance committee papers to confirm that shadow IT risk metrics include outcome-based indicators (e.g., time-to-remediate, recurrence rate, data exposure events) rather than activity metrics only. Update MI templates before examination if current reporting does not demonstrate risk treatment effectiveness to senior management.

10.17.title: Vulnerability and End-of-Life System Management
10.17.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised — not merely documented — its vulnerability and EOL management obligations. Examiners will cross-check patch registers against vendor advisories and CISA/CVE databases to verify timely remediation, particularly for critical and high-severity vulnerabilities, and will look for evidence that SLA targets are consistently met rather than aspirationally stated in policy. Red flags include stale security baselines that have not been updated following major OS or platform releases, undocumented EOL systems still in production, and exception registers that carry perpetually deferred timelines without genuine risk justification. Examiners specifically scrutinise management approval artefacts for EOL exceptions to confirm they are substantiated by current risk assessments — not templated sign-offs — and that the phase-out timeline is tracked and progressing. The audit will also probe whether digital services (internet-facing applications, APIs, mobile platforms) are explicitly in scope of patching and vulnerability scanning, as these are a common blind spot.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Security Baseline Standards
evidenceItems.description: Formal, versioned security hardening standards for each class of technology component (servers, endpoints, network devices, databases, middleware, cloud services) aligned to recognised frameworks such as CIS Benchmarks or DISA STIGs. These documents demonstrate that the institution maintains a current and accurate baseline as required by clause 10.17(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Each technology component class has a dedicated baseline document with a version number, effective date, and review date no older than 12 months from the last major platform update
Baselines explicitly reference the vendor or framework source (e.g., CIS Benchmark version number) and document the institution's rationale for any deviation from the source benchmark
The baseline review cycle is governed by a formal schedule in the IT security policy, and evidence of the last review (meeting minutes, approval email, change record) is attached
Baselines cover all in-scope assets including digital service components (web servers, API gateways, containerised workloads) and not just traditional on-premises infrastructure
A gap analysis or compliance scan report is linked to each baseline, showing current asset configuration versus the required standard
commonGaps: Baselines exist for legacy infrastructure but have not been extended to cloud workloads, containers, or third-party SaaS components used in digital services
Documents are dated two or more years old and have not been revised despite significant OS or platform version changes in the environment
Baselines are aspirational checklists with no evidence of enforcement or scanning — no configuration compliance report can be produced to demonstrate actual adherence
Deviations from the benchmark standard are undocumented, making it impossible to determine whether hardening shortfalls are accepted risk or oversight
Ownership of each baseline standard is unclear — no named custodian or team is accountable for keeping the document current
suggestedSources: Information Security or Cyber Security team SharePoint/Confluence document library
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) policy and standards module
Configuration Management tool (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) playbook repository as evidence of enforced baselines
Vulnerability management platform (Tenable.sc, Qualys, Rapid7) policy compliance module
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata; supplemented by configuration compliance scan reports in PDF or spreadsheet format

evidenceItems.name: Patch Management Register and SLA Compliance Report
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register tracking all identified vulnerabilities and vendor-released patches against the institution's technology estate, including patch criticality classification, target remediation SLA, actual remediation date, and status. This record demonstrates continuous monitoring and timely implementation of latest patch releases as required by clause 10.17(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: The register captures CVE or vendor advisory reference, CVSS score, affected assets, patch release date, SLA target (e.g., critical ≤7 days, high ≤30 days), remediation date, and closure evidence for every tracked vulnerability

SLA compliance is reported on a monthly basis to IT management with trend analysis, showing that critical and high vulnerabilities are resolved within defined timeframes consistently — not just on a best-efforts basis
The scope explicitly includes digital services, internet-facing systems, and cloud infrastructure, not only internal servers and endpoints
Exceptions to SLA targets are individually documented with a risk acceptance record and compensating controls, rather than being silently excluded from the dashboard
The register is reconciled against vulnerability scan results at least monthly, with a named owner per asset responsible for patch status, demonstrating operational accountability
commonGaps: The register exists but is incomplete — large portions of the asset estate (e.g., OT systems, cloud-native services, third-party managed platforms) are absent without explanation
SLA timelines are defined in policy but the register shows no consistent adherence, with critical vulnerabilities open well beyond the stated target with no exception records
Patch status is recorded as 'In Progress' indefinitely with no escalation trail, making it impossible to determine whether the patch was ever applied
Digital services and internet-facing applications are tracked separately or not at all, creating a compliance blind spot that examiners will specifically probe
The register is updated reactively after audits rather than continuously, evidenced by batch-update timestamps that cluster around audit periods
suggestedSources: ITSM platform patch management module (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
Vulnerability management platform (Tenable, Qualys VMDR, Rapid7 InsightVM) exported reports
GRC platform risk register linked to open vulnerability items
Monthly security operations report produced by the SOC or IT Security team
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system-generated report (PDF/Excel); monthly trend dashboard in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability Scanning Results and Remediation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Authenticated vulnerability scan outputs covering the full technology estate, including digital services, with evidence that identified findings have been triaged, assigned, and remediated. This demonstrates the institution's continuous monitoring capability underpinning clause 10.17(b) and provides the factual basis for remediation decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Scan reports are dated within the last 30 days for critical assets and within the last 90 days for the full estate, with documented scan frequency aligned to policy
Scans are authenticated (credentialed) for internal systems and cover all IP ranges and asset classes including virtual machines, cloud instances, containers, and network appliances
Each report is accompanied by a signed-off triage record that classifies findings by severity, assigns an owner, and sets a target remediation date consistent with the patch SLA policy
Rescan reports are retained to demonstrate closure — the institution can show before-and-after scans confirming that vulnerabilities were remediated rather than merely acknowledged
Scan scope documentation confirms that internet-facing digital services and APIs are included in external scanning, not only the internal network perimeter
commonGaps: Scans are run but results are not actioned in a structured way — there is no triage record linking scan findings to the patch register or change management system
Internal scans use unauthenticated configurations, significantly underreporting the actual vulnerability count and giving a false sense of security
Scan scope excludes certain asset classes (cloud workloads, containerised services, OT/IoT devices) because tooling has not been extended, leaving material gaps undetected
Historical scan reports cannot be retrieved to demonstrate trend improvement, making it impossible to evidence continuous monitoring over the audit period
Scanning frequency does not match policy — policy states monthly but scan timestamps show quarterly or ad hoc execution
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform scan history (Tenable.sc/Nessus, Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM, OpenVAS)
Cloud security posture management (CSPM) tool reports (AWS Security Hub, Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Prisma Cloud)
ITSM change records linked to scan-identified vulnerabilities as remediation evidence
SOC or IT Security monthly operational reports
evidenceItems.format: System-generated scan report (PDF or XML export); triage workbook in spreadsheet format

evidenceItems.name: EOL Technology Register and Remediation Plan
evidenceItems.description: A register of all technology components approaching or at end-of-life status, including vendor EOL dates, current usage context, risk classification, and a documented remediation or migration plan with milestones. This directly evidences compliance with clause 10.17(c) — the obligation to identify, plan, and implement remedial action for EOL systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: The register covers all technology layers — operating systems, middleware, databases, firmware, programming language runtimes, and third-party libraries — with vendor-confirmed EOL dates sourced from official documentation
Each EOL item has an assigned remediation track (upgrade, replacement, or exception) with a project plan, named owner, milestone dates, and current status that is updated at least quarterly

Systems within 12 months of EOL are flagged as 'approaching EOL' and actively tracked with a migration plan in place before the EOL date is reached, demonstrating proactive management rather than reactive crisis response

The register is integrated with or cross-referenced against the asset inventory (CMDB) to ensure completeness — all assets are checked against EOL dates systematically, not just those flagged by individual teams
Progress against remediation milestones is reported to IT management or the relevant governance committee on a regular basis, with escalation procedures for plans falling behind schedule
commonGaps: The register captures major OS EOL dates but omits EOL status for middleware, database versions, programming language runtimes, and open-source libraries — which are equally in scope under the clause
EOL dates are based on assumptions or undated internal knowledge rather than verified vendor documentation, leading to inaccurate tracking
Remediation plans exist as slide deck commitments from previous years but have not been updated to reflect actual progress, revised timelines, or blocking dependencies
The register is maintained by one team and has not been reconciled against the CMDB or asset inventory, meaning EOL systems not visible to that team are undiscovered
Systems that reached EOL status are removed from the register upon reaching EOL rather than being tracked as active exceptions until fully decommissioned, obscuring the true risk picture
suggestedSources: CMDB (ServiceNow CMDB, Lansweeper, Device42) with EOL date fields populated
GRC platform risk register or asset risk module
IT Architecture or Infrastructure team's technology lifecycle roadmap
Vendor product lifecycle pages cross-referenced during annual asset reviews
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export; project plan in PDF or project management tool export (Jira, MS Project)

evidenceItems.name: EOL Exception Records with Risk Assessment and Management Approval
evidenceItems.description: Formal exception records for each instance where the institution continues to operate unsupported or outdated technology beyond its EOL date, including the risk assessment substantiating the exception, compensating controls, phase-out timeline, and evidence of management approval and annual review. This directly addresses the obligations in clause 10.17(d).
whatGoodLooksLike: Each exception record uniquely identifies the EOL system, documents the business justification for continued use, lists compensating controls implemented to mitigate elevated risk, and states a firm phase-out date — not a rolling 'under review' placeholder

The risk assessment is substantive and current — it evaluates the specific threat landscape for the EOL component (available exploits, attack surface, data sensitivity), not a generic template statement that risk is 'medium and accepted'

Management approval is evidenced by a named approver at the appropriate authority level (e.g., CTO or CISO sign-off, or board risk committee approval for material exceptions), with an approval date and reference to the supporting risk assessment
Annual review evidence is retained for each exception — meeting minutes, updated risk assessment, or re-approval record — demonstrating that the exception is not simply left open indefinitely
The exception register is consolidated and reported to the risk governance committee, providing visibility at an enterprise level rather than exceptions being managed in siloed team documents
commonGaps: Exceptions exist informally (noted in emails or verbal agreement) without a structured record that can be produced to examiners as a standalone artefact

Risk assessments accompanying exceptions are templated and not tailored to the specific system — they do not reference known CVEs, exploit availability, or the specific data and services hosted on the EOL component
Phase-out timelines have been revised multiple times without fresh risk justification or re-approval, effectively allowing indefinite continuation of EOL use under a stale exception
Annual reviews are not evidenced — the exception was approved once and has not been formally revisited, which is a direct non-compliance with the clause requirement for at least annual review
Compensating controls listed in the exception record (e.g., network isolation, enhanced monitoring) cannot be evidenced as actually implemented and operating — they exist on paper only
suggestedSources: GRC platform exception management module (RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream)
IT Risk register maintained by the IT Risk or Information Security function
Risk governance committee or IT Steering Committee minutes confirming management approval
ITSM records evidencing implementation of compensating controls listed in the exception
evidenceItems.format: PDF or GRC platform export for each exception record; meeting minutes in PDF for approval and annual review evidence

evidenceItems.name: Patch and Vulnerability Management Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy and operational procedure document that defines the institution's framework for security baseline management, vulnerability scanning, patch classification and SLA targets, EOL identification, and the exception process including management approval requirements. This provides the policy foundation that all other evidence items must be consistent with.
whatGoodLooksLike: The policy explicitly defines patch SLA tiers by severity (e.g., critical ≤7 days, high ≤30 days, medium ≤90 days) and names the governance body responsible for approving EOL exceptions, directly aligning with the obligations in clauses 10.17(a) through (d)
The procedure covers the full lifecycle from vulnerability discovery through triage, remediation, verification, and closure, with clear role and responsibility assignments (RACI) for each step
Digital services, cloud-hosted systems, and third-party managed components are explicitly listed as in-scope, not implicitly assumed to be covered

The policy is reviewed at least annually with evidence of the last review date, reviewer, and approver, and version history shows it has been updated in response to changes in the technology environment

The exception process section specifies the required content of a risk assessment (threat analysis, compensating controls, phase-out timeline) and the minimum approval authority, leaving no ambiguity about what constitutes a valid exception
commonGaps: The policy states requirements at a high level but the procedure document is absent or too vague to operationalise — staff cannot determine from the documents alone how to execute patch management or raise an EOL exception
SLA targets are defined but the policy does not specify how SLAs are measured, reported, or escalated when breached, making SLA compliance reporting inconsistent and difficult to audit
The policy scope statement omits cloud services, SaaS platforms, or third-party managed systems, creating a documented gap that examiners will immediately identify
The EOL exception process is described in the policy but does not specify the required annual review or the minimum content of the risk assessment, leaving the process open to under-compliance

The policy has not been updated since a major technology change (e.g., cloud migration, adoption of containers) and therefore does not reflect the current technology architecture or risk profile of the institution
suggestedSources: Information Security Policy repository on SharePoint or Confluence
GRC platform policy management module with version history and approval workflow
IT Governance or IT Risk team document management system
Board or management committee approval records evidencing policy sign-off
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signatures; stored in a document management system with access audit trail

auditTips: Reconcile the patch register against an independent vulnerability scan export from the same period — if the scan shows open critical CVEs that are not reflected in the register as remediated or formally excepted, this signals a process breakdown that examiners will escalate. Resolve all discrepancies before the examination and document the resolution trail.

Map every EOL system in the environment against the exception register and confirm that each has a current, individually approved exception record. EOL systems that appear in the CMDB but have no corresponding exception record represent a direct 10.17(d) finding — even one undocumented instance is sufficient grounds for a supervisory observation.

Verify that annual review evidence exists for all active EOL exceptions, specifically looking for records older than 12 months without a re-approval event. Prepare a one-page summary for each exception showing the approval date, last review date, current phase-out timeline, and status of compensating controls, so examiners can assess the portfolio at a glance.

Test the completeness of your asset inventory by cross-referencing it against three independent sources — the network discovery tool, the vulnerability scanner's asset list, and the cloud billing or resource inventory. Assets appearing in one source but not others are likely unmanaged and therefore outside the patch management process, which is a systemic gap under 10.17(b).

Ensure that digital services and internet-facing systems can be traced through the entire evidence chain: from the security baseline document, through the vulnerability scan scope, into the patch register, and out to remediation records. If an examiner picks any internet-facing application and asks for its end-to-end compliance evidence, you must be able to produce it without relying on institutional memory.

Prepare a concise dashboard or one-page summary showing patch SLA compliance rates by severity tier over the last 12 months. Examiners will not want to manually calculate compliance from a raw register — presenting pre-computed metrics with trend lines demonstrates mature operational control and shifts the conversation from data retrieval to control effectiveness.

Cross-reference the EOL technology register against the vendor product lifecycle pages on the day of examination preparation and update any dates that have changed — vendors sometimes revise EOL dates, and using stale dates in front of an examiner who has independently verified the current date will undermine the institution's credibility on the entire clause.

10.18.title: Patch and EOL Management Framework
10.18.auditorFocus: BNM examiners evaluate whether the institution has operationalised a structured, risk-based patch and EOL management framework — not merely documented one. Examiners will cross-reference the patch policy against ITSM ticket records and vulnerability scan outputs to verify that stated turnaround times are actually met, with particular scrutiny on critical and high-severity patches. A key red flag is the presence of EOL systems in production without a documented exception, risk acceptance, or time-bound migration plan. Examiners also probe whether compatibility testing is a genuine gate in the deployment workflow or a paper formality bypassed under operational pressure. End-user awareness is assessed through training records and communication artefacts, ensuring staff impacted by EOL transitions were informed in an orderly and timely manner.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Asset Inventory with Patch and EOL Risk Register
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated register of all technology assets — hardware, operating systems, middleware, databases, and third-party software — that includes current patch status, vendor support lifecycle dates, EOL dates, and associated vulnerability risk ratings. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(a) by showing the institution has identified all assets and assessed their exposure to undeployed patches or EOL status.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory is comprehensive and includes all asset categories: servers, endpoints, network devices, databases, middleware, and third-party applications, with no unexplained gaps
Each asset record includes the current patch level, date of last patch, vendor EOL date, and a risk rating (e.g., Critical/High/Medium/Low) derived from a documented methodology
EOL assets are explicitly flagged and linked to either a remediation plan, a time-bound migration roadmap, or a formally approved risk acceptance with senior management sign-off
The register is refreshed at a defined frequency (at minimum quarterly) and shows evidence of recent updates, with a version history or audit trail demonstrating ongoing maintenance

Vulnerability scan outputs from tools such as Tenable Nessus, Qualys, or Microsoft Defender are reconciled to the asset inventory, confirming that scan coverage matches the registered asset population
commonGaps: Asset inventory covers only servers and endpoints, omitting network devices, OT/SCADA systems, or third-party SaaS components that are in scope for patch management
EOL dates are absent or outdated, leaving examiners unable to verify whether assets approaching end-of-support have been identified and risk-assessed
Risk ratings are assigned without a documented methodology, appearing subjective or inconsistently applied across asset types
The register has not been updated in over 90 days, indicating it is maintained episodically rather than as a living operational record
Vulnerability scan data is not reconciled to the inventory, leaving unscanned assets undetected and creating blind spots that examiners will flag
suggestedSources: GRC platform asset module (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Archer, MetricStream)
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system or CMDB maintained by IT Operations
Vulnerability management platform exports (Tenable.io, Qualys VMDR, Rapid7 InsightVM)
Network discovery tool outputs (e.g., Lansweeper, SolarWinds Network Atlas)
Vendor support lifecycle databases cross-referenced by the infrastructure team
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform report export (PDF/CSV), with version history

evidenceItems.name: Patch Management Policy and Prioritisation Framework
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy document that defines the institution's criteria, severity classifications, priority tiers, and mandatory turnaround times for patch deployment. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(b) by evidencing that the institution has formally established a risk-based framework for deciding what gets patched, in what order, and within what timeframe.
whatGoodLooksLike: The policy explicitly maps vulnerability severity ratings (e.g., CVSS scores or CVE classifications) to defined priority tiers, with each tier assigned a mandatory remediation turnaround time (e.g., Critical: 72 hours, High: 14 days, Medium: 30 days, Low: 90 days)
Criteria for patch prioritisation include factors beyond severity alone, such as asset criticality, exposure to the internet, regulatory or payment system relevance, and exploitability in the wild
The policy defines roles and responsibilities for each stage of the patch lifecycle, including who is accountable for assessment, approval, deployment, and verification
The document has been formally approved by the Chief Information Officer or Board-level technology risk committee, with a review cycle of at most annually and evidence of the most recent review

Scope of the policy explicitly covers all asset categories in the inventory, including third-party systems managed by vendors under outsourcing arrangements, with clear obligations placed on those vendors
commonGaps: Turnaround times are defined in vague terms (e.g., 'as soon as practicable' or 'within a reasonable timeframe') rather than specific, measurable SLAs tied to severity tiers
The policy does not address vendor-managed or outsourced systems, creating an ambiguity examiners will probe — particularly for cloud-hosted and SaaS platforms

Severity criteria rely solely on CVSS base scores without contextual adjustments for asset criticality or threat intelligence, resulting in low-risk assets being patched urgently while critical exposed systems are deprioritised

The policy has not been reviewed or updated to reflect changes in the asset landscape, regulatory expectations, or threat environment, with the last review date more than 24 months prior to examination
No exception-handling process is defined for cases where patches cannot be deployed within the mandated timeframe, leaving staff without a governed path for compensating controls or risk acceptance
suggestedSources: IT Governance or Technology Risk policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC document management module)
IT Operations team — patch management runbooks and standard operating procedures
Board Technology and Cyber Risk Committee approved policy register
ISO 27001 or NIST-aligned information security management framework documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with approval signature page, version control log, and review history

evidenceItems.name: Patch Compatibility Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that compatibility testing was conducted in a non-production environment prior to patch deployment in production. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(c) by evidencing that the institution has a structured testing gate to minimise disruption to connected systems from patch-induced failures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each patch deployment record references a corresponding test record that specifies the test environment used, the test cases executed, the systems and integrations tested, and the pass/fail outcome
Test records are maintained for a representative sample of deployments across all severity tiers, not only for critical patches, demonstrating consistent application of the testing requirement

The testing environment is documented as representative of production — including connected system dependencies — so that compatibility failures in test environments are meaningful predictors of production behaviour
Test records include regression testing results for dependent and connected systems, not merely confirmation that the patched system itself functions after update

Where expedited deployment was approved for critical patches bypassing full compatibility testing, a documented risk acceptance or compensating control (e.g., rollback plan, enhanced monitoring) is on file
commonGaps: Test records exist for operating system patches but are absent for application patches, database patches, or firmware updates, suggesting testing is applied inconsistently
The test environment is not documented as mirroring production dependencies, making it impossible for examiners to assess whether testing results are reliable indicators of production compatibility
Test records are informal (e.g., email threads or verbal sign-offs) with no structured test case documentation, making it difficult to demonstrate what was actually tested
Critical patches are routinely fast-tracked to production without any testing record or documented compensating control, and the policy does not provide a governed exception path for this
There is no evidence that regression testing covered downstream or laterally connected systems, meaning patch-induced failures in integrations would not have been detected in testing
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change and test management module (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
Test management tools (HP ALM/Quality Center, Zephyr, qTest)
IT Operations or DevOps team — patch deployment runbooks and test environment configuration records
Change Advisory Board (CAB) meeting minutes referencing test outcomes for approved changes
evidenceItems.format: System-generated test records (PDF export or ITSM report), supplemented by test scripts in XLSX or DOCX

evidenceItems.name: End-to-End Patch Deployment Workflow Records and Approval Logs
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating adherence to the institution's patch deployment workflow, including change requests, approvals, deployment logs, post-deployment monitoring, and tracking artefacts. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(d) by evidencing that the full end-to-end patch lifecycle — from identification through closure — is governed, approved, and auditable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each patch deployment is traceable through the ITSM system from initial vulnerability identification or vendor advisory through change request, CAB or delegate approval, deployment execution log, post-deployment verification, and ticket closure — with timestamps at each stage

Approval records demonstrate appropriate delegation of authority: routine patches approved at operational level, significant or high-risk patches escalated to a Change Advisory Board or senior IT risk owner
Deployment logs are system-generated and include the executing account, timestamp, target systems, and deployment outcome (success/failure/partial), providing a tamper-evident audit trail

Post-deployment monitoring records demonstrate that each deployment was followed by a defined observation period, with any anomalies, service degradations, or rollbacks documented and linked to the originating change record

SLA compliance reports show the percentage of patches deployed within the mandated turnaround time per severity tier, with breaches explained and evidenced with escalation or compensating control records
commonGaps: The workflow exists in policy but ITSM records show patches being deployed outside the change management process — particularly emergency patches — leaving gaps in the audit trail
Approval records are informal (e.g., email approvals) rather than captured within the ITSM workflow, making it difficult to demonstrate consistent governance and delegation of authority

Post-deployment monitoring is not documented as a formal step; examiners find no records of observation periods, health checks, or rollback decisions, undermining confidence that deployments are actively verified
SLA compliance is not tracked or reported, so the institution cannot demonstrate whether patches are meeting mandated turnaround times or quantify the extent of any overdue patches
Tracking of open, pending, and overdue patches is not centralised, resulting in fragmented records across multiple teams that cannot be reconciled to show a complete picture of patch currency
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy) — change records, approval logs, deployment tickets
Patch management platform reports (Microsoft SCCM/Intune, Red Hat Satellite, IBM BigFix, Ivanti Patch for Endpoint Manager)
Change Advisory Board (CAB) meeting minutes and decision registers
IT Operations patch compliance dashboards and SLA reporting
SIEM platform for post-deployment monitoring correlation (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar)
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV), supplemented by CAB minutes (PDF/DOCX) and patch compliance dashboards (PDF/screenshot)

evidenceItems.name: EOL System Register, Risk Acceptance, and Migration Roadmap
evidenceItems.description: A dedicated register of all technology assets that have reached or are approaching end-of-life, along with documented risk assessments, approved risk acceptances (where applicable), compensating controls in place, and time-bound migration or decommissioning plans. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(a) specifically in relation to EOL systems, and supports clause 10.18(e) by evidencing planned transitions.
whatGoodLooksLike: All EOL assets are captured in a dedicated register with the asset name, classification, vendor-confirmed EOL date, business owner, risk rating, and current status (in-migration, decommissioned, risk-accepted)

For each EOL asset still in production, a formally approved risk acceptance is on file, signed by an appropriate risk owner, and specifying the compensating controls implemented (e.g., network isolation, enhanced monitoring, virtual patching) and an expiry date for the acceptance

Migration or decommissioning plans are time-bound and specific, identifying the target replacement system, key milestones, responsible parties, and a planned completion date — and are tracked against actuals
The register distinguishes between assets approaching EOL (within 12 months) and assets that are already EOL, enabling proactive planning rather than reactive response
The EOL register is reviewed and reported to the Technology Risk Committee or equivalent governance body at least annually, with escalation of high-risk EOL assets to senior management
commonGaps: No dedicated EOL register exists; EOL assets are informally tracked in email or spreadsheets maintained by individual teams, preventing a consolidated view for examination

Risk acceptances for EOL systems are absent or undated, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate that continued operation of EOL assets is a deliberate, governed decision rather than an oversight
Compensating controls for EOL systems are not documented or tested, making it impossible to demonstrate that risk is being actively managed during the period between EOL and decommissioning

Migration roadmaps are high-level and aspirational, lacking specific milestones, assigned owners, or evidence of progress tracking — undermining confidence that transitions will occur in an orderly manner
The register does not cover EOL software components embedded in applications (e.g., deprecated libraries, unsupported frameworks), focusing only on operating systems and hardware
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk register or asset risk module
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system with vendor support lifecycle data
Infrastructure and Operations team — EOL tracking spreadsheets and project plans
Technology Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee papers and minutes
Vendor support lifecycle databases (Microsoft Product Lifecycle, Red Hat Life Cycle Dates, Oracle Support Policies)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF), with linked risk acceptance sign-off documents (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: End-User Awareness Programme Records for EOL Transitions
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that end-users impacted by EOL system transitions were informed through a structured awareness programme enabling orderly transition. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.18(e) by evidencing that communication, training, and support activities were planned and executed to minimise disruption caused by technology transitions.
whatGoodLooksLike: A communication plan for each material EOL transition is on file, specifying the target audience, communication channels, messaging, timeline, and accountable owner — demonstrating that awareness was planned rather than ad hoc

Training or briefing materials specific to the transitioning technology are available, and attendance or completion records show that affected staff received the relevant awareness before the transition date
Acknowledgement records (e.g., email confirmations, system-logged training completions, or signed acknowledgement forms) provide evidence that end-users received and acted upon the communication

Post-transition feedback or help-desk incident data is referenced to demonstrate that the awareness programme was effective — measured, for example, by a decline in transition-related support calls after go-live
The awareness programme covers not only technical end-users but also business users and third-party staff who interact with the transitioning system, reflecting a comprehensive scope
commonGaps: Awareness activities are limited to an email broadcast with no evidence of reach, comprehension, or acknowledgement — falling short of a structured programme as implied by 'orderly transition'

Training records do not distinguish between EOL transition awareness and general IT training, making it impossible to demonstrate that specific, relevant communication was provided for the transition in question

Communication plans exist for major system replacements but not for incremental EOL transitions (e.g., operating system upgrades, browser version changes), leaving gaps for smaller but operationally significant changes
No post-transition review or effectiveness measure is on file, so the institution cannot demonstrate that the awareness programme achieved its objective of enabling an orderly transition

Third-party or vendor staff who use affected systems are excluded from the awareness programme, creating a residual risk of disruption that examiners will probe if any third-party-related incidents occurred during transitions
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) — training completion records and course enrolment logs
Internal communications platform (SharePoint intranet, email distribution records, Microsoft Teams announcements)
HR or Training department — awareness campaign records and attendance registers
IT Service Desk — incident logs for transition-related support requests (pre- and post-transition comparison)
Project management records for EOL transition projects — communication plans, stakeholder registers, and status reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (communication plans, training materials), LMS export (PDF/CSV for completion records), email records (EML/PDF)

auditTips: Before examination, run a reconciliation exercise between the vulnerability scan output and the asset inventory to ensure scan coverage is 100% of registered assets — examiners will ask for this reconciliation and any unexplained gaps will be treated as a control deficiency under clause 10.18(a).

Prepare an SLA compliance report broken down by severity tier (Critical, High, Medium, Low) covering at least the preceding 12 months, showing the percentage of patches deployed within mandated turnaround times and a root-cause explanation for any breaches — this directly addresses clause 10.18(b) and demonstrates the framework is operative, not just documented.

For any EOL system in production at the time of examination, ensure a complete evidence pack is assembled per asset: the risk acceptance with senior management sign-off, a description of compensating controls and evidence they are functioning, and a specific migration or decommissioning plan with progress tracking — examiners view undocumented EOL systems as one of the highest-risk findings under clause 10.18.

Conduct a dry-run walkthrough of a recent patch deployment using only ITSM records to trace the end-to-end workflow from vulnerability identification through to closure — if the audit trail breaks down at any stage (e.g., testing records missing, approval not logged in system), identify and remediate the gap before examination, as clause 10.18(d) requires demonstrable adherence to the full workflow.

Cross-reference the patch management policy's stated turnaround times against actual ITSM ticket timestamps for a sample of deployments across each severity tier; where breaches are found, ensure exception records, escalation trails, or compensating control approvals exist — an examiner who finds policy-to-practice gaps without corresponding exception documentation will conclude the framework is non-operative.

Compile a single consolidated EOL register ahead of examination that draws from all asset types (infrastructure, applications, databases, network, OT/SCADA if applicable) and present it alongside governance evidence showing it was reviewed by the Technology Risk Committee — this prevents examiners from finding fragmented EOL tracking across multiple teams and demonstrates centralised oversight as expected by clause 10.18(a).

For each material EOL transition completed in the preceding examination period, assemble the end-user awareness evidence pack — communication plan, training materials, completion records, and at minimum one indicator of effectiveness (e.g., help-desk ticket data) — and map it explicitly to the asset in the EOL register; examiners assessing clause 10.18(e) will follow this thread from the EOL record to the awareness evidence and expect a clear, documented connection.

10.19.title: Technology Effectiveness Monitoring
10.19.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a systematic, forward-looking process for monitoring technology effectiveness — not merely reactive patching. Examiners specifically verify that the board is receiving substantive, timely advice on the evolving technology landscape, including emerging threats and disruptions to existing security controls, rather than generic IT update briefings. A key red flag is a long-term technology strategy that lacks explicit linkage to cyber adversary intelligence (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK developments, threat actor TTPs) or that is not resourced with clearly identified competent personnel. Examiners also scrutinise the system migration roadmap for concreteness — vague aspirational plans without milestones, owners, funding allocation, and risk assessments are treated as non-compliance. The 'S' marker signals this is a supervisory expectation that examiners will probe through direct questioning of CISO, CTO, and board members to confirm alignment between board-level understanding and operational execution.

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Effectiveness Advisory Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports presented to the board (or board risk/technology committee) advising on the impact of evolving technology developments and disruptions to existing security controls, fulfilling the obligation under clause 10.19(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are presented at least quarterly and include a dedicated section on technology landscape developments (e.g., quantum computing, AI-driven attacks, cryptographic shifts) and their specific security control implications for the institution
Each report explicitly maps identified technology developments to existing security controls that may be disrupted, with a materiality or risk rating assigned
Board minutes or resolutions confirm that the board discussed, questioned, and directed action on at least one technology risk advisory per reporting cycle
Reports reference external intelligence sources (e.g., BNM circulars, CISA advisories, FS-ISAC threat bulletins, NIST guidance) to demonstrate awareness is externally informed
Advisory reports are signed off by the CISO or CTO and show a clear escalation trail from technical teams to board level
commonGaps: Reports cover only operational IT metrics (uptime, incidents) without forward-looking technology landscape analysis — examiners view these as insufficient to satisfy clause 10.19(a)
Board minutes reflect perfunctory acknowledgement of reports with no recorded discussion, questions, or directives, indicating the board is not genuinely receiving or acting on the advice
Technology advisory is bundled into generic risk committee updates without a distinct focus on security control disruption from emerging technologies
Reports are produced annually rather than on a cycle that keeps pace with the speed of technology change, leaving months-long blind spots
No traceability between board advisory content and subsequent management action or strategy updates, breaking the governance chain required by the clause
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee or Board Technology Committee secretariat — board pack archives
Board meeting minutes repository (SharePoint / board portal such as Diligent or BoardEffect)
CISO / CTO office — periodic technology briefing decks and accompanying narrative reports
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — risk reporting module for board-level outputs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (board presentation decks and narrative reports); PDF (certified board minutes extracts)

evidenceItems.name: Long-Term Technology Risk and Cyber Strategy
evidenceItems.description: A documented multi-year strategy that addresses anticipated technology changes, allocates competent resources, and specifically incorporates evolving cyber adversary tactics and techniques, fulfilling the obligation under clause 10.19(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Strategy document spans a minimum 3-year horizon with annual review cycles, contains explicit technology evolution scenarios (e.g., post-quantum cryptography transition, zero-trust adoption), and maps each scenario to required capability changes
Cyber adversary tactics and techniques are addressed using a recognised framework (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK) with documented processes for tracking TTP evolution and updating the strategy accordingly

Resource allocation section identifies named competent roles (e.g., cloud security engineers, threat intelligence analysts), headcount plans, budget envelopes, and skills gap analysis with remediation timelines
Strategy is board-approved, version-controlled, and shows evidence of annual refresh with change history documenting what was updated and why
Strategy links explicitly to the institution's risk appetite statement and includes KPIs or OKRs that allow management to track execution progress
commonGaps: Strategy document exists but focuses solely on infrastructure refresh without addressing cyber adversary evolution — the 'new cyber adversary tactics and techniques' obligation under 10.19(b) is unmet
Resource allocation is stated at a high level (e.g., 'additional headcount will be considered') without concrete numbers, budget, or skills criteria, making competency commitments unverifiable
Strategy is not reviewed or updated on a regular cycle, resulting in documents that are 2-3 years stale and fail to reflect current threat actors or technology disruptions
Strategy is owned solely by IT operations without CISO involvement, meaning cyber adversary TTPs are not incorporated as a first-class input
Board approval is missing or informal — strategy exists as a management document only, without documented board endorsement or oversight
suggestedSources: CISO / CTO office — multi-year IT and cybersecurity strategy documents
Strategy and Planning department — enterprise technology roadmap
Human Resources — technology workforce planning and skills gap assessment records
GRC platform — risk treatment plans linked to technology risk categories
Threat Intelligence team — TTP tracking registers (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK Navigator layers)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (strategy document with version history); XLSX (resource allocation and skills gap matrix); PDF (board approval minutes)

evidenceItems.name: System Migration Roadmap
evidenceItems.description: A formal, time-bound roadmap documenting planned migration of technology systems to preserve security and reliability of the infrastructure in an orderly manner, fulfilling the obligation under clause 10.19(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: Roadmap covers all in-scope systems and explicitly prioritises migrations based on security risk, end-of-life proximity, and operational criticality, with each system assigned a target migration date and owner
Each migration entry documents the security and reliability rationale (e.g., cryptographic vulnerability, vendor EoL, unsupported OS), ensuring the roadmap is risk-driven rather than opportunistic
Roadmap includes defined milestones (e.g., discovery, design, pilot, cutover, decommission), progress tracking against those milestones, and escalation triggers for delays
Roadmap is integrated with the institution's IT asset register and patch management register so newly identified EoL systems are automatically fed into roadmap planning
Roadmap is reviewed and approved by senior management (CTO/CISO level) at least annually and after material technology events (e.g., vendor EoL announcements, critical CVE disclosures)
commonGaps: Roadmap exists for major platforms (e.g., core banking) but omits ancillary or legacy systems, leaving untracked EoL assets that represent security risks
Migration entries lack specific target dates or owners, making the 'orderly manner' requirement undemonstrable to examiners
Roadmap is not integrated with the asset register, so newly discovered EoL systems are not systematically fed into migration planning
Security rationale is absent — migrations appear driven by commercial refresh cycles rather than a deliberate analysis of security and reliability preservation
Roadmap progress is not formally tracked or reported, meaning delays accumulate without escalation, and the institution cannot demonstrate active management of migration risks
suggestedSources: IT Architecture team — enterprise technology roadmap and asset lifecycle plans
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — change management and project tracking modules
IT Asset Management (ITAM) platform (e.g., ServiceNow ITAM, Lansweeper) — EoL and vendor support status data
PMO (Project Management Office) — active and pipeline project registers
Patch and Vulnerability Management team — vulnerability registers linked to EoL status
evidenceItems.format: XLSX or GRC/ITSM system export (roadmap with milestones and owners); PDF (management approval sign-off); Gantt chart or project plan

evidenceItems.name: Technology Effectiveness Monitoring Programme
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the institution's structured, continual monitoring process for assessing the effectiveness and security of technology in use, including defined review cadences, methodologies, and outputs that demonstrate ongoing surveillance of security control adequacy.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined policy or procedure document establishes the monitoring programme scope, frequency, tools, responsible parties, and escalation procedures, with explicit reference to reviewing whether emerging technology developments disrupt existing controls

Monitoring outputs (e.g., technology effectiveness review reports, control testing results) are produced on a defined schedule and show trend analysis across review cycles rather than point-in-time snapshots

The programme incorporates external intelligence feeds (e.g., CERT/CC, vendor security advisories, FSISAC) as systematic inputs, with documented processes for assessing their relevance to in-use technology
Monitoring covers the full technology stack including cloud services, third-party platforms, and OT/IoT assets, not limited to on-premise core systems
Monitoring outputs demonstrably feed into strategy updates, board advisory reports, and migration roadmap adjustments, closing the loop between detection and response
commonGaps: Monitoring is conflated with vulnerability scanning or patch compliance reporting — these are necessary but insufficient; the clause requires assessment of whether technology remains effective against evolving security disruptions
No formal programme exists; monitoring is ad hoc and triggered only by incidents or vendor notifications, with no proactive horizon-scanning component
Coverage gaps exist for cloud-hosted systems, SaaS platforms, or third-party managed services, which are excluded from the monitoring scope without documented justification
Monitoring outputs are not traceably linked to board advisory reports or strategy updates, leaving a broken governance chain that examiners will flag
Frequency is inadequate relative to the pace of technology change — annual reviews in a context requiring 'continual' monitoring are likely to be challenged by examiners
suggestedSources: CISO office — technology risk monitoring policy and procedure documents
GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) — control monitoring schedules and results
Security Operations Centre (SOC) — technology health dashboards and periodic effectiveness reviews
Cloud Security team — cloud security posture management (CSPM) reports (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Wiz, Prisma Cloud)
Threat Intelligence team — intelligence feed ingestion logs and relevance assessment records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy and procedure); System export or PDF (monitoring reports and dashboards); XLSX (monitoring schedule and results register)

evidenceItems.name: Competent Resource Register and Skills Assurance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution has identified, allocated, and maintains competent resources to manage risks arising from anticipated technology changes and evolving cyber adversary tactics, directly supporting the resource allocation obligation under clause 10.19(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: A skills matrix maps required competencies (e.g., threat intelligence analysis, cloud security architecture, cryptographic engineering) to named individuals or roles, with current proficiency levels and target states documented

Training and certification records show that staff responsible for technology risk and cyber strategy hold current, relevant qualifications (e.g., CISSP, CISM, cloud security certifications, threat intelligence certifications) with renewal tracking
Resource adequacy is formally reviewed at least annually against the evolving technology risk landscape, with documented outcomes including headcount adjustments or upskilling investments
Third-party or managed service provider arrangements supplement internal competencies where gaps exist, with contractual evidence that specialist skills are available when needed
Succession plans or knowledge retention arrangements exist for critical technology risk roles to prevent single points of failure in competency coverage
commonGaps: Resource allocation in strategy documents is not matched by actual headcount or training investments — a commitment exists on paper but is not operationalised

Skills matrix is generic and does not address the specific cyber adversary TTP knowledge required by clause 10.19(b), such as familiarity with threat actor groups targeting Malaysian financial services
Training records exist but are not linked to the specific technology risks identified in the monitoring programme or strategy, making the connection between capability and risk management invisible
Competency review is triggered only by staff departures rather than by changes in the technology risk landscape, meaning the institution is reactive rather than proactive
Third-party reliance for specialist skills is undocumented or uncontracted, creating unverifiable competency claims
suggestedSources: Human Resources — staff training records, certification logs, and organisational charts
CISO / CTO office — skills matrix and competency framework documents
Learning Management System (LMS) — course completion and certification tracking
Procurement / Vendor Management — managed security service contracts and scope-of-service agreements
Succession Planning records — held by HR or senior management
evidenceItems.format: XLSX (skills matrix and training tracker); PDF (certificates and qualifications); PDF/DOCX (competency review reports and succession plans)

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Adversary Intelligence Integration Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution systematically incorporates developments in cyber adversary tactics and techniques into its technology risk management processes, strategy, and board advisory cycle, fulfilling the specific cyber threat dimension of clause 10.19(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented threat intelligence lifecycle process shows how external TTP data (e.g., from MITRE ATT&CK, FS-ISAC, BNM/MyCERT alerts, commercial threat intel feeds) is ingested, assessed for relevance to in-use technology, and escalated to risk management and strategy processes

Regular threat intelligence reports (at least quarterly) demonstrate that new or emerging adversary TTPs relevant to the financial sector are being tracked, with documented assessments of whether existing security controls adequately address them

Strategy documents and board advisory reports show direct references to specific threat actor groups or TTP developments, confirming that threat intelligence is influencing decisions rather than being siloed in the SOC
Control gap assessments triggered by significant TTP developments are documented, with remediation actions tracked to closure through the GRC or ITSM platform

Participation in sector-wide threat intelligence sharing (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM-coordinated exercises, NACSA engagement) is evidenced, demonstrating external situational awareness beyond the institution's own telemetry
commonGaps: Threat intelligence is consumed by the SOC for operational detection purposes but is not fed into the technology strategy or board advisory process — the strategic dimension of clause 10.19(b) is unmet
TTP tracking relies solely on generic commercial feeds without Malaysian or APAC financial sector-specific threat actor intelligence, limiting the relevance of assessments
No documented process exists for translating TTP developments into control gap assessments — threat intelligence and risk management operate as separate, disconnected functions
Threat intelligence reports are classified at a level that prevents sharing with board members, meaning the board advisory obligation under 10.19(a) cannot be fulfilled with the necessary specificity
Participation in sector intelligence sharing is informal or undocumented, making it impossible to demonstrate to examiners that the institution has external situational awareness
suggestedSources: Threat Intelligence team — intelligence platform (e.g., MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali, Recorded Future) and report archives
SOC — TTP tracking registers and detection rule change logs
GRC platform — risk records linked to threat intelligence inputs and control gap assessments
FS-ISAC or NACSA membership records and shared intelligence artefacts
CISO office — board advisory report inputs and threat briefing materials
evidenceItems.format: PDF (threat intelligence reports and board briefings); System export (GRC control gap assessments); PDF (FS-ISAC or NACSA membership and participation records)

auditTips: Map each sub-clause obligation (a), (b), and (c) to a distinct evidence item before the examination and prepare a compliance matrix that cross-references each obligation to specific document references, page numbers, and system exports — examiners will test each sub-clause separately and a consolidated 'technology strategy' document that conflates all three without clearly addressing each will be treated as a gap.

Prepare the CISO and CTO for direct examiner questioning on board engagement: examiners commonly ask board members directly whether they recall receiving advice on the evolving technology landscape, so ensure there is alignment between what board advisory reports contain and what board members can articulate — a disconnect is a significant red flag.

For the system migration roadmap under clause 10.19(c), pull a current point-in-time extract from the IT asset register and cross-reference it against the roadmap to confirm all EoL or high-risk systems are captured — examiners may request this cross-reference themselves, and an institution that cannot produce it on demand will struggle to demonstrate 'orderly' migration management.

Demonstrate that the technology monitoring programme is genuinely continual by preparing a timeline of monitoring outputs over the past 12 months — a single annual review will not satisfy the 'continual' requirement in clause 10.19's opening sentence, and examiners will look for evidence of regular cadence across the review period.

For the cyber adversary TTP dimension of clause 10.19(b), prepare a concrete example showing end-to-end traceability: a specific TTP development (e.g., a new ransomware technique targeting financial sector MFA) that was ingested from a named intelligence source, assessed for relevance, resulted in a control gap finding, was escalated to management and the board, and triggered a strategy or roadmap update — this narrative demonstrates the governance chain in action rather than as a paper process.

Validate that board advisory reports under clause 10.19(a) are substantively different from routine IT operational reports: examiners distinguish between reports that inform the board of what has happened (reactive) versus reports that advise the board on what technology developments are emerging and what their business operation impact could be (proactive) — review the last four board advisory packs before the examination and ensure at least one substantive forward-looking technology landscape section is present in each.

Before the examination, conduct a dry-run interview with the CISO, CTO, and a board technology committee member using BNM examiner-style questions (e.g., 'How does the institution ensure new cyber adversary techniques are reflected in your long-term strategy?' and 'Walk me through how a technology disruption would be escalated to the board') — misaligned or unprepared responses during the actual examination will prompt deeper scrutiny even where adequate documentation exists.

10.20.title: Cryptography Policy and Standards
10.20.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has moved beyond a high-level cryptography policy to operationally embedded controls across all five sub-requirements. Examiners will specifically test whether the cryptographic asset inventory is actively maintained and reconciled against production systems — a static or incomplete inventory is a primary red flag. They will probe whether the annual review of algorithms is substantive, documented with risk-based conclusions, and results in remediation actions where weakened algorithms are identified, rather than a superficial checklist exercise. Key lifecycle management is scrutinised end-to-end, with examiners looking for evidence that controls exist at each stage (generation through destruction) and that segregation of duties is enforced. The compromise-recovery plan is evaluated not only for its existence but for evidence of actual testing, including documented test outcomes, identified gaps, and remediation tracking — an untested plan is treated as a significant gap.

evidenceItems.name: Cryptography Policy Document
evidenceItems.description: The board- or senior management-approved cryptography policy that formally articulates the institution's commitment to strong cryptographic controls and explicitly addresses all five sub-requirements under clause 10.20 — algorithm standards, key lifecycle management, periodic review, asset inventory, and compromise-recovery planning.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references recognised standards bodies (e.g., NIST SP 800-series, ISO/IEC 18033, FIPS 140-2/3) and mandates specific approved algorithms for each cryptographic purpose (encryption, MAC, hashing, digital signatures, DRBG)
Policy version history shows at least annual review with a named approver, dated approval signatures, and change log entries reflecting updates to reflect evolving algorithm guidance
All five sub-requirements of clause 10.20 are individually and explicitly addressed in discrete policy sections — not bundled under a single generic paragraph
Policy clearly assigns ownership roles (e.g., CISO, CTO, Key Management Officer) with accountability for each control domain covered by the clause
Policy scope statement explicitly covers critical systems, external-linked systems, and transactional customer-facing applications, with no carve-outs that leave material systems ungoverned
commonGaps: Policy references outdated or deprecated algorithms (e.g., 3DES, SHA-1, RSA-1024) without a documented exception or migration timeline, indicating the annual review process is not feeding back into policy updates

Compromise-recovery planning and key lifecycle management are referenced in the policy but delegated entirely to separate procedural documents with no minimum standards or controls articulated at the policy level
Policy approval is by IT management only, with no evidence of board or senior management endorsement, undermining its standing as an institution-wide governance instrument
Policy scope is limited to data-at-rest or data-in-transit but does not address cryptographic controls for code signing, certificate management, or inter-system authentication
Policy lacks a defined exception management process — there is no mechanism for documenting, approving, or tracking deviations from mandated algorithm standards
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) — policy register module
SharePoint or Confluence policy library maintained by Information Security or Risk Management
Board or Risk Committee meeting minutes and resolution records confirming approval
ITSM document management system with version control and approval workflow audit trail
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Key Lifecycle Management Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented operational procedures covering each stage of the cryptographic key lifecycle as enumerated in clause 10.20(b) — generation, distribution, renewal, usage, storage, recovery, revocation, and destruction — together with system-generated logs or records demonstrating these procedures are being followed in production.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure document contains a discrete, numbered section for each of the eight lifecycle stages, with specific controls, responsible roles, and technical steps defined for each stage rather than high-level narrative

Key generation procedures mandate use of approved hardware security modules (HSMs) or certified software modules, with reference to FIPS 140-2/3 validation levels, and prohibit generation of keys in plaintext environments

Segregation of duties is enforced and documented — key generation, distribution, and destruction require dual control or split knowledge, with evidence from access control configurations or HSM audit logs
Key expiry and renewal schedules are defined per key type and system, and a system export or HSM report demonstrates active enforcement of those rotation schedules in production
Key destruction records exist as tamper-evident logs (e.g., HSM ceremony records, witnessed destruction certificates) confirming keys are cryptographically destroyed rather than merely deleted
commonGaps: Procedures exist for generation and storage but do not address recovery and revocation — leaving the institution without documented controls for two high-risk lifecycle stages

Key rotation is defined in policy but HSM or KMS audit logs show keys in production systems have not been rotated within the mandated period, demonstrating a gap between documented procedure and actual practice
Procedures do not specify minimum entropy requirements or approved random number generation methods for key generation, creating risk of weak keys even when the HSM itself is certified
There is no formal key ceremony record or dual-control log for master key generation — reliance is placed on informal verbal attestation rather than documented evidence
Key recovery procedures exist but have never been tested, and there is no documented recovery test outcome to demonstrate that the procedure is operationally viable
suggestedSources: HSM management console audit logs (e.g., Thales Luna, Entrust nShield, AWS CloudHSM)
Key Management Service (KMS) reports from cloud providers or on-premises KMS platforms
Certificate Authority (CA) system logs and PKI management console
Key ceremony documentation maintained by the Information Security or Infrastructure team
ITSM change and access management records for dual-control enforcement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (procedures); system-generated log exports in CSV/PDF for evidence of execution

evidenceItems.name: Annual Cryptographic Standards Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of the at-least-annual review of all cryptographic standards and algorithms in use across critical systems, external-linked systems, and transactional customer-facing applications, as required by clause 10.20(c), including the review methodology, findings, risk conclusions, and any remediation actions taken as a result.
whatGoodLooksLike: Review records are dated and demonstrate coverage of all three system categories specified in the clause — critical systems, external-linked systems, and customer-facing transactional applications — with a named reviewer and sign-off by a senior accountable owner

Review methodology cross-references current industry guidance on deprecated or weakened algorithms (e.g., NIST algorithm deprecation notices, ENISA reports) and explicitly assesses each algorithm in use against that guidance

Where weakened algorithms or protocols are identified (e.g., TLS 1.0/1.1, MD5, SHA-1 in signing chains), the review record contains a documented risk acceptance or a time-bound remediation plan with a named owner and target date
Review findings are tracked in the institution's risk register or issue management system, with evidence that prior-year remediation commitments were closed or formally re-assessed
Review is conducted at minimum annually, with evidence of an ad hoc trigger review process when material new vulnerabilities (e.g., POODLE, DROWN, ROBOT) are published mid-cycle
commonGaps: Review is conducted as a paper exercise covering only a subset of systems — typically data-at-rest encryption — while TLS configurations on customer-facing portals and API gateways are not assessed
Review findings identify deprecated algorithms but there is no remediation plan or risk acceptance document — the finding is noted and closed in the same review cycle without action
Reviews are not dated or signed off by a responsible owner, making it impossible to demonstrate the annual cadence required by the clause

The institution relies on vulnerability scanner output (e.g., Qualys, Nessus SSL reports) as the sole evidence of review, without a structured assessment against algorithm standards or a documented risk conclusion
Review cadence has slipped beyond 12 months — there is a gap of 14-18 months between the two most recent completed reviews, which directly fails the 'at least annually' requirement
suggestedSources: GRC platform risk register and issue tracker for remediation items arising from reviews
SSL/TLS scanning tool reports (Qualys SSL Labs, Nessus, Tenable) with timestamps
Information Security team review workpapers or assessment reports
Change management records showing remediation of deprecated algorithm findings
Architecture review board or security committee minutes where review findings were presented
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX review report; supporting spreadsheet or system exports as annexures

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Asset Inventory
evidenceItems.description: An expanded IT asset inventory that includes all cryptographic tools, algorithms, and keys in use across the institution, as required by clause 10.20(d), with the rationale for each cryptographic method documented and each entry mapped to the application systems it supports.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory records each cryptographic asset with fields for: algorithm or tool name, version, key length, purpose (encryption/signing/MAC/hashing/DRBG), the specific system(s) it supports, the documented business or technical rationale for its selection, and the asset owner

Inventory is actively maintained — a defined update trigger exists (e.g., change management gate requires inventory update for any system deployment or configuration change involving cryptographic components) and the last-updated date is current
Inventory is reconciled at least annually against production system configuration scans or PKI/KMS exports to identify shadow cryptographic usage not recorded in the inventory

Deprecated or legacy cryptographic methods in the inventory are flagged with an explicit risk status and linked to a remediation plan or formal risk acceptance, demonstrating the inventory is used as a living risk management tool

Inventory entries for customer-facing and critical systems include additional fields confirming the algorithm was selected following the annual review required by clause 10.20(c), creating a direct traceability link between the two controls
commonGaps: Inventory covers server-side encryption and PKI certificates but omits cryptographic algorithms embedded in application code (e.g., hardcoded cipher suites in mobile banking apps, API authentication token signing algorithms), leaving significant gaps in coverage
Inventory records the algorithm name but does not document the rationale for its selection — this is a direct gap against the explicit clause 10.20(d) requirement and will be highlighted by examiners

Inventory is a point-in-time spreadsheet with no defined update process — it has not been updated to reflect systems deployed or decommissioned in the past 12 months and cannot be reconciled against production state

Mapping between cryptographic assets and supporting application systems is one-directional or incomplete — some systems cannot be traced back to an inventory entry, and some inventory entries cannot be traced to a live system

Cryptographic tools used by third-party service providers or cloud-managed services are excluded from the inventory on the basis that they are vendor-managed, despite these tools protecting institution data
suggestedSources: Configuration Management Database (CMDB) in ServiceNow or equivalent ITSM platform — expanded with cryptographic attribute fields
