Certificate management platform (e.g., Venafi, DigiCert CertCentral, AWS Certificate Manager) for PKI certificate exports
KMS and HSM management consoles for key type and algorithm exports
Application security scan outputs (SAST/DAST) identifying cryptographic library usage in codebases
Cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools for cloud-deployed cryptographic configurations
SharePoint or Confluence maintained by IT Architecture or Information Security team
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export; supplemented by system-generated reports as evidence of reconciliation

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Key Compromise Recovery Plan and Test Records
evidenceItems.description: The documented compromise-recovery plan required by clause 10.20(e), covering the escalation process, key regeneration procedures, interim measures, changes to business-as-usual protocols, and containment strategies — together with records of at least one completed test of the plan, including test outcomes and remediation of identified gaps.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan contains distinct, actionable sections addressing all five elements mandated by clause 10.20(e): escalation process with named roles and contact trees, key regeneration procedures by key type, interim operational measures (e.g., suspension of affected services, fallback to out-of-band processes), BAU protocol changes, and containment/isolation strategies

Plan classifies compromise scenarios by severity (e.g., suspected vs. confirmed compromise, single key vs. master key vs. CA root key) with differentiated response procedures reflecting the varying operational impact of each scenario

Test records are dated and demonstrate end-to-end execution of the plan — not just a tabletop discussion — with participation documented by role, test scenario clearly defined, and outcomes recorded including services restored, time-to-recovery measured against RTO targets
Post-test gap analysis identifies weaknesses found during the test, with remediation actions assigned to named owners with target dates, and evidence that those actions were subsequently closed

Plan is integrated with the institution's broader incident response and business continuity frameworks — cross-references to the IS incident response plan and BCP are explicit, and escalation paths align with those documented in companion plans
commonGaps: Plan addresses key regeneration but does not cover interim measures or BAU protocol changes — leaving operational teams without guidance on how to maintain service continuity during the window between compromise identification and key rotation completion

Plan has never been tested — it exists as a document but there are no test records, tabletop exercise reports, or simulation outcomes, which examiners treat as equivalent to not having a plan for operational purposes

Test evidence consists only of a tabletop discussion summary with no technical execution, meaning critical operational steps (e.g., HSM re-keying, certificate reissuance, application reconfiguration) have never been validated under simulated compromise conditions

Escalation process identifies roles generically (e.g., 'CISO', 'IT Manager') without named individuals or deputies, and contact details are not documented — in a real compromise scenario, time-critical escalation would be impeded

Plan treats all cryptographic key compromises identically regardless of key type or system criticality — there is no tiered response reflecting that compromise of a root CA key has materially different containment requirements than compromise of a single application encryption key
suggestedSources: Information Security or IT Risk team — Business Continuity and Incident Response plan library
GRC platform — BCP/DR plan register and test exercise tracker
HSM and KMS vendor documentation supporting key regeneration procedures
Crisis management or incident response exercise records maintained by the IS Operations team
Change management records showing post-test remediation actions were implemented
SharePoint or Confluence site for Information Security policies and runbooks
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (plan document); PDF exercise report and gap register (XLSX) for test records

auditTips: Before the examination, reconcile the cryptographic asset inventory against live production configuration scans (e.g., SSL/TLS scan outputs, KMS exports, CMDB queries) and produce a written reconciliation report — examiners will test whether the inventory reflects actual production state, and an unreconciled inventory is a material finding.

Prepare a clause-mapping matrix that maps each of the five sub-requirements of 10.20 to specific policy sections, procedures, and evidence items — this allows examiners to quickly trace policy commitments through to operational evidence and demonstrates structured compliance rather than ad hoc document production.

Ensure the annual cryptographic standards review is cross-referenced to the cryptographic asset inventory: each algorithm in the inventory should have a corresponding line in the most recent review, with an explicit risk conclusion. Gaps in this traceability will be identified when examiners cross-reference the two documents.

For the key compromise recovery plan, assemble the test exercise report alongside the plan itself and highlight the gap register with closed remediation items — examiners specifically look for evidence that testing produced actionable findings and that those findings were resolved, not just recorded.

Check that algorithm rationale is documented for every entry in the cryptographic asset inventory, including third-party and cloud-managed cryptographic services — the explicit requirement for rationale documentation in clause 10.20(d) is frequently overlooked, and examiners will sample inventory entries and ask to see the rationale field populated.

Verify that key lifecycle procedures address all eight stages enumerated in clause 10.20(b) — generation, distribution, renewal, usage, storage, recovery, revocation, and destruction — and that you can produce system-generated evidence (HSM logs, KMS reports, key ceremony records) for each stage, not just policy statements.

Identify any systems using deprecated algorithms (3DES, RC4, MD5 for integrity, SHA-1 in signing chains, TLS 1.0/1.1, RSA keys below 2048 bits) before the examination and ensure each has either a time-bound remediation plan with a named owner or a formally approved risk acceptance — undocumented deprecated algorithm usage with no mitigation is the single most common critical finding under this clause.

10.21.title: Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence
10.21.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution has performed documented, structured due diligence on the cryptographic capabilities of every technology solution that processes or stores customer or sensitive information — not merely accepted vendor assurances at face value. A primary red flag is the absence of a current encryption key ownership register that clearly assigns custodianship and distinguishes critical from non-critical systems; examiners will probe whether the institution can demonstrate actual control of its keys rather than delegated reliance on a cloud or SaaS vendor. Where third-party key management is used, examiners will assess whether the institution has independently validated that industry standards (e.g., FIPS 140-2, ISO/IEC 11770) are met and whether that reliance has been formally assessed against the institution's stated risk appetite. Examiners also look for evidence that performance and capacity planning accounts for cryptographic overhead, and that the security operations team has addressed the visibility gap introduced by encrypted traffic — typically through TLS inspection or equivalent compensating controls.

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A structured assessment record documenting the evaluation of cryptographic controls for each material technology system, covering the five information security objectives: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation. This serves as the primary artefact demonstrating that the institution has actively evaluated — rather than passively assumed — the adequacy of cryptographic protections.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment covers all in-scope systems with a documented scope rationale, explicitly mapping each system to the five objectives (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, non-repudiation) with pass/fail findings

Each system entry references the specific cryptographic algorithm, key length, mode of operation, and protocol version evaluated (e.g., AES-256-GCM, TLS 1.3, RSA-2048) against a named industry standard
Assessment is dated, version-controlled, signed off by CISO or equivalent, and refreshed at least annually or upon material technology change
Findings include a risk rating with clear remediation actions, owners, and target dates where gaps are identified
Assessment methodology is documented and references recognised frameworks such as NIST SP 800-175B or ISO/IEC 18033
commonGaps: Assessment exists only as a generic checklist with no system-specific cryptographic algorithm or key length details, making it unverifiable
Non-critical systems are excluded from scope without a documented classification rationale or customer data determination
Assessment was completed at initial procurement and has not been refreshed to reflect protocol deprecations (e.g., TLS 1.0/1.1, SHA-1) or vendor updates
The five information security objectives are not explicitly addressed; the assessment covers only confidentiality and omits authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation
Findings have no assigned remediation owner or timeline, leaving identified weaknesses unactioned
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, RSA Archer) — assessment register module
Information Security department SharePoint or Confluence repository
Technology Risk team — vendor onboarding and periodic review records
Internal audit workpapers from most recent IT general controls review
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal assessment report with appendices per system)

evidenceItems.name: Encryption Key Ownership and Control Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register documenting ownership, custodianship, and control arrangements for all encryption keys across the institution's technology estate. This artefact directly evidences compliance with clause 10.21(a), demonstrating that the institution retains ownership except for formally classified non-critical systems without customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register maps every key to: system name, criticality classification, whether customer data is present, key owner (institution or third party), custodian name/entity, key management system used, and last review date

Non-critical systems without customer information are explicitly identified with supporting classification evidence linked to the institution's data classification policy, justifying the exception under clause 10.21(a)
For keys held by an independent key custodian, the custodian arrangement is governed by a formal agreement specifying access controls, audit rights, and SLAs
Register is maintained in a dedicated key management system (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS KMS with customer-managed keys, Thales CipherTrust) with automated expiry alerts
Quarterly reconciliation evidence shows the register is kept current and discrepancies are investigated and resolved
commonGaps: Register is a static spreadsheet maintained manually with no validation against live key management systems, resulting in undocumented orphan keys
System criticality classification is absent or inconsistently applied, making it impossible to determine which clause 10.21(a) exceptions are legitimately invoked
Keys for cloud-hosted SaaS platforms are recorded as 'vendor-managed' without any contractual evidence that the institution retains ownership or has access rights
Independent key custodian arrangements lack formal written agreements, meaning the institution has no enforceable access or audit rights
Register has not been updated following cloud migrations or system decommissions, leaving stale entries that inflate the apparent key estate
suggestedSources: Key Management System (KMS) — e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, Thales CipherTrust — exported inventory
Cloud services team — cloud key ownership configuration reports
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system — system criticality and data classification attributes
Legal/Contracts repository — independent key custodian agreements
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (master register) supported by system-generated exports and signed custodian agreements (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Key Generation Controls Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing the measures taken where the institution does not generate its own encryption keys, as required by clause 10.21(b). This includes the evaluation of the third party's key generation processes, adherence to industry standards, and the institution's own compensating controls to ensure robust key management.
whatGoodLooksLike: Institution maintains an inventory of all systems where key generation is delegated to a third party, with explicit justification for why self-generation is not feasible

For each such system, a controls assessment references the specific industry standard the third party claims conformance with (e.g., FIPS 140-2 Level 2+, ISO/IEC 11770-1, PCI DSS Key Management requirements) and provides independent evidence of that conformance (e.g., FIPS certificate number, third-party audit report)

Key lifecycle procedures covering generation, distribution, storage, rotation, and destruction are documented and tested for third-party key arrangements, with evidence the institution has contractual rights to enforce these
Key rotation schedules are defined, monitored, and adhered to, with records of the last rotation for each affected system
Compensating controls (e.g., dual-control procedures, split knowledge, M-of-N key access) are documented and tested where the institution cannot directly control generation
commonGaps: Institution accepts vendor SOC 2 reports as sufficient evidence without verifying that key management controls are explicitly in scope of the report
No documented controls or procedures exist for third-party key arrangements beyond a general contractual clause requiring 'industry best practices'
Key rotation is undefined or vendor-controlled without the institution setting or monitoring rotation schedules, violating the spirit of clause 10.21(b)
FIPS or equivalent certifications referenced are outdated or for a different product version than currently deployed
Institution has no contractual right to audit or test the third party's key management processes, leaving the assessment entirely reliant on vendor self-attestation
suggestedSources: Vendor management repository — third-party assurance reports (SOC 2 Type II, FIPS certificates, penetration test summaries)
Procurement/Legal — vendor contracts and data processing agreements with key management provisions
Cloud provider compliance portals (e.g., AWS Artifact, Azure Trust Center, Google Compliance Reports Manager)
Information Security team — key lifecycle procedure documents
evidenceItems.format: PDF (assessment report with annexed evidence) and DOCX (key lifecycle procedures)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Assessment Reliance Risk Evaluation
evidenceItems.description: A formal risk evaluation documenting whether the institution's reliance on third-party cryptographic assessments is consistent with its stated risk appetite and tolerance, as required by clause 10.21(c). This demonstrates that reliance on external assurance is a deliberate, governed decision rather than a default acceptance of vendor representations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk evaluation explicitly references the institution's current risk appetite statement and maps the level of third-party reliance to the defined risk tolerance thresholds for technology and information security risk
The evaluation assesses the quality, scope, and currency of each third-party assessment relied upon, including the assessor's independence, qualifications, and the standards applied

Where third-party reliance exceeds risk appetite thresholds, additional validation activities are documented (e.g., independent penetration testing, on-site inspection, contractual audit rights exercise)
The evaluation is reviewed and approved by the Chief Risk Officer or Risk Committee with documented sign-off, not solely by the technology function
Reliance decisions are revisited annually or upon material change in the third-party relationship, technology, or threat landscape
commonGaps: Institution relies on third-party assessments without any documented review of the assessor's independence or the scope of the assessment, accepting reports at face value
Risk appetite statement does not address third-party reliance for cryptographic controls specifically, creating a gap between policy and practice
Reliance evaluation is performed solely by the technology team without risk management or senior governance oversight, lacking the independence required for credible assessment
Evaluations are not updated when third-party assessment reports expire (typically annually), meaning the institution continues to rely on stale assurance
No escalation process exists for when third-party assessment findings reveal cryptographic weaknesses, leaving the institution without a defined response path
suggestedSources: Risk Management department — risk appetite statement and technology risk register
Third-party/vendor risk management platform (e.g., ProcessUnity, Prevalent, Venminder)
Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee meeting minutes and approved papers
Information Security team — third-party assurance register
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal risk evaluation paper with risk committee approval evidence)

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Performance and Capacity Impact Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has given due regard to system resource requirements for supporting cryptographic controls, as required by clause 10.21(d). This includes capacity planning evidence, performance benchmarking results, and records showing that cryptographic overhead has been factored into infrastructure provisioning decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Capacity planning documentation for critical systems explicitly quantifies the CPU, memory, and network overhead attributable to cryptographic operations (e.g., TLS termination, database encryption, HSM throughput) and shows headroom above defined thresholds

Performance benchmarking results are retained for key cryptographic operations, including before/after comparisons when cryptographic configurations were strengthened (e.g., key length increases, algorithm upgrades)
HSM (Hardware Security Module) sizing and throughput planning is documented for systems using HSMs, with evidence of load testing at peak transaction volumes
Monitoring dashboards or reports show ongoing tracking of cryptographic-related resource consumption with defined alert thresholds
Annual capacity review incorporates projected transaction growth and planned cryptographic changes (e.g., post-quantum migration planning)
commonGaps: Capacity planning documents make no reference to cryptographic overhead, treating encryption as a zero-cost operation in resource modelling
HSM capacity is sized for average load without stress-testing at peak transaction volumes, creating undetected bottlenecks during high-volume periods
No performance benchmarking was conducted when upgrading from weaker to stronger algorithms (e.g., RSA-1024 to RSA-2048, AES-128 to AES-256), leaving resource impact undocumented
System monitoring does not include cryptographic-specific metrics, meaning degradation from cryptographic overhead is only detected reactively during incidents
Post-quantum cryptography migration planning is absent, with no assessment of the significantly higher computational requirements of quantum-resistant algorithms
suggestedSources: Infrastructure/Platform engineering team — capacity planning and sizing documents
IT operations monitoring platform (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, SolarWinds, Prometheus/Grafana) — performance dashboards and historical reports
HSM vendor management console — throughput and utilisation reports
Change management records (ITSM platform, e.g., ServiceNow) — cryptographic upgrade change requests with impact assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (capacity planning reports) and system-exported performance reports (CSV/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Encrypted Traffic Visibility Risk Assessment and Controls
evidenceItems.description: Documentation addressing the risk of reduced network traffic visibility resulting from encryption, as specifically required by clause 10.21(d). This includes the institution's assessment of the inspection gap created by encrypted communications and the compensating or detective controls implemented to maintain security monitoring capability.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal risk assessment identifies all network segments and flows where encryption reduces or eliminates SOC/SIEM visibility, with a risk rating for each and documented risk acceptance or treatment decisions

Where TLS/SSL inspection is deployed, documentation covers the legal, privacy, and technical governance of the inspection solution including certificate management, scope of inspection, and employee/customer notification where applicable

Compensating controls for traffic not subject to inspection (e.g., endpoint detection and response, DNS-layer security, application-level logging, network flow analysis) are documented with evidence of their effectiveness
The encrypted traffic visibility risk assessment is reviewed by Information Security and approved by CISO, with linkage to the institution's overall threat detection capability framework

Annual review or post-incident review records show the assessment is kept current in response to changes in encrypted protocols, new application deployments, or emerging threats exploiting encrypted channels
commonGaps: Institution has deployed pervasive encryption without any documented assessment of the resulting SOC visibility gap, leaving detection capability blindspots unrecognised
TLS inspection is implemented without a formal governance document covering its legal basis, scope limitations, and privacy implications, creating regulatory risk beyond RMiT
Risk acceptance decisions for uninspected encrypted flows are absent or undocumented, meaning the institution has implicitly accepted unknown risk
SIEM/SOC team is unaware of which network flows are encrypted end-to-end and therefore cannot calibrate detection rules or acknowledge the absence of expected telemetry
No compensating controls exist for encrypted lateral movement within the internal network, leaving a significant threat detection gap for insider threats and advanced persistent threats
suggestedSources: SOC/SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) — traffic source inventory and coverage gap analysis
Network operations team — network architecture diagrams annotated with encryption boundaries
Information Security team — TLS inspection policy and encrypted traffic risk assessment
Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform — coverage reports as compensating control evidence
Firewall and proxy management console — SSL/TLS inspection configuration and scope documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF (risk assessment report with network architecture diagrams) and system-exported coverage reports

auditTips: Map each obligation in clause 10.21 sub-clauses (a) through (d) to a specific evidence artefact before the examination — present BNM examiners with a compliance matrix that cross-references each sub-clause to named documents, dates, and approving officers, demonstrating deliberate coverage rather than ad hoc responses.

Physically demonstrate key ownership by walking examiners through the Key Management System console during the examination — the ability to show live key metadata (creation date, owner, rotation schedule, access policy) is far more convincing than a static register and immediately resolves the most common examiner scepticism about cloud-hosted key control.

Pre-validate all FIPS certifications and third-party assurance reports relied upon for clause 10.21(b) and (c): check that certificates cover the exact product version deployed, confirm reports are dated within 12 months, and verify that key management is explicitly in scope — examiners routinely find that institutions cite reports where cryptographic controls are carved out of scope.

Prepare a classification decision log for every system claimed as a 'non-critical system without customer information' under the clause 10.21(a) exception — each entry should show who made the classification, on what date, based on what criteria, and when it was last validated; examiners will sample-test these exceptions and challenge unsupported classifications.

Commission an independent internal audit or penetration test specifically scoped to encrypted traffic visibility gaps prior to examination — having a recent independent assessment that quantifies the detection coverage gap and validates compensating controls allows the institution to front-foot examiner questions on clause 10.21(d) rather than respond reactively.

Ensure the risk appetite statement explicitly addresses cryptographic third-party reliance so the clause 10.21(c) risk evaluation has a clear anchor — if the current risk appetite statement is silent on this, prepare a board or risk committee paper that establishes the position and obtain approval before the examination; examiners will ask to see the governance linkage.

Conduct a dry run of the evidence package against clause 10.21 using a BNM examination simulation at least six weeks before the scheduled examination — focus on whether the due diligence assessment covers all five information security objectives verbatim (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, non-repudiation) since omission of any one of these is a common finding that is avoidable with a pre-examination checklist.

10.22.title: Cryptographic Protocol Implementation
10.22.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has implemented cryptographic controls grounded in internationally recognised standards — not merely selected them on paper — with particular scrutiny on whether secret and private key material is protected by hardware-backed environments such as HSMs or TEEs. Examiners look for evidence that protocol selection decisions are documented, risk-justified, and traceable to standards such as NIST SP 800-57, ISO/IEC 18033, or equivalent, and that those protocols have been independently tested. A key red flag is the absence of HSM or TEE coverage for high-risk key operations, or the use of software-only key stores for production cryptographic keys. Examiners also probe whether the institution can demonstrate that its cryptographic controls are subject to periodic review and that deprecated or weak protocols (e.g., DES, 3DES, RC4, RSA-1024, MD5, SHA-1) have been formally retired. Institutions that conflate policy documentation with operational implementation — producing strong paper trails but lacking observable technical controls — are routinely flagged during technical deep-dives.

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Protocol Standards and Selection Register
evidenceItems.description: A formal register or policy document that lists all approved cryptographic protocols in use across the institution, maps each to a recognised international standard, and records the risk-based rationale for selection. This demonstrates that protocol choices are deliberate, standards-aligned, and periodically reviewed rather than ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each approved protocol (e.g., TLS 1.2/1.3, AES-256-GCM, RSA-4096, ECDSA P-384) is explicitly mapped to its governing standard (NIST, ISO/IEC, IETF RFC) with version and publication date
The register includes a classification of key type (secret/symmetric vs. public/asymmetric) alongside each protocol entry, directly mirroring the clause's distinction
Deprecated or prohibited protocols are explicitly listed with their retirement dates and the compensating controls applied during transition
The document carries evidence of formal approval (CISO or equivalent) and has a documented review cycle of at least annually
A risk-tiering column or field links each protocol to the sensitivity level of assets it protects, demonstrating commensurate selection
commonGaps: Register exists but has not been updated since initial drafting, leaving recently deprecated algorithms (e.g., SHA-1, 3DES) still listed as approved
No explicit mapping to international standards — protocols are named but not linked to specific NIST, ISO, or IETF publications, making standard compliance unverifiable
Secret key and public key protocols are not differentiated, preventing examiners from assessing adequacy of protections specific to each type
Risk-tiering or classification rationale is absent, making it impossible to verify that protocol strength is commensurate with asset sensitivity
Document has been approved by IT management but lacks CISO or Board-level sign-off, falling short of the senior governance expectation implied by the 'S' marker
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow GRC, or OneTrust) — policy and standards library module
Information Security policy repository on SharePoint or Confluence
Enterprise Architecture team — technology standards register
CISO office — cryptographic standards working group minutes and approved standards catalogue
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy document with version history, or structured spreadsheet exported from GRC platform

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Protocol Testing and Validation Records
evidenceItems.description: Test evidence demonstrating that cryptographic protocols have been validated against their specified standards through recognised testing methodologies. This includes vulnerability assessments, penetration testing scoping records, and protocol configuration scans that confirm implementations are both correctly configured and free from known weaknesses.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated TLS/SSL configuration scan reports (e.g., Qualys SSL Labs, testssl.sh) for all externally and internally facing endpoints, showing only approved cipher suites and protocol versions are enabled
Penetration test reports explicitly scope cryptographic control testing and reference specific findings against OWASP Cryptographic Failures (A02) or equivalent framework, with remediation evidence
Results are dated and recurring — at minimum annually — with a clear remediation log showing all cryptographic findings were closed within defined SLA periods
Testing scope includes both network-layer protocol validation (TLS, SSH, IPSec) and application-layer cryptographic implementation review (key derivation, signing, encryption modes)
Vendor or third-party FIPS 140-2/140-3 validation certificates for HSM or cryptographic libraries are referenced and on file, providing external assurance of implementation correctness
commonGaps: SSL/TLS scans are performed only for external-facing systems, leaving internal service-to-service encryption and database encryption untested and undocumented
Penetration test reports mention cryptography in passing but do not contain dedicated findings sections or test cases mapped to specific protocols, making depth of testing unverifiable
No evidence of retesting after remediation of cryptographic findings — reports show issues identified but closure verification is absent
FIPS or equivalent validation certificates for HSM appliances are not on file or reference outdated firmware versions no longer covered by the validation
Testing cadence is ad hoc rather than scheduled, with gaps of two or more years between assessments, inconsistent with the 'tested accordingly' obligation
suggestedSources: IT Security or Red Team — penetration testing archives and remediation tracking in ITSM (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira)
Vulnerability Management platform (e.g., Qualys, Tenable Nessus) — cryptographic scan reports and historical trend data
Vendor portals — FIPS 140-2/140-3 validation certificates for HSM and cryptographic library vendors
PKI team or Certificate Management platform (e.g., Venafi, Keyfactor) — cipher suite enforcement policy and scan history
evidenceItems.format: PDF scan reports, ITSM-exported remediation logs, and vendor-issued validation certificates

evidenceItems.name: Key Management Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented policies and operational procedures governing the full lifecycle of cryptographic keys — generation, distribution, storage, use, rotation, revocation, and destruction — for both secret (symmetric) and private (asymmetric) key types. This directly evidences the institution's governance of the 'high degree of protection' obligation for key material.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly addresses both secret cryptographic keys and private cryptographic keys as distinct categories, with separate handling requirements and protection levels for each

Key lifecycle stages (generation, activation, distribution, storage, rotation, suspension, destruction) are all defined with responsible roles, procedural steps, and minimum technical controls at each stage

Storage and computation protections for each key classification are explicitly mapped to a risk tier that determines whether HSM, TEE, or software controls are required — with HSM mandated for high-risk keys
Key rotation frequencies are defined per key type and algorithm, with documented rationale aligned to NIST SP 800-57 or equivalent guidance
The policy is supported by documented procedures that can be demonstrated to be operationally in use (e.g., referenced in change records, key ceremony minutes, or HSM audit logs)
commonGaps: Policy treats all cryptographic keys generically without distinguishing between secret/symmetric and private/asymmetric types, missing the clause's explicit distinction
Key destruction procedures are documented for certificates but absent for symmetric keys, raw key material, and key-encrypting keys held in key management systems
Rotation frequencies are defined in policy but no operational evidence (rotation logs, ticket records) exists to demonstrate the policy is being followed
Policy is written at too high a level of abstraction to be operationally actionable — no step-by-step procedures exist, and staff cannot demonstrate how keys are actually managed day-to-day
The policy does not specify which key types or risk tiers require HSM protection, leaving the determination entirely to individual administrators and creating inconsistent implementation
suggestedSources: CISO or Information Security team — information security policy library
PKI / Cryptography team — key management standard operating procedures
GRC platform — policy management module with version history and approval workflows
HSM vendor management portal — key ceremony procedures and operator card holder documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy and procedure documents with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: HSM / TEE Inventory and Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: An asset inventory and configuration evidence set demonstrating that hardware security modules, trusted execution environments, or equivalently secured devices are deployed to protect secret and private key storage and cryptographic computation, commensurate with the assessed level of risk. This directly evidences the clause's hardware-backed protection requirement.
whatGoodLooksLike: A current asset inventory lists all HSM appliances and TEE-enabled systems with model, firmware version, FIPS 140-2/140-3 validation level, deployment location, and the key types or use cases each device protects

A documented risk assessment formally maps each cryptographic use case (e.g., payment HSM, TLS offload, code signing, database encryption) to its required protection tier, with HSM or TEE mandated for high and critical classifications
HSM configuration baselines are documented and include partition/role separation, dual-control and split-knowledge controls for key ceremonies, and audit logging enabled
HSM or TEE audit logs are regularly reviewed and alerts are generated for anomalous key access events — with evidence of log review activity (tickets, review sign-offs)
For TEE implementations, attestation reports or equivalent remote attestation evidence is available to demonstrate the integrity of the trusted execution environment
commonGaps: HSMs are deployed for payment systems but software key stores (e.g., plaintext key files, PKCS#12 files on file systems) are used for TLS private keys and code-signing keys without a documented risk justification for the lower protection level
HSM inventory is out of date — devices have been decommissioned or firmware updated but the register has not been refreshed, creating a gap between documented and actual state

Dual-control and split-knowledge requirements for key ceremonies are documented in policy but no key ceremony minutes, quorum card holder records, or witness logs exist to evidence operational compliance
HSM audit logs are collected but there is no evidence of regular review or alerting — logs exist but anomaly detection and review processes are absent

The institution relies solely on cloud provider KMS services for key protection but has not assessed whether these meet the HSM or 'similarly secured device' threshold required by the clause, and no equivalence documentation exists
suggestedSources: IT Infrastructure or Security Engineering team — HSM and cryptographic hardware asset register
ITSM (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) — hardware asset records for HSM appliances
HSM vendor management console (e.g., Thales CipherTrust Manager, Utimaco SecurityServer, nCipher nShield) — configuration exports and audit logs
Cloud provider security console (AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM, GCP Cloud HSM) — if applicable, with equivalence assessment documentation
Information Security Risk team — risk register entries mapping cryptographic use cases to required protection tiers
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet inventory, system-exported configuration baselines (PDF/XML), and audit log extracts

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Risk Assessment and Commensurate Control Mapping
evidenceItems.description: A formal risk assessment specifically addressing cryptographic controls, documenting how the institution has determined the appropriate level of cryptographic protection for different asset types and use cases, and demonstrating that the 'commensurate with the level of risk' determination is systematic rather than ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each significant cryptographic use case (data at rest, data in transit, authentication, code signing, payment processing) is assessed for likelihood and impact of key compromise, with risk ratings recorded and justified

The risk assessment explicitly links each use case's risk rating to a required minimum protection tier — specifying whether HSM, TEE, software-based controls, or a combination is required — and documents the decision rationale
The assessment identifies and formally accepts or mitigates residual risks where ideal protections are not yet implemented, with named risk owners and remediation timelines
The risk assessment has been reviewed and approved by both the CISO and a Risk Committee or equivalent governance body, evidencing senior oversight

The assessment references threat scenarios specific to the institution's operating environment (e.g., insider threat to key material, supply chain risk for HSM firmware, quantum computing transition planning)
commonGaps: Cryptographic risk is assessed generically as part of broader IT risk without a dedicated analysis of key material exposure scenarios, making it impossible to demonstrate 'commensurate' risk-based decision-making

Risk assessment exists but was completed at implementation and has not been reviewed since, meaning changes in the threat landscape (e.g., post-quantum concerns, newly deprecated algorithms) are not reflected
The assessment covers confidentiality risk but omits integrity and availability risks associated with key loss, HSM failure, or key ceremony disruption

Risk acceptance for lower-tier protection (e.g., software key stores for medium-risk use cases) is undocumented — controls simply exist without a formal risk-based justification that examiners can verify
Post-quantum cryptography transition risk is entirely absent from the assessment despite BNM and international regulators raising awareness of migration obligations
suggestedSources: Information Security Risk team — risk register and technology risk assessment repository in GRC platform
Enterprise Risk Management — technology risk register with cryptographic risk line items
CISO office — cryptography governance working group papers and risk acceptance records
Audit and Compliance team — previous internal audit findings on cryptographic controls that drove risk assessment updates
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX risk assessment report with risk register extract (spreadsheet or GRC platform export)

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Control Review and Continuous Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Periodic review records and continuous monitoring evidence demonstrating that the institution actively monitors the currency and effectiveness of its cryptographic implementations, including tracking of algorithm deprecation, certificate expiry, key rotation completion, and HSM health — evidencing that controls remain effective over time rather than at a single point in time.
whatGoodLooksLike: A certificate lifecycle management record or system report showing all TLS, code-signing, and client certificates with expiry dates, responsible owners, and renewal status — with no certificates expired or approaching expiry without a tracked renewal action
Key rotation completion records or automated key management system logs demonstrating that keys were rotated within policy-defined windows for each key type, with timestamps and operator records

A documented process for monitoring NIST, IETF, and other standards bodies for algorithm deprecation notices, with evidence that the institution has assessed and responded to recent deprecation announcements (e.g., SHA-1 retirement, RSA-2048 sunset timelines)
HSM health and availability monitoring alerts are configured and evidenced — including firmware currency tracking and scheduled HSM health checks
An annual cryptographic controls review report signed off by the CISO documents the overall state of cryptographic hygiene, outstanding gaps, and remediation plan for the review period
commonGaps: Certificate management is reactive rather than proactive — the institution relies on expiry alerts from browsers or monitoring tools rather than a managed renewal process, resulting in periodic expired certificate incidents
Key rotation logs exist for some systems but are absent for legacy platforms, creating blind spots where keys may not have been rotated for years without detection

No formal process exists to monitor NIST or international standards body communications for algorithm deprecation, meaning the institution is exposed to continued use of algorithms deprecated externally without internal awareness
HSM firmware versions are not tracked against vendor security bulletins, and there is no evidence of a process to assess and apply HSM firmware updates when security-relevant patches are released

The annual cryptographic review, if performed, produces a report that is circulated internally but never formally approved or tracked to remediation outcomes, limiting its value as governance evidence
suggestedSources: Certificate Management platform (e.g., Venafi Trust Protection Platform, Keyfactor Command, DigiCert CertCentral) — certificate inventory and renewal workflow records
Key Management System (e.g., Thales CipherTrust, HashiCorp Vault Enterprise) — key rotation audit logs and policy compliance reports
IT Security Operations — vulnerability management platform tracking HSM firmware advisories and cryptographic library CVEs
CISO office — annual cryptographic review reports and governance committee approval minutes
Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) — alerting rules and incident records related to cryptographic anomalies (key access outside hours, failed decryption events)
evidenceItems.format: System-exported reports (PDF/CSV), ITSM renewal tickets, and signed review reports (PDF)

auditTips: Before examination day, reconcile your HSM asset inventory against the CMDB and physically verify that all listed HSMs are operational and running firmware versions covered by their FIPS 140-2/140-3 validation certificates — examiners frequently request firmware version evidence and cross-check it against the NIST CMVP validated modules list.

Prepare a single-page cryptographic use case matrix that maps every significant system or data classification to its protocol, key type (secret or private), protection tier (HSM / TEE / software), and the international standard governing the protocol — this allows examiners to rapidly verify 'commensurate with risk' coverage without having to piece together evidence across multiple documents.

Ensure that your key ceremony records, quorum card holder registers, and split-knowledge documentation are current and that the named individuals are still employed and hold the roles described — examiners will cross-reference personnel records, and stale key ceremony documentation with departed staff is a recurring finding.

Run a full SSL/TLS scan across all internal and external endpoints using a reproducible toolset (e.g., testssl.sh or Qualys SSL Labs) within 30 days prior to examination and produce a signed-off clean scan report — this provides contemporaneous evidence of protocol implementation correctness and signals an active monitoring posture.

Map your cryptographic evidence set explicitly to the three distinct obligations in Clause 10.22: (1) effective implementation of suitable protocols for both key types, (2) selection based on recognised international standards with testing, and (3) protected environment with HSM/TEE for storage and computation commensurate with risk — examiners structure their questions around these three limbs and gaps in any one will be probed deeply.

Prepare a post-quantum cryptography transition position statement even if migration is not yet underway — examiners are increasingly asking about awareness and planning for NIST PQC standards (FIPS 203/204/205), and a documented gap assessment with a timeline demonstrates proactive risk management rather than ignorance.

Cross-reference your cryptographic risk assessment with findings from the most recent internal audit, external penetration test, and any BNM examination feedback — if all three sources identify the same gap (e.g., software key stores for medium-risk workloads), ensure either remediation evidence or a formal risk acceptance with a named owner and deadline is ready, as examiners treat unaddressed repeat findings as a material compliance failure.

10.23.title: Public Key Certificate Management
10.23.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a demonstrable, risk-tiered framework for managing the full lifecycle of public key certificates, with particular scrutiny on whether customer-facing certificates are issued by CAs that are formally recognised (i.e., trusted by major browser root stores or endorsed under applicable Malaysian legal frameworks such as the Digital Signature Act 1997). Examiners will probe whether authentication and digital signature implementations have technical and procedural controls that render private key usage legally binding and non-repudiable, which requires evidence of both technical enforcement (HSM storage, PIN/biometric activation) and legal/contractual acknowledgement by certificate holders. A key red flag is certificate sprawl — untracked certificates with expired or near-expiry states, which signals an absence of lifecycle governance. Examiners also verify that renewal and re-issuance procedures are tested and aligned with industry standards such as RFC 5280, CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements, and Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) or Controller of Certification Authorities (CCA) directives. Non-repudiation evidence — including audit logs capturing the specific certificate and key used per transaction — is critically evaluated to determine whether the institution could sustain a legal challenge over a disputed digital signature.

evidenceItems.name: PKI Governance Policy and Certificate Management Framework
evidenceItems.description: The overarching policy document that governs the institution's use of Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), including the criteria for selecting Certificate Authorities based on risk classification, the types of certificates permitted for different use cases (TLS, code signing, client authentication, digital signatures), and the institution's risk-tiered approach to CA selection distinguishing internal, commercial, and legally recognised CAs for customer-facing scenarios.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly distinguishes between CA requirements for internal systems versus customer-facing certificates, mapping each use case to a minimum CA trust level (e.g., commercially trusted CA for customer digital signatures)
Risk classification matrix is embedded in or annexed to the policy, defining 'appropriate to the level of risk' with concrete thresholds and examples specific to the institution's product lines
Policy references applicable Malaysian legal and regulatory instruments, including the Digital Signature Act 1997, CCA-licensed CA requirements, and BNM RMiT, with version-controlled cross-references
Document is reviewed and approved by CISO and a senior governance body (e.g., Technology Risk Committee or Board IT Committee) within the last 12 months

Policy includes explicit non-repudiation and legal bindingness requirements, mandating technical controls (e.g., HSM-backed private keys) and legal mechanisms (e.g., subscriber agreements, Terms of Use)
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not differentiate CA requirements by risk or use case — all certificates are treated uniformly regardless of whether they are customer-facing or internal-only
The concept of 'recognised Certificate Authority' is not defined, leaving ambiguity about which CAs are acceptable for customer certificate issuance and creating inconsistent implementation
Policy has not been updated following changes to CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements or following the issuance of new BNM or MCMC guidelines, making it non-current
Non-repudiation and legal bindingness obligations are absent from or vaguely stated in the policy, with no linkage to the Digital Signature Act 1997 or equivalent legal framework
Governance approvals are missing or outdated (over 24 months since last review), with no evidence of periodic review cycle being followed
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity Policy Repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or RSA Archer)
CISO Office document register
Technology Risk Committee or Board IT Committee meeting minutes confirming policy approval
Legal and Compliance department for cross-reference with Digital Signature Act 1997 obligations
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Certificate Inventory and Lifecycle Management Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive, continuously maintained inventory of all digital certificates deployed across the institution's environment, capturing certificate metadata, lifecycle status, ownership, and renewal schedules. This register demonstrates the institution's operational control over its certificate estate and its ability to detect and remediate expired, expiring, or non-compliant certificates before they pose security or service risks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory is automated or semi-automated via a certificate lifecycle management (CLM) tool (e.g., Venafi, DigiCert CertCentral, AppViewX) and captures all certificates including those on third-party or cloud infrastructure

Each entry records the issuing CA, certificate type, subject, SANs, validity period, responsible owner, associated system/application, and renewal status with automated alerting at 90, 60, and 30 days before expiry
Inventory is reconciled against active network discovery scans at least quarterly, demonstrating no untracked or shadow certificates exist in the environment
Customer-facing certificates are tagged distinctly, showing only recognised CA issuers, and are subject to more frequent reconciliation (e.g., monthly)
Expired certificate remediation actions are tracked with dates closed and root cause notes, evidencing a functioning operational process not just a static list
commonGaps: Inventory is maintained manually in spreadsheets with no automated discovery, resulting in certificates deployed on servers or cloud services that are unknown to the security team
Certificate ownership is not assigned at the individual or team level, creating accountability gaps when certificates expire undetected
Renewal alerting thresholds are set too close to expiry (e.g., 7 days) or notifications go to shared mailboxes that are not actively monitored, leading to outages from expired certificates
Cloud-native certificates (AWS ACM, Azure Key Vault, GCP Certificate Manager) and third-party SaaS platform certificates are excluded from the central inventory, creating blind spots
No reconciliation cadence exists — the inventory is a point-in-time snapshot rather than a living record, making it unreliable for audit purposes
suggestedSources: Certificate Lifecycle Management platform (Venafi Trust Protection Platform, DigiCert CertCentral, Sectigo Certificate Manager)
Cloud provider certificate dashboards (AWS ACM, Azure Key Vault certificates, GCP Certificate Authority Service)
ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for renewal and remediation ticket records
Vulnerability or asset management platforms (Qualys, Tenable.io) for certificate discovery scan results
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system-generated report (CSV/XLSX), supplemented by CLM platform exports

evidenceItems.name: Recognised CA Validation Records for Customer Certificates
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that all certificates issued to customers (including digital signature certificates used for e-signing transactions, client authentication certificates, and certificates embedded in mobile or internet banking tokens) have been sourced exclusively from Certificate Authorities that are formally recognised — either as CCA-licensed CAs under the Digital Signature Act 1997, or as CAs trusted in major browser root programs (Mozilla, Microsoft, Apple) appropriate to the risk context. This evidence substantiates the institution's compliance with the customer certificate recognition requirement.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal, approved list of recognised CAs is maintained and linked to the PKI policy, with each CA's recognition basis documented (e.g., CCA licence number, inclusion in Mozilla Root Store, contractual SLA with institution)

Procurement and onboarding records for each CA used for customer certificates demonstrate due diligence evaluation against the recognised CA criteria, including audit reports such as CA/Browser Forum WebTrust or ETSI EN 319 411 audits
Sample customer certificates can be traced to the approved CA list, with certificate chain validation confirming the issuing CA is the contracted recognised CA with no intermediate CA substitutions
Customer-facing products (e.g., digital signing for loan agreements, e-mandate, investment instructions) are mapped to specific recognised CA issuers with documented rationale for each selection
Annual or event-triggered review records confirm that each CA on the approved list remains in good standing (no distrust events, revocation of root, or WebTrust audit failures)
commonGaps: No formal approved CA list exists; CA selection has been ad hoc and project-driven, resulting in customer certificates from multiple CAs of varying trust levels without documented rationale
The institution relies on internal CAs (self-signed or enterprise CA) for customer-facing certificates, which do not meet the 'recognised CA' threshold for legally binding customer transactions
CA vetting records do not include review of WebTrust or ETSI audit reports, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate it assessed the CA's own operational integrity before relying on it
CA approval list is not updated when a CA faces distrust events (e.g., a CA being removed from browser root stores), leaving the institution potentially relying on an unrecognised CA
Customer certificate issuance is handled by a third-party vendor whose sub-CA relationship to the recognised root CA is not documented, creating an unverified chain of trust
suggestedSources: Vendor management or procurement records for CA contracts (Vendor Management System, SAP, or contract repository)
Controller of Certification Authorities (CCA) Malaysia licensed CA register (publicly available from MCMC/CCA website — institution should cross-reference its CA list against this)
Mozilla Root Store / Microsoft Trusted Root Program public lists for browser-trusted CAs
Product or solution design documentation for customer-facing digital signature features (e.g., e-KYC, e-signing, digital mandate)
Third-party CA audit reports (WebTrust, ETSI) obtained during vendor due diligence
evidenceItems.format: PDF (CA audit reports, contracts), DOCX (approved CA list and vetting records), system-generated certificate chain exports

evidenceItems.name: Authentication and Digital Signature Protocol Implementation Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical and procedural evidence demonstrating how authentication and digital signature protocols that rely on public key certificates are implemented with strong protection, specifically ensuring that private key usage is restricted to authorised certificate holders and is non-repudiable. This encompasses HSM configurations, private key protection controls, subscriber agreement frameworks, and the technical architecture of signing workflows to substantiate that the use of private keys is legally binding and irrefutable as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture documentation and configuration evidence shows that private keys for customer certificates are generated and stored in FIPS 140-2 Level 2 or higher HSMs or secure elements (e.g., smart cards, UICC, hardware tokens), with no key export capability enabled

Subscriber agreements or digital certificate terms and conditions signed by customers explicitly acknowledge sole control of their private key, the legal effect of their digital signature, and the binding nature of transactions executed using the certificate

Implementation evidence (e.g., code review records, security assessment reports) confirms that signing workflows require explicit user activation of the private key through a PIN, biometric, or equivalent factor, preventing silent or automated misuse

Audit log evidence demonstrates that each digital signature transaction captures the certificate serial number, signing timestamp, transaction hash, and user session context to support future non-repudiation claims

Penetration test or cryptographic review reports from a qualified assessor confirm that the signing protocol implementation is resistant to key extraction, replay attacks, and signature forgery attempts
commonGaps: Private keys for customer certificates are stored in software keystores (JKS, PKCS#12 files) rather than hardware security modules, failing to meet strong protection requirements and undermining non-repudiation claims

Subscriber agreements do not contain explicit acknowledgement of legal bindingness or reference to the Digital Signature Act 1997, weakening the institution's position in a legal dispute over a disputed signature

Signing workflows do not require user authentication at the point of signing (e.g., private key activation is automatic after session login), making it impossible to prove that the certificate holder personally authorised each specific transaction

Audit logs for digital signature events are incomplete — they record the user session but not the specific certificate or key used, making post-hoc non-repudiation evidence insufficient for legal proceedings

No formal cryptographic implementation review or assessment has been conducted, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate assurance that the protocol implementation is technically sound and resistant to known attack vectors
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture repository for system and integration diagrams (Confluence, SharePoint, ArchiMate models)
HSM vendor configuration and audit logs (Thales Luna, nCipher, AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM)
Legal department for subscriber agreement and certificate terms templates
SIEM or log management platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh) for digital signature transaction audit logs
Security assessment reports from internal red team, external penetration testers, or IS audit team
evidenceItems.format: PDF (architecture diagrams, assessment reports, subscriber agreements), System exports (HSM audit logs, SIEM query results)

evidenceItems.name: Certificate Issuance and Renewal Standard Operating Procedures with Compliance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures governing the initial issuance and subsequent renewal of certificates, demonstrating alignment with industry best practices (CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements, RFC 5280, NIST SP 800-57) and applicable legal and regulatory specifications (CCA regulations, BNM RMiT). Accompanied by operational evidence — such as completed checklists, renewal tickets, and post-issuance validation records — that demonstrates the procedures are actively followed rather than merely documented.
whatGoodLooksLike: SOPs explicitly reference and align with CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements and applicable Malaysian regulatory specifications, with named controls mapped to specific sections of each standard

Issuance checklists capture domain validation method, identity verification steps for OV/EV certificates, CSR review (key algorithm, key length, SAN correctness), and post-issuance validation, with completed samples available for sampled certificates

Renewal SOP includes automated alerting integration with the certificate inventory tool, a defined escalation path for certificates not renewed within the alert window, and emergency re-issuance procedures for compromised or revoked certificates

Evidence of procedure adherence exists for a sample of recently issued and renewed certificates — e.g., ITSM tickets showing request, approval, issuance, and validation stages with timestamps and responsible parties named

Annual review records confirm the SOPs are tested against current CA/Browser Forum requirements and any changes to BNM or CCA directives, with version history reflecting updates made in response to regulatory or industry changes
commonGaps: SOPs exist for TLS certificate renewal but not for customer-facing signature certificates, code signing certificates, or internal CA-issued certificates, leaving significant process gaps for non-TLS certificate types

Procedures reference outdated standards (e.g., SHA-1, 1024-bit RSA, 2-year validity for TLS — which violates current CA/Browser Forum requirements) without evidence of having been updated to reflect current requirements
Operational evidence is absent — procedures are documented but there are no completed checklists, ITSM tickets, or approval records to demonstrate the procedures are followed in practice

Emergency certificate re-issuance (in response to key compromise or CA distrust events) is not addressed in the SOP, leaving the institution without a documented response capability for urgent scenarios
No formal procedure exists for decommissioning or revoking certificates when systems are retired or staff leave, resulting in orphaned certificates remaining valid after their use case has ended
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for certificate request, approval, and renewal ticket records
GRC platform for SOP version control and review records (RSA Archer, MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC)
PKI operations team (typically within Cybersecurity or IT Infrastructure) for procedure documentation and completed checklists
Change management records for certificate-related changes as evidence of governance over issuance activities
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (SOPs and checklists), ITSM system exports (ticket records demonstrating procedural adherence)

evidenceItems.name: Certificate Revocation and Incident Response Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution maintains and exercises the capability to promptly revoke compromised or invalid certificates and that revocation events are managed through a documented incident response process. This demonstrates operational maturity in certificate lifecycle management and supports the legal bindingness requirement by ensuring that revoked certificates cannot be used to generate fraudulent signatures that could be mistaken for valid, binding transactions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Revocation capability is confirmed through technical evidence — CRL Distribution Points (CDP) and OCSP Responder URLs are present and functional in all issued certificates, with periodic testing records showing the revocation infrastructure responds correctly

A documented and tested process exists for emergency certificate revocation within 24 hours of a confirmed key compromise event, with named roles, contact details for the issuing CA, and internal escalation steps

Incident records from at least one revocation event (simulated or actual) demonstrate that the process was followed, the certificate was revoked within the target timeframe, affected systems were updated, and a post-incident review was conducted

Customer notification procedures for revoked customer certificates are documented, including communication templates and the process for assisting customers in obtaining replacement certificates with minimum disruption
Revocation events are logged in the certificate inventory and cross-referenced with the institution's incident management log, creating a complete audit trail from detection to closure
commonGaps: OCSP or CRL endpoints are configured in certificates but are not monitored for availability — during an actual revocation event, relying parties cannot check revocation status because the infrastructure is down or unreachable

No tested emergency revocation procedure exists; the assumption is that the CA vendor will handle revocation, with no internal process to initiate, track, or verify revocation within a defined timeframe

Customer certificate revocation is not addressed — if a customer's private key is compromised (e.g., through device theft), there is no documented process for the customer to report this or for the institution to action revocation on their behalf

Revocation events are treated purely as technical events with no linkage to the institution's incident management framework, resulting in no formal incident record, no root cause analysis, and no lessons-learned documentation

The institution uses certificates from a CA that does not support OCSP (only CRL), and CRL distribution points are internal URLs not accessible to external relying parties, making revocation checking impractical for external use cases
suggestedSources: Security Operations Centre (SOC) incident records and SIEM alerts related to certificate events
ITSM incident management module for revocation incident tickets
PKI or infrastructure team for OCSP/CRL monitoring dashboards and availability records
CA vendor portal (DigiCert, Sectigo, GlobalSign) for revocation confirmation receipts
Business continuity and crisis management documentation for customer communication templates
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (procedures, incident reports), System exports (OCSP/CRL monitoring logs, ITSM incident records)

auditTips: Map every customer-facing product that uses digital certificates (e-signing, e-mandate, digital banking tokens, API client authentication) to its issuing CA before the examination, and verify each CA against the CCA Malaysia licensed CA register and the Mozilla/Microsoft root trust programs — examiners will test whether the institution can produce this mapping on demand and justify the CA selection for each product based on documented risk criteria.

Conduct a pre-examination certificate discovery sweep using an automated tool to identify all deployed certificates across on-premises, cloud, and third-party environments, then reconcile the results against the official inventory — any certificate found in the discovery sweep but absent from the inventory is a finding that examiners will raise as evidence of inadequate lifecycle governance.

Validate that subscriber agreements for customer digital certificates contain explicit language on legal bindingness, sole control of private keys, and the binding nature of signatures under the Digital Signature Act 1997 — have the Legal team confirm the language is current and enforceable, as examiners may request a legal opinion on the non-repudiation framework.

Prepare a sample set of five to ten end-to-end certificate lifecycle records (from initial issuance request through renewal to current state) that can be walked through with the examiner, demonstrating that the SOP steps are reflected in actual ITSM tickets and approval records — this transforms a procedural document into credible operational evidence.

Test and document the OCSP responder and CRL endpoint availability for all customer-facing certificates in the month before the examination, and retain the results as evidence of a functioning revocation infrastructure — examiners may independently query OCSP endpoints during fieldwork and will note if revocation checking is unavailable.

Cross-reference the certificate inventory against active system and application registers to identify orphaned certificates — certificates still valid but attached to decommissioned systems or departed staff — and formally revoke these before the examination, as an unexplained discrepancy between active systems and the certificate inventory is a common examiner finding.

Ensure that HSM or secure element configuration records for private key storage are available and show FIPS 140-2 Level 2 or higher certification — if private keys for customer-facing signing are stored in software keystores, escalate this immediately as it represents a significant gap in the strong protection requirement and cannot be easily explained away during examination without a documented remediation plan and timeline.

10.24.title: Data Centre Resilience Objectives
10.24.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the institution has formally documented and approved resilience and availability objectives for each data centre tier, and whether those objectives demonstrably align with business recovery targets such as RTO and RPO defined in the Business Continuity Plan. Examiners will probe whether objectives are aspirational statements or operationally enforced commitments backed by SLAs, monitoring thresholds, and test results. A key red flag is the absence of traceability — where data centre uptime targets exist in isolation with no linkage to critical business services or BCP recovery objectives. Examiners will also assess whether management has endorsed the objectives through governance processes and whether periodic reviews reflect changes in business criticality or infrastructure topology.

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Resilience and Availability Objectives Policy
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy or framework document that specifies quantitative resilience and availability targets (e.g., uptime percentages, Tier classification, fault-tolerance levels) for each production and disaster recovery data centre operated or contracted by the institution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines specific availability targets (e.g., 99.99% uptime) per data centre site with explicit Tier ratings (Uptime Institute Tier III/IV or equivalent)
Document distinguishes between primary, secondary, and tertiary data centre sites with site-specific resilience objectives
Policy is formally approved by the Board or senior technology governance committee with visible approval signatures and version history
Objectives explicitly reference and align with BCP recovery tiers and critical business service classifications
Policy includes a defined review cycle (at minimum annually) with evidence of the most recent review having been completed
commonGaps: Availability targets are expressed as generic aspirational statements (e.g., 'high availability') rather than measurable numeric thresholds
Policy covers only the primary data centre and omits DR or co-location sites used for failover
Document has not been updated following a major infrastructure change, cloud migration, or outsourcing arrangement
No linkage to BCP or critical business service mapping — data centre objectives exist in a standalone IT document with no business context
Policy lacks approval records or exists only as a draft, indicating it has not passed governance review
suggestedSources: IT or Technology Risk Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as ServiceNow GRC or MetricStream)
Technology Governance or IT Steering Committee document library
Business Continuity Management team's policy register
Third-party or outsourced data centre SLA and contract annexures
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Business Recovery Objectives Alignment Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A traceability matrix or mapping document that explicitly links data centre resilience and availability objectives to the institution's business recovery objectives (RTO, RPO, MTPD) for each critical business service or application tier, demonstrating coherent alignment between IT infrastructure commitments and business continuity requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix maps each critical business service or application to the hosting data centre and the corresponding RTO/RPO, with the data centre's availability objective shown to support that recovery target
Document identifies the consequence of data centre unavailability on each mapped business service, enabling risk-informed prioritisation
Matrix is co-owned and validated by both the Technology and Business Continuity/BCM functions, evidenced by dual sign-off
Tier-1 and Tier-2 critical systems are explicitly called out with more stringent data centre availability requirements than non-critical systems
Matrix is maintained as a living document with change history reflecting system additions, decommissions, and BCP updates
commonGaps: RTO/RPO values in the BCP do not match the data centre availability SLAs documented in the technology policy, creating unresolvable gaps
Matrix covers only applications and omits supporting infrastructure layers (network, storage, power) whose failure would affect the same recovery objectives
Business units were not consulted in defining recovery objectives, resulting in IT-imposed targets that do not reflect actual business recovery needs
Matrix is outdated and does not reflect newly onboarded critical systems or changes in system criticality following a business impact analysis refresh
No evidence that the mapping has been tested or validated through a BCP exercise to confirm that data centre capabilities actually meet stated recovery objectives
suggestedSources: BCM or Business Continuity team's BCP documentation and BIA register
GRC platform (ServiceNow, Archer, or equivalent) — application criticality and recovery objective modules
IT Asset Management or CMDB (Configuration Management Database) with hosting location attributes
Technology Risk register linking infrastructure risks to business impact
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC system export in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Infrastructure Design and Tier Certification Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation evidencing the physical and logical resilience design of each data centre, including redundancy architecture (power, cooling, network), fault-tolerance specifications, and where applicable, third-party Tier certification or equivalent independent assessment, demonstrating that infrastructure design supports the stated availability objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams show N+1 or 2N redundancy for power (UPS, generators), cooling (CRAC/CRAH units), and network connectivity at each data centre site
Third-party Tier certification (Uptime Institute, TIA-942, or BNM-accepted equivalent) is current, valid, and on file for each data centre site
Design documentation identifies single points of failure and includes compensating controls or accepted residual risk acknowledgements signed by risk owners
Network connectivity diagrams demonstrate diverse physical routing to the data centre with at least two independent carrier-grade ISP connections
For outsourced or co-location data centres, vendor-provided infrastructure specifications and audit reports (SSAE 18 SOC 2, ISO 27001) are maintained and current
commonGaps: Tier certification has expired or applies only to part of the data centre facility, not the cages or suites occupied by the institution
Architecture diagrams are outdated and do not reflect recent hardware refreshes, cabinet relocations, or network re-patching
Single points of failure are identified in design reviews but no remediation plan or accepted risk record exists in the risk register
Institutions relying on third-party co-location facilities cannot produce the vendor's infrastructure design documentation or current certification
Design documents exist only for the primary data centre — the DR site infrastructure is undocumented or assumed to meet the same standard without verification
suggestedSources: Data Centre Operations team or Infrastructure Engineering team's technical document repository
Vendor management or third-party risk management system for co-location provider documentation
ISO 27001 or SOC 2 audit reports from managed/outsourced data centre providers
Facilities management records for on-premises data centres
evidenceItems.format: PDF (architecture diagrams, certification reports, vendor audit reports)

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Availability Performance Reports and SLA Monitoring Records
