evidenceItems.description: Periodic operational reports demonstrating actual data centre availability and resilience performance measured against the defined objectives, including uptime metrics, incident records affecting availability, and evidence that breaches of availability targets are escalated, investigated, and remediated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly or quarterly availability dashboards show measured uptime against defined targets per data centre site, with trend analysis over at least 12 months
Reports are generated from infrastructure monitoring tools (e.g., Nagios, Zabbix, Datadog, SolarWinds) and cross-referenced with incident management records to ensure completeness
Any periods of unplanned downtime are accompanied by incident reports and root cause analyses with corrective action tracking
SLA performance reports for outsourced or co-location data centres include vendor-provided metrics with the institution's own independent verification
Reports are reviewed and acknowledged by senior IT management or the CIO, with escalation evidence where availability targets were breached
commonGaps: Availability is measured only at the application layer and there are no data centre-level infrastructure availability metrics tracked independently
Monitoring coverage has gaps — certain critical components (e.g., storage arrays, out-of-band management networks) are not included in availability calculations
Incidents affecting availability are closed without root cause analysis or corrective actions, making it impossible to demonstrate continuous improvement
Vendor-reported SLA compliance for outsourced data centres is accepted without independent validation, and the institution has no ability to verify reported metrics
Historical availability data is not retained beyond the current year, making trend analysis and BNM examination of prior periods impossible
suggestedSources: Infrastructure monitoring platforms (Nagios, Zabbix, Datadog, PRTG, SolarWinds)
ITSM platform incident management module (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
Vendor-provided SLA reports and monthly service review meeting minutes
NOC (Network Operations Centre) operations logs and shift handover records
evidenceItems.format: PDF report exports or system-generated dashboards, supplemented by spreadsheet summaries

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Resilience Testing and Simulation Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of planned resilience tests, failover exercises, and simulation activities conducted to validate that the data centre can meet its stated availability objectives, including test scope, results, identified gaps, and remediation actions taken following each exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual test schedule covers all resilience scenarios including power failure (generator failover), cooling failure, network path failure, and full site failover to DR data centre
Test reports document pre-test objectives, actual test outcomes, measured recovery times, and a clear pass/fail assessment against stated availability objectives
Failover tests include business application validation — not just infrastructure-level confirmation — confirming that business services are recoverable within RTO from the DR data centre
Post-test gap reports are raised as formal remediation items tracked in the risk register or ITSM platform with assigned owners and target resolution dates
Tests are witnessed or independently validated by internal audit, risk management, or an external party, with sign-off records maintained
commonGaps: Resilience testing is limited to DR failover drills and does not include component-level failure simulations (power, cooling, network) within the primary data centre
Test results consistently show full success without any identified gaps — an indicator that test scope is insufficiently challenging or results are not objectively recorded
Recovery times measured during tests exceed the RTO specified in the BCP, but no escalation or remediation plan has been initiated to close the gap
Tests are conducted during off-peak hours with reduced load and results are not considered representative of peak business conditions
DR failover tests validate infrastructure availability but do not include business validation steps to confirm that critical systems are fully operational and data integrity is maintained
suggestedSources: BCM team's DR exercise reports and test schedule
IT Infrastructure team's maintenance and testing calendar
ITSM platform — change and problem management records related to resilience tests
Internal Audit's IT audit workpapers for data centre resilience reviews
evidenceItems.format: PDF test reports with sign-off pages, supplemented by ITSM records

evidenceItems.name: Board and Management Governance Records for Data Centre Resilience Objectives
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data centre resilience and availability objectives have been formally endorsed by appropriate governance bodies — Board Risk Committee, IT Steering Committee, or equivalent — and that senior management maintains oversight of compliance against those objectives, demonstrating institutional ownership beyond the IT function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes contain a formal resolution approving or reaffirming data centre resilience objectives within the past 12 months
Management reporting pack includes a standing agenda item on data centre availability performance with year-to-date metrics presented to senior management
Risk appetite statements approved by the Board explicitly include data centre availability thresholds as a quantified risk tolerance metric
Annual review of resilience objectives is documented with evidence that changes in business criticality, infrastructure, or regulatory expectations prompted updates to the objectives
Technology risk reporting to the Board includes data centre resilience as a tracked key risk indicator (KRI) with defined escalation triggers
commonGaps: Data centre resilience objectives are set and maintained entirely within the IT function with no Board or Risk Committee visibility or endorsement
Governance minutes reference data centre resilience only in the context of incidents — there is no proactive governance of the objectives themselves
Risk appetite does not quantify data centre availability as a measurable tolerance, making it impossible to determine when a breach requires Board escalation
Annual review of objectives is performed informally with no documented output — verbal confirmation by IT leadership is cited as evidence but no written record exists
Technology risk reporting aggregates all IT risks without specifically surfacing data centre resilience status, making it difficult to demonstrate that the Board has meaningful oversight
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee and IT Steering Committee secretariat records and approved minutes
Risk Management function's risk appetite framework and KRI dashboard
CRO or CIO management reporting pack submitted to governance committees
GRC platform — risk register and governance reporting modules
evidenceItems.format: PDF (approved committee minutes, signed risk appetite statements, management reports)

auditTips: Before the examination, compile a single-page Data Centre Resilience Objectives Summary that lists each data centre site, its Tier classification, stated availability target, corresponding BCP recovery tier, and the date objectives were last reviewed and approved — examiners frequently open with a request for exactly this overview and the absence of a ready document creates an unfavourable first impression.

Cross-reference the availability targets in the Data Centre Resilience Policy against the RTO and RPO values in the BCP for every Tier-1 critical system; any gap where the data centre availability objective is mathematically insufficient to support the stated RTO should be remediated or formally risk-accepted before the examination, as this misalignment is a primary finding trigger for this clause.

For institutions using outsourced or co-location data centres, prepare a vendor evidence pack in advance including current Tier certification, latest SOC 2 or ISO 27001 audit reports, and the most recent SLA performance report — examiners apply equal scrutiny to outsourced infrastructure and will expect the institution to demonstrate that it actively monitors vendor performance rather than relying solely on contractual protections.

Ensure that at least one full data centre resilience test has been conducted within the 12 months preceding the examination, covering both the primary and DR sites; if the most recent test revealed gaps, prepare a remediation tracker showing the status of each corrective action, as documented follow-through on identified weaknesses demonstrates control maturity even where the initial test result was imperfect.

Trace each data centre availability objective back to a formal governance approval record — retrieve the relevant committee minutes and have them indexed and ready; examiners will specifically ask who approved the objectives and when, and being unable to produce this evidence quickly implies that objectives are informal or aspirational rather than institutionally mandated.

Review the last 24 months of data centre incident records and confirm that any incident resulting in unplanned downtime has a completed root cause analysis and a closed or tracked corrective action in the ITSM system; examiners will sample incidents during the examination period and an incident with no RCA or open corrective actions with no owner is a reliable finding under this clause.

Validate that your infrastructure monitoring platform captures availability metrics at the data centre infrastructure layer — power, cooling, network — independently of application-layer monitoring; produce a sample availability report from the monitoring tool before the examination and confirm the figures are consistent with what is reported to governance committees, as discrepancies between raw monitoring data and management reports are a significant credibility risk during examination.

10.25.title: Data Centre Redundancy
10.25.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has demonstrably eliminated single points of failure across all critical data centre capacity components, including power, cooling, network, and compute infrastructure. Examiners look for evidence that redundancy architecture is not merely documented but is actively validated through periodic failover testing, with test results traceable to the institution's specific business recovery objectives (RTOs and RPOs). A primary red flag is the existence of N+0 configurations anywhere in the critical path — examiners expect at minimum N+1, and preferably N+2 or 2N, for components underpinning Tier 1 systems. Examiners also scrutinise whether redundant distribution paths are logically and physically diverse, flagging configurations where redundant paths share common cable trays, switch fabric, or power panels. Institutions that cannot map their redundancy architecture directly to approved BIA-derived recovery targets are considered non-compliant regardless of the physical infrastructure in place.

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Infrastructure Architecture Diagram
evidenceItems.description: Formal, version-controlled architecture diagrams depicting the physical and logical layout of all data centre infrastructure components, including power distribution units (PDUs), uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), generators, cooling units (CRAC/CRAH), network switches, routers, and compute racks. The diagrams must explicitly label redundancy tiers (e.g., N+1, 2N) and illustrate that all distribution paths serving computer equipment are diverse and do not share a common point of failure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagrams are current (reviewed within the past 12 months), version-controlled, and approved by the Head of Technology Infrastructure or equivalent.
All redundancy configurations are explicitly annotated (e.g., 2N power, N+1 cooling, dual-path networking with diverse entry points) for each infrastructure layer.
Physical path diversity is confirmed — power feeds, fibre runs, and network cables are shown traversing separate cable trays, conduits, and building entry points.
Diagrams cross-reference the Tier classification of the data centre (e.g., Uptime Institute Tier III/IV equivalent) and map redundancy levels to that classification.
Separate diagrams exist for the primary data centre and the disaster recovery/secondary site, with both demonstrating equivalent redundancy standards.
commonGaps: Diagrams are outdated, reflecting decommissioned or upgraded equipment, making it impossible to verify current redundancy posture during examination.
Redundancy annotations are absent or generic — diagrams show components but do not label N-tier configurations, leaving examiners unable to assess SPOF elimination.
Physical path diversity is assumed but not mapped — the diagram shows two network paths without confirming they use separate conduits or enter the building at different points.
Secondary/DR site diagrams are missing or held by a separate team, preventing a holistic view of end-to-end redundancy.
Diagrams conflate logical redundancy (software HA clustering) with physical redundancy, obscuring underlying hardware SPOFs.
suggestedSources: Technology Infrastructure team SharePoint/Confluence documentation repository
DCIM (Data Centre Infrastructure Management) platform exports (e.g., Nlyte, Sunbird, Vertiv)
Network management platform (e.g., SolarWinds, NetBox) for topology exports
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) asset and architecture register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/VISIO/draw.io export with version history log

evidenceItems.name: Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Risk Assessment Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured assessment documenting the methodology and findings of a systematic SPOF analysis conducted across all data centre capacity components — power, cooling, compute, storage, and network distribution paths. The register must capture identified SPOFs, their risk ratings, remediation actions taken or planned, and residual risk accepted by management, demonstrating a deliberate and ongoing process to eliminate SPOFs in alignment with business recovery objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment follows a defined methodology (e.g., FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) and covers all infrastructure layers: power, cooling, network, compute, and physical access.
Each identified SPOF is rated by criticality, linked to the systems it affects, and mapped to the institution's BIA-derived recovery objectives (RTO/RPO) to demonstrate business impact.
Remediation actions for each SPOF have assigned owners, target completion dates, and closure evidence, with residual SPOFs formally risk-accepted by the CTO or Risk Committee.
The register is reviewed and re-certified at least annually or following any significant infrastructure change, with review history documented.
The assessment distinguishes between SPOFs in the primary data centre and those in the DR/secondary site, treating both as in-scope.
commonGaps: The SPOF assessment covers only network and compute layers, omitting power distribution and cooling — both common sources of catastrophic failure.
Identified SPOFs are recorded but remediation actions lack owners or deadlines, indicating the register is a point-in-time exercise rather than a living control.
The assessment has not been refreshed following infrastructure upgrades or changes, meaning the register does not reflect the current environment.
Risk acceptance for residual SPOFs is undocumented or approved at too low a management level, indicating inadequate governance oversight.
SPOFs are assessed in isolation without mapping to affected business services or recovery objectives, making it impossible to demonstrate alignment with RMiT obligations.
suggestedSources: Technology Risk team's GRC platform (e.g., Archer RSA, ServiceNow IRM) risk register module
Infrastructure Change Management records in ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB/Change)
Internal Audit reports on data centre infrastructure reviews
DC Operations team's operational risk log
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Redundancy Component Testing and Failover Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented records of periodic tests conducted to validate that redundant components and distribution paths function as designed under simulated failure conditions. This includes generator failover tests, UPS switchover tests, cooling unit failover exercises, network path failover tests, and storage replication validation. Test records must demonstrate that redundancy actually works in practice and that the institution's business recovery objectives are achievable under real failure scenarios.
whatGoodLooksLike: Tests are conducted on a defined schedule (at minimum annually, quarterly for critical components) with a formal test plan approved before execution and test results signed off by the infrastructure lead.
Each test record documents the pre-test configuration, the failure scenario simulated, observed system behaviour, actual recovery time achieved, and comparison against the target RTO/RPO.
Test results demonstrate that multiple distribution path failover is seamless and that no single path failure causes service interruption to computer equipment.
Failed tests or tests that did not meet recovery objectives are formally tracked as deficiencies with root-cause analysis and remediation evidence documented.
Generator tests include full-load transfer tests (not just no-load starts) confirming the generator can sustain the data centre under realistic demand conditions.
commonGaps: Test records exist for network failover but not for power or cooling components, leaving significant portions of the redundancy architecture unvalidated.
Tests are conducted at partial load or during maintenance windows that do not reflect realistic operating conditions, making results unreliable.
Recovery times achieved during testing are not compared against approved RTOs, so it is unclear whether tests demonstrate compliance with business recovery objectives.
Test failures are closed without documented root-cause analysis or re-test evidence, creating doubt about whether deficiencies were actually resolved.
Test frequency is ad hoc rather than schedule-driven, with gaps of over 18 months between some component tests.
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change and test record repository (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Data centre operations runbooks and test execution logs maintained by the DC Operations team
BCP/DR test reports held by the Business Continuity Management team
Facilities management logs for physical infrastructure (power, cooling) test records
evidenceItems.format: PDF test reports with signed sign-off page; ITSM system export

evidenceItems.name: Business Recovery Objectives Traceability Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A traceability matrix that formally links the data centre redundancy architecture and configurations to the institution's approved business recovery objectives (RTOs and RPOs) as derived from the Business Impact Analysis (BIA). This document demonstrates that redundancy design decisions are not arbitrary but are deliberately calibrated to ensure the institution can meet its recovery commitments for each critical business service and system hosted in the data centre.
whatGoodLooksLike: The matrix maps each critical business service to its BIA-approved RTO/RPO and then to the specific redundancy mechanisms (e.g., 2N power, dual-path network, N+1 cooling) that enable the institution to meet those objectives.
The matrix is reviewed and updated whenever the BIA is refreshed or the infrastructure architecture changes, with version control demonstrating currency.
Recovery objectives reflected in the matrix are formally approved by the Board or Senior Management Risk Committee, establishing governance accountability.
The matrix is used as an input to failover test planning, ensuring that test scenarios are designed to validate the specific recovery targets each redundancy mechanism is intended to support.
Services with tighter RTOs (e.g., payment systems, core banking) are shown to have proportionally higher redundancy tiers, demonstrating risk-proportionate design.
commonGaps: Recovery objectives exist in the BIA document but have never been formally translated into infrastructure redundancy requirements, leaving redundancy design unjustified against business need.
The matrix covers Tier 1 systems only, omitting Tier 2 and Tier 3 systems that may nonetheless have regulatory or operational recovery requirements.
RTOs in the matrix conflict with actual recovery times demonstrated in failover testing, indicating either outdated objectives or inadequate redundancy.
The traceability matrix is maintained by a different team from those responsible for infrastructure design, leading to misalignment that is not caught until examination.
The matrix has not been updated following a BIA review, meaning redundancy configurations may be calibrated against obsolete recovery targets.
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management team's BIA repository (SharePoint or GRC platform)
Technology Risk team's GRC platform — control-to-requirement mapping module
IT Governance documentation library maintained by the CTO office
DR planning documentation held by the Technology Resilience team
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Capacity and Redundancy Monitoring Dashboard / Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports or real-time monitoring dashboard exports demonstrating that redundant capacity components are continuously monitored for availability, utilisation, and health status. This evidence demonstrates that the institution does not merely design redundancy in but actively maintains headroom and detects degradation of redundant components before they become SPOFs — for example, detecting that one UPS string has failed before the second string is also needed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monitoring covers all redundancy-critical components — UPS state-of-charge and bypass status, PDU load across feeds, CRAC/CRAH unit availability, network path utilisation across diverse links, and generator fuel and battery health.

Alerts are configured for conditions that reduce effective redundancy (e.g., one UPS string offline, one network path down, cooling unit in maintenance mode) and are routed to the on-call infrastructure team with defined response SLAs.

Monthly or quarterly capacity reports demonstrate that power, cooling, and network utilisation remain within design thresholds that preserve N+1 or 2N headroom — high utilisation consuming redundant capacity is flagged as a risk.
Monitoring reports are reviewed by management periodically (at least quarterly), with sign-off documented and anomalies actioned through the ITSM incident or change process.
The monitoring platform covers both the primary and DR data centres, with equivalent visibility into both sites.
commonGaps: Monitoring exists for compute and network but not for power and cooling components, creating blind spots in redundancy health visibility.

Alerts are configured but alert thresholds are set too conservatively (e.g., alerting only on complete component failure rather than degraded redundancy), meaning the team is unaware that redundancy has been compromised until the second component also fails.
Monitoring data is collected but not reviewed by management — there is no evidence of periodic reporting or management sign-off, only raw system logs.
Capacity reports show power or cooling utilisation consistently above 80% of total (not redundant) capacity, meaning the available headroom within the redundant design is already eroded.
The DR site has no equivalent monitoring coverage, so degraded redundancy at the secondary site is not detected until a failover event is attempted.
suggestedSources: DCIM platform (e.g., Nlyte, Vertiv Trellis, Sunbird) — dashboard exports and trend reports
Network monitoring platform (e.g., SolarWinds NPM, PRTG, Nagios) — availability and path utilisation reports
ITSM platform — incident records triggered by infrastructure monitoring alerts
Facilities management BMS (Building Management System) — power and cooling telemetry logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF dashboard export or system-generated report; CSV for raw telemetry

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Data Centre / Colocation Facility Compliance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Where the financial institution hosts critical infrastructure in a third-party colocation or cloud data centre facility, this evidence demonstrates that the institution has obtained and reviewed the facility operator's redundancy certifications, audit reports (e.g., SSAE 18 SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, Uptime Institute Tier certification), and contractual SLAs, and has validated that the facility's redundancy posture meets or exceeds the institution's own standards required to achieve its business recovery objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: Current Uptime Institute Tier III or Tier IV certification (or equivalent) for the colocation facility is on file, with certification scope confirmed to cover the physical space occupied by the institution's equipment.

The institution's vendor due diligence file includes the facility's most recent SSAE 18 SOC 2 Type II report reviewed by the institution's technology risk team, with noted exceptions tracked to resolution.

Contractual agreements with the colocation provider include explicit SLAs for power availability (e.g., 99.999% uptime), cooling capacity guarantees, and network path diversity commitments that align with the institution's RTOs.

The institution conducts or obtains results of annual site visits or audits of the colocation facility to verify that the physical redundancy described in certification documents is actually in place.

Where cloud infrastructure is used, cloud provider shared responsibility model documentation is retained and the institution's configuration (e.g., multi-AZ, multi-region deployment) is documented as meeting redundancy obligations.
commonGaps: The institution relies entirely on the colocation provider's assurances without obtaining independent audit reports (SOC 2 Type II) or certifications, making it impossible to verify redundancy claims.
Colocation SLAs reference uptime guarantees but do not specify the redundancy architecture (N+1, 2N) or physical path diversity commitments that underpin them.
Third-party data centre compliance documents are outdated (more than 12 months old) and have not been refreshed as part of the annual vendor management cycle.

The institution has not mapped the colocation facility's redundancy capabilities against its own business recovery objectives, so it is unclear whether the facility can support the institution's RTOs.
For cloud deployments, the institution's workloads are deployed in a single availability zone, and the shared responsibility model documentation has not been reviewed to confirm this creates a SPOF.
suggestedSources: Vendor Management team's third-party risk register and due diligence file repository (SharePoint or GRC platform)
Legal/Contracts team for colocation and managed services agreements
Colocation provider's customer portal for on-demand access to audit reports and certifications
Cloud provider compliance portals (e.g., AWS Artifact, Azure Trust Center, GCP Compliance Reports Manager)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (SOC 2 reports, certifications, contracts); GRC platform vendor record export

auditTips: Before the examination, conduct a walkthrough of both the primary and DR data centre facilities with the infrastructure team and physically trace the distribution paths for power (from utility feed to PDU to rack) and network (from external demarcation to core switch to rack) to confirm that no two redundant paths share a common physical segment — documented diagrams alone are insufficient if the physical reality diverges.

Cross-reference the SPOF register with the ITSM incident database: search for incidents over the past 24 months involving power, cooling, or network component failures and verify that each was caused by a component that was either already identified as a SPOF or was added to the register following the incident. Unexplained outages not reflected in the SPOF register are a significant red flag.

Request failover test records and then map each test's achieved recovery time against the corresponding RTO in the BIA traceability matrix — BNM examiners are specifically interested in whether recovery objectives are met under test conditions, not merely whether redundancy components exist. Gaps between tested recovery times and approved RTOs must be explained and remediated.

For each critical system identified in the BIA, verify that the redundancy tier of the infrastructure it runs on is proportionate to its recovery objective — a payment system with a 15-minute RTO must be hosted on infrastructure with a higher redundancy classification (2N power, dual cooling loops, dual diverse network paths) than a reporting system with a 24-hour RTO. Mismatches indicate the redundancy architecture was not designed against business requirements.

Review the monitoring alert history for the 12 months preceding the examination and specifically search for alerts indicating that effective redundancy was temporarily degraded (e.g., one UPS string offline for maintenance, one network path down for patching) — then confirm that the ITSM change records show these were planned, approved, and restored within a timeframe that aligns with the institution's risk appetite for operating in a degraded-redundancy state.

For third-party hosted infrastructure, verify that the vendor management team's annual review cycle has produced a current (within 12 months) assessment that includes review of the colocation facility's or cloud provider's redundancy certifications — BNM examiners will ask whether the institution can demonstrate it exercises oversight of third-party redundancy, not merely that a contract exists.

Prepare a one-page executive summary that maps the clause obligations (redundant capacity components, multiple distribution paths, SPOF elimination, business recovery objective achievement) directly to specific evidence items with document references and page numbers — BNM examiners find this mapping document valuable and it demonstrates that the institution understands the regulatory intent, not just the technical implementation.

10.26.title: Production Data Centre Physical Security
10.26.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution's production data centre is genuinely dedicated to that purpose and is not co-located with non-production systems or general office functions in a manner that degrades physical security controls. A primary red flag is evidence of single points of failure in power, cooling, or network connectivity — examiners will specifically request N+1 or 2N redundancy diagrams and cross-check them against maintenance records and incident logs. Examiners also scrutinise the physical location itself, looking for documented hazard assessments that demonstrate the site was selected away from flood plains, seismic zones, and other disaster-prone areas. Monitoring and alerting maturity is heavily weighted: examiners expect a continuous, automated monitoring regime with documented escalation thresholds, not periodic manual checks. Finally, examiners will test whether maintenance is planned and evidenced, looking for gaps between scheduled preventive maintenance and actual completion records that could indicate deferred risk.

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Site Risk and Location Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A formal documented assessment of the physical location of the production data centre, demonstrating that the site was evaluated and selected based on its distance from disaster-prone areas including flood zones, seismic risk areas, flight paths, industrial hazard zones, and areas of civil unrest. This record demonstrates due diligence in site selection and ongoing suitability review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment references recognised hazard maps (e.g., JMM flood risk maps, MET Malaysia seismic data) with coordinates and zone classifications explicitly documented
Report is dated and formally approved by senior management or the Board Risk Committee, with review frequency defined (at minimum annually or upon material environmental changes)
Assessment covers both natural and man-made risks including proximity to airports, chemical plants, and high-voltage infrastructure
Document includes a risk rating conclusion and any residual risk acceptance sign-off from the CRO or equivalent
Remediation or compensating controls are documented where any moderate risk was identified but site relocation was not feasible
commonGaps: Assessment exists only as a one-off exercise at the time of site selection with no evidence of periodic review despite changing environmental conditions (e.g., expanded flood zones)
Generic template used without site-specific geospatial data or reference to Malaysian hazard authority sources, rendering conclusions unsubstantiated
Assessment does not address man-made or anthropogenic risks, covering only natural disasters and therefore failing the holistic evaluation expected by examiners
No formal sign-off trail — document is unsigned or lacks version control, making it difficult to demonstrate Board or senior management awareness
Co-location or third-party hosted data centre arrangements lack a corresponding vendor-provided site risk report or contractual obligation to maintain one
suggestedSources: Facilities Management or Corporate Real Estate team (holds original site selection documentation)
GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) under the Physical Risk or Operational Risk module
Data centre operator's compliance portal if co-located (e.g., AIMS, Equinix customer portal)
Board Risk Committee or EXCO meeting minutes referencing site risk sign-off
SharePoint or document management system under the Enterprise Risk Management library
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with formal approval signatures and version history

evidenceItems.name: Physical Access Control Policy and Access Logs
evidenceItems.description: The policy governing physical access to the production data centre, together with supporting access logs demonstrating that only authorised personnel gain entry. This evidence bundle demonstrates that the dedicated space is actively secured against unauthorised access through layered physical controls and is subject to regular access review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy mandates multi-factor physical access (e.g., access card plus biometric or PIN) for the data centre perimeter and inner cage, with no single-factor entry points
Access logs are timestamped, automated, and retained in a tamper-evident system (e.g., PACS — Physical Access Control System) with at least 12 months of searchable history readily available
Quarterly or more frequent access rights reviews are documented, showing removal of access for departed or role-changed staff within defined SLA (e.g., within 24 hours of offboarding)
Visitor and third-party access is logged separately with escort requirements, purpose recorded, and approval trail from an authorised sponsor visible in the log
CCTV coverage is documented in a floor plan overlay, retention period meets policy (minimum 90 days), and footage retrieval procedures are tested and recorded
commonGaps: Access reviews are conducted annually rather than quarterly, leaving prolonged windows where terminated employees or role-changed staff retain physical access
Visitor logs are paper-based and not cross-referenced to the digital PACS log, creating reconciliation gaps that examiners can exploit to identify unlogged entries
CCTV footage retention is shorter than policy states or footage is overwritten due to storage limitations, meaning historical evidence cannot be produced on demand
The policy covers the main data centre floor but excludes ancillary spaces (UPS rooms, generator bays, cable management rooms) that provide indirect access to critical systems
No documented process for emergency access bypass — ad hoc emergency entries are unlogged, creating unaccountable access events in the audit trail
suggestedSources: Physical Access Control System (PACS) — exported logs from Lenel, Gallagher, or equivalent platform
IT Security or Facilities Security team for policy documents
HR system for cross-referencing active staff against access-provisioned list
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for access request and approval tickets
CCTV management system for footage retention settings and retrieval test records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy), CSV/system export (access logs), PDF (review sign-off records)

evidenceItems.name: Redundancy Architecture Documentation — Power, Cooling, and Network
evidenceItems.description: Formal architecture diagrams and engineering specifications demonstrating that there is no single point of failure in the electrical utility, thermal management, network connectivity, and hardware components of the production data centre. This evidence substantiates the SPOF-elimination obligation under the clause and is central to examiner assessment of resilience design.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams are current (reviewed within the past 12 months), version-controlled, and show explicit N+1 or 2N redundancy for UPS systems, PDUs, chillers, CRAC units, and core network switches
Diagrams are cross-referenced to a Bill of Materials or asset register, enabling examiners to validate that depicted redundant components physically exist and are correctly configured
Network connectivity diagrams show diverse fibre entry points from at least two geographically separate providers, with no shared conduit or entry point (carrier hotel diversity documented)
Generator capacity calculations are documented, showing runtime at full load exceeds the institution's RTO for the data centre, supported by load bank test results
A formal SPOF analysis report (e.g., FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is on record, signed off by an independent technical reviewer or third-party assessor
commonGaps: Diagrams exist but are outdated — they reflect the data centre as built several years ago and do not capture subsequent hardware upgrades, cage reconfigurations, or new network providers
Redundancy is depicted at the component level but shared management planes (e.g., a single out-of-band management switch) are not identified as SPOFs, which examiners will probe
Generator autonomy calculations assume partial load rather than full production load, meaning runtime under real failover conditions is shorter than documented
Network diversity is claimed but both fibre routes share the same street-level conduit for the last mile, which is not disclosed in the architecture documentation
No FMEA or equivalent structured SPOF analysis exists — redundancy claims are based solely on vendor marketing specifications rather than institution-specific engineering review
suggestedSources: Infrastructure Architecture team or Data Centre Engineering for Visio/Lucidchart/draw.io diagram files
Data centre operator's technical compliance pack if co-located (e.g., AIMS, Telekom Malaysia DC, Equinix)
GRC platform for FMEA or risk assessment artefacts linked to data centre infrastructure
Network Operations team for ISP diversity agreements and route documentation
Facilities Management for generator and UPS specification sheets and load test records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (architecture diagrams, FMEA report), XLSX (load calculations), PDF (ISP diversity agreements)

evidenceItems.name: Preventive Maintenance Schedule and Completion Records
evidenceItems.description: A documented preventive maintenance programme for all critical data centre components — including UPS, generators, PDUs, CRAC/CRAH units, fire suppression systems, and physical security systems — together with completion records demonstrating that maintenance was performed on schedule by qualified personnel. This evidence demonstrates the 'adequate maintenance' obligation within the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Maintenance schedule is approved by the Data Centre or Infrastructure manager and covers all critical components identified in the redundancy architecture, with defined frequencies aligned to manufacturer specifications and industry standards (e.g., TIA-942)
Completion records include technician identity, date performed, findings, and any corrective actions raised, stored in an ITSM or CMMS platform with an auditable trail
Overdue maintenance items are tracked in a formal risk register with documented escalation to management, ensuring no deferred maintenance is invisible to governance
Third-party maintenance contracts (e.g., for generators, CRAC units) include SLA terms and vendor completion certificates are retained alongside internal records
Post-maintenance test results are documented (e.g., UPS transfer time test, generator auto-start test) confirming components returned to service in a verified operational state
commonGaps: Maintenance records exist for high-visibility components (generators, UPS) but ancillary items (fire suppression inspection, PDU breaker testing, cable management) are absent or irregularly maintained
Records are held in spreadsheets without version control or access logging, making it impossible to demonstrate that records have not been retrospectively altered ahead of examination
Corrective actions identified during maintenance are not formally tracked to closure — open items from previous service visits remain unresolved with no documented risk acceptance
Third-party vendor completion certificates are not retained by the institution, meaning evidence of contracted maintenance cannot be produced independently of the vendor
Maintenance schedule has not been updated to reflect new equipment added to the data centre, creating undocumented gaps in the maintenance programme
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Maximo, or equivalent CMMS) under Planned Maintenance or Facilities work order modules
Facilities Management or Data Centre Operations team for physical maintenance logbooks
Vendor portals or service desk portals for third-party maintenance completion certificates
GRC platform for overdue maintenance items escalated as operational risks
SharePoint or document management system for historical maintenance schedule versions
evidenceItems.format: System export/PDF (ITSM work orders), PDF (vendor certificates), XLSX (maintenance schedule)

evidenceItems.name: Continuous Monitoring and Alerting Configuration with Alert Response Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the data centre environment — including temperature, humidity, power consumption, UPS status, cooling unit performance, and physical security events — is monitored continuously through automated tooling, with configured thresholds, defined alert escalation paths, and documented records of alert responses. This directly addresses the 'holistic and continuous monitoring with timely alerts' obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monitoring tool configuration documentation shows sensors deployed across all critical zones (hot aisle, cold aisle, UPS bays, generator room) with specific threshold values defined for temperature, humidity, and power metrics aligned to ASHRAE or manufacturer specifications
Alert escalation matrix is formally documented and tested, showing tiered notification paths (L1 NOC → L2 DC Engineering → On-call Manager) with defined response time SLAs per alert severity
Alert response records in the ITSM platform demonstrate that alerts are acknowledged, investigated, and closed within SLA, with root cause and resolution documented rather than simply dismissed
Dashboard or reporting evidence shows continuous uptime of the monitoring platform itself, with monitoring system availability tracked as a separate KPI to prevent blind spots
Annual or semi-annual alert simulation or tabletop exercises are documented, validating that escalation paths and contact details remain current and that on-call staff respond within SLA
commonGaps: Monitoring tool is deployed but alert thresholds have never been formally documented or approved — default vendor thresholds are in use without validation against the institution's specific hardware specifications or risk appetite

Alert logs show recurring alerts being closed without documented investigation (alarm fatigue), which examiners will highlight as evidence that monitoring is not resulting in timely or meaningful response

Monitoring coverage has gaps — sensors are deployed on primary equipment but secondary or redundant components (standby CRAC units, bypass UPS) are not monitored, creating blind spots in the supposedly continuous regime
No escalation matrix exists or the matrix is outdated with obsolete contact details, meaning alert notification cannot be guaranteed to reach the responsible party in a timely manner
Monitoring system availability is not tracked — there is no evidence of what happens when the monitoring platform itself goes offline, and no secondary alerting mechanism is documented
suggestedSources: DCIM platform (e.g., Schneider EcoStruxure, Vertiv Avocent, Nlyte) for sensor configuration and alert history exports
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) for alert-to-incident ticket records and resolution documentation
NOC or SOC team for escalation matrix and on-call roster documentation
Network Management System (NMS) for infrastructure-level availability monitoring records
GRC platform or intranet for alert response procedure documents and exercise records
evidenceItems.format: System export/PDF (alert logs, sensor configuration), PDF/DOCX (escalation matrix, procedures), PDF (exercise reports)

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Incident and Fault History Log
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated record of all data centre infrastructure incidents, faults, and near-miss events covering power disruptions, cooling failures, physical security breaches or attempts, hardware failures, and environmental exceedances. This log demonstrates the institution's ability to detect, respond to, and learn from data centre issues, validating the effectiveness of both the monitoring regime and the no-SPOF design in practice.
whatGoodLooksLike: Incident log is maintained in the ITSM platform with consistent categorisation, severity rating, timestamps for detection, escalation, and resolution, enabling examiners to calculate mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to resolve (MTTR)
Each significant incident has a linked Post-Incident Review (PIR) or Root Cause Analysis (RCA) document demonstrating structured investigation and documented lessons learned
Corrective and preventive actions from PIRs are tracked to closure in the same ITSM platform, with owner, due date, and sign-off evidence — not left as open recommendations
The log demonstrates that monitoring alerts successfully triggered incident records, validating the end-to-end effectiveness of the alerting and escalation chain
Trend analysis reports are produced periodically (quarterly minimum) from the incident log and presented to management, showing awareness of recurring fault patterns and proactive risk reduction
commonGaps: Minor faults and near-misses (e.g., brief power fluctuations absorbed by UPS, cooling unit failover events) are not logged because they did not cause outages, leaving examiners unable to assess the true frequency of stress events on the infrastructure

PIRs exist for major incidents but are not linked to the original incident records in the ITSM platform, creating a fragmented evidence trail that requires manual reconciliation under examination pressure

Corrective actions from previous PIRs remain open or overdue, suggesting that lessons learned from past incidents have not been implemented — a significant red flag for BNM examiners assessing governance maturity
Incident log categorisation is inconsistent — similar events are categorised differently across time, making trend analysis unreliable and preventing meaningful management reporting

Management reporting of data centre incident trends does not exist or is embedded in broader IT operations reports without dedicated visibility, indicating that the data centre risk profile is not being actively governed at a senior level
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira, Remedy) — Infrastructure or Facilities incident category exports
DCIM or BMS (Building Management System) event history for environmental exceedance records
Data Centre Operations team for manually logged or shift-report based fault records
GRC platform for PIR and corrective action tracking linked to data centre events
Management reporting portal or Board pack archives for quarterly/annual trend summaries
evidenceItems.format: System export/PDF (incident records), PDF/DOCX (PIRs and RCAs), PDF (management trend reports)

auditTips: Before examination, physically walk the data centre floor with the architecture diagrams in hand and verify that every redundant component shown on the diagrams is physically present, correctly labelled, and in active/standby status — BNM examiners may request an accompanied site walk and will cross-check diagrams against physical reality on the spot.

Compile a single 'evidence pack' indexed to each obligation in the verbatim clause text (dedicated space, physical security, non-disaster-prone location, no SPOF, maintenance, continuous monitoring) so that you can produce specific evidence against each obligation within minutes of the examiner's request, demonstrating preparedness rather than reactive searching.

Run a mock SPOF challenge internally before the examination — have your infrastructure team attempt to identify single points of failure that are not covered by your documented redundancy, and document the findings and remediation; presenting this self-assessment to examiners demonstrates proactive governance maturity that is explicitly valued by BNM.

Cross-reference the access control log exports against the HR offboarding records for the past 12 months and confirm that no terminated employee retained physical access beyond the policy SLA — prepare a summary attestation signed by HR and IT Security confirming this reconciliation was completed, as this is a common examiner probe point.

Validate that alert threshold configurations in your DCIM or BMS are formally documented and approved — extract the current threshold settings from the system and have them signed off by the Data Centre Engineering lead and a senior risk owner; undocumented or default thresholds are a reliable examiner finding that is straightforward to remediate before examination.

For co-located or third-party hosted data centre arrangements, obtain and review the provider's most recent independent audit report (e.g., SSAE 18 SOC 2 Type II, ISO/IEC 27001 certification, or TIA-942 certification) and document formally how the institution has assessed and accepted residual risks not covered by the provider's controls — BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the institution has exercised genuine oversight of third-party DC providers rather than wholesale reliance.

Ensure that the most recent generator load bank test and UPS switchover test results are available and show test conditions that simulate actual production load — examiners will probe whether tests were conducted at partial load (a common gap) and whether results demonstrate sufficient runtime and transfer performance to meet the institution's documented RTO for the data centre.

10.27.title: Data Centre Operations Control Procedures
10.27.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has formalised, end-to-end control procedures that govern all critical data centre activities — not merely that such procedures exist on paper, but that they are actively followed and evidenced. Examiners will specifically probe the adequacy of automated batch processing controls, looking for scheduler tools, job completion logs, and escalation mechanisms that demonstrate timely and accurate execution without manual dependency. A primary red flag is the absence of an integrated change management process for production systems, particularly where emergency changes bypass approval gates. Examiners also scrutinise error-handling workflows to verify that exceptional conditions (e.g., batch failures, hardware alerts, capacity breaches) trigger documented, auditable responses rather than ad hoc remediation. The 'S' marker signals that non-compliance in any of these areas — absent automation, undocumented exceptions, or informal change approvals — will be treated as a supervisory concern requiring prompt remediation.

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Operations Control Procedures Policy
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy or standard operating procedure (SOP) document that formally establishes control procedures for data centre operations. This document demonstrates the institution's overarching governance framework for data centre management and its alignment with RMiT obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly covers all four control domains mandated by 10.27: batch processing, production change management, error handling, and exceptional conditions management, with a dedicated section for each
Document is version-controlled, Board or Senior Management-approved, and reviewed at least annually, with a clear revision history and approval signatures visible
Scope clearly identifies all in-scope data centres (primary, DR, co-location) and references all critical batch-dependent systems by name or classification
Policy cross-references relevant downstream procedures (e.g., Change Management SOP, Incident Response Playbook) with document IDs, creating a traceable governance chain
Compliance obligations are explicitly mapped to RMiT clause 10.27 within the policy header or appendix, demonstrating regulatory awareness
commonGaps: Policy exists as a high-level document but lacks operational specificity — batch processing, change management, and error handling are mentioned but not defined as distinct control procedures with step-by-step guidance
Approval and review timestamps are outdated (more than 12 months since last review), indicating the document is not actively maintained to reflect current operations
DR and co-location sites are excluded from scope, leaving a material gap where the same control obligations apply but are not addressed
Policy does not define accountability — roles and responsibilities (e.g., Data Centre Manager, Operations Lead, Change Advisory Board) are absent or generic
Document is stored informally (email attachment, shared drive) without version control, making it impossible to confirm that staff are working from the current approved version
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Rsam) — Policy Management module
SharePoint or Confluence — Technology Operations policy repository
IT Governance team or Data Centre Operations Manager
Internal Audit's document register for last policy review findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata; accessible via GRC or document management platform

evidenceItems.name: Batch Processing Automation Tool Configuration and Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation and configuration records for the automated batch scheduling and management tools deployed by the institution. This evidence demonstrates that the institution has implemented relevant automated tools as required by Clause 10.27 and that these tools are properly configured to support timely and accurate batch execution.
whatGoodLooksLike: An authoritative inventory listing all batch scheduling tools in use (e.g., IBM Workload Scheduler, CA7, Control-M, ActiveBatch, Stonebranch) with tool name, version, owner, and scope of coverage per system
Configuration documentation shows job dependencies, SLA-based scheduling windows, retry logic, and automated alerting thresholds are defined for all critical batch jobs
Evidence of automated alerting integration (e.g., PagerDuty, ServiceNow) whereby batch job failures or SLA breaches trigger immediate notification to operations staff without manual monitoring
Tool access control records demonstrate that only authorised personnel can create, modify, or disable batch jobs, with segregation of duties enforced between scheduling and approval roles
Annual or periodic review of the batch job inventory confirms active jobs are validated against business requirements and obsolete or orphaned jobs have been decommissioned
commonGaps: Critical batch jobs continue to be managed manually via scripts or cron jobs without a centralised scheduler, exposing the institution to human error and SLA breaches that an automated tool would prevent
Automated alerting is configured but only notifies via email, with no escalation path — if the primary contact is unavailable, batch failures go undetected until business users report discrepancies
Batch job inventory is incomplete or not maintained; discovery during audit reveals undocumented jobs running in production that are not captured in any register or subject to change control
Retry logic and timeout thresholds are not defined for critical jobs, meaning a stalled job can hold resources indefinitely without triggering an exception condition
Tool configuration changes (e.g., modifying job schedules or dependencies) are not subject to formal change management, allowing ad hoc adjustments that bypass approval controls
suggestedSources: Batch scheduling platform (Control-M, IBM WS, CA7) — job catalogue and configuration exports
IT Operations team or Batch Operations Centre
Middleware or Platform Engineering team responsible for scheduler administration
CMDB (Configuration Management Database) for tool version and ownership records
evidenceItems.format: System-generated configuration export (PDF/CSV); supplemented by DOCX inventory register

evidenceItems.name: Batch Job Execution Logs and SLA Compliance Reports
evidenceItems.description: Operational logs and performance reports generated by the batch scheduling system, demonstrating that batch processes are completed timely and accurately in practice. This evidence validates that the automated tools are functioning as intended and that control procedures produce measurable, auditable outcomes.
whatGoodLooksLike: System-generated daily or monthly batch execution reports covering all critical jobs, showing job name, scheduled start time, actual start time, completion time, exit status, and SLA adherence rate

SLA breach and exception reports are automatically produced and distributed to operations management, with a defined threshold (e.g., 99% on-time completion) and trend data over a rolling 12-month period
Logs demonstrate that failed jobs triggered automated alerts and were escalated per documented procedures, with timestamps confirming response times met defined SLOs
Evidence of reconciliation between batch outputs and downstream systems (e.g., GL postings, settlement files, report generation) confirming accuracy of batch results, not merely completion
Logs are tamper-evident and retained in a system or archive where access is controlled and audit trails record any log access or modification attempts
commonGaps: Logs are available but are only reviewed reactively when a business user reports a problem — there is no proactive daily review process or automated SLA reporting to confirm batch health
Exit status codes in logs are not interpreted against a standard; a job marked 'completed' may have completed with warnings or partial data, and this distinction is not captured in reporting
Log retention is shorter than 7 years, or logs are stored on the production batch server itself without offloading to a secure archive, creating gaps in the historical audit trail
There is no reconciliation step to confirm batch accuracy — the institution can evidence timely completion but cannot demonstrate that outputs are correct and complete
SLA metrics are tracked for only a subset of batch jobs, typically the highest-profile ones, leaving lower-visibility jobs (which may still be critical) outside the monitoring framework
suggestedSources: Batch scheduling platform — execution history and SLA dashboards
SIEM platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk) for centralised log aggregation
IT Operations monitoring dashboard (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, Zabbix)
Operations Centre shift reports and daily batch status emails
evidenceItems.format: System-generated log export (CSV/PDF); dashboard screenshots with timestamp; monthly SLA report (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Production Change Management Procedure and Change Records
evidenceItems.description: The formal change management procedure governing all changes to production systems within the data centre, together with a representative sample of approved change records. This evidence demonstrates that the institution has implemented structured controls to ensure production changes are assessed, authorised, tested, and implemented in a controlled manner.
whatGoodLooksLike: Change Management SOP clearly distinguishes between standard, normal, and emergency change types, with separate approval workflows, risk assessment templates, and rollback requirements for each category

Change records (minimum 6-month sample) consistently show completion of all mandatory fields: change requestor, technical owner, risk rating, test evidence, Change Advisory Board (CAB) approval, implementation date, and post-implementation review outcome
Emergency change records exist but are demonstrably rare, and each is followed by a mandatory retrospective review and CAB ratification within a defined timeframe (e.g., 24–48 hours)

Segregation of duties is enforced — the same individual cannot raise, approve, and implement a change, and the ITSM tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) enforces this through workflow controls
Change success and rollback rates are reported to management on a regular cadence (monthly/quarterly), demonstrating oversight of change quality and trend analysis
commonGaps: Emergency change process is poorly controlled — changes are labelled 'emergency' to bypass the standard CAB approval process without meeting defined emergency criteria, inflating the emergency change rate

Post-implementation reviews (PIRs) are not consistently completed; approved changes are closed in the ITSM tool immediately after implementation without documented confirmation that the change achieved its objective

The change management process applies to application changes but does not cover infrastructure-level changes (network, storage, hypervisor), creating a blind spot for a significant category of production modifications

Rollback plans are present on change records but are templated and generic — they do not contain system-specific steps, meaning the rollback plan would not be executable during an actual rollback scenario
ITSM tool records show changes implemented outside of approved change windows without a documented deviation or emergency change designation, indicating informal production access is occurring
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy) — Change Management module
CAB meeting minutes and approval records
IT Change Manager or Release Management team
CMDB — linked configuration item change history
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) for change records; DOCX/PDF for the change management SOP; CAB minutes in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Error Handling Procedures and Error Resolution Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures for identifying, logging, escalating, and resolving errors arising from data centre operations, accompanied by operational records demonstrating that these procedures are applied consistently. This evidence confirms that the institution has a structured, repeatable approach to error management rather than relying on informal or ad hoc responses.
whatGoodLooksLike: Error handling procedures define a clear taxonomy of error types (batch errors, system alerts, hardware faults, data integrity errors) with specific response actions, escalation contacts, and resolution timeframes for each category

Error logs and incident tickets show a consistent pattern of timely detection, logging, escalation, and resolution that matches the documented procedure, with timestamps at each stage confirming adherence
Root cause analysis (RCA) is completed for all high-severity errors within a defined timeframe, and corrective actions are tracked to closure through the ITSM or GRC platform
Error trends are reported to senior management or an operations committee on a regular basis, demonstrating that error data informs process improvement rather than being filed and forgotten

Procedures include explicit guidance on data integrity verification following a batch error — confirming that partial or failed batch runs are identified, quarantined, and remediated before downstream systems consume the output
commonGaps: Error handling is described at a high level in the policy but there are no operational procedures with step-by-step guidance — staff rely on tribal knowledge, meaning error response varies significantly between individuals and shifts
Errors are logged in the ITSM system but RCA is only performed for incidents that caused visible business impact; recurring low-severity errors that indicate a systemic weakness are not analysed
Escalation contacts in the error handling procedure are outdated — staff listed have changed roles or left the institution, meaning the escalation chain is non-functional in practice

There is no documented procedure for handling data integrity errors specifically — the institution can demonstrate it responds to batch job failures but cannot evidence how it detects and remediates cases where a batch completed successfully but produced incorrect output

Error records lack sufficient detail to support audit — tickets are closed with resolution noted as 'resolved' without documenting the root cause, corrective action taken, or evidence that the fix was effective
suggestedSources: ITSM platform — Incident and Problem Management records
Batch scheduling platform — error and exception logs
IT Operations team — shift handover logs and error registers
GRC platform — corrective action tracking register
evidenceItems.format: ITSM export (PDF/CSV); DOCX SOP for error handling procedures; RCA reports in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Exceptional Conditions Management Procedure and Exception Register
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures and operational records governing the identification, management, and resolution of exceptional conditions beyond standard errors — including but not limited to capacity threshold breaches, environmental alerts (power, cooling, humidity), unplanned outages, and hardware failures. This evidence demonstrates the institution's preparedness and structured response to abnormal operating conditions in the data centre.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exceptional conditions management procedure explicitly defines what constitutes an exceptional condition, with examples spanning technical (storage capacity, CPU saturation, network degradation), environmental (UPS failure, cooling alarm), and operational (staff unavailability, vendor non-response) categories

An exception register or operations log captures all exceptional conditions encountered, including date, nature of condition, systems affected, response taken, escalation path, and resolution — demonstrating the procedure is operationally active

Procedures define clear thresholds that trigger exceptional condition status (e.g., storage utilisation exceeding 85%, temperature exceeding defined limits) and are linked to monitoring tools that generate automated alerts at those thresholds

Evidence shows that exceptional conditions are reported to senior management and, where applicable, to the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body, confirming appropriate oversight of material operational risks
Post-incident reviews for exceptional conditions result in documented lessons learned, and these feed back into updated procedures, demonstrating a continuous improvement cycle
commonGaps: The institution conflates exceptional conditions with standard incidents — there is no distinct classification or procedure for exceptional conditions, meaning high-impact or unusual operational scenarios are handled under a generic incident process not calibrated for their severity
Environmental monitoring alerts are generated but there is no documented procedure for what operations staff must do when an alert fires — response actions are informal and undocumented

Exception register is either absent or maintained only on an ad hoc basis; auditors find that significant exceptional conditions (e.g., a cooling failure, a UPS switchover) are not logged in any accessible register

Thresholds for exceptional condition triggers are not defined or are set at inappropriate levels (e.g., storage alerts at 95% utilisation, leaving insufficient headroom to respond before a critical failure)

Management reporting of exceptional conditions is inconsistent — some events are escalated appropriately while others of similar severity are managed at the operational level without senior management visibility, indicating the reporting threshold is not defined or is not applied consistently
suggestedSources: Data Centre Infrastructure Management (DCIM) platform — environmental and capacity alerts
IT Operations — exception register, shift logs, and NOC (Network Operations Centre) records
ITSM platform — major incident and problem records linked to exceptional conditions
Facilities Management team — environmental monitoring system (BMS) reports
GRC platform — operational risk event register
evidenceItems.format: DOCX/PDF for procedures; Spreadsheet or GRC system export for exception register; DCIM/BMS system reports in PDF

auditTips: Perform a pre-audit walkthrough of the batch scheduling platform with the operations team to confirm that all critical batch jobs are registered in the scheduler — cross-reference the scheduler's job inventory against the institution's application inventory to identify any critical system whose batch jobs are managed outside the authorised tool, as undocumented or manually-run batch jobs are a common examiner finding under this clause

Request a full 12-month extract of batch job SLA performance data before the examination and calculate the on-time completion rate per job and per system; proactively identify any jobs with recurring SLA breaches and prepare documented root cause analysis and corrective action evidence for each, as examiners will ask about outliers in the data

Conduct a targeted review of emergency change records over the past 12 months, calculating the ratio of emergency changes to total changes — a ratio above 10-15% is a red flag that examiners will probe; for each emergency change, verify that a CAB retrospective review was completed and documented within the required timeframe, and prepare a narrative explaining the business context for any spikes in emergency change activity

Test the error handling procedure end-to-end by tracing three to five recent batch errors from initial detection in the monitoring tool through to closure in the ITSM system, verifying that timestamps, escalation steps, and resolution documentation match the documented procedure at every stage; gaps in this trace are exactly what examiners look for when assessing whether procedures are operationally embedded versus existing only on paper

Map all four control domains in Clause 10.27 — batch processing automation, production change management, error handling, and exceptional conditions — to specific policy documents, SOPs, and operational tools, and present this mapping as a clause compliance matrix at the start of the examination; this demonstrates structured preparation and reduces the risk of examiners identifying areas not covered by existing documentation

Verify that the exception register and operations logs covering exceptional conditions are maintained by the Data Centre Operations team and are accessible with complete entries for at least the past 12 months; if the register is sparse or has gaps, conduct a retrospective exercise using ITSM records, DCIM alerts, and shift handover logs to reconstruct exceptional condition events and document them before the examination

Cross-reference the change management procedure and ITSM change records against the batch scheduling platform to confirm that changes to batch job schedules, dependencies, and configurations are subject to formal change control — examiners frequently find that batch scheduler configuration is treated as an operational activity outside the change management process, which constitutes a control gap directly relevant to this clause

10.28.title: Data Centre Activity Segregation
10.28.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised — not merely documented — the principle of segregating incompatible activities within the data centre environment, spanning both logical and physical dimensions. Red flags include the absence of a formal incompatible activities matrix, developers or vendors holding standing production access without time-bound approval, and monitoring logs that exist but are never reviewed or escalated. Examiners will request evidence that authorisation workflows for vendor and programmer production access are formally approved by accountable owners (not self-approved), and that monitoring is active and generates alerts rather than being a passive audit trail consulted only after incidents. Cross-referencing privileged access reviews against change management records is a common examiner technique to detect undisclosed production access that bypassed the formal authorisation gate.

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Activity Segregation Policy
evidenceItems.description: The formal policy document defining which data centre activities are deemed incompatible, the rationale for their segregation, and the governance framework governing exceptions — including when vendor or programmer access to the production environment is permissible.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly enumerates incompatible activity pairs (e.g., development vs. production operations, change implementation vs. change approval, physical access vs. logical superuser access) with clear rationale referencing operational risk principles

Policy contains a dedicated section on production access by vendors and programmers, specifying mandatory conditions (change ticket linkage, dual authorisation, time-bound access windows) before access is granted
Document is version-controlled with a minimum annual review cycle evidenced by dated Board IT Committee or MANCO approval signatures
Policy maps to and cross-references the institution's Segregation of Duties (SoD) framework and BNM RMiT obligations explicitly
Exception handling procedures with escalation paths and compensating controls are documented within the policy body, not in a separate ad-hoc memo
commonGaps: Policy exists at a generic level and does not enumerate specific incompatible activity pairs relevant to the institution's data centre topology, rendering it unverifiable during examination
Vendor and programmer production access is addressed in a separate IT security standard that is not referenced or reconciled with the main policy, creating governance gaps
Last review date is older than 12 months, or the approval authority is below the required governance tier (e.g., signed off by IT Manager rather than CIO or Board committee)
Policy covers logical access segregation but omits physical access controls within the data centre, leaving physical-logical crossover scenarios ungoverned
Exception process exists in theory but has no documented instances of approvals or denials, suggesting the process is not operationally active
suggestedSources: GRC platform policy repository (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or equivalent)
SharePoint / intranet document management system under IT Governance or Technology Risk function
Board IT Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes and resolution records
ISO 27001 / internal audit document register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: Incompatible Activities and Segregation of Duties Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured matrix mapping data centre roles, functions, and system access privileges to identify and formally document which combinations of duties are incompatible and how conflicts are controlled or mitigated, including detective controls where preventive segregation is not technically feasible.
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix covers all data centre operational roles (production operations, change management, development, vendor support, security administration) and maps each to specific system privileges and physical access zones, with conflicts clearly flagged

Each identified conflict is associated with a documented control — either preventive (technical enforcement via IAM rules) or detective (compensating control such as enhanced monitoring or dual-person integrity) — and the control owner is named
Matrix is reconciled against the institution's identity and access management (IAM) system at least quarterly, with dated reconciliation records demonstrating active maintenance
Roles occupied by vendors or third-party programmers are explicitly included and flagged with additional access conditions (time-limited, supervised, change-ticket-linked)
Matrix review is formally signed off by the data centre operations owner and the CISO or equivalent risk authority at each scheduled review cycle
commonGaps: Matrix covers application-layer SoD but excludes infrastructure and data centre operations roles, meaning the document does not address the specific scope of clause 10.28
Conflicts are identified but the associated compensating controls are vague (e.g., 'monitoring') without specifying the monitoring mechanism, frequency, or escalation path
Matrix has not been updated following organisational restructuring, system migrations, or new vendor onboardings, resulting in stale role definitions that no longer reflect actual access assignments
Vendor and contractor roles are excluded from the matrix on the assumption that they are governed by separate vendor management processes, creating a gap that examiners will specifically probe
No evidence that identified conflicts with compensating controls have been tested or validated — the matrix represents design intent but not operational effectiveness
suggestedSources: IAM / PAM system role and privilege reports (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust, SailPoint IIQ)
GRC platform SoD module
Technology Risk or Internal Audit maintained spreadsheets cross-referenced with HR role assignments
Data centre operations team organisational chart and RACI matrix
