evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC system export, with version control and approval audit trail

evidenceItems.name: Vendor and Programmer Production Access Authorisation Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal approval records for each instance where a vendor, third-party programmer, or internal developer was granted access to the production data centre environment, demonstrating that such access was explicitly authorised by accountable owners prior to access being provisioned, with defined scope and time limits.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each access request record contains: requestor identity, business justification, specific systems and access scope requested, duration of access, approver identity (minimum two-level authorisation including a business owner and IT security), and the associated change or incident ticket number

Approval workflow is enforced through a ticketing or PAM system (not email alone) that creates an immutable, timestamped audit trail from request submission through approval to access provisioning and revocation
Access is demonstrably time-bounded — system-generated evidence shows that accounts were automatically expired or revoked at the agreed time without requiring manual intervention
Records include a post-access review step confirming the scope of activity performed matched the authorised scope, signed off by the supervising internal owner
Vendor access for recurring maintenance activities is governed by a standing authorisation framework with defined conditions rather than ad-hoc approvals, with periodic revalidation at least annually
commonGaps: Approvals are captured in email threads rather than a formal workflow system, making it impossible to demonstrate the approval preceded access provisioning or that revocation was timely

Access scope is defined broadly (e.g., 'server administration') rather than specifying the minimum necessary privileges, making it impossible to assess whether the principle of least privilege was applied

Records exist for initial access grants but there is no corresponding revocation record, leaving examiners unable to confirm whether access was actually removed after the authorised activity concluded
Authorisations are approved by the vendor's own project manager or an IT operations staff member without involvement of the accountable system or data owner, constituting a self-approval conflict
Standing or recurring vendor access has not been formally revalidated within the past 12 months, meaning access may persist beyond the original business justification
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change and service request modules (e.g., ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management)
PAM system access request and approval logs (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust)
Vendor Management Office records and third-party access registers
IT Security access provisioning team records
evidenceItems.format: System-generated PDF export or ITSM/PAM report with timestamps and approver details

evidenceItems.name: Production Environment Access Monitoring Logs and Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that vendor, programmer, and privileged user activity within the production data centre environment is actively monitored in real time or near-real time, and that logs are regularly reviewed with findings documented, escalated where necessary, and retained for audit purposes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Session recordings or detailed command-level logs exist for all privileged and vendor access sessions to production systems, with logs stored in a tamper-evident repository outside the control of the users being monitored

A defined log review procedure is in place specifying review frequency (at minimum daily for privileged accounts), the reviewer's role, alert thresholds that trigger escalation, and the escalation path to IT Security or CISO
Evidence of actual reviews is documented — dated review sign-offs, alert disposition records, or GRC/SIEM dashboard exports showing review activity — not merely the existence of logs
Anomaly or policy violation alerts generated during vendor or programmer sessions are tracked through to resolution with documented investigative notes and outcomes

Monitoring coverage is validated periodically (e.g., quarterly) to confirm that all production access paths — including out-of-band management interfaces, VPN tunnels, and jump servers — are within scope and generating logs
commonGaps: Logs are collected and retained but there is no documented evidence of periodic review — examiners will treat an unreviewed log as equivalent to no monitoring for compliance purposes

Monitoring covers standard server access but excludes database administrator sessions, network device management interfaces, or hypervisor-level access, leaving significant production access vectors unmonitored
Alert thresholds and escalation procedures are not documented, meaning monitoring is reactive (consulted post-incident) rather than proactive detection of unauthorised activity
Logs are stored in systems accessible to the very administrators and vendors being monitored, undermining the integrity of the monitoring record

Session recording for vendor remote access exists but recordings are not reviewed unless an incident is reported, meaning the monitoring control is effectively post-hoc rather than preventive or timely-detective
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) log exports and alert records
PAM platform session recording repository (e.g., CyberArk Privileged Session Manager, BeyondTrust)
Security Operations Centre (SOC) daily review logs and incident records
Network and firewall access logs for production environment perimeters
Jump server or bastion host activity logs
evidenceItems.format: System-generated log exports (CSV/PDF), SIEM dashboard reports, and manual review sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Data Centre Physical Access Control Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that physical access to the data centre is controlled and segregated to prevent incompatible activities — for example, ensuring that developers and vendors do not have unescorted physical access to production hardware, and that physical access is logged and periodically reviewed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Physical access control system (PACS) records show differentiated access zones within the data centre (e.g., colocation cages, production racks, network equipment areas) with access rights assigned based on role and reviewed at least quarterly
Vendor or contractor physical visits are logged with entry and exit times, escorted by an authorised staff member, and linked to a prior access authorisation (work order or change ticket)
Quarterly or semi-annual access reviews demonstrate that physical access rights are recertified by the data centre operations manager and that stale or inappropriate access is promptly revoked
Visitor logs are reconciled against PACS badge records to detect tailgating or unescorted entry, with any discrepancies documented and investigated

Physical access rights for individuals who have changed roles or left the organisation are evidenced as having been revoked within the institution's defined SLA (typically within 24 hours of role change notification)
commonGaps: Physical access logs exist but are not linked to authorisation records, making it impossible to confirm that each physical visit was pre-approved rather than ad-hoc
Vendor personnel hold permanent physical access badges that are not time-limited or deactivated between scheduled visits, contradicting the principle of minimum necessary access
Physical access reviews are conducted annually rather than quarterly, and review records are generic sign-offs without evidence that individual access rights were examined

Production and non-production hardware coexists in the same physical zone without rack-level or cage-level segregation, undermining the intent of the clause even where logical access controls are in place
Physical visitor logs are maintained on paper and not reconciled with electronic PACS records, creating an easily manipulated record that does not satisfy BNM's integrity requirements
suggestedSources: Physical access control system reports (e.g., Lenel, Genetec, HID Global platform exports)
Data centre facilities management team visitor logs
IT Asset Management records for rack and zone assignment
HR offboarding checklist and physical access revocation confirmation records
Third-party data centre provider access reports (for co-location arrangements)
evidenceItems.format: System-generated PACS reports (PDF/CSV), manual visitor log (scanned or digitised), quarterly review sign-off records

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Compliance Review and Exception Reporting
evidenceItems.description: Records of formal periodic reviews assessing whether activity segregation controls in the data centre remain effective and consistently applied, including documentation of exceptions identified, risk decisions made, and remediation actions tracked to closure.
whatGoodLooksLike: A scheduled review cycle (at minimum semi-annual) is defined and evidenced, with each review producing a documented report signed by the data centre operations owner and reviewed by IT Risk or Internal Audit

Review reports include a reconciliation of actual access assignments against the authorised SoD matrix, with any deviations classified by risk severity and assigned to an accountable owner for remediation

Exceptions to the segregation policy (where incompatible activities could not be separated) are formally risk-accepted by the appropriate authority, documented with compensating controls and a defined review date, and tracked in the GRC platform
Trend data across review cycles is maintained, enabling examiners to assess whether the institution is reducing its exception count over time or whether systemic control weaknesses persist
Internal audit or the second line of defence independently validates a sample of access records against authorisation logs as part of the review, providing assurance beyond self-attestation
commonGaps: Reviews are conducted informally and findings are communicated verbally or via email without producing a formal report, leaving no auditable trail of the review's scope, findings, or conclusions

Exceptions are identified but not formally risk-accepted — they persist without documentation, making it impossible for examiners to distinguish known-and-managed exceptions from undetected control failures
Remediation actions from prior review cycles are not tracked to closure, with the same gaps recurring across multiple review periods without management accountability
Reviews are self-assessed by the data centre operations team without independent validation from IT Risk, Internal Audit, or the second line of defence, undermining the objectivity of findings

Review scope is limited to logical access controls and does not encompass physical access, monitoring effectiveness, or vendor authorisation compliance, resulting in partial assurance that does not satisfy the full scope of clause 10.28
suggestedSources: GRC platform compliance review module and exception register (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream)
Internal Audit reports and management action tracking system
Technology Risk Committee meeting papers and minutes
IT Risk function periodic risk reporting dashboard
External audit or BNM examination management letter and remediation tracking records
evidenceItems.format: PDF report with executive summary and detailed findings, GRC platform exception register export, management action plan tracker

auditTips: Before the examination, run a reconciliation exercise between your PAM or IAM system's current privilege assignments and your SoD matrix to identify any undisclosed conflicts — BNM examiners routinely cross-reference live access reports against the matrix and will flag discrepancies as evidence that controls are design-only rather than operationally enforced.

Pull the last 12 months of vendor and programmer production access authorisation records and verify that every access instance has a corresponding change or incident ticket number — any access record without a linked ticket suggests access occurred outside the formal authorisation process and will be treated as a potential unauthorised activity finding.

Demonstrate active monitoring by preparing a sample of SIEM or PAM alerts generated during the examination period, together with documented dispositions showing how each alert was investigated and closed — a log that exists but has no review records is treated by BNM as equivalent to no monitoring control.

Reconcile physical access control system records against logical privileged access logs for the same time period to confirm that physical presence in the data centre correlates with authorised logical sessions — discrepancies (physical access with no corresponding authorised session, or vice versa) are a common examination finding under this clause.

For each standing vendor access arrangement, prepare a one-page summary showing: the original business justification, the approval authority, the last revalidation date, the monitoring controls applied, and the revocation process — examiners expect standing access to be actively governed, not simply inherited from an initial approval.

Verify that your log retention architecture ensures that monitoring records are stored outside the administrative control of the users being monitored — if production system administrators or vendors can delete or modify their own access logs, BNM will assess the monitoring control as ineffective regardless of the volume of logs retained.

Cross-reference your exception register against the current SoD matrix and physical access records to ensure every known conflict is formally documented with a risk-accepted compensating control — an unregistered exception discovered during examination is a significantly worse finding than a registered exception with documented management awareness and compensating controls.

10.29.title: System Capacity Planning
10.29.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a formal, forward-looking capacity planning process that is actively integrated with business and technology strategy — not merely reactive monitoring. Examiners will look for evidence that peak processing periods (e.g., salary cycles, festive seasons, tax deadlines, regulatory reporting windows) are explicitly identified and planned for, and that capacity plans are revised in response to business growth projections and architectural changes such as cloud migrations or system consolidations. Red flags include capacity plans that are static documents updated only after incidents, absence of linkage between IT capacity data and business forecasts, and no documented review triggered by major architecture changes. Examiners will cross-reference capacity reports against incident logs to determine whether capacity shortfalls preceded outages, and will probe whether the Board or senior management receives meaningful capacity metrics.

evidenceItems.name: Capacity Planning Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A Board- or senior management-approved policy document that establishes the institution's methodology, roles, responsibilities, review frequency, and escalation procedures for system capacity planning across all critical technology assets.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references peak processing periods, business growth integration, and technology architecture changes as mandatory planning inputs
Defined roles and responsibilities assign capacity ownership to named positions (e.g., Head of IT Infrastructure, CTO) with clear accountability
Review and approval cadence is documented (at minimum annually, plus triggered reviews upon material architecture or business changes)
Scope covers all critical systems including core banking, payment rails, internet banking, and third-party hosted platforms
Policy references thresholds for escalation to senior management or the Board when capacity risks are identified
commonGaps: Policy exists but lacks explicit mention of peak period identification, making planning ad hoc and undocumented
Scope excludes outsourced or cloud-hosted systems, creating blind spots for critical services
Review triggers for architecture changes are absent, so capacity plans become stale after major infrastructure upgrades or migrations
Roles are assigned to generic team names rather than specific positions, diffusing accountability
Policy has not been updated to reflect current technology stack (e.g., still references legacy on-premise infrastructure after cloud adoption)
suggestedSources: IT Governance SharePoint or intranet policy repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) policy module
Board IT Committee or Risk Committee meeting minutes approving the policy
IT Operations document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: System Capacity Assessment Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports documenting current utilisation levels, headroom, trend analysis, and forward projections for critical infrastructure components including compute, storage, network bandwidth, database capacity, and application transaction throughput.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports cover all Tier-1 and Tier-2 systems with utilisation metrics expressed as percentage of defined thresholds, not raw numbers only
Trend analysis spans at least 12 months of historical data with projected growth curves tied to specific business assumptions
Each system has a documented capacity threshold (warning and critical) with evidence of management review when thresholds are breached
Reports are produced at a defined frequency (monthly or quarterly) with no unexplained gaps in the reporting cycle
Findings and risks identified in the report are tracked to closure with assigned owners and target dates
commonGaps: Reports cover infrastructure layers but omit application-level metrics such as transaction per second limits or database connection pool saturation
Utilisation data is presented without trend lines or projections, making it impossible to determine when capacity will be exhausted
Reports are produced but there is no documented evidence of management review, sign-off, or action taken
Frequency of reporting is irregular, with gaps during peak periods when capacity pressure is highest
Cloud resource capacity is tracked by the vendor but not consolidated into the institution's own reporting, creating governance gaps
suggestedSources: IT monitoring platforms (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, Zabbix, PRTG, SolarWinds)
ITSM reporting module (e.g., ServiceNow Performance Analytics)
Cloud provider cost and usage dashboards (AWS Cost Explorer, Azure Monitor)
Database administration team monthly reports
Network operations centre (NOC) capacity dashboards
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF dashboard export with timestamped data and management sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Peak Period Capacity Planning Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution proactively identifies known and anticipated peak processing periods and implements pre-emptive capacity measures, stress tests, or resource provisioning plans prior to those periods.
whatGoodLooksLike: An annual peak period calendar is maintained, covering events such as payroll cycles, Aidilfitri and Chinese New Year transaction surges, tax filing deadlines, year-end reporting, and regulatory submission windows
For each identified peak, there is a documented capacity review showing that headroom above projected peak load was confirmed or provisioned in advance
Stress test or load test results are available for critical systems prior to anticipated peak periods, with pass/fail determinations and remediation records
Pre-peak checklists or runbooks are maintained and executed, with sign-off confirming readiness
Post-peak reviews compare actual load against projections and feed back into the following year's planning cycle
commonGaps: Peak periods are informally understood by operations staff but not formally documented or tracked, leaving planning dependent on institutional memory
Stress or load testing is performed annually but not aligned to the specific timing of identified peak periods
Capacity is provisioned reactively during peak periods (e.g., emergency cloud scaling) rather than planned in advance with documented rationale
Post-peak review findings are not formally recorded, so lessons learned do not improve subsequent planning cycles
Planning focuses on internal transaction peaks but neglects regulatory reporting windows that simultaneously strain reporting and core infrastructure
suggestedSources: Business operations calendar maintained by Operations or Finance division
IT project and change management system (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow Change) for pre-peak provisioning tasks
Performance and load testing platform results (e.g., Apache JMeter, Gatling, k6)
NOC or IT operations pre-peak readiness review meeting minutes
Payment operations team peak planning memos
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX planning memo plus spreadsheet tracking matrix and test result exports

evidenceItems.name: Business Growth Forecast Integration Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that IT capacity planning inputs are formally linked to business growth projections from the business lines, ensuring infrastructure can support projected customer growth, transaction volume increases, and new product launches.
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented process exists for IT capacity planners to receive business volume forecasts from Finance or business divisions at least annually as part of the budgeting or strategic planning cycle
Capacity plans show a clear translation of business growth assumptions (e.g., projected 20% increase in active users) into specific infrastructure resource requirements
New product or service launches have a documented capacity impact assessment completed by IT prior to launch approval
Minutes or records from cross-functional planning meetings (IT and business) confirm that capacity discussions occurred and were actioned
Budget allocations for capacity expansion are traceable to specific business growth assumptions documented in the forecast
commonGaps: IT capacity planning and business planning occur in separate silos with no documented mechanism for sharing forecasts, creating misalignment
Business growth assumptions used in capacity plans cannot be traced to any formal business forecast, suggesting figures were estimated informally
New product launches proceed to production without a documented IT capacity impact assessment, discovered only after performance degradation
Capacity expansion budgets are based on prior year actuals with a fixed percentage uplift rather than business-driven projections
Integration is one-directional — IT receives volumes but provides no feedback to business on capacity constraints that may limit growth plans
suggestedSources: Annual IT budget and planning submissions linked to business strategic plan
Finance division business volume forecast documents
New product approval committee papers with IT capacity sign-off section
IT Steering Committee or ITGC meeting minutes referencing capacity and growth alignment
Enterprise architecture team demand management register
evidenceItems.format: DOCX/PDF planning documents and meeting minutes with cross-reference to business plan version

evidenceItems.name: Technology Architecture Change Capacity Impact Assessments
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that capacity planning is revisited and updated whenever significant changes to the technology architecture are planned or implemented, such as cloud migrations, system consolidations, decommissioning of platforms, or adoption of new middleware.
whatGoodLooksLike: Change management process formally mandates a capacity impact assessment as a mandatory gate for all Significant or Major change classifications before approval
Architecture change impact assessments include before-and-after capacity modelling, identifying any components that may become bottlenecks under the new architecture
Post-implementation reviews for major architecture changes include a capacity validation step confirming that actual performance matches pre-change projections
Cloud adoption or hybrid architecture changes include an assessment of shared-tenancy risk and cloud provider SLA alignment with the institution's capacity commitments
Capacity plans are formally updated and re-baselined within a defined period (e.g., 30 days) after major architecture changes are implemented
commonGaps: Change management process does not require a capacity assessment for infrastructure changes below a certain cost threshold, leaving mid-scale changes unreviewed
Architecture changes are assessed for security and availability but capacity modelling is not documented as a distinct step
Post-implementation reviews are conducted but do not include capacity validation, so degradation caused by architectural decisions goes undetected until incidents occur
Cloud migrations shift capacity accountability to vendor SLAs without a corresponding internal assessment of whether those SLAs meet the institution's peak period requirements
Capacity planning documents are not updated after architecture changes, creating a divergence between the documented baseline and actual infrastructure state
suggestedSources: Change Advisory Board (CAB) change records and impact assessment templates in ITSM platform
Enterprise architecture review board meeting minutes and decision logs
Project management office (PMO) post-implementation review reports
Cloud governance framework documentation
IT risk register entries related to architecture transitions
evidenceItems.format: ITSM change record exports (PDF) plus DOCX architecture assessment documents

evidenceItems.name: Capacity Threshold Breach and Escalation Log
evidenceItems.description: A log or register of instances where capacity thresholds were breached or at-risk, documenting the nature of the breach, root cause, business impact, escalation path, and remediation action, demonstrating that the institution's capacity management process is operationally active and responsive.
whatGoodLooksLike: Log is maintained continuously and captures all threshold breach events with timestamp, affected system, peak utilisation recorded, and duration
Each entry documents whether the breach was anticipated (planned peak) or unanticipated, with root cause analysis for unanticipated breaches
Escalation to appropriate management level is recorded for each breach, with evidence that senior management is notified for material or recurring issues
Remediation actions are tracked to closure with evidence (e.g., change record, procurement approval) that additional capacity was provisioned or demand managed
Trend analysis of breach frequency is reviewed quarterly to identify systemic capacity risks before they result in service degradation
commonGaps: Monitoring alerts are generated by tools but not consolidated into a managed log, making it impossible to demonstrate oversight or trend analysis
Breaches during peak periods are treated as expected and therefore not formally logged or reviewed, missing an opportunity to validate planning adequacy
Escalation to senior management is undocumented even when breaches were material, suggesting governance of capacity risk is informal
Remediation actions are implemented but not linked back to the breach log, making it impossible for auditors to confirm closure
Log covers compute and storage but omits network and application-layer capacity events, presenting an incomplete picture of capacity risk
suggestedSources: ITSM incident and problem management module (e.g., ServiceNow, Remedy) filtered for capacity-related incidents
IT monitoring platform alert history and threshold breach reports
IT Operations team capacity management register or tracker (SharePoint list or spreadsheet)
Senior management or IT Steering Committee escalation communications and meeting minutes
NOC shift handover logs referencing capacity events
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet register or ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) with management review sign-off

auditTips: Cross-reference the institution's capacity assessment reports (10.29-E2) against the incident management log for the same period — if capacity-related incidents occurred during gaps in threshold monitoring or after periods of sustained high utilisation, this is strong evidence that planning was inadequate rather than merely unlucky.

Request the most recent capacity plan and ask the institution to walk through exactly how business growth projections from the Finance or business division were translated into specific infrastructure sizing decisions — the inability to demonstrate this linkage clearly indicates a siloed planning process that does not satisfy the clause's business growth planning obligation.

Examine the change management register and select three to five major technology architecture changes from the past two years, then check whether each has a corresponding capacity impact assessment completed prior to implementation — missing assessments for even one Significant change are a material finding under 10.29.

Obtain the institution's peak period calendar and select two or three high-risk periods (e.g., the last Hari Raya cycle or December year-end), then request the pre-peak capacity review records and post-peak actual load reports for those periods — reconcile projected versus actual to assess planning accuracy and maturity.

Ask the institution to demonstrate how a capacity threshold alert in their monitoring platform triggers a documented escalation and response workflow — walk through a live example or a recent historical example to confirm that the governance process functions in practice and is not merely documented on paper.

Review Board IT Committee or Risk Committee papers for the past 12 months and confirm that capacity status, risks, and material breaches were reported to senior management — absence of capacity reporting at Board or senior management level is a governance gap that examiners treat as a finding regardless of the quality of operational-level controls.

Where capacity planning covers cloud or outsourced infrastructure, request the institution's assessment of vendor SLA alignment against internal peak period requirements — accept only documented assessments, not verbal assurances, and verify that vendor capacity commitments explicitly address the institution's peak load scenarios.

10.30.title: Real-Time Performance Monitoring
10.30.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised real-time monitoring — not merely deployed tools — with emphasis on three core obligations: (1) actual real-time visibility into capacity utilisation and performance of key processes and services, (2) alert mechanisms that are genuinely actionable, routed to responsible administrators, and tied to documented response procedures, and (3) evidence of periodic review ensuring monitoring scope, metrics, and thresholds remain calibrated to evolving risk. Red flags include monitoring dashboards that are deployed but unmonitored outside business hours, alert thresholds that have never been tuned since initial deployment, and monitoring coverage gaps for critical backend services or third-party-hosted systems. Examiners will cross-reference monitoring artefacts against incident records to test whether alerts actually preceded detection of past service interruptions.

evidenceItems.name: Real-Time Monitoring Architecture and Coverage Register
evidenceItems.description: A formal document defining the monitoring architecture, listing all key processes and services in scope, the monitoring tools deployed against each, and the metrics collected. Demonstrates that the institution has deliberately defined the scope of its monitoring coverage rather than relying on ad hoc tool deployment.
whatGoodLooksLike: All critical systems and services are explicitly enumerated with ownership assigned, and each is mapped to a monitoring tool with defined metric types (CPU, memory, latency, throughput, error rate, etc.)
The register distinguishes between real-time monitoring (sub-minute polling) and near-real-time or batch monitoring, with justification for any deviations from real-time for specific services
Third-party and cloud-hosted services are explicitly included in scope with documented monitoring arrangements (API integrations, vendor-provided dashboards, or contractual SLA reporting obligations)
The register was reviewed and approved by the CISO or Head of Technology Operations within the last 12 months and shows version history reflecting periodic updates
Coverage gaps are formally risk-accepted with compensating controls documented, rather than silently excluded from the register
commonGaps: Register lists infrastructure components (servers, network devices) but omits application-layer and business process metrics for key banking services such as payment processing, core banking transaction queues, or customer-facing portals
Third-party-hosted systems and cloud services are absent from the coverage register, creating blind spots for services the institution depends on but does not directly operate
The document is a one-time artefact with no version history or review date, indicating it has not been periodically updated as required by the clause
Monitoring tool coverage is described at a high level without specifying which specific metrics are collected for each service, making it impossible to assess whether coverage is meaningful
No formal risk acceptance or documented rationale exists for services that are excluded from or underrepresented in real-time monitoring
suggestedSources: IT Architecture or Infrastructure team — topology registers and CMDB exports
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or local equivalent) — technology asset and control registers
Monitoring platform documentation (Grafana, Zabbix, Datadog, PRTG, SolarWinds) — configured host and metric inventories
Third-party and vendor contract repository — SLA and monitoring obligations schedules
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control, or GRC platform record export

evidenceItems.name: Monitoring Dashboard Screenshots and Live System Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Contemporaneous evidence that real-time monitoring dashboards are operational and actively displaying capacity utilisation and performance metrics for key services. Demonstrates the monitoring mechanisms are live and functional, not merely configured in principle.
whatGoodLooksLike: Timestamped dashboard exports or screen captures showing real-time metrics for CPU utilisation, memory consumption, network throughput, transaction volumes, and response times across critical systems
Dashboards are accessible 24/7 and evidence shows they are actively used by operations staff, including during off-peak and weekend periods (supported by access logs or NOC shift handover records)
Metrics are displayed with visible thresholds or reference lines indicating warning and critical levels, confirming thresholds are configured and not just conceptually defined
Dashboard evidence covers both infrastructure-layer metrics and application/service-layer metrics, demonstrating end-to-end visibility rather than infrastructure monitoring alone
Historical metric data is retained within the monitoring platform to support trend analysis, and evidence shows this data was used in at least one capacity review during the audit period
commonGaps: Dashboard screenshots are produced on demand for the audit rather than being routine operational artefacts, with no evidence that dashboards are regularly consulted outside of audit preparation

Dashboards display infrastructure metrics only, with no application-level or business service performance indicators visible, limiting the ability to detect service degradation before it becomes an outage
No evidence of 24/7 monitoring coverage — dashboards are only monitored during business hours, creating a detection gap during nights, weekends, and public holidays
Alert thresholds are not visible on dashboards or are set at values that would only trigger at catastrophic failure levels (e.g., 99% CPU), providing no early warning capability
Monitoring data retention within the platform is insufficient (less than 90 days), preventing meaningful trend analysis or retrospective investigation of past performance issues
suggestedSources: Network Operations Centre (NOC) or IT Operations team — live dashboard access and shift logs
Monitoring platforms: Grafana, Kibana, Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, Zabbix, PRTG, SolarWinds NPM
Core banking and payment platform monitoring modules (vendor-supplied)
Cloud provider native monitoring consoles (AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) if applicable
evidenceItems.format: PDF export of dashboard snapshots with timestamps, or system-generated PDF reports

evidenceItems.name: Alert Configuration and Threshold Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of configured alert rules, threshold values, escalation paths, and notification routing for each monitored service. Demonstrates that alerts are actionable — defined, routed to responsible parties, and designed to enable timely response — rather than merely generated into a queue that no one monitors.
whatGoodLooksLike: Alert rules are documented with explicit threshold values (e.g., CPU >80% sustained for 5 minutes triggers warning; >90% triggers critical) for each key service, with clear rationale for threshold selection based on service baselines

Each alert type has a documented escalation path specifying the first responder, escalation contact, and maximum response time expectation, demonstrating alerts are routed to administrators capable of acting on them

Alert notifications are configured to reach responsible administrators via multiple channels (email, SMS, ITSM auto-ticketing, or paging systems) to ensure timely receipt regardless of individual availability

Alert suppression and maintenance window configurations are documented and controlled, with evidence that suppression is time-limited and approved, preventing alert fatigue from masking genuine incidents
Alert configuration is version-controlled, showing the history of threshold changes with dates, rationale, and approvals, supporting the periodic update requirement in the clause
commonGaps: Alert thresholds are set to vendor or tool defaults and have never been calibrated against the institution's actual service baselines, resulting in either excessive false positives or thresholds too high to provide early warning
Alert routing sends notifications to a shared group inbox or generic distribution list rather than to identified on-call administrators, reducing accountability and likelihood of timely action

No escalation procedure exists for unacknowledged alerts — if the first responder does not act, the alert silently expires without escalation, creating resolution gaps especially outside business hours
Alert suppression or maintenance windows are applied broadly or indefinitely, meaning monitoring coverage is effectively disabled for significant periods without risk acceptance

The alert configuration document exists but has no evidence of periodic review — thresholds defined at system commissioning have never been updated to reflect changes in workload, infrastructure, or service criticality
suggestedSources: Monitoring platform alert configuration exports (Grafana alerting rules, Zabbix trigger exports, Datadog monitor exports)
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — alert-to-ticket integration configuration
On-call scheduling system (PagerDuty, OpsGenie, or internal roster) — escalation policy documentation
Change management records — alert threshold change history
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy document plus system-exported alert rule configuration (JSON/XML/YAML acceptable as supplementary evidence)

evidenceItems.name: Alert Response and Incident Correlation Records
evidenceItems.description: Historical records demonstrating that alerts generated by monitoring systems led to timely administrator response and, where applicable, incident resolution. Provides empirical evidence that monitoring mechanisms are not only deployed but are operationally effective in enabling detection and resolution of service interruptions.
whatGoodLooksLike: ITSM incident or alert ticket records show a clear chain from monitoring system alert generation to administrator acknowledgement to resolution, with timestamps at each stage enabling measurement of mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR)

A sample of past service interruptions can be traced back to a monitoring alert that preceded or coincided with user-reported impact, demonstrating that monitoring is providing genuine early warning rather than lagging detection
Alert acknowledgement times are consistently within the institution's documented response time targets, and any breaches are accompanied by post-incident review documentation
Records show alerts are generated and responded to outside business hours, confirming 24/7 operational effectiveness rather than monitoring coverage limited to working hours

False positive and alert noise metrics are tracked, with evidence that high false positive rates have triggered threshold reviews, demonstrating a feedback loop between operational experience and monitoring configuration
commonGaps: Incidents in the ITSM system are predominantly created from user complaints or helpdesk calls rather than from monitoring alerts, suggesting monitoring is not functioning as the primary detection mechanism

Alert records exist in the monitoring platform but are not correlated with ITSM incident records, making it impossible to demonstrate the end-to-end detection and resolution chain required by the clause
Response time data shows alerts are routinely acknowledged hours after generation, with no evidence of escalation or post-incident review for delayed responses
Alert logs are only available for the current period because the monitoring platform does not retain historical alert data beyond 30-90 days, preventing audit of the full review period
All resolved alert records show rapid closure with no substantive notes, suggesting alerts are being cleared without genuine investigation, particularly for lower-severity alerts
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, Freshservice) — incident and alert ticket exports for the audit period
Monitoring platform alert history and acknowledgement logs
NOC or operations team shift logs and handover reports
Post-incident review reports for significant service interruptions during the audit period
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet export or PDF report from ITSM/monitoring platform covering the full audit period

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Monitoring Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of structured periodic reviews of monitoring scope, metrics, and thresholds to ensure they remain effective. Directly addresses the clause's explicit requirement that monitoring parameters are not static but are maintained as relevant over time.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal review records exist at a defined cadence (at minimum annually, ideally quarterly for critical services) with documented scope covering monitoring coverage gaps, threshold adequacy, metric relevance, and alert effectiveness

Review records show that findings led to tangible changes — new services added to monitoring scope, thresholds recalibrated, obsolete alerts removed — rather than consistently concluding that no changes are required

Reviews are triggered not only on a scheduled basis but also following significant changes such as new system deployments, major infrastructure changes, or post-incident reviews, demonstrating a risk-responsive approach
Review outputs are approved by the CISO or Head of Technology Operations and tracked through to implementation, with evidence that agreed changes were completed within a defined timeframe
The review process includes input from operations teams who work with the monitoring tools daily, ensuring practical effectiveness is assessed rather than only design adequacy
commonGaps: No formal periodic review process exists — monitoring configuration is only modified reactively following incidents or audit findings rather than through proactive scheduled reviews

Review records exist but show the same conclusion year-over-year with no changes recommended, which is implausible given evolving infrastructure and workloads and suggests the review is a checkbox exercise rather than genuine assessment

Reviews assess monitoring tool availability and dashboard uptime but do not evaluate whether the metrics and thresholds configured remain appropriate for the current risk profile and service baselines
Review findings identify gaps but there is no evidence of remediation tracking — recommended changes cannot be confirmed as implemented within the audit period
Reviews are conducted by the monitoring tool administrator without independent input, limiting the ability to identify blind spots in the current monitoring approach
suggestedSources: GRC platform — control review and testing records
IT Steering Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes
Change management system — records of monitoring configuration changes linked to periodic review outputs
IT Operations or Infrastructure team — internal review reports and action logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX review report with sign-off evidence, supplemented by change management records

evidenceItems.name: Capacity Utilisation Reporting and Trend Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Regular capacity utilisation reports demonstrating that the institution tracks and analyses capacity consumption over time across key systems and services. Demonstrates compliance with the specific capacity utilisation monitoring obligation and supports proactive capacity planning before service degradation occurs.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular capacity reports (at minimum monthly) cover CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth, and application throughput for all systems classified as key processes or services, with trend lines showing utilisation trajectory over time

Reports include forward-looking analysis identifying services approaching capacity thresholds, with projected breach dates based on utilisation trends, enabling proactive remediation before service impact
Capacity data is presented against defined thresholds (warning and critical levels) so that deviation from normal is immediately visible without requiring the reader to interpret raw figures

Reports are formally distributed to and reviewed by Infrastructure, Operations, and Technology Risk stakeholders, with evidence of discussion and action at management level when capacity concerns are identified

Capacity reporting covers peak period performance (e.g., month-end, salary processing periods, festive seasons) in addition to average utilisation, reflecting the institution's actual operational risk profile
commonGaps: Capacity reporting is produced but covers only infrastructure (servers, storage) without including application-tier throughput metrics such as transaction processing capacity, queue depths, or API call volumes relevant to financial services
Reports present point-in-time snapshots without trend analysis, making it impossible to identify gradual capacity degradation until it becomes acute
Capacity reports are generated but distribution and review are not evidenced — reports cannot be shown to have reached decision-makers or triggered any management action
Peak period capacity data is not separated from average utilisation reporting, obscuring the institution's true capacity risk during high-demand periods critical to financial services operations
Capacity thresholds in reports have not been updated following infrastructure changes (hardware upgrades, cloud migrations), causing reported figures to be measured against obsolete baselines
suggestedSources: Infrastructure or IT Operations team — capacity management reports and dashboards
Monitoring platforms with reporting capability (Grafana, Datadog, SolarWinds, PRTG)
Core banking and payment system performance reports from application teams or vendors
Technology Risk or IT Steering Committee — meeting packs containing capacity review items
evidenceItems.format: PDF report or Spreadsheet, produced on a documented recurring schedule

auditTips: Run a 'detection gap' test before the examination: select three to five past service interruptions from the ITSM incident log and trace each backward to identify whether a monitoring alert preceded user-reported impact. If incidents were consistently detected through user complaints rather than monitoring alerts, this is a critical finding that examiners will replicate — remediate before the examination by tightening thresholds and verifying alert routing.

Validate 24/7 monitoring coverage with concrete evidence rather than policy assertions. Pull monitoring platform access logs or NOC shift handover records showing alert acknowledgements at night, on weekends, and on public holidays during the audit period. Examiners will ask how alerts are handled outside business hours and will expect operational records, not just an on-call policy document.

Prepare a single master evidence map that links each monitoring obligation in Clause 10.30 (real-time coverage, actionable alerts, periodic review) to specific artefacts with document IDs, system locations, and page references. This prevents evidence being presented piecemeal and allows the examination team to efficiently verify completeness of the compliance posture.

Cross-reference alert configuration exports against the monitoring coverage register to demonstrate there are no services listed in scope that lack corresponding configured alerts. Examiners will probe for gaps between what the institution claims to monitor and what is actually alerting — producing this gap analysis proactively shows rigour and pre-empts examiner findings.

Bring dated before-and-after evidence of at least two threshold changes made as a result of periodic monitoring reviews. This directly addresses the 'periodically updated' obligation in the verbatim text and is far more persuasive than a review policy document alone. Change management records with approval timestamps are the strongest format for this evidence.

Ensure the periodic review records show a feedback loop: prior review findings should visibly connect to changes implemented, and the subsequent review should confirm those changes were effective. A chain of review records that each start fresh with no reference to prior findings will raise examiner doubts about whether the review process is substantive.

For any system excluded from real-time monitoring scope, prepare a formal risk acceptance record signed by an appropriate authority (CISO or equivalent) with a documented compensating control. Examiners understand that not every system warrants real-time monitoring, but undocumented exclusions are treated as compliance gaps — formal risk acceptance transforms an apparent gap into a managed and approved decision.

10.31.title: Digital Services Resilience Enhancement
10.31.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the institution has operationalised — not merely planned — the two hard-deadline capabilities: the early-warning monitoring system and the stand-in processing arrangement, both due by 30 September 2027. Examiners will test the 5% threshold escalation trigger in practice by reviewing incident logs and escalation records to confirm senior management was actually notified during qualifying disruptions, not just that a policy exists. A key red flag is monitoring dashboards that measure system uptime but cannot disaggregate customer counts or transaction volumes affected in near real-time. For stand-in arrangements, examiners will probe whether the institution has conducted end-to-end tests, whether customers have been formally notified of terms and limits, and whether fraud controls specific to the stand-in mode have been documented and tested. Interdependency reviews will be scrutinised for genuine risk analysis rather than superficial mapping exercises, particularly whether findings translated into remediation actions with tracked closure.

evidenceItems.name: Early Warning Monitoring System — Design and Capability Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical architecture documentation and configuration records proving the institution has implemented mechanisms to detect failed transactions, measure service availability, and monitor customer and transaction volume impact in near real-time, as required by clause 10.31(a)(i) and (ii).
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams show dedicated transaction-failure detection probes distinct from infrastructure uptime monitoring, with data lineage to the customer-impact dashboard

System configuration records demonstrate threshold alerts are set at the transaction level (e.g., error rate > X% over Y minutes) across all key digital channels (mobile app, internet banking, payment gateways)

Dashboard screenshots or reports show the system can display — in real time — the number of unique customers affected and transaction volume impacted expressed as a percentage of expected daily baseline

Test evidence (UAT/integration test records) confirms that synthetic transaction probes fire correctly and surface degradation scenarios including intermittent partial failures, not only complete outages
Go-live date and change records confirm full deployment no later than 30 September 2027, with a post-implementation review report
commonGaps: Monitoring measures server/API availability (HTTP 200 responses) but cannot distinguish failed business transactions (e.g., a payment that returned 200 but posted an error code internally)
Customer-impact counters rely on manual estimation from call centre volumes rather than automated real-time session or transaction data
The 'expected daily baseline' denominator for the 5% threshold calculation has not been formally defined, making the alert meaningless or inconsistently applied across channels
Monitoring coverage excludes third-party or outsourced channel components (e.g., white-label mobile app SDKs, payment switch), creating blind spots
Implementation was completed after the 30 September 2027 deadline without a formal dispensation or remediation plan approved by senior management
suggestedSources: IT Operations / NOC team — monitoring platform exports (Datadog, Dynatrace, Grafana, Splunk ITSI, or equivalent)
Enterprise Architecture team — technical architecture and integration design documents on SharePoint/Confluence
Digital Channels Product team — transaction volume baseline reports from core banking or payment switch
IT Project Management Office — project closure report and post-implementation review for the monitoring uplift initiative
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — technology risk controls register linked to this clause
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (architecture docs), System export/PDF (dashboard screenshots with timestamps), Spreadsheet (baseline volume definitions)

evidenceItems.name: 5% Threshold Escalation Policy and Incident Escalation Records
evidenceItems.description: The documented escalation policy defining the 5% threshold trigger and a historical sample of incident records demonstrating that senior management was actually notified — with timing evidence — when disruptions met or exceeded the threshold, as mandated by clause 10.31(a)(iii).
whatGoodLooksLike: The escalation policy explicitly states the 5% threshold in terms of both daily customer count and transaction volume, defines 'expected daily' baseline methodology, and names the senior management recipients by role title

Incident records for threshold-breaching events include a timestamped escalation notification (email, SMS, or incident management system alert) sent to the named senior management recipients within the required timeframe stated in the policy
Post-incident reports reference the escalation trigger, confirm senior management acknowledgement, and document any decisions made by management in response
The policy has been approved by the Board or a delegated senior committee and is version-controlled with a review date within the last 12 months
The ITSM tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) shows escalation workflows are automated where the monitoring system feeds the 5% breach trigger directly, minimising manual intervention
commonGaps: Policy defines the 5% rule but escalation in practice was performed via informal WhatsApp or verbal notification, leaving no auditable timestamped trail
The threshold was calculated differently across incidents — sometimes by transaction count, sometimes by customer count — with no standardised methodology documented
Minor incidents just below 5% were not logged, making it impossible for auditors to verify whether escalation was correctly withheld or whether underreporting occurred
Senior management recipients are defined by name rather than role, so the list is outdated following staff rotations
Escalation records exist in email inboxes only and have not been preserved in a central system accessible to compliance and audit teams
suggestedSources: IT Service Management (ITSM) system — incident records with escalation workflow logs (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira SM)
Technology Risk / BCM team — escalation policy document on SharePoint or GRC platform
Email system — archived escalation notifications (searchable by distribution list or subject line keyword)
Board/Senior Management secretariat — meeting minutes referencing disruption escalations
NOC shift logs — contemporaneous records of when thresholds were breached and who was notified
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy document), System export (ITSM incident and escalation records), PDF (email archive extracts)

evidenceItems.name: IT System Interdependency Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of regular structured reviews identifying vulnerable interdependencies among IT systems supporting digital services and delivery channels, with evidence of risk mitigation actions taken, as required by clause 10.31(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal interdependency mapping document covers all key digital services and delivery channels, identifying shared infrastructure, shared databases, shared middleware, and common third-party dependencies that could cause simultaneous failures
Review records show a defined periodicity (e.g., semi-annual) and include sign-off by a senior technology risk owner, with prior review versions demonstrating continuity over time
Each identified vulnerability has a corresponding risk treatment entry (accept, mitigate, transfer) with a named owner, target remediation date, and closure evidence where the date has passed

At least one review cycle demonstrates that a newly identified interdependency led to a concrete architecture change or operational control (e.g., circuit breaker pattern, separate network segments, staggered deployment pipelines)
The review methodology is documented and references industry standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-160, BCI Good Practice Guidelines) to demonstrate rigour
commonGaps: Interdependency maps are static diagrams last updated more than 12 months ago and do not reflect recent system changes, cloud migrations, or new third-party integrations
Reviews identify interdependencies descriptively but do not score or prioritise them by risk, making it unclear which vulnerabilities warrant urgent remediation
Remediation actions from prior reviews remain open with no updated target dates or escalation to management, suggesting the review process lacks teeth
Reviews cover application-layer dependencies but omit shared network infrastructure, shared DNS/NTP, and common cloud availability zones that could cause simultaneous failures
The review is performed by the same team that owns the systems, with no independent validation or challenge by a second line (Risk) or third line (Audit) function
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team — service dependency maps and CMDB (Configuration Management Database) exports from ServiceNow or iTop
Technology Risk team — risk register entries linked to interdependency vulnerabilities on GRC platform (Archer, MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC)
IT Change Advisory Board — change records showing architecture decisions made in response to interdependency findings
Third-party / vendor management team — contract and SLA documents for shared service providers
Internal Audit — audit findings and management responses related to previous interdependency reviews
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (review report with risk register extract), Visio/PDF (dependency maps), Spreadsheet (remediation tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Stand-In Processing Arrangement — Design, Policy, and Deployment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation proving the institution has established a stand-in processing capability by 30 September 2027, with evidence of its operational deployment during actual or simulated disruptions, prioritisation rationale for least-substitutable services, and customer disclosure materials, as required by clause 10.31(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: A stand-in processing policy defines the scope of covered services (prioritised by substitutability assessment), the activation trigger, transaction limits, permitted transaction types, and the roles responsible for activation and deactivation

Technical design documentation shows the stand-in system operates independently of the primary system's failure modes (e.g., separate processing engine, pre-loaded authorisation lists, offline capable), with a named vendor or in-house component

Deployment or drill records show the stand-in arrangement was activated — either during a real disruption or a scheduled exercise — with logs capturing activation time, transactions processed, and deactivation time

Customer-facing disclosures (website banners, in-app notifications, branch notices, terms and conditions updates) clearly state stand-in transaction limits, associated risks, liability allocation, and fraud reporting channels, and these materials have been reviewed for adequacy
Fraud risk controls specific to stand-in mode (e.g., reduced transaction limits, enhanced post-processing reconciliation, velocity checks) are documented and have been tested
commonGaps: Stand-in capability exists for ATM card transactions but has not been extended to mobile banking or internet banking channels, which are the least substitutable for many customer segments

The stand-in arrangement was designed but never tested end-to-end; only vendor documentation or proof-of-concept results are presented rather than live deployment evidence from the institution's own environment

Customer disclosure documents describe stand-in services in technical terms without clearly stating the transaction limit, the customer's responsibility for disputes, or how to report suspected fraud during stand-in mode
Reconciliation procedures for transactions processed in stand-in mode are undocumented or untested, creating financial integrity risk that examiners will flag as a control gap
The substitutability assessment that determined which services to prioritise for stand-in deployment is absent or was performed informally, with no documented methodology or customer impact analysis
suggestedSources: Digital Banking / Payments technology team — stand-in system technical design documents and configuration records
BCM / Operational Resilience team — stand-in activation procedures and drill/exercise reports on SharePoint or GRC platform
Product / Customer Experience team — customer disclosure materials, T&C documents, and in-app notification records
IT Operations — stand-in activation and transaction logs from the payment switch or authorisation host
Fraud Risk team — fraud controls matrix for stand-in mode and post-drill fraud risk assessment
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy, design, and procedures), System logs/export (activation and transaction records), PDF/HTML (customer disclosure materials)

evidenceItems.name: Substitutability Assessment for Digital Services Prioritisation
evidenceItems.description: A formal assessment documenting how the institution determined which digital services and delivery channels are 'least substitutable' for the purpose of prioritising stand-in processing deployment, demonstrating that prioritisation decisions under clause 10.31(c) were evidence-based and risk-informed.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment uses a defined methodology with explicit criteria (e.g., customer dependency ratio, availability of alternative channels, transaction criticality, vulnerable customer segment reach) applied consistently across all evaluated services

Results are ranked or tiered, clearly identifying which services received priority stand-in investment and explaining why others were deprioritised, with supporting data (e.g., channel usage statistics, customer demographic data)
The assessment was reviewed and approved by a senior governance body (e.g., Technology Risk Committee, EXCO) and is referenced in the stand-in processing policy
The assessment includes a review trigger that mandates reassessment when channel usage patterns change materially (e.g., following new product launch, significant shift in digital adoption)

Customer research or complaints data is cited to validate which services customers perceive as non-substitutable, grounding the assessment in actual customer behaviour rather than internal assumptions alone
commonGaps: Prioritisation decisions are presented as self-evident (e.g., 'mobile banking is critical') without a documented methodology, leaving examiners unable to verify the reasoning was systematic
The assessment was performed at a point in time and has not been reviewed since, despite significant changes in the institution's digital channel landscape or customer base
The assessment focuses exclusively on transaction volume without considering customer vulnerability factors (e.g., elderly customers reliant on a single channel, SME payroll processing windows)
Only channels the institution currently supports stand-in for are included in the assessment, creating a circular argument rather than an objective evaluation of all channels
The assessment does not consider the combined effect of simultaneous channel unavailability, which is directly relevant to the interdependency review obligation under clause 10.31(b)
suggestedSources: Strategy / Digital Banking team — channel usage analytics from digital banking platform or BI tool (Power BI, Tableau)
Customer Experience / Research team — customer journey maps and satisfaction surveys referencing channel dependency
Operational Resilience / BCM team — business impact analysis (BIA) documents that may already contain substitutability proxies
Retail / SME Banking business unit — product teams with knowledge of customer reliance on specific channels
GRC platform — risk assessment records linked to digital channel resilience
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (assessment report with supporting data appendix), Spreadsheet (scoring matrix), PDF (governance approval minutes)

evidenceItems.name: Clause 10.31 Implementation Roadmap and Milestone Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Project and programme records demonstrating the institution's planned and actual implementation trajectory toward the two 30 September 2027 deadlines (early-warning monitoring and stand-in processing), providing examiners with visibility of governance oversight and implementation progress prior to the compliance date.
whatGoodLooksLike: A Board or senior management-approved roadmap document identifies discrete workstreams for the monitoring capability and stand-in arrangement, with milestone dates, owners, and budget allocations

Project status reports presented to a governance committee (e.g., Technology Risk Committee, Project Steering Committee) at regular intervals show progressive milestone completion and flag delays with remediation plans

Programme charter or project initiation document explicitly references clause 10.31 and the regulatory deadline, confirming that the work is recognised as a regulatory obligation rather than discretionary IT improvement

Milestone completion evidence (e.g., system test sign-off, UAT report, go-live confirmation) is archived and cross-referenced to the roadmap, enabling auditors to verify that stated milestones were genuinely completed
For institutions examined before 30 September 2027, a credible gap analysis with confirmed remediation dates and escalation protocols for slippage demonstrates regulatory good faith
commonGaps: No dedicated project or workstream exists; implementation is being addressed through business-as-usual IT change tickets without executive sponsorship or regulatory tracking, making governance oversight invisible to examiners
The roadmap exists but has not been presented to or approved by the Board or a senior governance committee, suggesting the obligation has not been escalated to appropriate decision-makers
Milestone dates on the roadmap have slipped repeatedly without formal re-baselining or risk acceptance documentation, indicating weak programme governance
The roadmap conflates clause 10.31 requirements with unrelated IT resilience projects, making it difficult for examiners to trace regulatory compliance directly

Institutions past the September 2027 deadline cannot produce evidence of timely implementation — go-live dates in project records post-date the regulatory deadline with no formal dispensation from BNM
suggestedSources: IT Project Management Office — project charter, Gantt charts, and status reports from ITSM or PPM tool (MS Project, Jira, Planview)
Technology Risk / Compliance team — regulatory obligations tracker on GRC platform or SharePoint
Board / Risk Committee secretariat — board papers and minutes referencing clause 10.31 programme updates
Internal Audit — any pre-implementation readiness assessments or audit reviews of the programme
CFO / Finance team — capital expenditure approval records for stand-in and monitoring infrastructure investments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (programme charter, status reports, board papers), Spreadsheet (milestone tracker), PDF (committee meeting minutes)

auditTips: Before the examination, run a live demonstration of the monitoring dashboard with the BNM examiner team: select a recent historical disruption and show how the system surfaced customer impact counts and transaction volume percentages in real time — this directly addresses the 'detect and measure' requirements of 10.31(a)(i) and (ii) and is far more persuasive than screenshots alone.

Prepare a disruption event log covering the 24 months prior to examination, annotating each event with: channel affected, duration, estimated customers impacted, estimated transaction volume impacted as a percentage of expected daily, whether the 5% threshold was reached, and the escalation outcome — this single artefact cross-validates the monitoring system, the escalation policy, and incident management practices simultaneously.

Conduct a tabletop or live drill of the stand-in processing arrangement within three months before the examination and document it formally: capture activation time, transactions processed under stand-in mode, reconciliation steps, and deactivation. Examiners will probe whether the arrangement has ever actually been used, and a recent drill with a lessons-learned report is the strongest available substitute for a real-event deployment record.

Map every evidence item directly to the specific sub-clauses of 10.31 in a compliance evidence matrix: for example, evidence item X satisfies 10.31(a)(i), evidence item Y satisfies 10.31(c) customer disclosure. Present this matrix as the opening document in your examination pack so examiners can navigate evidence without inference, reducing the risk of being asked for items you have but did not present.

Cross-reference clause 10.31(b) interdependency review findings against your Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) — examiners will check whether interdependency risks identified in the review are reflected in recovery scenarios. Gaps between what the interdependency review found and what the BCP covers are a common and significant finding.

Validate that customer-facing stand-in disclosure materials (terms, transaction limits, fraud reporting) have been formally reviewed by Legal, Compliance, and a consumer protection lens before the examination — BNM examiners may specifically test whether the disclosures meet fair treatment of customers expectations under the Financial Services Act and PDPA, not just technical adequacy under RMiT.

If the examination occurs before 30 September 2027, prepare a written attestation from the CTO or equivalent confirming current implementation status against each of the two deadline-bound requirements (monitoring capability and stand-in arrangement), supported by the milestone evidence and a clear completion trajectory — proactive disclosure of credible progress is materially better than leaving examiners to discover gaps through their own testing.

10.32.title: Critical System High Availability Requirements
10.32.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus primarily on whether the institution can demonstrate a quantified, tracked record of unplanned downtime for each system designated as critical — specifically validating the rolling 12-month cumulative figure against the 4-hour threshold and the per-incident maximum against the 120-minute ceiling. Examiners will scrutinise the methodology used to define and classify 'critical systems' and will probe whether the institution's definition adequately captures all systems where customers or counterparties have a reasonable expectation of immediate service delivery, flagging omissions such as internet banking, real-time payment rails, or core banking interfaces. A key red flag is the absence of a formal, board-approved high availability architecture target for each critical system backed by engineering artefacts (e.g., active-active or active-passive cluster configurations, redundant data paths). Examiners also cross-reference incident management logs with downtime reports to detect discrepancies, particularly where incidents have been closed prematurely or partial outages affecting customer-facing interfaces have not been counted. Institutions that cannot produce an automated, near-real-time availability dashboard or that rely solely on manual log extraction are treated as higher risk, as this indicates weak operational controls over the availability obligation.

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Register with HA Classification
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved register identifying all systems classified as critical under the institution's technology risk framework, with explicit notation of whether each system is subject to the Clause 10.32 high-availability obligations (i.e., where immediate service delivery to customers or counterparties is expected). The register demonstrates that the institution has performed a deliberate scoping exercise and has not omitted material customer-facing systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register lists each critical system with a unique identifier, system owner, business function, and explicit flag indicating applicability of the 4-hour/120-minute HA thresholds
Scoping rationale is documented for each system, referencing the 'reasonable expectation of immediate delivery' criterion from Clause 10.32
Register has been reviewed and approved by the CIO and CISO within the last 12 months, with version history retained
Systems are mapped to the customer or counterparty touchpoints they serve (e.g., internet banking portal, interbank RENTAS interface, card authorisation switch)
Register is reconciled against the BCP/DR system inventory and the CMDB to ensure no material system is absent
commonGaps: Register exists but does not distinguish between systems subject to Clause 10.32 HA obligations versus other critical systems, making it impossible to scope downtime monitoring correctly
Payment infrastructure components (e.g., HSMs, message switches) and third-party-hosted customer-facing systems are omitted because ownership is attributed to a vendor
Register has not been updated following new system launches, acquisitions, or technology refreshes in the past 12 months
Scoping rationale is absent — systems are listed as 'critical' without documented reasoning tied to the regulatory criterion of immediate customer/counterparty delivery expectation
No formal approval signature or governance committee endorsement is attached to the register, making it inadmissible as a controlled document
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management function (owns the critical system taxonomy)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — Critical Asset Register module
CMDB (Configuration Management Database) in ITSM tool (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, BMC Helix)
Enterprise Architecture repository (for system landscape and dependency mapping)
BCP/DR steering committee meeting minutes and DR system inventory
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (approved register with sign-off page) or controlled GRC platform export

evidenceItems.name: High Availability Architecture Design Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical architecture artefacts — including architecture design documents, network/infrastructure diagrams, and engineering specifications — demonstrating that each Clause 10.32-scoped critical system has been engineered for high availability. These documents show the institution has proactively designed resilience into the system rather than relying on reactive incident response to meet the downtime thresholds.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture documents specify the HA pattern deployed (e.g., active-active, active-passive, N+1 redundancy) for each critical system tier: application, database, middleware, and network
Diagrams show elimination of single points of failure for customer-facing interfaces, including redundant load balancers, database clustering, and geographically separated data centre connectivity
Recovery time objectives (RTOs) documented in the architecture are equal to or more stringent than the 120-minute maximum tolerable downtime per incident required by Clause 10.32
Documents have been reviewed by an independent technology risk or architecture review board and carry version control with change history
HA design is validated by penetration or resilience test results (e.g., chaos engineering, failover simulation) that confirm the architecture performs as documented under failure conditions
commonGaps: Architecture documents exist for the core platform but omit downstream dependencies (e.g., API gateways, third-party payment switches, CDN layers) that, if unavailable, would sever the customer interface
HA design specifies active-passive clustering but RTO in the design exceeds 120 minutes for database failover, creating a latent breach of the per-incident threshold
