Documents are outdated and do not reflect infrastructure changes made during cloud migration or data centre consolidation projects
No evidence that the HA design has ever been tested under realistic failure conditions; architecture claims are theoretical and unvalidated
Third-party or vendor-hosted critical systems lack equivalent HA architecture documentation, and the institution has not obtained or reviewed vendor HA certifications
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team (architecture design records and decision logs)
Infrastructure and Cloud Operations team (infrastructure-as-code repositories, data centre design specs)
Technology Architecture repository (e.g., Confluence, SharePoint Architecture Library)
Vendor management files (for SLAs and HA architecture certifications from third-party system providers)
Internal audit or technology risk review reports referencing architecture adequacy assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (architecture design document with diagrams), supplemented by Visio/draw.io network topology diagrams

evidenceItems.name: Rolling 12-Month Unplanned Downtime Tracking Report
evidenceItems.description: A structured availability report — produced at minimum monthly and reconciled at least quarterly — that tracks cumulative unplanned downtime for each Clause 10.32-scoped critical system on a rolling 12-month basis. This is the primary quantitative evidence that the institution is actively monitoring compliance with the 4-hour cumulative threshold and can demonstrate adherence or identify trending risk before a breach occurs.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report covers each critical system individually, showing monthly and cumulative rolling 12-month unplanned downtime in hours and minutes against the 4-hour regulatory ceiling, with a clear traffic-light RAG status

Methodology note explains precisely how 'unplanned downtime affecting the interface with customers or counterparties' is defined and measured, including how partial degradation (e.g., intermittent errors affecting a subset of customers) is treated

Report is generated from an automated monitoring platform (e.g., APM tool, synthetic monitoring, ITSM incident data) rather than compiled manually, reducing the risk of human error or selective reporting
Historical reports are retained for a rolling 12-month period plus archive, allowing examiners to reconstruct any point-in-time compliance position
Report is reviewed and signed off by the Head of Technology Operations or equivalent at the close of each reporting period, with escalation records where thresholds are at risk
commonGaps: Report aggregates downtime at an infrastructure or platform level rather than specifically measuring downtime affecting the customer/counterparty interface — scheduled maintenance windows are incorrectly included in unplanned figures or vice versa

Measurement methodology is not documented; different teams calculate downtime differently (e.g., monitoring tool vs. incident ticket timestamps), leading to inconsistencies that an examiner will immediately probe
Reports are produced ad hoc or only when a breach is suspected rather than on a consistent monthly cadence, creating gaps in the rolling record

Partial or degraded service incidents (e.g., increased transaction failure rates, timeout errors) are not counted as downtime because the system was technically 'up', understating the true customer-impact duration
No governance review or escalation mechanism exists to alert senior management when cumulative downtime approaches the 4-hour ceiling, meaning the institution has no early warning control
suggestedSources: IT Service Management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — incident records with downtime timestamps
APM and synthetic monitoring tools (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, New Relic) — availability dashboards and SLA reports
Network Operations Centre (NOC) — availability monitoring exports
Technology Operations or Service Delivery Management function — monthly service reports
GRC platform — availability KPI/KRI tracking module
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets with automated feed) or PDF management report exported from monitoring/GRC platform

evidenceItems.name: Incident Records with Downtime Classification and Duration
evidenceItems.description: Complete incident management records for all unplanned outages or degradations affecting Clause 10.32-scoped critical systems over the rolling 12-month period, including timestamped records of incident detection, customer impact start/end times, root cause, and resolution actions. These records are the source-of-truth from which the rolling downtime report (10.32-E3) is derived and must be consistent with it.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each incident record contains: incident ID, affected system(s), detected time, customer/counterparty impact start and end times (clearly distinguished from internal detection time), total customer-facing downtime duration, severity classification, and whether the incident was planned or unplanned
Incident records are stored in the ITSM platform with tamper-evident audit trails; timestamps are system-generated rather than manually entered to prevent retrospective adjustment
Post-Incident Reviews (PIRs) or Root Cause Analysis (RCA) reports are linked to each P1/P2 incident, confirming the duration of customer impact independently of the initial incident ticket

Incidents involving third-party or vendor-managed components include vendor incident reports or communication logs confirming the impact window, ensuring the institution has not understated downtime attributable to outsourced services

A formal classification process exists and is evidenced — showing how operations staff determined whether an incident affected the customer interface and how impact duration was measured (e.g., through synthetic transaction monitoring, error rate thresholds)
commonGaps: Customer impact end time is recorded as the time the fix was applied internally rather than the time customers could demonstrably transact again, systematically understating downtime duration

Incidents are closed without a severity classification or without recording whether customer-facing interfaces were affected, making it impossible to filter records for Clause 10.32 compliance purposes
PIR/RCA reports are not consistently produced for all incidents exceeding 120 minutes, leaving the most material incidents with the weakest evidentiary record
Third-party incidents are recorded only as 'vendor issue' without capturing the verified customer impact window from vendor communications, creating unquantified downtime gaps

Multiple parallel incidents affecting the same system within the same window are recorded as separate tickets, and the combined customer impact duration is not aggregated — leading to understatement in the rolling report
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow Incident Management, Jira Service Management) — incident ticket exports filtered by critical system and 'unplanned' category
NOC shift logs and major incident manager (MIM) bridge call recordings/notes
PIR/RCA repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform)
Vendor management team — vendor incident notification emails, SLA breach reports, and vendor-issued PIR documents
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records — to cross-reference and confirm no unplanned incidents were miscategorised as planned changes
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/PDF) from ITSM platform, supplemented by linked PDF PIR/RCA documents

evidenceItems.name: High Availability Test and Failover Validation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution periodically tests the high availability mechanisms of each Clause 10.32-scoped critical system — including failover simulations, resilience drills, and recovery time validation — to confirm that the system can actually meet the 120-minute maximum tolerable downtime per incident in a real failure scenario. Testing records demonstrate that HA design (10.32-E2) is operationally verified rather than theoretical.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plans and results cover all HA failure modes relevant to each critical system (e.g., primary node failure, data centre power loss, network path failure, database cluster failover) and are conducted at least annually or following significant infrastructure changes
Test results record the actual recovery time achieved during the simulation and explicitly compare it against the 120-minute RTO ceiling, with pass/fail determination documented

Tests are conducted under conditions that replicate production load where possible, or include a documented rationale for why test conditions differ from production and how this affects result validity
Deficiencies identified during testing are tracked in a remediation log with owners and target dates; closure of remediation actions is evidenced with re-test results
Test execution is witnessed or independently validated — either by internal audit, technology risk, or an independent third party — to prevent the institution from self-certifying untested claims
commonGaps: Failover testing is conducted only for DR (full site failover) but not for component-level HA failures (e.g., individual node failure within a cluster), leaving the most common failure mode untested

Test records show that actual failover times exceeded 120 minutes in prior exercises, but no remediation plan was documented or implemented, indicating the institution is aware of a latent breach risk but has not addressed it
Tests are scheduled during low-traffic maintenance windows but results are not validated against production-equivalent load, making recovery time projections unreliable for peak-period incidents
Test plans and results exist in informal formats (e.g., email threads, spreadsheet notes) without formal sign-off, making it difficult to establish an authoritative record of what was tested and when

Third-party-managed critical systems have not been subjected to HA testing by the institution, and vendor assurances of HA capability are accepted without independent validation or contractual testing obligations
suggestedSources: IT Infrastructure / Cloud Operations team — DR and HA test plans and results
Business Continuity Management (BCM) team — resilience exercise records and BCP test reports
Internal Audit — technology audit reports covering HA and DR testing adequacy
Vendor management team — vendor-issued HA test certificates, SOC 2 Type II reports, or ISO 22301 certifications
Change management records (CAB minutes) — for HA tests conducted under change control
GRC platform — resilience testing tracker and remediation action log
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal test plan and results report with sign-off), supplemented by spreadsheet remediation tracker

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Availability Reporting and Governance Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the cumulative unplanned downtime position for Clause 10.32-scoped critical systems is reported to senior management and the Board (or a delegated Board committee) on a regular basis, and that governance decisions and escalations related to HA compliance are formally recorded. This demonstrates that the institution's HA obligations are treated as a board-level risk rather than a purely operational matter.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee minutes reflect periodic (at minimum quarterly) reporting of rolling 12-month downtime figures for each critical system, with explicit reference to the 4-hour regulatory threshold and RAG status
Management reporting packs include a trend analysis showing whether cumulative downtime is increasing, stable, or decreasing, enabling proactive governance rather than reactive breach management
Where downtime thresholds are approached or breached, escalation records show timely notification to senior management and the Board, with documented management responses and remediation commitments
The institution's Technology Risk Appetite Statement includes a quantified availability metric aligned to Clause 10.32 thresholds, and board-approved risk appetite breach protocols are in place
Minutes and reports carry formal chairperson or company secretary endorsement confirming they represent an accurate record of proceedings, meeting the standard for regulatory submission
commonGaps: Availability metrics are reported to an IT steering committee but do not reach the Board Risk Committee or equivalent, meaning the board cannot demonstrate oversight of this regulatory obligation

Board reports present availability in aggregated percentage uptime figures (e.g., '99.9% uptime') without converting to absolute downtime hours, making it impossible to assess compliance against the 4-hour ceiling without manual calculation
Escalation to senior management following a near-breach or actual breach of the downtime thresholds is not evidenced in committee minutes or documented communication records
The Technology Risk Appetite Statement does not reference Clause 10.32 thresholds explicitly, creating a disconnect between regulatory obligations and the institution's risk governance framework
Board minutes are cursory and do not reflect substantive discussion of availability risks, suggesting that availability reporting is a tick-box agenda item rather than a genuine governance control
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — Board Risk Committee and Board Technology Committee minutes and approved packs
Technology Risk Management function — management risk reporting packs
GRC platform — risk appetite monitoring dashboard and KRI breach escalation logs
Chief Risk Officer (CRO) office — Technology Risk Appetite Statement and associated board approval resolution
Internal Audit — audit committee reports referencing HA compliance findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board/committee minutes and approved management packs), supplemented by GRC platform dashboard exports

auditTips: Before examination day, reconcile the rolling downtime report (10.32-E3) against the ITSM incident log (10.32-E4) line by line for the past 12 months — BNM examiners routinely perform this cross-check and any discrepancy (e.g., an incident that appears in the ITSM system but is absent from the downtime tracker, or duration mismatches) will be treated as a control failure or data integrity concern requiring immediate explanation.

Map every system in the Critical System Register (10.32-E1) to its customer or counterparty touchpoint explicitly — examiners will test the completeness of your scoping by asking operational staff to name systems they use to serve customers and checking whether those systems appear in the register with Clause 10.32 flags. Prepare a one-page scoping rationale document showing why systems were included or excluded.

Convert all availability metrics in board and management reports to absolute hours and minutes against the 4-hour ceiling, not percentage uptime — a '99.95% uptime' figure for a critical system that is available 24x7x365 equates to approximately 4.38 hours of downtime annually, which already breaches the threshold. Presenting percentage figures to examiners without this conversion invites the calculation to be done against you.

For each incident in the rolling 12-month period where downtime exceeded 60 minutes, pre-prepare a two-page summary cross-referencing the ITSM ticket, the PIR/RCA, and the entry in the downtime tracker — examiners frequently sample the largest incidents in detail and an institution that can quickly produce a coherent, reconciled package for each major incident signals strong operational control.

Validate that your third-party and vendor-managed critical systems are covered: obtain and review vendor SLAs, SOC 2 Type II reports, or ISO 22301 certifications, and check that contracted RTOs are within 120 minutes. If a vendor SLA permits RTOs exceeding 120 minutes, this is a compliance gap that should be surfaced and remediation (contract renegotiation or compensating controls) documented before examination.

Conduct a dry-run failover test for at least one critical system within 3 months of the expected examination window, and retain the test plan, execution log, and results report with formal sign-off — examiners view recent, documented HA testing as a strong positive indicator of operational maturity, and the absence of recent tests is a consistent finding in BNM technology examination reports.

Prepare a single consolidated compliance evidence pack for Clause 10.32 that indexes all six categories of evidence with document references, locations, and version dates — examiners operate under time pressure and an institution that can produce a pre-organised evidence binder (physical or SharePoint folder) with a clear index demonstrates readiness and reduces the risk of ad hoc document retrieval causing inconsistencies or omissions during the examination.

10.33.title: Non-NCII Entity Service Availability Guidance
10.33.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing clause 10.33 focus primarily on whether the institution has made a documented, deliberate decision regarding voluntary adoption of the paragraph 10.31 resilience measures, rather than simply ignoring the guidance by default. Examiners will verify the institution's NCII designation status through authoritative records and confirm that the correct regulatory perimeter applies before evaluating the voluntary framework. A key red flag is the absence of any documented consideration of paragraph 10.31 measures — examiners interpret this as governance inattention rather than a legitimate opt-out. Where institutions have chosen not to fully adopt paragraph 10.31 measures, examiners expect a proportionate rationale supported by risk assessment, board awareness, and an articulation of compensating controls. The examination approach is qualitative and contextual: examiners weigh the institution's size, transaction volumes, customer exposure, and systemic risk profile against the adequacy of existing service availability arrangements.

evidenceItems.name: NCII Designation Status Confirmation
evidenceItems.description: Authoritative documentation confirming the institution's current designation status under the National Critical Information Infrastructure framework, establishing the regulatory basis for clause 10.33 applicability versus the mandatory requirements in clause 10.31. This record anchors the entire compliance position for this clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Official correspondence from NACSA (National Cyber Security Agency) or the relevant sector lead confirming non-NCII status, dated within the current review cycle

Internal legal or compliance memo cross-referencing the institution's entity type (e-money issuer, non-bank merchant acquirer, or intermediary remittance institution) against the NCII designation register
Board or EXCO briefing paper that acknowledges non-NCII status and explains the resulting applicability of clause 10.33 guidance rather than mandatory 10.31 obligations
Version-controlled register of regulatory applicability determinations that includes the 10.33 / 10.31 distinction with a named owner and review date
Evidence that the designation status is reviewed at least annually or upon any material change in the institution's licensing, ownership, or operational scope
commonGaps: Institution assumes non-NCII status without any documented confirmation, relying on informal understanding rather than a traceable determination
Status confirmation is outdated — originally obtained at licensing but never refreshed despite expansion of services or transaction volumes that could trigger re-designation
Documentation exists but is held informally in email threads rather than a controlled compliance repository, making it inaccessible during examination
No mapping between entity type classification (e-money issuer vs. acquirer vs. remittance) and the specific clause applicability, creating ambiguity during review
Legal and technology teams operate in silos, resulting in conflicting assumptions about designation status that surface only under examiner questioning
suggestedSources: NACSA official correspondence files held by the Chief Information Security Officer or Head of Compliance
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, LogicGate) regulatory applicability register
SharePoint or Confluence compliance policy repository under 'Regulatory Perimeter' or 'Licensing & Designation'
BNM licensing records and associated correspondence maintained by the company secretariat
evidenceItems.format: PDF (official correspondence) supported by DOCX or PDF internal memo; version-controlled in GRC or SharePoint

evidenceItems.name: Paragraph 10.31 Voluntary Adoption Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A structured gap analysis or self-assessment comparing the institution's current service availability controls against each measure prescribed in paragraph 10.31, with an explicit decision — documented and approved at management level — on whether to adopt, partially adopt, or defer each measure, accompanied by rationale. This is the central piece of evidence demonstrating that the guidance was actively considered.
whatGoodLooksLike: A clause-by-clause mapping table covering every sub-requirement within paragraph 10.31, with columns for current state, gap identified, adoption decision, owner, and target date
Management sign-off (CIO, CRO, or equivalent) on the overall adoption position, showing informed governance rather than passive non-action
Risk-based rationale for any measures not adopted, referencing the institution's transaction volumes, customer base size, and existing compensating controls
Clear differentiation between measures already implemented organically versus those deliberately deferred, avoiding conflation of the two
Assessment is dated, versioned, and scheduled for periodic review — at minimum annually or following any material change in technology architecture or business model
commonGaps: No formal gap analysis exists; the institution assumes existing controls are sufficient without a structured comparison against paragraph 10.31 specifics
Assessment was performed once at policy issuance but has not been refreshed, rendering it stale relative to the current operating environment
Adoption decisions are made by technology staff without escalation to management or board level, undermining governance credibility
Rationale for non-adoption is absent or generic ('not applicable to our size') rather than grounded in quantified risk assessment
The assessment conflates paragraph 10.31 measures with general BCP/DR obligations, creating confusion during examiner line-by-line review
suggestedSources: GRC platform compliance assessment module with paragraph 10.31 control library loaded
Internal audit workpapers from the most recent technology risk or business continuity audit
Technology Risk Management team SharePoint site or Confluence space
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) system risk treatment records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) for the gap mapping table; PDF for the approved management sign-off memo

evidenceItems.name: Service Availability Policy and Target Documentation
evidenceItems.description: The institution's current service availability framework documents — including defined Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs), Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs), and uptime commitments — demonstrating the baseline resilience posture against which paragraph 10.31 measures were evaluated. These records show whether the institution has independently converged on equivalent standards through its own governance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or EXCO-approved Service Availability or Business Continuity Policy that explicitly defines RTO and RPO targets for critical technology systems, with clear alignment to product and service obligations
Service-level definitions that distinguish between critical payment and settlement systems versus back-office platforms, with tiered availability targets
Documented RTO and RPO values that are quantified (e.g., RTO ≤ 4 hours, RPO ≤ 1 hour) rather than expressed as generic aspiration statements
Evidence that availability targets have been tested against actual incident or drill data, with test results referenced in the policy documentation
Policy version history demonstrating periodic review and update, with the most recent revision post-dating the institution's paragraph 10.31 assessment
commonGaps: RTO and RPO targets exist in BCP documents but are not reconciled with technology architecture capabilities, creating paper commitments that cannot be met operationally
Availability targets cover only the core banking or payment platform and omit dependent systems (authentication, network, cloud infrastructure) that are equally critical
Policy documents are approved by IT management rather than board or EXCO level, insufficient for a clause that links to systemic service availability
Targets are not formally linked to customer-facing service level agreements or regulatory commitments, making it difficult to demonstrate proportionality
No formal mechanism exists to escalate availability threshold breaches to management, leaving targets as aspirational rather than operationally enforced
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee or EXCO approved policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC document management)
Business Continuity Management (BCM) system or tool (e.g., Fusion Framework, Castellan, Archer)
IT Service Management (ITSM) platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for SLA configuration records
Cloud provider or data centre service agreements for contracted uptime commitments
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board-approved policy); XLSX or system export for RTO/RPO register

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Awareness Record
evidenceItems.description: Minutes, briefing papers, or reporting artefacts demonstrating that the board or a designated board committee has been informed of clause 10.33's applicability to the institution, the voluntary nature of paragraph 10.31 measures, and the management position taken on adoption. This evidence demonstrates governance accountability for a deliberate institutional choice.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or Audit Committee minutes containing a specific agenda item or resolution related to RMiT clause 10.33 and the institution's paragraph 10.31 adoption position
Technology or cyber risk dashboard presented to the board that includes a line item on service availability guidance compliance and adoption status
A management paper or briefing note presented to EXCO or equivalent body that summarises the gap assessment outcome and recommended adoption posture, with the decision recorded
Annual regulatory compliance attestation to the board that explicitly references the institution's position on voluntary RMiT guidance clauses including 10.33
Action items or follow-up commitments recorded in board minutes where partial adoption was approved, with completion tracking visible in subsequent meeting records
commonGaps: Board is aware of mandatory RMiT obligations but has never been briefed on voluntary guidance clauses, leaving clause 10.33 adoption decisions made at staff level without governance oversight
Board minutes reference BCP and DR generally but contain no specific discussion of RMiT paragraph 10.31 measures or the voluntary adoption decision under clause 10.33
Management papers presented to the board are too high-level to demonstrate informed decision-making — they acknowledge the clause exists but do not document the substantive adoption analysis
Board awareness is limited to the CIO or CRO verbal updates not captured in minutes, creating an evidential gap during examination
The institution's board reporting cycle means the adoption position has never been formally tabled despite being operationally decided by technology leadership
suggestedSources: Board secretariat for Risk Committee, Audit Committee, and full Board minutes
GRC platform board reporting module or board pack management system (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect)
EXCO meeting minutes held by the Office of the CEO or Head of Compliance
Regulatory compliance reporting tracker maintained by the Chief Compliance Officer
evidenceItems.format: PDF (certified board minutes extracts); PDF or PPTX for board briefing papers

evidenceItems.name: Compensating Controls Register (Where Paragraph 10.31 Measures Not Fully Adopted)
evidenceItems.description: For measures within paragraph 10.31 that the institution has decided not to adopt, a structured register of compensating controls demonstrating that equivalent risk mitigation is achieved through alternative means. This register provides the examiner with confidence that the voluntary opt-out is not a governance gap but a risk-managed decision.
whatGoodLooksLike: A control mapping document that pairs each unadopted paragraph 10.31 measure with one or more compensating controls, including control description, owner, and testing frequency

Risk quantification supporting the adequacy of each compensating control — e.g., residual risk ratings showing controls reduce risk to within appetite even without the specific paragraph 10.31 measure
Independent validation of compensating controls through internal audit, penetration testing, or DR drill results cited in the register
The register is reviewed and revalidated whenever a compensating control changes or is decommissioned, ensuring it reflects the current control environment
Clear escalation criteria defining the conditions under which a compensating control would be deemed insufficient and full paragraph 10.31 adoption would be triggered
commonGaps: Institution claims compensating controls exist but cannot produce a formalised register — reliance on tribal knowledge rather than documented controls

Compensating controls listed are operational procedures rather than technical controls, and have not been tested or validated against the specific risk scenario addressed by the paragraph 10.31 measure
The register exists but has not been reviewed following infrastructure changes, cloud migration, or new product launches that may have altered the control environment
Risk ratings in the register are not independently validated and reflect management optimism rather than objective assessment
No clear owner is assigned to compensating controls, making accountability and maintenance difficult to demonstrate during examination
suggestedSources: GRC platform control library with risk-control linkage mapping (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, OneTrust)
Internal Audit reports covering technology resilience and business continuity
Technology Risk team risk treatment plans and risk register exports
Penetration test reports and DR drill after-action reports from the CISO's office
evidenceItems.format: XLSX (control mapping register); PDF (supporting audit reports and test results)

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Review and Adoption Monitoring Record
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution's decision on paragraph 10.31 adoption is subject to ongoing monitoring and periodic reassessment rather than a one-time determination. This includes triggers for re-evaluation such as material changes in business model, transaction volumes, regulatory re-designation risk, or significant technology incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented review schedule — at minimum annual — for the paragraph 10.31 voluntary adoption assessment, with completed review records showing the review was actually conducted

A defined set of change triggers (e.g., exceeding a transaction volume threshold, launching a new product, acquiring a significant customer segment) that automatically prompt re-evaluation of the adoption position
Post-incident review records that assess whether a major service disruption warrants upgrading the institution's voluntary adoption of paragraph 10.31 measures
Regulatory horizon scanning records showing the institution monitors for any changes to NCII designation criteria or BNM policy updates affecting clause 10.33 applicability
Tracking evidence that review action items from prior cycles have been resolved, demonstrating a closed-loop process rather than a review that produces no follow-through
commonGaps: The initial adoption assessment is treated as a permanent decision with no scheduled review, leaving the institution exposed if its risk profile materially changes
Review triggers are defined in policy but not operationalised — no mechanism exists to detect when a trigger condition has been met and initiate the reassessment
Post-incident reviews consider operational response but do not feed back into the voluntary adoption position for paragraph 10.31, missing a critical learning loop
Regulatory monitoring is performed for mandatory clauses only; voluntary guidance clauses like 10.33 are excluded from the regulatory change management process
Review records exist but show no substantive analysis — they merely restate the prior adoption decision without evidence of fresh assessment against the current operating environment
suggestedSources: GRC platform compliance calendar and task management module
Regulatory Change Management system or SharePoint tracker maintained by the compliance team
Post-incident review and lessons-learned repository held by the CISO or Head of IT Risk
Technology Risk Committee meeting records documenting periodic risk profile reviews
evidenceItems.format: PDF or XLSX (review records and tracking logs); PDF (post-incident review reports)

auditTips: Establish NCII status in writing before any other preparation: obtain or locate the formal NACSA or sector-lead confirmation of non-designation and place it at the front of the clause 10.33 evidence pack. Examiners will ask for this first, and an inability to produce it immediately signals poor regulatory housekeeping.

Do not treat the 'G' marker as an excuse for inaction — prepare a brief narrative (one to two pages) explaining the institution's philosophy toward voluntary guidance adoption, referenced to its risk appetite. Examiners evaluate intent and governance quality, not just binary compliance, and an articulated position is far more defensible than silence.

Cross-reference the paragraph 10.31 gap assessment directly against the institution's existing BCP and DR test results. Where test outcomes already demonstrate convergence with paragraph 10.31 standards, highlight this explicitly rather than leaving the examiner to draw the connection — it demonstrates organic adequacy without additional investment.

Prepare a single-page summary matrix mapping each paragraph 10.31 sub-measure to one of three statuses: 'Adopted', 'Adopted via Compensating Control', or 'Deferred with Documented Rationale'. Present this matrix as an examiner-facing summary at the opening of the evidence pack — it controls the narrative and prevents examiners from interpreting absence of specific controls as a gap rather than a deliberate decision.

Verify that board minutes contain explicit references to clause 10.33 and the paragraph 10.31 adoption decision, not just general BCP governance. If this is missing from prior minutes, schedule a board paper before the examination cycle to create an authentic governance record — do not backfill minutes, which is a regulatory risk in itself.

Conduct a mock internal review simulating the BNM examiner's line-of-inquiry: assign a compliance officer to challenge the technology team on why each paragraph 10.31 measure was or was not adopted. This dry-run surfaces inconsistencies in the rationale before they are exposed in examination and ensures all team members are aligned on the institution's documented position.

Ensure the evidence pack distinguishes clearly between the institution's entity type and the clause 10.33 applicability criteria — e.g., whether it is an e-money issuer, non-bank merchant acquirer, or intermediary remittance institution. Examiners cross-reference entity classification against licensing records; any ambiguity in how the institution categorises itself relative to the clause scope will be pursued as a separate line of inquiry.

10.34.title: Technology Diversity for Resilience
10.34.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a deliberate, documented strategy for technology diversity rather than an ad-hoc or cost-driven approach that inadvertently creates concentration. Examiners will cross-reference the critical systems inventory against the technology stack to identify single points of failure — particularly where one vendor, one hypervisor, one cloud provider, or one hardware OEM underlies multiple critical systems simultaneously. A key red flag is the absence of a formal concentration risk threshold or the presence of such a threshold that has never triggered remediation action. Examiners will also probe whether diversity extends beyond hardware to include operating systems, middleware, database engines, network equipment vendors, and telecommunications carriers. Institutions that cannot demonstrate how their diversity posture has improved or been deliberately maintained over time — with documented board or senior management oversight — are at significant examination risk.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Diversity Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A board-approved or senior management-approved policy that explicitly mandates technology diversity as a resilience principle for critical systems, including defined concentration risk thresholds, diversity objectives, and governance accountability.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references technology diversity for resilience and maps directly to RMiT 10.34 obligations, with an approval date within the last three years.
Defined and quantified concentration risk thresholds are present (e.g., no single vendor to represent more than 60% of critical system dependencies) with a documented rationale.
Policy scope clearly covers hardware OEMs, hypervisors, operating systems, middleware, database platforms, cloud service providers, and telecommunications carriers.
Governance accountability is assigned to a named role (e.g., CTO or CISO) with defined review cycles and escalation paths to the Board Risk Committee.
Policy includes a remediation obligation when thresholds are breached, with timelines and exception approval requirements.
commonGaps: Policy exists but references only hardware or vendor diversity, omitting software stack components such as operating systems, database engines, and middleware.
Concentration thresholds are absent or expressed qualitatively ('avoid excessive exposure') without measurable limits, making compliance assessment impossible.
Policy has not been reviewed or updated following a significant technology acquisition, cloud migration, or infrastructure refresh, rendering it stale.
Board or senior management approval is missing or delegated too low, meaning the policy lacks the governance weight required for a material resilience control.
Remediation or exception processes are not defined, leaving the institution with no documented response when a concentration risk is identified.
suggestedSources: IT Governance or Technology Risk team SharePoint policy library
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes and resolution records
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream) policy module
Enterprise Risk Management framework documentation repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems Inventory with Technology Stack Taxonomy
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive and current register of all critical systems that maps each system to its full technology stack — including hardware vendor, hypervisor/virtualisation platform, operating system, database engine, middleware, network equipment vendor, cloud service provider, and telecommunications carrier — enabling concentration analysis.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every system classified as critical in the BIA is represented in the inventory, with the inventory and BIA cross-referencing each other by a consistent system identifier.
Each technology stack layer is populated with vendor and product names, version details, and end-of-life status, not left blank or marked 'TBC'.
The inventory is date-stamped with a clear owner and review frequency (ideally quarterly), and the most recent review is no older than six months.
Groupings or tags are applied to identify shared technology dependencies across multiple critical systems (e.g., 'VMware vSphere cluster', 'Oracle DB', 'AWS ap-southeast-1').
The inventory distinguishes between on-premises, co-location, and cloud-hosted systems, and identifies the relevant cloud service provider region and availability zone per system.
commonGaps: Inventory captures application names but omits underlying infrastructure layers (OS, hypervisor, hardware OEM), making concentration analysis at the infrastructure level impossible.
Third-party and outsourced critical systems are excluded from the inventory, creating blind spots for vendor concentration risks the institution does not directly manage.
The inventory is maintained in a spreadsheet with no version control or access audit trail, raising concerns about data integrity during an examination.
Network and telecommunications components (ISPs, MPLS providers, leased lines) are absent from the inventory, missing a significant category of concentration risk.
The inventory has not been updated to reflect recent cloud migrations or infrastructure changes, resulting in stale data that does not reflect the live technology environment.
suggestedSources: Configuration Management Database (CMDB) in ServiceNow, BMC Helix, or equivalent ITSM platform
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system export
Cloud provider consoles (AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, GCP Asset Inventory) for cloud-hosted systems
Network Operations Centre (NOC) asset register for network and carrier inventory
Technology Risk or IT Architecture team SharePoint register
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or CMDB system export (XLSX/CSV/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Concentration Risk Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A formal periodic assessment that analyses the critical systems inventory to identify and score concentration risks — instances where multiple critical systems share the same vendor, platform, technology, or geographic dependency — with risk ratings, remediation actions, and management sign-off.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment is structured against each technology stack layer (hardware, hypervisor, OS, database, middleware, network, cloud, carrier) and identifies specific concentration points with named vendors and system counts.
Each identified concentration is scored against a defined risk matrix (likelihood and impact) and compared against the concentration thresholds defined in the Technology Diversity Policy (10.34-E1).
Remediation actions for breached thresholds are documented with owners, target dates, and status, and open items are tracked in the GRC platform with aging metrics.
The assessment has been reviewed and accepted by Technology Risk and signed off by the CTO or equivalent senior officer within the last 12 months.
Trend analysis is present showing whether concentration levels have improved, remained stable, or worsened compared to the prior assessment cycle, demonstrating active management.
commonGaps: Assessment covers only vendor concentration and omits geographic, technology platform, and telecommunications carrier concentration, producing an incomplete risk picture.
Risk ratings are assigned without documented rationale or without reference to the institution's standard risk matrix, making scores difficult to defend under examination questioning.
Identified high-risk concentrations have open remediation items with no target dates or owners, indicating the assessment is a documentation exercise rather than an active risk management tool.
The assessment has not been updated following a material infrastructure change such as a cloud migration, data centre consolidation, or new critical system onboarding.
Management sign-off is absent or performed at too low a level (e.g., team lead rather than CTO or risk officer), undermining the governance posture of the control.
suggestedSources: Technology Risk team assessment reports stored in GRC platform (ServiceNow, RSA Archer) or SharePoint
Risk and Control Self-Assessment (RCSA) outputs from the Operational Risk Management team
IT Architecture review records for infrastructure change requests
Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) team vendor dependency analysis
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX report with supporting spreadsheet appendix

evidenceItems.name: Technology Procurement and Architecture Review Standards
evidenceItems.description: Documented standards and procedures that embed technology diversity requirements into the procurement and solution architecture approval process, ensuring new technology acquisitions are evaluated against existing concentration risks before approval.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture review or procurement approval checklists include a mandatory technology diversity assessment step that requires applicants to declare the proposed vendor, platform, and technology stack against the existing concentration inventory.

A formal sign-off from the Technology Risk or IT Architecture function is required before any new critical system procurement is approved, with documented evidence of this review in completed project records.
Procurement standards explicitly prohibit or require exception approval for sole-sourcing critical technology components where an alternative vendor is available in the market.
The standards are referenced in the institution's vendor management or IT procurement policy and are consistently applied across both on-premises and cloud technology acquisitions.
Recent procurement decisions for critical systems include completed diversity assessment checklists, demonstrating the standard is operationally embedded and not merely aspirational.
commonGaps: Procurement standards exist for cost and vendor due diligence but contain no requirement to assess technology concentration risk, leaving architecture diversity to the discretion of individual project teams.
Architecture review boards exist but their terms of reference do not include technology diversity as a mandatory assessment criterion, resulting in inconsistent application.
The diversity assessment step is present in the standard but is routinely waived or marked 'N/A' without documented justification, indicating the control is not effectively enforced.
Standards apply only to on-premises procurement and do not cover cloud service selection, creating a gap as the institution migrates workloads to cloud platforms.
Completed procurement checklists for recent critical system projects cannot be produced, indicating the standard is not consistently followed in practice.
suggestedSources: IT Architecture Review Board (ARB) terms of reference and meeting minutes
Procurement and vendor management policy documents in SharePoint
Project Management Office (PMO) project closure reports and architecture sign-off records
IT Steering Committee papers for major technology investment decisions
Technology Risk team architecture review checklists
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy with supporting completed checklist samples (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Resilience Testing Evidence Demonstrating Diversity Effectiveness
evidenceItems.description: Records of disaster recovery tests, failover exercises, or resilience simulations that specifically validate that technology diversity mechanisms function as intended — demonstrating that the loss or failure of one technology component does not cascade across systems sharing the same dependency.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plans explicitly state the technology diversity objective being validated (e.g., 'verify that failure of primary hypervisor cluster does not impact systems on secondary platform') rather than treating diversity as an implicit by-product of a generic DR test.

Test results document the actual technology components involved, including vendor, platform version, and the systems affected and unaffected, enabling auditors to verify diversity was genuinely tested.
Post-test reports identify any diversity gaps discovered during testing, with findings logged as risk items in the GRC platform and assigned remediation actions.
Tests cover multiple technology stack layers including network carrier failover, cloud region failover, and database platform redundancy — not solely server or application-layer failover.
Test frequency aligns with the criticality of the systems being tested, with critical system diversity validated at least annually and results reported to senior management.
commonGaps: DR test records demonstrate failover between primary and secondary sites but both sites use identical technology stacks, meaning the test validates availability but not technology diversity.
Test plans are generic and do not reference specific technology concentration scenarios, making it impossible to determine whether diversity objectives were assessed during the exercise.

Tests are conducted at the application layer only and do not exercise infrastructure-level diversity (hypervisor, hardware, network carrier), leaving the most material concentration risks unvalidated.
Post-test findings related to diversity gaps are not tracked as formal risk items, and there is no evidence that diversity failures identified during testing have been remediated.
Test results are not reported to senior management or the Board Risk Committee, meaning governance oversight of diversity effectiveness is absent.
suggestedSources: IT Operations or Business Continuity Management team DR test plans and results reports
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records for failover exercise change requests
ITSM platform (ServiceNow) incident and problem records from DR test execution
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee papers referencing resilience test outcomes
Cloud provider resilience test reports (AWS Resilience Hub, Azure Site Recovery test records)
evidenceItems.format: PDF test plan and results report, with system screenshots or log exports as supporting evidence

evidenceItems.name: Management Reporting on Technology Diversity Posture
evidenceItems.description: Regular management information (MI) reports presented to senior management or board-level committees that communicate the institution's current technology diversity posture, concentration risk levels, remediation progress, and any material changes to the diversity risk profile.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are presented at least quarterly to a board-level or senior management committee (e.g., Board Risk Committee, IT Steering Committee) with meeting minutes confirming receipt and discussion.
Reports include quantitative metrics such as the number of critical systems per vendor, concentration ratios by technology stack layer, and the number of open remediation items by risk rating.
Trend data spanning at least four reporting periods is present, enabling the committee to assess whether the institution's diversity posture is improving, stable, or deteriorating.

Reports are linked to the risk appetite and concentration thresholds defined in the Technology Diversity Policy, with a clear traffic-light or RAG status indicating whether thresholds have been breached.
Committee minutes reflect genuine challenge and decision-making on diversity risk items rather than passive receipt, demonstrating active board engagement with this regulatory obligation.
commonGaps: Technology diversity posture is reported as a subset of a broader IT risk dashboard with insufficient granularity — concentration by specific vendor and stack layer is not visible to the committee.
Reports are produced ad-hoc or only in response to incidents rather than on a defined periodic schedule, indicating diversity risk is not treated as a standing management concern.
Meeting minutes record that reports were 'noted' without evidence of committee challenge, discussion, or direction, suggesting governance is superficial.
Reports do not track remediation progress on identified concentration risks over time, making it impossible to demonstrate that management is actively reducing the risk.
The reporting chain does not reach board level — reports are consumed only at operational management level, below the governance threshold expected by BNM for a material resilience control.
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee and IT Steering Committee meeting packs and signed minutes
Technology Risk team MI reporting templates and distribution records in SharePoint
GRC platform dashboard exports (ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) used as source data for reports
Chief Risk Officer (CRO) or CTO quarterly risk report to the Board
evidenceItems.format: PDF management report with meeting minutes as corroborating evidence

auditTips: Before the examination, map every critical system identified in the BIA to the technology concentration assessment — BNM examiners frequently cross-reference these two documents and any system present in the BIA but absent from the concentration analysis will be treated as a gap.

Prepare a single-page concentration heat map showing the number of critical systems dependent on each major vendor, hypervisor platform, cloud provider, and telecommunications carrier — this visual demonstrates analytical depth and immediately answers the examiner's core question about 'excessive exposure to similar technology risks'.

Review all open remediation items in your concentration risk assessment before the examination; for each item that is overdue or has no owner, either close it with documented evidence of resolution or prepare a written management-approved extension with a credible revised target date, as unresolved items without governance oversight are a primary red flag.

Validate that your most recent DR or resilience test explicitly tested a diversity scenario — if your test records do not reference technology diversity objectives, prepare a supplementary memo from the test owner explaining which diversity controls were exercised, as this is a common examination question that catches institutions unprepared.

Confirm that your Technology Diversity Policy references RMiT 10.34 explicitly and that the concentration thresholds defined in the policy are quantitative — examiners will probe whether the institution can demonstrate a clear, measurable definition of 'excessive exposure' rather than relying on subjective judgement.

Pull at least two recent procurement or architecture review cases for critical systems and verify that completed technology diversity assessment checklists exist for each — the ability to produce operational evidence that diversity requirements are enforced at the point of procurement significantly strengthens the institution's compliance narrative.

Ensure the most recent board or senior management committee paper on technology diversity posture is dated within the last quarter and that meeting minutes reflect substantive discussion — examiners assess governance quality by reading minutes, not just reviewing reports, and 'noted' without recorded discussion will prompt follow-up questioning on the adequacy of board oversight.

10.35.title: Service Interruption Response
10.35.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing Clause 10.35 focus primarily on whether the institution has moved beyond documented intent to demonstrated operational capability. They will request evidence of actual service interruption incidents and trace the full lifecycle — from detection to escalation, customer notification, recovery, and post-incident disclosure — comparing observed timelines against the thresholds defined in Clause 10.32. A significant red flag is the absence of formalised third-party contractual arrangements that explicitly bind service providers to recovery performance obligations. Examiners will also probe whether the customer communication plan has been tested and whether customers were genuinely informed during past incidents with actionable alternatives, not merely retrospective apologies. For institutions approaching the October 2027 deadline, examiners will assess readiness of the quarterly disclosure mechanism, including the governance process for approving and publishing availability statistics within the mandated 15 calendar day window.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Escalation and Response Procedure
evidenceItems.description: A formalised, version-controlled procedure document defining the end-to-end response process for digital service interruptions, performance degradation, and intermittent failures. This document must map escalation paths, decision authorities, alternative arrangement triggers, and recovery thresholds tied explicitly to the timeframes in Clause 10.32, demonstrating that the institution can resume services promptly under all plausible scenarios.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure explicitly references Clause 10.32 recovery timeframes (RTO/RPO targets) and maps each severity tier to a specific escalation path and decision authority with named roles
Alternative arrangement triggers are defined with objective criteria (e.g., if primary channel unavailable > X minutes, activate fallback channel Y), not left to ad-hoc judgement
Document is version-controlled with an approval date within the last 12 months, signed by the CTO or equivalent, and reflects the current technology architecture
Procedure covers all plausible scenarios including third-party outages, partial degradation, and intermittent failures — not only total outages
A tested decision matrix or RACI chart is embedded, clearly identifying who has authority to invoke alternative arrangements at each escalation tier
commonGaps: Recovery timeframes in the procedure are generic or aspirational and do not align with the specific RTO/RPO thresholds approved under Clause 10.32, making cross-referencing impossible during examination
Alternative arrangements are listed without activation criteria, leaving escalation dependent on individual judgement rather than objective thresholds
Procedure covers only complete outages; performance degradation and intermittent failures — explicitly named in Clause 10.35 — are not addressed
Document has not been reviewed or updated following significant infrastructure changes, cloud migrations, or third-party onboarding, rendering it inaccurate
Escalation path does not identify decision-makers by role with sufficient specificity, resulting in ambiguity during actual incidents
suggestedSources: IT Service Management (ITSM) platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — procedure library or knowledge base module
GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) — policy and procedure register
SharePoint intranet document management system under Technology Risk or IT Operations section
Business Continuity Management system — technology recovery procedures annex
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signature page

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Drill and Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of tabletop exercises, simulation drills, and live failover tests conducted to validate that escalation procedures, decision-making chains, and alternative arrangements function as designed under realistic interruption scenarios. These records demonstrate that the institution's response capability is operationally proven, not merely documented, and that recovery within Clause 10.32 timeframes is achievable under plausible failure scenarios.
whatGoodLooksLike: Drill records cover multiple plausible scenarios including third-party provider outages, partial channel degradation, and peak-traffic interruptions — not only full system failures
Each drill record captures actual time-to-escalate, time-to-decision, and time-to-recovery metrics and compares them explicitly against Clause 10.32 thresholds, with pass/fail determination
After-action reports identify gaps found during the drill, assign remediation owners, and include evidence that remediation was completed before the next scheduled drill
Drills are conducted at least annually, with evidence that senior management (CISO, CTO, or Board Risk Committee) reviewed results and approved any remediation plans
Test records include participation logs confirming that third-party service providers were included in relevant scenarios, validating the coordination arrangements required under Clause 10.35(b)
commonGaps: Drills are conducted but results are not benchmarked against Clause 10.32 recovery timeframes, making it impossible to confirm regulatory compliance from test evidence alone
Only full-outage scenarios are tested; performance degradation and intermittent failure scenarios — which require different detection and escalation logic — are never exercised
After-action gaps are documented but remediation is not tracked to closure, leaving known weaknesses unresolved at the time of examination
Third-party service providers are excluded from drills, meaning coordination arrangements under Clause 10.35(b) have never been validated in practice
Drills occur less frequently than annually or are conducted only at the team level without senior management visibility or sign-off
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management system — drill and exercise management module
ITSM platform — major incident simulation records and post-incident review logs
GRC platform — control testing evidence repository
Technology Risk team SharePoint site — annual drill calendar and after-action report archive
evidenceItems.format: PDF after-action report with attendance register, scenario description, metrics table, and remediation tracker

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Service Provider Recovery Arrangements
evidenceItems.description: Formalised contractual and operational documentation establishing clear accountabilities between the institution and its third-party service providers for managing performance degradation and service interruptions. This includes contract clauses, service level agreements, escalation matrices, and coordination procedures that ensure effective coordination and timely recovery to normal performance levels as required under Clause 10.35(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Master Service Agreements and SLAs contain explicit recovery time commitments aligned to the institution's Clause 10.32 thresholds, with financial penalties or step-in rights for non-performance
A documented joint escalation matrix exists for each material third-party provider, identifying named contacts and alternates on both sides with response time obligations for each escalation tier
Coordination procedures specify how the institution and provider will communicate, share diagnostic information, and make joint recovery decisions during an active incident
Accountabilities for managing performance degradation — not only full outages — are explicitly defined in provider contracts, covering intermittent failures and partial service degradation
Third-party recovery performance is reviewed at least quarterly via service review meetings, with minutes evidencing that SLA breaches and near-misses are tracked and remediated
commonGaps: SLAs define uptime targets but do not specify recovery time obligations or coordination responsibilities during active incidents, leaving accountability gaps that are only discovered during an actual outage
Escalation matrices exist only at the account manager level; technical escalation paths to the provider's engineering or incident command teams are undefined

Arrangements cover only complete outages; performance degradation and intermittent failures are not addressed in contractual terms, creating a regulatory gap for the scenarios explicitly named in Clause 10.35
Provider contracts have not been reviewed following the issuance of RMiT or its amendments, meaning recovery obligations may not meet current regulatory expectations
Service review meeting minutes do not record SLA performance data or discussions of degradation incidents, providing no evidence that the institution actively oversees provider recovery capability
suggestedSources: Contract Management System (e.g., Icertis, Ironclad, or SharePoint contract library) — executed MSAs, SLAs, and addenda for material technology providers
Vendor Management team — third-party risk assessment register and provider performance scorecards
Technology Risk or Procurement — joint escalation matrix and coordination procedure documents
GRC platform — third-party risk management module with provider inventory and control evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF executed contracts with SLA schedules; DOCX/PDF joint escalation matrix; meeting minutes in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Service Interruption Customer Communication Plan
evidenceItems.description: A documented communication plan specifically governing how the institution will inform affected customers during service interruptions, manage elevated volumes of customer feedback, provide frequent recovery updates, and deliver actionable information on available alternatives for customers with urgent needs. This plan demonstrates compliance with Clause 10.35(c) and must be sufficiently operational to be executable by communications and operations staff during an active incident.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan specifies maximum time-to-first-notification thresholds from incident detection for each severity tier, with pre-approved message templates for SMS, push notification, website banner, and social media channels

Escalation procedures for managing high volumes of customer feedback are defined, including call centre surge capacity arrangements, chatbot routing rules, and authorised social media response protocols
Update frequency commitments are specified (e.g., customer-facing updates every 30 minutes during a Severity 1 incident) with a defined owner responsible for each update cycle

Actionable alternatives are pre-documented for each digital channel by product type (e.g., if mobile banking is unavailable: use internet banking, ATM, or call 1-800-XXX-XXXX for urgent transfers), enabling staff to direct customers immediately without improvisation

Plan has been reviewed and approved within the last 12 months, and evidence exists that call centre and social media teams have been trained on the plan and participated in at least one communication drill
commonGaps: Plan contains communication principles but no time-to-notification thresholds or update frequency commitments, making it impossible to assess whether communications were timely during past incidents
Message templates are absent or overly generic; staff must draft communications from scratch during an active incident, introducing delays and inconsistency in regulatory-sensitive customer messaging
Actionable alternatives are not pre-defined by channel and product type, meaning customers with urgent needs receive only a generic apology rather than specific guidance on what to do
The plan addresses initial notification but provides no structure for managing customer feedback volume surges — call centre and social media team responsibilities during an incident are undefined
Plan exists as a standalone document but has never been integrated into the incident response procedure or ITSM platform, so it is unlikely to be located or followed during an actual interruption
suggestedSources: Corporate Communications or Customer Experience team — incident communication runbook and message template library
ITSM platform — major incident management playbook with embedded communication tasks and SLA timers
Contact Centre Operations — surge capacity plan and escalation procedures
GRC or BCM system — business continuity communication plan annex
Social Media Management platform (e.g., Sprout Social, Hootsuite) — pre-approved response templates for service incidents
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX communication plan with embedded message templates and escalation procedures

evidenceItems.name: Real-Time Digital Service Availability Status Page
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of a customer-accessible mechanism for checking the real-time availability and performance status of the institution's digital services, as required under Clause 10.35(d). This includes screenshots, architecture documentation, and publishing governance records demonstrating that the status page is publicly accessible, reflects live system state, covers all material digital channels, and is updated promptly during and after incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Status page is publicly accessible without login, linked prominently from the corporate website homepage and digital banking login pages, ensuring customers can access it even when authenticated services are unavailable

All material digital services and delivery channels are listed individually (mobile banking app, internet banking portal, bill payment gateway, FPX, DuitNow, ATM network) with real-time status indicators
Status updates during incidents are timestamped and published within the timeframes specified in the customer communication plan, with audit logs available to prove publication times
Architecture documentation confirms that the status page is hosted on infrastructure independent of production banking systems, so it remains accessible even during a primary system outage

Historical incident logs are retained on the status page for at least 90 days, providing customers with transparency and providing the institution with a source record for quarterly disclosures under Clause 10.35(e)
commonGaps: Status page exists but is not linked from the corporate website homepage or banking login page, effectively making it unfindable by customers during an outage when navigation is impaired

Status page reflects only high-level system health and does not disaggregate by individual digital channel or service, providing insufficient information for customers to determine which specific services are affected
Status updates during past incidents were delayed well beyond the institution's own communication plan thresholds, and no audit log exists to verify publication times for regulatory review
Status page is hosted on the same infrastructure as primary banking systems, meaning it goes offline precisely when customers most need it — during a major service interruption
Page shows only current status with no historical incident log, preventing cross-referencing with quarterly availability disclosures and depriving customers of trend visibility
suggestedSources: IT Operations team — status page platform (e.g., Statuspage.io, Atlassian Statuspage, Freshstatus, or custom-built solution) with admin access and audit logs
Web analytics platform — visitor traffic logs confirming the status page was accessed by customers during past incidents
Corporate website content management system — page configuration and link placement records
Technology architecture documentation — hosting topology confirming independence of status page infrastructure
evidenceItems.format: Screenshots (PDF), architecture diagram (PDF/Visio), audit log export (CSV/PDF), platform configuration record

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly Service Availability Disclosure Records
evidenceItems.description: Published quarterly records of the institution's digital service availability track record, disclosed within 15 calendar days of each quarter-end in accordance with Clause 10.35(e), effective from 15 October 2027. Evidence must demonstrate both the governance process for preparing and approving the disclosure and the published output itself, including the methodology used to calculate availability metrics.
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented methodology defines how availability is measured for each digital channel (e.g., measured uptime as a percentage of total minutes in the quarter, excluding agreed scheduled maintenance windows), ensuring consistency and auditability across disclosure periods

Publication governance records show that each quarterly disclosure was approved by an appropriate authority (e.g., CTO, Chief Risk Officer, or Board Risk Committee) before publication, with approval dated within the 15 calendar day window

Published disclosures are accessible on the corporate website and timestamped, with the publication date provably within 15 calendar days of each quarter-end (31 March, 30 June, 30 September, 31 December)
Availability data in the disclosure is traceable to source system monitoring logs, enabling examiners to verify the accuracy of published figures against raw telemetry
Disclosures include all material digital channels individually and not only an aggregate figure, consistent with the disaggregated view required for meaningful customer transparency
commonGaps: No documented methodology exists for calculating availability, meaning figures cannot be independently verified and may be inconsistently computed across quarters
Publication dates fall outside the 15 calendar day window, breaching the explicit deadline in Clause 10.35(e) — even a single late disclosure constitutes a regulatory finding

Disclosures present only an aggregate availability figure across all digital services rather than service-by-service breakdowns, reducing transparency and preventing meaningful customer or regulatory assessment
Published figures cannot be reconciled to source monitoring data, creating an audit gap between the disclosed metric and the underlying system record
Institution has not yet established the governance process, publication platform, or data pipeline required to meet the October 2027 deadline, and no implementation roadmap exists
suggestedSources: IT Operations monitoring platform (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, Grafana, SolarWinds) — availability telemetry and uptime reports for each digital channel
Corporate website CMS — published disclosure pages with publication timestamps
GRC platform or governance secretariat — approval workflow records and sign-off documentation for each quarterly disclosure
Technology Risk or Compliance team — disclosure methodology document and quarterly preparation checklist
evidenceItems.format: Published HTML/PDF disclosure on corporate website; PDF approval record; source data export (CSV/PDF)

auditTips: Cross-reference every past major incident (Severity 1 and Severity 2) logged in the ITSM platform against the customer communication records for that incident — verify that first customer notification occurred within the threshold defined in the communication plan, and that update frequency matched the plan's commitments. Examiners will perform exactly this exercise, so identifying and explaining any gaps before examination is essential.

Map each sub-clause of 10.35 (a through e) to a specific document or record and present this mapping as an evidence schedule at the opening of the examination. This demonstrates structured compliance readiness and immediately signals to examiners that the institution has understood the regulatory obligations at a granular level rather than treating the clause as a single undifferentiated requirement.

For Clause 10.35(b), pull every material third-party technology provider contract and highlight the specific SLA clauses and joint escalation matrix provisions. Examiners will ask to see these provisions directly. If any material provider's contract lacks explicit recovery time obligations or coordination procedures, initiate a contract amendment or addendum before examination and document the remediation effort.

Prepare a service interruption case study for at least one recent incident, documenting the timeline from detection to resolution with evidence at each stage: ITSM incident ticket, escalation emails or call logs, customer notification timestamps from the messaging platform, status page update audit log, and customer feedback volume from the contact centre. This case study is the most compelling evidence that the institution's response capability is operational rather than merely documented.

Verify that the real-time status page remains accessible when production banking systems are degraded by running a test that simulates reduced connectivity and confirms the status page loads independently. Document this test with a timestamp and result. Examiners specifically probe whether the status page is hosted on independent infrastructure, as a status page that goes down with the services it monitors provides no regulatory value.

For institutions within 18 months of the 15 October 2027 quarterly disclosure deadline, present a readiness roadmap to examiners showing the methodology document, governance approval process, data pipeline from monitoring tools, and publication platform — even if the first disclosure has not yet been made. Examiners will expect evidence of preparation, not merely awareness of the deadline.

Validate that drill and test records specifically capture time-to-escalation and time-to-recovery metrics and compare them against Clause 10.32 thresholds. If drills show that recovery times exceed approved thresholds under any tested scenario, ensure after-action remediation plans are completed and documented before examination. Unresolved gaps from prior drills are among the most damaging findings an examiner can surface.

10.36.title: Enterprise Network Design
10.36.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a formally documented, board-approved enterprise network architecture that explicitly addresses reliability, scalability, and security as design principles — not merely as aspirational goals. Examiners look for evidence that the network design is deliberately aligned with current business activities and that capacity planning artefacts demonstrate forward-looking consideration of growth. A key red flag is a network topology that has grown organically without governance oversight, resulting in undocumented segments, shadow infrastructure, or capacity that lags business demand. Examiners will triangulate network architecture documents against incident logs, capacity utilisation data, and vendor contracts to identify gaps between documented design intent and operational reality. Particular scrutiny is applied to whether the institution can demonstrate that security controls are embedded in the network design itself, rather than bolted on reactively.

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Network Architecture Document
evidenceItems.description: A formal, version-controlled document describing the end-to-end logical and physical design of the institution's enterprise network, including topology diagrams, network segmentation strategy, routing architecture, and the design principles (reliability, scalability, security) underpinning each layer. This demonstrates that the network has been deliberately designed rather than assembled ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is version-controlled, dated within the last 12 months, and carries approval signatures from the CISO and CTO or equivalent governance authority
Includes both logical and physical network topology diagrams with clearly labelled segments (core, distribution, access, DMZ, OT/IoT where applicable)
Explicitly maps network design decisions to business requirements, including named business units or critical applications supported by each segment
Design rationale section articulates how reliability, scalability, and security were balanced as competing design constraints
Change history log demonstrates iterative updates in response to business changes, audits, or incident findings
