commonGaps: Architecture document exists but has not been reviewed or updated in over 24 months, rendering it unreliable as evidence of current design intent
Topology diagrams cover only the data centre core network and omit branch offices, remote access infrastructure, or cloud interconnects
Document describes the 'as-designed' state with no reconciliation against the 'as-built' state confirmed through network discovery tools
Reliability and scalability are referenced only in generic terms without quantified design targets such as uptime SLAs, throughput thresholds, or redundancy ratios
Approval by senior management or board-level technology committee is absent, making it difficult to demonstrate governance oversight of the design
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team repository (Confluence, SharePoint, or EA tool such as Sparx Enterprise Architect or LeanIX)
IT Steering Committee or Board Technology Committee meeting minutes referencing architecture approvals
Network Operations Centre (NOC) documentation portal
GRC platform document register (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded Visio or draw.io topology diagrams; version-controlled in a document management system

evidenceItems.name: Network Capacity Planning and Scalability Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A periodic assessment that analyses current network utilisation against capacity baselines and projects forward-looking capacity requirements aligned to the institution's business growth plans. This demonstrates that scalability is an active management discipline, not a passive design assumption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment is conducted at least annually and references the institution's strategic business plan or IT roadmap to derive growth assumptions for the projection period
Utilisation data covers all critical network segments and links with trend analysis spanning a minimum of 12 months of historical baseline
Report includes explicit thresholds at which capacity upgrade decisions are triggered, with ownership assigned to named roles
Scalability scenarios include at least one stress scenario (e.g., merger/acquisition, product launch, regulatory-driven volume increase) with quantified network impact
Remediation actions from prior capacity assessments are tracked and closed, with evidence of delivery against committed timelines
commonGaps: Capacity planning is performed reactively only when utilisation alerts fire, with no forward-looking projections tied to business growth assumptions
Assessment covers WAN and core links but omits internal LAN segments, internet egress points, or cloud connectivity bandwidth
Growth projections are not linked to any documented business plan and are instead based on flat percentage assumptions with no business justification
No defined capacity threshold or trigger criteria exist, so the point at which the institution must act to avoid congestion is undefined
Reports are produced by the network team but never reviewed or endorsed by business or senior IT leadership, undermining their credibility as governance artefacts
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platforms (SolarWinds NTA, PRTG, Cisco DNA Center, or equivalent) for raw utilisation data exports
IT capacity management process within ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Business planning or strategy documents from Finance or Corporate Planning division
Annual technology budget submissions that include network investment rationale
evidenceItems.format: PDF report with supporting spreadsheet data exports and graphed utilisation trend charts

evidenceItems.name: Network Security Architecture and Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that security is a core design principle embedded within the enterprise network architecture, including network segmentation policy, firewall rule-set governance, zero-trust or least-privilege access controls, and encryption standards applied across network tiers. This provides evidence that the 'secure' design obligation in clause 10.36 is met through deliberate architectural choices.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network segmentation policy is documented and enforced, with distinct zones for user endpoints, servers, DMZ, OT/IoT, and third-party connectivity, each with defined traffic control rules
Firewall rule-set reviews are conducted at least annually with evidence of stale or overly permissive rules being remediated and approved by the security authority
Encryption standards are specified for data in transit across all network tiers, including internal east-west traffic for sensitive segments, with non-compliant links identified and remediated
Network access control (NAC) or equivalent mechanism is documented and operational, ensuring only authorised and compliant devices can join the network
Security architecture documentation is cross-referenced to the institution's Information Security Policy and reviewed whenever significant changes to the network design are made
commonGaps: Firewall rules have accumulated over years without periodic review, resulting in rules for decommissioned systems or services that introduce unnecessary attack surface
Network segmentation is documented at a high level but microsegmentation between sensitive systems within the same zone is absent, increasing lateral movement risk
Encryption is enforced at the perimeter but unencrypted protocols remain in use for internal administrative traffic such as Telnet, SNMPv1, or HTTP management interfaces
No formal process exists to update security architecture documentation when network changes are made, causing the document to diverge from operational reality within months of approval
Security controls are described only in qualitative terms with no evidence of technical validation through penetration testing, configuration audits, or automated compliance scanning
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) for network security event correlation evidence
Firewall management platform (Tufin, AlgoSec, Palo Alto Panorama) for rule-set review reports
Vulnerability management platform for network device configuration compliance scan results
CISO office documentation repository in SharePoint or GRC platform
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy and architecture documents; system-generated firewall rule-set reports in PDF or CSV

evidenceItems.name: Network Reliability and Redundancy Design Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and operational records demonstrating that the enterprise network is designed for high availability through redundant paths, failover mechanisms, and resilient hardware configurations. This directly evidences the 'reliable' design obligation and provides assurance that network failures do not result in unacceptable business disruption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network architecture document explicitly identifies single points of failure and documents the redundancy mechanism deployed to mitigate each (e.g., dual ISP uplinks, HSRP/VRRP for gateway redundancy, stacked or dual-homed switches)
Uptime and availability metrics for critical network segments are captured and reported, demonstrating performance against defined SLAs for the preceding 12 months
Failover testing is conducted at least annually for all critical redundancy mechanisms, with documented test plans, results, and recovery time observations
Hardware lifecycle management records confirm that network devices are within vendor support lifecycles and covered by maintenance contracts with defined hardware replacement SLAs

Network availability incidents are logged in the ITSM platform and root cause analyses reference whether the design provided the expected resilience, creating a feedback loop into architecture reviews
commonGaps: Redundant links are physically diverse at the logical level but share common physical infrastructure such as the same duct, building entry point, or upstream provider, negating the resilience benefit
Failover mechanisms are configured but never tested end-to-end under realistic conditions, so actual recovery times and failure modes are unknown
Availability SLAs are defined for application tiers but not for underlying network segments, making it impossible to attribute availability failures to the network layer specifically
Hardware maintenance contracts have lapsed or provide only best-efforts support with no committed replacement times for critical devices
No formal process links network incidents back to architecture reviews, so recurring reliability failures do not drive design improvements
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform availability reports (SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix, or equivalent)
ITSM incident and problem management records (ServiceNow, Jira) filtered for network category
Failover and DR test plans and results from IT Operations or BCP team
Vendor maintenance contract register from Procurement or IT Asset Management
evidenceItems.format: PDF availability reports; ITSM-generated incident summaries; signed-off test results in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Network Change Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Records from the institution's formal change management process covering network infrastructure changes, demonstrating that modifications to the enterprise network are governed, assessed for risk, and implemented in a controlled manner that preserves the integrity of the approved architecture.
whatGoodLooksLike: All network changes are processed through a formal change advisory board (CAB) or equivalent governance process with documented risk and impact assessment prior to implementation
Change records reference the enterprise network architecture document to confirm that proposed changes are consistent with or formally update the approved design
Post-implementation reviews are completed for significant network changes, confirming that the change achieved its objective without unintended impact to reliability, scalability, or security
Emergency change records are proportionately small relative to normal changes, and each emergency change is followed by a post-incident review documenting why normal process was bypassed
Change success and failure rates are tracked as KPIs and reported to IT leadership, providing a governance-level view of network change quality
commonGaps: Network changes are frequently implemented outside the formal change process using informal approval channels, leaving no audit trail and undermining architecture governance
Change requests contain insufficient technical detail to assess impact, with risk assessments completed as formalities rather than substantive evaluations
No linkage exists between change records and the network architecture document, so it is impossible to determine whether cumulative changes have drifted the network away from its approved design
Post-implementation reviews are not consistently performed, meaning failed or partially successful changes are closed without documented lessons learned
Emergency changes are disproportionately frequent, suggesting that the normal change process is too slow to meet operational needs and is being routinely circumvented
suggestedSources: ITSM change management module (ServiceNow Change Management, Jira Service Management)
Change Advisory Board meeting minutes from IT Operations governance meetings
Network configuration management database (CMDB) change history
GRC platform for change management policy and compliance tracking
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system-generated change records exported as PDF or CSV; CAB meeting minutes in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Technology Roadmap Alignment Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the enterprise network design is explicitly connected to the institution's future business and technology growth plans, providing forward-looking assurance that the network will remain capable of supporting planned expansion, new products, digital channels, or regulatory requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: A multi-year network technology roadmap exists that is aligned to the institution's strategic plan and identifies planned network investments, upgrades, and architectural transitions (e.g., SD-WAN adoption, cloud on-ramp, 5G integration)
The roadmap is reviewed at least annually and updated to reflect changes in business strategy, with version history demonstrating iterative refinement
Investment cases or business cases for significant network projects reference both current business requirements and future growth assumptions, demonstrating deliberate forward planning
The network roadmap is endorsed by the IT Steering Committee or equivalent senior governance body, demonstrating that future scalability is a board-level concern
Dependencies between network roadmap milestones and business programme deliverables are explicitly mapped, preventing business initiatives from outpacing network readiness
commonGaps: No multi-year network roadmap exists; network investment is planned on an annual budgeting cycle only, with no visibility beyond 12 months
A roadmap exists but is maintained by the network team in isolation without reference to or endorsement by business strategy owners or senior IT governance
Roadmap milestones are described in vague terms such as 'network upgrade' without specifying the capability being delivered, the business need it addresses, or the scalability benefit achieved
The roadmap has not been updated to reflect significant business changes such as new digital product launches, branch network expansion, or cloud migration programmes, rendering it stale
No formal linkage exists between the network roadmap and the institution's IT budget cycle, meaning planned investments may not be funded in practice
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture function for technology roadmap documentation (LeanIX, Ardoq, or SharePoint-based EA repository)
IT Steering Committee or Board Technology Committee approved technology strategy documents
Finance or IT budget submissions referencing network infrastructure investment
Business transformation programme office for business growth plan alignment evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX strategic roadmap document; presentation deck approved at governance committee level

auditTips: Cross-reference the enterprise network architecture document against the output of an automated network discovery tool run within the past 90 days — discrepancies between documented and discovered topology are a reliable indicator that the architecture document does not reflect operational reality and will be a primary examiner finding.

Obtain the institution's three-year business plan or digital strategy and map specific growth initiatives (new branches, digital channels, volume projections) to corresponding network capacity planning artefacts to demonstrate that scalability planning is anchored to real business assumptions rather than generic percentages.

Pull the last 12 months of network-category incidents from the ITSM platform and analyse whether recurring reliability events point to design weaknesses; prepare a brief narrative explaining how each recurring issue has been or is being addressed at the architectural level to pre-empt examiner questions about reliability.

Verify that all critical network redundancy mechanisms — including dual ISP failover, gateway redundancy protocols, and link aggregation — have documented failover test results from within the past 12 months, and that test scripts and observed recovery times are on file, as BNM examiners frequently ask to see test evidence rather than accepting design assertions alone.

Prepare a 'design principles traceability matrix' that explicitly maps each of the three obligations in clause 10.36 (reliable, scalable, secure) to specific documented controls, policies, and test results, enabling examiners to quickly see how the institution has interpreted and operationalised each design obligation without extensive document navigation.

Audit the firewall change log and network CMDB change history for the past year and identify any changes that were implemented without a corresponding approved change record in the ITSM platform — unmanaged changes are a significant red flag and must be reconciled before examination, either by retrospectively documenting them or by demonstrating that they have since been remediated.

Confirm that the network architecture document, capacity assessment, and technology roadmap all carry consistent version dates and approval signatures, and that each references the others by document identifier — examiners look for a coherent document ecosystem rather than a collection of standalone artefacts, and inconsistency between related documents suggests governance gaps.

10.37.title: Network Service Reliability
10.37.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on two interlinked obligations: first, that the institution has affirmatively identified all critical systems and mapped their network service dependencies; and second, that each such dependency has demonstrable redundancy eliminating single points of failure at every layer — physical, logical, and provider. Red flags include reliance on a single ISP or carrier circuit for internet-facing critical systems, flat or insufficiently segmented network architectures, and the absence of documented and tested failover procedures. Examiners also assess whether protection against cyber threats (e.g., DDoS mitigation, intrusion prevention) is embedded into the redundant network design rather than bolted on as an afterthought. Evidence that failover paths have never been tested, or that test results show extended recovery times inconsistent with the institution's RTOs, will draw significant scrutiny.

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Network Architecture Diagrams
evidenceItems.description: Formal, version-controlled network topology diagrams that depict the end-to-end network path for each critical system, explicitly annotating all redundancy mechanisms, failover paths, and the elimination of single points of failure at physical, logical, and provider layers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagrams are maintained at an appropriate classification level and reviewed/approved at least annually by the CISO or Head of Infrastructure, with version history evidencing change control
Each critical system is individually traced to its upstream network services, ISP circuits, core switches, firewalls, and load balancers, with SPOF analysis annotations on the diagram itself
Redundant components (dual ISPs, diverse fibre paths, HA firewall pairs, redundant WAN links) are clearly labelled with the geographic or logical separation distance
Diagrams distinguish between active-active and active-passive configurations and document automatic versus manual failover mechanisms for each redundant path
Architecture includes cyber threat protection components (DDoS scrubbing, IPS clusters, WAF) integrated into the redundant design, not as a single inline choke point
commonGaps: Diagrams depict the intended target architecture rather than the current as-built state, with undocumented deviations discovered during walk-through interviews
SPOF analysis is absent or limited to the core network, omitting last-mile circuits, third-party managed links, or co-location cross-connects
Cyber threat protection appliances (firewalls, IPS) are shown as a single active instance with a cold-standby that has no documented or tested activation procedure
Diagrams have not been updated following significant infrastructure changes such as cloud migration, data centre moves, or vendor consolidation
Physical and logical diversity of fibre paths is assumed but not confirmed with carrier documentation, meaning two circuits appear redundant but share a common duct or exchange point
suggestedSources: Network Engineering team (Visio / Lucidchart / draw.io repositories)
IT GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — architecture artefacts module
SharePoint/Confluence infrastructure documentation library
Data centre colocation provider portal — cross-connect and cage layout records
Change Management System — approved RFCs that modified network topology
evidenceItems.format: PDF/Visio/PNG (versioned), with accompanying SPOF analysis register in XLSX

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Inventory with Network Dependency Mapping
evidenceItems.description: A formal register that cross-references the institution's approved list of critical systems (as defined under RMiT) against the specific network services, circuits, and providers they depend upon, enabling examiners to verify that every critical system is covered by the reliability and redundancy obligations of clause 10.37.
whatGoodLooksLike: The critical system register is formally approved by senior management and aligns with the institution's current BIA, with consistent system names and criticality ratings across both documents

Each critical system entry records its primary and secondary network service provider, circuit IDs, contracted SLA uptime, and the redundancy model employed (e.g., dual ISP BGP, MPLS with 4G failover)

Network dependency mapping identifies all intermediate network hops including third-party managed networks, cloud provider interconnects, and payment network gateways, not just the institution's own infrastructure
The register includes a residual SPOF column documenting any accepted risks with compensating controls and sign-off by the appropriate risk owner
Mapping is reviewed and reconciled against actual network configurations (e.g., router BGP tables, IPAM records) at least semi-annually
commonGaps: Critical system classification is inconsistent between the BIA, the network dependency register, and what is actually protected by redundant network paths, creating gaps for lower-tier systems that nonetheless process sensitive data
Third-party and outsourced network services (e.g., payment switching networks, cloud connectivity) are omitted from the dependency map, leaving external SPOFs unaddressed
The register lists provider names but lacks circuit IDs or technical identifiers, making it impossible to verify physical diversity claims with the carrier
Residual SPOFs are documented but have no associated remediation plan or risk acceptance that has been reviewed at an appropriate governance level
The register is maintained as a standalone spreadsheet with no integration into the ITSM or CMDB, resulting in drift as infrastructure changes occur
suggestedSources: IT GRC platform — critical asset register module
CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) — CI relationships between application CIs and network CIs
Business Continuity Management system — BIA critical system list
Procurement/vendor management system — ISP and carrier contract records
Network Operations Centre (NOC) — circuit inventory and provider SLA schedules
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export, cross-referenced to BIA document (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Network Redundancy and Failover Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence of periodic testing of redundant network paths and failover mechanisms for critical systems, demonstrating that redundancy is functional and operational rather than theoretical, and that recovery times meet the institution's defined RTOs.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plans are pre-approved by the Change Advisory Board and cover scheduled failover simulations for each redundant path (ISP failover, WAN link switchover, HA firewall pair failover, BGP re-convergence) at a frequency commensurate with criticality
Test reports document actual failover time, packet loss during transition, application-layer impact, and comparison against target RTO, with pass/fail determination
Tests include both planned failover (controlled cut-over) and unplanned simulation (e.g., pulling a physical link) to validate automatic failover mechanisms under realistic conditions
Results are formally reviewed by IT management and any failures or RTO breaches are tracked through the ITSM defect/remediation process with closure evidence
Test scope includes cyber threat protection path continuity — verifying that DDoS mitigation and IPS remain active and effective during a failover scenario
commonGaps: Failover testing is performed only at initial implementation and not repeated periodically, leaving no assurance that redundancy remains functional following subsequent changes
Tests are conducted on development or staging environments rather than production infrastructure, or during maintenance windows that do not reflect real-world conditions
Test reports record that failover was 'successful' without capturing actual failover time, making it impossible to verify RTO compliance
Only planned failover is tested; automatic failover (triggered by link-down detection) has never been validated, so BGP timers, HSRP/VRRP configuration, or BFD settings may not behave as expected
Tests are not scheduled in the annual technology operations calendar and occur ad hoc, resulting in multi-year gaps that examiners will identify from the test log timestamps
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — change records and test plans attached to maintenance windows
Network Engineering team — NOC runbooks and failover test scripts
IT Operations test calendar — annual DR/BCP schedule
Monitoring platform (e.g., Zabbix, SolarWinds, Grafana) — network availability graphs during test windows
SharePoint/Confluence — post-test review reports and lessons-learned documents
evidenceItems.format: PDF test plan + PDF/XLSX test report with network availability charts attached

evidenceItems.name: Network Availability and SLA Monitoring Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports demonstrating continuous monitoring of network service availability for critical systems, including uptime metrics, SLA compliance against contracted targets, and trend analysis identifying degradation before it becomes an outage.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monitoring is implemented with tools that measure availability from the perspective of the critical application (synthetic transactions or active probes) rather than only from the network device, ensuring end-to-end visibility
Monthly or quarterly reports present availability metrics per circuit and per critical system, compared against the institution's internal SLA targets and against carrier contractual SLAs
Reports include latency, jitter, and packet-loss trend data in addition to binary uptime, enabling early detection of degradation patterns that precede outages
Availability data is correlated with incident records so that every period of downtime has a corresponding incident ticket, root cause, and remediation action, providing an auditable chain
Reports are reviewed by IT management and escalated to governance forums (e.g., IT Risk Committee) when targets are consistently missed, with evidence of management discussion and action
commonGaps: Monitoring covers network devices (ping/SNMP) but not application-layer reachability, so network infrastructure reports 100% uptime while critical applications experienced connectivity failures that went undetected by monitoring

SLA reports are obtained from ISP portals and accepted without independent corroboration, meaning the institution has no internal measurement to dispute or validate carrier-reported availability figures
Reports are generated but not formally reviewed or signed off by management, existing as raw data exports without analysis or trend commentary
Availability metrics are not decomposed per critical system — a single aggregate uptime figure masks poor performance on specific circuits serving the most critical applications
Alerting thresholds are set at binary link-down events, with no proactive alerting on latency degradation or packet loss increases that precede full outages
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform (e.g., SolarWinds NPM, PRTG, Zabbix, Datadog) — availability dashboards and scheduled reports
NOC operations team — monthly/quarterly SLA review packs
ISP/carrier customer portals — contracted SLA performance reports
APM tools (e.g., Dynatrace, New Relic) — application connectivity and synthetic monitoring
IT GRC platform — KRI/KPI performance reports linked to network availability metrics
evidenceItems.format: PDF management report with supporting XLSX data and monitoring platform screenshots

evidenceItems.name: Network Incident Records for Critical System Outages
evidenceItems.description: Incident tickets and post-incident review reports for network faults and cyber-threat-induced network disruptions affecting critical systems, demonstrating the institution's ability to detect, respond to, and learn from network reliability failures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every network incident affecting a critical system is captured in the ITSM platform with consistent fields: affected systems, start/end time, duration, impact severity, root cause category, and resolution action

Post-Incident Reviews (PIRs) are completed for all P1/P2 network incidents, document the timeline and root cause analysis, and produce actionable remediation items with owners and due dates tracked to closure
Incident records demonstrate that redundant failover paths activated (automatically or manually) within RTO and that the incident log explicitly records the failover activation time and outcome

Trends are analysed at least quarterly to identify repeat incident patterns (e.g., recurring flapping on a specific circuit, repeated BGP instability) that may indicate a systemic SPOF or reliability gap

Cyber-threat-induced network incidents (e.g., DDoS events, BGP hijack attempts) are separately tagged and demonstrate that threat protection mechanisms (scrubbing, blackholing, IPS) operated as designed during the incident
commonGaps: Incident records for network faults are stored in the NOC's monitoring tool rather than the corporate ITSM platform, resulting in incomplete data and no linkage to PIRs or remediation tracking
PIRs are only conducted for incidents that caused prolonged outages, omitting incidents where failover activated successfully but the root cause of the primary path failure was never investigated
Incident records do not capture whether redundant failover was activated and how long it took, making it impossible to assess RTO compliance from incident evidence alone

Cyber-threat-induced network incidents are handled by the SOC and recorded in the SIEM but never integrated into the ITSM incident record, creating a blind spot in the overall network reliability picture
Repeat incidents on the same circuit or component are not escalated for root cause remediation because trend analysis is not performed, allowing systemic reliability issues to persist
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — incident queue filtered by network/connectivity category
NOC shift logs and event management system (e.g., PagerDuty, OpsGenie) — alert-to-incident linkage
SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh) — security incidents involving network threat events
DDoS mitigation provider (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai) — attack event reports and scrubbing activation logs
Change Management System — emergency changes triggered by network incidents
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) with PIR documents (PDF) attached per major incident

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Threat Protection Controls for Network Infrastructure
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and configuration evidence demonstrating that network-layer cyber threat protection controls — such as DDoS mitigation, intrusion prevention, and access control — are implemented with redundancy for critical systems and are operational, tested, and managed.
whatGoodLooksLike: A network security controls register maps each cyber threat protection control (DDoS mitigation service, IPS cluster, firewall HA pair, BGP route filtering, network ACLs) to the critical systems it protects, with evidence that each control has no unmitigated SPOF

DDoS mitigation provider contracts and activation runbooks are current, and test activations (e.g., simulated volumetric attack against a test prefix) have been conducted within the past 12 months with documented results

IPS and firewall rule sets are reviewed at a defined frequency (at least annually), with evidence of management sign-off on the current approved rule base and a process for emergency rule changes during active threats

Vulnerability and configuration assessments of network devices (e.g., CIS Benchmark compliance scans) are conducted periodically, with remediation tracking for identified findings above accepted risk thresholds

Threat intelligence feeds are integrated into network controls (e.g., automated BGP blackhole for known malicious prefixes, IPS signature updates) with evidence of the integration and its operational cadence
commonGaps: DDoS mitigation is contracted with a single upstream provider but the activation process (BGP community signalling or traffic redirection) has never been tested end-to-end, so it is unknown whether the integration works under actual attack conditions

Firewall high-availability configurations have config-sync enabled but asymmetric routing issues mean that failover causes session drops for long-lived TCP connections to critical banking applications, a gap not discovered until a real failover event

IPS is deployed in detection-only (IDS) mode for critical system segments due to concerns about false positives blocking legitimate traffic, effectively removing the prevention capability the architecture assumes is in place
Network device hardening is applied at initial deployment but configuration drift is not detected because no periodic compliance scanning or configuration backup comparison is performed

Threat intelligence integration is manual — the SOC analyses feeds and manually creates firewall rules — with no SLA on how quickly new threat indicators are operationalised, leaving a window of exposure during active threat campaigns
suggestedSources: SOC/CSIRT platform — threat intelligence feeds and network indicator enforcement logs
Firewall management console (e.g., Panorama, FortiManager, Check Point SmartConsole) — HA status, policy version history, and change logs
DDoS mitigation provider portal — service configuration, protected prefixes, and attack event history
Vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable, Qualys) — network device scan reports
Network configuration management tool (e.g., Oxidized, SolarWinds NCM) — configuration backup diffs and compliance audit reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF controls register + system-exported configuration reports (PDF/XML) + vendor-provided DDoS event reports (PDF)

auditTips: Before the examination, reconcile the critical system list used in your BIA against the systems explicitly covered by redundant network paths in your architecture diagrams — any system classified as critical that relies on a single network path is an immediate finding under clause 10.37 and must be remediated or formally risk-accepted prior to examination.

Prepare a single consolidated 'clause 10.37 evidence pack' that sequences from critical system inventory → network dependency map → architecture diagrams → failover test results → availability reports → incident records, so examiners can follow the logical chain of evidence without requiring the institution to retrieve documents ad hoc.

For every claimed redundant path, obtain written confirmation from your ISP or carrier (on provider letterhead or portal export) that the two circuits are physically diverse — BNM examiners may specifically ask for carrier confirmation that fibre paths do not share a common duct, exchange, or point of presence, as logical redundancy with physical convergence is a common SPOF.

Cross-reference network incident records against the availability monitoring reports for the same period — any gap where monitoring shows 100% uptime but incident tickets record a network fault is a credibility red flag that examiners will probe, and such discrepancies must be explained by differences in monitoring scope rather than data inconsistency.

Ensure your most recent failover test was conducted within 12 months of the examination date and that the test report explicitly states the failover time against the RTO — if the gap exceeds 12 months or the report lacks RTO comparison, schedule an emergency test and document it before the examination window.

Map each network cyber threat protection control (DDoS mitigation, IPS, firewall HA) back to the 'cyber threats' limb of clause 10.37 verbatim, and prepare a one-page control summary that examiners can use to confirm that threat protection is integrated into the redundant architecture rather than relying on a single inline device whose failure would both expose systems to threats and create a network SPOF simultaneously.

Interview your NOC and network engineering leads before the examination to ensure they can articulate the redundancy design and failover procedures verbally — BNM examiners conduct staff interviews, and inconsistency between what documentation says and what technical staff describe is treated as evidence that documentation does not reflect operational reality.

10.38.title: Network Fault Prevention Measures
10.38.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing clause 10.38 will verify that the institution has operationalised all three prescribed fault-prevention mechanisms — component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths — as a coherent and tested ensemble rather than as isolated controls. Examiners place particular weight on whether redundancy is active-active or merely passive standby, since passive configurations frequently fail to prevent service disruption during real faults. A common red flag is documentation that describes redundancy architecturally but cannot be corroborated by test records, monitoring dashboards, or incident post-mortems demonstrating that failover actually occurred as designed. Examiners will also scrutinise whether service diversity extends to third-party telecommunications providers, ISPs, and last-mile connections, or whether purported diversity collapses to a single carrier at a physical aggregation point. Cross-referencing with clause 10.37 network resilience requirements is standard practice, so institutions should expect examiners to trace a thread from risk assessment findings through control design to evidence of control effectiveness.

evidenceItems.name: Network Architecture Diagram — Redundancy and Alternate Paths
evidenceItems.description: Current-state network topology diagrams, maintained under change management, that explicitly annotate component redundancy (dual switches, routers, firewalls), service diversity (multiple ISPs/telcos), and alternate network paths (diverse physical routes). These diagrams serve as the primary artefact demonstrating architectural compliance with the three prescribed control categories.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagrams carry a version number, approval signature, and review date within the last 12 months, confirming currency under change management
Each redundant component pair (e.g., dual core switches, dual WAN routers) is explicitly labelled with device names or IDs that can be cross-referenced to asset inventory records
Alternate network paths are traced to physically diverse cable routes or ducts, with annotations identifying the different carriers or conduit owners for each path

Service diversity is demonstrated by naming at least two independent ISPs or telco providers, including reference to distinct AS numbers or peering points to rule out convergence at a single upstream node
Diagrams are accompanied by a narrative legend or design rationale document explaining the redundancy strategy and any deliberate single points of failure with accepted risk justification
commonGaps: Diagrams show logical redundancy (e.g., HSRP/VRRP configured) but physical cabling maps reveal both uplinks share a single conduit or patch panel, negating path diversity
Carrier diversity is claimed but both ISP contracts are with subsidiaries of the same parent telco, sharing last-mile infrastructure — examiners will request carrier route verification letters
Diagrams are outdated relative to recent infrastructure changes (data centre migrations, SD-WAN deployments) and do not reflect the current production environment
Critical network segments such as OT/SWIFT networks or cloud interconnects are omitted from the redundancy scope without a documented risk-acceptance rationale
No clear delineation between production redundant paths and disaster-recovery paths, making it impossible to assess normal-operations resilience independently
suggestedSources: Network Operations Centre (NOC) — authoritative topology diagrams maintained in Visio, Lucidchart, or NetBrain
IT Infrastructure team's SharePoint/Confluence repository for approved architecture documents
Change Management System (ServiceNow, Jira) — RFC records that triggered diagram updates
Data centre colocation provider's cross-connect records for physical path verification
Telecoms/ISP carrier route confirmation letters held by the Vendor Management team
evidenceItems.format: PDF/Visio/PNG (approved and version-controlled), supplemented by PDF narrative design document

evidenceItems.name: Component Redundancy Inventory and Configuration Baseline
evidenceItems.description: A structured inventory of all network components designated as redundant (firewalls, core and distribution switches, WAN routers, load balancers, DNS/DHCP servers), paired with configuration baseline records confirming that redundancy protocols (HSRP, VRRP, LACP, BGP multi-path, OSPF multi-area) are correctly configured and active. This evidence demonstrates that redundancy is operationally implemented, not merely planned.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory is maintained in a CMDB (ServiceNow, Device42) with fields capturing redundancy role (primary/secondary or active/active), redundancy protocol, and last configuration review date
Configuration baselines exported from network management platforms (Cisco DNA Center, SolarWinds NCM, Ansible AWX) confirm that redundancy protocols are enabled and in a converged/healthy state
Active-active versus active-passive classification is explicit for each component pair, with active-passive pairs accompanied by documented switchover time objectives and test results
Power redundancy for network components (dual PSUs, separate UPS feeds, diverse PDUs) is captured alongside network-layer redundancy to demonstrate end-to-end resilience
The inventory scope is demonstrably aligned with the network diagram in 10.38-E1, with no critical components appearing in one artefact but absent from the other
commonGaps: CMDB records are incomplete or stale — devices upgraded or decommissioned have not been updated, creating a mismatch with actual production equipment
Redundancy protocols are listed as configured but configuration exports show them in a passive or non-preemptive state that would delay or prevent automatic failover
Power-layer redundancy is not captured, leaving open the risk that dual network devices share a single power feed and would both fail on a PDU or UPS fault
Redundancy scope excludes out-of-band management networks and console servers, which are themselves critical for incident response during primary path failures
No evidence that configuration baselines have been reviewed and re-approved following firmware upgrades or major changes, meaning documented configs may not reflect running configs
suggestedSources: CMDB platform (ServiceNow CMDB, Device42, Infoblox) — network asset and relationship records
Network configuration management tools (SolarWinds NCM, Cisco DNA Center, Ansible AWX) — running configuration exports
Data centre team's power distribution records and UPS topology diagrams
Network Engineering team's hardening and baseline standards documentation in Confluence/SharePoint
Automated compliance scanning tool reports (Cisco Compliance Suite, Skybox, Nipper) confirming protocol states
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (CMDB export) + PDF/text configuration exports, cross-referenced to network diagrams

evidenceItems.name: Service Diversity Agreements — ISP and Telco Contracts
evidenceItems.description: Executed contracts, service schedules, and route diversity confirmation letters from at least two independent telecommunications providers or ISPs, demonstrating that the institution's connectivity services are sourced from diverse carriers with distinct physical infrastructure. This evidence substantiates the 'service diversity' obligation in clause 10.38 at the contractual and physical layers.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contracts name at least two providers with provably distinct AS numbers, and the institution holds carrier route verification letters confirming physical path separation to the nearest exchange or PoP

Service schedules include explicit diversity commitments (e.g., no shared last-mile, no shared exchange building) and the institution has conducted site visits or requested infrastructure maps to validate claims
Contracts include individual SLAs covering uptime, MTTR, and notification obligations, enabling the institution to measure each provider's contribution to overall resilience independently
The institution's Vendor Risk Register captures each telco/ISP as a critical third-party with periodic resilience assessments, and the most recent assessment is on file
Contract renewal dates and diversity re-validation checkpoints are tracked in a contract management system, preventing undetected erosion of diversity over time (e.g., provider mergers)
commonGaps: Both ISP contracts are with subsidiaries of the same holding company or share physical infrastructure at the local exchange, making claimed diversity commercially real but physically illusory
Route diversity letters are dated at contract inception and have not been refreshed after network changes by the carrier, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate current-state diversity

Contracts specify diverse entry points to the institution's premises but internal records show both feeds terminate in the same MDF/IDF cabinet, creating a physical single point of failure within the building
Only internet connectivity is covered — dedicated private circuits (MPLS, leased lines) for inter-branch or data-centre connectivity lack equivalent diversity documentation
The institution relies solely on the carrier's verbal assurance of diversity rather than written contractual commitment, which BNM examiners will view as insufficient evidence
suggestedSources: Procurement and Vendor Management team — executed ISP/telco contracts and service schedules
Contract management system (Ironclad, SharePoint contract library, Ariba) — current contract repository
Network Engineering team — carrier route diversity letters and site survey records
Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform — vendor risk assessments for critical telco providers
Facilities/Data Centre team — cable plant records confirming physical entry points and termination locations
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed contracts and diversity letters), supplemented by Spreadsheet (vendor risk register extract)

evidenceItems.name: Network Failover and Path Switchover Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented results of scheduled failover tests and drills that validate each redundancy mechanism — component redundancy, service diversity switchover, and alternate path activation — under controlled conditions. Test records provide the evidentiary bridge between architectural design and operational effectiveness, which BNM examiners require to move beyond paper compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plans define specific pass/fail criteria (e.g., failover completes within RTO, no application sessions dropped beyond accepted threshold) and results record actual measured outcomes against each criterion

Tests cover all three control categories: component-level failover (e.g., pulling primary firewall), ISP/telco switchover (e.g., withdrawing BGP routes from primary carrier), and alternate path activation (e.g., simulating primary circuit failure)
Test records include timestamps, names of personnel who conducted the test, approvals, and a post-test restoration confirmation — sufficient to reconstruct the test event during an examination
Deficiencies or unexpected behaviours discovered during tests are captured in a remediation register with assigned owners and closure dates, demonstrating a closed-loop improvement process
Tests are conducted at least annually or following significant infrastructure changes, and a forward schedule of upcoming tests is maintained and approved by IT management
commonGaps: Tests are conducted only at the component level (e.g., switch failover) but never at the ISP/carrier level, leaving service diversity untested under real switching conditions
Test records document that failover 'was successful' without quantifying actual switchover duration, packet loss, or application impact — examiners will probe for measurable outcomes
Tests are performed only in maintenance windows when traffic is near-zero, meaning they do not validate resilience under production load conditions
Remediation items from previous test cycles remain open without evidence of resolution, indicating the institution is aware of gaps but has not addressed them
Test scope excludes critical applications (e.g., core banking, payment gateways) because business units refused downtime risk, leaving the most consequential resilience paths unvalidated
suggestedSources: IT Service Management system (ServiceNow, Jira) — change and test records linked to network resilience tests
Business Continuity and DR team — BCP/DR test reports that include network component exercises
Network Operations Centre (NOC) — maintenance window logs, NOC test reports, and monitoring platform exports during test windows
GRC platform (Archer, MetricStream, OneTrust) — resilience test schedule and results repository
Technology Risk team — risk register entries for untested resilience paths and corresponding remediation plans
evidenceItems.format: PDF test reports with sign-off, supplemented by system-generated evidence (monitoring screenshots, traffic graphs) embedded or annexed

evidenceItems.name: Network Monitoring and Alerting Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Configuration records and live dashboard exports from the network monitoring platform demonstrating continuous visibility over the health of all redundant components, diverse service paths, and alternate network routes. This evidence shows that the institution will detect a fault or redundancy degradation before it escalates into an outage, supporting the preventive intent of clause 10.38.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monitoring platform (SolarWinds, Zabbix, PRTG, Datadog) is configured with alerts for redundancy-state changes (e.g., HSRP role change, BGP neighbour down, LACP member link failure) with defined escalation thresholds and NOC response procedures

Dashboards provide real-time visibility into the status of each ISP/carrier circuit independently, enabling NOC staff to immediately identify which path has failed without relying on carrier notification

Alert records for the preceding 12 months demonstrate that redundancy-related events were detected, acknowledged within SLA, and escalated appropriately — confirming monitoring is operationally active
Monitoring scope explicitly covers out-of-band management networks and critical upstream BGP peers, not only internal LAN/WAN segments
Alert tuning records show that thresholds have been reviewed and adjusted to minimise false positives while ensuring genuine redundancy failures are not suppressed
commonGaps: Monitoring is configured for availability (device up/down) but does not capture redundancy-protocol state changes — a standby device may appear healthy while its failover capability is silently broken
ISP circuit monitoring relies entirely on carrier-provided portals rather than institution-controlled probes, creating a dependency on the same provider whose failure needs to be detected
Alert notification chains are documented but not tested — escalation contacts have changed and alerts are routing to distribution lists with departed staff
Monitoring platform itself lacks redundancy, meaning a monitoring infrastructure failure would leave the institution blind to network faults at the worst possible time
Historical alert logs are not retained beyond 90 days due to storage constraints, preventing examiners from reviewing the institution's detection history over a meaningful period
suggestedSources: NOC team — network monitoring platform (SolarWinds NMS, PRTG, Datadog, Zabbix) configuration exports and dashboard screenshots
ITSM platform — incident records and alert-to-ticket correlation reports
Network Engineering team — monitoring scope documentation and alert threshold registers
Security Operations Centre (SOC) — SIEM correlation rules related to network device state changes
Technology Operations team — on-call and escalation runbooks linked to network monitoring alerts
evidenceItems.format: PDF (monitoring configuration reports and dashboard exports), supplemented by CSV alert log extracts for the review period

evidenceItems.name: Network-Related Incident and Post-Mortem Records
evidenceItems.description: Incident management records and root-cause analysis (RCA) reports for network faults occurring in the review period, demonstrating how the institution's redundancy controls actually performed during real events. These records provide BNM examiners with empirical evidence of control effectiveness and show whether identified weaknesses were remediated in a timely manner.
whatGoodLooksLike: RCA reports for network incidents explicitly assess whether redundancy controls activated as designed, and where they did not, document the technical reason (e.g., misconfiguration, simultaneous component failure, vendor delay)
Incident records capture Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Restore (MTTR) by incident type, allowing examiners to assess whether resilience controls meaningfully reduced customer impact
Post-mortem action items are tracked through to closure in the ITSM system, with evidence of verification testing confirming that identified gaps have been resolved rather than just documented

The institution can demonstrate a reducing trend in network-fault severity or frequency attributable to redundancy control improvements, or provide a credible explanation where such a trend is not yet evident

Incident classification correctly identifies whether faults were prevented from becoming outages by redundancy controls (near-misses) — a populated near-miss register signals mature operational awareness
commonGaps: RCA reports conclude that redundancy 'should have' prevented the outage without examining why it did not — providing no assurance that the same failure mode has been addressed
Minor network incidents that caused brief redundancy degradation are closed as P3/P4 without RCA, meaning systematic weaknesses in the redundancy architecture go unidentified and uncorrected
Post-mortem action items are marked closed based on planned completion dates rather than verified testing, creating a false record of remediation
Incident records do not distinguish between faults where alternate paths activated automatically and those requiring manual intervention, masking operational dependencies that increase actual risk

The institution has experienced no significant network incidents in the review period but also has no near-miss or redundancy-activation records, suggesting detection or logging gaps rather than genuine absence of events
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — network incident tickets, RCA reports, and post-mortem action registers
Technology Risk team — risk event log and loss event database for technology incidents
NOC team — shift handover logs and incident chronology records
GRC platform — control effectiveness assessments referencing incident outcomes
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records — emergency changes triggered by network faults, cross-referenced to incident tickets
evidenceItems.format: PDF (RCA/post-mortem reports), supplemented by ITSM system exports (incident register with status and closure evidence)

auditTips: Map each of the three specific control categories in the verbatim text — component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths — to at least one named evidence item before the examination. Examiners will structure their line of inquiry around this exact triplet, and an institution that cannot produce distinct evidence for each category will face follow-up findings even if its overall network is mature.

Request and review carrier route diversity confirmation letters from all ISPs and telcos at least three months before the examination. Last-mile convergence (both circuits sharing a single duct from the street to the building) is the most common physical diversity gap and is not visible from logical diagrams — it requires written carrier confirmation or a physical site walk.

Run an end-to-end redundancy test specifically for the examination cycle and ensure the results document quantitative outcomes (switchover time in seconds, packet loss percentage, application impact duration) rather than qualitative pass/fail assessments. BNM examiners will probe whether test evidence is sufficient to confirm RTO objectives are achievable, not merely that a test occurred.

Cross-reference the network redundancy inventory against the institution's Business Impact Analysis (BIA) to confirm that all systems classified as critical or high-availability in the BIA are supported by the network resilience controls documented under clause 10.38. A discrepancy — where a critical payment system sits on a network segment without documented redundancy — is a high-severity finding.

Prepare a one-page resilience control summary matrix that maps each clause 10.38 control type to: the specific components or circuits implementing it, the test record verifying it, the monitoring alert covering it, and the responsible owner. Examiners appreciate institutions that can navigate their own evidence efficiently, and this matrix demonstrates control integration rather than isolated compliance artefacts.

Review all open audit, risk, and post-mortem action items related to network resilience before the examination and either close them with verified evidence or prepare a credible, time-bound remediation plan with executive sponsorship. Examiners weight unresolved prior findings heavily; an institution that surfaces its own gaps with an active remediation plan fares significantly better than one where examiners discover the same gaps independently.

Ensure that monitoring and alerting evidence covers the period from the last examination to the present — not just a recent snapshot. Examiners may request a 12-month alert log to assess how consistently redundancy events were detected and acted upon. Gaps in log retention or monitoring coverage for historical periods are themselves treated as a resilience deficiency.

10.39.title: Network Bandwidth Monitoring
10.39.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will prioritise confirming that real-time bandwidth monitoring is genuinely operational — not merely configured but unmonitored — by reviewing live dashboards, alert histories, and incident logs side-by-side. A key red flag is the absence of defined network service resilience metrics with documented thresholds, as the clause explicitly requires these to trigger over-utilisation flags rather than relying on ad hoc observation. Examiners will probe whether traffic analysis is systematic and recurring, looking for evidence of trend reports and anomaly detection outputs that feed into management decisions or capacity planning cycles. Institutions that cannot demonstrate a closed loop — from monitoring alert through to remediation action — will be viewed as having a process that exists on paper only. The 'S' marker signals a Supervisory expectation, meaning examiners will assess both design adequacy and operational effectiveness, often by interviewing Network Operations Centre (NOC) staff to validate that documented procedures reflect actual practice.

evidenceItems.name: Real-Time Network Bandwidth Monitoring Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy document and operational procedures that mandate real-time bandwidth monitoring across the institution's network infrastructure, defining roles, responsibilities, monitoring scope, and escalation paths.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references RMiT 10.39 and is approved by a senior technology governance body (e.g., IT Steering Committee or Board Risk Committee) within the last review cycle
Procedures specify the tools, platforms, and polling intervals used for real-time monitoring (e.g., sub-5-minute refresh rates for critical links)
Defined RACI matrix clearly assigns ownership of monitoring tasks to NOC, Network Engineering, and IT Risk teams
Escalation matrix documents threshold breach notification chains including on-call contacts and SLA timelines for response
Document version history shows regular review (at least annually) with change rationale recorded
commonGaps: Policy exists but contains no reference to real-time monitoring requirements — only periodic or daily checks are described
Procedures do not specify which network segments or critical links are in scope, leaving core banking or inter-datacenter links potentially unmonitored
Escalation paths are defined but not tested — no records of tabletop exercises or drills validating the escalation chain works as documented
Policy has not been updated to reflect infrastructure changes such as SD-WAN adoption, cloud interconnects, or branch network expansions
Roles are assigned at department level rather than named roles, making accountability difficult to establish during examination
suggestedSources: IT Governance SharePoint or intranet policy repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or RSA Archer) policy register
IT department document management system with version control
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) linked procedure articles
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata

evidenceItems.name: Network Service Resilience Metrics Register
evidenceItems.description: A formal register or configuration artefact that defines the institution's network service resilience metrics — including bandwidth utilisation thresholds, latency, packet loss, and jitter targets — against which real-time monitoring alerts are calibrated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Metrics are differentiated by criticality tier (e.g., core banking links vs. internet breakout vs. branch WAN), with tighter thresholds for higher-criticality segments
Each metric has a documented baseline, a warning threshold, and a critical/over-utilisation threshold that directly triggers automated alerts
Thresholds are reviewed and approved by Network Architecture and IT Risk at least annually, with sign-off evidence retained
Register maps each metric to a corresponding monitoring tool or dashboard widget, confirming the metric is actively measured and not theoretical
Historical threshold review records show adjustments made in response to traffic growth trends, demonstrating the metrics remain fit for purpose
commonGaps: Thresholds are hard-coded in monitoring tools but never formally documented in a reviewed and approved register, making it impossible to audit their rationale
A single generic utilisation threshold (e.g., 80%) is applied uniformly across all links regardless of criticality, failing to reflect the risk profile of different segments
Metrics cover bandwidth utilisation only — latency, jitter, and packet loss are not defined, leaving key resilience dimensions unmonitored
Thresholds have not been reviewed since initial deployment despite significant traffic growth, meaning alerts fire too late or too frequently to be actionable
Register exists but is maintained by a single engineer with no formal review or governance process, creating a key-person dependency risk
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform configuration exports (e.g., SolarWinds NTA, PRTG, Zabbix, Grafana alert rules)
IT Risk register or capacity management artefacts in GRC platform
Network Engineering team SharePoint or Confluence documentation space
Change management records in ITSM tool showing threshold approval history
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF with approval signatures; system configuration export as supplementary evidence

evidenceItems.name: Real-Time Bandwidth Monitoring Dashboard and Alert Configuration
evidenceItems.description: System-level evidence demonstrating that real-time monitoring is operationally active, including dashboard screenshots, monitoring tool configurations, and alert rule exports that show bandwidth over-utilisation and fault detection is automated and continuous.
whatGoodLooksLike: Dashboard covers all critical network segments including internet uplinks, inter-datacenter links, core switching fabric, and third-party/BNM connectivity, with no material gaps in scope
Alert configurations show automated notifications are sent via multiple channels (email, SMS, ITSM ticket auto-creation) within defined response windows upon threshold breach
Monitoring tool is configured with redundancy — if the primary monitoring server fails, a secondary instance or failover mechanism maintains alerting capability
Dashboard timestamps confirm polling intervals meet the institution's defined real-time standard (e.g., 1–5 minute intervals) for all monitored links
Alert suppression or maintenance window logs are available and show these features are not being used to mask genuine performance issues
commonGaps: Dashboard screenshots provided are point-in-time exports with no corroborating evidence that monitoring was continuous — gaps in historical data are unexplained
Monitoring covers only the perimeter or internet-facing links, with internal core banking network segments absent from scope
Alerts are configured but routed to a generic mailbox that is not actively monitored, meaning over-utilisation events go unnoticed until service degradation occurs
Monitoring tool itself has no high-availability configuration — a single point of failure could render the institution blind to network events during a critical outage
Alert thresholds in the tool do not match the formally approved metrics register, indicating the tool was configured independently of the governance process
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform admin console (SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix, Grafana/Prometheus, Cisco DNA Center)
NOC operations SharePoint or Confluence runbook pages
ITSM platform showing auto-generated tickets from monitoring alerts
IT infrastructure team — direct demonstration during on-site examination is common BNM practice
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF/PNG dashboard screenshots, XML/JSON alert configuration export, tool-native report)

evidenceItems.name: Bandwidth Over-Utilisation and Network Fault Alert History
evidenceItems.description: A historical log of bandwidth over-utilisation alerts and network fault notifications generated by the monitoring system over a defined lookback period, demonstrating that the alerting mechanism has been active and that alerts have been acted upon through documented remediation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Alert log covers at least 12 months and includes timestamp, affected segment, metric breached, threshold value, actual value, and acknowledging engineer for each event
Each significant alert (warning or critical) has a corresponding ITSM incident or problem ticket showing investigation, root cause, and resolution actions

Log shows a credible pattern — some alerts with prompt resolution — rather than either zero alerts (suggesting monitoring is not effective) or an overwhelming volume with no response (suggesting alert fatigue)
Recurring alerts on the same segment are traceable to a capacity planning review or a permanent remediation action, demonstrating continuous improvement
Alert acknowledgement timestamps versus resolution timestamps confirm SLA compliance with the institution's defined response time objectives
commonGaps: Alert log shows zero events over a 12-month period, which is implausible for a production network and suggests alerting is not properly configured or thresholds are set too high to fire
Alerts are logged in the monitoring tool but no corresponding ITSM tickets exist, indicating alerts were seen but not formally actioned or tracked to resolution
Log retention in the monitoring tool is shorter than 7 years due to default storage settings, creating a compliance gap for historical review during examination
High-volume periods (e.g., month-end batch processing, public holiday surges) show no corresponding utilisation spikes, suggesting monitoring gaps during high-load windows
Alert logs contain timestamps only — no information on which team member acknowledged, investigated, or resolved each alert, preventing accountability tracing
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform historical alert/event logs (SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix)
ITSM platform incident management module (ServiceNow, Jira, Remedy)
NOC shift handover logs or operations reports
Security information and event management (SIEM) platform if network events are forwarded there
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/PDF report from monitoring tool); corroborated by ITSM incident extract

evidenceItems.name: Network Traffic Trend Analysis and Anomaly Detection Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic analytical reports generated from traffic analysis tools or processes that identify bandwidth utilisation trends, seasonal patterns, and anomalous traffic events, demonstrating that the institution's monitoring extends beyond real-time alerting to include predictive and investigative traffic analysis.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are produced on a defined regular cycle (e.g., monthly for trend analysis, weekly for anomaly review) and are formally distributed to Network Engineering, IT Risk, and senior technology management
Trend reports include multi-period comparisons (e.g., month-on-month, year-on-year) that surface growth trajectories and inform capacity planning decisions with documented outputs

Anomaly detection reports identify specific events — unusual traffic volumes, unexpected protocol distributions, or off-hours spikes — and include analyst commentary on whether each was investigated and explained
Reports are generated from a flow-analysis or deep packet inspection tool (e.g., NetFlow, sFlow, Cisco StealthWatch, ntopng) rather than derived solely from SNMP utilisation polling
Evidence shows reports feed into a formal capacity management or IT risk process — action items are tracked to completion, not merely noted and filed
commonGaps: Institution conflates real-time utilisation monitoring with traffic analysis — no flow-level or protocol-level analysis is performed, meaning anomalies such as DDoS reconnaissance or misconfigured applications are undetectable
Trend reports are produced ad hoc rather than on a scheduled cycle, and no evidence exists that they are reviewed by management or feed into any governance process
Reports cover aggregate bandwidth only and do not break down traffic by application, user group, or destination, limiting the institution's ability to identify the source of anomalies
Anomaly detection relies entirely on manual review of utilisation graphs rather than automated anomaly detection algorithms or baselining, making subtle trend deviations easy to miss
Reports are generated but not formally approved or signed off, and distribution evidence is absent — making it impossible to confirm management awareness of network risk posture
suggestedSources: NetFlow/sFlow collector and analysis platform (SolarWinds NTA, ManageEngine NetFlow Analyzer, ntopng, Elastic with network module)
Network Engineering monthly capacity and performance reporting SharePoint folder
IT Risk Committee or Technology Risk Working Group meeting packs and minutes
SIEM platform with network anomaly detection use cases (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel)
evidenceItems.format: PDF report with supporting charts; spreadsheet data annexes; meeting minutes referencing report findings

evidenceItems.name: Network Capacity Management and Remediation Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that bandwidth monitoring outputs are translated into concrete capacity management actions — including bandwidth upgrades, traffic engineering changes, and QoS policy adjustments — evidencing that the monitoring process drives operational resilience improvements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Capacity review records reference specific monitoring data and trend reports as inputs, establishing a clear data-driven basis for capacity decisions

Change records in the ITSM platform document bandwidth upgrade projects, circuit provisioning, or load-balancing configurations that were initiated in response to monitoring-identified over-utilisation risks
QoS or traffic shaping policy documents are current, reflect the institution's actual traffic profile, and have been updated following trend analysis findings
Capacity management records include forward-looking projections (e.g., 12–18 month headroom analysis) demonstrating proactive rather than purely reactive management
Post-implementation reviews for capacity changes reference pre- and post-change monitoring data to confirm the remediation was effective
commonGaps: Bandwidth upgrades are procured and implemented but change records contain no reference to monitoring data or capacity analysis, making it impossible to demonstrate the monitoring process drove the decision
Institution has no formal capacity management process — upgrades are reactive and initiated only after service degradation incidents rather than being triggered by proactive monitoring thresholds
QoS policies are documented but have not been reviewed or updated in over two years, and traffic analysis shows the current policy no longer reflects the institution's actual application mix
Capacity planning is performed by network vendor or managed service provider with no evidence of institution oversight, review, or approval of the outputs
Remediation actions following over-utilisation alerts are resolved by temporary workarounds (e.g., rate limiting non-critical traffic) without a permanent fix or a tracked problem management record
suggestedSources: ITSM change management module (ServiceNow, Remedy, Jira) — change advisory board records
Network Engineering capacity planning register or SharePoint site
IT Steering Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes and decision logs
Vendor/managed service provider monthly service reports and capacity review outputs
Problem management records in ITSM platform linked to recurring bandwidth events
evidenceItems.format: ITSM change records (system export); capacity planning spreadsheets; committee minutes (PDF/DOCX)

auditTips: Before the examination, run a live demonstration of the real-time monitoring dashboard with an examiner present — BNM examiners frequently request this for RMiT 10.39 to confirm the system is genuinely operational and not a pre-prepared static export. Ensure NOC staff can navigate the dashboard fluently and explain what each panel represents.

Cross-reference the alert log (10.39-E4) against the ITSM incident register for the same period: every significant bandwidth alert should have a corresponding incident ticket. Unexplained gaps — alerts in the monitoring tool with no ITSM record — are a red flag that examiners will probe as evidence of alert fatigue or process breakdown.

Validate that the thresholds configured in the monitoring tool exactly match the values in the approved metrics register (10.39-E2). Discrepancies between the documented and deployed thresholds suggest the governance process is disconnected from operational reality, which is a common finding that undermines the credibility of all other evidence.

Prepare a one-page network monitoring scope map showing every network segment in scope, the monitoring tool covering it, the threshold applied, and the alert routing destination. Examiners will use this to probe for coverage gaps — particularly for cloud interconnects, SWIFT/PayNet links, DR site connectivity, and third-party managed segments that institutions often omit.

Ensure traffic trend analysis reports (10.39-E5) contain explicit anomaly findings sections, even if the conclusion is 'no anomalies detected this period' with supporting rationale. Reports that contain only utilisation charts with no analytical commentary fail to demonstrate that the institution is actively interpreting monitoring data rather than passively collecting it.

Link capacity management actions (10.39-E6) back to specific monitoring outputs with document cross-references. For example, a change record for a bandwidth upgrade should cite the trend report or alert ID that triggered the capacity review. This traceability chain demonstrates the monitoring process produces operational outcomes, which is the core intent of the clause.

Interview preparation: brief NOC and Network Engineering staff on the specific requirements of RMiT 10.39 before the examination. Examiners commonly ask frontline staff to explain what happens when a bandwidth threshold is breached — if staff cannot describe the escalation process or are unaware of the defined thresholds, this creates a material finding even if the documentation is complete.

10.40.title: Network Security for Critical Systems
10.40.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether network services supporting critical systems are architected with deliberate security controls that address all three pillars of the CIA triad — confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Examiners will scrutinise network segmentation designs to confirm that critical systems are isolated from general-use environments and that east-west traffic between segments is governed by explicit allow-list policies. Red flags include flat network topologies, missing encryption for data in transit between critical system components, absence of redundant network paths, and undocumented or unapproved network changes. Examiners also verify that the institution's network security posture is periodically validated through independent technical assessments such as penetration tests and vulnerability scans, and that findings are tracked to closure. Particular attention is paid to whether the institution can demonstrate that confidentiality, integrity, and availability requirements were explicitly considered during the design phase — not retrofitted after deployment.

evidenceItems.name: Network Architecture and Segmentation Design Document
evidenceItems.description: A formal, board- or CISO-approved document depicting the logical and physical network topology for environments hosting critical systems, including segmentation zones, DMZ configurations, inter-zone trust policies, and the rationale for each design decision relative to CIA requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network diagrams are version-controlled, dated, and reflect the current production state with clearly labelled critical system segments (e.g., Core Banking, Payment Switch, Treasury).
