Design document explicitly maps each network zone to CIA objectives, explaining how confidentiality is enforced (encryption, access controls), integrity is maintained (routing integrity, flow control), and availability is assured (redundant links, failover paths).
Segmentation policy defines permitted traffic flows using an explicit allow-list, with all inter-zone communication documented and approved by the network security team.
Document includes a change history log and has been reviewed and approved by a named senior technology or security officer within the last 12 months.
Architecture accounts for third-party and vendor connectivity, showing how external network access to critical systems is controlled and monitored.
commonGaps: Diagrams are outdated and do not reflect recent infrastructure changes, cloud migrations, or newly onboarded critical systems — creating a disconnect between documented and actual architecture.
CIA objectives are referenced generically without explicit mapping to specific network controls, leaving examiners unable to trace how each obligation is operationalised.
Flat or insufficiently granular segmentation where critical systems share network segments with less-sensitive workloads, defeating the purpose of tiered access controls.
Third-party and vendor network access is undocumented or depicted at a high level only, with no clarity on which critical systems are reachable from external connections.
The document exists but carries no formal approval signature or review date, indicating it may not have been actively maintained or endorsed by accountable leadership.
suggestedSources: Network Engineering team (Visio, Lucidchart, or draw.io source files and exported PDFs)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — architecture artefact repository
SharePoint or Confluence — IT Architecture governance library
Change Management System (ITSM) — approved change records referencing network design updates
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded or attached network diagrams (Visio/PNG/SVG)

evidenceItems.name: Firewall and Access Control Rule Documentation
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive record of firewall rule sets, network access control lists (ACLs), and micro-segmentation policies governing traffic to and from critical systems, demonstrating that only explicitly authorised flows are permitted and that rules are reviewed, approved, and kept current.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each firewall rule protecting a critical system segment is documented with a business justification, rule owner, approval date, and review expiry — no orphaned or undocumented rules exist.
Rule review logs confirm that rule sets are assessed at least annually, with evidence that unused, overly permissive, or any-any rules have been identified and remediated.
Rule sets implement a default-deny posture with only explicitly approved source/destination/port combinations permitted into critical system zones.
Separation of duties is evidenced: rule change requests, approvals, and implementation are performed by different named individuals or teams.
Critical system firewall rules are reconciled against the network architecture document, with no rules permitting flows that contradict the approved segmentation design.
commonGaps: Firewall rules contain broad 'any source' or 'any destination' entries protecting critical systems, indicating insufficient access control granularity.
Rule review records are absent or show reviews conducted infrequently (e.g., every two or three years), with no evidence of remediation of identified issues.
Legacy rules accumulated over time have no documented business owner or justification, making it impossible to determine if they are still required.
Change management records for firewall rule modifications are missing or incomplete, preventing auditors from establishing an authorised change trail.
Firewall policy documentation is held only in the firewall management console with no formal exported record, making it inaccessible for audit review without live system access.
suggestedSources: Firewall management platform (Palo Alto Panorama, Fortinet FortiManager, Check Point SmartConsole) — policy export reports
ITSM change management tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — firewall change requests and approvals
GRC platform — firewall rule review schedules and completion evidence
Security Operations team — rule review worksheets and sign-off records
evidenceItems.format: PDF export or spreadsheet (XLSX) from firewall management platform; change request records as PDF

evidenceItems.name: Encryption in Transit Policy and Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and technical evidence confirming that data traversing networks between critical system components, and between critical systems and end-users or third parties, is encrypted using approved protocols and cipher suites, protecting confidentiality and integrity in transit.
whatGoodLooksLike: An approved encryption standard document specifies minimum protocol versions (e.g., TLS 1.2 or higher) and approved cipher suites, with explicit prohibition of deprecated protocols such as SSL, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1.
Technical scan results (e.g., from Qualys, Nessus, or testssl.sh) evidence that critical system endpoints enforce the approved encryption standards, with no deprecated protocol support detected.

Certificate inventory lists all TLS/PKI certificates protecting critical system communications, including issuing CA, expiry dates, and assigned owners — with no expired or self-signed certificates in production critical paths.

Encryption controls cover all data flows: internal API calls, database replication links, administrative management traffic, and external-facing interfaces, with no unencrypted critical data paths identified.
Remediation records are available showing timely resolution of any encryption weaknesses identified in prior assessments, demonstrating an active management cycle.
commonGaps: Internal network traffic between critical system components (e.g., application to database, microservice to microservice) is unencrypted under the assumption that the internal network is trusted, contradicting zero-trust and CIA requirements.
Deprecated protocols (TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, SSLv3) remain enabled on critical system endpoints, presenting a confidentiality and integrity risk that violates the policy.
Certificate inventory is incomplete or not actively managed, resulting in expired certificates discovered during audit — a clear availability and integrity risk.
Encryption policy exists as a document but has not been validated against the actual implementation through technical scans, creating an unverifiable compliance assertion.
Cipher suite configurations on critical system services have never been reviewed, potentially permitting weak ciphers that undermine the confidentiality objective.
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform (Qualys VMDR, Tenable.io, Nessus) — TLS scan reports
PKI/Certificate management system (DigiCert, Venafi, AWS Certificate Manager) — certificate inventory export
IT Security Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence) — encryption standards document
Application Security or Infrastructure team — SSL/TLS configuration baselines
evidenceItems.format: PDF (policy document and scan reports); XLSX (certificate inventory); system-generated HTML/PDF scan output

evidenceItems.name: Network Redundancy and Availability Design Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation and testing evidence demonstrating that network services supporting critical systems are designed with sufficient redundancy — including redundant links, failover mechanisms, and alternate routing — to meet availability requirements and withstand single points of failure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network design documents identify all single points of failure in critical system network paths and document the redundancy controls (active-active links, HSRP/VRRP gateway redundancy, BGP multi-homing) implemented to mitigate each.

Failover test records confirm that network redundancy mechanisms have been tested within the past 12 months, documenting the test scenario, outcome, recovery time achieved, and sign-off by a responsible technology officer.
Uptime and availability metrics for critical system network segments are tracked and reported, with evidence that SLA targets are being met and exceptions are investigated.

ISP and WAN connectivity for critical systems is provided via at least two diverse carriers with physically separate cable routes, evidenced by provider contracts and route diversity confirmation letters.
Capacity management reports confirm that network links supporting critical systems are not routinely operating above 70–80% utilisation, avoiding congestion-driven availability degradation.
commonGaps: Network redundancy is documented in design but has never been tested through controlled failover exercises, leaving the institution unable to confirm that failover actually works as designed.
Redundant links exist but share the same physical path or carrier, meaning a single physical event can eliminate both links simultaneously — negating the redundancy benefit.
Network availability is not formally monitored or reported for critical system segments, meaning outages or degradation events may go undetected or unrecorded.
Failover recovery times have not been validated against the critical systems' RTO requirements, creating a potential gap between network recovery capability and business continuity obligations.
Capacity planning for network links is absent, and historical utilisation data shows periods of sustained high utilisation that risk impacting availability during peak loads or incidents.
suggestedSources: Network monitoring platform (SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix, Datadog) — uptime and availability reports
ISP/carrier contracts and route diversity certificates
BCM/DR test records — network failover test results
Network Engineering team — topology and redundancy design documentation
Capacity management reports from the IT Operations team
evidenceItems.format: PDF (design and test reports); system-generated availability dashboards (PDF/PNG export); XLSX (capacity utilisation reports)

evidenceItems.name: Network Security Assessment Reports (Penetration Test and Vulnerability Scan)
evidenceItems.description: Independent technical assessment reports covering the network infrastructure supporting critical systems, including penetration test findings, vulnerability scan results, and evidence that identified risks have been remediated or formally accepted within the institution's risk framework.
whatGoodLooksLike: Penetration test reports are produced by an independent and suitably qualified party (internal red team or approved external firm) at least annually, with scope explicitly covering network segments hosting critical systems.

Vulnerability scan results for critical system network infrastructure are generated at least quarterly, with critical and high findings tracked to remediation within defined SLAs (e.g., critical within 30 days, high within 60 days).

Assessment reports include specific findings related to confidentiality (e.g., information disclosure, weak encryption), integrity (e.g., spoofing, man-in-the-middle risks), and availability (e.g., DoS susceptibility, resource exhaustion), demonstrating CIA-aligned scope.
A remediation tracker links each finding from assessment reports to a resolution record (change ticket, patch record, or risk acceptance) with completion dates and verifying evidence.
Prior assessment findings are re-tested in subsequent assessments, with evidence that previously identified vulnerabilities have been successfully remediated.
commonGaps: Penetration testing scope excludes production critical system network segments due to operational risk concerns, leaving the most sensitive environments unvalidated.
Vulnerability scans are conducted irregularly or only when triggered by incidents, rather than on a scheduled, risk-based cadence.
Assessment reports contain findings that remain open beyond their remediation SLA with no formal risk acceptance or escalation recorded, indicating inadequate follow-through.
Assessments are conducted by internal teams without sufficient independence, and no external validation has been performed — reducing the credibility of findings and the assurance provided.
Reports document vulnerabilities but are not formally reviewed and signed off by accountable technology or security leadership, limiting their evidential value for regulatory purposes.
suggestedSources: External penetration testing firm engagement deliverables
Vulnerability management platform (Qualys, Tenable, Rapid7) — scan reports and remediation dashboards
GRC platform — findings register and risk acceptance records
ITSM — remediation change requests linked to assessment findings
Information Security team — assessment scheduling calendar and tracker
evidenceItems.format: PDF (penetration test and scan reports); XLSX (remediation tracker); GRC platform export

evidenceItems.name: Network Change Management Records for Critical System Environments
evidenceItems.description: Formal change management records evidencing that all network changes affecting critical system environments are subject to a governed approval process incorporating security review, ensuring that changes do not degrade the confidentiality, integrity, or availability controls in place.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every network change affecting a critical system segment is recorded in the ITSM change management system with a completed security impact assessment section addressing potential CIA impacts before approval.
Change approval records show that a named network security or information security owner has reviewed and approved changes to critical system network configurations prior to implementation.

Emergency change records for critical system network environments are retrospectively reviewed and approved within a defined window (e.g., 48 hours), with post-implementation testing evidence attached.
Post-implementation reviews (PIR) for significant network changes confirm that CIA controls remain effective after the change, with no unintended security regressions introduced.
Change records are reconciled against actual network configuration snapshots at least quarterly, confirming no unauthorised or undocumented changes have been introduced.
commonGaps: Network changes are implemented directly by technical teams without formal change request records, making it impossible to establish an authorised change history for critical system environments.
Security impact assessments are included as a pro forma step in change records but are routinely left blank or completed with generic responses, providing no meaningful assurance.
Emergency changes — which tend to have the highest risk — are processed outside the standard approval chain and never retrospectively reviewed, creating an uncontrolled change category.
Configuration drift is undetected because network device configurations are not baselined or compared against approved change records, allowing undocumented modifications to persist.

Change management records do not distinguish between changes affecting critical system environments and those affecting less sensitive segments, preventing auditors from focusing their review efficiently.
suggestedSources: ITSM change management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy) — change request records and approval workflows
Network configuration management tool (SolarWinds NCM, Cisco DNA Center, Rancid/Oxidized) — configuration snapshots and diff logs
GRC platform — change risk assessment records
IT Change Advisory Board (CAB) meeting minutes
Network Security team — post-implementation review records
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/XLSX); configuration diff reports (TXT/PDF); CAB minutes (PDF/DOCX)

auditTips: Before the examination, reconcile your live network architecture diagrams against the approved design document — examiners will ask technical staff to walk through the actual topology and any discrepancy between what is documented and what exists will be treated as a significant control gap under this clause.

Prepare a CIA control mapping matrix specific to clause 10.40: create a table listing each critical system, its network segment, and the specific controls in place addressing confidentiality (encryption, access control), integrity (routing controls, change management), and availability (redundancy, capacity) — this gives examiners a structured artefact that directly mirrors the regulatory language and demonstrates deliberate design.

Run a TLS/SSL scan against all critical system network endpoints immediately before the examination and remediate any deprecated protocol support or weak cipher findings — examiners may request evidence of current encryption posture, and a clean scan result paired with a remediation history is far stronger than a policy document alone.

Cross-reference your penetration test findings register against your change management system to produce a closed-loop evidence package: for each network security finding, show the finding report, the associated change ticket implementing the fix, and the retest result confirming closure — this demonstrates a functioning vulnerability management cycle rather than isolated point-in-time assessments.

Conduct a tabletop or live failover exercise for at least one critical system network path in the three months prior to examination, and ensure the test record captures the scenario, actual recovery time, and a named approver sign-off — examiners specifically look for tested (not just designed) redundancy and will probe whether the institution knows its actual recovery time versus its RTO target.

Review all firewall rules protecting critical system segments for any 'any source', 'any destination', or broad port-range rules and either remove them or attach documented business justifications before the examination — overly permissive rules are one of the most commonly cited findings under network security clauses and will attract immediate examiner attention.

Ensure your network change management records for the past 12 months include completed security impact assessments, particularly for emergency changes — compile these into a single indexed package organised by critical system environment so that examiners can sample and trace records efficiently, reducing examination time and demonstrating a well-governed change process.

10.41.title: Network Design Blueprint
10.41.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the network design blueprint is a living, maintained artefact rather than a static diagram produced for audit purposes. Examiners specifically verify that the blueprint comprehensively covers all internal and external network interfaces — including third-party connectivity, cloud links, and vendor access tunnels — and that it is consistent with the institution's actual deployed network configuration. Red flags include diagrams that omit internet-facing or partner-facing segments, blueprints with stale version dates, or a mismatch between logical and physical diagrams (e.g., VLANs shown in logical diagrams that do not correspond to switch configurations). Examiners also assess whether network segmentation boundaries are clearly demarcated in the blueprint and whether the blueprint is formally governed under a change management process so updates are triggered by any network topology change.

evidenceItems.name: Master Network Design Blueprint
evidenceItems.description: The primary network architecture document that serves as the authoritative record of the institution's entire network topology, capturing all internal zones (corporate, production, DMZ, management, OT/SWIFT if applicable) and all external connectivity points. This document demonstrates that the institution has formally established and actively maintains the blueprint as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Blueprint carries a document owner, version number, approval signature, and a review date no older than 12 months, demonstrating active maintenance
All internal network segments are labelled with their function, IP addressing scheme, and VLAN IDs, providing complete internal interface coverage

All external connectivity is enumerated including internet breakouts, inter-branch WAN links, cloud provider peering, payment network connections (RPT, RENTAS, SWIFT), and third-party/vendor VPN tunnels
The document explicitly maps network segmentation boundaries and identifies the security controls (firewalls, ACLs, IDS/IPS) enforced at each boundary
Blueprint version history log is embedded or attached, showing that topology changes triggered documented updates aligned with the change management register
commonGaps: Blueprint exists but has not been updated following infrastructure changes such as cloud migration, new branch connectivity, or SDWAN rollout — version date is more than 12 months old
External interfaces are incompletely catalogued — partner API gateways, payment gateway links, or regulatory reporting endpoints are absent from the diagram
Document is maintained informally by the network team without formal ownership, version control, or governance approval, making it impossible to confirm it represents the authorised design
Network segmentation is depicted at a high level only, without VLAN IDs, subnet ranges, or identification of the enforcement points controlling traffic between segments
Blueprint does not reference or cross-link to related security artefacts such as firewall rule sets, network access control policies, or the asset register, making consistency verification difficult
suggestedSources: Network engineering team SharePoint or Confluence documentation library
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, RSA Archer) network architecture record
Enterprise architecture repository (e.g., LeanIX, Sparx EA, or equivalent)
IT Change Management system — search approved RFCs referencing network topology changes for corroboration
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded or linked Visio/Lucidchart/Draw.io diagram files; version-controlled in a document management system

evidenceItems.name: Physical Network Topology Diagrams
evidenceItems.description: Detailed diagrams depicting the physical layer of the network — data centre rack layouts, physical cabling paths, hardware device placement (routers, switches, firewalls, load balancers), and physical port connectivity. These demonstrate compliance with the blueprint's obligation to highlight physical connectivity between network components.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagrams identify each physical device by hostname, make/model, and physical location (data centre, floor, rack, rack unit position), enabling asset-to-diagram reconciliation
Physical port interconnections between devices are explicitly shown, including uplink speeds and media type (fibre/copper), reflecting the actual cabling infrastructure
Diagrams cover both primary and disaster recovery / secondary data centre physical topology, demonstrating resilience architecture is documented
Diagrams are reconcilable against the institution's CMDB or asset register — every device on the diagram appears in the asset inventory with matching identifiers
Out-of-band management network (IPMI, iDRAC, console server paths) is documented separately or clearly distinguished on the diagram
commonGaps: Physical diagrams exist only for the primary data centre; the DR site or co-location facility network is undocumented or represented by a placeholder
Diagrams show logical topology mislabelled as physical — devices are shown without rack/location context, port-level connectivity is absent, or cloud-style icons are used for physical appliances
Devices on the physical diagram cannot be reconciled against the CMDB or asset inventory because naming conventions differ or the asset register is outdated
Out-of-band / management network paths are absent, leaving a gap in the documented physical connectivity picture
Decommissioned hardware has not been removed from diagrams, creating discrepancies between documented and actual physical topology
suggestedSources: Data centre management team — rack elevation diagrams and cabling records
CMDB / asset management system (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Device42, Racktables)
Facilities or data centre operations SharePoint / DCIM platform
Network operations team Visio/Lucidchart diagram repository
evidenceItems.format: Visio/Lucidchart/Draw.io exported as PDF; supplemented by DCIM or rack elevation reports from CMDB

evidenceItems.name: Logical Network Topology Diagrams
evidenceItems.description: Diagrams depicting the logical network architecture including IP addressing schemes, VLAN assignments, routing domains, VPN overlays, software-defined networking constructs, and traffic flow paths. These demonstrate compliance with the obligation to highlight logical connectivity between network components.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each network segment is labelled with its VLAN ID, IP subnet, and function (e.g., production servers, management, payment processing, user workstations), leaving no unnamed or undocumented segments
Routing topology is documented including OSPF/BGP domains, route redistribution points, and static route summaries, demonstrating a complete logical forwarding picture
VPN and tunnelling overlays (IPSec site-to-site, SD-WAN, MPLS) are shown as logical overlays on the diagram with endpoints and encryption parameters noted
Cloud connectivity is represented in the logical diagram including VPC/VNET peering, ExpressRoute/Direct Connect circuits, and hybrid DNS resolution paths
Logical diagrams are dated and version-controlled consistently with the master blueprint, confirming they are maintained as a unified documentation set
commonGaps: Logical diagrams omit cloud workload segments — AWS VPCs, Azure VNETs, or SaaS interconnects are not represented, leaving external logical interfaces undocumented
VLAN IDs and subnet ranges are absent from the diagrams, making it impossible to verify network segmentation boundaries from the documentation alone
Diagrams do not reflect routing topology — how traffic traverses between segments is not shown, and routing redundancy (HSRP/VRRP) is not depicted
Legacy or decommissioned VLANs remain on diagrams because the logical diagram update process is not triggered by change management events
VPN tunnel endpoints and associated access policies are not documented, leaving third-party logical connectivity partially visible in the blueprint
suggestedSources: Network team Confluence/SharePoint diagram library
Network management platform (e.g., SolarWinds NMS, PRTG, Cisco DNA Centre) — automated topology exports
Cloud provider console network topology views (AWS VPC diagrams, Azure Network Watcher topology)
Firewall management console (e.g., Panorama, FortiManager) — zone and policy topology views
evidenceItems.format: Visio/Lucidchart/Draw.io exported as PDF; supplemented by automated topology exports from NMS tools

evidenceItems.name: Network Segmentation Register and Boundary Control Evidence
evidenceItems.description: A structured record documenting all network segmentation boundaries, the security rationale for each segment, and the technical controls enforced at each boundary. This directly evidences the blueprint's requirement to highlight network segmentations and demonstrates that segmentation is governed rather than incidental.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register enumerates all segments with a stated business/security purpose (e.g., PCI-DSS cardholder data environment, SWIFT secure zone, DMZ, third-party access zone), demonstrating risk-based segmentation design
Each segmentation boundary entry references the specific enforcement control (firewall policy name and version, ACL applied, NAC policy), enabling direct verification against firewall configurations
Inter-segment traffic flows are documented in an approved traffic flow matrix that has been reviewed and signed off by information security, confirming only authorised communication paths exist
The register includes the date of last review and any exceptions to standard segmentation policy with documented risk acceptance and compensating controls
Segmentation aligns with and cross-references other compliance obligations documented in the institution's RMiT posture (e.g., clauses addressing third-party access and critical system isolation)
commonGaps: Segmentation is depicted on diagrams but no formal register or traffic flow matrix exists — examiners cannot determine whether observed segmentation is deliberate policy or coincidental network design
Firewall rules across segment boundaries have not been reviewed or recertified recently, meaning the documented segmentation intent may not match the enforced rule set
Flat or insufficiently segmented zones exist (e.g., production servers and user workstations share the same broadcast domain) with no documented risk acceptance
Third-party or vendor network access segments are not explicitly identified or governed, creating undocumented external logical interfaces within the network
Traffic flow matrix has not been updated to reflect new applications or integrations, leaving undocumented inter-segment flows that may violate segmentation policy
suggestedSources: Information security team — network segmentation policy and zone documentation
Firewall management platform (Panorama, FortiManager, Check Point SmartConsole) — zone definitions and policy export
GRC platform — network segmentation controls record and risk acceptance register
PCI-DSS / ISO 27001 scope documentation (often contains segmentation rationale that can be repurposed)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform record; supplemented by firewall configuration exports as PDF

evidenceItems.name: External Interface and Third-Party Connectivity Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register of all external network interfaces and third-party connectivity arrangements, including cloud providers, payment networks, correspondent banks, outsourced service providers, and regulatory reporting connections. This evidences the blueprint's obligation to identify all external network interfaces and connectivity.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register lists each external connection with: counterparty name, business purpose, connection type (MPLS, IPSec VPN, dedicated circuit, internet), bandwidth, and criticality classification
Each entry references the corresponding contractual or SLA document governing the connection, demonstrating that external interfaces are subject to formal governance
Connectivity to payment systems (RPT, RENTAS, IBG, SWIFT) and BNM-mandated reporting channels are explicitly called out with connection architecture details and resilience arrangements
The register is reconcilable against the network diagram — every external interface on the diagram has a corresponding register entry, and vice versa, with no orphan connections
Register includes the date the connection was established, last reviewed, and the responsible business owner, enabling accountability and periodic recertification
commonGaps: Register exists for primary provider connections but omits indirect connectivity such as SaaS application internet paths, cloud API integrations, or data exchange arrangements with analytics providers
Shadow IT or legacy integrations established without IT governance approval appear in network device configurations but are absent from the register
No formal recertification process exists for the register — connections remain listed long after vendor contracts have ended or connectivity has been decommissioned
Business purpose and data classification for each connection are not documented, preventing examiners from assessing whether connection controls are proportionate to data sensitivity
Payment network connectivity details are described generically without specifying the physical and logical path, resilience arrangements, or failover connectivity documentation
suggestedSources: IT vendor/supplier management register in GRC platform or SharePoint
Procurement and contract management system — filter for connectivity and network service contracts
Network operations team — BGP peer list, VPN peer configuration exports from firewall/router
Cloud management portal — VPC peering connections, ExpressRoute / Direct Connect circuit inventory
Payment operations team — payment network membership documentation and connectivity records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform register exported as PDF; supplemented by router/firewall BGP and VPN peer configuration extracts

evidenceItems.name: Blueprint Maintenance and Change Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that the network design blueprint is actively maintained and updated in response to network topology changes, including change request records, periodic review minutes, and version history logs. This supports the 'establish and maintain' obligation in the clause, distinguishing a living document from a one-time artefact.
whatGoodLooksLike: Change management records (RFCs) include a mandatory field or checklist item requiring the network diagram to be updated as part of change closure, with diagram version number recorded
An annual (or more frequent) formal review of the blueprint is evidenced by meeting minutes or a sign-off record from information security and network leadership confirming accuracy
Document version history within the blueprint or the document management system shows revisions correlated with significant network changes (e.g., data centre migration, cloud onboarding, new branch)
A documented procedure or RACI defines who is responsible for initiating, updating, reviewing, and approving blueprint changes, demonstrating institutional governance rather than ad hoc updates
Post-implementation review records for major network changes reference the blueprint update as a completed action item, creating an auditable trail linking changes to documentation
commonGaps: Change management process does not mandate blueprint updates — network changes are implemented and closed without a documentation update requirement, leading to blueprint drift over time
Periodic review of the blueprint is not evidenced — the institution claims annual review occurs but no meeting minutes, sign-off record, or dated review annotation exists
Version history is absent or incomplete — the document shows a current version but prior versions and the change log describing what was modified are not available
Blueprint ownership is informal — no named document owner or accountable executive is assigned, meaning there is no party responsible for ensuring maintenance occurs
Emergency or out-of-band network changes (e.g., incident response firewall rule changes, emergency circuit activation) are never back-ported into the blueprint documentation
suggestedSources: IT Service Management (ITSM) platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — change request records with network diagram update closure evidence
Document management system version history (SharePoint, Confluence page history)
IT governance or change advisory board (CAB) meeting minutes
Information security team — annual architecture review or network security assessment records
evidenceItems.format: ITSM change records exported as PDF; document management system version history report; meeting minutes in PDF/DOCX

auditTips: Reconcile the blueprint against the CMDB and firewall configuration: pull a device inventory from the CMDB and a BGP/VLAN/zone listing from firewall and switch management platforms, then verify every device and network segment appears on the blueprint — unexplained gaps are high-risk findings that examiners will escalate.

Verify the blueprint's currency by cross-referencing the document version date against the ITSM change log — identify all RFC records closed in the past 12 months that involved network topology changes and confirm each one resulted in a blueprint version increment; a mismatch demonstrates the maintenance obligation is not being met.

Prepare a side-by-side comparison of physical and logical diagrams to demonstrate they are consistent and complementary — examiners will specifically check that VLANs shown on logical diagrams correspond to switch configurations and that physical device placements match DCIM or rack records.

Explicitly map external connectivity to third-party and regulatory obligations before the examination: annotate the external interface register to reference the relevant vendor contracts, BNM-mandated payment network membership documents, and outsourcing notifications, so examiners can trace each external interface to its governance framework without requiring additional requests.

Conduct a walkthrough test of the segmentation controls documented in the blueprint by requesting a firewall rule review or network penetration test result that validates inter-segment traffic flows match the approved traffic flow matrix — presenting this as proactive assurance evidence demonstrates mature compliance posture to examiners.

Ensure the blueprint document includes cloud and hybrid connectivity in a dedicated section or sub-diagram — cloud network topology is frequently missing from blueprints that were designed before cloud adoption and examiners treating cloud segments as external interfaces will scrutinise whether they are documented to the same level of detail as on-premises infrastructure.

Brief the network and infrastructure team leads on the examination scope before the BNM visit — examiners may request live demonstrations such as navigating the network management platform to validate a specific device's connectivity or pulling a real-time topology view, so the team should be prepared to corroborate the blueprint with live system evidence on short notice.

10.42.title: Network Device Log Retention
10.42.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing clause 10.42 focus on three core obligations: sufficiency of log coverage across all network devices, relevance of captured log data for forensic reconstruction, and demonstrable retention of at least three years of intact, unaltered logs. Examiners will request evidence that network devices — including firewalls, routers, switches, IDS/IPS, load balancers, and VPN concentrators — are all enrolled in centralised log collection, and will probe whether any gaps exist in device coverage. A common red flag is log retention policies that exist on paper but have not been enforced technically, resulting in logs being overwritten or purged before the three-year threshold. Examiners also scrutinise log integrity controls, looking for cryptographic hashing, write-once storage, or SIEM-based tamper detection to confirm logs have not been altered post-collection. Institutions that cannot produce complete, retrievable logs for a specific past incident or time window — even within the three-year window — are considered non-compliant regardless of policy documentation.

evidenceItems.name: Network Device Log Retention Policy
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy document that defines the institution's requirements for collecting, storing, and retaining logs from all network devices, specifying the minimum three-year retention period, scope of devices covered, log types required, and responsibilities for enforcement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly names device categories in scope (firewalls, routers, core switches, IDS/IPS, load balancers, VPN gateways, Wi-Fi controllers) with no carve-outs
Retention period is stated as at least three years with a clear effective date and version history showing the policy has been maintained over time
Policy defines 'sufficient and relevant' log types (e.g., authentication events, configuration changes, ACL hits, session establishment/teardown, administrative access) aligned to forensic use cases
Document carries current approval signatures from CISO and CTO or equivalent, with evidence of periodic review (at least annually)
Policy references enforcement mechanisms such as SIEM retention rules, log storage quotas, and automated alerting for log source failures
commonGaps: Policy covers only perimeter firewalls and omits internal switches, IDS/IPS, or cloud-hosted network appliances
Retention period is defined as '90 days online plus archival' without specifying that archived logs remain accessible and retrievable for the full three years
Policy has not been reviewed or re-approved in more than 12 months, raising questions about whether it reflects the current network architecture
Log types listed are generic (e.g., 'all syslog') without mapping to specific forensic use cases, making sufficiency difficult to assess
Policy exists but there is no evidence it has been communicated to or acknowledged by the network operations and security operations teams responsible for implementation
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or local equivalent) — policy register module
SharePoint or Confluence policy library maintained by Information Security or Compliance team
ITSM document management system with version control and approval workflow records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Network Device Log Source Inventory
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive inventory listing every network device within scope, confirming each device is enrolled in centralised log collection, the log types being forwarded, and the date from which log collection commenced for each device.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory is generated or reconciled against the authoritative CMDB and covers all environments — production, DR, DMZ, branch, cloud-hosted network appliances — with no unexplained exclusions
Each device record includes hostname, IP address, device type, OS/firmware version, log forwarding destination (SIEM/syslog server), and log collection start date
Inventory includes a 'last verified' timestamp and is reviewed at least quarterly, with evidence of updates following network changes or device onboarding
A reconciliation process is documented showing how newly provisioned devices are automatically detected and enrolled in log collection before being placed into production
Coverage percentage metric is tracked and reported (target: 100% of in-scope devices actively forwarding logs)
commonGaps: Inventory is maintained in a spreadsheet that is not reconciled with the CMDB, resulting in decommissioned or shadow devices appearing as active log sources while newly added devices are missing
Cloud-hosted network appliances (e.g., AWS Network Firewall, Azure Firewall, virtual load balancers) are not included, creating blind spots in log coverage
Log collection start dates are not recorded, making it impossible to confirm logs exist for the full three-year lookback period for recently migrated devices
Inventory does not distinguish between devices forwarding logs and devices where log forwarding has been configured but is silently failing
Branch or subsidiary network devices are excluded from the inventory without documented risk acceptance or compensating controls
suggestedSources: CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Lansweeper, or NetBox) — network device asset register
SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) — active log source inventory or data inputs report
Network management system (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG, Cisco Prime) — device inventory export
Cloud provider consoles (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Logging) for cloud-hosted network devices
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or CMDB system export (CSV/XLSX) with date-stamped reconciliation records

evidenceItems.name: SIEM / Log Management Platform Retention Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence from the centralised log management or SIEM platform confirming that retention policies are configured to retain network device logs for at least three years, including storage tier configurations, archival rules, and automated deletion or purge schedules.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM or log management platform configuration screenshots or exports show network device log indices/data sources assigned to a retention tier of at least 1,095 days (three years)
Archival pipeline configuration demonstrates that logs transition from hot/warm storage to cold/archive storage without deletion, with retrieval procedures tested and documented
Automated alerts or monitoring rules are configured to notify operations staff when log storage utilisation approaches capacity thresholds or when retention policies are modified
Configuration is under change control — any modification to retention settings requires an approved change request, preventing accidental or unauthorised reduction of retention periods
A sample retrieval test result is available showing that logs from a date more than one year ago can be successfully queried or restored within a defined RTO (e.g., within 24 hours for archived logs)
commonGaps: SIEM retention policy is set to 90 days for online search, with archived logs technically stored for three years but no tested retrieval procedure, making forensic use impractical
Retention configuration applies globally and does not specifically protect network device log indices from early purging when storage is under pressure
Cloud-based SIEM or log aggregator has a default retention setting that has not been overridden, resulting in logs being retained for only 30 or 90 days
Log retention configuration has been changed without a corresponding change request, indicating controls over the SIEM configuration are insufficient
Storage capacity planning has not been performed to ensure sufficient space exists to retain three years of logs without purging, resulting in undocumented ad-hoc deletions
suggestedSources: SIEM platform administration console — retention policy configuration export (Splunk index settings, QRadar log source retention, Sentinel workspace retention settings)
Log management platform (e.g., Graylog, Elastic Stack, Loki) — index lifecycle management policy configuration
Cloud storage configuration (e.g., AWS S3 lifecycle policies, Azure Blob Storage retention policies) for archived log buckets
Change management system — change records related to SIEM or log platform configuration changes
evidenceItems.format: System configuration export (JSON/XML/screenshot) with change management records; PDF for archived test results

evidenceItems.name: Log Integrity and Tamper-Evidence Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that network device logs are protected against unauthorised modification, deletion, or tampering after collection, ensuring logs remain forensically valid and admissible for investigation purposes throughout the three-year retention period.
whatGoodLooksLike: Cryptographic hashing (e.g., SHA-256) is applied to log files at ingestion, with hash values stored separately to enable tamper detection at any point during the retention period

Log storage uses write-once or immutable storage configurations (e.g., AWS S3 Object Lock, Azure Immutable Blob Storage, WORM-enabled storage arrays) preventing deletion or modification of retained logs
Access to log storage is restricted through role-based access controls, with privileged access to modify or delete logs requiring multi-party authorisation or a formal break-glass procedure
Audit trails of administrative access to the log management platform are themselves retained and monitored, creating a chain of custody for the log repository
Periodic integrity verification runs are scheduled and documented, with results showing no detected tampering events across the retention window
commonGaps: Logs are stored on standard network-attached storage with no immutability controls, allowing any administrator with storage access to modify or delete logs without detection
Hashing is applied to log files but hash values are stored in the same system as the logs themselves, making it trivial to replace both the log and its hash
Access control reviews for the log management platform have not been performed, and former employees or service accounts retain write/delete access
No audit trail exists for administrative actions performed within the SIEM or log management platform, creating an undetectable pathway for log tampering
Integrity verification is described in policy but has never been operationalised — no scheduled jobs, results, or evidence of verification runs exist
suggestedSources: SIEM platform — access control configuration and privileged user access logs
Storage platform administration console — immutability or WORM configuration settings
PAM solution (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust) — session recordings and access logs for log infrastructure administrators
Vulnerability and configuration management platform — evidence of integrity check scheduling and results
evidenceItems.format: System configuration export and access control review report (PDF/DOCX); integrity verification job logs (CSV/TXT)

evidenceItems.name: Log Monitoring and Availability Health Reports
evidenceItems.description: Operational reports demonstrating that log collection from network devices is continuously monitored for gaps, failures, and interruptions, ensuring the three-year log corpus remains complete and without unexplained gaps that would undermine forensic investigations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly or weekly log health reports show each enrolled network device's log forwarding status, volume trends, and any detected outages with documented remediation actions and timestamps
Automated alerting is configured to trigger within a defined SLA (e.g., within 15 minutes) when a network device stops forwarding logs, with escalation procedures documented and tested
Log volume anomaly detection is in place to flag both cessation of logs (potential device failure or network issue) and unexpected log volume spikes (potential attack or misconfiguration)

Reports cover a continuous rolling period demonstrating no unresolved gaps longer than a defined threshold (e.g., no gap exceeding four hours without documented explanation) over the trailing 12 months
Incident tickets for log source outages are linked to health reports, showing the full lifecycle from detection to resolution including the duration and scope of any gap in log collection
commonGaps: Log health monitoring only checks whether the SIEM is running, not whether individual device log sources are actively forwarding, resulting in silent gaps going undetected for extended periods
Alerting thresholds are set too loosely (e.g., alert only if a device has sent no logs for 24 hours), allowing forensically significant gaps to accumulate before detection
Health reports are generated but not reviewed or actioned — log source failures remain unresolved for weeks because no owner tracks the reports
No log volume baseline exists, making it impossible to distinguish between a device that has genuinely stopped logging and one that is simply quiet due to low network activity
Historical health reports are not retained, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate to examiners that log collection was continuous over the three-year period
suggestedSources: SIEM platform — data source health dashboard exports (Splunk Monitoring Console, QRadar System Health, Sentinel workspace health)
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — incident and problem tickets related to log source failures
Network monitoring platform (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix) — syslog forwarding health alerts
Security operations daily/weekly operational reports maintained by SOC team
evidenceItems.format: PDF or system-generated HTML reports; ITSM ticket exports (CSV/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Log Retrieval and Forensic Readiness Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution has tested its ability to retrieve and utilise historical network device logs for investigation and forensic purposes, validating that retained logs are not merely stored but are practically accessible and usable within the three-year window.
whatGoodLooksLike: At least one documented log retrieval test per year demonstrates successful extraction of network device logs from a date more than 12 months prior, with results showing completeness, readability, and chain of custody

Forensic readiness exercises or tabletop incident response simulations include a step requiring retrieval of historical network logs, with outputs documenting time to retrieve, format of logs, and tools used

Test results specify which device types and log categories were retrieved, the date range queried, and the number of log events returned, enabling examiners to assess whether retrieval is representative of the full scope
Any failures or delays encountered during retrieval tests are documented as findings with remediation actions tracked to closure, demonstrating continuous improvement of forensic readiness
Retrieval procedures are formally documented so that any authorised analyst — not just the original system administrator — can execute a log retrieval request during an investigation
commonGaps: No retrieval tests have ever been performed — logs are assumed to be accessible based on storage configuration alone, but actual recoverability has never been validated
Retrieval tests cover only recent logs (last 30–90 days) and have never tested access to logs approaching the three-year boundary, leaving the oldest archived logs unvalidated

Test results show that archived logs are stored in a proprietary or legacy format that requires specific software versions to read, creating a forensic readiness risk if those tools become unavailable

Retrieval procedures are known only to one or two senior staff members and are undocumented, creating a key-person dependency that would impair forensic investigations if those individuals are unavailable
Retrieval test outcomes are not formally recorded or signed off, making it impossible to demonstrate to examiners that tests were actually conducted
suggestedSources: SOC or IT Security team — forensic readiness exercise reports and log retrieval test records
Incident response platform or ITSM — documented IR simulation or tabletop exercise outputs
GRC platform — control testing records and evidence repository for technology risk controls
Business continuity and disaster recovery test records where log retrieval is included as a dependency
evidenceItems.format: PDF test report with approver sign-off; supporting log extracts or screenshots as annexures

auditTips: Before the examination, run a reconciliation between the CMDB network device inventory and the SIEM active log source list, and produce a one-page coverage matrix showing percentage of devices actively forwarding logs per device category — examiners will ask for this and an on-the-spot gap is a red flag

Pull a sample of raw logs from a network device dated exactly three years ago (or as close as possible) and document the retrieval steps, time taken, and log completeness — presenting this proactively demonstrates that the three-year retention requirement is met in practice, not just on paper

Validate that cloud-hosted network appliances (virtual firewalls, cloud-native load balancers, SD-WAN controllers) are included in both the log source inventory and the SIEM — examiners are increasingly aware of hybrid infrastructure and will probe whether cloud network logs are captured under the same policy

Review SIEM retention configuration exports and cross-reference them against the log retention policy — confirm that no index or data source has a retention period shorter than 1,095 days, and document any exceptions with approved risk acceptance records signed by the CISO

Check that privileged access to the log management platform (ability to delete, modify, or alter retention settings) is restricted, reviewed quarterly, and that any changes to retention configuration in the past 12 months have a corresponding approved change request — examiners look for evidence that retention commitments cannot be silently undermined

Prepare a one-page forensic readiness summary showing the end-to-end log lifecycle: collection → normalisation → storage tier → archival → retrieval, with estimated retrieval times for each storage tier — this demonstrates that logs are not just retained but are operationally usable for investigations

Ensure log health monitoring alerts and the corresponding ITSM incident tickets for log source outages are archived and retrievable — examiners may request a 12-month history of log source failures and their resolution times to assess whether gaps in the log corpus exist that could undermine the three-year continuity requirement

10.43.title: Network Segmentation Safeguards
10.43.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the financial institution has implemented demonstrable, enforceable safeguards that prevent lateral movement of a compromise from any group entity into the financial institution's environment. Examiners will scrutinise the actual network architecture diagrams against firewall rule sets and access control lists to verify that logical segmentation is not merely documented but is technically enforced. A key red flag is flat or loosely segmented group networks where inter-entity trust relationships, shared credentials, or broad firewall rules could allow a compromise in a non-financial-institution subsidiary to propagate into the regulated entity. Examiners will also test whether monitoring controls exist at the segmentation boundary to detect anomalous cross-entity traffic, and whether the institution can demonstrate periodic testing or validation that segmentation controls remain effective. Institutions that rely solely on policy statements without technical evidence of enforcement, or that cannot map their group entity boundaries in network terms, are likely to receive adverse findings.

evidenceItems.name: Group Network Architecture Diagram
evidenceItems.description: A current, approved network topology diagram depicting all group entities, their interconnection points, and the logical segmentation boundaries (VLANs, DMZs, network zones) separating the financial institution from other entities within the group. This document demonstrates that segmentation has been deliberately designed and formally documented.
whatGoodLooksLike: Diagram is dated within the last 12 months and carries formal approval signatures from the CISO or Head of IT Infrastructure
All group entities are clearly labelled and their network zones are colour-coded or demarcated with distinct VLAN IDs or IP subnet ranges
All inter-entity interconnection points (MPLS links, site-to-site VPNs, shared service hubs) are explicitly shown with the corresponding control layer (firewall, proxy, NAC) at each boundary
The diagram distinguishes between production, non-production, and shared services zones across the group, with the regulated financial institution's perimeter clearly bounded
Version control history is maintained showing how the architecture has evolved, with change rationale documented
commonGaps: Diagrams are outdated (more than 12 months old) and do not reflect recent infrastructure changes such as cloud onboarding or subsidiary acquisitions
Group entity boundaries are shown at a high level only, with no detail on the actual segmentation controls enforced at each interconnect point
Shared services (Active Directory, DNS, SIEM, backup) that traverse entity boundaries are omitted from the diagram, understating the actual attack surface
The diagram exists in an uncontrolled format (e.g., informal Visio on a personal drive) with no version history or formal approval record
Cloud-hosted workloads and SaaS integrations shared across group entities are not represented, creating a blind spot in the segmentation picture
suggestedSources: IT Infrastructure or Network Engineering team SharePoint/Confluence documentation repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or internally built) under the IT Asset and Architecture register
Enterprise Architecture management tool (e.g., LeanIX, Ardoq) if used by the group
Change Management system for the most recent approved network change records that should align with the diagram
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formally approved version for submission) with source Visio/Lucidchart/draw.io file retained internally

evidenceItems.name: Firewall Rule Sets and Access Control Lists at Group Boundaries
evidenceItems.description: Exported firewall policy configurations and ACLs governing all traffic permitted between the financial institution and other group entities. This evidence demonstrates that segmentation is technically enforced, not merely conceptual, and that the principle of least privilege governs inter-entity connectivity.
whatGoodLooksLike: Rule sets are exported directly from the firewall management platform (e.g., Cisco FMC, Palo Alto Panorama, Fortinet FortiManager) with a timestamp confirming currency
Inter-entity rules are explicitly tagged or grouped and follow a default-deny posture, with only business-justified traffic permitted on named protocols and ports
Each rule has an associated change ticket reference and business owner, demonstrating that rules were approved through a formal process rather than informally added
Rules prohibit or strictly limit high-risk protocols (e.g., unrestricted RDP, SMB, WMI) from traversing the group boundary, reducing lateral movement risk
A periodic rule review record (at least annual) is attached, confirming that stale or overly permissive rules have been identified and remediated
commonGaps: Rule sets contain large blocks of 'any-to-any' or broadly scoped rules between group entity subnets that effectively nullify segmentation
Rules have no associated change ticket, making it impossible to demonstrate that they were formally approved and business-justified
Firewall exports are provided only as screenshots rather than machine-readable configuration exports, preventing thorough analysis
Rule review has not been performed within the last 12 months, and the institution cannot demonstrate that legacy rules from historical integrations have been decommissioned
Separate firewall instances for cloud-hosted group entity interconnects are not included, leaving cloud-based lateral movement paths unaddressed
suggestedSources: Firewall management console export (Palo Alto Panorama, Cisco FMC, Fortinet FortiManager, Check Point SmartConsole)
ITSM change management system (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for linked change records
Network Security team's policy review register or GRC platform under the network security control domain
Cloud security posture management tool (e.g., Prisma Cloud, Microsoft Defender for Cloud) for cloud boundary ACLs
evidenceItems.format: Structured configuration export (XML/CSV from firewall platform) accompanied by PDF change approval records

evidenceItems.name: Network Segmentation Policy and Group-Level Interconnection Standards
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy document that establishes the institution's requirements for logical network segmentation from other group entities, defines permissible inter-entity connectivity patterns, and assigns accountability for maintaining and reviewing segmentation controls. This demonstrates that the institution has translated the regulatory obligation into enforceable internal standards.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references the group structure and names or categorises the entity types (e.g., insurance subsidiary, fund management arm, overseas branches) to which segmentation requirements apply
Policy mandates a default-deny stance at group boundaries and requires formal business justification and approval for any inter-entity network connectivity
Roles and responsibilities for network segmentation design, approval, and periodic review are clearly defined and assigned to named roles (e.g., Group CISO, Network Security Lead)
Policy includes a minimum review cycle (at least annually or upon material group structure changes) and carries evidence of the most recent review and board/senior management endorsement
Policy cross-references related standards such as the institution's vulnerability management, access control, and incident response policies to demonstrate an integrated control framework
commonGaps: Policy addresses segmentation in general terms (e.g., 'segment the network') without specifying group entity boundaries or the types of controls required at those boundaries
Policy has not been updated following group mergers, acquisitions, or the onboarding of new shared services, rendering it inconsistent with the current operating environment
Accountability for compliance with the policy is not assigned, making it impossible to identify who is responsible when gaps are found during examination
Policy exists but has not been communicated to or acknowledged by the relevant technical and operational teams, meaning it functions as a paper control only
Policy lacks measurable compliance criteria, making it difficult to objectively assess whether the institution is compliant
suggestedSources: Policy management repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or dedicated policy management tool such as PolicyHub or MetaCompliance)
GRC platform policy register with version history and approval workflow records
Group IT Security Governance committee minutes confirming policy endorsement
Legal and Compliance document management system if policy forms part of the broader regulatory compliance framework
evidenceItems.format: PDF with embedded approval metadata and version history table

evidenceItems.name: Network Segmentation Testing and Validation Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic technical testing — such as penetration tests, network segmentation validation exercises, or vulnerability assessments — that verify the operational effectiveness of segmentation controls at group entity boundaries. This evidence demonstrates that the institution does not rely solely on design-time controls but actively validates their continued efficacy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test reports are produced by a qualified internal red team or an accredited external penetration testing firm, with scope explicitly including group entity interconnect boundaries
Reports document specific test cases that attempted lateral movement from a simulated compromised group entity host into the financial institution's network, with findings and outcomes clearly stated
Any segmentation bypass or weaknesses identified in testing are linked to remediation tickets in the ITSM system, with closure dates and retesting confirmation
Testing is performed at least annually and additionally following material changes to the group network architecture or after a significant security incident
Test methodology is documented and consistent with recognised frameworks (e.g., PTES, OWASP, NIST SP 800-115), providing credibility to the results
commonGaps: Penetration tests are scoped to the financial institution's internal network only and do not include group entity boundary testing, leaving the key regulatory concern untested
Test reports are available but remediation tracking is absent — findings are noted but there is no evidence that vulnerabilities were closed within agreed timelines
Testing has not been performed within the preceding 12 months, and no compensating controls or rationale for the gap are documented
Tests were conducted by internal IT staff who also manage the network, creating an independence conflict that undermines the credibility of results
Only automated vulnerability scanning (e.g., Nessus, Qualys) is presented, with no evidence of manual validation that segmentation controls cannot be bypassed
suggestedSources: Information Security team's penetration test register and report repository (SharePoint or GRC platform)
ITSM vulnerability and finding tracking system (ServiceNow, Jira) for remediation records linked to test findings
External penetration testing vendor engagement records and final reports
Threat and Vulnerability Management function's periodic reporting to the Risk or IT Security Committee
evidenceItems.format: PDF penetration test report with executive summary and technical findings, plus linked ITSM remediation records exported as spreadsheet or PDF

evidenceItems.name: Inter-Entity Connectivity Register and Approval Records
evidenceItems.description: A maintained inventory of all approved network connections between the financial institution and other group entities, including the business justification, data flows, approvals, and the specific controls applied at each connection. This demonstrates disciplined governance over the inter-entity attack surface and provides an auditable record of every deliberate segmentation boundary exception.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register is comprehensive and maps every inter-entity link by source entity, destination entity, protocol, port range, data classification, and the control layer enforcing the connection (firewall rule ID, VPN tunnel ID, etc.)
Each entry includes a named business owner accountable for the connection, a reference to the original change approval ticket, and a periodic revalidation date

The register is reconciled against live firewall rule sets at least annually, with a documented reconciliation record confirming that all live rules appear in the register and all register entries are reflected in live rules
Connections involving high-sensitivity data (e.g., customer PII, payment data) are flagged and subject to enhanced review, demonstrating risk-tiered governance
Decommissioned connections are tracked with closure dates and confirmation that associated firewall rules were removed, preventing rule accumulation
commonGaps: No centralised register exists; connectivity knowledge is distributed across individual engineers and is not formally documented or governed
Register is incomplete — it captures only new connections established after a certain date, with legacy connectivity from before a governance programme was initiated remaining undocumented
Business owners are listed as generic team names rather than named individuals, making accountability for revalidation unenforceable
Reconciliation against live firewall rules has never been performed, meaning discrepancies between the register and actual network state are unknown
Temporary connections established for projects or incidents are not tracked or have no expiry mechanism, resulting in persistent access paths that were intended to be time-limited
suggestedSources: GRC platform's network asset or connectivity register module (ServiceNow, Archer, or custom-built)
IT Infrastructure team's SharePoint-based asset and connection register
ITSM system change records as a source for populating and cross-checking the register
Network management platform (SolarWinds, NetBrain, or equivalent) for automated discovery data to validate manual register entries
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/CSV) or GRC platform export with linked PDF change approval records

evidenceItems.name: Cross-Entity Traffic Monitoring and Alerting Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution actively monitors traffic traversing group entity boundaries and has configured alerts for anomalous or policy-violating cross-entity communication. This demonstrates that segmentation is supported by detective controls, ensuring that any degradation of preventive controls or attempted lateral movement is identified and investigated.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM use cases or detection rules are documented that specifically target anomalous inter-entity traffic patterns, such as unexpected protocol usage, high-volume data transfers, or connections to undocumented group entity subnets
Network flow data (NetFlow, sFlow, or equivalent) from group boundary interfaces is ingested into the SIEM and retained in accordance with the institution's log retention policy
Alert records from the past review period demonstrate that cross-entity monitoring is operational, with at least some alerts generated, triaged, and closed through the incident management process
Monitoring coverage is validated through periodic reviews confirming that all group boundary interfaces are included as log sources, with no blind spots
Threat hunting records or periodic traffic analysis reports show that the security operations team proactively reviews inter-entity traffic trends, not just reactive alerting
commonGaps: SIEM monitoring is configured for intra-institution traffic but group boundary interfaces are not included as log sources, leaving cross-entity movement entirely undetected
Network flow logs are collected but no use cases or alert rules are tuned specifically for group boundary anomalies, meaning the data is present but not actionable
Alerts exist but triage and closure records are absent or cursory, suggesting that cross-entity alerts are not being meaningfully investigated
Log retention for cross-entity traffic is shorter than the institution's standard or BNM's recommended retention period, limiting the ability to perform retrospective analysis
Monitoring focuses only on north-south (internet-facing) traffic with no east-west (group-internal) monitoring, which is precisely the traffic path a lateral movement attack would use
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Wazuh) — use case library and alert history exports
Security Operations Centre (SOC) incident and alert management records (ServiceNow Security Operations, Jira)
Network detection and response (NDR) platform (Darktrace, ExtraHop, Vectra) if deployed at group boundaries
Log management platform retention configuration records and ingestion source inventory
evidenceItems.format: PDF SIEM use case documentation plus spreadsheet/CSV of alert records for the review period; log source inventory as spreadsheet

auditTips: Before the examination, perform a self-assessment reconciliation: compare the group network architecture diagram against live firewall rule sets to identify any connections that exist in reality but are absent from the diagram, or diagram elements with no corresponding enforcement rule. Unexplained discrepancies are a primary examiner focus and should be resolved prior to submission.

Map every shared service that crosses group entity boundaries — Active Directory domains, DNS resolvers, backup systems, centralised logging, and shared application platforms — and prepare a documented rationale for each, along with the compensating controls applied. Examiners will probe shared services as potential lateral movement vectors that standard segmentation does not address.

Ensure that penetration test scope letters and final reports explicitly name group entity boundary testing as an in-scope objective. If existing reports do not cover this, commission a targeted network segmentation validation exercise before the examination rather than submitting general penetration test reports that do not address the regulatory concern.

Prepare a concise one-page narrative for examiners that explains the group structure, names each entity, and describes the connectivity model — which entities are connected, for what business purpose, and through which controls. Examiners who must piece this together themselves from fragmented documents are more likely to form an adverse view; a clear narrative demonstrates command of the control environment.

Cross-reference the inter-entity connectivity register against the outcome of the most recent change management review to demonstrate that all connections were formally approved and that no informal or undocumented connections exist. Highlight any connections that were identified and decommissioned as a result of this review, as this demonstrates a functioning governance process rather than a static paper exercise.

Validate that incident response runbooks include specific procedures for group-contained incidents — scenarios where a compromise is detected in a group entity and the financial institution must decide whether to sever inter-entity links. The existence of a tested playbook for this scenario demonstrates that the institution has operationalised its segmentation strategy beyond the preventive layer.

Collect evidence of the most recent governance forum (IT Security Committee, Risk Committee, or equivalent) where the status of group network segmentation controls was formally reported. Board or senior management visibility of this control domain, evidenced by committee minutes and management information packs, demonstrates tone-from-the-top and satisfies examiners that segmentation is treated as a governed risk rather than a purely technical matter.

10.44.title: Backup Strategy and Procedures
10.44.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution's backup strategy is operationally effective, not merely documented. Examiners will trace specific obligations through the full lifecycle: from documented procedures (10.44a), through backup inventory completeness covering all asset classes in 10.44b, to physical and logical controls at the backup site (10.44c), removable media governance against Appendix 1 (10.44d), evidence of actual test execution with root-cause remediation (10.44e), and finally the independence and rigour of the risk assessment under 10.44f. Red flags include gaps in coverage of critical systems, untested or repeatedly failed backups without documented remediation, absence of an independently conducted risk assessment, and reliance on a single backup site or single backup generation. Examiners give particular weight to the independence of the risk assessment and to whether remedial actions from failed backup tests are closed promptly and verifiably.

evidenceItems.name: Backup Strategy Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy and operational procedures that define the institution's backup data life cycle — including backup schedules, retention tiers, rotation schemes, labelling standards, restoration workflows, roles and responsibilities, and escalation paths. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.44(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is formally approved by senior management or the Board IT Risk Committee and reviewed within the last 12 months, with version history and change log maintained
Procedures explicitly define backup frequency (full, incremental, differential) for each criticality tier of data, system, and application, aligned to documented RPO and RTO targets
The data life cycle is end-to-end: procedures cover creation, verification, labelling, transportation, storage, retrieval, restoration testing, and secure disposal of backup media
Roles and responsibilities for each life cycle stage are unambiguously assigned to named roles (not individuals), with RACI or equivalent accountability matrix included
Procedures reference and are consistent with the institution's BCP/DR framework, data classification policy, and Appendix 1 requirements for sensitive data handling
commonGaps: Policy exists but procedures are high-level only — no step-by-step operational guidance, leaving execution dependent on undocumented tribal knowledge
Retention schedules are defined for primary data but backup media retention tiers are absent or inconsistent with regulatory minimum periods
