Policy has not been reviewed or re-approved following material changes to infrastructure (e.g., cloud migration, new core banking platform), making it stale
Roles are assigned to individuals rather than job titles, creating gaps when staff leave or change roles
Restoration procedures describe how to restore files but omit system-level restoration sequences, recovery priority order, and dependency mapping for critical applications
suggestedSources: IT Governance / Information Security SharePoint or document management repository (e.g., Confluence, OpenText)
GRC platform policy register (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, LogicGate)
IT Operations runbook repository maintained by Infrastructure or Data Centre team
Board/ITSC/MANCO meeting minutes approving the current version of the policy
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control header, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems and Backup Coverage Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured inventory mapping every critical system, application, database, and file type enumerated in clause 10.44(b) — including operating system images, production programs, system utilities, master files, transaction files, and event logs — to its corresponding backup job, schedule, retention period, and storage location. Demonstrates that backup coverage is complete and adequate relative to the institution's critical asset universe.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register is derived from and reconciled against the authoritative CMDB or asset inventory, with a documented reconciliation date showing no unprotected critical assets

Each entry specifies the backup type (full/incremental/differential), frequency, last successful backup timestamp, retention period, and number of generations maintained — meeting or exceeding RPO requirements
Coverage explicitly includes all asset classes cited in 10.44(b): OS software, production programs, system utilities, master and transaction files, and event logs, with no asset class omitted
Register is reviewed and signed off by the system/application owner on at least a quarterly basis, evidencing business ownership of backup adequacy
Backup storage locations (on-site, off-site, cloud) are mapped per asset, enabling examiners to confirm geographic and media diversity for critical systems
commonGaps: Event logs and system utilities are excluded from the backup inventory, with coverage limited only to databases and application data — directly missing 10.44(b) asset classes
Register is manually maintained and has not been reconciled against the CMDB in over six months, resulting in newly onboarded systems without backup coverage
Backup frequency is documented as a target (e.g., 'daily') but actual last-run timestamps are not recorded, preventing verification of compliance with the stated schedule
Number of backup generations or copies retained is not specified per system, making it impossible to confirm 'adequate number' as required by 10.44(b)

Register is owned by IT Operations and has never been reviewed or attested by business system owners, resulting in criticality classifications that are outdated or misaligned with business impact assessments
suggestedSources: Backup software management console (e.g., Veeam, Commvault, Veritas NetBackup, AWS Backup, Azure Backup) — exportable job and schedule reports
CMDB / asset management platform (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Lansweeper)
IT Operations team — backup coverage matrix or register (spreadsheet or GRC platform)
BCP/DR team — critical system inventory and RTO/RPO register for cross-referencing
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export with system owner sign-off, dated reconciliation

evidenceItems.name: Backup Site Security Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the off-site backup storage facility meets clause 10.44(c) requirements for environmental security and access control. This encompasses physical security assessments, environmental monitoring records, access logs, and any third-party certifications for co-location or cloud backup providers. Demonstrates the institution's ability to protect backup integrity and confidentiality at rest in the storage site.
whatGoodLooksLike: Physical security controls at the backup site are formally documented and include multi-factor physical access control (e.g., biometric + card), CCTV coverage with retention period, and manned security or equivalent compensating controls

Environmental controls are evidenced by monitoring logs showing temperature, humidity, fire suppression, and flood protection parameters maintained within vendor-specified tolerances, with alerts for threshold breaches
Access to backup media is restricted to authorised personnel only, with access logs reviewed at least monthly; all exceptions or anomalies are documented and investigated

For third-party off-site storage or cloud providers, a current vendor assessment report or independent certification (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, SS-564 for data centres) is on file, with right-to-audit provisions in the contract

The backup site is geographically separated from the primary data centre by a distance assessed as sufficient to avoid simultaneous impact from the same localised disaster event, documented in the DR site selection rationale
commonGaps: Access logs for the backup vault or tape library exist in theory but are not reviewed periodically, and no review records or exception reports are maintained
Environmental monitoring is in place but alerting thresholds are not formally set or documented; historical logs cannot be produced for the past 12 months
The institution relies on a third-party off-site storage vendor but has not conducted or obtained any independent security assessment of that vendor's facility within the past two years
Backup site is a secondary room within the same building as the primary data centre — failing the geographic separation requirement and not constituting a genuine off-site backup site

Cloud backup storage is used but encryption key management documentation is absent, leaving it unclear whether the institution or the cloud provider controls decryption, and whether access control obligations are met
suggestedSources: Data Centre / Facilities Management team — physical access control system logs and environmental monitoring dashboards
Security Operations team — CCTV retention policy and access review reports
Third-party off-site storage provider contracts and latest security assessment or certification report
Cloud provider compliance portal (e.g., AWS Artifact, Azure Compliance Manager) for certifications
IT Infrastructure team — DR site selection assessment document
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports, system-exported access logs (CSV/PDF), vendor certificates (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Removable Media Security Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution's storage and transportation of sensitive data in removable media (tapes, external drives, USB devices) meets the minimum controls specified in Appendix 1 of RMiT or an equivalent standard. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.44(d), including encryption requirements, chain of custody controls, authorised handling procedures, and secure disposal.
whatGoodLooksLike: All removable media containing sensitive backup data is encrypted using a recognised algorithm (AES-256 or equivalent), with encryption key management procedures documented and keys stored separately from media

A chain-of-custody manifest accompanies every removable media shipment, recording media identifiers, custodian names, transfer timestamps, seals, and recipient acknowledgements — with records retained for the full regulatory period
Transportation is conducted by an approved, contracted courier with documented security standards; the courier agreement includes non-disclosure, liability, and audit provisions aligned to Appendix 1
An authorised media handler list is maintained and reviewed at least annually; only listed personnel may handle, transport, or access removable backup media
Secure disposal of end-of-life removable media is documented through degaussing or physical destruction certificates, with a destruction log referencing each media identifier
commonGaps: Backup tapes are encrypted but the encryption key management process is undocumented, and keys are stored on systems accessible to the same administrators who manage the media — creating a single point of compromise
Chain-of-custody records exist for outbound media shipments but are not maintained for the return journey or for media retrieved for restoration testing, leaving gaps in the end-to-end custody trail

The institution has migrated primarily to cloud backup but retains residual removable media usage without updated procedures; Appendix 1 controls have not been formally re-assessed in the context of cloud vs. physical media

Transportation is conducted by general courier services without specific security provisions in the contract rather than by an approved secure courier — a direct gap against Appendix 1 minimum controls
No formal secure disposal process exists; decommissioned tapes are stored in a locked cabinet indefinitely rather than being degaussed or physically destroyed, creating data exposure risk
suggestedSources: IT Operations / Infrastructure team — removable media register and chain-of-custody manifests
Procurement / Vendor Management team — secure courier contracts and vendor assessments
Information Security team — encryption standards documentation and key management procedures
Data Centre Management — media destruction certificates and disposal log
GRC or ISMS platform — Appendix 1 controls mapping and compliance register
evidenceItems.format: PDF policy/procedure, spreadsheet chain-of-custody log, PDF destruction certificates

evidenceItems.name: Backup and Restoration Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic backup restoration tests, including test plans, test results, identified failures, root-cause analyses, and evidence of remedial actions taken to address unsuccessful backups — as required by clause 10.44(e). Demonstrates that backup restoration capabilities have been validated in practice, not only by monitoring backup job completion status.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal testing schedule exists and is adhered to, with tests conducted at a frequency appropriate to system criticality (e.g., quarterly for critical systems, annually for non-critical), evidenced by test records spanning at least the past 12 months

Test records document the specific systems tested, data restored, restoration method used, time taken to restore, comparison of restored data against source (integrity check), and pass/fail determination — not just backup job completion logs

Every test failure is linked to a documented root-cause analysis and a tracked remediation action in the ITSM or GRC platform, with closure evidence confirming the root cause was resolved before the next scheduled test

Restoration tests include recovery of all critical asset types cited in 10.44(b) — not limited to databases alone — with explicit confirmation that OS images, utilities, and logs were successfully restored
Test results are reported to IT management or the relevant governance committee, with sign-off evidencing management awareness and oversight of backup restoration capability
commonGaps: Backup monitoring dashboards show successful backup job completions, but no actual restoration tests have been conducted — the distinction between a successful backup write and a verified restoration is not understood or acted upon
Restoration tests are conducted but limited to a subset of non-critical systems for convenience; core banking, payment systems, and event logs have never been included in a restoration test

Failed backup tests are logged in the ITSM system but root-cause analysis is marked as 'network timeout' or similar superficial descriptions without investigation; remediation actions are not tracked to closure
Testing frequency is annual for all systems regardless of criticality, which BNM examiners will view as insufficient for critical financial systems that require quarterly or more frequent validation

Test records are held informally in email threads or spreadsheets with no version control, making it impossible to demonstrate a consistent, auditable testing history over the required retention period
suggestedSources: ITSM platform — change and incident records for backup restoration tests (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
GRC platform — control testing register and remediation action tracker
IT Operations / DR team — restoration test plans, scripts, and results reports
Backup software console — job history and error logs as supporting evidence
Governance committee minutes (ITSC, Risk Committee) where test results were reported
evidenceItems.format: PDF test reports, ITSM exports (PDF/CSV), committee minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Independent Risk Assessment of End-to-End Backup Storage and Delivery
evidenceItems.description: The report and supporting working papers from an independent risk assessment of the institution's end-to-end backup storage and delivery management, as required by clause 10.44(f). The assessment must evaluate whether existing controls are adequate to protect sensitive data at all times across the full backup lifecycle — from creation through transportation, storage, retrieval, and disposal. Independence requires that assessors are not directly responsible for the backup operations being assessed.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment is conducted by a party independent of the IT Operations and Infrastructure function responsible for backups — either Internal Audit, an external consultant, or a qualified third party — with the assessor's independence explicitly documented

Scope of assessment covers the full end-to-end lifecycle as specified in 10.44(f): backup creation, verification, labelling, transportation, off-site storage, access controls, encryption, restoration capability, and media disposal
The report includes a formal control adequacy opinion with findings rated by risk severity, specific control gaps identified, and management responses with committed remediation timelines

Assessment methodology is documented and references applicable standards (RMiT Appendix 1, ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-34, or equivalent), enabling examiners to assess the rigour of the evaluation approach
The assessment has been conducted within the past 12-24 months (or following material changes to backup infrastructure), with a clear schedule for the next assessment cycle documented and approved
commonGaps: The 'independent' assessment was conducted by the same IT Operations team responsible for backup management — this does not meet the independence requirement of 10.44(f) regardless of how it is labelled

An assessment exists but its scope is limited to backup job monitoring and storage capacity, omitting transportation security, access controls, encryption key management, and media disposal — missing the 'end-to-end' requirement

The assessment report lists findings but management responses are generic ('will be addressed') with no committed timelines; there is no evidence that high-risk findings were remediated and the assessment was closed

The last independent assessment was conducted more than three years ago with no subsequent review, despite significant infrastructure changes (e.g., introduction of cloud backup, new DR site, staff turnover in key roles)
Internal Audit conducted the assessment but the work programme was scoped entirely by IT Operations, compromising the independence of the exercise and limiting coverage of sensitive areas
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — audit report repository and findings tracker
External auditor or IT assurance firm — engagement report and management letter
Risk Management team — risk assessment register and third-party assurance log in GRC platform
Board Audit Committee or Risk Committee — minutes recording receipt and approval of the assessment report
Vendor Management team — engagement letter and terms of reference for external assessor
evidenceItems.format: PDF assessment report with scope, methodology, findings, ratings, and management responses

auditTips: Map every evidence item directly to a lettered sub-clause (a) through (f) before the examination. BNM examiners structure their review around each sub-obligation and will expect the institution to demonstrate coverage of each one distinctly — a single 'backup policy' document will not satisfy sub-clauses (c), (d), (e), and (f) on its own.

Prepare a restoration test evidence pack that clearly distinguishes between backup job completion logs and actual restoration test records. Examiners frequently encounter institutions that conflate monitoring dashboards (which confirm data was written) with restoration testing (which confirms data can be recovered). Have at least one recent restoration test report ready that shows data integrity validation post-recovery.

For the independent risk assessment under 10.44(f), prepare a one-page independence attestation that documents who conducted the assessment, their reporting line, and why they are considered independent of backup operations. If the assessment was conducted by Internal Audit, ensure the audit charter and work programme confirm they had unrestricted scope — this is a common examiner challenge.

Conduct a pre-examination reconciliation between the backup coverage register and the CMDB, with the output dated within 30 days of the examination. Examiners will cross-reference the critical system inventory against backup records; any system present in the CMDB but absent from the backup register is an immediate finding that is difficult to explain on the spot.

For removable media controls under 10.44(d), prepare a side-by-side mapping of the institution's actual controls against each minimum control in Appendix 1. If the institution uses an equivalent standard rather than Appendix 1 directly, document the equivalence mapping explicitly — examiners will require a justification for why the alternative standard is considered equivalent.

Trace the remediation closure cycle for at least the three most recent backup test failures end-to-end: from the initial failure record in the ITSM system, through the root-cause analysis document, to the remediation action closure record and the subsequent successful test. This demonstrates that the '10.44(e) prompt remediation' obligation is operationally embedded, not just a policy statement.

Verify that backup site access logs have been reviewed and any exceptions investigated within the past quarter, and retain the review records. Examiners will request access logs for the off-site backup facility and will look for evidence that the institution actively monitors access rather than simply collecting logs — an unreviewed log is treated as an absent control.

10.45.title: Tamper-Proof Backup and Isolated Recovery
10.45.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised—not merely documented—both a tamper-proof backup arrangement and a functionally separate isolated recovery environment. Examiners specifically probe the technical controls that enforce immutability (e.g., WORM storage, air-gap, cryptographic integrity checks) and will demand evidence that these controls have been independently verified rather than self-attested. A key red flag is the absence of ransomware-specific recovery scenarios in test exercises, particularly where recovery time objectives have never been validated under simulated destructive-attack conditions. Examiners will cross-reference tolerable downtime figures declared in the Business Impact Analysis against actual recovery test results to confirm the institution can genuinely meet its own stated thresholds. Governance oversight—Board or senior management sign-off on backup architecture and test outcomes—is also scrutinised to confirm accountability at the appropriate level.

evidenceItems.name: Tamper-Proof Backup Policy and Architecture Documentation
evidenceItems.description: The formal policy, standards, and architecture design records that define the institution's tamper-proof backup arrangement, including the technical mechanisms used to enforce immutability and protect backups from modification or deletion by ransomware or insider threat.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates immutable or write-once storage (WORM tapes, object-lock-enabled S3-compatible storage, or equivalent) with versioning enabled for all critical system backups
Architecture diagram shows logical and physical separation between production systems and backup repositories, including network segmentation controls preventing lateral movement to backup targets
Document specifies cryptographic integrity verification (hash chaining or digital signatures) applied to each backup job, with procedures for detecting and alerting on hash mismatches
Policy scope explicitly covers all critical banking and payment systems as classified in the institution's IT asset register, with no unexplained exclusions
Document carries an approval date, named senior owner (CISO or CTO level), and a review cycle of no more than 12 months
commonGaps: Policy describes backup schedules but is silent on immutability mechanisms, leaving examiners unable to determine how tamper-proofing is technically enforced
Architecture documentation is outdated (more than 18 months old) and does not reflect current cloud or hybrid infrastructure changes
Scope carve-outs exclude third-party-hosted payment systems or outsourced core banking components without documented compensating controls
Integrity verification is described as a manual step performed ad hoc rather than an automated, logged process integrated into the backup job pipeline
Document exists only as a draft or has never received formal sign-off by senior management, undermining its status as an authorised control
suggestedSources: IT Security or Infrastructure team's policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as ServiceNow GRC or Archer)
Configuration management database (CMDB) for backup infrastructure asset records
Cloud provider console exports (e.g., AWS S3 Object Lock configuration, Azure Immutable Blob Storage policy screenshots)
Enterprise Architecture team's solution design documents and network topology diagrams
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded architecture diagrams; version-controlled in GRC or document management system

evidenceItems.name: Backup Immutability Technical Evidence and Audit Logs
evidenceItems.description: System-generated records and configuration exports that provide objective, technical proof that tamper-proof controls are active and functioning, including storage configuration screenshots, access control logs, and backup job integrity reports.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exported storage configuration showing Object Lock or WORM retention locks are enabled in Compliance mode (not Governance mode) for the defined retention period, making deletion impossible even by privileged users
Access control audit logs showing that backup repository administrative accounts are segregated from production system administrators, with no shared credentials or cross-access
Automated backup integrity reports (hash verification results) for at least the preceding 12 months, showing consistent pass results with any failures logged and remediated
Out-of-band alerting configuration evidence showing that any attempted modification or premature deletion of backup objects triggers an immediate alert to the SOC independent of production channels
Air-gap or network isolation evidence (firewall rules, VLAN configuration exports) confirming backup storage is not reachable from production workloads via standard network paths
commonGaps: Storage is described as 'immutable' in documentation but configuration exports reveal Object Lock is set to Governance mode, which privileged accounts can still override
Backup administrator accounts share the same Active Directory domain as production system accounts, creating a single point of compromise exploitable by ransomware
Integrity verification logs exist only for the most recent backup cycle; historical logs have been overwritten or are unavailable, preventing trend analysis
No alerting is configured on the backup storage layer itself; the institution relies solely on production-side backup agent logs that a ransomware operator could disable
Network segmentation is applied at the firewall level but not enforced at the storage system level, leaving a residual path via compromised firewall rules
suggestedSources: Backup software management console (Veeam, Commvault, Veritas NetBackup, or equivalent) — export job reports and configuration
Cloud provider IAM and storage configuration exports (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs)
Network operations team — firewall rule exports, VLAN configuration, and network topology documentation
SIEM platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, IBM QRadar) — backup-related alert rule configurations and triggered alert history
Identity and Access Management (IAM) system — privileged access reports for backup administrator accounts
evidenceItems.format: System-generated PDF/CSV exports, configuration screenshots (timestamped), SIEM alert rule exports

evidenceItems.name: Isolated Recovery Environment Design and Configuration Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and technical evidence demonstrating the existence and design of a dedicated, isolated recovery environment that is operationally separate from production and protected against contamination by a ransomware event affecting the primary environment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Dedicated recovery environment architecture document shows physical or logical isolation from production, including separate compute, storage, and network resources with independent management credentials
Recovery environment is maintained in a clean, known-good state with documented baseline configuration, ensuring it cannot be pre-infected before activation

Network architecture confirms the recovery environment has no persistent connectivity to production infrastructure; connection is established only on-demand through an authenticated, audited jump process
Recovery environment capacity has been formally assessed and documented as sufficient to host the minimum viable set of critical banking and payment services within the institution's declared RTO
Change management records show that updates to the recovery environment baseline are controlled, tested, and approved through a separate change process from production changes
commonGaps: The 'isolated recovery environment' is a VLAN within the same physical data centre as production with no additional access controls, providing minimal isolation against a threat actor with existing network access
Recovery environment uses the same hypervisor management plane and vCenter credentials as production, meaning a compromised production admin account can reach the recovery environment
Capacity planning documentation is absent or dated; the recovery environment was sized years ago and has not been re-assessed against current critical system footprints
The recovery environment doubles as a development or UAT environment during non-incident periods, introducing configuration drift and unknown software that could undermine clean recovery
There is no documented process for transitioning the recovery environment back to isolation after a test or actual activation, leaving persistent connectivity that degrades its isolation posture
suggestedSources: Infrastructure or cloud engineering team — recovery environment architecture design documents, capacity reports, and configuration baselines
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records — change requests specific to the recovery environment
Virtualisation or cloud management console exports (VMware vCenter, AWS VPC configuration, Azure Virtual Network exports)
Network operations team — inter-environment connectivity diagrams and firewall rule sets
GRC platform — linked risk and control records for the recovery environment asset
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX architecture documents, Visio/draw.io network diagrams, configuration management system exports

evidenceItems.name: Ransomware Recovery Testing Records and Results
evidenceItems.description: Documented records of exercises that specifically test the institution's ability to recover critical banking and payment services from tamper-proof backups within the isolated recovery environment under simulated ransomware or destructive cyber-attack conditions, validating that tolerable downtime thresholds are achievable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test scenario is explicitly framed as a ransomware or destructive cyber-attack exercise (not a generic DR test), covering encryption of production data, unavailability of production infrastructure, and potential compromise of standard recovery tooling

Test records capture actual measured recovery times for each critical system and payment service, compared directly against the RTO/RPO declared in the Business Impact Analysis or Recovery Time Objective register
Test scope includes restoration of data integrity verification — confirming restored data matches the cryptographic hashes stored at backup time — not merely that services start up
Test results are reviewed and formally accepted by senior management or the Board-level IT risk committee, with a signed attestation confirming the institution met its tolerable recovery thresholds
Gaps or failures identified during the test are captured in a formal remediation register with assigned owners, target dates, and evidence of closure prior to the next scheduled test
commonGaps: Tests are labelled as 'DR tests' but the scenario does not simulate ransomware-specific conditions (e.g., production encryption, backup accessibility challenges), meaning the test does not validate Clause 10.45 compliance
Recovery time measurements are recorded as estimated or approximate rather than precisely timestamped, making it impossible to confirm compliance with declared RTO thresholds

Tests are conducted on a subset of non-critical systems and extrapolated to cover critical systems, without actual validation that payment and banking services can be recovered within tolerable limits
Test reports are not presented to or approved by senior management; they remain at operational team level and do not form part of Board or ERMC reporting
Remediation actions from previous test gaps remain open at the time of the next test, with no evidence of re-testing to confirm the gaps were resolved
suggestedSources: IT Disaster Recovery or Business Continuity team — test plans, test scripts, and after-action reports
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — incident and problem records created during recovery testing
Board or senior management committee minutes — references to DR/cyber resilience test results
GRC platform — linked control test records and remediation tracking against this clause
Backup software console — job execution logs timestamped during the test window
evidenceItems.format: PDF test plan and results report, spreadsheet with timestamped recovery metrics, signed management attestation

evidenceItems.name: Tolerable Downtime and Recovery Objective Alignment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has formally defined and Board-approved tolerable downtime thresholds for critical banking and payment services, and that these thresholds are directly reflected in the design and testing of its tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery arrangements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Business Impact Analysis (BIA) or equivalent document formally defines Maximum Tolerable Period of Disruption (MTPD), RTO, and RPO for each critical banking and payment service, with explicit approval by senior management or Board

Backup frequency and retention settings documented in technical configuration are demonstrably aligned with the RPO for each critical system (e.g., a 4-hour RPO is supported by backup jobs running at minimum every 4 hours)
Recovery environment capacity and runbook steps have been formally validated to achieve the declared RTO, with evidence from the most recent ransomware recovery test confirming the target was met

A traceability matrix or control mapping document explicitly links each critical service's RTO/RPO to the specific backup and recovery controls in place, enabling an examiner to follow the chain from BIA to technical implementation

Tolerable thresholds have been reviewed and reaffirmed following any significant change to critical systems, infrastructure, or the threat landscape, with evidence of the review in meeting minutes or GRC platform records
commonGaps: BIA documents define RTO/RPO at a business process level but these have never been translated into technical backup and recovery configuration parameters, leaving a gap between business expectations and IT implementation
Tolerable thresholds were set several years ago and have not been updated to reflect new digital banking channels, real-time payment services, or revised regulatory expectations
No traceability exists between the BIA-stated recovery objectives and the actual backup job schedules or recovery environment specifications; compliance relies entirely on verbal assurance
The BIA is owned by the Business Continuity team but has not been shared with or accepted by the IT Infrastructure or Cyber Security teams responsible for implementing the backup controls

Tolerable downtime figures are defined only at aggregate institution level and do not differentiate between individual critical services, making it impossible to prioritise recovery sequencing or validate service-level compliance
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management team — BIA reports, MTPD/RTO/RPO registers
GRC platform — control records linking BIA outputs to technical controls
Board or ERMC minutes — approval records for tolerable downtime thresholds
IT Infrastructure team — backup configuration documentation showing job frequency aligned to RPO
Enterprise Risk Management team — cyber resilience risk appetite statements referencing tolerable disruption levels
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX BIA report, spreadsheet traceability matrix, Board/committee minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Governance and Oversight Records for Backup and Recovery Arrangements
evidenceItems.description: Board and senior management governance records confirming that the tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery arrangements are subject to periodic oversight, with findings, test results, and material risks reported at the appropriate level and tracked through to resolution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or IT/Cyber Risk Committee terms of reference explicitly assign oversight responsibility for cyber resilience controls, including backup and recovery, to a named committee with documented reporting lines

Committee meeting minutes from at least the preceding 12 months contain standing agenda items or periodic reports on backup arrangement health, recovery test outcomes, and any material gaps identified
Annual attestation or sign-off by the CTO, CISO, or equivalent confirms that tamper-proof backup and isolated recovery controls remain fit for purpose and compliant with Clause 10.45 obligations

Internal Audit or an independent third party has assessed the backup and recovery arrangements within the past two years, with a report addressing tamper-proof and isolation controls specifically, and management responses to findings are documented
Material changes to backup architecture or recovery environment are escalated through the governance structure before implementation, evidenced by relevant committee approval records
commonGaps: Backup and recovery is reported to management only when a failure occurs; there is no routine positive assurance reporting to demonstrate ongoing compliance
Internal Audit reviews cover DR processes generically but have never specifically tested the tamper-proof and isolation controls required by Clause 10.45, leaving a gap in independent assurance
Senior management sign-off exists for the backup policy document but there is no periodic re-attestation confirming that the controls described in the policy remain operationally effective

Committee minutes reference 'DR readiness' without recording specific recovery test results, RTO achievement data, or outstanding remediation items, making the minutes insufficient as governance evidence

Ownership of the isolated recovery environment is ambiguous between IT Infrastructure and Business Continuity teams, resulting in neither team taking full accountability for governance reporting on this control
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — Board and committee minutes, terms of reference
Internal Audit department — audit reports and management letter responses
GRC platform — risk and control owner attestation records, issue tracking
CISO or CTO office — annual cyber resilience attestation sign-off documents
Business Continuity Management team — BCP/DR governance reporting packs submitted to management
evidenceItems.format: PDF Board/committee minutes, Internal Audit reports (PDF), signed attestation letters (PDF)

auditTips: Before the examination, request a live demonstration of the tamper-proof storage configuration — not a screenshot — so examiners can directly observe that Object Lock or equivalent is set to Compliance mode and that even a privileged administrator account cannot delete or modify a backup object within the retention window. Self-attested documentation alone is insufficient for this control.

Map every critical banking and payment service identified in the BIA to a specific backup job, storage target, and recovery runbook entry, then produce a one-page traceability matrix. Examiners will look for gaps between what the BIA declares as critical and what the backup architecture actually covers; an unexplained exclusion is an immediate finding.

Ensure the most recent recovery test was explicitly scoped as a ransomware scenario, not a generic failover. If the last test predates the institution's adoption of real-time payment services or new digital channels, schedule a supplementary test before the examination and document the results, as examiners will assess whether test scope reflects the current threat and business environment.

Confirm that the isolated recovery environment has not been used for any non-recovery purpose (development, UAT, capacity overflow) in the period under review. If it has, document how configuration baseline integrity was verified and restored, as dual-use environments raise immediate questions about the reliability of the isolation control.

Cross-reference the access control lists for backup storage systems against the production Active Directory and privileged access management (PAM) reports. If any production system administrator account has write or delete access to the backup repository, remediate before the examination. Examiners routinely pull IAM exports and compare them against the production account register.

Prepare a gap remediation register that shows all findings from the most recent internal audit and DR test, with closure evidence attached. Examiners view an institution more favourably when it can demonstrate a disciplined remediation cycle than when no findings have ever been recorded, as a clean history with no prior gaps suggests the assurance process itself is superficial.

Validate that out-of-band alerting for backup integrity failures routes to a channel that ransomware cannot disable — for example, an SMS gateway, a separate email domain, or a SIEM with an independent network path. Document this channel explicitly in the backup monitoring procedure and be prepared to demonstrate it during the examination, as examiners will probe whether the institution would detect a compromised backup before relying on it during a ransomware incident.

10.46.title: Third Party Service Provider Oversight
10.46.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the board and senior management have institutionalised—not merely delegated—oversight of third party service providers (TPSPs) engaged for critical technology functions. Examiners look for evidence that the board receives substantive risk reporting on TPSP performance and incidents, not just routine dashboards, and that senior management has clear accountability structures documented in charters or ToRs. A primary red flag is where operational staff manage TPSP relationships in isolation without board-level visibility, or where the institution treats accountability as transferable to the vendor. Examiners also scrutinise whether the institution's TPSP risk appetite is formally approved by the board, and whether residual risks from critical TPSP arrangements are tracked, escalated, and actioned. Cross-referencing TPSP registers against board papers and risk committee minutes is a standard examination technique to test whether oversight is substantive rather than procedural.

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management TPSP Oversight Framework
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved governance framework or policy that establishes the board's and senior management's explicit roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and accountability structures for overseeing critical TPSP arrangements. This demonstrates that oversight is institutionalised at the highest level and not confined to operational teams.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board-approved TPSP oversight policy with clearly defined roles for the board, risk committee, CIO, CRO, and operational owners, including review frequency
Terms of Reference for a TPSP oversight committee or equivalent body chaired at senior management level, with documented quorum and escalation triggers
Board risk appetite statement that explicitly addresses tolerable exposure from critical TPSP dependencies, approved within the last 12 months
Documented escalation matrix specifying thresholds (e.g., SLA breach severity, incident classification) that trigger board or risk committee notification
Annual board attestation confirming the adequacy of the TPSP oversight framework, signed by the Chairman or relevant board committee
commonGaps: TPSP governance policy exists but has not been reviewed or re-approved by the board in over two years, making it stale relative to current vendor landscape
Responsibilities are assigned only to IT or procurement teams with no documented board-level ownership, implying accountability has been effectively delegated downward
Risk appetite for TPSP exposure is not articulated separately and is subsumed in a generic operational risk statement that examiners cannot map to specific TPSP arrangements
Escalation matrix references generic severity tiers but does not distinguish between critical and non-critical TPSP incidents, resulting in under-escalation in practice
Board committee ToRs do not mention TPSP oversight as an explicit standing agenda item, so coverage is ad hoc and unprovable
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board and risk committee minutes, approved policies, and ToRs
GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — policy register with version history and approval records
SharePoint or internal intranet policy library — TPSP governance policy with sign-off metadata
CRO or Risk Management office — risk appetite statements and annual review sign-offs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (board-approved policy), supplemented by meeting minutes in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Critical TPSP Register and Classification Evidence
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive, current register of all third party service providers engaged for critical technology functions and systems, with documented classification criteria that identify which providers are deemed 'critical'. This underpins all downstream oversight activities and demonstrates the institution can identify the full scope of its TPSP exposure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register contains all active critical TPSPs with fields capturing: provider name, service description, technology function supported, business criticality rating, contract expiry, and assigned relationship owner

Documented classification methodology that defines 'critical technology function' consistently with RMiT definitions (e.g., functions that would materially disrupt operations if unavailable for more than 4 hours)
Register is reviewed and certified by senior management (e.g., CIO sign-off) at least semi-annually with version-controlled change history
Register cross-references each TPSP to the systems or processes it supports, enabling impact assessment and dependency mapping
Register distinguishes between cloud service providers, managed service providers, and software vendors, with sub-classification consistent with the institution's TPSP policy
commonGaps: Register is incomplete — shadow IT or departmentally procured SaaS tools supporting technology functions are not captured, creating blind spots in the oversight perimeter
Criticality classification is not based on documented criteria; entries are classified by individual judgment, producing inconsistent ratings across the register
Register has not been updated following a significant vendor change (e.g., contract renewal, service scope change, or vendor merger), rendering it unreliable for examination
No linkage between the TPSP register and the institution's application or asset inventory, making it impossible to demonstrate which systems depend on which vendors
Register is maintained in an uncontrolled spreadsheet with no audit trail of changes, approvals, or periodic review sign-offs
suggestedSources: Procurement or Vendor Management system (e.g., Coupa, SAP Ariba) — vendor master data and contract repository
GRC platform — vendor risk register with classification metadata
IT Asset Management (ITAM) or CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) — system-to-vendor dependency mapping
IT Governance or Technology Risk team — TPSP inventory maintained for BNM reporting purposes
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC platform export in PDF/CSV, with sign-off evidence

evidenceItems.name: TPSP Risk Assessments for Critical Technology Functions
evidenceItems.description: Documented risk assessments conducted on each critical TPSP prior to engagement and refreshed periodically thereafter, demonstrating that the institution proactively identifies, evaluates, and addresses technology, security, and operational risks before and during the engagement lifecycle.
whatGoodLooksLike: Pre-engagement risk assessment reports covering cybersecurity posture, financial viability, operational resilience, regulatory compliance, and data protection for each critical TPSP
Risk assessment methodology is standardised, board-approved, and consistently applied across all critical TPSP assessments, with risk scoring rationale documented
Periodic reassessment (at minimum annual) is evidenced for all critical TPSPs, with date-stamped reports and sign-off by the risk owner
Risk assessments explicitly identify residual risks and link to documented risk acceptance, mitigation, or escalation decisions approved at the appropriate seniority level
Assessment outputs are fed into the institution-wide risk register and reported to the risk committee, evidencing integration with enterprise risk management
commonGaps: Risk assessments were conducted at onboarding but not refreshed annually, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate continuous oversight as required

Assessments rely solely on self-reported vendor questionnaires without independent validation (e.g., third-party audit reports, ISO 27001 certificates, SOC 2 reports), reducing their evidential weight
Residual risks identified in assessments are not formally accepted or escalated; they remain open without documented owner accountability or target resolution dates
Risk assessment scope does not cover the supply chain of the critical TPSP (i.e., fourth-party risks), which examiners increasingly scrutinise
Assessment findings and risk ratings are not consistently linked to contractual obligations or monitoring activities, creating a disconnect between the assessment and ongoing oversight
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., Archer, OneTrust, ServiceNow VRM) — vendor risk assessment workflows and completed assessment records
Technology Risk or Information Security team — risk assessment reports and supporting evidence packages
Third-party assurance reports obtained from vendors: SOC 2 Type II, ISO/IEC 27001 certificates, penetration test summaries
Procurement or Legal team — due diligence files assembled at vendor onboarding
evidenceItems.format: PDF (risk assessment report with supporting annexes), supplemented by GRC system screenshots or exports

evidenceItems.name: Critical TPSP Contractual Agreements with Regulatory Compliance Obligations
evidenceItems.description: Executed contracts, service level agreements, and addenda with critical TPSPs that embed the institution's regulatory obligations under RMiT, ensuring the provider is contractually bound to support the institution's compliance posture, security requirements, and audit rights.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contracts contain explicit clauses requiring TPSPs to comply with BNM RMiT requirements applicable to the services provided, and to notify the institution promptly of any non-compliance
SLAs define measurable service performance metrics (availability, RTO/RPO for critical systems, incident response times) with financial or remedial consequences for breach
Contracts include unambiguous right-to-audit clauses permitting the institution and BNM to inspect the TPSP's systems, controls, and records without restriction
Data security and confidentiality obligations are explicitly stated, including requirements for encryption standards, access controls, and breach notification timelines consistent with RMiT
Exit and transition clauses specify data return, service continuity obligations, and minimum notice periods to protect the institution from concentration risk upon termination
commonGaps: Standard vendor contracts are used without RMiT-specific addenda, leaving regulatory compliance obligations entirely to the institution without TPSP commitment
SLAs reference availability metrics but do not specify RTO/RPO targets aligned to the institution's business continuity requirements for critical functions
Right-to-audit clauses are present but include carve-outs or require advance notice periods so lengthy that they effectively nullify the right in practice
Contracts for legacy critical vendors predate RMiT and have not been renegotiated to include updated regulatory obligations, creating a compliance gap
Subcontracting provisions are absent or permissive, allowing the TPSP to engage fourth parties for critical functions without the institution's prior approval or knowledge
suggestedSources: Legal or Contract Management team — executed contract repository (physical or electronic)
Procurement system (e.g., SAP Ariba, Coupa) — contract lifecycle management module with executed documents
SharePoint or document management system — contract register with version history
GRC platform — TPSP profile linked to contract metadata and expiry alerts
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed contracts and addenda), with contract register in spreadsheet or GRC export

evidenceItems.name: Ongoing TPSP Performance Monitoring and Board Reporting Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of continuous monitoring of critical TPSP performance against agreed SLAs and risk indicators, together with escalation records and board or senior management reporting, demonstrating that oversight is exercised on an ongoing basis and not confined to onboarding.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly or quarterly SLA performance reports for each critical TPSP, showing actual metrics against targets, trend analysis, and documented responses to breaches
Board or risk committee papers that include a dedicated TPSP oversight section, tabled at a minimum quarterly, with named senior management ownership of each critical provider
Key Risk Indicator (KRI) dashboard or equivalent that tracks TPSP-specific indicators (e.g., incident frequency, SLA breach rate, audit finding closure rate) reported to senior management
Evidence of formal TPSP review meetings (e.g., quarterly business reviews) with minutes, action logs, and escalation items documented and tracked to closure
Records of corrective action plans issued to underperforming critical TPSPs, with target dates, follow-up evidence, and sign-off by the responsible senior manager
commonGaps: SLA reports are produced by the vendor and accepted without independent validation by the institution, undermining the credibility of oversight
Board papers mention TPSPs only when a significant incident occurs; routine performance reporting is absent from board-level governance, evidencing reactive rather than proactive oversight
KRI thresholds for TPSP performance are not defined, so there is no systematic trigger for escalation when service quality deteriorates
Formal TPSP review meetings are held irregularly or informally, with no minutes or action log retained, making it impossible to evidence the regularity or substance of oversight
Corrective action plans are issued but not tracked to resolution; open actions accumulate without senior management attention or formal closure sign-off
suggestedSources: GRC or vendor management platform — SLA tracking module and KRI dashboards
Board Secretariat — board and risk committee board papers and minutes
IT Operations or Vendor Management team — quarterly business review records and correspondence with critical TPSPs
Service Management tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — TPSP-related incident and problem records linked to SLA tracking
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers, QBR minutes, corrective action plans), Spreadsheet or GRC export (SLA/KRI dashboards)

evidenceItems.name: TPSP Incident and Accountability Tracking Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that when technology incidents arise from or involve critical TPSPs, the institution maintains accountability for risk management outcomes, conducts root cause analysis, drives remediation, and reports appropriately — evidencing that accountability is not transferred to the vendor.
whatGoodLooksLike: Incident records for TPSP-related events classify the TPSP's role explicitly, with the institution's internal incident owner identified separately to confirm retained accountability
Root cause analysis (RCA) reports for material TPSP incidents are completed by or validated by the institution's own teams, not accepted uncritically from the vendor
Post-incident review documents show that the institution assessed whether TPSP engagement terms (SLA, contract obligations) were triggered and enforced remedial provisions where applicable
TPSP-related incidents are reported to the board or risk committee where they meet materiality thresholds, with evidence that the institution assessed regulatory notification obligations to BNM
Remediation action plans arising from TPSP incidents are tracked by the institution to closure, with documented confirmation that recurrence controls are implemented and tested
commonGaps: Incident records do not distinguish TPSP-caused incidents from internally caused ones, making it impossible to assess the frequency or severity of TPSP-driven risk materialisation
RCA reports for TPSP incidents are prepared solely by the vendor and accepted without independent challenge, evidencing that the institution has ceded accountability in practice

Contractual remedies (e.g., SLA credits, step-in rights, termination triggers) are not invoked after material incidents, suggesting the institution lacks the operational discipline to enforce its contracts
Incidents involving TPSPs are not escalated to the board even when they affect critical systems, indicating a breakdown in the oversight chain required by this clause
Remediation action closure is self-certified by the TPSP without the institution verifying effectiveness through independent testing or review
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow Incident Management, Jira) — incident tickets tagged to TPSP, with linked RCA documents
Technology Risk or Information Security team — major incident review reports and post-incident action logs
Board Secretariat — risk committee minutes referencing TPSP incidents and management responses
Legal or Contract Management team — records of SLA credit claims, notices issued to vendors, and correspondence relating to contract enforcement
evidenceItems.format: PDF (RCA reports, post-incident reviews, board papers), ITSM system export (incident log with TPSP filter)

auditTips: Map every evidence item back to the board approval chain: examiners will specifically test whether the board has formally approved the TPSP oversight framework and risk appetite, so ensure board minutes explicitly reference approval of these documents by agenda item and resolution number — verbal approvals or implied endorsements will not satisfy scrutiny.

Compile a 'critical TPSP dossier' for each provider on the critical register before examination: a single package containing the risk assessment, executed contract with RMiT-specific addenda, most recent SLA performance report, and latest QBR minutes. This enables examiners to assess the completeness of the oversight lifecycle for each vendor in one review.

Cross-reference the TPSP register against the institution's business continuity plan and disaster recovery documentation: examiners will check whether all critical TPSPs are reflected in BCP/DR scenarios and whether recovery dependencies are explicitly acknowledged, since gaps here indicate the institution has not fully mapped its third-party risk exposure.

Validate that right-to-audit clauses have actually been exercised at least once for each critical TPSP: produce audit engagement letters, fieldwork records, or TPSP-provided assurance reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 surveillance audit) as evidence that the right is operational and not merely contractual. An unexercised audit right raises examiner doubts about effective oversight.

Prepare a clear accountability matrix showing which member of senior management is personally responsible for each critical TPSP relationship, with documented evidence (e.g., KPI in performance review, board paper authorship) that this responsibility is actively discharged. This directly addresses the 'senior management must exercise effective oversight' obligation in the verbatim text.

Review board and risk committee minutes from the past 24 months and tag all TPSP-related agenda items: if coverage is sparse or reactive (appearing only after incidents), prepare a remediation narrative and evidence of enhanced reporting frequency going forward. Examiners trace the history of board engagement with TPSP risk to assess whether oversight is embedded or episodic.

Ensure that contracts for legacy critical TPSPs have been reviewed and updated within the last three years to include current RMiT obligations: examiners are alert to outdated contractual frameworks that predate the 2019 RMiT policy, and the institution should be able to demonstrate a systematic contract refresh programme with documented completion status for all critical providers.

10.47.title: Third Party Due Diligence
10.47.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised a structured, risk-based due diligence process that spans both pre-engagement screening and continuous monitoring throughout the service lifecycle. Examiners specifically verify that due diligence outputs are documented and traceable — not merely policy commitments — and that the scope of assessment explicitly covers the risk categories enumerated in Appendix 8 of RMiT, including financial, operational, legal, reputational, concentration, and country risks. A key red flag is a 'point-in-time' approach where due diligence is only conducted at onboarding but lapses into inactivity thereafter; examiners will probe for evidence of periodic reviews triggered by both calendar schedules and material events such as ownership changes, incidents at the provider, or shifts in the macroeconomic or geopolitical risk environment. Examiners also scrutinise whether due diligence findings are escalated to the appropriate governance body and whether identified risks are tracked to remediation, or explicitly accepted with Board or senior management sign-off.

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Due Diligence Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: The institution's governing policy document that establishes the mandatory requirements, scope, methodology, risk criteria, and accountability for conducting due diligence on third party service providers — both pre-engagement and on a continuing basis. This document demonstrates that the institution has formally institutionalised the obligations under Clause 10.47 and mapped them to the risk categories in Appendix 8.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references RMiT Clause 10.47 and Appendix 8, listing all required risk dimensions (financial, operational, legal, reputational, concentration, country/cross-border) as mandatory assessment areas

Distinct procedural requirements are articulated for pre-engagement due diligence (including minimum information to be obtained) and for periodic ongoing reviews, with defined review frequencies (e.g., annually for critical providers, bi-annually for non-critical)
Policy defines tiering criteria (e.g., critical, significant, non-critical) that calibrate the depth of due diligence proportionate to the risk exposure of each provider
Roles and responsibilities are clearly assigned — including the business owner, risk function, legal, and a designated approval authority — with accountability for sign-off documented

Policy includes an event-trigger mechanism requiring an out-of-cycle review upon material changes such as provider financial distress, significant incidents, regulatory sanctions, or geopolitical events affecting the provider's jurisdiction
commonGaps: Policy covers only pre-engagement screening and contains no provisions for ongoing or periodic due diligence, creating a direct non-compliance with the 'throughout the service engagement' obligation
Appendix 8 risk categories are not explicitly enumerated in the policy, leaving the scope of due diligence undefined and inconsistently applied across business units
No tiering or risk-based calibration exists — all providers are subjected to the same template regardless of criticality, indicating a checkbox rather than risk-driven approach

Policy has not been reviewed or updated following changes to the RMiT policy document or shifts in the risk environment (e.g., post-pandemic, geopolitical disruptions), evidenced by stale version dates
Approval authority for due diligence sign-off is ambiguous or vested too low in the organisation (e.g., operational team level) rather than at a risk committee or senior management tier
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) — policy register module
SharePoint/Confluence policy repository maintained by the Risk or Compliance function
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes evidencing policy approval
Internal Audit's policy universe register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history, approval signatures, and effective date

evidenceItems.name: Pre-Engagement Due Diligence Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Completed due diligence questionnaires, assessment reports, and supporting documentation gathered from or about prospective third party service providers prior to contract execution or formal onboarding. These records demonstrate that the institution evaluated the provider against Appendix 8 risk dimensions before committing to the engagement and that identified risks were surfaced and managed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each assessment record is linked to a specific provider and engagement, timestamped prior to contract signing, and contains responses to all Appendix 8 risk categories with supporting evidence (e.g., audited financials, ISO/SOC certifications, BCP documentation, legal entity verification)
A consolidated risk rating or risk summary is produced at the conclusion of the assessment, clearly articulating residual risks and any conditions or mitigants required before onboarding proceeds
Financial viability assessment is substantiated by at least two years of audited financial statements or equivalent solvency indicators, not solely self-declaration by the provider

Where the provider operates in or routes data through a foreign jurisdiction, a country risk assessment is documented covering regulatory, geopolitical, and data sovereignty considerations relevant to that jurisdiction
The completed assessment is formally signed off by the designated approval authority prior to contract execution, and any risk acceptance decisions are documented with rationale
commonGaps: Due diligence records are incomplete — certain Appendix 8 risk dimensions (commonly country risk or concentration risk) are absent from the assessment template or left blank without documented justification

Assessment records postdate the contract execution date, indicating due diligence was conducted retrospectively rather than prior to onboarding, a direct breach of the clause's pre-engagement requirement

Provider-supplied self-assessment responses are accepted at face value without independent verification or corroboration from third-party sources (credit bureaus, regulatory sanction lists, published incident databases)
No formal risk rating or conclusion is recorded — the file contains raw questionnaire responses but no synthesised risk opinion, making it impossible to determine what risk decision was made
Sign-off by an authorised approver is absent or delegated to a level below that required by policy, indicating governance controls are not functioning as designed
suggestedSources: Vendor management module within GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow Vendor Risk Management, Coupa Risk Assess)
Procurement or vendor onboarding workflow system with audit trail
Shared drive or document management system (SharePoint) maintained by Procurement or Vendor Risk team
Contract management system linking due diligence records to executed agreements
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX assessment report with supporting annexures (financial statements, certificates, questionnaire responses)

evidenceItems.name: Ongoing Due Diligence Review Schedule and Completed Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution maintains a structured programme of periodic due diligence reviews for active third party providers throughout the service engagement. This includes a forward-looking review schedule tied to provider risk tier and completed review records evidencing that assessments were conducted, findings evaluated, and outcomes actioned — confirming that business performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired.
whatGoodLooksLike: A maintained register or calendar documents the scheduled due diligence review dates for all active providers, differentiated by risk tier, with clear ownership assigned for each upcoming review

Completed ongoing review records are structured comparably to pre-engagement assessments, updated for the current risk environment, and include re-evaluation of all Appendix 8 risk dimensions rather than only monitoring service performance metrics

Review records explicitly assess whether the provider's ability to meet business continuity and recovery objectives (RTO/RPO) remains unimpaired, with reference to BCP/DR test results or attestations obtained from the provider during the review period

Material changes at the provider level (e.g., change of ownership, regulatory action, significant incident, financial rating downgrade) are documented as triggering out-of-cycle reviews with evidence that such triggers were identified and actioned promptly
Findings from ongoing reviews are formally reported to the relevant risk committee or governance body, with escalation and remediation actions tracked to closure
commonGaps: No structured review schedule exists — ongoing due diligence is conducted on an ad hoc basis or only when a contract renewal prompts activity, leaving multi-year gaps between formal assessments

Ongoing reviews are limited to SLA/KPI performance monitoring and do not re-assess the broader Appendix 8 risk dimensions, particularly financial health and country risk, which can deteriorate between reviews

There is no mechanism to identify and act on material event triggers; changes such as a provider being placed under regulatory supervision or suffering a significant cyber incident do not automatically prompt a review

Business continuity and recovery capability of the provider is not re-verified during ongoing reviews — institutions rely on contractual commitments made at onboarding without current evidence that the provider can still meet agreed RTO/RPO targets
Review findings are not escalated or reported to governance; they remain at the operational level with no documented risk committee awareness, creating a governance blind spot
suggestedSources: GRC platform vendor risk module — review scheduling and workflow tracking
Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) programme register maintained by Risk or IT Risk function
Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee meeting packs and minutes reflecting periodic vendor risk reporting
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) for tracking review tasks and completion evidence
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (review schedule/register) and PDF/DOCX completed review reports

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 8 Risk Coverage Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured document or artefact that maps all Appendix 8 risk categories to the institution's due diligence assessment methodology, demonstrating that every prescribed risk dimension is explicitly covered in both pre-engagement and ongoing due diligence activities. This evidence item establishes the completeness of the institution's risk coverage and provides a defensible audit trail for BNM examiners reviewing scope of assessment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each Appendix 8 risk category is listed in a cross-reference matrix with corresponding assessment questions, data requirements, and evaluation criteria — demonstrating deliberate design rather than incidental coverage

The matrix distinguishes which risk categories are assessed at pre-engagement versus ongoing review stages, and documents the rationale for any difference in frequency or depth of assessment across stages

For each risk category, the matrix identifies the information sources used (e.g., audited financials for financial risk, regulatory sanction databases for legal/compliance risk, BCP documentation for operational risk) and the minimum acceptable evidence standard
The matrix has been validated by the Risk, Legal, and Compliance functions and reviewed against the current version of the RMiT policy document, with version control demonstrating currency
Completed assessments are traceable back to the matrix — examiners can verify that an individual provider's assessment file addresses each matrix row without significant omissions
commonGaps: No matrix or equivalent cross-referencing artefact exists — the institution asserts Appendix 8 compliance verbally but cannot demonstrate systematic coverage through documentation

The matrix exists but omits one or more Appendix 8 categories, most commonly concentration risk (especially relevant for shared or dominant providers) and cross-border/country risk for providers with offshore operations
The matrix covers pre-engagement assessment only and has not been extended to ongoing review, resulting in a narrowing scope of risk surveillance over the life of the engagement

Assessment questions within the matrix are too generic to be risk-sensitive — they do not escalate in depth for higher-risk providers or higher-risk jurisdictions, undermining the institution's risk-based approach

The matrix has not been updated following revisions to RMiT or the emergence of new risk categories (e.g., AI/algorithmic risk introduced by providers' adoption of AI-driven services), rendering coverage incomplete against the current regulatory landscape
suggestedSources: Risk Management division — TPRM methodology documentation
GRC platform assessment template configuration (demonstrating how questions map to risk categories)
Internal Audit workpapers referencing the framework used to assess Appendix 8 compliance
SharePoint/Confluence — Third Party Risk Management framework documentation library
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF matrix with version control

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Risk Register with Current Risk Environment Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A maintained register of all active third party service providers that documents each provider's risk profile, current due diligence status, risk ratings, identified risk exposures, and risk treatment actions — updated to reflect the latest risk environment. This register demonstrates continuous monitoring of the institution's overall third party risk exposure and supports governance-level oversight of provider-related risks.
whatGoodLooksLike: The register is actively maintained and reflects the current population of active providers, with entries for each provider including risk tier, last review date, next scheduled review date, Appendix 8 risk ratings, and open risk items

Risk ratings within the register are refreshed following each periodic review and upon material event triggers, with timestamps demonstrating currency and a clear audit trail of rating changes over time

The register includes a 'risk environment' column or annotation that captures recent developments relevant to each provider — such as geopolitical events, sector-wide cyber incidents, or macroeconomic deterioration in the provider's jurisdiction — demonstrating that assessments consider the latest risk context as required by Clause 10.47

Concentration risk is explicitly tracked at both individual provider level and aggregate level, enabling the institution to assess its cumulative exposure to single providers, provider categories, or geographic concentrations

The register is reported to the risk committee or equivalent governance body on a defined cadence, and the reporting pack evidences committee review and challenge of the overall third party risk exposure
commonGaps: The register is static or infrequently updated — entries reflect due diligence conducted at onboarding years prior with no subsequent refresh, providing a misleading picture of current risk

The register does not capture the latest risk environment — assessments make no reference to contemporaneous developments such as geopolitical instability, sector incidents, or provider financial distress, directly undermining the clause's 'latest risk environment' obligation

Concentration risk is not tracked — the register records individual provider risk but does not aggregate exposure by provider type, geography, or service criticality, preventing a systemic view of concentration vulnerability

The register is maintained by the business or procurement function and is not visible to or reviewed by the risk function or governance committee, creating a disconnect between operational activity and institutional risk oversight

Terminated or expired provider relationships are removed from the register without retention of historical records, preventing audit trail reconstruction and potentially losing evidence needed for regulatory examination
suggestedSources: GRC platform vendor risk register module
Enterprise risk management system (ERM) — third party risk sub-register
Risk Committee reporting packs (Board or Management level) referencing the third party risk landscape
Procurement or Vendor Management Office — active vendor register cross-referenced with risk ratings
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export with version history

evidenceItems.name: Business Performance and Recovery Objective Verification Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution actively monitors and verifies, on an ongoing basis, whether engaged third party service providers continue to meet the business performance benchmarks and recovery objectives (RTO/RPO) agreed at onboarding. This evidence directly substantiates the clause's specific obligation that due diligence ensures 'achievement of business performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired.'
whatGoodLooksLike: SLA performance reports from providers are received, reviewed, and formally acknowledged on a defined frequency, with a documented process for escalating breaches to the risk or governance function rather than managing them solely at operational level

Business continuity and disaster recovery capability of the provider is re-verified at least annually through receipt and review of the provider's BCP/DR test results, third-party audit reports, or equivalent attestations confirming that agreed RTOs and RPOs remain achievable

Where the provider's performance or recovery capability is found to be impaired or degraded, documented remediation plans are in place and tracked to closure, with escalation to senior management if remediation is not achieved within agreed timelines

The institution has conducted or commissioned independent BCP/DR testing (e.g., joint simulation exercises or tabletop exercises involving the provider) and documented outcomes, rather than relying solely on provider self-attestation

Recovery objectives verified through ongoing monitoring are explicitly linked back to the institution's own BIA (Business Impact Analysis) — demonstrating that provider capability is assessed against the institution's internal criticality benchmarks, not generic standards
commonGaps: The institution monitors SLA/KPI performance metrics but does not separately verify BCP/DR recovery capability — service availability is conflated with recovery readiness, leaving a significant blind spot for resilience risk

Provider BCP/DR attestations are received at onboarding but not refreshed during the service lifecycle — the institution has no current evidence that recovery objectives agreed years earlier remain achievable given changes in the provider's infrastructure, staffing, or operating model

SLA breach management is handled entirely at an operational level with no escalation to risk governance; breaches are resolved bilaterally without formal risk assessment of their implications for business continuity

The link between provider recovery capabilities and the institution's own BIA-derived RTOs/RPOs is not documented — recovery objectives in provider contracts may have been set without reference to the institution's actual criticality requirements, creating a gap between contracted and required performance

Independent verification of provider BCP/DR capability (through joint testing or third-party audits of the provider) is absent — the institution relies entirely on provider-supplied attestations, which may not reflect actual recovery performance under real incident conditions
