suggestedSources: Contract management system — SLA schedules and performance reports
Business Continuity Management (BCM) platform or register — provider BCP/DR attestation records
IT Risk or Technology Risk team — records of BCP/DR exercises involving third party providers
Vendor management review meeting minutes and performance scorecards
Business Impact Analysis (BIA) documentation cross-referenced with provider SLA/RTO-RPO commitments
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX reports, spreadsheet scorecards, and exercise records

auditTips: Map your evidence files directly to the two temporal obligations in Clause 10.47 — pre-engagement and throughout the engagement — and ensure you can produce distinct, timestamped records for each stage. BNM examiners will test both limbs separately; a strong onboarding file with no ongoing review records is a partial failure.

Prepare a populated Appendix 8 risk coverage matrix before examination and be ready to walk the examiner through how every risk category is assessed in your due diligence methodology. For each category, have at least one completed provider assessment on hand that demonstrates the category was genuinely evaluated, not merely acknowledged in the template.

Construct a master third party register that includes 'last due diligence review date' and 'next scheduled review date' for every active provider, sorted by risk tier. Examiners frequently request this register in the opening information request and use it to select samples — gaps or stale dates are immediate red flags.

For sampled providers, ensure the full due diligence file is retrievable within a reasonable timeframe (ideally within one business day) and is logically organised — questionnaire, supporting documents, risk rating, approval sign-off, and any conditions precedent to onboarding. Disorganised or incomplete files signal weak process governance regardless of underlying quality.

Demonstrate that the 'latest risk environment' obligation is operationalised by including a contemporaneous risk environment note in each periodic review record — explicitly referencing relevant developments (geopolitical shifts, sector incidents, provider financial news, regulatory actions) assessed during the review period. This directly addresses the clause's language and distinguishes a compliance-aware institution from one conducting mechanical checkbox reviews.

Cross-reference your due diligence review outcomes against your Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee minutes to demonstrate governance visibility. If risk findings from provider reviews are not surfaced in committee packs, examiners will question whether the governance oversight framework is functioning — prepare to show the escalation pathway from operational findings to board-level awareness.

Identify and pre-empt concentration risk gaps by running an aggregation analysis of your third party register by provider, service category, and geography before examination. If your institution relies heavily on a single cloud provider or a small set of dominant vendors, prepare a documented concentration risk assessment and mitigation rationale — examiners specifically probe this dimension given its systemic implications for financial sector resilience.

10.48.title: Service Level Agreement Requirements
10.48.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will first verify that a written SLA exists for every material third party service provider engagement, then systematically assess whether each of the nine enumerated minimum elements under sub-clauses (a) through (i) is explicitly and operationally captured — not merely referenced by boilerplate. Key red flags include SLAs that are silent on regulator access rights, confidentiality obligations that lapse upon contract termination, exit arrangements that do not address data recoverability timelines, and cyber incident notification clauses that lack defined response windows. Examiners pay particular attention to whether SLOs are measurable and monitored, whether sub-contracting notification thresholds are defined, and whether there is documentary evidence that the institution actively enforces these SLA provisions rather than treating them as paper obligations. Institutions relying on vendor-standard agreements without tailored annexures to address Malaysian regulatory requirements are at heightened examination risk.

evidenceItems.name: Third Party SLA Master Register and Executed Agreements
evidenceItems.description: A centralised inventory of all third party service provider engagements linked to their executed SLA documents, demonstrating that a compliant SLA exists for each engagement and that all nine minimum elements under clause 10.48 are present within the agreement text.
whatGoodLooksLike: A GRC-maintained register cross-referencing each active third party engagement to its executed SLA, with a clause-mapping matrix confirming coverage of sub-clauses (a) through (i) for each agreement
SLA documents bear execution dates, authorised signatories from both parties, and version control histories showing periodic review cycles of no more than three years or upon material change
A structured SLA review checklist completed by Legal/Compliance at onboarding and renewal, explicitly attesting to the presence of all BNM-mandated minimum elements
Critical and high-criticality vendors identified in the TPSP risk register are individually mapped to their SLA, with material gaps escalated to Board Risk Committee or equivalent
Executed SLA annexures or addenda specifically incorporating Malaysian regulatory requirements where the base agreement was a vendor-standard template
commonGaps: Institution relies on vendor-provided master service agreements that omit or restrict regulator access rights, with no locally negotiated addendum to remedy the deficiency
The TPSP register and the SLA document repository are maintained in separate systems with no automated linkage, making it impossible to demonstrate end-to-end coverage during examination
SLAs for legacy or long-standing engagements have never been updated to incorporate RMiT-compliant clauses, creating a population of non-compliant agreements hidden within the broader register

Clause-mapping matrices exist but are completed at a generic level, referencing section headings rather than confirming that specific BNM minimum elements are addressed in substantive operative language
There is no defined review trigger for SLA updates when a third party undergoes material corporate change (e.g., acquisition, change of jurisdiction), leaving agreements structurally outdated
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, RSA Archer) — TPSP module containing vendor register and linked document repository
Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) system (e.g., Ironclad, DocuSign CLM, SAP Ariba) — executed agreement vault with clause-tagging capability
Legal department SharePoint or intranet site — SLA template library and completed review checklists
Procurement / Vendor Management team — onboarding approval records and periodic vendor review minutes
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (executed SLA), Spreadsheet or GRC report (register and clause-mapping matrix)

evidenceItems.name: Regulator and Auditor Access Rights Provisions and Exercise Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that all SLAs explicitly grant BNM and any party appointed by the financial institution unfettered rights to access records, files, data, management information, and decision-making minutes held by the third party, together with records demonstrating that this right has been invoked or tested in practice.
whatGoodLooksLike: SLA operative clauses use unambiguous language granting access rights to 'any regulatory authority having jurisdiction over the financial institution' without requiring further consent from the third party at the time of access

The access rights clause explicitly enumerates scope: records, files, data, management information, and minutes of all consultative and decision-making processes — mirroring the language of sub-clause (a)

Evidence of at least one invocation of the access rights clause per major TPSP — such as an internal audit review, external auditor data request, or BNM inspection — with the third party's cooperation formally documented

For cloud and offshore service providers, the access rights clause is supplemented by a jurisdictional provision ensuring physical or virtual access is not limited by the provider's operating country laws
Annual TPSP compliance attestations obtained from third parties confirming their continued ability and willingness to facilitate regulator access as stipulated in the SLA
commonGaps: Access rights clauses are present but scoped narrowly (e.g., limited to 'financial records' or 'transaction logs'), excluding management information and decision-making minutes as required by sub-clause (a)
The SLA grants access rights subject to the third party's prior written consent or a notice period, effectively allowing the provider to obstruct or delay regulatory inspection

For multi-tenant cloud arrangements, the institution has not confirmed in writing that the provider can segregate and produce the institution's specific data and records in a format usable for examination
No records exist to demonstrate that the access rights have ever been tested; the clause exists on paper but there is no evidence of practical operationalisation
SLAs with offshore providers do not address the risk that the provider's local jurisdiction may impose data sovereignty restrictions that conflict with the access rights obligation
suggestedSources: Legal department CLM system — executed SLA clause library with access rights tagging
Internal Audit SharePoint or audit management platform (e.g., TeamMate, AuditBoard) — records of third party audits conducted under SLA access rights provisions
BNM examination correspondence files — records of regulator access facilitated through third party environments
TPSP compliance attestation repository — annual self-certification letters from service providers
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed SLA extract, attestation letters, audit access records)

evidenceItems.name: Sub-contracting Notification Framework and Confidentiality Undertaking Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that SLAs contain defined thresholds and timelines for prior notice of substantial sub-contracting by the third party, combined with executed written undertakings on compliance with secrecy provisions that expressly survive contract termination — addressing sub-clauses (b) and (c) of clause 10.48.
whatGoodLooksLike: SLA clauses define 'substantial sub-contracting' with objective criteria (e.g., sub-contracting of core service functions, engagement of sub-contractors with access to customer data, or sub-contracts exceeding a defined monetary threshold) to avoid ambiguity at the time of notification

The prior notice requirement specifies a minimum number of calendar days (typically 30–90 days) and a defined notification channel, enabling the institution to assess and if necessary object to or condition the sub-contracting arrangement

The confidentiality and secrecy clause explicitly references the Financial Services Act 2013 (FSA) and Islamic Financial Services Act 2013 (IFSA) secrecy provisions, binding the third party to equivalent obligations

Survival clauses are present and unambiguous, stating that integrity and confidentiality obligations remain binding for a defined post-termination period (typically matching the institution's 7-year retention obligation)
A log of sub-contracting notifications received from each TPSP is maintained, with the institution's formal acknowledgement or conditional approval recorded for each notification received
commonGaps: The SLA requires notification of sub-contracting but does not define what constitutes 'substantial', creating a loophole that allows providers to sub-contract material functions without triggering the notification obligation

Confidentiality clauses do not cite applicable Malaysian legislation by name, relying instead on general data protection language that may not satisfy BNM's requirement for compliance with statutory secrecy provisions
Post-termination survival of confidentiality obligations is either absent or limited to a period shorter than the institution's regulatory data retention requirement, creating a gap window
The institution cannot produce a log or register of sub-contracting notifications received, making it impossible to demonstrate that the notification mechanism is actively monitored and actioned
Written confidentiality undertakings exist within the SLA body but have not been obtained as separate executed documents from key sub-contractors identified by the primary TPSP
suggestedSources: Vendor Management team — sub-contracting notification log and approval correspondence files
Legal department CLM — executed SLA confidentiality and survival clause extracts
Compliance department archive — written undertakings received from TPSPs and their identified sub-contractors
GRC platform — TPSP risk assessments updated upon receipt of sub-contracting notifications
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed SLA extracts, undertaking letters, notification correspondence), Spreadsheet (sub-contracting notification log)

evidenceItems.name: Disaster Recovery, Backup, Exit and Business Continuity Arrangements
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that SLAs address disaster recovery and backup capability (where applicable) and include documented arrangements to secure business continuity upon exit or termination — including defined timelines for the recovery of data held by the third party — corresponding to sub-clauses (d) and (f) of clause 10.48.
whatGoodLooksLike: SLA annexures for applicable engagements specify DR objectives including Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) commitments from the third party that are consistent with the institution's own BCP requirements under RMiT

Exit management provisions include a data return and destruction protocol specifying the format of data repatriation, the maximum timeline for delivery (e.g., within 30 days of termination notice), and cryptographic verification or checksums to confirm data integrity upon receipt
Annual DR test results provided by the third party are on file, with the institution's formal review and sign-off documented; gaps identified during tests are tracked to remediation
Exit plans have been rehearsed or table-top tested at least once for critical TPSPs, with documented outcomes and identified remedial actions
For cloud providers, the SLA specifies data residency and portability standards (e.g., open formats, API-accessible exports) that prevent vendor lock-in from obstructing timely data recovery
commonGaps: DR and backup provisions in the SLA are aspirational rather than contractually binding, using language such as 'best efforts' rather than defined RTO/RPO obligations with associated remedies for breach

Exit provisions address return of data but do not specify timelines, formats, or the institution's right to direct destruction of residual data after repatriation — leaving the institution exposed post-termination
The institution has never requested or received evidence of the third party's DR test results, meaning the SLA commitment to backup capability has never been independently verified

Exit management plans exist at a high level but have not been rehearsed, and the institution cannot demonstrate that data held by the TPSP could be recovered within a timeframe that meets operational needs

For multi-cloud or SaaS arrangements, SLAs do not address data portability or the cost of egress, creating a practical barrier to timely data recovery that is not reflected in the contractual exit provisions
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management team — TPSP DR test reports, exit plan documentation, and rehearsal records
IT Operations / Infrastructure team — backup configuration records and DR test schedules for TPSP-hosted systems
Legal department CLM — exit and termination provisions within executed SLAs
GRC platform — TPSP risk assessments incorporating DR capability ratings and exit readiness scores
Vendor Management team — annual TPSP review minutes addressing DR and exit plan status
evidenceItems.format: PDF (SLA annexures, DR test reports, exit plan documents, rehearsal minutes), Spreadsheet (exit readiness tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Service Level Objectives Monitoring and Performance Reporting Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that SLAs contain measurable service level objectives for uptime or availability and that the institution actively monitors TPSP performance against these objectives, with reporting, breach tracking, and escalation records — addressing sub-clause (e) of clause 10.48.
whatGoodLooksLike: SLAs specify numeric uptime or availability SLOs (e.g., 99.9% monthly availability) with clearly defined measurement methodology, exclusion windows, and the data source used for calculation — leaving no ambiguity in how performance is assessed

Monthly or quarterly SLO performance reports are received from the TPSP and independently validated by the institution against its own monitoring data (e.g., from network monitoring platforms or ITSM tools)

A breach log is maintained tracking all instances where the TPSP failed to meet its SLO, including the nature of the breach, duration, root cause analysis received, and remedies or service credits applied

SLO performance trends are reported to senior management or a relevant governance committee (e.g., Technology Risk Committee) on a periodic basis, with deteriorating trends triggering formal vendor review

SLAs for critical systems incorporate tiered SLO structures that distinguish between different service components (e.g., core processing vs. reporting), reflecting the institution's internal criticality classification
commonGaps: SLAs state uptime targets but rely solely on the TPSP's self-reported availability figures, with no independent monitoring or validation mechanism established by the institution

SLO breach thresholds are defined but the associated remedies (e.g., service credits, escalation rights, termination triggers) are either absent or so nominal as to create no incentive for the provider to maintain performance
Performance reports are received from the TPSP but not formally reviewed or acknowledged by the institution, and there are no records of escalation or follow-up on persistent underperformance
SLOs apply at an aggregate level without distinguishing the availability of individual critical service components, making it difficult to assess impact on specific regulated functions
The institution cannot produce historical SLO performance data for examination, as records have not been systematically retained beyond the current reporting period
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — incident and availability reporting dashboards linked to TPSP services
Network and application monitoring tools (e.g., Dynatrace, Datadog, SolarWinds) — uptime and availability logs for TPSP-hosted systems
Vendor Management team SharePoint — TPSP performance report archive and breach log
Technology Risk Committee / Board Risk Committee minutes — periodic TPSP performance reporting records
Finance / Procurement team — service credit claims and SLA penalty invoices raised against underperforming providers
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC report (SLO performance tracker, breach log), PDF (TPSP performance reports, committee minutes)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Notification Records, Standards Compliance Attestations, and Security Awareness Participation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Composite evidence demonstrating that SLAs require and that TPSPs have operationalised: prompt disclosure of service disruptions and cyber incidents affecting the institution or its customers; compliance with internationally recognised standards with supporting certifications for key staff; and participation in the institution's security awareness and education programme — addressing sub-clauses (g), (h), and (i) of clause 10.48.
whatGoodLooksLike: Incident notification clauses specify a defined maximum response window (e.g., within 4 hours of discovery for cyber incidents affecting customer data) and a prescribed notification channel, with escalation paths named rather than generic; the institution holds a log of all notifications received with timestamps demonstrating TPSP adherence to the obligation

Annual compliance attestations or assurance reports (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001 certificates, SOC 2 Type II reports, PCI-DSS Attestations of Compliance) are on file for each critical TPSP, with the institution's documented assessment of whether the scope and findings are satisfactory for the services rendered

Evidence of key TPSP staff certifications (e.g., CISM, CISSP, CISA, relevant cloud provider certifications) is collected annually through a structured attestation process, with a register maintained by the institution showing certification currency for named individuals

Security awareness programme participation records show that TPSP personnel with access to the institution's systems or data have completed the institution's mandatory security awareness training within prescribed timelines, with completion certificates or system-generated records on file

For cyber incidents notified by TPSPs, the institution's formal incident response records reference the TPSP notification, the institution's own investigation actions, and any customer impact assessment — demonstrating end-to-end incident management integration with the third party
commonGaps: Incident notification clauses exist but do not specify a maximum notification timeline, allowing TPSPs to delay disclosure of cyber incidents affecting customer data for days or weeks without breaching the SLA

The institution collects ISO or SOC certification summaries from TPSPs at onboarding but does not perform annual refresh, meaning certifications on file may be expired or for a scope that no longer covers the services being rendered
Key staff certification requirements are written into the SLA but no mechanism exists for the institution to receive or verify evidence of compliance — the obligation is untested and unenforced

Security awareness programme participation is required for TPSP staff 'where appropriate' but the institution has not defined criteria for when it is appropriate, and no records exist of any TPSP having been enrolled or having completed the programme

Incident notification logs are maintained by the institution's Security Operations Centre but are not linked to the TPSP register, making it impossible to demonstrate during examination that a specific notification was received under a specific SLA provision
suggestedSources: Security Operations Centre (SOC) incident management platform (e.g., Splunk SOAR, IBM QRadar SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR) — TPSP-sourced incident notification log
GRC platform — TPSP compliance attestation repository with certificate expiry tracking
Human Resources or Learning Management System (LMS) (e.g., Workday Learning, SAP SuccessFactors) — security awareness training completion records, filterable by external/TPSP participants
Vendor Management team SharePoint — TPSP key staff certification register and annual attestation correspondence
CISO office — TPSP cyber incident post-mortem reports and formal notification acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (attestation letters, certification copies, training completion certificates, incident reports), Spreadsheet (TPSP notification log, certification register, training completion tracker)

auditTips: Construct a clause 10.48 compliance matrix before examination: list every active TPSP engagement in one axis and sub-clauses (a) through (i) across the other, then mark each cell as Compliant, Partially Compliant, or Gap with a reference to the specific SLA section that addresses it — this enables examiners to verify coverage systematically and demonstrates institutional rigour

Pre-examination, pull a random sample of at least five to ten TPSP SLAs spanning different criticality tiers and service categories, and verify each one against the nine minimum elements — do not rely on the SLA template alone, as executed agreements frequently deviate from the approved template through negotiated carve-outs

For sub-clause (a) regulator access rights, prepare a briefing note that identifies any TPSP where access rights are restricted by the provider's jurisdiction or terms of service, together with the compensating controls or escalation path the institution has in place — examiners will probe this specifically for offshore and cloud arrangements

Produce a TPSP incident notification drill record: simulate a scenario where a critical TPSP experiences a cyber incident and trace the notification pathway through the SLA obligation, the institution's TPSP contact list, the SOC intake process, and the regulatory reporting obligation under BNM's incident reporting requirements — gaps in this chain are a common examination finding

Validate that confidentiality survival clauses in executed SLAs use a post-termination duration that meets or exceeds the institution's seven-year regulatory retention obligation — many vendor standard agreements cap post-termination confidentiality at two to three years, creating a compliance gap that is not apparent without examining the specific clause language

Prepare a consolidated TPSP performance dashboard covering the preceding twelve months of SLO performance data for all critical TPSPs, with breach instances, remedies applied, and trend commentary — examiners assess not only whether SLOs are contractually defined under sub-clause (e) but whether the institution is actively managing performance against them

Cross-reference the TPSP SLA register against the institution's Business Continuity Management records to confirm that every TPSP identified as supporting a critical business service has an SLA with explicit exit and data recovery provisions under sub-clause (f), and that the exit provisions have been exercised or table-top tested — untested exit arrangements are a recurring gap BNM examiners flag during Technology Operations reviews

10.49.title: Continuous Third Party Cybersecurity Monitoring
10.49.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the institution has moved beyond point-in-time vendor assessments toward a demonstrable, operationalised continuous monitoring programme. Examiners specifically test for an approved roadmap with measurable milestones, evidence that IT footprint and customer data exposure are quantified and actively managed, and proof that automated tooling — not manual reviews — drives metric testing. A key red flag is institutions that rely solely on periodic questionnaires or annual audits to satisfy sub-clauses (a) through (g); examiners expect real-time or near-real-time telemetry, defined threshold-breach response workflows, and documented escalation records showing the institution actually acted on failed assertions. Cross-references to clause 10.51 (third party audits) and 10.52 (exit management) will be checked to ensure the continuous monitoring posture is coherent with the broader third-party risk framework.

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Continuous Monitoring Roadmap
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved strategic roadmap document that articulates the institution's phased plan to achieve continuous cybersecurity monitoring of third party service providers, including milestones, target states, owners, and timelines. Demonstrates compliance with the overarching obligation to 'formulate a roadmap' under clause 10.49.
whatGoodLooksLike: Roadmap is Board or Board Risk Committee-approved, with version history and review dates evidencing active governance oversight
Each phase maps explicitly to sub-clauses (a) through (g), with defined acceptance criteria and measurable KPIs per milestone
Current-state gap assessment is embedded in the roadmap, showing baseline maturity scores against which progress is tracked
Named accountable owners (e.g., CISO, Head of Third Party Risk) and budget allocation are documented for each workstream
Progress updates against the roadmap are tabled at a senior governance forum (e.g., Technology Risk Committee) at least quarterly
commonGaps: Roadmap exists only as a slide deck with no formal approval trail or Board-level endorsement, making governance accountability unclear
Milestones are described in vague, non-measurable terms (e.g., 'improve monitoring') without specific completion criteria or dates
Roadmap addresses some sub-clauses but omits automation (10.49(f)) and threshold-breach response (10.49(g)) entirely
No evidence of periodic progress reviews — the roadmap was created once and never updated, suggesting it is not operationally embedded
Roadmap is not linked to the institution's broader technology risk appetite or TPRM framework, leaving it siloed and unsupported
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — roadmap artefact and approval workflows
Board and Board Risk Committee minutes and resolutions
CISO or Technology Risk function SharePoint/Confluence programme management space
Technology Risk Committee papers and meeting packs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal approved document) with supporting PPTX progress decks

evidenceItems.name: Third Party IT Footprint and Customer Data Exposure Register
evidenceItems.description: A living inventory that quantifies the IT infrastructure components, system access entitlements, and categories and volumes of customer information accessible to each material third party. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.49(a) — measuring the IT infrastructure footprint and customer information accessible to third parties and managing external attack surface exposure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register is maintained in a dedicated TPRM or asset management system and updated at least quarterly or upon material change in third party access scope
Each third party entry includes network access paths, privileged account counts, data classification tiers (e.g., PII, financial records), and estimated data volumes
Attack surface reduction actions are tracked against each entry, with risk ratings, remediation owners, and closure dates visible
Integration with the institution's data loss prevention (DLP) or CASB tooling provides automated alerts when third party data access deviates from baseline
Residual exposure scores are reviewed at the Technology Risk Committee and feed directly into the continuous monitoring roadmap progress reports
commonGaps: Register is a static spreadsheet last updated over six months ago, with no process to trigger updates when third party contracts or access scopes change
Customer data exposure columns are incomplete — data classification, volume, and sensitivity are not recorded, only system names
Attack surface metrics exist but are not linked to any remediation workflow; no evidence that exposures identified were actually mitigated
Register covers only Tier 1 vendors; lower-tier providers with access to customer data are absent, creating blind spots
No DLP or CASB integration — footprint measurement is entirely manual and dependent on vendor self-declarations
suggestedSources: Third Party Risk Management platform (e.g., Prevalent, ProcessUnity, OneTrust) — vendor access inventory module
Privileged Access Management system (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust) — third party privileged session reports
Data governance platform or CASB (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps) — data access logs and classification reports
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system — infrastructure components mapped to vendor connections
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF/CSV) or GRC platform report; supplemented by CASB/PAM screenshots

evidenceItems.name: Third Party Security Control Framework and Product-Specific Risk Policy
evidenceItems.description: Policy and control documentation that establishes leading security requirements applied to third party service providers, segmented by service type and product risk profile. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.49(b) — adopting leading security policies and controls to mitigate product-specific third party risks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy references recognised leading frameworks (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST CSF, CIS Controls) and maps BNM RMiT obligations to specific control requirements imposed on vendors
Product-specific annexes exist for high-risk categories (e.g., cloud services, payment processors, software vendors) with tailored control baselines for each
Policy is contractually embedded — evidence of third party acceptance (signed schedules or addenda) is traceable to each vendor agreement
Annual policy review cycle is documented with change log; policy version is consistent with what is reflected in active vendor contracts
Priority control tiers are defined (aligning with sub-clause (d)), distinguishing controls requiring continuous assertion from those subject to annual review
commonGaps: Policy is generic and does not differentiate control requirements by product type, vendor criticality, or data sensitivity — a one-size-fits-all approach that fails sub-clause (b)
Policy references outdated frameworks or earlier BNM circulars without reconciliation to current RMiT obligations
Vendor contracts pre-date the policy; no evidence that controls were flowed down contractually to existing third parties
Priority control sets required by sub-clause (d) are not defined — all controls are treated with equal assurance frequency
Policy has not been reviewed in over 24 months despite significant changes in the vendor landscape or threat environment
suggestedSources: Policy management system (e.g., PolicyTech, SharePoint policy library) — current approved version and prior versions
Legal and Procurement — signed vendor contract schedules and security addenda
GRC platform — control framework mapping and vendor control assignments
CISO function — product risk classification methodology documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy document); supporting contract schedules in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan — Third Party Protocols and Software Update Provisions
evidenceItems.description: The institution's incident response plan (or a dedicated annex) that explicitly incorporates escalation, containment, and communication protocols with third party service providers, including specific provisions for security vulnerabilities arising from software updates. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.49(c) and (e).
whatGoodLooksLike: IRP contains a dedicated third party incident annex with named vendor contact matrices, escalation SLAs, and mutual notification obligations for each material provider

Software update vulnerability scenario is explicitly addressed — the plan defines detection triggers (e.g., vendor security advisories, CSIRT feeds), containment playbooks (e.g., isolation of affected integrations), and rollback procedures
Evidence of joint tabletop exercises with critical third parties testing the protocols within the last 12 months, with after-action reports documenting gaps and remediation

Monitoring frequency obligations (sub-clause (e)) are embedded — the plan specifies channels and cadence for receiving third party incident disclosures (e.g., vendor security portals, ISAC feeds, direct notification SLAs)

IRP version control and approval chain are current; the plan was reviewed following any material third party incident or significant software supply chain event (e.g., analogous to SolarWinds-type scenarios)
commonGaps: IRP mentions third parties generically but lacks specific protocols — no named contacts, no defined notification SLAs, and no vendor-specific containment steps
Software update vulnerability scenario is entirely absent; the plan does not address how the institution would detect and respond to a compromised update from a critical vendor
No evidence of third party participation in incident response exercises — drills are conducted in isolation, leaving coordination gaps untested

Monitoring channels for third party incident disclosures are undefined — the institution cannot demonstrate how it tracks vendor security advisories at 'higher frequency' as required by sub-clause (e)
IRP has not been updated following known industry supply chain events, suggesting it is not dynamically maintained against the evolving threat landscape
suggestedSources: ITSM or incident management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — IRP document repository and incident records
Cyber Threat Intelligence function — vendor advisory tracking logs and ISAC membership records
Business Continuity and Crisis Management team — tabletop exercise records and after-action reports
Vendor management portal — documented third party notification SLAs and contact directories
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (IRP and annexes); exercise reports in PDF; advisory logs as system exports

evidenceItems.name: Continuous Monitoring Technology Platform Evidence and Automated Metric Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution has deployed technology solutions to automate cybersecurity metric testing against third party service providers, including platform configuration records, dashboards, and automated test results. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.49(f) — implementing technology solutions to automate metric testing.
whatGoodLooksLike: A deployed third party risk rating or attack surface monitoring platform (e.g., BitSight, SecurityScorecard, RiskRecon) produces continuous, timestamped security ratings for material vendors with audit trail of score changes

Automated metric tests cover the priority control set defined under sub-clause (d), with test frequency matching the defined assurance schedule (e.g., daily checks for critical controls, weekly for standard controls)
Dashboard evidence shows real-time or near-real-time visibility of vendor security posture across multiple domains (e.g., patching cadence, TLS configuration, open ports, dark web exposure)
Automated alerts are configured with defined thresholds; evidence shows alerts firing and being routed to responsible owners within documented SLAs
Platform outputs are integrated into the GRC system so that automated findings feed directly into vendor risk records and remediation workflows without manual rekeying
commonGaps: No dedicated continuous monitoring technology is deployed; the institution relies on periodic questionnaires and annual audits, which directly contradicts sub-clause (f)'s automation requirement
A platform exists but covers only a subset of critical vendors — coverage mapping cannot demonstrate that material third parties are monitored
Automated alerts are configured but not actioned; logs show numerous threshold breaches with no corresponding investigation or remediation records
Metric testing is automated for external-facing signals (e.g., domain ratings) but does not extend to internal control assertions, leaving a significant monitoring gap
Platform outputs are reviewed manually in spreadsheets rather than integrated into the GRC system, introducing latency and defeating the real-time insight objective
suggestedSources: Continuous monitoring platform (e.g., BitSight, SecurityScorecard, RiskRecon, UpGuard) — vendor dashboards, score histories, and alert logs
GRC platform — integration configuration and automated finding records
SIEM (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) — third party telemetry feeds and correlation rules
SOC/CISO function — platform licensing records and coverage inventories confirming which vendors are enrolled
evidenceItems.format: System-generated reports and dashboard screenshots (PDF/PNG); integration configuration documents (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Threshold Breach Response and Failed Assertion Remediation Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational records demonstrating that the institution has defined threshold breach response processes, investigated failed control assertions from third party providers, and tracked remediation of identified control gaps to closure. Demonstrates compliance with clause 10.49(d) and (g).
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented threshold breach response procedure defines escalation tiers, investigation timelines, and remediation SLAs for each priority control category, with clear ownership at each step

The GRC or ITSM platform contains a log of all failed assertions received from third parties in the review period, with investigation notes, root cause determinations, and remediation actions for each

Remediation tracking records show time-to-close metrics against SLAs, with escalation evidence where breaches exceeded defined thresholds, demonstrating active governance rather than passive recording
Trend analysis reports are produced at least quarterly, identifying repeat offenders among vendors and systemic control weaknesses, and these reports are tabled at the Technology Risk Committee
Closed remediation items include evidence of re-assertion by the third party (e.g., updated attestation, retest results from the monitoring platform) before the finding is marked resolved
commonGaps: Threshold breach response procedure exists on paper but no operational records of its application — no investigation logs, no remediation tickets, and no escalation evidence can be produced
Failed assertions are recorded in the GRC system but many are aged beyond their SLA with no investigation notes or escalation, indicating the process is not enforced
Remediation records do not include re-assertion evidence — findings are closed based on vendor representations alone, with no independent verification
Priority control tiers required by sub-clause (d) are undefined, making it impossible to demonstrate that higher-risk controls receive more frequent or more rigorous assurance
No trend analysis or management reporting on threshold breaches — each failure is treated in isolation, preventing systemic risk identification and senior management visibility
suggestedSources: GRC platform — assertion tracking module, finding registers, and remediation workflow records
ITSM system (e.g., ServiceNow) — vendor risk remediation tickets and SLA compliance reports
Technology Risk Committee and Audit Committee meeting packs — trend analysis reports tabled for governance review
Continuous monitoring platform — historical breach alert logs and re-test results
Third Party Risk team — email and portal correspondence with vendors on remediation commitments
evidenceItems.format: GRC system exports (PDF/CSV); ITSM ticket reports; governance committee papers (PDF)

auditTips: Before the examination, map each of the seven sub-clauses (a) through (g) to a specific artefact in your evidence pack — examiners will test each sub-clause independently, and gaps in any one will be cited as a separate finding. Prepare a clause-to-evidence cross-reference matrix as a cover sheet for your submission.

Demonstrate operationalisation, not just documentation: for sub-clauses (f) and (g), pull two to three real examples of automated alerts that fired, were investigated, and were remediated — including timestamps — to show the process works end-to-end rather than existing only in policy.

Validate that your continuous monitoring platform coverage list matches your critical and material third party register exactly. Examiners will cross-check these two lists; any vendor appearing in the TPRM register but absent from the monitoring platform will be flagged as a coverage gap under sub-clause (f).

For sub-clause (c), ensure your incident response tabletop exercise records explicitly reference a software supply chain compromise scenario (e.g., a compromised vendor update). Generic disaster recovery exercises will not satisfy this sub-clause — the scenario must name the third party coordination protocols being tested.

Prepare a dashboard or summary report from your monitoring platform covering the 12 months prior to examination, showing trend lines for vendor security ratings and the frequency of threshold breaches. This single artefact simultaneously evidences sub-clauses (e), (f), and (g) and demonstrates the 'higher frequency' monitoring posture BNM expects.

Confirm that the roadmap required under the opening obligation has been presented to and approved by the Board or an equivalent senior governance body within the past 12 months, and that progress against milestones has been formally reported. Roadmaps without governance endorsement or progress tracking will be treated as non-compliant with the foundational obligation regardless of the quality of operational evidence.

Cross-reference this clause with your clause 10.51 (third party audit) evidence to ensure consistency: the priority control set defined under sub-clause (d) should align with the scope of controls tested in third party audits. Inconsistency between these two artefacts — such as controls flagged as priority here but not audited under 10.51 — will draw examiner scrutiny and undermine both submissions.

10.50.title: Cloud Services Risk Assessment
10.50.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that a formal, documented risk assessment was completed before any cloud workload was provisioned — not retrospectively. Examiners look closely at whether all ten risk domains prescribed in clause 10.50(a)–(j) are individually and substantively addressed, not treated as a generic checklist. A common red flag is a risk assessment that recycles the cloud provider's own marketing material rather than reflecting the institution's specific threat model, data classification, and regulatory obligations under Malaysian law. Examiners will cross-reference the assessment against the institution's actual cloud architecture diagrams, data flow maps, and the signed cloud service agreement to test whether identified risks are accurately characterised and whether stated controls exist in practice. Institutions that cannot demonstrate a clear pre-adoption approval workflow — including sign-off from the CTO, CISO, and Board IT Risk Committee — will attract close scrutiny regardless of the assessment's content.

evidenceItems.name: Pre-Adoption Cloud Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: The primary risk assessment document completed before cloud adoption, demonstrating that the institution systematically evaluated all ten risk domains in clause 10.50(a)–(j). This report is the anchor artefact for the entire clause and must evidence a structured methodology, risk ratings, control recommendations, and senior approval prior to the go-live date of any cloud workload.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report is dated and version-controlled with evidence of completion before the cloud service provisioning date, confirmed by comparing the report timestamp against cloud contract execution and first-invoice records

Each of the ten risk domains in clause 10.50(a)–(j) has a dedicated section with an inherent risk rating, current control description, residual risk rating, and named control owner — no domain is lumped together or left blank
The assessment methodology references an established framework (e.g., CSA Cloud Controls Matrix, ISO/IEC 27017, NIST SP 800-144) and maps findings back to BNM RMiT obligations explicitly

Sign-off page includes dated signatures or electronic approvals from at least the CISO, CTO, and Head of Compliance, with a Board IT Risk Committee or equivalent resolution approving adoption at an acceptable residual risk level
The report includes a residual risk treatment plan with specific control actions, accountable owners, target remediation dates, and a scheduled review cycle — not just a static point-in-time snapshot
commonGaps: Assessment was completed after the cloud service was already live, making it a post-hoc rationalisation rather than a genuine pre-adoption evaluation — detectable by comparing the report date against the cloud contract signing date or first billing date

Ten sub-risks in clause 10.50(a)–(j) are addressed at a surface level with generic statements such as 'provider is SOC 2 certified' without institution-specific analysis of how that certification mitigates the risk in the institution's context

No independent or internal audit validation — the assessment was prepared and approved entirely by the same IT team that owns the cloud environment, creating a self-assessment conflict with no second-line review

Risk ratings are assigned without supporting evidence or scoring rationale, making it impossible for an examiner to verify how an inherent risk of 'High' was calculated or why a residual risk of 'Low' was accepted

The document is never updated after initial adoption despite material changes to the cloud architecture, new service tiers adopted, or changes in the regulatory environment — no re-assessment cadence is defined or evidenced
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, RSA Archer) — risk assessment module where the cloud risk record was created and approved
SharePoint or Confluence — Technology Risk and Compliance team's document library for pre-adoption assessments
Board secretariat system — Board IT Risk Committee minutes and resolutions approving cloud adoption
Project management platform (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) — project inception documents and gateway approval records for cloud migration projects
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded approval workflow record or wet/electronic signatures; version history must be preserved

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Architecture and Deployment Model Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation describing the cloud deployment model chosen (public, private, hybrid, or community cloud) and the service model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), with a risk analysis of the architectural sophistication and the data classification of workloads placed in each tier. This evidence satisfies clause 10.50(a) and underpins risk ratings across all other sub-clauses by establishing the baseline architecture context.
whatGoodLooksLike: Architecture diagrams are current, version-controlled, and show data flows, network segmentation, integration points with on-premises systems, and the boundaries between the institution's responsibilities and the cloud provider's responsibilities

Each cloud service tier or workload is tagged with a data classification label (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) aligned to the institution's data classification policy, with a documented rationale for why that classification is appropriate for cloud hosting

The sophistication of the deployment model is explicitly risk-rated — for example, a multi-cloud or hybrid architecture is assessed for increased complexity risk including misconfiguration exposure, identity federation sprawl, and monitoring blind spots

The documentation is maintained in a configuration management database (CMDB) or equivalent, and changes to the architecture trigger a mandatory re-assessment trigger as defined in the change management policy

The deployment model selection is traced back to a business requirements document and a security-by-design review, showing that architecture decisions were risk-informed rather than purely cost-driven
commonGaps: Architecture diagrams are outdated — reflecting the initial deployment state but not incorporating subsequent additions of SaaS tools, new regions, or PaaS services adopted informally by business units outside IT governance

The distinction between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS risk profiles is not addressed; all three service models are treated identically in the risk assessment despite having materially different responsibility boundaries and security customisation capabilities

Data classification for cloud-hosted workloads is absent or applied inconsistently, so examiners cannot determine whether sensitive customer or financial data is co-located with lower-sensitivity workloads on the same cloud tenancy

No formal security-by-design or threat modelling artefact exists for the cloud architecture, meaning risks from the chosen topology (e.g., direct internet egress from a cloud VPC) were never explicitly evaluated
Shadow IT cloud usage — business units subscribing to SaaS tools independently — is not captured in the architecture documentation, leaving significant risk surface unaccounted for in the assessment
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team — architecture repository (e.g., LeanIX, Ardoq, or SharePoint-based EA library)
CMDB in ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) — cloud asset inventory and configuration records
Cloud provider native tools — AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, or GCP Asset Inventory for current-state architecture exports
Information Security team — threat modelling records and security design review sign-off documents
evidenceItems.format: Visio/Lucidchart diagrams exported to PDF, accompanied by a DOCX narrative; CMDB extract in spreadsheet format

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Migration Risk Assessment and Data Residency Analysis
evidenceItems.description: A dedicated risk assessment covering the risks of migrating existing systems to cloud infrastructure (clause 10.50(b)) and the geographic location of cloud infrastructure including geo-political and legal risks (clause 10.50(c)). This document must demonstrate that the institution evaluated data sovereignty, cross-border transfer obligations under Malaysian law, potential regulatory access by foreign governments, and the technical and operational risks of the migration process itself.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment explicitly identifies the physical data centre regions selected and maps them against the jurisdiction's data protection laws, mutual legal assistance treaties (MLATs), and any provisions under which foreign authorities could compel disclosure of Malaysian financial customer data

Migration risks are categorised by workload type and include specific scenarios such as data corruption during transfer, downtime risk to critical systems, rollback capability limitations, and the risk of running hybrid configurations during extended migration windows

The institution has documented legal counsel opinion or obtained a regulatory no-objection from BNM (where required) confirming that the proposed data residency arrangement satisfies PDPA 2010 obligations and any sector-specific BNM data localisation expectations

A data flow map shows exactly which data elements leave Malaysian jurisdiction, under what circumstances, for what purposes (e.g., cloud provider operations, AI model training, backups), and what contractual protections govern those transfers

Post-migration validation procedures are documented and evidenced, including data integrity checks, security control equivalence testing, and a sign-off confirming the migrated system meets the same or higher security baseline as the on-premises predecessor
commonGaps: The assessment identifies the cloud region (e.g., 'Southeast Asia') but does not drill into the specific country or jurisdiction, leaving geo-political risk (e.g., government access laws of the hosting country) unanalysed

Cross-border data transfer risks are treated as mitigated solely by the cloud provider's contractual terms, without independent legal analysis under Malaysian law or BNM guidance — the institution cannot demonstrate it understood the legal risk it accepted

Migration risk assessment covers the technical migration plan but omits operational risks such as staff retraining, support model changes, incident response capability gaps during the transition period, and the risk of extended hybrid-state operation

No rollback or fallback plan is documented for the migration, meaning the institution has no evidence of how it would recover critical systems to on-premises infrastructure if the cloud migration encountered a critical failure post-cutover

Data residency assessment is a one-time exercise and is not revisited when the cloud provider announces new regions, changes its data processing locations for specific services, or when BNM issues updated guidance on data localisation
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance department — legal opinion letters on cross-border data transfer and PDPA compliance
Project Management Office — cloud migration project documentation, run books, and post-migration validation reports
Cloud provider portals — data residency commitment documentation and Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) specifying data location
GRC platform — risk records associated with data residency and migration workstreams
BNM correspondence files — any regulatory submissions or no-objection letters related to cloud adoption
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX assessment report with embedded data flow diagrams; legal opinion in PDF; migration validation report in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Multi-Tenancy Controls Assessment and Cyber Risk Exposure Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that the institution assessed and controls risks arising from cloud multi-tenancy and data co-mingling (clause 10.50(d)), the ability to customise security configurations to achieve a high level of protection (clause 10.50(f)), and exposure to cyber-attacks via cloud service providers (clause 10.50(g)). This evidence set should combine technical configuration evidence with a documented risk assessment narrative.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment documents specific multi-tenancy isolation mechanisms relied upon (e.g., hypervisor isolation, network micro-segmentation, dedicated tenancy options) with an evaluation of the residual risk of co-mingling, including whether the institution opted for dedicated or shared hosting based on data sensitivity

Security configuration baseline documents (e.g., CIS Benchmark compliance reports, cloud security posture management tool outputs) show the institution's cloud environment is hardened to a defined standard, with deviations formally risk-accepted and documented

Cyber risk exposure via the cloud supply chain is assessed using a structured methodology — including evaluation of the cloud provider's own security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II), third-party penetration test reports shared by the provider, and the institution's own cloud penetration testing results

The institution maintains evidence of cloud security posture management (CSPM) tooling deployment (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud, AWS Security Hub, Prisma Cloud) with periodic reports showing continuous monitoring of misconfigurations and policy drift

The assessment includes a documented analysis of supply chain attack scenarios (e.g., compromise of cloud provider's management plane, malicious insider at the provider) and the institution's compensating controls such as customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) and privileged access monitoring
commonGaps: Multi-tenancy risk is dismissed as 'mitigated by the provider' without the institution demonstrating it tested or validated the isolation controls — no penetration testing, no independent review, and no technical evidence of logical separation between tenant environments

Security configuration customisation is limited to default settings — the institution accepted the cloud provider's out-of-box configuration without conducting a hardening exercise or mapping configurations to a recognised security baseline such as CIS Benchmarks

Cyber risk assessment for the cloud supply chain focuses only on the institution's own cloud environment and does not consider the risk of the cloud provider itself being breached, nor does it document the institution's response plan for a provider-side security incident

CSPM tooling generates alerts but findings are not tracked to resolution — audit logs show persistent high-severity misconfigurations that were acknowledged but not remediated, indicating the monitoring programme is not operationally effective

The assessment does not address shared technology vulnerabilities in virtualisation layers or container orchestration platforms (e.g., escape vulnerabilities, side-channel attacks in shared CPU environments) that are specific to multi-tenant cloud architectures
suggestedSources: Cloud provider portal — tenant isolation architecture documentation, shared responsibility model documentation, and available audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001 certificates)
CSPM platform — Microsoft Defender for Cloud, AWS Security Hub, or Prisma Cloud — policy compliance dashboards and historical reports
Information Security team — penetration testing reports for cloud environments and cloud security hardening baselines
ITSM platform — vulnerability and misconfiguration remediation tickets linked to cloud findings
Third-party assessor reports — external cloud security reviews or red team exercises targeting cloud infrastructure
evidenceItems.format: PDF assessment report; CSPM tool exports in PDF or CSV; penetration test reports in PDF; cloud provider audit certificates in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Vendor Lock-in Assessment and Cloud Exit Strategy
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution assessed vendor lock-in risk, application portability and interoperability (clause 10.50(e)), and the risks associated with termination of the cloud service provider including the ability to recover the institution's data following termination (clause 10.50(h)). This evidence must show that a viable and tested exit strategy exists before the cloud service is adopted.
whatGoodLooksLike: The vendor lock-in assessment quantifies dependency on provider-proprietary services (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Cosmos DB, GCP BigQuery) and rates the portability risk of each workload, with a documented decision to accept or mitigate lock-in for each proprietary service consumed

An exit strategy document defines the specific procedures, timelines, tooling, and resource requirements for migrating each workload to an alternative provider or back to on-premises, with a realistic cost estimate and a maximum tolerable migration duration aligned to business continuity requirements

The institution has tested the exit strategy through a tabletop exercise or partial data extraction test, with documented results demonstrating that data can be exported in an open, usable format within the timeframes stated in the contract and exit plan

Cloud service agreements are reviewed and the exit strategy document references specific contractual provisions governing termination notice periods, data retrieval windows, data deletion timelines, and any provider assistance obligations during transition

An interoperability assessment evaluates API standards used (REST, open standards versus proprietary SDKs) and documents where the institution has implemented abstraction layers or open-source alternatives to reduce proprietary dependency
commonGaps: Exit strategy exists as a high-level narrative but has never been tested — no tabletop exercise, no data extraction drill, and no validation that data volumes can be exported within contractual timelines, leaving its viability unproven

Vendor lock-in risk is assessed at the contract level only (reviewing termination clauses) without a technical assessment of how deeply proprietary services are embedded in the architecture — the institution underestimates the actual switching cost

The exit strategy does not account for data held in cloud provider-managed services such as managed databases, object storage, or SaaS platforms where the export mechanism is controlled by the provider and may be slow, costly, or incomplete

Termination scenarios are limited to voluntary exit — the exit strategy does not address involuntary termination scenarios such as provider insolvency, regulatory suspension of the provider's licence, or provider unilateral contract termination due to sanctions or legal orders

Post-termination data deletion obligations are not documented or verified — the institution cannot demonstrate that it has a process to obtain and retain evidence of secure data deletion by the provider after contract termination
suggestedSources: Vendor Management or Procurement team — cloud service contract, service schedules, and data processing agreements
Technology Risk team — vendor lock-in risk assessment records in the GRC platform
IT Architecture team — cloud workload dependency maps and portability assessments
Business Continuity Management team — exit strategy and cloud resilience playbooks
Internal Audit or Technology Risk — tabletop exercise records or data extraction test results
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX exit strategy document; spreadsheet workload portability assessment; tabletop exercise report in PDF; contract clauses extracted in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Shared Responsibility Matrix and Regulatory Compliance Mapping
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution has formally documented the demarcation of responsibilities, limitations, and liability between the institution and the cloud service provider (clause 10.50(i)), and has assessed and continues to monitor its ability to meet BNM regulatory requirements and applicable international standards on cloud computing (clause 10.50(j)). This evidence spans both the contractual and the compliance assurance dimensions of cloud governance.
whatGoodLooksLike: A shared responsibility matrix is documented at the service model level (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and at the individual workload level, clearly attributing each security control domain (e.g., identity management, network security, data encryption, incident response, audit logging) to either the institution, the cloud provider, or a shared obligation with specific party accountabilities

The regulatory compliance mapping document cross-references each BNM RMiT obligation and applicable international standard (ISO/IEC 27017, ISO/IEC 27018, CSA CCM, BNM RMiT Part G) against the control owner in the shared responsibility matrix, demonstrating that no regulatory obligation falls into an unaddressed gap

The institution holds current copies of the cloud provider's independent audit certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, ISO 27017) and has a documented process for tracking the renewal and scope of those certifications annually, including a procedure for escalating if a certification lapses

The cloud service agreement explicitly addresses liability limitations including data breach notification timelines, financial liability caps, service credit mechanisms, and the provider's obligation to support BNM regulatory examinations — and the institution has obtained legal review confirming these terms are acceptable

A continuous compliance monitoring programme is in place — including periodic self-assessments against the shared responsibility matrix, automated policy compliance checks, and an annual third-party cloud compliance review — with evidence of the last review cycle and findings remediation
commonGaps: The shared responsibility matrix is adopted verbatim from the cloud provider's generic documentation without customisation to the institution's specific service consumption, leaving ambiguity about who is responsible for controls in services the institution actually uses

Regulatory compliance mapping is a one-time exercise performed at adoption and not updated when BNM issues new circulars, guidance, or when the institution adopts new cloud services — the mapping is stale and no longer reflects the current regulatory landscape

The institution holds the cloud provider's ISO 27001 certificate but has not verified whether the specific services and data centres used by the institution fall within the certification scope — a common gap where institutions assume full coverage when the audit scope is narrower

Liability and data breach notification clauses in the cloud contract conflict with BNM's mandatory incident reporting timelines (e.g., the provider's contractual notification window exceeds the regulatory deadline), but this discrepancy was never identified or escalated

There is no evidence of ongoing regulatory compliance monitoring post-adoption — the institution cannot demonstrate it reviewed its cloud compliance posture in response to BNM's subsequent cloud guidance updates or international standard revisions such as the ISO/IEC 27017 amendment
suggestedSources: Cloud provider portal — shared responsibility model documentation, compliance programme pages, and downloadable audit reports (SOC 2, ISO certificates)
Legal and Compliance team — cloud service agreement review memos and regulatory mapping spreadsheets
GRC platform — compliance control library mapped to BNM RMiT and ISO 27017 requirements
Vendor Management team — certificate tracking register and vendor compliance monitoring records
Internal Audit — cloud compliance review reports and findings registers
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (shared responsibility matrix and compliance mapping); PDF (cloud service agreement extract and legal review memo); PDF (provider audit certificates and scope letters)

auditTips: Verify the date sequence first: obtain the pre-adoption risk assessment report, the cloud service contract execution date, and the first provisioning or billing record, then confirm the risk assessment predates the contract. Any institution that cannot produce a pre-adoption assessment dated before contract signing will struggle to demonstrate compliance regardless of the quality of the document itself.

Use the architecture documentation and CMDB as a ground-truth test: map every cloud service in the institution's cloud billing console or CMDB against the risk assessment coverage. Services that appear in billing or the CMDB but not in the risk assessment indicate shadow adoption — a significant gap that examiners will treat as evidence of an immature cloud governance framework.

Test the exit strategy's viability by requesting evidence of a tabletop exercise or data extraction drill, not just the written document. Ask for the exercise report, participant list, and any identified gaps. An exit strategy that has never been exercised provides limited assurance — probe whether the institution can articulate realistic extraction timelines for its largest data stores.

Cross-reference the shared responsibility matrix against the actual cloud services consumed: many institutions document the matrix at the IaaS level but then consume dozens of PaaS and SaaS services where responsibilities shift significantly. Request a service-by-service breakdown and look for PaaS services (managed databases, serverless functions, AI/ML platforms) where the institution has assumed the provider handles security controls it is actually responsible for.

Probe geo-political risk depth by asking the compliance team to explain the specific legal risk of the chosen data centre jurisdiction — not just that it is 'in Asia Pacific'. Examiners should ask about foreign government access laws (e.g., the US CLOUD Act, China's National Security Law) and whether Malaysian customer financial data in that jurisdiction is subject to compelled disclosure. A strong response will reference legal counsel opinion; a weak response will cite only the provider's contractual commitments.

Request the CSPM or cloud security posture management tool's dashboard history, not just a current snapshot. Reviewing the historical trend of misconfiguration findings reveals whether the institution is actively remediating issues or allowing findings to accumulate — persistent high-severity findings over multiple months indicate a monitoring-without-action problem that suggests the security customisation obligation in clause 10.50(f) is not being met in practice.

Check whether the regulatory compliance mapping has been updated since the initial cloud adoption by requesting the document's version history and cross-referencing it against the dates of subsequent BNM guidance updates and any new cloud services adopted. An institution that adopted cloud in 2021 but has a compliance mapping last updated in 2021 — despite BNM issuing further guidance and despite adding new SaaS tools — demonstrates a static compliance posture that does not satisfy the 'continuing basis' requirement in clause 10.50(j).

10.51.title: Public Cloud Key Risks and Controls
10.51.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has systematically mapped each critical system hosted on public cloud against the specific key risks and control measures enumerated in Appendix 10 of the RMiT, and whether this mapping is actively maintained rather than a one-time exercise. A primary red flag is the absence of a documented critical system classification that clearly delineates which workloads are subject to Appendix 10 obligations. Where the institution departs from Appendix 10 prescribed controls, examiners will probe whether the rationale is substantiated with quantitative or qualitative evidence demonstrating equivalence or superiority — mere assertions without supporting data are treated as non-compliant. Examiners also look for evidence that senior management and the Board are aware of deviations and have formally approved them. The evaluation approach typically involves cross-referencing the institution's cloud risk register against Appendix 10 line-by-line to identify gaps, unapproved deviations, or controls that exist on paper but lack operational evidence.

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems Register — Public Cloud Footprint
evidenceItems.description: A current, Board-endorsed register that identifies all systems classified as critical, specifies which are hosted on public cloud, names the cloud service provider and service model (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), and serves as the foundational scoping document for Appendix 10 obligations. This register demonstrates that the institution has formally determined which systems trigger clause 10.51 requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each system entry carries a documented criticality classification rationale aligned to the institution's IT risk framework, not merely a label
The register distinguishes between public cloud, private cloud, hybrid, and on-premises hosting with specific CSP names, regions, and account/subscription identifiers
Review date and version history confirm the register is refreshed at least annually and after any material infrastructure change
Ownership is clearly assigned — a named system owner and a named cloud custodian appear for each critical system entry
The register is cross-referenced to the institution's business impact analysis so that criticality ratings are traceable to RTO/RPO and business function dependencies
commonGaps: Criticality classification is applied inconsistently — some systems are labelled critical without a documented threshold or scoring methodology, making the scope of Appendix 10 obligations arbitrary
The register conflates private and public cloud deployments, obscuring which systems are actually subject to clause 10.51
Stale entries exist for decommissioned systems while newly migrated workloads are absent, indicating the register is not integrated into the change management process
No Board or senior management approval record accompanies the register, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate governance oversight of its cloud footprint
CSP details are incomplete — missing region, availability zone, or service model — preventing examiners from assessing data residency and sovereignty exposures
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or equivalent) — Configuration Management Database (CMDB) module
IT Asset Management system filtered by hosting environment and criticality tag
Cloud management portal exports (AWS Organizations, Azure Management Groups, GCP Resource Manager) corroborated against internal records
Technology Risk or IT department SharePoint/Confluence repository where the formal register is version-controlled
Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery team for BIA-linked criticality ratings
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (PDF-printable), with version control history

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 10 Risk and Control Mapping Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured, line-by-line assessment document that maps each key risk and corresponding control measure prescribed in Appendix 10 of the RMiT against the institution's implemented controls for each critical public cloud system. This matrix is the primary artefact demonstrating that the institution has genuinely considered Appendix 10 obligations rather than treated them as a checkbox.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every Appendix 10 risk category is explicitly addressed — no rows are skipped or merged without documented justification, covering areas such as data sovereignty, multi-tenancy, access management, encryption, business continuity, and exit strategy

For each control, the matrix specifies the control owner, implementation status (implemented/partially implemented/not applicable/alternative control), and references supporting evidence by document ID or system name
The matrix is reviewed and signed off by the Chief Information Security Officer and Head of Technology Risk at least annually, with review dates visible
Where a control is assessed as 'not applicable', a written rationale is provided that is specific to the system's architecture — not a blanket exclusion
The matrix version-history shows iterative improvement over successive review cycles, demonstrating active management rather than a static document
commonGaps: The matrix is populated at the institution level rather than per critical system, meaning system-specific risk profiles and control implementations are obscured and cannot be verified by examiners
Control implementation status is self-assessed without reference to independent testing results, audit findings, or configuration evidence, making assertions unverifiable
Entire Appendix 10 risk categories (commonly exit strategy, multi-tenancy isolation, and concentration risk) are omitted or marked not applicable without rationale
The matrix exists as a one-time compliance exercise with no subsequent review dates, making it unreliable as evidence of ongoing governance
Control descriptions are paraphrased from Appendix 10 without any institution-specific detail, suggesting the document was completed theoretically rather than based on actual implementation review
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management team — primary owner of the RMiT compliance mapping artefact
GRC platform risk and control library linked to cloud risk domain
Internal Audit workpapers from the most recent cloud infrastructure audit
Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool exports (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Cloud, AWS Security Hub, Prisma Cloud) as supporting technical evidence
Third-party cloud security assessment reports commissioned by the institution
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet with structured columns (Risk ID, Appendix 10 Reference, Control Description, Implementation Status, Evidence Reference, Owner, Review Date) or equivalent GRC platform report

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Risk Register — Appendix 10 Aligned
evidenceItems.description: An operational risk register that captures identified cloud-specific risks for each critical public cloud system, directly traceable to the risk categories in Appendix 10.
