This register demonstrates that Appendix 10 risk consideration is embedded in the institution's ongoing risk management cycle, not confined to policy documentation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each risk entry references the specific Appendix 10 risk category it corresponds to, enabling traceable coverage of all prescribed risk areas
Risk entries include inherent risk rating, current control effectiveness rating, and residual risk rating using a consistent and documented methodology
The register is reviewed in the institution's risk committee at a cadence commensurate with the criticality of the systems — at minimum quarterly for critical systems
Risk treatment plans with named owners and target dates are attached to all residual risks rated above the institution's risk appetite threshold
The register captures risk trends over time, showing whether exposures are increasing, stable, or reducing as controls mature
commonGaps: Cloud risks are embedded in the generic IT risk register without Appendix 10 tagging, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic coverage of prescribed risk categories
Risk ratings are inconsistently applied — some entries lack inherent risk ratings or control effectiveness assessments, preventing a coherent residual risk picture
The register has not been updated to reflect new cloud workloads onboarded since the last review, indicating a disconnect between change management and risk processes
Risk treatment plans exist but are overdue with no documented escalation, suggesting the risk management process is not functioning effectively
Concentration risk — particularly CSP dependency for multiple critical systems — is absent from the register despite being an Appendix 10 concern
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform — Technology Risk domain, cloud sub-category
Technology Risk Management team's operational risk reporting pack submitted to the Risk Committee
Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes evidencing review of the cloud risk register
Internal Audit or Risk Assurance function's independent risk register validation reports
evidenceItems.format: GRC platform export or spreadsheet (PDF for committee presentation), with audit trail of updates

evidenceItems.name: Alternative Control Justification and Equivalence Assessment
evidenceItems.description: For any Appendix 10 prescribed control measure from which the institution departs, a formal documented assessment that articulates the nature of the departure, the alternative risk management practice adopted, and a structured argument demonstrating that the alternative is at least as effective as, or superior to, the Appendix 10 measure. This evidence is required by clause 10.51 to sustain any deviation from Appendix 10.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each deviation is documented individually with a unique reference, identifying the specific Appendix 10 control being departed from and the system(s) affected

The equivalence argument is supported by quantitative or qualitative evidence — such as penetration test results, independent audit findings, industry benchmarks, or threat modelling outputs — rather than narrative assertion alone
A named approver at senior management level (CISO, CTO, or equivalent) has formally approved each documented deviation, with approval date recorded
The assessment acknowledges residual risk arising from the deviation and confirms that residual risk remains within the institution's approved risk appetite
A periodic review schedule is documented for each deviation, ensuring that equivalence remains valid as the threat landscape and technology environment evolve
commonGaps: Deviations are undocumented — the institution has informally adopted alternative controls without any written record, meaning it cannot 'explain and demonstrate' to BNM as required by the clause
The equivalence argument is limited to a narrative statement without supporting evidence, which BNM examiners will treat as insufficient under the clause's 'demonstrate' standard
Approvals are absent or delegated below the appropriate seniority level, failing to demonstrate adequate governance oversight of the departure
The assessment does not quantify residual risk or confirm it is within appetite, making it unclear whether the institution has genuinely accepted the risk or overlooked it
Deviations lack a review date, meaning they may have been approved under different circumstances and the equivalence assessment is no longer current
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management team — deviation and exception register maintained within GRC platform
Information Security policy exception log maintained by the CISO office
Board Risk Committee or Technology and Cybersecurity Committee minutes evidencing senior approval of material deviations
Third-party assessor reports (penetration testing, cloud security assessments) cited as supporting evidence in the equivalence argument
Internal Audit findings and management responses where deviations were identified and subsequently formalised
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX formal assessment document with approval signatures, cross-referenced to GRC exception register entry

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Governance Framework and Board-Level Oversight Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing that the institution's governance structure encompasses public cloud risk, including Board and senior management awareness of Appendix 10 obligations, approval of the institution's cloud risk posture, and ongoing oversight of cloud-related risks for critical systems. This demonstrates that clause 10.51 compliance is a governed activity, not a purely operational one.
whatGoodLooksLike: A Board-approved Cloud Services Policy or Cloud Governance Framework exists that explicitly references RMiT Appendix 10 obligations and assigns accountability for compliance

Board Risk Committee or Technology and Cybersecurity Committee minutes contain substantive discussion of cloud risks for critical systems at least annually, not merely a notation that a report was tabled

Management information packs presented to the Board include cloud-specific metrics — such as control coverage against Appendix 10, open risk items, and CSP concentration exposure — enabling informed oversight
Escalation records demonstrate that material cloud risk issues (including unresolved Appendix 10 control gaps) are surfaced to Board level within defined timelines
The institution's three lines of defence model is visibly applied to cloud risk — first line ownership, second line risk oversight, and third line audit coverage are all documented and functioning
commonGaps: Cloud risk governance is treated as an IT operational matter only, with no evidence that the Board has been briefed on Appendix 10 obligations or the institution's compliance posture
Board and committee minutes reference cloud as a topic but lack substantive discussion, suggesting reporting is superficial and oversight is nominal
The Cloud Services Policy predates the current RMiT version and has not been updated to reflect Appendix 10 requirements, creating a gap between policy and regulatory obligation
The three lines of defence structure for cloud risk is unclear — first line ownership of Appendix 10 controls is not assigned, and second line challenge is absent from risk review records
Management information does not differentiate between critical and non-critical cloud systems, preventing the Board from exercising proportionate oversight over the systems subject to clause 10.51
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — Board Risk Committee and Technology and Cybersecurity Committee minutes and approved papers
CISO or CTO office — Cloud Services Policy and Cloud Governance Framework documents
Technology Risk Management team — management information packs and dashboard reports presented to governance committees
Internal Audit — audit charter and cloud audit coverage plan confirming third-line oversight of Appendix 10 compliance
GRC platform — policy register with version history and approval records
evidenceItems.format: PDF policy documents, committee minutes (PDF), and dashboard/MIS reports (PDF or presentation slides)

evidenceItems.name: Independent Assurance and Audit Reports — Cloud Appendix 10 Coverage
evidenceItems.description: Reports from internal audit, external auditors, or qualified third-party assessors that independently verify the institution's compliance with Appendix 10 risk and control measures for critical public cloud systems. This evidence demonstrates that the institution does not rely solely on self-assessment and that control effectiveness is subject to objective scrutiny.
whatGoodLooksLike: Internal audit reports explicitly reference Appendix 10 of the RMiT as the audit criterion and structure findings against specific Appendix 10 risk categories, enabling direct mapping of audit coverage to regulatory obligations
The most recent audit of cloud controls was conducted within the past 12 months for critical systems, reflecting a risk-based audit frequency proportionate to the exposure

Management responses to audit findings include specific remediation actions, owners, and target dates — and subsequent follow-up audits confirm closure of findings, demonstrating a functioning audit cycle

Third-party assessments (penetration tests, cloud security posture reviews, CSP SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 reliance assessments) supplement internal audit and are referenced in the institution's overall assurance picture

Where audit findings identified Appendix 10 control gaps that were remediated through alternative controls, the alternative control equivalence assessment (10.51-E4) is cross-referenced in the audit closure record
commonGaps: Internal audit reports cover IT and cloud infrastructure generally but do not specifically reference Appendix 10, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate targeted assurance over its regulatory obligations
Audit findings related to Appendix 10 controls remain open beyond agreed remediation dates with no documented escalation or revised timelines, indicating systemic issues with the remediation process

The institution relies on CSP-issued compliance certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2) as a substitute for its own Appendix 10 assessment, without acknowledging that these certifications cover CSP infrastructure obligations, not the institution's own control responsibilities
Third-party assessments are commissioned infrequently (every two or three years) for critical systems that change regularly, meaning assurance is outdated relative to the current risk environment
Audit coverage does not extend to alternative control equivalence assessments, meaning deviations from Appendix 10 operate outside the assurance framework
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — audit universe, audit plan, and completed audit reports for cloud and technology domains
CISO or Technology Risk team — third-party penetration test reports and cloud security assessment reports
Cloud Service Provider portals — SOC 2 Type II reports, ISO 27001 certificates, and shared responsibility model documentation
Audit Committee minutes evidencing review and acceptance of cloud audit findings and remediation progress
GRC platform — issue and action tracking module for audit findings with status and closure evidence
evidenceItems.format: PDF audit reports with management response sections; GRC platform action tracker export as supporting evidence

auditTips: Before the examination, conduct a structured walk-through of Appendix 10 with system owners for each critical public cloud system and produce a pre-examination gap analysis — this demonstrates proactive governance and allows remediation of obvious gaps before BNM examiners identify them. Document that this walk-through occurred, including attendees and date.

Prepare a dedicated Appendix 10 binder or digital evidence pack that organises all evidence items (critical systems register, risk and control mapping, risk register, deviation assessments, governance records, and audit reports) by Appendix 10 risk category rather than by document type — this allows examiners to trace end-to-end coverage of each prescribed risk area without having to cross-reference multiple unlinked files.

For any Appendix 10 departure, ensure the equivalence assessment is examiner-ready: print the relevant Appendix 10 control text, place it alongside the institution's alternative control description, and attach the supporting evidence (test results, benchmarks, or audit findings) as numbered annexes. BNM examiners will test the 'demonstrate' standard by asking probing questions — rehearse the narrative with the CISO and system owner in advance.

Verify that the critical systems register is reconciled to actual cloud billing and usage data from CSP portals before the examination — examiners may cross-check the declared cloud footprint against CSP account structures, and unexplained discrepancies (shadow IT, unregistered accounts) are a serious red flag signalling inadequate governance over cloud adoption.

Cross-reference open audit findings against the Appendix 10 mapping matrix and the alternative control deviation register to identify any finding that affects a control the institution has represented as implemented or equivalent — an unresolved audit finding on a control claimed as effective is a material inconsistency that examiners will probe, so reconcile these records and prepare a coherent narrative on remediation status.

If the institution relies on CSP compliance certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001) as part of its Appendix 10 control evidence, prepare a written analysis of the shared responsibility model that clearly delineates which Appendix 10 controls are addressed by the CSP and which remain the institution's own responsibility — examiners frequently find that institutions conflate CSP certifications with their own compliance, and this analysis demonstrates that the institution understands the boundary.

Commission a targeted pre-examination review of concentration risk across the critical systems register — if multiple critical systems rely on the same CSP or the same CSP region, prepare a documented concentration risk assessment and the associated contingency or exit strategy, as BNM has signalled heightened focus on CSP dependency as a systemic risk for the financial sector.

10.52.title: Cloud Data Protection Safeguards
10.52.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised data protection controls rather than merely documented them. Primary scrutiny falls on three areas: (1) evidence that cryptographic key management is genuinely controlled by the institution rather than delegated entirely to the cloud service provider (CSP), including whether keys are stored in customer-managed hardware security modules (HSMs) or key management services; (2) whether data ownership and access governance arrangements are contractually enforceable and technically implemented, not just stated in policy; and (3) whether the institution can demonstrate, through logs and audit trails, that unauthorised disclosure events would be detected and that access to regulated data is continuously monitored. Red flags include institutions that rely solely on CSP-native key management with no customer-controlled key material, absence of data residency controls for Malaysian customer data, and gap between policy commitments and actual technical configurations evidenced during technical walkthroughs.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Data Protection Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: The formal institutional policy governing protection of customer, counterparty, and proprietary data hosted in cloud environments, including data ownership principles, classification-based protection controls, and responsibilities for maintaining control over cloud-hosted data. This document demonstrates that the institution has a governance foundation mandating safeguards aligned to Clause 10.52 obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly distinguishes between customer and counterparty information, proprietary data, and other data types, with differentiated protection requirements for each category
Ownership and control responsibilities are unambiguously assigned to named internal roles (e.g., Data Owner, CISO, Cloud Security team) rather than deferred to the CSP
Policy mandates customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) or customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) as the default posture for regulated data
Document is reviewed and approved at Board or senior management level, with version history showing at least annual review cycles
Policy cross-references the institution's data classification framework and maps protection requirements to each sensitivity tier
commonGaps: Policy language conflates cloud-specific obligations with general IT security policy, making it impossible to demonstrate targeted compliance with RMiT cloud clauses
Data ownership statements are aspirational rather than operationally enforced — policy says the institution owns all data but contracts or technical configurations contradict this
Cryptographic key management obligations are absent or vague, with no specification of whether keys are CSP-managed, customer-managed, or hybrid
Policy has not been reviewed since initial cloud adoption, pre-dating current CSP configurations and failing to reflect production data flows
Proprietary data is not defined with sufficient precision, leaving ambiguity about which datasets are subject to the highest protection tier
suggestedSources: Information Security / CISO office document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform policy library (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream)
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes approving the policy
Document management system with version control and approval workflow records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with tracked approval signatures and version history

evidenceItems.name: Cloud-Hosted Data Inventory and Classification Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive, maintained register of all customer information, counterparty information, and proprietary data assets stored or processed in cloud environments, including their classification tier, associated CSP and service, data residency location, and applicable protection controls. This register demonstrates that the institution has visibility and ownership over what data exists in the cloud and where.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all cloud deployments (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) across all CSPs in use, not just primary providers, and is reconciled against the institution's cloud asset inventory
Each dataset entry specifies data residency region and confirms Malaysian customer data is stored in-country or under an approved cross-border arrangement with documented legal basis
Classification tier is linked to specific protection controls (encryption standard, key management type, access tier) so the register functions as a control mapping document
Register is updated at defined intervals (at minimum quarterly) with a documented review process, including sign-off by the designated Data Owner
Register includes fields for retention period, deletion/purging schedule, and confirmation that data lifecycle obligations are met even in cloud storage
commonGaps: Register is incomplete — shadow IT and SaaS applications adopted by business units without IT approval are not captured, creating blind spots for customer data exposure
Data residency is recorded at the CSP account level rather than the storage-bucket or database level, making it impossible to verify actual data location for specific datasets
Classification entries are self-reported by data owners without independent validation, leading to systematic under-classification of sensitive datasets
Register does not track which encryption keys protect each dataset, severing the link between data inventory and key management controls
Stale entries for decommissioned cloud services remain in the register without deletion confirmation, overstating the scope of data governance
suggestedSources: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool exports (e.g., Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Microsoft Defender for Cloud)
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) platform discovery scan results
GRC platform data asset module
CMDB records from ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) with cloud-tagged assets
CSP-native inventory services (AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, GCP Asset Inventory)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export with filter and audit trail capability

evidenceItems.name: Cryptographic Key Management Framework and Key Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the institution's cryptographic key management programme for cloud-hosted data, including key generation, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction procedures, together with a current inventory of all active keys, their custodians, the datasets they protect, and the hardware or software key management infrastructure used. This evidence demonstrates that the institution retains genuine ownership and management of cryptographic keys as required by Clause 10.52.
whatGoodLooksLike: Keys protecting customer and counterparty information are stored in customer-controlled hardware security modules (HSMs) or cloud HSM services (e.g., AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM) where the institution holds exclusive access to key material, preventing CSP access
Key rotation schedules are documented, enforced, and evidenced by rotation logs showing compliance with the defined frequency (e.g., annual rotation for data-at-rest keys)
Key custodianship is assigned to named individuals within the institution with documented segregation of duties — no single individual can generate, escrow, and approve key operations unilaterally

Key destruction procedures are tested and evidenced, demonstrating that the institution can render cloud-hosted data cryptographically inaccessible upon contract termination or data deletion requirements
The key inventory cross-references the data inventory (10.52-E2), providing a traceable mapping from each regulated dataset to its protecting key and the key's management status
commonGaps: Institution relies on CSP-managed keys (SSE-S3, Google-managed encryption) with no customer key material, meaning the CSP can technically access plaintext data — this is the single most common and critical finding under this clause
Key management procedures exist on paper but HSM deployment evidence is absent — examiners cannot confirm that key material is actually held in tamper-resistant hardware
Key rotation logs are not retained or are overwritten, making it impossible to demonstrate historical compliance with rotation requirements

There is no documented or tested key recovery procedure, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate that data remains accessible following key custodian departure or HSM failure without CSP assistance
Bring-your-own-key (BYOK) arrangements are in place for primary workloads but legacy cloud migrations retain CSP-managed keys, creating inconsistent protection posture across the cloud estate
suggestedSources: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) console exports and CloudTrail logs for key operations
Azure Key Vault audit logs and access policies
GCP Cloud KMS key version and rotation history
HSM management console reports from thales CipherTrust, AWS CloudHSM, or Azure Dedicated HSM
ITSM change records for key rotation and key ceremony documentation from Information Security team
evidenceItems.format: PDF (procedures), Spreadsheet (inventory), System export (rotation logs) — package as combined evidence bundle

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Identity and Access Management (IAM) Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that access to customer information, counterparty information, and proprietary data in cloud environments is controlled, regularly reviewed, and enforced through least-privilege principles. This includes IAM policies, role definitions, access review certifications, and privileged access management (PAM) logs, collectively demonstrating that unauthorised disclosure is prevented through access governance.
whatGoodLooksLike: IAM role and policy configurations confirm that no CSP support or administrator roles have standing access to production data storage buckets or databases containing regulated data, with break-glass access documented and audited

Quarterly access reviews are evidenced by signed certification records showing that Data Owners have attested to the appropriateness of all users and service accounts with access to regulated cloud data

Multi-factor authentication (MFA) enforcement is confirmed for all human identities with access to cloud management consoles and data services, evidenced by IdP configuration exports or conditional access policy screenshots
Service account and workload identity permissions are scoped to minimum necessary access, with no wildcard (*) permissions on regulated data resources, evidenced by IAM policy analysis reports
Privileged access to cloud environments is routed through a PAM solution with session recording, and logs are retained and searchable for the full retention period
commonGaps: CSP root account or equivalent global administrator credentials lack MFA enforcement, creating a critical unauthorised access risk that directly contradicts the clause's protection requirements

Access review certifications are bulk-approved by a single reviewer without evidence of individual assessment — certifications are signed on the same day for hundreds of accounts, indicating a tick-box exercise
Service accounts accumulated over time hold excessive permissions (often Owner or Editor at project level) because deprovisioning processes do not consistently apply to non-human identities
There is no evidence that CSP support access — including vendor access invoked during incident resolution — is logged, reviewed, or subject to institution approval, leaving a gap in access governance

Access controls are documented at the cloud platform level but shadow SaaS applications accessed by business units are entirely outside the IAM governance framework, exposing customer data processed in those tools
suggestedSources: CSP-native IAM policy exports (AWS IAM Access Analyzer, Azure AD access reviews, GCP IAM recommender reports)
Identity Provider (IdP) configuration records (Azure AD / Entra ID, Okta, Ping Identity)
Privileged Access Management platform reports (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)
GRC platform access review campaign records
SIEM platform (Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) user access event reports
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (access review records), PDF (IAM policy reports), System export (PAM session logs)

evidenceItems.name: Data Residency and Sovereignty Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution has implemented and verified technical controls ensuring customer and counterparty information remains within approved geographic boundaries, consistent with data residency commitments made to customers and obligations under Malaysian data protection requirements. This includes CSP configuration records, independent verification outputs, and any approved cross-border data transfer arrangements with documented legal basis.
whatGoodLooksLike: CSP service control policies (SCPs), Azure Policy, or GCP organisation policy constraints are configured to deny storage resource creation outside approved regions, with configuration exports showing enforcement at the organisation or management account level
Independent technical verification (e.g., CSPM scan or penetration test finding) confirms no regulated data is stored in unapproved regions, supplementing policy configuration with empirical evidence

Where cross-border data transfers exist (e.g., disaster recovery in a secondary region), a documented legal basis is on file including customer disclosure obligations, risk assessment, and CISO approval

CSP contractual terms (Data Processing Agreements, DPAs) confirm that the CSP itself does not replicate or process data outside agreed regions for platform operations, support, or AI training purposes
Data residency controls are included in the institution's cloud onboarding checklist, demonstrating that residency requirements are enforced for new cloud deployments by design, not retrofitted
commonGaps: Data residency policy exists but no technical guardrails enforce it — a misconfigured storage bucket in an unapproved region would not be automatically detected or prevented

CSP logging services (CloudTrail, Azure Activity Logs, GCP Audit Logs) that capture data access events are themselves stored in a region different from the primary data, creating a residency gap in the audit infrastructure

Institution is unaware that CSP managed services (e.g., database backup, CDN edge caching, support tooling) may temporarily replicate data outside the primary region — DPAs have not been reviewed for these carve-outs
Disaster recovery configuration uses a CSP paired region that is outside Malaysia without a documented and approved cross-border transfer arrangement, creating an unacknowledged compliance exposure
SaaS applications procured by business units process customer data without IT review of their data residency commitments, leaving residency controls entirely dependent on vendor defaults
suggestedSources: CSP organisation policy or SCP configuration exports from cloud management console
CSPM tool residency compliance reports (Wiz, Prisma Cloud, Orca Security)
Vendor contracts and Data Processing Agreements held by Procurement or Legal
Cloud onboarding checklist records from IT or Cloud Centre of Excellence (CCoE)
Third-party cloud audit reports (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certificates) reviewed for residency scope
evidenceItems.format: PDF (DPAs, policy configurations), System export (CSPM residency reports), Spreadsheet (SaaS residency register)

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Security Monitoring and Unauthorised Access Detection Logs
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that continuous monitoring is in place to detect and alert on unauthorised or anomalous access to cloud-hosted customer and counterparty data, and that the institution can demonstrate the effectiveness of these controls through sample alerts, investigation records, and threat detection configuration. This evidences the 'protect against unauthorised disclosure and access' obligation in Clause 10.52.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM or cloud-native security tooling (e.g., AWS GuardDuty, Microsoft Defender for Cloud, GCP Security Command Center) is configured with specific detection rules for data exfiltration patterns, unusual bulk downloads, and cross-region data access on regulated datasets

Sample alert investigation records demonstrate the end-to-end response process: alert triggered, analyst assigned, investigation completed, and disposition documented — showing the monitoring programme is operationally active

Cloud audit logs (CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Logging) are enabled for all data-access events on regulated storage services, with log integrity protection (log file validation, WORM storage) preventing tampering

Threat detection coverage is tested periodically through purple team exercises or detection validation activities, with results documented and gaps remediated — demonstrating that monitoring actually detects the threats it is configured to catch
Monitoring scope explicitly covers CSP support access events, so that any vendor access to customer data during support engagements is visible to the institution's security operations team
commonGaps: Cloud audit logging is enabled at the account level but data-plane logging (S3 object access, Azure Blob read events, GCP Data Access audit logs) is disabled due to cost concerns, creating a blind spot for the most relevant access events

Alerts are generated by cloud-native tooling but are not ingested into the institution's SIEM, meaning cloud security events are siloed from the institution's broader threat detection and investigation capability

Log retention in cloud logging services defaults to 90 days, far below the 7-year regulatory retention requirement — logs are not exported to long-term storage, making historical investigation impossible

Monitoring configuration has never been tested — there is no evidence that detection rules have been validated against simulated attack scenarios, so there is no assurance that they would fire on actual data exfiltration
Cloud monitoring scope excludes SaaS applications, which are instead subject only to the SaaS provider's native audit logs — these logs may not be accessible to or retained by the institution
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) cloud data source configuration and alert dashboards
CSP-native security tooling consoles (AWS GuardDuty findings, Microsoft Defender for Cloud alerts, GCP SCC findings)
Security Operations Centre (SOC) incident and alert investigation records from ITSM platform
Cloud audit log export configuration and long-term storage evidence (S3 with Object Lock, Azure immutable storage, GCP Locked retention policy)
Purple team or red team exercise reports covering cloud data exfiltration scenarios
evidenceItems.format: System export (log configuration, alert records), PDF (SOC investigation reports, purple team reports)

auditTips: Before the examination, conduct a technical walkthrough of cryptographic key configurations across all cloud environments and confirm that keys protecting customer and counterparty data are customer-managed — produce a one-page summary showing each regulated dataset, its encryption method, and who holds the key material. BNM examiners will probe this specifically; having a pre-prepared, accurate summary prevents examiner-led discovery of CSP-managed key gaps.

Cross-reference the cloud data inventory (10.52-E2) against cloud billing and CSP-native asset discovery outputs to identify shadow IT and unregistered cloud usage before the examination. Examiners may request a live demonstration of CSPM tooling during the walkthrough, and any discrepancy between the documented inventory and what the scanner finds will be treated as a control failure.

Obtain and review all active Data Processing Agreements with CSPs and verify that they explicitly prohibit the CSP from using customer data for training AI or analytics models, and that they specify approved data residency regions matching the institution's documented commitments. Highlight any DPA gaps to Legal and remediate before the examination — undisclosed residency carve-outs in standard DPA annexes are a recurring finding.

Test the data deletion and key destruction procedure end-to-end for at least one non-production dataset and document the exercise, including evidence that data was rendered unrecoverable. Examiners assess whether the institution's claimed control over cloud data is real by asking how the institution would enforce data return or deletion upon CSP contract exit — a tested, documented drill is far more persuasive than a policy statement.

Ensure cloud audit logs for data-access events are enabled at the data-plane level (not just management-plane) and verify that logs are exported to immutable, long-term storage meeting the 7-year retention requirement. Run a retention audit across all cloud accounts before the examination and remediate any gaps — cost-driven logging gaps are common and will be identified if examiners request historical access records for a specific dataset.

Prepare a consolidated cloud security controls matrix that maps each Clause 10.52 obligation to the specific technical control, the system implementing it, and the evidence location. Provide this to BNM examiners at the outset of the review as it demonstrates structured governance and allows the examination to proceed efficiently — institutions that cannot map their controls to specific obligations invite broader investigative scrutiny.

Verify that access review certifications for cloud IAM roles are completed by Data Owners who have genuine knowledge of the access scope, not delegated to IT administrators bulk-approving access they did not grant. Prepare for examiners to interview a Data Owner directly about their last access review — if the owner cannot describe the process or the outcome, the certification will be treated as a compensating control failure rather than positive evidence.

10.53.title: Access Control Policy
10.53.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the access control policy is a substantive, operationalised document — not a high-level statement of intent. They will verify that the policy explicitly covers all three pillars: identification, authentication, and authorisation, and assess whether the granularity of controls scales proportionately with the risk classification of the underlying IT assets and data. A key red flag is a uniform, one-size-fits-all policy that applies identical controls to low-risk internal tools and high-risk core banking or payment systems — examiners expect tiered or risk-differentiated controls. Examiners will also trace the policy through to implementation by cross-referencing it against actual system configurations, user provisioning records, and access review outputs to verify that the written policy reflects operational reality. Institutions that cannot demonstrate a clear link between their risk assessment of IT assets and the granularity of controls defined in the policy will draw significant scrutiny.

evidenceItems.name: Access Control Policy Document
evidenceItems.description: The formal, board-or-senior-management-approved policy document that establishes the institution's framework for identification, authentication, and authorisation of all users to IT assets and data. This document must articulate the risk-based rationale for the level of granularity applied to different asset classes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines identification, authentication, and authorisation requirements as distinct obligations, with specific minimum standards for each (e.g., unique user IDs mandatory, MFA required for privileged and remote access, RBAC as the default authorisation model)

Policy contains a tiered or risk-differentiated framework — for example, distinguishing control requirements for Tier 1 (critical/core banking), Tier 2 (business applications), and Tier 3 (internal tools) — with progressively granular controls for higher-risk tiers
Document includes a clear scope statement covering all user categories: employees, contractors, third parties, service accounts, and non-human identities
Policy references and is consistent with the institution's IT asset classification and risk assessment methodology, with cross-references to those governing documents

Policy has a documented review cycle (at minimum annual), shows evidence of the most recent review date, version history, and approval by an appropriate authority (e.g., CIO, CISO, or Board Risk Committee)
commonGaps: Policy conflates identification and authentication — for example, treating a username as both, without specifying that authentication requires a separate credential verification step — resulting in a policy that does not meet the tripartite obligation in the clause

Granularity of controls is uniform across all IT assets, with no differentiation based on risk level; the policy applies the same password complexity rules to a low-risk intranet portal and a core banking system
Policy scope excludes or is silent on non-human identities (service accounts, API keys, system-to-system credentials), leaving a significant population of access subjects ungoverned

Document has not been reviewed or updated to reflect changes in the institution's IT environment (e.g., cloud adoption, new critical systems) — version date is more than 12 months old with no evidence of interim review

Policy exists as a standalone document but contains no linkage to the institution's risk assessment outputs or IT asset inventory, making it impossible to demonstrate that granularity is commensurate with risk
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cyber Risk department (policy owner)
GRC platform document repository (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream)
SharePoint or internal document management system under IT Governance library
Board or Risk Management Committee meeting minutes confirming policy approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signature block

evidenceItems.name: IT Asset Risk Classification Register
evidenceItems.description: A register or inventory of IT assets that includes a risk classification or sensitivity rating for each asset (or asset category), which forms the basis for determining the appropriate level of access control granularity required by the policy. This demonstrates that the 'commensurate with risk' obligation is operationalised rather than stated in principle only.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all material IT assets — servers, applications, databases, network devices, cloud services — and assigns each a risk or sensitivity tier using a defined, documented methodology (e.g., based on data classification, business criticality, regulatory impact)
Each asset record explicitly maps to the access control requirements applicable to its risk tier, creating a traceable link from asset risk to policy granularity
Register is maintained as a living document with a named owner per asset or asset category, and shows evidence of periodic review (at minimum annually or upon material change)

High-risk assets (e.g., core banking, payment systems, customer data repositories) are clearly distinguished with more granular access control requirements such as MFA, session recording, just-in-time access, and privileged access management (PAM) integration
Register is reconciled with the access control policy's tiering framework — the tiers described in the policy correspond to named categories in the register
commonGaps: Asset register exists for IT asset management purposes but does not include a risk or sensitivity classification column, making it unusable as evidence that granularity is commensurate with risk
Classification methodology is undocumented or informal — assets are classified without a written criteria framework, so the classification cannot be validated or reproduced by an examiner
Cloud-hosted assets, SaaS platforms, and third-party-managed systems are excluded from the register, creating blind spots in the risk landscape that the access control policy ostensibly governs
Register has not been updated following material changes to the IT environment (e.g., new system go-lives, decommissions, or significant configuration changes), resulting in stale classifications

No traceability between the register and the access control policy tiers — the register uses different terminology or scales than the policy, making cross-referencing impossible without manual interpretation
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management (ITAM) system (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Lansweeper, SolarWinds)
Information Security team's data classification and risk register
GRC platform risk register module
Enterprise Architecture repository
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or system-generated report with version/export date stamp

evidenceItems.name: User Provisioning and Access Request Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of the formal process by which user identities are created (identification), credentials are issued (authentication provisioning), and access rights are granted (authorisation) for IT assets and data. These records demonstrate that the access control policy's identification, authentication, and authorisation requirements are operationally enforced at the point of user onboarding and access changes.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every access request record includes: the requestor, the data owner or system owner approval, the IT/security team fulfilment action, and a timestamp — creating a complete, auditable chain of custody for every access grant

Records demonstrate that authentication credentials (e.g., MFA enrolment, certificate issuance) are provisioned in accordance with the risk tier of the asset being accessed — higher-risk systems show MFA enrolment as a mandatory step before access is activated
Authorisation records reflect adherence to least-privilege and need-to-know principles, with role or entitlement assignments documented and justified rather than bulk-granted
Records are retained systematically in an ITSM or IAM system with immutable audit logging, not managed via email or spreadsheet

Joiner-mover-leaver (JML) records are complete, with leaver records showing timely access revocation and mover records showing both revocation of prior access and grant of new access within defined SLAs
commonGaps: Access requests are approved and fulfilled via informal email chains, with no structured workflow system capturing the approval decision and rationale — records cannot be systematically produced for examination

Authentication provisioning (e.g., MFA enrolment) is not captured as a distinct step in the access provisioning workflow, making it impossible to confirm that authentication controls were applied before access was activated

Authorisation records show generic role assignments (e.g., 'Standard User', 'Admin') without documentation of the specific entitlements within those roles, making least-privilege compliance unverifiable
Leaver records show delays of days or weeks between the HR termination date and IT access revocation, indicating that the JML process is not consistently enforced in line with policy
Records for contractor, third-party, and privileged (service account) access are managed in separate, ad-hoc processes and cannot be produced coherently alongside employee records
suggestedSources: ITSM platform access request workflows (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Identity and Access Management (IAM) system audit logs (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, SailPoint, Saviynt)
Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution provisioning records (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust)
HR system integration logs showing JML triggers
Active Directory or LDAP provisioning audit trail
evidenceItems.format: System-generated report or export (PDF/CSV) with immutable audit trail reference

evidenceItems.name: Privileged Access Management Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and system-generated evidence demonstrating that elevated or privileged access to high-risk IT assets is governed with a higher degree of granularity — including stricter identification, stronger authentication, and more tightly scoped authorisation — consistent with the risk-commensurate requirement of the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Privileged accounts (domain admins, database admins, system service accounts, network device administrators) are inventoried in a PAM solution with clearly documented justification for each account's existence and assigned entitlements

MFA or certificate-based authentication is enforced for all privileged access sessions, with PAM solution logs confirming that no session can be initiated without passing the stronger authentication gate

Just-in-time (JIT) or time-limited access is implemented for the most sensitive assets, with checkout logs showing that privileged access is granted on-demand and automatically revoked after session completion
Privileged sessions to critical systems are recorded (video or keystroke logging) and logs are stored in a tamper-evident repository accessible to the security operations team

Privileged account reviews are conducted at a frequency commensurate with risk (e.g., quarterly for Tier 1 critical systems) and review records show that orphaned or unnecessary privileged accounts are promptly removed
commonGaps: Privileged accounts are managed via a shared spreadsheet or team password vault without individual accountability — the institution cannot demonstrate that a specific named individual was responsible for a privileged action at a given time
Service accounts are excluded from PAM controls and use static, long-lived passwords that are rarely if ever rotated, contradicting the risk-commensurate granularity requirement for high-risk assets
MFA is applied to end-user remote access but not to privileged administrative access to core systems, creating an inverted risk profile where the most sensitive access has weaker authentication
No session recording is in place for privileged access to critical systems, making forensic investigation of potential insider threats or access misuse impossible after the fact
Privileged account reviews are conducted annually at best and do not differentiate review frequency by the risk level of the systems the accounts can access
suggestedSources: PAM solution console and reports (e.g., CyberArk Vault, BeyondTrust Password Safe, Delinea Secret Server)
SIEM platform privileged access alert rules and session logs (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel)
Active Directory privileged group membership reports
Network device access control lists and TACACS+/RADIUS logs
Cloud IAM privileged role assignment reports (e.g., AWS IAM Access Analyzer, Azure PIM)
evidenceItems.format: System-generated PAM reports (PDF/CSV) and session log exports with integrity hash

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Access Review and Recertification Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of formal, periodic access reviews (also called user access recertification or entitlement reviews) conducted by data or system owners to verify that all current users' access rights to IT assets remain appropriate, authorised, and least-privileged. These records demonstrate that the authorisation component of the access control policy is continuously enforced, not merely applied at provisioning.
whatGoodLooksLike: Access reviews are conducted at a cadence commensurate with the risk of the asset — critical and high-risk systems are reviewed quarterly or more frequently, while lower-risk systems are reviewed at minimum annually, with the cadence documented and justified in the access control policy

Review records capture each reviewer's decision for each entitlement (certify, revoke, or escalate for investigation), with a timestamp and, where access is revoked, evidence that the revocation was actioned in the IAM system within the defined SLA
Reviews cover all user categories — employees, contractors, third parties, and service accounts — without carve-outs or exemptions that are not risk-justified
Exceptions or deviations (e.g., access retained despite a reviewer flagging concern) are escalated through a formal risk acceptance process with named approver and documented rationale

Summary metrics from each review cycle (e.g., total accounts reviewed, percentage revoked, average time to revocation) are reported to senior management or a risk committee, demonstrating governance oversight
commonGaps: Access reviews are conducted as a one-time annual exercise with a uniform scope across all systems, with no differentiation in frequency or depth based on asset risk level — failing the commensurate-with-risk standard

Reviews are paper-based or conducted via email confirmation, with no system-generated evidence that the reviewer actually examined current entitlements versus a static list — the review is a rubber-stamp exercise

Revocations identified during access reviews are not actioned promptly — follow-up records show significant lag (weeks or months) between the review decision and actual removal of access in the source system

Service accounts and shared accounts are systematically excluded from access recertification on the grounds that they are 'not user accounts', leaving a large and often over-privileged population unreviewed
There is no escalation or risk acceptance process for access that cannot be revoked immediately — any access retained beyond the review period is undocumented and lacks a named accountable owner
suggestedSources: IAM governance platform recertification campaign reports (e.g., SailPoint IdentityNow, Saviynt, One Identity Manager)
GRC platform access review workflow records
Application-level user access reports exported at review dates and annotated with reviewer decisions
ITSM tickets raised for access revocations following review
Risk management committee or IT steering committee minutes referencing access review summary reports
evidenceItems.format: System-generated campaign report (PDF/CSV) and evidence of downstream revocation actions

evidenceItems.name: Authentication Standards and MFA Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation and system configuration evidence demonstrating that the institution has defined and implemented authentication standards that are appropriately strong — and risk-differentiated — across its IT assets, satisfying the authentication obligation within the access control policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Authentication standards document specifies minimum requirements by risk tier: for example, Tier 1 (critical) requires phishing-resistant MFA (hardware token or FIDO2), Tier 2 requires software OTP MFA, and Tier 3 requires at minimum strong password policy — all defined and consistently applied

System configuration evidence (e.g., Entra ID Conditional Access policy exports, IAM policy JSON, network access control configurations) confirms that MFA enforcement is technically mandated and cannot be bypassed by end users for in-scope asset tiers

Password policy technical configurations are exported and validated against the written standards — minimum length, complexity, history, and lockout thresholds match the documented policy and are enforced at the directory or system level, not merely advisory
Remote access authentication (VPN, remote desktop, cloud console) is demonstrably subject to stronger authentication requirements than internal network access, consistent with a risk-based approach

Authentication failure monitoring is implemented and evidence of alerting on threshold breaches (e.g., repeated failed logins, credential stuffing indicators) is available, demonstrating that authentication controls extend to detection as well as prevention
commonGaps: MFA is implemented for remote access but not enforced for access to core banking or critical internal applications from within the corporate network — a significant gap given that insider threat and lateral movement scenarios represent material risk

Authentication standards are documented at a high level (e.g., 'strong authentication shall be used') without specifying what 'strong' means technically, making compliance unverifiable and creating inconsistency across system implementations

Technical configuration exports cannot be produced to substantiate that password policies are enforced at the directory level — the institution can produce a policy document but not evidence that the settings are applied in Active Directory or the relevant IAM system

Legacy systems or third-party applications are granted MFA exemptions that are not risk-assessed, documented, or time-limited — these exemptions are typically discovered only during examination and represent significant compliance exposure
Session management controls (e.g., automatic timeout, concurrent session limits) are absent or inconsistently applied, undermining the authentication controls that establish the initial session
suggestedSources: Identity provider configuration exports (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access policies, Okta policy configuration, Ping Identity)
Active Directory Group Policy Object (GPO) reports for password and lockout policy
VPN and remote access gateway authentication configuration documentation
SIEM or UEBA platform authentication anomaly detection rule configuration
Cloud IAM MFA enforcement reports (AWS, Azure, GCP)
evidenceItems.format: System-generated configuration export (JSON/XML/PDF) and policy document (PDF/DOCX)

auditTips: Before examination, conduct a traceability walkthrough: take 3-5 high-risk IT assets from your asset register and manually trace each one from its risk classification → to the access control policy tier that applies → to actual system configurations → to current user access records → to the most recent access review. If this chain breaks at any point, you have a gap that an examiner will find.

Cross-validate your policy's stated granularity against operational reality by running a spot-check: extract the current user list and entitlements for at least one Tier 1 (critical) system and verify that every account has a corresponding approved access request, an MFA enrolment record, and has been reviewed within the required recertification period. Unexplained accounts or missing steps are immediate red flags.

Ensure your access control policy explicitly names the three pillars — identification, authentication, and authorisation — as distinct requirements using terminology consistent with the RMiT clause. Examiners will read the policy against the verbatim clause text; a policy that addresses only 'access management' generically without distinguishing these three obligations will be questioned.

Prepare a one-page risk-to-granularity mapping document as a stand-alone exhibit that explains, in plain language, how your institution determined that the level of control applied to each asset tier is commensurate with the risk. This narrative summary helps examiners quickly understand the logic and reduces the likelihood of a finding based on apparent rather than actual non-compliance.

Audit your non-human identity population separately: extract all service accounts, API keys, and system-to-system credentials and verify that each has a documented owner, a defined purpose, a credential rotation schedule, and is included in access reviews. This population is consistently under-governed and draws disproportionate examiner attention.

Reconcile your HR system's active employee and contractor list against your IAM system's active accounts at least two weeks before examination — any orphaned accounts (IAM active, HR terminated) must be investigated, documented, and remediated before examination day. Orphaned accounts are one of the most common and most cited findings under access control clauses.

Where legacy systems or applications cannot support the authentication standard required by their risk tier (e.g., cannot enforce MFA), ensure that each such system has a formally documented compensating control accepted through a risk acceptance process with a named approver, a defined review date, and a remediation plan. Examiners understand technical constraints but expect a disciplined risk management response — undocumented exceptions are treated as non-compliance.

10.54.title: Access Control Principles
10.54.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised all six sub-principles of clause 10.54 as a coherent, enforced framework rather than as paper-only policies. Examiners will request evidence that a default-deny posture is technically enforced at the directory and firewall level, not merely stated in policy, and will trace a sample of active user accounts to confirm permissions do not exceed role requirements. Red flags include any account with standing privileged access where time-bound or just-in-time controls are absent, SoD conflicts between development and operations or between security and system administration roles, critical systems lacking MFA for remote access, and shared or generic credentials that cannot be uniquely attributed to a single individual. Examiners typically cross-reference the access matrix against HR records to detect orphaned accounts, role creep from historical access accumulation, and SoD violations, and will probe whether dual-authorisation criteria are formally defined and technically enforced rather than applied ad hoc.

evidenceItems.name: Access Control Policy and Deny-All Configuration Records
evidenceItems.description: The Board- or senior management-approved Access Control Policy that formally mandates a deny-all default posture, supplemented by technical configuration exports (firewall rules, Active Directory/LDAP default group membership settings, cloud IAM policy baselines) demonstrating that access is blocked unless explicitly granted. This evidence demonstrates that sub-clause (a) is both documented and technically enforced.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly states 'deny all by default' and requires a formal access request and approval workflow before any permission is provisioned, with a version history showing periodic review (at least annually)
Technical exports from AD/LDAP, cloud IAM (e.g., AWS SCPs, Azure Policy), and network ACLs show no implicit allow rules and confirm that new accounts are created with zero permissions by default
Policy is cross-referenced to the institution's IT Asset Criticality Register so that stricter deny-all controls apply proportionately to critical systems
Change records in the ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) show that every access grant is traceable to an approved request ticket with documented business justification
Evidence of policy communication and attestation — staff (particularly IT administrators) have acknowledged the policy within the current review cycle
commonGaps: Policy states deny-all in principle but technical defaults (e.g., Everyone group with read access in AD, overly permissive default security groups in cloud environments) contradict the stated posture
Policy has not been updated following major infrastructure or cloud migration changes, creating a disconnect between documented controls and actual configurations
No evidence of access request tickets for legacy accounts provisioned before the ITSM tool was adopted, leaving a historical blind spot examiners will probe
Firewall rule exports show legacy 'permit any' or broad VLAN allow rules that were never rationalised, indicating deny-all was never fully implemented at the network layer
Policy scope excludes service accounts, application accounts, or API keys, leaving non-human identities outside the deny-all framework
suggestedSources: Information Security / Policy Management team — approved policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as RSA Archer or ServiceNow GRC)
Network Security team — firewall rule exports from Palo Alto, Fortinet, or equivalent NGFW management consoles
IAM / Directory Services team — Active Directory default group policy objects (GPOs), Azure AD/Entra ID baseline configurations, AWS SCPs
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy) — access request workflow configuration and audit log
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy), PDF exports of configuration screenshots, CSV/XLSX (firewall rule exports), ITSM workflow configuration screenshots

evidenceItems.name: Role-Based Access Matrix and Least-Privilege Review Records
evidenceItems.description: A formal Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) matrix mapping each job role to the minimum set of permissions required, combined with periodic user access review (UAR) records confirming that actual permissions do not exceed role entitlements. This evidence demonstrates compliance with sub-clause (b) — least privilege on a need-to-have basis.
whatGoodLooksLike: Role definitions are documented with explicit permission sets and are signed off by the relevant business owner and Information Security, showing that entitlements were determined based on documented job functions rather than convenience
UAR records show reviews conducted at least every six months for privileged accounts and annually for standard users, with line-manager or system-owner approval documented for each account
UAR exceptions (accounts where permissions exceed the role baseline) are formally logged, risk-accepted by an appropriate authority, and tracked to remediation closure

System exports (e.g., Active Directory group membership reports, Saviynt or SailPoint access certification reports) can be reconciled line-by-line against the role matrix with no unexplained discrepancies
Evidence that access accumulation ('role creep') from internal transfers is actively detected and remediated, with revocation records in the ITSM platform
commonGaps: Role definitions exist in the ITSM tool but have not been updated to reflect organisational restructuring, meaning the matrix no longer accurately represents current job functions
UAR is performed but limited to a checklist confirmation from managers who lack visibility into actual system permissions, resulting in rubber-stamp reviews with no real scrutiny
Privileged accounts (domain admins, DBAs, cloud root/owner accounts) are excluded from the standard UAR cycle or reviewed at a lower frequency than required
No reconciliation is performed between the HR joiner-mover-leaver process and IAM provisioning, leaving orphaned or over-entitled accounts for staff who have changed roles
Access reviews are conducted but remediation actions (revocations) are not tracked to completion, with open items remaining unresolved beyond the agreed SLA
suggestedSources: IAM Governance team — RBAC matrix document and access certification reports from SailPoint IdentityNow, Saviynt, or equivalent IGA platform
HR system (SAP SuccessFactors, Workday) — joiner-mover-leaver event logs for cross-referencing against IAM provisioning records
System owners / Business unit heads — signed UAR sign-off sheets or approval records in the GRC platform
Active Directory / LDAP — group membership exports; Cloud IAM consoles (AWS IAM Access Analyzer, Azure AD Access Reviews) — certification reports
evidenceItems.format: XLSX/CSV (role matrix and access exports), PDF (signed UAR reports), GRC platform screenshots, IGA platform certification reports

evidenceItems.name: Time-Bound Access Provisioning and Expiry Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that time-limited access is technically enforced for temporary, project-based, vendor, and privileged access scenarios, including access request tickets with defined start and end dates, automated expiry configurations, and evidence of timely revocation upon expiry. This evidence demonstrates compliance with sub-clause (c).
whatGoodLooksLike: Access request tickets for temporary or privileged access include a mandatory end-date field, with the ITSM workflow configured to reject submissions lacking a defined access duration

Technical evidence (e.g., Privileged Access Management (PAM) tool configurations such as CyberArk, BeyondTrust, or Delinea) shows that privileged sessions are time-boxed and that standing privileged access is not permitted for critical systems
Automated expiry notifications and revocation logs confirm that access is systematically removed at the agreed end date without requiring manual intervention, with exceptions flagged and escalated

Vendor and third-party access records show that external accounts are provisioned with contractually-aligned time windows and that expired vendor accounts are deactivated within 24 hours of contract end
Management reporting demonstrates the percentage of access grants with defined expiry dates versus open-ended grants, with a trend toward increased time-bound provisioning over audit cycles
commonGaps: Time-bound access is policy-mandated but not technically enforced — expiry depends entirely on manual revocation, with no automated mechanism to disable accounts at the agreed end date
PAM tools are deployed for some privileged accounts but standing access remains for legacy or application service accounts that were excluded from the PAM scope
Vendor accounts provisioned during a project are not linked to contract end dates in the IAM system, persisting indefinitely after project completion
The ITSM access request form has an optional expiry date field that requestors routinely leave blank, and approvers do not enforce its completion
Revocation logs show accounts disabled days or weeks after the documented expiry date, indicating that the automated control has exceptions that are not being tracked or remediated
suggestedSources: PAM platform (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) — session policy configurations, access request records with expiry dates, and revocation audit logs
ITSM platform (ServiceNow) — access request tickets with start/end date fields and automated closure workflows
IAM / Directory Services team — AD account expiry attribute reports; cloud IAM temporary credential records (AWS STS, Azure PIM eligible role assignments)
Vendor Management / Procurement team — third-party access contracts and associated IAM provisioning records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/XLSX (access request tickets and expiry reports), PAM platform screenshots and exported audit logs, CSV (account expiry attribute exports)

evidenceItems.name: Segregation of Duties Matrix and Conflict Detection Records
evidenceItems.description: A formally documented SoD matrix covering the four incompatible function pairs identified in sub-clause (d) — system development vs. technology operations, security administration vs. system administration, network operation vs. network security, and IT operations environment roles — supplemented by automated conflict detection reports and records of SoD violation remediation.
whatGoodLooksLike: The SoD matrix explicitly maps the four incompatible function pairs listed in 10.54(d) to specific system roles, AD groups, and application entitlements, and is approved by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and Internal Audit

Automated SoD conflict detection reports from the IGA platform or GRC tool are generated at least quarterly, showing the number of conflicts detected, the risk-acceptance decisions, and compensating controls applied

Where SoD conflicts cannot be eliminated (e.g., in small IT teams), formal risk acceptance with documented compensating controls (enhanced logging, independent review) is evidenced and approved at an appropriate seniority level
Change management records confirm that developers are restricted from deploying to production environments without a separate change approver from operations, with no exceptions in the sample tested

Personnel records cross-referenced against system entitlements confirm that individuals do not hold conflicting role combinations, with any exceptions tied to approved risk acceptance or compensating controls
commonGaps: The SoD matrix covers application-level roles but does not extend to underlying infrastructure roles (e.g., server administrator access that could circumvent application-layer controls), leaving a material gap

Developers have been granted read or execute access to production environments under the justification of 'incident support', but this access is standing rather than time-bound, violating both SoD and sub-clause (c)

SoD conflicts are identified in automated reports but the remediation workflow is unclear — conflicts remain open for extended periods without formal risk acceptance or compensating controls documentation
The security administration role is held by the same individual who administers the SIEM and log management platform, creating a conflict where security events could be suppressed without detection

Network operation and network security functions are both handled by a single small team with no compensating controls or independent monitoring in place, and no risk acceptance has been formally documented
suggestedSources: IAM Governance / Information Security team — SoD matrix document (SharePoint or GRC platform) and IGA platform conflict reports (SailPoint, Saviynt)
Change Management team (ITSM platform) — change approval records showing separation between requestor and approver for production deployments
Internal Audit team — SoD review reports and management response records
HR / Organisational chart — reporting line data to confirm functional independence of roles assessed for SoD
evidenceItems.format: XLSX (SoD matrix), PDF (IGA platform conflict detection reports, Internal Audit findings), ITSM change records (PDF/screenshots)

evidenceItems.name: Dual-Authorisation Criteria and Enforcement Records
evidenceItems.description: A formally defined list of activity types requiring dual authorisation, the technical or procedural controls that enforce the four-eyes requirement, and a sample of transaction or change records demonstrating that dual authorisation was obtained prior to execution. This evidence demonstrates compliance with sub-clause (e).
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented and approved schedule of activities requiring dual authorisation — including examples such as privileged account creation, firewall rule changes, production database modifications, cryptographic key operations, and wire transfers — with rationale for each category

Technical enforcement is evidenced (e.g., change management workflows in ServiceNow requiring two distinct approvers, PAM platform requiring a second approver for session checkout, or payment systems with maker-checker logic) rather than relying solely on manual procedure

A sample of dual-authorisation records (change tickets, transaction logs) confirms that two distinct, appropriately authorised individuals approved each action, with timestamps showing sequential approval rather than simultaneous or post-hoc sign-off
The criteria are reviewed at least annually and updated to reflect new technology deployments or changes in operational risk, with version-controlled approval records
Exceptions to dual-authorisation (e.g., emergency break-glass scenarios) are formally defined with compensating controls, logged, and subject to independent post-event review
commonGaps: The institution has not formally documented which activities require dual authorisation — the control is applied inconsistently based on individual judgement rather than a defined and approved criteria list
Dual authorisation is required in policy but the technical workflow permits a single approver in certain system configurations, or self-approval is technically possible for senior administrators
Break-glass emergency access is used frequently and without post-event review, effectively bypassing dual authorisation for a significant volume of high-risk activities
Dual authorisation records exist for IT change management but are absent for administrative activities such as privileged account creation, bulk data exports, or cryptographic key ceremonies
Approvers in the dual-authorisation workflow are not independent of the requestor — they are from the same team or report to the same manager, undermining the control's effectiveness
suggestedSources: Information Security / IT Governance team — dual-authorisation criteria document (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform)
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy) — change approval workflow configuration and change records with multi-approver audit trails
PAM platform (CyberArk, BeyondTrust) — access checkout approval logs and break-glass session records
Core banking / payment system — maker-checker transaction logs; Treasury/Finance systems with dual-control payment release records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (criteria document), ITSM workflow configuration screenshots, PDF exports of change and transaction approval records

evidenceItems.name: Authentication Strength and Unique Credential Assurance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that authentication mechanisms are calibrated to the criticality of IT assets and risk profile of access scenarios, including MFA enforcement records for critical systems and remote access, identity verification records for credential provisioning, and technical controls ensuring credentials are uniquely attributable to a single individual. This evidence demonstrates compliance with sub-clause (f).
whatGoodLooksLike: An IT Asset Criticality Register or authentication standards document maps asset criticality tiers to required authentication strength (e.g., Tier 1 critical systems require hardware token or FIDO2 MFA; remote access requires certificate + OTP), with configurations confirmed in the identity provider (Azure AD Conditional Access, Okta policies, or equivalent)

MFA enforcement reports from the identity provider show the percentage of accounts with MFA enabled by system and user type, with privileged and remote access accounts at or near 100% coverage, supported by conditional access policy exports

Identity verification records for user onboarding (e.g., HR-verified identity document checks, out-of-band credential delivery logs) demonstrate that credentials are issued to verified individuals with documented proof, preventing impersonation at provisioning

Technical controls preventing shared credentials are evidenced — e.g., PAM vaulting all shared service account passwords, directory policy enforcing unique UPNs, SIEM alert rules detecting concurrent logins from multiple geographies on a single account

Remote access VPN and privileged access logs confirm that all remote sessions are authenticated with at least two factors, with a sampling of session logs attributable to uniquely identified individuals without shared or generic account usage
commonGaps: MFA is enforced for external-facing systems but not for internal critical infrastructure (core banking, SWIFT, HSM management consoles) on the assumption that network perimeter controls are sufficient, contradicting sub-clause (f)(i)
Some legacy systems cannot support MFA and no compensating controls (privileged access gateways, session recording, network-layer restrictions) have been formally documented and risk-accepted

Generic or shared accounts (e.g., 'admin', 'sysadmin', 'dba') remain active on critical systems with no PAM vaulting, making it impossible to attribute actions to a specific individual in violation of sub-clause (f)(iii)

Identity verification at credential provisioning relies on manager confirmation via email rather than HR-verified identity documentation, providing insufficient assurance against impersonation risk as required by sub-clause (f)(ii)

Remote access is enforced via VPN with username and password only — MFA has been planned but not implemented, or is enforced only for specific user groups (e.g., executives) rather than all remote users
suggestedSources: IAM / Identity Provider team — Azure AD/Entra ID Conditional Access policy exports, Okta MFA enforcement reports, or on-premises RADIUS configuration for VPN MFA
IT Security Operations — SIEM (Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) alerts for concurrent session anomalies and shared credential usage
HR / Onboarding team — identity verification checklists and credential issuance records from the joiner process
PAM platform (CyberArk, BeyondTrust) — shared account vaulting configuration, session recording inventory, and credential checkout logs
Network / Remote Access team — VPN gateway logs (Cisco ASA, Palo Alto GlobalProtect) confirming MFA challenge and response records for remote sessions
evidenceItems.format: PDF (policy and standards documents), Identity provider policy exports (JSON/PDF), MFA coverage reports (XLSX/PDF), SIEM alert configuration screenshots, PAM platform configuration exports
