auditTips: Before the examination, generate a live access rights report for all critical systems and reconcile it against the approved RBAC matrix and HR records — examiners will request this on day one, and unexplained discrepancies (orphaned accounts, entitlements exceeding role definitions, active accounts for departed staff) are among the most common findings under this clause.

Prepare a single consolidated evidence pack that maps each of the six sub-clauses (a) through (f) to specific documents and technical artefacts — examiners assess all six sub-principles, and institutions that address only deny-all and MFA while neglecting time-bound access or dual-authorisation criteria will receive findings on the unaddressed sub-clauses.

Run a targeted SoD conflict report covering the four specific incompatible function pairs listed in 10.54(d) and document the remediation status of each conflict — examiners are familiar with these exact pairs and will request evidence for each one; an undocumented conflict is treated as a finding even if compensating controls exist informally.

For any legacy system that cannot technically enforce deny-all, time-bound access, or MFA, prepare a formal risk acceptance signed by an appropriate authority (typically CISO or CTO level) along with documented compensating controls — examiners will accept documented and approved exceptions far more readily than undocumented gaps discovered during fieldwork.

Conduct a targeted sample test before the examination: select five to ten recent privileged access grants and trace each one end-to-end through the access request ticket, approval record, provisioning log, and (where applicable) revocation record — if this chain is broken for any sample, remediate before the examination because examiners perform exactly this walkthrough.

Verify that dual-authorisation controls are technically enforced rather than procedurally dependent — pull a sample of changes or transactions from the past three months and confirm that two distinct approver identities appear in each record, including confirming that the approvers are genuinely independent of the requestor; self-approval enabled by elevated permissions is a critical finding.

Cross-reference the authentication standards document against the IT Asset Criticality Register and generate a gap analysis showing which critical systems currently lack MFA for remote access — present this analysis with a time-bound remediation roadmap, as examiners view a documented and actively managed gap far more favourably than an undiscovered one, particularly for a Standard (S) marker clause.

10.55.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
10.55.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on three core obligations: whether MFA is deployed across all critical systems without exception, whether the chosen MFA solution can credibly defend against social engineering attacks (particularly phishing and SIM-swap attacks), and whether the authentication factors genuinely span two distinct categories from the knowledge/inherent/possession taxonomy. A major red flag is institutions that treat SMS OTP as a possession factor when it is susceptible to SIM-swapping and SS7 interception — examiners will probe whether the institution has assessed this risk. Examiners also look for scope gaps where MFA is applied to front-end portals but absent from backend administrative consoles, privileged access workstations, or API access paths. The 'S' marker means non-compliance cannot be offset by compensating controls — examiners will expect full implementation, not a roadmap.

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems Inventory with MFA Coverage Mapping
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register of all systems classified as 'critical' under the institution's IT asset classification framework, cross-referenced against the MFA mechanism deployed for each system. This demonstrates that the institution has formally scoped all critical systems and can account for MFA coverage with no gaps.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each critical system entry explicitly states the MFA method deployed (e.g., FIDO2 hardware key, biometric + PIN) and maps it to the knowledge/inherent/possession factor taxonomy
The inventory is dated, version-controlled, and reviewed at least annually with a named system owner approving each entry
Systems without MFA are flagged with a formal risk acceptance signed by CISO and approved by board-level risk committee, with a remediation target date
The register distinguishes between user-facing access paths and administrative/privileged access paths, with MFA coverage confirmed for both
The inventory links each system's criticality rating back to the institution's IT asset classification policy, providing a defensible basis for scope
commonGaps: Critical systems list is maintained by IT Operations but not reconciled with the Risk or Business Continuity team's critical system definitions, creating scope inconsistencies
Backend administrative consoles, database management interfaces, and CI/CD pipelines are omitted from scope, leaving privileged access paths unprotected
MFA column records the vendor product name (e.g., 'RSA SecurID') without specifying which factor types are combined, making taxonomy mapping impossible for auditors
The inventory has not been updated following recent system acquisitions, cloud migrations, or third-party integrations
No evidence of periodic review sign-off — the document exists but shows no governance trail of it being validated or challenged
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — IT asset register with criticality classifications
CMDB (Configuration Management Database) — authoritative system inventory
Information Security team — access control matrix and IAM platform (e.g., CyberArk, SailPoint) system onboarding records
IT Risk register for any formal risk acceptances related to MFA gaps
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export, with version history

evidenceItems.name: MFA Solution Architecture and Anti-Social-Engineering Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation describing the MFA architecture deployed, accompanied by a formal security assessment that evaluates the solution's resistance to social engineering attacks including phishing, vishing, SIM-swapping, MFA fatigue (push bombing), and adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) attacks. This is the primary evidence that the institution has satisfied the specific 'defend against social engineering attacks' obligation in the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment explicitly names and evaluates each relevant social engineering attack vector (phishing, SIM-swap, push fatigue, AiTM) and explains how the deployed MFA mechanism mitigates each

Phishing-resistant MFA (e.g., FIDO2/WebAuthn, certificate-based authentication) is used for the highest-risk systems, with documented rationale for why less phishing-resistant methods are used elsewhere
The assessment is authored or reviewed by a qualified security practitioner (e.g., CISSP, CISM) and endorsed by the CISO
SMS OTP usage, if present, is explicitly acknowledged as a weaker factor with a documented compensating control strategy and a time-bound migration plan to a stronger mechanism
The architecture diagram shows end-to-end authentication flows including fallback/recovery procedures, demonstrating that bypass paths do not circumvent MFA
commonGaps: The institution has deployed MFA but has no documented assessment of its social engineering resistance — the clause obligation is partially unmet even if MFA is technically present
SMS OTP is classified as a possession factor without any acknowledgement of SIM-swap or SS7 vulnerabilities, indicating the institution has not assessed the attack surface
MFA push notifications are enabled without number matching or additional context, leaving the deployment vulnerable to MFA fatigue attacks with no documented awareness of this risk
Architecture documentation describes the solution at a conceptual level but omits fallback authentication flows, which are often the weakest link exploited by social engineering
The assessment is performed by the vendor rather than an independent party, reducing credibility for examination purposes
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity Architecture team — solution design documents
Vendor technical documentation and security whitepapers for the deployed MFA product
Internal risk assessment records from the IAM or Identity team
Third-party penetration test reports or red team assessments covering authentication mechanisms
SharePoint / document management system under the Cybersecurity or Technology Risk folder
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — formal security assessment report with architecture diagrams

evidenceItems.name: MFA Policy and Procedure Documentation
evidenceItems.description: The institution's formal MFA policy stating the mandatory requirement for MFA on critical systems, the approved factor types and combinations, approved MFA technologies, and the procedures governing enrolment, management, exception handling, and recovery. This establishes the governance framework that the technical implementation is built upon.
whatGoodLooksLike: The policy explicitly references BNM RMiT clause 10.55 or equivalent regulatory obligation and uses the knowledge/inherent/possession factor taxonomy
Approved and prohibited authentication methods are explicitly listed — e.g., FIDO2 hardware keys and biometrics are approved; standalone password is prohibited for critical systems
The policy defines 'critical systems' by reference to the institution's asset classification framework, ensuring the scope is deterministic and auditable
Exception and waiver procedures require CISO approval, are time-limited (no open-ended exceptions), and mandate compensating controls
The policy shows a clear review and approval trail — version history, review date, approver names and designations, and board or risk committee endorsement
commonGaps: The policy permits SMS OTP as a fully acceptable possession factor with no caveats, contradicting the social engineering resilience requirement without any risk acknowledgement
Exception waivers are approved informally via email with no central register, making it impossible to demonstrate how many systems are operating outside policy
The policy does not address privileged or administrative accounts separately from standard user accounts, leaving a governance gap for the highest-risk access
Recovery and self-service enrolment procedures are undocumented, meaning the security of the bypass path is ungoverned
The policy has not been reviewed or updated in more than 12 months, predating major cloud migrations or system changes that altered the critical system landscape
suggestedSources: Information Security Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform)
Board Risk Committee or ITSC (IT Steering Committee) meeting minutes evidencing policy approval
HR / Compliance shared drive for the approved policy library
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — formal policy document with version control and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: MFA Enrolment and Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: System-generated records demonstrating that MFA is actively enforced across all in-scope critical systems, including enrolment rates, enforcement configurations, and any accounts operating without MFA. This provides empirical proof that the policy is operationally implemented rather than aspirational.
whatGoodLooksLike: IAM platform or MFA solution dashboard export showing 100% MFA enrolment for all user accounts with access to critical systems, or a clear reconciliation for any exceptions

Conditional access or authentication policy configurations (e.g., Azure AD Conditional Access, Okta policies) are exported and demonstrate that MFA is enforced — not just offered — for critical system access

The report distinguishes between human user accounts and service accounts, with service accounts addressed through separate compensating controls (certificates, managed identities) rather than MFA bypass
Periodic recertification records show that dormant or orphaned accounts without valid MFA enrolment are identified and deprovisioned
Configuration evidence shows that MFA cannot be bypassed via legacy authentication protocols (e.g., SMTP AUTH, basic auth) which are either disabled or blocked for critical system access
commonGaps: MFA enrolment reports show high but not complete coverage — the remaining unenrolled accounts (often service accounts or legacy application accounts) have no documented justification or compensating control
Conditional access policies have broad emergency-access exclusions or named account exclusions that effectively create standing MFA bypass paths
Legacy authentication protocols remain enabled in critical systems, allowing MFA to be bypassed by clients that use basic authentication
Enrolment data is a point-in-time snapshot with no evidence of ongoing monitoring or alerting for accounts that lose their MFA enrolment (e.g., after device loss)
Service accounts are included in user MFA reports as 'compliant' but are actually using shared credentials without any second factor, artificially inflating compliance percentages
suggestedSources: IAM platform reports (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID / Azure AD, Okta, Ping Identity) — MFA registration and enforcement status exports
Privileged Access Management (PAM) platform (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust) — privileged account MFA enforcement settings
SIEM (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk) — authentication event logs showing MFA challenge and completion events
Active Directory / LDAP — group membership and account attribute exports
Cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools for cloud-hosted critical systems
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet/CSV export from IAM platform, supplemented by configuration screenshots or policy exports

evidenceItems.name: MFA Exception Register and Waiver Records
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register of all formally approved exceptions to the MFA requirement, including the rationale, risk acceptance, compensating controls, approver details, and expiry date for each exception. This demonstrates that governance exists around deviations and that the institution maintains situational awareness of its MFA coverage gaps.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every exception entry includes: the system or account name, the specific MFA requirement being excepted, the business justification, the compensating controls in place, the risk acceptance approver (CISO or above), and a defined expiry date
The register is reviewed quarterly with evidence of review (dated sign-off), and expired exceptions are either renewed with fresh approval or the gap is remediated
Compensating controls for exceptions are specific and verifiable (e.g., network access restriction to a named IP range, session recording enabled in PAM) rather than generic statements
The total count of open exceptions is reported to a risk committee or equivalent governance body on a regular basis, evidenced by board pack or committee minutes
The register shows a trend of exceptions being closed over time, demonstrating active remediation rather than indefinite deferral
commonGaps: No centralised exception register exists — exceptions are tracked informally in emails or individual team logs, making aggregated risk visibility impossible
Exceptions lack expiry dates, effectively becoming permanent waivers that are never reviewed or challenged
Compensating controls stated in the exception record are not verified as actually implemented — they exist on paper only
The exception register has not been reported to a risk governance body, meaning senior management and the board are unaware of the institution's true MFA compliance posture
Legacy system exceptions that were granted temporarily several years ago remain open with no remediation progress, indicating a systemic failure to drive resolution
suggestedSources: GRC platform exception/waiver module (e.g., ServiceNow Risk, Archer, LogicGate)
Information Security team — risk acceptance log maintained by the CISO office
Risk committee meeting packs and minutes evidencing exception reporting
IT Risk register maintained by Technology Risk function
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export, with linked approval records

evidenceItems.name: MFA Audit Logs and Authentication Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Log evidence and monitoring records demonstrating that MFA events (successful authentications, failed challenges, bypass attempts, and fallback activations) are captured, retained, and actively monitored for anomalies. This evidences operational continuity of the control and the institution's capacity to detect MFA bypass or abuse.
whatGoodLooksLike: Authentication logs from critical systems capture MFA challenge and result events (success/failure) with sufficient detail: timestamp, user identity, source IP, device, system accessed, and factor type used

SIEM rules or use cases are configured to alert on MFA anomalies including: repeated MFA failures, successful authentication after multiple MFA rejections (potential push-fatigue acceptance), authentication from unusual geographies, and MFA bypass via legacy protocol
Log retention configurations demonstrate that authentication logs are retained for at least 7 years (or as per BNM retention requirements) in a tamper-evident store
Evidence of periodic log review or automated alerting exists — for example, SIEM alert tickets or SOC analyst reports referencing MFA-related detections
Incident response records show at least one example of an MFA anomaly being investigated and resolved, demonstrating that the monitoring capability is operationally effective
commonGaps: MFA logs are generated by the identity provider but not forwarded to the SIEM, creating a detection blind spot for MFA-related threats
Logs capture authentication success/failure but do not record which factor type was used or whether MFA was challenged, making it impossible to detect silent MFA bypass
Log retention is set to 90 days or less in the identity platform, meaning historical evidence is unavailable for examinations or incident investigations
No SIEM use cases or alerting rules are defined for MFA abuse patterns — logs exist but are not actively monitored, reducing the control to a detective artefact rather than an active defence
Cloud-hosted critical system authentication logs are stored in the cloud provider's default log service without export to the institution's centralised SIEM, creating siloed visibility
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) — authentication log dashboards and alert configurations
Identity provider audit logs (e.g., Microsoft Entra Sign-in logs, Okta System Log, Ping audit trail)
SOC (Security Operations Centre) — alert runbooks and incident tickets related to authentication anomalies
Log management platform — log retention policy configuration and storage evidence
PAM platform session logs for privileged access MFA events
evidenceItems.format: SIEM dashboard export (PDF/screenshot), log configuration evidence, and sample alert tickets

auditTips: Before the examination, run a reconciliation between the institution's critical system inventory (as defined by business continuity or IT risk) and the IAM platform's MFA enforcement configurations — discrepancies between these two sources are the most common finding, where systems appear critical in one register but are not treated as such in access controls.

Specifically test whether the MFA implementation is phishing-resistant for the highest-risk systems (core banking, treasury platforms, privileged admin consoles). Pull the MFA solution's technical specification and map it explicitly to known attack vectors — examiners trained on NIST SP 800-63B or equivalent will ask whether the deployment meets 'verifier impersonation resistance' standards, and institutions that cannot answer this question clearly will face scrutiny.

Audit the fallback and account recovery procedures as thoroughly as the primary MFA mechanism — social engineering attacks frequently target recovery paths (helpdesk reset calls, backup codes, email-based recovery) rather than the primary factor. Obtain the helpdesk identity verification procedure and assess whether it can be bypassed by a caller armed with publicly available information.

Prepare a clean, signed exception register before the examination and brief the CISO on every open exception — examiners will ask for this document and will cross-reference it against the IAM enrolment report to check for undisclosed gaps. An institution that proactively presents a complete exception register with time-bound remediation plans demonstrates mature governance; one that cannot produce it signals control weakness.

Cross-reference MFA enforcement configurations against all access paths to each critical system, not just the primary portal. Check whether administrative interfaces, API endpoints, VPN access, jump-server access, and out-of-band management networks also enforce MFA — examiners specifically probe whether alternative access routes bypass the primary MFA control.

Obtain a sample of authentication logs from the SIEM or identity provider covering the past 3 months and verify that MFA challenge events are visible, distinguishable from password-only events, and that the SOC has defined at least one active alert for MFA anomalies. Examiners will ask to see both the logs and the detection capability — having logs with no monitoring is insufficient.

Validate that the MFA policy and the critical systems inventory have both been reviewed within the past 12 months and that review evidence is documented. The 'S' marker on this clause means examiners will look for a living governance process, not a one-time implementation — stale policy documents or inventories that predate major technology changes are a reliable red flag that will attract follow-up questions.

10.56.title: User Access Matrix
10.56.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a formally documented user access matrix that is comprehensive, current, and actively governed — not merely a static artefact. Examiners will cross-check the access matrix against live system configurations to verify that documented roles and entitlements reflect actual provisioned access, flagging discrepancies as evidence of inadequate controls. A key red flag is an access matrix that has not been reviewed within the institution's stated review cycle, or where review sign-offs lack clear evidence of authorising and approving authority involvement. Examiners also scrutinise whether the matrix captures all systems in scope — including privileged accounts, shared accounts, and third-party access — and whether role definitions enforce least-privilege principles. Institutions that conflate the access matrix with an unstructured spreadsheet lacking role-to-system mapping, or that cannot demonstrate a formal periodic review process with audit trails, are considered non-compliant with the structural and governance obligations of this clause.

evidenceItems.name: User Access Matrix Document
evidenceItems.description: The primary access matrix artefact that maps each user role or profile to specific systems, applications, and data assets with the corresponding access rights (read, write, execute, admin, etc.) and identifies the designated authorising and approving authorities for each role. This document demonstrates the institution has formalised the structure and scope of access entitlements across its technology environment.
whatGoodLooksLike: The matrix covers all in-scope systems, applications, databases, and infrastructure components, with no critical systems omitted, and explicitly marks privileged and shared accounts as distinct categories
Each row or entry maps a named role or profile to specific entitlements using a defined classification (e.g., Read, Write, Execute, Admin, No Access) rather than vague descriptors
Authorising authority (role owner or business unit head) and approving authority (IT Security or CISO function) are explicitly named or titled for each role in the matrix
The document carries version control metadata (version number, effective date, last review date, document owner) and is maintained under a formal document management system
The matrix is granular enough to distinguish between roles at the application function level, not just broad system-level access, enabling meaningful least-privilege assessment
commonGaps: The matrix covers only selected applications and omits critical infrastructure components such as databases, network devices, cloud platforms, or privileged access management systems

Access rights are described ambiguously (e.g., 'standard user', 'full access') without mapping to specific system permissions or function-level entitlements that can be validated against live configurations

Authorising and approving authorities are either absent, listed as generic department names rather than specific roles, or the same individual is recorded for both functions — undermining segregation of duties
The document lacks version history, making it impossible to demonstrate what changed between review cycles or to reconstruct entitlements at a point in time for forensic or audit purposes

Privileged accounts (domain admins, DBA accounts, service accounts) and shared/generic accounts are not differentiated from standard user roles, obscuring the risk profile of high-impact access entitlements
suggestedSources: IT Security or Identity and Access Management (IAM) team documentation repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) access governance module
Enterprise IAM or Identity Governance and Administration (IGA) platform (e.g., SailPoint, Saviynt, Microsoft Entra ID Governance)
CISO or IT Risk function document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (approved policy document) or Spreadsheet (XLSX) with version control metadata; IGA platform export acceptable as supplementary evidence

evidenceItems.name: Role Definition and Authorisation Register
evidenceItems.description: A register or catalogue that formally defines each user role or profile referenced in the access matrix, including the business justification for the role, the business owner accountable for the role, the IT owner responsible for technical provisioning, and the approval authority. This artefact demonstrates that roles are deliberately designed, owned, and authorised rather than organically accumulated over time.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every role in the access matrix has a corresponding entry in the role register with a unique role ID, plain-language business justification, and named business and IT owners
The register captures the date on which each role was formally authorised and by whom, providing a traceable approval lineage for every entitlement set in the matrix
Roles that have been deprecated or modified carry a change history log, demonstrating active lifecycle management rather than indefinite accumulation of legacy roles
The register distinguishes between standard roles, privileged roles, and emergency/break-glass roles, with elevated approval requirements documented for higher-risk categories
Role definitions are cross-referenced to segregation of duties (SoD) conflict rules, with documented compensating controls where conflicts are accepted
commonGaps: Role definitions exist only within the IAM system configuration and have never been translated into a human-readable, formally approved document that non-technical auditors or senior management can review
Business owners are not assigned to roles, meaning access entitlements have no accountable party outside of IT — a significant governance gap under BNM's principle of business ownership of risk
The register does not capture deprecated or dormant roles that remain provisioned in systems, leaving ghost entitlements unaccounted for in the access matrix
Emergency or break-glass roles are absent from the register entirely, suggesting they are managed ad hoc without formal authorisation or review
No SoD conflict analysis is linked to the role register, making it impossible to demonstrate that incompatible duties have been considered and controlled
suggestedSources: IAM or IGA platform role catalogue (SailPoint IdentityNow, Saviynt, Microsoft Entra ID, CyberArk)
IT Security team's access governance SharePoint or Confluence space
GRC platform access management module
HR system for business owner nomination records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC/IGA platform export; formal approval records in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Access Matrix Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence that the access matrix is reviewed at defined intervals, capturing the review activities performed, findings identified, actions taken, and sign-offs by both authorising and approving authorities. This artefact demonstrates that the matrix is a living control subject to ongoing governance, not a one-time compliance exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Review records exist for each review cycle within the past 12–24 months and align with the frequency committed to in the institution's access control policy (typically annual as a minimum, with more frequent reviews for high-privilege roles)

Each review record captures the scope reviewed (systems, roles, user population), the methodology used (e.g., role-by-role attestation, exception-based review), and the outcome including number of entitlements confirmed, modified, or revoked
Sign-offs are obtained from both the authorising authority (business/role owner) and the approving authority (IT Security or CISO) as separate, distinct approvals — not a single consolidated sign-off
Findings from the review (e.g., excessive entitlements, orphaned accounts, SoD conflicts) are tracked in an action log with assigned owners, remediation timelines, and closure evidence
The review cycle is driven by a formal schedule documented in policy or a governance calendar, demonstrating proactive rather than reactive compliance
commonGaps: Review records exist but show only a blanket 'no changes required' attestation with no evidence that individual roles or entitlements were actually examined — suggesting a tick-box exercise rather than substantive review

Business owners sign off on access reviews without documented evidence of how they verified the accuracy of entitlements assigned to their roles (e.g., no supporting user list or entitlement report attached)
The most recent review is outside the institution's stated review cycle, meaning the access matrix is operating on a lapsed governance schedule at the time of examination
Action items identified during prior reviews have not been tracked to closure, with overdue remediation items outstanding at the time of audit — indicating weak follow-through on review outcomes
Reviews only cover a subset of systems (e.g., core banking but not email, network infrastructure, or cloud platforms), leaving significant portions of the access matrix unreviewed
suggestedSources: IT Security or IAM team's review tracker (SharePoint, Jira, ServiceNow)
GRC platform's access review workflow and attestation records
Board IT Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes referencing access review outcomes
CISO or IT Risk governance calendar and review completion reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed review completion report) with supporting XLSX (reviewed entitlement extract); workflow system screenshots or exports as supplementary evidence

evidenceItems.name: Access Matrix Update and Change Log
evidenceItems.description: A historical record of all updates made to the access matrix since its establishment, capturing what changed, why the change was made, who requested and authorised the change, and when it was implemented. This artefact demonstrates that the matrix is kept current through a controlled change process and that updates do not bypass the authorising and approving authority framework.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every material change to the access matrix (new role creation, role modification, role retirement, entitlement scope change) is logged with a unique change reference, timestamp, requester, and approval chain aligned to the matrix's authorisation structure
Changes are linked to corresponding IT change requests or access request tickets in the ITSM platform, enabling end-to-end traceability from business request to matrix update to system provisioning
Emergency or out-of-cycle changes are identifiable in the log and carry evidence of expedited approval and post-change review, demonstrating that urgency does not bypass governance
The log is maintained in a system that prevents retrospective alteration (e.g., GRC platform, ITSM with audit trail), providing an immutable record of the matrix's evolution
Version numbers in the access matrix document correspond directly to entries in the change log, enabling examiners to reconstruct the access matrix at any historical point
commonGaps: Changes to the access matrix are made directly in the document without a corresponding formal change request or approval trail, making it impossible to distinguish authorised updates from unauthorised modifications
The change log is maintained informally in email chains or chat messages rather than a controlled system, creating gaps and inconsistencies in the audit trail
Role or entitlement changes implemented in the live system are not reflected in the access matrix document, resulting in divergence between the documented matrix and actual provisioned access
Emergency access changes made outside the standard change process are not captured in the change log or retroactively approved, leaving a governance gap for high-risk out-of-band modifications
The change log does not capture role retirements, meaning the matrix accumulates obsolete roles over time and overstates the institution's active entitlement footprint
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy, Jira Service Management)
IAM or IGA platform provisioning workflow audit trail
Document version history in SharePoint or Confluence
GRC platform access governance change records
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) or structured change log spreadsheet (XLSX); document version history extract

evidenceItems.name: Authorising and Approving Authority Delegation Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A documented framework that specifies which individuals or roles hold authorising authority (business justification approval) and approving authority (IT Security/risk sign-off) for granting, modifying, or revoking access rights across different systems and risk tiers. This artefact demonstrates that the access governance structure mandated in clause 10.56 is formally defined, delegated appropriately, and enforced consistently.
whatGoodLooksLike: The delegation matrix explicitly separates authorising authority (typically the business unit head or data owner) from approving authority (IT Security, CISO, or designated risk function), with no individual holding both authorities for the same access request
Approval tiers are risk-differentiated, with standard user access requiring lower-tier approval and privileged or sensitive access requiring elevated authorisation (e.g., CISO or CTO sign-off)
Delegation of authority is formally documented and signed, with clear succession arrangements for when named individuals are unavailable — preventing access provisioning delays or governance bypasses
The framework is embedded in the access request workflow in the ITSM or IAM platform, so approvals are system-enforced rather than dependent on manual compliance
The authority matrix is reviewed annually or whenever organisational restructuring occurs, ensuring that named authorities remain current and relevant
commonGaps: Authorising and approving authorities are not formally separated — the same individual approves both the business need and the technical provisioning, undermining the dual-control principle implicit in the clause
The delegation matrix exists for standard user access but does not address privileged access, third-party access, or emergency access — leaving high-risk categories without defined governance
Named authorities in the delegation matrix are outdated due to staff turnover or restructuring, meaning live approvals are being obtained from individuals who no longer hold the designated authority

The delegation framework is documented in policy but not enforced in the ITSM or IAM workflow, allowing access to be provisioned with informal approvals that do not match the documented authority structure
There is no documented escalation path for access requests that are denied at one authority level, leaving it unclear how contested or borderline requests are resolved
suggestedSources: IT Security or IAM team's governance policy documents (SharePoint)
ITSM platform approval workflow configuration (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
HR system for authority delegation and organisation structure records
CISO or IT Risk function's authority and delegation register
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal policy or procedure document) with supporting XLSX (delegation matrix table); ITSM workflow configuration screenshots as supplementary evidence

evidenceItems.name: Access Matrix vs. Live System Reconciliation Report
evidenceItems.description: A periodic reconciliation report that compares the access rights documented in the access matrix against the entitlements actually provisioned in live systems, identifying discrepancies such as access granted beyond the matrix definition, access not yet provisioned, or access for roles that no longer exist in the matrix. This artefact demonstrates that the access matrix is not only documented but operationally enforced.
whatGoodLooksLike: The reconciliation is performed at least annually (or more frequently for high-risk or privileged systems) and covers all systems in the access matrix scope without exclusions
The report quantifies discrepancies (number of accounts with excess access, number of orphaned accounts, number of active accounts with no corresponding matrix entry) and classifies them by severity
Discrepancies identified in the report are tracked to remediation with assigned owners and target closure dates, and follow-up reports show closure rates from prior cycles
The reconciliation methodology is documented and repeatable, using automated exports from IAM/IGA platforms rather than manual sampling — reducing the risk of incomplete or biased results
The report is reviewed and signed off by IT Security and relevant business owners, demonstrating accountability for both the findings and the remediation commitments
commonGaps: No formal reconciliation process exists between the access matrix document and live system entitlements — the institution assumes that provisioning controls prevent divergence without verifying this assumption periodically
Reconciliation is performed only for selected high-profile systems and excludes legacy applications, development environments, or cloud platforms where entitlement drift is often most pronounced
The reconciliation report identifies discrepancies but lacks a remediation tracker, meaning findings are documented without accountability for resolution — a significant control weakness
Reconciliation relies on manual sampling rather than a full population extract, making it statistically insufficient to provide assurance over the entire access matrix scope

Prior reconciliation reports show recurring discrepancies of the same type with no evidence of root cause analysis or systemic remediation — indicating the control is not effectively preventing entitlement drift
suggestedSources: IAM or IGA platform automated access certification reports (SailPoint, Saviynt, Entra ID Access Reviews)
Privileged Access Management (PAM) platform account discovery reports (CyberArk, BeyondTrust)
IT Security team's quarterly or annual access review outputs
GRC platform access risk and compliance reporting module
Active Directory, LDAP, or directory service account exports cross-referenced against the matrix
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed reconciliation report) with supporting XLSX (discrepancy register and remediation tracker); IGA platform certification completion report

auditTips: Before the examination, perform a live reconciliation between the access matrix document and actual provisioned entitlements in at least three critical systems (e.g., core banking, email, Active Directory) — BNM examiners routinely request system exports on the spot and compare them to the matrix, and unexplained discrepancies are treated as evidence of inadequate control.

Compile a single consolidated evidence pack that traces the full access governance lifecycle for one representative role: from the initial role definition and authorisation, through the access matrix entry, to the provisioning record in the ITSM system, to the most recent periodic review sign-off — this end-to-end traceability narrative directly addresses all obligations in clause 10.56 in a single logical sequence.

Verify that the most recent access matrix review falls within the review cycle committed to in the institution's access control policy, and that both the authorising authority (business/role owner) and the approving authority (IT Security or CISO) have provided separate, documented sign-offs — a single consolidated sign-off from IT alone is a common finding that examiners flag as a governance gap.

Ensure that privileged accounts, service accounts, shared accounts, and third-party vendor accounts are explicitly included in the access matrix and carry their own distinct role definitions — examiners specifically probe whether the matrix covers the full account population or only standard named user accounts, and omissions for high-risk account types are treated as material non-compliance.

Cross-reference the access matrix against the institution's application inventory or CMDB to verify that all systems in scope are represented in the matrix — prepare a documented rationale for any system that is intentionally excluded (e.g., isolated test environments), as undocumented omissions are interpreted as oversight rather than intentional scoping decisions.

Review the change log for the access matrix and confirm that every change in the past 24 months has a corresponding ITSM change request or access request ticket with the appropriate authority sign-off — spot-check three to five changes by tracing them from the change log back to the original request and forward to the system provisioning record, as examiners use the same technique to test control effectiveness.

Prepare a one-page summary for senior management that describes the access matrix governance framework, the review cycle, responsible owners, and the outcomes of the most recent review — BNM examiners may request management interviews and expect senior leaders to demonstrate awareness of access governance commitments, not just technical staff.

10.57.title: Access Monitoring and Logging
10.57.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised — not merely documented — access monitoring across all enterprise-wide systems, with particular scrutiny on whether anomaly detection results in documented, time-stamped investigations rather than passive alerting. Examiners will pull a sample of flagged anomalies and trace them end-to-end from detection through investigation closure to verify the containment loop is functional. A major red flag is the absence of logs for privileged or administrative accounts, or gaps in log coverage for critical systems such as core banking, payment gateways, and IAM platforms. Examiners will also verify that logs are immutable, centralised, and retained for at least three years with evidence of periodic review — not merely stored. Institutions that conflate log storage with log review, or that cannot demonstrate a regular review cadence with documented outcomes, are typically rated as non-compliant.

evidenceItems.name: Access Control Monitoring Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy document that defines the institution's approach to enterprise-wide access monitoring, including roles responsible for monitoring, monitoring frequency, escalation thresholds, and the link between anomaly detection and incident response. Demonstrates that management has formally committed to the obligations under 10.57(a) and (b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly names enterprise-wide systems in scope and defines 'critical systems' with a rationale aligned to the institution's risk classification framework
Procedures specify monitoring frequency (e.g., real-time SIEM alerting plus daily/weekly review cycles) with named roles and teams accountable for each activity
Document includes a clear escalation matrix linking anomaly severity tiers to incident response procedures and containment timelines
Version history shows the policy has been reviewed and approved within the last 12 months by a senior governance body (e.g., CISO, IT Steering Committee)
Policy cross-references related documents including the Incident Response Plan, Privileged Access Management standard, and log retention schedule
commonGaps: Policy defines monitoring obligations in general terms without specifying which systems are in scope, making it impossible to assess coverage gaps
Anomaly escalation thresholds are absent or defined so broadly (e.g., 'unusual activity') that they provide no operational guidance to monitoring teams
Document has not been reviewed in over 12 months and does not reflect current system inventory or organisational changes
Procedures describe monitoring but do not establish a feedback loop confirming that flagged anomalies trigger formal investigation records
Policy is approved by IT Operations alone without sign-off from Risk Management or the Board-level committee, undermining governance credibility
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity Policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as RSA Archer or ServiceNow GRC)
IT Governance document management system with version control and approval audit trail
CISO or Head of Technology Risk office
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Access Monitoring Coverage Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A system-level inventory mapping every enterprise-wide and critical system to its configured monitoring controls, log sources, and SIEM ingestion status. Demonstrates that monitoring is comprehensive across the estate rather than limited to a subset of systems, directly evidencing compliance with 10.57(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Matrix covers all critical systems identified in the institution's system classification register, including core banking, payment systems, IAM/AD, email gateways, network perimeter devices, and cloud platforms
Each row confirms whether the system feeds into the centralised SIEM, the log types captured (authentication, privileged access, data access, configuration changes), and the alerting rules applied
Matrix is dated and reflects the current system inventory, with a column indicating the last validation date for each log source
Coverage gaps are explicitly acknowledged, risk-accepted, and linked to compensating controls or a remediation timeline
Document is owned by a named role (e.g., SOC Manager or IT Risk) and reviewed at least quarterly
commonGaps: Matrix exists but covers only IT infrastructure, omitting application-layer systems such as internet banking portals, middleware, and API gateways
Log source entries list systems without confirming that log forwarding to SIEM is active and validated — many institutions assume connectivity without testing it
Cloud-hosted or SaaS systems are excluded entirely, leaving significant blind spots in the monitoring estate
Matrix is a point-in-time snapshot with no evidence of periodic refresh, meaning decommissioned systems remain listed and new systems are absent
No owner or accountability is assigned per system, making it unclear who is responsible for remediating coverage gaps
suggestedSources: SOC or Security Operations team (SIEM platform: Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, or Wazuh)
IT Asset Management system or CMDB (ServiceNow, Lansweeper)
GRC platform system risk register
Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) tool for cloud system coverage
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export, with system owner sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Anomaly Detection and Alert Investigation Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented records of security anomalies flagged by monitoring systems, including the alert details, the investigation undertaken, findings, and closure or escalation outcome. Directly evidences compliance with 10.57(b) by demonstrating that anomalies result in prompt, structured investigations rather than passive logging.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each alert record includes a timestamp of detection, the system source, the nature of the anomaly, the analyst assigned, investigation steps taken, and a documented disposition (true positive, false positive, or escalated incident)
Time-to-investigate metrics are tracked and demonstrate that critical anomalies are actioned within the SLA defined in the monitoring policy (e.g., within 15 minutes for P1 alerts)
Records are stored in a ticketing or SIEM case management system with immutable audit trail (e.g., ServiceNow Security Incident Response, Splunk SOAR, or JIRA with appropriate controls)
A statistically meaningful sample of false positive dispositions includes evidence of tuning actions taken to reduce alert fatigue, demonstrating active management
Escalation records link anomaly tickets to formal Incident Response records where containment actions were triggered, closing the loop between detection and response
commonGaps: Alerts are generated by the SIEM but investigations are conducted informally in email threads or verbal discussions, leaving no traceable investigation record
Investigation records exist but omit timestamps, making it impossible to assess whether investigations were prompt as required by 10.57(b)
High volumes of alerts are closed as false positives without documented rationale or tuning follow-up, suggesting the monitoring system is generating noise rather than signal
Records are stored in analyst-owned spreadsheets or local files rather than a centralised system, creating completeness and integrity risks
Escalations from anomaly to incident are not documented, breaking the chain of evidence between detection and containment
suggestedSources: SIEM case management module (Splunk SOAR, Microsoft Sentinel Incidents, IBM QRadar SOAR)
ITSM ticketing system (ServiceNow Security Incident Response module, Jira Service Management)
SOC daily/weekly shift handover reports
Incident Response platform or security incident register
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/PDF) from SIEM or ITSM, covering a rolling 12-month sample

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Activity Logs with Integrity Controls
evidenceItems.description: Raw or aggregated activity logs from critical systems demonstrating that user actions — including authentication events, privileged access, data queries, and configuration changes — are captured in full and stored with integrity controls. Directly evidences the logging obligation under 10.57(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs capture the minimum required fields: timestamp (UTC-synchronised), user identity, source IP, system/resource accessed, action performed, and outcome (success/failure)
Logs from privileged accounts (system administrators, DBAs, vendor support accounts) are captured separately and subjected to enhanced review controls
Log integrity is protected through cryptographic hashing, write-once storage, or forwarding to an immutable SIEM so that stored logs cannot be altered or deleted without detection
Log coverage is validated periodically (e.g., quarterly) by SOC or IT Audit through test events to confirm that expected log entries are generated and forwarded correctly
Logs are centralised in a SIEM or log management platform with role-based access controls preventing the subjects of the logs from modifying or deleting their own records
commonGaps: Logs are stored locally on individual servers rather than forwarded to a centralised platform, creating gaps when servers are decommissioned or storage is overwritten
Log records omit user identity fields, capturing only system account names or IP addresses, making it impossible to attribute actions to individual users during an investigation
Privileged access sessions (e.g., DBA queries, root-level commands) are not logged at the application or session level, only at the network perimeter
No integrity controls are applied to logs — logs are stored in mutable flat files or databases accessible to system administrators, undermining their evidentiary value
Log coverage has never been independently validated; the institution assumes logging is working without testing it against known events
suggestedSources: SIEM platform log ingestion inventory (Splunk, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel, QRadar)
Database Activity Monitoring (DAM) tools (Imperva, IBM Guardium) for database logs
Privileged Access Management (PAM) platform session logs (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)
Active Directory / Entra ID audit logs for authentication and access events
Application-level audit logs from core banking, payment, and internet banking platforms
evidenceItems.format: SIEM query export (CSV/JSON) or PAM platform report; log schema documentation in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Log Retention Schedule and Verification Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation confirming that activity logs for critical systems are retained for a minimum of three years as required by 10.57(c), including the institutional retention schedule, storage architecture, and evidence that logs remain accessible and retrievable throughout the retention period.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal log retention schedule exists and specifies a minimum three-year retention period for critical system activity logs, with the institution's own schedule set at seven years to align with BNM regulatory document standards

Storage architecture documentation confirms that logs are retained in a tiered structure (e.g., hot storage for 90 days, warm for 12 months, cold/archive for the remainder) with retrieval SLAs defined for each tier
Annual retrieval tests are documented, demonstrating that archived logs from prior periods can be successfully retrieved and are readable in their original format
Automated retention enforcement is configured in the SIEM or log management platform, preventing early deletion, with evidence of the configuration settings
Retention policy is reviewed annually and approved by a governance body, confirming alignment with current regulatory requirements
commonGaps: Institution retains logs for only 12 months due to storage cost constraints, directly violating the three-year minimum requirement in 10.57(c)
Retention schedule exists but applies a blanket policy to all logs without specifically identifying critical system logs as a higher-priority retention category
Archived logs have never been retrieved or tested for readability; the institution cannot demonstrate that historical logs are actually accessible when needed
Log deletion is not automated and relies on manual processes, creating risk that logs are deleted prematurely or retained inconsistently across systems
Retention documentation does not address cloud-hosted log storage, where default vendor retention settings may be shorter than the regulatory minimum
suggestedSources: IT Records Management or Data Governance team (retention schedule register)
SIEM platform retention configuration (Splunk index retention settings, Microsoft Sentinel workspace retention, Wazuh index lifecycle policies)
Cloud provider log storage configuration (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, GCP Cloud Logging retention settings)
IT Infrastructure or Storage team for archive tiering documentation
Internal Audit log retrieval test reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX retention policy with storage architecture diagram; XLSX retrieval test log

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Log Review Reports and Governance Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that activity logs from critical systems are regularly reviewed in a timely manner as required by 10.57(c), including scheduled review reports, reviewer sign-offs, findings, and escalation records where anomalies are identified during review. Demonstrates that log retention is coupled with active oversight rather than passive storage.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined log review schedule exists specifying review frequency by system criticality (e.g., daily automated review via SIEM rules, weekly manual review of privileged access logs, monthly summary reports to IT Risk Committee)
Review reports are dated, signed by the responsible reviewer, and include findings — including explicit confirmation of no anomalies where none were found — rather than blank attestations
Findings from log reviews that identify anomalies are traceable to investigation records (cross-referenced by ticket ID), demonstrating that review triggers action
Review coverage is tracked against the full list of critical systems, with metrics showing the proportion of systems reviewed within each period and any backlogs addressed
Quarterly or semi-annual management reporting to the IT Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee includes log review completion rates and material findings, demonstrating governance-level oversight
commonGaps: Log reviews are conducted ad hoc or only when an incident occurs, with no scheduled cadence and no documented evidence of routine review activity
Review reports exist but contain no substantive findings or commentary — they are checkbox exercises with no evidence that logs were actually analysed
Reviews focus exclusively on authentication logs and ignore privileged access, data access, and configuration change logs, creating blind spots in critical monitoring areas
Findings from log reviews are not escalated or linked to investigation records, meaning the review process is disconnected from the anomaly investigation process required under 10.57(b)
Management and board-level reporting does not include log review metrics, meaning governance oversight of the monitoring programme cannot be demonstrated to examiners
suggestedSources: SOC monthly operational reports and SIEM dashboard exports
IT Risk Committee or Technology Risk Working Group meeting minutes and management packs
Internal Audit review reports covering access monitoring
GRC platform task completion records for scheduled log review activities
Privileged Access Management (PAM) platform periodic access review reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports with reviewer signatures; meeting minutes in PDF/DOCX; GRC platform task audit trail export

auditTips: Before the examination, run a log coverage validation exercise by generating known test events (e.g., a failed privileged login, a configuration change) across each critical system and confirming the events appear in the SIEM within the expected timeframe — documented results of this exercise serve as contemporaneous evidence that monitoring is functional, not just configured.

Produce a single consolidated evidence pack that traces the 10.57 obligations end-to-end: system inventory → monitoring coverage matrix → SIEM alert → investigation ticket → closure or escalation — examiners assess the chain of evidence, and any broken link (e.g., alerts that have no associated investigation records) will be treated as a compliance gap regardless of what policies say.

Prepare a log retention demonstration by retrieving a sample of logs from exactly 36 months ago across at least three different critical systems and documenting the retrieval process, format, and completeness — this directly addresses the three-year minimum requirement and pre-empts examiner requests during on-site review.

Reconcile your critical system list against the log review schedule and SIEM coverage matrix immediately before the examination to identify and remediate any systems that are classified as critical but absent from monitoring — examiners frequently cross-reference these lists and will flag discrepancies as evidence of inadequate management of access controls under 10.57(a).

Compile a metrics summary showing the volume of anomalies flagged over the past 12 months, the mean time to investigate, the proportion escalated to formal incidents, and false positive rates with tuning actions taken — this demonstrates active, risk-based management of the monitoring programme rather than passive alert generation, which is a key examiner distinction under 10.57(b).

Ensure that privileged access logs are explicitly included in your evidence set and subject to enhanced review controls — BNM examiners pay disproportionate attention to administrator, DBA, and vendor access logging because these accounts represent the highest residual risk, and institutions that can only evidence end-user access monitoring are typically assessed as partially compliant at best.

Cross-reference your 10.57 evidence with related clauses including 10.55 (Privileged Access Management) and 10.56 (User Access Reviews) to present a coherent access control narrative — examiners assess RMiT holistically, and evidence that demonstrates integration across clauses is more persuasive than isolated point-in-time records for a single clause.

11.1.title: Enterprise-Wide Cyber Risk Management
11.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether cyber risk management is genuinely embedded across the enterprise rather than siloed within the IT or technology function alone. Examiners specifically look for evidence that business lines — including retail banking, treasury, operations, and finance — have documented cyber risk ownership and are active participants in risk identification, assessment, and treatment processes. A key red flag is the presence of cyber risk frameworks or policies that reference only the CISO or technology division without explicit accountability assigned to business unit heads. Examiners will also scrutinise Board and senior management reporting to determine whether cyber risk is presented in business-relevant terms and acted upon at an enterprise level, not merely acknowledged. Institutions that cannot demonstrate cross-functional cyber risk governance structures, joint risk acceptance records, or business-line participation in cyber risk forums are likely to face findings under this clause.

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Cyber Risk Management Framework
evidenceItems.description: The overarching policy or framework document that formally establishes enterprise-wide cyber risk management, defining scope, objectives, governance structure, and the shared accountability model between business and technology lines.
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework explicitly assigns cyber risk ownership to both business unit heads and technology leadership, naming roles rather than departments generically
Document scope covers all business lines, subsidiaries, and critical third parties — not limited to IT systems or infrastructure
Framework is approved at Board or Board Risk Committee level, with approval signatures and version history visible
Cyber risk appetite statements are expressed in business-impact terms (e.g., revenue loss thresholds, customer data exposure limits) that business lines can operationalise
Framework includes a defined review cycle (at minimum annual) with evidence of the most recent review and update reflected in the change log
commonGaps: Framework is titled 'IT Risk Policy' or 'Information Security Policy' and scoped only to technology functions, with no explicit reference to business line accountability
Roles and responsibilities section lists only CISO, IT risk, and cybersecurity team without naming business unit risk owners or their obligations
Document approved by CIO or CISO only, lacking Board or senior management endorsement that would signal enterprise-level commitment
Cyber risk appetite is expressed in purely technical metrics (e.g., CVSS scores, patch SLAs) without translation into business risk language meaningful to non-technical stakeholders
Framework has not been updated to reflect material changes in the institution's digital footprint, new products, or evolving threat landscape within the past 12 months
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — Policy Management module
SharePoint or Confluence — Governance & Compliance document library
Board Secretariat records for approval minutes
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) function for integrated policy repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Risk Governance Committee Terms of Reference and Meeting Records
evidenceItems.description: Terms of Reference (ToR) for the governance body responsible for enterprise-wide cyber risk oversight, along with minutes and action logs demonstrating active participation from both business and technology lines.
whatGoodLooksLike: ToR explicitly includes representation from business unit heads (e.g., Head of Retail Banking, CFO, COO) alongside technology and security leadership, not solely IT representatives
Meeting minutes show substantive discussion of cyber risks in business-impact terms, with business line representatives raising issues and contributing to decisions
Action items and risk treatment decisions are tracked to closure, with named business and technology owners assigned for each item
Meeting frequency meets or exceeds the institution's own governance policy (e.g., quarterly minimum) with full quorum achieved and documented
Escalation records exist showing how cyber risk items were elevated from the committee to Board Risk Committee or equivalent, with corresponding Board-level responses
commonGaps: Committee is composed entirely of technology and security personnel, with business line attendance listed as 'ad hoc' or absent from the attendance register
Minutes are superficial, recording only that topics were 'noted' without capturing debate, dissenting views, or substantive risk decisions made by business representatives
Action item logs show persistent overdue items with no documented accountability or escalation, indicating the committee lacks enforcement authority
ToR has not been reviewed or reapproved following changes in senior leadership or organisational restructuring
Separate IT risk committee and cyber risk committee exist with overlapping mandates but no documented integration, creating governance fragmentation
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Company Secretary records for approved ToR and signed minutes
GRC platform — Committee Management or Governance module
SharePoint — Risk Governance folder with version-controlled ToR and minutes archive
Enterprise Risk function for consolidated risk committee calendar and quorum records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Business Line Cyber Risk Register with Ownership Attribution
evidenceItems.description: A risk register that captures cyber risks identified at the business unit level, demonstrating that individual business lines actively identify, own, and manage cyber risks relevant to their operations — not solely risks catalogued by the technology or security function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk register entries include a designated business line risk owner (by name and role) distinct from the IT or cybersecurity team, evidencing distributed accountability
Risks are described in terms of business processes and outcomes (e.g., 'unauthorised access to customer loan origination system leading to data breach') rather than purely technical descriptors
Register captures risk treatment status, residual risk ratings, and treatment plan owners, with regular update timestamps confirming active maintenance
Risk register is linked or reconciled with the enterprise-level cyber risk register, showing how business-line risks roll up to entity-wide risk reporting
Evidence of business line risk owners formally accepting residual cyber risk in writing, with acceptance records traceable to specific register entries
commonGaps: All cyber risk entries are owned by 'IT Risk' or 'CISO Office' with no business unit owner named, revealing that risk identification has not been devolved to business lines

Risk descriptions use exclusively technical language (e.g., 'unpatched CVE-XXXX') without articulating the business impact, making it impossible to assess whether business owners understand the risks they nominally own
Register is static, with 'last updated' dates more than six months prior, suggesting it is treated as a compliance artefact rather than a living management tool
No reconciliation exists between business line registers and the enterprise cyber risk register, so aggregated exposure cannot be assessed and double-counting or omissions go undetected
Risk acceptance records are absent or unsigned, meaning there is no audit trail of informed business leadership accepting residual cyber risk
suggestedSources: GRC platform — Risk Register module (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow IRM, SAP GRC)
Business unit risk coordinators or Risk Champions network
Enterprise Risk Management function for consolidated register export
SharePoint — Business Unit Risk folders if decentralised management is in use
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / GRC system export (PDF for submission)

evidenceItems.name: Enterprise Cyber Risk Reporting to Board and Senior Management
evidenceItems.description: Periodic risk reports presented to the Board, Board Risk Committee, and senior management that demonstrate enterprise-wide visibility of cyber risk, framed in a manner accessible and actionable for non-technical decision-makers across business and technology lines.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports present cyber risk in business-impact language — financial exposure, operational disruption, regulatory consequence — alongside technical metrics, enabling informed decision-making by business leadership
Reporting covers all business lines and significant subsidiaries, with entity-level aggregation and the ability to drill down to specific business unit risk posture
Board minutes or resolution records reference the cyber risk report and show evidence of Board-level challenge, decision, or direction, not merely acknowledgement
Reports include trend analysis comparing current period to prior periods, enabling assessment of whether enterprise cyber risk is improving, stable, or deteriorating
Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) in the report are tied to the Board-approved cyber risk appetite, with clear traffic-light or threshold indicators showing appetite compliance status
commonGaps: Reports are highly technical (e.g., vulnerability counts, patch compliance percentages) with no translation into business risk impact, limiting their utility for Board oversight
Reporting is provided to the IT Steering Committee only, with no formal mechanism for cyber risk visibility at Board Risk Committee or Board level
Board minutes show cyber risk reports as standing agenda items that are consistently 'noted' without recorded discussion, challenge, or follow-up, suggesting passive rather than active oversight
Reports cover only the technology estate and do not reflect cyber risks arising from business processes, third-party dependencies, or new product launches
No linkage between reported cyber risk levels and the institution's stated risk appetite, making it impossible to assess whether the institution is operating within tolerance
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat for Board Risk Committee packs and approved minutes
CISO Office or Enterprise Risk for management dashboard and report templates
GRC platform — Reporting and Dashboard module
Business Intelligence tools (e.g., Power BI, Tableau) used for risk dashboards
evidenceItems.format: PDF (Board pack), Dashboard export

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Risk Training and Awareness Records for Business Lines
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that cyber risk management responsibilities are understood and exercised by business line personnel, evidencing that collective accountability is operationalised through structured awareness and training programmes targeting non-technology staff.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records show structured cyber risk programmes delivered to business line employees, including senior management and business unit heads — not limited to IT and security staff
Training content is differentiated by role: business risk owners receive training on risk identification and acceptance responsibilities, distinct from general phishing awareness for all staff
Risk champion or liaison network exists across business units with documented appointment letters and training records for designated individuals who bridge business and technology risk management
Post-training assessments or attestations confirm that business line participants understand their specific cyber risk responsibilities under the enterprise framework
Training programme is reviewed and updated at least annually to reflect emerging threats and changes to the institution's cyber risk profile
commonGaps: Training records show only IT and security staff completed cyber risk management training, with no evidence that business line heads or risk owners received targeted role-based training
General phishing awareness completion rates are reported as the primary evidence of business line cyber risk awareness, conflating security hygiene with risk management competency
No risk champion or liaison programme exists to operationalise business-technology collaboration on cyber risk, leaving business lines without a structured conduit to the security function
Training content is generic and not tailored to the specific cyber risks facing individual business lines (e.g., fraud risks in retail banking versus market data risks in treasury)
Training records are maintained inconsistently across business units, with some divisions unable to produce completion evidence, undermining the enterprise-wide claim
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) — e.g., SAP SuccessFactors, Cornerstone, Workday Learning
HR function for training attendance and completion records
CISO Office or Information Security function for security awareness programme documentation
Business unit risk coordinators for role-based training logs
evidenceItems.format: LMS export (PDF/spreadsheet), Attendance registers

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Risk Integration in New Product and Project Approval Process
evidenceItems.description: Documentation showing that cyber risk assessment is a mandatory gate in the institution's new product development, project approval, and significant change management processes — evidencing that business lines are required to consider cyber risk as part of their business decision-making, not as an afterthought.
whatGoodLooksLike: New Product Approval (NPA) or Project Initiation frameworks include a mandatory cyber risk assessment gate, with sign-off required from both business sponsor and information security before launch
Sample NPA or project approval records show completed cyber risk assessments that were reviewed and approved, with business line sponsors visibly involved in risk acceptance decisions
Risk assessment templates used in the process require business line input on data assets, customer impact, and operational dependencies — not just technical security controls
Post-implementation reviews include a cyber risk component, with records showing that residual risks identified during assessment were tracked to resolution after go-live
Change Advisory Board (CAB) or equivalent records show cyber risk considerations documented for significant changes, with business representation present in approval decisions
commonGaps: New product or project approval process exists but cyber risk assessment is conducted solely by IT security as an internal review, with no documented business sponsor engagement or risk acceptance
Cyber risk gate exists on paper but project records show approvals granted without a completed security assessment attached, indicating the gate is bypassed in practice
Risk assessments focus exclusively on technical controls (e.g., encryption, access controls) without capturing the business process risks, data classification implications, or customer impact
No evidence of post-implementation cyber risk review, meaning risks identified at inception are not tracked to closure and the effectiveness of controls cannot be demonstrated
Change management records show IT-only approval for significant technology changes with no business risk owner sign-off, indicating cyber risk decisions are made unilaterally by technology
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — Change Management and Project modules
Project Management Office (PMO) for project gateway documentation
Product Management or Innovation function for NPA records
GRC platform — Risk Assessment module linked to project records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (assessment forms), System exports from ITSM or PMO tools

auditTips: Map every cyber risk governance document to a specific named business unit owner before the examination — examiners will trace accountability from the framework all the way down to who signed the last risk acceptance, and gaps in this chain are immediate findings. Prepare a one-page accountability matrix showing cyber risk owners by business line, their appointment basis, and the documents they have formally approved or acknowledged.

Pull at least three Board Risk Committee or equivalent meeting packs from the past 12 months and cross-reference the cyber risk section against the corresponding Board minutes. If the minutes show only 'noted' with no recorded discussion, escalation, or Board direction, rehearse a narrative explaining how challenge and oversight occurred informally — but expect examiners to probe this gap specifically, as passive Board oversight is a common finding under enterprise-wide governance clauses.

Conduct a dry-run interview with two or three business unit heads (not technology staff) and ask them to describe their cyber risk responsibilities and the last cyber risk decision they made or approved. If they cannot answer confidently without referring to IT, the enterprise-wide accountability model is not operationally embedded — identify and close this gap before examination day, as examiners may conduct similar spot interviews.

Reconcile the business-line cyber risk registers against the enterprise-level register and resolve any disconnects before submission. Examiners will look for risks that appear at business line level but are absent from enterprise reporting (indicating gaps in roll-up), and risks in the enterprise register with no traceable business owner (indicating technology-only risk identification). Document the reconciliation process as evidence of active enterprise-wide risk management.

Review your new product and change approval records from the past 12 months and identify any instances where the cyber risk gate was waived, abbreviated, or completed retrospectively.
