Prepare a documented rationale for each exception, including compensating controls applied and residual risk accepted by a named business sponsor. Examiners frequently sample three to five recent approvals to test whether process gates are enforced in practice, not just described in policy.

Verify that your cyber risk reporting KRIs are explicitly linked to the Board-approved risk appetite thresholds. Prepare a single exhibit showing each KRI, its current value, its appetite threshold, and the trend over the last three reporting periods. This exhibit demonstrates enterprise-wide risk monitoring in business-relevant terms and is highly effective in examination settings for showing the Board has visibility over risk appetite compliance.

Cross-reference training completion records with the list of named business line cyber risk owners and risk champions to confirm that every individual with a documented cyber risk responsibility has completed role-appropriate training in the current 12-month period. Any gap between the accountability register and training records will be flagged as evidence that responsibilities are assigned on paper but not operationally supported — close these gaps and retain the updated completion evidence for the examination file.

11.2.title: Cyber Resilience Framework Development
11.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) exists as a substantive, board-approved policy document rather than a nominal or template-driven artefact. Examiners look specifically for evidence that the CRF explicitly articulates governance accountabilities (including Board and senior management responsibilities), documented cyber resilience objectives tied to measurable outcomes, and a formally adopted risk tolerance statement that is calibrated against the institution's threat profile. A major red flag is a CRF that does not map to the IPDRR lifecycle or treats it as a conceptual reference rather than an operationalised control structure. Examiners will also probe whether the CRF expressly covers systems and data held by third-party service providers, as omission of third-party scope is a common finding. The framework must demonstrate responsiveness to the evolving threat environment through periodic review cycles, with evidence that threat intelligence inputs have influenced CRF objectives or risk tolerance decisions.

evidenceItems.name: Board-Approved Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) Policy Document
evidenceItems.description: The primary CRF policy document that serves as the foundational governance instrument. It must articulate the institution's governance structure for cyber risk management, defined cyber resilience objectives, and formally adopted risk tolerance boundaries. This document demonstrates that the institution has satisfied the core obligation under clause 11.2 to develop and maintain a CRF.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document carries a Board or Board Risk Committee resolution approving the CRF, with date, version number, and signatures of accountable signatories including the CISO or equivalent
A dedicated section explicitly states cyber resilience objectives in measurable or outcome-based terms, including the objective of operational resilience against extreme but plausible cyber-attacks

Risk tolerance is expressed in quantitative or semi-quantitative terms (e.g., maximum acceptable downtime, recovery time objectives by system criticality tier) and linked to the institution's overall risk appetite statement
The CRF scope statement explicitly names on-premises systems and third-party/cloud-hosted systems and data as within scope, with no carve-outs that would exclude material outsourced arrangements
A governance accountability matrix or RACI assigns CRF ownership, review responsibilities, and escalation paths from operational teams up to Board level
commonGaps: CRF exists as a standalone document but has not received formal Board or Board Risk Committee approval, with only management-level sign-off — this fails to demonstrate adequate governance oversight

Cyber resilience objectives are stated in vague aspirational language (e.g., 'achieve best-practice cybersecurity') without measurable targets, making it impossible to assess whether objectives have been met

Risk tolerance statement is absent or is simply cross-referenced to a generic enterprise risk appetite without cyber-specific thresholds such as breach tolerance, data exposure limits, or system recovery benchmarks
Third-party service providers are excluded from the CRF scope or addressed only in a brief footnote, creating a significant coverage gap for cloud-hosted or outsourced critical systems

The document has not been reviewed or updated following material changes to the threat landscape, regulatory environment, or the institution's technology footprint, rendering it stale and non-responsive
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat or Company Secretary's office for board resolution records and meeting minutes approving the CRF
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) policy repository for the current approved version
CISO or Information Security function's SharePoint/Confluence policy library
Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework documentation for alignment with risk appetite statement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control header, approval signatures page, and change log

evidenceItems.name: CRF Governance Accountability and Oversight Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the governance structure articulated in the CRF is operationally active — including Board and committee charters, terms of reference, meeting minutes, and management reporting that evidence ongoing cyber risk oversight. This satisfies the CRF requirement to 'clearly articulate governance for managing cyber risks' by showing the governance structure functions in practice.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee charter explicitly assigns oversight responsibility for the CRF, with cyber risk as a standing agenda item evidenced by at least four sets of meeting minutes per year

Management-level cyber risk committee (e.g., Information Security Steering Committee) has documented terms of reference, regular meeting cadence, and minutes that reference CRF performance or exceptions

A defined escalation path is evidenced in practice — for example, a cyber risk threshold breach or a material incident that was escalated from the CISO to the Board with documented rationale and Board response
Role-specific accountability for the CRF (CISO, CRO, CIO) is reflected in position descriptions or delegation of authority documents, cross-referencing the CRF
Annual or semi-annual Board-level cyber risk reporting pack demonstrates that Board members receive substantive updates on cyber resilience posture, threat environment changes, and CRF effectiveness
commonGaps: Governance structure is documented in the CRF but not operationalised — committee meetings are infrequent, quorum was not achieved, or cyber risk is only addressed as a sub-item under broader IT risk reporting
Board meeting minutes reference cybersecurity in passing but do not demonstrate substantive engagement with CRF objectives, risk tolerance levels, or resilience testing outcomes

Accountability for the CRF is not clearly assigned — multiple functions claim ownership without clear delineation, or the CISO role lacks formal authority to enforce CRF requirements across business lines
No evidence that the governance structure was invoked during a cyber incident or near-miss, suggesting the governance model is theoretical rather than embedded in operational response
Management reporting to the Board uses qualitative traffic-light dashboards without underlying data or methodology, which examiners view as insufficient to demonstrate informed Board oversight
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat for Board and committee meeting minutes and attendance records
CISO or Information Security function for management committee terms of reference and minutes
HR or People function for role profiles and delegation of authority documents
GRC platform for automated cyber risk dashboard exports used in Board reporting
evidenceItems.format: PDF (meeting minutes, charters, reports); system-generated dashboard exports in PDF or XLSX

evidenceItems.name: IPDRR Control Mapping and Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the CRF operationally supports the full Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover (IPDRR) lifecycle as required by clause 11.2. This includes a structured control mapping that links each IPDRR function to specific implemented controls, responsible owners, and evidence of operational effectiveness across both on-premises and third-party hosted systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal IPDRR control mapping document or register that maps every CRF-mandated control to one of the five IPDRR functions, with control owner, implementation status, and evidence reference for each control
The IPDRR mapping explicitly distinguishes controls applicable to on-premises systems from those applicable to third-party hosted systems, with no material coverage gaps for either category

Each IPDRR function has documented key performance indicators (KPIs) or key risk indicators (KRIs) with current performance data — for example, mean time to detect (MTTD) for Detect, and mean time to recover (MTTR) for Recover

Evidence of periodic control testing or assurance activity (e.g., penetration tests, red team exercises, tabletop simulations) mapped to IPDRR functions, with findings tracked to closure in an issue management system
The IPDRR mapping has been reviewed and updated following material changes to the institution's systems landscape or third-party service provider arrangements
commonGaps: The CRF references the IPDRR model conceptually but does not map specific controls to IPDRR functions, making it impossible to assess control completeness or identify lifecycle gaps

Control mapping exists but covers only on-premises systems — third-party hosted systems (cloud platforms, outsourced data centres) are absent, creating a material scope gap inconsistent with the clause requirement

Detect and Respond functions are significantly under-controlled relative to Identify and Protect — a common imbalance where institutions invest heavily in prevention but lack mature detection and response capabilities
IPDRR metrics are reported but lack baseline targets, making it impossible to assess whether performance meets the institution's stated cyber resilience objectives
Control testing results are available for some IPDRR functions but remediation of identified gaps is not tracked to closure, suggesting the assurance cycle is incomplete
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow GRC) for the control register and IPDRR mapping
Security Operations Centre (SOC) for MTTD/MTTR metrics and detection coverage reports
Vulnerability management and penetration testing reports from the Information Security function or third-party assessors
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) for remediation tracking and issue closure evidence
evidenceItems.format: XLSX or GRC platform export for control mapping; PDF for testing reports; system exports for KPI/KRI dashboards

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Risk Tolerance Statement and Risk Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of the institution's cyber risk tolerance, including the methodology used to establish it, its linkage to the broader enterprise risk appetite, and the risk assessment records that informed it. This evidences the CRF's obligation to articulate risk tolerance 'with due regard to the evolving cyber threat environment', demonstrating that tolerance levels are data-driven and periodically validated.
whatGoodLooksLike: A dedicated cyber risk tolerance statement approved at Board level, expressed with specific thresholds across multiple dimensions such as confidentiality breaches (e.g., number of records), system availability loss (e.g., hours of downtime), financial impact, and reputational harm

The methodology for deriving cyber risk tolerance is documented and references quantitative inputs such as threat intelligence data, historical incident data, industry benchmarking, and business impact analysis outcomes
A cyber risk register or risk heat map shows current cyber risk exposure measured against the stated tolerance, enabling examiners to assess whether the institution is operating within appetite

Records show that risk tolerance has been reviewed and if necessary revised following material threat environment changes, regulatory updates, or significant cyber incidents affecting the institution or the financial sector
The cyber risk tolerance statement is explicitly referenced in the CRF and is consistent with (but more specific than) the institution's enterprise-wide risk appetite statement
commonGaps: Cyber risk tolerance is defined only qualitatively (e.g., 'low tolerance for data breaches') without quantitative thresholds, making it unverifiable whether the institution is operating within tolerance

Risk tolerance was set during initial CRF development and has not been reviewed despite material changes to the cyber threat landscape, regulatory expectations, or the institution's technology profile
The cyber risk register does not consistently measure risk exposure against the stated tolerance, so there is no mechanism to detect when risk levels exceed acceptable bounds

Cyber risk tolerance is treated as identical to the enterprise risk appetite statement rather than being a cyber-specific elaboration — this fails to demonstrate the level of specificity required for effective cyber risk governance
The basis for the risk tolerance level is not documented, so examiners cannot assess whether it was informed by threat intelligence, business impact analysis, or other substantive inputs
suggestedSources: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) function for the risk appetite framework and Board-approved risk tolerance statements
GRC platform for the cyber risk register and risk heat map exports
Threat intelligence function or subscription service for threat environment inputs used in risk tolerance calibration
Business continuity and business impact analysis documentation from the BCM function
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for risk tolerance statement; XLSX or GRC platform export for risk register; PDF for Board approval records

evidenceItems.name: Threat Intelligence and Threat Environment Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution monitors the evolving cyber threat environment and incorporates threat intelligence findings into CRF governance decisions, risk tolerance calibration, and resilience objective-setting. This evidences the CRF's requirement to be developed 'with due regard to the evolving cyber threat environment' and to address 'extreme but plausible cyber-attacks'.
whatGoodLooksLike: A documented threat intelligence programme or process that identifies, ingests, and analyses threat intelligence from multiple sources (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM advisories, commercial threat intelligence feeds, internal SOC telemetry) on a defined frequency

Threat landscape assessments or reports produced at least annually, identifying sector-specific threats relevant to Malaysian financial institutions, including advanced persistent threats (APTs), ransomware, and supply chain attacks that constitute 'extreme but plausible' scenarios

Evidence that threat intelligence outputs have been formally reviewed by the governance structure (e.g., Information Security Steering Committee, Board Risk Committee) and have informed decisions on CRF objectives, risk tolerance, or control enhancements

Extreme-but-plausible cyber-attack scenarios are documented and explicitly referenced in the CRF or in associated stress testing and resilience testing frameworks, with a clear methodology for scenario selection
Records of integration between threat intelligence findings and third-party risk assessments, ensuring that threats relevant to outsourced or cloud-hosted systems are captured within the CRF's scope
commonGaps: Threat intelligence is consumed at the operational SOC level but findings are not formally escalated or reported to governance bodies, creating a disconnect between threat awareness and CRF governance decisions

Extreme-but-plausible scenarios referenced in the CRF are generic (e.g., 'ransomware attack') without institution-specific elaboration based on the institution's threat profile, critical asset inventory, or sector threat context
Threat landscape assessments are produced ad hoc or only in response to BNM advisories rather than on a defined, proactive review cycle
Threat intelligence coverage does not address third-party or supply chain threats, leaving a gap in the CRF's ability to assess risks to systems hosted by third-party service providers

There is no documented feedback loop between threat intelligence findings and updates to cyber resilience objectives or risk tolerance — threat data is collected but does not drive governance decisions
suggestedSources: SOC or Threat Intelligence function for threat landscape reports and intelligence feed subscription records
BNM advisories and FS-ISAC membership records for regulatory and sector threat intelligence inputs
Governance committee meeting minutes for evidence that threat intelligence was presented and actioned by oversight bodies
GRC platform or SharePoint for threat scenario documentation and scenario selection methodology records
evidenceItems.format: PDF for threat landscape reports and scenario documentation; system exports for threat intelligence ingestion logs; PDF for committee meeting minutes referencing threat intelligence reviews

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Cyber Risk Coverage Within the CRF
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the CRF explicitly and operationally covers systems and data hosted by third-party service providers, as required by clause 11.2. This includes third-party cyber risk assessment records, contractual cyber obligations, and IPDRR control coverage for outsourced and cloud-hosted environments, evidencing that the CRF's scope is not limited to on-premises infrastructure.
whatGoodLooksLike: A third-party cyber risk register or inventory that maps all material outsourced arrangements and cloud service providers to the CRF scope, with each entry referencing applicable IPDRR controls and assessment status

Contractual agreements with material third-party service providers contain cyber resilience obligations aligned with the CRF, including requirements for incident notification, resilience testing participation, and audit rights
Third-party cyber risk assessments (pre-engagement and periodic) are conducted using a methodology aligned to the CRF's IPDRR framework, with assessment reports retained for material providers

Evidence that the institution has exercised its audit rights or obtained independent assurance (e.g., SOC 2 Type II reports, ISO 27001 certificates, penetration testing results) for material third-party service providers on at least an annual basis

The CRF's governance structure includes a defined process for escalating third-party cyber risk findings to the appropriate oversight body, with at least one example of such escalation evidenced in committee records
commonGaps: The CRF's scope statement covers on-premises systems comprehensively but treats third-party hosted systems as subject only to vendor management processes rather than full CRF governance, creating a bifurcated and incomplete framework

Third-party cyber risk assessments are conducted by the procurement or vendor management function using generic questionnaires that do not align with the CRF's IPDRR structure, resulting in an assessment gap

Contractual agreements with cloud service providers do not include cyber resilience obligations, incident notification timelines, or the institution's right to conduct or commission cyber resilience assessments

Material third-party service providers have not provided independent assurance reports (SOC 2, penetration testing) within the past 12 months, and the institution has not pursued alternatives or compensating controls

Third-party cyber risk findings are managed entirely within the procurement or vendor management function and are not reported to the cyber risk governance structure, preventing Board-level visibility of third-party cyber exposures
suggestedSources: Vendor management or third-party risk management function for the third-party inventory and risk assessment records
Legal or Contracts function for executed agreements with material service providers containing cyber resilience obligations
GRC platform for third-party risk register and assessment tracking
Cloud service providers' trust portals (e.g., AWS Artifact, Azure Compliance Manager) for independently obtained assurance reports such as SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificates
evidenceItems.format: XLSX or GRC platform export for third-party risk register; PDF for assessment reports, contracts excerpts, and assurance certificates

auditTips: Conduct a scope gap analysis before the examination by mapping every system and data asset — both on-premises and third-party hosted — against the CRF's documented scope. Any material system not covered by the CRF is an immediate finding under clause 11.2; address gaps proactively and document the remediation rationale.

Prepare a single-page CRF lineage document that traces each IPDRR function through four layers: (1) CRF policy obligation, (2) specific implemented controls, (3) key metrics or KPIs demonstrating effectiveness, and (4) most recent testing or assurance activity. Examiners will walk this chain and any broken link — a control with no metrics, or metrics with no testing — will attract scrutiny.

Cross-reference the Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes from the past 12 months against the CRF's governance requirements and flag any months where cyber risk was not substantively addressed. Gaps in Board oversight cadence are a common examiner finding that can be remediated by preparing a retrospective briefing note and scheduling an interim Board update before the examination.

Validate that the cyber risk tolerance statement uses specific quantitative thresholds and can be demonstrated as 'in use' — pull the most recent cyber risk register and show that current risk exposure is plotted against each tolerance threshold. An unused tolerance statement that cannot be tied to active monitoring will be treated as a paper control.

For the threat environment requirement, prepare a chronological log showing that threat intelligence inputs from the past 12 months (BNM advisories, FS-ISAC alerts, commercial feeds) were received, reviewed at a named governance forum, and resulted in a documented decision — even if the decision was to maintain existing controls. The absence of a feedback loop between threat intelligence and CRF governance is a frequent examiner observation.

Test the third-party coverage of the CRF by selecting two or three material third-party service providers at random and tracing their cyber risk coverage end-to-end: contractual cyber obligations, most recent risk assessment, assurance report, and any findings tracked to closure. Examiners frequently sample third-party arrangements in this way; having these packages prepared in advance demonstrates control maturity.

Ensure the CRF document version history and Board approval audit trail are immediately accessible. Examiners often begin by requesting the CRF and its approval documentation simultaneously. If the document has been revised since initial Board approval, prepare evidence that each revision was subject to Board or delegated authority review — a document with multiple revisions but only one original approval record suggests inadequate governance of the framework itself.

11.3.title: CRF Minimum Elements
11.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess clause 11.3 by verifying that the Cyber Risk Framework is not merely a policy document but an operationally live framework evidenced across all nine mandated elements. Examiners pay close attention to whether the CRF reflects the institution's actual risk posture — not a generic template — by cross-referencing the stated cyber risk context in sub-clause (a) against real-world threat intelligence, business operations, and interconnectivity maps from sub-clause (b). A primary red flag is a CRF that lists international standards such as zero-trust and defence-in-depth in sub-clause (d) without corresponding technical architecture records, configuration baselines, or micro-segmentation evidence to substantiate the claim. Examiners will specifically probe whether the cyber risk management function under sub-clause (i) has genuine independence and access to senior management and the board, looking for board papers, escalation records, and Terms of Reference rather than a nominal organisational chart. Non-integration between the centralised asset inventory in sub-clause (h), the critical systems register in sub-clause (b), and the threat-and-vulnerability process in sub-clause (c) is treated as a material control gap indicating that the CRF exists in silos rather than as a coherent, enterprise-wide framework.

evidenceItems.name: CRF Master Document & Cyber Risk Context Assessment
evidenceItems.description: The governing Cyber Risk Framework document itself, accompanied by the institutional cyber risk context assessment that establishes the organisation's overall cyber risk profile, business operating environment, exposure landscape, and current cybersecurity posture. This evidence demonstrates compliance with sub-clause (a) by showing that the institution has a structured, approved framework with a board-endorsed understanding of its cyber risk context relative to its specific business lines, digital channels, geographic footprint, and third-party dependencies.
whatGoodLooksLike: CRF document is board-approved, version-controlled, and reviewed at least annually, with dated approval signatures from the board or relevant board committee clearly visible on the face of the document
The cyber risk context assessment explicitly maps identified risks to specific business units, product lines, and digital service channels rather than describing risks in generic industry-wide terms

A current-state cybersecurity posture statement is included, referencing the most recent internal or third-party maturity assessment score and comparing it against a defined target maturity level with a gap-closure roadmap

The document articulates the institution's regulatory obligations, customer data categories handled, and interconnectivity with payment infrastructure (e.g., RPP, RENTAS, IBG) as drivers of its cyber risk exposure
A structured review cycle is evidenced through change-log entries showing updates triggered by material operational changes, new digital product launches, or emerging threat events
commonGaps: CRF document is approved at management level only and lacks board-level endorsement, which fails the governance threshold implied by sub-clause (a) and sub-clause (i) read together

Cyber risk context describes the institution using generic language (e.g., 'financial services sector faces cyber threats') without linking risks to specific systems, business processes, or third-party relationships unique to the institution
Posture assessment is more than 12 months old or was last updated before a material change such as a cloud migration or new digital banking product launch, rendering it stale for examination purposes

The framework document references supporting policies and procedures that cannot be produced during examination because they were never created, indicating the CRF is aspirational rather than operational

No explicit linkage exists between the cyber risk context section and the institution's broader enterprise risk appetite statement, making it impossible for examiners to assess whether cyber risk is managed within board-approved tolerances
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee or Board Technology Committee minutes and resolutions approving the CRF
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) housing the master CRF document and version history
Annual cyber risk posture report prepared by the CISO or equivalent and presented to senior management
Internal audit or third-party cybersecurity maturity assessment reports (e.g., NIST CSF, CRI Profile assessments)
SharePoint or document management system housing the approved policy register with version control and approval workflow records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (board-approved master document) with supplementary PPTX or PDF posture assessment report

evidenceItems.name: Critical Systems, Information Asset Classification & Interdependency Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive and current register of critical systems, information assets, and data classifications, combined with an interdependency mapping that documents internal and external connectivity. This evidence addresses sub-clauses (b) and (h) simultaneously by demonstrating that the institution has both identified and prioritised its assets and implemented the centralised automated tracking system mandated for technology asset inventory management. The register must show classification criteria, assigned criticality ratings, and connectivity to internal systems and external parties including third-party service providers, payment networks, and group entities.
whatGoodLooksLike: The asset inventory is maintained in a centralised, automated platform (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Qualys AssetView, or equivalent ITAM/CMDB tool) that is demonstrably integrated with vulnerability scanning and patch management systems, providing near-real-time accuracy rather than relying on manual spreadsheet updates

Each asset entry includes a business criticality rating, data classification tier, responsible asset owner, hosting environment (on-premises, private cloud, public cloud), and date of last verification, allowing examiners to immediately assess scope and completeness

A network and data-flow diagram accompanies the register, mapping internal system interdependencies and external connectivity to third parties (cloud service providers, interbank payment systems, outsourced service providers) in alignment with the interconnectivity scope specified in sub-clause (b)

Critical system designations are formally justified against defined criticality criteria (e.g., impact on financial stability, customer data volume, regulatory reporting function) and reviewed by a governance committee at least annually
The automated inventory system generates exception reports for unregistered or shadow IT assets discovered through network scanning, and these reports are actioned with documented resolution evidence
commonGaps: The asset inventory is maintained as a static spreadsheet with no automated discovery integration, failing the specific 'centralised automated tracking system' requirement of sub-clause (h) and rendering asset data unreliable between manual refresh cycles

External dependencies — particularly cloud service providers, Software-as-a-Service platforms, and intragroup shared services — are absent from the interdependency register, creating blind spots in the institution's cyber risk profile contrary to sub-clause (b)

Criticality classification criteria are undefined or inconsistently applied, resulting in assets that clearly support critical business functions being rated as non-critical, undermining the reliability of the prioritisation required by sub-clause (b)

The register has not been reconciled against the IT environment for more than six months, with newly commissioned systems or decommissioned assets not reflected, indicating the inventory is not operationally maintained

No documented process exists for updating the register when new systems are onboarded or third-party integrations are established, meaning the register degrades in accuracy over time without a structural control to prevent it
suggestedSources: CMDB or ITAM platform export (ServiceNow, Flexera, ManageEngine AssetExplorer, or equivalent)
Network operations team for topology and data-flow diagrams (Visio, Lucidchart, or network management platform exports)
Vulnerability management platform asset inventory (Qualys, Tenable.io, Rapid7 InsightVM)
Third-party and vendor management register maintained by Procurement or Vendor Management teams
Cloud infrastructure inventory from cloud provider consoles (AWS Config, Azure Resource Graph, GCP Asset Inventory)
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/XLSX) from CMDB platform + PDF network/data-flow diagrams; automated platform screenshots demonstrating integration

evidenceItems.name: Threat & Vulnerability Assessment Records with Countermeasure Mapping
evidenceItems.description: Documented records of ongoing threat identification, vulnerability assessments, and the countermeasures implemented or planned in response, as required by sub-clause (c). This evidence demonstrates that the institution systematically identifies cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities relevant to its digital service delivery and maps them to security controls, thereby showing a closed-loop process from threat identification through to remediation that protects against cyber-attacks and reputational damage.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal threat intelligence programme is evidenced, with subscriptions to financial sector threat feeds (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM-facilitated sharing mechanisms, commercial threat intelligence platforms) and documented processes for consuming, contextualising, and actioning intelligence relevant to the institution's specific asset profile

Vulnerability assessment results from periodic penetration tests, red team exercises, and automated scanning are catalogued with risk ratings, affected assets cross-referenced to the asset register, and remediation timelines that are tracked to closure with evidence of sign-off

A threat-to-countermeasure mapping document or matrix exists that links identified threat actors and threat scenarios to the specific technical and procedural controls deployed, demonstrating that countermeasures are deliberately chosen in response to specific threats rather than implemented ad hoc

Penetration testing is conducted at minimum annually and after material changes to the environment, with test scope, findings, and remediation status documented; reports are presented to senior management or a relevant governance committee

Vulnerability remediation SLAs are defined by severity (e.g., critical within 15 days, high within 30 days) and tracking evidence shows actual remediation performance against these SLAs, with overdue items subject to documented risk acceptance by an authorised approver
commonGaps: Vulnerability assessments are conducted infrequently (e.g., annually only) or do not cover the full scope of the critical systems register, leaving material portions of the attack surface unassessed contrary to the continuous nature implied by sub-clause (c)

Threat intelligence is consumed passively through generic industry feeds but is never contextualised to the institution's specific technology stack, digital products, or threat actor profile, rendering it of limited operational value for targeted countermeasure selection

Remediation tracking is managed informally through email or shared documents rather than a structured ITSM workflow, making it impossible for examiners to verify that identified vulnerabilities were addressed within approved timeframes

The countermeasure inventory is not maintained as a living document and has not been updated to reflect newly deployed controls or changes in the threat landscape, meaning the mapping between threats and defences is outdated

Penetration test reports are produced but findings are not escalated through the governance structure, with senior management and the board unaware of material vulnerabilities identified during testing
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform reports (Qualys, Tenable, Rapid7) exported from the central scanning console
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for vulnerability remediation tickets and SLA tracking
Penetration testing and red team engagement reports from internal red team or approved third-party security vendors
Threat intelligence platform or SIEM threat intelligence feeds (Anomali, ThreatConnect, IBM X-Force Exchange)
GRC platform for risk treatment records linking threats to countermeasures and residual risk assessments
evidenceItems.format: PDF penetration test reports; system-exported vulnerability tracking reports (CSV/XLSX); PDF/DOCX threat-to-countermeasure mapping matrix

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Defence Architecture & Layered Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical and governance documentation demonstrating the implementation of layered cyber defences aligned to current international standards including zero-trust architecture, defence-in-depth through micro-segmentation, and security-by-design principles, as required by sub-clause (d). This evidence establishes that the institution's defensive posture is grounded in established frameworks and contemporary security principles rather than legacy perimeter-only approaches, and that controls are systematically applied to protect data, infrastructure, and assets against evolving cyber threats.
whatGoodLooksLike: A security architecture document articulates the institution's adoption of zero-trust principles with explicit reference to how identity verification, device health validation, and least-privilege access enforcement are implemented across specific systems and user populations, including privileged users and third-party access

Network segmentation and micro-segmentation designs are documented with current network diagrams showing zone boundaries, firewall rule policies, and east-west traffic controls; configuration evidence (firewall policy exports, network access control logs) corroborates the diagrammatic representation

A security-by-design policy and corresponding development standards are in place, with evidence that security requirements are incorporated at the design phase of new systems and digital products through threat modelling records, secure development lifecycle (SDL) gate approvals, and pre-production security assessments

Control mapping to at least one current international standard (e.g., NIST CSF 2.0, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, CIS Controls v8) is documented and maintained, with coverage gaps identified, risk-accepted, or remediated on a tracked schedule

Records of regular control effectiveness testing (e.g., firewall rule review outcomes, privileged access recertification results, penetration test findings mapped to defence layers) demonstrate that the layered defence is operationally effective and not merely documented
commonGaps: Zero-trust and defence-in-depth are cited as adopted principles in the CRF but no supporting technical architecture documentation, network diagrams, or configuration evidence exists to substantiate these claims, making compliance assertions unverifiable

Network segmentation diagrams are outdated and do not reflect the current environment following cloud adoption, digital banking platform deployments, or network changes, rendering them unreliable as evidence of current micro-segmentation posture

Security-by-design is referenced in policy but no threat modelling records, SDL gate approvals, or pre-production security assessment evidence exists for recently launched digital services, indicating the policy is not operationally enforced

International standards alignment claims are based on self-assessment worksheets that have not been independently validated, and the assessments have not been updated to reflect current versions of referenced standards (e.g., assessments reference NIST CSF 1.1 rather than the current 2.0 version)

Privileged access management controls are described as a defence layer in documentation but privileged account inventories are incomplete, and evidence of regular recertification of privileged access is absent, indicating the control is not functioning as described
suggestedSources: Enterprise architecture team for security architecture documents and current network topology diagrams
Firewall management platform or SIEM for firewall rule-set exports and network segmentation configuration evidence (Palo Alto Panorama, Cisco Firepower Management Center)
Identity and Access Management platform for zero-trust policy configurations, MFA enforcement records, and privileged access review outputs (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Microsoft Entra ID)
Secure Development Lifecycle records from the application development team or DevSecOps pipeline (Jira, Confluence, GitLab CI/CD security scan outputs)
GRC platform for international standards alignment mapping and control effectiveness test results
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX security architecture document; Visio/PDF network diagrams; system-exported configuration evidence; XLSX control mapping workbook

evidenceItems.name: Security Operations & Continuous Monitoring Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that the institution has implemented continuous surveillance and monitoring capabilities capable of timely detection of cybersecurity incidents, as required by sub-clause (e). This encompasses SIEM platform configuration and coverage records, SOC operational metrics, alert triage and escalation workflows, and monitoring scope documentation proving that critical systems and assets are under active surveillance around the clock.
whatGoodLooksLike: A SIEM platform is deployed with documented log source coverage that maps onboarded log sources to the critical systems register, demonstrating that all critical systems generate events that are ingested and monitored, with a formal gap analysis for any critical system not yet onboarded

SOC operational records — including shift handover logs, daily monitoring reports, or security operations dashboards — demonstrate 24x7 monitoring capability and provide examiners with evidence of continuous rather than business-hours-only surveillance

Use-case and detection rule documentation shows that alerting logic is tuned to the institution's specific risk profile, with evidence of periodic rule review and tuning to reduce false-positive fatigue and maintain detection fidelity

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and Mean Time to Respond (MTTR) metrics are tracked over time and reported to senior management, demonstrating that the institution measures the effectiveness of its detection capability against defined performance thresholds

Threat hunting activities are documented with records of hypothesis-driven hunts conducted by the SOC or threat intelligence team, including scope, methodology, findings, and any resulting rule or control improvements — demonstrating proactive rather than purely reactive detection
commonGaps: SIEM log source coverage is significantly incomplete, with critical systems such as internet-facing applications, privileged access management systems, or cloud environments not onboarded for monitoring, creating detection blind spots that would be immediately apparent to examiners cross-referencing the SIEM coverage map against the critical assets register

Monitoring operates on business hours only or relies on after-hours alerting to on-call staff without a documented escalation procedure, failing to meet the 'continuous surveillance' standard of sub-clause (e)

Alert thresholds and detection rules have not been reviewed or tuned since initial deployment, resulting in either excessive false positives that overwhelm analysts or miscalibrated rules that miss genuine threat activity

No performance metrics exist for the monitoring function; the institution cannot demonstrate MTTD or MTTR data, making it impossible to show that detection is actually timely as required by sub-clause (e)

SOC function is outsourced to a managed security service provider but no evidence of oversight exists — no service performance reviews, no SLA breach records, no independent testing of the MSSP's detection capability — making it appear the institution has delegated rather than governed the monitoring obligation
suggestedSources: SIEM platform administration console for log source inventory, coverage maps, and detection rule library (Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Splunk SIEM)
SOC operational dashboards, shift logs, and monthly security operations reports prepared for management
ITSM platform for security incident tickets showing MTTD and MTTR timestamps (ServiceNow Security Incident Response module)
MSSP service review records if monitoring is outsourced, including SLA reports and quarterly service reviews
Threat hunting activity logs and hunt reports maintained by the threat intelligence or SOC team
evidenceItems.format: System-exported SIEM coverage reports (PDF/CSV); SOC operational reports (PDF); ITSM metric dashboards (PDF/XLSX); MSSP SLA performance reports (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan, Crisis Playbook & Information Sharing Framework
evidenceItems.description: The suite of operational response documentation mandated by sub-clauses (f), (g), and (i), comprising detailed incident handling policies and procedures, a crisis response management playbook, and policies governing secure information sharing with other financial institutions and financial market infrastructure participants. This evidence must collectively demonstrate that the institution is operationally prepared to respond to and recover from cybersecurity incidents, manage board and regulatory communications under crisis conditions, and participate in the financial sector's collective cyber resilience through structured information sharing. Sub-clause (i) evidence — the cyber risk management function's Terms of Reference, escalation records, and board reporting — is also captured here.
whatGoodLooksLike: The incident response policy and procedures are granular and role-specific, defining escalation thresholds with explicit time-bound triggers (e.g., escalate to CISO within one hour of P1 classification, notify BNM within defined regulatory timeframes), and are supplemented by scenario-specific playbooks for high-probability incident types such as ransomware, DDoS, data exfiltration, and insider threat

The crisis response management playbook documents the full crisis lifecycle including activation criteria, command structure, internal and external communication templates (including BNM regulatory notification drafts and public communications scripts), board engagement procedures, and recovery decision points — with evidence of at least one tabletop or full simulation exercise conducted within the past 12 months

Information sharing participation is evidenced through membership records in sector sharing forums (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM-facilitated CERT coordination mechanisms) and documented internal procedures governing what intelligence can be shared, through which authorised channels, and with what information classification controls applied to protect sensitive operational data

The cyber risk management function's Terms of Reference explicitly define its mandate to analyse threats and escalate high-risk matters, its reporting line to senior management and the board, and whether it operates as an in-house team or leverages a parent or group function — with organisational charts, staffing records, and evidence of qualified personnel filling the defined roles

Board and senior management reporting records (board papers, committee minutes) demonstrate that the cyber risk management function regularly escalates threat assessments and risk positions to the appropriate governance level, with evidence that the board has received and actioned cyber risk escalations within the review period
commonGaps: Incident response procedures exist at a high level but lack the operational specificity needed for crisis execution — no escalation time thresholds, no pre-drafted regulatory notification templates, no defined roles for each response phase — meaning the procedures would not guide staff effectively during an actual incident

The crisis response playbook has never been tested through a tabletop exercise or simulation, and the institution cannot demonstrate that response teams understand their roles or that the playbook's assumptions (e.g., system recovery timeframes, communication channel availability) have been validated against operational reality

Information sharing participation is limited to passive receipt of threat intelligence circulars with no reciprocal sharing capability or process, and no documented procedure governs how received intelligence is actioned internally — failing the proactive and collaborative intent of sub-clause (g)

The cyber risk management function exists organisationally but lacks adequate resourcing or qualified personnel, with key roles unfilled or filled by staff without demonstrated cybersecurity competency, undermining the function's ability to deliver the analytical and escalation mandate of sub-clause (i)

Board and senior management reporting records show cyber risk updates are infrequent, superficial, or limited to status dashboards without evidence of substantive escalation of high-risk threats — failing to demonstrate that the function performs the 'timely escalation of high-risk cyber threats' required by sub-clause (i)
suggestedSources: ITSM or GRC platform for incident response policy documents, procedure workflows, and incident handling records (ServiceNow, Archer, RSA Archer)
Crisis management team documentation repository (SharePoint, Confluence) for the crisis response playbook and tabletop exercise after-action reports
FS-ISAC membership portal, BNM regulatory correspondence files, and sector CERT coordination records for information sharing participation evidence
HR and organisational records for the cyber risk management function's staffing structure, job descriptions, and professional qualification records
Board secretariat or Company Secretary records for board committee papers, minutes, and cyber risk escalation records presented to the board or board risk committee
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX incident response policy and playbooks; PDF tabletop exercise after-action reports; PDF/DOCX information sharing policy; PDF board papers and committee minutes

auditTips: Conduct a pre-examination CRF completeness check against each of the nine sub-clauses (a) through (i) as a formal gap assessment — produce a traceability matrix that maps each sub-clause obligation to a specific named document or system record, the document owner, the last review date, and where it is stored. Examiners will expect this kind of structured evidence mapping and its absence suggests the institution has not systematically verified its own compliance.

Cross-reference the critical systems register (sub-clause b), the SIEM log source coverage map (sub-clause e), and the automated asset inventory (sub-clause h) to identify assets that appear in one list but not the others. Any uncovered critical system represents a simultaneous gap across three sub-clauses and will draw immediate examiner scrutiny; resolving these gaps before examination demonstrates operational integration of CRF elements.

For sub-clause (d) compliance, prepare a specific zero-trust implementation evidence pack rather than relying solely on policy statements. This should include identity governance platform screenshots showing MFA enforcement rates and privileged access recertification completion, network segmentation diagrams with current-state validation dates, and at least one control effectiveness test result (e.g., a red team finding that was detected and contained by a specific defence layer). Policy without technical corroboration will not satisfy BNM examiners.

Validate that the cyber risk management function under sub-clause (i) has a documented reporting line that reaches the board, and collect at least 12 months of board and senior management meeting minutes showing actual cyber risk agenda items, presented reports, and board decisions or acknowledgements. A function that exists on an organisational chart but cannot produce evidence of regular board-level engagement will be treated as non-functional by examiners.

Test the crisis response playbook's regulatory notification provisions by walking through a simulated ransomware scenario and identifying exactly when the BNM notification obligation would be triggered, who is authorised to make the notification, and where the pre-drafted notification template is stored. Ensure the template references current BNM reporting requirements. Examiners frequently test whether institutions know their own notification timelines and have operational procedures to meet them.

For the information sharing obligation under sub-clause (g), prepare documentation showing not just membership of sharing forums but the institution's actual participation — threat indicators submitted, alerts actioned, or joint exercises attended. Passive membership without demonstrated two-way engagement may be assessed as partial compliance only, particularly given BNM's emphasis on collective resilience across the financial sector.

Sequence your evidence review to mirror the examiner's likely workflow: start with the CRF master document and trace each of its nine elements to supporting operational evidence. If the CRF references a procedure or system that cannot be produced or demonstrated during examination, treat that as a finding-in-waiting and either produce the evidence or formally document a remediation commitment with a realistic timeline prior to examination commencement.

11.4.title: NCII Compliance Requirements
11.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will first confirm whether the institution has received a formal NCII designation under the Cyber Security Act 2024 and, if so, verify that the institution has operationalised a structured compliance programme specifically aligned to NCII obligations — not merely mapped existing controls. A key red flag is an institution that treats NCII compliance as synonymous with general RMiT compliance without demonstrating awareness of additional NACSA-specific directives, standards, or reporting channels. Examiners will scrutinise whether the institution maintains an active register of NACSA-issued directives and can demonstrate timely implementation and attestation against each. They will also probe governance accountability, looking for a named NCII compliance owner at senior management level and evidence that the Board or Risk Committee receives periodic NCII-specific reporting distinct from the general cybersecurity dashboard. Institutions that cannot produce a NACSA correspondence log, a gap-closure plan tied to NCII standards, or evidence of participation in NACSA-coordinated exercises will be considered materially non-compliant regardless of overall cybersecurity posture.

evidenceItems.name: NCII Designation Certificate and Sector Classification Record
evidenceItems.description: The official gazette notification, designation letter, or formal written communication from the relevant NCII Sector Lead (Ministry of Finance / BNM acting as Sector Lead under the Cyber Security Act 2024) confirming the institution's status as a designated NCII entity, including the sector classification and effective date of designation. This establishes the legal trigger for all downstream NCII compliance obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Official designation instrument citing the Cyber Security Act 2024 is on file, undated less than the Act's effective date, and retained in the institution's regulatory correspondence archive
A Board or Senior Management resolution acknowledges the NCII designation and formally assigns accountability for NCII compliance to a named Chief Information Security Officer or equivalent
The designation record is cross-referenced in the institution's GRC platform against all derived NCII obligations so that designation status automatically triggers related control requirements
Annual reconfirmation or self-declaration of NCII status is documented, reflecting awareness that designation criteria may be revised by NACSA
Legal and Compliance department has annotated the designation with a summary of obligations triggered, reviewed by external counsel familiar with the Cyber Security Act 2024
commonGaps: Institution is unaware of or cannot produce the formal designation instrument, relying only on internal assumption that it qualifies as NCII based on asset criticality
Designation is filed in Legal but not shared with the CISO or technology risk function, creating a disconnect between compliance ownership and operational accountability
No Board-level acknowledgement of NCII designation exists, meaning governance accountability for NCII-specific obligations has never been formally assigned
The designation document predates the Cyber Security Act 2024 and has not been refreshed under the new legislative framework, leaving the institution operating under a superseded reference
Sector classification is recorded incorrectly or the institution has not confirmed with its Sector Lead whether sub-sector standards apply to its specific business lines
suggestedSources: Legal and Regulatory Affairs department — correspondence files and regulatory register
Company Secretary's office — Board resolutions and regulatory notifications folder
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or Archer) — regulatory obligations register
CISO office — regulatory engagement tracker
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scanned official letter or gazette extract) + Board resolution (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: NACSA Directives and Standards Compliance Register
evidenceItems.description: A living register that inventories every directive, circular, standard, and guideline issued by NACSA applicable to the institution as an NCII entity, mapped against the institution's implemented controls, compliance status, responsible owner, and target/actual remediation dates for any identified gaps. This register is the primary operational artefact demonstrating that the institution monitors, tracks, and acts on NACSA issuances systematically rather than reactively.
whatGoodLooksLike: The register is maintained in the GRC platform or a version-controlled document with a clear update frequency (at minimum quarterly) and a named custodian responsible for monitoring NACSA publications
Each NACSA issuance is logged with its reference number, issuance date, compliance deadline, applicability rationale, and a direct mapping to the institution's internal control framework or policy

Compliance status for each directive shows evidence of implementation (policy update, technical configuration, training completion) with supporting artefact references rather than self-attestation alone
Gap items carry a documented risk acceptance or remediation plan approved at the appropriate management level, with escalation to the Risk Committee for items breaching defined thresholds
The register distinguishes between mandatory directives (S-marker equivalent) and guidance-level publications, applying proportionate rigour to each category
commonGaps: Register exists but covers only NACSA publications received after a certain date, with no retrospective review of directives issued at or after the Cyber Security Act 2024 commencement
Compliance status column is populated with 'Compliant' without any referenced evidence artefacts, making assertions unverifiable during examination
NACSA standards are listed but not decomposed into control-level requirements, leaving it unclear which specific controls address each obligation
The register is updated only when an examination is imminent rather than on a continuous or periodic basis, evidenced by bulk update timestamps immediately preceding the audit date

No process exists to detect newly issued NACSA directives; the institution relies on informal notification rather than a formal monitoring mechanism such as NACSA portal subscriptions or Sector Lead circulars
suggestedSources: GRC platform — regulatory change management module (ServiceNow, Archer, MetricStream, or OneTrust)
CISO or Technology Risk team — NCII compliance workgroup SharePoint site
Legal and Compliance — regulatory horizon-scanning tracker
NACSA official portal and Sector Lead communication records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (XLSX/PDF), version-controlled with change history

evidenceItems.name: NCII Compliance Assessment and Gap Closure Evidence Pack
evidenceItems.description: Documented results of periodic compliance assessments conducted against NACSA-issued standards and the institution's NCII obligations, including gap analysis findings, remediation plans, and closure evidence. This pack demonstrates that the institution conducts structured self-assessments — not merely point-in-time audits — and systematically closes identified deficiencies. It is the primary evidence that compliance is an ongoing process rather than a documentation exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessments are conducted at minimum annually or upon issuance of a material new NACSA directive, with scope explicitly tied to NCII-specific requirements rather than reusing the general cybersecurity audit programme
Each assessment report is signed off by the CISO and presented to the Board Risk Committee or equivalent, with minutes confirming discussion and oversight

Gap items from prior assessments are tracked to verified closure, with supporting evidence (screenshots, configuration exports, updated policy versions, training completion reports) attached to each closure record
The assessment methodology references the specific NACSA standards or NCII framework being assessed, demonstrating deliberate alignment rather than generic control testing
Independent validation of gap closure is performed by Internal Audit or an approved third party, separate from the team that implemented the remediation
commonGaps: Assessment reports exist but are indistinguishable from general IT audit reports, with no specific callout of NCII obligations or NACSA standard references
Gap closure is self-certified by the remediation team without independent validation, creating a conflict of interest that examiners will flag
Assessments are conducted irregularly, triggered by upcoming examinations rather than a defined assessment calendar, undermining the credibility of the compliance programme
Remediation plans lack specific milestones and accountable owners, making it impossible to verify whether progress is on track between assessment cycles
The most recent assessment predates a material NACSA directive issuance, meaning the institution has not evaluated its posture against current requirements
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — audit universe and findings tracker (TeamMate, AuditBoard, or equivalent)
CISO office — NCII compliance programme SharePoint or confluence site
GRC platform — issue management and remediation tracking module
Board Risk Committee or IT Risk Committee — meeting minutes and board papers
evidenceItems.format: PDF assessment report + XLSX gap tracker + PDF closure evidence attachments

evidenceItems.name: NACSA and Sector Lead Correspondence Log
evidenceItems.description: A chronological log of all formal communications between the institution and NACSA or the designated Sector Lead under the Cyber Security Act 2024, including submissions, notifications, enquiries, responses, and directives received. This log demonstrates that the institution maintains an active and responsive regulatory relationship with NACSA as required of a designated NCII entity, and that no mandatory reporting or notification obligations have been missed.
whatGoodLooksLike: The log is comprehensive, capturing inbound directives, outbound submissions (including mandatory incident notifications), and acknowledgement receipts, with no gaps in the chronological sequence since the Cyber Security Act 2024 commencement
Each entry references the relevant NACSA issuance number or correspondence reference, the internal action taken, the responsible officer, and the resolution date

Mandatory NCII incident reports are cross-referenced to the institution's internal cyber incident register, confirming that reporting timelines stipulated by NACSA were met for every qualifying incident
Correspondence related to NCII exercises, drills, or sector-level engagements (e.g., NACSA-coordinated threat briefings) is included, demonstrating active participation
The log is reviewed by the CISO and Legal and Compliance on a quarterly basis, with sign-off documented to evidence senior management oversight of the regulatory relationship
commonGaps: Correspondence with NACSA is scattered across multiple email inboxes with no centralised log, making it impossible to produce a complete record during an examination
Incident notifications to NACSA are not recorded in the log or are recorded without confirmation of receipt, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate timely reporting compliance
The log commences from an arbitrary date rather than from the institution's NCII designation date, creating a gap period with unverifiable compliance history
Communications from NACSA that contain implicit compliance obligations (e.g., sector-level threat advisories requiring action) are filed as informational only with no action tracking

There is no defined process for routing NACSA communications to the correct internal function within a specified timeframe, resulting in delays between receipt and action that are visible in the log timestamps
suggestedSources: Legal and Regulatory Affairs — regulatory correspondence management system or SharePoint archive
CISO office — regulatory engagement folder in document management system
Cyber incident management platform (e.g., ServiceNow Security Incident Response) — NACSA notification sub-records
Company Secretary's office — official inward correspondence register
evidenceItems.format: XLSX log with email/letter attachments in PDF, stored in a version-controlled repository

evidenceItems.name: NCII Incident Reporting Records and Timeline Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has identified, classified, and reported cybersecurity incidents to NACSA and the Sector Lead within the prescribed timelines under the Cyber Security Act 2024 and applicable NACSA directives. This includes incident classification criteria, internal escalation records, NACSA notification submissions, and post-incident reports. For institutions with no qualifying incidents in the review period, documented evidence of the classification process and its application to all incidents must still be produced.
whatGoodLooksLike: The institution's cyber incident classification framework explicitly incorporates NCII-specific reporting thresholds as defined by NACSA, distinguishing between incidents requiring notification to NACSA versus those reportable only internally or to BNM

For every incident classified as NCII-reportable in the review period, a complete reporting package exists: initial notification (with timestamp), NACSA acknowledgement, preliminary report, and final investigation report, all within stipulated timeframes
Incident timelines are corroborated by SIEM or SOAR platform logs showing detection time, classification time, and notification dispatch time, enabling objective verification of reporting timeliness

Where no NCII-reportable incidents occurred in the review period, the institution can demonstrate that all incidents were assessed against NCII reporting thresholds with documented classification rationale
Post-incident reviews for NCII-reportable incidents include lessons learned that feed back into the NACSA compliance register and, where applicable, triggered updates to NCII-specific controls
commonGaps: The institution's incident classification taxonomy does not reference NACSA reporting thresholds, meaning classification decisions for NCII reporting purposes are made ad hoc without a documented framework
Notification timestamps cannot be verified independently because submissions to NACSA were made verbally or via informal channels without documentary confirmation of the exact dispatch time
Post-incident reports submitted to NACSA are not retained internally in a retrievable format, with the institution only able to produce the initial notification but not the full reporting lifecycle
Incidents that were not classified as NCII-reportable have no documented rationale for the classification decision, leaving open questions about whether underreporting occurred
The CISO was not involved in the NCII reporting decision for significant incidents; classification was delegated to operational staff without the requisite knowledge of NCII thresholds
suggestedSources: Cyber incident management platform — incident register with NCII classification flag (ServiceNow SIR, Jira Service Management, or equivalent)
SIEM / SOAR platform — incident timeline and alert correlation logs
CISO office — NACSA submission copies and acknowledgement records
Legal and Regulatory Affairs — NACSA correspondence log (cross-reference with 11.4-E4)
evidenceItems.format: PDF incident reports + system exports from SIEM/SOAR (PDF/CSV) + PDF NACSA submission confirmations

evidenceItems.name: NCII Governance Framework and Board Reporting Records
evidenceItems.description: Governance documentation establishing the institution's NCII compliance programme structure, including the NCII compliance policy, terms of reference for the NCII-specific oversight committee or working group, role assignments, and Board or Risk Committee reporting records showing periodic NCII compliance status updates. This evidence demonstrates that NCII obligations are governed at the appropriate organisational level and that accountability is embedded in the institution's governance architecture — not treated as a purely operational matter.
whatGoodLooksLike: A standalone NCII compliance policy or a clearly delineated NCII section within the cybersecurity policy exists, approved by the Board, referencing the Cyber Security Act 2024 and NACSA as the regulatory basis

A named senior officer (CISO or equivalent) holds documented accountability for NCII compliance, with this responsibility reflected in their role description, performance objectives, and annual attestation

Board Risk Committee or equivalent receives an NCII compliance report at minimum semi-annually, with meeting minutes showing substantive engagement (questions raised, decisions made) rather than passive receipt

An NCII compliance working group or steering committee with defined membership, meeting cadence, and terms of reference operates and produces documented meeting minutes capturing actions, risks, and escalations

The institution's Three Lines of Defence model explicitly addresses NCII compliance: first line operational ownership, second line Technology Risk monitoring, and third line Internal Audit coverage — each with documented outputs
commonGaps: NCII compliance is governed entirely within existing cybersecurity committee structures without any specific agenda items, resolutions, or reports dedicated to NCII obligations, making it impossible to demonstrate NCII-specific Board oversight

The NCII compliance policy references only RMiT and does not acknowledge the Cyber Security Act 2024 or NACSA, indicating the governance framework has not been updated to reflect the current regulatory landscape

Accountability for NCII compliance is informally understood but not formally documented in any role description, committee charter, or delegation of authority, creating a gap that examiners will characterise as governance failure

Board reporting on NCII compliance is included as a line item in a broader cybersecurity dashboard without sufficient granularity for directors to assess NCII-specific risk posture or compliance status
Internal Audit has not included NCII compliance within its audit universe, meaning no independent assurance over the NCII compliance programme has been provided to the Board
suggestedSources: Company Secretary's office — Board and Risk Committee meeting minutes and board papers
CISO office — NCII governance framework documentation and working group minutes
Human Resources — CISO and technology risk officer role descriptions
Internal Audit — audit universe, audit plan, and NCII-related audit reports
GRC platform — policy register with approval workflow and review history
evidenceItems.format: PDF policy documents + PDF Board/committee papers and minutes + DOCX terms of reference

auditTips: Before the examination, obtain written confirmation from the institution's Sector Lead or NACSA of the current designation status and applicable sector-specific standards — BNM examiners will verify this directly and any discrepancy between the institution's records and NACSA's registry will be treated as a critical finding.

Conduct a dedicated pre-examination drill specifically on NCII evidence retrieval: test whether the NACSA correspondence log, directives register, and incident reporting records can be produced within 24 hours, as BNM may request these on short notice during the on-site examination.

Map every NACSA directive and standard in the compliance register to a specific RMiT control or internal policy reference, then cross-reference this mapping during evidence review to ensure that no obligation is addressed by a control that has already been found deficient in a previous RMiT examination — overlapping gaps compound risk ratings.

Interview the CISO and at least one Board Risk Committee member prior to the examination to verify that both can articulate the institution's NCII obligations and recent compliance activities from memory — BNM examiners frequently conduct interviews separate from document review and inconsistencies between documentary evidence and oral responses are weighted heavily.

Verify that the institution's cyber incident classification matrix contains an explicit NCII-reporting decision tree, then walk through two or three historical incidents (including at least one that was assessed but not reported to NACSA) to confirm the classification rationale is documented and defensible against the Cyber Security Act 2024 notification thresholds.

Ensure the NCII compliance programme documentation post-dates the Cyber Security Act 2024 commencement — examiners will discount any governance framework, policy, or register that was clearly drafted before the Act as not genuinely responsive to the NCII designation, even if the content is otherwise sound.

Prepare a one-page NCII compliance status summary for senior management to present at the examination opening meeting: include the designation date, total number of NACSA directives received and their compliance status, open gaps with target closure dates, and incidents reported to NACSA in the review period — this demonstrates command of the subject matter and typically sets a constructive tone for the remainder of the examination.

11.5.title: Cybersecurity Control Measures
11.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has systematically implemented the full suite of cybersecurity controls enumerated in Appendix 5 of RMiT — not merely acknowledged them. Examiners look for evidence of deliberate operationalisation: each control domain (endpoint protection, network security, identity and access management, patch management, security monitoring, and data loss prevention) must be demonstrably active and periodically validated. A key red flag is gap-only documentation — institutions that produce a controls register showing outstanding items without a remediation timeline attract immediate scrutiny. Examiners will cross-reference the controls register against independent assurance outputs such as penetration test reports and vulnerability assessment results to verify that self-assessed compliance is substantiated. Institutions that cannot trace each Appendix 5 control to an owner, an implementation artefact, and a testing record are considered non-compliant regardless of policy-level commitments.

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 5 Controls Implementation Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured mapping document that lists every cybersecurity control specified in RMiT Appendix 5, records its implementation status, assigns an accountable owner, and links to supporting artefacts. This demonstrates that the institution has systematically addressed each mandated control rather than selectively implementing convenient measures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every control in Appendix 5 is enumerated row-by-row with no omissions, including sub-controls where applicable

Each control row shows implementation status (Implemented / Partially Implemented / Not Applicable with documented justification), the accountable business or technology owner, and a hyperlink or reference to supporting evidence
Controls marked Partially Implemented are accompanied by a time-bound remediation plan with milestone dates reviewed by senior management
The register carries a version history, a last-reviewed date within the past 12 months, and a sign-off from the CISO or equivalent
Not-Applicable exclusions are supported by a formal risk acceptance or compensating control rationale approved by the Risk Committee
commonGaps: Register exists but was created once at policy rollout and has not been updated to reflect infrastructure or threat landscape changes — dates reveal it is 18–36 months stale
Controls are marked 'Implemented' based on intent or policy existence rather than validated deployment; no link to technical evidence such as configuration screenshots or tool exports
Not-Applicable exclusions lack documented business justification and have not been reviewed or approved by a risk governance body
Ownership column is blank or assigned to a generic team rather than a named individual, making accountability untraceable during examination
Partial implementations carry no remediation timeline, leaving examiners unable to assess whether the institution is progressing toward full compliance
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — Controls library module
SharePoint or Confluence — Cybersecurity governance repository maintained by the CISO office
Internal audit management system — Control self-assessment workpapers
CISO or IT Risk department — Annual cybersecurity posture review documentation
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF/CSV)

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability and Patch Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence of the institution's vulnerability identification and patch remediation lifecycle, demonstrating that the Appendix 5 requirements for timely patching and exposure reduction are operationally enforced across the technology estate including endpoints, servers, network devices, and internet-facing systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: Authenticated vulnerability scan reports generated at least monthly (or more frequently for critical systems), covering all in-scope assets with no undocumented exclusions
