Patch management policy states defined SLA timings (e.g., Critical patches within 7 days, High within 30 days) and scan results show adherence rates tracked and reported to management
Exceptions and deferred patches are logged on a risk register with a documented compensating control, an expiry date, and risk-owner sign-off
Scan results are trended over time, showing a declining or consistently low open critical vulnerability count — demonstrating continuous improvement rather than point-in-time compliance
Evidence that vulnerability findings feed into the broader risk management cycle — for example, recurring critical gaps escalated to the IT Risk Committee with minutes reflecting discussion
commonGaps: Scans are performed but results are not acted upon systematically — open Critical and High findings persist beyond SLA with no documented justification or compensating control
Scan scope excludes operational technology (OT), legacy systems, or network appliances — creating blind spots that examiners specifically probe
Patch exception log is absent or informal; deferred patches cannot be traced to a risk approval, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate managed risk acceptance
Scan credentials are unauthenticated, resulting in significantly under-reported vulnerability counts and providing a false picture of the security posture
No trend data is presented to senior management or the board; vulnerability management is treated as an operational activity rather than a risk indicator
suggestedSources: Vulnerability scanner platform (e.g., Tenable Nessus/SC, Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM) — scan report exports
ITSM system (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) — patch ticket queues and SLA compliance dashboards
IT Risk register — patch exception entries
IT Steering Committee or Risk Committee minutes — vulnerability status reports tabled as agenda items
evidenceItems.format: PDF scan reports, Spreadsheet (SLA tracking), ITSM system export

evidenceItems.name: Identity and Access Management (IAM) Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution enforces Appendix 5 identity and access management requirements, including least-privilege access provisioning, privileged access management, multi-factor authentication (MFA) deployment, and periodic access recertification — collectively preventing unauthorised access that could enable cyber-attacks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Access review reports (at minimum quarterly for privileged accounts, semi-annually for general users) with documented evidence of review completion, accounts revoked or modified as a result, and sign-off by access owners

Privileged Access Management (PAM) tool deployment evidence showing that all privileged accounts (domain admins, database admins, cloud IAM admins) are vaulted, session-recorded, and subject to just-in-time access workflows

MFA enforcement reports from identity provider (e.g., Azure AD / Entra ID Conditional Access, Okta) demonstrating MFA is mandatory for remote access, privileged accounts, and internet-facing applications with no standing exemptions

Joiners-Movers-Leavers (JML) process records showing timely deprovisioning — leavers' access revoked within the institution's stated SLA (ideally same day for involuntary departures), evidenced by HR-to-IT workflow tickets
Documented role-based access control (RBAC) matrix or access entitlement catalogue aligned to the principle of least privilege, reviewed and signed off annually
commonGaps: Access recertification is performed but certifiers rubber-stamp approvals in bulk without evidence of genuine review — certifier comments are absent and no accounts are ever revoked during the review cycle
Orphaned accounts (accounts of departed staff) exist in Active Directory or application directories, indicating the JML process is not reliably executed — a critical finding for examiners
MFA is deployed for remote access but not enforced for internal privileged account use or cloud console access, leaving a significant attack surface unaddressed
Shared or generic accounts (e.g., svc_admin, oracle_admin) are in active use without individual accountability, making attribution of privileged actions impossible
PAM solution is deployed but session recording is disabled or logs are not retained for the required period, defeating the accountability purpose of the control
suggestedSources: Identity provider (Azure AD / Entra ID, Okta, PingIdentity) — access reports, MFA compliance dashboards, Conditional Access policy exports
PAM platform (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) — vault account inventory, session recording audit logs
ITSM system — JML workflow tickets (onboarding/offboarding requests and completions)
HR system — termination records cross-referenced against IT deprovisioning timestamps
GRC platform — access review campaign completion reports
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/PDF), GRC platform report, ITSM ticket extract

evidenceItems.name: Security Monitoring and Incident Detection Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution operates continuous security monitoring capabilities consistent with Appendix 5 requirements, including a functioning Security Operations Centre (SOC) or equivalent, SIEM deployment with use-case coverage, and alert triage records demonstrating timely detection and escalation of security events.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM use-case catalogue mapping detection rules to the MITRE ATT&CK framework or equivalent threat model, showing coverage of the top threat scenarios relevant to the institution's risk profile

SOC operational records evidencing 24x7 monitoring capability — shift handover logs, analyst triage tickets, and escalation trails for security events, demonstrating the monitoring is live and not simply tool-deployed
Mean-Time-to-Detect (MTTD) and Mean-Time-to-Respond (MTTR) metrics reported to management at defined intervals, with trend data showing performance against defined SLAs

Threat intelligence integration evidence — records showing that external threat feeds (e.g., FS-ISAC, MyCERT, commercial threat intel) are ingested into the SIEM and used to tune detection rules or block indicators of compromise

Regular SIEM health-check or tuning reports confirming that log sources are active, ingestion is complete, and detection rules are maintained and tested (e.g., purple team exercises or detection validation results)
commonGaps: SIEM is deployed but critical log sources (Active Directory, endpoint agents, cloud audit logs, OT/SCADA systems) are not ingested, creating monitoring blind spots that examiners identify by reviewing the SIEM source inventory

Alert triage records do not exist or are incomplete — high-severity alerts are raised but there is no documented evidence of analyst investigation, containment decision, or escalation, suggesting alerts are silently suppressed or ignored
SOC coverage is business-hours only with no documented out-of-hours escalation path, leaving the institution exposed outside standard working hours

Detection use cases have not been reviewed or updated in over 12 months and do not reflect current threat intelligence or the institution's changed infrastructure (e.g., cloud migration not reflected in monitoring scope)
MTTD/MTTR metrics are not tracked or reported to senior management, meaning governance oversight of the monitoring function's effectiveness is absent
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar, Elastic SIEM) — use-case registry, log source inventory, alert/incident queue
SOC ticketing system (ServiceNow Security Operations, PagerDuty, Jira) — incident triage and escalation records
SOC management reporting — monthly or quarterly security operations metrics dashboards tabled to the IT Risk Committee
Threat intelligence platform (MISP, Recorded Future, Anomali) — feed integration and IOC blocking evidence
MyCERT / FS-ISAC membership and alert acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.format: System exports (PDF/CSV), Dashboard screenshots, Management reports (PDF/PPTX)

evidenceItems.name: Independent Security Assessment Reports (Penetration Testing and Red Team)
evidenceItems.description: Reports from independent third-party or sufficiently independent internal assessments that validate the effectiveness of Appendix 5 cybersecurity controls in practice, including penetration testing of external perimeter, internal network, web applications, and where applicable, red team or adversary simulation exercises.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual (or more frequent) penetration test reports covering external perimeter, internal network, and critical applications, conducted by an independent and suitably qualified party — assessor credentials (e.g., CREST-accredited, OSCP-certified) are documented

Findings are risk-rated and each finding has a corresponding remediation entry in the vulnerability or risk register with an assigned owner, target remediation date, and evidence of closure (retest results)

Scope of assessments is comprehensive and aligns with the institution's critical asset inventory — cherry-picked or narrow scopes that exclude legacy systems or cloud environments are flagged as a gap
Executive summary and board-level reporting exists, demonstrating that assessment results are escalated to governance bodies rather than contained within the IT function

Retest or remediation validation reports confirm that critical and high findings from prior assessments have been resolved, showing a closed-loop remediation process rather than findings that recur across consecutive assessments
commonGaps: Penetration tests are conducted but findings are not tracked to closure — the same critical or high vulnerabilities reappear across consecutive annual assessments, indicating that remediation is not enforced
Scope limitations are excessive and undocumented — key systems such as internet banking platforms, payment gateways, or cloud infrastructure are excluded without a documented risk-based rationale
Tests are conducted by internal IT staff who also manage the systems under test, compromising independence and the reliability of findings
Remediation evidence is absent — the institution asserts findings are closed but cannot produce a retest report, configuration change record, or patch evidence to substantiate closure

Assessment frequency does not reflect risk — material infrastructure changes (cloud migration, new payment channel, M&A integration) occur between assessment cycles without triggering an ad hoc reassessment
suggestedSources: CISO office or IT Risk department — retained third-party penetration test reports and statements of work
Risk or vulnerability register — findings imported from assessment reports with remediation tracking
IT Steering Committee or Risk Committee minutes — agenda items tabling assessment results and remediation progress
Vendor management system — contracts and scope-of-work documents for retained security testing providers
Internal Audit department — any co-sourced or supervised penetration testing workpapers
evidenceItems.format: PDF (assessment report), Spreadsheet (findings tracker), PDF (retest confirmation report)

evidenceItems.name: Endpoint and Network Security Controls Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical and governance artefacts demonstrating that endpoint protection and network security controls mandated by Appendix 5 are deployed, configured to an approved baseline, and continuously monitored — including endpoint detection and response (EDR), network segmentation, firewall rule management, and data loss prevention (DLP).
whatGoodLooksLike: EDR or endpoint protection platform (EPP) deployment report showing coverage rate across all managed endpoints (workstations, servers, mobile devices) with targets defined in policy and gaps tracked to remediation; configuration confirms real-time protection, tamper protection, and central management are enabled

Network segmentation diagram accompanied by firewall rule review records — critical network zones (e.g., payment processing, core banking, DMZ) are isolated with documented and justified access control lists, reviewed at least annually
Firewall change management records showing all rule changes are subject to formal request, risk assessment, approval, and post-implementation review — no undocumented or standing temporary rules

DLP policy documentation and incident log demonstrating that sensitive data exfiltration controls (email DLP, endpoint DLP, cloud DLP) are active, tuned to the institution's data classification scheme, and that DLP alerts are reviewed and acted upon

Security configuration baseline (hardening standards) for key asset classes (Windows endpoints, Linux servers, network devices, cloud workloads) with compliance scan results showing adherence rate, and non-compliant items tracked to remediation
commonGaps: EDR coverage is below 100% with no documented gap remediation plan — servers, legacy systems, or recently onboarded subsidiaries are unprotected and the gap is unknown to management until the examination

Network segmentation diagrams are out of date and do not reflect current architecture — actual firewall rules permit broader lateral movement than the diagram implies, which examiners discover by comparing diagrams to live rule exports
Firewall rules contain large numbers of undocumented, long-standing temporary rules or 'any-to-any' rules that have never been reviewed, indicating that change control is applied inconsistently
DLP is deployed but operates in monitor-only or audit mode rather than blocking mode for critical data classifications, providing visibility without prevention — a key distinction examiners probe
No security hardening baseline exists for cloud workloads or containerised environments, reflecting that security configuration management has not kept pace with infrastructure modernisation
suggestedSources: EDR/EPP platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne) — deployment coverage dashboard, policy configuration export
Firewall management platform (Tufin, AlgoSec, Palo Alto Panorama) — rule review reports, change request history
Network documentation repository (SharePoint, Confluence) — current network segmentation and data flow diagrams
DLP platform (Microsoft Purview, Symantec DLP, Forcepoint) — policy configuration, incident log exports
Configuration management database (CMDB) or vulnerability scanner — hardening compliance scan results per asset class
evidenceItems.format: System exports (PDF/CSV), Network diagrams (Visio/PDF), Spreadsheet (compliance tracking)

auditTips: Before the examination, perform a control-by-control walkthrough of Appendix 5 against your implementation register — confirm that every control has a named owner, a dated implementation artefact, and a test result. Address any rows where evidence cannot be produced within 24 hours, as examiners will request specific items on-site.

Prepare a single consolidated evidence pack with a cover sheet that maps each Appendix 5 control to its corresponding evidence item using clear cross-references (e.g., 'Control A5.3 — see Tab 3: Patch SLA Report Q4 2025'). Examiners respond positively to institutions that demonstrate organised, pre-mapped evidence rather than producing documents reactively.

Cross-reference penetration test findings against your vulnerability register and patch records — examiners frequently test whether findings from the most recent assessment appear as open items in the risk register and whether the remediation timeline is being met. Unexplained gaps between test findings and register entries are a major red flag.

Validate your security monitoring scope by extracting the SIEM log source inventory and comparing it against your critical asset register. Any critical asset not sending logs to the SIEM should be documented as a risk-accepted gap with a compensating control, or remediated before examination. Undisclosed monitoring blind spots discovered by examiners carry greater weight than self-disclosed gaps.

Ensure that governance escalation trails are complete — for each control domain, confirm that metrics and exception reports have been tabled at a named governance body (IT Risk Committee, Board Risk Committee) with minutes that record discussion and decisions. Evidence of controls without governance visibility will lead examiners to question whether the board has adequate oversight of cyber risk as required under RMiT 11.1.

Prepare your CISO or Head of IT Security to walk examiners through the end-to-end lifecycle of a security incident detected by the SOC — from alert trigger through triage, escalation, containment, and closure. This narrative demonstration validates that monitoring controls are operationally effective and not simply tool-deployed, which is a common examiner technique for distinguishing genuine from paper compliance.

Review access recertification records immediately before examination and confirm that the most recent review cycle produced documented outcomes — specifically, accounts that were revoked or modified as a result of review. A recertification process where no accounts are ever revoked in any cycle will be challenged by examiners as evidence that the review is not genuinely performed.

11.6.title: Red Team Simulation Attacks
11.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has conducted a genuine, adversary-simulated Red Team exercise — not a standard penetration test — against its production infrastructure within the mandatory three-year cycle. Examiners will scrutinise the realism and breadth of the engagement scope, looking for evidence that attack scenarios reflect current threat-actor tactics relevant to the Malaysian financial sector (e.g., APT groups, ransomware operators). A key red flag is conflating a vulnerability assessment or standard penetration test with a Red Team exercise; examiners will probe whether the engagement involved multi-stage attack chains, lateral movement, and objective-based scenarios without prior disclosure to defensive teams. Examiners also verify that findings were formally tracked to closure and that the exercise outcomes informed updates to the institution's cyber risk posture, not merely filed away. Institutions that cannot demonstrate executive oversight, a formal remediation programme, and a documented next-exercise schedule will be viewed as failing the spirit of this obligation.

evidenceItems.name: Red Team Engagement Charter and Scope Agreement
evidenceItems.description: The formal authorisation document that defines the rules of engagement, scope boundaries, objectives, attack scenarios, timeline, and legal indemnity for the Red Team exercise. Demonstrates that the institution formally commissioned a realistic, structured adversary simulation rather than an ad-hoc or informal test.
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed authorisation from the CISO and at least one C-suite executive (e.g., CEO or CRO) confirming formal approval before exercise commencement

Scope document explicitly covers production infrastructure, not solely test environments, and identifies in-scope assets (networks, applications, third-party integrations, physical access where applicable)
Objectives are framed as adversarial goals (e.g., 'exfiltrate customer PII', 'achieve Domain Admin privilege', 'disrupt payment processing') rather than generic vulnerability discovery

Rules of engagement include clearly defined 'get-out-of-jail' contact procedures and a deconfliction process with the SOC, confirming the exercise was conducted without prior disclosure to defensive teams
Document references specific threat-actor profiles or TTPs (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK framework) relevant to Malaysian financial institutions that informed scenario design
commonGaps: Scope limited to non-production or isolated lab environments, meaning the exercise does not reflect real infrastructure risk as required by 'realistic' simulation language in the clause
Authorisation signed only at middle-management level (e.g., IT Manager) without CISO or board-level visibility, indicating insufficient governance of a high-risk activity
Objectives describe vulnerability discovery rather than adversarial goal attainment, making the exercise indistinguishable from a penetration test in the documentation
No documented deconfliction protocol, raising doubt about whether defensive teams were notified in advance and undermining the realism of the simulation
Missing or generic threat-actor framing — scenarios not grounded in threats relevant to the Malaysian financial sector, weakening the regulatory justification for the chosen attack scenarios
suggestedSources: CISO office or Cyber Risk team document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or internally managed risk register)
Third-party Red Team provider's contract and statement of work, held by Procurement or Legal
Board or Risk Committee secretariat for signed authorisation records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (signed, version-controlled)

evidenceItems.name: Red Team Final Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: The comprehensive technical and executive report produced by the Red Team at the conclusion of the exercise, detailing attack narratives, techniques used, vulnerabilities exploited, objectives achieved, and recommendations. This is the primary artefact demonstrating that a realistic simulation was conducted and its findings captured.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report includes a full attack narrative ('kill chain' or 'attack path') documenting each phase — initial access, persistence, lateral movement, privilege escalation, and objective achievement — using MITRE ATT&CK TTP references
Executive summary translates technical findings into business risk language, explicitly linking compromised assets to potential regulatory, financial, or reputational impact for the institution
Findings are risk-rated using a consistent, documented methodology (e.g., CVSS, or a custom risk matrix aligned to the institution's risk appetite) with clear prioritisation for remediation
Report distinguishes between vulnerabilities exploited by the Red Team versus those discovered but not exploited, providing a complete picture of the attack surface assessed

Report is dated, version-controlled, and clearly identifies the Red Team provider's credentials (e.g., CREST certification, OSCP/OSEP-certified testers), evidencing the exercise was conducted by qualified practitioners
commonGaps: Report reads as a standard penetration test output — a flat list of vulnerabilities with CVSS scores — rather than a narrative of objective-based attack scenarios, indicating a penetration test was mislabelled as a Red Team exercise
Attack paths are not mapped to MITRE ATT&CK or equivalent framework, making it impossible for examiners to assess the realism and sophistication of the simulation
No executive summary or board-level risk translation, suggesting findings were never meaningfully communicated to senior leadership as required by good governance

Report covers only technical findings with no reference to people or process weaknesses (e.g., phishing susceptibility, SOC detection failures), meaning the 'realistic' nature of the simulation is incomplete
Provider credentials and qualifications are absent from the report, raising questions about whether the exercise meets the standard implied by a genuine Red Team simulation
suggestedSources: CISO or Cyber Risk team secure document store (SharePoint with restricted access, or DLP-controlled file repository)
Third-party Red Team provider's secure delivery portal
GRC platform linked to the exercise record (ServiceNow, RSA Archer, or equivalent)
Internal Audit team document management system if the report was reviewed by Internal Audit
evidenceItems.format: PDF (encrypted at rest, access-controlled)

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability Remediation Plan and Closure Evidence
evidenceItems.description: A structured tracking record demonstrating that all Red Team findings were formally accepted, assigned to owners, remediated or risk-accepted within defined timelines, and verified as closed. This evidence satisfies the clause's requirement to 'manage' identified vulnerabilities, not merely identify them.
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation plan is formally approved by the CISO and relevant system owners, with each finding assigned a unique ID traceable back to the Red Team report, an accountable owner, and a target remediation date based on severity

High and critical severity findings have documented remediation timelines that are materially shorter than those for low-severity items, reflecting risk-based prioritisation aligned to the institution's risk appetite

Closed findings include verification evidence (e.g., retest results, vulnerability scan outputs, configuration screenshots) confirming the remediation was effective and not merely marked closed administratively

Any finding that is risk-accepted rather than remediated includes a formal risk acceptance sign-off from the appropriate authority (e.g., Risk Committee or CRO) with documented rationale and compensating controls
Remediation plan status is reported to senior management or the Board Risk Committee at defined intervals, with documented meeting minutes or board papers evidencing oversight
commonGaps: Remediation plan exists but findings are tracked only by generic status (Open/Closed) without linked closure evidence, making it impossible to verify actual remediation versus administrative closure
Critical findings remain open beyond the institution's own stated remediation SLAs with no documented risk acceptance or escalation, indicating a breakdown in vulnerability management governance
No evidence of remediation verification (retesting) — findings are closed on the word of system owners without independent confirmation, particularly for high-severity exploited vulnerabilities
Risk acceptances for unresolved findings lack proper authorisation — signed off by the same team that owns the vulnerability rather than an independent governance body
Remediation status was never formally reported to Board or Risk Committee, meaning senior leadership cannot demonstrate awareness of the institution's post-exercise cyber risk posture
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira, or equivalent) — vulnerability or risk treatment ticket queue
GRC platform risk treatment module (MetricStream, RSA Archer, or equivalent)
Cyber Risk team's remediation tracker (SharePoint list or Excel with audit trail, if no GRC platform)
Board Risk Committee and CISO steering committee meeting minutes and board papers (Board Secretariat)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (with audit trail), plus supporting closure evidence in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Three-Year Exercise Frequency Compliance Record
evidenceItems.description: Documentation proving that Red Team exercises have been conducted at intervals not exceeding three years, satisfying the mandatory frequency obligation in the clause. For institutions that have completed multiple cycles, this record demonstrates ongoing compliance rather than a one-time activity.
whatGoodLooksLike: A chronological register of all Red Team exercises conducted, showing commencement date, completion date, provider, and linked report reference, making the three-year cadence immediately verifiable by an examiner
The most recent exercise completion date is within three years of the examination date, with a documented next-exercise scheduled date that falls within the next three-year window

Where the institution is approaching the three-year deadline, a commissioning or procurement record (e.g., RFP, vendor selection, signed contract) evidences that the next exercise is actively in progress

The frequency register is maintained within the GRC platform or a formal policy compliance tracker, rather than existing only in informal emails or spreadsheets, indicating institutionalised compliance management

Scope of each successive exercise is documented and shows evolution — addressing prior gaps, expanding coverage, or incorporating new infrastructure — demonstrating continuous improvement rather than repetitive checkbox compliance
commonGaps: Institution cannot produce a consolidated register — historical exercise records exist in disparate folders or with departed staff, creating a documentation gap that cannot be quickly reconstructed for examination

The three-year calculation is made from report issuance date of the previous exercise rather than from exercise completion, creating a compliance risk if report issuance was significantly delayed after the actual engagement
No scheduled next-exercise date exists, meaning compliance beyond the current cycle is unplanned and the institution risks inadvertently breaching the three-year requirement
Only one historical exercise is documented with no evidence of how the institution satisfied this requirement in prior years, leaving an unexplained gap in the compliance history
Scope of successive exercises is identical with no documented rationale, suggesting exercises may be conducted as a formality rather than as genuine, evolving adversary simulations
suggestedSources: GRC platform compliance calendar or control testing schedule
CISO office policy compliance tracker (SharePoint, Confluence, or dedicated GRC module)
Internal Audit's multi-year audit plan, which may reference planned Red Team exercises
Procurement or vendor management records for historical and upcoming engagements
evidenceItems.format: GRC platform record or PDF/Spreadsheet register with linked report references

evidenceItems.name: Red Team Provider Qualifications and Independence Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation confirming the qualifications, independence, and professional standing of the Red Team conducting the exercise. Demonstrates that the 'realistic' simulation was delivered by practitioners with credible adversary simulation expertise, not repurposed from internal staff or unqualified vendors.
whatGoodLooksLike: Provider holds recognised industry certifications relevant to adversary simulation (e.g., CREST Certified Simulated Attack Specialist/Manager, OSEP, CRTO) and these are referenced in the engagement contract or report

Clear independence from the institution is established — the Red Team provider has no operational or reporting relationship with the institution's IT or security teams that could compromise the objectivity or realism of the exercise

Due diligence record for the provider selection exists, including assessment of the provider's track record, references from comparable financial institution engagements, and relevant regulatory familiarity (e.g., BNM-regulated entities)
Provider's non-disclosure agreement (NDA) and data handling obligations are documented, protecting the confidentiality of sensitive infrastructure details shared during scoping
Contract explicitly prohibits the provider from disclosing the institution's vulnerabilities to third parties and includes liability provisions proportionate to the sensitivity of the engagement
commonGaps: Exercise conducted by the institution's own internal penetration testing team relabelled as a Red Team — internal teams lack independence and their prior knowledge of the environment undermines simulation realism
Provider credentials are not documented beyond a generic vendor profile — no evidence of adversary simulation-specific certifications, only generic IT security accreditations
No vendor due diligence record exists, indicating the provider was selected without assessment of their qualifications or experience with financial institution environments
NDA exists but data handling obligations for infrastructure documentation shared during scoping are absent, creating confidentiality and regulatory risk

Contract was not reviewed by Legal or Procurement — the engagement was initiated directly by the IT team without formal procurement governance, leaving the institution exposed to liability and quality risks
suggestedSources: Procurement or vendor management system (contract repository, vendor risk assessment records)
Legal department for NDA and contract documentation
Third-party risk management (TPRM) platform for vendor due diligence records
CISO office for provider credential verification records and correspondence
evidenceItems.format: PDF (contract, NDA, due diligence report, certification copies)

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Reporting on Red Team Outcomes
evidenceItems.description: Formal records demonstrating that Red Team exercise outcomes — including key findings, risk exposure identified, and remediation progress — were reported to and acknowledged by the Board or Board-level risk governance committee. Evidences that the exercise informed strategic cyber risk decision-making at the highest level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or equivalent board-level meeting minutes explicitly reference the Red Team exercise, record discussion of material findings, and document any decisions made (e.g., risk acceptance, budget allocation for remediation)
A management paper or briefing note prepared for the Board translates technical Red Team findings into business risk impact, regulatory implications under RMiT, and recommended governance actions
Subsequent board papers include remediation progress updates, demonstrating that the Board exercised ongoing oversight rather than receiving a one-time report and disengaging
The institution's cyber risk register or risk appetite statement was formally reviewed and updated following Red Team findings, with Board endorsement of any changes documented in meeting minutes
Red Team outcomes are cross-referenced in the institution's Technology Risk Report or equivalent regulatory submission where applicable, demonstrating regulatory transparency
commonGaps: Red Team report was shared with the CISO and IT leadership but never formally tabled at Board or Risk Committee level, meaning senior governance oversight required for a material cyber risk activity is absent

Board minutes reference the Red Team exercise only superficially (e.g., 'Red Team exercise completed — noted') without evidence of substantive discussion, suggesting the report was filed rather than genuinely reviewed
No remediation progress updates were provided to the Board following the initial report, leaving a governance gap between finding identification and closure that examiners will flag
The Board paper was prepared in highly technical language without executive translation, casting doubt on whether board members had sufficient information to exercise meaningful oversight

Cyber risk register was not updated to reflect Red Team findings, meaning the exercise outcomes did not feed back into the institution's formal risk management framework as required by good governance
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — board and committee meeting minutes, approved papers
CISO office — management papers and briefing notes prepared for board reporting
GRC platform — cyber risk register and risk appetite statement version history
Risk Management function — Technology Risk Report and regulatory submissions
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers, meeting minutes, risk register extracts)

auditTips: Verify that the engagement is genuinely a Red Team exercise and not a penetration test by examining the report for objective-based attack narratives, multi-stage kill chains, and MITRE ATT&CK TTP references — a flat vulnerability list with CVSS scores is the hallmark of a penetration test, which does not satisfy clause 11.6. Prepare a one-page comparison document for the examiner if needed.

Calculate the three-year compliance window from the exercise completion date (not the report issuance date) and confirm the next scheduled exercise is contractually commissioned before the deadline lapses — present this as a forward-looking compliance calendar in the GRC platform so examiners can verify at a glance.

Cross-reference the Red Team scope document against the institution's technology asset register to confirm that production systems and critical infrastructure were included in scope — examiners will specifically question any material exclusions (e.g., core banking system, payment infrastructure) and the institution should have documented risk-based rationale for any exclusions.

Prepare a remediation evidence pack prior to examination: for each Red Team finding, compile a dossier containing the original finding, the assigned owner, the target and actual closure date, and the closure evidence (retest output or configuration screenshot). Organise this pack by severity tier so examiners can immediately focus on critical and high findings.

Ensure that the Red Team provider's qualifications are readily accessible in the vendor management system — retrieve CREST or equivalent certification documentation, the due diligence assessment, and the signed contract with scope of work before the examination, as examiners may request these to validate the 'realistic' standard implied by the clause.

Confirm that Board Risk Committee minutes from the period immediately following the Red Team exercise explicitly reference the exercise, record substantive discussion of findings, and document any governance decisions. If minutes are thin, prepare a supplementary board paper trail showing subsequent remediation reporting to demonstrate ongoing oversight.

Map the Red Team exercise findings against any concurrent regulatory submissions or internal risk assessments to demonstrate that the exercise outcomes were integrated into the institution's broader cyber risk framework — examiners look for evidence that the exercise was a genuine risk management tool, not an isolated compliance checkbox, so demonstrating downstream usage of findings significantly strengthens the institution's position.

11.7.title: Crowdsourced Security Testing
11.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has implemented crowdsourced security testing as a genuine complement to — not a replacement for — its existing security assessment programme, with particular scrutiny on whether the institution can demonstrate that the programme meaningfully extends coverage beyond conventional assessments. Examiners place heavy emphasis on the due diligence conducted to establish the 'reputable and credible' standing of the service provider, looking for structured vendor selection processes rather than informal or relationship-based engagements. A key red flag is the absence of a formal programme charter or rules of engagement, as this suggests the programme may not be properly controlled or legally bounded. Examiners also probe whether findings from the programme feed into the institution's vulnerability management and risk treatment cycle, and whether appropriate legal, data protection, and third-party risk controls govern researcher participation. Institutions that treat crowdsourced testing as a marketing exercise without demonstrable security improvement outcomes are viewed critically.

evidenceItems.name: Crowdsourced Security Testing Programme Policy and Charter
evidenceItems.description: A formal policy or programme charter that establishes the institution's governance framework for crowdsourced security testing, including its scope, objectives, relationship to existing security assessments, and the controls that bound researcher activity. This document demonstrates that the programme is a deliberate, managed complement to the broader security assessment strategy rather than an ad hoc arrangement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly positions crowdsourced testing as supplementary to VAPT, red team exercises, and other scheduled assessments, with a rationale for how coverage gaps are addressed
Charter defines clear in-scope and out-of-scope systems, prohibited testing techniques (e.g., DDoS, social engineering of staff), and safe harbour provisions for good-faith researchers
Document is Board- or CISO-approved, version-controlled, and reviewed at least annually or after significant IT environment changes
Policy references integration with the institution's vulnerability management lifecycle, incident response plan, and third-party risk management framework
Objectives are measurable (e.g., target number of unique vulnerabilities identified per cycle, mean time to remediate critical findings)
commonGaps: Policy exists only as a brief paragraph within the broader cybersecurity policy rather than as a standalone, operationally detailed document
No explicit linkage to existing security assessments, making it impossible to demonstrate the 'complement' requirement — the programme appears to operate in isolation
Out-of-scope systems and prohibited activities are vague or absent, creating legal and operational risk and signalling poor programme governance to examiners
Policy has not been updated following material changes to the IT environment (e.g., cloud migration, new digital banking channels), rendering scope definitions stale
No approval record or sign-off trail from senior management or the Board, undermining the programme's governance standing
suggestedSources: Information Security Policy repository on SharePoint or GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, OneTrust)
CISO or Head of Cybersecurity office for the authoritative document version
Board IT/Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee meeting minutes for approval evidence
Third-party risk management framework documentation maintained by Vendor Risk team
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Service Provider Due Diligence and Selection Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the structured due diligence process used to evaluate and select the crowdsourced security testing platform or facilitating service provider, demonstrating that the institution verified the provider's reputation, credibility, and capability before engagement. This is the primary evidence for satisfying the 'reputable and credible service providers' obligation in Clause 11.7.
whatGoodLooksLike: Vendor assessment scorecard or due diligence questionnaire responses covering the provider's researcher vetting processes, background check practices, legal frameworks governing researchers, and insurance/indemnity arrangements
Evidence of market comparison or RFP process showing at least two or three providers were evaluated, with documented rationale for final selection
Reference checks or independent reputation verification, such as industry analyst reports (Gartner, Forrester), peer institution endorsements, or regulatory body recognition in other jurisdictions
Provider's accreditations, certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II), and track record with comparable financial institutions documented in the selection file
Legal and compliance sign-off confirming the provider's researcher agreement terms, data handling practices, and jurisdictional compliance were reviewed prior to engagement
commonGaps: No formal vendor selection process documented — provider was chosen based on informal recommendation without a structured evaluation, which directly contradicts the 'reputable and credible' requirement
Due diligence focuses only on commercial terms and pricing rather than the provider's security practices, researcher vetting rigour, or programme management capability
Assessment is point-in-time with no periodic re-assessment schedule, so changes in the provider's operational posture or reputation are not detected
Researcher vetting practices of the platform are not assessed, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate it understands who has access to its systems
Legal review of the provider's researcher participation agreement and liability allocation is absent from the file, creating unquantified legal exposure
suggestedSources: Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) system or vendor register (e.g., Ariba, Coupa, ServiceNow VRM)
Procurement or vendor management team records including RFP documentation and evaluation matrices
Legal and Compliance department for contract review sign-off records
Information Security team for technical due diligence questionnaire responses
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX, Spreadsheet

evidenceItems.name: Service Provider Contract and Legal Agreements
evidenceItems.description: Executed contracts between the financial institution and the crowdsourced security testing platform provider, including any researcher-facing participation terms that the provider administers. These documents demonstrate that the institution has legally bounded the programme, allocated liability appropriately, and established obligations around confidentiality, data handling, and responsible disclosure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Master services agreement includes provisions for confidentiality of discovered vulnerabilities, researcher non-disclosure obligations, data handling and retention by the provider, and liability allocation for researcher conduct
Contract specifies the provider's obligations regarding researcher identity verification, background screening standards, and the legal basis under which researchers access the institution's systems
Responsible disclosure timeline and escalation obligations are contractually defined, including maximum time the provider has to notify the institution of critical findings
Contract includes a right-to-audit clause allowing the institution to inspect the provider's security and operational practices
Data processing agreement (DPA) or equivalent is in place if the testing programme involves exposure of personal data or customer information, consistent with PDPA obligations
commonGaps: Contract is generic platform terms and conditions with no financial-institution-specific customisation for scope controls, regulatory notification obligations, or customer data protections
Liability and indemnity provisions are unclear or unbalanced, with no legal review evidence in the contract file
No contractual obligation on the provider to notify the institution within a defined timeframe of critical or high-severity findings, creating response delays
Researcher participation agreements managed by the provider have not been reviewed by the institution's legal team, leaving researcher conduct obligations opaque
Contract has expired or operates on auto-renewal without formal re-review, meaning terms may not reflect current programme scope or regulatory requirements
suggestedSources: Legal department contract management system (e.g., Ironclad, ContractSafe, DocuSign CLM)
Procurement team executed agreement repository
Information Security team for programme-specific annexes or statements of work
Data Protection Officer for DPA review records
evidenceItems.format: PDF

evidenceItems.name: Programme Scope, Rules of Engagement, and Test Plans
evidenceItems.description: Technical and operational documentation defining exactly which systems, applications, and environments are in-scope for the crowdsourced testing programme, the testing techniques permitted, safe harbour conditions, and how the programme interfaces with the institution's change and incident management processes. This evidence demonstrates controlled, governed execution of the programme.
whatGoodLooksLike: Scope document maps in-scope assets to the institution's IT asset register and is updated with each programme cycle to reflect environment changes such as new applications, cloud workloads, or decommissioned systems

Rules of engagement explicitly list permitted testing techniques (e.g., web application testing, API fuzzing) and prohibited techniques (e.g., physical intrusion, volumetric attacks, social engineering), with researcher acknowledgement process
Safe harbour declaration is clearly communicated to researchers through the platform, providing legal protection for good-faith security research within defined boundaries
Integration with the change management process is documented, ensuring testing windows are coordinated and production incidents triggered by researchers are distinguishable in the SIEM/ITSM

Escalation procedures define how researchers submit critical findings out-of-band (e.g., direct security contact) rather than only through the standard platform queue, ensuring timely response to severe vulnerabilities
commonGaps: Scope is defined at a high level (e.g., 'all public-facing web applications') without specific asset enumeration, making it impossible to verify coverage or detect scope drift
Rules of engagement have not been updated following IT environment changes, meaning newly deployed services may be inadvertently out of scope or unrestricted
No safe harbour clause is published or communicated to researchers, creating legal ambiguity around researcher conduct and potentially deterring legitimate security research
No integration with the SIEM or incident management process, so testing activity cannot be distinguished from actual attacks, creating alert fatigue or missed escalations
Production databases containing customer data are in-scope without appropriate data handling controls or masking requirements documented for researchers
suggestedSources: IT Asset Register or CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Jira Assets)
Information Security team for rules of engagement documentation
Platform provider's programme management portal for published scope and safe harbour statements
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records and ITSM system (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX, Platform configuration export

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability Findings Reports and Remediation Tracking Records
evidenceItems.description: Consolidated records of all vulnerabilities submitted through the crowdsourced testing programme, including triage outcomes, severity classifications, remediation assignments, and closure evidence. This evidence demonstrates that the programme produces actionable security improvements and that findings feed into the institution's vulnerability management lifecycle, fulfilling the objective of 'thoroughly testing the security of their IT environment'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each finding is recorded with a unique ID, submission date, researcher pseudonym or ID, affected asset, CVSS or internal severity rating, triage outcome (valid, duplicate, informational, out-of-scope), and remediation owner

Remediation timelines are tracked against the institution's SLAs for vulnerability remediation (e.g., critical within 30 days, high within 60 days) and exceptions are formally documented with risk acceptance sign-off
Trend analysis across programme cycles shows measurable improvement in the institution's security posture (e.g., reduction in recurring vulnerability classes, improvement in mean time to remediate)
Duplicate and informational findings are recorded and analysed to identify patterns, demonstrating that the triage process extracts value beyond individual valid findings
Remediation evidence is linked to findings (e.g., patch records, code commit references, configuration change tickets), enabling end-to-end traceability from discovery to closure
commonGaps: Findings exist only in the platform provider's portal without export or local retention, creating a dependency on the provider and a gap if the contract is terminated
Triage outcomes are not documented with rationale, making it impossible to verify that valid vulnerabilities were not incorrectly closed as duplicates or out-of-scope
Remediation tracking is disconnected from the institution's vulnerability management system, so findings are not subject to the same SLA governance as other vulnerability sources
No management reporting or trend analysis derived from programme findings, meaning senior management cannot assess the programme's security impact or value
Critical and high findings submitted out-of-hours show delayed triage times significantly exceeding SLAs, with no compensating control or escalation evidence
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management system (e.g., Tenable.sc, Qualys VMDR, Jira with security tracking)
Crowdsourced testing platform portal (e.g., HackerOne, Bugcrowd, Synack) — exported reports
ITSM system for remediation tickets linked to findings
GRC platform for risk acceptance records on overdue remediation items
Security Operations team for SIEM correlation records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet, PDF (platform export), System export

evidenceItems.name: Integration Evidence: Linkage to Existing Security Assessment Programme
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating how the crowdsourced security testing programme complements and does not duplicate or substitute for the institution's existing security assessment activities such as VAPT, red team exercises, and application security reviews. This evidence is critical to satisfying the 'complement to existing security assessments' obligation and demonstrating that the institution has a coherent, layered security testing strategy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Security testing calendar or programme map shows how crowdsourced testing, scheduled VAPT, red team exercises, and application security reviews collectively cover the institution's IT environment without significant duplication or coverage gaps

Annual security assessment report or CISO report references crowdsourced testing findings alongside findings from other assessment types, with analysis of how each programme contributes unique coverage

Coverage analysis demonstrates that crowdsourced testing reaches attack surfaces not typically covered by point-in-time assessments, such as complex business logic flaws or novel attack chains across multiple systems
Lessons learned from crowdsourced findings are fed back into the scope design of scheduled VAPT and red team engagements, showing cross-programme learning
Board or senior management reporting distinguishes between vulnerability sources (crowdsourced vs. scheduled assessments) with commentary on the incremental value of each programme
commonGaps: Crowdsourced testing programme operates entirely independently of the scheduled security assessment calendar, with no documented strategy showing how the two programmes are complementary
The same assets are tested repeatedly by both crowdsourced and scheduled VAPT with no rationale for duplication, suggesting the institution does not have a coordinated testing strategy
Management and Board reporting on cybersecurity does not reference the crowdsourced programme, indicating it is not integrated into the institution's security governance reporting
No coverage gap analysis exists showing what attack surfaces or vulnerability classes crowdsourced testing addresses that scheduled assessments do not, undermining the 'complement' justification

Institution is unable to demonstrate that crowdsourced testing has produced findings that were not already identified through existing assessment methods, suggesting the programme adds limited incremental value
suggestedSources: Information Security team for the annual security testing programme calendar and coverage mapping
GRC platform for consolidated security assessment tracking across all testing types
Board IT/Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee papers for integrated security reporting
CISO annual review or security posture report
VAPT and red team engagement reports from the same period for cross-reference
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX, Spreadsheet, Board paper extract

auditTips: Since Clause 11.7 carries a 'G' (Guidance) marker, BNM examiners will not cite non-implementation as a direct violation, but if the institution has implemented a programme, every control obligation in the clause becomes fully examinable — confirm whether the institution has a programme before assuming this clause is out of scope for the examination.

Cross-reference the crowdsourced testing scope document (11.7-E4) against the IT asset register and the scopes used in the most recent VAPT engagement to demonstrate a coherent, complementary testing strategy — gaps between these documents are a common finding that examiners probe during walkthroughs.

Request the platform provider's researcher vetting and onboarding documentation proactively, as examiners frequently ask how the institution assures itself that researchers accessing its systems are legitimate and vetted — the inability to answer this question directly contradicts the 'reputable and credible' requirement.

Validate that vulnerability findings from the crowdsourced programme are imported or mirrored into the institution's primary vulnerability management system and are subject to the same remediation SLAs and escalation governance as findings from scheduled VAPT — a separate, siloed tracking spreadsheet is a red flag for examiners assessing maturity.

Prepare a one-page programme overview or security testing coverage map for the examination team showing how crowdsourced testing, VAPT, red team, and application security review collectively cover the institution's IT environment — this pre-empts examiner questions about the 'complement' requirement and signals programme maturity.

Ensure the contract file (11.7-E3) and due diligence records (11.7-E2) are co-located and readily retrievable, as examiners assessing third-party risk obligations often request these together; a disorganised or incomplete file is viewed as a governance deficiency even if the underlying controls are sound.

If the institution operates a private (invite-only) crowdsourced testing programme rather than a public bug bounty, prepare documentation explaining the researcher selection methodology, as examiners may question whether a closed programme genuinely represents 'crowdsourced' testing in the spirit of the clause.

11.8.title: Cybersecurity Operations Responsibilities
11.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has translated the cyber-attack lifecycle into concrete, assigned responsibilities rather than leaving it as a theoretical framework. Examiners will trace accountability from policy through to named roles (not just job titles) and verify that each of the seven kill-chain phases has a documented mitigating control with an identifiable owner. A primary red flag is a controls matrix that lists tools or capabilities but cannot be mapped back to specific personnel or teams responsible for operating them. Examiners also test whether the institution can demonstrate operational evidence — logs, alerts, hunting reports — showing the controls are active, not merely documented. Institutions that conflate 'having a SIEM' with 'addressing the kill chain' without phase-level mapping will draw significant scrutiny, as will those whose incident response playbooks do not reference the seven phases explicitly.

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Operations Responsibility Matrix (RACI)
evidenceItems.description: A formal RACI or accountability matrix that assigns named roles and teams to cybersecurity operations functions, explicitly cross-referenced to each of the seven cyber-attack lifecycle phases. This demonstrates that the institution has fulfilled the obligation to establish 'clear responsibilities' as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each of the seven kill-chain phases (reconnaissance through exfiltration) has at least one Responsible and one Accountable role explicitly named in the matrix
The matrix distinguishes between detection responsibilities, response responsibilities, and escalation authorities for each phase
Role names align directly with the institution's HR-approved job descriptions and organisational chart, with no references to phantom or vacant positions
The document carries a version number, approval date, and sign-off from the CISO or equivalent with evidence of annual review
Cross-references to the specific playbooks, runbooks, or standard operating procedures that each responsible role must execute are included in the matrix
commonGaps: Matrix assigns responsibilities to generic teams (e.g., 'SOC') rather than named roles, making accountability untraceable during an examination
Only a subset of kill-chain phases are covered — typically delivery through exploitation — with reconnaissance and exfiltration left unassigned
Document exists in an approved policy repository but has not been reviewed or updated following organisational restructures or outsourcing changes
No escalation path is defined, so the matrix shows who detects but not who decides on containment or regulatory notification
Third-party or MSSP responsibilities are absent, leaving gaps where the institution has outsourced kill-chain phase coverage without formalising accountability
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate) — policy and control ownership module
SharePoint or Confluence — cybersecurity policy library maintained by the CISO office
HR system — for cross-validation that named roles exist and are filled
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control, or GRC platform export as PDF

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Kill Chain Controls and Mitigations Framework
evidenceItems.description: A structured controls mapping document that details the specific technical, procedural, and administrative mitigating measures deployed against each of the seven attack lifecycle phases. This is the primary artefact demonstrating that 'appropriate mitigating measures' exist and are phase-specific rather than generic.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each of the seven phases has a dedicated section listing preventive controls, detective controls, and responsive controls with specific tool or process names (not just control categories)
Controls are rated for coverage and maturity (e.g., using a defined scale such as CMM or the institution's internal framework), allowing examiners to assess proportionality
The framework maps each control to the responsible team from E1 (the RACI), creating an auditable thread between obligation, control, and ownership

Evidence of a threat-informed approach is visible — controls are justified by reference to threat intelligence relevant to the Malaysian financial sector (e.g., FS-ISAC advisories, BNM threat bulletins)
The document includes a gap register or residual risk log where known coverage weaknesses are acknowledged and tracked to remediation timelines
commonGaps: Framework lists tools (e.g., 'we have a WAF') without explaining which kill-chain phase the tool addresses or how it is configured to provide that coverage
Reconnaissance and weaponisation phases are treated as entirely external and therefore excluded, with no controls for detecting OSINT enumeration or supply-chain tampering
Controls are listed at a category level only (e.g., 'endpoint protection') with no evidence they are actually deployed, licensed, and operational
The framework has not been updated to reflect changes in the institution's technology environment, leaving retired systems still listed as active controls
Residual risks are not documented, giving examiners no basis to assess whether senior management has accepted gaps or is actively remediating them
suggestedSources: GRC platform — control library and risk treatment module
Security architecture team documentation in SharePoint or Confluence
MITRE ATT&CK Navigator exports, if the institution uses ATT&CK as an underlying framework
Vendor assessment reports from security tool vendors confirming deployment scope
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or GRC platform export; may include embedded spreadsheet annexes for control details

evidenceItems.name: SOC Detection and Response Playbooks (Kill-Chain Aligned)
evidenceItems.description: Operational playbooks or runbooks used by the Security Operations Centre (or equivalent function) that are explicitly structured around the seven phases of the cyber-attack lifecycle. These demonstrate that mitigating measures are operationalised, not merely documented in policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: A distinct playbook or playbook section exists for each kill-chain phase, describing detection logic (alert rules, thresholds), triage steps, containment actions, and escalation triggers
Playbooks reference specific detection tools, log sources, and SIEM query logic by name, providing examiners with a traceable link between documentation and operational capability
Each playbook records the last test or tabletop exercise date and outcome, demonstrating that the procedures have been validated against realistic scenarios
Playbooks include decision trees that guide analysts on when to escalate to incident response, invoke the Business Continuity Plan, or notify BNM under the cyber incident reporting obligation
Version history shows playbooks are updated following real incidents or threat intelligence updates, not left static after initial creation
commonGaps: Playbooks exist for common incident types (malware, phishing) but are not mapped to kill-chain phases, making it impossible to demonstrate phase-specific mitigation
Detection logic references SIEM rules that have not been tuned and are either suppressed due to alert fatigue or have not fired in months, indicating they are not operationally effective
Escalation thresholds are undefined or set so high that the playbook would never trigger regulatory notification, creating compliance exposure
Playbooks have never been tested — no tabletop, red team, or purple team exercise records exist to validate them
Third-party SOC or MSSP playbooks are referenced but the institution cannot produce them or confirm they align with the institution's specific obligations under RMiT
suggestedSources: ITSM or SOAR platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Splunk SOAR, Palo Alto XSOAR) — playbook library
SOC wiki or Confluence — operational runbook repository
SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) — detection rule exports linked to playbooks
MSSP contractual documentation if SOC is outsourced
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or SOAR platform export; SIEM rule exports in native format

evidenceItems.name: Security Technology Coverage Evidence (Per Kill-Chain Phase)
evidenceItems.description: System-generated evidence demonstrating that the security tools and controls identified in the E2 framework are active, configured, and producing outputs for each kill-chain phase. This bridges the gap between documented controls and operational proof, a distinction BNM examiners actively probe.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM dashboard exports or scheduled reports show active alert rules tagged or labelled to each kill-chain phase, with non-zero detection activity over the review period
Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform exports confirm coverage across the full server and endpoint estate, with no significant gaps in agent deployment
Email gateway and web proxy logs demonstrate active filtering, with quarantine and block statistics showing the delivery and weaponisation controls are functioning
Network traffic analysis or NDR platform reports show east-west traffic monitoring is active, supporting detection of command-and-control and lateral movement (exploitation/installation phases)
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) or CASB reports confirm exfiltration controls are configured with active policies on data egress channels including email, USB, and cloud upload
commonGaps: Tool deployment reports show agents installed on servers but endpoint coverage of user workstations is below 80%, leaving significant blind spots in installation and exploitation phase detection
SIEM contains detection rules for external-facing phases but no rules targeting internal lateral movement, meaning installation and command-and-control phases are effectively unmonitored
DLP is deployed but operating in monitor-only mode with no blocking policies active, meaning exfiltration controls are detective only and 'appropriate mitigation' is debatable
Log ingestion gaps exist — critical systems such as core banking or payment switches are not forwarding logs to the SIEM, leaving entire environments outside the detection perimeter
Tool licensing or subscription lapses mean certain capabilities (e.g., threat intelligence feeds, sandbox detonation) are inactive during the review period despite being listed in policy
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar) — detection rule inventory and alert volume reports
EDR platform (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) — deployment coverage reports
Email security gateway (Proofpoint, Mimecast) — threat statistics and policy reports
DLP platform (Forcepoint, Symantec DLP, Microsoft Purview) — policy status and incident reports
Vulnerability management platform (Tenable, Qualys) — for reconnaissance-phase mitigation evidence on attack surface reduction
evidenceItems.format: System-generated reports exported as PDF or CSV; dashboard screenshots with timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Threat Intelligence and Threat Hunting Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of threat intelligence consumption and proactive threat hunting activities that demonstrate the institution actively monitors for threats at the reconnaissance and early kill-chain phases, rather than relying solely on reactive alerting. This evidences a forward-leaning interpretation of 'appropriate mitigating measures'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Threat intelligence reports or feed integration logs show the institution receives and acts on indicators of compromise (IOCs) relevant to financial sector threats, with timestamps showing timely ingestion into defensive controls
Threat hunting reports document hypothesis-driven hunts that reference specific kill-chain phases and ATT&CK techniques, with findings and closure status recorded
Records show that threat intelligence is operationalised — IOCs from feeds are being blocked or alerted on in the SIEM, firewall, or email gateway, not just stored in a repository

Participation in sector-specific information sharing (e.g., FS-ISAC, JCSC advisories, BNM cyber threat bulletins) is documented, with evidence that advisories triggered control reviews or configuration updates
Hunting cadence and coverage is defined in policy and actual hunt records demonstrate adherence — examiners can verify that hunts occur at stated frequency and cover stated phases
commonGaps: Institution subscribes to a commercial threat intelligence feed but cannot demonstrate that IOCs are being ingested into any defensive control, making the subscription evidentially worthless
Threat hunting is performed ad hoc with no documented hypotheses, scope, or outcomes, so records cannot demonstrate systematic coverage of kill-chain phases
Reconnaissance-phase monitoring is absent — no external attack surface management, no dark web monitoring, and no process for discovering credential or data exposure before exploitation occurs
Threat intelligence reports are circulated to management but not downstreamed to SOC analysts or control owners, creating a disconnect between strategic and operational awareness
Hunt records are informal (e.g., analyst notes in email threads) rather than tracked in a GRC or ITSM system, making it impossible to demonstrate frequency or completeness to examiners
suggestedSources: Threat intelligence platform (TIP) such as MISP, ThreatConnect, or Anomali — IOC ingestion logs and hunt records
SIEM — IOC-based detection rule activity reports
GRC or ITSM platform — threat hunting task records with open/close timestamps
External attack surface management tools (e.g., RiskIQ/Microsoft Defender EASM, Censys) — exposure scan reports
Email records of FS-ISAC or BNM advisory receipts and any resulting control actions
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports, ITSM/GRC platform exports, TIP exports in PDF or CSV

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Operations Governance and Review Records
evidenceItems.description: Board and management-level governance records showing that cybersecurity operations performance — including kill-chain phase coverage — is periodically reviewed, reported, and subject to executive oversight. This demonstrates that responsibilities are not only assigned but also supervised and held accountable at the institutional level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee minutes reference cybersecurity operations performance metrics on a regular cadence (at minimum quarterly), with specific discussion of coverage gaps or incidents

A cybersecurity operations Key Risk Indicator (KRI) dashboard is produced and tabled at management committees, with metrics traceable to kill-chain phase coverage (e.g., mean time to detect per phase, unresolved alerts by phase)

Annual independent assessments (red team, penetration test, or purple team exercise) report findings against the kill-chain framework and their results are formally reviewed by the CISO and tabled to the appropriate governance committee

Management responses to audit or assessment findings related to cybersecurity operations are documented with committed remediation dates and evidence of closure, demonstrating a functioning accountability loop

The cybersecurity operations function's budget, headcount, and tooling decisions are documented in governance forums, showing the institution invests proportionately in the mitigating measures it claims to have in place
commonGaps: Board minutes mention 'cybersecurity' generically but contain no metrics, no kill-chain coverage assessment, and no discussion of specific control gaps, suggesting cybersecurity is reported up but not genuinely governed

KRI dashboards measure lagging indicators (number of incidents) but not leading indicators (detection coverage by phase, mean time to detect early kill-chain phases), limiting the board's ability to assess pre-breach posture
Red team or penetration test results are filed but never formally tabled to a governance committee, meaning executive management cannot be shown to have reviewed or accepted residual risks

Audit findings on cybersecurity operations are tracked in a spreadsheet outside the GRC platform, with no formal management responses or evidence of closure, making the remediation trail difficult to follow

Annual review of the cybersecurity operations framework occurs on paper but is not evidenced — no meeting records, no version-controlled updated documents, and no sign-off trail from accountable executives
suggestedSources: Board and committee secretariat — minutes and board packs from Risk Committee, IT Steering Committee, or EXCO
GRC platform — audit finding and remediation tracking module
Internal audit department — cybersecurity operations audit reports and management responses
CISO office — cybersecurity KRI/KPI dashboard with publication history
Red team, penetration testing, or purple team engagement reports from external or internal assurance providers
evidenceItems.format: PDF minutes, GRC platform exports, PDF assessment reports; all with version control and approval signatures

auditTips: Map all seven kill-chain phases to your controls framework before the examination and prepare a single-page visual summary — examiners frequently ask for a quick overview and a clear visual demonstrating phase-by-phase coverage prevents the conversation from stalling on definitional questions about what each phase means to the institution.

Do not rely solely on policy documents to demonstrate compliance with this clause — examiners will ask to see operational evidence. For each phase, prepare at least one system-generated artefact (SIEM report, EDR coverage export, DLP policy status) dated within the examination review window to prove controls are live, not merely documented.

Validate your RACI against your actual organisational chart before examination. Examiners may request to interview role holders named in the matrix; if named roles are vacant, have been outsourced, or belong to staff who are unaware of their responsibilities, this will be a significant finding. Conduct a brief walkthrough with named owners prior to the examination.

Treat reconnaissance and exfiltration as the phases most likely to be under-evidenced. Most institutions have reasonable delivery and exploitation controls (email gateway, EDR, patching) but struggle to show external attack surface monitoring for reconnaissance and DLP enforcement for exfiltration. Prioritise collecting evidence for these two phases specifically.

Cross-reference your playbooks against your most recent red team or penetration test findings. If the test identified kill-chain phases where detection failed or response was slow, examiners will expect to see how the playbooks were updated in response. Being able to show this improvement cycle demonstrates a mature, functioning operations programme rather than static documentation.

If cybersecurity operations are partially or fully outsourced to an MSSP, obtain and be prepared to produce the contractual RACI, the MSSP's playbooks, and evidence that service levels covering each kill-chain phase are being met. BNM does not accept outsourcing as a reason for accountability gaps — the institution remains responsible and must demonstrate oversight of the MSSP's performance against phase-specific obligations.

Prepare a concise narrative linking Clause 11.8 obligations to your internal control framework reference numbers. If your institution uses NIST CSF, ISO 27001, or another standard as the underlying control framework, having a pre-prepared crosswalk showing how your controls satisfy each of the seven phases reduces examination time and demonstrates mature compliance management rather than reactive evidence-gathering.

11.9.title: Continuous Security Monitoring and SOC
11.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has moved beyond passive, reactive monitoring toward a genuinely proactive security posture. Examiners will scrutinise whether the SOC is adequately staffed with competent resources — not merely contracted out with no internal oversight — and whether tooling is fit-for-purpose rather than checkbox installations. A key red flag is monitoring scope that excludes legacy or non-standard infrastructure, creating blind spots in critical systems coverage. Examiners will also cross-reference VA/PT findings against remediation records to determine whether the institution acts on identified vulnerabilities or allows them to persist, and will probe whether alert thresholds, detection use cases, and escalation runbooks are regularly reviewed and updated to reflect the evolving threat landscape.

evidenceItems.name: SOC Charter and Operating Model
evidenceItems.description: Formal document defining the SOC's mandate, organisational structure, staffing model (in-house, hybrid, or outsourced), roles and responsibilities, operating hours, and governance accountability. Demonstrates that the institution has formally established a SOC with clearly defined authority and competency requirements as required under clause 11.9(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter is board- or senior management-approved, dated within the last two years, and references RMiT clause 11.9 explicitly
Staffing model specifies minimum competency requirements (e.g., certifications such as GCIH, GCFE, CEH) and headcount rationale aligned to monitoring scope
Escalation matrix is embedded within the charter, mapping alert severity levels to named roles and maximum response timeframes
Operating model clearly distinguishes Tier 1/2/3 analyst responsibilities and defines when managed security service provider (MSSP) involvement is triggered
Document is subject to at least annual review with version history evidencing updates post-significant incidents or threat landscape changes
