commonGaps: SOC charter exists only as an informal email approval or slide deck, lacking formal sign-off from senior management or the board
Competency requirements are vague (e.g., 'relevant experience') with no defined certifications, training pathways, or minimum tenure criteria
Outsourced or hybrid SOC arrangements lack a retained internal oversight function, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate meaningful oversight of the MSSP
Charter has not been reviewed or updated following significant organisational changes, mergers, or new product lines that expanded the technology footprint
Operating hours are limited to business hours only, with no documented after-hours monitoring arrangement or on-call escalation procedure
suggestedSources: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) office — primary custodian of SOC governance documents
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — policy and charter repository with version control
SharePoint or internal document management system under the Cybersecurity or IT Risk division
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes approving the SOC operating model
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (Board or senior management approved policy document with version history)

evidenceItems.name: SOC Tool and Technology Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive record of all security monitoring tools deployed within the SOC environment, including SIEM, SOAR, EDR/XDR, IDS/IPS, UEBA, and threat intelligence platforms. Demonstrates that the SOC is equipped with necessary tools and technologies for proactive monitoring as required under clause 11.9(a), and that tooling is actively maintained and licensed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory lists each tool with its version, vendor, license expiry date, and designated owner, and is reconciled at least quarterly
SIEM deployment evidence includes log source onboarding records showing all critical systems feed into the SIEM with documented ingestion volumes and health metrics
Threat intelligence platform integration is evidenced by feed subscription records, IoC ingestion logs, and documented use cases mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques
SOAR playbook library is documented with a register of automated response workflows, last-tested dates, and coverage mapped against top threat scenarios
Tool health dashboards or availability reports demonstrate that monitoring infrastructure operates with documented uptime SLAs (e.g., ≥99.5%) with alerting for tool outages
commonGaps: SIEM is deployed but log source coverage is incomplete — critical systems such as privileged access management solutions, cloud workloads, or OT/SCADA systems are not onboarded
Threat intelligence feeds are subscribed to but there is no evidence of operationalisation — IoCs are not actioned or correlated against internal telemetry
Tool inventory is maintained as a static spreadsheet last updated over 12 months ago, failing to reflect decommissioned or newly deployed technologies
SOAR playbooks exist but have never been tested end-to-end, with no exercise records or tabletop validation documented
Monitoring tool licenses have lapsed or are insufficient for current log volumes, resulting in data dropping or truncation without documented risk acceptance
suggestedSources: SIEM administration console (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) — log source inventory and ingestion health reports
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system — licensed software register with expiry tracking
SOAR platform (e.g., Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) — playbook library and execution logs
Vendor management records maintained by Procurement or IT Operations for subscription and support contracts
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system export (CMDB/ITAM export) supplemented by SIEM health dashboard screenshots

evidenceItems.name: Monitoring Scope Register and Coverage Map
evidenceItems.description: Documented register mapping all critical systems and supporting infrastructure to the SOC monitoring perimeter, confirming that nothing within the institution's critical technology estate falls outside security monitoring coverage. Directly evidences compliance with clause 11.9(b) requiring that monitoring scope covers all critical systems including supporting infrastructure.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register cross-references the institution's critical system inventory (as defined under RMiT) and explicitly confirms SIEM/monitoring onboarding status for each system with dates
Coverage map distinguishes between network, endpoint, application, database, cloud, and third-party/outsourced infrastructure layers, with monitoring method documented for each
Any systems currently out of scope are accompanied by a formal risk acceptance record, approved by the CISO or equivalent, with a defined remediation timeline
Register is reviewed and updated at least semi-annually, or upon commissioning of new critical systems, with an audit trail of changes
Supporting infrastructure (firewalls, routers, DNS, Active Directory, PKI) is explicitly listed and confirmed as log-generating sources feeding into the SIEM
commonGaps: Coverage register exists for core banking and payment systems but omits supporting infrastructure such as network devices, hypervisors, and identity platforms
Cloud environments (IaaS, SaaS, PaaS) adopted through digital transformation are not reflected in the monitoring scope, creating unmonitored attack surfaces
Third-party or outsourced systems processing critical data lack contractual log-sharing provisions, resulting in a documented gap with no compensating controls
The register has not been updated following significant infrastructure changes (e.g., data centre migration, new branch connectivity), rendering it inaccurate
No formal process exists to validate that systems onboarded to the SIEM are actually generating and forwarding logs — gaps may go undetected for extended periods
suggestedSources: SIEM platform — log source management console showing onboarded assets and last-event timestamps
GRC platform or IT Risk register — critical system inventory maintained by IT Risk or the CISO office
CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) — authoritative source of infrastructure assets cross-referenced against monitoring coverage
Network Operations Centre (NOC) or IT Operations — network topology diagrams used to validate infrastructure coverage
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (structured table with system name, criticality rating, monitoring method, SIEM onboarding date, and gap status)

evidenceItems.name: Security Alert and Incident Detection Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational records demonstrating that the SOC is actively detecting, triaging, and escalating anomalous activity in a timely manner. Includes SIEM alert logs, incident tickets, and detection metrics that collectively evidence the 'continuous and proactive monitoring' and 'timely detection' obligations under clause 11.9.
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM alert logs are exportable and show a realistic, non-trivial volume of alerts being generated, triaged, and closed or escalated with analyst notes within documented SLA timeframes
Incident management records (e.g., ServiceNow tickets) correlate to SIEM alerts, demonstrating an end-to-end detection-to-response chain with timestamps for each stage
SOC performance metrics dashboard tracks mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR) trends over rolling 12-month periods with defined targets
Use case library is maintained in the SIEM documenting detection rules, tuning history, and false-positive rates, evidencing proactive rather than default-only monitoring
Monthly or quarterly SOC performance reports are presented to senior management or the IT Risk Committee, evidencing governance oversight of monitoring effectiveness
commonGaps: SIEM alert volumes are suspiciously low or consist predominantly of auto-closed false positives, suggesting detection rules are not tuned to the institution's environment
Alert-to-incident correlation is absent — alerts are logged in the SIEM but there is no corresponding incident ticket trail, indicating alerts are not being acted upon
MTTD and MTTR metrics are not formally tracked, making it impossible to demonstrate 'timely detection' to examiners
Detection use cases are limited to out-of-the-box SIEM rules with no institution-specific tuning, leaving threat-actor TTPs relevant to financial services undetected
SOC performance reports do not reach senior management or the board, indicating a governance gap in oversight of the institution's security posture
suggestedSources: SIEM platform — alert and event logs with analyst disposition records (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar)
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — security incident and alert ticket history
SOC manager or CISO office — monthly/quarterly SOC KPI and performance reports
IT Risk or Security Governance Committee meeting minutes — evidence of senior management oversight of detection metrics
evidenceItems.format: System export (SIEM/ITSM) in CSV or PDF, supplemented by management reports in PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability Assessment Reports and Remediation Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of all vulnerability assessments (VA) conducted across the critical technology estate, including scope, findings, risk ratings, and remediation status. Directly evidences compliance with clause 11.9(c) requiring regular review of security posture through VA in line with Appendix 5.
whatGoodLooksLike: VA reports are dated and scoped to cover all critical systems, with authenticated scans performed at least quarterly, or after significant infrastructure changes, consistent with Appendix 5 frequency requirements
Each report includes an executive summary, vulnerability findings with CVSS scores, affected assets mapped to the critical system register, and a prioritised remediation plan with due dates
Remediation tracking records demonstrate that critical and high-rated vulnerabilities are closed within documented SLA timeframes (e.g., critical within 14 days, high within 30 days)
Vulnerability trends over successive assessments are tracked, showing measurable reduction in open critical/high findings, evidencing an improving security posture
VA results are formally reviewed by the CISO or IT Risk Committee with documented acceptance or escalation of residual risk items that cannot be remediated within SLA
commonGaps: VA is conducted only annually rather than on a frequency commensurate with the institution's risk profile and Appendix 5 requirements, leaving extended windows of undetected exposure
Authenticated scans are not used, resulting in incomplete vulnerability discovery that significantly understates the true risk profile of the environment
Remediation plans lack assigned owners and due dates, and follow-up scans to confirm closure of findings are not performed or documented
VA scope consistently excludes certain system categories (e.g., legacy mainframes, OT systems, cloud environments) without documented justification or compensating controls
Findings are tracked in disconnected spreadsheets with no linkage to the organisation's risk register, preventing accurate risk aggregation at the enterprise level
suggestedSources: Vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable.sc, Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM) — scan reports and remediation tracking dashboards
GRC platform — vulnerability findings imported as risk items with remediation workflow and approval trails
IT Operations or Infrastructure team — patch management records corroborating vulnerability closure claims
CISO office or IT Risk Committee — meeting minutes reflecting review and acceptance of VA findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF (VA reports from scanning platform) with supporting spreadsheet or GRC export for remediation tracking

evidenceItems.name: Penetration Testing Reports and Remediation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Full penetration testing reports and associated remediation records demonstrating that the institution regularly subjects its critical systems to adversarial testing by qualified parties, consistent with clause 11.9(c) and Appendix 5 requirements. Distinguishes PT from automated VA and demonstrates human-led, scenario-based adversarial assessment.
whatGoodLooksLike: PT reports are conducted by qualified independent testers (internal red team with demonstrable competency or accredited third-party firm) at a frequency consistent with Appendix 5, with scope covering internet-facing and internal critical systems
Reports include a defined scope, test methodology (e.g., PTES, OWASP, OSSTMM), attack narratives, evidence of exploitation (screenshots, proof-of-concept), and impact assessment per finding
Findings are risk-rated and a formal remediation plan is produced within a defined period post-report delivery, with ownership assigned to specific teams
Retesting reports or remediation verification records confirm that identified vulnerabilities have been addressed, with close-out sign-off from the CISO or equivalent

PT scope and scheduling are governed by a formal PT programme document reviewed annually, ensuring coverage rotates across different systems and attack scenarios (e.g., web application, network, social engineering)
commonGaps: Penetration tests are conflated with automated vulnerability scans — reports contain only tool output without manual exploitation attempts, failing to meet the adversarial intent of Appendix 5 requirements
PT scope is limited to internet-facing systems only, with no internal network, lateral movement, or assumed-breach scenarios tested
Remediation of PT findings is not verified — close-out is documented only by the remediation team self-attesting, with no independent retesting conducted
Testing is performed by the same internal team responsible for building and operating the systems under test, undermining independence and credibility of findings
PT reports are classified and stored but not formally reviewed at the governance level, with no evidence that senior management or the board is aware of findings and remediation status
suggestedSources: CISO office or Cybersecurity team — PT report archive and engagement contracts with testing firms
Third-party penetration testing firm — raw reports, retest reports, and attestation letters
GRC platform — PT findings logged as risk items with remediation workflow and approval records
Vendor management — statements of work (SOW) and penetration tester qualification records (CVs, certifications such as OSCP, CREST, GPEN)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (PT report and retest report from testing party) with supporting remediation tracking in GRC platform or spreadsheet

auditTips: Cross-reference the SOC monitoring scope register (11.9-E3) against the institution's critical system inventory submitted under RMiT clause 10.x — any system classified as critical that does not appear as a monitored SIEM source is an immediate red flag that examiners will pursue; prepare a reconciliation worksheet in advance.

Before the examination, pull a 12-month export of SIEM alert and incident records and calculate MTTD and MTTR by severity tier — examiners increasingly request this data on-site, and being unable to produce it suggests the institution cannot demonstrate 'timely detection' as required by clause 11.9.

Validate that VA and PT scheduling records align with Appendix 5 frequency requirements for the institution's risk tier; prepare a forward-looking assessment calendar showing planned assessments for the next 12 months alongside completed assessments for the prior 24 months to demonstrate a sustained programme rather than ad-hoc activity.

For outsourced or hybrid SOC arrangements, prepare the MSSP contract alongside service level reports and the institution's internal oversight records — examiners will test whether the institution can demonstrate meaningful oversight of the MSSP's activities rather than simply delegating responsibility; absence of an internal oversight function is a common finding.

Conduct a pre-examination walkthrough with the SOC team to ensure analysts can demonstrate a live use case from alert firing through triage, escalation, and incident creation — examiners may request a live demonstration of SOC capabilities, and the inability to demonstrate this end-to-end is damaging to the institution's credibility.

Ensure PT findings from the most recent two cycles are loaded into the GRC or risk register with remediation status updated — examiners will ask to see what was found, what was done about it, and how long remediation took; unresolved critical or high findings from prior PT cycles with no documented risk acceptance are a significant compliance weakness.

Prepare a single consolidated SOC governance pack that sequences the charter (11.9-E1), tool inventory (11.9-E2), coverage map (11.9-E3), and performance reports (11.9-E4) in logical order — presenting these as a coherent narrative rather than disconnected documents significantly strengthens the institution's demonstration of a mature, managed SOC programme.

11.10.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence
11.10.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the institution has operationalised a structured, repeatable CTI lifecycle — not merely subscribed to threat feeds. Examiners look for evidence that collected intelligence is analysed and contextualised to the institution's specific environment (sector, geography, technology stack) rather than consumed raw. A primary red flag is the absence of a documented process linking CTI outputs to detection rules, incident response playbooks, or vulnerability prioritisation, indicating that intelligence is collected but not acted upon. Examiners also specifically probe coverage of two non-negotiable use cases: detection of data breach indicators (e.g., credential leaks, dark web exposure) and monitoring for misleading or fraudulent information about the institution circulating online (impersonation, phishing lures, false announcements). Institutions that cannot demonstrate prompt detection — evidenced by timelines from intelligence receipt to alert or escalation — will face significant findings.

evidenceItems.name: CTI Programme Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy and accompanying standard operating procedure documenting the institution's end-to-end CTI lifecycle: collection sources, ingestion methods, analysis methodology, evaluation criteria, dissemination channels, and integration with cybersecurity operations. This demonstrates that the CTI process is deliberate, governed, and repeatable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly names the CTI lifecycle stages (collect, analyse, evaluate, disseminate) and maps them to responsible roles or teams
Procedure specifies minimum required intelligence sources including commercial feeds, ISACs, government feeds (e.g., CyberSecurity Malaysia, BNM FinCERT), and open-source intelligence
Document includes defined SLAs for analysis turnaround and escalation timelines, distinguishing between critical, high, and informational intelligence
Scope explicitly covers both technical threat intelligence and reputational/narrative threats such as impersonation campaigns and false information spread online
Policy version-controlled, board or senior management approved within the last 12 months, and reviewed at least annually
commonGaps: Policy exists but treats CTI as a subset of vulnerability management, with no explicit mention of monitoring for misleading information or data breach indicators
Procedure describes collection only — analysis methodology and evaluation criteria are absent, leaving the process undefined beyond feed subscription
No defined SLAs for intelligence-to-action timelines, making it impossible to demonstrate 'prompt detection' as required by the clause
Roles and responsibilities for CTI are unassigned or defaulted generically to 'SOC team' without named accountabilities or RACI matrix
Document has not been reviewed following a significant threat landscape change (e.g., major sector-wide incident), suggesting governance is nominal
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity department policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate) under the Cybersecurity domain
Board Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee approval minutes confirming policy endorsement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Threat Intelligence Feed Subscription and Source Inventory
evidenceItems.description: A current inventory of all CTI sources subscribed to or monitored by the institution, including commercial threat intelligence platforms, government and regulatory feeds, industry ISACs, open-source intelligence tools, and dark web monitoring services. This demonstrates breadth of collection and deliberate source selection relevant to the institution's threat environment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Inventory documents each source with feed type (strategic, operational, tactical), data format (STIX/TAXII, CSV, API), ingestion frequency, and owning team
At least one dedicated dark web and credential leak monitoring service is subscribed, with evidence of integration into alerting workflows
Financial sector-specific sources are included (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM FinCERT notifications, regional CERTs such as MyCERT)
Inventory distinguishes between automated feeds ingested into SIEM/TIP and manually reviewed sources, with clear accountabilities for each
Annual review record confirms sources remain active, relevant, and that gaps in coverage have been assessed and addressed
commonGaps: Institution relies exclusively on a single commercial feed with no supplementary government, ISAC, or open-source coverage, creating blind spots
Dark web and credential leak monitoring is absent or outsourced informally with no SLA or escalation path back to the institution's SOC
Feed inventory exists as a spreadsheet but is not reconciled against what is actually ingested into the SIEM or TIP, leading to stale or broken feeds
No financial sector-specific intelligence sources are included, demonstrating that the CTI programme is not contextualised to the institution's operating environment
Costs are tracked but relevance and quality of feeds have never been formally reviewed, with several feeds returning zero actionable indicators over the review period
suggestedSources: Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) such as MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali, or Recorded Future — source configuration and subscription records
Procurement or vendor management system for commercial feed contracts and renewal dates
SOC operations documentation in Confluence or SharePoint listing active monitoring integrations
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or TIP platform export (PDF/XLSX)

evidenceItems.name: CTI Analysis and Evaluation Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented records of intelligence analysis outputs over a representative review period, demonstrating that collected intelligence is systematically analysed, contextualised to the institution's environment, and evaluated for relevance and severity before action is taken. This is the primary evidence that the institution moves beyond passive collection to active intelligence work.
whatGoodLooksLike: Threat intelligence reports or bulletins are produced at defined intervals (e.g., weekly tactical, monthly strategic) and distributed to relevant stakeholders including IT risk, SOC, and senior management
Each analysis record links raw indicators or intelligence to the institution's specific assets, systems, or threat actors relevant to Malaysian financial services
Evaluation records show a prioritisation or scoring methodology (e.g., TLP classification, CVSS contextualisation, MITRE ATT&CK mapping) applied consistently
Analysis outputs contain explicit recommendations — detection rule updates, patch prioritisation, or awareness actions — demonstrating the intelligence is actioned
Records show analyst attribution and sign-off, confirming human judgement is applied rather than purely automated feed processing
commonGaps: Institution receives threat feeds and forwards raw IOC lists to the SOC without any analysis layer, conflating collection with intelligence production
Reports exist but are generic sector summaries with no contextualisation to the institution's environment, systems, or specific exposures
Analysis is performed ad hoc following incidents only, with no evidence of proactive, ongoing intelligence analysis during normal operations
MITRE ATT&CK or equivalent framework is referenced in policy but not applied in practice — analysis records contain no TTP mapping
Intelligence reports are produced but distribution lists are limited to the SOC, with no evidence that strategic intelligence reaches risk management or senior leadership
suggestedSources: TIP platform (MISP, Anomali, Recorded Future) — report and analysis modules
SharePoint or internal intranet where CTI bulletins are published
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — CTI-related tickets or tasks linked to analysis outputs
Email distribution records for threat intelligence bulletins to senior management
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports, TIP platform exports, or DOCX intelligence bulletins

evidenceItems.name: Data Breach and Dark Web Monitoring Logs
evidenceItems.description: Operational logs, alerts, and case records from dark web monitoring, credential leak detection, and data breach indicator monitoring services. This evidence directly demonstrates the institution's capability to detect data breach incidents as explicitly required by the clause, and shows that alerts are reviewed and escalated in a timely manner.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monitoring service logs show continuous coverage with timestamped alerts over the review period, confirming no gaps in dark web surveillance
Alert records include the full triage lifecycle: detection timestamp, analyst review timestamp, escalation decision, and closure — enabling prompt detection timelines to be verified

At least one end-to-end case record exists demonstrating how a dark web credential exposure alert was detected, triaged, escalated to IT security, and resulted in a defined response action (e.g., forced password reset)
Monitoring scope covers the institution's domains, brand names, executive names, customer-facing application identifiers, and known third-party relationships
False positive rate and tuning records are maintained, demonstrating active management of alert quality rather than passive log accumulation
commonGaps: Dark web monitoring is in place but alert logs show no escalations over a 12-month period, suggesting alerts are being dismissed without adequate investigation
Monitoring scope is limited to the institution's primary domain only, missing subsidiaries, product brands, and executive personal credential exposure
Alerts are generated by the monitoring platform but there is no documented triage process, meaning detection capability exists without a response workflow
Service is outsourced to a third party but SLA reports are not reviewed by the institution's security team, and no internal escalation records exist
Monitoring logs are retained in the vendor portal only with no internal archival, creating a gap if the vendor relationship ends
suggestedSources: Dark web monitoring platform (e.g., Recorded Future, Digital Shadows, Kroll, ZeroFox) — alert logs and case management exports
SOC SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) — correlation rules and alert history for credential leak IOCs
ITSM platform — incident or case records linked to dark web alert escalations
Third-party vendor's periodic monitoring reports delivered under managed service agreements
evidenceItems.format: Platform export (PDF/CSV), ITSM case records, or vendor service reports

evidenceItems.name: Online Impersonation and Misleading Information Monitoring Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of a structured process to monitor for misleading, fraudulent, or false information about the institution circulating on the Internet, including social media impersonation, fake websites, phishing lures mimicking the institution's brand, and false announcements. This directly addresses the second explicit use case named in the clause verbatim text.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined digital risk protection or brand monitoring process exists with documented tooling, coverage scope (social media platforms, app stores, phishing feeds, domain registrations), and responsible team
Monitoring logs show regular sweeps with timestamped findings, demonstrating continuous rather than reactive surveillance
Takedown request records exist for at least one identified impersonation or fraudulent site, showing the end-to-end response process from detection to remediation
Escalation path is documented and tested — findings feed into a defined workflow that notifies communications, legal, and cybersecurity teams as appropriate
Metrics are tracked (e.g., number of incidents detected, average time to takedown) and reported to management, demonstrating programme effectiveness
commonGaps: Institution monitors for technical threats but has no process for detecting misleading information or impersonation, leaving this explicit clause obligation entirely unaddressed
Social media monitoring exists in the communications or marketing department but is not connected to cybersecurity operations, resulting in no formal escalation or security response
Monitoring is limited to the institution's own social media accounts and does not cover external impersonation accounts, fake domains, or fraudulent mobile applications
Takedown capability relies entirely on ad hoc reporting to platform providers with no formal process, SLA, or vendor relationship for expedited removal
No records are retained of identified impersonation incidents, making it impossible to demonstrate detection timeliness or programme effectiveness to examiners
suggestedSources: Digital risk protection platform (e.g., ZeroFox, Brandwatch, Cyberint, Group-IB) — monitoring logs and takedown case records
Communications or corporate affairs team — social media monitoring reports and incident records
Legal department — cease and desist correspondence and domain dispute records
Phishing intelligence feeds integrated into TIP — fake domain and phishing site detection alerts
evidenceItems.format: Platform export (PDF/CSV), incident case records, or compiled monitoring reports

evidenceItems.name: CTI Integration Evidence — Detection Rules and Playbook Updates
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that CTI outputs are operationally integrated into the institution's cybersecurity defences — specifically, evidence that intelligence drives updates to SIEM detection rules, firewall or proxy blocklists, and incident response playbooks. This closes the loop between intelligence collection and prompt threat detection as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Change records in the SIEM or SOAR platform show detection rules or correlation logic updated in response to specific CTI findings, with the originating intelligence referenced
IOC blocklists (IP addresses, domains, file hashes) maintained in security controls (firewall, proxy, EDR) are traceable to CTI sources and include creation and expiry timestamps
Incident response playbooks are versioned with a change log referencing CTI-driven updates, demonstrating that threat intelligence shapes response procedures
A regular cadence (e.g., weekly or bi-weekly) of CTI-to-operations integration meetings or review cycles is evidenced by meeting minutes or ITSM tickets
Metrics on rule effectiveness (e.g., number of IOC hits, true positive rate for CTI-derived rules) are tracked, demonstrating that intelligence quality is measured
commonGaps: IOC blocklists exist but contain only static, vendor-provided entries with no evidence of CTI-driven updates, suggesting the integration between intelligence and controls is absent
SIEM detection rules have not been updated for an extended period despite active CTI subscriptions, indicating that intelligence and detection engineering operate in silos
Playbooks are generic and not updated to reflect threat-actor TTPs identified through CTI, meaning intelligence has no influence on incident response approach
IOCs are imported into security controls without expiry management, leading to bloated, stale blocklists and no evidence of active intelligence lifecycle management
No audit trail exists linking a specific CTI finding to a specific control change, making it impossible to demonstrate that intelligence drives defensive action
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, QRadar) — detection rule version history and change records
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — change requests linked to CTI-driven rule updates
SOAR platform (Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) — playbook version history
Firewall and proxy management console — IOC blocklist update logs
EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) — custom indicator management records
evidenceItems.format: System-generated change logs (PDF export or ITSM reports), versioned playbook documents

auditTips: Map your evidence directly to the three clause obligations before the examination: (1) collect, (2) analyse and evaluate, and (3) prompt detection — including specifically data breach incidents and misleading information online. Prepare a one-page evidence matrix showing which document satisfies each obligation, so you can respond to examiner queries without searching during the assessment.

Pre-validate your dark web and impersonation monitoring coverage by running a targeted test sweep against your institution's domains, brand names, key executive names, and major product identifiers at least four weeks before examination. Any gaps discovered can be remediated before they become examiner findings.

Prepare at least one end-to-end case study — ideally a real incident, otherwise a tabletop simulation — that traces a CTI alert from initial detection through analysis, escalation, and defensive action. Examiners respond well to concrete narratives that demonstrate the process works in practice, not just on paper.

Reconcile your active threat intelligence feed list against what is actually ingested and operational in your SIEM or TIP before examination. A common examiner technique is to ask for a live demonstration of feed ingestion or to request IOC query results; feeds that appear in your inventory but are broken or inactive will immediately undermine credibility.

Ensure your CTI programme explicitly documents coverage of Malaysian and ASEAN regional threat actors and campaigns. BNM examiners expect threat intelligence to be contextualised to the local financial sector threat landscape — generic global threat reports without regional relevance signal that intelligence is not tailored to the institution's environment.

Cross-reference CTI evidence with your incident log for the review period. If incidents occurred during that period, examiners may ask whether your CTI programme provided any advance warning or relevant intelligence — the ability to demonstrate CTI's contribution to incident detection and response significantly strengthens your compliance posture.

Confirm that your misleading information monitoring is operationally connected to cybersecurity, not siloed in communications or marketing. Before examination, obtain written confirmation from the communications team of the escalation path to cybersecurity operations and produce at least one joint process record (e.g., a shared incident register entry or escalation email chain) demonstrating the integration works.

11.11.title: Anomalous Activity Response
11.11.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has moved beyond merely detecting anomalous activities to having documented, operationalised response procedures that are explicitly calibrated to complexity tiers. Examiners will probe whether the institution can demonstrate a clear linkage between how an alert is classified and the corresponding response workflow invoked — a flat, one-size-fits-all playbook is a red flag. They will request evidence of actual investigations conducted, looking for consistency between documented procedures and real case records, including escalation paths, timelines, and closure rationale. The absence of complexity-tiering criteria, untested playbooks, or a gap between policy and operational reality (e.g., analysts unable to articulate the procedure during walkthrough interviews) are primary concerns. Examiners also evaluate whether response capabilities are adequately resourced and whether lessons learned from past anomalies feed back into procedure refinement.

evidenceItems.name: Anomalous Activity Response Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: The formal, board- or senior management-approved policy and accompanying standard operating procedures (SOPs) that govern how the institution identifies, classifies, investigates, and responds to flagged anomalous activities. This document demonstrates that response obligations are institutionalised in writing and that complexity-based tiering is explicitly defined.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines at least three complexity tiers (e.g., Low, Medium, High/Critical) with clear criteria distinguishing each tier based on factors such as asset sensitivity, potential business impact, and attack sophistication
Each complexity tier maps to distinct response workflows, escalation authorities, and target response/resolution timeframes, leaving no ambiguity about what action is required at each level

Document carries current version control metadata, a review date within the last 12 months, and approval signatures from CISO and relevant governance body (e.g., IT Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee)
SOPs reference integration with the broader Incident Response Plan and contain step-by-step procedural guidance sufficient for a new analyst to execute without additional verbal instruction
Scope explicitly covers all detection sources feeding anomalous activity alerts (SIEM, EDR, UBA, network monitoring, fraud systems) so there are no blind spots in coverage
commonGaps: Policy exists but complexity tiering is absent or purely narrative — no objective criteria are defined to help analysts consistently classify an anomaly, resulting in ad-hoc, inconsistent responses

SOPs are generic incident response procedures repurposed without tailoring to anomalous activity scenarios, meaning they lack specificity on investigation techniques, evidence preservation, or containment steps relevant to anomaly types

Document has not been reviewed or updated following significant changes to the threat landscape, technology environment, or organisational structure, indicating it is stale and may not reflect current controls
Escalation paths and authority matrices are missing or reference outdated role titles and personnel, making it impossible to verify that escalation will function correctly during an actual event
The policy does not reference or integrate with detection tooling outputs, leaving a disconnect between how alerts are surfaced and how response is triggered
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cybersecurity department — master policy and procedure repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate) — policy management module
SharePoint or institutional intranet document management system
IT Risk or Cybersecurity Committee secretariat — approved policy register and meeting minutes recording approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy), PDF/DOCX (SOP), with version history log

evidenceItems.name: Anomaly Complexity Classification Framework
evidenceItems.description: A standalone or embedded classification matrix or decision tree that operationalises the tiering criteria referenced in the response policy. This artefact demonstrates how analysts systematically assign a complexity level to a flagged anomalous activity so that the correct response procedure is invoked. It is the bridge between detection and response.
whatGoodLooksLike: Classification framework uses objective, multi-factor scoring covering dimensions such as affected asset criticality, data sensitivity, lateral movement indicators, attacker persistence, and business process impact — reducing reliance on analyst subjectivity
Framework is presented as a decision tree, scoring rubric, or matrix that an analyst can apply within minutes of receiving an alert, with worked examples for each complexity tier
Criteria are cross-referenced to the institution's Critical Asset Register and Data Classification Policy, ensuring consistency with broader risk management frameworks
Framework has been validated through tabletop exercises or real case retrospectives, with evidence of adjustment based on outcomes — demonstrating it is operationally tested, not merely theoretical
Version control and ownership are clearly documented, and the framework is embedded within or directly linked from the SOPs so analysts access a single, authoritative source
commonGaps: Classification relies entirely on SIEM rule severity labels (Low/Medium/High) inherited from vendor defaults without institutional calibration, meaning complexity assignment reflects tool configuration rather than actual business risk context
Framework does not account for asset criticality or data sensitivity, resulting in identical response procedures being applied to a compromise of a core banking server and a developer workstation
No worked examples or case studies are provided, making the framework aspirational rather than operationally usable by analysts of varying experience levels
Framework exists in a separate document from SOPs, with no cross-referencing, so analysts may apply response procedures without consistently performing classification first
Classification criteria have never been tested or challenged through exercises, meaning their validity and discriminatory power under real-world conditions is unproven
suggestedSources: Security Operations Centre (SOC) — analyst runbook repository
SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) — alert triage workflow configuration
GRC platform — risk classification and asset register modules
Cybersecurity team SharePoint or Confluence wiki — SOC knowledge base
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX, Spreadsheet, or embedded within SOC runbook (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Anomalous Activity Investigation and Response Records
evidenceItems.description: Closed and in-progress case records from the ITSM or Security Incident Management platform documenting actual investigations into flagged anomalous activities. These records demonstrate that the institution's response procedures are operationalised in practice, that complexity classification is consistently applied, and that investigations are conducted and closed with appropriate rigour.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each case record captures the full investigation lifecycle: initial alert receipt, complexity classification with rationale, assigned analyst and escalation trail, investigation actions taken with timestamps, containment and remediation steps, and formal closure with root cause determination

Case records show variation in response depth and escalation that is proportionate to the assigned complexity tier — high-complexity cases demonstrate involvement of senior analysts, management, and potentially third-party forensic support, while low-complexity cases follow streamlined workflows

Timestamps across the case lifecycle demonstrate adherence to the target response and resolution timeframes defined in the SOPs for each complexity tier, with documented justification for any breaches

Investigation notes include technical artefacts referenced (e.g., log extracts, packet captures, memory dumps) with evidence of structured analysis methodology, demonstrating analytical depth rather than superficial review

A statistically representative sample across all complexity tiers can be produced, covering at least the past 12 months, showing the classification framework and procedures are consistently applied across different analysts and shifts
commonGaps: Case records are sparse and lack investigation detail — entries consist of brief notes such as 'Reviewed alert, no issue found' with no supporting analysis, making it impossible to verify that a genuine investigation was conducted

Complexity classification is absent from case records or is inconsistently applied, with many cases defaulting to 'Low' regardless of characteristics, suggesting the classification framework is not operationally embedded
Escalation trail is missing or incomplete, so for high-complexity cases there is no evidence that senior staff or management were involved as required by the SOPs
Case closure rationale is generic or missing — cases are closed without documented root cause, making it impossible to determine whether the anomaly was fully investigated or merely dismissed
Response timeline data is not captured or is captured inconsistently, preventing any assessment of whether the institution is meeting its own defined SLA targets for each complexity tier
suggestedSources: ITSM platform — security incident/case management module (e.g., ServiceNow Security Incident Response, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
SIEM platform — case/investigation workflow and alert disposition logs
SOAR platform (e.g., Palo Alto XSOAR, Splunk SOAR) — playbook execution logs and case records
SOC shift handover logs and analyst workbooks
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF or CSV), ITSM case report

evidenceItems.name: Anomalous Activity Response Playbooks
evidenceItems.description: Scenario-specific response playbooks that operationalise the general SOPs for common anomaly categories encountered by the institution (e.g., impossible travel, after-hours privileged access, bulk data exfiltration indicators, lateral movement patterns). These demonstrate that response is calibrated not only to complexity level but also to the nature of the anomaly, as required by the clause's reference to 'appropriate response'.
whatGoodLooksLike: Playbooks exist for the institution's most prevalent or highest-risk anomaly types, as identified through the threat intelligence and risk assessment process, demonstrating that response design is risk-informed rather than generic

Each playbook specifies the detection source and alert signature that triggers it, the classification criteria for each complexity tier within that scenario, and step-by-step investigation and containment actions tailored to that anomaly type

Playbooks include decision points with explicit branching logic (e.g., 'If lateral movement is confirmed, escalate to High tier and invoke Containment SOP'), ensuring analysts follow a structured methodology rather than improvising
Playbooks have been reviewed and approved within the last 12 months and carry evidence of testing through tabletop exercises or purple team activities, with post-exercise amendments documented

Playbooks reference integration touchpoints with other institutional processes (e.g., when to invoke the Crisis Management Framework, when to notify Compliance for potential regulatory breach, when to engage Legal for evidence preservation)
commonGaps: Institution relies on a single generic incident response playbook for all anomaly types, with no scenario-specific guidance — this fails to demonstrate that response is appropriately calibrated to the nature and complexity of different anomaly categories

Playbooks exist for common IT incidents (malware, phishing) but not for behavioural anomalies (UBA alerts, insider threat indicators, anomalous transaction patterns), leaving significant detection coverage without corresponding response guidance
Playbooks are theoretical documents that have never been tested — no tabletop exercise records, simulation logs, or after-action reviews exist to validate their operational effectiveness

Integration with fraud detection and AML monitoring systems is absent from playbooks, creating a siloed response where financial anomalies and cybersecurity anomalies are handled without coordination despite potential overlap
Playbooks do not specify when to invoke digital forensics and evidence preservation procedures, risking destruction of forensic evidence in the course of remediation activities
suggestedSources: SOC runbook repository — Confluence, SharePoint, or SOAR platform playbook library
SOAR platform — automated playbook configurations and manual playbook documentation
Cybersecurity team — scenario-specific procedure documents
Red Team / Purple Team exercise outputs — playbook validation and gap analysis reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX, or SOAR platform export with workflow diagrams

evidenceItems.name: SOC Analyst Training and Competency Records
evidenceItems.description: Training completion records, competency assessments, and exercise participation logs demonstrating that personnel responsible for investigating and responding to anomalous activities are equipped with the skills and knowledge to execute the procedures. This addresses the implicit requirement that 'appropriate response' cannot be achieved without adequately trained responders.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training records cover both technical skills (SIEM operation, log analysis, malware triage, network forensics) and procedural knowledge (complexity classification, escalation authority, evidence handling), with completion dates within an appropriate currency window (e.g., annually)
Competency assessments or practical evaluations are documented for each analyst, confirming that training has translated into operational capability — not merely attendance certificates

Participation records for tabletop exercises, red team engagements, or simulation scenarios show that analysts have rehearsed anomalous activity response under realistic conditions, with performance observations recorded

Training plan is mapped to the institution's anomaly classification framework and playbook library, ensuring training content directly supports the documented response procedures rather than being generic security awareness content
Skills gap analysis is conducted periodically and informs the training plan, demonstrating a continuous improvement approach to capability development
commonGaps: Training records exist only for general security awareness (e.g., phishing simulations, annual compliance training) with no evidence of role-specific, technical training for SOC analysts on anomaly investigation and response
Training is conducted once at onboarding with no evidence of refresher training, leaving analysts' skills potentially outdated as the threat landscape and response procedures evolve
No competency assessments or practical evaluations are conducted — the institution cannot demonstrate that trained analysts can actually execute the procedures, only that they attended sessions
Exercise participation is limited to senior analysts or management, with frontline SOC analysts who handle day-to-day alert triage not included in scenario rehearsals
Training records are maintained informally (e.g., email confirmations) without a centralised HR or LMS system record, making it impossible to produce a comprehensive training register on request
suggestedSources: Human Resources — Learning Management System (LMS) training completion records
Cybersecurity team — internal training programme records, exercise participation logs
External training providers — course completion certificates (e.g., SANS, EC-Council, vendor training)
Tabletop exercise and red team engagement reports — participant lists and performance notes
evidenceItems.format: LMS system export (PDF/spreadsheet), certificates (PDF), exercise reports (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Post-Investigation Review and Lessons Learned Register
evidenceItems.description: Records of structured post-investigation reviews (post-incident reviews or after-action reviews) conducted following the closure of anomalous activity investigations, particularly for Medium and High complexity cases. These demonstrate that the institution continuously refines its response capability and that the clause's requirement for 'appropriate response' is treated as a dynamic standard subject to ongoing improvement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-investigation reviews are conducted systematically for all Medium and High complexity cases, with a documented review template capturing root cause, timeline analysis, response effectiveness assessment, and identified improvement actions

Improvement actions from reviews are tracked to completion in the GRC or ITSM platform, with owners and target dates assigned — demonstrating that lessons learned translate into operational changes rather than remaining as paper observations

Lessons learned are periodically aggregated and reviewed at the SOC management or IT Risk Committee level, with evidence that systemic findings (e.g., recurring classification errors, persistent tooling gaps) are escalated and addressed at an institutional level

Review records show evidence of procedure updates, playbook amendments, training enhancements, or detection rule tuning attributable to specific investigation findings, demonstrating a closed feedback loop

A register of completed reviews is maintained and can be produced as a summary document, enabling examiners to assess the volume, tiering distribution, and improvement trajectory of the institution's anomalous activity response programme
commonGaps: Post-investigation reviews are conducted only for declared security incidents that escalate to crisis level — routine anomaly investigations, including significant Medium complexity cases, are closed without structured review, meaning the majority of learning opportunities are lost

Reviews are conducted informally in verbal team debriefs with no documented record, making it impossible to demonstrate to examiners that structured review processes exist or that specific findings were identified
Improvement actions are identified in reviews but are not tracked to completion — follow-up records show the same gaps recurring across multiple review cycles without resolution
Lessons learned are not shared across the SOC or fed back into playbook and procedure updates, meaning knowledge remains siloed with individual analysts and institutional learning does not accumulate

The lessons learned register is maintained as a spreadsheet with infrequent updates and no governance oversight, giving examiners no confidence that the improvement process is managed with appropriate rigour
suggestedSources: ITSM platform — post-incident review records linked to closed anomaly investigation cases
GRC platform — action tracking and risk treatment module
IT Risk Committee or Cybersecurity Committee — meeting minutes referencing lessons learned agenda items
SOC management — internal review registers, improvement action logs
SharePoint or document management system — post-investigation review templates and completed reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (review reports), Spreadsheet or GRC system export (action register)

auditTips: Before the examination, conduct an end-to-end walkthrough of at least three real closed cases — one from each complexity tier — and verify that the documented response procedure was actually followed: check that classification rationale is recorded, escalation occurred as specified, and closure documentation meets the SOP standard. Gaps found internally are far less costly than gaps found by examiners.

Pull a 12-month statistical summary from your ITSM or SIEM platform showing the volume of anomalous activity cases by complexity tier, average time-to-respond and time-to-close by tier, and escalation rates. Examiners will ask for this data to assess whether the tiering framework is functioning and whether response SLAs are being met — having it pre-prepared demonstrates operational maturity.

Interview two or three frontline SOC analysts independently and ask them to walk through how they would classify and respond to a specific anomaly scenario without referencing documentation. If analysts struggle to articulate the classification criteria or cannot identify the correct playbook, this is a strong signal that documented procedures are not operationally embedded — a finding examiners actively probe through similar walkthroughs during examination.

Cross-reference the coverage of your anomaly response playbooks against the detection use cases active in your SIEM and UBA tools. Any detection rule that generates alerts for which no specific response guidance exists represents a procedural gap — prioritise closing these gaps, particularly for high-frequency or high-severity alert types, before the examination.

Ensure your post-investigation review records can demonstrate a closed improvement loop: select two or three findings from reviews conducted in the past 12 months and verify that the corresponding procedure update, playbook amendment, or training change has been completed and documented. Examiners will test whether lessons learned translate into concrete changes or whether the review process is performative.

Verify that your complexity classification criteria explicitly reference asset criticality by linking to the institution's Critical Asset Register. If anomaly response is calibrated purely to technical severity indicators without reference to business impact or asset sensitivity, this is a significant gap under clause 11.11 — the clause requires response to be appropriate to complexity, and BNM expects complexity to be understood in a business risk context, not just a technical one.

Prepare a document lineage map showing how clause 11.11 obligations flow from policy to SOP to playbook to case record — this gives examiners a clear navigation guide through your evidence and demonstrates governance coherence. Institutions that present fragmented, disconnected documents without a clear thread from obligation to implementation consistently receive more scrutiny than those who guide examiners through a well-structured evidence package.

11.12.title: Cyber Crisis Management
11.12.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether cyber crisis management is genuinely integrated into the institution's enterprise-wide crisis governance framework, not treated as a standalone IT document. Examiners look for explicit cyber-attack scenarios embedded in crisis management plans and tested through tabletop or simulation exercises, with evidence of Board and senior management awareness. A key red flag is when BCPs and DRPs reference only natural disasters or operational failures without cyber-specific triggers, recovery objectives, or threat-actor-aware response procedures. Examiners also scrutinise the stakeholder communication plan for completeness — institutions frequently define internal escalation but lack structured, pre-approved templates and authorities for regulatory notifications to BNM, customer advisories, and board-level briefings. The 'S' marker signals a mandatory supervisory expectation, meaning gaps are treated as findings rather than recommendations.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Crisis Management Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The formal policy document and associated procedures that govern the institution's response to cyber crises, demonstrating that cyber-attack scenarios are explicitly incorporated into the overarching crisis management framework with defined scope, ownership, and review cycles.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is Board-approved, version-controlled, and reviewed at least annually or after a material cyber incident
Scope explicitly covers cyber-attack scenarios (e.g., ransomware, data breach, DDoS, supply-chain compromise) cross-referenced to the enterprise crisis management plan
Roles and responsibilities are clearly assigned including a named Cyber Crisis Manager or equivalent with delegated authority
Procedures include decision-tree flowcharts for crisis declaration, escalation thresholds, and stand-down criteria
Document references linkages to BCP, DRP, and the institution's Incident Response Plan with version numbers ensuring alignment
commonGaps: Policy exists as a standalone cybersecurity document with no formal integration clause or cross-reference to the enterprise crisis management plan
Cyber-attack scenarios are absent or limited to a generic 'cyber incident' category without threat-specific response procedures
Roles are assigned to job titles that no longer match the current organisational structure, indicating the document is not actively maintained
Policy has not been reviewed or re-approved following significant changes such as cloud migration, M&A activity, or a prior incident
Procedures lack defined escalation thresholds (e.g., severity levels, financial impact triggers) leaving crisis declaration to individual judgement
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — policy register module
SharePoint or internal intranet policy library maintained by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or Risk Management function
Board and Board Risk Committee secretariat for approval records and meeting minutes
ISO 27001 / ISMS document management system if the institution is certified
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Attack Scenario Library and Crisis Response Playbooks
evidenceItems.description: A structured set of documented cyber-attack scenarios and corresponding crisis response playbooks that demonstrate the institution has operationalised threat-specific responses within its crisis management framework, covering realistic threat vectors relevant to the Malaysian financial sector.
whatGoodLooksLike: Scenario library covers at least five distinct attack vectors relevant to financial institutions — including ransomware/destructive malware, large-scale data exfiltration, core banking system compromise, third-party/supply-chain attack, and DDoS against critical services
Each playbook contains defined trigger conditions, immediate containment actions, crisis declaration criteria, recovery time objectives, and communications actions
Playbooks reference specific internal systems, data assets, and third-party dependencies relevant to each scenario, demonstrating they are institution-specific rather than generic templates
Scenarios are threat-intelligence informed, updated when new threat actors or TTPs emerge, with evidence of review dates and threat intel inputs
Each playbook assigns named roles (e.g., Incident Commander, Legal Counsel, Communications Lead) with backups identified for key-person risk
commonGaps: Playbooks are generic templates lifted from industry frameworks (e.g., NIST, SANS) without customisation to the institution's technology environment or risk profile
Scenario library covers only technical response steps without crisis management actions such as regulatory notification, customer communication, or board escalation
Playbooks exist for IT incidents but are not formally linked to or recognised within the enterprise crisis management plan hierarchy
Recovery Time Objectives and Recovery Point Objectives are absent from scenario playbooks, preventing meaningful DRP alignment
Scenarios have not been updated to reflect current threat intelligence, leaving outdated vectors (e.g., no cloud-specific or AI-assisted attack scenarios)
suggestedSources: CISO or Cyber Defence team's secure document repository (e.g., Confluence, SharePoint restricted site)
Security Operations Centre (SOC) runbook library — ITSM platform such as ServiceNow or Jira Service Management
Threat Intelligence platform (e.g., Anomali, MISP) for evidence of threat intel inputs to scenario updates
Crisis Management or Business Continuity team's GRC or BCP management tool
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX playbooks with version history; scenario matrix as spreadsheet or GRC platform record

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Incident Escalation Framework and Authority Matrix
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing the escalation processes for cyber incidents and crises, including severity classification criteria, escalation paths, decision authorities at each tier, and timelines for escalation to senior management, the Board, and regulatory bodies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Escalation matrix defines at least three severity tiers with clear, measurable criteria (e.g., data records affected, financial impact, system availability loss percentage, reputational exposure)
Each tier specifies notification timelines, required decision-makers (e.g., CISO, CRO, CEO, Board Risk Committee), and expected actions, with no ambiguity about who declares a crisis
Regulatory escalation path explicitly references BNM notification obligations including reporting timeframes consistent with RMiT and any supervisory letters received
Escalation procedures include out-of-hours contact protocols with maintained call trees, tested at least annually
Document is integrated into the enterprise crisis management plan and cross-referenced in cyber response playbooks
commonGaps: Escalation criteria are subjective (e.g., 'significant incident') with no measurable thresholds, leading to inconsistent escalation decisions evidenced in past incident post-mortems
BNM notification requirements are absent or reference an incorrect timeframe, reflecting gaps in regulatory awareness at the operational level
Contact lists and call trees are outdated, with departed employees or incorrect numbers identified during testing
Escalation matrix covers IT and operational teams but does not include Legal, Compliance, Corporate Communications, or the Board as required escalation recipients for a declared cyber crisis
No documented decision log template or authority to declare and stand down a cyber crisis, leaving audit trails incomplete
suggestedSources: GRC or incident management platform — escalation workflow configuration and documented authority matrix
ITSM tool (ServiceNow, BMC Remedy) — incident severity classification rules and escalation routing records
Human Resources or Business Continuity team for current contact directory and call tree records
Compliance or Regulatory Affairs team for BNM notification obligation register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX authority matrix; call tree as spreadsheet or HR system export; ITSM escalation workflow screenshot

evidenceItems.name: BCP and DRP Cyber Integration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating that cyber-attack scenarios are formally embedded within the institution's Business Continuity Plans and Disaster Recovery Plans, with cyber-specific recovery objectives, alternate operating procedures, and tested recovery capabilities.
whatGoodLooksLike: BCP documentation explicitly lists cyber incidents as a recognised disruption scenario alongside traditional threats, with cyber-specific alternate operating procedures for critical business functions

DRP includes cyber-specific recovery procedures for core systems distinguishing between cyber-induced outages and traditional failures, including clean-restore procedures, integrity verification steps, and isolation protocols
RTO and RPO for cyber scenarios are defined, documented, and demonstrably achievable based on test results — not simply inherited from infrastructure failure scenarios
BCP/DRP documents reference the cyber crisis management policy and playbooks as upstream inputs, with version alignment confirming documents are maintained together
Business Impact Analyses (BIAs) include cyber threat scenarios as a recognised threat category with impact ratings reviewed at least annually
commonGaps: BCP and DRP documents treat cyber incidents as a subset of 'system failure' without acknowledging the unique characteristics of cyber recovery (e.g., forensic preservation requirements, integrity verification before restoration, attacker persistence risk)
RTOs and RPOs for cyber scenarios have never been independently tested; figures are aspirational rather than evidence-based
BCP covers people and process continuity but DRP focuses solely on infrastructure, with no integration point ensuring both are activated cohesively during a cyber crisis
BCP/DRP documents and cyber crisis management policy are maintained by separate teams (BCM and CISO respectively) with no formal synchronisation process, leading to version misalignment
Backup and recovery procedures do not address ransomware-specific risks such as encrypted backup sets or compromised backup infrastructure
suggestedSources: Business Continuity Management (BCM) team's BCP/DRP repository on GRC platform or SharePoint
IT Operations or Infrastructure team — DRP runbooks and recovery procedure documentation
BCP testing and exercise records including after-action reports from the past two exercise cycles
Business Impact Analysis reports maintained by BCM or Enterprise Risk Management team
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX BCP and DRP documents with BIA supporting annexes; exercise test reports

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Cyber Incident Communication Plan
evidenceItems.description: A documented communication plan specifying how the institution will engage all required stakeholder groups — shareholders, BNM and other regulatory authorities, customers, and employees — in the event of a cyber incident, including pre-approved templates, designated spokespersons, approval workflows, and channel-specific strategies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication plan explicitly addresses all four stakeholder groups named in clause 11.12 (shareholders, regulatory authorities, customers, employees) with tailored content, timing, and channel guidance for each
Pre-approved communication templates exist for each stakeholder group and for common cyber incident types (data breach, service outage, ransomware), reducing response time under crisis pressure

Regulatory notification process for BNM specifies the responsible officer, approved communication channels, mandatory content (incident nature, scope, containment actions, remediation timeline), and required notification timeframe
Designated spokespersons and approval authorities are named for each communication channel (media, regulator, customer portal, internal all-staff) with backups identified
Communication plan is exercised during tabletop or crisis simulation exercises with documented results and improvement actions
commonGaps: Communication plan addresses internal escalation and regulatory notification but omits customer-facing and shareholder communications entirely, or defers them to a generic corporate communications policy
Templates exist for data breach scenarios but not for service unavailability or other cyber crisis types, requiring drafting from scratch under time pressure
BNM notification content requirements are undefined — templates lack mandatory fields such as estimated number of affected customers, data categories involved, and containment measures taken
Approval workflows for crisis communications are undocumented, causing delays in real incidents when legal, compliance, and communications teams dispute approval authority
Communication plan has never been exercised; no tabletop or simulation records exist to validate that templates are fit-for-purpose and approvals can be obtained within required timeframes
suggestedSources: Corporate Communications or Group Communications team's crisis communication playbook and template library
Compliance or Regulatory Affairs team — BNM notification procedures and correspondence templates
Legal team — customer and shareholder disclosure protocols and approved template sign-off records
Crisis management exercise records maintained by BCM or Risk Management team
Investor Relations team for shareholder communication protocols and Bursa Malaysia disclosure obligations if applicable
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX communication plan with template annexes; exercise after-action reports

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Crisis Exercise Records and Post-Exercise Improvement Tracking
evidenceItems.description: Records of cyber crisis management exercises — including tabletop simulations, full-scale drills, and regulatory-mandated exercises — demonstrating that the institution tests its cyber crisis management capabilities, identifies gaps, and implements improvements in a structured and documented manner.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exercise programme includes at least one cyber-specific tabletop or simulation exercise per year, with scenarios drawn from the cyber attack scenario library and involving senior management and Board-level participants

Exercise records include pre-exercise objectives, scenario injects, participant list (with seniority indicated), observed findings, and a formal after-action report signed off by the CISO or Crisis Management lead
After-action reports generate a remediation action log with assigned owners, target completion dates, and evidence of closure — not merely a list of findings left unresolved
Communication plan elements are tested within exercises, including drafting and approving stakeholder notifications against realistic time constraints
Exercise history spans at least the current and prior review period, demonstrating a sustained programme rather than a one-off event ahead of examination
commonGaps: Exercises are conducted by IT or security teams only, without participation from Legal, Compliance, Corporate Communications, or senior management, meaning crisis governance and communication capabilities are never tested
After-action reports document findings but remediation actions have no assigned owners or deadlines; follow-up tracking shows most actions as open or overdue
Exercises test technical recovery only (e.g., system restore from backup) without testing crisis declaration, escalation, or stakeholder communication components
Exercise scenarios are repeated year-on-year without introducing new threat vectors, providing false assurance without improving institutional resilience
Records are informal (e.g., email summaries or verbal debriefs) with no structured after-action report, preventing auditors from assessing what was actually tested and what was found
suggestedSources: BCM or Enterprise Risk team's exercise management records on GRC platform or SharePoint
CISO office — tabletop exercise facilitation reports and scenario documentation
Board Risk Committee and EXCO meeting minutes referencing exercise outcomes and management responses
Remediation action tracker maintained in GRC platform, Jira, or equivalent project tracking tool
evidenceItems.format: PDF after-action reports; spreadsheet or GRC platform remediation tracker with closure evidence

auditTips: Cross-reference the version dates and approval dates of the cyber crisis management policy, BCP, DRP, and stakeholder communication plan side-by-side — misaligned version dates are a reliable indicator that these documents are maintained in silos and not genuinely integrated as required by clause 11.12.

Request the last three cyber incident post-incident reports or after-action records and trace whether escalation procedures were actually followed — deviations from documented processes in real incidents reveal whether the documented framework is operational or merely aspirational.

Test the stakeholder communication plan by asking the compliance team to demonstrate how they would notify BNM within the required timeframe: who drafts the notification, who approves it, and what template is used. The inability to produce pre-approved templates or name the approving officer on the spot is a strong indicator of a documentation gap.

Examine BCP and DRP documents for cyber-specific language: search for terms such as 'ransomware', 'data breach', 'forensic', and 'integrity verification'. Generic references to 'system failure' without cyber-specific considerations indicate that cyber integration is superficial rather than substantive.

Verify that exercise participants at the most recent tabletop included representation from Legal, Corporate Communications, Compliance, and at least one EXCO-level executive — attendance lists from exercises are a reliable proxy for whether the institution treats cyber crisis as a cross-functional governance matter or an IT-only concern.

Review the remediation action log from the most recent cyber exercise after-action report and assess closure rates: a high volume of open or overdue items relative to the exercise date suggests the institution exercises for compliance purposes rather than genuine capability improvement.

Check whether the escalation matrix explicitly references BNM notification obligations and the correct reporting timeframes — inconsistency between the escalation matrix and the institution's regulatory obligation register indicates that the Compliance and CISO functions are not coordinating effectively on cyber crisis governance.

11.13.title: Cyber Incident Response Plan
11.13.auditorFocus: BNM examiners scrutinise whether the CIRP is a living, operationalised document rather than a shelf artefact — they will test whether staff can actually execute it under pressure. Examiners pay particular attention to the completeness and currency of CERT governance structures, verifying that roles, deputies, and escalation thresholds are named and unambiguous. A major red flag is a CIRP that has never been formally invoked or exercised, or where post-incident review records are absent or superficial, suggesting the plan is not embedded in institutional culture. Examiners also cross-reference detection and forensic evidence-preservation procedures against actual incident records to confirm the plan was followed in practice, not just documented in theory. Recovery strategies are evaluated for specificity — generic statements about resuming operations without measurable RTOs, RPOs, or documented contingency options will attract findings.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) Master Document
evidenceItems.description: The authoritative CIRP policy document that consolidates all five response phases — Preparedness, Detection and Analysis, Containment and Eradication, Recovery, and Post-Incident Activity — into a single, Board or senior management-approved framework. This document demonstrates the institution has fulfilled the foundational obligation to establish a comprehensive written plan.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is formally approved by the Board or delegated senior management committee, with a visible approval date and version number updated within the last 12 months
All five phases mandated under clause 11.13(a)–(e) are explicitly addressed in dedicated sections with clear procedural steps, not just high-level descriptions

CERT membership is named with primary and backup contacts, reporting lines are mapped to organisational structure, and invocation thresholds are expressed in measurable criteria (e.g., severity ratings tied to impact categories)
The plan cross-references related documents such as the BCP, DRP, crisis communications protocol, and regulatory notification obligations under BNM guidelines
Scope covers all material technology assets, third-party service providers, and cloud environments, with explicit applicability statements
