commonGaps: CIRP exists as a high-level policy without operational procedures — phases are described in one or two sentences with no actionable steps or decision trees
Document has not been reviewed or re-approved for more than 12–18 months, and does not reflect changes in the institution's technology landscape or personnel
CERT roles list job titles only, with no named individuals or alternates, making real-world invocation ambiguous
Recovery phase lacks defined RTOs and RPOs, and contingency strategies are referenced by name only without supporting runbooks or tested procedures
Third-party and outsourced service providers are excluded from scope despite being material to the institution's critical systems
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — policy repository module
SharePoint or Confluence — Information Security policy library
Board/Risk Committee secretariat — board paper approvals and minutes
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) office — master policy register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: CERT Governance Charter and Escalation Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A dedicated governance document that defines the Cyber Emergency Response Team's mandate, membership hierarchy, decision-making authority, escalation thresholds, and communication protocols. This evidence demonstrates compliance with clause 11.13(a) by showing that governance processes and reporting structures are formalised and unambiguous prior to any incident occurring.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter names the CERT Lead, deputy, and functional representatives (IT, Legal, Compliance, Communications, Business Lines) with contact details updated quarterly

Escalation matrix maps each incident severity tier to specific internal escalation actions, timelines (e.g., notify CISO within 1 hour, Board within 4 hours for Severity 1), and regulatory notification obligations including BNM reporting windows
Invocation criteria are objective and measurable — defined by severity scoring rubric (e.g., number of systems affected, data classification of compromised assets, financial impact threshold)
Document includes a tested out-of-band communication channel (e.g., encrypted messaging platform, bridge line) separate from potentially compromised corporate infrastructure
Charter has been formally adopted by the Risk Management Committee or equivalent governance body, with dated approval records
commonGaps: CERT membership does not include representation from Legal, Communications, or senior business lines, limiting the team's ability to manage regulatory and reputational dimensions of an incident
Escalation thresholds are qualitative and subjective (e.g., 'significant incident') with no measurable criteria, creating ambiguity during high-stress response situations
No out-of-band or alternative communication channel is designated, meaning a network-compromising attack would disable the primary coordination mechanism
Charter has not been updated to reflect organisational restructuring, personnel changes, or the addition of new critical systems since original approval
Regulatory notification timelines are absent or do not align with BNM's required reporting windows under the incident reporting framework
suggestedSources: GRC platform — risk and incident governance module
Human Resources system — for verifying named personnel and their current roles
IT Service Management (ITSM) tool (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — incident management configuration
Compliance department — regulatory notification obligation register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with org chart attachments and version-controlled escalation matrix (spreadsheet or table)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Detection, Triage, and Forensic Preservation Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented operational procedures covering the Detection and Analysis phase under clause 11.13(b), including processes for identifying indicators of compromise, triaging alerts, assessing the scope and severity of damage, and preserving digital forensic evidence in a manner admissible for investigation and regulatory inquiry.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures specify the authoritative detection sources (SIEM, EDR, threat intelligence feeds, user reporting channels) and define how alerts from each source are triaged and correlated
Forensic evidence preservation steps follow a documented chain-of-custody process, specifying imaging tools, hash verification methods, storage media, and personnel authorised to handle evidence
Damage assessment methodology includes a defined scope-expansion checklist — analysts are guided to systematically identify lateral movement, persistence mechanisms, and affected data classifications
Procedures reference specific tools in use (e.g., SIEM platform name, EDR solution, forensic tooling) rather than generic placeholders, demonstrating operational readiness
Log retention settings and forensic data sources are documented and cross-referenced to the institution's log management policy to confirm evidence will be available when needed
commonGaps: Forensic evidence preservation is mentioned in the CIRP but no chain-of-custody process or approved tooling is specified, leaving analysts without a defensible methodology during an actual incident
Detection procedures rely solely on automated SIEM alerts with no documented process for handling incidents reported by users, third parties, or external threat intelligence
Triage criteria do not distinguish between security events and security incidents, leading to inconsistent severity classification and inappropriate escalation decisions
Log retention periods are insufficient or inconsistently configured across systems, meaning forensic evidence required to determine the full scope of an incident may be unavailable
Procedures have not been updated to cover cloud-hosted environments or SaaS platforms, leaving detection and evidence collection gaps for a material portion of the institution's attack surface
suggestedSources: Security Operations Centre (SOC) — SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) playbook library
Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform — alert triage runbooks
Digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) team documentation repository
Log management platform — retention configuration exports
SharePoint or Confluence — SOC standard operating procedures library
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX operational runbooks and SIEM playbook exports

evidenceItems.name: Containment and Eradication Runbooks with Incident Execution Records
evidenceItems.description: Tactical runbooks detailing containment and eradication actions for common cyber incident scenarios (e.g., ransomware, data exfiltration, insider threat, DDoS), paired with records from actual or simulated incident executions demonstrating that procedures were followed. This evidence addresses clause 11.13(c) by showing both documented remedial action capability and evidence of practical application.
whatGoodLooksLike: Runbooks are scenario-specific and include step-by-step containment actions (e.g., network segmentation commands, account lockout procedures, firewall rule changes) with responsible roles assigned to each step

Each runbook includes explicit eradication verification steps — criteria that must be met before declaring a threat removed, such as clean AV scans, log analysis confirming no persistence mechanisms, and credential rotation confirmation
At least three to five distinct attack scenario runbooks exist covering threats relevant to the institution's risk profile and sector (e.g., BEC, ransomware, supply chain compromise)

Incident execution records from actual events or tabletop/simulation exercises show that containment decisions were logged with timestamps, decision-makers identified, and deviations from runbooks documented with rationale
Runbooks define explicit criteria for transitioning from containment to eradication to recovery, preventing premature resumption of operations before threats are fully removed
commonGaps: Runbooks are generic and technology-agnostic, providing no actionable commands or platform-specific steps, making them unusable under time pressure during a real incident
No eradication verification criteria exist — the plan describes removing threats but does not specify how analysts confirm the threat has been fully eliminated before recovery begins
Runbooks cover only one or two scenarios (commonly ransomware) while leaving other high-probability attack vectors for the sector undocumented
Incident execution records are absent or incomplete — containment decisions during past incidents cannot be reconstructed, suggesting the plan was not actively used or documented during response
Containment procedures do not address third-party or supply chain compromise scenarios, leaving a gap for incidents originating from vendor access
suggestedSources: SOC and CERT shared repository — scenario runbook library (Confluence, SharePoint, or ITSM knowledge base)
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — incident and change records linked to security events
Vulnerability management and EDR platforms — remediation action logs
Tabletop exercise facilitator reports and after-action records
Network Operations Centre (NOC) — firewall change logs and network segmentation records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX runbooks; ITSM system export or PDF for execution records

evidenceItems.name: Incident Recovery Plans and Business Resumption Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the recovery strategies and contingency plans required under clause 11.13(d), including defined RTOs and RPOs for critical systems, failover and redundancy configurations, and evidence that recovery procedures have been tested. Execution records from actual recovery events or Business Continuity Plan (BCP) tests demonstrating the institution's ability to swiftly resume operations following a cyber incident.
whatGoodLooksLike: Recovery plans define system-specific RTOs and RPOs that are consistent with the institution's BIA findings and Board-approved risk appetite, and are explicitly linked to the CIRP rather than treated as a standalone BCP artefact

Multiple recovery strategies are documented for critical systems (e.g., hot standby failover, cold backup restoration, manual workaround procedures), demonstrating strategic redundancy as required by clause 11.13(d)

Recovery test records from the past 12 months include actual RTO achievement metrics, issues encountered, and sign-off from technology and business owners confirming successful resumption of operations
The recovery phase includes explicit criteria for declaring an incident resolved and transitioning to normal operations, with documented approval authority (e.g., CISO or CTO sign-off required)
Third-party dependencies critical to recovery (e.g., cloud providers, payment processors, core banking vendors) are identified with documented escalation contacts and SLA references
commonGaps: Recovery plans exist within the BCP/DRP but are not integrated into or referenced by the CIRP, creating a disconnect between cyber incident response and actual recovery execution
RTOs and RPOs are defined but have never been validated through testing — the institution cannot demonstrate it can actually achieve its stated recovery targets
Recovery strategies are singular (relying on a single backup restoration approach) without documented alternatives if the primary method fails or is compromised by the same attack
Recovery procedures do not account for scenarios where backup systems or data are also compromised (e.g., ransomware affecting backup repositories), leaving no documented fallback
Third-party recovery dependencies are unidentified or undocumented, creating blind spots where an institution believes it can recover but is actually dependent on an uncontracted vendor capability
suggestedSources: BCP/DRP documentation repository — SharePoint, GRC platform, or Business Continuity management tool
Infrastructure and Cloud Operations team — DR configuration documentation and failover architecture diagrams
ITSM platform — change and incident records from past recovery events
Backup management platform (e.g., Veeam, Commvault) — restore test logs and reports
Third-party contract management system — vendor SLAs and escalation contact registers
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX plans; spreadsheet or system export for RTO/RPO tracking; PDF for test reports

evidenceItems.name: Post-Incident Review Reports and Risk Mitigation Tracking Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal post-incident review (PIR) reports and associated remediation tracking artefacts covering clause 11.13(e). These records demonstrate that each material cyber incident or exercise is followed by a structured lessons-learned process, that findings are translated into documented long-term risk mitigations, and that action items are tracked to closure by accountable owners.
whatGoodLooksLike: PIR reports are completed for all material incidents within a defined timeframe (e.g., within 14 calendar days of incident closure) and follow a consistent structured template covering timeline reconstruction, root cause analysis, impact assessment, and CIRP effectiveness evaluation

Lessons learned are specific and actionable — each finding is mapped to a named control gap, an assigned owner, a remediation action, and a target completion date, rather than being recorded as vague observations
Long-term risk mitigation actions are tracked in the institution's GRC platform or risk register with status updates, providing an auditable chain from incident to control improvement
PIR findings that identify systemic or recurring weaknesses are escalated to the Risk Management Committee or Board Risk Committee, with evidence of this escalation in meeting minutes
The CIRP itself is updated following PIR completion when procedural gaps are identified, and the revision is documented with change rationale referencing the originating incident
commonGaps: PIR reports are produced inconsistently — only completed for high-profile incidents while lower-severity events that could reveal systemic weaknesses are left unreviewed

Lessons-learned sections contain generic observations (e.g., 'improve detection capabilities') with no specific findings, owners, or timelines, making them unenforceable and unverifiable at examination

Remediation actions identified in PIR reports are not tracked to completion — the institution cannot demonstrate that past findings have actually been addressed or that repeat incidents have been prevented
PIR process does not feed back into CIRP updates, meaning the plan remains static despite recurring findings that expose the same procedural gaps incident after incident

Post-incident reviews are conducted only by the IT/SOC team without involvement from Legal, Compliance, or senior business management, limiting the review's ability to identify regulatory, reputational, or enterprise risk dimensions
suggestedSources: GRC platform — incident risk register and remediation tracking module
ITSM platform — post-incident problem management records and linked change requests
SOC/CERT shared documentation repository — PIR report archive (SharePoint, Confluence)
Risk Management Committee and Board Risk Committee secretariat — meeting minutes evidencing escalation of material findings
CISO office — CIRP version history and change log documenting PIR-driven updates
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX PIR reports; GRC platform export or spreadsheet for remediation tracking

auditTips: Map each of the five CIRP phases — (a) through (e) — explicitly to a section of your submitted evidence package, using a cross-reference index. BNM examiners assess completeness against the verbatim sub-clauses, and any phase without dedicated evidence will be treated as a gap regardless of how comprehensive the overall CIRP document appears.

Produce a CIRP exercise or simulation record dated within the past 12 months showing the full invocation cycle from detection through post-incident review. A CIRP that has never been exercised or invoked is a critical finding — examiners specifically look for evidence the plan can be operationalised, not merely documented.

Cross-reference your CIRP against actual incident records from the past two years. Pull ITSM tickets and SOC logs from any material security events and annotate where CIRP procedures were followed, deviations taken, and decisions made. This retrospective linkage is powerful evidence of genuine operationalisation.

Verify that CERT governance documents reflect current personnel before submission — examiners frequently check whether named CERT members are still in their stated roles by cross-referencing with HR records or directory services. Stale rosters are a common, easily avoidable finding that undermines confidence in overall CIRP readiness.

Prepare a consolidated PIR log showing all incidents subject to post-incident review over the past two to three years, with status of each remediation action (open, in-progress, closed). Examiners will probe whether the same root causes recur across incidents, which would indicate lessons-learned reviews are not resulting in durable risk mitigations as required by clause 11.13(e).

Align RTO and RPO figures in your CIRP recovery section with the values approved in your Business Impact Assessment and communicated to the Board — any discrepancy between these documents will be identified by examiners comparing artefacts and will raise questions about governance oversight of recovery capability.

Obtain written confirmation from your material third-party service providers that their incident response procedures are compatible with and support your CIRP — particularly regarding notification timelines, evidence sharing, and cooperative containment actions. Clause 11.13 does not exempt third-party-hosted systems, and examiners increasingly request evidence of vendor IR alignment as institutions shift workloads to cloud and outsourced environments.

11.14.title: CERT Member Readiness
11.14.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether each CERT member can operationally demonstrate familiarity with the incident response plan (IRP) and handling procedures — not merely that they received a document. Examiners will request training records, signed acknowledgements, and exercise participation logs to verify active comprehension rather than passive distribution. A key red flag is a gap between the IRP version in force and the training materials or test scenarios used, indicating members may be rehearsing outdated procedures. Examiners also verify 24/7 contactability through on-call rosters, escalation directories, and evidence of periodic reachability tests, paying particular attention to whether deputy or backup contacts are designated and tested. Institutions that rely solely on email distribution of the IRP without follow-up competency checks are routinely cited for non-compliance with this clause.

evidenceItems.name: CERT Member Training and Competency Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal records demonstrating that each CERT member has received structured training on the current incident response plan and handling procedures, including dates, delivery method, trainer credentials, and assessment outcomes. This evidence establishes that familiarity is verified and not self-declared.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records are tied to named individuals with role designations and mapped to specific IRP sections they are responsible for executing
Competency assessments (written tests, scenario walkthroughs, or structured Q&A) are documented with pass thresholds and remediation records for failures
Training records reflect the current IRP version; version numbers and effective dates appear on both the training material and the completion certificate
Refresher training is conducted whenever the IRP is materially updated, with evidence of re-acknowledgement within 30 days of any revision
Training records cover both primary CERT members and designated backups to ensure no single point of failure in knowledge coverage
commonGaps: Training records exist for initial onboarding only, with no evidence of refresher training after subsequent IRP revisions
Completion records show attendance but no competency verification, making it impossible to confirm members are truly conversant with procedures
Training materials reference a superseded IRP version, creating a disconnect between documented familiarity and current obligations
Deputy or alternate CERT members are excluded from training records, leaving backup coverage unverified
Records are stored inconsistently across departments with no single authoritative register, making completeness difficult to demonstrate during examination
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) such as Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors, or internal training portals — training completion and assessment exports
HR system records cross-referenced against current CERT roster to confirm all active members are covered
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or MetricStream) where training obligations are tracked against policy requirements
CERT Secretariat or Information Security team for manually maintained training logs if LMS is not used
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (training certificates, assessment results); Spreadsheet (training register); LMS system export

evidenceItems.name: IRP Acknowledgement and Read-Receipt Records
evidenceItems.description: Signed or system-logged acknowledgements confirming that each CERT member has read, understood, and accepted responsibility under the current version of the incident response plan. This provides direct evidence of individual-level familiarity as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each acknowledgement record identifies the individual by name and role, specifies the IRP version acknowledged, and includes a date of signature
Acknowledgements are collected within a defined period (e.g., 14 days) of each IRP issuance or revision, with a tracked compliance rate reported to CISO or senior management
Digital acknowledgement workflows (e.g., DocuSign, SharePoint approval workflows, or GRC task completion) generate an auditable trail with timestamps
Outstanding acknowledgements are escalated through a formal reminder and escalation process, with closure evidence available
Acknowledgements cover the full IRP document including annexes and procedure appendices, not merely the policy cover page
commonGaps: Acknowledgements are collected only for the initial IRP issuance, with no records for subsequent amendments or annual reviews
Email read-receipt or distribution list evidence is presented as acknowledgement, which does not confirm comprehension or intent
Acknowledgement records cannot be matched to the current CERT roster because staff changes are not reflected in the tracking register
Acknowledgement is obtained from team leads only, who are assumed to cascade to their members, leaving individual member compliance unverified
No escalation or remediation evidence exists for CERT members who did not acknowledge within the required window
suggestedSources: Document management system (e.g., SharePoint, Confluence) with version-controlled IRP and built-in approval workflow logs
GRC platform task or attestation module where acknowledgement obligations are assigned and tracked per individual
DocuSign or equivalent e-signature platform with audit trail exports
Information Security or CERT Secretariat for manually maintained sign-off registers where digital tooling is absent
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed acknowledgement forms); System export (digital workflow audit trail); Spreadsheet (acknowledgement tracking register)

evidenceItems.name: CERT On-Call Roster and Escalation Directory
evidenceItems.description: A maintained, current-version roster documenting primary and backup contact details for all CERT members, including 24/7 reachability information (mobile, alternate phone, and preferred out-of-hours channel). This directly evidences the 'contactable at all times' obligation in the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: The roster identifies primary and at least one backup contact for every CERT role, ensuring no single point of failure in the escalation chain
Contact details include multiple channels: direct mobile, secondary number, and secure messaging platform (e.g., Signal, Teams, or institutional equivalent), with a clear indication of priority order
The roster carries a document version number, effective date, and review date, demonstrating it is actively maintained rather than a static document
The roster is accessible to all CERT members and relevant stakeholders through a system that is reachable during a major incident (e.g., not solely dependent on internal network availability)
A documented process exists for updating the roster within a defined SLA (e.g., 48 hours) when personnel changes occur, with evidence of adherence
commonGaps: The roster contains outdated contact numbers for former employees or members who have changed roles, undermining the 'contactable at all times' assurance
Only office landline numbers are listed without mobile or out-of-hours contact details, making 24/7 contactability unsubstantiated
The roster has no version history or last-reviewed date, making it impossible to confirm it reflects the current state of the CERT
No backup or deputy contact is designated for key CERT roles, creating gaps during leave or unavailability
The roster is stored exclusively on internal systems that may be inaccessible during a major cyber incident, defeating its operational purpose
suggestedSources: CERT Secretariat or Information Security Operations team maintaining the authoritative roster
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) where on-call assignments and escalation groups are configured
HR system for current employment status cross-reference to validate all listed members are active staff
Secure out-of-band communication platform (e.g., PagerDuty, Everbridge) configured for CERT alerting and escalation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (controlled document); Spreadsheet (contact register); System export (on-call tool configuration)

evidenceItems.name: CERT Reachability and Contactability Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic tests conducted to verify that all CERT members are reachable through the channels documented in the on-call roster. These tests provide empirical evidence that the 'contactable at all times' obligation is operationally met and not merely documented.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reachability tests are conducted at a defined frequency (e.g., quarterly), with results logged per individual including time of contact attempt, channel used, response time, and outcome
Tests are conducted outside of business hours to validate 24/7 contactability specifically, not just availability during working hours
Failed contact attempts trigger a documented remediation action, such as updating contact details or escalating to alternate contacts, with closure evidence
Test results are reported to the CISO or relevant governance body, demonstrating management oversight of CERT readiness
The test methodology covers all channels listed in the roster (mobile call, SMS, secure messaging) to confirm each channel is functional and monitored
commonGaps: No evidence of out-of-hours reachability tests exists; tests are conducted only during business hours, which does not satisfy the 'at all times' requirement
Test results are recorded informally (e.g., email threads) without a structured log, making it difficult to demonstrate systematic compliance
Failed contacts are noted but no remediation or follow-up evidence is available, suggesting the roster was not updated in response
Tests are conducted for primary contacts only, leaving backup contact reachability unverified
Test frequency is ad hoc or annual, which is insufficient to demonstrate sustained contactability across the examination period
suggestedSources: CERT Secretariat or Information Security Operations team maintaining test logs
On-call management platform (e.g., PagerDuty, Everbridge, OpsGenie) with automated alert dispatch and acknowledgement logs
Incident management or ITSM system where test outcomes are logged as recurring tasks or service requests
Internal audit or second line of defence team records if reachability tests are included in periodic CERT health checks
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (test log with timestamps and outcomes); System export (on-call platform dispatch and acknowledgement records); PDF (test summary report)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Tabletop Exercise and Drill Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of structured simulations (tabletop exercises, red team drills, or functional tests) in which CERT members actively demonstrate their ability to apply the incident response plan and handling procedures. This provides the strongest evidence that members are operationally conversant, not merely document-familiar.
whatGoodLooksLike: Exercise records include a scenario description, participant attendance list with roles, facilitator notes, and a debrief summary documenting decisions made and procedures applied during the exercise
Exercises are conducted at a frequency aligned with regulatory expectations (at least annually) and following significant changes to the IRP, threat landscape, or CERT composition
After-action reports identify gaps between actual member responses and IRP-prescribed procedures, with remediation actions tracked to closure
Exercises test scenarios relevant to the institution's specific threat profile (e.g., ransomware, DDoS, insider threat) rather than generic hypotheticals, demonstrating meaningful preparedness
Exercise outcomes are reported to senior management or the Board Risk Committee, demonstrating governance-level oversight of CERT readiness
commonGaps: Exercises are conducted but debrief records and after-action reports are absent, making it impossible to assess quality or outcomes
Only senior CERT leadership participates in exercises, leaving operational-level members with no demonstrated familiarity with hands-on procedures
Exercise scenarios are identical across multiple cycles with no variation, failing to test member adaptability or familiarity with the full scope of the IRP
Remediation actions identified in after-action reports are not tracked to closure, suggesting lessons learned are not embedded into member capability
Exercises are scheduled but cancelled or deferred without rescheduling, leaving multi-year gaps in documented drill activity
suggestedSources: CERT Secretariat or Cyber Crisis Management team maintaining exercise planning and records
GRC platform where exercise obligations are tracked as recurring tasks with evidence attachment capability
Business Continuity or Crisis Management team records where CERT exercises are coordinated with BCP/DR drills
External facilitator or Red Team provider engagement records and final exercise reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (exercise scenario, attendance register, after-action report); Presentation slides (debrief materials); Spreadsheet (remediation action tracker)

evidenceItems.name: CERT Member Role Assignment and Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation defining which staff are designated as CERT members, their assigned roles and responsibilities within the incident response structure, and the acceptance of those obligations (including out-of-hours availability). This establishes the defined population of 'relevant CERT members' as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: The CERT Terms of Reference or equivalent governance document explicitly names or categorises roles designated as CERT members, aligned to the IRP organisational structure
Each designated CERT member has a role profile or job function description that includes incident response responsibilities and out-of-hours availability obligations
The CERT membership register is reviewed and reaffirmed at least annually, with a documented approval from the CISO or equivalent authority
Changes to CERT membership (additions, removals, role changes) are documented with effective dates and triggered updates to training, acknowledgement, and contact records
The scope of 'relevant' CERT members is clearly defined, distinguishing core response roles from supporting functions, to avoid ambiguity during examination
commonGaps: CERT membership is informally understood but not formally documented, making it impossible to enumerate the population of members whose compliance must be demonstrated
The CERT roster includes individuals no longer employed or no longer in relevant roles, without a decommissioning process to reflect organisational changes

Out-of-hours availability and contactability obligations are not explicitly included in role descriptions or terms of reference, leaving the 'at all times' requirement unanchored to individual accountability
The distinction between primary CERT members and supporting or advisory participants is undefined, creating confusion over whose training and reachability must be verified
Terms of reference have not been reviewed or updated following organisational restructuring, leaving them misaligned with the actual incident response structure in operation
suggestedSources: Information Security or CISO office maintaining CERT governance documentation
HR system for cross-referencing active employment and role assignments against the CERT register
GRC platform where CERT membership and role obligations are formally recorded and linked to policy requirements
Board or management committee minutes where CERT composition or mandate changes were approved
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (Terms of Reference, role profiles); Spreadsheet (CERT membership register with effective dates); System record (GRC role assignment)

auditTips: Before examination, reconcile the CERT membership register against HR records to confirm all listed members are currently employed and in relevant roles — examiners routinely cross-check this and roster discrepancies are a common finding.

Map each evidence item to the specific obligation it satisfies: training records satisfy 'conversant with procedures', acknowledgements satisfy 'conversant with the IRP', and reachability test logs satisfy 'contactable at all times' — this traceability matrix speeds up document production during examination and demonstrates structured compliance thinking.

Validate that all training records, acknowledgements, and contact tests reference the current IRP version number and effective date; mismatches between document versions across your evidence set are a red flag examiners specifically look for when assessing whether familiarity reflects current obligations.

Ensure at least one set of reachability test records covers an out-of-hours time window (evenings or weekends); if all tests were conducted during business hours, examiners will question whether '24/7 contactability' has actually been demonstrated, even if the roster looks complete.

Prepare a consolidated CERT Readiness Dashboard or summary report that aggregates training completion rates, acknowledgement rates, last exercise date, and last contact test date across all CERT members — presenting this proactively demonstrates mature governance oversight and reduces the volume of ad-hoc document requests during examination.

Cross-reference exercise after-action reports against the current IRP to show that identified gaps resulted in IRP updates or targeted training, demonstrating a closed-loop improvement process rather than a static compliance posture.

Confirm that the on-call roster and escalation directory are accessible through an out-of-band channel (e.g., a printed copy in a secured location, or a cloud-hosted document accessible without VPN) — examiners may ask how CERT members would obtain contact details if internal systems were compromised during the very incident requiring the CERT to activate.

11.15.title: Out-of-Band Communication Infrastructure
11.15.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised — not merely documented — a discrete communication channel that remains functional when primary networks and systems are compromised. Examiners pay close attention to whether the out-of-band infrastructure is genuinely independent from primary systems (separate carrier, separate authentication, separate power supply) and whether it covers both internal stakeholders (board, senior management, crisis teams) and external stakeholders (BNM, CERT, payment networks, peer institutions). A key red flag is an out-of-band plan that relies on the same ISP, email domain, or identity provider as the primary environment, rendering it equally vulnerable during an incident. Examiners also probe whether the solution has been tested end-to-end under simulated crisis conditions, whether contact directories are current, and whether personnel at all relevant levels are trained and familiar with activation procedures.

evidenceItems.name: Out-of-Band Communication Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy or framework document that defines the institution's requirement, scope, architecture principles, governance, and maintenance obligations for its out-of-band communication infrastructure, demonstrating that management has committed to a structured programme independent of primary communication channels.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly defines 'out-of-band' and mandates technological and network independence from primary infrastructure (separate ISP, separate authentication provider, separate power path)
Scope section names both internal stakeholders (board, EXCO, crisis management team, IT/security staff) and external stakeholders (BNM, payment networks, correspondent banks, CERT/CC)
Document is approved by the Board or a Board-level committee and carries a review date no older than 12 months
Policy references specific fallback channels (e.g., encrypted satellite phone, dedicated VPN over secondary carrier, out-of-band management network) rather than generic statements
Roles and responsibilities for maintaining, testing, and activating the infrastructure are clearly assigned to named functions
commonGaps: Policy exists but treats out-of-band communication as a sub-section of BCP/DRP without specifying independence requirements, leaving ambiguity about whether the channel truly separates from primary infrastructure
External stakeholder coverage limited to BNM only; no mention of payment network operators, correspondent banks, or sector-wide crisis coordination bodies
Policy has not been reviewed or updated in over 12 months, predating the institution's cloud migration or network architecture changes
No explicit prohibition against using the same email domain, identity provider, or ISP that may be compromised during a cyber incident
Responsibilities are assigned to a generic 'IT department' rather than specific roles, making accountability difficult to verify during examination
suggestedSources: Enterprise GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — Policy Management module
SharePoint/Confluence policy repository maintained by the Chief Information Security Officer's office
Board Secretariat document management system for Board-approved versions and meeting minutes
Technology Risk or Cybersecurity function document register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — signed, version-controlled policy document with approval signature page

evidenceItems.name: Out-of-Band Infrastructure Architecture and Asset Register
evidenceItems.description: Technical documentation describing the physical and logical architecture of the out-of-band communication infrastructure, including network diagrams, hardware inventory, software components, carrier/service provider details, and independence verification — demonstrating that the institution has a concrete, deployable solution that is separate from its primary environment.
whatGoodLooksLike: Network topology diagram explicitly shows separation from primary LAN/WAN, using a distinct carrier, IP address range, and authentication directory (e.g., standalone LDAP or pre-shared key, not corporate Active Directory)

Asset register lists every component (satellite terminals, encrypted handsets, dedicated routers, hardened laptops, backup SIM cards) with serial numbers, firmware versions, and responsible custodians
Architecture documentation confirms redundant power path (UPS and generator) for all critical out-of-band nodes, independent of primary data centre power feeds
Carrier/service provider contracts are referenced and confirm SLA commitments for availability during widespread outages (e.g., satellite or LTE fallback with documented coverage map)
Diagram is version-controlled and reflects current state following the most recent infrastructure change or DR review cycle
commonGaps: Architecture documentation exists at a high level but omits physical layer details — examiners cannot determine whether the 'separate network' actually traverses the same physical fibre termination point or last-mile provider
Asset register is out of date; devices listed have been decommissioned or replaced without the register being updated
Authentication to the out-of-band environment relies on corporate Single Sign-On or Active Directory, which would be unavailable or compromised in the scenarios the infrastructure is meant to address
Power independence is assumed but not documented — no reference to UPS or generator sizing calculations for out-of-band nodes
No carrier diversity: primary and fallback paths use the same telecommunications provider, defeating the purpose of the alternate channel
suggestedSources: Network Operations Centre (NOC) — network diagrams maintained in Visio, Lucidchart, or similar diagramming tools
IT Asset Management system (e.g., ServiceNow ITAM, Lansweeper) for hardware and software inventory
Vendor/carrier contract repository managed by Procurement or Vendor Management function
Data Centre facilities team for power topology documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/Visio/PNG network diagrams + spreadsheet asset register, packaged together

evidenceItems.name: Crisis Contact Directory (Internal and External Stakeholders)
evidenceItems.description: A maintained, access-controlled directory of contact details for all internal and external stakeholders reachable via out-of-band channels during a crisis, demonstrating that the institution can actually reach the right people when primary communication channels are unavailable.
whatGoodLooksLike: Directory is stored in a medium accessible without primary network connectivity (e.g., printed hardcopy in crisis kits at key sites, encrypted offline file on dedicated devices, or hosted on out-of-band portal)

Internal coverage includes board members, EXCO, crisis management team members, IT/security on-call staff, and branch/regional leads — each with at least two independent contact methods (e.g., personal mobile and satellite phone)

External coverage includes BNM supervisory contacts, JPCERT/MyCSERT, SWIFT/payment network operations centres, correspondent bank crisis lines, and key outsourced IT/cloud provider escalation contacts
Directory carries a 'last verified' date no older than 90 days, with evidence of a regular verification cycle (e.g., quarterly call-tree test acknowledgements)
Access controls prevent unauthorised disclosure while ensuring authorised responders can retrieve the directory without logging into primary corporate systems
commonGaps: Directory exists only as a file on the corporate SharePoint intranet — inaccessible if the primary network or identity provider is compromised, which is precisely the scenario it is meant to address
External stakeholder section lists generic published phone numbers rather than verified crisis-line contacts confirmed through direct relationship management
Directory has not been verified in over six months; contact details for departed employees or reorganised teams render portions of it unreliable
Board and EXCO members are omitted on the assumption that senior management will self-organise — no documented escalation path for board-level crisis notification via out-of-band means
No documented process for updating the directory following staff movements, organisational changes, or vendor/regulator contact changes
suggestedSources: Crisis Management Office or Business Continuity function — contact tree maintained in BCP platform (e.g., Fusion Risk Management, Riskonnect) or offline roster
Human Resources system for staff mobile numbers, cross-referenced against crisis roster
Vendor Management function for outsourced provider escalation contacts
Regulatory Affairs or Compliance function for BNM and authority contact details
evidenceItems.format: PDF hardcopy (maintained in sealed crisis kits at primary and alternate sites) + encrypted digital copy on out-of-band device

evidenceItems.name: Out-of-Band Communication Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic tests of the out-of-band communication infrastructure — including tabletop exercises, technical activation tests, and crisis simulation exercises — demonstrating that the institution has validated the operability, coverage, and usability of the infrastructure under realistic conditions and has remediated identified gaps.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test records show at least one full end-to-end technical activation test per year, confirming that all components (devices, network paths, authentication, contact directory) functioned as designed

Testing scope explicitly includes both internal stakeholder communication (crisis team activation) and external stakeholder notification (simulated contact with BNM, payment networks, or key vendors via out-of-band channel)
After-action reports document issues identified during testing and link each issue to a remediation action with an owner and target completion date — closed items show evidence of verification

Test scenarios include a simulated primary infrastructure compromise (e.g., assumed corporate email and VPN unavailable) to validate that the out-of-band channel is the sole communication method tested
Participation records confirm that personnel beyond the IT/security team — including senior management — have been trained and have participated in at least one test exercise
commonGaps: Testing is limited to tabletop exercises that discuss the procedure without technically activating the infrastructure — no evidence that devices power on, networks connect, and messages actually transmit
Test scenarios assume primary email remains available as a backup, undermining the independence requirement and masking gaps in the out-of-band channel
After-action reports list findings but subsequent tests show the same issues recurring, with no evidence that remediation was completed or verified
Testing frequency is annual at best and does not reflect changes to infrastructure, personnel, or threat landscape since the last test
External stakeholder communication is not tested — institutions simulate internal coordination only and assume external channels will work without validation
suggestedSources: Technology Risk or Cybersecurity function — exercise and test records maintained in GRC platform or dedicated BCM tool
Business Continuity / IT DR team — DR test logs and after-action reports
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — change and test records for out-of-band infrastructure components
Crisis Management Office — exercise management records and attendance registers
evidenceItems.format: PDF test reports, after-action review documents, and remediation tracking spreadsheets or GRC platform exports

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Incident Response Plan — Out-of-Band Communication Activation Procedures
evidenceItems.description: The documented activation procedures within the Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) or Crisis Communication Plan that specify how and when personnel switch to out-of-band communication channels during a cyber crisis, demonstrating that the infrastructure is integrated into the institution's actual response workflow rather than existing as a standalone artefact.
whatGoodLooksLike: CIRP contains a dedicated section or runbook for out-of-band communication activation, with clear trigger criteria (e.g., primary email system unavailable for more than 30 minutes, suspected compromise of Active Directory, ransomware affecting corporate network) that remove ambiguity about when to switch

Activation procedure specifies step-by-step instructions that a responder can follow without access to primary systems, including device location, power-on steps, network connection verification, and first contacts to notify
Plan assigns a named role (e.g., Crisis Communication Coordinator) responsible for declaring activation and notifying all stakeholders via out-of-band channel within a defined timeframe
External notification procedures within the CIRP reference out-of-band contact methods for BNM and other mandatory notification recipients, consistent with the contact directory (11.15-E3)
CIRP version history shows the out-of-band section was updated following each test cycle (11.15-E4) and reflects the current infrastructure architecture (11.15-E2)
commonGaps: CIRP references out-of-band communication in principle but delegates procedure to a separate document that is stored only on the corporate intranet — inaccessible during the crisis scenarios the plan addresses
Trigger criteria for switching to out-of-band are vague ('when primary systems are unavailable') with no measurable threshold, leading to delayed or inconsistent activation decisions
Plan does not address the scenario where the person responsible for activating the out-of-band channel is themselves unreachable via primary systems — no backup activation authority is named
External notification section of CIRP still references corporate email addresses for BNM and authority notifications, inconsistent with the out-of-band channel requirement
CIRP has not been updated to reflect infrastructure or personnel changes since the last major review, creating discrepancies between the plan and the actual out-of-band environment
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity function — CIRP maintained in GRC platform or document management system, with printed hardcopy in crisis kits
Business Continuity Office — integrated with the institution's overall Crisis Management Plan
Crisis Management Team leads — for operational runbooks and quick-reference activation cards
Regulatory Affairs / Compliance — for external notification procedures aligned with BNM reporting obligations
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX — version-controlled plan with printed hardcopy in crisis kits at primary and alternate sites

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party and Vendor Service Agreements for Out-of-Band Channels
evidenceItems.description: Contracts, SLAs, and service documentation covering any third-party providers that underpin the out-of-band communication infrastructure (e.g., satellite communication providers, dedicated LTE/5G carrier, encrypted messaging platform, managed out-of-band network provider), demonstrating that the institution has contractually secured the availability and security commitments necessary for the channel to function in a crisis.
whatGoodLooksLike: Contracts specify guaranteed availability or uptime commitments that are distinct from and not contingent on the primary carrier infrastructure, with explicit provisions for crisis scenarios or major network outages

Security requirements in contracts mandate encryption standards (e.g., minimum AES-256 for data at rest and in transit), key management responsibilities, and incident notification obligations applicable to the vendor
SLAs include response and restoration time commitments relevant to a crisis context (e.g., restoration within 4 hours, 24/7 technical support line), not generic business-hours commitments

Vendor due diligence records (e.g., SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 certification) confirm that the third-party provider meets the institution's security standards and would not itself become a single point of failure
Contracts are current (not expired), and a vendor review cycle is documented confirming that the institution actively monitors the provider's performance and financial health
commonGaps: Institution relies on consumer-grade or uncertified communication tools (e.g., standard WhatsApp, personal mobile carrier) for out-of-band communication with no formal contract or security assessment
Contracts exist but contain no security requirements, leaving data classification, encryption standards, and incident notification undefined
SLA uptime commitments are expressed as annual averages, providing no meaningful guarantee that the service will be available during the specific crisis window when it is needed most
Vendor due diligence has not been conducted or renewed, and the provider's security posture is unknown — creating a risk that the out-of-band channel itself could be compromised
Contract renewal was missed and the agreement is operating outside its term, or the vendor has been acquired or changed its service terms without the institution reviewing the implications
suggestedSources: Procurement / Vendor Management function — contract repository (e.g., Icertis, Coupa, or SharePoint contract library)
Technology Risk function — third-party risk assessments and vendor due diligence records
Legal department — executed agreements and amendment schedules
Finance / Accounts Payable — for identifying active vendor relationships and renewal dates
evidenceItems.format: PDF executed contracts, SLA schedules, and vendor assessment reports

auditTips: Before examination, physically activate the out-of-band channel end-to-end in the presence of your compliance team and document the test with timestamped logs and photographs — examiners frequently request a live demonstration or recent activation evidence, and paper-based assertions without proof of operability are a common finding.

Map every component of your out-of-band infrastructure against your primary environment and explicitly document the independence points: different ISP, different authentication directory, different power feed, different physical site. Prepare a one-page independence attestation that an examiner can review quickly, supported by the detailed architecture in 11.15-E2.

Cross-reference your contact directory (11.15-E3) against your Cyber Incident Response Plan (11.15-E5) line by line to confirm that every external party named in the CIRP's notification obligations has a corresponding out-of-band contact method — BNM examiners will probe this linkage specifically.

Retrieve your last three test after-action reports (11.15-E4) and prepare a remediation status summary showing each finding, its resolution, and the verification date. Unresolved or recurring findings from prior tests are a significant red flag; close all open items before examination and have closure evidence ready.

Conduct a tabletop walkthrough with your crisis management team in the 30 days prior to examination, using the scenario that corporate email, VPN, and Active Directory are all unavailable simultaneously — document attendance and outcomes. This demonstrates that staff muscle memory and the out-of-band runbook are aligned, not just that infrastructure exists.

Verify that printed copies of your contact directory and CIRP activation runbook are physically present in crisis kits at both your primary and alternate sites, and confirm the kits were last reviewed within 90 days. Examiners may ask where a responder would go to retrieve this information at 2am with no internet access — the answer must be a physical, accessible location.

Reconcile your vendor contracts (11.15-E6) with your asset register (11.15-E2) to confirm every third-party component has an active, security-reviewed contract. Identify any consumer or uncertified tools currently in use and either replace them or document a time-bound remediation plan before examination — informal tools are routinely cited as non-compliances under this clause.

11.16.title: Annual Cyber Drill Exercise
11.16.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the annual cyber drill is substantive and not a box-ticking formality — they scrutinise whether scenarios are genuinely threat-informed (aligned to current and emerging threats, not recycled from prior years) and whether participation by board members, senior management, and third-party service providers is documented with sign-off evidence rather than merely listed as invitees. A primary red flag is the absence of out-of-band communication testing as a distinct, documented component of the drill. Examiners also verify that results were formally reported to the board within a reasonable and defined timeframe, and that the report contains identified gaps and remediation actions rather than a superficial pass/fail summary. Particular attention is paid to whether escalation chains, multi-level impact decision-making, and CERT readiness — including third-party CERT or managed SOC involvement — are explicitly tested, documented, and traceable back to CIRP provisions.

evidenceItems.name: Annual Cyber Drill Exercise Programme and Scenario Design
evidenceItems.description: A formal programme document that establishes the scope, objectives, methodology, threat scenarios, and participant roster for the annual cyber drill exercise. Demonstrates that scenario selection is grounded in current and emerging threats, that all required stakeholder categories are planned for inclusion, and that out-of-band communication methods are explicitly scoped within the exercise design.
whatGoodLooksLike: Programme document is dated, version-controlled, and approved by CISO or equivalent senior authority before the drill commences
Scenarios are explicitly linked to current threat intelligence sources (e.g., BNM advisories, FS-ISAC reports, recent industry incidents) with rationale documented for each scenario selected
At least one scenario tests social engineering or human-layer attack vectors as referenced in the clause, alongside technical compromise scenarios covering multiple CIRP impact levels
Participant list explicitly names board members, C-suite or senior management representatives, and relevant third-party providers with their designated roles in the exercise
Out-of-band communication methods (e.g., dedicated hotlines, encrypted messaging platforms, courier protocols) are listed as distinct test items with success criteria defined upfront
commonGaps: Scenarios are reused year-over-year with cosmetic changes, with no documented threat intelligence rationale justifying scenario relevance to the current threat landscape
Board and senior management participation is listed in the programme but attendance is not confirmed until after the exercise, leaving no pre-exercise commitment record
Out-of-band communication testing is described vaguely (e.g., 'communications will be tested') without specifying which channels, fallback sequences, or contact directories will be validated
Third-party service providers are omitted from the programme scope or only added as optional participants, contrary to the clause's explicit requirement for their involvement
The programme conflates the cyber drill with routine tabletop exercises or BCP tests run under a separate framework, making it difficult to demonstrate a standalone annual CIRP-focused exercise
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cyber Defence team SharePoint or GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) exercise planning module
Cyber Incident Response team document repository
Third-party engagement records and vendor participation agreements maintained by Vendor Risk Management or Procurement
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal programme document with approval signatures)

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Drill Exercise Execution Records and Attendance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Contemporaneous records captured during or immediately after the exercise that prove the drill was actually conducted as planned. Includes sign-in sheets, facilitator logs, inject timelines, decision logs, and communication transcripts. Demonstrates actual — not merely planned — participation by board members, senior management, and third parties, and provides a factual basis for the after-action report.
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed attendance register or digital confirmation records (e.g., calendar invites with accepted responses, video conference participant reports) showing named board members and senior management were present for material portions of the exercise
Inject log or master scenario events list (MSEL) with timestamps showing each scenario inject, participant responses, and facilitator observations recorded in real time
Communication transcripts or screenshots capturing out-of-band channel activations, demonstrating which channels were used, by whom, and at what stage of the simulated incident
Third-party service provider participation evidenced by separate log entries, their specific actions during the exercise, and any escalation or handoff interactions with internal teams
Exercise facilitator's contemporaneous notes capturing deviations from the planned programme, improvised decisions, and unexpected gaps observed in real time
commonGaps: Attendance records show only internal staff; board and senior management participation is referenced in the after-action report narrative but lacks supporting attendance evidence
Inject logs are reconstructed after the fact rather than captured in real time, making it impossible to demonstrate the actual decision timelines and escalation sequence
Out-of-band communication channels are described as 'tested' in the summary but no transcripts, call logs, or message records are retained to substantiate the claim
Third-party providers submitted a separate internal report that was not cross-referenced or integrated into the institution's exercise records, creating an evidence silo

The exercise log records actions taken but not the impact levels or escalation thresholds that triggered each decision, making it impossible to verify that multi-level impact decision-making was tested
suggestedSources: Cyber Defence / SOC exercise coordination platform (e.g., SimSpace, Cyberbit, or internal ITSM ticketing system used to simulate incident tickets)
Video conferencing platform participant reports (e.g., Microsoft Teams, Zoom admin export)
Physical sign-in sheets held by the exercise coordinator or Business Continuity Management team
evidenceItems.format: Mixed: PDF scans of signed registers, XLSX inject logs, email/chat exports, system-generated participant reports

evidenceItems.name: After-Action Report and Gap Remediation Tracker
evidenceItems.description: A structured post-exercise report that objectively evaluates CIRP effectiveness against the tested scenarios, documents findings across escalation, communication, decision-making, and CERT/third-party readiness dimensions, and establishes a time-bound remediation plan. This is the primary document evidencing that the drill produced actionable outcomes rather than a compliance attestation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report structure maps findings directly to specific CIRP sections, identifying which procedures held up under testing and which contained gaps, with supporting evidence from the exercise logs

Separate assessment sections for each clause 11.16(a) and 11.16(b) obligation: escalation effectiveness, communication effectiveness at each impact level, decision-making quality, and CERT/third-party readiness ratings
Each finding is accompanied by a root cause classification (process gap, capability gap, tool gap, or awareness gap) and a named owner responsible for remediation
Remediation tracker included within or appended to the report, with target completion dates and a defined review mechanism to confirm closure
Out-of-band communication findings are reported as a distinct section with channel-by-channel assessment results and any recommended updates to the out-of-band contact directory or fallback sequence
commonGaps: Report is written as a narrative exercise summary rather than a structured gap assessment, making it difficult for auditors to identify what specifically failed and what was remediated
Findings are graded as pass/fail without qualitative assessment of why gaps occurred or how severe they are relative to CIRP response time requirements
Remediation items from the prior year's drill are not referenced, so there is no evidence that previous gaps were tracked to closure — creating a recurring deficiency pattern

CERT and third-party provider performance is assessed only by internal staff without input from the providers themselves, limiting the objectivity and completeness of the readiness assessment under clause 11.16(b)
Report is finalised months after the exercise, undermining the 'timely reporting' obligation and raising questions about the accuracy of reconstructed findings
suggestedSources: GRC platform issue and remediation tracking module (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow IRM, MetricStream)
Information Security team SharePoint document library with version history
Cyber Incident Response team post-incident/post-exercise review repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal report with executive summary) + XLSX (remediation tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Board Reporting Record for Cyber Drill Results
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the results of the annual cyber drill exercise were formally presented and reported to the board within a timely and defined period after the exercise. Demonstrates fulfilment of the specific board reporting obligation in clause 11.16 and confirms board-level awareness of CIRP effectiveness, identified gaps, and remediation plans.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or Board Risk Committee (BRC) meeting minutes explicitly reference the cyber drill results as a standing agenda item, with the date of presentation falling within a documented maximum timeframe (e.g., next scheduled board meeting after exercise completion or within 30–60 days)
Board paper or management report submitted ahead of the meeting is retained and cross-referenced in the minutes, confirming it was circulated and considered rather than verbally summarised
Minutes record substantive board engagement — questions raised, decisions made, or directives issued — rather than a passive notation that the report was 'received and noted'
Any board directives arising from drill findings are documented in the minutes and subsequently traceable to remediation actions in the gap tracker (evidence item 11.16-E3)
The institution has a defined policy or procedure specifying the maximum elapsed time between exercise completion and board reporting, demonstrating that 'timely' has been quantified and governed
commonGaps: Drill results are reported to the IT Steering Committee or EXCO but not elevated to the full board or Board Risk Committee, falling short of the clause's explicit board reporting obligation

Board reporting occurs at the next annual cycle, meaning results from an October exercise are only presented the following February — no documented policy defines what 'timely' means for this institution
Board paper is retained but minutes do not confirm it was tabled or discussed, leaving ambiguity about whether the board actually reviewed the findings

The board paper summarises the drill as 'successfully completed' without presenting specific findings, gaps, or remediation actions, depriving the board of the substantive visibility the clause intends
There is no linkage between prior year board directives arising from drill findings and the current year's remediation status, breaking the governance accountability chain
suggestedSources: Company Secretary's board and committee minutes repository
Board portal (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect) for board paper submissions and version history
GRC platform for linking board directives to open remediation items
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board minutes extract and board paper), with access log from board portal as supporting evidence

evidenceItems.name: Out-of-Band Communication Method Test Records
evidenceItems.description: Dedicated records documenting the testing of out-of-band communication methods as a distinct component of the annual cyber drill, as well as any additional periodic tests conducted separately from the main drill. Demonstrates compliance with the clause's dual requirement that out-of-band methods are tested both as part of the annual drill and 'regularly' through standalone tests.
whatGoodLooksLike: A register of all designated out-of-band communication channels (e.g., dedicated emergency hotlines, encrypted messaging apps such as Signal or Wickr, satellite phones, physical courier protocols, pre-shared key contact directories) with test dates and results recorded for each channel

Test records show the full out-of-band communication sequence was exercised end-to-end — including activation, message transmission, receipt confirmation, and fallback to secondary channel where primary failed — not merely that a channel was 'available'

Frequency of standalone out-of-band tests (outside the annual drill) is defined in policy and evidenced by test logs showing regularity, e.g., semi-annual or quarterly spot tests of hotline reachability
Contact directory used during out-of-band communication is validated as current during each test, with version-controlled directory updates documented where contact details changed

Test results clearly distinguish between internal out-of-band communications (board, EXCO, CERT) and external communications (regulators, third-party providers, media/PR channels) with findings reported separately
commonGaps: Out-of-band communication testing is recorded as a single checkbox in the drill summary rather than a standalone section with channel-by-channel results, making it impossible to verify which channels were tested and what the outcomes were
Only the primary out-of-band channel (e.g., the emergency hotline) is tested; secondary and tertiary fallback channels are not tested on the assumption they would work if needed
The contact directory used during the test is not dated or version-controlled, so it cannot be confirmed that the tested contacts were current at the time of the exercise
No evidence of out-of-band communication tests conducted outside of the annual drill, despite the clause's requirement for regular testing — annual-only testing is insufficient to satisfy 'regularly'

External out-of-band communication paths (to BNM, peer institutions under mutual aid, or key third-party providers) are excluded from testing on the grounds that they involve external parties, leaving a material gap in tested coverage
suggestedSources: IT Operations or Cyber Defence team test logs maintained in ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management)
Business Continuity Management system for out-of-band contact directory version history
Telecommunications provider call records for dedicated emergency hotline activation logs
evidenceItems.format: XLSX or PDF test log, contact directory with version history, telecommunications provider confirmation

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party and CERT Readiness Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence demonstrating the participation and assessed readiness of the institution's internal CERT (or equivalent) and relevant third-party service providers during the cyber drill, as required under clause 11.16(b). Includes invitations, participation confirmations, role assignments during the exercise, and the institution's formal assessment of their recovery support effectiveness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Named third-party providers (e.g., managed SOC, cloud service providers, core banking system vendor, incident response retainer firm) are contractually or by agreement obligated to participate in annual drills, with this obligation documented in service agreements or engagement letters
Each third-party participant is assigned a specific role in the exercise scenario with defined triggers for their involvement, and their actual response to those triggers is logged and evaluated

CERT (internal or outsourced) readiness is assessed against predefined criteria covering: activation time, staffing adequacy, tooling availability, escalation to external authorities (e.g., CyberSecurity Malaysia, BNM), and communication with internal teams
Third-party providers submit a post-exercise self-assessment or debrief report that the institution reviews and integrates into its own after-action report, demonstrating a two-way assurance process
Gaps identified in third-party or CERT readiness are communicated to the relevant provider and tracked in the remediation register with agreed closure dates, not merely noted internally
commonGaps: Third-party providers are invited to the drill but their participation is not confirmed in advance, resulting in low or no actual participation on the day with the institution recording them as 'invited' rather than 'participated'
CERT readiness is assessed informally by the exercise facilitator with no defined criteria, making the assessment subjective and difficult to compare year-over-year or defend to an examiner

Third-party participation is limited to a single vendor (e.g., the managed SOC) while other critical providers — cloud infrastructure, payment network operators — are excluded despite being material to recovery

The institution does not obtain a post-exercise debrief from third-party providers, meaning their self-assessed readiness and lessons learned are unknown, creating a one-sided picture of recovery capability
There is no mechanism to track or verify that gaps identified in third-party performance during the drill have been addressed by the provider before the next annual exercise
suggestedSources: Vendor Risk Management system for third-party engagement records and participation obligations
Incident response retainer agreement and managed SOC service contract held by Procurement or Legal
GRC platform for CERT capability assessment records and third-party gap remediation tracking
Internal CERT ticketing system for activation and response time metrics captured during the exercise
evidenceItems.format: PDF (participation agreements, debrief reports, assessment records), XLSX (readiness assessment scorecard, remediation tracker)

auditTips: Cross-reference the cyber drill programme's scenario list against the institution's current threat intelligence reports (e.g., BNM cybersecurity advisories, FS-ISAC bulletins, and the institution's own threat landscape assessment) to demonstrate that scenarios are genuinely threat-informed rather than arbitrarily selected. Examiners will look for this linkage explicitly.

Prepare a single 'evidence spine' document that maps each obligation in clause 11.16 — annual frequency, CIRP testing, out-of-band testing, board and senior management involvement, third-party involvement, board reporting, clause 11.16(a) escalation/communication/decision-making testing, and clause 11.16(b) CERT/third-party readiness — to specific pages or sections in each piece of evidence. This prevents examiners from having to hunt for coverage and signals thorough preparation.

Verify that board and senior management attendance is evidenced independently of the drill organiser's own records — use the Company Secretary's board portal access logs, signed registers, or video conference platform participant exports. Self-reported attendance in the after-action report alone will not satisfy a skeptical examiner.

Audit the out-of-band communication contact directory for staleness before examination: confirm that all named contacts (board members, senior management, regulators, key third parties) are current, that the directory itself is version-controlled with an update date, and that the most recently tested version matches the current operational version. A directory that has not been updated since the last drill is a common examiner finding.

Obtain written confirmation from each material third-party provider that they participated in the drill, specifying the name and role of their representative, the duration of their involvement, and their own assessment of the outcome. This protects the institution against an examiner finding that third-party participation was nominal rather than substantive.

Document the institution's internal definition of 'timely' for board reporting of drill results — embed this in the cyber drill policy or CIRP — and ensure the actual reporting date for each year's exercise is recorded alongside the exercise completion date so the elapsed time can be demonstrated. Undefined timelines are an easy examiner criticism.

Before examination, run a prior-year remediation closure review: for each gap identified in last year's after-action report, confirm that the remediation action was completed, that evidence of closure exists in the GRC platform, and that the current year's drill programme incorporated any CIRP updates that arose from those remediation actions. Demonstrating a closed-loop improvement cycle is the strongest signal of a mature programme.

11.17.title: Cyber Insurance and Loss Provision
11.17.auditorFocus: BNM examiners evaluating clause 11.17 focus first on whether loss provision arrangements have been stress-tested against quantified, institution-specific extreme adverse cyber scenarios — they will probe whether scenario analysis is genuinely rigorous or a superficial desktop exercise. Where cyber insurance is in place, examiners scrutinise the coverage mapping to confirm the policy scope aligns with the institution's actual threat profile, including third-party liability, regulatory fines, and business interruption, rather than relying on generic off-the-shelf wording. A key red flag is the absence of a mechanism to assess how IT service changes, cloud migrations, or new vendor onboarding might inadvertently void or narrow coverage; examiners expect this to be embedded in change management gates. On the conflict-of-interest dimension, examiners look for evidence that senior management has actively considered and documented scenarios where the insurer's cost-minimisation objective could conflict with the institution's duty to customers — and that crisis response procedures explicitly address decision-making authority in such situations. Institutions that hold cyber insurance but have not read it into their incident response playbooks, or that cannot demonstrate Board/senior management awareness of policy obligations and exclusions, are routinely cited.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Loss Provision Review and Scenario Analysis Report
evidenceItems.description: A formal documented review of the institution's loss provision arrangements, including quantified scenario analysis of extreme adverse cyber events (e.g., ransomware shutdown of core banking, data breach affecting all customer records, prolonged SWIFT system compromise). The report must demonstrate that provisioning levels have been tested against these scenarios and found adequate, or that remediation actions have been taken where gaps were identified.
