whatGoodLooksLike: Report is dated within the last 12 months and explicitly references the RMiT 11.17 obligation as the governing requirement

Scenarios are quantified with financial impact estimates (direct losses, regulatory penalties, reputational damage, third-party liability) derived from the institution's own risk quantification model rather than generic industry figures
At least three extreme adverse scenarios are analysed, including a prolonged operational outage, a catastrophic data exfiltration, and a supply chain compromise affecting critical third parties
Loss provision adequacy conclusion is supported by a Board Risk Committee or ALCO resolution, not just management sign-off
Report documents the methodology used (e.g., FAIR, actuarial modelling) and assumptions, enabling reproducibility and challenge by examiners
commonGaps: Scenario analysis exists for operational risk generally but has no cyber-specific scenarios, relying instead on broad 'technology failure' categories that do not satisfy RMiT 11.17's extreme adverse event requirement
Financial quantification is absent — scenarios describe events narratively without estimating monetary exposure, making provision adequacy impossible to validate
Review is performed ad hoc or only when the insurance policy renews, rather than on a defined annual or trigger-based cycle tied to the threat landscape
Provision adequacy conclusions are made by the risk team in isolation without reference to the institution's balance sheet capacity, Treasury input, or Board approval
Scenarios are not updated to reflect the institution's current IT architecture — a post-cloud-migration institution still runs pre-migration scenarios
suggestedSources: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) team — owner of the loss provision review cycle
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — risk register and scenario analysis records
Board Risk Committee or ALCO meeting minutes and papers
Finance/Treasury for loss provision levels reflected in financial statements
Cyber threat intelligence function for scenario threat assumptions
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX report with supporting spreadsheet model; Board or committee resolution as separate PDF

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Insurance Policy Coverage Mapping and Gap Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A structured mapping document that cross-references the institution's cyber insurance policy coverage against its identified information security event types and liability exposures. This demonstrates compliance with clause 11.17(a) by showing that the institution has actively verified the policy scope aligns with its actual risk profile, including areas such as first-party losses, third-party liability, regulatory fines, notification costs, and business interruption, with any coverage gaps explicitly identified and risk-accepted or mitigated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Coverage mapping table lists every material cyber risk category from the institution's risk register and maps each to specific policy clauses, coverage limits, and sub-limits, with clear notation where coverage is absent or insufficient

Assessment distinguishes between first-party coverage (direct losses to the institution) and third-party liability (claims by customers, counterparties, regulators) and validates both against the institution's exposure profile
The document is reviewed and signed off by both the CISO/Head of Information Security and the Chief Risk Officer, confirming it represents a combined technical and risk view
Any identified coverage gaps are escalated to Board/senior management with a documented risk acceptance decision or a remediation plan including timeline
Assessment is refreshed upon each policy renewal and upon material changes to the institution's IT environment or threat landscape, with version control maintained
commonGaps: The institution holds a cyber insurance policy but no one has systematically compared its coverage scope to the institution's actual risk register — the policy was purchased without a formal fit-for-purpose assessment

Coverage mapping focuses only on headline limits and ignores sub-limits, co-insurance clauses, retentions, and exclusion carve-outs that materially reduce effective coverage for high-frequency or high-severity events
Regulatory fines and penalties coverage is assumed but not verified against jurisdiction-specific enforceability of such coverage under Malaysian law
Third-party liability arising from customer data breaches — a core exposure for financial institutions — is either absent from the policy or subject to exclusions not identified in the mapping
The assessment is prepared by the insurance broker rather than internally owned and validated, creating a dependency and potential conflict of interest in the adequacy determination
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance team — policy document custody and regulatory enforceability review
Group Insurance/Treasury for the executed policy schedule, endorsements, and broker correspondence
CISO office — risk register of information security event types
External cyber insurance broker or legal counsel — for policy interpretation support
GRC platform — risk register entries cross-referenced to insurance controls
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) as the primary mapping artefact; policy PDF as supporting document; sign-off via PDF or GRC workflow

evidenceItems.name: Insurance Policy Terms Integration into Crisis Response Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution has reviewed the cyber insurance policy's terms, conditions, warranties, and attestations and has reflected relevant obligations into its crisis response policies and procedures. This addresses clause 11.17(b) by demonstrating that the institution's incident response team knows what the policy requires of them (e.g., notification timelines to insurer, preservation of evidence, approved vendor lists, cooperation obligations) and that these are embedded in operational playbooks rather than residing only in the policy document.
whatGoodLooksLike: Crisis response policy or incident response playbook contains a dedicated section referencing the cyber insurance policy, listing specific obligations (notification windows, insurer contact details, evidence preservation requirements, approved forensic vendors) with step-by-step procedural guidance
Notification timelines to the insurer are mapped against internal escalation timelines, and a responsible owner is named for triggering insurer notification at each incident severity level

Warranties and attestations required by the insurer (e.g., attestations of security control standards such as MFA, patch currency, backup integrity) are documented and mapped to internal controls, with evidence that these standards are actively maintained
Procedures are reviewed and updated as part of the annual insurance renewal process, with a documented review sign-off confirming continued alignment between policy terms and internal procedures

Crisis response tabletop exercises incorporate insurance obligation scenarios (e.g., a scenario where the insurer's notification window is about to lapse during a chaotic incident response) to validate that staff know the requirements under pressure
commonGaps: The incident response playbook makes no reference to the cyber insurance policy — the two documents are managed entirely separately and the response team has no visibility of policy obligations during an incident

Insurer notification timelines exist in the policy (e.g., 72-hour notification requirement) but are not reflected in the incident escalation matrix, creating a risk that the institution inadvertently breaches notification obligations and voids coverage

Warranties requiring maintenance of specific security controls (e.g., MFA on all privileged accounts, patching within defined SLAs) are buried in the policy schedule but have never been communicated to the IT operations or security teams responsible for maintaining those controls
Policy terms are reviewed only by the legal or insurance team at renewal — operational teams such as the SOC, IT, and incident response never receive a briefing on what the policy requires of them

Procedures document what to do during an incident but do not specify what must NOT be done without insurer approval (e.g., paying a ransom, engaging unauthorised third-party vendors), leaving gaps that could trigger coverage disputes
suggestedSources: CISO office — incident response plan and crisis management playbook
Legal and Compliance — policy terms review and warranty/attestation schedule
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) — incident escalation workflows
Tabletop exercise records and after-action reports
Human Resources / Training — records of incident response team awareness training on insurance obligations
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (incident response policy with insurance section); supporting training attendance records; tabletop exercise reports

evidenceItems.name: IT Change Management Insurance Impact Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution has a mechanism within its IT change management process to assess whether material changes to IT services, architecture, or security controls could create unintended exclusions or voids in cyber insurance coverage. This addresses the second part of clause 11.17(b) by showing that coverage integrity is actively maintained as the IT environment evolves, rather than assuming the original policy remains adequate after significant technological changes.
whatGoodLooksLike: The IT change management policy or procedure contains an explicit step requiring assessment of insurance coverage impact for changes classified as major or significant (e.g., cloud migrations, new SaaS adoptions, retirement of security tools, changes to network architecture)

A standard change assessment checklist includes an insurance impact question with guidance on what types of changes require escalation to Legal/Compliance for policy review (e.g., introduction of new processing locations, engagement of new critical third parties, changes to security control standards warranted by the policy)

Records show instances where the insurance impact step was completed, with documented outcomes — including at least one instance where a proposed change triggered a policy endorsement or insurer notification
Change Advisory Board (CAB) terms of reference include Legal/Compliance representation or a defined escalation path to Legal/Compliance for insurance-sensitive changes

Annual review of the policy exclusion clauses is cross-referenced against the IT change log to retrospectively identify whether any changes executed during the year may have created coverage gaps, with results reported to the CRO
commonGaps: Change management process has no insurance impact assessment step — changes such as migration to public cloud or introduction of new remote access technologies proceed without any consideration of whether they trigger policy exclusions or warranty breaches

The insurance policy contains exclusions for changes to geographic processing locations or use of specific third-party cloud providers, but IT has never been briefed on these exclusions and cannot identify relevant changes
Assessment step exists on paper in the change form but is treated as a checkbox exercise — responses are consistently 'No impact' without any substantive analysis or reference to policy terms

Material changes (e.g., retirement of a security control warranted by the policy, such as a SIEM or DLP tool) are processed through the change management process but the insurance impact is not assessed because the policy warranties are not cross-referenced during review
There is no retrospective or annual look-back review — coverage gaps created by prior changes are only discovered when a claim is made and the insurer denies coverage
suggestedSources: IT Change Management team — CAB records, change request forms, and change log
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management, Jira Service Management) — change records with insurance impact assessment fields
Legal and Compliance — records of insurance impact escalations and endorsement requests
CISO office — records of security control changes and decommissions
Group Insurance/Treasury — endorsement history and insurer correspondence
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) of change records with insurance assessment fields; CAB minutes (PDF); endorsement correspondence (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Conflict of Interest Management Framework for Insurer Obligations
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has identified, assessed, and established governance arrangements to manage potential conflicts of interest that may arise between its cyber insurance policy obligations and its duty to act in the best interests of the institution and its customers. This addresses clause 11.17(c) by showing that decision-making authority during a cyber incident is clearly defined and not impaired by insurer-imposed obligations, and that the institution has anticipated scenarios where insurer and institutional interests may diverge.
whatGoodLooksLike: A conflict of interest analysis or legal opinion is documented, identifying specific scenarios where insurer obligations (e.g., mandatory use of insurer-appointed forensic vendors, insurer approval required before paying affected customers, insurer direction on public communications) could conflict with the institution's regulatory obligations, customer duty, or BNM reporting requirements

Crisis response policy explicitly establishes that institutional decision-making authority — particularly decisions affecting customer safety, regulatory notifications, and business continuity — is not delegable to or overrideable by the insurer, with the CEO/CRO/Board as the final decision authority

Legal review of the policy has assessed whether specific insurer-imposed obligations (e.g., appointment of insurer-nominated experts, insurer approval of settlements) are legally enforceable versus advisory, and this assessment is reflected in the institution's response protocols

Escalation matrix defines the point at which the institution would override insurer guidance in the interests of customers or regulatory compliance, and how this decision is documented to protect coverage under the policy to the maximum extent possible

Conflict of interest scenarios are included in tabletop exercises (e.g., a scenario where the insurer's appointed forensic firm is slow to respond, impeding the institution's ability to notify affected customers within the PDPA 72-hour window) with documented outcomes
commonGaps: Institution has accepted all insurer-imposed obligations without legal review of whether any of them — particularly mandatory use of insurer-appointed vendors or insurer approval of public communications — could impair fulfilment of BNM reporting obligations or customer notification duties

Crisis response procedures are silent on what happens when the insurer's instructions conflict with institutional or regulatory obligations, leaving incident response teams without guidance in a high-stress scenario

No conflict of interest analysis has been performed — the institution assumes insurer and institutional interests are aligned without having examined specific policy clauses that could create divergence

Insurer-appointed experts (forensic firms, legal counsel, PR advisers) are accepted as the default without the institution maintaining its own independent expert relationships, creating over-reliance that could impair independent decision-making

Board and senior management are unaware of the insurer obligations embedded in the policy — the conflict of interest analysis, if it exists, sits with Legal/Compliance but has not been escalated or socialised at governance level
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance — legal opinion or policy interpretation on insurer obligations and enforceability
CISO office — crisis response and incident management policy
Board Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee — minutes reflecting discussion of conflict of interest risks
Group Insurance/Treasury — policy schedule and insurer obligation clauses
Tabletop exercise records — scenarios testing insurer-institution conflict situations
evidenceItems.format: PDF (legal opinion or analysis); PDF/DOCX (crisis response policy extract); Board/committee minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Oversight Evidence for Cyber Insurance Governance
evidenceItems.description: Records evidencing that the Board and senior management are actively engaged in overseeing the institution's cyber insurance and loss provision arrangements, including periodic reporting on coverage adequacy, scenario analysis results, and any material changes to coverage. This demonstrates the governance overlay required for all S-marker obligations and confirms that cyber insurance is managed as a strategic risk instrument rather than an operational procurement matter.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or Board papers include at least annual reporting on cyber insurance coverage adequacy, scenario analysis outcomes, and loss provision levels, with substantive discussion reflected in minutes rather than passive note-taking
Management Risk Committee receives periodic (at minimum semi-annual) updates on the insurance programme, including coverage mapping results, any identified gaps, and the status of remediation actions

Chief Risk Officer (CRO) or Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) formally owns the cyber insurance governance framework and is accountable to Board for its adequacy — this ownership is documented in a terms of reference or RACI

Insurance renewal decisions are presented to Board or a delegated Board committee for approval, with supporting analysis of coverage adequacy relative to the institution's risk profile and scenario analysis results

Board has been briefed on the conflict of interest risks under clause 11.17(c) and has approved the institution's governance framework for managing those risks, with this briefing and approval documented in committee papers
commonGaps: Cyber insurance is managed entirely at an operational level by Legal, Compliance, or Treasury — Board has no visibility of coverage scope, adequacy assessments, or scenario analysis results and has never formally approved the insurance governance framework

Board papers reference insurance renewal as a line item cost approval rather than a substantive coverage adequacy discussion — minutes show no evidence of Board engagement with whether the policy actually covers the institution's risk profile
There is no named senior management owner for the cyber insurance programme — it falls between Legal, IT Risk, and Finance, resulting in fragmented governance and accountability gaps

Scenario analysis results that show the loss provision is inadequate are not escalated to Board — findings remain at working group level and no formal risk acceptance or remediation decision is made at the appropriate governance tier
Reporting to Board conflates cyber insurance with general insurance and does not provide sufficient cyber-specific granularity for informed oversight
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — Board Risk Committee and Management Risk Committee minutes and papers
CRO/CISO office — governance frameworks, RACI matrices, and reporting templates
GRC platform — risk reporting dashboards and Board pack generation
Group Insurance/Treasury — renewal approval records and Board submissions
Internal Audit — audit reports on cyber insurance governance adequacy
evidenceItems.format: PDF (Board/committee papers and minutes); PDF/DOCX (governance framework); GRC platform report export

auditTips: Request the cyber insurance policy schedule, all endorsements, and the full exclusions section before the on-site examination begins, and map each exclusion against the institution's current IT architecture and security control inventory — this frequently reveals undisclosed coverage gaps caused by cloud adoption, new third-party integrations, or decommissioned security controls that were warranted by the policy

Ask the institution to walk you through a simulated invocation of the cyber insurance policy by narrating the steps the incident response team would follow when a covered event occurs — gaps between the policy's notification timelines, approved vendor requirements, and the institution's actual incident response playbook become immediately visible when tested procedurally rather than reviewed on paper

Cross-reference the institution's IT change log from the past 12 months against the policy's warranty and attestation requirements, specifically looking for changes (e.g., expansion of privileged access, changes to patching SLA, retirement of endpoint security tools) that may have inadvertently breached a warranty without an insurer notification or endorsement being sought

Probe the conflict of interest assessment by asking the CISO or CRO a direct scenario question: 'During a ransomware incident at 2am, your insurer's appointed forensic vendor cannot deploy for 18 hours, but you are approaching the PDPA notification deadline — who makes the decision to engage your own vendor at potential coverage risk, and how is that documented?' — the quality of the answer reveals whether clause 11.17(c) has been genuinely operationalised or merely acknowledged on paper

Validate that the scenario analysis underpinning the loss provision review uses institution-specific financial quantification, not generic industry benchmarks — request the model inputs and assumptions, and check whether the scenarios reflect the institution's current critical asset inventory, transaction volumes, and customer data holdings rather than a prior-year baseline

Check whether tabletop exercise after-action reports from the past two years include any insurance-related scenarios or findings — the complete absence of insurance obligations from exercise scenarios is a red flag indicating the integration of clause 11.17(b) into crisis response procedures is theoretical rather than operationally tested

Verify Board-level engagement by reviewing Board Risk Committee minutes for substantive discussion of cyber insurance coverage adequacy — look for evidence that Board members asked questions, challenged assumptions, or requested changes rather than simply receiving reports for information, as passive receipt without engagement does not satisfy the governance expectations of an S-marker clause

11.18.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.18.title: Cyber Incident Notification to BNM
11.18.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has operationalised a multi-policy notification framework that integrates obligations under Operational Risk Reporting Part C, Business Continuity Management Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26 into a single coherent cyber incident reporting process. Examiners will specifically cross-reference actual cyber incidents logged in the institution's ITSM or GRC platform against BNM notification records to verify timeliness and completeness of submissions, looking for evidence that thresholds and timelines were respected. A key red flag is fragmented ownership — where the SOC, Operations Risk, and IT teams each hold partial records with no unified audit trail of who notified BNM, when, and via which mechanism. Examiners also probe whether the institution's notification content meets the substantive requirements of each referenced policy document (not merely that a notification was sent), and whether lessons from past notification failures have been embedded into updated procedures. Institutions with no documented incidents will be scrutinised particularly carefully to determine whether the absence reflects genuine resilience or a reporting gap.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Incident Notification Policy and SOP
evidenceItems.description: A formal policy and standard operating procedure that defines the institution's end-to-end process for notifying BNM of cyber incidents, explicitly cross-referencing the triggering conditions, timelines, and content requirements mandated by Operational Risk Reporting Part C, Business Continuity Management Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly maps each referenced BNM policy document (ORM Part C, BCM Part C, MAS 19.25–19.26) to specific notification triggers, timelines, and responsible owners within the institution
SOP includes a decision tree or flowchart that guides staff from incident detection through classification to BNM notification, with clear escalation paths and time-bound action steps
Policy is Board or senior management approved, with version history showing review within the past 12 months or following any material cyber incident
Notification templates aligned to BNM's expected format are embedded in or appended to the SOP, covering initial, interim, and final notification stages
Policy explicitly addresses multi-jurisdictional scenarios where the institution must notify both BNM and other regulators, with guidance on sequencing and content consistency
commonGaps: Policy references only one of the three BNM policy documents (typically ORM Part C), leaving BCM Part C and MAS 19.25–19.26 obligations undocumented and operationally unaddressed
Notification timelines are stated in generic terms ('as soon as practicable') rather than defined hours matching each referenced policy document's specific requirements
SOP ownership is ambiguous — Cyber/SOC team and Operational Risk team each reference different procedures with no single authoritative document, creating confusion during an actual incident
Policy has not been updated to reflect amendments to any of the three referenced BNM policy documents, resulting in stale thresholds or outdated notification channels

Merchant Acquiring Services obligations (paragraphs 19.25–19.26) are absent from the policy entirely because the policy was authored by the IT/Cyber function without input from the Payments or Cards business line
suggestedSources: Operational Risk Management department — policy register and document management system (e.g., SharePoint Policy Library)
Information Security / CISO office — cyber incident response playbooks and runbooks
Compliance department — regulatory obligations register linking BNM policy documents to internal procedures
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Riskonnect) — policy management module
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata; supporting flowcharts may be in Visio or embedded diagrams

evidenceItems.name: BNM Cyber Incident Notification Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive log of all cyber incidents that triggered a notification obligation to BNM, recording the incident details, classification rationale, notification timeline, submission channel, acknowledgement received, and final resolution status for each event.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register captures all incidents across the full three-year lookback period with no chronological gaps, and each entry cross-references the specific BNM policy document clause that triggered the notification obligation
Each record includes timestamps at four key stages: incident detection, internal escalation to Operational Risk or Compliance, submission of notification to BNM, and receipt of BNM acknowledgement
Register distinguishes between initial, interim, and final notifications for multi-stage reporting events, with separate entries or linked sub-records for each stage

Classification rationale column documents why the incident met or did not meet each relevant threshold (ORM Part C, BCM Part C, MAS 19.25–19.26), including cases where an incident was assessed and determined not to require notification
Register is subject to periodic independent review (e.g., internal audit or Compliance second-line) with review dates and sign-off recorded within the register itself
commonGaps: Register covers only incidents that were ultimately notified to BNM, omitting the population of assessed incidents that were determined not to meet reporting thresholds — leaving no audit trail of the institution's classification decisions

Timestamps are recorded inconsistently: detection time is logged by SOC in the ITSM system but notification time is manually entered in a separate spreadsheet, making timeline reconstruction unreliable

Incidents involving the Merchant Acquiring business are logged in a separate payments operations register and never reconciled against the cyber notification register, creating a blind spot for MAS 19.25–19.26 obligations
Register does not capture BNM's acknowledgement or any follow-up queries from BNM, making it impossible to demonstrate closed-loop notification management
The register is maintained in an uncontrolled spreadsheet with no access controls, version history, or change log, undermining its evidentiary integrity
suggestedSources: Operational Risk Management — regulatory incident reporting register
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — cyber incident ticket history with timestamps
GRC platform — regulatory event management or breach notification module
Compliance department — regulatory submissions log
CISO / SOC — security incident management system export
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC system export in PDF; must include timestamps, classification fields, and submission evidence

evidenceItems.name: Actual BNM Notification Submissions and Correspondence
evidenceItems.description: The complete set of actual notification documents, emails, or portal submissions transmitted to BNM for each notifiable cyber incident, together with any BNM acknowledgements, requests for additional information, and the institution's responses — demonstrating that notifications were substantively compliant with the content requirements of the relevant BNM policy documents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Submissions are stored in a secure, access-controlled repository with a consistent naming convention linking each submission to its corresponding incident record in the notification register

Each submission contains all substantive content fields required by the relevant BNM policy document (e.g., incident description, impact assessment, affected systems, customer impact, containment actions, and proposed remediation timeline)
Interim and final notifications reference and update the initial notification, demonstrating a coherent progression of information rather than standalone unrelated submissions
BNM acknowledgement receipts or portal confirmation screens are retained alongside each submission, confirming delivery and establishing the official notification timestamp
Where BNM requested additional information or clarification, the institution's responses are retained and cross-referenced to the original submission within the same record
commonGaps: Only the initial notification email is retained; interim updates and final notifications are missing because they were sent verbally or via phone and never documented

Submission content is generic and does not address the specific data fields required by the referenced BNM policy document, indicating the institution used an ad hoc format rather than a structured template
Notifications are stored in the personal email inbox of the former Chief Risk Officer and cannot be retrieved following staff departure, creating a critical evidentiary gap
The institution cannot demonstrate when BNM received the notification because submissions were sent by email without delivery confirmation or portal acknowledgement, and no follow-up was sought

Notifications for incidents affecting the Merchant Acquiring business line were submitted by the business unit directly and are not held by the Compliance or Risk function, making complete retrieval for audit purposes impossible
suggestedSources: Compliance department — BNM correspondence file (SharePoint or secure document management system)
BNM RENTAS or supervisory portal — submission history and acknowledgement records (where applicable)
Chief Risk Officer / Head of Compliance — email archive for regulatory correspondence
Operational Risk Management — regulatory submissions folder
Legal department — copies held where notifications had legal significance
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scanned or native) for notification letters; email exports in PDF or MSG format with metadata; portal screenshots where applicable

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Incident Classification and Threshold Assessment Framework
evidenceItems.description: A documented framework that defines the classification criteria and materiality thresholds used to determine whether a cyber incident triggers notification obligations under Operational Risk Reporting Part C, Business Continuity Management Part C, and Merchant Acquiring Services paragraphs 19.25–19.26, enabling consistent and auditable classification decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Framework maps specific incident characteristics (e.g., system downtime duration, number of customers affected, financial loss quantum, data records compromised) to the notification thresholds defined in each of the three referenced BNM policy documents, with no ambiguity about which threshold applies

Classification is performed using a documented scoring matrix or structured checklist that requires explicit assessment against all three policy document obligations, not just the one most familiar to the assessor

Framework is supported by worked examples or anonymised case studies from past incidents that illustrate how borderline cases were classified, demonstrating institutional judgement is consistent and documented

The framework has been validated against the actual text of ORM Part C, BCM Part C, and MAS 19.25–19.26 by Compliance, with a sign-off that confirms alignment as at the most recent policy amendment date
Classification decisions made using the framework are recorded and retained with each incident record, showing the assessor's identity, the scores assigned, and the resulting classification outcome
commonGaps: The classification framework addresses ORM Part C thresholds but has never been extended to incorporate BCM Part C or MAS 19.25–19.26 criteria, leaving staff without guidance on how to apply these parallel obligations

Thresholds are expressed in qualitative terms ('significant impact', 'material disruption') without quantitative anchors, resulting in inconsistent classification outcomes across different assessors or business units
The framework has not been updated following BNM amendments to any of the referenced policy documents, meaning staff may be applying outdated thresholds

Classification decisions are made verbally in incident bridge calls and not formally documented, so there is no audit trail showing how the institution concluded that a given incident did or did not require notification
The framework sits solely within the SOC's incident response runbook and is unknown to the Operational Risk and Compliance teams who are responsible for actual notification to BNM
suggestedSources: Information Security / CISO office — incident response playbooks and classification matrices
Operational Risk Management — risk event classification policy and materiality thresholds
Compliance department — regulatory threshold mapping document
GRC platform — incident classification workflows and decision rules
Payments / Cards operations — MAS-specific threshold documentation held by the Merchant Acquiring business line
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy document with embedded matrix or decision tree; supporting spreadsheet for quantitative threshold tables

evidenceItems.name: Notification Timeline Compliance Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records that demonstrate the institution met the specific notification timelines prescribed by each referenced BNM policy document for each notifiable cyber incident, enabling examiners to calculate elapsed time from incident detection (or trigger event) to BNM submission and verify adherence to regulatory deadlines.
whatGoodLooksLike: A timeline analysis document for each notifiable incident that calculates, in hours, the elapsed time from detection to BNM notification against the applicable policy deadline, with source timestamps cited from ITSM, SIEM, or email metadata

Where notification was made within deadline, evidence demonstrates the full chain: detection alert timestamp (SIEM), SOC ticket creation time (ITSM), escalation to Compliance timestamp, and notification submission timestamp (email metadata or portal log)

Where notification was delayed beyond the prescribed timeline, a documented root cause analysis and remediation action exists, showing the institution identified the breach proactively rather than only when challenged by BNM

Timeline records cross-reference the specific BNM policy document and clause that prescribed the applicable notification deadline, confirming the institution applied the correct timeline rather than a generic internal standard

An annual summary report reviewed by senior management or Board Risk Committee that presents notification timeline performance across all incidents in the review period, demonstrating governance oversight of reporting obligations
commonGaps: The institution cannot reconstruct accurate detection timestamps because the SIEM alert that first flagged the incident was not correlated with the SOC ticket, and the ticket was opened hours later using an estimated time

Timeline calculations use 'business hours' rather than calendar hours, but the applicable BNM policy document prescribes calendar-hour deadlines — resulting in apparent compliance that is actually a breach
No timeline analysis is performed after incidents are resolved; the institution assumes compliance without ever calculating the actual elapsed time against the regulatory deadline

Multiple handoffs between SOC, IT, Operational Risk, and Compliance during the notification process consumed hours that are undocumented, making it impossible to identify where timeline slippage occurred
Timeline evidence covers only the initial notification but not interim notifications required under BCM Part C or MAS 19.25–19.26, which have their own separate update deadlines
suggestedSources: SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) — alert and event timestamps
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — ticket creation, escalation, and closure timestamps
Email system — metadata from notification emails sent to BNM (sent timestamps, delivery receipts)
Operational Risk Management — regulatory event log with milestone timestamps
GRC platform — notification workflow audit trail showing each stage completion time
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet with calculated timeline fields; supported by system exports (PDF or CSV) providing source timestamps; incident-specific timeline narrative in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Post-Notification Review and Lessons Learned Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented post-incident reviews covering the institution's notification process performance following each notifiable cyber incident, recording whether the notification process functioned as intended, any deviations from the SOP, and corrective actions taken — demonstrating continuous improvement of the BNM notification capability.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each post-incident review explicitly evaluates the notification process as a distinct workstream, separate from the technical incident response review, with specific findings on timeline compliance, content quality, and cross-policy alignment
Where notification process deficiencies are identified, corrective actions are recorded with owners, target dates, and a formal closure mechanism confirming the action was completed and verified

Lessons learned from notification exercises and actual incidents are consolidated into an annual review that informs updates to the notification policy, SOP, and classification framework, with a documented link between lessons and policy changes
Reviews are reviewed and signed off by the Chief Risk Officer or Head of Compliance, evidencing senior management accountability for notification process quality

The post-incident review log is reviewed by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent at least annually, with minutes recording the Committee's assessment and any additional directions on improving BNM notification readiness
commonGaps: Post-incident reviews focus entirely on technical containment and recovery, with no evaluation of whether the notification process met regulatory requirements — the BNM reporting obligation is treated as a checkbox rather than a process to be reviewed

Corrective actions identified in previous post-incident reviews were never formally closed, and the same notification process failures (e.g., unclear escalation path, missing MAS 19.25–19.26 checklist) recur in subsequent incidents
Reviews are conducted only for high-severity incidents, leaving moderate-severity incidents — which may still trigger notification obligations — without any process review or learning extraction
Lessons learned are documented in a report that is filed and never referenced again; there is no mechanism to ensure findings are translated into SOP amendments or training updates

Board or senior management oversight of notification process performance is absent; the Board Risk Committee receives incident reports but these contain no assessment of whether BNM notification obligations were met on time and in full
suggestedSources: Information Security / CISO office — post-incident review reports and action logs
Operational Risk Management — risk event closure reports
Compliance department — regulatory breach register (where notification timeline was missed)
GRC platform — post-incident action tracking and closure workflow
Board Risk Committee secretariat — minutes recording discussion of notification performance
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX post-incident review reports; action tracking spreadsheet or GRC system export; Board/Committee minutes in PDF

auditTips: Before the examination, reconcile the BNM notification register against the ITSM/SIEM incident log for the full lookback period — examiners will perform exactly this cross-check, and any incident present in the ITSM system that is absent from the notification register will require a documented explanation of why it was assessed as not meeting reporting thresholds under ORM Part C, BCM Part C, or MAS 19.25–19.26.

Prepare a three-column mapping document that places the verbatim notification obligation from each of the three referenced policy documents (ORM Part C, BCM Part C, MAS 19.25–19.26) side-by-side with the corresponding section of your internal SOP — examiners assess whether internal procedures operationalise all three sets of obligations, and gaps in this mapping are a common finding for institutions that drafted their SOP around only one policy document.

Retrieve and pre-package all actual BNM notification submissions as a single indexed bundle, with each submission labelled by incident date, incident name, notification stage (initial/interim/final), and the BNM policy document that triggered the obligation — examiners may request this evidence on short notice and the inability to produce it quickly creates an adverse impression of document management maturity.

Calculate and document the notification timeline for every incident in the lookback period using calendar hours (not business hours), comparing each against the applicable BNM deadline — if any timeline breach is identified, prepare a written root cause analysis and evidence of corrective action before the examination, as self-identified and remediated breaches are viewed more favourably than breaches discovered by examiners.

Engage the Merchant Acquiring business line specifically to confirm that MAS paragraphs 19.25–19.26 obligations are reflected in their incident escalation procedures and that any qualifying incidents from the payments/cards business have been captured in the institution-wide notification register — this cross-business-line gap is frequently missed because the MAS policy is perceived as a payments policy rather than a cyber policy.

Conduct a dry-run tabletop exercise simulating a notifiable cyber incident specifically to test the notification process — document the exercise, record identified process gaps, and retain evidence of corrective actions taken — as examiners view documented exercises as strong evidence that the institution has operationally tested its compliance with clause 11.18 rather than relying solely on paper policy.

Verify that all staff with a role in the BNM notification process (SOC analysts, Operational Risk officers, Compliance officers, relevant business line heads) have completed training or awareness sessions on the notification obligations and can articulate the thresholds and timelines without referring solely to documentation — examiners may conduct staff interviews and a staff member's inability to explain the notification process creates doubt about whether the written policy is genuinely embedded in operating practice.

11.19.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.19.title: Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing
11.19.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has an operationalised, two-way cyber threat intelligence (CTI) sharing programme — not merely passive membership in sharing platforms. Examiners specifically verify that the institution actively contributes threat indicators, TTPs, and advisories to BNM-recognised or industry platforms (e.g., FinCERT, MySEF, FSA-ISAC) and can demonstrate timely submission records. A key red flag is institutions that hold memberships but have no documented outbound sharing activity, or where sharing is ad hoc and undocumented. Examiners also scrutinise whether dedicated resources (budget lines, personnel, or time allocations) are formally assigned to industry CTI initiatives, and whether data protection compliance has been considered and documented as part of the sharing process. Cross-referencing sharing logs against incident timelines is a common technique to verify that intelligence was shared on a timely rather than retrospective basis.

evidenceItems.name: CTI Sharing Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: A formal, board or senior management-approved policy and supporting standard operating procedures (SOPs) governing the institution's obligations, processes, and controls for sharing cyber threat intelligence with external parties, including data classification, anonymisation requirements, approval workflows, and the list of authorised sharing platforms.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references BNM RMiT clause 11.19 and maps obligations to internal roles and responsibilities
Procedures define clear timelines for sharing (e.g., initial notification within 24 hours of validated IOC, full report within 72 hours) that operationalise 'timely basis'
Data protection compliance section references PDPA 2010 and internal legal/DPO sign-off requirements before sharing PII-adjacent data
Approved list of authorised sharing platforms is maintained and reviewed at least annually, including BNM FinCERT, MySEF, and law enforcement channels
Document carries version control, owner designation (e.g., CISO), and evidence of annual review with change history
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not define 'timely' — no SLA or timeframe is specified, making compliance unmeasurable
Data protection requirements are absent or generic, with no reference to PDPA 2010 or internal DPO approval steps
Sharing platforms listed are outdated or do not include BNM-sanctioned channels, suggesting policy has not been reviewed since initial drafting
Policy is approved at operational level only; no evidence of senior management or board endorsement for a regulatory obligation of this nature
Procedures describe inbound threat feed consumption only, with no outbound sharing obligations or workflows documented
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — Policy Management module
SharePoint or Confluence intranet policy repository
Information Security Management System (ISMS) document register
CISO office or Cybersecurity Governance team
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: CTI Platform Membership and Registration Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation proving the institution's active, registered membership in BNM-recognised and industry-approved cyber threat intelligence sharing platforms and communities, including onboarding correspondence, membership certificates, user account records, and terms of participation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Membership certificates or confirmation letters from at least two recognised platforms (e.g., BNM FinCERT, MySEF, FS-ISAC or regional equivalent, national CERT/law enforcement portal)
Active user account records showing named institutional representatives with current access credentials or access logs
Terms of participation or memoranda of understanding (MoUs) are signed and on file, with data protection and confidentiality clauses reviewed by legal counsel
Annual renewal or revalidation records confirm memberships are current and have not lapsed
Escalation contacts and liaison officer designations for each platform are documented and kept up to date
commonGaps: Institution holds membership on only one platform, creating a single point of dependency and limited industry reach
Membership records are present but user accounts belong to staff who have since left the organisation, indicating access was never transferred
MoUs or terms of participation have not been reviewed by legal or DPO since signing, creating unmitigated PDPA exposure
No escalation contact or designated liaison documented, meaning sharing activity is person-dependent and not institutionalised
Membership exists at IT team level with no awareness at CISO or senior management level, indicating insufficient resource allocation
suggestedSources: CISO office correspondence files and contract repository
IT Security team email archives and vendor management system
Legal/Compliance contract management platform (e.g., Ironclad, ContractSafe)
HR system — to cross-check named representatives are current employees
evidenceItems.format: PDF/Email export

evidenceItems.name: Outbound CTI Sharing Activity Log
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive log or register of all outbound cyber threat intelligence shared by the institution with external platforms, including the type of intelligence shared (IOCs, TTPs, advisories, vulnerability data), destination platform, date and time of submission, and approvals obtained, demonstrating timely sharing in accordance with policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Log entries include structured fields: date/time of sharing, intelligence type (IOC, TTP, advisory), platform/recipient, internal approver, and reference to originating incident or threat event
Timestamps on log entries can be cross-referenced against incident records to verify 'timely basis' — sharing occurs within policy-defined SLAs, not weeks after detection
Log reflects consistent sharing activity across multiple periods (not solely during examination preparation), indicating an embedded operational practice
STIX/TAXII or platform-native export records from threat intelligence platforms (e.g., OpenCTI, MISP, ThreatConnect) corroborate manual log entries with system-generated audit trails
Data anonymisation or redaction steps applied before sharing are documented in the log or linked approval records, evidencing PDPA compliance
commonGaps: Log only captures inbound feed consumption; no outbound sharing records exist, the most critical gap for this clause
Sharing activity is present but timestamps show submissions occurring weeks after incidents were resolved, contradicting 'timely basis' obligations
Log is maintained as an informal spreadsheet with no version control, ownership, or access restrictions, undermining its evidentiary value
Intelligence shared is limited to generic, public-domain indicators with no institution-specific or operationally useful data, suggesting nominal compliance only
Approvals for sharing are absent from the log, meaning no evidence that PDPA or data classification checks were performed before outbound sharing
suggestedSources: Threat Intelligence Platform (TIP) export — OpenCTI, MISP, Anomali ThreatStream, or ThreatConnect
SIEM platform sharing integration logs (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel)
GRC platform — Incident Management or Threat Intelligence module
SOC ticketing system (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM, Jira) — threat intelligence workflow records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / System export (CSV/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Industry Initiative Participation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of the institution's active participation in industry-wide initiatives aimed at improving collective threat intelligence capabilities, including attendance records, meeting minutes, working group contributions, exercise participation, and training completions related to CTI platforms and communities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Attendance registers or certificates for BNM FinCERT briefings, MySEF exercises, FSTEP cybersecurity programmes, or equivalent industry CTI workshops within the past 12 months
Named institutional representatives (not generic email aliases) are evidenced participating in at least two separate industry CTI initiatives annually
Working group contributions are documented — meeting minutes show institution's representatives providing input, not merely attending as passive observers
Cyber exercise participation records (e.g., BNM-coordinated simulations, NACSA exercises) with after-action reports demonstrating learning outcomes applied internally
Budget or resource allocation records show approved headcount or time allocation for staff dedicated to CTI community participation, evidencing the 'allocate resources' obligation
commonGaps: Attendance records exist for one-time events only, with no evidence of sustained, recurring participation across multiple reporting periods
Participation is limited to a single individual whose departure would leave the institution with no active engagement, indicating no institutional embedding
Records show attendance at general cybersecurity conferences rather than CTI-specific industry initiatives as required by the clause
No budget line or resource allocation document exists; participation is treated as informal and unsanctioned, contradicting the 'must allocate resources' obligation
Meeting minutes and participation records are held personally by attending staff and not filed centrally, making evidence retrieval for examination unreliable
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) — training completion records
CISO office calendar and correspondence archives
Finance system — training and conference budget approvals
SharePoint or Teams — working group collaboration spaces and meeting minutes
HR or L&D platform — external conference and professional development records
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Spreadsheet / System export

evidenceItems.name: Data Protection Compliance Assessment for CTI Sharing
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has assessed and addressed its obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (PDPA) and other applicable data protection laws before sharing cyber threat intelligence, including a legal assessment, anonymisation procedures, and DPO or legal sign-off.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal legal or DPO assessment addressing what categories of data are permissible to share externally under PDPA 2010 and any BNM-specific guidance, referenced in the CTI sharing policy

Documented anonymisation and data minimisation procedures applied to threat intelligence before outbound sharing, with worked examples for common intelligence types (e.g., IP addresses linked to customer activity, email headers)
Approval workflow records show DPO or legal sign-off obtained for each new category of intelligence type added to the sharing programme
Staff handling CTI sharing have completed PDPA awareness training relevant to sharing scenarios, with completion records on file
Periodic review of the data protection assessment (at least annually or upon regulatory change) with version history evidencing updates
commonGaps: No data protection assessment exists — institution assumes CTI sharing is exempt from PDPA without legal basis, creating regulatory exposure
Anonymisation is applied informally and inconsistently, with no documented procedure and no validation step to confirm PII has been removed before sharing
DPO is unaware of the CTI sharing programme, indicating it was established without required privacy governance oversight
Assessment was conducted once at programme inception and has not been reviewed following the 2024 PDPA amendments or BNM guidance updates
Legal sign-off covers only one platform; as new platforms were added to the sharing programme, no incremental assessment was performed
suggestedSources: Legal/Compliance department — PDPA assessment register
DPO office — privacy impact assessments and data sharing agreements
GRC platform — Privacy or Risk Assessment module
SharePoint — Compliance and Legal folder
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: CTI Sharing Programme Review and Reporting to Management
evidenceItems.description: Periodic management reporting and programme review records demonstrating that the institution's senior management or board-level committee maintains oversight of the CTI sharing programme, including its effectiveness, resource adequacy, participation coverage, and compliance with BNM obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or IT/Cyber Committee meeting minutes include at least annual agenda items reviewing CTI sharing programme performance metrics (volume of intelligence shared, platforms active, initiatives participated in)
Management reports include trend data comparing sharing activity across periods, evidencing continuous improvement rather than static compliance
Resource adequacy review is documented — reports assess whether current headcount and tooling are sufficient for ongoing CTI sharing obligations and recommend adjustments where gaps exist
Programme review outcomes feed directly into the institution's cybersecurity strategy or annual cybersecurity plan, demonstrating integration with broader security governance
Corrective actions from prior BNM examinations or internal audit findings on CTI sharing are tracked and closed within documented timelines
commonGaps: CTI sharing is never tabled as a standalone agenda item at senior management or board level; it is buried in broad cybersecurity updates with no specific metrics or discussion
Reports present activity counts (e.g., number of IOCs shared) without qualitative assessment of whether sharing is timely, useful, or compliant with policy
No corrective action tracking mechanism exists for CTI sharing gaps identified in audits or examinations, suggesting a culture of finding acknowledgement without remediation
Resource adequacy is never formally assessed — sharing programme relies on staff absorbing CTI duties into existing roles without approved allocation
Management reporting on CTI sharing is prepared only in response to examination notices, rather than as a standing governance practice
suggestedSources: Board secretariat — Board Risk Committee or IT/Cyber Committee minutes and papers
CISO office — Cybersecurity management reporting pack
GRC platform — Committee reporting and action tracking module
Internal Audit — findings register and management action tracking
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Meeting minutes

auditTips: Cross-reference the outbound CTI sharing log (E3) timestamps directly against the institution's incident management records and SOC tickets: BNM examiners will specifically test whether intelligence sharing occurred within policy-defined SLAs or was submitted retrospectively after threat resolution, as backdated or delayed sharing is a primary indicator of nominal compliance.

Request a live demonstration of the institution's access to at least two active CTI sharing platforms during fieldwork — verify that named liaison officers can log in, that accounts are current, and that the institution can show a recent submission; static membership certificates without operational access are a common evasion technique.

Trace the resource allocation trail from the CTI sharing policy's 'allocated resources' commitment through to Finance (approved budget lines), HR (role descriptions or time allocation approvals), and calendar records — the gap between policy commitment and actual resource assignment is frequently where institutions fall short of the 'must allocate resources' obligation.

Engage the DPO directly and independently of the IT Security team to assess whether they are aware of and have formally reviewed the CTI sharing programme; if the DPO has no knowledge of the programme or has never issued a data protection opinion on it, this indicates the PDPA compliance obligation in the verbatim text has not been addressed in substance.

Review at least 24 months of management and board committee papers to establish whether CTI sharing has been a standing governance topic or only appears in periods preceding an examination — institutions that can only produce recent reporting are demonstrating examination-driven compliance rather than embedded practice.

When evaluating industry initiative participation (E4), distinguish between passive event attendance and active contribution: request meeting minutes, working group outputs, or exercise after-action reports where the institution's specific contributions are identifiable; attendance at public conferences does not satisfy the 'industry-wide initiatives aimed at improving collective threat intelligence capabilities' obligation.

Map every sharing platform listed in the institution's policy and membership records against BNM's current recognised platform list and any MySEF or FinCERT guidance in force at the time of examination — outdated platform lists that include decommissioned or non-recognised channels without corresponding updates to active, recognised channels indicate the policy and programme are not being actively maintained.

11.20.subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
11.20.title: Stakeholder Collaboration on Cyber Threats
11.20.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has established structured, active, and documented engagement with relevant stakeholders — including BNM itself, CyberSecurity Malaysia (CSM), the Financial Services Information Sharing and Analysis Center (FS-ISAC), sector peers, and law enforcement — in combating cyber threats. Examiners look beyond the existence of membership or registration; they probe for evidence of two-way information exchange, demonstrable contribution to threat intelligence sharing ecosystems, and timely responsiveness to threat advisories received from authorities. Red flags include passive or nominal participation (e.g., receiving bulletins without acting or contributing), the absence of documented escalation paths to regulatory bodies during incidents, and no evidence of participation in cybersecurity exercises or industry working groups. The evaluation approach cross-references MOU/agreement registers, threat intelligence platform logs, incident correspondence with BNM and CSM, and meeting minutes from inter-agency or industry forums to verify that collaboration is substantive and ongoing rather than performative.

evidenceItems.name: Stakeholder Collaboration Register and MOU/Agreement Repository
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated register of all formal and informal collaboration arrangements with external cybersecurity stakeholders, including BNM, CyberSecurity Malaysia, FS-ISAC, GCIX, law enforcement agencies, and sector peers. Supported by copies of signed MOUs, information-sharing agreements, participation letters, and membership certificates that authorise the exchange of cyber threat intelligence.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register is current, reviewed at least annually, and covers all active relationships with named contact persons, scope of collaboration, and agreement expiry dates
MOUs and information-sharing agreements clearly define the classification, handling, and permissible use of shared threat intelligence in accordance with TLP (Traffic Light Protocol) or equivalent
Each arrangement maps to specific threat categories or incident types, demonstrating purposeful rather than blanket collaboration
Agreements include bilateral obligations — the institution both receives and contributes threat intelligence, evidencing genuine partnership
Version history shows agreements have been renewed or updated in response to evolving threat landscapes or regulatory guidance
commonGaps: Register exists but contains expired agreements that have not been renewed, suggesting collaboration has lapsed
MOUs are generic or template-based with no institution-specific schedules defining what intelligence is shared, at what frequency, and through which channels
Membership in FS-ISAC or similar bodies is documented but no participation logs or downloaded intelligence reports are retained to prove active use
Collaboration arrangements are siloed within the CISO's office with no visibility at Board or senior management level, indicating governance weakness
Agreements with law enforcement (e.g., PDRM, NACSA) are absent or undocumented, leaving a gap for mandatory incident referrals
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC, or in-house) — Third-Party and Regulatory Relationship module
CISO Office SharePoint or document management system — Legal and Agreements folder
Legal / Compliance department contract management system
Corporate Secretariat records for board-approved collaboration frameworks
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (agreements and MOUs); Spreadsheet or GRC export (register)

evidenceItems.name: Threat Intelligence Sharing and Receipt Logs
evidenceItems.description: Operational records demonstrating active, two-way participation in threat intelligence exchange with external stakeholders. This includes logs from threat intelligence platforms (TIPs), records of indicators of compromise (IOCs) submitted to or received from CyberSecurity Malaysia, BNM, FS-ISAC, or sector peers, and evidence that shared intelligence was actioned internally.
whatGoodLooksLike: TIP logs (e.g., MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali) show timestamped ingestion and contribution of IOCs, TTPs, and threat reports with attribution to the external source or recipient organisation

A reconciliation log links received intelligence to internal actions taken — such as firewall rule updates, IOC ingestion into SIEM, or alerts issued to operations teams — demonstrating closed-loop processing
Intelligence shared with external parties is clearly marked with sensitivity classification and approved by the appropriate authority (e.g., CISO sign-off) before transmission
Frequency of exchange is consistent with the agreed cadence in MOUs — daily, weekly, or event-triggered — with no unexplained gaps exceeding 30 days
Reports received from BNM or CSM cyber advisories are tracked through an advisory register showing acknowledgement, risk assessment, and disposition within defined SLA
commonGaps: Institution receives threat feeds but has no evidence of contributing intelligence back to the community, making collaboration one-directional and potentially non-compliant with sharing agreement terms
IOC ingestion is automated but there is no human review log, so it cannot be demonstrated that received intelligence was assessed for relevance or actioned
Advisory tracking register is missing or managed informally in email inboxes, making it impossible to demonstrate timely response to regulator-issued advisories
TIP logs are not archived in a tamper-evident or auditable format, undermining their evidential value during examination
No evidence that intelligence shared externally went through an internal approval and sanitisation process to remove proprietary or customer data
suggestedSources: Threat Intelligence Platform (MISP, ThreatConnect, Anomali, or OpenCTI) — event and sharing logs
SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh) — IOC ingestion correlation reports
Security Operations Centre (SOC) ticketing system (ServiceNow, Jira) — advisory handling tickets
Email/collaboration platform (Outlook, Teams) — archived communications with BNM Cyber Supervision and CSM MyCERT
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/JSON from TIP or SIEM); PDF (advisory tracking register); Email archive

evidenceItems.name: BNM and Regulatory Authority Cyber Incident Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of all cyber incident notifications, disclosures, and ongoing communications made to BNM, CyberSecurity Malaysia (MyCERT/NACSA), and other mandated authorities in accordance with reporting obligations under RMiT. Demonstrates the institution's fulfilment of its duty to collaborate with authorities during active threat events.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each reportable incident has a corresponding BNM notification record with timestamp, channel used, incident reference number, and name of the BNM contact notified — meeting the prescribed reporting timelines (e.g., within 1 hour for critical incidents)
Notifications include all required fields: nature of incident, affected systems, estimated impact, containment actions taken, and a defined next-update commitment
Post-incident, follow-up reports and closure notifications are documented, showing the institution maintained collaborative communication throughout the incident lifecycle
A centralised incident notification log tracks all regulatory submissions with cross-references to the internal incident ticket, enabling rapid retrieval during examination
Root cause analysis reports shared with BNM upon request are version-controlled and demonstrate collaborative willingness to provide transparency
commonGaps: Notification records exist only in individual email threads without a centralised log, making it difficult to demonstrate systematic compliance across multiple incidents
Timestamps on notifications cannot be independently verified (e.g., sent from personal email or informal channels), weakening the audit trail for timeliness
Only initial notifications are retained; follow-up updates and closure reports are missing, giving the impression of incomplete collaboration
Minor or near-miss cyber events are not reported to authorities even where classification thresholds are ambiguous, reflecting an overly conservative disclosure posture
Notification templates are outdated and do not align with current BNM incident reporting format requirements, risking submission of incomplete information
suggestedSources: Incident Management System (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — regulatory notification workflow
BNM RENTAS/BNM Regulatory Reporting Portal — submission acknowledgements
Compliance department SharePoint — Regulatory Correspondence folder
Legal department — preserved email correspondence with BNM Supervision
evidenceItems.format: PDF (notification letters and BNM acknowledgements); Spreadsheet (notification log); System export (incident management platform)

evidenceItems.name: Industry Forum and Working Group Participation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of the institution's active participation in industry cybersecurity forums, working groups, and collaborative exercises relevant to the Malaysian financial sector. This includes Association of Banks in Malaysia (ABM) cybersecurity committees, FS-ISAC Asia Pacific working groups, BNM-convened industry exercises, and bilateral engagements with sector peers on common threats.
whatGoodLooksLike: Attendance records, minutes, or participation certificates for a minimum of four relevant industry cybersecurity forums or working groups per year, with named institution representatives identified
Action items from forum meetings are tracked internally and assigned owners, demonstrating that intelligence gathered is operationalised rather than merely noted
The institution has contributed presentations, case studies, or threat briefings to at least one industry forum in the past 12 months, evidencing active rather than passive participation

Participation spans multiple levels — technical (SOC analysts sharing IOCs), management (CISO-level strategy forums), and governance (Board or Risk Committee receiving industry threat briefing summaries)
Records include joint cyber exercises or tabletop simulations conducted with industry peers or regulatory bodies, with after-action reports documenting lessons learned
commonGaps: Participation is limited to receiving industry newsletters or bulletins with no interactive engagement, which does not satisfy the 'collaborate and cooperate' standard
Only the CISO attends industry forums with no mechanism to cascade intelligence or action items to operational teams, creating an information silo
Meeting minutes are not formally retained, leaving no auditable record of the institution's positions, contributions, or commitments made in collaborative forums
Participation is concentrated in a single forum or channel, with no diversification across technical, strategic, and law enforcement collaboration contexts
After-action reports from joint exercises are not produced or are too superficial to demonstrate meaningful learning and improvement
suggestedSources: Corporate calendar and travel records — forum attendance verification
CISO Office SharePoint — Working Group folder with minutes and action logs
Human Resources training platform — cybersecurity exercise participation records
ABM Secretariat or FS-ISAC member portal — participation certificates and attendance registers
evidenceItems.format: PDF (meeting minutes, attendance certificates, after-action reports); Spreadsheet (participation tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Internal Stakeholder Escalation and Communication Policy
evidenceItems.description: The institution's documented policy and procedures governing how cyber threat intelligence received from external stakeholders and authorities is escalated internally, and how internal decisions to share information externally are governed. Demonstrates that external collaboration is embedded in internal operating procedures rather than managed ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy clearly defines roles and responsibilities for managing external cyber collaboration, including who is authorised to receive, assess, escalate, and respond to threat intelligence from BNM, CSM, and peers
Escalation thresholds are explicitly defined — specifying what types of external threat intelligence trigger immediate escalation to CISO, Board Risk Committee, or CEO within defined timeframes
The policy references specific external stakeholders by name (BNM, NACSA, MyCERT, FS-ISAC, PDRM) and describes the nature and cadence of collaboration expected with each
Information-sharing governance includes a pre-transmission review checklist to ensure intelligence shared externally is appropriately sanitised, classified, and approved
Policy is reviewed annually, with the most recent review evidenced by CISO sign-off and Board Risk Committee endorsement
commonGaps: Policy exists for internal incident escalation but makes no reference to external stakeholder collaboration, leaving a governance gap specifically for clause 11.20
Roles are described at department level rather than by named position, making it unclear who specifically bears accountability for regulatory collaboration
The policy does not address outbound information sharing — only inbound intelligence receipt — failing to capture the 'cooperate closely' obligation
No training records demonstrate that staff responsible for external collaboration have been briefed on the policy and their specific obligations under it
Policy has not been updated following the most recent BNM RMiT revision or following a significant incident that exposed collaboration process weaknesses
suggestedSources: Policy management system (Confluence, SharePoint, or GRC platform) — Cybersecurity Policy Library
CISO Office — Information Security Policy and Standards repository
Human Resources / Learning Management System — policy acknowledgement and training records
Board Risk Committee secretariat — endorsed policy documents with approval minutes
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy document with version history and approval signatures)

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Threat Collaboration Metrics and Management Reporting
evidenceItems.description: Periodic management and Board-level reports that include metrics on the institution's external cyber threat collaboration activities. Demonstrates that collaboration is monitored, measured, and subject to governance oversight — not just an operational function without strategic visibility.
whatGoodLooksLike: Monthly or quarterly CISO dashboard includes quantified collaboration metrics: number of threat advisories received and actioned, IOCs shared/received, regulatory notifications made, and forum engagements attended
Board Risk Committee or equivalent receives an annual summary of external collaboration activities, outcomes, and improvements recommended, with evidence of board-level discussion (minutes)
Metrics are trended over time, enabling management to identify declining engagement or missed escalation SLAs before they become examination findings
Reports include qualitative assessment of collaboration effectiveness — e.g., percentage of received IOCs that were relevant and actioned — rather than purely activity counts
KPIs or KRIs linked to collaboration obligations are defined, with breach thresholds that trigger management escalation, demonstrating an active control framework
commonGaps: Collaboration activities are tracked operationally within the SOC but never aggregated into management or board reporting, creating a governance blind spot
Metrics focus on volume (number of advisories received) without quality indicators (percentage actioned, time to action), making it impossible to assess collaboration effectiveness
Board reports reference cybersecurity broadly but contain no specific disclosure of regulatory collaboration activities or compliance with clause 11.20 obligations
No defined KPIs or benchmarks exist for collaboration, so the institution cannot objectively assess whether its engagement level is adequate relative to its risk profile
Historical metrics are not retained across reporting periods, preventing trend analysis and masking degradation in collaboration posture over time
suggestedSources: GRC platform — compliance metrics and KRI dashboards
SIEM / TIP platform — exportable collaboration activity reports
Board Risk Committee secretariat — board pack archives
CISO Office — monthly cybersecurity management reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board and management reports); Spreadsheet (metrics tracker); Dashboard export (GRC or SIEM platform)

auditTips: Map your evidence directly to named stakeholders: BNM examiners will expect to see that collaboration spans at minimum BNM itself, CyberSecurity Malaysia (MyCERT/NACSA), FS-ISAC Asia Pacific, and law enforcement.
