Prepare a one-page stakeholder map showing each relationship, the nature of collaboration, the communication channel, and the frequency — this becomes your anchor document for the entire clause examination.

Prove two-way collaboration, not just reception: Examiners specifically probe whether the institution contributes to threat intelligence communities or merely receives. Before the examination, compile a summary of IOCs, threat reports, or advisories the institution has shared outbound in the past 12 months, with timestamps and recipient confirmation where available. Passive membership will not satisfy the 'collaborate and cooperate closely' standard.

Reconcile your incident notification log against BNM's records: Prior to examination, request your internal incident register and cross-reference every reportable cyber incident against your BNM notification correspondence. Any incident that triggered internal escalation but has no corresponding regulatory notification record will be a red flag. Resolve discrepancies proactively and document the rationale for any incident classified below the reporting threshold.

Demonstrate closed-loop processing of external threat advisories: For a sample of the last 10–15 advisories received from BNM or CSM, prepare a one-page walkthrough showing receipt date, internal triage decision, action taken (or documented rationale for no action), and closure date. This evidence trail directly addresses examiner concerns about whether intelligence received is operationalised.

Validate that your collaboration policy references specific external bodies by name and is Board-endorsed: A generic information security policy referencing 'relevant authorities' without naming them will be treated as insufficient. Ensure your escalation and collaboration procedures explicitly name BNM, NACSA, MyCERT, FS-ISAC, and where applicable PDRM, and that the document carries a Board Risk Committee approval signature dated within the last 12 months.

Prepare attendance and contribution evidence for industry forums at multiple organisational levels: Compile attendance certificates or meeting minutes for at least four cybersecurity industry forum engagements in the past year, and ensure these span both technical (IOC sharing, peer SOC collaboration) and governance (CISO or senior management forums) levels. Evidence that only one employee engages with external stakeholders will raise concerns about the institution's collaboration depth.

Pre-position your metrics narrative: Examiners will ask how management knows collaboration is effective. Prepare a two-cycle trend comparison of your collaboration KPIs (advisories actioned, response times, IOCs shared) covering at least two reporting periods. If metrics show improvement, highlight the management actions that drove it. If metrics show decline, have a documented remediation plan ready — proactive transparency is viewed more favourably than gaps discovered during examination.

12.1.title: Digital Services Security Controls
12.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has formally extended its Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) to explicitly cover digital service delivery channels — they look for documented evidence that the CRF is not a generic policy but one tailored to each channel (mobile banking, internet banking, API-based services, kiosks). A primary red flag is the absence of channel-specific minimum security control assessments linked to the controls listed in Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11 of RMiT, particularly where assessments are conducted only at onboarding and not on a regular, risk-calibrated schedule. Examiners scrutinise authentication and transaction authorisation mechanisms with particular care, looking for strong multi-factor authentication (MFA) evidence, session management controls, and anomaly detection that collectively reduce impersonation and fraud risk. Physical and logical access control deficiencies — such as unreviewed privileged access, missing segregation of duties, or inadequate data centre physical controls — are consistently cited findings. Examiners cross-reference findings from Appendix 3 minimum controls against actual system configurations, penetration test results, and vulnerability scan outputs to validate that documented controls are operationally effective rather than merely policy-level commitments.

evidenceItems.name: CRF Extension Document for Digital Services
evidenceItems.description: The formal policy or framework document that extends the institution's Cyber Risk Framework to cover digital service delivery channels. This demonstrates that management has explicitly scoped digital services into the CRF governance structure and defined accountability, control objectives, and minimum security standards per channel in alignment with Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11.
whatGoodLooksLike: The CRF extension document explicitly names each active digital delivery channel (mobile app, internet banking portal, open banking APIs, ATM/kiosk, USSD) and maps them to their respective minimum security control sets from Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11
Board or senior management approval is evidenced by dated signatures or minutes, and the document carries a version history showing periodic review (at minimum annually or upon material change)
Control objectives for confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty data are explicitly stated per channel, not as generic principles
The document cross-references the institution's overarching CRF policy, data classification framework, and incident response plan to demonstrate integration rather than standalone treatment
Roles and responsibilities for maintaining and assessing channel-specific controls are clearly assigned to named functions (e.g., CISO office, Digital Banking Technology, Operational Risk)
commonGaps: The CRF policy covers cybersecurity in general terms but contains no channel-specific annexures or addenda, meaning digital services are not demonstrably in scope
Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11 are referenced by name but the institution has not mapped its actual controls to the specific line items listed in those appendices, leaving traceability gaps
The document was approved at initial digital launch but has not been reviewed following material changes such as new channel additions, API integrations, or cloud migrations
Ownership of the CRF extension is unclear — cybersecurity, technology, and digital business units each maintain separate artefacts with no single authoritative document
The document exists as a draft without formal approval evidence, or approval is evidenced only by email rather than a governance committee minute
suggestedSources: CISO or Information Security department SharePoint/Confluence repository
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) under the Cyber Risk Framework module
Board Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee meeting minutes and approved policy register
Digital Banking or Technology division policy library
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Delivery Channel Minimum Security Control Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Periodic assessment records demonstrating that the institution has evaluated the minimum security controls applicable to each digital delivery channel against the requirements in Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11. These records evidence that the assessments are recurring, channel-specific, and result in documented remediation where gaps are identified.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment records exist for every active digital delivery channel and are indexed against the specific control items in Appendices 2, 3, 4, and 11, showing a pass/fail or maturity rating for each line item

Assessment frequency is risk-calibrated and documented — high-risk channels (e.g., mobile banking, real-time payment APIs) are assessed more frequently than lower-risk channels, with the rationale recorded
Each assessment is dated, names the assessor (internal audit, second-line risk, or qualified third party), and is reviewed and signed off by a responsible senior officer
Gap findings from assessments are tracked to closure in a remediation register with target dates, owners, and evidence of completion — not left as open items indefinitely

Assessment methodology references the specific control categories from the relevant Appendices (e.g., encryption standards, session timeout, input validation) rather than generic security checklist items
commonGaps: Assessments are performed once at product launch and not repeated, with no documented schedule for recurring review despite material changes to channel technology or threat landscape
A single assessment template is applied uniformly across all channels without tailoring to channel-specific Appendix requirements, resulting in incomplete coverage of applicable controls
Assessment findings identify gaps but the remediation register is not maintained or is disconnected from the assessment artefact, making it impossible to demonstrate closure
Assessments are conducted by the technology team that owns the channel, with no independent second-line or third-party validation, undermining objectivity
Confidentiality and integrity controls are assessed in isolation without evaluating end-to-end transaction flows, leaving inter-system or API integration gaps unaddressed
suggestedSources: GRC platform assessment module (ServiceNow, Archer, MetricStream) with channel-specific assessment workflows
Internal Audit management system (e.g., TeamMate, AuditBoard) for audit workpapers
Technology Risk or Operational Risk division SharePoint for second-line assessments
Qualified external assessor reports (penetration testing firms, Big 4 advisory) retained in contract management system
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC platform export; supporting evidence as PDF

evidenceItems.name: Authentication and Transaction Authorisation Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical and policy documentation demonstrating that all digital channels implement the minimum authentication and transaction authorisation controls specified in Appendix 3, including evidence of MFA deployment, session management configurations, transaction signing mechanisms, and anomaly detection capabilities that collectively mitigate impersonation and fraud risk.
whatGoodLooksLike: An authentication architecture document per channel describes the authentication factors deployed (knowledge, possession, inherence), maps them to Appendix 3 minimum requirements, and includes configuration screenshots or system export evidence

Transaction authorisation controls are documented per transaction type and risk tier — higher-value or higher-risk transactions require stronger authorisation (e.g., dynamic OTP, step-up authentication, biometric confirmation) as evidenced by system configuration records
MFA enforcement is validated by system-generated reports showing the percentage of sessions authenticated via MFA, with near-zero exceptions for non-exempted user segments
Session management parameters (timeout thresholds, concurrent session limits, re-authentication triggers) are documented in system configuration records and validated by periodic technical review

Fraud detection and anomaly monitoring systems are integrated with authentication events, with evidence of alert generation, investigation, and escalation for suspicious authentication or transaction patterns
commonGaps: MFA is deployed for initial login but transaction authorisation for high-value payments relies solely on session continuity with no step-up authentication, contrary to Appendix 3 requirements
Authentication controls documentation describes intended design but is not reconciled against actual live system configurations, meaning documented controls may not reflect operational reality
Session timeout values are set to policy-compliant thresholds on paper but system configuration exports show longer timeouts, and the discrepancy has not been identified or remediated
Exemptions from MFA requirements (e.g., for certain user segments or low-value transactions) are applied without a documented risk acceptance process or periodic review of the exemption scope

Anomaly detection rules for authentication events exist but alert thresholds have not been tuned since initial deployment, resulting in either excessive false positives that are routinely suppressed or insufficient sensitivity to detect genuine impersonation attempts
suggestedSources: Identity and Access Management (IAM) platform (e.g., ForgeRock, Okta, Azure AD B2C) configuration exports and audit logs
Fraud management system (e.g., NICE Actimize, SAS Fraud Management, Featurespace) rule and alert configuration records
Digital channel middleware or API gateway (e.g., Apigee, Kong, AWS API Gateway) authentication policy configurations
Security Architecture team Confluence/SharePoint for authentication design documents
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) for MFA exception request and approval records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX for policy and architecture documents; system configuration exports (JSON/XML/PDF); dashboard screenshots with timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Physical Access Control Records for Digital Service Infrastructure
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and records demonstrating that physical security controls are in place and effective for the infrastructure supporting digital services, including data centres, server rooms, network equipment rooms, and any third-party co-location or cloud provider facilities. This evidences compliance with the physical control dimension of clause 12.1(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: Physical access control policy defines access tiers for digital service infrastructure locations, specifies approved access methods (biometric, access card, PIN), and mandates visitor escorting and logging requirements with documented review frequency

Access logs for data centres and server rooms are maintained, regularly reviewed by a responsible officer, and anomalies (after-hours access, unescorted visitors, tailgating events) are investigated and documented

Periodic physical security reviews or audits are evidenced, including CCTV review records, access list recertification records, and environmental control inspection reports (fire suppression, flood detection, temperature monitoring)

For co-location or cloud providers supporting digital services, third-party assurance reports (ISO 27001 certificates, SOC 2 Type II reports, or BNM-approved equivalent) are obtained annually and reviewed by the institution's risk function
Decommissioning and media disposal records for hardware supporting digital channels exist, demonstrating secure disposal processes that protect customer and counterparty data confidentiality
commonGaps: Physical access lists for server rooms hosting digital service infrastructure include former employees or contractors whose access was not revoked upon departure, evidenced by discrepancies between HR termination records and access system records

Physical access reviews are performed annually but digital service infrastructure is expanding through cloud adoption, and cloud provider physical controls are not included in the institution's assurance programme
CCTV footage retention periods are shorter than the institution's incident response investigation window, meaning footage is unavailable for post-incident forensic review
Environmental monitoring alerts (temperature, humidity, power) for digital service infrastructure are not routed to an on-call team, leaving risks of undetected physical failures

Third-party data centre or co-location contracts exist but do not mandate the provision of physical security audit reports or right-to-audit clauses, leaving the institution unable to verify physical controls
suggestedSources: Physical Access Management system (e.g., Lenel, Honeywell Pro-Watch, Genetec) access logs and access list reports
Facilities Management or Corporate Real Estate division for physical security policy and inspection records
Vendor management or third-party risk platform for co-location and cloud provider assurance reports
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system for hardware lifecycle and disposal records
HR system cross-referenced with access management system for leaver reconciliation
evidenceItems.format: System-generated access reports (PDF/CSV); third-party assurance certificates (PDF); inspection checklists (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Logical Access Control Records for Digital Services
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that robust logical access controls govern who can access digital service systems, data, and infrastructure, including privileged access management, role-based access control configurations, access recertification records, and segregation of duties enforcement. This evidences the logical control dimension of clause 12.1(c) and supports confidentiality and integrity obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Privileged access to digital service systems (production databases, application servers, API gateways, security tooling) is managed through a Privileged Access Management (PAM) tool, with session recording enabled for all privileged sessions and records retained for the required period

Access recertification is conducted at minimum semi-annually for privileged accounts and annually for standard accounts, with evidence of manager certification, revocation actions taken for inappropriate access, and sign-off by the responsible system owner

Segregation of duties (SoD) controls are documented and enforced for critical digital service functions — development, testing, and production deployment access are held by different individuals with SoD conflict reports reviewed periodically
Role-based access control (RBAC) matrices for digital service applications are documented, current, and reconciled against live system access configurations at each recertification cycle

Joiners, movers, and leavers (JML) process records show that access provisioning, modification, and revocation for digital service systems follows defined timelines and is evidenced by ITSM tickets linked to HR events
commonGaps: Generic or shared privileged accounts (e.g., 'admin', 'sysadmin') are used for digital service system administration without individual accountability, making it impossible to attribute actions to specific individuals

Access recertification is performed as a bulk approval by managers without genuine review — large batches of accounts are certified in minutes with no evidence that individual access rights were evaluated against job responsibilities
Developer accounts retain production access that was granted for incident resolution and never revoked, creating persistent SoD violations that accumulate over time

Logical access controls for digital service infrastructure hosted in cloud environments are managed separately from on-premises systems, with no unified access governance view and inconsistent recertification processes
Leaver access revocation records show delays of more than 24 hours for high-privileged accounts supporting digital services, with no risk escalation process triggered for delayed revocations
suggestedSources: Privileged Access Management platform (e.g., CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) session logs and account inventory reports
Identity Governance and Administration (IGA) platform (e.g., SailPoint, Saviynt, One Identity) for recertification campaign records and access request history
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) for JML process tickets linked to HR triggers
Cloud IAM consoles (AWS IAM Access Analyzer, Azure AD Access Reviews, GCP IAM) for cloud-hosted digital service access records
HR system for leaver dates cross-referenced with access revocation timestamps in IAM/PAM platforms
evidenceItems.format: System-generated reports (PDF/CSV); recertification campaign exports; ITSM ticket exports

evidenceItems.name: Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing Reports for Digital Channels
evidenceItems.description: Reports from vulnerability assessments and penetration tests conducted against digital service delivery channels, demonstrating that the institution actively identifies and remediates technical security weaknesses that could compromise the confidentiality and integrity of customer information or expose authentication mechanisms to exploitation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Penetration test reports are channel-specific (mobile application, web portal, API layer, backend infrastructure) and conducted by qualified independent testers (CREST-certified or equivalent), with scope explicitly covering Appendix 2 and Appendix 3 control areas such as authentication bypass, session hijacking, injection vulnerabilities, and cryptographic weaknesses

Remediation tracking records show all critical and high findings from penetration tests are assigned owners, remediated within defined SLAs (e.g., critical within 30 days), and verified through retest before closure

Vulnerability scan results from automated tools (DAST, SAST, SCA, infrastructure scanning) are produced on a regular schedule, reviewed by the security team, and reconciled against the penetration test findings to demonstrate continuous coverage between point-in-time tests
Mobile application security assessments include both static and dynamic analysis, cover platform-specific risks (iOS and Android), and reference OWASP Mobile Top 10 mapped to Appendix 2 requirements

Management sign-off on residual risk is evidenced for findings that cannot be immediately remediated, with compensating controls documented and risk acceptance approved at an appropriate authority level
commonGaps: Penetration tests are conducted annually on the web portal but the mobile application and open banking APIs have never been independently tested, leaving significant attack surfaces unassessed

Critical findings from penetration test reports are marked as remediated in the tracking register but no retest evidence exists to confirm effective remediation, meaning vulnerabilities may persist in production

Penetration test scope is limited to the application layer and explicitly excludes network infrastructure and backend systems supporting digital services, creating gaps in coverage relative to Appendix 2 requirements
Vulnerability scan results are produced by the technology team but are not reviewed by the security or risk function, and findings are not escalated or tracked through a formal remediation process

The institution relies solely on the vendor's own security attestations for third-party digital components (e.g., payment SDKs, authentication libraries) without independent verification of the security controls those components implement
suggestedSources: CISO or Information Security team secure document repository for penetration test reports (classified as confidential)
GRC or vulnerability management platform (e.g., Tenable.io, Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM) for scan results and remediation tracking
ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) for vulnerability remediation tickets linked to penetration test findings
Vendor management system for third-party security assurance documentation
DevSecOps pipeline tooling (e.g., Veracode, Checkmarx, Snyk) for SAST/SCA results on digital service codebases
evidenceItems.format: PDF (penetration test reports); system export CSV/PDF (vulnerability scan results); ITSM export (remediation tracking)

auditTips: Map every active digital delivery channel to the specific Appendix (2, 3, 4, or 11) that governs it before the examination — create a single-page channel-to-appendix matrix and use it as the backbone for organising your evidence pack, so examiners can instantly see coverage without needing to reconstruct the mapping themselves.

Reconcile documented controls against live system configurations for all authentication and authorisation mechanisms referenced in Appendix 3 at least 60 days before examination — specifically extract MFA enforcement rates, session timeout settings, and transaction step-up authentication rules from production systems and compare them line-by-line against the controls stated in your security architecture documents to identify and close any configuration drift.

Prepare a remediation closure matrix that links every open finding from your most recent channel security assessments and penetration tests to a status, owner, target date, and closure evidence reference — BNM examiners will specifically probe whether identified gaps from prior assessments have been actioned, and an incomplete or undated remediation register is a significant red flag.

Conduct a dry-run access reconciliation exercise covering both privileged logical access and physical access for digital service infrastructure — pull live access lists from IAM/PAM and physical access management systems, compare against current HR records, and revoke or document any exceptions before the examination window, since access anomalies (leavers with active accounts, undocumented shared credentials) are among the most commonly cited findings under clause 12.1(c).

Obtain and file current third-party assurance reports (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, or BNM-accepted equivalent) for all external parties hosting or processing data for digital services — including cloud providers, co-location facilities, payment processors, and technology vendors — and prepare a summary that maps each provider to the digital channel it supports, so examiners can verify that third-party physical and logical controls are within scope of the institution's assurance programme.

Cross-reference the authentication and fraud controls in your Appendix 3 compliance evidence against actual fraud incident data from the past 12 months — if there are patterns of impersonation or account takeover incidents, be prepared to demonstrate that root cause analysis was conducted, controls were updated in response, and the CRF was revised to reflect lessons learned, as examiners assess whether controls are adaptive rather than static.

Ensure that the CRF extension document for digital services explicitly cites each sub-clause of 12.1 (a), (b), and (c) and is cross-referenced to living operational artefacts (assessment schedules, authentication standards, access control procedures) rather than referencing those artefacts in abstract — examiners look for a demonstrable governance chain from board-approved policy down to operational control records, and any break in that chain will prompt deeper inquiry.

12.2.title: Alternative Controls and Liability
12.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a formal, documented process for tracking deviations from the prescribed controls under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9 and whether each deviation is accompanied by a rigorous equivalence justification — not merely a narrative explanation, but one supported by risk quantification, compensating control mapping, and independent validation. Examiners are particularly alert to blanket exceptions granted without time-bound remediation plans, as this signals a systemic culture of non-compliance rather than a managed transition. A key red flag is the absence of board or senior management acknowledgment of assumed fraud liability, which the clause explicitly imposes on institutions that operate with control gaps. Examiners will also cross-reference identified gaps against actual fraud incident logs to determine whether the institution has experienced losses attributable to the missing controls, and whether those losses were correctly attributed and reported. Institutions that cannot demonstrate continuous monitoring of their alternative measures or show that alternatives have been independently tested will draw heightened scrutiny.

evidenceItems.name: Control Gap Register with Equivalence Justifications
evidenceItems.description: A formal register that catalogues every instance where a prescribed control under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9 has not been fully implemented, documenting the nature of the gap, the alternative or compensating measure deployed, and a structured equivalence assessment demonstrating that the alternative achieves at minimum an equivalent risk reduction outcome.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each gap entry maps explicitly to the specific RMiT paragraph (e.g., 12.3, 12.5) and control requirement it deviates from, with no ambiguity about scope

Equivalence justifications include quantitative or qualitative risk analysis comparing residual risk under the alternative control versus the prescribed control, referencing the institution's own risk appetite statement
Each entry carries a time-bound remediation target, an accountable owner at minimum at Head of Department level, and a milestone tracking column updated no less than quarterly
Compensating controls are cross-referenced to evidence of actual implementation (e.g., ITSM change records, policy version numbers, system configuration screenshots)
The register has been reviewed and formally signed off by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and noted by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent within the last 12 months
commonGaps: The register exists but maps gaps only to high-level policy sections rather than the specific sub-clauses of 12.1 to 12.9, making equivalence assessment unverifiable
Equivalence justifications are narrative-only with no supporting risk quantification, threat modelling output, or reference to a recognised control framework such as NIST CSF or ISO 27002
Remediation timelines are absent or have lapsed without documented extensions approved by senior management, indicating passive acceptance of the gap rather than active management

The register has not been updated following significant changes to the digital services environment (e.g., new product launches, infrastructure migrations), creating a stale and unrepresentative record
Sign-off authority is limited to IT operations staff with no evidence of CISO or Board-level visibility, failing to demonstrate adequate governance over material control gaps
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — control exception or deviation module
Information Security team — maintained as a standalone ITSM-linked spreadsheet or register
Internal Audit department — may hold a parallel version produced during the last technology audit cycle
Compliance department — RMiT compliance tracking workbook updated for each BNM examination cycle
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal register with version control) or Spreadsheet with access-controlled edit history; GRC platform export acceptable if it includes full audit trail

evidenceItems.name: Alternative Control Effectiveness Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation evidencing that each alternative or compensating control recorded in the gap register has been independently tested or validated to confirm it operates effectively and achieves the risk reduction claimed in the equivalence justification. This includes test plans, results, and sign-off by a party independent of the control owner.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plans are pre-approved and define measurable success criteria explicitly tied to the risk outcome the alternative control is intended to achieve (e.g., fraud detection rate, transaction anomaly capture rate)

Testing has been performed by a party independent of the team responsible for implementing the alternative control, such as Internal Audit, a second-line risk function, or an accredited external assessor
Test results include both passed and failed scenarios with documented remediation actions where the alternative control underperformed against success criteria
Testing cadence is risk-proportionate — higher-severity gaps (e.g., absence of strong customer authentication) are tested at least semi-annually, with evidence of each cycle retained
Results are formally reported to senior management with a clear conclusion on whether the alternative control remains adequate or requires escalation
commonGaps: Testing is performed by the same team that owns and operates the alternative control, creating a conflict of interest that invalidates independence of the effectiveness assessment

Test scope is limited to functional testing (e.g., does the control activate?) rather than adversarial or scenario-based testing that reflects the actual fraud threats the missing prescribed control was designed to address
Test records exist for initial implementation but there is no evidence of periodic re-testing, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate continued effectiveness over time
Results documentation does not link back to the specific RMiT control gap, making it impossible for an examiner to match a test outcome to a declared exception in the gap register
Failures identified during testing have not triggered escalation or remediation within the institution's defined response timeline, indicating the testing process is not operationally integrated
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — technology audit working papers and control testing schedules
Information Security team — vulnerability assessment and penetration testing reports commissioned for digital banking channels
Third-party assessors — independent control validation reports, red team exercise reports
GRC platform — control testing module with linked evidence attachments and sign-off workflows
evidenceItems.format: PDF (test plans and results reports with independent sign-off); supporting evidence may include screenshots, system logs, or spreadsheet annexes

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Liability Acknowledgement
evidenceItems.description: Formal board or senior management committee resolution or paper explicitly acknowledging that, for each identified control gap under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, the institution assumes full liability for any fraud losses attributable to that gap, as required by clause 12.2(b). This demonstrates that the liability assumption is a deliberate and governed decision rather than an inadvertent oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: A board paper or Board Risk Committee minute explicitly references clause 12.2 of RMiT and names each active control gap for which liability is being assumed, rather than making a generic acknowledgement

The resolution records that the board has reviewed the equivalence justifications and is satisfied that alternative controls are in place, distinguishing informed assumption of residual liability from unmanaged exposure

Where gaps are material in terms of fraud risk exposure, the paper includes an estimated financial impact assessment or a reference to the institution's fraud loss provisioning under the assumed liability
The document records the date of acknowledgement and is refreshed at least annually or whenever a new gap is added to or removed from the control gap register
The paper is signed by the Board Chairman or Committee Chair and countersigned by the CEO or CRO, reflecting dual accountability at the highest governance level
commonGaps: No board-level paper exists — liability assumption under clause 12.2(b) has never been explicitly presented to or acknowledged by the board, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate governed acceptance of the obligation
The acknowledgement is generic, referencing only 'technology risks' without identifying specific RMiT control gaps, making it non-responsive to the clause's specific liability trigger
The paper was produced only once (e.g., at initial RMiT implementation) and has not been refreshed as the control gap register evolved, meaning newer gaps carry no board acknowledgement
Sign-off is limited to C-suite executives without board committee ratification, which may be insufficient given the regulatory nature of the liability assumption

The paper does not distinguish between gaps subject to active remediation (and therefore temporary) and gaps where the institution has made a deliberate long-term decision to rely on alternatives, conflating managed risk with unaddressed deficiency
suggestedSources: Company Secretary — Board Risk Committee or Board Technology Committee minutes and papers
Risk Management department — board risk appetite papers that may incorporate technology control gap summaries
Compliance department — RMiT board reporting pack prepared for each examination cycle or annual compliance attestation
CEO or CRO office — executive sign-off records linked to the control gap register approval workflow
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board paper and signed minutes extract); must include date, attendees, resolutions, and signatures to be valid as regulatory evidence

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Incident Attribution and Loss Tracking Log
evidenceItems.description: A structured record of all fraud incidents that occurred during periods when a prescribed RMiT control under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9 was absent, including an assessment of whether the gap contributed to or enabled the fraud. This demonstrates the institution's transparency in attributing losses consistent with its assumed liability under clause 12.2(b) and supports honest self-assessment of alternative control adequacy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each fraud incident record includes a field capturing whether any active RMiT control gap was present at the time of the incident, with a root-cause analysis conclusion on causal or contributory relationship
Where a gap is assessed as causally linked to a fraud incident, the loss amount is separately flagged and reported to senior management as a materialisation of assumed clause 12.2(b) liability

The log is reconciled at least quarterly against the control gap register to ensure completeness — any gap that has existed for more than one reporting period is actively checked against fraud incident data
Root-cause analyses are performed by a function independent of the fraud operations team (e.g., Internal Audit, second-line risk) and conclusions are documented with supporting evidence
The log feeds into the institution's regulatory fraud reporting obligations and shows consistent treatment between internal records and externally reported figures, demonstrating integrity of data
commonGaps: Fraud incident records exist but contain no field or annotation linking incidents to RMiT control gaps, making it impossible to demonstrate compliance with the liability attribution requirement of clause 12.2(b)

Root-cause analysis is performed only for high-value fraud cases, leaving a population of lower-value incidents uninvestigated that may collectively indicate systematic failures attributable to a control gap
The fraud log and the control gap register are maintained by separate departments with no cross-referencing process, creating an organisational blind spot where gap-linked losses go unidentified

Attribution assessments default to 'customer negligence' or 'social engineering' as root causes without adequately considering whether a prescribed control (e.g., real-time transaction monitoring, step-up authentication) would have prevented the loss

Loss figures in the fraud log do not reconcile with figures reported to BNM or disclosed in annual reports, raising questions about the completeness and accuracy of the institution's fraud data management
suggestedSources: Fraud Operations or Financial Crime Compliance department — fraud case management system (e.g., NICE Actimize, FIS FCRM, or in-house case tracking system)
Internal Audit — fraud investigation reports and root-cause analysis working papers
Risk Management — operational risk loss event database (often maintained in GRC platform or spreadsheet under Basel operational risk framework)
Finance department — fraud loss provisioning records and write-off approvals
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system export from fraud case management platform; PDF root-cause analysis reports as supporting annexes; must include dates, amounts, and attribution conclusions

evidenceItems.name: Alternative Control Implementation and Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Concrete artefacts demonstrating that the alternative controls declared in the gap register are actually implemented and operational within the institution's digital services environment, rather than existing only on paper. This closes the loop between documented intent and operational reality.
whatGoodLooksLike: System configuration screenshots or exports from the relevant digital banking platform, fraud monitoring system, or security tool that confirm the alternative control settings are active and correctly configured as described in the equivalence justification

Change management records (e.g., ITSM change tickets, deployment records) that establish the date the alternative control was implemented, providing a clear timeline relative to the onset of the RMiT control gap

Operational metrics or monitoring dashboards showing the alternative control is generating outputs (e.g., alert volumes, intervention rates, detection counts) consistent with active operation, not a dormant or misconfigured state
Policy or procedure documents governing the alternative control are version-controlled, approved, and accessible to all relevant staff, with training completion records confirming staff awareness

Regular review records (e.g., monthly operational reviews, quarterly risk reviews) that include the alternative control's performance metrics, demonstrating it is actively monitored and not simply deployed and forgotten
commonGaps: The gap register describes an alternative control (e.g., 'enhanced manual review process') but no procedure document, staffing record, or operational metric exists to confirm the control is actually functioning
System configuration evidence is outdated — screenshots or exports are from the original implementation date with no subsequent verification that configurations have not drifted or been overridden

The alternative control was implemented as a temporary measure but has been in place for an extended period without formal confirmation that it continues to be fit for purpose as the threat landscape and digital services environment evolve

Implementation evidence exists for the primary production environment but not for disaster recovery, failover, or secondary processing environments, leaving gaps in coverage that may be exploited during incidents

Operational metrics are collected but not reviewed against defined thresholds — anomalies (e.g., sudden drop in alert generation) are not triggering investigation, indicating monitoring is performative rather than substantive
suggestedSources: IT Operations or Digital Banking Technology team — system administration consoles, configuration management database (CMDB), and deployment records
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — change records and implementation tickets for alternative control deployments
Security Operations Centre — SIEM dashboards, alert management platform, and detection rule configuration exports
Digital Channels or Product team — fraud prevention platform configuration (e.g., Featurespace, ThreatMetrix, RSA Fraud and Risk Intelligence)
evidenceItems.format: Screenshots (timestamped), PDF system configuration reports, ITSM change ticket exports; operational metrics may be provided as dashboard PDF exports or spreadsheet summaries

evidenceItems.name: Remediation Roadmap with Progress Tracking
evidenceItems.description: A structured roadmap showing the institution's plan to close each control gap and achieve full compliance with the prescribed controls under paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, with documented progress updates demonstrating active pursuit of remediation rather than indefinite reliance on alternative controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: The roadmap breaks each gap closure into discrete, trackable milestones (e.g., procurement approval, vendor selection, UAT, production deployment) with target dates and accountable owners for each milestone
Progress is formally reviewed and updated at least quarterly by a governance body such as the Technology Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee, with meeting minutes evidencing the review

Where milestones have been missed, the roadmap includes documented root causes for the delay, a revised timeline approved by an appropriate authority, and any additional interim controls implemented to manage the extended gap period

The roadmap distinguishes between gaps where full remediation is planned (with a committed completion date) and gaps where the institution has made a formal long-term decision to rely on alternative controls, with the latter requiring stronger board-level justification
Completion of each roadmap milestone is evidenced by a linked artefact (e.g., change record, test completion certificate, policy approval) rather than a self-attested status update
commonGaps: The roadmap is a high-level document with completion dates at the project level rather than milestone level, making it impossible to assess actual progress or identify slippage at an early stage
Repeated timeline slippages have occurred without documented justification or revised approvals, indicating the roadmap is aspirational rather than operationally managed
The roadmap does not address all gaps in the control gap register — some gaps are tracked in the register but absent from the roadmap, implying no active remediation plan exists for those items

Progress updates are self-reported by the gap owner without independent validation, and the governance body reviewing the roadmap does not challenge or verify the reported status against underlying evidence

The roadmap has not been shared with or acknowledged by the board, leaving senior governance without visibility of the institution's trajectory toward full RMiT compliance on digital services security
suggestedSources: IT Programme Management Office (PMO) — project tracking tools (e.g., Jira, MS Project, Smartsheet) with milestone and dependency views
Compliance or Risk Management department — RMiT compliance programme tracker maintained for BNM engagement purposes
Technology Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee — meeting packs and minutes where roadmap progress is a standing agenda item
GRC platform — remediation workflow module linked to the control gap register, with automated escalation on missed milestones
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal roadmap document with version history) or project management tool export; meeting minutes confirming governance review should be attached as supporting evidence

auditTips: Before the examination, map every item in your control gap register directly to the specific RMiT sub-paragraph it deviates from (e.g., 12.3(a), 12.5(b)) — examiners will test whether you know exactly which obligations are unmet, and a vague reference to 'paragraph 12' will signal inadequate governance of your own compliance position.

Prepare a pre-examination summary document that presents the gap register, equivalence justifications, board liability acknowledgement, and remediation roadmap as a coherent package — examiners respond well to institutions that can walk them through the narrative of 'gap identified, alternative implemented, effectiveness validated, remediation planned' without needing to retrieve documents ad hoc.

Conduct a reconciliation exercise prior to examination: pull all fraud incidents from the past two years and cross-reference each against the period your gap register shows each control was absent — any unattributed losses during gap periods are a significant examination risk and should be proactively assessed, documented, and if necessary escalated to the board before the examiner identifies them.

Validate that your board liability acknowledgement papers are current — if your last board sign-off on RMiT control gaps predates any new gap added to the register, or is older than 12 months, refresh the paper before examination as an outdated acknowledgement will suggest the board is not actively governing the clause 12.2(b) liability it has assumed.

Request that Internal Audit perform a pre-examination walkthrough of your alternative control implementation evidence, specifically testing whether declared alternative controls are verifiably operational (not just documented as operational) — if Internal Audit cannot confirm operationality from available evidence, the examiner will reach the same conclusion and draw an adverse finding.

Ensure your equivalence justifications cite a recognised control framework (ISO 27002, NIST CSF, PCI DSS, or similar) to provide an objective benchmark against which the alternative control's effectiveness can be assessed — justifications that rely solely on internal expert opinion without external reference points are routinely challenged by BNM examiners as insufficiently rigorous.

Prepare a clear ownership matrix showing which executive is accountable for each control gap, its alternative control, its remediation milestone, and its fraud liability exposure — examiners frequently ask 'who is accountable for this gap?' during examination interviews, and an institution that cannot give a clear, consistent answer across interviewees will appear to lack the governance discipline expected of a regulated entity under RMiT.

12.3.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.3.title: CRF Enhancement for Digital Fraud
12.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will assess whether the institution has substantively enhanced its Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) — not merely updated a policy document — to address digital fraud targeting customers' mobile devices and access points. Examiners will trace the CRF's threat identification scope to confirm mobile endpoints and customer-facing access points are explicitly covered, then verify that layered controls (application hardening, runtime protection, data encryption) are deployed and tested. A primary red flag is a mismatch between the documented CRF and actual technical controls: examiners will request evidence of continuous mobile application surveillance, penetration test results, and patch/upgrade timelines to validate that monitoring is genuine and remediation is swift. Cross-functional incident response arrangements will be scrutinised for operational readiness — not merely the existence of a procedure — with examiners looking for tabletop exercise records, clear RACI matrices, and evidence that multiple business functions (IT, fraud operations, customer contact, legal) coordinate in practice. Governance trail integrity is critical: examiners will verify an unbroken escalation chain from operational detection through senior management review to formal board appraisal, with board papers demonstrating substantive engagement rather than passive receipt.

evidenceItems.name: Enhanced CRF Document with Mobile Threat Scope
evidenceItems.description: The current, board-approved version of the institution's Cyber Resilience Framework explicitly incorporating customers' mobile devices and access points within the threat identification and countermeasures scope, demonstrating alignment with paragraphs 11.2 and 11.3 of RMiT and clause 12.3(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: CRF document contains a dedicated section or annex explicitly listing customers' mobile devices (iOS and Android), third-party access points, and mobile banking channels as in-scope threat surfaces with mapped threat actors and attack vectors
Version history and change log shows a deliberate enhancement cycle post-RMiT issuance, with named reviewers, approval dates, and a summary of what was added to address digital fraud requirements

Threat taxonomy within the CRF references mobile-specific threats such as malicious SDK injection, overlay attacks, SIM swapping, fake base stations, and rogue Wi-Fi access points with corresponding countermeasures for each

CRF cross-references paragraphs 11.2 and 11.3 explicitly, demonstrating the institution understands these enhancements are extensions of existing cybersecurity obligations rather than standalone requirements
Board or Board Risk Committee approval signature page is present with approval date, and the document reflects a review cycle of no longer than 12 months or after significant fraud events
commonGaps: CRF scope statement references 'digital channels' or 'internet banking' generically without explicitly naming customers' mobile devices as a distinct threat surface, leaving an ambiguity that examiners will flag

Countermeasures section lists controls applicable to institution-owned infrastructure but fails to address threats originating from or targeting the customer's device environment (e.g., compromised routers, malicious apps on the same device)
CRF was last approved before the institution launched its mobile banking application, meaning the document predates the risk it is supposed to govern and has not been retroactively updated

Threat identification methodology relies solely on internal threat modelling without referencing external mobile threat intelligence feeds (e.g., FS-ISAC, BNM advisories, MITRE ATT&CK Mobile), limiting coverage of emerging attack techniques

The CRF exists as a high-level policy document with no linkage to technical implementation standards or operational runbooks, making it impossible to verify that documented countermeasures are actually deployed
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or RSA Archer) — Cyber Resilience Framework module under Policy Management
Information Security department SharePoint or Confluence repository — Governance and Frameworks section
Board Risk Committee secretariat — minutes and approved policy registers
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) office document management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with tracked version history and approval signatures; supplementary spreadsheet mapping threats to countermeasures

evidenceItems.name: Mobile Application Defense-in-Depth Security Controls Register
evidenceItems.description: A technical controls register documenting the layered security controls applied to the institution's customer-facing digital service applications deployed on mobile devices, covering application hardening, runtime protection, data protection, and network security layers, demonstrating compliance with clause 12.3(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Controls register is organised by security layer (application, data, network, device) and for each layer identifies the specific control, implementation standard (e.g., OWASP MASVS level), responsible owner, and evidence of implementation such as configuration screenshots or audit trail

Application-layer controls include code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, anti-tampering mechanisms, and screenshot prevention for sensitive screens, with each control verified through a recent penetration test finding confirming effectiveness

Data protection controls explicitly address banking data at rest (AES-256 encrypted local storage, no sensitive data in logs or clipboard) and in transit (TLS 1.2/1.3 enforcement, certificate pinning), with a data flow diagram showing all data paths from device to backend

Controls register references an accepted mobile application security standard (OWASP MASVS, NIST SP 800-163, or equivalent) as the baseline, with a gap assessment showing the institution's current posture against each requirement
Controls have documented compensating measures or accepted risk decisions for any gaps, each signed by an appropriate risk owner and reviewed within the last 12 months
commonGaps: Controls register exists for server-side infrastructure but the mobile application client itself has not been subject to a structured defense-in-depth assessment, leaving customer-side banking data unaddressed

Certificate pinning is documented as a control but penetration test results reveal it was bypassed during testing, and the finding has not been remediated or formally risk-accepted, indicating the register overstates actual security posture

Data classification has not been applied to data stored locally on the mobile device, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate which banking data fields require encryption at rest and whether controls are proportionate to sensitivity

Controls are documented at policy level but lack corresponding technical implementation evidence (configuration extracts, vendor attestations, SAST/DAST scan results), making it impossible to verify deployment in production

The controls register has not been updated following the last major application version release, meaning new features (e.g., biometric authentication, QR payments) are not covered and their security controls are undocumented
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity or Application Security team — Mobile Application Security Assessment reports and controls tracker
DevSecOps pipeline outputs — SAST/DAST scan results, dependency vulnerability reports (e.g., from SonarQube, Checkmarx, or Veracode)
Mobile application vendor or in-house development team — technical security design documentation
GRC platform — Technology Risk Register filtered for mobile banking application assets
Third-party penetration testing firm — latest mobile application penetration test report
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (controls register) with linked PDF penetration test report and technical architecture diagram (VISIO/PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Mobile Application Surveillance and Vulnerability Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Operational records demonstrating continuous surveillance and monitoring of the customer-facing digital service application deployed on mobile devices, including detection of exploitation attempts, vulnerability disclosure and patching timelines, and evidence of swift security control upgrades, demonstrating compliance with clause 12.3(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: SIEM or mobile threat intelligence platform dashboard exports show continuous monitoring coverage for the mobile application, with alert rules specifically targeting mobile fraud indicators such as emulator usage, automated script detection, unusual geolocation patterns, and known malicious IP connections

Vulnerability management records show a documented SLA for mobile application patches (e.g., critical: 24 hours, high: 72 hours, medium: 14 days) and a compliance report demonstrating the institution has met or formally risk-accepted deviations from those SLAs for every vulnerability identified in the past 12 months

App store monitoring evidence (screenshots or system exports) shows the institution actively monitors for rogue or cloned versions of its mobile application on official and unofficial marketplaces, with takedown request records where applicable

Monthly or quarterly threat intelligence reports specific to mobile banking threats consumed by the security operations team, with evidence that actionable intelligence has been operationalised into detection rules or control enhancements

Post-incident or post-advisory upgrade records show a traceable end-to-end workflow: threat or vulnerability identified → risk assessed → remediation developed and tested → production deployment → monitoring rule updated, with timestamps demonstrating 'swift' response as required by the clause
commonGaps: Institution has SIEM coverage for core banking infrastructure but mobile application activity (client-side telemetry, API abuse patterns) is not ingested, meaning the 'continuous surveillance' obligation for mobile devices is not met

Vulnerability management records show patching of backend systems but mobile application releases follow a commercial App Store release cycle with no defined SLA for security patches, resulting in customers running vulnerable versions for weeks or months
No proactive monitoring for fraudulent or cloned mobile applications on third-party app stores, meaning the institution is unaware of impersonation attacks until customers report them

Threat intelligence consumption is limited to generic cybersecurity feeds and does not include mobile-specific intelligence sources, meaning the institution lacks awareness of mobile malware families, overlay attack tools, or accessibility service abuse techniques targeting its application

Alert thresholds for mobile application anomalies have not been tuned since initial deployment, generating excessive false positives that the SOC team has learned to ignore, undermining the effectiveness of continuous surveillance
suggestedSources: Security Operations Centre (SOC) — SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel) alert logs and mobile application monitoring dashboards
Vulnerability management platform (e.g., Qualys, Tenable, Rapid7) — mobile application scan results and remediation tracking
Mobile Application Security Testing (MAST) or Runtime Application Self-Protection (RASP) platform — exploitation attempt logs
Threat Intelligence platform or vendor reports — mobile threat landscape briefings
IT Service Management (ITSM) platform (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM) — change records for mobile application security patch deployments
evidenceItems.format: System exports (PDF/CSV dashboards), ITSM change records (PDF), and threat intelligence reports (PDF); monthly vulnerability compliance report (Spreadsheet)

evidenceItems.name: Digital Services Incident Handling Procedures and Customer Assistance Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented and tested incident handling procedures specifically addressing cybersecurity breaches involving digital services, with clearly defined steps to assist affected customers in containing potential damage, demonstrating compliance with clause 12.3(d).
whatGoodLooksLike: Incident response playbook contains a dedicated digital fraud or mobile banking breach scenario with step-by-step procedures covering detection, triage, customer notification, account containment, evidence preservation, and regulatory reporting obligations under BNM's incident reporting requirements

Customer assistance procedures are specific and actionable: defined scripts for contact centre agents, criteria for immediate account freeze or transaction reversal, escalation paths for high-value fraud victims, and a dedicated fraud hotline with documented availability and response time SLAs

Procedures define clear roles and responsibilities (RACI matrix) across IT security, fraud operations, customer service, legal, compliance, and communications teams, with named alternates for each role to ensure coverage during incidents

Tabletop exercise or simulation records from the past 12 months demonstrate the procedures were tested against a realistic digital fraud scenario (e.g., mass overlay attack on mobile banking app), with a post-exercise gap analysis and tracked remediation actions

Procedures include customer-facing communication templates (SMS, email, in-app notification) pre-approved by compliance and legal, with defined trigger thresholds and timelines for customer notification that meet or exceed regulatory expectations
commonGaps: Incident response procedures are generic cybersecurity IR documents that reference server and network incidents; there is no dedicated scenario or annex addressing digital service breaches affecting customers' mobile devices and the specific customer assistance obligations these trigger

Customer assistance steps are high-level (e.g., 'notify affected customers') without operational detail on how to identify impacted customer accounts, what specific remediation to offer (refund, account reinstatement), or how to prioritise response based on victim impact

RACI matrix exists on paper but the contact directory is outdated, and key roles (e.g., fraud operations manager, media relations) have not been briefed on their responsibilities under the procedure, meaning the institution would be unable to execute a coordinated response under pressure

Procedures have never been tested through a tabletop exercise or simulation; the institution's only 'test' is reference to a real incident that occurred, but no formal lessons-learned document or procedure update followed that incident

Customer notification templates do not exist or have not been pre-approved, meaning during an actual incident the institution would spend critical hours obtaining legal and compliance clearance before being able to warn affected customers
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cyber Resilience team — Incident Response Playbook repository (SharePoint or GRC platform)
Fraud Operations department — fraud case management system and customer assistance standard operating procedures
Customer Experience or Contact Centre operations — escalation scripts and fraud hotline operating procedures
Business Continuity Management team — crisis communication templates and stakeholder notification registers
GRC platform — incident simulation and tabletop exercise records with action tracking
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (procedures and playbooks); PDF (tabletop exercise report); Spreadsheet (RACI matrix and open action tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Cross-Functional Digital Fraud Response Operational Arrangement
evidenceItems.description: Formalised documentation of the operational arrangement enabling swift, coordinated response across multiple business functions to counter advanced digital fraud tactics, demonstrating compliance with clause 12.3(e). Relevant where the institution relies on multiple internal or external functions to deliver its digital services.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formalised Digital Fraud Response Charter or Terms of Reference establishes a standing cross-functional team (or activation protocol for a virtual team) comprising representatives from IT Security, Fraud Operations, Digital Banking, Compliance, Legal, Customer Experience, and Communications, with defined activation criteria tied to fraud severity thresholds

Operational arrangement documentation specifies decision-making authority at each escalation tier, including pre-authorised actions that can be taken without waiting for executive approval (e.g., disabling a specific app feature, blocking transaction types) to enable rapid countermeasure deployment

Service Level Agreements or Memoranda of Understanding with third-party service providers (mobile application vendors, cloud hosting providers, CDN providers, fraud analytics vendors) include security incident response obligations, escalation contacts, and maximum response times

Records show that the cross-functional arrangement has been activated or rehearsed within the past 12 months, with documented outcomes demonstrating improved coordination speed or countermeasure effectiveness compared to prior incidents

The arrangement includes a defined process for rapid countermeasure upgrades — from identification of an advanced fraud tactic through threat assessment, countermeasure selection, emergency change approval, deployment, and post-deployment validation — with target timelines documented and measured
commonGaps: No formalised cross-functional arrangement exists; instead, coordination occurs informally during incidents, leading to delays, duplicated effort, and unclear accountability when multiple business units must respond simultaneously

The arrangement covers internal teams but does not address third-party service providers whose platforms are integral to the digital service; during an advanced fraud event the institution has no contractual mechanism to compel rapid response from vendors

Pre-authorised emergency actions are not defined, meaning every countermeasure during an active fraud event requires real-time executive approval, slowing response to advanced tactics that evolve within hours

The operational arrangement was drafted once and has not been updated to reflect organisational changes (team restructuring, outsourcing of functions, new technology vendors), rendering the documented contacts and escalation paths obsolete

The arrangement's effectiveness has never been measured; there are no metrics for coordination response time, countermeasure deployment speed, or victim impact reduction that would allow senior management to assess whether the arrangement is achieving its purpose
suggestedSources: Digital Banking or Technology Risk management team — Digital Fraud Response Charter and cross-functional meeting records
Procurement or Vendor Management team — third-party contracts and SLAs with security incident response provisions
ITSM platform — emergency change request records for fraud-related countermeasure deployments
GRC platform — operational risk and business continuity documentation for digital services
Fraud Operations — incident coordination logs and after-action review records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (Charter, Terms of Reference, SLAs); PDF (after-action review reports); Spreadsheet (vendor contact and escalation matrix)

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Review and Board Reporting Records on Digital Fraud Management
evidenceItems.description: Records of regular senior management reviews assessing the effectiveness of digital fraud management — including defined escalation thresholds accounting for victim impact and the emerging fraud environment — and formal board appraisals of review outcomes, demonstrating compliance with clauses 12.3(f) and 12.3(g).
whatGoodLooksLike: Senior management review minutes or management committee papers show structured, periodic reviews (at minimum quarterly) of digital fraud management effectiveness, covering fraud loss trends, customer impact metrics, control performance indicators, open vulnerabilities, and emerging fraud typologies — not merely incident counts

A documented escalation threshold framework exists, defining quantitative and qualitative criteria (e.g., number of victims, financial loss value, fraud typology novelty, media exposure risk) that trigger escalated countermeasures or emergency board notification, reviewed and approved by senior management

Board or Board Risk Committee papers include a dedicated digital fraud management agenda item at least annually (or after significant events), presenting management review outcomes, trend analysis, strategic countermeasure decisions, and management's assessment of residual risk to public confidence and institutional reputation

Board papers demonstrate active board engagement: minutes record board questions, directives given to management, and follow-up items tracked to closure — not passive receipt of information, but evidence of substantive board oversight

Management review records show that escalation thresholds have been calibrated to actual victim impact data (average customer financial loss, demographic vulnerability of affected customers) and updated in response to the emerging fraud environment (new fraud typologies, peer institution incidents, BNM advisories)
commonGaps: Digital fraud management is reported within a broader technology or operational risk report without dedicated analysis; senior management receives fraud statistics but no structured effectiveness assessment of the CRF enhancements, meaning the review obligation is nominally met but substantively hollow

Escalation thresholds are undefined or set as static financial loss figures that have not been updated for years, failing to account for victim impact (e.g., a small number of severely impacted vulnerable customers) or the reputational dimension of the emerging fraud environment

Board papers on digital fraud are produced reactively after major incidents rather than as a regular governance rhythm, meaning the board is informed only of crises rather than receiving proactive assurance that management's fraud management approach remains effective

Board minutes do not reflect substantive engagement with digital fraud management content — papers are noted rather than discussed, with no recorded board questions, directives, or follow-up actions, indicating the governance process is procedural rather than effective

Management review records and board papers are not cross-referenced to each other, making it impossible to demonstrate the required audit trail from operational detection through management review to board appraisal; examiners cannot verify that the escalation chain functions as documented
suggestedSources: Board secretariat — Board Risk Committee meeting minutes and approved board papers register
Senior management committee secretariat — Management Risk Committee or Digital/Technology Committee minutes and supporting papers
Fraud Analytics or Financial Crime team — fraud management effectiveness dashboard and trend reports
Compliance or Regulatory Reporting team — internal management information reports on digital fraud KPIs and KRIs
GRC platform — risk committee action item tracker linking management review findings to board reporting outcomes
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers and meeting minutes); PDF/PPTX (management review presentations); Spreadsheet (escalation threshold framework and KPI/KRI dashboard)

auditTips: Map each sub-clause (a) through (g) explicitly to a named document or system record before the examination — examiners will work through the sub-clauses sequentially and expect the institution to produce evidence on a one-to-one basis; a single omnibus 'digital fraud policy' that addresses all items at a high level will not satisfy the granularity expected for a 'S' (Specific) marker clause.

Validate that the CRF document version on the GRC platform matches the version approved by the board — examiners frequently find that working drafts with tracked changes have been mistakenly submitted as approved versions; confirm the document bears a valid approval signature page with the correct approval date and authority.

Prepare a penetration test bridging analysis: pull the most recent mobile application penetration test report and map every finding to either a closed remediation record in the ITSM system (with deployment date) or a formally risk-accepted exception signed by the appropriate risk owner — examiners will select open findings at random and ask to see evidence of remediation or acceptance, and any untracked finding is a critical gap.

Test the escalation chain for clauses (f) and (g) by retrieving three consecutive senior management review cycles and tracing each material finding or threshold breach to the corresponding board paper — if the chain breaks (i.e., a management finding was not escalated to the board), document the rationale proactively and be prepared to explain how the exception was governed rather than waiting for the examiner to discover it.

For the cross-functional operational arrangement under clause (e), request confirmation in writing from each named participant (internal and third-party) that they are aware of their role, have reviewed the current version of the arrangement, and can confirm their contact details are current — this simple step prevents the common finding that arrangements are documented but participants are unaware of them.

Construct a fraud event timeline for any digital fraud incident in the past 24 months and annotate it with the CRF controls that were active at the time of detection, the surveillance mechanism that generated the alert, the time from detection to customer notification, and the countermeasure upgrade that followed — this narrative demonstrates the CRF operates as a living framework rather than a compliance artefact and directly addresses clauses (c) and (d) in a way examiners find compelling.

Cross-reference the mobile application controls register against the current production version of the application: retrieve the current App Store version number, compare it to the version that was last assessed in the penetration test or MAST scan, and if there is a version gap, prepare a delta security review or a risk attestation from the application owner confirming no new security-relevant changes were introduced — version mismatches between assessed and deployed code are among the most common and most impactful findings under clause 12.3(b) and (c).

12.4.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.4.title: Fraud Detection Capabilities
12.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the institution has a demonstrable, time-bound process for translating newly identified fraud modus operandi into updated detection rules and system configurations — the critical question is how quickly this conversion happens and whether it is governed. Examiners will look for evidence that fraud intelligence ingestion (internal incidents, industry alerts, MFPC/BNM advisories) is systematically linked to rule-change workflows, and will probe whether rule updates are subject to testing and approval before deployment. A key red flag is the absence of timestamped records that correlate a detected fraud typology to a corresponding rule change, leaving examiners unable to verify timeliness. Compliance with Appendix 11 Fraud Detection Standards will be assessed through direct comparison of the institution's deployed capabilities against each standard specified therein, and examiners will look for evidence of periodic self-assessment against that appendix. Risk analytics maturity — including model performance metrics, false-positive and false-negative rates, and evidence of model retraining cycles — will also be examined as proof of continuous upgrade.

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Modus Operandi Intelligence Log and Rule Update Register
evidenceItems.description: A contemporaneous register linking each newly identified fraud modus operandi — sourced from internal incident reports, MFPC alerts, BNM advisories, peer-bank intelligence, or law enforcement — to a corresponding fraud detection rule update. Each entry must record the date the modus operandi was identified, the date the rule was updated in the production fraud detection system, and the approval authority. This document directly evidences the 'timely manner' obligation in clause 12.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each entry contains a clear timestamp chain: date modus operandi detected, date rule change drafted, date tested in UAT, and date deployed to production
The register covers the full examination period with no unexplained gaps, and is reconcilable against fraud incident logs and change management records
Update turnaround times are defined in policy (e.g., critical typologies within 24 hours, standard within 5 business days) and actual entries demonstrate adherence to those SLAs
Entries reference the originating intelligence source (e.g., BNM circular reference, MFPC bulletin ID, internal incident ticket number) enabling cross-verification
The register is owned by a named function (e.g., Fraud Risk, Cyber Threat Intelligence) and reviewed by a governance body such as a Fraud Risk Committee at defined intervals
commonGaps: Register exists but dates are retrospectively populated — examiners will cross-check against change management system timestamps and ITSM ticket creation dates to detect back-dating
Intelligence from external sources (MFPC, BNM, industry) is not captured; only internal incidents are logged, leaving gaps for industry-wide typologies
No SLA or turnaround-time policy exists, making it impossible to assess whether updates were 'timely' against any defined benchmark
Rule updates are documented in the fraud system but not reconciled to the modus operandi register, breaking the audit trail from threat to control response
The register is maintained informally in email threads or chat logs rather than a governed, version-controlled repository such as a GRC platform or SharePoint with access logging
