suggestedSources: Fraud Risk Management team's GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — fraud typology register module
ITSM/change management system (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM, Jira) — change tickets tagged to fraud rule updates
Fraud Detection System (FDS) vendor admin console — rule version history and deployment logs
Compliance SharePoint or document management system — BNM/MFPC circular acknowledgement records
Fraud Operations team's case management platform — incident reports that triggered rule reviews
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (PDF/XLSX), with embedded hyperlinks or references to supporting change tickets

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Detection Rule Change Management Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal change management documentation for each fraud detection rule modification, covering the change request, technical specification of the rule change, results of pre-deployment testing in a non-production environment, approval sign-off by authorised personnel (e.g., Fraud Risk Head, CISO), and post-deployment validation. These records demonstrate that rule updates are controlled, tested, and approved — not deployed ad hoc — and that the institution's change governance applies to fraud detection logic.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every fraud rule change has a corresponding approved change request in the ITSM system, with no out-of-band deployments detectable in the fraud system's version history
Pre-deployment test results are documented with explicit pass/fail criteria, false-positive impact assessments, and sign-off by both technical and fraud risk business owners
Emergency change procedures exist and are documented for critical fraud typologies, with expedited approval chains and post-implementation review obligations
Change records reference the specific modus operandi or intelligence input that triggered the change, enabling cross-reference to the Intelligence Log (12.4-E1)
Post-deployment monitoring reports are attached or linked, confirming the rule performed as intended within a defined observation window
commonGaps: Fraud detection rules are updated directly in the production system by vendor or technical teams without formal change records, creating an ungoverned shadow process
Test environments do not mirror production data characteristics, resulting in test results that cannot meaningfully predict production performance
Approval authority matrix for fraud rule changes is undefined or not enforced — changes are approved by whoever is available rather than a defined authority
Post-deployment validation is not performed or is performed but not documented, leaving no evidence that the rule functioned correctly after go-live
Emergency change fast-track is used routinely for non-emergency updates, circumventing pre-deployment testing and obscuring the true change frequency
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management, BMC Remedy) — change request records filtered by fraud system CI
Fraud Detection System vendor portal — rule deployment audit logs and version control history
IT or Cybersecurity team's change advisory board (CAB) meeting minutes
UAT/staging environment test reports — QA team's test management platform (e.g., Zephyr, qTest)
Fraud Operations runbooks stored in Confluence or SharePoint
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF) with attached test evidence (PDF/XLSX) and approval records

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 11 Fraud Detection Standards Compliance Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A structured self-assessment or gap analysis mapping the institution's deployed fraud detection capabilities against each requirement specified in Appendix 11 of the RMiT policy document (Fraud Detection Standards). This assessment must be current, periodically reviewed, and demonstrate that all mandatory standards are met or that remediation plans with target dates are in place for any gaps. This is the primary evidence that the institution actively manages compliance with the Appendix 11 obligation referenced in clause 12.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment is structured as a clause-by-clause mapping of every Appendix 11 requirement to a specific deployed control, system, or process, with evidence references for each
Assessment was conducted or reviewed within the preceding 12 months, with a named accountable owner and approval by a senior governance body (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Management Risk Committee)
Where gaps are identified, a formal remediation plan with milestones, responsible owners, and target completion dates is appended and tracked through a GRC tool

The assessment distinguishes between technical capabilities (e.g., behavioural analytics, device fingerprinting) and operational processes (e.g., alert triage SLAs), covering both dimensions of Appendix 11
Version history of the assessment is maintained, enabling examiners to see how the institution's compliance posture has evolved over time in response to remediation activities
commonGaps: Assessment was conducted at RMiT implementation and not updated since — examiners will note the gap between assessment date and examination date as evidence of stale compliance posture
The mapping is high-level and self-declaratory, stating 'compliant' without referencing specific systems, configurations, or evidence that could be independently verified
Appendix 11 requirements relating to advanced analytics (e.g., machine learning model requirements) are marked compliant based on vendor marketing materials rather than actual deployed configurations
Gaps are identified but not tracked — no remediation plan exists or remediation plan items have passed their target dates without resolution or re-baselining
Assessment does not cover all delivery channels (e.g., mobile banking is assessed but internet banking or third-party payment gateways are omitted)
suggestedSources: Compliance or Fraud Risk function's GRC platform — RMiT compliance register module
SharePoint compliance library — RMiT self-assessment workbooks
Board Risk Committee or Management Risk Committee meeting packs — Appendix 11 compliance status reporting
Fraud Detection System vendor documentation and configuration reports confirming deployed features
Internal Audit reports on fraud detection capability reviews
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export with embedded evidence references, approved as PDF

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Risk Analytics Model Performance Reports
evidenceItems.description: Periodic performance monitoring reports for risk analytics models and rule engines deployed in the fraud detection stack. These reports must demonstrate that the institution measures analytical accuracy — including false-positive rates, false-negative rates, detection rates, and model drift indicators — and uses those metrics to drive continuous improvement. The reports evidence the 'appropriate risk analytics' and 'continuous upgrade' obligations in clause 12.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are produced at a defined cadence (e.g., monthly operational, quarterly strategic) and cover all material analytics models in the fraud detection stack, not just the primary rules engine
Metrics include false-positive rate, false-negative rate, precision, recall, and a fraud loss-detected versus fraud loss-escaped ratio, presented with trend lines across the reporting period
Model retraining or rule recalibration decisions are documented and traceable to specific performance triggers (e.g., false-positive rate exceeding 15% threshold triggers recalibration review)
Reports are reviewed and acted upon by a named governance forum (e.g., Fraud Model Risk Committee) with documented decisions on model adjustments or escalations
Performance reporting covers emerging channel-specific analytics (e.g., biometric behavioural analytics for mobile, device fingerprinting) separately, not just aggregate system-level metrics
commonGaps: Fraud system vendor provides monthly statistics but the institution does not analyse or govern them — reports are filed without review sign-off or follow-up actions

Only transaction decline rates and fraud loss amounts are reported; model-level accuracy metrics (false-positive/false-negative) are not measured, making it impossible to demonstrate analytical effectiveness
Model performance is assessed annually at best — no continuous monitoring is in place to detect model drift between formal reviews
Reports cover the primary fraud detection engine but omit ancillary analytics components such as device intelligence, IP reputation scoring, or behavioural biometrics
There is no documented threshold or trigger that mandates model retraining or rule recalibration, meaning degrading performance does not automatically prompt a remediation response
suggestedSources: Fraud Detection System vendor management portal — analytics and reporting module exports
Data Analytics or Model Risk team — model monitoring dashboards (e.g., Databricks, SAS Model Manager, internal BI tools such as Power BI or Tableau)
Fraud Operations MIS reports — monthly fraud performance packs shared with senior management
Model Risk Management framework documentation and model inventory register
Fraud Risk Committee meeting minutes — performance review agenda items
evidenceItems.format: PDF management report with underlying data in XLSX, or BI platform dashboard export

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Intelligence Intake and Escalation Procedure
evidenceItems.description: A documented, approved procedure governing how the institution receives, assesses, and acts upon fraud intelligence — including BNM directives, MFPC alerts, law enforcement advisories, CERT-MY notifications, and internal fraud incident signals. The procedure must specify roles, responsibilities, escalation thresholds, and the linkage between intelligence intake and the rule update process. This underpins the institution's ability to respond 'in a timely manner' as required by clause 12.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: The procedure defines explicit response timelines for different intelligence severity levels (e.g., critical BNM-issued alert within 4 hours, standard industry advisory within 2 business days) and these timelines are ratified by senior management

Clear ownership is assigned: a named team or role is designated as the single point of entry for all fraud intelligence sources, with a documented escalation path to Fraud Risk, IT Security, and the CISO as required

The procedure includes a triage step with defined criteria for determining whether incoming intelligence warrants a rule change, a monitoring uplift, a customer alert, or no action, with documentation required for each decision
The procedure references and integrates with the change management process (12.4-E2) to ensure rule update requests generated by intelligence intake are formally governed
The procedure was reviewed and approved within the preceding 12 months and version-controlled, with evidence of staff awareness training or communications upon each revision
commonGaps: The procedure exists but applies only to BNM/regulatory sources — industry peer intelligence, law enforcement referrals, and internal fraud operations signals feed into the rule update process ad hoc without a documented intake pathway
Response timelines are not defined in the procedure, leaving 'timely manner' undefined and unenforceable internally
The procedure does not specify what constitutes a 'new modus operandi' as distinct from a variant of an existing one, creating inconsistency in when the rule update obligation is triggered
Procedure is stored informally and staff are unaware of its contents — no training records, acknowledgement log, or testing via tabletop exercises to verify operational effectiveness
The procedure has not been reviewed following significant fraud events or after receipt of new BNM guidance, indicating it is not actively maintained
suggestedSources: Fraud Risk Management team's procedure library on SharePoint or document management system
Cybersecurity or CISO function — Cyber Threat Intelligence intake procedures (may overlap with fraud intelligence)
Compliance team's policy register — cross-referenced to RMiT clause 12.4
GRC platform — procedure review and approval workflow audit trail
HR or L&D training records — fraud intelligence procedure awareness training completion rates
evidenceItems.format: PDF (approved policy/procedure document) with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Account Takeover Prevention Controls Evidence and Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that fraud detection capabilities specifically address account takeover (ATO) using stolen customer credentials, as explicitly referenced in clause 12.4. This includes evidence of controls such as credential stuffing detection, impossible travel logic, device anomaly detection, and step-up authentication triggers, as well as records of periodic testing or red team exercises validating that these controls function as intended.
whatGoodLooksLike: A controls inventory maps each account takeover attack vector (credential stuffing, SIM swap, phishing-derived credentials, social engineering) to specific detection rules or analytics models in the fraud detection stack, with configuration details
Penetration testing or red team exercise reports from the preceding 12 months include ATO-specific scenarios, and findings have been tracked to remediation with evidence of closure
Fraud detection system logs demonstrate that ATO-pattern alerts are generated, triaged, and resolved within documented SLAs, with a sample of closed cases reviewable by examiners

Step-up authentication triggers linked to fraud risk scoring are documented and the thresholds are formally approved, evidencing that high-risk sessions are challenged without requiring manual intervention

Performance metrics specific to ATO detection (e.g., number of ATO attempts detected, prevented, and escaped) are reported separately from general transaction fraud, demonstrating targeted visibility into this specific threat
commonGaps: ATO controls exist within broader fraud detection rules but are not inventoried or labelled separately, making it impossible to demonstrate to examiners that ATO as a specific threat category is addressed

Penetration testing covers network and application layers but does not include ATO-specific credential-based attack simulations such as credential stuffing or password spraying against authentication endpoints
Step-up authentication is implemented but threshold calibration is not documented — examiners cannot determine whether thresholds are risk-appropriate or were set arbitrarily at implementation

ATO detection capability addresses login-stage anomalies but not post-authentication behavioural anomalies (e.g., unusual fund transfer patterns immediately after login from a new device), leaving a detection gap
No case review sample is available — fraud operations case records are purged or inaccessible, preventing examiners from verifying that detected ATO alerts are acted upon appropriately
suggestedSources: Cybersecurity or Fraud Risk function — ATO controls mapping documentation
Fraud Detection System admin console — rule configuration exports for session anomaly and credential misuse rules
Penetration testing vendor reports — engagement scope and findings registers
Authentication platform logs and risk-scoring engine configuration (e.g., ForgeRock, Ping Identity, in-house IAM)
Fraud Operations case management system — ATO case type reports and closure records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (controls inventory and test reports) with supporting system configuration exports (PDF/TXT)

auditTips: Reconstruct the end-to-end timeline for at least three recent fraud typology updates before the examination: pull the originating intelligence source document (BNM circular, MFPC bulletin, internal incident ticket), the corresponding change request in the ITSM system, the UAT test results, and the production deployment log. Present these as a coherent pack — examiners will use this to assess whether 'timely manner' was achieved and whether the governance chain was intact for each update.

Run a reconciliation exercise between the Fraud Modus Operandi Intelligence Log (12.4-E1) and the fraud system's rule version history before the examination. Any rule version increment in the system that cannot be traced to a corresponding intelligence log entry — or vice versa — is a finding waiting to be discovered. Resolve unexplained discrepancies and document the resolution before submission.

Produce a current Appendix 11 compliance heatmap at least 60 days before the examination date. For any capability marked as partially compliant or non-compliant, ensure a formal remediation plan with an executive-approved target date exists in the GRC platform. Examiners will compare remediation target dates against actual completion dates — overdue items with no re-baselining approval are a significant red flag.

Validate that model performance reports for the fraud analytics stack are current and contain explicit false-positive and false-negative metrics, not just transaction volumes or fraud loss amounts. If the fraud system vendor provides raw statistics, ensure an internal analyst has reviewed and formally signed off on those reports — a vendor report filed without internal governance sign-off does not constitute evidence of appropriate risk analytics oversight.

Conduct a tabletop exercise simulating receipt of a critical BNM fraud advisory and walk the response through the documented Fraud Intelligence Intake Procedure end-to-end. Document the exercise, identify any procedural gaps, and update the procedure before the examination. The exercise record itself becomes supporting evidence that the procedure is operationally tested and not merely a paper control.

Ensure account takeover-specific controls are explicitly labelled in your controls inventory rather than embedded within generic fraud detection rules. Prepare a one-page ATO controls summary mapping each attack vector referenced in clause 12.4 (fraudulent transactions, account takeover via stolen credentials) to a specific deployed control, its configuration threshold, and its last test date. This summary significantly accelerates examiner review and demonstrates deliberate control design.

Cross-reference your Fraud Risk Committee or equivalent governance body meeting minutes from the preceding 12 months to confirm that fraud detection rule performance, Appendix 11 compliance status, and modus operandi updates were standing agenda items with documented decisions. Absence of these topics from governance records signals to examiners that fraud detection is managed operationally but not strategically governed, which undermines the overall compliance posture for clause 12.4.

12.5.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.5.title: Digital Service Delivery Risk Mitigation
12.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the institution has operationalised each of the three specific obligations in clause 12.5 with documented evidence rather than policy statements alone. A primary red flag is reliance on SMS or email as sole communication channels without compensating controls such as in-app messaging, DMARC/DKIM enforcement, or registered sender ID frameworks — examiners will trace whether a formal channel risk assessment underpins any compensating control adoption. For customer education (clause 12.5(b)), examiners will verify that advance notification — not retrospective announcement — was issued before new controls went live, and will assess whether the institution tracked customer acknowledgement or measured adoption impact. On call authenticity (clause 12.5(c)), examiners look beyond policy existence to whether the verification mechanism is practically accessible to a typical customer, whether outsourced service providers are explicitly covered, and whether the institution has tested or audited the mechanism's real-world effectiveness.

evidenceItems.name: Secure Communication Channel Risk Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A formal risk assessment evaluating each communication channel used for digital service delivery (SMS, email, push notification, in-app messaging, IVR, etc.) against phishing exploitability, with documented decisions on channel adoption, retirement, or compensating control application. Demonstrates compliance with clause 12.5(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Each active channel is rated against a defined phishing risk taxonomy, with ratings justified by threat intelligence or industry benchmarks (e.g., GSMA, MCMC guidance on SMS SIM-swap fraud)

The assessment explicitly identifies SMS and email as phishing-prone and documents the specific compensating controls applied (e.g., Sender ID registration with MCMC, DMARC enforcement policy of 'reject', in-app notification mirroring)
Document is reviewed and reapproved at least annually and after any new channel is introduced, with version history and sign-off by CISO or equivalent
Risk treatment decisions reference the institution's risk appetite and are tracked to closure in the GRC platform with residual risk ratings
The assessment covers both first-party channels and channels used by appointed outsourced service providers acting on behalf of the institution
commonGaps: Assessment covers only email and SMS but omits newer channels such as WhatsApp Business API, RCS, or third-party aggregator platforms used by outsourced call centres
Compensating controls are listed generically (e.g., 'user education') without mapping to specific technical implementations or policies, making verification impossible
Assessment is dated more than 12 months ago with no evidence of refresh despite the institution launching new digital products in the interim
Risk ratings are assigned without supporting threat intelligence data, meaning examiners cannot validate that the ratings reflect current exploitation trends
Document exists as a draft or internal working paper without formal approval signatures, making it inadmissible as governance evidence
suggestedSources: Information Security department — channel security assessment register
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer) — risk treatment records
Digital Banking or e-Channel team — channel architecture documentation
CISO / IT Risk committee meeting minutes approving the assessment
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded approval signatures and version control table

evidenceItems.name: Phishing Compensating Controls Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical and procedural evidence demonstrating that compensating controls are actively deployed for phishing-prone channels. This includes configuration records, vendor certifications, and monitoring outputs that prove controls are operational, not merely planned. Directly evidences clause 12.5(a) compensating control obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: DMARC DNS record exports showing a 'p=reject' policy enforced on all sending domains, accompanied by DKIM and SPF alignment reports from the reporting period
MCMC Sender ID registration certificates or telco agreement records showing the institution's registered SMS alphanumeric sender IDs, preventing spoofing of the institution's name

In-app secure messaging configuration documentation showing that sensitive communications (OTP context, transaction alerts) are delivered through the authenticated app channel in parallel with or replacing SMS
Phishing simulation or red team exercise report showing the compensating controls were tested and results were used to refine controls, with management sign-off
Monitoring dashboard exports or SIEM alerts log showing phishing attempt detections and response actions taken within the review period
commonGaps: DMARC policy is set to 'p=none' (monitoring only) rather than 'p=quarantine' or 'p=reject', meaning the control provides no active protection against spoofed emails reaching customers
Sender ID registration covers the main brand but not subsidiary brands, product names, or outsourced service provider identities used in customer-facing communications
Compensating controls are documented in policy but no technical configuration evidence (screenshots, exports, vendor attestation) is retained to prove deployment

Anti-phishing controls for outbound communications are present but inbound phishing (e.g., fraudulent sites impersonating the institution) is not addressed, with no takedown SLA or monitoring evidence
Controls are applied to production systems but not to UAT or staging environments used in customer-facing pilots, creating an inconsistency the examiner may probe
suggestedSources: IT/Infrastructure team — DNS configuration exports, email gateway reports
Cybersecurity Operations Centre (SOC) — SIEM logs, phishing detection alerts
MCMC Sender ID portal — registration records
Vendor/outsourcer attestation letters for shared communication platforms
Red team or penetration testing provider — phishing simulation reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF report + system configuration exports (TXT/XML) + screenshots with timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Customer Advance Notification Records for New Controls
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that customers were notified in advance — prior to go-live — whenever new security controls affecting digital service delivery were introduced, covering all notification channels used and confirmation of timing relative to the control launch date. Directly evidences clause 12.5(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: A change log or release register cross-referencing each new security control with its go-live date and the date(s) customer notifications were issued, demonstrating advance timing for every entry

Sample notification artefacts (email templates, SMS text, in-app banner screenshots, website notice screenshots) are retained for each notification campaign, showing plain-language explanations of what is changing and why

Notification reach metrics are recorded — showing percentage of active customers notified, delivery success rates, and channel breakdown — to demonstrate that the institution made reasonable efforts to reach all customers
Customer feedback or complaint log from the notification period is retained, with evidence that inconvenience-related complaints informed future notification design
For major controls (e.g., mandatory re-authentication, biometric enrolment), evidence of multi-channel notification (in-app, email, SMS, and branch notice) rather than single-channel is present
commonGaps: Notifications were issued on the same day as the control launch (or retrospectively), meaning customers were informed but not in advance as required by clause 12.5(b)
Notification records exist for new product launches but not for security control changes embedded within product updates (e.g., session timeout reduction, step-up authentication additions)
Notifications are retained as sent templates but delivery confirmation records (bounce reports, delivery receipts) are absent, making it impossible to evidence that customers actually received them
Notifications use technical or legal language that does not clearly explain the practical impact on customers, undermining the 'ease adoption and minimise inconvenience' intent of the clause
Evidence covers retail customers but excludes corporate or SME banking customers who are subject to different onboarding controls
suggestedSources: Marketing or Customer Communications team — campaign management platform (e.g., Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Braze) export logs
Digital Banking team — in-app notification dispatch records
Change Management register — linking security changes to customer communication obligations
Customer Feedback and Complaints system — complaints tagged to new control launches
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (notification log) + PDF/image (notification samples) + system export (delivery metrics)

evidenceItems.name: Call Authenticity Verification Mechanism Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the practical mechanism(s) available to customers to verify that an inbound call originates from the institution or its appointed outsourced service providers, including user guides, system configuration evidence, and awareness materials. Directly evidences clause 12.5(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: The verification mechanism is clearly described in a published customer-facing document (website FAQ, mobile app help section, welcome pack), specifying step-by-step instructions a customer can follow during or after receiving a call

If a callback verification feature is offered (e.g., customer can initiate a call back to a verified number to confirm the original call was genuine), the technical implementation is documented including the dedicated number, IVR routing, and integration with CRM to confirm the active call record

The mechanism explicitly extends to appointed outsourced service providers — either by including their names and numbers in a published register or by routing all outsourced calls through the institution's authenticated infrastructure
Evidence of customer awareness of the mechanism is present — such as training campaign reach metrics, FAQ page analytics, or inclusion in onboarding materials with acknowledgement records
The mechanism has been tested end-to-end (e.g., mystery shopping, internal audit walkthrough) with results documented showing it functions as intended from a customer perspective
commonGaps: The institution publishes a customer service number for callback verification but does not maintain a registry of outsourced service provider identities, leaving a gap in clause 12.5(c)'s explicit outsourcer coverage

The verification mechanism exists in the institution's internal policy but is not published in customer-facing channels in plain language, meaning customers cannot practically use it without knowing to ask

Mechanism relies solely on advising customers to call the number on the back of their card, which does not address scenarios where the fraudster has convinced the customer the card number is compromised
No testing or audit of the mechanism's real-world effectiveness has been conducted — the institution cannot demonstrate the mechanism works as intended under realistic fraud conditions

Documentation covers inbound calls to the contact centre but does not address outbound calls initiated by the institution (e.g., fraud alerts, account verification calls) which are the primary phishing vector
suggestedSources: Contact Centre / Customer Experience team — IVR configuration documentation, call routing procedures
Digital Banking / Mobile App team — in-app callback feature specifications
Fraud Management team — outbound call procedures and outsourcer communication protocols
Compliance / Legal team — outsourced service provider register and contractual obligations
Corporate Communications / Website team — published customer FAQ and awareness content
evidenceItems.format: PDF (policy/procedure) + URL/screenshot (published customer-facing content) + system configuration export

evidenceItems.name: Outsourced Service Provider Communication Control Register
evidenceItems.description: A maintained register of all outsourced service providers authorised to communicate with customers on behalf of the institution, documenting the channels they use, the controls applied to those channels, and oversight activities conducted. Evidences compliance with the outsourcer dimension of clauses 12.5(a) and 12.5(c).
whatGoodLooksLike: The register lists each appointed outsourced service provider with their authorised communication channels, the types of customer interaction permitted, and the specific compensating controls applied to their phishing-prone channels
Contractual clauses in outsourcer agreements are cited, requiring providers to comply with the institution's communication security standards and prohibiting use of unapproved channels
Periodic oversight reviews (at least annual) of outsourcer communication practices are documented, with findings and remediation actions tracked to closure in the GRC platform
The register is maintained in real time — additions and removals of outsourcers are reflected promptly, with a clear owner and review cadence
Customer-facing disclosures listing authorised outsourcers are cross-referenced in the register, ensuring the published list matches internal records
commonGaps: The institution has a vendor register but it does not specifically capture communication channel authorisations or phishing control requirements, making it impossible to verify clause 12.5 compliance for outsourcers

Some outsourced functions (e.g., debt collection, insurance agency, loyalty programme partners) are omitted from the register because they are not classified as 'technology' outsourcers, despite making customer-facing calls
Oversight reviews exist for IT outsourcers but not for customer communication outsourcers such as call centre BPOs or SMS aggregators
Contracts with legacy outsourcers predate the RMiT 2020 requirements and have not been updated to include communication security obligations
The register is maintained by Procurement without input from Information Security, meaning communication channel risks are not assessed and documented alongside the vendor relationship
suggestedSources: Procurement / Vendor Management team — outsourced service provider register
Legal / Contracts team — outsourcer agreements and communication control clauses
GRC platform — third-party risk management module
Compliance team — BNM outsourcing notification records
Contact Centre management — BPO operational procedures and communication guidelines
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (register) + PDF (contract extracts) + GRC platform export (oversight records)

evidenceItems.name: Digital Fraud Incident and Near-Miss Log Related to Communication Channels
evidenceItems.description: A log of fraud incidents and near-misses attributable to phishing or social engineering via digital communication channels, with root cause analysis documenting whether control failures are linked to clause 12.5 obligations and evidence of remediation actions taken. Demonstrates ongoing monitoring and continuous improvement of digital service delivery risk.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each incident record identifies the communication channel exploited, maps it to the institution's channel risk register, and notes whether existing compensating controls functioned as expected or were bypassed

Root cause analysis for phishing incidents explicitly assesses whether the clause 12.5(a) compensating controls were adequate, and any identified gap triggers a formal control enhancement with a tracked remediation date
Trends analysis is conducted quarterly and presented to the Board or relevant risk committee, showing whether the frequency and severity of channel-based fraud is stable, improving, or deteriorating
Near-miss events (e.g., customer reports of suspicious calls or SMS) are captured in the log even when no financial loss occurred, demonstrating proactive monitoring
Incidents involving outsourced service provider communication channels are separately tagged and escalated to the vendor management process, with evidence of outsourcer notification and remediation
commonGaps: Fraud logs capture financial loss incidents but near-misses and customer-reported suspicious contacts are routed to a separate complaints system and never consolidated for channel risk analysis
Root cause analysis does not trace incidents back to specific communication channels or controls, making it impossible to assess whether clause 12.5 controls are effective
Trends analysis is performed ad hoc rather than on a defined schedule, and there is no evidence it was presented to senior management or the risk committee in the review period

Incidents involving third-party or outsourcer impersonation are categorised as external fraud rather than linked to the institution's clause 12.5 obligations, creating a blind spot in control effectiveness assessment
Remediation actions following incidents are logged but do not include a validation step confirming the corrective control was implemented and tested before closure
suggestedSources: Fraud Management team — fraud incident management system
Customer Care / Complaints team — suspicious contact reports and escalation records
Risk committee — board or management committee minutes referencing fraud trend reports
SOC / Cybersecurity team — phishing detection and response incident records
GRC platform — control deficiency and remediation tracking
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (incident log) + PDF (root cause analysis reports) + PDF (board/committee presentation extracts)

auditTips: Map each sub-clause (a), (b), and (c) to a distinct evidence owner before the examination. Clause 12.5 spans three separate obligations — channel security (Information Security), customer notification (Customer Experience/Marketing), and call verification (Contact Centre/Fraud) — and examiners frequently find that one obligation is well-evidenced while another has no designated owner and no documentation.

For clause 12.5(a), do not rely solely on policy statements. Request technical configuration exports from your email gateway (DMARC aggregate reports), DNS records, and MCMC Sender ID registration certificates before the examination and verify they are current. An examiner who checks your DNS live during the review and finds a DMARC policy of 'p=none' will override any policy document claiming compliance.

Construct a notification timeline table that lists every security control change in the past 12-24 months alongside the date customer notifications were issued and the go-live date. This single artefact directly addresses clause 12.5(b) and immediately shows whether advance notification was consistently achieved. Gaps where notification postdates go-live should be investigated and explained before the examination.

For clause 12.5(c), conduct a mystery shopping exercise internally — have a compliance team member attempt to verify the authenticity of a call from the institution as if they were a customer with no prior knowledge of the process. Document the steps taken, barriers encountered, and outcome. This test report is compelling evidence that the mechanism is practically accessible, which is the standard the clause sets.

Pull your outsourced service provider register and cross-check it against the institution's published customer-facing list of authorised third parties. Discrepancies — providers who call customers but appear in neither list, or listed providers whose communication controls have not been assessed — are a common examiner finding under clause 12.5(a) and (c) and should be rectified before examination.

Prepare a cross-reference matrix linking clause 12.5 sub-clauses to related internal policies (Anti-Phishing Policy, Outsourcing Policy, Customer Communication Policy) and to related RMiT clauses (e.g., 12.4 on fraud controls, 12.6 on customer awareness programmes). Examiners assess whether controls are integrated into the institution's governance fabric, not implemented as isolated clause-by-clause checklists.

Review the past 12 months of fraud incidents and customer complaints for any involving phishing, smishing, vishing, or call impersonation. For each, confirm that a root cause analysis was completed and that findings were either closed with evidence or are actively tracked. Unresolved control gaps from prior incidents that are still open at the time of examination significantly increase examiner concern about the institution's remediation culture.

12.6.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.6.title: Customer Fraud Risk Empowerment
12.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised customer self-service card controls — specifically the ability to activate and deactivate payment cards for card-not-present (CNP) and overseas transactions — through accessible digital channels. They assess whether these controls are genuinely functional, discoverable by customers, and supported by clear user guidance rather than being superficially available but practically obscure. A key red flag is institutions that offer toggle controls only in branch or via call centre, or where the feature exists in mobile banking but is buried behind multiple navigation layers with no customer awareness. Examiners also probe the boundary of liability: they will test whether any customer-facing communications, terms, or incident-handling procedures improperly shift blame to customers who did not use available controls, as the clause explicitly preserves the institution's duty of care regardless of customer action or inaction. Cross-referencing with complaint and fraud loss data is standard practice to identify whether the absence or inaccessibility of controls contributed to customer harm.

evidenceItems.name: Card Control Feature Functional Specification and Design Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Technical and functional documentation describing the card control capabilities offered to customers, including CNP toggle, overseas transaction toggle, and any channel through which these controls are accessible (mobile app, internet banking, IVR, branch). Demonstrates that the institution has deliberately designed and implemented customer-facing fraud mitigation controls as required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Specification explicitly distinguishes CNP transaction controls from overseas transaction controls as separate, independently operable toggles
Documentation confirms real-time or near-real-time effect of customer-initiated control changes, with maximum processing lag defined and measured
Multi-channel availability is documented (e.g., mobile app, internet banking, at minimum), with fallback channel (call centre or branch) also specified
Accessibility considerations are addressed, including options for customers without smartphones or digital access
Version history shows iterative improvement based on usability testing or customer feedback, indicating ongoing commitment rather than a point-in-time implementation
commonGaps: Documentation covers only one channel (e.g., mobile app) with no fallback for digitally excluded customers, raising reasonableness concerns
CNP and overseas controls are conflated into a single toggle, preventing granular customer control and limiting fraud mitigation effectiveness
Specification lacks latency SLA for control activation, making it impossible to assess whether the control provides meaningful protection
Documentation is outdated relative to the live production system, indicating governance over the feature has lapsed
No accessibility or inclusivity assessment is present, leaving open questions about whether all customer segments are practically empowered
suggestedSources: Digital Channels / Product Technology team SharePoint or Confluence wiki for feature specifications
Cards and Payments product team for product requirements documents (PRDs)
Enterprise architecture repository (e.g., LeanIX, Sparx EA) for system capability maps
IT service management platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) for feature delivery records and change logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or Confluence export with version control timestamps

evidenceItems.name: Customer-Facing Card Control User Interface Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Screenshots, screen recordings, or UAT artefacts demonstrating the actual customer experience of activating and deactivating CNP and overseas transaction controls across supported channels. Provides direct observable proof that controls are accessible, clearly labelled, and functional in production, not merely documented in specifications.
whatGoodLooksLike: Screenshots or recordings are date-stamped from the production environment and reflect the current app or portal version
UI labels unambiguously identify CNP and overseas transaction controls using plain language consistent with customer communications
Confirmation or feedback mechanism is visible after a customer changes a control state, confirming the change has been applied
Navigation path to reach controls requires no more than three steps from the main authenticated screen, supporting genuine discoverability
UAT sign-off records confirm end-to-end testing of both activation and deactivation flows, including edge cases such as attempting a CNP transaction while the control is disabled
commonGaps: Screenshots are from a UAT or staging environment rather than production, providing limited assurance of live functionality
Control labels use internal jargon (e.g., 'e-commerce flag') rather than customer-intelligible language, undermining informed use
No confirmation screen or notification is shown, leaving customers uncertain whether their control change was successfully applied
Navigation path involves six or more taps from login, making the feature practically inaccessible for many customers under time pressure
Evidence only covers mobile app; no screenshots for internet banking or other advertised channels are included
suggestedSources: Digital Channels QA team for UAT artefacts and regression test packs
Mobile app and internet banking product owners for production screenshots
Customer Experience (CX) team for usability test recordings
App store or internal MDM-deployed application for live walkthrough during examination
evidenceItems.format: PDF screenshot compilation or MP4 screen recording with date-stamp overlay

evidenceItems.name: Customer Awareness and Communication Records for Card Controls
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of proactive customer communications — including onboarding materials, in-app notifications, SMS/email campaigns, and website disclosures — that inform customers of the existence, location, and use of CNP and overseas transaction controls. Demonstrates the institution's effort to ensure customers can meaningfully exercise the fraud risk empowerment controls, not merely that the controls technically exist.
whatGoodLooksLike: New customer onboarding communication explicitly references card control features with direct navigation instructions or deep-link to the relevant app section
Annual or periodic awareness campaigns are evidenced, showing the institution does not rely solely on one-time onboarding disclosure
Communication content is available in Bahasa Malaysia and English at minimum, with other languages where the institution's customer demographics warrant
Website FAQ or help centre contains a dedicated, indexed article on card controls with step-by-step instructions that is findable via common search terms
Campaign distribution metrics (reach, open rates, click-throughs) are captured to demonstrate that communications were actually delivered and not merely drafted
commonGaps: Card controls are mentioned only in the full terms and conditions document, with no standalone proactive customer communication
Communications reference the feature generically (e.g., 'manage your card settings') without explaining CNP or overseas transaction controls specifically
Awareness content is English-only, potentially excluding a significant customer segment
No evidence of communication updates following product changes, leaving customers with outdated instructions that may erode trust in the feature
Distribution records are absent, meaning it cannot be demonstrated that communications were received by customers rather than merely published
suggestedSources: Marketing and Corporate Communications department for campaign records and distribution reports
Customer Relationship Management (CRM) platform (e.g., Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics) for email and SMS send logs
Digital Channels team for in-app notification content management system records
Website Content Management System (CMS) for help centre article publication history
Onboarding workflow system for welcome communication templates and trigger logs
evidenceItems.format: PDF copies of communications, CRM export of send metrics, CMS publication history

evidenceItems.name: Card Control Transaction Event Logs and Audit Trail
evidenceItems.description: System-generated logs recording customer-initiated card control changes (activation and deactivation of CNP and overseas transaction permissions) with timestamps, channel, customer identifier, and resulting card state. Demonstrates that the control mechanism is operationally active, auditable, and produces a reliable record that can be used in fraud investigations and dispute resolution.
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs capture each control change event with precise timestamp (to the second), customer ID, card masked PAN, channel used, previous state, and new state
Log integrity is protected — records are write-once or stored in a tamper-evident system, with no evidence of post-hoc modification capability
Logs are retained and queryable for the full 7-year regulatory retention period without degradation or archival inaccessibility
Correlation between control change events and subsequent transaction approval or decline events is technically possible, supporting fraud investigation workflows
Sample queries demonstrate that fraud investigation teams and compliance staff can retrieve complete control change history for a specific customer or card within minutes
commonGaps: Logs exist but are stored in application log files rather than a structured, queryable data store, making retrieval slow and unreliable during examination
Log records do not capture the channel through which the control change was made, preventing analysis of whether certain channels have reliability issues
Retention policy for control event logs is shorter than 7 years or is undefined, creating potential gaps for older fraud dispute investigations
No mechanism exists to correlate a declined transaction with the customer's control state at the time of the transaction, weakening fraud investigation capability
Logs are stored alongside application debug logs without separation, making extraction of compliance-relevant records labour-intensive
suggestedSources: Core banking or card management system (e.g., FIS, Temenos, i2c) audit log exports
Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform (e.g., Wazuh, Splunk, QRadar) for aggregated event logs
Data warehouse or operational data store maintained by Technology Operations
Fraud Management System for correlated transaction decline and control state records
IT Operations or Database Administration team for log retention configuration evidence
evidenceItems.format: Structured log export (CSV/JSON) or SIEM query result with field-level documentation

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Liability Policy and Customer Terms Confirming Retained Institutional Duty of Care
evidenceItems.description: Documented internal policy and customer-facing terms that explicitly confirm the institution's retained liability, responsibilities, and duty of care for digital services security, notwithstanding any customer use or non-use of available fraud controls. Directly addresses the clause's preservation of institutional liability and prevents the institution from using customer empowerment controls as a liability shield.
whatGoodLooksLike: Internal fraud liability policy explicitly states that the availability of customer card controls does not reduce or transfer the institution's legal and regulatory obligations to the customer
Customer terms and conditions do not contain clauses that condition fraud reimbursement eligibility on whether the customer activated or used available controls
Fraud dispute handling procedures require investigation of institutional security failures as a primary step, independent of whether the customer had controls enabled
Policy is reviewed and approved by Legal, Compliance, and senior management on at least an annual basis, with evidence of most recent review and sign-off
Staff training materials for fraud operations and customer service teams reflect the correct liability position, demonstrating operational embedding of the policy
commonGaps: Customer terms include language stating that customers 'should' enable card controls, which could be interpreted as creating a contributory negligence position that reduces institution liability
Fraud reimbursement decision tree used by operations staff implicitly uses absence of control activation as a factor in declining claims, inconsistent with the institutional policy
Internal policy exists but has not been reviewed since the RMiT policy took effect, meaning it may not reflect current regulatory expectations
Legal team and Fraud Operations team have inconsistent interpretations of the liability boundary, creating operational risk in dispute resolution
No evidence that customer-facing terms were reviewed by Legal specifically against clause 12.6 obligations, leaving unanswered whether the current terms comply
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance department for fraud liability policy documents
Cards and Retail Banking product team for customer terms and conditions master document
Fraud Operations for dispute handling standard operating procedures (SOPs)
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Riskonnect, CURA) for policy version control and approval workflows
Human Resources or Learning Management System (LMS) for fraud operations staff training records
evidenceItems.format: PDF policy document with approval metadata; PDF customer terms with version history

evidenceItems.name: Periodic Review and Effectiveness Assessment of Customer Card Controls
evidenceItems.description: Management reporting and review records demonstrating that the institution periodically assesses whether customer card control mechanisms remain adequate, accessible, and effective in reducing fraud exposure. Demonstrates ongoing governance rather than a static implementation, consistent with the 'reasonably practical' standard in the clause which implies a duty to continuously assess and improve.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual or more frequent management review report analyses card control adoption rates segmented by customer demographic and channel, identifying segments with low uptake warranting targeted outreach
Review benchmarks control effectiveness against fraud loss data, specifically CNP and overseas transaction fraud rates, to assess whether controls are achieving risk reduction
Findings from each review result in documented action items with owners and completion dates, and evidence of closure is maintained
Review process includes input from Fraud Risk, Technology, Customer Experience, and Legal teams, reflecting cross-functional governance

Review reports are presented to and approved by a relevant governance committee (e.g., Technology and Cyber Security Risk Committee, Retail Risk Committee) with meeting minutes as corroborating evidence
commonGaps: No periodic review process exists — the control was implemented once and has not been formally assessed for effectiveness since launch
Review is conducted informally with no documented output, meaning there is no evidence trail for examination purposes
Adoption rate metrics are tracked but not acted upon — low adoption in certain customer segments has not triggered any outreach or usability improvement programme
Fraud loss data is not disaggregated to isolate CNP and overseas transaction fraud, making it impossible to assess whether the controls are achieving their intended purpose
Review findings are not escalated to a governance committee, meaning senior management oversight of control effectiveness cannot be demonstrated
suggestedSources: Fraud Risk Management function for quarterly and annual fraud analytics reports
Digital Channels / Product Management for feature adoption and usage analytics (e.g., from mobile app analytics platform)
GRC platform for risk control assessment records and action tracking
Board and management committee secretariat for Technology Risk Committee or equivalent meeting minutes and papers
Business Intelligence / Data Analytics team for customer segmentation and control uptake dashboards
evidenceItems.format: PDF management report with committee approval evidence; dashboard export with underlying data

auditTips: Before the examination, obtain a live demonstration of the CNP and overseas transaction toggle controls in the production mobile banking app and internet banking portal — do not rely solely on screenshots. Examiners may request a live walkthrough, and being able to demonstrate end-to-end in real time is significantly more convincing than static artefacts. Identify the exact navigation path and rehearse it to avoid awkward delays during the session.

Cross-reference customer complaint and fraud dispute records against dates when card control features were unavailable due to system downtime or degraded performance. If controls were inaccessible during periods of elevated fraud activity, this creates a directly observable gap between the institution's stated empowerment commitment and operational reality that examiners will probe.

Review all customer-facing terms and conditions, FAQs, and fraud dispute SOPs specifically for language that could be read as conditioning fraud reimbursement on whether a customer had activated controls. Flag and escalate any such language to Legal before the examination — this is a high-likelihood finding and a relatively straightforward remediation if caught early.

Prepare a concise card control adoption metrics pack showing what percentage of active card customers have engaged with CNP and overseas controls, segmented by channel and demographic where available. Examiners will ask about this to assess whether the empowerment is theoretical or practically realised. Have a narrative ready that connects low-adoption segments to specific outreach or usability improvement initiatives.

Ensure audit trail and log evidence is pre-extracted and formatted before the examination. Request a sample extraction from IT covering a three-month window that includes both control change events and correlated transaction outcomes. Validate that the extraction is complete and that retention for the full 7-year period is confirmed in writing from the Data Management or IT Operations team.

Map the evidence items to the three distinct obligations in the clause verbatim text: (1) empowering customers where reasonably practical, (2) specifically offering CNP and overseas transaction card controls, and (3) retaining institutional liability and duty of care. Structure your examination evidence folder with these three themes so examiners can navigate directly to the relevant artefacts without requiring explanation.

Check whether any recent changes to the card control feature — including UI changes, channel additions or removals, or back-end processing changes — went through a formal change management process with risk assessment. Uncontrolled changes to a regulatory-mandated control are a secondary finding risk. Pull the relevant change management records from your ITSM platform and include them in the evidence pack as proof of controlled governance over the feature.

12.7.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.7.title: Customer Fraud Awareness Programmes
12.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution treats customer fraud awareness as a living programme rather than a static campaign — they look for evidence of a structured review cycle with documented outcomes and measurable effectiveness metrics. Key priorities include verifying that modus operandi updates are timely and tied to actual threat intelligence (not generic annual reminders), that security measure explanations are accessible and accurate across all digital channels, and that real-time alert mechanisms are operationally active and tested. Red flags include awareness materials that have not been refreshed following known fraud incidents or industry alerts from bodies such as BNM's Financial Consumer Alert, absence of interactive or simulation-based elements, and an inability to demonstrate that alert thresholds and triggers for clause 12.7(c) are formally defined and monitored. Examiners will cross-reference awareness programme content against the institution's own fraud incident records and industry threat feeds to test whether the programme reflects current fraud typologies.

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Awareness Programme Policy and Review Records
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy or framework document that establishes the institution's customer fraud awareness programme, including review frequency, ownership, and scope. Supporting records must demonstrate that the programme is continuously reviewed and formally enhanced, not merely maintained at a steady state.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy document specifies a defined review cycle (at minimum annual, with triggered ad-hoc reviews upon new fraud typologies emerging) and is signed off by a named senior owner such as the Chief Digital Officer or Head of Retail Banking.

Version-controlled policy history shows at least two substantive revisions within the past 24 months, with change logs referencing specific threat triggers or regulatory inputs that prompted each update.
Review records include documented outcomes — not just sign-offs — capturing what was assessed, what gaps were identified, and what enhancements were approved and actioned.
Programme scope explicitly covers all active digital channels (mobile banking, internet banking, e-wallet, in-app notifications) and assigns channel-specific content ownership.
Board or Board Risk Committee minutes or management committee records confirm governance oversight of the programme at least annually, demonstrating tone from the top.
commonGaps: Policy exists but review records are superficial sign-off sheets with no documented assessment of effectiveness or specific enhancement decisions, making continuous improvement claims unsubstantiated.
Programme scope is limited to internet banking and does not address mobile application channels, in-app messaging, or third-party digital service partners.
Review cycles are calendar-driven only (e.g., January each year) with no evidence of triggered reviews following BNM Financial Consumer Alert bulletins or the institution's own fraud spike events.
Ownership is assigned to Marketing rather than a function with fraud intelligence input (e.g., Fraud Risk, Cybersecurity), resulting in content that is promotional rather than threat-informed.
No linkage documented between the awareness programme review and outputs from the institution's fraud incident management process or threat intelligence feeds.
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or local equivalent) — policy registry and review task completion records
SharePoint or document management system — version history and approval audit trail for the programme framework document
Board secretariat records — Board Risk Committee or management committee minutes referencing programme governance
Fraud Risk or Cybersecurity department — internal review reports and action trackers
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy and review reports); meeting minutes as PDF

evidenceItems.name: Fraud Modus Operandi Content Library and Update Log
evidenceItems.description: A catalogued repository of customer-facing fraud awareness content (articles, advisories, push notifications, SMS alerts, website banners, social media posts) that demonstrates continuous and timely updates on how to identify fraud, including specific coverage of new and common modus operandi as required under clause 12.7(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Content library is tagged by fraud typology (e.g., phishing, vishing, SIM swap, Macau scam, investment fraud) and each item carries a publication date and a 'triggered by' field referencing the threat intelligence input that prompted it.

Update timestamps show content was published within a defined SLA (e.g., 14 days) of the corresponding BNM Financial Consumer Alert, police advisory, or internal fraud trend report — demonstrating timeliness.
Content explicitly names specific modus operandi with actionable identification steps rather than generic 'beware of scams' language, and is available in Bahasa Malaysia and English at minimum.
A content calendar or release schedule demonstrates planning and cadence, showing that awareness is continuous rather than reactive spikes around major fraud events only.
Retirement or archival records exist for outdated content, ensuring customers are not encountering stale or superseded fraud guidance on active channels.
commonGaps: Content library lacks metadata on what triggered each update, making it impossible to demonstrate that new modus operandi were addressed in a timely manner rather than as part of routine calendar updates.

Awareness materials describe fraud generically without naming specific current schemes (e.g., no reference to prevalent local typologies such as Macau scam variants or e-commerce impersonation), failing the 'specific information' requirement of 12.7(a).

Content exists only on the public website and is absent from the in-app notification centre, push messaging, or at-login banners within the digital banking application where customers are most at risk.
No multilingual content or Easy Read versions exist, limiting reach to customers with lower digital literacy who may be higher-risk fraud targets.
Update frequency evidence shows content has not been refreshed in over six months despite documented fraud incidents in that period, undermining the 'continuous' requirement.
suggestedSources: Content Management System (CMS) for public website and mobile banking portal — publication logs and version history
Digital Marketing or Corporate Communications team — campaign management platform (e.g., Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Adobe Experience Manager) with content scheduling records
Fraud Risk Management team — internal fraud typology tracker and threat intelligence reports used to trigger content updates
BNM Financial Consumer Alert portal — external reference for cross-validation of timeliness
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (content inventory and update log); PDF or web archive (published materials)

evidenceItems.name: Security Measure Explanation and Customer Communication Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution clearly explains new and existing security measures to customers, including how to verify genuine e-banking websites and mobile applications, as required under clause 12.7(b). This covers pre-launch communications, in-product guidance, and ongoing educational content.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every material change to digital security controls (e.g., introduction of biometric authentication, adoption of kill-switch, deployment of transaction cooling-off) is accompanied by a documented customer communication plan executed prior to or concurrent with the rollout.

Communications include specific, step-by-step guidance on verifying genuine digital touchpoints — for example, official App Store listing URLs, the institution's registered domain verified against BNM's Financial Institution Directory, and indicators of tampered or cloned applications.
Content distinguishes clearly between what the institution will and will not ask customers to do (e.g., 'We will never ask for your TAC via phone') to counteract social engineering tactics.

Explanatory materials are accessible within the digital banking application at the point of use (e.g., 'Why we ask for this' tooltips, security FAQ within app settings) rather than only on external web pages.
Post-deployment customer comprehension is tested — even via short in-app surveys or call centre feedback logs — with results documented to demonstrate that the explanation was understood.
commonGaps: Security feature communications are issued after rollout rather than before, leaving a window during which customers encounter new controls without understanding them and may abandon legitimate transactions or fall for social engineering exploiting the confusion.
Verification guidance for genuine e-banking websites and mobile apps is buried in lengthy Terms and Conditions or FAQ pages with no prominent, accessible placement at login or during onboarding.
Communications focus on 'what' the security measure is but not 'why' it protects customers or 'how' to use it, reducing customer adoption and the protective effect of the control.
No evidence of customer communications being reviewed for plain-language readability or tested with a representative sample of customers before broad release.

Records of security measure communications are held only within Marketing and are not cross-referenced in the Fraud Risk or Cybersecurity function's programme documentation, creating an audit trail gap.
suggestedSources: Digital channel management team — pre-launch communication plans and deployment records within project or change management tools (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow Change Management)
CMS and mobile banking back-end — in-app content logs for security guidance tooltips and FAQs
Customer Experience or Marketing team — campaign briefs, email distribution records, push notification logs
Call centre knowledge management system — scripts and guidance updated to reflect new security measures
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (communication plans and scripts); system export (push notification and email dispatch logs)

evidenceItems.name: Real-Time Customer Alert Configuration and Operational Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical and operational evidence demonstrating that the institution issues real-time alerts to customers when security measures are absent or have not yet been implemented, as required under clause 12.7(c). This includes alert rule configurations, test results, and records of actual alerts triggered.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formally documented alert rules specify the precise conditions that trigger a customer notification (e.g., transaction attempted over unencrypted connection, access from unrecognised device, login without MFA where MFA has not been enrolled) with named system owners and review dates.

System configuration screenshots or exported rule sets from the digital banking platform, fraud monitoring engine (e.g., NICE Actimize, SAS Fraud Management, or equivalent), or mobile application back-end confirm that alert logic matches documented specifications.

End-to-end test records — executed at least annually or following any change to alert logic — demonstrate that alerts fire correctly, are delivered within the defined latency threshold, and contain actionable guidance for the customer.

Production alert volume reports show the alert mechanism is operationally active, with logs of actual alerts triggered, delivery channel (push, SMS, email), delivery success rate, and customer response or acknowledgement data where captured.

Escalation procedure exists and is tested for scenarios where the alert cannot be delivered to the customer (e.g., invalid mobile number, push notification not enabled) so that alternative protective actions are triggered.
commonGaps: Alert rules are defined for fraud detection purposes (flagging suspicious transactions) but no distinct real-time alert exists for the specific scenario of a security measure being absent — for example, no warning when a customer accesses internet banking without having enrolled a second factor.

Alert configuration is undocumented or exists only as institutional knowledge within the IT operations team, with no formal specification that can be presented to an examiner or reviewed for adequacy.
Test records are limited to unit testing at deployment and do not include periodic operational testing to verify that alerts continue to function correctly as the platform evolves.

Alerts are delayed or batched rather than real-time — for example, a daily digest of security events rather than an immediate notification — which does not satisfy the 'real-time' requirement of 12.7(c).

No evidence that alert content has been reviewed for customer comprehensibility; alerts contain technical jargon or codes that do not provide the customer with a clear understanding of the risk or action required.
suggestedSources: Digital banking platform or core banking vendor — alert rule configuration exports and delivery logs
Fraud monitoring system (e.g., Actimize, Feedzai, or in-house system) — rule management console and alert production reports
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow) — change records for alert rule modifications and associated test evidence
IT operations or cybersecurity team — penetration test or functional test reports covering alert triggering scenarios
Telecoms or notification gateway provider — SMS and push notification delivery success rate reports
evidenceItems.format: System export or screenshot (rule configuration); Spreadsheet or PDF (alert logs and test results)

evidenceItems.name: Interactive Fraud Awareness Simulations and Engagement Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution employs interactive or simulation-based methods to improve customer understanding of fraud risks and controls, as required under clause 12.7(d). This goes beyond passive content delivery and demonstrates active customer engagement with fraud awareness material.
whatGoodLooksLike: At least one interactive awareness mechanism is deployed and active within the digital banking application or customer-facing platforms — for example, a phishing simulation quiz, a mock social engineering scenario, a security settings self-assessment, or a guided walkthrough of genuine versus fake banking interfaces.

Participation and completion data is captured, showing meaningful customer reach — records include number of customers exposed, completion rates, and where applicable, quiz scores or simulation pass/fail rates — demonstrating programme effectiveness beyond mere content publication.

Interactive content is refreshed in line with current fraud typologies, with version history and update rationale documented, so that simulations reflect the modus operandi customers are actually encountering.

Customers who fail simulations or score below a defined threshold receive targeted follow-up content or alerts, demonstrating that the mechanism actively improves individual customer understanding rather than being a passive exercise.
Effectiveness findings from interactive engagements are fed back into the programme review cycle (cross-referenced with 12.7-E1 review records), demonstrating a closed-loop improvement process.
commonGaps: Awareness programme relies exclusively on static content (articles, videos, banners) with no interactive or simulation component, directly failing to meet the explicit requirement in 12.7(d) for simulations or equivalent interactive measures.

Interactive elements exist (e.g., a quiz on the public website) but participation data is not captured or is not linked to customer identities, making it impossible to demonstrate meaningful customer reach or individual improvement.
Simulations are deployed as a one-time campaign rather than as a continuous programme feature, contradicting the 'continuous efforts' requirement of the clause.

Interactive content is deployed only for new customer onboarding and is not available to or targeted at the existing customer base, leaving the majority of the portfolio without access to the measure.

No outcome data is collected on whether interactive engagements change customer behaviour (e.g., whether customers who completed a phishing simulation subsequently reported phishing attempts at a higher rate), limiting the institution's ability to evidence effectiveness.
suggestedSources: Digital banking application back-end — user engagement analytics for in-app interactive features
Customer education or financial literacy platform (e.g., custom-built module, third-party platform such as Knowledgeable or similar) — completion and score reports
Marketing or Digital team — campaign performance reports for interactive awareness campaigns run via email, app, or social channels
Fraud Risk Management team — linkage reports connecting simulation participation data with downstream fraud incident or reporting rates
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF (participation and completion reports); system export (engagement analytics); PDF (simulation content and scenario scripts)

evidenceItems.name: Programme Effectiveness Measurement and Enhancement Records
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution objectively measures the effectiveness of its fraud awareness programme and uses the results to drive documented enhancements — evidencing the 'review and enhance effectiveness' obligation that overarches all sub-clauses of 12.7.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined effectiveness measurement framework exists, specifying quantitative and qualitative metrics — for example, customer fraud loss rates, fraud reporting rates, phishing click-through rates from simulation exercises, customer survey scores on fraud awareness, and call centre enquiry volumes on fraud topics — with targets and review frequency.

Periodic effectiveness reports (at minimum annual, aligned to programme review cycles) present trend data against defined metrics, with narrative analysis identifying which programme elements are working and which require enhancement.

Enhancement decisions are formally documented with specific actions, owners, and target completion dates, and subsequent reports evidence whether enhancements were implemented and whether they improved metric outcomes.

Customer feedback mechanisms (e.g., post-interaction surveys, focus groups, call centre feedback logs) are systematically captured and incorporated into effectiveness assessments, ensuring customer perspective informs programme evolution.

Effectiveness measurement is presented to a governance forum (e.g., Fraud Risk Committee, Customer Risk Committee) at least annually, with minutes recording discussion and any additional enhancements directed by the committee.
commonGaps: No formal effectiveness metrics are defined — the programme is assessed qualitatively by the team that runs it with no independent measurement, making it impossible to demonstrate objective evidence of continuous improvement.

Metrics used are input-based (e.g., number of articles published, number of alerts sent) rather than outcome-based (e.g., change in customer fraud victimisation rate or improvement in correct identification of phishing simulations), failing to evidence actual effectiveness.
Effectiveness reports exist but enhancements identified are not tracked to completion, meaning the review cycle produces findings but no demonstrable programme improvement follows.

Customer feedback data is collected through general Net Promoter Score or satisfaction surveys that do not contain fraud awareness-specific questions, providing no usable signal for programme effectiveness.

Effectiveness measurement is not linked to fraud loss data or incident trends from the Fraud Risk function, siloing the awareness programme from the institution's actual fraud experience and preventing data-driven enhancement.
suggestedSources: Fraud Risk Management team — fraud loss and incident trend reports segmented by fraud typology and customer segment
GRC platform or management reporting system — programme effectiveness dashboard and periodic review reports
Customer Experience team — survey platform exports (e.g., Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey) with fraud awareness question results
