Fraud Risk Committee or equivalent governance body — meeting minutes and action trackers referencing programme effectiveness reviews
Digital analytics platform (e.g., Adobe Analytics, Google Analytics for institutional use) — customer engagement data for awareness content and interactive features
evidenceItems.format: PDF (effectiveness reports and governance minutes); Spreadsheet (metrics tracking and action log)

auditTips: Cross-reference the timestamps on fraud awareness content updates (12.7-E2) against BNM Financial Consumer Alert bulletins and the institution's own internal fraud incident records — examiners will do this, and any gap of more than 14-30 days between a documented threat emerging and customer-facing content being updated is a material finding. Prepare a timeliness matrix before examination.

Ensure that real-time alert evidence (12.7-E4) distinguishes clearly between fraud detection alerts (flagging suspicious transactions) and security posture alerts (notifying customers that a protective measure is absent or unenrolled) — these are separate obligations and conflating them is a common examination failure point. Prepare a classification table mapping each alert type to the specific sub-clause it satisfies.

For interactive simulations (12.7-E5), be ready to demonstrate not just that the feature exists but that customers are actively using it — pull participation reports before the examination and have a narrative prepared for why reach is at its current level and what the institution is doing to improve it. An unused simulation feature is treated similarly to no feature at all.

Map every evidence item to a named regulatory obligation in the verbatim clause text using a compliance traceability matrix — examiners will assess sub-clauses (a) through (d) individually, so ensure your evidence pack has a clear document that links each item to the specific sub-clause it addresses, preventing any sub-clause from appearing unevidenced.

Validate that the governance trail for programme reviews (12.7-E1 and 12.7-E6) runs from working-level review documentation up through a named committee with minuted discussion — examiners assess whether governance is substantive or ceremonial. If committee minutes show only 'noted' with no questions or directions, this signals a governance gap.

Before examination, conduct a dry-run walkthrough of the real-time alert mechanism end-to-end in a test or UAT environment, capturing screen recordings or logs — this gives examiners a live demonstration of clause 12.7(c) compliance and is far more compelling than static configuration screenshots alone.

Interview the call centre or customer service team leads as part of examination preparation — they often have ground-truth insight into whether awareness content is actually helping customers (evidenced by reduced repeat enquiries on fraud topics) or is falling short, and their feedback can strengthen or flag weaknesses in your effectiveness narrative before examiners surface them independently.

12.8.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.8.title: Customer Account Suspension and Kill Switch
12.8.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the kill switch solution is genuinely self-service, accessible without requiring a functioning login session, and operable across all digital channels offered by the institution. Examiners scrutinise the end-to-end customer journey — from fraud detection through suspension, validation, and reinstatement — paying close attention to whether timeframes are formally defined, monitored, and met in practice. A major red flag is a kill switch that is buried within authenticated app menus, making it inaccessible precisely when customers need it most after credential compromise. Contact centre adequacy is evaluated through staffing models, queue metrics, and escalation logs, particularly during peak fraud event periods. Examiners cross-reference complaint records, incident logs, and customer feedback to detect cases where distressed customers could not reach the institution promptly or where account reinstatement was delayed without documented justification.

evidenceItems.name: Kill Switch Feature Design and Functional Specification
evidenceItems.description: Technical and functional documentation describing the architecture, user interface, access pathways, and security controls of the self-service kill switch solution, demonstrating that it is secure, convenient, and accessible to customers under fraud conditions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Specification explicitly documents that the kill switch is accessible pre-authentication (e.g., from the app login screen or a public URL) so that customers with compromised credentials can still invoke it.
Design includes multi-channel accessibility — mobile app, internet banking, USSD, and IVR — with documented fallback pathways for each channel.
Security controls section details authentication alternatives (e.g., OTP to registered mobile, biometric challenge, or NRIC verification) that do not rely solely on the primary login credential.
User journey wireframes or screenshots demonstrate three-click or fewer access from app entry point to account suspension confirmation.
Document carries version history, approval signatures from Technology and Compliance, and a date linking it to the current production deployment.
commonGaps: Kill switch is only accessible from within the authenticated dashboard, rendering it unusable when credentials have been stolen — the most common scenario requiring its use.
Specification covers mobile app only, with no equivalent pathway documented for customers who do not have the app installed or whose devices are lost or stolen.
Authentication method for kill switch invocation is identical to the primary login, providing no additional security benefit and creating circular dependency.
Document has not been updated following material UI or architecture changes, creating a mismatch between specification and live system.
No security assessment or penetration test result is referenced, leaving the 'secure' requirement unsubstantiated.
suggestedSources: Digital Banking / Technology team — product specification repository (Confluence, SharePoint, or Jira)
Enterprise Architecture team — solution design records
Information Security team — security review and penetration test reports linked to the feature
Change Management / ITSM platform (ServiceNow, Jira) — change request records for kill switch deployment
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (functional spec), supplemented by wireframe exports (PDF/PNG) and system architecture diagrams

evidenceItems.name: Kill Switch End-to-End Testing and UAT Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Test plans, test execution results, and user acceptance testing records demonstrating that the kill switch has been validated to suspend accounts effectively and within acceptable timeframes across all supported channels and device types.
whatGoodLooksLike: Test plan covers positive paths (successful suspension), negative paths (expired OTP, invalid NRIC), and edge cases (customer initiates suspension mid-transaction, dual-device scenarios).
Test results include measured suspension latency from customer action to account freeze, with timestamps evidencing near-real-time or sub-minute execution.
UAT sign-off includes representation from Fraud Operations, Digital Product, and Customer Experience teams, not solely Technology.
Regression test results are present for every subsequent release that touched authentication or account management modules, confirming kill switch integrity was preserved.
Defect log from UAT is included alongside resolution evidence, demonstrating that all critical and high-severity defects were closed before production release.
commonGaps: Testing was performed only in controlled lab conditions using test accounts, with no evidence of staging-environment testing against production-equivalent data volumes.
Suspension latency was not measured or recorded, making it impossible to demonstrate 'swiftness' as required by the clause.
UAT covered only Android and iOS app channels, omitting internet banking portal and contact centre-initiated suspension pathways.
Test plan has not been refreshed following major platform upgrades, so existing results do not reflect the current production system.
No re-activation pathway was tested — only the suspension leg was evidenced, leaving the restoration obligation unvalidated.
suggestedSources: Quality Assurance team — test management platform (Zephyr, qTest, Azure Test Plans)
Digital Banking project records — UAT sign-off documents in SharePoint or project management tool
DevOps/CI-CD pipeline — automated regression test reports
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records — release approval documentation referencing test completion
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF export from test management platform; PDF sign-off memo

evidenceItems.name: Account Suspension and Reinstatement Transaction Logs
evidenceItems.description: System-generated logs capturing every kill switch invocation, account suspension event, and subsequent reinstatement, including timestamps, channel used, initiating party, and outcome — demonstrating operational effectiveness and measurable response timeframes in practice.
whatGoodLooksLike: Logs capture timestamp of customer-initiated suspension request and timestamp of confirmed account freeze as separate fields, enabling calculation of actual suspension latency.
Each reinstatement record includes the validation steps completed (identity verification method, agent ID or automated rule applied) and the elapsed time from suspension to restoration.
Log data is immutable and stored in a tamper-evident system (SIEM, audit database) separate from the operational platform it monitors.
Monthly or quarterly management reports summarising suspension volumes, average suspension latency, and reinstatement timeframes are derived from these logs and reviewed by senior management.
Log retention aligns with regulatory requirements and the institution's data classification policy, with access controls restricting modification to system accounts only.
commonGaps: Logs record only the account status change but not the customer-initiated request timestamp, making it impossible to calculate actual response time from the customer's perspective.
Reinstatement events are logged in a different system from suspension events with no common key, preventing end-to-end journey reconstruction during examination.
Logs are stored in the same application database as the kill switch feature itself, creating integrity risk and non-compliance with segregation expectations.
No aggregated reporting is produced from the logs, so management has no visibility of whether performance standards are being met across the customer population.
Log retention policy is not formally documented or enforced, with evidence of records being purged after 90 days due to storage cost controls.
suggestedSources: Core Banking System / Digital Banking Platform — audit log exports
SIEM platform (Splunk, IBM QRadar, Wazuh) — correlated event records
Fraud Management System — account action logs
Data Warehouse / BI platform — management dashboard extracts
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/XLSX or PDF report); SIEM query output

evidenceItems.name: Contact Centre Resourcing and Service Level Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Workforce management records, service level agreement (SLA) documentation, and operational performance reports demonstrating that the contact centre is adequately staffed and operating effectively to assist customers in distress related to suspected fraud, including during peak periods and outside business hours.
whatGoodLooksLike: Workforce management plan explicitly accounts for fraud-related call surges, with documented contingency staffing levels and escalation protocols for high-volume fraud events.

SLA document defines specific performance targets for fraud-distress calls — e.g., average speed of answer (ASA) of 60 seconds or less, abandonment rate below 5% — and distinguishes these from general customer service queues.
Monthly performance reports show actual ASA, call abandonment rate, and first-call resolution rate for fraud-related interactions tracked as a separate queue or category.

Evidence of 24/7 availability (or clearly documented out-of-hours protocol directing customers to self-service kill switch) is present, with out-of-hours call routing logs confirming operational continuity.

Training records confirm that all contact centre agents handling fraud calls have completed fraud response certification, including account suspension and reinstatement procedures, within the past 12 months.
commonGaps: SLAs exist for general customer service but no distinct performance target is defined for fraud-distress calls, making it impossible to assess adequacy for this specific obligation.
Performance reports aggregate all call types, obscuring whether fraud-related queue performance meets acceptable standards during peak periods.
Staffing model does not include a fraud surge scenario — capacity planning is based solely on average daily volume without stress-testing against major fraud incidents.

Contact centre operates only during business hours with no documented procedure for customers who discover fraud outside those hours, leaving the 'convenient means' requirement unmet for a significant window.
Training records cannot be produced for a material proportion of active fraud queue agents, or training has not been refreshed following procedural changes to the kill switch workflow.
suggestedSources: Contact Centre Operations team — workforce management system (Verint, NICE, Genesys) reports
Customer Experience / COO office — SLA documentation and performance dashboards
Human Resources / Learning Management System (LMS) — agent training completion records
Telephony platform — call queue analytics and IVR routing configuration exports
evidenceItems.format: PDF/XLSX reports from workforce management and telephony platforms; PDF SLA policy document; LMS training completion exports

evidenceItems.name: Account Reinstatement Policy and Validation Procedure
evidenceItems.description: Formally approved policy and operational procedure governing the validation steps required before reinstating customer access following a kill switch suspension, including defined timeframes for reinstatement and the controls preventing premature or unauthorised restoration.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy defines 'reasonable timeframe' numerically (e.g., reinstatement within 24 hours of completed validation) and assigns accountability to a named role or team for meeting that target.

Procedure details a minimum validation checklist — identity re-verification method, fraud investigation status check, customer consent confirmation — that must be completed before any reinstatement action is taken.
Document addresses scenarios where the customer is confirmed as a fraud victim and where the suspension was triggered in error (false positive), with different validation pathways for each.
Policy has been approved by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body and carries a review frequency of at least annually.
Procedure includes a maker-checker control requiring two authorised staff members to approve reinstatement for accounts suspended due to confirmed or suspected fraud.
commonGaps: 'Reasonable timeframe' is referenced in policy but not quantified, making compliance measurement and examiner assessment impossible and leaving the institution exposed to subjective interpretation.
Validation procedure is documented at a high level only, with no step-by-step operational guide for contact centre or fraud operations staff — increasing the risk of inconsistent execution.
Policy does not distinguish between self-service kill switch suspensions and institution-initiated suspensions, creating ambiguity about which reinstatement pathway applies.
Document has not been reviewed or updated following significant changes to the digital banking platform or fraud typologies in the past 24 months.
No escalation path is defined for disputed reinstatement decisions, leaving customers with no recourse if reinstatement is unreasonably delayed.
suggestedSources: Fraud Risk / Financial Crime Compliance team — policy register
GRC platform (Archer, ServiceNow GRC, LogicGate) — policy management module
Digital Banking / Operations team — standard operating procedure (SOP) repository on SharePoint
Board / Board Risk Committee secretariat — meeting minutes evidencing approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (policy and procedure documents); Board committee minutes extract (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Customer Fraud Complaints and Escalation Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of customer complaints, feedback, and escalations related to the kill switch experience, contact centre responsiveness, and account reinstatement delays — demonstrating that the institution monitors service quality, investigates failures, and implements corrective actions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Complaint records are categorised with a specific taxonomy tag for kill switch or fraud-account-suspension complaints, enabling trend analysis distinct from general service complaints.
Each complaint record captures the full timeline: date of incident, date of complaint, resolution action taken, and date of closure, enabling calculation of resolution cycle time.
Root cause analysis is documented for complaints involving reinstatement delays or contact centre inaccessibility, with evidence of corrective action implemented and effectiveness monitored.
Quarterly complaint trend reports are reviewed by the Customer Experience and Fraud Risk committees, with management attestation recorded in meeting minutes.
Customer satisfaction scores or qualitative feedback specific to fraud recovery experience are collected post-incident and trended over time.
commonGaps: Complaint management system does not have a specific category for kill switch or fraud account suspension complaints, making it impossible to isolate and analyse this population.
Complaints are recorded but root cause analysis is not performed systematically — recurring issues such as contact centre queue abandonment are not identified or escalated for remediation.
No feedback mechanism exists for customers who successfully used the kill switch, meaning the institution has no positive baseline against which to measure the impact of problems.
Management reporting on fraud-related complaints does not reach Board Risk Committee level, indicating inadequate governance oversight of this customer protection obligation.
Complaint resolution records do not document whether reinstatement occurred within the defined timeframe, creating a disconnect between complaint outcome and compliance measurement.
suggestedSources: Customer Experience / Complaints Management team — CRM platform (Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics) complaint records
GRC platform — complaint and incident register
Contact Centre Operations — escalation queue logs
Board Risk Committee / Customer Experience Committee — meeting minutes and management information packs
evidenceItems.format: CRM system export (CSV/PDF); Management Information pack (PDF/PPTX); Committee meeting minutes (PDF)

auditTips: Conduct a live demonstration of the kill switch during the examination preparation phase — walk through the suspension flow as an unauthenticated user on both the mobile app and internet banking portal, and time the process from entry point to suspension confirmation. Examiners may request a similar demonstration, and any friction or authentication barrier discovered during this rehearsal must be remediated before examination.

Produce a single integrated evidence pack that traces one complete customer journey end-to-end: kill switch invocation log entry → contact centre interaction record (if applicable) → fraud investigation record → reinstatement log entry → customer notification. Examiners assess the clause holistically, and fragmented evidence across disconnected systems is a common cause of adverse findings.

Cross-reference kill switch suspension logs against complaint records to proactively identify cases where a customer complained about delayed reinstatement after invoking the kill switch. Prepare a root cause summary and corrective action narrative for any such cases — examiners will sample complaint records and link them to operational logs independently.

Obtain and retain contact centre performance data specifically for the fraud-distress call queue during the three most recent major fraud incident periods (e.g., phishing campaigns, e-wallet scam waves). Examiners specifically test whether the contact centre remains 'adequately resourced and operating effectively' under stress, not just on average days — average performance data alone is insufficient.

Verify that the defined reinstatement timeframe in policy has actually been met in practice by running a data query across all reinstatement events in the past 12 months and calculating the percentage meeting the target. Prepare a management-signed attestation with this calculation. If the target is not being met, prepare a remediation plan before the examination rather than allowing examiners to discover the gap.

Ensure the kill switch is documented in customer-facing communications — mobile app onboarding, internet banking help pages, and fraud awareness materials — with evidence that customers are informed of its existence and how to use it. Clause 12.8 requires 'convenient means' which implies customers must be aware of and able to find the mechanism; undisclosed features do not satisfy the obligation.

Map all evidence items to the three specific sub-obligations in clause 12.8(a), (b), and (c) in a compliance matrix before the examination. Examiners assess each sub-obligation independently and institutions often have strong evidence for the kill switch feature itself (a) but weak documentation for contact centre adequacy (b) and reinstatement timeframes (c) — the matrix forces completeness across all three limbs.

12.9.subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
12.9.title: Post-Incident Customer Protection
12.9.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised a structured, time-bound response that goes beyond generic incident handling to specifically protect customers once a fraud or data compromise event is declared. Examiners will trace a live or recent incident end-to-end — from detection through account monitoring escalation, customer notification dispatch, and credential or payment instrument revocation — to confirm that all three sub-obligations in clause 12.9(a)–(c) were executed in sequence and within documented SLAs. A key red flag is the absence of evidence showing heightened monitoring was activated at the account level rather than at aggregate or system level only. Examiners will also scrutinise whether customer notifications contained actionable mitigating guidance or were merely generic breach alerts, and whether credential revocation was applied comprehensively to all affected accounts rather than selectively. Institutions that cannot produce timestamped, per-incident artefacts demonstrating each sub-obligation was met — as opposed to policy documents alone — are assessed as non-compliant.

evidenceItems.name: Post-Incident Customer Protection Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: A formal, board-approved or CISO-approved policy and accompanying operational procedure that explicitly defines the institution's obligations under clause 12.9(a)–(c), including roles, responsibilities, escalation paths, and SLA timelines for heightened monitoring activation, customer notification, and credential revocation following a fraud or data compromise incident.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly maps to clause 12.9(a), (b), and (c) with individual SLA targets (e.g., heightened monitoring within 1 hour of incident declaration, customer notification within 24 hours, credential revocation within 4 hours of confirmed compromise)
Procedure defines clear criteria and thresholds for triggering each sub-obligation, including what constitutes 'potential risk of exploitation' under clause 12.9(c)
Roles and responsibilities are assigned to named functions (e.g., Fraud Operations, Digital Banking, Information Security) with a RACI matrix covering all three sub-obligations
Document version history shows annual review and post-incident review cycles, with the most recent version dated within 12 months
Policy includes a customer communication template register and a credential revocation decision tree as annexed artefacts
commonGaps: Policy covers general incident response but does not separately address customer-facing protection measures, conflating clause 12.9 obligations with generic IR procedures
SLA timelines are absent or aspirational without corresponding monitoring metrics or breach escalation triggers
Credential revocation criteria are vague ('where appropriate') with no defined risk threshold or decision authority documented
Policy has not been updated following a significant incident or regulatory change, showing a review gap of more than 12 months
Customer notification content requirements are not specified, leaving template selection to individual staff discretion during high-stress incidents
suggestedSources: Information Security / Cyber Risk policy repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform such as ServiceNow GRC or Archer)
Fraud Risk Management department policy register
Digital Banking or Digital Services operations procedure library
Board Risk Committee or CISO approval records in board paper management system
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Heightened Account Monitoring Activation Records
evidenceItems.description: System-generated logs, configuration records, and operational reports demonstrating that heightened monitoring was activated at the individual customer account level for all accounts identified as affected by a fraud or data compromise incident, in accordance with clause 12.9(a). Records must show what monitoring rules were applied, when they were activated, and who authorised the activation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Fraud monitoring platform (e.g., FICO Falcon, SAS Fraud Management, Actimize) exports showing account-level flags applied within the SLA window, with timestamps and analyst IDs

Heightened monitoring rule set is documented and distinct from standard monitoring — showing lower transaction thresholds, additional velocity checks, or manual review queues applied specifically to affected accounts
Monitoring activation is linked to the parent incident ticket via incident ID, providing an auditable chain from incident declaration to account-level action
A post-activation monitoring report is produced at defined intervals (e.g., 24-hour, 72-hour, 7-day) showing alerts generated and dispositions taken on affected accounts
Evidence that monitoring remained heightened until a formal risk closure decision was made, not simply until the incident ticket was closed
commonGaps: Monitoring logs exist at the system or channel level but cannot be filtered or attributed to specific customer accounts affected by the incident
Heightened monitoring is applied only to accounts where fraud loss was confirmed, rather than to all accounts where data was potentially compromised
Activation timestamps show heightened monitoring was applied days after incident declaration, evidencing SLA breach with no documented exception or escalation
Monitoring rule changes were made verbally or informally and are not captured in a system audit trail or change management record
No documented process or evidence for deactivating heightened monitoring — accounts remain on heightened status indefinitely without review, indicating the process is not actively managed
suggestedSources: Fraud monitoring and analytics platform (Actimize, FICO Falcon, Featurespace, or equivalent)
Core banking system account flag audit trail
ITSM platform incident record (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) showing linked account monitoring activation tasks
Fraud Operations team shift logs or daily operational reports
SOC or Fraud Operations team runbook with evidence of execution sign-off
evidenceItems.format: System export (CSV/XLSX) and PDF operational reports with incident ID cross-reference

evidenceItems.name: Customer Notification Records and Communication Templates
evidenceItems.description: Documented evidence that affected customers were notified following a fraud or data compromise incident, including the notification templates used, the channels deployed, the population reached, dispatch timestamps, and the specific mitigating guidance provided to customers, in compliance with clause 12.9(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification dispatch records show all identified affected customers received communication within the SLA window, with per-customer delivery confirmation (SMS receipt, email delivery log, push notification acknowledgement) traceable to the incident

Notification content includes specific, actionable mitigating guidance tailored to the nature of the incident (e.g., instructions to change password, freeze card, enable transaction alerts) rather than generic 'we take security seriously' language

Customer communication templates are version-controlled and pre-approved by Compliance and Legal, with incident-specific variables (affected service, recommended actions, helpline number) populated for each notification event

A post-notification reconciliation report confirms the count of customers notified matches the count of affected customers identified in the incident record, with documented handling for customers who could not be reached
Notifications are issued through multiple channels (SMS, email, in-app) proportionate to the severity and reach of the incident, with channel selection rationale documented
commonGaps: Notification was issued to a subset of affected customers only, with no documented rationale or escalation for the gap between affected population and notified population
Customer notification content is generic and does not contain specific mitigating actions, failing the 'necessary information to apply mitigating measures' requirement of clause 12.9(b)
Notification timeline cannot be evidenced because dispatch logs were not retained or are held in a system without forensic-grade audit trail
Templates were drafted ad hoc during the incident without Compliance or Legal review, creating regulatory and reputational risk
There is no evidence of follow-up communication to customers who did not respond to or acknowledge the initial notification, leaving residual risk unaddressed
suggestedSources: Customer communication platform (Twilio, Infobip, or internal SMS/email gateway) dispatch logs
CRM system (Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics) customer contact records
Digital banking platform push notification logs
Customer Communication / Marketing Operations team archive
Compliance-approved template repository in SharePoint or document management system
evidenceItems.format: System export logs (CSV/XLSX), PDF notification templates, and reconciliation report (PDF/XLSX)

evidenceItems.name: Credential and Payment Instrument Revocation Records
evidenceItems.description: System-generated evidence demonstrating that affected user credentials (passwords, PINs, OTP seeds, authentication tokens) and/or designated payment instruments (debit/credit cards, virtual cards, e-wallet accounts) were revoked and re-issued for all customers identified as at potential risk of exploitation, as required under clause 12.9(c), with timestamps, authorisation records, and re-issuance completion confirmation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Identity and access management (IAM) or banking platform audit logs show forced credential resets applied to all affected customer accounts, with timestamps within the defined SLA and a senior authorisation record for bulk revocation actions

Card management system records confirm card blocking and re-issuance requests were raised for all affected cardholders, with card status transitions (active → blocked → re-issued) traceable per customer and linked to the incident ID

Re-issuance completion tracking shows the percentage of affected customers who completed credential reset or received new payment instruments, with a follow-up process for customers who did not complete the process

A risk-based decision record documents the scope determination for revocation — which credentials and instruments were revoked, the rationale for inclusions and exclusions, and the risk owner who approved the scope
Post-revocation confirmation report confirms no affected accounts retained active compromised credentials or payment instruments beyond the SLA window
commonGaps: Revocation was applied only to customers who self-reported fraud rather than to all accounts within the potentially compromised population, understating the scope of clause 12.9(c) obligations

Credential revocation and card blocking actions are not linked to the incident record, making it impossible to demonstrate the actions were taken in response to the specific incident under examination
Re-issuance process was initiated but completion rate is not tracked, leaving unknown exposure for customers who did not reset credentials or activate replacement cards
The scope determination for revocation (which accounts were included) is not documented, suggesting the decision was made informally without risk owner accountability
Physical card re-issuance was handled by a third-party card bureau with no SLA evidence or completion confirmation retained by the institution
suggestedSources: IAM platform or internet banking system credential management audit logs (e.g., ForgeRock, Ping Identity, proprietary core banking)
Card management system (FIS, Temenos, or equivalent) card lifecycle audit trail
Fraud Operations or Information Security incident action log in ITSM platform
Third-party card bureau service reports and SLA delivery confirmations
Digital banking platform OTP seed or token management system logs
evidenceItems.format: System audit log exports (CSV/XLSX), PDF incident action authorisation records, and completion status report

evidenceItems.name: Incident-Linked Post-Incident Protection Activity Report
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated, per-incident report that brings together all three sub-obligations under clause 12.9(a)–(c) for each qualifying incident, demonstrating end-to-end compliance with a single auditable artefact. This report links heightened monitoring activation, customer notification, and credential or payment instrument revocation actions to the parent incident, with timestamps, population counts, and completion status for each obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each qualifying incident (fraud event or data compromise) has a corresponding post-incident customer protection report generated within a defined timeframe (e.g., within 5 business days of incident closure) and signed off by the Fraud Risk or Information Security owner

Report contains a structured summary table mapping each clause 12.9 sub-obligation to the specific action taken, the population affected, the timestamp of action, the SLA target, and a compliant/breached status flag
SLA breaches are identified in the report with a documented root cause and corrective action, demonstrating the institution's self-monitoring capability
Reports are stored in a searchable, access-controlled repository that allows examiners to retrieve all reports within a specified date range without reliance on individual staff to locate records

The report references supporting evidence artefacts (monitoring logs, notification dispatch records, revocation system exports) by document ID or hyperlink, enabling quick triangulation during examination
commonGaps: No consolidated per-incident report exists — evidence for clause 12.9 sub-obligations is dispersed across multiple systems and departments with no single artefact providing an auditable overview
Reports exist for major incidents but not for smaller fraud events or lower-severity data compromise incidents that also trigger clause 12.9 obligations
Reports are narrative only and do not contain population counts or timestamps, making it impossible to objectively assess SLA compliance or coverage

SLA breaches are not disclosed or analysed in the report, suggesting either the report is produced retrospectively to present a clean picture or the institution is not monitoring its own SLA performance
Reports are held in individual staff email folders or shared drives without version control, access logging, or assured retention, undermining their evidentiary value
suggestedSources: GRC platform incident management module (ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or LogicGate)
Fraud Risk Management department SharePoint site or document management system
Information Security post-incident review repository
Integrated ITSM platform with custom post-incident protection activity workflow
Internal audit evidence management platform
evidenceItems.format: PDF consolidated report with linked supporting artefacts, stored in GRC or document management system

evidenceItems.name: Testing and Simulation Records for Post-Incident Customer Protection Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution periodically tests and validates its clause 12.9 procedures through tabletop exercises, simulation drills, or controlled testing of heightened monitoring activation, customer notification dispatch, and credential revocation workflows — demonstrating that procedures are operationally effective and not merely documented in policy.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual or bi-annual tabletop exercise or simulation drill records specifically covering the clause 12.9 scenario (fraud or data compromise with customer impact), with participant sign-in sheets, scenario scripts, and documented outcomes

Test results identify measurable SLA performance against each sub-obligation (e.g., time to activate heightened monitoring, time to dispatch notification, time to complete revocation in a simulated environment)
Gaps and weaknesses identified during testing are tracked in a findings register with remediation owners and target closure dates, and closure evidence is retained

Testing records show cross-functional participation from Fraud Operations, Information Security, Digital Banking, Customer Service, and Communications teams, reflecting the multi-team nature of clause 12.9 execution
Test frequency and scope are defined in the post-incident customer protection procedure, and testing records confirm adherence to the defined schedule
commonGaps: No testing records exist for clause 12.9 specifically — general crisis communication drills are cited as equivalent but do not cover credential revocation or heightened monitoring activation
Testing was conducted once at implementation and has not been repeated annually, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate that procedures remain effective as systems and staff change

Test scenarios are unrealistically simplified (e.g., single affected customer) and do not validate the institution's capability to execute at scale during a real incident affecting thousands of accounts
Findings from previous exercises were not remediated or tracked, and the same gaps recur in subsequent test cycles without documented management acceptance of residual risk
Testing is conducted by the procedure owners themselves without independent validation or challenge, reducing the objectivity and rigour of the exercise
suggestedSources: Business Continuity / Cyber Resilience team exercise records and after-action reports
Fraud Risk Management team drill logs and scenario documentation
GRC platform testing and assurance module
Internal Audit work papers for technology and cyber risk reviews
Information Security team tabletop exercise repository in SharePoint or Confluence
evidenceItems.format: PDF after-action report, exercise scenario script, findings register (XLSX), and remediation closure evidence

auditTips: Compile a complete register of all incidents in the examination period that involved potential fraud or customer data compromise — including lower-severity events — and confirm that each incident has a corresponding set of clause 12.9 artefacts (monitoring activation, notification, revocation records). BNM examiners will cross-reference the institution's incident log against its clause 12.9 evidence, and gaps for any qualifying incident constitute a standalone finding regardless of the quality of evidence for other incidents.

Perform a timestamp walkthrough for at least two incidents prior to examination: trace the incident declaration time, the heightened monitoring activation time, the customer notification dispatch time, and the credential or revocation completion time, and calculate elapsed duration against documented SLAs. This exercise will reveal SLA compliance rates and expose any instances where actions were recorded after-the-fact rather than in real time — a pattern examiners specifically look for.

Validate that customer notification content for past incidents meets the 'necessary information to apply mitigating measures' threshold in clause 12.9(b) by having Compliance review archived notification copies against a structured checklist: does the notification identify the nature of the risk, the specific actions the customer should take, a dedicated helpline or support channel, and a realistic timeframe for re-issuance of credentials or instruments? Generic notifications that fail this checklist should be treated as a gap and the template library updated before examination.

Confirm that the scope of credential revocation and payment instrument revocation in past incidents matched the full population of potentially affected customers, not only confirmed fraud victims. Obtain a reconciliation between the incident's affected customer count (as documented in the incident record) and the count of accounts on which revocation actions were executed. Any unexplained variance should be investigated and documented with a risk rationale before examination, as unexplained scope gaps are a significant red flag for examiners.

Ensure all clause 12.9 evidence artefacts are stored in a centrally accessible, access-controlled repository with consistent naming conventions that allow retrieval by incident ID and date. Examiners may request evidence for multiple incidents simultaneously on short notice during fieldwork, and an inability to produce records promptly is itself treated as a control weakness. Avoid situations where key artefacts reside in individual staff mailboxes or informal shared drives.

Cross-reference clause 12.9 evidence with related clauses in the RMiT policy — particularly those covering incident response (clause 11), customer data protection, and digital fraud management — to ensure the institution's evidence demonstrates a coherent, integrated response rather than isolated point-in-time actions. Examiners assess clause 12.9 compliance in the context of the broader incident management and customer protection framework, and contradictions between evidence sets across clauses will attract heightened scrutiny.

Brief Fraud Operations, Information Security, and Digital Banking operational staff on the specific obligations under clause 12.9(a)–(c) before examination, so they can speak knowledgeably to their role in executing these obligations during interviews. Examiners frequently conduct staff interviews as a parallel track to document review, and staff who are unfamiliar with the specific regulatory requirements or who describe a process that differs materially from documented procedures will create inconsistencies that extend the examination scope.

13.1.title: Technology Audit Scope and Frequency
13.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution has operationalised a risk-based audit methodology that demonstrably links audit scope, frequency, and intensity to an objective assessment of each system's complexity, sophistication, and criticality — not merely to regulatory schedules or budget cycles. Examiners will probe whether the audit universe is complete, whether criticality ratings are formally documented and periodically refreshed, and whether high-risk or critical systems receive proportionately deeper scrutiny than commodity systems. Red flags include a flat annual audit cycle applied uniformly across all systems regardless of risk profile, audit scope statements that are generic rather than system-specific, and evidence that internal audit resources are allocated without reference to a criticality tiering framework. Examiners will also assess whether the Board Audit Committee exercises meaningful oversight of audit planning decisions and whether management responses to technology audit findings are tracked to closure within defined timelines.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Universe and Criticality Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive inventory of all technology systems, applications, and infrastructure components within audit scope, annotated with assigned criticality tiers, complexity ratings, and the rationale for each classification. This demonstrates that the institution has formally identified what must be audited and has applied a consistent, documented methodology to determine the relative risk weight of each asset.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every system in the institution's IT asset inventory is mapped to an entry in the audit universe, with no unexplained omissions or exclusions

Each entry carries a formally approved criticality tier (e.g., Critical / High / Medium / Low) derived from a documented scoring methodology that considers business impact, regulatory sensitivity, interconnectivity, and data classification
The register is reviewed and formally re-approved at least annually, with a clear version history and sign-off trail from Internal Audit leadership and the Board Audit Committee
Tier assignments are cross-referenced to the institution's Business Impact Analysis (BIA) and the RMiT Critical Systems list, ensuring alignment across governance functions
Changes to criticality ratings following material system changes, incidents, or new regulatory guidance are documented with effective dates and approver identities
commonGaps: The audit universe covers only applications and excludes underlying infrastructure (databases, middleware, cloud services, network devices), creating blind spots that examiners immediately identify
Criticality ratings are assigned subjectively by system owners without a standardised scoring rubric, resulting in inconsistent classifications across business units
The register has not been updated following significant technology changes such as cloud migrations, new product launches, or M&A activity, making it stale at the time of examination
Systems designated as Critical under RMiT are rated lower in the audit universe due to operational pressure, with no documented justification or escalation record
There is no evidence of Board Audit Committee or Audit Committee review and approval of the audit universe, reducing its governance weight
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — Audit Universe module
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system — authoritative system inventory
CMDB (Configuration Management Database) — for infrastructure-level completeness
Board Audit Committee meeting minutes and approved charter appendices
RMiT Critical Systems register maintained by Group Technology Risk
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (PDF for point-in-time evidence); version-controlled with approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Risk-Based Technology Audit Plan
evidenceItems.description: The annual (or rolling multi-year) technology audit plan that explicitly maps each planned audit engagement to the criticality tier and complexity assessment of the target system, demonstrating that audit frequency and resource intensity are proportionate to risk. This is the primary artefact showing that clause 13.1 obligations have been operationalised in planning decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: The plan document contains a narrative or table that directly cross-references each audit engagement to the system's criticality tier, providing auditable justification for why each system is audited at its assigned frequency

Critical and high-complexity systems are scheduled for annual or more frequent audits, while lower-risk systems have extended cycles (e.g., biennial or triennial), with the differential explicitly justified
Planned audit intensity (e.g., full scope, targeted review, follow-up only) varies by tier and is defined in terms of minimum test coverage, sample sizes, and specialist resource requirements
The plan is approved by the Chief Audit Executive (CAE) and the Board Audit Committee before the commencement of the audit year, with the approval date documented

Mid-year plan amendments triggered by significant risk events (e.g., major incidents, regulatory changes, new critical system deployments) are formally recorded with the rationale and re-approved by the relevant authority
commonGaps: The audit plan presents a uniform annual cycle for all technology systems with no differentiation by complexity or criticality, indicating a compliance-calendar approach rather than a risk-based approach
Frequency decisions are driven by available audit headcount rather than system risk profiles, with no documented risk rationale for gaps or deferrals
The plan covers application-layer audits but omits technology infrastructure, cybersecurity controls, and third-party technology service provider audits, leaving significant scope gaps
There is no mechanism for unplanned or ad-hoc audits triggered by material incidents or emerging threats, suggesting the plan is inflexible
Board Audit Committee approval is evidenced only through generic meeting minutes rather than a formal plan document, making it difficult to confirm what was actually sanctioned
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — annual audit plan document and Board Audit Committee approval papers
GRC platform — Audit Plan module with linkage to audit universe
Board Audit Committee meeting packs and resolutions (for the relevant approval period)
CAE's annual audit planning memorandum to Board/Audit Committee
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal plan document) with accompanying spreadsheet detailing system-level scheduling; Board-approved version retained

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Scope Statements and Engagement Planning Documents
evidenceItems.description: Engagement-level planning documents for each technology audit, including the defined audit scope, objectives, test approach, and resource allocation. These demonstrate that scope and intensity are tailored to the specific system being audited rather than applied generically, satisfying the 'commensurate with complexity, sophistication and criticality' obligation at the individual engagement level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each engagement planning document explicitly states the system's criticality tier and complexity characteristics that informed the scope boundary, test depth, and sampling approach

Scope statements identify specific control domains to be tested (e.g., access management, change control, backup and recovery, encryption) that are relevant to that system's risk profile, rather than using a generic template applied uniformly
Resource allocation (auditor days, specialist skills such as cybersecurity or data analytics) is documented and justified by reference to system complexity and the scope of testing required
For critical systems, the planning document evidences pre-audit engagement with Technology Risk and Information Security teams to identify current risk hotspots that should be incorporated into scope
Scope limitations or exclusions are formally documented with risk acceptance or compensating control rationale, approved by the CAE
commonGaps: Scope statements are templated and largely identical across different system audits, with only the system name changed, indicating that intensity is not genuinely calibrated to each system
There is no documented link between the complexity and criticality assessment of the system and the chosen sample sizes, control domains, or depth of testing applied
Specialist technical resources (e.g., penetration testing support, data analytics) are absent from critical system audits due to budget constraints without documented risk acceptance
Scope exclusions are undocumented, meaning examiners cannot determine what was intentionally left out versus overlooked
Engagement planning documents are created retrospectively or updated after fieldwork commences, undermining the integrity of the planning rationale
suggestedSources: Internal Audit — engagement files (physical or electronic audit management system such as TeamMate, AuditBoard, or Diligent)
Audit management platform — engagement workspace with planning section
Internal Audit methodology manual for required planning document components
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX stored in the audit engagement file within the audit management system; must be version-controlled and dated

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Reports and Findings Register
evidenceItems.description: Completed audit reports for technology engagements and the consolidated findings register tracking all open, closed, and overdue audit issues. These demonstrate that audits are being executed as planned, that findings are risk-rated and recorded, and that the overall body of audit work reflects proportionate scrutiny of the technology estate.
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit reports explicitly state the system criticality tier and confirm whether the planned scope was fully executed, with any deviations explained and approved
Findings are rated using a consistent severity framework (e.g., Critical / High / Medium / Low) that aligns to the system's risk profile, and the rating methodology is documented in the report
The findings register is current and maintained in a GRC or audit management platform, providing a real-time view of outstanding issues by system, severity, age, and responsible owner
Critical and high-severity findings from technology audits have documented management action plans with specific remediation milestones, and evidence of progress tracking against those milestones
Audit reports for Critical systems are presented to the Board Audit Committee within a defined timeframe (e.g., within 30 days of report issuance), as evidenced by committee meeting packs
commonGaps: Audit reports for critical systems are not presented to the Board Audit Committee on a timely basis, or are summarised at such a high level that the committee cannot exercise meaningful oversight
The findings register is maintained in a spreadsheet with no version control, making it difficult to demonstrate point-in-time status during an examination period
Repeat findings (same control weakness identified in successive audits) are present without adequate escalation, suggesting that management responses are superficial
Finding severity ratings are inconsistently applied across business units, with evidence that ratings are negotiated down by system owners rather than independently determined by Internal Audit
Overdue remediation actions lack documented extensions or risk acceptances, indicating a breakdown in the findings management process
suggestedSources: Internal Audit — completed engagement files and issued reports
GRC platform or audit management system — findings register and issue tracking module
Board Audit Committee meeting packs — technology audit summary reports
Management response tracking logs maintained by the relevant technology team
evidenceItems.format: PDF (issued audit reports); Spreadsheet or GRC system export (findings register); Board paper extracts (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Internal Audit Methodology — Technology Audit Standards
evidenceItems.description: The Internal Audit function's documented methodology or charter provisions that govern how technology audit scope, frequency, and intensity are determined, including the risk-based planning framework, criticality tiering criteria, and standards for test depth calibration. This provides the policy foundation that BNM examiners use to assess whether the institution has a structured, repeatable approach to complying with clause 13.1.
whatGoodLooksLike: The methodology explicitly defines a criticality tiering model for technology systems, including the criteria (e.g., revenue impact, regulatory sensitivity, data volume, interconnectivity) and scoring thresholds that determine each tier
Minimum audit frequency requirements are specified per tier (e.g., annual for Critical, biennial for High) and are clearly linked to the risk rationale rather than arbitrary scheduling conventions

The methodology addresses how complexity and sophistication of technology (e.g., bespoke legacy systems, cloud-native architectures, AI/ML models) influence scope design and the required technical competencies of the audit team
The methodology is reviewed and updated at least annually or following significant changes in the technology or regulatory environment, with version history and approval records maintained
The methodology references RMiT and other applicable BNM policy documents as the regulatory basis for technology audit requirements, demonstrating regulatory alignment
commonGaps: The methodology is generic and does not contain technology-specific guidance, requiring auditors to use ad-hoc judgment with no documented standards — a significant red flag for examiners
Criticality tier definitions exist but are not operationalised into concrete audit frequency or intensity requirements, leaving the link between risk and audit planning implicit rather than explicit
The methodology has not been updated to reflect cloud, API-driven, or AI/ML technology environments, meaning it is not fit for purpose for modern system architectures
There is no provision in the methodology for specialist or co-sourced technical audit support, meaning complex systems may receive insufficiently deep scrutiny
The methodology is approved by Internal Audit management only, with no evidence of Board Audit Committee endorsement, limiting its governance standing
suggestedSources: Internal Audit — methodology manual, audit charter, and planning standards documents
Board Audit Committee — approved Internal Audit charter and methodology sign-off
SharePoint or document management system — Internal Audit policy library
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX; formally version-controlled and Board Audit Committee approved

evidenceItems.name: Board Audit Committee Oversight Records — Technology Audit Coverage
evidenceItems.description: Board Audit Committee (BAC) meeting minutes, technology audit summary reports, and annual audit coverage assessments that demonstrate the BAC exercises active oversight of technology audit scope, frequency, and intensity decisions. This satisfies the governance dimension of clause 13.1 by showing that Board-level scrutiny is applied to whether technology audits are commensurate with system risk.
whatGoodLooksLike: BAC meeting minutes reflect substantive discussion of technology audit results, not just noting receipt of reports — including committee questions on scope adequacy, coverage gaps, and findings trends

An annual technology audit coverage report is presented to the BAC, comparing planned versus actual audit coverage, explaining any shortfalls, and confirming that critical systems have been audited at the required frequency
The BAC receives a forward-looking view of the technology audit plan with explicit commentary on how the plan addresses the complexity and criticality profile of the technology estate
BAC minutes document instances where the committee has challenged or directed changes to technology audit scope or frequency, evidencing active rather than passive oversight
The BAC receives reporting on the age and remediation status of critical technology audit findings, with escalation protocols triggered for overdue high-severity issues
commonGaps: BAC minutes record only that technology audit reports were 'noted' or 'received', with no evidence of substantive discussion, challenge, or direction — indicating passive rather than active oversight
Technology audit results are reported to the BAC in aggregate only, with no system-level detail that would allow the committee to assess whether coverage is commensurate with criticality
The BAC is not informed of audit plan deferrals or scope reductions, meaning the committee cannot exercise informed oversight of gaps in technology audit coverage

There is no annual audit coverage assessment presented to the BAC, making it impossible to demonstrate at examination that the Board has visibility of the overall adequacy of technology audit activity

BAC reporting conflates technology audit findings with operational risk events and other issues, making it difficult to isolate and demonstrate the governance oversight trail for technology audits specifically
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — Board Audit Committee meeting minutes (approved and signed)
Internal Audit — BAC presentation packs and technology audit summary papers
Board portal or document management system — BAC agenda packs and resolutions
Annual Report — Internal Audit coverage statement (for publicly listed institutions)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed BAC minutes and presentation packs); retained as board-level records

auditTips: Before examination, reconcile the technology audit universe against the institution's CMDB and RMiT Critical Systems register simultaneously — any system present in one but absent from another is a scope gap that examiners will identify within the first hour of document review.

Prepare a single 'coverage matrix' document that maps each system in the audit universe to its criticality tier, the last audit date, the planned next audit date, and the audit intensity applied — this pre-built artefact answers the core clause 13.1 question directly and signals a mature audit function.

For any critical or high-complexity system that has not been audited within its scheduled frequency, prepare a documented risk acceptance or deferral memo signed by the CAE and acknowledged by the BAC, as unexplained gaps in coverage of high-risk systems are the most common examination finding under this clause.

Cross-reference the intensity of completed audits (auditor days, scope breadth, sample sizes) against the system's criticality tier to confirm that critical systems consistently received deeper scrutiny than lower-risk systems — be prepared to show this comparison to examiners quantitatively.

Review BAC minutes for the past two years and highlight all references to technology audit scope, frequency, and coverage decisions — if substantive discussion is absent, prepare a briefing for the next BAC meeting before examination to demonstrate active oversight and improve the contemporaneous record.

Ensure the Internal Audit methodology document references RMiT explicitly and has been updated within the last 12 months — an outdated methodology that predates current cloud or digital banking environments will be questioned by examiners as evidence that audit scope calibration is not keeping pace with technology sophistication.

Conduct a dry-run walkthrough of the end-to-end evidence chain for one critical system: start with its entry in the audit universe, trace through the audit plan, engagement planning document, completed report, and BAC presentation — if you cannot narrate a complete, coherent story for that system, gaps in your evidence package will surface during examination.

13.2.title: Annual Technology Audit Plan
13.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution's technology audit plan is formally reviewed at least annually and is not merely a static or rolled-over document from prior cycles. Examiners specifically scrutinise whether the plan's scope explicitly addresses all five mandated coverage areas: critical technology services, third-party service providers, material external system interfaces, delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects, and post-implementation reviews (PIRs). A key red flag is an audit plan that lacks a documented rationale for audit frequency — examiners expect risk-based justification, not uniform scheduling. They will also trace whether the plan was approved by the Audit Committee or Board, and whether actual audit execution aligns with the approved plan. Examiners cross-reference the audit plan against the institution's technology risk register, vendor register, and project portfolio to identify coverage gaps — items that appear in those registers but are absent from the audit plan are treated as material non-compliance.

evidenceItems.name: Annual Technology Audit Plan Document
evidenceItems.description: The formally approved, current-year technology audit plan that defines the scope, schedule, audit universe, frequency rationale, and coverage of all mandated areas under Clause 13.2. This document demonstrates that the institution has a structured, risk-based approach to technology auditing and that the plan has been reviewed and approved by appropriate governance bodies.
whatGoodLooksLike: Plan explicitly identifies and maps each of the five mandated coverage categories (critical technology services, third-party providers, material external system interfaces, delayed/terminated projects, PIRs) with named audit engagements assigned to each
Audit frequency for each item is supported by a documented risk-based rationale, referencing the institution's technology risk rating or criticality classification
Document carries formal approval signatures or Board Audit Committee (BAC) resolution with a dated approval, confirming annual review has occurred
Plan version history or change log shows a prior-year plan and the current review cycle, demonstrating continuity and incremental improvement
Resource allocation (internal audit FTEs or co-sourced specialists) and indicative timelines are assigned to each audit engagement within the plan
commonGaps: Plan is a copy of the prior year's document with updated dates but no substantive revision to scope or coverage, with no evidence that a formal review was conducted
Coverage of third-party service providers and material external system interfaces is absent or limited to a single generic 'vendor audit' without identifying specific providers or interfaces
Audit frequency decisions are undocumented — the plan schedules audits annually by default without any risk-based rationale or linkage to criticality ratings
No evidence of BAC or Board approval; plan exists as an internal audit document only, without formal governance sign-off during the review cycle
Delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects are not tracked as a distinct audit category, resulting in missed PIR obligations for cancelled or stalled programmes
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department SharePoint or GRC platform (e.g., TeamMate+, Archer, MetricStream) — Audit Universe and Annual Plan module
Board Audit Committee meeting minutes and resolutions for the relevant approval period
Chief Audit Executive's (CAE) office — formal plan submission package to BAC
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (approved plan) with supporting BAC resolution in PDF

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Universe and Risk-Based Coverage Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured inventory of all auditable technology entities — systems, platforms, vendors, interfaces, and projects — mapped against their criticality rating, last audit date, next scheduled audit, and assigned audit frequency. This demonstrates that the institution has systematically identified its full technology audit universe and applied risk-based frequency to coverage decisions, as required by Clause 13.2.
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit universe is cross-referenced against the institution's approved list of critical technology services (as defined under RMiT Clause 10.1 or equivalent internal policy), with all critical services appearing in the universe

Each auditable entity has a documented criticality or risk rating (e.g., High/Medium/Low) with a corresponding audit frequency (e.g., annual for High, biennial for Medium) and the rationale is traceable to the institution's risk methodology
Third-party service providers in the vendor register are individually listed in the audit universe with audit coverage planned, not aggregated into a single vendor category
Material external system interfaces (e.g., interbank payment gateways, SWIFT, regulatory reporting interfaces) are explicitly listed as distinct auditable entities
Universe is dated and shows a review date within the current annual cycle, confirming it was refreshed as part of the annual plan review
commonGaps: Audit universe is limited to internal systems and does not include third-party providers or external system interfaces as distinct auditable entities
Criticality ratings in the audit universe do not align with the institution's technology risk register or BCP asset classification, suggesting the universe was built in isolation
Last audit date and next scheduled date columns are unpopulated for a significant proportion of entries, making it impossible to assess whether adequate frequency is maintained
Universe has not been updated following the onboarding of new critical systems or material third-party providers, leaving recent additions uncovered
No documented methodology or policy governing how entities are added to, or removed from, the audit universe, raising questions about completeness
suggestedSources: GRC platform audit universe module (e.g., TeamMate+, Galvanize HighBond, SAP GRC)
IT Asset Management (ITAM) system or CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow) — cross-referenced for system completeness
Vendor/Third-Party Risk Management register maintained by Procurement or IT Risk
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (PDF/CSV)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Technology Service Provider Audit Coverage Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that third-party technology service providers — particularly those supporting critical systems — are included within the technology audit plan and have been subject to audit or assessment during the review cycle. This demonstrates compliance with the specific third-party coverage obligation in Clause 13.2 and aligns with the broader third-party risk management framework under RMiT.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined list of in-scope third-party technology providers is maintained, prioritised by criticality, with each provider's audit or assessment status tracked within the current plan cycle

Audit methodologies used for third-party coverage are documented — whether direct on-site audit, review of provider's independent assurance reports (e.g., SOC 2 Type II, ISAE 3402), or questionnaire-based assessments — with clear criteria for selecting the appropriate method

Where third-party assurance reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001 certifications) are relied upon as audit substitutes, the institution has documented a gap analysis confirming the reports adequately cover the institution's specific risk concerns
Findings from third-party audits or assessments are tracked in the institution's issue management system with assigned remediation owners and target dates
Coverage includes cloud service providers, outsourced IT operations, and payment processors, not limited to traditional IT vendors
commonGaps: Institution relies entirely on vendor-provided SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certificates without conducting any independent verification or gap analysis against institution-specific risks
Only Tier 1 or contracted critical vendors are audited; providers supporting material functions through sub-contracting arrangements are not identified or included in scope
Third-party audit results are not consolidated into a single tracking register, making it impossible to demonstrate aggregate coverage at plan review time
Audit rights clauses in vendor contracts are not exercised — the institution has audit rights but has not scheduled or conducted audits for multi-year periods
New third-party providers onboarded during the year are not added to the audit plan until the following annual cycle, leaving a coverage gap during the interim period
suggestedSources: Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform or register (e.g., Prevalent, ProcessUnity, or GRC module)
Procurement/Vendor Management system for contract audit rights clauses
Internal Audit's completed engagement workpapers and third-party assessment reports
IT or Outsourcing Risk team's vendor criticality assessment database
evidenceItems.format: PDF (audit reports, SOC 2 reliance assessments), Spreadsheet (coverage tracker)

evidenceItems.name: Material External System Interface Audit Coverage Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that material external system interfaces — such as interbank payment gateways, regulatory reporting systems, SWIFT connectivity, API integrations with financial market infrastructure, and core banking interfaces — are identified, risk-rated, and included in the annual technology audit plan with appropriate frequency.
whatGoodLooksLike: A maintained inventory of material external system interfaces exists, cross-referenced with the institution's application portfolio and network architecture documentation, with each interface assigned a materiality or criticality rating

The audit plan includes specific audit engagements or reviews covering interface controls, including data integrity checks, access controls, encryption standards, and monitoring mechanisms for each material interface
Interface audit scope addresses both inbound and outbound data flows, with testing of exception handling, reconciliation processes, and failure notification mechanisms
Interface inventory is reviewed as part of the annual audit plan review, with additions or retirements of interfaces reflected in updated coverage
Audit findings specific to external interfaces are tracked separately and escalated appropriately, given the systemic risk implications of interface failures
commonGaps: No formal inventory of external system interfaces exists; the audit plan references 'system integration' generically without identifying specific material interfaces by name or criticality
Interface audits focus exclusively on cybersecurity controls (e.g., firewall rules) without assessing data integrity, reconciliation adequacy, or operational resilience of the interface
Interfaces added through technology projects or new product launches are not captured in the audit plan until the next annual cycle review

Audit coverage of payment system interfaces (e.g., RPP, IBG, FPX) is not coordinated with the institution's payment system risk management function, resulting in duplicated or inconsistent assessments
Interface decommissioning is not audited — legacy interfaces that remain technically active after functional retirement are overlooked as an access control and data leakage risk
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture or IT Architecture team — interface and integration register or API catalogue
Network Operations Centre (NOC) or middleware team — system integration documentation
IT Security team — network segmentation and data flow diagrams
Payment Operations team — payment gateway and clearing system interface documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF (audit reports), XLSX (interface inventory), Architecture diagrams (PDF/Visio)

evidenceItems.name: Delayed and Prematurely Terminated Critical Technology Projects Register and Audit Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution tracks critical technology projects that have been significantly delayed or prematurely terminated, and that these projects are subject to audit or review within the annual technology audit plan. This addresses the specific obligation in Clause 13.2 to audit such projects, which carry heightened residual risk, sunk-cost exposure, and potential control gaps from incomplete implementation.
whatGoodLooksLike: A project portfolio register or PMO dashboard identifies critical technology projects, flags those meeting defined delay thresholds (e.g., >3 months behind baseline schedule) or terminated status, and feeds this list to the Internal Audit function for plan inclusion

The annual audit plan contains a specific workstream for reviewing delayed or terminated critical projects, with defined audit objectives covering control gaps, data disposition, access revocation, licence management, and lessons-learned documentation

For prematurely terminated projects, audit evidence confirms that partially deployed components (e.g., infrastructure, integrations, user accounts) have been properly decommissioned or rolled back, and that residual risks have been formally accepted or mitigated
Delay thresholds and triggers for escalation to the audit plan are defined in policy or the audit plan methodology, removing ambiguity about which projects qualify for mandatory review
Audit findings for delayed/terminated projects are reported to the Project Steering Committee and BAC, with remediation tracked to closure
commonGaps: No defined threshold or policy criterion exists for when a delayed project triggers mandatory audit inclusion — the decision is ad hoc and subject to management discretion

Prematurely terminated projects are removed from the project portfolio without a post-termination audit, leaving control gaps (orphaned accounts, unlicensed software, partially migrated data) undetected
The Internal Audit function does not have systematic access to the PMO's project status reports and relies on management to self-report delays, creating an independence and completeness risk
Audit scope for delayed projects focuses only on timeline and budget variance rather than technology controls, leaving security and data risks unexamined
Projects that are descoped or restructured (rather than formally terminated) are not treated as terminated projects, bypassing the audit trigger
suggestedSources: Project Management Office (PMO) — project portfolio dashboard (e.g., Microsoft Project Online, Jira Portfolio, Clarity PPM)
IT Steering Committee or Technology Committee meeting minutes — project status reporting
Change Advisory Board (CAB) records — project change and closure notifications
Internal Audit's project audit workpapers and findings tracker
evidenceItems.format: PDF (audit reports, project closure reports), XLSX/System export (project register)

evidenceItems.name: Post-Implementation Review (PIR) Audit Coverage Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the annual technology audit plan includes post-implementation reviews for new technology services and material enhancements, and that PIRs have been completed for qualifying implementations during the audit cycle. PIRs validate that technology deployments achieved their intended objectives, controls were correctly implemented, and residual risks from the implementation were addressed.
