whatGoodLooksLike: A defined PIR policy or procedure specifies which technology implementations trigger a mandatory PIR (e.g., new critical systems, material enhancements above a defined change risk threshold) and the required timeframe after go-live (e.g., 3-6 months post-implementation)
The annual audit plan lists specific PIR engagements with named systems, planned review dates, and assigned audit resources, demonstrating forward planning rather than reactive scheduling

PIR audit scope covers: achievement of original business and technical objectives, adequacy of testing conducted pre-go-live, effectiveness of data migration (where applicable), user access provisioning accuracy, and ongoing monitoring control effectiveness
PIR findings are tracked to closure and fed back to the project team and technology risk function, demonstrating a closed-loop improvement process
The list of implementations subject to PIR is derived from the Change Advisory Board (CAB) or Release Management process, ensuring completeness and independence from management self-selection
commonGaps: PIRs are performed by the project team rather than Internal Audit or an independent function, compromising objectivity and conflating project closure activities with independent audit
No formal policy defines which implementations require a PIR, resulting in PIRs being conducted selectively for high-profile projects while material enhancements to existing systems are overlooked
PIRs are scheduled too close to go-live (within 30 days) before operational stability is established, resulting in incomplete evidence of system performance under normal operating conditions
PIR scope does not include a review of whether pre-implementation risk assessments and BIA assumptions proved accurate post-go-live, missing an opportunity for risk calibration
PIR completion rates against the plan are not tracked or reported to BAC, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic coverage across the full year
suggestedSources: Change Advisory Board (CAB) records and Release Management logs — source of truth for qualifying implementations
Internal Audit GRC platform — PIR engagement records and findings tracker
Project Closure Reports maintained by PMO
IT Risk team — post-implementation risk assessment records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (PIR audit reports, project closure reports), Spreadsheet (PIR tracking register)

auditTips: Before the BNM examination, produce a single consolidated evidence pack that maps each of the five mandated coverage categories in Clause 13.2 verbatim text to specific named audit engagements in the current year's plan — this cross-reference table is the single most effective tool for demonstrating systematic compliance and pre-empts the examiner's coverage gap analysis.

Pull the institution's approved critical system list (under RMiT Clause 10.1 or internal IT Risk policy) and reconcile it line-by-line against the technology audit universe — any critical system absent from the audit universe must be documented with a risk-accepted explanation signed by the CAE or CRO, or immediately added to the plan as an unscheduled audit.

Obtain BAC meeting minutes from the annual plan approval meeting and ensure they explicitly record that the Board Audit Committee reviewed and approved the technology audit plan, including acknowledgment of the five mandatory coverage areas — a BAC resolution that only approves the 'Internal Audit Plan' without specific mention of the technology coverage scope is insufficient for examiners.

For third-party coverage, prepare a summary matrix showing each material third-party provider, the audit method used (direct audit, SOC 2 reliance, questionnaire), the date of last assessment, and next scheduled review — where SOC 2 or equivalent reports are relied upon, attach the gap analysis memo confirming their adequacy for the institution's specific risk profile, as examiners will specifically ask for this documentation.

Request a current snapshot from the PMO of all critical technology projects showing status (on-track, delayed, terminated) and verify that any project meeting the institution's delay or termination threshold appears in the audit plan — if delays occurred during the year after the plan was approved, evidence a mid-year plan amendment reviewed by BAC, demonstrating the plan is a living document rather than a static annual artefact.

Compile a PIR completion rate dashboard showing planned PIRs versus completed PIRs for the current audit cycle, with reasons for any deferred PIRs documented and approved by the CAE — a high PIR deferral rate without documented justification is a significant red flag for examiners assessing whether the plan provides 'appropriate frequency and coverage'.

Conduct a dry-run internal walkthrough six to eight weeks before the examination where the compliance or internal audit team presents the technology audit plan and supporting evidence to a senior reviewer acting as examiner — use the verbatim Clause 13.2 text as the interview script, asking whether each mandatory element can be evidenced on demand, and remediate any gaps before the actual examination date.

13.3.title: Technology Audit Competency
13.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the internal audit function maintains genuinely dedicated technology audit resources — not generalist auditors occasionally assigned to IT reviews — and whether those resources hold current, relevant professional certifications. Examiners will probe whether the certifications are commensurate with the institution's actual technology risk profile, particularly where complex systems such as cloud infrastructure, open banking APIs, or AI-driven decisioning are deployed. A key red flag is a mismatch between the institution's stated technology sophistication and the depth of competency evidenced by the audit team's credentials and training records. Examiners also assess whether technology auditors demonstrate working familiarity with the institution's specific delivery channels, core banking platforms, and emerging technologies through audit work papers that reflect substantive technical inquiry rather than checkbox compliance. Institutions that rely heavily on co-sourced or outsourced technology audit capacity without demonstrating adequate internal oversight and knowledge retention will attract heightened scrutiny.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Resource Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured register of all personnel assigned to the technology audit function, documenting their roles, dedicated allocation (full-time vs. co-sourced), years of experience, areas of specialisation, and reporting lines within internal audit. This demonstrates that technology audit resources are formally designated and not ad hoc assignments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register clearly distinguishes dedicated technology auditors from generalist auditors with incidental IT responsibilities, with headcount ratios documented
Each resource entry maps specialisation areas (e.g., cybersecurity, cloud, data governance) to the institution's actual technology risk domains
Co-sourced or outsourced contributors are explicitly identified with scope limitations and oversight arrangements noted
Register is reviewed and signed off by Chief Audit Executive (CAE) at least annually and updated upon any resourcing change
Headcount and skill coverage is benchmarked against peer institutions or industry guidance such as IIA standards, with gaps formally acknowledged and remediated
commonGaps: Register conflates general IT auditors with dedicated technology audit specialists, obscuring true resource adequacy
Co-sourced provider personnel are omitted entirely, making the function appear self-sufficient when it is not
Specialisation fields are left blank or populated with broad labels (e.g., 'IT') that do not map to specific technology risk areas audited
Register is not updated when staff leave or are reassigned, creating a stale record that cannot withstand examination scrutiny
No evidence of CAE sign-off or periodic review, suggesting the register is a point-in-time artefact rather than a living governance document
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department's HR or capacity planning records
GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC) — staff module or engagement assignment records
SharePoint or intranet-based audit function documentation repository
HR system (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors, Workday) for headcount and role classification data
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (PDF for submission)

evidenceItems.name: Professional Certification Records
evidenceItems.description: Verified records of active professional certifications held by technology audit personnel, demonstrating that the function meets the 'professionally certified' obligation under Clause 13.3. Certifications must be current, relevant to technology audit, and commensurate with the institution's technology risk profile.
whatGoodLooksLike: Certification portfolio includes recognised technology audit credentials such as CISA, CRISC, CISSP, CCSP, CEH, or equivalent, with certificate copies and issuing body details retained
Each certification is mapped to the specific audit domains the individual is responsible for (e.g., CCSP mapped to cloud audit engagements)
Renewal and CPE (Continuing Professional Education) compliance records are maintained, confirming certifications are active and not lapsed
Certification requirements are formalised in the Technology Audit function's resourcing policy or charter, establishing minimum standards for all technology auditors
At least one senior technology auditor holds a certification directly relevant to cybersecurity or IT risk given BNM's emphasis on these areas in RMiT
commonGaps: Certifications are held but lapsed due to missed CPE requirements, with no tracking mechanism in place to flag renewals
Certifications are misaligned with audit scope — e.g., a CISA-certified auditor conducting cloud security reviews without cloud-specific credentials
Only one or two auditors are certified, creating key-person dependency that examiners will flag as a resourcing adequacy concern
Certification records are held by HR but not accessible to or reviewed by the CAE as part of audit function governance
Certifications listed are outdated or from non-recognised bodies and do not meet the threshold of 'professionally certified' as understood by BNM
suggestedSources: HR learning and development system (e.g., Cornerstone OnDemand, SAP SuccessFactors) — certification tracking module
Internal Audit department's shared document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
Issuing body verification portals (e.g., ISACA Verify, (ISC)² Member portal) for real-time certificate validation
Annual CPE compliance reports submitted to certification bodies
evidenceItems.format: PDF certificate copies with supporting CPE logs; consolidated summary spreadsheet

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Competency Framework and Training Plan
evidenceItems.description: A documented competency framework that defines the required knowledge, skills, and certification standards for technology audit roles, paired with an annual training and development plan demonstrating how the institution maintains and enhances auditor competency as technology sophistication evolves. This evidences proactive management of the 'adequately conversant' obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Competency framework defines role-specific requirements covering the institution's actual technology environment — including core banking systems, cloud platforms, digital channels, and cybersecurity domains

Training plan is refreshed annually and linked to the institution's technology risk profile, ensuring coverage of emerging areas such as AI governance, open API security, or cloud-native architectures
Completed training records show auditors have attended relevant courses, conferences, or workshops (e.g., ISACA conferences, BNM-organised awareness sessions, vendor-specific training)
Framework includes a gap analysis mechanism that is triggered when new technology systems or delivery channels are onboarded, prompting targeted upskilling
Training investments are reported to the Audit Committee, demonstrating board-level visibility of competency management
commonGaps: Competency framework exists as a generic document not tailored to the institution's actual technology stack, rendering it meaningless for examination purposes
Training plan is aspirational only — records show low completion rates or that planned training was deferred without remediation
Framework is not updated when the institution adopts new technology (e.g., migrating to cloud, launching a mobile wallet), leaving competency requirements misaligned with audit scope
Training is limited to generic IT audit courses and does not address the institution's specific high-risk technology areas identified in the risk assessment
No evidence of Audit Committee oversight of competency development, suggesting it is managed operationally without appropriate governance
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department's annual planning documents and Audit Committee papers
HR learning management system (LMS) — training completion records and course catalogues
GRC platform — competency or skills management module
Chief Audit Executive's annual report to the Board Audit Committee
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (framework document); Spreadsheet or LMS export (training records)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Work Papers Demonstrating Technical Depth
evidenceItems.description: Selected audit work papers from completed technology audit engagements that demonstrate auditors possess sound knowledge of the areas audited, as required by Clause 13.3. Work papers should evidence substantive technical analysis rather than procedural compliance, confirming that auditors understand the systems, delivery channels, and risks they review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Work papers contain technically substantive findings — e.g., specific configuration weaknesses, misaligned access controls, or architectural risks — rather than generic observations lifted from audit templates
Audit programs reference institution-specific technology components (named systems, platforms, and APIs) and demonstrate tailored testing procedures
Findings narratives demonstrate auditor understanding of business context and technology interdependencies, not just checklist adherence

Work papers for delivery channel audits (e.g., mobile banking, internet banking) include technical testing evidence such as API security test results, penetration test review, or infrastructure configuration reviews
Reviewer sign-offs by senior technology auditors are present, evidencing quality assurance and knowledge oversight within the function
commonGaps: Work papers consist primarily of interview notes and document reviews with no technical validation, suggesting auditors lack the competency to test controls directly
Audit programs are generic and not updated to reflect the institution's specific technology environment, indicating a lack of system familiarity
Findings are superficial — limited to policy non-compliance observations without technical root cause analysis — raising doubts about auditor depth of knowledge
Work papers for complex technology areas (e.g., cloud, AI models) are thin or outsourced entirely with no evidence of internal oversight or knowledge transfer
No evidence of work paper review by a qualified senior technology auditor, suggesting quality assurance processes are absent or ineffective
suggestedSources: Internal Audit management system (e.g., TeamMate+, AuditBoard) — completed engagement files
GRC platform — audit findings and evidence repository
SharePoint or network-based audit file archive
Audit Committee reporting packages containing summarised findings from technology audits
evidenceItems.format: System export or PDF (work papers); DOCX (audit reports)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Charter and Function Mandate
evidenceItems.description: The Internal Audit Charter or a dedicated Technology Audit mandate document that explicitly defines the scope, authority, independence, and resourcing requirements of the technology audit function, including the requirement for dedicated resources and professional certifications. This establishes the governance foundation for Clause 13.3 compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter explicitly mandates dedicated technology audit resources and defines minimum certification requirements by role level
Scope of technology audit is defined to encompass all significant technology systems, digital delivery channels, and technology risk domains relevant to the institution
Charter is approved by the Board Audit Committee and reviewed at least annually, with the most recent version bearing current approval signatures
Charter references applicable standards (e.g., IIA Standards, BNM RMiT) and establishes accountability for maintaining auditor competency
Any use of co-sourcing or outsourcing for technology audit is explicitly governed within the charter, including oversight and quality assurance obligations
commonGaps: Charter makes no specific reference to technology audit as a dedicated function — technology is simply subsumed within a general internal audit mandate
Certification and competency requirements are absent from the charter, leaving resourcing standards undefined and unenforceable
Charter has not been reviewed or updated to reflect the institution's evolved technology risk profile (e.g., cloud adoption, digital banking expansion)
Board Audit Committee approval is absent or outdated (more than 12 months without re-affirmation), undermining the governance legitimacy of the document
Co-sourcing arrangements are operational practice but are not referenced or governed within the charter, creating an undocumented dependency
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — Board Audit Committee approved documents repository
Internal Audit department's governance documentation (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform — policy and charter management module
Annual Audit Committee meeting minutes referencing charter approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with Board Audit Committee approval signature page

evidenceItems.name: Annual Audit Committee Reporting on Technology Audit Resourcing
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports presented to the Board Audit Committee that provide visibility of technology audit function resourcing adequacy, competency levels, certification status, and any identified gaps with remediation plans. This evidences board-level oversight of the institution's compliance with the dedicated resourcing and competency obligations in Clause 13.3.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are presented at least annually and include a structured assessment of technology audit headcount, certification coverage, and competency alignment against the institution's technology risk profile
Any resourcing or competency gaps identified during the period are explicitly reported with root cause, impact assessment, and time-bound remediation plans
Reports reference changes in the institution's technology environment (new systems, channels, or risks) and assess whether audit competency has kept pace
Audit Committee minutes reflect substantive discussion of technology audit resourcing — not merely passive receipt — evidencing active board oversight
Reports include forward-looking statements on planned training, certification targets, and co-sourcing arrangements for the coming period
commonGaps: Technology audit resourcing is not separately reported to the Audit Committee — it is embedded within general internal audit updates and receives no dedicated scrutiny
Reports are descriptive but lack gap analysis, giving the Audit Committee no basis to assess whether the function is adequately resourced against technology risk
Audit Committee minutes record receipt of the report but show no substantive discussion, suggesting the board is not actively discharging its oversight obligation
Reports do not track certification renewal status or CPE compliance, leaving the Audit Committee unaware of potential lapses
Remediation plans for identified gaps are not followed up in subsequent reports, making it impossible to demonstrate closure of known deficiencies
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat — Audit Committee meeting packs and approved minutes
Internal Audit department — CAE reporting templates and historical submissions
GRC platform — Audit Committee reporting module
SharePoint governance repository — Board and Committee papers
evidenceItems.format: PDF presentation or DOCX report with accompanying Audit Committee minutes

auditTips: Conduct a certification-to-scope mapping exercise before examination: list every significant technology domain audited in the last 12 months and verify that at least one certified, dedicated technology auditor was responsible for each engagement. Any domain audited by uncertified or generalist staff is an immediate finding risk under Clause 13.3.

Cross-reference the Technology Audit Resource Register against actual audit engagement assignments in the audit management system (e.g., TeamMate+, AuditBoard) to confirm that individuals listed as dedicated technology auditors were the ones who actually led and executed technology audit work — not merely listed as reviewers.

Verify certification currency by running spot checks against issuing body verification portals (ISACA Verify, (ISC)² Member lookup) immediately before examination preparation begins. Lapsed certifications discovered by examiners carry significantly more weight than those proactively disclosed with a remediation plan already in place.

Review the last three technology audit reports submitted to the Audit Committee and assess whether findings reflect genuine technical depth — specific vulnerabilities, named systems, configuration-level observations — rather than generic policy compliance statements. If findings are superficial, engage senior technology auditors to supplement work papers with technical annexures before examination.

Ensure the Technology Audit Charter has been reviewed and approved by the Board Audit Committee within the last 12 months, and that it explicitly references dedicated resourcing and certification requirements. Examiners will request the Charter early in the examination process and treat an outdated or silent charter as a structural governance gap.

Prepare a forward-looking competency gap analysis that maps the institution's technology roadmap (planned system upgrades, cloud migration milestones, new digital channels) against current auditor certifications and training. This demonstrates to BNM examiners that the institution is proactively managing the 'adequately conversant with developing sophistication' obligation rather than reactively responding to gaps.

Where co-sourced or outsourced technology audit resources are used, prepare a clear narrative and supporting documentation showing internal oversight arrangements — including review of co-sourcer work papers, qualification checks on co-sourcer personnel, and knowledge transfer mechanisms. BNM examiners are alert to institutions that have offloaded technology audit capability without retaining meaningful internal oversight competency.

13.4.title: Technology Audit Advisory Role
13.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assessing Clause 13.4 focus primarily on whether the institution has a formal, documented framework that governs when technology auditors may take on advisory or consulting roles, and whether that framework explicitly addresses the independence impairment risk. Examiners will scrutinise instances where an auditor who provided advisory input during planning or development phases subsequently conducted or participated in a post-implementation review of the same product, system, or operation, treating any such overlap as a presumptive independence violation unless the institution can demonstrate a rigorous, documented impairment assessment was performed and appropriate safeguards were applied. Red flags include the absence of a conflict-of-interest register for audit advisory engagements, no evidence that independence assessments were escalated to the Chief Audit Executive (CAE) or Audit Committee, and post-implementation review reports that do not disclose prior advisory involvement. Examiners will also test whether the institution's audit charter or methodology explicitly distinguishes advisory engagements from assurance engagements and prescribes mandatory cooling-off or role-segregation requirements.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Audit Charter — Advisory Role Provisions
evidenceItems.description: The approved internal audit charter or technology audit methodology document that explicitly defines the permissible scope of advisory and consulting roles for technology auditors, establishes conditions under which such roles may be undertaken, and mandates independence impairment assessments before and after advisory engagements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Charter contains a dedicated section distinguishing assurance engagements from advisory/consulting engagements with explicit applicability to technology audit functions.

The charter defines mandatory triggers for independence impairment assessment, including involvement in planning or development of major products, systems, or third-party technology service provider adoptions.
Escalation and approval authorities are named (e.g., CAE sign-off required before advisory role is accepted; Audit Committee notification for material impairment risk).

The charter includes a cooling-off or exclusion requirement specifying that an auditor who materially participated in an advisory role is recused from leading or significantly contributing to the subsequent post-implementation review.
Document is approved by the Audit Committee, version-controlled, and reviewed at least annually with revision history maintained.
commonGaps: Charter addresses advisory roles generically without specific provisions for technology audit contexts (e.g., new system implementations, third-party technology adoptions) as enumerated in the RMiT clause.
Independence impairment criteria are vague — the charter references 'objectivity' without defining what constitutes 'material' impairment or prescribing a structured assessment methodology.
No escalation path is specified; the decision to accept an advisory role is left entirely to the individual auditor without supervisory or committee oversight.
Cooling-off periods or recusal requirements are absent, permitting the same auditor to both advise during development and lead the post-implementation review.
The charter has not been updated to reflect RMiT requirements and pre-dates the institution's current technology operating model (e.g., cloud adoption, fintech partnerships).
suggestedSources: Internal Audit Department — Charter repository (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform document library)
Audit Committee secretariat — Board-approved charter with sign-off evidence
Chief Audit Executive's office — Latest version with amendment log
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (Board/Audit Committee approved)

evidenceItems.name: Advisory Engagement Register — Technology Audit
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register or log maintained by the Internal Audit function that records all instances where technology auditors have been enlisted to provide advisory or consulting input during the planning and development phases of major products, systems, third-party service provider adoptions, or technology operations, including the nature of advice given and the auditors involved.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register captures each advisory engagement with fields for: engagement name/project, nature and scope of advisory input, names and roles of technology auditors involved, dates of advisory participation, and the corresponding assurance/post-implementation review scheduled.
Register is maintained in a GRC or audit management platform (e.g., TeamMate, AuditBoard, ServiceNow GRC) ensuring completeness and tamper-evident records.
Each entry cross-references the independence impairment assessment (see 13.4-E3) performed for that advisory engagement.
Register is reviewed quarterly by the CAE and presented to the Audit Committee at least annually as part of the audit independence reporting.
Advisory engagements covering major third-party technology service provider adoptions are tagged distinctly, enabling targeted review of third-party oversight obligations.
commonGaps: No centralised register exists; advisory engagements are tracked informally through emails or individual auditors' workpapers, making population completeness unverifiable.
Register exists but is inconsistently maintained — entries lack scope descriptions, making it impossible to assess whether advisory input was substantive enough to impair independence.
Advisory engagements with external consultants or co-sourced audit providers are excluded from the register, creating a blind spot for independence risks.
The register is not linked to the audit universe or audit plan, so upcoming post-implementation reviews cannot be screened against prior advisory involvement.
Register is maintained in a spreadsheet with no access controls or version history, undermining its integrity as a governance record.
suggestedSources: Internal Audit Department — GRC platform (AuditBoard, TeamMate+, or equivalent)
SharePoint or Confluence — Audit engagement tracking workspace
Project Management Office (PMO) — Project intake records cross-referenced with audit advisory requests
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (PDF for submission)

evidenceItems.name: Independence Impairment Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented assessments performed by the technology auditor and reviewed by the CAE for each advisory engagement, evaluating whether the nature and extent of advisory or consulting input could materially impair the auditor's independence or objectivity in any subsequent post-implementation review of the same product, system, or operation.
whatGoodLooksLike: A structured assessment template is used for every advisory engagement, covering factors such as: depth of involvement in design decisions, access to sensitive pre-implementation data, degree of influence over control design, and the planned timeline to post-implementation review.
Assessments are completed prior to accepting the advisory role and updated if the scope of advisory involvement changes materially during the engagement.
Each completed assessment is reviewed and signed off by the CAE, with Audit Committee notification documented where material impairment risk is identified.

Where impairment risk is assessed as present, the assessment records the specific safeguard applied (e.g., auditor recusal, supplemental independent reviewer assigned, disclosure in post-implementation review report).
Assessments are retained in the GRC platform or audit workpaper management system and linked to both the advisory engagement record and the subsequent post-implementation review workpapers.
commonGaps: Independence assessments are performed verbally or informally, with no documented output — compliance cannot be evidenced to BNM examiners.
Assessments are performed only at engagement initiation and not revisited when advisory scope expands (e.g., auditor transitions from observing to actively recommending control design).
The assessment methodology conflates independence with objectivity without separately evaluating both dimensions as required by IIA standards and consistent with RMiT expectations.
CAE review and sign-off is absent; assessments are self-certified by the individual auditor with no supervisory oversight.
Assessments do not identify specific safeguards or document why identified risks were deemed non-material, making the rationale for continued auditor involvement unsubstantiable.
suggestedSources: Internal Audit Department — Workpaper management system (TeamMate, AuditBoard, Caseware)
CAE's office — Independence declaration files
GRC platform — Linked to advisory engagement register (13.4-E2)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (signed assessment template with CAE approval notation)

evidenceItems.name: Post-Implementation Review Reports — Independence Disclosures
evidenceItems.description: Technology audit post-implementation review (PIR) reports for major products, systems, or third-party technology service provider adoptions that transparently disclose any prior advisory or consulting involvement of the audit team in the subject matter, and that document the safeguards applied to protect independence and objectivity.
whatGoodLooksLike: PIR reports contain a mandatory independence statement section that explicitly discloses whether any member of the audit team provided advisory input during the planning or development phase of the subject system, product, or operation.

Where prior advisory involvement is disclosed, the report details the nature of that involvement, the independence impairment assessment outcome, and the specific safeguards applied (e.g., recusal, supplemental reviewer, scope limitation).
Reports are reviewed by a senior auditor or CAE with no prior advisory involvement in the subject engagement before issuance.
Distribution of PIR reports includes the Audit Committee, with the independence disclosure section prominently highlighted for board-level governance visibility.
PIR reports cross-reference the advisory engagement register entry and impairment assessment (13.4-E2, 13.4-E3) in their working papers, enabling an end-to-end audit trail.
commonGaps: PIR reports contain no independence statement or disclosure section, making it impossible to determine whether prior advisory involvement was considered.
Disclosure is present but generic ('auditors maintained independence throughout') without specifying the advisory engagements evaluated or safeguards applied.
The same lead auditor who provided substantive advisory input on control design conducts and signs off the PIR without any disclosure, recusal, or compensating control.
PIR reports are not provided to the Audit Committee in cases where prior advisory involvement existed, depriving the board of material governance information.
Working papers for PIR engagements do not link back to the advisory engagement register, preventing examiners from tracing the full independence governance trail.
suggestedSources: Internal Audit Department — Completed PIR workpapers and issued reports (TeamMate, AuditBoard, SharePoint)
Audit Committee secretariat — Minutes and report distribution records confirming PIR reports were tabled
GRC platform — Linked audit engagement records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (issued report) with supporting DOCX workpapers

evidenceItems.name: Audit Committee Reporting on Advisory Engagements
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports presented to the Audit Committee that summarise technology audit advisory engagements undertaken during the reporting period, independence impairment assessments performed, safeguards applied, and any instances where prior advisory involvement affected the conduct or scope of subsequent post-implementation reviews.
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit Committee papers include a standing agenda item or periodic report specifically covering technology audit advisory engagements and independence management.
Reports provide a summary of all advisory engagements in the period, cross-referenced to the advisory engagement register, with an explicit statement on independence status for each.
Any instance of assessed material impairment risk and the safeguard applied is reported individually to the Audit Committee with management commentary.
The Audit Committee minutes record that members reviewed, questioned, and accepted the independence reporting, demonstrating active board-level governance oversight.

Annual reporting includes a trend analysis of advisory engagements versus assurance engagements, enabling the Audit Committee to monitor the proportion of advisory activity and its independence implications over time.
commonGaps: Advisory engagements are not reported to the Audit Committee at all; independence governance is managed entirely within the Internal Audit function without board-level visibility.
Audit Committee papers mention advisory activity in passing within broader CAE reports without sufficient detail for the committee to assess independence adequacy.
Minutes do not reflect substantive Audit Committee discussion or challenge on independence matters, suggesting the reporting is pro forma rather than genuinely scrutinised.
Reporting is annual rather than periodic, creating extended windows where undisclosed independence impairment risks could affect assurance quality without committee awareness.
Reports do not distinguish technology audit advisory engagements from other advisory activities, obscuring the technology-specific independence risks relevant to RMiT compliance.
suggestedSources: Audit Committee secretariat — Board paper archives and meeting minutes
CAE's office — Periodic independence and advisory activity reports
Board secretariat system (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardPad) — Committee paper repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF (committee papers) and PDF (certified meeting minutes)

evidenceItems.name: Technology Auditor Training Records — Independence and Advisory Role Obligations
evidenceItems.description: Training completion records demonstrating that technology auditors have received adequate training on the obligations, risks, and governance requirements associated with undertaking advisory or consulting roles, including the requirement to assess independence impairment and the conditions under which recusal or disclosure is required.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training records show completion of a module specifically covering the distinction between assurance and advisory roles, independence impairment assessment, and obligations under IIA standards and RMiT Clause 13.4.
Training is refreshed at least annually and updated when material changes occur to the institution's advisory role policy or relevant regulatory guidance.
New technology auditors complete independence and advisory role training as part of their onboarding programme before being eligible to participate in any advisory engagement.

Training content includes worked case studies specific to technology audit advisory scenarios (e.g., advising on controls for a new core banking system, reviewing a third-party cloud provider's security architecture).
Training completion is tracked in the HR or Learning Management System (LMS) with individual completion certificates or timestamps, enabling population-level compliance verification.
commonGaps: Training on advisory role obligations exists only at onboarding and has not been refreshed to reflect current RMiT requirements or the institution's updated advisory role policy.
Training covers independence concepts generically without technology audit-specific scenarios, leaving auditors without practical guidance for the situations enumerated in Clause 13.4.
Training records are maintained in disparate systems (paper certificates, email confirmations) rather than the LMS, making population completeness difficult to evidence.
Senior or co-sourced technology auditors are excluded from mandatory training requirements, creating a gap for the most experienced individuals who are most likely to be enlisted for advisory roles.
No assessment or competency check is performed post-training, so records show attendance but not comprehension of independence obligations.
suggestedSources: HR Learning Management System (LMS) — Training completion records
Internal Audit Department — Onboarding checklists and CPD logs
Third-party training providers — Certificates of completion for external IIA or independence training
evidenceItems.format: LMS system export (PDF/CSV) or individual training certificates (PDF)

auditTips: Before the examination, run a reconciliation between the advisory engagement register (13.4-E2) and all post-implementation reviews completed in the examination period: for every PIR, confirm whether any participating auditor is listed in the register as having provided advisory input on that same system or product. Any overlap without a documented impairment assessment and safeguard is an immediate finding — resolve these gaps before the examiner identifies them.

Request the institution's Audit Committee minutes for the past two to three years and verify that advisory engagements are explicitly reported and discussed, not merely embedded in omnibus CAE activity reports. BNM examiners will read minutes for evidence of genuine committee oversight, not just information receipt — look for recorded questions, challenges, or decisions from committee members on independence matters.

Test the independence impairment assessment process by selecting two or three advisory engagements from the register and tracing end-to-end: confirm the assessment was completed before the advisory role commenced (check dates), that the CAE signed off, and that the subsequent PIR report either discloses the prior involvement or confirms the involved auditor was recused. Incomplete chains at any step indicate a control breakdown.

Examine the technology audit charter specifically for the cooling-off or recusal provisions: these are the most commonly missing element in Malaysian financial institutions' audit charters. If the charter is silent on recusal timelines or conditions, raise this as a design gap to be remediated before the examination — a verbal assurance from the CAE that recusal is applied in practice will not satisfy BNM examiners without written policy support.

For third-party technology service provider adoptions specifically, cross-reference advisory engagements against the institution's vendor onboarding register or TPSA (Third-Party Service Agreement) register. Advisory involvement in vendor selection or due diligence is a particularly sensitive independence impairment trigger — ensure each such engagement has a documented assessment addressing whether the auditor's subsequent ability to independently review the vendor relationship is preserved.

Review the independence disclosure language in the most recent three to five PIR reports before the examination. Disclosures should be specific and substantive — flag any report that uses boilerplate language ('the audit team maintained independence throughout') without referencing the advisory engagement assessment or safeguards applied, as BNM examiners will treat vague disclosures as equivalent to no disclosure.

Verify that co-sourced or guest technology auditors (external consultants brought in to supplement the internal audit function) are covered by the same advisory role governance framework as internal auditors. Institutions frequently apply independence controls only to employees, leaving co-sourced arrangements unregulated — if the charter or policy does not explicitly extend to co-sourced resources, recommend an immediate policy amendment and document any retrospective assessments for current co-sourced engagements.

14.1.title: Data Centre Resilience Assessment
14.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will focus on whether the financial institution has appointed a technically competent external service provider to conduct the DCRA, scrutinising the provider's qualifications and independence from the institution. Examiners will verify the assessment frequency — confirming it was performed within the last three years and that any material infrastructure changes triggered an earlier reassessment — and will look for documented evidence of scope covering all major risks and paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28. For third-party-managed data centres, examiners will assess whether reliance on independent assurance reports is documented, justified against the institution's risk appetite, and whether those reports demonstrably cover equivalent risks. A critical red flag is the absence of a board-level committee deliberation record on the DCRA outcome, as this is an explicit governance obligation that examiners will trace from the DCRA report through to board committee minutes.

evidenceItems.name: DCRA Engagement and Provider Qualification Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing the appointment of the external DCRA service provider, including the provider's professional credentials, technical competency evidence, and contractual engagement terms. Demonstrates that the institution performed due diligence in selecting a technically qualified and independent assessor.
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed engagement letter or statement of work explicitly scoping the DCRA against RMiT requirements including paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28
Provider profile or credentials package evidencing relevant industry certifications (e.g., Uptime Institute, TÜV, BICSI RCDD) and prior DCRA experience
Vendor due diligence record confirming the provider's independence from the financial institution and absence of conflicts of interest
Internal approval documentation (e.g., technology procurement committee sign-off) authorising the appointment of the specific provider
Clear delineation in the scope document of which data centre sites are covered, distinguishing between institution-owned and third-party-managed facilities
commonGaps: Provider appointed without documented competency assessment — credentials on file are generic corporate profiles rather than role-specific technical qualifications
Engagement scope does not explicitly reference RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28, making it impossible to confirm regulatory coverage during examination
No independence or conflict-of-interest declaration from the provider, raising questions about objectivity of the assessment
Procurement records exist but internal approval for the specific DCRA appointment is missing or misfiled outside the compliance evidence pack
Third-party-managed data centres are excluded from the engagement scope without documented justification or alternative assurance arrangement
suggestedSources: Vendor Management System or Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform (e.g., ServiceNow VRM, Ariba)
Technology Procurement or Sourcing department contract repository
GRC platform vendor register (e.g., MetricStream, RSA Archer, Compliance.ai)
SharePoint or document management system under IT Risk or Technology Governance folder
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed contract/engagement letter), PDF/DOCX (provider credentials package), PDF (internal approval memo)

evidenceItems.name: Production Data Centre Resilience and Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: The full DCRA report produced by the appointed external service provider, documenting all major risks identified, the assessed current resilience level of the production data centre, and findings against the requirements in RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28. This is the primary substantive evidence of compliance with the assessment obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report explicitly maps findings to each of RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28, with a compliance status (met, partially met, not met) for each sub-requirement
Assessment covers all major risk categories relevant to data centre resilience, including physical security, power, cooling, network connectivity, fire suppression, and structural integrity
Report contains a clear overall resilience rating or maturity score with supporting evidence and methodology explanation
Findings section distinguishes between critical, high, medium, and low-risk observations with specific remediation recommendations and suggested timeframes
Report is dated and signed by the lead assessor, with the assessment scope, methodology, and site visit dates clearly stated
commonGaps: Report does not explicitly reference or map to RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28, requiring examiners to manually infer coverage — a significant examination risk
Assessment covers only a subset of the institution's production data centres (e.g., primary site only) without documented rationale for excluding DR or secondary sites
Risk assessment is high-level and qualitative without sufficient technical depth to substantiate the resilience rating assigned
Report is more than three years old and no material change assessment was performed to determine whether an earlier reassessment was required
Remediation recommendations from the prior DCRA cycle have not been tracked to closure, with no evidence of follow-up action
suggestedSources: IT Risk or Technology Governance team document repository (SharePoint, Confluence)
GRC platform risk assessment module (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow IRM)
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or Chief Technology Officer (CTO) office records
Internal Audit shared drive for externally commissioned reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF (final signed report), with supporting appendices including site photographs, test results, and technical data

evidenceItems.name: DCRA Assessment Frequency and Trigger Log
evidenceItems.description: A record demonstrating that the DCRA has been conducted at least once every three years and that a process exists to identify and respond to material changes in data centre infrastructure that would trigger an earlier assessment. Demonstrates compliance with the frequency obligation and the material-change threshold.
whatGoodLooksLike: A schedule or register showing the dates of all DCRA assessments conducted, confirming no gap exceeds three years between successive assessments

A defined and documented procedure or policy specifying what constitutes a 'material change' in data centre infrastructure (e.g., major power upgrades, cooling system replacement, new cage or cage expansion, change in Tier classification)
Change management records or IT infrastructure change log entries showing that each material change was evaluated against the DCRA trigger criteria
Evidence that at least one material change evaluation was documented and a formal decision (triggered reassessment or documented rationale for non-trigger) was recorded
Upcoming DCRA scheduled date is tracked in a compliance calendar or GRC platform with ownership assigned
commonGaps: No formal definition of 'material change' exists, meaning the institution cannot demonstrate consistent application of the trigger threshold across infrastructure change events
Change management process does not include a DCRA trigger assessment step, so material changes are processed without compliance review
Three-year schedule is tracked informally (e.g., individual email reminders) rather than in a system that provides audit-trail evidence
Prior DCRA was commissioned based on elapsed time only, with no documented review of whether earlier material changes should have prompted an earlier assessment
DCRA schedule shows a gap exceeding three years between the most recent assessment and the prior one, with no remediation or explanation on file
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM, Jira Service Management)
GRC platform compliance calendar or obligation register
Data Centre Operations team infrastructure change log
Technology Risk team compliance tracking spreadsheet or dashboard
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (assessment schedule), PDF/DOCX (material change definition policy), ITSM system export (change records with DCRA assessment field)

evidenceItems.name: Board-Level Committee Deliberation Record on DCRA Outcome
evidenceItems.description: Minutes or resolution records of the designated board-level committee (as specified in RMiT paragraph 8.3) documenting its deliberation of the DCRA outcome. This evidence item demonstrates fulfilment of the explicit governance escalation obligation and that the board has oversight of data centre resilience risks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board committee minutes clearly identify the agenda item as the DCRA outcome, reference the specific assessment report by title and date, and record substantive discussion of key findings and risks
Minutes record the committee's deliberation on remediation actions, including assigned ownership, target dates, and any resource commitments approved
The deliberating committee is confirmed as the designated board-level committee per paragraph 8.3 (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Board IT Committee), not a management-level forum
Minutes are signed or formally approved, with attendance records confirming quorum and participation of board members rather than management delegates only
Where the DCRA identified critical or high-risk findings, minutes reflect proportionate board scrutiny and documented decisions rather than a mere acknowledgement
commonGaps: DCRA outcome was presented to and deliberated by a management-level committee (e.g., Technology Risk Committee) rather than the designated board-level committee, failing the explicit paragraph 8.3 requirement
Board committee minutes record only that the DCRA report was 'noted' or 'received', with no evidence of substantive deliberation, discussion, or decision-making
Time gap between completion of the DCRA report and board committee deliberation exceeds the next scheduled meeting cycle, suggesting deliberation was not prioritised
Minutes do not reference the DCRA report by name or date, making it impossible to trace the deliberation back to the specific assessment without additional investigation
Remediation actions agreed at board level are not subsequently tracked in a formal action register, severing the governance chain from deliberation to resolution
suggestedSources: Company Secretary office — board and board committee minutes repository
Board governance portal (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect, SharePoint board committee folder)
Chief Risk Officer or Technology Risk team records for board paper submissions
GRC platform board reporting module
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed/approved board committee minutes), PDF (board paper or management report submitted to committee as supporting document)

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Data Centre Independent Assurance Reliance Assessment
evidenceItems.description: For data centres managed by third-party service providers, documentation demonstrating that the financial institution has assessed any independent third-party assurance reports relied upon, confirmed their equivalence to DCRA requirements, and validated consistency with the institution's risk appetite and tolerance. This evidence is required only where the institution relies on third-party assurance in lieu of commissioning its own DCRA for a hosted facility.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal reliance assessment document for each third-party data centre, mapping the third-party assurance report's scope and findings against RMiT paragraph 14.1 DCRA requirements and paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28
Risk appetite and tolerance statement that explicitly addresses reliance on third-party assurance for data centre resilience, with an approved threshold for acceptable residual risk
Gap analysis identifying any areas where the third-party assurance report does not cover equivalent risks, with documented compensating controls or escalation decisions
Evidence that the third-party assurance report is from an independent assessor (not the data centre operator's internal team), with the assurance provider's credentials verified
Annual or periodic review record confirming the third-party assurance remains current, valid, and sufficient — including confirmation that the report has not lapsed beyond its stated validity period
commonGaps: Institution relies on the third-party provider's SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 certificate without performing a gap analysis to confirm coverage of RMiT DCRA-specific requirements and paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28

Risk appetite statement does not address third-party data centre assurance reliance specifically, making it impossible to demonstrate that reliance is consistent with the institution's approved tolerance
Third-party assurance report is relied upon despite being more than 12 months old or outside its stated validity window, with no refresh or escalation on file
Reliance decision was made informally by the technology team without documented approval from Technology Risk or the Risk Management function
No record exists of the institution ever requesting or reviewing the third-party assurance report — reliance is assumed based on contractual provisions rather than actual evidence review
suggestedSources: Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform — vendor assurance report repository
IT Risk team or Outsourcing Risk team records for hosted/cloud data centre providers
Legal and vendor contracts repository for third-party data centre SLAs and assurance provisions
Risk Management function — risk appetite framework and tolerance register
evidenceItems.format: PDF (reliance assessment memo or report), PDF (third-party assurance report copy), PDF/DOCX (gap analysis), PDF (risk appetite extract or board-approved tolerance statement)

evidenceItems.name: DCRA Remediation Action Plan and Closure Evidence
evidenceItems.description: A tracked action plan derived from the DCRA findings, documenting remediation actions assigned to owners with target completion dates, status updates, and evidence of closure for completed items. Demonstrates that the institution acts on DCRA outcomes rather than merely commissioning assessments for compliance optics.
whatGoodLooksLike: Action register maps each DCRA finding to a specific remediation action, with a named owner, priority rating consistent with the finding's risk severity, and a documented target completion date
Progress updates are recorded at regular intervals (e.g., monthly for critical/high findings) with evidence of substantive progress rather than status labels alone
Closed actions are supported by closure evidence (e.g., updated configuration records, test results, completed works orders) reviewed and signed off by the action owner and a second-line reviewer
Overdue or re-scoped actions are escalated through a defined governance process, with documented approvals for revised timelines rather than informal extensions
The action register is presented as a standing agenda item to the designated board-level committee, linking DCRA governance to ongoing remediation oversight
commonGaps: DCRA findings are acknowledged in the board committee minutes but no formal action register is established, leaving remediation untracked and unverifiable
Action register exists but closure dates are repeatedly extended without escalation or documented risk acceptance, indicating systemic non-remediation rather than genuine progress
Actions are assigned to teams rather than named individuals, diffusing accountability and making it impossible to confirm ownership during examination
Closed items lack supporting closure evidence — status is marked 'complete' based on owner self-attestation without independent verification or documentary proof
Action register is not linked back to the specific DCRA report cycle, so it is unclear which assessment cycle generated which findings, hindering trend analysis across successive DCRAs
suggestedSources: GRC platform issue and action management module (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow IRM, MetricStream)
IT Risk team action tracker (SharePoint list, Jira project, or dedicated risk register)
Internal Audit follow-up tracking system for externally commissioned assessment findings
Board committee secretariat records for management updates presented on DCRA remediation progress
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export (action register with status history), PDF (closure evidence attachments), PDF (board committee papers showing remediation updates)

auditTips: Trace the complete governance chain from DCRA report to board deliberation: obtain the DCRA report, identify its completion date, then locate the corresponding board committee minutes and confirm the deliberation occurred at the next scheduled meeting. Any unexplained time gap or absence of substantive discussion in the minutes is a finding against the explicit board governance requirement.

Verify provider technical competency specifically for DCRA work — do not accept a general IT security or consulting firm's credentials at face value. Request the CVs or certification records of the individuals who actually conducted the on-site assessment and confirm their data centre-specific qualifications (e.g., Uptime Institute Accredited Tier Designer, BICSI RCDD) are current and valid as at the assessment date.

Cross-reference the DCRA scope document against the institution's actual production data centre footprint — obtain the IT asset register or infrastructure topology diagram and confirm every production data centre site is either covered in the DCRA scope or has a documented third-party assurance reliance assessment. Undocumented sites are an automatic gap.

For the three-year frequency obligation, do not only check whether the most recent DCRA falls within three years of today. Also check the gap between the current and immediately preceding DCRA to confirm continuity, and review the change management records for the intervening period to confirm no material infrastructure changes occurred that should have triggered an earlier assessment.

When reviewing third-party data centre reliance, request both the third-party assurance report and the institution's internal gap analysis. Perform your own high-level mapping of the report's scope against RMiT paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28 and compare it to the institution's analysis — institutions frequently claim equivalence without performing a genuine gap assessment.

Interview the Technology Risk or IT Risk Manager to test whether the institution's definition of 'material change' is operationalised. Ask for the last three significant infrastructure changes at each data centre and trace whether they were evaluated against the DCRA trigger criteria. A lack of documented evaluations, or evaluations performed only retrospectively, indicates the process exists on paper only.

Confirm that the board committee deliberating the DCRA outcome is the specific committee designated under paragraph 8.3, not a substitute or delegated management body. Request the board governance framework or terms of reference for the committee that received the DCRA report and verify its board-level status — a management IT steering committee presenting to the board does not satisfy the deliberation requirement.

14.2.title: Network Resilience Assessment
14.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners scrutinise whether the institution has genuinely engaged an independent, technically qualified external party rather than relying on internal teams or affiliated vendors with conflicts of interest. Examiners will verify the three-year cadence is being adhered to and will specifically probe whether material network changes triggered an out-of-cycle assessment, looking for evidence of a formal change-trigger mechanism rather than ad hoc decision-making. A critical focus area is whether the NRA scope explicitly covered paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43 requirements — examiners cross-reference the assessment report against those specific network security controls to confirm coverage depth. Red flags include board committee minutes that are perfunctory or show no substantive deliberation on assessment findings, assessments that pre-date major infrastructure migrations, and controls that cannot be demonstrably traced to the institution's documented risk appetite.

evidenceItems.name: External Assessor Appointment and Competency Record
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that a technically competent external service provider was formally engaged to conduct the NRA, including evidence of their qualifications, independence, and the contractual scope of the engagement. This establishes the institution satisfied the 'technically competent external' requirement before the assessment commenced.
whatGoodLooksLike: Signed engagement letter or statement of work explicitly scoping NRA coverage including paragraphs 10.36–10.43 of RMiT and risk appetite alignment
Vendor due diligence record including relevant certifications (e.g., CREST, CHECK, ISO 27001 lead auditor, OSCP-credentialed staff) and professional indemnity insurance confirmation
Independence declaration or conflict-of-interest attestation signed by the external assessor confirming no commercial relationship with the institution's network vendors
Internal vendor selection evaluation showing at least one alternative was considered, with scoring against technical competency criteria
Third-party assurance register entry linking the vendor to this engagement, with periodic review date
commonGaps: Assessor credentials are listed generically on a CV without mapping specific team member qualifications to the NRA scope — examiners expect individual-level competency evidence, not just firm-level accreditations
Engagement scope in the contract omits explicit reference to RMiT paragraphs 10.36–10.43, making it impossible to demonstrate the assessment was designed to meet the regulatory requirement
Vendor is an affiliate or subsidiary of a major network equipment supplier used by the institution, creating an independence concern with no mitigating disclosure or waiver
No evidence of vendor selection rationale — institution simply reappointed the previous assessor without a documented competency review against current network complexity
Contract predates the assessment by more than 12 months with no refreshed scope or addendum, leaving unclear whether the engagement covered material network changes that occurred in the interim
suggestedSources: Procurement or vendor management system (e.g., SAP Ariba, Coupa) for signed contracts and vendor evaluation scorecards
GRC platform (e.g., RSA Archer, ServiceNow GRC) third-party assurance module for vendor register entries
Legal/Contracts SharePoint repository for executed engagement letters and statements of work
Compliance or IT Risk team's vendor due diligence folder for certification and independence records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (contract, SOW, due diligence report); Spreadsheet (vendor scoring matrix)

evidenceItems.name: Network Resilience and Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: The formal, final NRA report produced by the external assessor covering all major network risks, current resilience levels, and adherence to the specific requirements in RMiT paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43. This is the primary substantive evidence of clause compliance and will receive the most intensive scrutiny from examiners.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report contains a dedicated section explicitly mapping findings to each of paragraphs 10.36–10.43, with a compliance rating (e.g., compliant, partially compliant, non-compliant) and supporting technical evidence for each paragraph

Risk assessment methodology is documented, clearly explaining how major risks were identified and scored, with the institution's network topology (including third-party connectivity and cloud segments) fully reflected in the scope

Current resilience level is expressed against a recognised framework or quantified metric (e.g., NIST CSF maturity tiers, network redundancy metrics, RTO/RPO validation results) rather than a generic narrative
Report includes a prioritised remediation roadmap with risk-ranked findings, responsible owners, and target completion dates that the institution has accepted and tracked
Assessment date and version control are clearly stated, and the report is addressed to the institution (not a generic template), with assessor firm and lead assessor name visible
commonGaps: Report is structured as a generic penetration test or vulnerability scan output rather than a holistic resilience and risk assessment — network availability, redundancy, and capacity risks are absent

Coverage of paragraphs 10.36–10.43 is incidental rather than deliberate — examiners find the report addresses some of those requirements but the assessor was not explicitly tasked with evaluating them
Resilience determination is purely qualitative with no quantification, leaving BNM examiners unable to assess proportionality of controls or improvement over prior assessment cycles
Material network segments such as cloud environments, inter-branch WAN links, or third-party API gateways are excluded from scope without documented justification
Report is not final — institution presents a draft with tracked changes and no sign-off from the assessor, making it inadmissible as formal evidence of completed assessment
suggestedSources: IT Risk or Cyber Security team's secure document repository (SharePoint with restricted access) for the final signed report
GRC platform for linked risk register entries referencing NRA findings
Project management tool (Jira, Azure DevOps) for remediation tracking tickets linked to the report's findings
External assessor's delivery portal or secure file transfer for the original unedited report
evidenceItems.format: PDF (final signed report); Spreadsheet (findings tracker / paragraph-level compliance matrix)

evidenceItems.name: NRA Schedule and Material Change Trigger Log
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution operates a formal mechanism to maintain the three-year assessment cycle and to identify material network design changes that would trigger an out-of-cycle NRA. This evidences that the 'whichever is earlier' obligation is operationalised and not left to ad hoc judgement.
whatGoodLooksLike: A rolling NRA schedule approved by the CISO or equivalent, showing the last assessment date, next scheduled date, and confirmation the interval does not exceed three years, embedded in the institution's IT assurance calendar

A defined and documented threshold for what constitutes a 'material change in network design' (e.g., addition of new data centre, adoption of SD-WAN, significant cloud migration, acquisition integration) approved by the IT risk committee

A change trigger log or register linked to the Change Advisory Board (CAB) process, where each significant network change is assessed against the materiality threshold with documented rationale for the trigger/no-trigger decision

Evidence that at least one out-of-cycle or cycle-advanced NRA was initiated in response to a material change, or documented confirmation that no qualifying change occurred during the period under review
Integration with the institution's ITSM change management workflow so that network design changes automatically flag for NRA trigger assessment by the IT Risk team
commonGaps: No documented definition of 'material change' exists — the institution relies on informal judgement, meaning BNM examiners cannot confirm the trigger mechanism functions as intended
The NRA schedule exists but is only in the CISO's spreadsheet with no formal approval or integration into the enterprise IT risk assurance programme, indicating it is not institutionalised

A major network re-architecture (e.g., full SD-WAN deployment or hybrid cloud migration) occurred during the three-year window but no out-of-cycle NRA was triggered, and there is no documented assessment of whether a trigger was required
Assessment dates show a gap slightly exceeding three years (e.g., 37–38 months) with no documented reason for the delay, which constitutes a direct breach of the frequency requirement
Change trigger reviews are conducted verbally at CAB meetings with no written record of the trigger/no-trigger decision for individual network changes
suggestedSources: IT Risk or Cyber Security team's assurance calendar (SharePoint or GRC platform)
ITSM tool (ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) CAB minutes and change records for network-category changes
Technology governance committee minutes referencing NRA scheduling decisions
IT architecture review board records for major network design change approvals
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform record (schedule and trigger log); PDF (CAB minutes, committee approvals)

evidenceItems.name: RMiT Paragraphs 10.36–10.43 Compliance Assessment Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured mapping artefact — either produced by the external assessor or validated by the institution — that demonstrates explicit coverage and evaluation of each sub-requirement in paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43 as part of the NRA. This ensures examiners can trace every specific network security control requirement to a documented assessment outcome.
whatGoodLooksLike: A clause-by-clause compliance matrix with columns for the paragraph reference, requirement summary, assessment finding, compliance status, supporting evidence cited, and remediation action if applicable
Each of paragraphs 10.36 through 10.43 is individually addressed with a substantive finding — not a blanket 'compliant' without supporting rationale
The matrix is cross-referenced to specific pages or sections of the NRA report so examiners can verify the matrix accurately reflects the assessor's conclusions
Non-compliant or partially compliant items are linked to dated remediation plans with owner accountability, and the matrix is versioned to show subsequent compliance uplift
The matrix was reviewed and formally accepted by the institution's CISO or IT Risk Director, evidencing internal validation rather than sole reliance on the assessor's output
commonGaps: The NRA report addresses network security broadly but paragraphs 10.36–10.43 are never individually cited — the institution cannot demonstrate the specific RMiT requirements were evaluated rather than general best practices

Matrix exists but was self-completed by the institution's internal team using the NRA report as a secondary source, with no external assessor validation — examiners may not accept this as satisfying the 'external assessment' requirement for those paragraphs
Several paragraphs within 10.36–10.43 are marked 'not applicable' without documented justification, and examiners find the exclusions are not defensible given the institution's network architecture
Compliance status for multiple paragraphs is marked 'in progress' with remediation dates that have already passed at the time of examination, indicating open compliance gaps with no updated tracking
Matrix is version-controlled but the most recent version predates a material network change, meaning the paragraph-level compliance posture has not been re-evaluated against the current network state
suggestedSources: GRC platform compliance module where RMiT clause responses are maintained (RSA Archer, MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC)
External assessor's deliverable appendix within the NRA report
IT Risk team's SharePoint compliance evidence folder
Regulatory compliance tracking tool maintained by the Compliance or Legal department
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (compliance matrix with cross-references); PDF (if embedded in NRA report as an annex)

evidenceItems.name: Board-Level Committee Deliberation Minutes and Action Tracking
evidenceItems.description: Minutes of the designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 (typically the Board Risk Committee or Board IT/Technology Committee) demonstrating that the NRA outcomes were formally tabled, deliberated — not merely noted — and that resulting actions were tracked to closure. This satisfies the governance escalation obligation at the top of the clause's accountability chain.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board committee minutes explicitly identify the NRA as a standing agenda item, include a summary of key findings and the current resilience level as presented, and record questions, challenge, and discussion from committee members — not simply 'noted and received'
The specific committee is identifiable as the paragraph 8.3 designated body, either by reference in its terms of reference or a cross-reference in the board governance framework
Minutes record the committee's formal acceptance or rejection of the risk appetite alignment proposed by management in response to NRA findings, demonstrating substantive deliberation
A board action log or dashboard shows NRA-derived remediation items with board visibility, updated status as at each subsequent meeting, and formal closure notation when completed
The meeting at which NRA was deliberated occurred within a reasonable timeframe after receipt of the final report (typically within one to two board meeting cycles), not months or years later
commonGaps: Minutes record the NRA as 'presented and noted' with no evidence of substantive discussion, challenge, or decision — examiners treat this as a procedural pass-through rather than genuine deliberation

The committee that reviewed the NRA is the Management Technology Committee, not the board-level committee designated under paragraph 8.3, meaning the governance obligation was not satisfied at the correct level
NRA findings were reported to the board via a dashboard summary that omitted critical or high-rated findings, meaning the committee deliberated on an incomplete picture
There is a lag of more than six months between receipt of the final NRA report and the board committee meeting at which it was deliberated, with no documented reason for the delay
Board action log does not track NRA remediation items, or items are tracked but show persistent overdue status with no board-level escalation or re-prioritisation decision recorded
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat for board and board committee minutes and resolutions (restricted access)
Board portal (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardEffect) for board papers and pre-read packs tabled at the relevant committee meeting
GRC platform action tracking module linked to board committee decisions
CISO or CRO office for the board risk reporting pack that accompanied the NRA presentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed and certified board committee minutes); Spreadsheet or GRC export (board action log)

evidenceItems.name: Risk Appetite Alignment and Proportionate Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the controls implemented following the NRA are explicitly calibrated against the institution's documented risk appetite, demonstrating that the 'set proportionate controls aligned with its risk appetite' obligation was met rather than treating the NRA as a pure compliance checklist exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: The institution's technology or cyber risk appetite statement contains specific, quantified network resilience metrics (e.g., maximum tolerable network downtime, acceptable percentage of critical links without redundancy) against which NRA findings are benchmarked

A post-NRA controls uplift plan shows each new or enhanced control is explicitly mapped to a risk appetite threshold it is designed to maintain or restore, with a cost-benefit justification for proportionality

Where the NRA identifies risks that management proposes to accept rather than remediate, there is a documented risk acceptance record approved by the appropriate authority that references the risk appetite statement and is time-bound

The risk appetite statement was reviewed or reaffirmed by the board committee at the same meeting at which NRA outcomes were deliberated, demonstrating the two are treated as connected governance activities

Post-implementation review evidence (e.g., updated vulnerability metrics, redundancy test results) demonstrates that implemented controls achieved the intended resilience improvement consistent with risk appetite targets
