commonGaps: Risk appetite statement exists but contains only qualitative language (e.g., 'low tolerance for network outages') with no quantified thresholds, making it impossible to objectively assess whether controls are proportionate

NRA remediation plan and risk appetite statement are managed by different teams in separate documents with no documented linkage — controls were selected based on assessor recommendations rather than against the institution's own risk appetite

Multiple high-rated NRA findings have been formally risk-accepted but the acceptance approvals are signed at management level below the authority required by the institution's risk delegation framework

Risk appetite for network resilience has not been reviewed since the previous NRA cycle, meaning the current NRA was benchmarked against thresholds that may no longer reflect the institution's actual risk profile or strategic direction

Controls implemented following the NRA were not proportionate — the institution adopted all assessor recommendations without a cost-benefit or risk appetite calibration, which while not unsafe may indicate the institution does not genuinely understand its own risk posture
suggestedSources: Board-approved Risk Appetite Statement document (held by Chief Risk Officer or Enterprise Risk Management team)
GRC platform risk register for network resilience risk categories and appetite thresholds
IT Risk team's post-NRA remediation tracking plan with risk appetite cross-references
Technology Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee papers where risk appetite for technology is reviewed
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (risk appetite statement, risk acceptance records); Spreadsheet (controls mapping against appetite thresholds)

auditTips: Before the examination, prepare a single-page NRA timeline that plots every assessment date, the corresponding material change trigger evaluations, and the board committee deliberation date — examiners appreciate being able to confirm the three-year frequency and governance chain at a glance without excavating multiple documents.

Cross-reference the NRA report's table of contents against paragraphs 10.36–10.43 line by line and produce a gap-fill memo before examination if the report does not explicitly cite those paragraphs; bring both the original report and the memo to demonstrate you identified and addressed the coverage gap internally.

Verify that the board committee referenced in your NRA governance workflow is the exact entity designated under paragraph 8.3 of RMiT — misidentifying the deliberating body (e.g., using the Management Risk Committee instead of the Board Risk Committee) is a common finding that invalidates the governance trail regardless of how thorough the assessment was.

Review the external assessor's credentials at the individual consultant level, not just the firm level — compile a brief competency evidence pack (CVs, certifications, project experience relevant to Malaysian financial sector networks) for each assessor who participated, as examiners may request this to substantiate 'technically competent' status.

Audit the material change trigger log against the institution's CAB records and IT architecture review board approvals for the period under review; identify any network changes (cloud migrations, WAN re-architecture, new data centre additions) where the trigger assessment decision is not documented, and prepare a retrospective rationale before the examiner finds the gap independently.

Confirm that board committee minutes for the NRA deliberation session reflect genuine engagement — if the minutes only say 'noted', obtain the board paper that was tabled and the pre-read risk summary as supplementary evidence of substantive deliberation, as these demonstrate the committee was adequately informed even if the minutes are terse.

Ensure the remediation actions arising from the NRA are tracked in a system with an auditable history (GRC platform or ITSM tool) rather than a standalone spreadsheet; be prepared to demonstrate to examiners that overdue items have documented extension approvals with risk-based justifications, as unexplained overdue items are treated as open compliance gaps.

15.1.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Education
15.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a structured, institution-wide cybersecurity awareness programme that is conducted at minimum annually and demonstrably reaches every staff member regardless of role or seniority. A key examiner priority is evidence that programme content is actively updated to reflect the evolving threat landscape — static, unchanged curricula across successive years is a significant red flag. Examiners will scrutinise the effectiveness measurement methodology, looking for quantifiable metrics (assessment scores, phishing simulation results, incident attribution data) rather than attendance registers alone. Institutions are also assessed on whether role-based differentiation exists, ensuring that staff in higher-risk functions receive appropriately targeted training. Finally, examiners will verify whether material third-party service providers with access to institutional systems or data are incorporated into the training perimeter as required under paragraph 10.48(i).

evidenceItems.name: Cybersecurity Awareness Programme Policy and Annual Training Plan
evidenceItems.description: The governing policy document and annual training calendar that establishes the institution's mandatory cybersecurity awareness programme, defining scope (all staff and applicable third parties), minimum frequency, content update obligations, and ownership. Demonstrates that the institution has formally committed to the regulatory requirement and planned execution accordingly.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly mandates at minimum annual completion for all staff categories and references RMiT paragraph 15.1 and 10.48(i) as regulatory drivers
Annual training plan includes dated delivery windows, responsible owners, target audience segmentation by role or risk profile, and designated content review cycles

Policy contains a documented process for incorporating emerging threats into curriculum — including trigger events (e.g., significant industry incidents, new BNM advisories) that prompt out-of-cycle updates
Policy has been formally approved by the Board or senior management (CISO/CTO level) and version-controlled with a current review date within the past 12 months
Third-party inclusion criteria are explicitly defined, referencing categories of service providers within scope consistent with paragraph 10.48(i) obligations
commonGaps: Policy exists but does not define how or when content is updated to reflect the evolving threat landscape, leaving currency of training content undemonstrated
Training plan covers employees only and makes no reference to third-party service providers, creating a gap against the 10.48(i) cross-reference obligation
Policy has not been reviewed or re-approved within the past 12 months, suggesting the programme governance is not actively maintained
Role-based differentiation is absent — the plan treats all staff identically with no escalated content for privileged users, IT staff, or senior management
Policy owner is not clearly designated, making it difficult for examiners to establish accountability for programme delivery and measurement
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, LogicGate) — policy repository module
SharePoint or institutional intranet document management system under Information Security or Compliance folders
CISO or Cybersecurity function — annual operating plan or programme charter documents
Board/EXCO secretariat — approved minutes or resolutions referencing the programme
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version history; supplemented by calendar export (PDF or spreadsheet) for the annual training plan

evidenceItems.name: Training Curriculum and Content Version History
evidenceItems.description: The actual training materials, course content, and a documented history of revisions made to reflect the evolving cyber threat landscape. Demonstrates that curriculum is not static and that the institution actively monitors the threat environment to keep awareness content current and relevant.
whatGoodLooksLike: Curriculum version log shows at minimum one substantive content update per annum, with change notes citing specific threat developments (e.g., incorporation of AI-generated phishing, ransomware trends, BNM or CyberSecurity Malaysia advisories)

Content covers topics commensurate with current threat landscape — including phishing, social engineering, credential hygiene, mobile device security, and incident reporting procedures — not limited to generic IT hygiene
Role-specific modules exist for higher-risk groups such as IT administrators, finance staff, senior management, and remote workers, with content tailored to their threat exposure
Training materials reference or incorporate threat intelligence inputs — such as Cyber999 alerts, BNM cybersecurity circulars, or internal incident learnings — as evidence of real-world relevance
Content has been reviewed by a subject matter expert (internal SOC team or external cybersecurity provider) and sign-off is documented with date and reviewer identity
commonGaps: Curriculum content is identical across two or more consecutive years with no documented rationale, indicating the institution has not reflected the evolving threat landscape
Training covers only basic password hygiene and email safety but omits current high-prevalence threats such as business email compromise, deepfake social engineering, or supply chain attacks

No role-differentiation in content — privileged IT administrators receive the same awareness module as general staff, with no additional depth on topics like insider threat, privileged access misuse, or secure coding awareness
Version history is absent or maintained informally in email threads rather than a structured document management system, making audit trail reconstruction unreliable
Content review is not supported by any threat intelligence input — updates are driven by administrative schedules rather than the observed threat landscape
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) such as Cornerstone OnDemand, SAP SuccessFactors Learning, or Moodle — course content and version archives
Cybersecurity awareness vendor platform (e.g., KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness, Cofense) — module library with release and update logs
CISO or Security team SharePoint site — curriculum design documents and review records
Threat intelligence team or SOC — documented feed of threat advisories reviewed during curriculum refresh cycles
evidenceItems.format: LMS export (PDF/spreadsheet for version log); training slide decks or e-learning packages (SCORM/PDF); content review sign-off sheet (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Staff Training Completion Records
evidenceItems.description: System-generated records confirming that all staff have completed the mandatory cybersecurity awareness training within each required annual cycle. Demonstrates institution-wide reach of the programme and enables examiners to identify coverage gaps by business unit, grade, or staff category.
whatGoodLooksLike: Completion report is drawn directly from the LMS and includes employee ID, name, department, role, training module title, completion date, and assessment score — enabling cross-referencing against the HR staff register
Overall completion rate meets or exceeds the institution's defined target (typically 95–100%) within the annual cycle deadline, with documented escalation records for non-completions
Records reflect tracking across all staff categories including permanent employees, contract staff, secondees, and — where applicable — third-party personnel with system access
Historical completion records are available for at least the prior three annual cycles, enabling examiners to assess consistency and trend over time
Non-completion follow-up process is evidenced — escalation emails, manager notifications, and HR consequence management records demonstrate that the institution enforces completion obligations
commonGaps: Completion records exist only in aggregate (e.g., '92% completion') with no individual-level data, making it impossible for examiners to verify coverage or identify which functions have persistent non-compliance
Records are limited to permanent employees and exclude contractors, temporary staff, or third parties who nonetheless have access to institutional systems or data
The completion deadline extends beyond 12 months from the previous cycle, creating a gap in the annual cadence required by the clause
No evidence of follow-up action for non-completions — the institution records the shortfall but cannot demonstrate it was remediated or that consequences were enforced
LMS records and HR headcount records have not been reconciled, leaving uncertainty about whether the denominator (total staff in scope) is accurate
suggestedSources: LMS platform — completion and assessment reports exportable by period, department, and employee group
HR Information System (HRIS) such as SAP HR, PeopleSoft, or Workday — staff headcount register for reconciliation
GRC platform — compliance tracking dashboard if LMS data is integrated
Business unit coordinators or HR business partners — escalation correspondence records for non-compliant staff
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX/CSV) system export from LMS; supplemented by PDF summary report signed off by programme owner

evidenceItems.name: Programme Effectiveness Measurement Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the methodology and results used to measure the effectiveness of the cybersecurity awareness and education programme. Demonstrates that the institution goes beyond delivering training to actively assessing whether it produces meaningful behavioural or risk-reduction outcomes, as explicitly required by the clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: Effectiveness measurement framework is formally documented and uses multiple indicators — including pre/post knowledge assessment scores, phishing simulation click rates, simulated vishing or pretexting exercise results, and incident reporting volumes attributable to staff vigilance

Phishing simulation results show a statistically meaningful trend over successive cycles (e.g., declining click-through rates) and are reported at department or business unit granularity to enable targeted remediation

Annual effectiveness summary report is produced, reviewed by senior management or the Board Risk Committee, and informs curriculum adjustments for the following cycle — demonstrating a closed feedback loop
Repeat offenders or high-risk staff identified through simulations are enrolled in targeted remediation training, and completion of that remediation is tracked and documented
Baseline metrics are established at programme inception and tracked year-on-year, allowing examiners to observe whether the programme produces measurable improvement over time
commonGaps: Effectiveness measurement is limited to pass/fail assessment scores at training completion with no behavioural indicators — the institution cannot demonstrate whether the training changed staff behaviour
Phishing simulations are conducted but results are not linked back to the training programme or used to update curriculum content, creating a disconnect between measurement and improvement
Simulation and assessment results are reported only in aggregate without business unit breakdown, preventing identification of high-risk pockets within the institution

No annual effectiveness report is produced — data is collected but never synthesised into a formal review presented to management, so the institution cannot evidence that measurement outputs drive programme decisions
Measurement methodology has not changed in multiple years and does not account for new attack vectors, meaning the institution is measuring staff resilience against outdated threat scenarios
suggestedSources: Phishing simulation platform (e.g., KnowBe4, Cofense, Proofpoint) — campaign result dashboards and historical exports
LMS — pre- and post-assessment score records per training module and cohort
Security Operations Centre — incident log data filtered for staff-reported phishing or social engineering attempts
CISO or Security Awareness programme owner — annual effectiveness review reports submitted to management or Board Risk Committee
GRC platform — risk metric dashboards if awareness effectiveness KPIs are tracked therein
evidenceItems.format: PDF report (annual effectiveness review); spreadsheet (simulation result data); system-exported dashboards (PDF/PNG) from simulation or LMS platforms

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Service Provider Training Records
evidenceItems.description: Records evidencing that material third-party service providers have been incorporated into the institution's cybersecurity awareness training programme where appropriate, as required by the cross-reference to paragraph 10.48(i). Demonstrates that the institution's security awareness perimeter extends beyond direct employees to encompass vendors with relevant access or risk exposure.
whatGoodLooksLike: A defined list of third-party service providers assessed as requiring inclusion in awareness training exists, with documented criteria for inclusion (e.g., access to production systems, handling of customer data, on-site presence)

Training completion records for in-scope third parties are maintained separately but with equivalent rigour to staff records — capturing vendor name, individual participant identity, training module, and completion date
Contractual clauses with in-scope third parties require cybersecurity training participation and allow the institution to verify compliance — copies of relevant contract provisions are available

Where the institution delivers training directly to third-party personnel, session attendance registers or LMS guest access records are available; where vendors self-certify, formal attestation letters are on file
Annual review of the in-scope third-party list is conducted to account for new vendor relationships, changed access profiles, or terminated contracts — evidenced by a dated review record
commonGaps: No formal criteria or list exists for determining which third parties are 'appropriate' to include — inclusion decisions are ad hoc and undocumented, making it impossible to demonstrate proportionate and risk-based scoping
Third-party training records consist only of vendor self-declarations with no supporting evidence (e.g., attendance lists, platform certificates), which BNM examiners will treat as unverifiable
Contracts with key technology service providers do not include clauses requiring cybersecurity awareness training participation, undermining the institution's ability to enforce or verify compliance
The in-scope third-party list has not been reviewed following significant vendor relationship changes — new high-access vendors onboarded during the year are absent from training records
Third-party training content is generic and not tailored to the specific risks associated with the vendor's access profile or the institution's environment, reducing its practical effectiveness
suggestedSources: Vendor management or Third-Party Risk Management (TPRM) platform — vendor register and risk tier classification
Procurement or Legal department — signed vendor contracts with cybersecurity training obligations
CISO or Security Awareness team — third-party training completion tracking spreadsheets or LMS guest accounts
GRC platform — third-party risk assessments that document access scope and training requirements
Business relationship managers — vendor attestation letters or evidence collected during annual vendor reviews
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (third-party training register); PDF (vendor attestation letters, contract excerpts); LMS export for guest participant records

evidenceItems.name: Management Reporting and Programme Governance Records
evidenceItems.description: Board and senior management reporting records demonstrating that cybersecurity awareness programme performance is subject to active oversight at the appropriate governance level. Demonstrates that the programme is not managed in isolation but is integrated into the institution's broader technology risk governance framework.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or equivalent receives at minimum an annual update on the cybersecurity awareness programme covering completion rates, effectiveness metrics, and planned improvements — with meeting minutes confirming discussion and any directions issued
Management reporting includes a clear escalation pathway — programme metrics are reviewed at CISO or CTO level on a defined frequency (e.g., quarterly) before being summarised for Board reporting

Programme budget allocation is documented and proportionate to the institution's risk profile and staff population, evidencing that the institution has committed adequate resources as required by the 'adequate' standard in the clause

Remediation actions arising from effectiveness measurement (e.g., curriculum updates, targeted retraining) are tracked through a formal action register with owners and due dates, visible in governance records

Governance records show the programme has been benchmarked against industry standards or peer institutions (e.g., through BNM JCCC participation, financial sector ISACs, or external assessment) to validate its adequacy
commonGaps: Cybersecurity awareness programme performance is reported to IT management only and has never been presented to the Board or Board Risk Committee, indicating insufficient senior oversight of a mandatory regulatory obligation

Management reports present only completion percentages without linking programme outcomes to the institution's broader cyber risk profile or incident trends, limiting their utility for governance decisions
No action register exists for programme improvement items — recommendations arising from effectiveness reviews are noted in minutes but not formally tracked to closure
Budget allocation for the awareness programme is not documented or is bundled within a general IT operating budget, making it impossible to demonstrate that 'adequate' resources have been committed

Governance records do not show any external benchmarking or independent validation of the programme's adequacy, which examiners may use to question whether the institution's self-assessment of adequacy is objective
suggestedSources: Board and Board Risk Committee secretariat — approved minutes, board papers, and action trackers
CISO or CTO office — management dashboard reports and quarterly security reviews
Finance or IT procurement — budget allocation records for cybersecurity awareness activities
GRC platform — programme action register and risk treatment tracking
External training or assessment providers — benchmarking reports or third-party programme assessment findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF (board papers and approved minutes); DOCX/PDF (management reports); spreadsheet (action register); PDF (budget allocation summary)

auditTips: Reconcile LMS completion records against the current HR headcount register before the examination — examiners frequently request a reconciliation report to verify that the denominator used for completion percentages reflects the true staff population, including contractors and secondees. Unexplained gaps between headcount and training records are an immediate red flag.

Prepare a threat landscape justification document for each annual curriculum review — for every content update made to the training materials, maintain a one-page rationale citing the specific threat developments (BNM cybersecurity advisories, Cyber999 alerts, high-profile industry incidents) that prompted the change. This directly substantiates the 'evolving cyber threat landscape' obligation in the verbatim text.

Map your third-party training coverage to the TPRM risk tier register and be ready to walk examiners through the scoping rationale — examiners will ask why certain vendors were or were not included. Having a documented, risk-based criteria matrix (access type, data classification, on-site presence) demonstrates proportionate and deliberate scoping rather than arbitrary inclusion.

Do not rely solely on assessment pass rates as your effectiveness evidence — prepare a multi-metric effectiveness pack that combines phishing simulation trend data, year-on-year knowledge assessment improvements, and incident reporting statistics. BNM examiners are sceptical of programmes where 'effectiveness' is defined as 100% completion of a multiple-choice test with a low pass mark.

Verify that the annual training cycle cadence is strictly maintained — calculate the exact gap between successive completion deadlines across the past three years and confirm no cycle exceeded 12 months. Where a cycle boundary was missed (e.g., due to a system migration), prepare a management-approved rationale and evidence of compensating controls implemented during the gap period.

Cross-reference governance records to ensure the programme has a visible Board-level audit trail — retrieve Board Risk Committee minutes for each of the past three years and confirm that cybersecurity awareness programme performance was formally tabled, discussed, and minuted. The absence of Board visibility on a mandatory clause is consistently cited as a significant finding by BNM examiners.

Engage your external auditor or a specialist cybersecurity awareness assessor to conduct a pre-examination independent review of programme adequacy — having a dated third-party assessment report on file demonstrates that the institution's assertion of 'adequate' training is not self-referential. This also signals to examiners that senior management exercises genuine oversight rather than treating the programme as a checkbox exercise.

15.2.title: Continuous Training for Technology Staff
15.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has a structured, ongoing training programme specifically targeting staff in technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management — not generic staff-wide awareness training. Examiners will scrutinise whether training is role-appropriate, whether completion is tracked with evidence of actual attendance and assessment outcomes, and whether staff hold current and relevant industry certifications proportionate to their responsibilities. A key red flag is training that is ad hoc, infrequent, or limited to annual e-learning modules without deeper technical content. Examiners also look for a competency framework or role-based skills matrix to confirm the institution can demonstrate a systematic approach to identifying training needs and closing skill gaps, rather than simply ticking a compliance box.

evidenceItems.name: Technology Staff Training Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A formal, Board- or senior management-approved policy document that mandates continuous training for technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management staff, including defined training frequencies, minimum hours, role categorisation, and accountability ownership.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly names the three staff groups covered: technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management, with role examples for each
Minimum annual training hours or CPD points are specified per role category, with a separate higher threshold for cybersecurity and critical technology roles
Approval signatures from Board IT Committee or CIO/CISO are present, with a clear review cycle (at least annual) and evidence of most recent review date
Policy references a competency framework or skills matrix that drives training needs identification and curriculum selection
Escalation and consequence clauses are included for staff non-compliance with mandatory training requirements
commonGaps: Policy covers general staff awareness training but does not differentiate obligations for technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management roles specifically
No minimum training hours or frequency requirements are defined, leaving 'adequate and continuous' open to interpretation
Policy has not been reviewed or updated within the past 12 months, predating significant changes in the threat landscape or technology stack
Ownership of training compliance monitoring is ambiguous — HR, IT, and Risk all disclaim responsibility, resulting in enforcement gaps
Policy exists but is not operationalised; there is no linked training calendar, budget allocation, or tracking mechanism referenced
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or internal policy management system) for the approved policy document
SharePoint or intranet policy repository under the IT Governance or HR Policy section
Board/IT Steering Committee meeting minutes approving the policy
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Role-Based Competency and Training Needs Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A documented competency framework or skills matrix that maps specific technical competencies to each technology, cybersecurity, and risk management role, and records periodic training needs assessments (TNA) conducted to identify individual skill gaps requiring remediation.
whatGoodLooksLike: Competency matrix covers all in-scope roles with clearly defined required skills, proficiency levels, and certification expectations per role tier (e.g., analyst, engineer, manager)
Annual TNA records show structured assessments conducted by line managers, with individual gap analyses documented and signed off by both staff member and supervisor
TNA outputs are directly linked to individual training plans or learning agreements for the following period, demonstrating a closed-loop process
Cybersecurity roles reference industry frameworks such as NIST NICE or CREST to anchor competency definitions to recognised standards
Matrix is version-controlled and reflects current role structure, including any new roles arising from cloud adoption, OT security, or third-party risk management
commonGaps: Competency framework exists at a high level but does not break down to individual role granularity, making it impossible to demonstrate targeted training decisions
TNA process is informal or conducted verbally during appraisals, with no documentary evidence of gap identification or agreed remediation actions
Matrix has not been updated to reflect new technology platforms or emerging risk domains (e.g., AI governance, cloud security) introduced in the past 12–18 months
Assessment is self-reported by staff without manager validation or independent verification, undermining objectivity
No linkage between TNA findings and the subsequent training budget allocation or course selection, breaking the audit trail
suggestedSources: HR Learning Management System (LMS) such as SAP SuccessFactors, Cornerstone OnDemand, or equivalent, for TNA records and individual development plans
IT/Cybersecurity department SharePoint site for the skills matrix spreadsheet or competency register
Annual performance appraisal documentation held by HR or line managers
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / PDF

evidenceItems.name: Annual Training Plan and Calendar for Technology Staff
evidenceItems.description: A structured annual training plan that identifies planned training programmes, course titles, target audiences, delivery modes, and schedules for the current and prior year, demonstrating that training provision is systematic and forward-planned rather than reactive.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training plan is segmented by staff group (technology operations, cybersecurity, risk management) with separate tracks and curricula relevant to each group's responsibilities
Plan covers both foundational training (role onboarding) and advanced/specialised topics (e.g., threat hunting, cloud security architecture, regulatory risk modelling) to demonstrate depth
Budget allocation is documented alongside the plan, evidencing that the institution has committed financial resources sufficient to execute the programme
Training plan is reviewed and updated at mid-year to incorporate emerging threats, regulatory changes, or lessons learned from incidents, with a version history
Delivery mix includes internal workshops, external vendor-led courses, industry conferences, and self-paced platforms, reflecting a varied and comprehensive approach
commonGaps: Training plan is a generic HR calendar not tailored to technology-specific roles; cybersecurity and risk management staff share the same plan as all other departments
Plan covers only mandatory compliance modules (e.g., AML, PDPA awareness) with no technical cybersecurity or technology operations content
No evidence that the plan was actually followed — courses listed were not delivered, dates were not met, and there is no record of deferral or rescheduling decisions
Plan is reactive rather than planned — training is only scheduled after incidents or audit findings, with no proactive calendar for the year
Training budget is not documented or is demonstrably insufficient relative to the size and complexity of the technology and cybersecurity workforce
suggestedSources: HR or Learning & Development department's annual training calendar, typically maintained in the LMS or a shared SharePoint drive
IT/Cybersecurity department budget documents and procurement records for external training vendors
CISO or CIO office for the cybersecurity-specific training roadmap
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / PDF

evidenceItems.name: Training Completion and Attendance Records
evidenceItems.description: System-generated and manually maintained records demonstrating that individual staff in technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management have completed assigned training, including attendance logs, assessment scores, certificates of completion, and overall completion rate reports.
whatGoodLooksLike: LMS-generated completion reports show training completion rates by department and role, with timestamps, staff IDs, and pass/fail outcomes for assessed modules
External training attendance is supported by certificates of completion or attendance letters from course providers, filed against individual employee records
Assessment results where training includes a knowledge test are retained, showing that staff demonstrated understanding rather than merely clicking through content
Completion reports are reviewed by line managers and CISO/CIO on a quarterly basis, with evidence of management sign-off and follow-up for non-completions
Records distinguish mandatory training (100% completion required) from elective development training, with escalation records for staff who failed to complete mandatory courses
commonGaps: Records exist in the LMS but are not reconciled against the full headcount of in-scope staff, leaving it unclear whether all technology and cybersecurity personnel are captured
External training certificates are held informally by individual staff members and not centrally filed or tracked, creating gaps in the institutional record
Completion rate is reported as a headline percentage but underlying data is not available to verify which specific staff completed which courses
No evidence that management reviewed completion reports or took action on non-completions; training records are generated but not actively governed
Records cover the current year only; prior year records have been archived or deleted, making it impossible to demonstrate 'continuous' training over time
suggestedSources: LMS (e.g., Cornerstone, Moodle, TalentLMS) training completion reports exported by HR or IT Training team
HR personnel files for externally issued certificates and attendance letters
CISO office or IT Governance team for cybersecurity-specific training dashboards
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / System export (PDF/CSV)

evidenceItems.name: Professional Certification Register for Technology and Cybersecurity Staff
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register documenting current professional certifications held by technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management staff, including certification name, issuing body, issue date, expiry date, and renewal status, demonstrating that staff are 'suitably certified' per the clause requirement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register covers all in-scope staff and lists role-relevant certifications such as CISSP, CISM, CEH, CISA, CRISC, CompTIA Security+, AWS/Azure security certifications, and CREST qualifications as appropriate to role
Expiry dates are tracked with automated or calendar-based alerts at 90 and 30 days prior to expiry, and renewal records show that certifications are maintained without lapse
Register is cross-referenced to the competency matrix, confirming that certification requirements per role are being met at the individual level
Institutional funding or sponsorship for certification examinations and renewals is documented, demonstrating organisational commitment to maintaining certified staff
Senior cybersecurity and technology leadership roles are held by staff with at least one recognised advanced certification commensurate with their seniority and scope of responsibility
commonGaps: No centralised register exists; certifications are self-reported on CVs or LinkedIn profiles with no institutional verification or tracking of expiry
Register exists but is not maintained — multiple certifications shown as current have since expired, and renewals have not been tracked or funded by the institution
Certifications held are not mapped to role requirements; staff hold certifications from previous roles that are irrelevant to current responsibilities, inflating the apparent certification coverage
Only senior staff (managers and above) hold certifications; hands-on technical staff in operations and cybersecurity roles hold none, despite being directly involved in critical technology functions
Certification register is not reviewed by management; no one has accountability for ensuring role-relevant certifications are maintained across the department
suggestedSources: HR information system (HRIS) or personnel files for individual certification records and institutional funding approvals
IT/Cybersecurity department SharePoint or GRC platform for the centralised certification register
Certification issuing body portals (e.g., (ISC)² portal, ISACA portal) for verification of active certifications
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet / PDF

evidenceItems.name: Training Effectiveness Review and Programme Improvement Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of periodic reviews assessing the effectiveness of training delivered to technology, cybersecurity, and risk management staff, including post-training evaluations, knowledge retention assessments, linkage to incident metrics, and evidence that programme improvements are actioned based on findings.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-training evaluations (Kirkpatrick Level 1 and 2 at minimum) are collected for all significant training programmes, with aggregate results reviewed by management and used to inform curriculum decisions
Effectiveness is measured beyond satisfaction scores — knowledge retention tests administered 30–90 days post-training demonstrate sustained competency improvement
Annual training programme review report documents lessons learned, changes made to curriculum or delivery based on evaluation data, staff performance data, or incident trends
Training gaps identified through post-incident reviews (e.g., after a security incident or technology failure) are formally linked back to the training programme with documented remediation actions
Board IT Committee or senior management receives an annual summary of training programme effectiveness, demonstrating governance-level oversight of staff competency
commonGaps: Training evaluation is limited to post-session satisfaction surveys ('happy sheets') with no assessment of actual knowledge acquisition or behavioural change
Evaluation data is collected but not analysed or actioned — there is no evidence that any training course was modified or discontinued based on poor evaluation results
No linkage between technology or cybersecurity incidents and training programme adjustments; incidents are managed operationally without feeding into competency improvement
Effectiveness reviews are conducted informally or verbally by the training coordinator, with no documented output or management sign-off
No evidence that Board or senior management receive reporting on training effectiveness, indicating a gap in governance oversight of the staff competency programme
suggestedSources: LMS evaluation and survey modules for post-training feedback data
IT Risk and Cybersecurity team for post-incident review reports that reference training actions
Board IT Committee or Risk Committee meeting packs for management reporting on training effectiveness
HR Learning & Development team for annual training programme review reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Spreadsheet

auditTips: Reconcile the training completion register against the full list of staff in technology operations, cybersecurity, and risk management roles before the examination — BNM examiners will request a headcount of in-scope staff and will cross-check it against completion records to identify anyone who fell through the gap, including contractors and secondees with system access privileges.

Prepare a one-page summary matrix showing the three staff groups named in the clause (technology operations, cybersecurity, risk management), the number of staff in each group, the training programmes assigned to each group in the past 12 months, completion rates, and average assessment scores — this allows examiners to quickly validate systemic coverage rather than reviewing individual records piecemeal.

Verify that all certifications listed in the certification register are currently valid by checking issuing body portals immediately before the examination; an expired certification presented as current is a significant finding that undermines confidence in the institution's controls and suggests the register is not actively managed.

Cross-reference training content against the actual threat landscape and technology environment — if the institution operates cloud infrastructure or OT environments, examiners expect to see cloud security or OT-specific training in scope; generic on-premises cybersecurity training alone will not satisfy 'adequate' training for environments where those topics are irrelevant.

Ensure that training records for the past three years are accessible and not archived in a system that requires lengthy retrieval — BNM examiners may request historical records to validate 'continuous' training rather than accepting only the current year's evidence, and an inability to produce prior years' records promptly signals a records management weakness.

Prepare evidence that management actively governs training compliance, not just HR — produce examples of management escalation or follow-up actions taken against staff who did not complete mandatory training on time, as this demonstrates accountability and distinguishes a genuinely managed programme from one that simply runs without oversight.

Link at least one training programme update to a specific trigger (e.g., a change in the threat landscape, a BNM circular, a technology incident, or a post-incident review finding) to demonstrate that the training programme is dynamic and responsive rather than static — examiners responding positively to evidence that the institution treats training as a living control rather than a fixed annual obligation.

15.3.title: Board Training on Technology Developments
15.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether board-level technology training is substantive, structured, and recurring — not merely ceremonial or ad hoc. Examiners will scrutinise whether training content covers material technology risks relevant to the institution (e.g., cloud migration, AI/ML adoption, cyber threats, operational resilience) rather than generic IT awareness. A key red flag is a training register showing infrequent or irregular sessions, or where attendance records reveal that a significant proportion of board members consistently miss sessions. Examiners will also probe whether training outcomes demonstrably inform board deliberations — by cross-referencing board minutes for evidence of technology topics being raised, challenged, or actioned by members. Institutions that conflate management-level briefings with board-specific training, or that cannot produce a forward training calendar approved by the board itself, are likely to attract findings.

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Training Policy and Framework
evidenceItems.description: A board-approved policy or framework document that mandates regular technology training for board members, defines minimum frequency, scope of topics, and accountability for delivery. Demonstrates that the obligation is institutionalised and not ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references RMiT obligations and names the board or a board committee as the accountable body for oversight of training delivery
Minimum training frequency is defined (e.g., at least twice per year) with a requirement for annual review of the training curriculum
Scope of technology topics is enumerated and linked to the institution's technology risk profile (e.g., cloud, cyber, AI, third-party technology risk)
Document carries board approval signature and version history showing it has been reviewed within the past 12 months
Policy assigns ownership to a named function (e.g., Group CIO, Company Secretary) for scheduling, tracking, and reporting training completion to the board
commonGaps: Policy exists for staff security awareness but no dedicated board-level technology training policy is maintained as a separate or distinct document
Frequency requirements are vague (e.g., 'periodic' or 'as required') without defining a minimum number of sessions per year
Policy scope is limited to cybersecurity awareness and does not cover broader technology governance topics such as emerging technology, digital strategy, or technology-related regulatory developments
Document has not been reviewed or re-approved by the board for more than two years, making it outdated relative to the institution's current technology landscape
No mechanism is defined for escalating non-attendance or gaps in board member participation
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat document repository (SharePoint, BoardPad, Diligent Boards)
GRC platform policy register (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, SAP GRC)
Board or Risk Committee terms of reference documents
Corporate governance framework maintained by the Company Secretary
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Annual Board Technology Training Calendar
evidenceItems.description: A forward-looking training schedule approved or noted by the board or its relevant committee, listing planned technology training sessions for the year including topics, delivery dates, facilitators, and intended audience. Demonstrates structured and proactive planning rather than reactive delivery.
whatGoodLooksLike: Calendar is presented to and noted by the board or Risk/Audit Committee at the start of each calendar or financial year, with evidence of this in meeting minutes
Topics are varied and cover strategic technology themes (e.g., AI governance, cloud risk, operational resilience, cyber threat landscape) aligned to the institution's risk register
Facilitators include a mix of internal subject matter experts (CIO, CISO, CTO) and independent external experts to ensure objectivity and currency of knowledge
Calendar is reviewed and updated mid-year to reflect any material technology developments or emerging risks that have arisen since initial planning
Schedule includes contingency dates or provisions for make-up sessions for board members who are unable to attend a scheduled session
commonGaps: No forward training calendar exists; sessions are scheduled reactively on an ad hoc basis with no documented planning process
Calendar exists but was never formally presented to or acknowledged by the board or a board committee, so there is no evidence of board ownership
Topics repeat year-on-year without refresh, failing to reflect the evolving technology and threat landscape relevant to the institution
Calendar does not identify facilitators or distinguish between internal and external subject matter experts
Make-up or alternative attendance arrangements are not provided for, resulting in board members missing training without remedy
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat board meeting planner (Diligent Boards, BoardPad, or equivalent)
Board secretariat annual work plan documentation
CIO or CISO office annual governance calendar
GRC platform task or event scheduling module
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or Spreadsheet

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Training Attendance Register
evidenceItems.description: A structured record of all board technology training sessions conducted, capturing session date, topic, facilitator, duration, and individual attendance by each board member. Demonstrates regularity of delivery and board member participation rates.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register records every session conducted within the review period, with no unexplained gaps of more than six months between sessions
Individual attendance is captured by name for each board member, enabling calculation of per-member and aggregate participation rates
Participation rate is high, with at least 80% of board members attending each scheduled session or a compensating make-up session
Register is maintained and signed off by the Company Secretary or equivalent governance officer after each session
Where board members were absent, the register records the reason and any follow-up action taken (e.g., distribution of session materials, individual briefing)
commonGaps: Attendance is captured only at an aggregate level (e.g., quorum met) without identifying which individual board members were present or absent
Register exists but covers only recent sessions, with gaps in historical records making it impossible to demonstrate regularity over the full review period
Absences are not followed up with compensating measures, resulting in board members who have not received training for 12 months or more
Sessions delivered via video conference are not tracked with the same rigour as in-person sessions, creating inconsistent records
Training conducted by external facilitators is not consolidated into the internal register, leaving gaps when only internally-organised sessions are recorded
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat meeting management system (Diligent Boards, BoardPad, board portal)
Board training tracker maintained by the CIO or CISO office
HR learning management system (LMS) if board members are enrolled (e.g., Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors)
GRC platform compliance tracking module
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or System export (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Training Materials and Session Records
evidenceItems.description: The presentation decks, briefing papers, pre-read materials, and any post-session summaries distributed to board members for each training session. Demonstrates that content is substantive, relevant to the institution's technology risk profile, and pitched at an appropriate level for board oversight rather than operational management.
whatGoodLooksLike: Materials are tailored to the institution's specific technology context (referencing actual systems, initiatives, and risk exposures) rather than being generic industry content
Content addresses strategic technology risk themes relevant to the board's oversight role, including implications for risk appetite, capital, and regulatory compliance
External facilitator materials are accompanied by an internal cover note from the CIO or CISO contextualising the content to the institution's circumstances
Post-session summaries or key takeaways are documented and circulated to all board members, including those who were absent from the live session
Materials are dated, versioned, and clearly attributed to the session they were used in, enabling easy cross-referencing with the attendance register
commonGaps: Materials are generic vendor presentations or public industry reports with no customisation to the institution's technology risk profile or strategic context
Content is operationally detailed and technical, demonstrating it was prepared for management audiences rather than for board-level governance oversight
Session materials are not retained centrally; delivery is informal (e.g., verbal briefings only) with no documentation of content covered
Materials do not evolve year-on-year; identical decks from previous cycles are reused without update to reflect new developments
No post-session summary or key takeaways document is produced, making it impossible to evidence what board members actually learned from each session
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat board paper repository (SharePoint, Diligent Boards)
CIO or CISO office presentation archive
Board portal document library (BoardPad, Nasdaq Boardvantage)
External training provider delivery records and materials archive
evidenceItems.format: PDF/PPTX/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Board and Committee Minutes Evidencing Technology Oversight Discussions
evidenceItems.description: Extracts from board and relevant committee minutes (e.g., Risk Committee, Audit Committee, Technology Committee) that demonstrate board members applying technology knowledge in their deliberations — asking informed questions, challenging management, and making decisions on technology matters. Serves as the ultimate outcome evidence that training has enabled effective oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: Minutes record substantive board-level questions or challenges on technology risk topics (e.g., cloud concentration risk, cyber incident response adequacy, AI model governance) beyond routine noting of management reports
Technology risk appetite statements or technology-related board resolutions can be traced to informed board deliberation, not merely management proposals passively approved
Minutes from the period following a training session demonstrate application of recently covered topics in subsequent board discussions
Board members individually identified in minutes as raising technology-specific points, evidencing individual engagement rather than collective passive receipt of reports
Minutes record the board directing management to address specific technology concerns or provide further information, demonstrating active rather than passive oversight
commonGaps: Technology items in board minutes are consistently recorded as 'noted' or 'approved as presented' with no record of board challenge, questions, or deliberation
Technology risk is addressed only in sub-committee minutes (e.g., Risk Committee) with no evidence of escalation or reporting to the full board
Minutes are templated and formulaic, capturing decisions without recording the substance of board member contributions to technology discussions
The same board member (e.g., a single technology-specialist director) is the only one raising technology questions, suggesting other members are not equipped to contribute

Minutes for the review period contain no reference to major technology developments known to have occurred at the institution (e.g., cloud migration, major cyber incident), suggesting technology was not meaningfully discussed at board level
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat minutes archive (SharePoint, Diligent Boards, BoardPad)
Board and committee secretariat office
GRC platform board reporting module
Regulatory affairs unit (for any technology-related regulatory correspondence discussed at board)
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Board Technology Training Needs Assessment and Effectiveness Review
evidenceItems.description: A documented periodic assessment of the technology training needs of individual board members (recognising varying levels of technology familiarity) and a review of training programme effectiveness. Demonstrates that the institution actively manages the quality and relevance of board training rather than treating it as a checkbox exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training needs assessment is conducted at least annually, considering changes in board composition, the institution's technology strategy, and the evolving risk landscape
Assessment accounts for individual board member backgrounds and identifies where specific members may require additional foundational or advanced coverage on particular topics
Post-session feedback is collected from board members and analysed to refine future training content, facilitators, and format
Effectiveness review is presented to the board or nominating/governance committee and documented in minutes, closing the loop on programme quality
Assessment is used to inform the next cycle's training calendar, with a traceable linkage between identified gaps and subsequent training topics
commonGaps: No training needs assessment process exists; training topics are selected solely at management discretion without reference to board-level knowledge gaps
All board members are treated as a homogeneous group regardless of their individual technology literacy, resulting in content that is too basic for some and too advanced for others
Post-session feedback is not collected, or where collected, is not analysed or used to improve the programme
Effectiveness review is performed by the same function responsible for delivering training, creating a conflict of interest with no independent validation
Board composition changes (new directors appointed) do not trigger an updated needs assessment or onboarding technology training component
suggestedSources: Board nomination and governance committee records
Company Secretariat board induction and development programme documentation
CIO or CISO office governance reporting
HR business partner to the board (where applicable)
GRC platform survey and assessment module
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or Spreadsheet

auditTips: Construct a consolidated training evidence pack before the examination: map each board member by name against every training session in the review period, producing a visual participation matrix that immediately demonstrates compliance with the 'regular' training obligation. This prevents examiners from having to manually reconcile scattered attendance records and reduces examination friction.

Cross-reference training session topics against the institution's technology risk register and board-approved risk appetite statements to demonstrate that training content is directly tied to material technology risks — examiners will ask how the institution determines what technology topics the board needs to be trained on.

Pull board and committee minutes from the 30-day window following each training session and bookmark any passages where board members raised technology questions or exercised informed challenge — this is the most compelling evidence that training has achieved its regulatory purpose of enabling effective oversight.

If any board member has a participation rate below 80% for the review period, prepare a written explanation for each absence and document what compensating measures were taken (e.g., individual briefing, distribution of materials with confirmation of receipt). Examiners will notice individual gaps and will ask what the institution did about them.

Verify that training materials in the evidence pack are institution-specific and not purely generic vendor or industry content. Where external facilitators were used, attach the internal cover note or contextualisation memo from the CIO/CISO that links external content to the institution's own technology posture — this demonstrates that management actively curated the training rather than outsourcing it wholesale.

Ensure the training calendar and policy document each carry a board approval date or a board-noted minute reference. Examiners specifically check whether the board owns its training programme or whether it is purely a management-driven activity, and a calendar that has never been formally presented to the board is a common finding.

Prepare a concise one-page narrative for the examination team that traces the full lifecycle for the review period: policy → needs assessment → calendar → sessions conducted → attendance → board deliberation outcomes → effectiveness review. This narrative demonstrates programme maturity and makes it straightforward for examiners to follow the evidence chain without having to reconstruct it themselves.

16.1.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.1.title: Notification Prior to Digital Services Changes
16.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners under Clause 16.1 primarily verify that the institution has an institutionalised, pre-emptive notification habit — not a reactive or after-the-fact disclosure practice. Examiners will cross-reference the institution's digital service release history against BNM notification records to identify any launches or material enhancements that proceeded without prior notification. A key red flag is the absence of a formalised internal trigger mechanism that flags qualifying changes at the planning or design stage, before go-live decisions are made. Examiners also assess whether the institution applies a consistent and documented definition of what constitutes a 'new digital service' or a notifiable 'enhancement,' since vague scoping is the most common root cause of notification failures. The evaluation approach typically includes interviews with both technology and compliance personnel to confirm that the notification obligation is operationally embedded in the project lifecycle, not managed solely as a compliance afterthought.

evidenceItems.name: Digital Services Change Register
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register or inventory that records all digital services (new and existing) and tracks planned introductions or enhancements, including their notification status with BNM. This register demonstrates that the institution maintains a systematic, institution-wide view of notifiable digital service changes and that notification is tracked as a gate before go-live.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register includes columns for service name, change type (new/enhancement), planned go-live date, BNM notification date, BNM reference number, and outcome — all populated without gaps
Every entry is linked to a corresponding project record in the ITSM or project management system, confirming traceability from planning to notification
The register is actively maintained and reviewed at defined intervals (e.g., monthly governance committee), evidenced by dated review sign-offs
Entries clearly distinguish between notifiable enhancements and routine maintenance, with a written rationale applied consistently using an approved classification framework
Historical entries spanning at least the prior 7 years are retained and retrievable, demonstrating sustained compliance across multiple product generations
commonGaps: Register exists but is incomplete — entries are missing BNM reference numbers or notification dates, making it impossible to verify timely submission
Register is maintained by a single team (e.g., IT) without compliance oversight, resulting in changes being logged post-launch rather than at the planning stage
No distinction is documented between notifiable enhancements and routine updates, exposing the institution to risk that material changes were misclassified and never notified
Register is stored in an informal tool (e.g., personal spreadsheet, email thread) with no version control, audit trail, or access governance
Entries for legacy or sunset digital services are absent, preventing examiners from reconstructing the full notification history over the examination period
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream) — Digital Change or Technology Notification module
Project Management Office (PMO) SharePoint or Confluence — Digital Initiatives Register
IT Change Management system — filtered view of change records tagged as 'Digital Service'
Compliance team's regulatory submission tracker (Excel/SharePoint list with BNM correspondence references)
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC platform export (PDF/CSV)

evidenceItems.name: Internal Notifiability Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented assessments conducted for each digital service change to determine whether it triggers BNM notification obligations under Clause 16.1, referencing the criteria in paragraphs 16.2 to 16.7. These records demonstrate that the institution applies a deliberate, structured evaluation — not an ad hoc or informal judgment — at the earliest feasible stage of the project lifecycle.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each assessment is completed using a standardised template or checklist that maps the proposed change against the notifiability criteria defined internally in reference to paragraphs 16.2–16.7
Assessments are dated and completed at the project initiation or design phase, not retrospectively after go-live, with timestamps that predate the go-live date by a meaningful margin
Both 'notify' and 'not notify' decisions are documented with written rationale, and 'not notify' decisions include explicit articulation of why each criterion under 16.2–16.7 was not triggered
Assessments are reviewed and approved by the Chief Compliance Officer or a designated compliance authority, with evidence of the approval signature and date
The assessment references the specific version of the institution's internal Digital Services Notification Policy in effect at the time, ensuring the correct criteria were applied
commonGaps: Assessments exist only for cases where notification was ultimately submitted, leaving 'not notify' decisions undocumented and unverifiable
Templates are generic and not tailored to Clause 16.1 criteria, making it unclear which specific regulatory obligation the assessment is addressing
Assessments are completed by the project or IT team without compliance review or approval, undermining the independence and reliability of the determination
Dates on assessments post-date the go-live of the digital service, revealing a retroactive compliance exercise rather than a genuine pre-launch gate

No version history is maintained on assessment templates, making it impossible to verify that assessments conducted in prior years used criteria aligned with the then-current regulatory interpretation
suggestedSources: Compliance department's regulatory assessment folder on SharePoint or network drive
GRC platform — Regulatory Change Assessment or Project Compliance Gate module
ITSM change request records (e.g., ServiceNow Change) — attached compliance assessment form at the 'Assess' workflow stage
Legal and Compliance intranet portal — Notification Assessment Archive
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed assessment form) or structured GRC workflow export

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification Submission Packages
evidenceItems.description: The formal notification submissions sent to BNM for each qualifying new digital service or enhancement, including the complete submission package (cover letter, supporting annexes, and any supplementary materials required under paragraphs 16.2–16.7). These records are the primary documentary proof that the institution fulfilled its prior notification obligation and that the content of each submission met the required standard.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each submission package is complete, containing the cover letter addressed to BNM, all prescribed annexes as required under 16.2–16.7, and any technical or risk documentation referenced in the body of the submission
Cover letters are dated prior to the go-live date of the digital service or enhancement, demonstrating that notification was made before launch — not concurrently or after

Submissions are transmitted through an approved and evidenced channel (e.g., BNM FAST portal, registered post with acknowledgment, or official email to BNM's designated inbox) with transmission receipts retained

The scope description within each submission is specific and unambiguous — it clearly identifies the digital service, the nature of the change, the affected customer segment, and the proposed go-live timeline
Submissions are indexed by BNM reference number and cross-referenced to the Digital Services Change Register (16.1-E1), enabling end-to-end traceability
commonGaps: Submission packages are incomplete — cover letters exist but supporting annexes required under 16.3 or 16.4 are missing or filed separately without linkage
Submission dates are on or after the go-live date, indicating that notification was concurrent or retroactive rather than prior, which is a direct breach of Clause 16.1
Submissions describe the change in vague or high-level terms (e.g., 'system upgrade') without identifying the specific digital service, affected functionality, or customer impact
No transmission evidence is retained — the institution holds the submission document but cannot prove it was actually sent to or received by BNM
Submissions for enhancements to existing services are absent, revealing that only new service launches were notified while material enhancements were overlooked
suggestedSources: BNM FAST (Flexible Automated Submission Tool) portal — submission history and exported records
Compliance department's regulatory correspondence archive on SharePoint
Outbound email archive (e.g., Microsoft 365 compliance centre) — emails to BNM's designated notification inbox
Legal department's document management system — filed regulatory submissions folder
Registered mail logbook or courier dispatch records (for physical submissions)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (cover letter and annexes as a single indexed package)

evidenceItems.name: BNM Acknowledgment and Response Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of BNM's acknowledgment of each notification submission and any subsequent correspondence or feedback from BNM (including requests for additional information, no-objection confirmations, or directives). These records demonstrate that the institution not only submitted notifications but also received and tracked BNM's response, and that go-live decisions were made with awareness of BNM's position.
whatGoodLooksLike: Every BNM notification submission in 16.1-E3 has a corresponding acknowledgment record, with no submissions showing as unacknowledged beyond a reasonable response window
Acknowledgment records are indexed with BNM's reference number and stored alongside the original submission package, enabling instant pairing during examination
Where BNM requested additional information or raised concerns, the institution's response and BNM's subsequent reply are retained as a complete correspondence thread

Internal records show that go-live decisions were consciously sequenced relative to BNM's position — for example, board or ITSC approval minutes reference the BNM notification status as a go-live prerequisite
A summary reconciliation (e.g., quarterly compliance report) confirms that all submissions in the register have received documented BNM responses, with any outstanding items escalated
commonGaps: Acknowledgment records are missing for a subset of submissions, leaving the institution unable to confirm BNM received and processed those notifications
BNM's requests for supplementary information are on file but the institution's responses are not retained, creating an incomplete correspondence record
Acknowledgment records are stored in individual staff email inboxes rather than a shared compliance repository, creating institutional knowledge risk if personnel depart
The institution cannot demonstrate that BNM's feedback or any conditions attached to its acknowledgment were acted upon before go-live
No escalation process exists for cases where BNM has not acknowledged within a defined timeframe, leaving potential notification failures undetected
suggestedSources: BNM FAST portal — received correspondence and response records
Compliance team's shared mailbox archive (Microsoft 365 or equivalent)
SharePoint regulatory correspondence library — BNM Notifications subfolder
Board/ITSC meeting minutes referencing BNM notification status (cross-reference with governance secretariat)
evidenceItems.format: PDF (acknowledgment letter or email printout) or portal export

evidenceItems.name: Internal Digital Services Notification Policy
evidenceItems.description: The institution's internal policy or procedure that operationalises Clause 16.1 obligations, including a definition of what constitutes a 'digital service' and a 'notifiable enhancement,' the roles responsible for triggering and submitting notifications, and the process integration with the SDLC or change management lifecycle. This document demonstrates that the institution has translated the regulatory obligation into repeatable internal processes rather than relying on ad hoc individual awareness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy contains an explicit and bounded definition of 'digital service' and 'enhancement' for notification purposes, aligned to the language in Clause 16.1 and informed by the criteria in 16.2–16.7, with worked examples for clarity

The policy maps the notification process to specific stages of the institution's SDLC or change management framework, identifying the precise gate at which a notifiability assessment must be completed

Named roles (not individuals) are assigned clear accountability — including who initiates the assessment, who approves the notification decision, who drafts and signs the BNM submission, and who tracks BNM's response
The policy has been approved by senior management or the Board Risk Committee, reviewed at least annually, and version-controlled with a clear change history
Policy includes a section on escalation and exception handling — specifying what occurs if a notification-triggering change is identified late in the project lifecycle
commonGaps: Policy exists but predates the current RMiT version and has not been updated to reflect the notification criteria in paragraphs 16.2–16.7, creating misalignment between the policy and the regulation
The definition of 'digital service' is absent or so broad/narrow that it fails to reliably catch notifiable changes — either over-notifying trivial updates or missing material enhancements
Policy describes the notification process in isolation from the SDLC or change management framework, meaning project teams are unaware of their triggering obligations at the planning stage
Policy has never been tested or validated against actual notification cases — there is no evidence that the process described on paper reflects what actually happens in practice
Accountability is assigned to individuals by name rather than roles, meaning the policy becomes inoperative when those individuals change without a formal handover
suggestedSources: Compliance department's policy management system (e.g., PolicyHub, Converge, SharePoint Policy Library)
IT Governance and SDLC procedure documentation on Confluence or SharePoint
Risk and Compliance intranet portal — Regulatory Compliance Procedures section
Board/Management Committee approval minutes where policy was tabled and approved
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal policy document with version control header and approval signature)

evidenceItems.name: SDLC/Change Management Notification Gate Evidence
evidenceItems.description: System-generated or process-generated evidence from the institution's SDLC or IT change management workflow demonstrating that a BNM notification compliance gate is embedded as a mandatory checkpoint before digital service changes proceed to go-live. This evidence proves that the notification obligation is structurally enforced — not dependent on individual vigilance — and that no qualifying change can bypass the gate.
whatGoodLooksLike: ITSM or project workflow system shows a mandatory 'BNM Notification Status' field or approval step that must be completed before the change record can progress to the go-live or deployment approval stage

Workflow audit logs demonstrate that the notification gate has been applied consistently across all digital service change records within the examination period, with no instances of the gate being bypassed or overridden without documented justification

The gate requires attachment of the notifiability assessment (16.1-E2) and, where applicable, proof of BNM submission (16.1-E3), before the workflow advances — enforcing evidence linkage at the point of control
Exception overrides of the notification gate are subject to a separate approval workflow requiring sign-off from the Chief Compliance Officer or equivalent, with override reasons logged and auditable

Training completion records confirm that project managers, change managers, and product owners have been trained on the notification gate requirements and understand their responsibilities within the workflow
commonGaps: The notification gate exists in the SDLC documentation but is not technically enforced in the ITSM or project system — it is a manual checkbox that can be skipped without system-level control

The gate applies only to formal IT change records but not to agile product sprints or rapid deployment pipelines, creating a blind spot for digital services delivered through modern delivery methodologies
Audit logs from the ITSM or workflow system are not retained beyond 90 days, making it impossible to reconstruct the notification gate history for the full examination period
Training on the notification gate is not documented or tracked — the institution claims awareness but cannot produce records showing who was trained, when, and on what content

The notification gate does not distinguish between new services and enhancements, resulting in all changes being treated uniformly and notification assessments being skipped for changes incorrectly assumed to be routine
suggestedSources: ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) — Change Request workflow configuration and audit log exports
Project Management system (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) — Release approval workflow with compliance gate configuration
SDLC process documentation on Confluence or SharePoint — showing gate placement in the delivery lifecycle
Learning Management System (LMS) — training completion records for compliance and IT governance modules
Internal Audit reports referencing SDLC control effectiveness assessments
evidenceItems.format: System export/screenshot (ITSM workflow configuration), PDF (process flowchart), Spreadsheet (training completion report)

auditTips: Perform a backward reconciliation before the examination: extract all digital services launched or materially enhanced in the past 3 years from your product catalogue, release notes, and app store update history, then verify each one appears in both the Digital Services Change Register and the BNM notification archive — any gap is a finding waiting to be made.
