Obtain and review BNM's prior examination findings and industry guidance on Clause 16.1 notification thresholds, then cross-check your institution's internal definition of 'notifiable enhancement' against that benchmark — vague or overly narrow internal definitions are the single most common root cause of notification failures under this clause.

Interview at least one project manager and one product owner who has led a digital service delivery in the past 12 months and ask them to describe, unprompted, how and when they engage the compliance team for BNM notification — their answers will reveal whether the notification obligation is genuinely embedded in operational practice or exists only in policy documents.

Verify the completeness of BNM acknowledgment records by cross-referencing the submission dates in your regulatory correspondence archive against BNM's FAST portal submission history — discrepancies between internal records and portal records indicate either incomplete filing or submissions made outside the approved channel.

Review the audit log of your ITSM or project delivery workflow to identify any digital service change records where the BNM notification gate was bypassed, overridden, or marked as 'not applicable' — for each such instance, confirm that a documented notifiability assessment exists justifying the classification and that it was approved by compliance.

Ensure that your evidence packages are indexed and retrievable by BNM reference number as well as by internal project code — examiners frequently request specific submissions by BNM reference during the on-site examination, and an inability to retrieve a specific record promptly undermines confidence in the institution's recordkeeping discipline.

Confirm that your internal Digital Services Notification Policy explicitly addresses agile and DevOps delivery models — if your institution uses sprint-based or continuous deployment pipelines, demonstrate how the notification gate is operationalised in those contexts, since examiners are increasingly alert to compliance frameworks designed for waterfall delivery that have not been adapted to modern engineering practices.

16.2.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.2.title: First-Time Digital Services Notification
16.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the notification package submitted prior to first-time digital service launch is complete, timely, and substantive — not a checkbox exercise. Examiners specifically scrutinise whether the risk assessment under 16.2(a) is tailored to the specific digital channel (e.g., mobile banking, e-wallet, digital onboarding) rather than a generic enterprise risk document, and whether named accountabilities are traceable to actual roles. A common red flag is a notification submitted after go-live or one that lacks evidence paragraph 16.4 measures were completed beforehand. Examiners also cross-check the security arrangements in 16.2(b) against actual technical configurations to detect misalignment, and confirm third-party arrangements in 16.2(f) are fully finalised and not described as 'pending' at notification time.

evidenceItems.name: Digital Services Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: A formal risk assessment document specific to the digital service being launched, identifying inherent and residual risks, the risk management strategies adopted, named accountabilities (role/committee), and the policies and controls in place to address each identified risk. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(a).
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment is scoped specifically to the digital channel (e.g., internet banking, mobile app, digital onboarding) and not a reused enterprise-level document
Each identified risk has a corresponding control, a named accountable owner (by designation, not just department), and a residual risk rating
Document is dated and version-controlled, with evidence of sign-off by a senior authority such as the CRO or Risk Committee before notification submission
Policies referenced in the assessment are hyperlinked or appended, confirming they are approved and in force at time of submission
Assessment explicitly references the digital service's threat landscape including cyber threats, fraud vectors, and operational risks relevant to the service type
commonGaps: Risk assessment is a generic ICT or enterprise risk document not tailored to the specific digital service, rendering it non-responsive to clause 16.2(a)
Accountabilities listed are department names rather than designated roles, making it impossible to trace responsibility to a specific individual
Controls are described at a conceptual level only (e.g., 'access controls will be implemented') with no reference to specific mechanisms or policy frameworks
Document is undated or lacks approval signatures, undermining its status as a pre-launch control artifact
Residual risk ratings are absent or all marked 'Low' without supporting rationale, suggesting the assessment was not genuinely conducted
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, or equivalent) — risk register for the project or initiative
Project Management Office (PMO) — project risk log or risk and issues register for the digital service launch
Risk Management division — technology risk assessment templates and completed assessments
Board or Management Risk Committee minutes evidencing review and approval of the assessment
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded approval signatures or DocuSign audit trail

evidenceItems.name: Security Arrangements and Controls Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Documented security architecture, controls, and arrangements applicable to the digital service, covering authentication mechanisms, encryption standards, access controls, monitoring capabilities, incident response integration, and any relevant third-party security dependencies. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Document maps security controls to specific components of the digital service (e.g., API gateway, mobile application layer, backend core banking interface) rather than describing controls generically

Authentication and authorisation mechanisms are specified with technical detail (e.g., MFA type, token validity periods, session timeout values) consistent with RMiT Appendix 5 or BNM CISSA requirements
Encryption standards are explicitly stated for data in transit and at rest, referencing algorithm and key length (e.g., TLS 1.2/1.3, AES-256)
Security monitoring and alerting coverage for the digital service is described with reference to the SOC or SIEM platform, including escalation thresholds
Document has been reviewed by the CISO or Information Security function and is cross-referenced with a penetration test or vulnerability assessment conducted before launch
commonGaps: Security documentation describes intended future controls rather than controls confirmed as implemented at time of notification, creating a compliance gap
No evidence of penetration testing or security assessment of the digital service prior to submission, leaving the security claims unvalidated
Encryption and authentication specifications are vague (e.g., 'industry standard encryption') without citing specific standards or configurations
Third-party security dependencies (e.g., payment gateway, identity verification provider) are listed but their security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2) are not appended or referenced
Security controls matrix does not map to a recognised control framework (e.g., NIST CSF, ISO 27002), making it difficult for examiners to assess adequacy
suggestedSources: Information Security division — security architecture review documents and security baseline checklists for the digital service
SOC/SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) — alert rule documentation scoped to the digital service
Penetration testing vendor reports — pre-launch ethical hacking or VAPT reports for the application
Change Management system (e.g., ServiceNow ITSM) — security sign-off tasks within the change record for the digital service go-live
evidenceItems.format: PDF with appendices for architecture diagrams (Visio/PNG) and VAPT report summary

evidenceItems.name: Significant Terms and Conditions
evidenceItems.description: The approved terms and conditions governing the digital service as presented to customers, specifically the version finalised prior to launch and submitted to BNM as part of the notification. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(c) and establishes the contractual baseline for the service.
whatGoodLooksLike: Terms and conditions are the final approved version, version-controlled and dated, with legal counsel sign-off documented
Document clearly identifies the specific digital service it governs, not a single set of generic terms covering all products
Key terms covering liability limitations, fees, dispute resolution, service availability, and data handling are present and clearly articulated
Terms have been reviewed for compliance with relevant consumer protection legislation (e.g., Financial Services Act 2013, Consumer Protection Act 1999) with a compliance sign-off note
Evidence exists that terms were loaded into the digital service platform and were live and accessible to customers from launch date onward
commonGaps: Terms and conditions submitted are a draft version rather than the final approved document, or lack a version number and approval date
Generic product terms are submitted without customisation for the specific digital service, failing to address channel-specific risks and obligations
No evidence of legal review or board/management approval of the terms prior to submission to BNM
Terms do not address digital-service-specific matters such as electronic communications consent, digital signature validity, or session authentication responsibilities
No confirmation that the terms submitted to BNM match exactly what was published on the institution's platform at go-live
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance division — document management system holding approved legal templates and final executed versions
Product Management — product approval committee papers including the terms approval agenda item
Digital platform CMS or website archive — published terms and conditions with effective date metadata
SharePoint or equivalent intranet DMS — version history of terms document showing draft-to-final progression
evidenceItems.format: PDF (final approved, signed by authorised signatories) and web-published HTML version for reference

evidenceItems.name: Client Charter for Digital Service
evidenceItems.description: The published client charter specific to or inclusive of the digital service, setting out the institution's service commitments, customer rights, complaint handling timelines, and service standards applicable to the digital channel. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(d).
whatGoodLooksLike: Client charter explicitly references or covers the digital service by name and sets out measurable service level commitments (e.g., transaction processing times, downtime notification windows)
Charter includes complaint escalation procedures specific to the digital channel, including contact methods (in-app, email, hotline) and resolution timeframes
Document has been approved by senior management and published on both the institution's website and within the digital service interface before launch
Charter aligns with BNM's Financial Consumer Protection Framework obligations, including fair treatment and transparency commitments
Version and effective date are clearly stated, and the charter submitted to BNM matches the version published to customers
commonGaps: Client charter is a generic corporate document that does not address the digital service channel specifically, offering no digital-specific service commitments
Measurable service standards are absent, replaced by aspirational language (e.g., 'we strive to provide excellent service') that cannot be audited or enforced
Charter was not published on the digital platform at launch, meaning customers had no access to it from day one of service availability
No evidence of management approval for the charter or of its alignment review against the Financial Consumer Protection Framework
Complaint handling procedures described in the charter do not match the actual complaint workflow configured in the institution's CRM or complaints management system
suggestedSources: Customer Experience or Consumer Protection division — approved client charter documents and publication records
Digital platform CMS — published client charter page with effective date metadata
Complaints Management System (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud, Zendesk) — complaint workflow configuration matching charter commitments
Board or Management Committee papers — approval minutes for the client charter
evidenceItems.format: PDF (approved version) and web/in-app published version for cross-verification

evidenceItems.name: Privacy Policy Statement
evidenceItems.description: The approved privacy policy statement governing the collection, use, storage, and disclosure of personal data by the digital service, demonstrating that the institution has addressed data privacy obligations under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (PDPA) and BNM requirements. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(e).
whatGoodLooksLike: Privacy policy is specific to or clearly covers the digital service and identifies the categories of personal data collected through the digital channel (e.g., biometric data for eKYC, device identifiers, behavioural data)
Policy states the legal basis for data collection and processing, data retention periods, and customer rights under the PDPA including access and correction rights
Third parties with whom data may be shared are categorised (e.g., credit bureaus, payment processors, fraud screening vendors) and the basis for sharing is explained
Policy was approved by the Data Protection Officer (DPO) or equivalent and published on both the institution's website and within the digital service before go-live
Cross-border data transfer arrangements, if any, are disclosed and the adequacy or safeguards in place are described
commonGaps: Privacy policy is a generic corporate document that fails to disclose digital-service-specific data collection activities such as device fingerprinting, location data, or behavioural analytics
Data retention periods are not specified, or a single blanket retention period is applied without differentiation by data category
Third-party data sharing is described vaguely (e.g., 'trusted partners') without identifying the categories of recipients or the safeguards in place
No evidence that the privacy policy was reviewed by the DPO or legal counsel for PDPA compliance prior to submission to BNM
Policy does not describe how consent is obtained and recorded within the digital service, leaving the consent mechanism unsubstantiated
suggestedSources: Data Protection Officer (DPO) or Legal division — approved privacy policy and PDPA compliance review notes
Digital platform CMS — published privacy policy page with effective date and version metadata
Data mapping or data inventory register — to cross-verify data categories disclosed in the policy against actual data flows in the digital service
Consent management platform or CRM — consent recording configuration demonstrating how customer consent is captured and stored
evidenceItems.format: PDF (approved and signed by DPO/Legal) and web-published version for cross-verification

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party and Outsourcing Arrangements Register
evidenceItems.description: A consolidated disclosure of all outsourcing arrangements, website link arrangements, strategic alliances, and third-party partnerships that have been finalised in connection with the digital service, including copies or summaries of executed agreements. This demonstrates compliance with clause 16.2(f).
whatGoodLooksLike: Register lists all finalised third-party arrangements by category (outsourcing, technology vendor, strategic alliance, referral/website link) with the counterparty name, nature of arrangement, and contract execution date
For outsourcing arrangements, the register references compliance with RMiT paragraph 16.4 measures and cross-links to outsourcing risk assessments and due diligence records
Executed contracts or formal letters of agreement are appended or referenced with document IDs, confirming arrangements are finalised — not 'in negotiation' or 'subject to final approval'
Where website link arrangements exist, the linked sites and the purpose of each link are disclosed, with evidence that the legal basis for the link (e.g., disclaimer, agreement) is documented
Register has been reviewed by Compliance or Legal and signed off as complete and accurate at the time of BNM notification submission
commonGaps: Arrangements described as 'finalised' are actually still under negotiation at notification time, directly breaching the requirement of clause 16.2(f) and raising concerns about the notification's accuracy

Technology vendors providing critical functions (e.g., cloud hosting, API providers, eKYC vendors) are omitted from the register on the basis that they are 'technology purchases' rather than outsourcing, when BNM's definition captures material third-party dependencies
No due diligence or vendor risk assessment records are cross-referenced for the third parties listed, leaving the adequacy of vendor selection unsubstantiated
Website link arrangements are not disclosed, typically because they are not perceived as requiring notification, when the clause explicitly requires their inclusion
Register is not version-controlled or dated, making it impossible to confirm it reflects the arrangement landscape at the precise time of notification submission
suggestedSources: Vendor Management or Procurement division — executed contract repository and vendor register (SharePoint, Ariba, or equivalent contract management system)
GRC platform — outsourcing risk assessment records and third-party due diligence reports
Legal division — executed agreement copies and alliance or partnership deeds
IT/Enterprise Architecture — third-party technology dependency maps for the digital service identifying all external service integrations
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or PDF register with hyperlinked contract references; executed agreements as PDF appendices

auditTips: Verify notification submission date against the digital service go-live date: clause 16.2 requires notification before or at launch, not after. Request the BNM notification acknowledgement receipt and compare it against the change management record or App Store/Play Store publication date for the digital service.

Cross-check the risk assessment submitted under 16.2(a) against the paragraph 16.4 pre-launch checklist: the clause requires 16.4 measures to be completed before submission. If the 16.4 measures were not demonstrably complete at notification time, the notification itself is non-compliant regardless of its content quality.

For each of the six notification components (16.2(a) through (f)), create a coverage matrix mapping each sub-requirement to a specific section of the submitted notification package. This matrix allows you to rapidly identify any component that was omitted or addressed by a cross-reference to a document that does not actually satisfy the obligation.

Request the actual notification package submitted to BNM, not a retrospectively prepared document set. Compare document version numbers and approval dates on all supporting materials against the notification submission date to confirm they were finalised before submission — not backdated.

For third-party arrangements under 16.2(f), specifically probe for website link arrangements, which institutions frequently omit. Ask IT or the digital channel team to produce a list of all external URLs embedded in the digital service at launch and verify each is accounted for in the notification.

Where the institution has launched multiple digital services since RMiT came into effect, create a tracker of each launch event and confirm a separate 16.2-compliant notification was submitted for each first-time service. A single notification covering multiple services is only acceptable if the services launched simultaneously under one project scope.

Validate that the privacy policy and terms and conditions submitted to BNM are byte-for-byte identical to what was published to customers at launch by comparing version numbers, effective dates, and a spot-check of key clause wording. Discrepancies between the BNM submission and the customer-facing version indicate that one was produced specifically for regulatory purposes rather than being the genuine operational document.

16.3.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.3.title: Simplified Notification for Enhancements
16.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on two primary obligations under Clause 16.3: (1) whether the institution correctly identified that the enhancement qualifies for the simplified notification pathway by meeting the Appendix 6 criteria, and (2) whether the submitted notification package contains a substantive description of the enhancement and a genuine risk assessment — not a perfunctory checklist. A major red flag is institutions treating the simplified pathway as an exemption from rigour; examiners will probe whether risk assessments identify material impacts and propose concrete, tested mitigations rather than boilerplate language. Examiners also verify that notifications were submitted to BNM prior to or at the appropriate stage of deployment, not retrospectively. Cross-referencing the change management register against the notification log is a standard examination technique to detect unreported enhancements that should have triggered this obligation.

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 6 Eligibility Assessment Record
evidenceItems.description: A documented assessment confirming that the digital service enhancement meets the Appendix 6 criteria that trigger the simplified notification pathway rather than the full notification or prior approval route. This record demonstrates that the institution applied a deliberate, auditable decision process before selecting the simplified pathway.
whatGoodLooksLike: A completed eligibility checklist mapping each Appendix 6 criterion against the specific enhancement, with a named approver and date
Clear rationale recorded for each criterion, showing why the enhancement qualifies — not just tick-boxes but brief written justification
Approval sign-off from a senior technology or compliance officer confirming the simplified pathway is appropriate
Version-controlled template that references the current RMiT policy version and Appendix 6 as issued by BNM
The record is filed alongside the notification submission package so that the eligibility decision and submission are traceable as a single evidence set
commonGaps: Eligibility assessment is not documented at all — the simplified pathway is selected informally without any recorded rationale
Checklist exists but contains only tick-marks with no written justification, making it impossible to verify the decision during examination
Assessment was completed after deployment rather than before submission, indicating it was created retrospectively to satisfy audit
The Appendix 6 version referenced is outdated and does not reflect the current BNM policy criteria
No named approver on the record, leaving accountability unclear when examiners ask who authorised the simplified pathway selection
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer) change or project intake module where pathway classification is captured
Compliance team's SharePoint or Confluence library under 'BNM Notifications / RMiT'
Technology Risk Management team's project intake form or ITSM service request attached to the change record
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (completed checklist with signatures or digital approvals)

evidenceItems.name: Enhancement Description Document
evidenceItems.description: A written description of the enhancements made to the existing digital service, as required under Clause 16.3(a). This document forms the core of the simplified notification and must provide BNM with sufficient clarity about what changed, why, and the scope of the change relative to the existing service baseline.
whatGoodLooksLike: Narrative description covering: what the existing service does, the specific functional or technical changes introduced, the technology components affected (e.g., APIs, infrastructure, authentication), and the intended business outcome
Includes version or release identifiers that can be cross-referenced to change management records and system release notes
Clearly distinguishes the enhancement from a new service, reinforcing the eligibility basis for the simplified pathway
Written at a level of detail sufficient for a non-technical BNM examiner to understand the nature and scope of the change without requiring supplementary verbal explanation
Document is dated and signed by the accountable technology or product owner prior to or at the time of submission
commonGaps: Description is vague or uses internal jargon without explanation, making it insufficient for BNM to assess the nature of the change
Document describes only the business benefit of the enhancement with no technical detail on what systems or components were modified
Enhancement scope is understated — significant architectural changes are described minimally to avoid triggering a higher notification tier
No baseline reference to the existing service, making it impossible to determine what changed versus what was already in place
Document is undated or post-dated, suggesting it was not prepared contemporaneously with the notification submission
suggestedSources: Project or product management platform (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) — release notes and feature specifications
Technology change management system — change request description fields and business justification sections
Digital banking or product team's SharePoint site — product release documentation and business requirements documents
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX

evidenceItems.name: Risk Assessment for Enhancement
evidenceItems.description: A risk assessment conducted specifically for the proposed enhancement, as required under Clause 16.3(b). The assessment must identify risks arising from the enhancement, evaluate their impact, and document measures taken to mitigate those risks. This is a substantive obligation — not a reference to a generic product risk register.
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment addresses technology-specific risk domains relevant to the enhancement: cybersecurity, operational resilience, data integrity, third-party dependencies, and customer impact
Each identified risk has an inherent risk rating, a named mitigation control, a residual risk rating after control application, and a risk owner
Mitigation measures reference actual implemented or scheduled controls (e.g., penetration test scheduled for date X, WAF rule updated, rollback plan documented) rather than generic statements
Assessment was reviewed and approved by the Technology Risk Management function and, where relevant, the Information Security Officer prior to submission
Assessment explicitly addresses customer-facing impact scenarios, including service disruption, data exposure, and transaction integrity risks
commonGaps: Risk assessment is copied from a previous similar project with minimal modification, resulting in risks that do not match the specific enhancement
Mitigations are described in aspirational or future-tense language ('will implement') without evidence that they were completed before submission
Assessment omits third-party or vendor risk even where the enhancement involves a third-party technology component or API integration
No residual risk rating is provided after mitigations, making it impossible to determine whether the institution accepted, transferred, or reduced risk to an acceptable level
Assessment is not signed off by an appropriate risk authority, indicating it may not have been formally reviewed before the notification was submitted
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management function's risk register within the GRC platform (e.g., Archer, ServiceNow IRM, RSA Archer)
Project risk log maintained in the project management tool (Jira, MS Project, Confluence)
Information Security team's security assessment repository for enhancements involving authentication, encryption, or data handling changes
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX or Spreadsheet (structured risk register format with sign-off)

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification Submission Record
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the simplified notification was formally submitted to BNM in accordance with the regulatory requirement, including proof of submission timing, the channel used, and BNM's acknowledgement or reference number. This record demonstrates the institution met its procedural obligation to notify and did so at the required stage.
whatGoodLooksLike: Copy of the notification package as submitted (enhancement description and risk assessment), timestamped and version-locked to match the submission date
BNM acknowledgement email, portal reference number, or other confirmation that the Bank received the notification
Submission date falls within the regulatory window — pre-deployment or at the stage prescribed by BNM, not after the enhancement was already live
Notification log or register maintained by the compliance function records this submission alongside all other RMiT notifications, enabling portfolio-level review
Clear linkage between the submission record and the internal change management record (e.g., change request ID cross-referenced) for traceability
commonGaps: Institution cannot produce BNM's acknowledgement or a delivery confirmation, making it impossible to prove the notification was received
Submission date post-dates the enhancement go-live, indicating the notification was made retrospectively rather than within the required window
Notification register is not maintained, so the submission exists only as an isolated email with no institutional tracking
Submitted package differs from the final version (e.g., risk assessment was updated after submission but the submitted version was not retained), creating a discrepancy
No clear ownership of the submission — it is unclear which function (IT, Compliance, Risk) is responsible for submitting and tracking notifications, leading to inconsistent practice
suggestedSources: BNM's designated submission portal or secure email correspondence archive
Compliance team's notification tracker (spreadsheet, SharePoint list, or GRC workflow) recording all RMiT submissions
Email archive of the Compliance or Technology Risk function for submission correspondence with BNM
evidenceItems.format: PDF (submitted package) + Email/portal export (proof of submission)

evidenceItems.name: Change Management Record Linkage
evidenceItems.description: The internal change management or project record for the enhancement, demonstrating that the BNM simplified notification was embedded as a mandatory step within the institution's standard change lifecycle rather than managed as an ad hoc compliance activity. This linkage provides auditors confidence that no qualifying enhancement can be deployed without triggering the notification obligation.
whatGoodLooksLike: The ITSM or change management record contains a mandatory field or gate for 'BNM Notification Status' with permissible values (e.g., Not Required, Simplified Notified, Full Notification, Prior Approval) and the field is populated for this change
Change approval workflow includes a compliance or risk review checkpoint that validates notification status before deployment approval is granted
Change record cross-references the notification submission reference number, creating a bidirectional audit trail between the regulatory obligation and the operational change
Post-implementation review or change closure documentation notes the notification status and confirms it was completed before go-live
Change categorisation criteria within the ITSM tool are aligned with Appendix 6 criteria, enabling automated flagging of changes that require simplified notification
commonGaps: Change management process has no field or gate for BNM notification status, meaning the obligation relies entirely on individual memory rather than process control
Notification is managed in a separate compliance system with no linkage to the change record, creating a risk of changes being deployed without the notification being filed
Change records show deployment occurred before the compliance gate was completed, indicating the gate is present but not enforced
Only major changes are routed through the compliance gate; minor or fast-tracked changes bypass the step, creating a population of potentially unreported enhancements
Change management records for the enhancement cannot be produced because they were not retained or were archived in a system that is no longer accessible
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management, Jira Service Management, BMC Remedy)
DevOps or CI/CD pipeline release records where deployment gates are enforced (e.g., Azure DevOps release pipelines with approval stages)
Project governance repository — project closure reports that include regulatory compliance sign-off checklists
evidenceItems.format: System export (PDF or CSV from ITSM) or screenshot with metadata

evidenceItems.name: Simplified Notification Policy and Procedure
evidenceItems.description: The institution's internal policy or standard operating procedure governing how simplified notifications under Clause 16.3 are identified, prepared, approved, and submitted. This demonstrates that the institution has institutionalised the requirement rather than addressing it on a case-by-case basis, and that staff responsible for digital service enhancements understand their obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure explicitly references Clause 16.3 and Appendix 6 of RMiT by name, with a review schedule tied to BNM policy update cycles
Procedure defines roles and responsibilities: who assesses Appendix 6 eligibility, who prepares the description and risk assessment, who approves the package, and who submits to BNM
Procedure specifies the required content of the notification package — aligning to the two components in Clause 16.3(a) and (b) — and provides templates or examples
Procedure includes a decision tree or flowchart distinguishing between simplified notification, full notification, and prior approval pathways based on enhancement characteristics
Procedure has been reviewed and approved within the last 12 months, or following any BNM update to RMiT, and training records confirm that relevant staff have been briefed on it
commonGaps: No documented procedure exists — the notification process is managed informally by individuals with institutional knowledge, creating key-person dependency and inconsistent outcomes

Procedure exists but does not distinguish between the simplified and full notification pathways, leading to either under-notification (simplified used when full is required) or over-notification (unnecessary submissions)
Procedure has not been updated since the initial RMiT adoption and does not reflect subsequent BNM guidance or policy revisions
Procedure assigns responsibility to a role that no longer exists in the current organisational structure, leaving the obligation unowned
No training or awareness programme accompanies the procedure, so technology and product teams who originate enhancement projects are unaware of the notification obligation
suggestedSources: Compliance or Technology Risk function's policy management system (e.g., PolicyTech, SharePoint policy library, Confluence)
HR or Learning Management System — training completion records for RMiT awareness programmes
Internal audit working papers from prior RMiT reviews, which may have flagged gaps in notification procedures
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (approved policy document with version history and sign-off)

auditTips: Before the examination, reconcile your internal change management register against the BNM notification log for the review period. Any digital service enhancement that was classified as qualifying under Appendix 6 should have a corresponding simplified notification record — unexplained gaps are the single most common examination finding under this clause.

Do not treat the simplified pathway as a light-touch obligation. Pre-stage both the Appendix 6 eligibility assessment (16.3-E1) and the risk assessment (16.3-E3) together in a single submission folder per enhancement, so examiners can clearly see the full package for each notification without needing to chase documents across multiple systems.

Verify submission timing for every notification in your sample: compare the BNM submission date against the change management record's go-live date. Any notification submitted after deployment will be treated as a retrospective notification, which undermines the regulatory intent of advance or contemporaneous notice and is a significant red flag for examiners.

Ensure the risk assessment language is specific and evidence-based rather than generic. Replace phrases like 'standard security controls will be applied' with references to actual controls deployed — for example, 'WAF rule set updated on [date], penetration test completed [date] with no high findings, rollback plan documented in change record CR-XXXX'. Examiners are trained to distinguish substantive assessments from boilerplate.

Cross-reference your Appendix 6 eligibility criteria against recent BNM guidance letters and any supervisory feedback received from prior examinations. If BNM has issued clarifications on what qualifies for simplified versus full notification, ensure your eligibility assessment template reflects those clarifications and that prior notifications are not invalidated by a revised interpretation.

Confirm that your ITSM or change management workflow enforces notification status as a hard gate — not an advisory field — before deployment approval is granted. If examiners find that the gate can be bypassed or overridden without documented escalation, they will question the reliability of your entire notification population, not just individual cases.

Prepare a summary matrix for the examination team that lists all simplified notifications submitted during the review period, with columns for: enhancement name, submission date, BNM reference number, go-live date, Appendix 6 criterion met, risk rating, and linkage to change record. This proactive disclosure demonstrates control maturity and significantly reduces examiner information requests.

16.4.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.4.title: Assurance and Confirmation for Material Enhancements
16.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus primarily on two hard obligations: the existence of a credible, independent external assurance engagement and a properly executed senior-level confirmation, both completed before the notification to BNM is submitted. Examiners will scrutinise whether the external party meets genuine independence criteria — internal audit, group entities, or incumbent technology vendors will raise immediate red flags. They will also verify that the assurance report and confirmation letter strictly follow the formats prescribed in Part A and Part B of Appendix 7 respectively, and that both documents predate the notification submission timestamp. A recurring examiner concern is institutions treating the assurance as a checkbox exercise by engaging parties with limited scope or pre-agreed conclusions; examiners will probe the scope of work, the methodology used, and whether identified findings were actually remediated prior to sign-off. The seniority and authority of the confirmation signatory is also scrutinised to ensure it aligns with paragraph 8.3 — signatures from IT managers or mid-level compliance officers will not satisfy the requirement.

evidenceItems.name: Independent External Assurance Report (Appendix 7 Part A)
evidenceItems.description: The formal assurance report issued by a qualified, independent external party confirming that technology risks and security controls associated with the digital service or material enhancement have been assessed and adequately addressed. This is the primary mandatory deliverable under clause 16.4(a) and must conform to the BNM-prescribed format in Part A of Appendix 7.
whatGoodLooksLike: Report is issued by a named external party with no commercial, ownership, or consulting relationship with the institution that could impair independence, and the independence basis is explicitly declared in the report
Scope section clearly maps to the specific digital service or enhancement being notified, referencing system names, version numbers, and the change boundary assessed
Report follows the structure and headings prescribed in Appendix 7 Part A verbatim, including all mandatory sections on risk coverage, control assessment, and the formal assurance opinion paragraph

All findings identified during the assessment are listed with their remediation status, and the positive assurance opinion is conditional on completion of all critical and high findings before go-live
Report date and the assurance firm's authorised signatory date both precede the date of the BNM notification submission
commonGaps: External party is the institution's existing technology vendor, group internal audit function, or a consultant embedded in the project team — none of whom qualify as independent for this purpose
Report scope is defined so narrowly (e.g., only penetration testing) that it does not constitute holistic assurance over technology risk and security controls as required by the clause
Report format deviates materially from Appendix 7 Part A, omitting mandatory sections or substituting institution-preferred templates without BNM approval

Outstanding high or critical findings are noted in the report but the confirmation letter is still signed before documented evidence of remediation exists, creating a gap between the assurance opinion and actual control status
Report is dated after the BNM notification was submitted, making it non-compliant with the 'prior to notifying the Bank' requirement
suggestedSources: Engagement files and final deliverables folder maintained by the institution's Technology Risk or Information Security department
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, or local equivalents) under the project risk register for the relevant digital service
Vendor/third-party management portal where the external assurance firm's engagement letter and deliverables are stored
Project governance SharePoint or Confluence site for the digital service launch, under the 'Pre-Launch Governance' section
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed, non-editable) — original issued on external firm letterhead with wet or digital signature

evidenceItems.name: External Assurance Party Independence Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing that the external party engaged under clause 16.4(a) meets the independence criteria required by BNM. This may take the form of a conflict-of-interest declaration, due diligence output, or an internal memo from the CISO or Technology Risk function confirming the basis for the independence determination.
whatGoodLooksLike: A signed conflict-of-interest declaration from the external assurance firm explicitly listing relationships with the institution and attesting that none impair independence
Internal due diligence memo from the CISO or procurement team documenting how the institution evaluated and confirmed the external party's independence prior to engagement
The assurance firm is a recognised professional services firm, specialist cybersecurity assessor, or qualified external auditor with no active technology delivery contracts with the institution

Engagement letter between the institution and the assurance firm clearly delineates the assurance scope and confirms the firm is acting in an independent advisory capacity, not as a project participant
Board Risk Committee or equivalent approval of the external assurance engagement, evidenced in meeting minutes, where the firm's independence was tabled and approved
commonGaps: No formal independence assessment is performed — the institution selects the external party informally and relies solely on the firm's own assertion of independence without documented verification
The assurance firm holds or recently concluded a material technology implementation or managed services contract with the institution, and this was not identified or disclosed during selection
Independence documentation is created retrospectively after the assurance report is issued, rather than being completed at the point of engagement

The institution conflates ISO or other certification body audits (conducted for certification purposes) with BNM-mandated independent assurance, which have different independence and scope requirements
suggestedSources: Procurement or vendor management system where the assurance firm's onboarding and conflict-of-interest screening records are held
CISO office or Technology Risk department records for the specific project engagement
Legal and Compliance department files where third-party engagement letters and independence declarations are archived
Board or Risk Committee secretariat for meeting minutes referencing the assurance engagement approval
evidenceItems.format: PDF or DOCX — signed declaration and supporting due diligence memo; supplemented by engagement letter (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: CISO / Senior Management / Board Confirmation Letter (Appendix 7 Part B)
evidenceItems.description: The formal confirmation issued by the CISO, a senior management officer, or the chairman of the board or designated board-level committee as specified in paragraph 8.3, attesting to the institution's readiness to provide the digital service or implement the material enhancement. This document must conform to the format prescribed in Part B of Appendix 7 and is a mandatory pre-notification deliverable under clause 16.4(b).
whatGoodLooksLike: Confirmation is signed by an officer whose title and authority is explicitly aligned with the roles specified in paragraph 8.3 — CISO, a named senior management officer, or the board/committee chairman — and the signatory's designation is stated in the letter
Letter follows the Appendix 7 Part B format precisely, including all mandatory attestation statements regarding technology risk management, security control adequacy, and institutional readiness
Confirmation letter is dated after the external assurance report is received, evidencing that the senior officer reviewed the assurance outcome before signing
Letter explicitly references the specific digital service or enhancement and the scope of the associated external assurance engagement, demonstrating it is not a generic or recycled template
The original signed letter is retained with the full notification package submitted to BNM, and an internal copy is filed in the institution's governance records
commonGaps: Signatory does not meet the authority threshold in paragraph 8.3 — letters signed by IT Directors, Deputy CISOs, or compliance managers who do not hold the requisite senior management designation are non-compliant
Confirmation letter is signed before the external assurance report is finalised, meaning the senior officer could not have reviewed assurance outcomes when attesting to readiness
Format deviates from Appendix 7 Part B — institutions use their own confirmation templates or board resolution formats that omit mandatory attestation language
Letter is generic and does not reference the specific digital service, enhancement scope, or the assurance report, making it impossible to demonstrate it was prepared for this specific notification
The designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 was not the one that issued the confirmation, and no delegation of authority documentation exists to justify the alternative signatory
suggestedSources: CISO office or Group Technology Risk function where the signed original is held
Board secretariat or Company Secretary records if the confirmation is issued at board or committee level
BNM notification submission package archived by the Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department
GRC platform under the relevant project's regulatory notification workflow, where the confirmation is uploaded as a pre-submission gate document
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed, on institution letterhead) — wet signature or qualified electronic signature acceptable

evidenceItems.name: Material Enhancement Classification Determination
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the institution's internal assessment and decision that the relevant change to the digital service constitutes a 'material enhancement' subject to clause 16.4 rather than a simplified notification under 16.3. This record demonstrates the institution applied a deliberate and auditable classification process before determining its notification obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal change classification assessment, signed by the Technology Risk or Regulatory Affairs function, that evaluates the enhancement against the materiality criteria and explicitly concludes that clause 16.4 applies

The assessment references the institution's internal materiality threshold policy (aligned to RMiT definitions) and documents the specific characteristics of the change that drive the 16.4 classification
Review and concurrence from the CISO or CTO is evidenced, ensuring senior oversight of the classification decision before the assurance and confirmation process is initiated
The classification document is dated at project initiation or pre-go-live governance stage, not created retrospectively after the notification is submitted
Clear linkage exists between the classification record and the subsequent assurance engagement scope, demonstrating the scope was designed to match the assessed materiality of the enhancement
commonGaps: No documented classification process exists — the institution defaults to 16.4 or 16.3 treatment based on informal judgement without a traceable decision record
Classification criteria used by the institution are not formally defined in policy, making it impossible to demonstrate consistent or defensible materiality determinations across different projects
The same classification template is reused across projects without project-specific analysis, appearing as a rubber-stamp exercise rather than genuine risk-based assessment

Classification is performed exclusively by the project team without independent review from Technology Risk or Compliance, creating a conflict of interest where project timelines may influence the determination
suggestedSources: Change Advisory Board (CAB) records or ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira Service Management) where change classification is recorded as part of the change request workflow
Technology Risk department's project intake or governance checklist for digital service changes
Regulatory Affairs or Compliance function's notification planning records
GRC platform's risk assessment module where pre-project regulatory obligation screening is documented
evidenceItems.format: DOCX or PDF — formal memo or governance checklist with sign-off fields; or structured record in ITSM/GRC platform with audit trail

evidenceItems.name: Assurance Findings Remediation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that all findings identified during the external assurance engagement have been remediated or formally accepted with compensating controls prior to the institution signing the confirmation letter and submitting the BNM notification. This evidence bridges the assurance report and the confirmation of readiness, validating that the positive attestation is substantiated.
whatGoodLooksLike: A findings tracker or remediation log maintained by the Technology Risk or Information Security function, listing each assurance finding, its severity, the remediation action taken, the responsible owner, and the closure date

Closure evidence for each critical and high finding is attached or referenced in the tracker — this may include system screenshots, updated configuration records, policy document versions, or retesting results

The external assurance firm provided written confirmation (via a closure letter or updated report addendum) that critical and high findings were retested and validated as remediated before the final assurance opinion was issued
The CISO or Technology Risk head has formally signed off the findings closure register before the confirmation letter under clause 16.4(b) was executed, establishing a clear chronological dependency
Residual medium and low findings that remain open at notification time are formally risk-accepted with documented rationale, approval authority, and remediation timelines
commonGaps: No remediation tracker exists — findings are communicated verbally or via email threads without a structured record, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic closure

Critical or high findings are listed as 'in progress' or 'planned' at the time the confirmation letter is signed, meaning the attestation of readiness is not supported by evidence of actual control adequacy
Remediation evidence is limited to internal self-attestations without independent validation — the external assurance firm was not engaged to verify that fixes were correctly implemented
Risk acceptance decisions for open findings are not formally documented or approved at the appropriate authority level, leaving unresolved audit exposure

The remediation tracker is updated retrospectively to show closure dates that predate the confirmation letter, but underlying evidence (e.g., change tickets, system logs) shows remediation actually occurred after notification
suggestedSources: Information Security or Technology Risk function's vulnerability and findings management system (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow Security Operations, or dedicated GRC platform)
External assurance firm's closure letter or retest report, held in the engagement file
ITSM change management records showing the specific remediation changes implemented in response to assurance findings
Risk Committee or CISO sign-off records where findings closure was formally approved as a pre-notification gate
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform export (findings tracker) supplemented by PDF closure evidence and/or external firm closure letter

evidenceItems.name: BNM Pre-Notification Submission Package with Chronological Timeline
evidenceItems.description: The complete, compiled notification package submitted to BNM for the digital service or material enhancement, together with a document-level chronological timeline demonstrating that the external assurance report and the senior management confirmation were both completed and dated prior to the notification submission date. This package provides the overarching audit trail tying all clause 16.4 obligations together.
whatGoodLooksLike: A single, version-controlled submission package containing: the BNM notification form, the Appendix 7 Part A assurance report, the Appendix 7 Part B confirmation letter, and any supplementary materials required by BNM

A cover sheet or transmittal memo produced by the Regulatory Affairs function that lists all enclosed documents with their respective dates, explicitly showing the assurance report date and confirmation letter date both precede the notification submission date
Proof of submission to BNM is retained — this may be a BNM acknowledgement receipt, the submission timestamp from BNM's regulatory reporting portal, or an email confirmation from BNM
The notification package is version-controlled and access-restricted, with any post-submission correspondence or BNM queries and responses filed in the same record set

Internal governance approval for the notification submission (e.g., sign-off from the Chief Compliance Officer or equivalent) is evidenced, confirming that completeness and format compliance were verified before dispatch
commonGaps: Documents within the submission package carry dates that are inconsistent — the confirmation letter predates the assurance report, or the notification date precedes both, suggesting the pre-notification requirements were not actually met in the correct sequence
The submission package is incomplete at the time of BNM examination because documents were submitted piecemeal over multiple communications and were never consolidated into a single auditable record
No proof of submission is retained — institutions rely on memory or verbal confirmation from relationship managers rather than documentary evidence of the notification date
The notification package on file is a draft version and not the final version actually submitted to BNM, creating uncertainty about what was formally notified
Post-submission BNM queries and the institution's responses are not filed with the original notification package, leaving an incomplete picture of the regulatory interaction for examiner review
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department's regulatory submissions register and document archive
BNM's regulatory reporting or notification portal (submission receipts and correspondence)
Secure SharePoint or document management system maintained by the Compliance function for BNM correspondence
Legal department records where formal regulatory submissions are archived for litigation and examination readiness purposes
evidenceItems.format: PDF submission package (compiled, paginated, and indexed) with email or portal confirmation of receipt as supplementary evidence

auditTips: Before examination, reconstruct the chronological sequence for each clause 16.4 notification: verify that the external assurance report date, the confirmation letter date, and the BNM notification submission date appear in that strict order — any inversion in this sequence is an immediate compliance failure that examiners will cite.

Verify the independence of every external assurance party engaged under 16.4(a) by cross-referencing the assurance firm against the institution's vendor register and active contracts list — if the firm appears as a technology services vendor, managed services provider, or active project consultant anywhere in that register, escalate for legal review before the examination.

Pull the Appendix 7 Part A and Part B templates directly from the current version of the RMiT policy document and perform a section-by-section format comparison against every assurance report and confirmation letter on file — institutions frequently use outdated or localised templates that omit mandatory sections, which examiners treat as non-compliant regardless of the substantive content.

Cross-reference the signatory on each Appendix 7 Part B confirmation letter against the institution's paragraph 8.3 governance structure documentation (board charter, terms of reference, and management authority matrix) — prepare a one-page signatory authority mapping that can be handed to examiners immediately if the delegation chain is questioned.

For each open finding identified in assurance reports, ensure the remediation tracker shows a documented closure date with supporting evidence that predates the confirmation letter signature — if any critical or high finding lacks closure evidence, obtain a formal risk acceptance memo approved by the appropriate authority level before the examination and ensure it is filed with the original notification package.

Prepare a master register of all digital service enhancements from the examination period, including those assessed as simplified notifications under 16.3, so examiners can see the full population and the classification rationale for each — this proactively demonstrates a systematic approach to materiality determination and prevents examiners from independently identifying enhancements that should have triggered 16.4 treatment but did not.

Brief the CISO and relevant board committee members on the specific attestations they made in each Appendix 7 Part B confirmation letter before the examination — examiners may conduct interviews with signatories and will probe whether they understood the obligations they were confirming, so signatories who are unfamiliar with the specific enhancement or the assurance findings at the time of signing represent a significant examination risk.

16.5.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.5.title: Independent External Party Assurance Quality
16.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will scrutinise whether the financial institution conducted a structured, documented assessment of the external assurance provider's competency and track record prior to engagement — not merely relied on brand recognition or incumbency. Examiners will verify that the assurance report substantively addresses all mandatory matters prescribed in Appendix 7 Part C (covering pre-launch technical and security assessments) and Part D (covering operational resilience and controls), rather than providing superficial or templated commentary. A key red flag is an assurance report that omits specific Appendix 7 line items, contains boilerplate findings without evidence of actual testing, or was issued by a party with no demonstrable experience in Malaysian financial services technology assessments. Examiners will also assess whether the institution critically evaluated the report's findings and follow-up actions, rather than treating it as a tick-box exercise. The independence of the external party from the institution's technology vendors and internal teams will be closely examined.

evidenceItems.name: External Assurance Provider Competency Assessment Record
evidenceItems.description: A structured evaluation record documenting the institution's due diligence on the external assurance provider's professional qualifications, relevant certifications, domain expertise, and technical capabilities specific to the technology or digital service being assessed. This demonstrates that the institution made an informed, evidence-based selection rather than a relationship-driven appointment.
whatGoodLooksLike: The evaluation records specific professional certifications held by key personnel (e.g., CISA, CISSP, OSCP, ISO 27001 Lead Auditor) that are directly relevant to the scope of the assurance engagement
The assessment includes a skills and experience matrix mapping the provider's capabilities to the specific technology domains covered by Appendix 7 Parts C and D
Evaluation was conducted using a documented scoring or weighting methodology with clear pass/fail thresholds applied consistently across all shortlisted providers
The record includes verified CVs or firm profiles of the specific personnel assigned to the engagement, not merely the firm's general credentials
The assessment was reviewed and approved by the institution's CISO or equivalent technology risk governance body prior to formal engagement
commonGaps: Competency assessment relies solely on the firm's marketing brochure or website profile without verifying individual assessor credentials assigned to the engagement
Evaluation does not map provider capabilities to the specific technical domains in Appendix 7 Parts C and D, making it impossible to confirm suitability for scope
No documented scoring methodology — selection is retrospectively justified after an appointment had already been made informally
CVs of assigned personnel are absent or not verified against claimed certifications through the relevant certification body
Assessment was performed by the project team rather than an independent internal function (e.g., Group Procurement or Technology Risk), creating a conflict of interest in the selection process
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — vendor risk or third-party assessment module
Group Procurement or Vendor Management SharePoint repository — RFP evaluation scorecards
Technology Risk or CISO Office — vendor due diligence records and approval minutes
Human Resources or external verification services for credential validation records
Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting packs where vendor approval was noted
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (evaluation report with scoring matrix and approval sign-off)

evidenceItems.name: External Assurance Provider Track Record Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution assessed the external party's historical performance and reputation in conducting technology assurance engagements for financial institutions, particularly those subject to BNM regulatory requirements. This evidence supports the institution's determination that the provider has a 'good track record' as required by clause 16.5.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reference checks were conducted with at least two prior clients of the provider, with written responses documenting the quality and timeliness of prior assurance work
The provider's track record includes prior engagements specifically involving BNM-regulated institutions or equivalent central bank-regulated entities in comparable jurisdictions

Any prior regulatory findings, disciplinary actions, or quality failures associated with the provider's assurance work have been identified, documented, and explicitly considered in the selection decision
The institution obtained and reviewed anonymised samples of prior assurance reports (redacted for confidentiality) to assess depth, rigour, and alignment with Appendix 7 structure
Track record assessment covers a meaningful historical period of at least three years, demonstrating sustained performance rather than a single successful engagement
commonGaps: Track record assessment is limited to the provider's self-reported client list with no independent verification or reference contact
No evidence that prior client references were actually contacted — the assessment references 'references available upon request' without follow-through
Track record is assessed at the firm level only, with no consideration of whether the specific team assigned to the engagement has relevant prior experience
The institution failed to check whether the provider has been subject to any professional body sanctions, regulatory censure, or quality review findings
Track record evidence is undated or pre-dates significant changes in the provider's ownership, key personnel, or practice area focus
suggestedSources: Group Procurement or Vendor Management — reference check records and client feedback forms
ISACA, (ISC)², PIKOM, or other professional body websites — member directories and disciplinary records
SC Malaysia or BNM public registers for any regulatory actions against audit/assurance firms
Legal and Compliance department — contract history and performance records for prior engagements with the same provider
Internal audit department records if the provider was previously engaged for any internal assurance work
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (reference check summaries, prior engagement samples, track record assessment report)

evidenceItems.name: Independence Declaration and Conflict of Interest Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation confirming that the external assurance provider is genuinely independent of the institution's technology vendors, system integrators, and internal teams involved in developing or implementing the digital service being assessed. Independence is a prerequisite for assurance credibility and is implicit in the clause's requirement for an 'independent external party'.
whatGoodLooksLike: A signed independence declaration from the assurance provider explicitly listing all relationships with the institution's technology vendors and confirming none create a conflict of interest
The institution's own conflict of interest review cross-referenced the provider against all technology vendors, cloud providers, and system integrators involved in the in-scope digital service
Independence assessment was conducted by a function separate from the project team commissioning the assurance (e.g., Legal, Compliance, or Procurement)
The declaration covers not only financial relationships but also prior employment links, board memberships, and subcontracting relationships between the assurance firm and in-scope vendors
Any identified relationships are escalated with documented management decisions on whether independence is sufficiently preserved, rather than being silently dismissed
commonGaps: Independence declaration is a generic template that does not reference the specific vendors and technology components relevant to the in-scope digital service
The institution did not conduct its own conflict of interest check and relied solely on the provider's self-declaration without verification
Declaration was obtained after the engagement had commenced rather than prior to engagement commencement
Subcontractors used by the assurance provider were not included in the independence assessment, leaving a potential blind spot
No process exists to refresh the independence assessment if the scope of the digital service changes materially during the assurance engagement
suggestedSources: Legal and Compliance department — conflict of interest registers and declaration filing system
Group Procurement — vendor relationship register and supply chain mapping
GRC platform — third-party risk module with conflict of interest workflow
Contract management system — executed engagement letters containing independence representations
Technology Risk or CISO Office — assurance engagement governance files
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed declaration with institutional countersignature and date)

evidenceItems.name: Assurance Report Appendix 7 Part C and D Coverage Matrix
evidenceItems.description: A structured traceability matrix or coverage analysis demonstrating that the assurance report issued by the external party explicitly addresses every mandatory matter prescribed in Appendix 7 Part C and Part D of the RMiT policy document. This is the primary evidence that the assurance 'addresses the matters covered in, and complies with, Part C and D of Appendix 7' as required by clause 16.5.
whatGoodLooksLike: A line-by-line mapping table cross-referencing each Appendix 7 Part C and Part D requirement against the specific section, page, and finding in the assurance report
The coverage matrix was prepared or reviewed by an internal function (e.g., Technology Risk or Compliance) independently of the project team, confirming completeness
Each Appendix 7 requirement is marked with a disposition (addressed, partially addressed, not applicable with justification) and the corresponding report reference
Where any Appendix 7 requirement is assessed as not applicable to the specific digital service, the justification is documented and approved by the CISO or equivalent authority
The assurance report itself explicitly states that it was conducted with reference to Appendix 7 of the RMiT policy document and identifies the specific version/date of the policy used
commonGaps: No coverage matrix exists — the institution assumes the assurance report is compliant without systematically verifying against Appendix 7 line items
The assurance report addresses generic technology security domains but omits specific RMiT Appendix 7 requirements such as cyber resilience testing or third-party dependency assessment
The coverage matrix was prepared by the project team that commissioned the assurance rather than an independent reviewer, creating a self-assessment bias
Appendix 7 Part D operational matters (e.g., incident management capability, business continuity) are absent from the assurance scope due to an overly narrow technical focus
The assurance report references an outdated version of the RMiT policy or Appendix 7, meaning post-amendment requirements may not have been assessed
suggestedSources: Technology Risk or Compliance department — RMiT compliance tracking workbooks and coverage matrices
GRC platform — regulatory requirement mapping module linked to assurance engagement records
Project Management Office (PMO) SharePoint — digital service launch governance documentation
CISO Office — assurance report repository with coverage annotations
Internal Audit department — if internal audit reviewed the external assurance report quality
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or PDF with embedded traceability table

evidenceItems.name: Assurance Engagement Scope and Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: The formal engagement letter, terms of reference, or scope document agreed between the institution and the external assurance provider prior to commencement of work. This document demonstrates that the assurance scope was deliberately designed to cover all Appendix 7 Part C and Part D matters, and that the institution exercised governance over the assurance process rather than delegating scope definition entirely to the provider.
whatGoodLooksLike: The terms of reference explicitly reference RMiT Appendix 7 Parts C and D by name and require the assurance provider to address each prescribed matter

The scope document specifies the technology components, data flows, third-party integrations, and operational environments included within the assurance boundary, with clear exclusion justifications where applicable
The engagement letter includes delivery milestones, quality review rights for the institution, and a process for the institution to request remediation of report deficiencies before finalisation
The scope was reviewed and approved by the institution's Technology Risk or Compliance function, not solely by the project team, prior to engagement commencement
Any amendments to the agreed scope during the engagement are documented through a formal change control process with re-approval by the relevant governance authority
commonGaps: Terms of reference are generic and do not reference RMiT or Appendix 7, meaning the provider may not have been explicitly directed to address mandatory requirements
Scope was defined by the project team and signed off without review by Technology Risk or Compliance, resulting in RMiT-specific requirements being omitted
No provision in the engagement terms for the institution to reject or require revision of a report that does not meet Appendix 7 coverage requirements
Scope document does not define the assurance boundary clearly, allowing the provider to exclude critical components (e.g., cloud infrastructure, third-party APIs) without formal justification
The engagement letter is a vendor standard form without institution-specific RMiT obligations incorporated, reflecting a procurement failure rather than a regulatory compliance-driven engagement
suggestedSources: Legal department or contract management system — executed engagement letters and statements of work
Group Procurement — vendor contract repository with associated scope schedules
PMO SharePoint or project governance repository — project initiation documents referencing assurance scope
Technology Risk or CISO Office — assurance governance approval records
GRC platform — third-party engagement records with scope documentation attachments
evidenceItems.format: PDF (executed engagement letter with scope schedule and approval signatures)

evidenceItems.name: Assurance Findings Remediation and Management Response Record
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of the institution's structured response to findings raised in the external assurance report, including management acceptance, remediation plans with owners and target dates, and evidence of closure or escalation. This demonstrates that the institution treated the assurance as a substantive governance mechanism rather than a procedural requirement, and that findings were actioned before or contemporaneously with the digital service launch.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each assurance finding is captured in the institution's issue management system with a risk rating, assigned owner, agreed remediation action, and target closure date
Critical and high-rated findings are escalated to the Technology Risk Committee or equivalent governance body with explicit acceptance or remediation decisions documented in meeting minutes
Evidence of finding closure includes artefacts produced after remediation (e.g., retest results, updated configurations, revised procedures) rather than mere management attestation

The digital service launch decision records explicitly reference the status of outstanding assurance findings and document management's acceptance of any residual risks for findings not closed pre-launch
A post-launch review mechanism is documented for findings agreed to be remediated post-launch, with defined accountability and escalation triggers if target dates are missed
commonGaps: Assurance findings were not entered into the institution's formal issue management or GRC system, making it impossible to demonstrate systematic tracking and governance
Critical findings were closed based on management attestation without supporting evidence of the remediation action actually being implemented and independently verified
The digital service launch proceeded with unresolved high-rated findings and no documented risk acceptance by an appropriately senior authority
Remediation plans lack defined owners and specific target dates, making accountability and progress monitoring impossible to evidence
Post-launch remediation commitments have no monitoring mechanism, resulting in findings remaining perpetually open without escalation
suggestedSources: GRC platform or ITSM system (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) — issue and findings management module
Technology Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee meeting minutes — findings escalation and acceptance records
Internal Audit department — validation evidence for closed findings if internal audit performed closure testing
PMO SharePoint — go/no-go launch decision documentation referencing assurance finding status
CISO Office — risk acceptance registers for findings accepted without full remediation
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (findings tracker) and PDF (committee minutes, risk acceptance forms, closure evidence packages)

auditTips: Before the examination, prepare a single consolidated assurance dossier for each notified digital service that contains in sequence: the competency assessment, track record evidence, independence declaration, terms of reference, the assurance report, the Appendix 7 coverage matrix, and the findings remediation tracker — BNM examiners frequently request all these documents together and gaps become immediately visible when they are collated rather than held in disparate systems.

Conduct an internal 'Appendix 7 dry-run' before submitting the assurance report to BNM — have your Technology Risk or Compliance team independently verify the report covers every Part C and Part D line item, because examiners will perform exactly this check and a single omitted mandatory matter can trigger a material finding against clause 16.5.
