Cross-reference the assurance provider's declared independence against your institution's complete technology vendor register, not just the primary system integrator — examiners have identified conflicts arising from subcontractor relationships and cloud sub-processor arrangements that were not surfaced in provider self-declarations.

Ensure the terms of reference for the assurance engagement are negotiated and finalised before work commences, and explicitly include a contractual right for your institution to require report revisions if Appendix 7 coverage is found to be deficient — this creates a quality control mechanism that demonstrates to examiners that the institution governed the process proactively.

For the provider's track record, obtain and retain written reference responses rather than verbal confirmations — examiners may ask to see the reference check records, and undocumented verbal references are treated as equivalent to no reference check being performed.

Maintain a register of all external assurance providers used across all RMiT-notified digital services, updated annually, showing each provider's credentials, engagement history, and any quality issues — this demonstrates a programme-level governance approach rather than ad-hoc engagement management and signals institutional maturity to examiners.

Where assurance findings remain open at the time of BNM examination, prepare a clear remediation status summary with objective evidence of progress for each item — examiners distinguish between findings that are genuinely in remediation with demonstrable progress and findings that have simply aged without action, and the latter will significantly increase examination scrutiny of your overall assurance programme.

16.6.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.6.title: Provision of Documents for Bank Review
16.6.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution maintains a structured, readily accessible repository of all documentation pertaining to digital service introductions or enhancements notified to the Bank. Examiners look specifically at the institution's ability to produce requested documents promptly and completely, treating delays or incomplete submissions as indicators of weak governance over technology change management. Red flags include inability to locate historical notification packages, version-controlled artefacts that are inconsistent with what was submitted to BNM, and absence of a documented custodianship or retrieval process. Examiners also verify that document sets are complete end-to-end — covering pre-notification, assessment, approval, and post-implementation phases — rather than selectively curated. Institutions that cannot demonstrate a systematic document-provision capability signal broader weaknesses in their technology governance and regulatory engagement frameworks.

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification Submission Package
evidenceItems.description: The complete, version-controlled document package submitted to BNM for each digital service introduction or enhancement notification, including cover letters, technical annexes, risk assessments, and any supplementary materials provided at the time of notification.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each submission package is stored as a single, date-stamped archive (ZIP or equivalent) with a manifest listing all included documents and their versions
Cover letters bear authorised signatories (e.g., CTO or CRO) and reference the specific BNM notification reference number assigned at receipt
All technical annexes, architecture diagrams, and risk assessments are internally consistent — dates, system names, and scope align across documents
The repository is indexed by notification reference number, product name, and submission date, enabling retrieval within one business day
Supplementary materials provided in response to BNM follow-up queries are appended to the original package and clearly labelled as post-submission addenda
commonGaps: Submission packages are fragmented across email threads, shared drives, and individual laptops without a centralised repository, making complete retrieval unreliable
Cover letters and technical annexes have mismatched dates or scope descriptions, suggesting documents were assembled retrospectively rather than at time of submission
BNM acknowledgement receipts or reference numbers are not retained alongside the submission package, making it impossible to verify what was formally notified
Only the final approved version is retained; earlier drafts and revision histories that demonstrate the evolution of the submission are discarded
No manifest or index exists, so auditors cannot confirm that all required components of the notification package were actually submitted
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — regulatory submissions module
SharePoint or Confluence — dedicated BNM Notifications library with access controls
Compliance team email archive — BNM correspondence folders
Document management system (DMS) such as OpenText or M-Files — regulatory affairs collection
Regulatory Affairs or Technology Risk team — custodian of submission records
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed cover letters and attestations), PDF/DOCX (technical annexes), ZIP archive (complete package), with accompanying submission register in XLSX

evidenceItems.name: Document Provision Register and Retrieval Log
evidenceItems.description: A formal register recording every instance where BNM has requested documents under this clause, capturing request details, documents provided, provision timelines, and custodian accountability — demonstrating the institution's responsiveness and process discipline.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each BNM request is logged with a unique tracking ID, date of request, BNM officer contact, specific documents requested, and required response deadline
Actual provision dates are recorded against each request, enabling calculation of response time and demonstrating compliance with any BNM-specified timeframes
The register captures the name and role of the internal custodian who compiled and dispatched each document set, establishing clear accountability
Escalation entries are present where deadlines were at risk, showing that the institution has a functioning escalation protocol rather than ad hoc responses
The register is reviewed and signed off quarterly by the Chief Compliance Officer or equivalent, confirming active oversight of regulatory document provision obligations
commonGaps: No formal register exists; document provision is managed informally via email with no audit trail of what was requested, when, and what was actually sent
Response timelines are not tracked, so the institution cannot demonstrate whether it met BNM's requested provision deadlines
The register records only completed requests, omitting instances where BNM requests were partially fulfilled or where follow-up was required
Custodian accountability is absent — there is no record of who was responsible for compiling and dispatching documents, making it impossible to assess process ownership
The register is maintained by a single individual with no backup custodian, creating a single point of failure in the document provision process
suggestedSources: GRC platform — regulatory engagement or examination management module
SharePoint list or Teams channel — BNM correspondence tracker
ITSM tool (e.g., Jira Service Management, ServiceNow) — regulatory request ticket queue
Compliance Operations team — manual register if no platform exists
Legal and Compliance department — examination readiness tracker
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export with timestamped audit trail; PDF for signed quarterly sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Internal Document Custodianship and Retrieval Policy
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy or standard operating procedure defining how the institution maintains, organises, and retrieves documents related to BNM digital service notifications — including roles, responsibilities, storage locations, retrieval timelines, and escalation paths.
whatGoodLooksLike: The policy explicitly references RMiT clause 16.6 and the obligation to provide documents to BNM upon request, grounding procedural requirements in regulatory obligation
Defined retrieval SLAs are stated (e.g., documents to be assembled and provided within X business days of a BNM request), with escalation triggers if SLAs are at risk
Named roles (not just titles) or organisational units are designated as custodians for each category of notification document, with a documented handover process when personnel change
The policy covers the full document lifecycle from creation through submission, post-submission addenda, and long-term retention, with no gaps in custodianship
The policy has been reviewed within the past 12 months and approved by a senior governance body (e.g., Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee)
commonGaps: The policy exists but does not reference BNM document provision obligations specifically, making it unclear whether regulatory requests are treated differently from internal records requests
No retrieval SLAs are defined, so there is no basis for assessing whether the institution's response to BNM requests is timely or adequate
Custodianship is assigned to a role that has since been restructured, and the policy has not been updated to reflect current organisational responsibilities
The policy covers document creation and submission but is silent on post-submission retention and retrieval, creating a gap in the regulatory document lifecycle
The policy has not been reviewed or tested against an actual BNM document request scenario, so its practical effectiveness is unverified
suggestedSources: Policy management system (e.g., PolicyTech, Convercent, SharePoint policy library)
Technology Risk or Compliance department — regulatory document management procedures
Records and Information Management team — document retention schedules
Internal Audit — policy inventory and review schedule
Board or Board Risk Committee secretariat — approved governance documents
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed and version-controlled policy document) with approval minutes from governing body

evidenceItems.name: BNM Correspondence Records
evidenceItems.description: All written correspondence exchanged with BNM in relation to digital service notifications — including BNM requests for documents, the institution's responses and transmittal letters, BNM acknowledgements, and any follow-up queries — forming a complete audit trail of regulatory engagement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Correspondence is filed chronologically per notification reference, linking each BNM request to the specific document set provided in response
Transmittal letters accompanying document submissions are signed by an authorised officer and explicitly list the documents enclosed, preventing disputes about what was provided
BNM acknowledgements and any feedback or queries received are retained alongside outgoing correspondence, creating a full dialogue record
Correspondence is stored in a system with access controls and immutable audit logging, preventing retrospective alteration of records
A correspondence index is maintained showing all communications per notification reference, enabling examiners to trace the full regulatory engagement history
commonGaps: BNM correspondence is stored in individual email inboxes rather than a shared, access-controlled repository, making records inaccessible when staff depart or change roles
Transmittal letters do not list the specific documents enclosed, making it impossible to confirm after the fact exactly what was provided to BNM
Incoming BNM acknowledgements and queries are retained but outgoing responses are not systematically filed, leaving incomplete correspondence records
Correspondence records are commingled across multiple regulatory topics without clear linkage to specific notification references, making retrieval time-consuming and error-prone
Physical correspondence (if any) is not scanned and filed digitally, creating a risk of loss and non-retrievability during examination
suggestedSources: Compliance or Regulatory Affairs team — dedicated BNM correspondence folder in shared mailbox
Document management system (DMS) — regulatory correspondence module
SharePoint — BNM Engagement site with per-notification subfolders
Legal department — external correspondence archive
Board secretariat — formal regulatory letters register
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scanned originals and digital correspondence), with metadata tagging for notification reference, date, and correspondent

evidenceItems.name: Document Readiness Self-Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A periodic internal assessment — conducted at least annually or ahead of anticipated BNM engagement — confirming that all notification document packages are complete, retrievable, and current, identifying and remediating gaps before a formal BNM request is received.
whatGoodLooksLike: The self-assessment uses a standardised checklist aligned to BNM's expected notification document requirements under RMiT, covering all active and recently closed notifications
Gaps identified during the assessment are logged as findings with assigned owners, remediation actions, and target completion dates, demonstrating a closed-loop process
The assessment is conducted by a function independent of the teams that prepared the original notification documents (e.g., Internal Audit or a second-line compliance review team)
Results are reported to a senior governance forum (e.g., Technology Risk Committee) with evidence of discussion and approval of remediation plans
Prior assessment findings are tracked to closure before the next assessment cycle, showing continuous improvement in document readiness
commonGaps: No self-assessment is conducted; the institution operates reactively, only discovering document gaps when BNM makes a formal request
The self-assessment checklist does not align to RMiT notification requirements and misses document categories that BNM examiners routinely request
Assessments are conducted by the same team that prepared the notification packages, compromising independence and objectivity
Gap findings are noted informally but not tracked to closure, so the same deficiencies recur across assessment cycles
The frequency of self-assessment is ad hoc rather than scheduled, and there is no evidence that assessments were conducted ahead of known BNM examination or engagement windows
suggestedSources: Internal Audit — technology and compliance audit workpapers
Second-line Technology Risk team — compliance self-assessment tracker
GRC platform — control testing and assessment module
Compliance Operations team — examination readiness programme
Technology Risk Committee — meeting packs and minutes recording assessment results
evidenceItems.format: XLSX or GRC platform export (assessment checklist with findings); PDF (assessment report with sign-off); meeting minutes (governance discussion evidence)

evidenceItems.name: Staff Training and Awareness Records for Document Provision Obligations
evidenceItems.description: Training completion records and awareness materials demonstrating that personnel responsible for managing, assembling, and providing documents to BNM understand their obligations under RMiT 16.6 and the institution's internal document provision procedures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Training records identify the specific staff groups covered (e.g., Technology Risk, Compliance, IT Project Managers) with 100% completion rates for mandatory training modules
Training content explicitly references RMiT 16.6 and the obligation to provide documents to BNM upon request, not just generic records management principles
Training is refreshed at least annually and following any significant update to BNM notification requirements or internal procedures, with version history maintained
Competency assessments or attestations are conducted post-training to verify understanding, with pass thresholds documented and remedial training assigned for failures
Training records are maintained in an LMS or HR system with exportable completion reports, enabling rapid production of evidence during examination
commonGaps: No targeted training on BNM document provision obligations exists; staff rely solely on generic records management training that does not address regulatory-specific requirements
Training completion records are incomplete or cannot be extracted efficiently from the LMS, making it difficult to demonstrate that responsible personnel have been trained
Training content is outdated and does not reflect current BNM notification requirements or recent changes to internal document custodianship arrangements
Training is provided to compliance staff only, excluding IT project managers and technology teams who are often the primary custodians of technical notification documents
No competency verification follows training, so there is no evidence that attendees understood their obligations beyond mere attendance
suggestedSources: Learning Management System (LMS) — training completion and assessment records
HR system — mandatory training tracker
Compliance team — regulatory training programme materials and attendance logs
Technology Risk team — awareness session records
Internal communications platform — awareness campaign artefacts (newsletters, briefing decks)
evidenceItems.format: LMS export (CSV/PDF completion report), PDF (training materials and assessment questions), signed attendance registers for in-person sessions

auditTips: Conduct a dry-run BNM document request simulation at least six months before any anticipated examination: issue an internal mock request for the three most complex recent notification packages and time how long it takes to produce complete, verified document sets — anything beyond two business days warrants immediate remediation of the retrieval process.

Cross-reference the BNM notification submission register against the internal project portfolio and change management log to confirm that every digital service introduction or enhancement notified to BNM has a corresponding, retrievable document package — gaps in this mapping are a primary examiner red flag.

Verify that document version numbers and dates in retained packages match what was recorded in BNM correspondence at time of submission; retrospectively assembled or backdated documents are detectable through metadata inconsistencies and will severely undermine examiner confidence in the institution's governance.

Map custodian responsibilities explicitly: for each notification package retained, confirm that a named individual and a named backup currently hold the access credentials and knowledge to retrieve the package — personnel turnover without custodianship handover is one of the most common causes of retrieval failure during examination.

Ensure that BNM correspondence related to document provision requests is stored in a shared, access-controlled repository (not individual email inboxes), and test that at least two members of the Compliance team can independently retrieve any correspondence record within 30 minutes of a request — single points of failure in correspondence retrieval are a process maturity red flag.

Review the document provision SLAs in your internal policy against BNM's typical turnaround expectations and any timeframes communicated in prior engagement; if your policy states five business days but BNM has historically expected two, align the policy to reality and ensure staffing and workflow support the tighter timeline.

Before examination, prepare a master index — a single document listing every BNM notification submitted in the past seven years, the reference number, submission date, document package storage location, and current custodian — and present this proactively to examiners as evidence of systematic document governance rather than waiting for individual document requests to expose gaps.

16.7.subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
16.7.title: Immediate Offering Upon Notification
16.7.auditorFocus: BNM examiners evaluating clause 16.7 focus primarily on establishing a clear, documented sequence of events: that the institution submitted a valid notification under paragraph 16.1 and demonstrably satisfied all requirements under paragraphs 16.2 to 16.6 before or simultaneous with the go-live of the digital service or enhancement — not after. A primary red flag is post-hoc notification, where evidence shows the service launched days or weeks before the notification was formally submitted or where pre-launch compliance artefacts are backdated. Examiners will cross-reference notification submission timestamps against deployment logs, change management records, and production release notes to validate the sequence. They also look for evidence that the institution understood clause 16.7 as a self-assessment regime requiring internal discipline rather than a passive administrative step, and will probe whether a formal internal gate existed to prevent go-live absent completed 16.2–16.6 compliance artefacts. Institutions that conflate the notification submission date with the compliance completion date, or that cannot produce contemporaneous evidence of each prerequisite, are considered high-risk findings.

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification Submission Receipt
evidenceItems.description: The official acknowledgement or submission record confirming that the notification required under paragraph 16.1 was formally lodged with BNM via the prescribed channel (e.g., BNM FAST portal or equivalent regulatory submission system), including a machine-generated timestamp, reference number, and summary of the notified initiative. This record is the foundational proof that the institution triggered the clause 16.7 entitlement to offer the service immediately.
whatGoodLooksLike: Portal-generated submission receipt with a unique BNM reference number, submission timestamp accurate to at least the minute, and the submitting officer's identity
Submission content matches the actual service or enhancement launched — scope, system name, and nature of change are consistent across both the notification and the go-live artefacts
Submission date is on or before the production go-live date, with no gap in the opposite direction
Retention is in a tamper-evident repository (e.g., compliance document management system) with an unbroken audit trail showing no post-submission edits
A covering email or internal memo from the CISO or CIO confirming submission to the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body on the same day
commonGaps: Submission receipt is a screenshot rather than a portal export, making it difficult to verify authenticity or confirm it was not edited
The notification reference number cannot be traced back to BNM's records during examination, suggesting a draft was saved but never formally submitted
Submission date is later than the go-live date recorded in the ITSM change management system, directly contradicting the clause 16.7 sequence requirement
Notification scope in the submission describes a narrower feature set than what was actually deployed, raising questions about completeness of disclosure
No named individual is associated with the submission, making it impossible to confirm the authorised signatory per the institution's notification governance policy
suggestedSources: BNM FAST (Financial Accessibility Submission Terminal) portal submission history and exported receipts
Compliance team's regulatory submissions register maintained in GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Riskonnect, or MetricStream)
Email archive of the Regulatory Affairs mailbox for submission confirmation threads
SharePoint or Confluence compliance submissions library with access-controlled document versioning
evidenceItems.format: PDF export from BNM portal or certified digital receipt; supplemented by DOCX/PDF covering memo

evidenceItems.name: Pre-Launch Compliance Gate Checklist (Paragraphs 16.2–16.6)
evidenceItems.description: A structured, completed compliance checklist that documents the institution's self-assessment against each of the requirements in paragraphs 16.2 to 16.6 as a condition precedent to go-live. Each checklist item should be individually signed off by the responsible function (e.g., Risk, IT Security, Legal, Operations) with a completion date, and the aggregate checklist must be approved by a senior authority before deployment. This is the primary evidence that the institution treated clauses 16.2–16.6 as substantive prerequisites rather than administrative formalities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each paragraph from 16.2 to 16.6 is mapped to a discrete checklist line item with a completion status, responsible owner, and evidence reference (hyperlink or document ID) rather than a blanket 'complied' statement
Checklist completion dates for all line items precede or match the notification submission date under 16.1, confirming the correct sequence
The checklist is countersigned by the CISO, CTO, or equivalent senior officer, and reviewed by Internal Audit or the Risk function independently
Supporting evidence artefacts referenced in the checklist (e.g., risk assessments, security testing results, user acceptance test sign-offs) are retrievable and themselves dated prior to go-live
Version history shows the checklist was created as part of the initiative lifecycle, not compiled retrospectively after the launch date
commonGaps: Checklist exists as a generic template with tick-boxes but no evidence references, individual sign-offs, or dates — indistinguishable from a template that was never applied to this specific initiative
Several 16.2–16.6 line items are marked 'N/A' without documented rationale, suggesting the institution did not assess applicability rigorously
Checklist completion date is weeks after the production deployment date, indicating compliance was assessed after the service was already live
Only one function (e.g., IT) completed the checklist — risk, legal, and operations sign-offs are absent, undermining the cross-functional nature of the requirements
The checklist version used does not reflect the current RMiT policy edition, meaning line items may not map to the correct paragraph obligations
suggestedSources: GRC platform initiative/project compliance module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC project risk module, Archer)
Project management platform (Jira, Azure DevOps) with compliance gate tasks linked to sprint or release milestones
Internal Audit's pre-launch advisory review workpapers stored in the audit management system (e.g., TeamMate, AuditBoard)
SharePoint compliance document library under the relevant digital initiative folder with version control enabled
evidenceItems.format: PDF or XLSX with digital signatures or workflow approval audit trail; or GRC system export

evidenceItems.name: Production Deployment and Go-Live Authorisation Record
evidenceItems.description: The formal change management record and go-live authorisation that documents the exact date and time the digital service or enhancement was released to production and made available to end-users, along with the identity of the authorising officer. When cross-referenced against the 16.1 notification receipt and the 16.2–16.6 compliance checklist, this record provides the definitive timeline evidence that the institution's offering was contemporaneous with — or after — completed prerequisites, satisfying the clause 16.7 entitlement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Change management record (RFC) in the ITSM system shows an approved change with a scheduled and actual deployment window that is on or after the notification submission timestamp
Go-live authorisation sign-off is by a named senior technology or business owner, and references the BNM notification reference number as a prerequisite condition in the change record
Deployment logs from the CI/CD pipeline or release management tool provide a machine-generated timestamp of the production deployment that is independently verifiable
Post-deployment verification steps (smoke tests, user acceptance confirmation) are documented and dated, confirming the service was operationally live at the stated time
A change freeze or deployment hold was in place prior to notification submission, evidenced by the ITSM change calendar, confirming no premature release occurred
commonGaps: The ITSM change record shows an earlier 'planned go-live' date that predates the notification submission, and the institution cannot explain why the planned date was not the actual go-live date
Production deployment logs are not retained or are overwritten after 30–90 days, making it impossible to independently verify the deployment timestamp during an examination
The go-live authorisation does not reference the BNM notification or compliance prerequisites, suggesting the deployment process is disconnected from the regulatory notification workflow
There are multiple deployment records for the same initiative (e.g., a partial release followed by full release) and it is unclear which constitutes the 'offering' for the purposes of clause 16.7
Change management records are in an unstructured format (e.g., email approvals) rather than an ITSM system, making timeline reconstruction unreliable
suggestedSources: ITSM change management system (ServiceNow ITSM, Jira Service Management, Remedy) — change request and approval history
CI/CD pipeline logs (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps Pipelines) with timestamped deployment artefacts
Release management register maintained by the Technology Change Advisory Board (CAB)
Application performance monitoring or observability platform (Dynatrace, Datadog) showing first live traffic after deployment as corroborating evidence
evidenceItems.format: ITSM system export (PDF/CSV) and CI/CD pipeline log export; supplemented by CAB minutes (PDF/DOCX)

evidenceItems.name: Digital Initiative Regulatory Notification Governance Policy
evidenceItems.description: The institution's internal policy or standard operating procedure that operationalises the BNM RMiT clause 16.7 regime — defining which types of digital service introductions or enhancements trigger the notification obligation, the internal workflow for satisfying paragraphs 16.2–16.6 prior to notification, and the governance gate preventing go-live absent completed prerequisites. This policy demonstrates that the institution has institutionalised clause 16.7 compliance as a systemic control rather than a one-off exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy explicitly references RMiT paragraphs 16.1 to 16.7 and defines the institution's interpretation of 'introduction or enhancement to digital services' with worked examples relevant to the institution's product set

A mandatory internal compliance gate (e.g., a pre-notification review board or technology risk committee sign-off) is defined as a prerequisite before BNM notification submission, ensuring 16.2–16.6 completion is independently verified
Roles and responsibilities are clearly assigned — identifying who is responsible for preparing the 16.1 notification, completing each 16.2–16.6 requirement, and authorising the go-live
Policy version history shows it was last reviewed against the current RMiT edition and is approved by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent senior governance body
The policy includes a clause stating that no digital service may be released to production without a confirmed BNM notification submission receipt on file
commonGaps: The policy covers notifications generically but does not specifically map internal workflow steps to individual RMiT paragraph obligations (16.2, 16.3, 16.4, 16.5, 16.6), leaving compliance interpretation to individual project teams
The policy has not been updated since the current RMiT edition took effect, meaning it may reference superseded paragraph numbering or obligations
Go-live authorisation is handled entirely by the Technology function without a mandatory compliance or risk sign-off, creating a single point of failure in the governance gate
The policy scope excludes minor enhancements or 'low-risk' changes without defining thresholds, potentially causing the institution to under-notify BNM on changes that meet the RMiT definition
There is no training record showing that project managers, product owners, and technology leads responsible for digital initiatives have been trained on the policy
suggestedSources: Compliance and regulatory affairs policy library (SharePoint, Confluence, or dedicated policy management platform such as PolicyHub or Navex PolicyTech)
Board Risk Committee or Technology Risk Committee meeting minutes approving the policy
HR or learning management system (LMS) for training completion records linked to the policy
Internal audit's policy inventory and last review date register
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX policy document with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: Digital Initiatives Notification Register
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register maintained by the Compliance or Technology Risk function that logs every digital service introduction and enhancement subject to the RMiT clause 16.1 notification regime, recording for each initiative: the initiative name, notification submission date and BNM reference, 16.2–16.6 compliance completion date, go-live date, and current status. This register provides BNM examiners with a population view of all notifiable events and enables rapid sampling to test whether the clause 16.7 sequence was maintained across multiple initiatives.
whatGoodLooksLike: Register is complete and current, covering all digital service introductions and enhancements during the examination period without unexplained gaps, including initiatives that were abandoned prior to launch
For every entry, the notification submission date is equal to or earlier than the go-live date, with no exceptions — any exceptions are accompanied by a documented escalation and remediation record
Register is maintained in a system with an audit trail (GRC platform, SharePoint list with versioning) preventing retrospective alteration of dates without detection

Each register entry contains a hyperlink or document reference to the underlying notification receipt (E1), compliance checklist (E2), and go-live authorisation (E3), enabling rapid cross-referencing during examination
Register ownership is assigned to a named compliance officer and is subject to periodic internal audit sampling to validate accuracy of recorded dates against source systems
commonGaps: Register only captures major product launches and omits feature enhancements or infrastructure-layer changes that may qualify as 'enhancements to digital services' under RMiT
Dates in the register are manually entered and have not been reconciled against source system timestamps (ITSM, BNM portal), making them unreliable as standalone evidence
The register has no evidence of periodic review or sign-off, suggesting it is a static spreadsheet updated inconsistently rather than a governed compliance tool
Several entries show a go-live date but no corresponding BNM notification reference, indicating either the notification was not submitted or was not linked back to the register entry
The register is maintained in a personal OneDrive or local drive folder rather than a shared, access-controlled system, creating both single-point-of-failure and chain-of-custody concerns
suggestedSources: GRC platform regulatory submissions module (Riskonnect, MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC) configured with a custom notification tracking object
SharePoint list with mandatory field validation and column-level audit logging enabled
Compliance team's regulatory reporting tool or internal tracker reviewed by the Technology Risk Committee quarterly
Integration with ITSM change management system to auto-populate go-live dates from approved change records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export (CSV/PDF) with embedded audit trail metadata

evidenceItems.name: Board and Senior Management Reporting on Notification Compliance
evidenceItems.description: Minutes, management reports, or dashboard extracts showing that the institution's Board Risk Committee, Technology Risk Committee, or equivalent senior governance body receives periodic reporting on the status of BNM digital service notifications — including confirmation that clause 16.7 prerequisites were met for each initiative launched during the reporting period. This evidence demonstrates that compliance with the notification regime is subject to senior-level oversight rather than being managed solely at the operational level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or committee reports include a standing agenda item or periodic section on RMiT notification status, covering the number of digital initiatives notified, any delays or exceptions, and remediation actions

Reports explicitly confirm, by reference to individual initiatives, that the go-live sequence complied with clause 16.7 (notification submitted before or at launch, all 16.2–16.6 prerequisites completed)
Management reporting distinguishes between notifications in progress, completed, and any exceptions requiring escalation, demonstrating active monitoring rather than retrospective reporting
Minutes record that committee members reviewed, questioned, and accepted the notification compliance report, not merely that it was tabled — evidencing genuine governance oversight
Reporting cadence is at least quarterly, or triggered ad-hoc for significant digital launches, ensuring timely senior visibility
commonGaps: Notification compliance is reported only as a line item in a broader regulatory compliance dashboard without initiative-level detail, making it impossible to confirm that any specific launch complied with clause 16.7
Reports to the Board are prepared by the same function responsible for submissions (i.e., no independent second-line or internal audit input), weakening assurance quality
Minutes do not record any questions or deliberations on the notification report, suggesting it was tabled without substantive review — a governance adequacy concern
Reporting frequency is annual, meaning multiple digital launches during the year are never subject to senior review until well after the fact
Reports use forward-looking language ('we plan to notify') rather than confirming completed submissions and go-live sequence, providing no retrospective assurance
suggestedSources: Board Risk Committee and Technology Risk Committee secretariat — minutes and board papers archives (SharePoint, Diligent Boards, or equivalent board portal)
CRO or CISO management reporting packs prepared for senior governance meetings
Compliance dashboard or regulatory affairs periodic report maintained in the GRC platform
Internal audit committee reports referencing results of notification compliance testing
evidenceItems.format: PDF board/committee papers and certified minutes; dashboard exports in PDF or XLSX

auditTips: Build a timeline matrix for every digital initiative launched in the examination period, plotting three key dates side-by-side: (1) the BNM notification submission timestamp from the portal receipt, (2) the 16.2–16.6 compliance checklist completion date, and (3) the production go-live date from the ITSM system. Any initiative where date 3 precedes date 1 or date 2 is a direct clause 16.7 finding — identify and remediate these before examination and prepare a documented root cause and corrective action plan for each.

Do not rely on a single source of truth for go-live dates. Cross-reference the ITSM change management system, CI/CD pipeline deployment logs, and application monitoring first-traffic timestamps to triangulate the actual production deployment moment. BNM examiners will probe whether stated go-live dates are accurate, and discrepancies between internal systems will be treated as a record-keeping control weakness independent of the substantive notification compliance question.

Conduct a pre-examination scope mapping exercise to identify initiatives that may have been classified internally as 'minor enhancements' and therefore excluded from the notification register, but which could qualify as 'enhancements to digital services' under BNM's interpretation of RMiT. Review the definition in paragraphs 16.2–16.6 and apply it to the full change management backlog. Undiscovered notifications are a higher-risk finding than documented exceptions with remediation.

Verify that each entry in the digital initiatives notification register can be supported by three independently retrievable artefacts: the BNM notification receipt (E1), the signed pre-launch compliance checklist (E2), and the ITSM go-live authorisation record (E3). Walk through a dry-run evidence pull for a sample of 3–5 initiatives before examination — if you cannot retrieve the full artefact set within 30 minutes for each sample, your evidence organisation needs remediation before BNM arrives.

Review the internal governance policy (E4) to confirm it contains an explicit hard gate preventing production deployment without a BNM notification submission receipt on file. If your current change management process does not technically enforce this gate — for example, if a deployment can be approved in the ITSM system without a regulatory reference number field being populated — raise this as a process control gap and implement a compensating control (such as a mandatory compliance sign-off field or a workflow integration with the GRC platform) before examination.

Prepare a concise narrative for the opening meeting that explains how clause 16.7 is operationalised end-to-end within the institution — from initiative identification, through 16.2–16.6 compliance, to notification submission, to go-live authorisation. Examiners assess not just whether the institution complied on each occasion, but whether it has a mature, repeatable process. Being able to walk through the workflow with reference to policy documents, system screenshots, and a live example initiative demonstrates systemic compliance rather than ad-hoc execution.

Ensure that the digital initiatives notification register (E5) reflects the examination period population completely, including initiatives that were notified but subsequently withdrawn or changed in scope before launch. BNM may ask about notifications that were submitted but where no corresponding go-live is recorded — have a documented explanation ready for each such case, as incomplete launches are not a compliance failure but unexplained gaps will invite further scrutiny.

17.1.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.1.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.1.title: First-Time Cloud and Emerging Technology Consultation
17.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners focus on whether the institution completed all three mandatory pre-consultation deliverables — the risk assessment, the readiness confirmation, and the third-party review — before any consultation was initiated with the Bank, not merely before go-live. A primary red flag is evidence of production deployment or procurement commitment preceding the BNM consultation, which signals that the institution treated the consultation as a formality rather than a genuine gating control. Examiners will verify that the risk assessment follows the prescribed format of Appendix 7 Part A and addresses the specific risk dimensions in Appendix 10 (for public cloud) or Appendix 9 (for emerging technology), and will probe whether identified risks were substantively addressed rather than acknowledged and deferred. The third-party reviewer's independence, scope coverage, and timing relative to the consultation submission are scrutinised closely, particularly for higher-risk services involving customer data or cross-border transmission. Examiners also assess whether the confirming officer — CISO, senior management, or board-level committee chair — had genuine visibility into the risk assessment findings before signing, or merely countersigned a document prepared below them.

evidenceItems.name: Cloud/Emerging Technology Risk Assessment Report (Appendix 7 Part A)
evidenceItems.description: A formal, structured risk assessment document prepared prior to BNM consultation that comprehensively evaluates all risks associated with the proposed public cloud or emerging technology adoption for a critical system. For public cloud, it must follow the risk dimensions in paragraph 10.50 and Appendix 10; for emerging technology, it must follow Appendix 9. The document must conform to the prescribed format in Part A of Appendix 7 and demonstrate that identified risks have been adequately considered and mitigated.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is structured exactly to the Appendix 7 Part A template with all mandatory sections completed, including system classification, risk inventory, impact ratings, and mitigation measures — no sections marked 'N/A' without justification

Risk coverage explicitly maps to each risk domain in Appendix 10 (for cloud) or Appendix 9 (for emerging tech), with a traceability matrix linking each risk category to the institution's control response

Each identified risk includes a clearly defined residual risk rating, the specific control or mitigation measure applied, the responsible owner, and a target implementation date that pre-dates or aligns with go-live

Document version history and approval signatures show the assessment was finalised and endorsed before the BNM consultation submission date, with no post-consultation amendments that materially change risk conclusions

The assessment addresses cross-border data transmission, data sovereignty, cloud provider lock-in, and regulatory access to data as distinct risk items with specific mitigations, not generic statements
commonGaps: Risk assessment is adapted from a generic internal template rather than the Appendix 7 Part A prescribed format, omitting mandatory fields or restructuring sections in ways that cannot be directly mapped to BNM's expected output

Risks are listed and rated but mitigations are vague or aspirational (e.g., 'controls will be implemented') rather than specifying the actual control, the implementing party, and evidence that it is in place
The assessment covers only technical risks and omits legal, regulatory, vendor concentration, and operational continuity risks that Appendix 10 explicitly requires
Document is dated after the BNM consultation meeting or after a contract was signed with the cloud service provider, invalidating the pre-consultation sequencing requirement

Customer data processing and cross-border transmission risks are not identified as higher-risk services triggering the Part C third-party review obligation, despite the system clearly handling personal financial data
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, Archer, MetricStream) — risk assessment module where the formal report is authored and version-controlled
Information Security department's SharePoint or Confluence project workspace for the cloud adoption initiative
IT Risk and Governance team's document repository or policy management system
Cloud Centre of Excellence (CCoE) or Cloud Adoption Programme project records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (must match Appendix 7 Part A template structure); supporting spreadsheets for risk registers are acceptable as annexures

evidenceItems.name: Readiness Confirmation by CISO / Senior Management / Board Committee (Appendix 7 Part B)
evidenceItems.description: A formal written confirmation, in the format prescribed by Part B of Appendix 7, signed by the institution's CISO, a designated senior management officer, or the chairman of the board or board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3. The confirmation attests that the institution is ready to adopt public cloud or emerging technology for a critical system and that the signatory has reviewed the risk assessment findings and is satisfied that identified risks are adequately addressed.
whatGoodLooksLike: The confirmation is signed by an officer whose designation exactly matches one of the three categories specified in clause 17.1(b) — CISO, senior management officer, or board/committee chairman per paragraph 8.3 — and the signatory's title is explicitly stated on the document
The document references the specific Risk Assessment Report (17.1-E1) by title, version, and date, establishing a clear evidentiary link between the assessment findings and the readiness declaration
The signed confirmation is dated after the risk assessment was finalised and before the BNM consultation submission date, confirming correct sequencing

The confirmation follows the Appendix 7 Part B template precisely, with no omitted fields, and includes the system name, the technology type (public cloud or emerging technology), and the intended use for critical systems

Board or committee endorsement is supported by meeting minutes or a resolution excerpt that confirms the agenda item was discussed and the confirmation was formally authorised, not merely signed offline
commonGaps: The signatory is a Head of IT or IT Director rather than the CISO or a qualified senior management officer or board-level chair as explicitly required, making the confirmation non-compliant on its face
The confirmation is a generic letter drafted by the project team and signed by the officer without reference to the Appendix 7 Part B format or explicit attestation of risk review
The document is undated or bears a date that post-dates the BNM consultation, destroying the evidence of sequential compliance
No board or committee minutes exist to corroborate that the readiness confirmation was discussed and endorsed at the appropriate governance level, making the signature unverifiable
The same template is used for multiple cloud adoptions with only the system name changed, with no evidence that the confirming officer reviewed each system's specific risk profile before signing
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat's board and committee minute books and resolutions register
CISO's office or Chief Risk Officer's document management system for signed governance attestations
GRC platform — governance attestation or certification module
Board governance portal (e.g., Diligent Boards, BoardPad) where committee papers and resolutions are archived
evidenceItems.format: PDF with wet or digital signature (must match Appendix 7 Part B template); supporting board/committee minutes as annexure

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Pre-Implementation Review Report (Appendix 7 Part C)
evidenceItems.description: An independent review report commissioned from a qualified third party, completed prior to BNM consultation, assessing the institution's readiness to adopt the public cloud or emerging technology. The review scope must cover the areas in Appendix 10 (for public cloud) or Appendix 9 (for emerging technology) and must follow the format in Part C of Appendix 7, particularly for higher-risk services involving customer information processing, storage, or cross-border data transmission. The third party must be independent of both the institution and the cloud or technology vendor.
whatGoodLooksLike: The third-party reviewer's engagement letter explicitly defines a scope that maps to each area listed in Appendix 10 or Appendix 9, and the final report includes a scope-to-findings traceability matrix confirming all areas were reviewed

The reviewer's independence is clearly established — they have no contractual relationship with the proposed cloud service provider or technology vendor, and the institution's conflict-of-interest assessment for the reviewer is documented

The report includes findings, exceptions, and recommendations categorised by severity, with the institution's management responses and remediation commitments included as a separate section or annexure

For higher-risk services, the Part C format requirements are fully addressed, including data classification, cross-border transmission controls, access controls for customer information, and incident response readiness

The report is dated before the BNM consultation submission and the third-party firm's credentials, methodology, and relevant qualifications (e.g., CISA, ISO 27001 lead auditor, cloud security certifications) are disclosed in the report
commonGaps: The review was conducted by the institution's internal audit team or an external consultant who also advises the cloud vendor, failing the independence requirement
The third-party scope was limited to a high-level readiness assessment and did not cover all Appendix 10 or Appendix 9 risk areas, with large sections of the required scope absent from the report

The institution classified the service as lower-risk to avoid the Part C requirements despite the system clearly processing or storing customer financial information, and no documented risk classification rationale exists
The report was issued after the BNM consultation meeting, meaning it could not have informed the Bank's assessment of institutional readiness at the time of consultation
Management responses to third-party findings are absent, incomplete, or commit to remediation after go-live rather than before, leaving open risks unresolved at the point of consultation
suggestedSources: Procurement and vendor management system — third-party engagement contracts and statements of work
Internal Audit or Risk department's repository for externally commissioned review reports
Project management office (PMO) document library for the cloud adoption or technology implementation project
GRC platform — third-party assurance and vendor risk management module
evidenceItems.format: PDF report from the third-party firm on their letterhead; engagement letter, scope definition, and management response as supporting annexures

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Classification Records
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the system subject to the cloud or emerging technology adoption has been formally classified as a critical system under the institution's IT criticality framework, triggering the clause 17.1 consultation obligation. This evidence establishes jurisdiction — that the clause applies — and demonstrates the institution identified its own obligation proactively rather than reactively.
whatGoodLooksLike: The system appears on the institution's formally approved critical systems register with a classification date and the criteria used to designate it as critical (e.g., impact on regulated functions, customer data, or operational continuity)

The criticality assessment methodology is documented and aligns with BNM RMiT definitions of critical systems, and the system's classification has been reviewed and approved by senior management or the relevant governance committee within the required review cycle
The critical systems register is version-controlled, with an audit trail showing when the system's classification was established relative to the cloud adoption decision

There is a documented process linking the critical systems register to the cloud adoption governance workflow, showing that classification as critical automatically triggers the pre-consultation checklist

Where a system's criticality is borderline, a documented escalation and decision record exists showing the institution considered the classification question and resolved it with appropriate governance oversight
commonGaps: The critical systems register exists but was not updated to include the system prior to the cloud adoption decision, meaning the institution had no documented basis for knowing clause 17.1 applied
Criticality criteria are defined loosely and the classification is applied inconsistently across systems of similar function, making it difficult to demonstrate the methodology is rigorous
The register is maintained by IT operations without formal senior management or committee approval, reducing its evidential weight as a governance artefact

No linkage exists between the critical systems register and the cloud/technology adoption governance process, so the trigger for BNM consultation was identified ad hoc rather than through a systematic control

Systems supporting customer-facing financial services are not classified as critical despite meeting the definitional criteria, suggesting under-classification that may suppress consultation obligations
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management System (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Ivanti) — system classification and asset criticality fields
Business Continuity Management platform or BCMS register where system criticality is formally designated
IT Risk and Governance SharePoint — critical systems register maintained by the CISO's office
Board IT/Risk Committee papers where the critical systems register is periodically reviewed and approved
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system export (critical systems register); PDF for committee approval minutes

evidenceItems.name: BNM Consultation Submission Package and Correspondence Records
evidenceItems.description: The complete package submitted to BNM as the formal pre-adoption consultation, together with all official correspondence (submissions, acknowledgements, queries, and outcomes) between the institution and BNM relating to the first-time cloud or emerging technology adoption. This evidence demonstrates that the consultation occurred, that it was supported by the required deliverables, and that the Bank was satisfied before adoption proceeded.
whatGoodLooksLike: The submission package contains all three mandatory pre-consultation deliverables — the Appendix 7 Part A risk assessment, the Appendix 7 Part B readiness confirmation, and the Appendix 7 Part C third-party review — and a covering letter that explicitly references each
The submission date is documented and demonstrably precedes the system's go-live date and any contractual commitment to deploy the system on public cloud, establishing correct sequencing

All BNM correspondence — including acknowledgement of receipt, requests for additional information, BNM's queries and the institution's responses, and any outcome communication — is retained in a single organised file

Where BNM raised concerns or requested additional assurances, the institution's response documents the specific actions taken, and evidence of those actions (e.g., additional controls implemented) is cross-referenced
The file includes internal records showing how the BNM outcome was communicated to the project team and governance bodies, and how any conditions imposed by BNM were tracked to closure before go-live
commonGaps: The submission was made verbally or informally and no written record of the consultation package or BNM's response exists, making it impossible to evidence that the Bank was satisfied
The covering letter is present but one or more of the three required pre-consultation deliverables was not included in the submission, meaning the consultation was technically incomplete
BNM queries received during the consultation process are not retained or are stored in individual staff email inboxes without centralised archiving, creating gaps in the correspondence chain
Deployment proceeded before a clear indication of BNM's satisfaction was received, and the institution cannot produce evidence that it waited for the Bank's confirmation before proceeding
Only the initial submission is retained; BNM's follow-up correspondence, the institution's responses, and the final outcome are missing, leaving the consultation record incomplete
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department's regulatory submissions register and correspondence management system
Legal and Compliance SharePoint or document management system — BNM correspondence folder
Email archiving platform (e.g., Microsoft Exchange Archive, Mimecast) — correspondence with BNM's supervisory contact
Board or management committee papers where BNM consultation outcomes were reported
evidenceItems.format: PDF (submission package and all correspondence); email exports as supplementary evidence

evidenceItems.name: Pre-Consultation Sequencing and Governance Checklist
evidenceItems.description: An internal governance record demonstrating that the institution systematically verified the completion and sequencing of all three mandatory pre-consultation steps — risk assessment, readiness confirmation, and third-party review — before initiating the BNM consultation. This evidence demonstrates process discipline and regulatory awareness, showing the institution has embedded the clause 17.1 obligations into its cloud adoption governance workflow rather than relying on ad hoc compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: A formal pre-consultation checklist exists as an institutionalised artefact within the cloud adoption governance framework, with completion sign-offs by the accountable officers for each of the three deliverables and a final authorisation before the BNM submission is dispatched

The checklist records the completion dates of each deliverable alongside the BNM submission date, providing an auditable timeline that demonstrates correct sequencing without requiring manual reconstruction from multiple documents
The checklist is approved by the CISO or Chief Risk Officer and retained alongside the BNM consultation package, creating a consolidated governance file for the first-time adoption event
Where deviations from the standard sequence were identified during preparation, a documented exception or escalation record shows how they were resolved before submission

The checklist references the applicable regulatory source (RMiT clause 17.1, Appendix 7, Appendix 9 or 10) against each step, demonstrating that the institution has mapped its process directly to the regulatory obligation
commonGaps: No pre-consultation checklist exists and sequencing compliance can only be inferred retrospectively from document dates, which introduces ambiguity about whether the sequence was planned or accidental
The checklist exists as a project management task list rather than a formal governance artefact, lacking sign-off by accountable officers and not retained as a regulatory evidence document
Completion dates on the checklist are inconsistent with the dates on the underlying deliverables, suggesting the checklist was completed retrospectively to create the appearance of compliance
The checklist does not distinguish between mandatory and optional steps, treating the three pre-consultation requirements as equivalent to routine project milestones without regulatory weight

The institution relies on the project manager's judgment to initiate BNM consultation rather than a formal governance gate, and no evidence exists of an independent compliance or legal review confirming readiness to consult
suggestedSources: Cloud governance framework documentation maintained by the Cloud Centre of Excellence or IT Governance team
Project management platform (e.g., Jira, Azure DevOps) — governance gate tasks and sign-off records for the cloud adoption project
Compliance department's regulatory checklist library or policy management system
CISO's office — cloud adoption governance sign-off register
evidenceItems.format: PDF or DOCX (formal checklist with sign-off fields); project management system export as supplementary evidence

auditTips: Establish a clear timeline artefact — a single document or table showing the dates of: (1) system criticality classification, (2) risk assessment finalisation, (3) readiness confirmation signature, (4) third-party review report issuance, (5) BNM consultation submission, and (6) system go-live or contract execution. BNM examiners will reconstruct this timeline independently from document metadata, email timestamps, and contract dates, so any inconsistency between the institution's narrative and the documentary evidence will be immediately apparent.

Cross-reference the risk assessment (17.1-E1) against the Appendix 10 or Appendix 9 risk taxonomy line by line before the BNM examination, and prepare a gap analysis demonstrating that every prescribed risk area has a corresponding section in the assessment. Examiners use the appendix as a checklist and will flag any missing risk dimension as a deficiency, even if the institution has addressed equivalent risks under different headings.

Verify that the officer who signed the Appendix 7 Part B readiness confirmation holds a designation that unambiguously falls within the three permitted categories in clause 17.1(b) — CISO, senior management officer, or board/committee chair per paragraph 8.3. Prepare a one-page designation mapping document showing the signatory's title, their position in the governance structure, and the specific paragraph 8.3 category they satisfy, as examiners may challenge titles that are not self-evidently within the prescribed categories.

For the third-party reviewer (17.1-E3), prepare a conflict-of-interest declaration and a credentials summary showing the reviewer's qualifications, relevant experience, and absence of any relationship with the cloud or technology vendor. If the same firm has previously advised the institution on cloud strategy, escalate the independence question to legal counsel before the examination and prepare a documented justification if the institution believes the prior engagement does not impair independence.

If the system processes or stores customer information or involves cross-border data transmission, confirm that the institution formally classified it as a higher-risk service and triggered the Part C third-party review requirement. Document the classification decision with a rationale, as BNM examiners frequently test whether institutions have correctly self-identified the higher-risk trigger rather than defaulting to the lighter review scope to reduce pre-adoption effort.

Retain the BNM consultation correspondence file (17.1-E5) in Regulatory Affairs, not only in the email inboxes of the individuals who managed the submission. Examiners may request the full correspondence chain including any BNM queries and the institution's responses, and an incomplete correspondence record — even if the underlying work was done — signals weak regulatory engagement management.

Conduct an internal dry-run examination at least four weeks before any scheduled BNM assessment: assign an internal audit or compliance officer to independently request each evidence item from the responsible team and verify that it can be produced within 24 hours, is in the correct format, and covers the correct scope. Gaps identified during the dry run can be remediated before the Bank's examiners arrive, and the dry-run report itself serves as evidence of the institution's proactive compliance posture.

17.2.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.2.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.2.title: Subsequent Cloud and Emerging Technology Notification
17.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the institution has correctly distinguished between a first-time consultation (paragraph 17.1) and a subsequent notification (paragraph 17.2), and whether all four pre-conditions are demonstrably satisfied before the notification was submitted — not after. A key red flag is submitting a notification that lacks the full 17.1 information set, or where the prior consultation record does not clearly document that the Bank raised no concerns. Examiners pay particular attention to whether the enhanced technology risk management framework represents substantive improvement over the baseline and is not merely a renaming or cosmetic revision of existing documents. The independent assurance requirement is scrutinised for genuine independence — internal audit reliance without an external or second-line-of-defence component is routinely challenged. Incident response plan sufficiency is evaluated not on its existence but on whether it explicitly addresses cloud-specific or emerging technology failure scenarios with tested and time-bound recovery procedures.

evidenceItems.name: First-Time Consultation Record and BNM Response (Paragraph 17.1 Precedent)
evidenceItems.description: The formal documentation package from the institution's first-time public cloud or emerging technology consultation submitted to BNM under paragraph 17.1, together with BNM's written response confirming no concerns were raised. This establishes the foundational pre-condition for the subsequent notification pathway and must be retained and referenced in every subsequent 17.2 submission.
whatGoodLooksLike: A complete, date-stamped submission dossier to BNM under paragraph 17.1 with acknowledgement receipt from the Bank, demonstrating the consultation occurred prior to first adoption
An explicit written communication from BNM — letter, regulatory correspondence, or formal acknowledgement — stating no concerns were raised or that the institution may proceed
Clear cross-referencing within the 17.2 notification package to the specific 17.1 consultation reference number, date, and BNM response document
The 17.1 submission and BNM response are stored together as a linked record in the institution's GRC or regulatory correspondence management system with access controls preserving document integrity
A compliance register entry or board/ERMC paper noting the outcome of the 17.1 consultation and authorising use of the subsequent notification pathway for future adoptions
commonGaps: Institutions retain the submission but cannot produce BNM's response confirming no concerns, leaving the pre-condition unverifiable during examination
The first-time consultation was conducted informally (e.g., verbal or email discussion) without a formal submission dossier, making it impossible to trace to the paragraph 17.1 process
The 17.2 notification does not explicitly reference or attach the prior 17.1 consultation record, requiring examiners to piece together the compliance chain independently
BNM's response is stored in a personal email inbox or shared drive without version control, creating authenticity and retrieval risk during examination
The institution conflates a general regulatory briefing or industry dialogue with BNM as the required paragraph 17.1 consultation, which does not satisfy the pre-condition
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department — formal BNM correspondence register
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer) — regulatory engagement module
Board Secretariat records — ERMC or Board Technology Committee meeting minutes approving first-time cloud adoption
Legal and Compliance SharePoint site — BNM submissions and responses library
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scanned BNM correspondence) + PDF/DOCX (original submission dossier), stored as a linked document set

evidenceItems.name: Enhanced Technology Risk Management Framework (Cloud and Emerging Technology)
evidenceItems.description: The revised and board-approved technology risk management framework (TRMF) demonstrating substantive enhancement to specifically govern cloud and emerging technology risks. This document must show a clear before-and-after evolution from the baseline TRMF, incorporating cloud-specific risk domains such as shared responsibility models, multi-tenancy risks, data sovereignty, API security, and vendor lock-in, as well as controls and metrics tailored to the technology type being adopted.
whatGoodLooksLike: A versioned document with a clear change log showing additions or revisions specifically addressing cloud or emerging technology risk categories, with the enhancement dated after the 17.1 consultation outcome
Explicit coverage of cloud-specific risk domains: shared responsibility delineation, data residency and sovereignty obligations, cloud service provider (CSP) concentration risk, and portability risk
Board or ERMC approval resolution referencing the framework enhancement as a condition precedent for subsequent cloud adoption notifications to BNM

Risk appetite statements, key risk indicators (KRIs), and escalation thresholds that are quantitative and calibrated to cloud environment characteristics rather than copied verbatim from the on-premises TRMF

Integration of the enhanced framework into the institution's three lines of defence model, with documented roles and responsibilities for technology risk ownership at business unit, risk management, and internal audit levels
commonGaps: The enhanced TRMF adds a cloud annex that merely lists CSP names and contract dates without addressing risk treatment strategies, controls, or monitoring mechanisms specific to cloud environments
Framework enhancements are approved at CTO or CRO level only, without Board or designated board-level committee approval, which is insufficient for a document this material to regulatory compliance

The framework references generic ISO 27017 or CSA CCM control objectives without mapping them to the institution's actual cloud architecture, rendering the document aspirational rather than operational
Version control is absent or inconsistent, making it impossible to demonstrate that the enhancement occurred before the subsequent notification was submitted to BNM

Emerging technology risk coverage is superficial — often limited to one paragraph — when the notification concerns an emerging technology (e.g., AI/ML, DLT), leaving that risk domain inadequately governed
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management or Enterprise Risk Management department — TRMF repository
Board Secretariat — Board Technology Risk Committee or ERMC resolution records
GRC platform — policy and framework version management module
Cloud Centre of Excellence (CCoE) — cloud governance framework documentation
Internal Audit — framework review workpapers confirming the enhanced version reviewed
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (board-approved policy document with version history and change log)

evidenceItems.name: Independent Assurance Report on Cloud or Emerging Technology Risk Management Framework
evidenceItems.description: A formal independent assurance report assessing the design adequacy and operating effectiveness of the enhanced cloud or emerging technology risk management framework. Independence is critical — the report must originate from a party that is not responsible for designing or implementing the framework, such as external auditors, a qualified third-party assessor, or the institution's Internal Audit function operating under a mandate clearly separate from any advisory role in framework development.
whatGoodLooksLike: An assurance report from an external auditor, qualified cloud security assessor (e.g., CSA STAR certified), or internal audit with a scope statement that explicitly evaluates the enhanced TRMF against the RMiT requirements and relevant cloud security standards
Clear articulation of the assurance scope, methodology, sample selection rationale, and the basis for conclusions — not a high-level opinion letter without supporting workpapers
The report is addressed to the Board or Audit Committee and includes a management response to findings, demonstrating governance ownership of the assurance process
The assurance engagement was completed and signed off prior to submission of the 17.2 notification to BNM, with dates on the report and the notification submission forming a traceable sequence

Any material gaps or recommendations identified in the report are accompanied by remediation plans with owners and target dates, demonstrating the institution acted on assurance findings rather than filing the report without follow-up
commonGaps: The 'independent assurance' provided is a self-assessment checklist completed by the same technology risk team that designed the framework, which does not satisfy the independence requirement
Internal Audit conducted the review but was also involved in advisory capacity during framework drafting, creating an independence impairment that examiners will challenge
The assurance report covers only the policy document design without testing whether controls are actually implemented and operating effectively in the cloud environment
The report is undated or post-dated relative to the BNM notification submission, making it impossible to establish that assurance preceded notification as a pre-condition

Third-party certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 of the CSP) are presented as substitutes for the institution's own framework assurance, conflating CSP-level assurance with institution-level framework assurance
suggestedSources: Internal Audit department — audit engagement files and issued reports register
External auditors or specialist technology risk assurance firms — engagement deliverables
Audit Committee Secretariat — Audit Committee papers and resolution records
GRC platform — audit and assurance module with report tracking
Vendor or third-party management system — CSP assessment and assurance repository
evidenceItems.format: PDF (formal assurance report with sign-off, addressed to Board/Audit Committee, with management response appended)

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan — Cloud and Emerging Technology Sufficiency Assurance
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution's incident response plans explicitly cover adverse or unexpected events arising from public cloud or emerging technology use, together with assurance provided to BNM confirming their sufficiency. This includes the incident response plan itself, evidence of testing (tabletop exercises, simulation results), and the assurance statement or attestation submitted as part of the 17.2 notification package.
whatGoodLooksLike: An incident response plan with dedicated playbooks for cloud-specific failure scenarios such as CSP outage, cloud account compromise, data exfiltration from cloud storage, API gateway failure, and misconfigured cloud resources impacting critical systems

A test record — tabletop exercise report, red team exercise outcome, or BCP/DR drill results — conducted within the prior 12 months, specifically simulating cloud or emerging technology failure scenarios, with identified gaps remediated

A formal assurance statement signed by the CRO, CTO, or equivalent senior accountable officer, included in the 17.2 notification, explicitly attesting to the sufficiency of incident response plans for cloud or emerging technology adverse events
Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) defined for critical systems hosted on cloud, with evidence that these were tested and met during the most recent exercise

Escalation procedures within the incident response plan that include cloud service provider engagement protocols (e.g., CSP support tiers, shared responsibility matrix for incident response), regulatory notification timelines, and customer communication procedures
commonGaps: The institution submits its generic enterprise incident response plan without cloud-specific playbooks or appendices, and BNM examiners find no mention of CSP engagement, cloud-specific failure modes, or shared responsibility considerations

Incident response plan testing records exist but the scenarios tested are traditional IT failures (server crash, ransomware on-premises) with no cloud environment simulation, leaving cloud-specific sufficiency undemonstrated
