The assurance statement in the notification is a generic paragraph asserting plans are 'adequate' without referencing specific plan versions, test dates, or coverage of cloud or emerging technology scenarios

RTOs and RPOs are defined in policy documents but have never been validated against actual cloud architecture recovery capabilities, and no test results exist to substantiate the stated recovery objectives

Post-incident review records from prior cloud-related incidents (if any) are not referenced in the notification, missing an opportunity to demonstrate that incident response plans are continuously improved based on real experience
suggestedSources: Technology Operations or IT Security department — incident response plan repository and test records
Business Continuity Management team — BCP/DR exercise reports and scenario library
ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow, Jira) — incident records and post-incident review documentation
Cloud Centre of Excellence — cloud-specific runbooks and operational playbooks
CRO or CTO office — board-level attestation and sign-off records included in BNM notification package
evidenceItems.format: PDF (incident response plan with version control) + PDF (test exercise report) + PDF (signed assurance statement for BNM submission)

evidenceItems.name: Subsequent Notification Package to BNM (Complete Submission with Updated 17.1 Information)
evidenceItems.description: The complete and self-contained notification package submitted to BNM for each subsequent public cloud or emerging technology adoption for a critical system. This package must include all updated information required under paragraph 17.1 — not a summary or partial update — as well as documentation demonstrating compliance with all four pre-conditions stipulated in paragraph 17.2. The submission package is the primary artefact an examiner will review to assess the institution's compliance with this clause.
whatGoodLooksLike: A structured submission document with a cover letter, table of contents, and explicit mapping of each 17.2 pre-condition (a through d) to the corresponding supporting evidence included in the package, enabling examiners to verify compliance without requesting additional documents

All paragraph 17.1 information fields updated to reflect the specific cloud service or emerging technology being adopted, including updated risk assessments, critical system classification rationale, and vendor/CSP due diligence outcomes
Submission is date-stamped and submitted to BNM prior to adoption of the cloud service or emerging technology for the critical system — not retrospectively

A compliance attestation signed by a senior executive (CEO, CRO, or Board-authorised officer) confirming that all four pre-conditions in paragraph 17.2 have been met and that supporting documentation is accurate and complete

A submission register is maintained internally tracking each 17.2 notification by reference number, submission date, BNM acknowledgement date, system or technology covered, and current status — enabling the institution to demonstrate a complete history of notifications
commonGaps: The submission provides only a delta update referencing the prior 17.1 consultation rather than the complete updated information set, which does not satisfy the requirement to include 'necessary updates to all the information required under paragraph 17.1'

The package includes references to supporting documents ('see attached framework' or 'refer to prior submission') without attaching those documents, leaving BNM examiners unable to verify compliance from the submission alone
The institution cannot produce a submission register or acknowledgement from BNM, making it impossible to establish the precise submission date and confirm pre-adoption timing

Critical system classification for the newly adopted technology is not explicitly argued — the institution assumes the classification from the prior 17.1 applies, when BNM expects each subsequent adoption to be independently justified

The notification is submitted after the cloud service or emerging technology has already gone live in production for the critical system, inverting the notification obligation into a retrospective reporting exercise
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department — BNM submissions register and correspondence files
GRC platform — regulatory notification workflow and submission tracking module
Board Secretariat — board or ERMC approval records authorising each subsequent cloud adoption
Cloud Centre of Excellence or Enterprise Architecture — new cloud adoption pipeline and approval gate records
Legal department — outgoing regulatory correspondence registry
evidenceItems.format: PDF (complete submission package with all attachments compiled as a single versioned dossier, with a separate internal register in spreadsheet or GRC platform)

evidenceItems.name: Critical System Classification and Cloud Adoption Inventory
evidenceItems.description: A maintained inventory that identifies all systems classified as critical, maps each to any public cloud or emerging technology adoption decision, and records whether that adoption was handled under a first-time 17.1 consultation or a subsequent 17.2 notification. This inventory provides BNM examiners with a complete view of the institution's cloud adoption trajectory and confirms that no critical system was moved to cloud without the appropriate regulatory engagement.
whatGoodLooksLike: A live, board-approved register of critical systems with classification basis documented against BNM's definition of critical systems, reviewed and updated at least annually or upon material changes to the IT environment

Each critical system entry in the register explicitly records the cloud or emerging technology adoption status, the paragraph (17.1 or 17.2) under which BNM was engaged, the submission date, BNM reference number, and confirmation of no concerns (for 17.1) or notification acknowledgement (for 17.2)

The inventory is governed with a formal change management process requiring sign-off from CTO, CRO, and Compliance before a new cloud or emerging technology adoption is initiated for any critical system

Historical entries are preserved with point-in-time snapshots so examiners can reconstruct the state of the inventory at any prior date, demonstrating ongoing compliance across multiple adoption cycles

The inventory is reconciled against the institution's enterprise architecture asset register and cloud cost management tool outputs at least quarterly to detect unapproved or shadow cloud workloads hosting critical systems
commonGaps: The critical system list is maintained by IT Operations for change management purposes but has never been formally aligned with BNM's critical system definition, resulting in systems being misclassified and cloud adoptions proceeding without BNM engagement

The inventory records the initial 17.1 consultation but does not track subsequent 17.2 notifications, creating a gap in the compliance history that examiners must reconstruct from unstructured correspondence files

Shadow IT or business-unit-led cloud adoptions (e.g., SaaS applications processing customer data) are absent from the inventory because procurement or vendor onboarding controls do not feed into the critical system classification process
The register is a static spreadsheet last updated at the time of the initial 17.1 submission, with no evidence of subsequent reviews despite additional cloud services being adopted

Classification decisions lack documented rationale — systems are marked 'critical' or 'non-critical' without reference to the BNM criteria (e.g., impact on financial stability, customer data, payment processing), making the classification vulnerable to challenge
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture team — application and infrastructure asset register
Technology Risk Management — critical system classification framework and register
Cloud Centre of Excellence — cloud adoption pipeline and CSP usage inventory
Procurement or Vendor Management — SaaS and cloud service contracts repository
IT Change Management (ITSM platform, e.g., ServiceNow) — change records for cloud migration projects
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform module (with version history enabled) + PDF (annual board/ERMC approval of the register)

auditTips: Establish a clear documentary trail linking each 17.2 submission to its 17.1 predecessor: before any examination, assemble a single 'regulatory engagement chronology' showing the date of 17.1 consultation, BNM's no-concerns response, and each subsequent 17.2 notification submission in sequence. BNM examiners will test this chain of compliance as a threshold question — if it cannot be reconstructed quickly, the examination will stall on foundational issues.

Validate the independence of assurance on the cloud risk management framework before submission: document the assurer's mandate, qualifications, and the absence of any conflict of interest (e.g., no advisory involvement in framework drafting). Prepare a one-page independence memorandum that can be presented at the start of examination to pre-empt the most common challenge to this pre-condition.

Test incident response plans against cloud-specific scenarios within 12 months of each 17.2 submission, and retain the exercise report, participant list, scenario descriptions, and gap remediation log. During examination, be prepared to walk examiners through a specific cloud failure scenario from the test and demonstrate how the plan was activated — narrative-only assurance statements without test evidence are routinely discounted.

Cross-reference the 17.2 notification package against the full paragraph 17.1 information checklist line by line before submission, and retain this cross-reference matrix within the submission package itself. Examiners will verify that 'necessary updates to all the information required under paragraph 17.1' are present — a checklist demonstrating this review signals institutional diligence and accelerates the examination process.

Conduct a shadow cloud audit reconciling the institution's formal critical system inventory against actual cloud usage data from CSP billing consoles, network traffic analysis, and procurement records at least quarterly. If BNM examiners discover cloud workloads touching critical data or critical system functions that are absent from the inventory and not covered by a 17.1 or 17.2 submission, this will constitute a material finding regardless of the quality of other documentation.

Maintain a consolidated 17.2 compliance pre-condition checklist as a standing governance artefact: for each adoption decision, the four pre-conditions (a) through (d) should be formally sign-off items in the institution's cloud adoption approval gate, with the accountable owner and evidence reference documented. This transforms compliance from a submission-time scramble into an embedded governance control that produces audit-ready evidence as a by-product.

Prepare a briefing document for the examination opening meeting that describes the institution's end-to-end cloud adoption governance lifecycle — from critical system classification through BNM engagement pathway selection (17.1 vs 17.2), framework enhancement governance, assurance commissioning, and incident response testing. Framing the compliance story proactively before document requests begin gives examiners context and reduces the risk of misinterpretation of individual documents reviewed in isolation.

17.3.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.3.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.3.title: Enhancement Notification Exemption
17.3.auditorFocus: BNM examiners scrutinise whether the institution has a documented, consistently applied materiality determination framework that distinguishes 'enhancements' from changes that materially alter prior assessments or representations submitted to the Bank. A key red flag is institutions invoking the 17.3 exemption broadly without traceable, contemporaneous rationale — examiners will cross-reference each exempted change against the original BNM notification or consultation record to verify that the prior representations remain accurate. Examiners also look for governance structures that ensure the materiality determination is made at an appropriate seniority level and is not self-certified at the operational layer without oversight. Institutions that lack a closed-loop process linking the change management lifecycle to BNM submission records will struggle to demonstrate that the exemption has been applied appropriately. Where a pattern of repeated 'non-material' enhancements is observed on the same cloud service or technology, examiners may probe whether the cumulative effect constitutes a material alteration that should have triggered notification.

evidenceItems.name: Materiality Determination Framework for Cloud and Emerging Technology Changes
evidenceItems.description: A formally approved policy or procedure that defines the criteria and thresholds used to classify a change to an existing cloud or emerging technology adoption as either a non-material enhancement (exempt under 17.3) or a material alteration (requiring notification under 17.2). This document demonstrates that the institution has operationalised the distinction required by the clause rather than applying it ad hoc.
whatGoodLooksLike: Explicitly enumerates materiality criteria mapped to the dimensions of prior BNM assessments — e.g., risk profile, data classification, geographic footprint of cloud processing, third-party dependency, and business criticality — with clear pass/fail or threshold language for each.

Distinguishes between change types (e.g., capacity scaling, feature enablement within an existing contracted service, new cloud service provider, new use case involving personal or financial data) and maps each type to the appropriate notification pathway.
Board or senior management committee approval is evidenced on the document, with version history showing at least one formal review cycle.
Includes worked examples or a decision tree that an analyst can follow to produce a reproducible materiality determination, reducing interpretive inconsistency across teams.
References the specific BNM representations and assessment dimensions documented in prior 17.2 notifications so that analysts know precisely what must remain unaltered for the exemption to apply.
commonGaps: Criteria are defined in general terms (e.g., 'significant change') without quantitative or qualitative anchors, making determinations subjective and difficult for examiners to validate.
The framework covers cloud changes but omits emerging technology adoptions (e.g., AI/ML models, distributed ledger), leaving a partial coverage gap that examiners will probe.
Document is undated or lacks approval signatures, preventing examiners from confirming it was in place at the time exemptions were claimed.
No linkage to the institution's BNM notification register, meaning analysts cannot cross-check what prior representations were made when applying the materiality test.
Framework has not been reviewed or updated after a BNM consultation or notification cycle, so it may not reflect the representations actually submitted to the Bank.
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management (TRM) or Cloud Governance policy repository (SharePoint / Confluence)
GRC platform policy module (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, Archer)
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee secretariat — approval minutes and signed policy
Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or Chief Risk Officer (CRO) office records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control metadata and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification and Consultation Submission Register
evidenceItems.description: A centralised register of all prior notifications and consultation submissions made to BNM under paragraph 17.2 (and predecessor requirements), capturing the exact assessments and representations made at each submission. This is the baseline against which every subsequent 17.3 materiality determination is measured, and examiners will use it to verify that exempted enhancements genuinely did not alter the represented position.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each entry records the submission date, cloud service provider or emerging technology involved, scope of adoption, risk assessment summary, key representations made (e.g., data residency, encryption standard, exit strategy, concentration risk position), and the Bank's response or acknowledgement reference.
The register is maintained in a system that produces an auditable change log, so examiners can confirm no retrospective alterations have been made to recorded representations.
Cross-referenced to the institution's cloud service inventory and technology asset register, enabling a reviewer to trace from a specific cloud deployment to the corresponding BNM submission.
Reviewed and attested to by a senior compliance or risk officer at least annually, with attestation records retained alongside the register.
Accessible to the change assessment team at the point of conducting 17.3 materiality determinations, evidenced by access control logs or documented workflow integration.
commonGaps: Register exists as a static spreadsheet with no change history, making it impossible to confirm the recorded representations match what was actually submitted to BNM at the time.
Prior submissions are stored in individual email threads across multiple mailboxes rather than in a single searchable register, creating retrieval risk during examination.
Register does not capture the specific risk assessment dimensions or representations made — only the fact that a submission occurred — rendering it useless for 17.3 materiality comparison.
Submissions made under legacy frameworks or by predecessor teams are not included, leaving historical gaps for long-standing cloud adoptions.
No formal attestation or periodic reconciliation process to confirm the register is complete and accurate against actual correspondence with BNM.
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance department — BNM correspondence management system
GRC platform regulatory engagement module
Secure document management system (e.g., SharePoint with restricted access, OpenText)
Legal and Compliance shared drive with BNM correspondence folders
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC system export with version history; supporting PDF copies of actual BNM submissions

evidenceItems.name: Enhancement Materiality Assessment Records
evidenceItems.description: Contemporaneous, per-change records documenting the materiality determination made for each enhancement to an existing cloud or emerging technology adoption, including the rationale for invoking the 17.3 exemption. These records form the primary audit trail demonstrating that the institution applied the exemption deliberately and on defensible grounds rather than as a default to avoid notification.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each record identifies the specific cloud service or emerging technology, describes the enhancement in sufficient technical and business detail, and maps it explicitly against the prior BNM representations recorded in the notification register (17.3-E2).
Materiality determination is completed using the approved framework (17.3-E1) with each criterion evaluated and recorded, producing a traceable conclusion rather than a bare assertion.
The record is approved by an authorised assessor at the appropriate seniority level as defined in the governance framework, with approval date captured.
Where the determination is borderline, escalation to a senior governance body (e.g., Technology Risk Committee) is documented, evidencing conservative and deliberate application of the exemption.
Records are created at or near the time of the change decision — not retrospectively — as evidenced by timestamps that precede or coincide with the change implementation date in the ITSM system.
commonGaps: Materiality assessments are produced only upon examiner request rather than as part of the standard change process, indicating the determination was not genuinely contemporaneous.
Assessment records reference the enhancement description but do not identify which specific prior BNM representations could or could not be affected, making the analysis superficial.

Approval is recorded as a generic ITSM ticket sign-off rather than a deliberate regulatory compliance determination by an authorised individual, conflating change management approval with regulatory assessment.
Cumulative impact of multiple sequential enhancements on the same system is not assessed — each change is evaluated in isolation, masking potential aggregate materiality.
Records are inconsistent in depth and format across business units, suggesting the framework is not uniformly applied and that some determinations may be poorly supported.
suggestedSources: ITSM platform change management module (e.g., ServiceNow Change Management, Jira Service Management) — linked to RFC records
GRC platform risk assessment or regulatory compliance module
Cloud governance committee or Technology Risk Committee — meeting papers and decision records
Technology Risk Management team — assessment workpapers
evidenceItems.format: Structured form (PDF/DOCX or GRC system record) with embedded approval workflow and timestamp metadata

evidenceItems.name: Cloud and Emerging Technology Adoption Inventory
evidenceItems.description: A current and complete inventory of all cloud services and emerging technology adoptions for which BNM has been notified or consulted under paragraph 17.2, serving as the authoritative reference for identifying the population of adoptions to which the 17.3 exemption may apply. Examiners use this to confirm that enhancements claimed as exempt are genuinely associated with a prior notified adoption and not a new adoption that should have triggered its own 17.2 process.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each entry records the cloud service provider or emerging technology name, adoption scope and business function, date of BNM notification, relevant BNM submission reference, data classification of information processed, and current operational status.
Inventory is maintained in a system with version control and an audit trail, allowing examiners to confirm the state of the inventory at any historical point.
Directly linked or cross-referenced to the BNM notification register (17.3-E2) and to the ITSM/configuration management database (CMDB), ensuring consistency across systems of record.
Reviewed and formally attested to by the cloud governance or technology risk function on at least a quarterly basis, with attestation records retained.
Includes a field indicating any pending or recently completed enhancements, with linkage to the corresponding materiality assessment record (17.3-E3), enabling end-to-end traceability.
commonGaps: Inventory captures currently active cloud services but does not tag which have been notified to BNM under 17.2, making it impossible to identify the notified population to which 17.3 applies.
Inventory is maintained by the IT department without visibility to the Compliance or Regulatory Affairs function, so it is not integrated into the notification and exemption workflow.
Emerging technology adoptions (e.g., AI platforms, RPA, blockchain) are managed separately from cloud services and are absent from the primary inventory, creating a blind spot.
Inventory is updated infrequently and may not reflect services that have been substantially modified, decommissioned, or migrated since the original BNM notification.
No formal process to flag when a service in the inventory undergoes a proposed enhancement, meaning the change management and regulatory compliance workflows are not connected.
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management or CMDB (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Device42)
Cloud Centre of Excellence or Cloud Governance team — cloud service register
Vendor management system — third-party cloud contracts and service schedules
GRC platform — technology risk register linked to vendor/asset records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC/CMDB system export; PDF attestation records

evidenceItems.name: Governance Body Oversight Records for Exemption Decisions
evidenceItems.description: Minutes, decision papers, and reporting packs from the relevant governance committee (e.g., Technology Risk Committee, IT Steering Committee, or Cloud Governance Board) demonstrating that the application of the 17.3 exemption is subject to appropriate senior oversight, periodic review, and escalation for borderline cases. These records demonstrate that exemption decisions are not made solely at the operational level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Committee terms of reference or charter explicitly assigns responsibility for overseeing the 17.3 exemption process and periodic review of exempted enhancements.
Meeting minutes or papers include a standing agenda item or periodic reporting on the volume and nature of enhancements assessed as exempt, enabling trend analysis and cumulative impact monitoring.
Records show that at least one borderline or escalated determination was reviewed by the committee and a documented decision was reached, demonstrating the escalation pathway is functional.
Reporting to the committee distinguishes between enhancements assessed as exempt and those that triggered a 17.2 notification, providing a balanced view of the notification landscape.

Committee composition includes representation from Technology Risk, Compliance/Regulatory Affairs, and a senior business sponsor, evidencing multi-disciplinary oversight of regulatory compliance decisions.
commonGaps: No governance body has been assigned explicit oversight of the 17.3 exemption process; cloud change decisions are managed entirely within the IT function without Compliance involvement.
Committee minutes record the outcome of escalated decisions but not the deliberation or rationale, leaving examiners unable to assess the quality of the governance review.
Reporting to the committee presents only exempted enhancements without context of the total change population, making it impossible to assess whether the exemption is being over-applied.
Committee meets infrequently (e.g., annually), resulting in governance oversight that is too lagged to provide effective real-time checks on exemption decisions.
Terms of reference do not reference RMiT paragraph 17.3 or the regulatory notification obligation, suggesting the committee's oversight role in this area is informal and potentially incomplete.
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee secretariat — minutes and board papers
Compliance or Regulatory Affairs function — governance reporting packs
Board Risk Committee secretariat — where escalated items are tabled upward
GRC platform governance module — committee action tracking and decision records
evidenceItems.format: PDF minutes and papers; signed attendance registers; action tracking logs

evidenceItems.name: Cumulative Change Impact Assessment
evidenceItems.description: A periodic analysis — at least annually or triggered by a defined threshold of sequential exemptions on the same cloud service or technology — that evaluates whether the aggregate effect of multiple individually non-material enhancements has cumulatively altered the prior assessments and representations made to BNM. This evidence demonstrates that the institution monitors for 'death by a thousand cuts' scenarios where repeated small changes collectively constitute a material alteration.
whatGoodLooksLike: Assessment methodology is documented and approved, specifying the trigger conditions (e.g., five or more exempted enhancements within 12 months on a single system, or any change to data classification or geographic processing scope) that prompt a cumulative review.

Each periodic assessment compares the current operational state of the cloud service or emerging technology against the representations made in the original BNM notification, dimension by dimension, and reaches a documented conclusion on whether a notification obligation has arisen.

Where cumulative impact is found to be material, the assessment record shows that a 17.2 notification was prepared and submitted, with the BNM submission cross-referenced in the notification register.
Assessments are signed off by the Chief Risk Officer, Chief Information Officer, or equivalent senior officer, reflecting the significance of the determination.
The assessment cadence and trigger thresholds are reviewed at least annually and adjusted based on the institution's change velocity and risk profile.
commonGaps: No cumulative assessment process exists — each enhancement is evaluated in isolation and there is no mechanism to aggregate their impact over time or across related changes.
Cumulative assessments are performed informally by the same operational team that approved the individual exemptions, without independent review or a structured methodology.

Assessment scope is limited to changes within a single calendar year, missing multi-year cumulative drift where a cloud service's operational profile has substantially evolved from the notified state.

Assessments do not compare the current state against the specific representations made to BNM — they compare against internal risk assessment criteria — meaning the regulatory baseline is not the reference point.
Trigger thresholds for a cumulative review have not been formally set, resulting in assessments only being performed when an examiner or internal auditor raises the question.
suggestedSources: Technology Risk Management function — periodic risk assessment workpapers
GRC platform — aggregated change log reports linked to cloud service records
Cloud governance committee — periodic review agenda items and decision records
Internal Audit — technology risk audit workpapers covering cloud governance
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX assessment report with supporting data appendix; approval signatures and date

auditTips: Map every exempted enhancement back to a specific prior BNM notification or consultation record before the examination — examiners will ask for this linkage on the spot, and any gap between an exempted change and a traceable BNM submission reference will be treated as a potential undisclosed new adoption requiring a 17.2 notification.

Prepare a summary table cross-referencing each cloud service and emerging technology in your inventory against its BNM notification reference, the date of last enhancement, the materiality determination outcome, and the assessor's name — this single artefact answers the examiner's primary line of inquiry on clause 17.3 and demonstrates systemic rather than ad hoc compliance.

Review the cumulative change history for any cloud service that has had three or more exempted enhancements since the original BNM notification, and document a contemporaneous cumulative impact assessment before the examination; examiners are specifically trained to look for aggregated drift on frequently modified systems as a signal that a notification obligation was avoided rather than genuinely inapplicable.

Confirm that your materiality determination framework uses the exact dimensions and language from your prior BNM submissions as the reference baseline — not internal risk thresholds — because the clause test is whether prior representations to the Bank are materially altered, not whether internal risk appetite is exceeded; a mismatch between these two references is a common finding.

Brief the ITSM and change management teams so that every change request affecting a BNM-notified cloud service or emerging technology automatically triggers a regulatory materiality check field in the change ticket; the absence of this integration is a structural gap that examiners interpret as evidence that exemptions are not being deliberately considered at the point of change decision.

Ensure that at least one governance committee — with Compliance representation — has a documented mandate to review exemption decisions on a periodic basis and that meeting minutes contain substantive discussion, not just a ratification note; examiners will interview committee members to test whether oversight is genuine, and thin minutes that do not reflect deliberation will undermine confidence in your governance framework.

Where any enhancement involved a change to the cloud service provider's sub-processors, data residency location, or encryption architecture, conduct a specific assessment of whether those dimensions were represented to BNM in the original notification and flag them for heightened scrutiny — these three areas are the most common vectors for a change that appears minor operationally but is material from a regulatory representation standpoint.

17.4.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.4.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.4.title: Bank Discretionary Direction
17.4.auditorFocus: BNM examiners assess whether the financial institution has a standing governance mechanism to receive, track, and respond to discretionary directives from the Bank — particularly where such directives extend consultation or notification obligations beyond the standard thresholds in paragraphs 17.1 and 17.2. Examiners will scrutinise the institution's ability to demonstrate prompt compliance, looking for evidence of defined internal escalation paths, documented response timelines, and accountability structures that activate without delay upon receipt of a directive. A key red flag is the absence of records covering non-critical systems or scenarios where the standard pre-requisites were not met, as this signals that the institution treats clause 17.4 as a theoretical obligation rather than an operational one. Examiners will also probe whether explanations of deviations from Appendix 9 and Appendix 10 are substantive and formally documented, rather than produced reactively during the examination itself. Institutions that cannot demonstrate a clear audit trail from directive receipt through to BNM-satisfactory resolution are considered high risk for this clause.

evidenceItems.name: BNM Discretionary Directive Register
evidenceItems.description: A centralised log or register recording all directives received from BNM under clause 17.4, including directives to consult, notify, observe Appendix 9/10 guidance, or explain deviations. This register demonstrates that the institution systematically captures, tracks, and monitors the status of every Bank-issued directive regardless of whether the standard thresholds in paragraphs 17.1 or 17.2 apply.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each directive entry includes the date received, BNM reference number, directive type (consult/notify/observe/explain), scope (system or technology affected), and assigned responsible officer
The register distinguishes between directives triggered under standard pre-requisites and those issued at BNM's discretion for non-critical systems or where pre-requisites were not met
Status tracking fields show current compliance stage (acknowledged, in-progress, submitted, closed) with target and actual completion dates
Register entries cross-reference supporting response documents, deviation explanations, and any BNM acknowledgement correspondence
Register is reviewed and signed off by the Chief Compliance Officer or equivalent on a monthly basis with evidence of periodic management reporting
commonGaps: Register exists only for consultation and notification under 17.1/17.2 and does not capture discretionary directives issued outside those thresholds, leaving clause 17.4 coverage incomplete
No distinction is made between directive types, making it impossible to demonstrate the institution understood whether it was directed to consult, notify, or explain deviations
Status fields are incomplete or stale, with no evidence of active monitoring or escalation when deadlines are approaching
Register is maintained ad hoc in email folders or informal spreadsheets with no version control, access governance, or audit trail of updates
Non-critical systems that received a BNM discretionary directive are absent from the register, suggesting selective or incomplete capture
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, Archer) — regulatory correspondence module
Compliance team SharePoint or Confluence site — regulatory register section
Legal and Secretariat department — BNM correspondence files
Chief Compliance Officer's office — regulatory engagement tracker
Board Risk or Audit Committee reporting packs — regulatory directive summary annexes
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets) or GRC platform export (PDF/CSV), with version history enabled

evidenceItems.name: Consultation and Notification Response Packages (Directive-Triggered)
evidenceItems.description: Complete response packages prepared and submitted to BNM following a discretionary directive to consult under paragraph 17.1 or notify under paragraph 17.2. These packages demonstrate that the institution operationalised the Bank's directive by producing substantive, timely submissions aligned to the consultation or notification requirements, even where those requirements would not ordinarily have been triggered.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each package is clearly labelled as having been prepared in response to a clause 17.4 directive, with the original directive reference cited in the cover letter or submission form
The submission content meets the same standard as a voluntary 17.1 consultation or 17.2 notification, covering system scope, risk assessment, controls, and timelines
Submission timestamps and BNM acknowledgement receipts confirm that the response was provided promptly, with internal records showing the elapsed time from directive receipt to submission
Where the directive was issued for a non-critical system, the package includes explicit articulation of why BNM directed consultation or notification despite the non-critical classification
Internal approvals (e.g., CTO, CRO, CCO sign-off) are documented prior to submission, demonstrating governance over the response content
commonGaps: Response packages prepared under a 17.4 directive are filed alongside standard 17.1/17.2 submissions with no distinguishing label, making it impossible to trace which submissions were directive-triggered
Submissions for non-critical systems are thin or generic, lacking the contextual justification for why BNM's discretionary direction applied to the system in question
No BNM acknowledgement or correspondence confirming receipt and acceptance of the submission is retained, creating a gap in demonstrating satisfactory compliance
Internal approval records are absent, suggesting submissions were made without appropriate senior sign-off and governance oversight
Timeliness cannot be demonstrated because submission dates are not recorded against directive receipt dates, precluding any assessment of prompt compliance
suggestedSources: Compliance team document management system — regulatory submissions folder
BNM Regulatory Submission Portal — submission history and acknowledgement receipts
IT Governance team — cloud and technology change records
SharePoint or Teams channels used for BNM engagement coordination
Legal department — outgoing regulatory correspondence archives
evidenceItems.format: PDF submission package with cover letter, supporting annexes, and BNM acknowledgement receipt

evidenceItems.name: Appendix 9 and Appendix 10 Deviation Explanation Records
evidenceItems.description: Formal documented explanations prepared in response to a BNM directive requiring the institution to explain deviations from the guidance in Appendix 9 (cloud services guidance) or Appendix 10 (emerging technology guidance). These records demonstrate that the institution can articulate, justify, and document non-conformance with BNM guidance on demand, with compensating controls or remediation plans where deviations are identified.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each deviation explanation identifies the specific Appendix 9 or Appendix 10 guidance paragraph(s) that are not fully observed, with a precise description of the nature and scope of the deviation
Explanations are substantive and technical, providing risk context and detailing the compensating controls or mitigating factors that justify the deviation or reduce residual risk
Where a deviation is not justified by compensating controls, a time-bound remediation plan with accountable owners and milestone dates is included and tracked
Deviation records are reviewed and attested by the relevant system owner and the Chief Risk Officer or Technology Risk head before submission to BNM
The explanation document cross-references the relevant system's risk assessment, Technology Risk Framework classification, and any prior BNM correspondence on the system
commonGaps: Institutions are unable to produce deviation explanations promptly because no baseline mapping of their systems against Appendix 9 and Appendix 10 exists, forcing reactive and incomplete gap analyses during the examination
Deviation explanations are vague, acknowledging non-conformance without articulating compensating controls, leaving BNM unable to assess whether residual risk is adequately managed
Remediation plans are absent for unjustified deviations, or plans that exist are not tracked and show no evidence of progress since the directive was received
Explanations cover only cloud-critical systems and omit non-critical systems for which BNM issued a discretionary directive, directly contravening the scope of clause 17.4

Sign-off by senior risk officers is missing, and deviation explanations appear to have been prepared by technical staff without compliance or risk oversight, undermining their credibility with examiners
suggestedSources: Technology Risk team — Appendix 9/10 compliance mapping workbooks
Cloud governance team — cloud security and architecture review records
GRC platform — control deficiency and exception register
IT Architecture review board minutes and decisions
Internal Audit — technology audit findings and management responses related to cloud or emerging technology
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX structured deviation report with appendix cross-reference table, risk narrative, and remediation plan

evidenceItems.name: Prompt Compliance Timeliness Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Records demonstrating that the institution responded to BNM discretionary directives promptly and to the satisfaction of the Bank. This evidence includes timestamped directive receipt records, internal workflow documentation showing response preparation timelines, submission confirmation, and any BNM feedback indicating satisfactory compliance. It directly addresses the 'promptly and to the satisfaction of the Bank' requirement in clause 17.4.
whatGoodLooksLike: A clear timeline for each directive showing: date directive received, date acknowledged to BNM, date internal response team convened, date draft submitted for internal review, and date submitted to BNM

Internal SLA or response protocol documentation defining what 'prompt' means operationally (e.g., acknowledgement within 1 business day, substantive response within defined period), demonstrating that the institution has operationalised the promptness requirement
BNM correspondence confirming that submissions were received and either accepted or subject to follow-up, with follow-up items tracked to resolution
Board or senior management reporting showing directive receipt and status, evidencing that compliance timeliness is monitored at an appropriate governance level
Where BNM indicated that a response was unsatisfactory, records show the institution's corrective response, revised submission, and eventual BNM acceptance
commonGaps: No internal definition or SLA for what constitutes 'prompt' compliance, leaving the institution unable to demonstrate it has operationalised the promptness requirement beyond ad hoc efforts
Directive receipt dates are not formally recorded, making elapsed time from directive to submission impossible to calculate and audit
BNM correspondence acknowledging satisfactory compliance is not retained or is retained only informally in individual inboxes rather than the compliance document management system
Instances where BNM indicated dissatisfaction are not documented or tracked, suggesting the institution does not have a closed-loop process for achieving satisfactory resolution
Senior management and board are not informed of directive receipt or compliance status, indicating a governance gap where BNM directives are handled at operational level without appropriate oversight
suggestedSources: Legal and Secretariat department — BNM incoming and outgoing correspondence log
Compliance team — regulatory engagement calendar and workflow tracker
GRC platform — regulatory action item module with timestamp audit trail
Email management system — BNM correspondence thread with received/sent timestamps
Board Risk Committee and Executive Risk Committee minutes — regulatory matters agenda items
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC export showing directive-to-resolution timeline, plus PDF copies of BNM correspondence confirming satisfactory compliance

evidenceItems.name: Non-Critical System Governance and Readiness Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the institution maintains governance readiness for non-critical systems to be subject to consultation, notification, or Appendix 9/10 compliance requirements at BNM's discretion. This includes system inventory records showing the non-critical classification, and documentation demonstrating that relevant information (risk assessments, architecture diagrams, control records) is available and can be mobilised for a BNM-directed submission without significant delay.
whatGoodLooksLike: The institution's technology system inventory includes all non-critical systems with classification rationale, ownership, and links to available risk and architecture documentation, enabling rapid response to a BNM directive on any system

Risk assessments and control records for non-critical systems are maintained at a level of detail sufficient to support a consultation or notification submission, not merely basic asset register entries
The governance framework explicitly addresses the possibility of BNM-directed obligations for non-critical systems, with policy language or procedures referencing clause 17.4
A periodic exercise or simulation has been conducted testing the institution's ability to prepare a clause 17.4 response for a non-critical system, with results and lessons learned documented
Non-critical systems subject to past BNM directives have dedicated files demonstrating how the institution complied, serving as precedent documentation for future examinations
commonGaps: System inventory covers only critical systems in sufficient detail for regulatory submissions, with non-critical system records too sparse to support a prompt consultation or notification response

The institution's governance framework and internal policies do not reference the possibility of BNM-directed obligations for non-critical systems, leaving staff unprepared when such directives are issued
No readiness testing or simulation has been performed for non-critical systems, meaning the first real test of the institution's preparedness occurs during an actual BNM examination
Non-critical system classification rationale is not documented or is based on outdated assessments, undermining the institution's ability to defend the classification if BNM queries it
Ownership and accountability for non-critical systems is unclear, resulting in delays when a BNM directive requires rapid mobilisation of subject matter experts and documentation
suggestedSources: IT Asset Management system (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB, Freshservice) — full system inventory including non-critical classifications
Technology Risk team — system risk assessment repository
Enterprise Architecture team — system and architecture documentation repository
Compliance policy document management system — Technology Risk and Cloud governance policies
Internal Audit — IT audit universe, which typically includes non-critical systems
evidenceItems.format: System inventory export (spreadsheet/GRC platform), supplemented by PDF risk assessment templates and governance policy extracts

evidenceItems.name: Internal Escalation and Response Procedure for BNM Directives
evidenceItems.description: Documented internal procedures governing how the institution receives, escalates, coordinates, and responds to discretionary directives from BNM under clause 17.4. This evidence demonstrates that the institution has institutionalised its response capability rather than relying on ad hoc arrangements, and that roles, responsibilities, timelines, and approval authorities are clearly defined for all directive types.
whatGoodLooksLike: A written procedure or runbook specifically addressing BNM discretionary directives covers all directive types (consult, notify, observe Appendix 9/10, explain deviations) and explicitly references clause 17.4
The procedure defines escalation chains with named roles (not individuals), approval authorities for submissions, and escalation triggers if internal timelines are at risk of being missed
Training or awareness records show that relevant compliance, technology risk, and IT governance staff have been briefed on the procedure, with sign-off confirming understanding
The procedure has been reviewed and approved by senior management within the past 12 months and incorporates lessons learned from any prior BNM directives received
The procedure includes a section on non-critical systems and on scenarios where standard pre-requisites in 17.1 or 17.2 were not met, directly addressing the scope of clause 17.4
commonGaps: No written procedure exists for handling BNM discretionary directives; the institution relies on institutional memory or informal understanding, which cannot be demonstrated to BNM examiners

Existing regulatory response procedures address standard consultation and notification under 17.1 and 17.2 but contain no provisions for clause 17.4 discretionary directives or non-critical system scenarios
Approval authorities for BNM submissions are not defined, resulting in submissions being made without appropriate senior sign-off or, conversely, delays caused by unclear decision-making authority

The procedure has not been updated following receipt of previous BNM directives, indicating that lessons learned are not being incorporated and the institution is not improving its response capability over time
Training records are absent, and compliance and technology risk staff interviewed during the examination are unfamiliar with the procedure, undermining its operational credibility
suggestedSources: Compliance department — regulatory engagement and response procedure library
Technology Risk team — risk management procedures and frameworks
HR or Learning Management System — training completion records for compliance and technology risk staff
Document management system (SharePoint, Confluence) — policy and procedure repository with version history
Board or management committee — procedure approval minutes or sign-off records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX procedure document with version control, approval signatures, and training acknowledgement records as annexes

auditTips: Before the examination, map every BNM directive received in the past seven years to the clause 17.4 register and verify that each directive has a complete response package — examiners will ask to see the full audit trail from directive receipt through to BNM-satisfactory resolution, and gaps in any single directive will call the entire register's completeness into question.

Conduct a mock clause 17.4 readiness drill for a non-critical system: select a non-critical system at random, task the compliance and technology risk teams to prepare a hypothetical consultation or notification package within a defined window, and document the exercise outcomes — this produces contemporaneous evidence of operational readiness that examiners find highly credible.

Prepare a consolidated Appendix 9 and Appendix 10 compliance matrix covering all cloud and emerging technology systems, including non-critical ones, with each row showing the guidance paragraph, observed/not observed status, compensating controls, and remediation plan — this allows the institution to produce a substantive deviation explanation within hours of receiving a BNM directive rather than days.

Review internal procedures explicitly for clause 17.4 language: if the procedure only references paragraphs 17.1 and 17.2, update it to include discretionary directive scenarios, non-critical system scenarios, and the 'promptly and to the satisfaction of the Bank' compliance standard — examiners frequently find that procedures are silent on clause 17.4, which signals the institution has not operationalised the obligation.

Verify that BNM correspondence (including emails) confirming receipt and acceptance of directive-triggered submissions is captured in the compliance document management system and not left in individual inboxes — the loss of BNM acknowledgement records through staff turnover is a recurring finding that prevents institutions from demonstrating satisfactory compliance with past directives.

Cross-reference the clause 17.4 directive register against the Technology Risk team's system change log and the cloud governance committee minutes to identify any system deployments or changes that BNM could reasonably have directed consultation or notification on, and confirm that these are either in the register or documented as not having attracted a directive — proactive coverage analysis demonstrates maturity and reduces examiner scepticism.

Brief the Chief Compliance Officer, Chief Risk Officer, and Chief Technology Officer on the specific obligations of clause 17.4 prior to the examination — examiners frequently conduct senior management interviews and assess whether leadership understands that BNM's discretionary direction powers extend beyond standard thresholds, including to non-critical systems; uninformed senior management responses are a significant red flag.

17.5.section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.5.subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
17.5.title: Cloud and Emerging Technology Roadmap
17.5.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution's annual outsourcing plan explicitly contains a forward-looking roadmap covering both cloud services and emerging technology adoption across critical and non-critical systems, not merely a retrospective list of existing arrangements. Examiners will cross-reference the roadmap against the IT Profile submission and the Outsourcing policy document requirements to confirm alignment and timeliness of submission. A key red flag is a roadmap that lacks granularity — vague aspirational statements without project-level detail, timelines, or system classifications will be challenged. Examiners will also confirm that risk assessments are pre-prepared and retrievable on demand: institutions that cannot promptly produce the paragraph 10.50 cloud risk assessment or the Appendix 9 emerging technology risk assessment when requested are considered non-compliant regardless of whether the roadmap exists. The completeness of the risk assessment documentation relative to every technology initiative listed in the roadmap is a critical audit dimension.

evidenceItems.name: Annual Outsourcing Plan with Cloud and Emerging Technology Roadmap
evidenceItems.description: The formally submitted annual outsourcing plan that includes a dedicated section or annex containing the institution's multi-year roadmap for adoption of cloud services and emerging technology, covering both critical and non-critical systems. This document demonstrates compliance with the obligation to submit the roadmap to BNM in accordance with the Outsourcing policy document requirements.
whatGoodLooksLike: The plan contains a clearly labelled roadmap section that lists all planned and in-flight cloud and emerging technology initiatives by system name, criticality classification, anticipated adoption timeline, and responsible owner
Both critical and non-critical systems are explicitly addressed, with separate categorisation consistent with the institution's system criticality framework
The roadmap covers at least a rolling 12-month forward horizon and ideally 3 years, with year-on-year phasing visible
The submission is dated and evidences formal Board or senior management approval prior to submission to BNM
Version history or amendment logs demonstrate the roadmap is updated annually and any material mid-year changes are tracked
commonGaps: Roadmap is embedded in a dense outsourcing plan without clear labelling, making it difficult for examiners to locate cloud or emerging technology content quickly
Non-critical systems are omitted entirely from the roadmap, giving the false impression that only critical system migrations are planned
The roadmap contains only technology categories (e.g., 'public cloud migration') without naming specific systems, vendors, or timelines, rendering it unverifiable
The plan is submitted after the BNM deadline or the submission date cannot be evidenced, creating a gap in demonstrating timely compliance
The roadmap was never formally approved by the Board Risk Committee or equivalent governance body before submission
suggestedSources: Group Technology or CTO office — custodian of the annual outsourcing plan master document
GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, MetricStream, or local equivalent) where outsourcing plan versions and approval workflows are stored
BNM submission portal records or correspondence log maintained by Regulatory Affairs / Compliance
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting minutes confirming plan approval
SharePoint or document management system under the Compliance or Technology Risk function
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (formal submission copy with approval signatures or digital approval trail)

evidenceItems.name: IT Profile Reporting Extract — Cloud and Emerging Technology Section
evidenceItems.description: The relevant section of the institution's IT Profile report submitted to BNM that captures cloud services and emerging technology adoption data, serving as an alternative or complementary regulatory reporting channel to the annual outsourcing plan for roadmap disclosure purposes.
whatGoodLooksLike: The IT Profile extract maps directly to the fields prescribed by BNM's IT Profile reporting template, with cloud adoption metrics and emerging technology initiatives fully populated
The extract is consistent with the annual outsourcing plan roadmap, showing no material discrepancies between the two submissions
Submission timestamps and acknowledgement receipts from BNM (or the regulatory portal) are retained alongside the extract
All technology categories reported align with definitions in RMiT (e.g., the distinction between public cloud, private cloud, and hybrid deployments is clearly applied)
Commentary fields within the IT Profile are substantive, not left blank or completed with generic statements
commonGaps: IT Profile data is populated by a different team than the outsourcing plan, resulting in inconsistencies in technology listings or system counts that examiners flag as a governance weakness
Emerging technology section is left blank or marked 'not applicable' without documented rationale, even where the institution has ongoing proofs-of-concept or pilots
The institution cannot produce submission receipts or confirmation that the IT Profile was accepted by BNM, making it impossible to evidence timely compliance
Cloud deployment models are misclassified (e.g., a managed private cloud reported as on-premise), undermining the accuracy of the regulatory picture
The IT Profile reflects a point-in-time snapshot that has not been reconciled against the more detailed outsourcing plan, creating version drift
suggestedSources: Regulatory Reporting team or Compliance function — owners of the IT Profile submission process
BNM's regulatory portal submission history and acknowledgement records
Technology Risk or Enterprise Architecture team — data providers for the IT Profile technology inventory fields
GRC or compliance calendar tool tracking submission deadlines and completion status
evidenceItems.format: PDF or system export (BNM IT Profile template format); retain alongside submission confirmation

evidenceItems.name: Cloud Services Risk Assessment (Paragraph 10.50 Compliance)
evidenceItems.description: The documented risk assessment for each cloud services adoption initiative, conducted in accordance with the requirements of RMiT paragraph 10.50. This document demonstrates that the institution has formally assessed the risks of cloud adoption before or concurrent with each initiative listed in the roadmap, and that it is maintained and retrievable for BNM review on demand.
whatGoodLooksLike: A separate, versioned risk assessment document exists for each material cloud services initiative referenced in the roadmap, cross-referenced by initiative name and system
The assessment addresses all risk dimensions specified in paragraph 10.50, including data sovereignty, concentration risk, vendor lock-in, exit strategy, and cloud security controls
Risk ratings are supported by evidence (e.g., vendor audit reports, penetration test results, contractual terms review) rather than being asserted without basis
The document records the date of assessment, the assessor, and formal sign-off by the Chief Risk Officer or Technology Risk function
Reassessment dates are scheduled and tracked, with evidence that assessments are refreshed when material changes occur to the cloud arrangement
commonGaps: A single generic cloud risk assessment is used for all cloud initiatives rather than initiative-specific assessments, failing to reflect the unique risk profile of each deployment
The assessment is completed post-implementation rather than prior to adoption, negating its purpose as a pre-decision risk control
Paragraph 10.50 requirements are only partially addressed — commonly, concentration risk and exit/portability risks are omitted or superficially treated
The assessment exists but is stored in an ad hoc location (e.g., a personal drive or email) and cannot be retrieved promptly when BNM requests it during examination
Risk ratings have not been updated following significant changes to the cloud service agreement, vendor sub-contracting arrangements, or regulatory guidance updates
suggestedSources: Technology Risk or Cloud Centre of Excellence — primary authors of cloud risk assessments
GRC platform risk register (e.g., ServiceNow Risk, Archer) where cloud risk assessments are stored and tracked
Third-party risk management system where vendor-specific cloud risk files are maintained
Internal Audit working papers referencing cloud risk assessment reviews
Chief Risk Officer's office — sign-off records and risk acceptance documentation
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with version control, cross-referenced to the relevant outsourcing plan initiative

evidenceItems.name: Emerging Technology Risk Assessment (Appendix 9 Compliance)
evidenceItems.description: The documented risk assessment for each emerging technology initiative, prepared in accordance with RMiT Appendix 9 requirements. This provides BNM examiners with assurance that the institution has systematically evaluated the risks of emerging technology adoption and that the documentation is maintained in a state ready for immediate regulatory production.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each emerging technology listed in the roadmap has a corresponding Appendix 9-structured risk assessment, with the technology type, use case, and affected systems clearly identified
The assessment addresses all Appendix 9 dimensions including technology maturity, legal and regulatory implications, operational resilience impact, and consumer protection considerations
The document clearly distinguishes between proof-of-concept, pilot, and production-stage deployments, with risk treatment appropriate to each stage
Sign-off by senior management (minimum Technology Risk Head or equivalent) is evidenced, with Board Risk Committee notification for material emerging technology adoption
The assessment includes a monitoring plan specifying how risks will be tracked post-adoption, with assigned risk owners
commonGaps: Emerging technology initiatives in the proof-of-concept stage are not assessed at all, on the incorrect assumption that Appendix 9 only applies once a technology reaches production
The Appendix 9 template is used as a checkbox exercise with minimal narrative, particularly for novel technologies where deeper analysis is most critical
No clear linkage exists between the emerging technology listed in the outsourcing plan roadmap and its corresponding Appendix 9 assessment, making cross-referencing during examination laborious
The institution cannot define which technologies qualify as 'emerging' under RMiT and has inconsistently applied the Appendix 9 requirement across similar technology types
Legal and regulatory implication sections of the assessment are left to legal counsel who are not engaged in the process, resulting in blank or deferred fields
suggestedSources: Technology Innovation or Digital Transformation team — initiators of emerging technology programmes
Technology Risk function — responsible for Appendix 9 completeness and storage
GRC platform risk register linked to the emerging technology initiative project record
Innovation Committee or equivalent governance body — meeting minutes evidencing risk assessment review
Enterprise Architecture repository documenting emerging technology proof-of-concept outcomes
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX structured against Appendix 9 framework, with version history and approval signatures

evidenceItems.name: System Criticality Classification Register for Cloud and Emerging Technology Initiatives
evidenceItems.description: A register or classification matrix that formally designates each system involved in cloud or emerging technology adoption as either critical or non-critical, underpinning the institution's ability to demonstrate that the roadmap covers both categories as required by clause 17.5. This document provides the classification basis that gives the roadmap its regulatory completeness.
whatGoodLooksLike: The register covers all systems in scope for cloud or emerging technology adoption and explicitly states the criticality classification with the rationale for each classification decision
Classifications are consistent with the institution's overarching system criticality framework referenced in other RMiT compliance artefacts, with no contradictions
The register has been reviewed and approved within the current annual cycle, with a named accountable owner for each classification
Non-critical systems are actively included and not treated as outside the scope of the roadmap or risk assessment process
The classification is linked to or embedded within the outsourcing plan roadmap so that examiners can trace each initiative directly to its criticality status
commonGaps: The register only captures critical systems, with non-critical systems entirely absent despite the explicit obligation in clause 17.5 to cover both categories
Criticality classifications are outdated — systems that have grown in operational importance since the last review remain classified as non-critical
Classification decisions lack documented rationale, making it impossible to demonstrate that the institution applied a consistent and defensible methodology
The register is maintained in a different system from the roadmap, creating reconciliation effort and version mismatch risks that are visible to examiners
New systems added during the year through cloud migration or emerging technology pilots are not added to the register until the next annual cycle
suggestedSources: Enterprise Architecture or Technology Risk team — owners of the system inventory and criticality framework
CMDB (Configuration Management Database) within ITSM platform (e.g., ServiceNow CMDB) where system attributes including criticality are recorded
IT Asset Management function
Business Continuity Management team — who maintain their own criticality classification aligned to RTO/RPO requirements that should be reconciled with this register
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or system export (PDF for submission purposes), with version control and approval sign-off

evidenceItems.name: Evidence of On-Demand Retrieval Capability for BNM Review
evidenceItems.description: Documentation demonstrating that the institution has a defined, tested process for retrieving and producing all required risk assessments (paragraph 10.50 and Appendix 9) promptly upon BNM request. This may include an evidence index, document retrieval procedure, or results of an internal readiness drill, demonstrating that 'made available for the Bank's review as and when requested' is operationally achievable.
whatGoodLooksLike: A centralised evidence index or document register exists that maps each cloud and emerging technology initiative to its corresponding risk assessment documents, with direct retrieval links or file paths
The institution has conducted at least one internal readiness exercise (mock BNM request) within the past 12 months, with the outcome documented showing retrieval within an acceptable timeframe
Document ownership is assigned so that a named individual is responsible for producing each category of evidence, with a deputy or backup identified
All risk assessment documents are stored in a system accessible to the compliance or regulatory affairs team without requiring IT intervention or multi-department escalation
The evidence index is reconciled against the current outsourcing plan roadmap at least annually, with reconciliation sign-off documented
commonGaps: Risk assessments are dispersed across multiple teams, drives, and systems with no central index, meaning a BNM request triggers a time-consuming internal search that may not succeed within the examination window
The institution has never tested whether it can actually retrieve documents on demand — the assumption of retrievability has not been validated

Access controls on document repositories are overly restrictive, preventing the compliance team from retrieving risk assessments without involving the originating business unit, which is unavailable during an examination
Documents are stored in formats that require specialist software or access credentials that are not maintained by the compliance team responsible for regulatory production
There is no nominated owner for the evidence retrieval process, resulting in confusion and delays when a BNM request is actually received
suggestedSources: Compliance or Regulatory Affairs team — responsible for maintaining the evidence index and managing BNM examination logistics
GRC platform document management module — ideally the single source of truth for all regulatory evidence
SharePoint or institutional document management system with appropriate access controls for compliance team
Internal Audit team — who may have conducted prior readiness assessments that can serve as baseline evidence
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or GRC platform report (evidence index); PDF (readiness drill report)

auditTips: Before the examination, perform a line-by-line reconciliation between the roadmap in the annual outsourcing plan and the complete inventory of cloud and emerging technology risk assessments — every initiative on the roadmap must have a corresponding paragraph 10.50 or Appendix 9 document, and every assessment must map back to a named roadmap initiative. Any orphaned assessments or unassessed roadmap items are immediate examination findings.

Confirm that both the annual outsourcing plan and the IT Profile submission cover the same set of cloud and emerging technology initiatives, and prepare a reconciliation table that examiners can review. Discrepancies between these two regulatory submissions are a high-priority red flag that signals weak governance over regulatory reporting.

Prepare a one-page roadmap summary that visually presents cloud and emerging technology initiatives by system, criticality classification (critical vs. non-critical), adoption phase, and timeline. This is not required by the clause but significantly accelerates the examiner's review and demonstrates that the institution has a clear, governed view of its technology trajectory.

Verify that non-critical systems are explicitly included in the roadmap — not just noted as 'covered' — with at least enough detail to demonstrate intentional scoping rather than accidental omission. Examiners are specifically alert to roadmaps that only address critical systems, as the verbatim text of clause 17.5 explicitly requires both.

Designate a single evidence custodian in the compliance or technology risk team who can retrieve and produce the paragraph 10.50 cloud risk assessments and Appendix 9 emerging technology assessments within two business hours of a BNM request. Test this capability by running a mock BNM document request drill at least 60 days before the expected examination window, and document the outcome.

Check the submission dates on all annual outsourcing plan versions and confirm they fall within the deadline prescribed by the Outsourcing policy document. Late submissions — even by one day — are recorded as compliance failures under clause 17.5. Retain BNM submission portal timestamps or email acknowledgements as proof of timely submission.

Cross-reference the system criticality classifications used in the roadmap against the institution's formal criticality framework and any classifications cited in business continuity, disaster recovery, or other RMiT compliance artefacts. Inconsistent criticality classifications across documents is a common examination finding that undermines confidence in the entire submission.

18.1.title: Gap Analysis and Action Plan
18.1.auditorFocus: BNM examiners will verify that the institution performed a structured, documented gap analysis comparing existing technology risk management practices against every requirement in the RMiT policy document — not just high-visibility domains. Examiners specifically check that the initial gap analysis and action plan were submitted within the 90-day deadline from the policy issuance date, and will treat a missed submission as a material breach of a 'shall' obligation. For institutions that previously submitted under an earlier RMiT version, examiners will probe whether a delta assessment was conducted to capture new or enhanced requirements, rather than simply resubmitting the prior document. A major red flag is an action plan that lacks specific owners, realistic timelines, or measurable milestones, or where remediation evidence shows no meaningful progress against committed closure dates. Examiners will also request the most recent annual compliance assessment to confirm that gap-tracking is a living process and not a one-time exercise conducted solely for the initial submission.

evidenceItems.name: Initial RMiT Gap Analysis Report
evidenceItems.description: A formal documented assessment comparing the institution's existing technology risk management practices clause-by-clause against the requirements in the current RMiT policy document, identifying compliance status and highlighting key implementation gaps for each applicable requirement.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each RMiT clause is mapped row-by-row with a compliance status rating (e.g., Compliant, Partially Compliant, Non-Compliant) supported by a brief rationale and reference to supporting evidence
The report is dated within 90 days of the RMiT issuance date and bears approval signatures from senior management or the Board Risk Committee
Key implementation gaps are clearly distinguished from minor gaps, with risk ratings assigned to prioritise remediation effort
The scope explicitly covers all applicable entity types (e.g., bank, subsidiary, outsourced functions) and does not arbitrarily exclude technology domains
The report version history shows it was reviewed by Internal Audit or an independent function before submission to BNM
commonGaps: Gap analysis covers only high-profile RMiT domains (e.g., cyber, cloud) while omitting operational technology, third-party risk, or business continuity requirements
Compliance status is self-assessed as 'Compliant' across broad sections without clause-level granularity or supporting evidence references
The report is undated or carries a date beyond the 90-day submission window, with no documented escalation or extension request
Gaps are described in vague terms ('under review', 'to be assessed') rather than specific, observable control deficiencies
The document has no approval signatures, indicating it was not formally endorsed by governance bodies before submission
suggestedSources: GRC platform (e.g., ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream) — gap register or compliance module
Technology Risk department SharePoint or document management system
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee meeting packs and minutes
Internal Audit shared drive — technology risk audit workpapers
Regulatory submissions folder maintained by Compliance or Legal
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded clause mapping matrix (may include linked spreadsheet annex)

evidenceItems.name: BNM Submission Proof — Gap Analysis and Action Plan
evidenceItems.description: Documentary evidence confirming that the completed gap analysis and action plan were formally submitted to Bank Negara Malaysia no later than 90 days after the RMiT policy issuance date, including any official acknowledgement received from BNM.
whatGoodLooksLike: A dated submission letter or email addressed to BNM's supervisory team, referencing the specific RMiT policy document and submission deadline, with the gap analysis and action plan attached
BNM's written acknowledgement or receipt confirmation is retained alongside the submission record
The submission timestamp (email sent date or courier receipt) falls on or before the 90-day deadline with margin
The cover letter is signed by the CEO, CRO, or an authorised signatory consistent with the institution's regulatory correspondence policy
A submission log or regulatory calendar entry cross-references this submission with the specific RMiT issuance date and deadline
commonGaps: No physical or electronic record of the actual submission exists — only a draft of the gap analysis document
The submission was made after the 90-day deadline with no documented extension request or BNM dispensation on file
BNM acknowledgement is absent, leaving the institution unable to confirm receipt
The submission letter does not reference the correct policy version or issuance date, creating traceability ambiguity
Submission evidence is held informally in a personal email inbox rather than a centralised regulatory correspondence system
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs or Compliance team email archive and correspondence log
