Centralised regulatory submission register (SharePoint, Confluence, or GRC platform)
CEO or CRO executive assistant records for signed outgoing correspondence
BNM FAST (Financial Institutions' Application for Submission and Tracking) portal submission history, if applicable
Legal department file for dated regulatory submissions
evidenceItems.format: Email thread with attachments (PDF) or scanned signed letter with courier proof of delivery

evidenceItems.name: Technology Risk Remediation Action Plan
evidenceItems.description: A structured plan documenting the specific actions, responsible owners, target closure dates, and key milestones required to remediate each gap identified in the RMiT gap analysis, demonstrating that the institution has a credible and time-bound path to full compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each gap from the gap analysis has a corresponding action item with a named accountable owner (by role, not just department), an interim milestone date, and a final target closure date
Actions are categorised by priority (e.g., Critical, High, Medium) aligned to the gap risk rating, with Critical items targeted for closure within defined short-term windows
The plan includes a baseline status column that is updated periodically to reflect percentage completion or revised timelines, creating an auditable change history
The action plan is formally approved by the Technology Risk Committee or equivalent governance body and re-tabled at each subsequent governance meeting
Remediation tasks reference specific RMiT clause numbers, enabling direct traceability from gap to action to evidence of closure
commonGaps: Action items are stated at a high level (e.g., 'improve cyber controls') without breaking down the specific control implementations needed to close the gap
Responsible owners are assigned to departments rather than named individuals, making accountability diffuse and difficult to enforce
Target dates are uniformly set to year-end or to a generic future period without realistic phased milestones that allow progress tracking
The action plan was prepared for the initial submission but has not been updated since, showing no evidence of ongoing monitoring
No governance escalation mechanism is documented for action items that breach their target closure date
suggestedSources: GRC platform project tracking module (ServiceNow, Jira, or equivalent)
Technology Risk Committee or Board Risk Committee meeting packs
Project Management Office (PMO) — regulatory compliance project portfolio
CRO or CISO office — strategic risk remediation tracker
Internal Audit follow-up tracking register
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet (XLSX) or GRC platform export — tabular format with status columns; accompanied by governance approval minutes (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Delta Gap Analysis — Enhanced or Revised Requirements
evidenceItems.description: For institutions that previously submitted under an earlier version of the RMiT policy document, this is a documented assessment identifying new or materially changed requirements introduced in the latest version and the corresponding new gaps in the institution's practices, demonstrating continuous compliance monitoring.
whatGoodLooksLike: A side-by-side comparison matrix mapping prior RMiT requirements to the equivalent revised requirements, with changed or new clauses clearly flagged
For each new or enhanced requirement, the assessment records whether the institution's existing controls are sufficient or whether a new gap exists, with a rationale
The delta analysis is dated and demonstrates that it was conducted promptly following publication of the revised RMiT policy
New gaps identified in the delta analysis are incorporated into the remediation action plan with updated timelines and owners
The document acknowledges explicitly that it builds on the prior submission and does not simply resubmit the original gap analysis unchanged
commonGaps: The institution resubmits the original gap analysis with superficial edits rather than conducting a genuine delta assessment of revised requirements
The delta analysis only addresses clauses that changed in numbering or wording but misses substantive enhancements to existing obligations
No evidence of the prior version of the RMiT document being retained or referenced, making it impossible to demonstrate a meaningful comparison was performed
The delta assessment is performed by the same team that owns the gaps, with no independent review to challenge completeness
New gaps identified in the delta assessment are not formally added to the remediation action plan and governance tracker
suggestedSources: Technology Risk or Compliance team's RMiT policy version control archive
GRC platform — policy change impact assessment module
Regulatory change management register maintained by Compliance
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee minutes confirming review of revised policy requirements
Prior BNM submission folder for the earlier RMiT version, used as a baseline reference
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded comparison matrix (XLSX annex recommended for clause-level delta tracking)

evidenceItems.name: Annual RMiT Compliance Assessment
evidenceItems.description: An updated annual assessment of the institution's level of compliance with the RMiT policy document, maintained on a rolling basis and made available to BNM upon request, demonstrating that compliance monitoring is an ongoing process rather than a one-time submission exercise.
whatGoodLooksLike: The assessment is formally dated and clearly identifies the assessment period, confirming it is conducted at least annually and not simply carried forward from the prior year
Each RMiT clause is assigned a current compliance status that reflects actual control testing or self-assessment evidence gathered during the assessment period, not a rollover of prior ratings
The annual assessment is reviewed and approved by a senior governance body (e.g., Board Risk Committee, Audit Committee) and the approval is minuted
Outstanding gaps from the action plan are reflected in the assessment with updated closure dates, and newly closed items cite the specific evidence of remediation
The document is stored in a location that allows rapid retrieval and production to BNM within a reasonable timeframe (e.g., within 24–48 hours of request)
commonGaps: The annual assessment has not been updated for more than 12 months, meaning it does not reflect the current year's compliance position
Compliance ratings in the assessment are not supported by current-year evidence and appear to replicate prior year assessments without fresh review
The assessment is maintained informally in a spreadsheet with no governance approval trail, making it unsuitable for regulatory production
The document cannot be located or retrieved quickly because it is stored inconsistently across different team members' personal drives
The annual assessment does not cross-reference the remediation action plan, creating a disconnect between stated compliance status and actual gap closure progress
suggestedSources: GRC platform — annual compliance reporting module or dashboard
Technology Risk department document management system (SharePoint or Confluence)
Board Risk Committee or Audit Committee meeting packs and resolution minutes
CRO office — regulatory compliance reporting pack
Internal Audit — annual technology risk audit report, used as input to the compliance assessment
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX with embedded compliance matrix; governance approval evidenced by signed minutes or resolution (PDF)

evidenceItems.name: Governance Oversight Records — Gap Analysis Review
evidenceItems.description: Board and senior management governance records demonstrating that the gap analysis, action plan, and ongoing compliance assessments have been formally tabled, reviewed, and approved at appropriate governance forums, establishing accountability at the highest organisational level.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board Risk Committee or equivalent minutes explicitly reference the RMiT gap analysis and action plan as agenda items, with substantive discussion recorded rather than a bare notation of receipt
Minutes record that individual committee members challenged assumptions in the gap analysis or questioned progress on high-priority action items
Annual compliance assessment is a standing agenda item at the relevant governance committee, with each year's pack retained in sequence to demonstrate continuity
Escalation records exist for any action items that breached their committed closure dates, showing that governance actively monitored remediation progress
Committee terms of reference reference oversight of regulatory compliance submissions as an explicit responsibility, establishing structural accountability
commonGaps: Meeting minutes record only that the gap analysis was 'noted' without evidence of substantive Board or committee engagement with the content
The gap analysis and action plan were reviewed only at an operational-level working group and never escalated to a Board or senior management committee
Minutes are templated and generic, with no clause-specific discussion points that demonstrate the committee understood the content of the gap analysis
Governance records are incomplete — some committee meetings where RMiT compliance was discussed have no minutes or only informal notes
There is no standing agenda item for annual RMiT compliance review, meaning assessments are presented ad hoc and their governance history is fragmentary
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat — Board and Board committee minutes archive
Technology Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee secretariat records
CRO or CFO office — regulatory governance calendar and agenda packs
Board portal (e.g., Diligent, BoardEffect) — historical meeting packs and resolution records
Internal Audit — committee effectiveness review workpapers
evidenceItems.format: PDF — signed and certified meeting minutes; agenda packs (PDF/DOCX); attendance registers

auditTips: Before the examination, reconstruct a complete submission timeline: identify the exact RMiT policy issuance date, calculate the 90-day deadline, and confirm that the submission email or courier record predates that deadline. If the deadline was missed even by one day, prepare a documented explanation and any BNM correspondence acknowledging the late submission, as examiners will raise this as a finding without context.

Map every evidence item in your action plan directly back to a specific RMiT clause number using a traceability matrix. Examiners frequently cross-check the gap analysis register against the action plan to confirm that every identified gap has a corresponding remediation task — disconnected documents with inconsistent gap counts are a common finding that signals poor governance.

For institutions on the revised RMiT version, produce a formal delta comparison document before the examination that shows, clause by clause, what changed between the prior and current policy and how each change was assessed against existing controls. Do not rely on verbal explanations during the examination — the delta analysis must be a standalone written record.

Stress-test the retrievability of the annual compliance assessment: simulate a BNM request by asking a team member who was not involved in preparation to locate and produce the document within two hours. If this cannot be done, the document is effectively inaccessible and remediation of storage and indexing should happen before any examination.

Review the Board or risk committee minutes for the most recent three years and confirm that RMiT compliance appears as a substantive agenda item — not just a standing attachment — with recorded discussion. If minutes show only 'noted', work with the Company Secretariat to ensure future minutes capture specific points raised, questions asked, and management responses, as examiners read minutes carefully to assess the quality of governance oversight.

Cross-reference the action plan closure dates against actual remediation evidence for the top 10 highest-risk gaps. For each closed item, confirm that the supporting evidence (e.g., completed policy, implemented control, test results) is already packaged and accessible. Examiners routinely ask for closure evidence on the spot, and an action plan marked 'Closed' without retrievable proof will be re-opened as a finding.

Assign a single named compliance owner for clause 18.1 who can speak authoritatively during the examination to the submission history, the gap count, gap severity ratings, remediation progress, and the schedule for the next annual assessment. Examiners are alert to institutions where no individual has end-to-end visibility of the compliance assessment process, as this indicates the process is not genuinely embedded in governance.

18.2.title: Gap Analysis Submission
18.2.auditorFocus: BNM examiners verify that the institution has formally submitted the self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan to the correct supervisory department — not merely completed the documents internally. A primary red flag is submission to the wrong department (e.g., a licensed bank routing to Jabatan Penyeliaan Insurans dan Takaful), which signals weak governance ownership and unclear regulatory mapping. Examiners will cross-reference the submission record against BNM's internal receipt logs to confirm both the fact and timeliness of submission, and will probe whether the Board or senior management formally endorsed the package prior to dispatch. The absence of a formal transmittal letter, acknowledgement receipt, or evidence of management sign-off are the most common deficiencies encountered during examination.

evidenceItems.name: Formal Submission Transmittal Letter
evidenceItems.description: The official cover letter or transmittal document addressed to the correct BNM supervisory department (Jabatan Penyeliaan Konglomerat Kewangan, Jabatan Penyeliaan Perbankan, Jabatan Penyeliaan Insurans dan Takaful, or Jabatan Pemantauan Perkhidmatan Pembayaran), enclosing the self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan. Demonstrates that the submission obligation was formally discharged to the appropriate regulatory recipient.
whatGoodLooksLike: Letter is addressed explicitly to the correct BNM supervisory department corresponding to the institution's licence type, with the department name spelled in full
Letter references the specific RMiT paragraph (18.1 and 18.2) and the submission deadline or regulatory cycle it fulfils
Letter bears the wet or digital signature of a senior officer (CIO, CISO, CEO, or equivalent) authorised to correspond with BNM
Letter includes a document schedule listing all enclosures (self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan) with version numbers and page counts
A copy of the letter is retained on file with a date-stamped outgoing reference number traceable in the institution's document management system
commonGaps: Letter is addressed to a generic 'Bank Negara Malaysia' contact rather than the specific supervisory department, making it impossible to confirm routing compliance
Transmittal letter is undated or uses a date that post-dates the regulatory submission deadline, indicating late submission
Letter is unsigned or signed by an officer below the authorised signatory threshold defined in the institution's delegation of authority
Enclosure schedule is absent, so auditors cannot confirm the gap analysis and action plan were actually enclosed rather than just the self-assessment
No outgoing reference number or despatch record exists, preventing the institution from proving the letter was actually sent and not merely drafted
suggestedSources: Corporate Secretariat or Regulatory Affairs outgoing correspondence register
SharePoint or document management system (e.g., OpenText, M-Files) under the regulatory submissions folder
Executive Assistant or CEO/CIO office filing system for BNM correspondence
GRC platform (e.g., MetricStream, ServiceNow GRC, Archer) regulatory submission tracker
evidenceItems.format: PDF (signed and scanned original) or PDF with digital signature

evidenceItems.name: BNM Acknowledgement or Receipt Confirmation
evidenceItems.description: Documentary proof that the correct BNM supervisory department received the submission package. This may take the form of an email acknowledgement from BNM, a stamped acknowledgement copy of the transmittal letter, a registered post delivery confirmation, or a BNM supervisory portal submission receipt. Demonstrates successful delivery, not merely dispatch.
whatGoodLooksLike: Acknowledgement identifies the receiving BNM department by name and matches the department named in the institution's transmittal letter
Acknowledgement is dated, enabling calculation of submission lead time relative to the regulatory deadline
If delivered via BNM's supervisory portal, a system-generated submission ID or reference number is captured and stored
If submitted by hand or registered post, a stamped copy or Track & Trace delivery confirmation with the recipient's name and date is retained
Acknowledgement is cross-referenced in the institution's GRC or compliance calendar against the specific RMiT 18.1/18.2 obligation
commonGaps: Institution retains the transmittal letter but has no evidence of receipt, relying solely on the act of sending as proof of compliance
Email acknowledgement exists but is stored only in a personal inbox rather than the corporate document management system, creating a single point of failure
Submission was made verbally or via an informal channel (e.g., WhatsApp) with no written acknowledgement retained
Receipt confirmation references a different document version than what was ultimately submitted, creating a chain-of-custody discrepancy
Portal submission receipt was not downloaded and archived at the time of submission and is no longer retrievable from the external system
suggestedSources: Regulatory Affairs team email archive (corporate mailbox, not personal)
BNM supervisory portal submission history (if applicable)
Post and courier logbook maintained by the institution's mailroom
SharePoint regulatory submissions folder with receipt scan attached to the submission record
evidenceItems.format: PDF (scanned stamped copy, email printout, or portal export)

evidenceItems.name: Supervisory Department Routing Justification
evidenceItems.description: An internal record documenting the rationale for selecting the specific BNM supervisory department to which the submission was addressed. This demonstrates that the institution applied deliberate governance in identifying 'as the case may be' — confirming that the correct department was chosen based on the institution's licence type or conglomerate classification, rather than by default or error.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document explicitly maps the institution's licence category (e.g., licensed bank, takaful operator, payment service provider, financial conglomerate) to the corresponding BNM supervisory department
Routing decision is approved or noted by the Chief Compliance Officer or Head of Regulatory Affairs, not determined ad hoc by a junior officer
Document is reviewed and updated each regulatory cycle to account for any changes in BNM supervisory structure or the institution's licensed activities
Where the institution operates across multiple licence types, the document clearly identifies which entity within the group submits to which department
Routing justification is referenced in the submission checklist so that preparers consistently apply the correct addressee
commonGaps: No formal routing justification exists; the correct department was identified informally and is not documented, creating risk of error in future cycles
Routing document has not been updated to reflect BNM's supervisory reorganisation, resulting in submissions addressed to a superseded department name
For conglomerate groups, a single submission is sent to one department without analysis of whether subsidiary entities require separate submissions to different departments
The routing decision was made by a junior compliance analyst without sign-off from a senior officer, undermining governance credibility
Routing justification conflates the submission addressee with the institution's primary BNM supervisor, without verifying alignment with BNM's current departmental mandate for RMiT oversight
suggestedSources: Compliance or Regulatory Affairs department policy and procedures manual
RMiT implementation project governance documents
GRC platform regulatory obligation register with department mapping field
Legal or Company Secretariat records confirming current licence classifications
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX (internal memo or policy annex)

evidenceItems.name: Board or Senior Management Endorsement Record
evidenceItems.description: Minutes of a Board, Board Risk Committee, or senior management committee meeting at which the self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan were approved prior to submission to BNM. Demonstrates that the submission was formally sanctioned at the appropriate governance level before being dispatched to the supervisory department, rather than submitted unilaterally by the compliance function.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board or committee minutes explicitly reference approval of the RMiT 18.1 self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan for submission to the named BNM department
Minutes record the date of approval, confirming that endorsement predates the submission date on the transmittal letter
The resolution or approval note specifies the version of the documents approved (version number or document date), enabling traceability to the submitted package
Minutes are signed by the Board Chairman or Committee Chair and the Company Secretary, conforming to the institution's standard minutes governance
Action items in the minutes assign accountability for effecting the submission, with a named officer and target date
commonGaps: Self-assessment was submitted to BNM before Board approval was obtained, with minutes only ratifying the submission after the fact — reversing the correct governance sequence
Minutes refer generically to 'RMiT compliance updates' without specifically recording approval of the submission package, making it unclear what was sanctioned
Approval was obtained via email circulation rather than at a formal committee meeting, and no resolution is captured in formal minutes
The version of documents approved by the Board differs from the version ultimately submitted, with no change control record bridging the two
Minutes have not been finalised and signed at the time of BNM examination, existing only as draft notes
suggestedSources: Company Secretariat Board and committee minutes archive (SharePoint, BoardEffect, Diligent, or equivalent board portal)
Board Risk Committee or IT Steering Committee minute books
GRC platform workflow audit trail showing approval stages before submission
Regulatory Affairs calendar showing the committee meeting date relative to the submission date
evidenceItems.format: PDF (certified extract of minutes)

evidenceItems.name: Submission Deadline Compliance Record
evidenceItems.description: A documented record confirming that the submission was made within any deadline communicated by BNM (whether via a supervisory letter, circular, or the RMiT effective date provisions). Demonstrates timely discharge of the obligation under paragraph 18.2 and shows the institution's awareness of and responsiveness to regulatory deadlines.
whatGoodLooksLike: Compliance calendar or regulatory obligation register records the BNM-prescribed submission deadline alongside the actual submission date, with both fields populated
Where a deadline was communicated by BNM in a supervisory letter or circular, that letter is cross-referenced and retained alongside the submission record
If an extension was sought and granted, the BNM approval for the extension is documented and attached to the submission file
Submission date on the transmittal letter and the BNM receipt date both fall within the prescribed deadline window
A post-submission compliance confirmation memo is issued internally, signed by the CCO, confirming the obligation was discharged on time
commonGaps: Institution cannot produce the original BNM communication specifying the deadline, relying on institutional memory of the date rather than documentary evidence
Compliance calendar records a target date but does not record the actual submission date, preventing confirmation of timely completion
Submission was made after the deadline with no extension approval from BNM on file, creating an unmitigated compliance breach
Different departments hold conflicting records of the submission date (e.g., Compliance says one date, Corporate Secretariat another), with no single authoritative record
Deadline tracking is managed in a personal spreadsheet rather than a governed GRC system, creating fragility and audit credibility issues
suggestedSources: GRC platform regulatory calendar module (e.g., MetricStream, Archer, ServiceNow GRC)
Regulatory Affairs compliance obligations register (SharePoint or equivalent)
BNM supervisory letters archive maintained by Corporate Secretariat
Compliance dashboard or reporting tool used by the CCO for board reporting
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet extract or GRC system report (PDF export)

evidenceItems.name: Submission Package Version Control Record
evidenceItems.description: A version control log or document register confirming that the finalised version of the self-assessment, gap analysis, and action plan submitted to BNM is identical to the Board-approved version and has not been altered after endorsement. Establishes integrity of the submission and prevents disputes about what was actually provided to the regulator.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document management system shows a 'final submitted' version locked after Board approval, with subsequent edits tracked under a new version number marked as post-submission internal working copies
Each submitted document bears a version number and date consistent with the version referenced in the Board minutes and the transmittal letter enclosure schedule
Hash value or checksums of the submitted PDF files are recorded at the time of submission and retained, enabling later verification that files were not altered
Change log clearly distinguishes between pre-submission revisions (incorporated into the submitted package) and post-submission updates (internal action plan tracking only)
A named document custodian is identified in the version control record, accountable for maintaining the integrity of the submission archive
commonGaps: Institution cannot demonstrate that the version submitted to BNM is the same as the version approved by the Board, because document versioning was not applied consistently
The submitted PDF is overwritten or replaced in the document management system after submission, destroying the audit trail of what was actually sent
No distinction is made between the regulatory submission copy and internal working drafts, creating ambiguity about which version represents the official submission
Version numbers are applied informally (e.g., 'final', 'final v2', 'FINAL ACTUAL') without a governed versioning scheme, making chronological reconstruction unreliable
Action plan is updated post-submission to reflect progress without creating a clearly labelled post-submission working copy, conflating the submitted baseline with subsequent internal tracking
suggestedSources: Document management system version history (SharePoint, OpenText, Confluence)
GRC platform document register with version control fields
IT department file server audit logs confirming file creation and modification timestamps
Regulatory Affairs document control register
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or DMS system export (PDF)

auditTips: Map your institution's licence type to the correct BNM supervisory department before drafting any submission documentation — BNM examiners will specifically verify that the submission was addressed to the department with actual supervisory jurisdiction over your institution, and an incorrect addressee is treated as a procedural non-compliance regardless of document quality.

Establish a dual-confirmation protocol: retain both the outgoing transmittal letter (proof of dispatch) and the BNM acknowledgement or registered post delivery confirmation (proof of receipt) in the same submission file — examiners distinguish between evidence of sending and evidence of delivery, and only the latter fully satisfies the submission obligation.

Ensure Board or Board Risk Committee approval of the submission package is minuted with explicit reference to the RMiT 18.2 submission, and that the approval date in the minutes predates the submission date on the transmittal letter — an examiner performing a timeline check will flag any instance where the submission precedes the governance approval.

Lock the submitted document versions in your document management system immediately after submission and apply a clear naming convention (e.g., 'RMiT18.1_SelfAssessment_v3.0_SUBMITTED_YYYYMMDD') to prevent the submission baseline from being overwritten by subsequent internal revisions to the action plan.

Cross-reference your compliance calendar entry for this obligation against the BNM supervisory letter or circular that communicated the deadline — if the original deadline communication cannot be produced during examination, BNM may question whether your compliance tracking is reactive rather than proactive.

For financial conglomerate groups, explicitly document and retain a routing analysis confirming which group entities submitted separately to which BNM departments, as examiners for Jabatan Penyeliaan Konglomerat Kewangan will assess whether subsidiary submissions to other departments were also completed consistently across the group.

Conduct a pre-examination dry run in which the compliance team assembles the complete submission evidence chain — transmittal letter, enclosure schedule, receipt confirmation, Board minutes, version control log, and deadline compliance record — as a single linked file set, so that during examination all supporting documents can be produced together without gaps that raise further questions.
